label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Family members, family mother,  
Open the little doors.  
The Kadris have come from far away,  
From far away, from high places.  
Kadri’s fingers are freezing,  
Kadri’s toes are aching.  
Kadri asks for beds.  
Kadri wants to be fed,  
To be dressed in linen.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
There are indeed men in our village!  
Handsome boys, proud boys,  
Painful boys, pale boys!  
There were no girls for the dishes,  
No wreaths for the heads,  
All the girls were smiling,  
All were mocking Piia,  
Scolding Sõlerinna.  
Ten girls in the village,  
Nine across the street,  
Eight by the herd's path.  
They do not come swinging on the swing,  
Nor singing on the swing board.  
They fear their gold will wear out,  
Their bravado will fade,  
Their money bags run empty.  
We have goldsmiths at home,  
Coppersmiths at the gate,  
Tinsmiths by the road.  
I go to the smith with a request:  
Little smith, little fellow!  
Take my paua shells and fix them,  
Make my bravado whole again,  
Make my money bags carry weight.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, old brothers,  
Do not ask me for eggs;  
The hen stayed home to lay eggs,  
The rooster stayed crowing by the ear,  
The cat stayed watching by the root.  
If I come to swing tomorrow,  
Then I will bring eggs, then I will bring hens,  
And then I will bring ducks with long necks.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
How many girls are in the village?  
Ten girls in the village:  
Nine across the street,  
Eight by the herd’s path;  
Seven nearby,  
They were not allowed to come to the swing,  
They fear gold will wear out,  
They fear copper will sink,  
Silver beads to break,  
Money jugs to drain,  
Clover jugs to tear apart,  
Young maidens, young ladies!  
Do not grumble, do not fear!  
Your gold will not wear out here;  
Your copper will not sink here,  
Here only men’s gold wears out:  
Here only men’s copper sinks,  
Men’s silver rusts!  
"Young maidens, young ladies!  
Do not grumble, do not fear!  
We have home goldsmiths:  
At the gate are coppersmiths;  
Tinsmiths by the road."

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let's go sledding
Flax sled, long fiber
Hemp fiber
Flax for the sledder
Velcro for the pusher
Lints for the front guard
Let it be, let it be, I swear
Let's go sledding again
Let it become smoky at home
Let it settle in front of the stove
Let it mushroom along the wall
Let it grow in the greenhouse on the neck
On the catkins of the willow
On the shoulders of the apple tree
On the rack on the other thigh

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let's go to the midsummer bonfire  
To keep the bonfire  
To protect the herd of the kite  
The fire wanders into buildings  
The kite climbs onto the roof  
Come to the midsummer bonfire!  
For Peeter, for Paaver  
To hug the two dear ones  
You made paths to the sky  
You carved pathways  
To climb up the apple trees  
To come down to the berry fields

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Rowing swing, strength swing  
Swingsmiths, gentle brothers!  
What do I promise to the rower:  
To the rower, to the strong one?  
Over the beam to the pusher  
Over the shaft to the stepper:  
Over the axle to the roller.  
From the flax, a white maiden  
The longest, the finest  
The straightest little pig  
The brightest shoulder  
This I promise to the rower  
To the rower, to the strong one  
Over the beam to the pusher  
Over the shaft to the stepper  
Over the axle to the roller.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let me try the new swing  
Does this swing carry a maiden?  
Does it carry two footed:  
Two footed clothing  
Five maidens in rows  
If the swing does not carry us  
Then bring a sturdy, chopped swing  
Bring a brave, free swing  
Bring a vest, a vested swing  
Let me say it again,  
Let me sing it back:  
A swing is not sturdy chopping  
Nor is it vesting a vest  
The swing was brought from far away  
The boards were brought from Laiuse  
The poles were brought from Puivere  
The supports were brought from Ammuta  
Toomas was bringing, Toomas was getting  
Toomas was buying a thousand  
Getting a hundred footboards  
The brothers were pulling the shafts.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row across three forests  
There were three forests side by side:  
One was the golden spruce forest  
The second was the dear birch forest,  
The third truly an apple orchard!  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
It was the forest of young men  
What was the dear birch forest?  
It was the forest of young women:  
What was the true apple orchard?  
It was the forest of young maidens.  
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row the swing to the deserted land  
Where the roosters drink gold  
Roosters of gold, hens beware  
The swans are from the silver lake  
Soon for us two young ones:  
We will have a perfect pair of young ones.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row across three forests  
There were three forests side by side:  
One was the golden spruce forest  
The second was the dear birch forest,  
The third truly an apple orchard!  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
It was the forest of young men  
What was the dear birch forest?  
It was the forest of young women:  
What was the true apple orchard?  
It was the forest of young maidens.  
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row the swing to the deserted land  
Where the roosters drink gold  
Roosters of gold, hens beware  
The swans are from the silver lake  
Soon for us two young ones:  
We will have a perfect pair of young ones.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row across three forests  
There were three forests side by side:  
One was the golden spruce forest  
The second was the dear birch forest,  
The third truly an apple orchard!  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
It was the forest of young men  
What was the dear birch forest?  
It was the forest of young women:  
What was the true apple orchard?  
It was the forest of young maidens.  
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row the swing to the deserted land  
Where the roosters drink gold  
Roosters of gold, hens beware  
The swans are from the silver lake  
Soon for us two young ones:  
We will have a perfect pair of young ones.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Ten girls in the village!  
Nine across the street  
Eight on the edge of the herd:  
Seven on this side of it  
Six on the other's side;  
Five on the brother's side.  
They were not allowed to come to the swing!  
They feared the gold would wear out,  
They feared the copper would fade,  
The brass pitcher would lose its color;  
The clover pitcher would rust  
The silver beads would tarnish  
Our two poor children!  
We are going, we are walking  
We are going under the swing  
Let our gold wear out,  
Let our copper fade;  
The silver beads to shine again  
We have goldsmiths at home  
Outside there are silversmiths:  
Tinsmiths by the road;  
Foundry smiths in the pile  
Our bag rises with gold smoke  
From outside, silver smoke  
Copper smoke from the side  
Tin smoke by the road  
Foundry smoke from under the pile

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Ten girls in the village!  
Nine across the street  
Eight on the edge of the herd:  
Seven on this side of it  
Six on the other's side;  
Five on the brother's side.  
They were not allowed to come to the swing!  
They feared the gold would wear out,  
They feared the copper would fade,  
The brass pitcher would lose its color;  
The clover pitcher would rust  
The silver beads would tarnish  
Our two poor children!  
We are going, we are walking  
We are going under the swing  
Let our gold wear out,  
Let our copper fade;  
The silver beads to shine again  
We have goldsmiths at home  
Outside there are silversmiths:  
Tinsmiths by the road;  
Foundry smiths in the pile  
Our bag rises with gold smoke  
From outside, silver smoke  
Copper smoke from the side  
Tin smoke by the road  
Foundry smoke from under the pile

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Granddaughter, little maiden  
Get up from the bed  
Sit on the edge of the bed  
Put your feet on the chair;  
Blow on the fire from the rooster’s mouth  
Honk like a chicken from the hen’s nose,  
If the mirror is not in the house,  
Pull a piece from the blanket,  
Pull splinters from the doorstep,  
Keep the fire in the room,  
Then you will be called good,  
Good old woman’s daughter,  
Wise old man’s plant.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Family, family members  
Get up from the bed,  
Family, little grandmother  
Go to the cellar  
Across the yard to the barns,  
Bring a piece of raw meat  
A plate of white beans  
God bless your yard  
Adorn the cattle sheds  
God grant the barns be full,  
Filling the barns with grain  
Filling the barns with sheep's fodder.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
In our village, ten girls  
Do not come even to the swing.  
They fear their gold will wear away,  
Silver beads will tarnish,  
Banknotes will lose value.  
Let our gold wear away,  
Let our copper sink,  
We have a home for the goldsmith,  
Outside is a silversmith,  
At the gate is a coppersmith,  
A tinsmith on the road.  
From home rises gold smoke,  
From the gate copper smoke,  
Tinsmith's smoke from the road.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
Let me down, I beg you.  
If you don’t let me, I won’t want to:  
Surely I will swing until evening,  
Swing until midday,  
Sing until broad daylight.  
Surely the alder will milk the cow,  
Surely the birch will drive the herd.  
The maple tree will water the calves,  
Drive the herd far away,  
The calves to the willow grove,  
The bulls to Änn’s hayfield,  
The goats to Kiili’s kopel,  
The pigs to Siim’s rye field.  
If you don’t let me, I won’t ask.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Why does the swing creak,  
The base plank groan,  
The top wood crack?  
The swing wants to be swung,  
The base plank to be given,  
The top wood red ribbons.  
But wait, little swing:  
Let my brother take a wife,  
The second brother another wife,  
The third brings a girl home,  
The fourth brings a bride inside—  
Then the swing will want to be swung,  
The base plank will be given,  
The top wood red ribbons.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Where did this swing come from?  
The swing came from Turkey,  
The swing posts from Germany;  
The swing axle from Võivere.  
The main boards from Põltsamaa,  
The braces from Harju-Jaani as well.  
Let's go try the swing,  
Will this swing hold us?  
If it doesn’t hold, let it break.  
Young men, dear brothers,  
Came to swing on purpose,  
To make us fall.  
Why do you swing on purpose,  
To make us fall?  
If I fall, who will pay for me?  
I will leave behind many mourners:  
Father will be without a daughter,  
Mother without a daughter,  
Sister without a sister,  
Brother without a sister,  
Fiancé without a bride.  
Dear brothers,  
Do not swing on purpose,  
Do not make us fall.  
I have no brother under the swing,  
No nephew under the table.  
Who will bring pillows under the swing,  
Wide sheets under the table?  
If I fall, who will pay for me?  
Father will pay for a pair of oxen,  
Another father for another pair.  
Ears like golden ears of grain,  
Eyes bluish-gray,  
Nose like a young horse’s.  
A thousand pays for a girl’s hair,  
A hundred pays for a girl’s sash,  
Fifty for the girl’s hat.  
Put a thousand on the hair,  
A hundred on the sash,  
Fifty on the hat.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Linen slip, long fiber,  
Flax for the slider,  
Knots for the pusher behind,  
Fibers for the front puller,  
Tufts for the one sitting at the end.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I come to your swing  
To look at your swing  
Will this swing carry me,  
Will it carry two girls,  
Two girls’ dresses,  
Four girls’ jewelry,  
Five girls’ felt shoes,  
Six girls’ bonnets.  
If the swing does not carry me,  
Bring the axe, chop the swing  
Bring fire, burn the swing,  
Bring the knife, carve the swing.  
Wait, let me say it again  
Let me sing it back again:  
The swing is not for chopping with an axe,  
There is no burning,  
Nor carving with a knife,  
The swing was brought from far away;  
It was brought from the village of Moe,  
Brought from Võlla in Võiveri,  
The rails were brought from Ambela,  
The main boards from Põltsamaa.  
The swing costs a painted yoke,  
In Võlla it costs a butter stake,  
The rails cost a gray stake.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Mother, where have they made the swing:  
They made the swing on the threshing field,  
On the guests' ravaging land,  
On the little ones' ruin land,  
Here on Sia's stony, rugged land.  
The village weeps for the threshing field,  
The guests' ravaging land,  
The little ones' ruin land,  
Here on Sia's stony, rugged land.  
We have dear swing smiths:  
They made the swing on the hay meadow,  
Among the bluebells,  
Between the meadowsweet,  
In the campanula's cluster.  
When I come onto the swing  
I step down onto a berry,  
Onto a bluebell's eye,  
Onto a campanula's eyebrow,  
Between the meadowsweet.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Ten girls in the village  
Nine across the street:  
They were not allowed to come on the swing  
Afraid the gold would wear out  
Afraid the copper would bend  
Silver beads would break  
Banknotes would fade.  
Let our gold wear out  
Let our copper bend  
Let silver beads break  
Let banknotes fade  
We have goldsmiths at home  
Silversmiths outside  
At the gate are coppersmiths  
Tinsmiths on the path.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Wait, wait, swing, noo-noo swing  
What have you whined, what have you wished,  
The pike is the baseboard,  
You asked for an outer board.  
The swing whines for a glove's fabric,  
It was given a baseboard,  
And bound with an outer board.  
Let my brother take a wife,  
Another brother another wife,  
The third walks to the doorstep,  
The fourth brother goes to bring a girl,  
The fifth brother goes to drink wine,  
The sixth brother comes out of the crown.  
The brother makes a new swing in the yard,  
Behind the room with a roof tile,  
On the thigh a strap,  
On the pile a rowan branch,  
Outside an apple tree,  
In front of the barn green spruces,  
On the garden a maple tree.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
What are you fixing on the swing  
The swing is being fixed in the shed  
The baseboard is being nailed  
The beam is being wrapped with a red ribbon  
Wait, wait, swing, no, no, swing  
Let my brother take a wife  
The second brother take another wife  
The third brings home a daughter-in-law  
Then the swing will be fixed in the shed  
The baseboard will be nailed  
The beam will be wrapped with a red ribbon.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I heard a cricket chirping  
A spider spinning its web  
A lizard crawling  
I hit the cricket on the head  
The spider on the back  
The grasshopper on the leg  
The cricket went to Sweden to bring grass  
For its sick side butter  
The spider’s back butter  
The grasshopper’s pig butter.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Why does this swing creak  
The baseboard rattles  
The outer layer rustles  
The swing groans like a glove keeper  
The baseboard is giving way  
The outer layer is tied  
Wait, wait, swing, well, well, swing  
Let my brother take a wife  
One wife to another wife  
The third brother has been courtshiped  
The fourth brother brings a maiden  
The fifth brother has drunk vodka  
The sixth brother is announced  
Then the swing groans like a glove keeper  
The baseboard is giving way  
The outer layer is tied.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Young men, tender brothers  
Mother, where did they make the swing  
They made the swing at Karjasmaa  
At Karjasmaa, on Marjamäe  
And they made the swing narrow  
A goose can't swim underneath  
A little mouse can’t crawl on top  
Inside there's no silk bird  
Between there’s no Swedish beetle  
Girls swung on the swing  
Girls swung, their wreaths glistened  
Men watched under the swing  
Under the swing, on top of the swing  
If there were a rifle, they would catch the girls  
If there were a net, they would take the girls  
If there were a seine, they would gather the girls  
The girls heard, immediately they heard  
The wolf is caught with a rifle  
Fish is caught with a net  
Crayfish is gathered with a seine  
A thousand is the price of a troop of girls  
A hundred is the price of a girl’s drink

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Young men, tender brothers  
Mother, where did they make the swing  
They made the swing at Karjasmaa  
At Karjasmaa, on Marjamäe  
And they made the swing narrow  
A goose can't swim underneath  
A little mouse can’t crawl on top  
Inside there's no silk bird  
Between there’s no Swedish beetle  
Girls swung on the swing  
Girls swung, their wreaths glistened  
Men watched under the swing  
Under the swing, on top of the swing  
If there were a rifle, they would catch the girls  
If there were a net, they would take the girls

The wolf is caught with a rifle

A hundred is the price of a girl’s drink

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Ten girls in the village  
Nine across the street  
The tenth is the blacksmith's daughter in the village  
They were not allowed to come to the swing  
They were afraid that the gold would wear out  
The silver beads would tarnish  
The money purses would collapse  
Let our gold wear out now  
Let the silver beads tarnish  
Let the money purses collapse  
There is a goldsmith in the village  
From home comes the smoke of gold  
To the yard, the smoke of silver  
From the gate, the smoke of copper  
From the street, the smoke of tin  
Let the damned go to hell  
The devil's choir  
The great swamp fools  
I will live without a necklace  
Without a ring on my finger  
Growing without a necklace.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I went to see the swings,  
What is done on that swing.  
I found the kerbu swinging,  
The lizard rowing,  
The bee's wedding.  
I struck the kerbu on the eyebrow,  
On the lizard's eye,  
On the bee's beak.  
Kerbu went to Sweden to bring herbs  
For his golden eyebrow.  
On the lizard's eye,  
On the bee's beak.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Where this swing was brought to  
Brought here, received here  
The swing was woven in Kilvandu  
The beams were brought from Ambela  
The posts from Sinisalu  
Toomas was a thousand strong  
Getting a hundred reins  
The bulls of the brother were pulling

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, brothers Ella  
I came to try the swing  
Will this swing carry me  
If it doesn’t carry, then let it perish  
My brother will make a new swing  
A new swing, a splendid swing  
A brass swing for the gate  
A spotted one for the street  
He brings the swing from Kuuramaa  
Swing posts from Germany  
Swing fathers from Harjumaa,  
Axle from Voiveri  
Main planks from Põltsamaa  
Supports from the Turkish ridge  
Then I will swing high  
High up, far up  
So that I can see much land  
See much, pay much  
So that I appear to the healer  
I shine on the sea waves  
My wreath shines to the clouds  
The wreath’s ends shine to the rain  
My skirt shines to Kungla land  
Embroidery shines to Pikerill  
So that a boy will come, a son of the sun  
A suitor, a son of the moon  
A better suitor, a star’s son  
A dear suitor from a distant land.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let's go swing on the swing,  
Sing songs on the baseboard.  
Swing smiths, gentle brothers,  
The swing moves high along,  
The boyfriend’s swing moves softly.  
Swing smiths, gentle brothers,  
Let me down, I would ask.  
If you won’t let me down, don’t ask either.  
I will wait until evening,  
Swing until the middle of the morning,  
Sing until broad daylight.  
Surely the spruce yields milk,  
Surely the alder milks the cow,  
Surely the birch guides the herd,  
The maple tree waters the calf.  
Mund’s herd is far away,  
Mund’s heifers are in the meadow.  
Our herd is in the pasture,  
Our heifers are in the mud,  
Our sheep are in the barn,  
The geese are still in the fodder.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Old lady from the household,  
Old woman of the household,  
Rise from the bed,  
Lift your head from the pillows,  
Feet from the hundred linens,  
Look upon poor Kadri,  
Let Kadrike in,  
Kadri has come from afar,  
Over the marsh, through the slippery place;  
Kadri has a birch horse,  
A hazel sleigh,  
A rowan whip.  
Let Kadrike in,  
Let her in, I ask,  
Kadri does no harm:  
Kadri brings luck, luck for the herd,  
Luck for the cattle at the gate,  
Luck for the pigs in the dung,  
Luck for the chickens in the yard,  
Straw for the pigsty,  
Barns full of lambs,  
Yards full of chickens.  
Old lady from the household,  
Old woman of the household,  
Let the Kadris in:  
Kadri’s fingers get cold  
Toes begin to ache,  
Old lady from the household:  
Take the keys from the fence,  
Creep into the cellar,  
Creep into the chamber,  
Across the yard towards the barn;  
Look for a share for Kadri,  
Offer a treat for Kadri:  
Two or three cabbage heads,  
Five or six sausages,  
A bowl of white peas.  
Old lady from the household,  
Old woman of the household,  
Let her in, I ask,  
If you do not let her in, I will not ask again,  
I will knock loudly on the door.  
Among me are locksmiths at the door,  
I myself am a blacksmith,  
My sons are locksmiths too.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
How many girls in the village  
Ten girls in the village  
Nine across the street  
They are not allowed to come to the swing  
They are afraid of wearing out the gold  
Silver beads becoming less  
Money jars moving away  
Little sister, dear one  
Our two poor children  
We have a home with a goldsmith  
In the yard, a silversmith  
At the gate, a coppersmith.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadris have come from afar  
Over the swamp, through the slippery.  
Kadri has a birch horse  
A twiggy sleigh  
A hazel sled,  
A cowberry whip.  

Kadris have come from afar  
Around this curved moon,  
Around this red day,  
Around five rainbows.  

"Kadri spins, Kadri knits,  
Kadri spins wool,  
Knits with six threads –  
Kadri herself without a belt,  
Kadri’s children without a cap.  

"Old woman, granny,  
Rise up from your bed  
Shake the pillows out,  
Straighten your silk clothes.  
Go quickly, go briskly,  
Go quickly to the cellar,  
Go to the chamber,  
Across the yard to the barns.  
Look for Kadri’s ossikene (little bone),  
Try Kadri’s kakukene (little cake).  
Daughter of the house, maiden,  
Rise up from your bed –  
Blow this fire into the room,  
Take the tinder from behind the wall,  
Strike the kindling from the ceiling,  
From the bench the splint,  
If there is no fire in the house  
If there is no fire in the house  
Bring the horse into the room,  
Set the tail on fire,  
Push the smoke up into the chandelier."

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the Martin guests come in,  
Martin’s nails are frostbitten,  
Martin’s toes are aching.  
Raise the glass, Leenukene,  
Open the door, Annekene,  
But grandfather and grandmother:  
Rise from under the gray blanket,  
From above, the white linen sheets,  
Pull on the smoked wool socks,  
Put on the shoe bindings.  
Hop to the cellar,  
Creep to the chamber,  
Look for a share for Martin,  
Give Martin a morsel,  
Five sausages, six small sausages...  
Fill the pigsty with straw,  
Fill the sheepfold with hay.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Now the mardis are marching,  
The mardisants are traveling.  
Here comes Mart, totters Mart,  
Goes Mart, shines Mart.  
Mart has come in the year of the sky,  
Mart has come in the year of the earth,  
Wandering through the wool,  
Along the golden roads.  
The housewife was an old woman,  
The housefather was an old man:  
Go knock on the cellar,  
Try to bring food to Mart,  
A piece of raw meat,  
A dish of white peas.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadri has come from afar  
Over the swamp, through the slippery  
Kadri has a birch horse  
A brier-covered sleigh  
A gravelly little sled  
Kadri has come from afar  
Around five rainbows  
Around this crooked moon  
Around this red day  
Rise up, little old woman  
Rise up from the bed  
Stretch out of the silk clothes  
Take the pillows out  
Go to the cellar, go to the cellar  
Go to the pantry  
Across the yard to the granaries  
Kadri spins, Kadri knits  
Kadri spins wool  
Knits with six threads  
Pick wool, pick wool  
If it is not full of wool  
Take the wool into five pieces  
The daughter of the family, a maiden  
Rise up from the bed  
Blow this fire into the room  
Fan the fire into flames  
If you are not yet at your home  
Take a stick from behind the wall  
Strike a little chip from the ceiling  
A splinter from the bench  
If there is no chip from the ceiling  
A splinter from the bench  
Bring that horse into the room  
Set its tail on fire  
Push it into the barn like a chandelier.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadri has come from far away,
Over the marsh, over the bogs.
Kadri has a birch stick,
A slender little whip,
A reed rod,
Kadri’s fingers are freezing,
Kadri’s toes are aching,
Gloves are torn 
Socks tied on the feet.
I don’t want a little lamb,
Nor a woolly coat for it.
Pinch the wool into five pieces,
Cut the wool into six pieces...

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Answer, little St. Martins!
The Martins have come from afar,
Over the bog, over the marshes,
The Martin’s claws are freezing,
Toes causing pain.
The Martin has not come from the earth,
The Martin has come from the sky's edge.
Martin brings luck to the yard,
The bleating of sheep to the barn,
The grunting of piglets to the straw.
Answer, little St. Martins!
The Martins have come from afar,
Over the bog, over the marshes;
The Martin’s claws are freezing,
Toes causing pain.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Take the kadris on the way!
The kadris have come from afar,
over five fields,
over three dawns.
Kadri's fingers are cold,
toes causing pain.
Kadri knits with wool,
knits with six threads.
Take the kadris on the way!
The kadris have come from afar,
over five fields

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
If only the little Christmas people could come,
if only the dear times would arrive,
so we could quickly eat sausage,
and take part with our own hands.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Mart has come from a distant land,  
Across the swamp, across the open field,  
Across the long Lake Peipus.  
Mart has a birch-gray horse,  
A slender whip,  
A reed-gray coat.  
Rise up, family maiden,  
Blow that fire into the room,  
Stoke the fire for the poor fellow.  
If there is no barley on the floor,  
Take the rye and arrange it.  
If there is no rye arranged,  
Then pull the firewood to the ceiling.  
Rise up, family aunt,  
Rise up, family lady,  
Take those keys in your hands,  
And turn those hands to fists,  
Click-clack into the chamber,  
Across the yard to the storerooms,  
Look for Mart’s little bone,  
Try Mart’s little cake,  
Our Marts are pleasuring Marts,  
The village, however, has quarrelsome Marts,  
Other Marts are egg Marts.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadri's toes hurt,  
Kadri's nails are cold.  
This Kadri is not from here,  
Kadri has come from far away,  
Over the marsh, over the bogs,  
Through pitch-black darkness.  
Dear little father,  
Dear little mother,  
Come to the cellar,  
Come to the cellar to pick,  
Across the yard to the barns.  
This Kadri does not want a calf,  
Nor a lamb.  
The Kadris want a little bone for her.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
There are men in our parish,  
Proud and beautiful grooms,  
Painful and pale grooms!  
No maiden made demands,  
No wreath taken from the head,  
No brooch removed from the chest.  
All are maidens laughing,  
All are piias mocking,  
Brooches on the chest reproached.  
Ten girls in the village,  
Nine across the street,  
Eight on the trail of the herd.  
Swings do not come swinging,  
No singing on the swing board.  
Afraid that gold will wear out,  
Silver beads will become less,  
Money handles will fall off,  
We have goldsmiths at home,  
At the gate are coppersmiths,  
Tin smiths on the trail.  
I go to the smith’s workshop,  
Dear little smith.  
Take my drums, repair them,  
Make the silver beads whole,  
Make the money handles last.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Improvisatsioonid
translation:
Dear swing-makers, kind brothers!  
I came to try the swing,  
To see if this swing will carry us,  
If it doesn't carry, then let it be lost.  
My brother will make a new swing  
For the spotted street.  
He will bring the swing from Kuuramaa,  
The swing posts from Germany,  
The swing beams from Harjumaa,  
He will bring the swings from Võivere,  
The main boards from Põltsamaa,  
The supports from the edge of Turkey.  
Then I will swing high,  
High up, far up.  
So I will be seen far across the land,  
Be seen far away, pay dearly,  
So I will be seen by the shining sun,  
Shining on the sea waves,  
My wreath will be seen by the clouds,  
The tail of the wreath by the rain,  
My dress will be seen in Kungla land  
With embroidered long flowers.  
That a boy will come, a son of the day,  
A suitor, a son of the moon,  
A better suitor, a son of the star,  
A dear suitor from a distant land.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I went to see the swings.  
To see what is done on the swing.  
I found a flea swinging,  
A lizard rowing,  
A spider driving.  
I hit the flea on the eyebrow,  
The lizard on the eye,  
The spider on the beard.  
The flea went to Sweden to get medicine,  
For its golden eyebrow,  
For the lizard’s eye,  
For the spider’s beard.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the little Christmas forces arrive,  
The little New Year forces;  
So that the children can eat bread  
And take a bite with their own hand.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the beggars in,  
Marti's claws are freezing,  
Toes are aching.  
Blow that fire into the rooms,  
Bellows the blaze onto the floor.  
The beggars are not taken from the earth.  
Marti has come from under the sky,  
Along the silvering banks,  
Along the golden road.  
Let it be, let me curse.  
Let your pigs die,  
Let the hens lay far away.  
Empty the straw from the pigsty,  
Empty the barn of sheep.  
Marti brings you luck with the herd,  
From afar horse’s luck,  
From nearby cow’s luck.  
Fill the straw in the pigsty,  
Fill the barn in the sheepfold,  
Roofs full of hen nests.  
Kadri carding, Kadri spinning,  
Kadri carding the wool,  
Spinning six-thread yarn.  
Kadri has come from afar,  
Through swamps and bogs,  
Over long pipe forest,  
Over the wide grove island.  
Kadri’s horse is aspen-colored,  
Birch-colored wagon,  
Rowan-colored whip.  
Kadri’s claws are freezing,  
Kadri’s toes are aching.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Master master’s wife  
old mother, little old woman,  
go quickly to the cellar  
across the yard to the sheds  
look for St. Martin what you find.  
give something to St. Martin  
try to give fish to St. Martin  
a piece of raw meat  
a bowl of white peas.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadri’s nails are cold,  
her toes are hurting.  
Kadri has come from afar  
over wet mountains.  
Kadri brings luck to the herd,  
bride’s luck to the family’s son.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the St. Martin’s visitors in!
The earth is black and muddy,
The sky is wide and bare,
May the rains enrich my letter of blessing,
May the mud enrich my sled cover,
Frost for the good horse.
The stone pulled my shoe laces,
Willow roots tied the sandal strings.
Now my nails are cold,
Now my toes ache.
Daughter of the household, golden shoe,
Long-haired little swallow,
Rise from your silk bed.
Take a log from the top of the stove,
Take a peeler from the woodpile.
So there will be light in the room,
So there will be twilight in the corner.
May you have beautiful hay,
May curly oats grow,
Peas perfectly round,
Lentils broad and gleaming,
May the stallion be free of lice,
Free of lice, free of worms.
Mistress of the house, little woman,
Daughter of the household, tiny one,
Young maidens, little ones.
Give horned bread,
Give berry-filled pies,
Put chicken necks in the sack.
Tie boar bristles on your back.
Martin’s mouth is not a crack in the wall,
Martin’s hand is a branch of a birch tree.
The spurs will run out of oil,
The stall will drip a drop,
Then let us go down to the cellar.
We will bring large beer barrels,
We will bring small vodka barrels.
What takes sweet men's salvation,
Thanks to wise women’s salvation.
Thanks, house mothers,
Thanks, house fathers.
Be thanked for giving to Martin,
Be praised for carrying to Martin,
To house mothers the milking cows,
To house fathers the stocky bulls,
To the daughter of the household proud suitors,
To the son of the household fat brides.
May your corners cry
And your chambers shout.
May your elbows be wooden elbows,
May your shoulder blades be like apple trees,
The threshold flat and maple.
May you have bugs inside,
May you have beetles under your liver,
Big toads under your lungs.
May your horse in the stable be moldy,
May your bulls drown in meadows,
May your plowshares be burned.
May the alder bring happiness to the mistress of the house,
Maple to the luck of the calf,
Oak to the beautiful lamb’s fortune.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the St. Martin's Day guests in,  
Martin's nails are freezing,  
Martin's toes are hurting,  
Martin's toes have gotten wet.  
Where did those toes get wet?  
They got wet in feeding the pig,  
In carrying water to the cattle,  
In licking the lambs.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the Martins come inside,  
to be annoying at the door.  
The Martins have come from afar  
around the beautiful round one,  
through the moon and behind the stars,  
coming from beneath the gray sky,  
from the top of the red sun.  
Through the woods and groves,  
through the marshes and bogs,  
sticking an eye into the reeds,  
cutting a finger on the fern.  

Now the Martin's claws are cold,  
their toes are aching.  
Mistress, dear young lady,  
rise up from the bed,  
from between the white rugs,  
rise up from under the gray blanket,  
from the ancient straw on top,  
blow the fire into the room,  
stoke the fire into the hearth,  
look for a share for the Martin,  
look for morsels, look for scraps,  
the Martin's hunger is pinching,  
an empty stomach....

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Oh, this is our young maiden,  
a maiden as fresh as a meadow bud:  
she weaves a wreath for Pärnumaa,  
a brooch that shines for Saaremaa,  
her head is seen through the forest,  
her feet rarely under the forest,  
you will not snatch this maiden,  
you boys will not marry her,  
at home she has a dear husband,  
a dear husband, a fine groom!

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Good evening, family grandmother  
Good evening, family aunt  
Blow the fire in the room  
The accordion fire in front of the stove  
May Kadri be allowed to read  
To say a little prayer  
Kadri has not come from here, the land  
Kadri has come from far away land  
Over the bogs, over the marshes  
Over the long Peisi lake  
Over the swamps, this way and that  
Through the forests, back and forth  
Through the fields, along the ridges  
Along the paper fields  
Along the golden fields  
Dear family grandmother  
Dear family aunt  
Will you accept Kadri?  
Kadri has a birch foal  
A willow whip  
A juniper rake  
A pine needle broom  
Dear family grandmother  
Dear family aunt  
Will you accept Kadri?  
Kadri's toes hurt  
Kadri's nails are cold  
Kadri spins, Kadri knits  
Kadri spins wool yarn  
Kadri knits thread yarn  
Dear family grandmother  
Dear family aunt  
Will you accept Kadri?  
No, Kadri does not want a calf  
Does not want a little soul  
She wants a little fleece  
She wants a little rake  
Thank you, family grandmother  
Thank you, family aunt  
For accepting Kadri  
I wish you cow luck  
Pig luck on the floor  
Calf luck behind the wall  
Good sheep luck on the threshing floor  
The hay is still full, pig mother.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let's go sledding!
Flax sled long fibers,
Hemp fibers,
Flax for the sledder
Burrs for the pusher
Lint for the remover.
---
Let it be, let it be, I curse!
Where are you going to sled
Let the home get musty
Let it rest in front of the stove
Let it mold by the wall
Let it grow on the neck in the greenhouse;
Willow for the ears of grain,
Apple tree for the straws:
Chunk for the other thigh.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let's go from Jaanipäev to the fire,  
To keep the Jaanipäev fire!  
To protect the flock of letters,  
The fire wanders to the nest,  
The letter creeps onto the roof,  
Come from Jaanipäev to the fire!  
For Peeter and Paaver  
Two dear names,  
You made your way to the sky,  
You carved the road.  
To go up into the apple trees,  
To come down to the berry fields.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Rowing swing, pitching swing  
Swing smiths, shafts, brothers!  
What do I promise to the rowing time  
To the rowing time, to the pitching time?  
Over the pulley to the pusher;  
Over the shaft to the roller.  
From the shaft, a white maiden  
The longest, the finest:  
The straightest seam;  
The brightest between,  
This I promise to the rowing time.  
To the rowing time, to the pitching time  
Over the pulley to the pusher  
Over the yoke stepper  
Over the shaft to the roller.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let me try a new swing,
Will this swing carry the maiden?
Will it carry two of the beloved:
Two fine ladies,
Five maidens in a line.
If the swing does not carry us?
Then bring a sturdy swing,
Bring a free swing: -
You bring the swing to rest.
Let it be said again,
Let me sing again in return:
The swing is not just sturdy wood,
It is not mere idle talk,
The swing is brought from afar.
The boards were brought from Laiuse;
The rods were brought from Puivere
The beams were brought from Ammuta.
Toomas was bringing, Toomas was coming,
Toomas was buying a thousand;
He was getting a hundred reins:
He was calling the brothers together.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
What then swings our swing,  
Or what groans our voice?  
But the swing swings the glove mother,  
The lace of the mother’s leg groans.  
Let my brother take a wife,  
Then the swing will have a glove mother,  
A base cloth gift mother,  
An outer cloth lace mother.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Village girls, sisters,  
Village boys are half-brothers.  
Let's go and try the swing,  
See if the swing can carry us,  
If it can't carry, then let it perish.  
Is the swing made by a brother,  
Or made by the road boys?  
If the swing were made by a brother,  
The brother would have signed the swing.  
The road boys made the swing,  
The helpful spirits carved the beams.  
They made a narrow swing;  
From below, ants crawl in,  
From above, sparrows don't enter,  
In between, a poor little louse. -  
Swing me, drop me,  
Don't push me over,  
Don't swing me over the axle.  
If I fall off the swing,  
I have no brother on the swing,  
To send the brother under the table,  
Who puts a pillow under it.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Yesterday the mummers came to us,  
The day before yesterday the other mummers;  
Then they beat our asses,  
Tapped the assholes,  
Rummaged the money holes,  
Poked the owl holes.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Please, family matron:  
Take the key to the Kadrike chest,  
The Kadris have come from afar,  
Over the marsh, over the plain,  
Over the long Lake Peipus;  
Kadri’s fingers are cold,  
Kadri’s toes are aching.  
Please, family matron:  
Take the key from your pocket  
To the barns of Kõpsi,  
You take the wool for five pieces,  
I will throw in wool luck,  
The family daughter’s groom luck,  
The family son’s bride luck.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Oh, you crazy swing-makers!  
Look where you have made the swing!  
You made it on the village threshing floor,  
On the guests’ straw field,  
On the village boys’ stick field,  
The village was left crying on the threshing floor,  
The village children on the straw field,  
The village boys on the stick field.  
Swing-makers, dear brothers,  
Do not ask me for eggs.  
The hens stayed silent on the eggs,  
And the roosters started crowing in the ears.  
Row the swing, pull the swing,  
Row the swing to the faraway land,  
Where the roosters drink gold,  
Roosters of gold, hens beware,  
Ducks of green silver.  
Ten girls in the village,  
Nine across the street,  
Seven towards this side.  
They are not allowed to come to the swing,  
They complain about golden wear,  
Silver beads smelling of envy  
And purses rattling.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Oh, you crazy swing-makers!  
Look where you have made the swing!  
You made it on the village threshing floor,  
On the guests’ straw field,  
On the village boys’ stick field,  
The village was left crying on the threshing floor,  
The village children on the straw field,  
The village boys on the stick field.  
Swing-makers, dear brothers,  
Do not ask me for eggs.  
The hens stayed silent on the eggs,  
And the roosters started crowing in the ears.  
Row the swing, pull the swing,  
Row the swing to the faraway land,  
Where the roosters drink gold,  
Roosters of gold, hens beware,  
Ducks of green silver.  
Ten girls in the village,  
Nine across the street,  
Seven towards this side.  
They are not allowed to come to the swing,  
They complain about golden wear,  
Silver beads smelling of envy  
And purses rattling.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing-swing, gentle girls,  
Who have those swift lads,  
Set the swings to swinging,  
Set the rowing legs to rowing:  
The swing boards are sturdy,  
The swing handrails are square,  
The swing posts are pillar-like,  
The swing supports are strong:  
Come, village girls to the swing,  
Come, village lads to the swing,  
Bring bread rolls, bring curds,  
Bring beers, bring eggs:  
Our swing is made of fine wood,  
The swing handrails from oak trunk,  
Not made from anything cheap,  
The base boards do not creak:  
First, the charming boys from Valla,  
The strong lads from Köisi village,  
Come to try the swing,  
To look for your bride-to-be:  
The swing in Koordi is the best,  
The swing in Pordi even much better,  
There row the silk stocking wearers,  
The strong givers of men's life ...  
Let the swing move well,  
Let us call out the swing masters,  
Koordi’s swing is praised,  
Pordi's swing is exalted.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come swing, dear Juuli,  
leave Tõnu in bed,  
Tie his eyes with silk,  
Cover his eyebrows with fabric,  
Hands in the handkerchief,  
soft pillows under the head,  
spread wide sheets below,  
put a thick quilt on top,  
Our swing goes high above,  
our song sounds far away.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come to the swing, village women,  
Bring eggs, bring hens,  
Bring geese to hatch,  
Bring ducks in pairs.  
The hen stayed at home to lay eggs,  
The rooster stayed to crow,  
The goose stayed to hatch goslings,  
The duck stayed to brood goslings.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come together, village women,  
Come to swing on the swing,  
To sing the swing song,  
The haying time is coming,  
The time to harvest oats is near,  
The wheat will be gathered,  
The barley ear is already visible,  
The pea pods are opening,  
The pen is made for the pigsty,  
The cabbage is turning toward the sun.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadri carded wool,
spun six threads.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
Half brothers of our boys,  
Mother, where did they make the swing,  
They made the swing in the grazing land,  
In the grazing land, on the grazing path,  
On the pasture of the herds,  
They made the swing on the berry hill,  
On the berry hill, on the berry path,  
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
Do not ask me for eggs,  
The chick stayed to lay eggs,  
The other stayed to tend the nest,  
The rooster stayed to crow,  
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
Do not swing me too hard,  
Throw me over the beams.  
I have chicks under my wings,  
I have to watch out for twins.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Row, swing, row,
Row over three fields.
There were three fields side by side:
One was a field of rye with rust
The second a precious field of oats,
The third a green field of barley.
The rye field belonged to the young woman,
The barley field was the maiden’s field,
The oats field was the suitor’s field.
Row, swing, row,
Row over three forests.
There were three forests side by side:
One was a precious birch forest,
The second a golden spruce forest,
The third a generous pine forest.
The one that was the precious spruce forest,
That was the young maiden’s forest.
The one that was the golden spruce forest,
That was the young suitor’s forest.
The one that was the generous pine forest,
That was the young boys’ forest.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
Do not ask me for eggs.  
I will come tomorrow the second time,  
And the day after tomorrow the third time.  
Then I will bring eggs, then I will bring hens,  
I will bring a pair of ducks.  
I will bring geese under my shawl.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come swing, village women.  
Bring eggs, bring hens,  
Bring ducks in pairs,  
Bring ducks in the barn in pairs,  
Birds wearing feathers on their legs.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Flax tow, long fiber.  
Flax tow for the flax breaker  
Locks for the one sitting indoors  
Waste for the one pulling the fibers.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I heard the village swinging  
The village rocking in its cradle.  
I took to making a frame  
I took steps to move forward  
I braided hay in the barn on the hill  
In the middle of the yard in the cellar  
I took off the lid of the chest  
The goat's hide from the chest lid.  
I took whiter from the frame  
I took an axe from the chest  
Only thin threads were visible  
Fine strands on top  
The hem rustled down to the heel  
The linen moved down to the toes  
Put on a wide belt  
Like the rainbow in the rain  
Wrapped around with a wide sash  
Like half of a reindeer hide,  
In front laid out  
Like a snake on the barn door  
Put a large brooch on the chest  
Like the moon in the sky  
Laid a necklace of beads  
Like the hen that lays eggs on the straw  
Put rings on the fingers  
Like these new shoes  
Put on rustling stockings  
Lastly, I put on quick shoes.  
Then I went to swing in the village  
To rock the village cradle.  
The snake was full of daring  
The district was full of mischief  
Bread made from the finest flour  
Bread stacked in a basket  
I found a kitchen apron  
New desires on top of fog.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
This swing is made of sow's meat  
From a basket of chicken eggs  
Catching a zander from the table.  
Who came to swing for us  
To rock our cradle?  
From Riga came a knight's son  
From Paide came a priest's son  
From Finland came the son of a great lord.  
There was a dog behind the thicket  
Behind the shore by the rake  
There was a little red puppy  
This is not from the sow's meat  
This is not from a basket of chicken eggs  
Nor from catching a zander from the table.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing, little swing,  
Swing, swing to the high place,  
To the high, distant place.  
What did you see from there up high?  
From there I saw three oats.  
Each oat had a pod,  
Each tip had a clinging husk.  
One oat was free-spirited,  
The second was covered in wax,  
The third was written in gold.  
What about the free-spirited oat?  
I gave that to my mother.  
What was covered in wax?  
That I gave to my father.  
What was written in gold?  
That I put under my armpit,  
Behind my armpits—  
That was  
Secretly taken along.  
Peti (a suitor) into the chamber,  
His wife into customs,  
Is there a wolf or is there a bear?  
Is there a wolf near the beer,  
Or is there a bear near the well?  
Sometimes there was a wolf, sometimes a bear,  
Sometimes a wolf near the beer,  
Sometimes a bear near the well.  
Again I say differently,  
I say in another way.  
There was spirit, there was mind,  
There were heads full of brains,  
Silk shirts full on the back,  
Big socks on the feet,  
Colorful gloves on the hands.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
We had a brother swinging on the swing  
Brother was imagining the swing  
Knitting a rooster's feather  
Chasing a duck's feather  
Humming a bird's song  
Brought a rooster's feather from Kuramaa  
A goose feather from Harjumaa  
A dove feather from Medemaa man  
From there I swing high  
High, far away  
From there I am seen in Paide  
From there I am heard in Helme  
That I am not even visible  
My face appears red  
That I am not even waving  
My flag is in the linen hair  
That I am not even swinging  
My rattle makes a sound.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Oh my dear cradle rocker  
Gentle silver rockier  
Do not throw in anger  
Nor pull in truth.  
Here is a cradle beneath the grave  
A deep grave at the bottom of the iron  
One fathom deep  
Two fathoms clever  
Ten cubits wide  
It can make ears wither  
Tie clover hay  
Let the storm rain down.  
Neither father nor mother is under the cradle  
The Russian is under the cradle  
The Pole is under the tree.  
They do not take my hard work  
They do not strip my cross from the land  
They do not take my dear from the land.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let them in, the Kadris!
Kadri's fingertips are cold,
Toes hurt.
The Kadris have come from afar,
Over the marsh, over the rivers,
Over the dear birch forest,
Over the entire open forest.
Kadri has a silky horse,
A broom twig carriage,
A whip made of birch bark.
Kadri's basket was full of water,
Hairs full of dew –
Kadri splashed into the swamp,
Kadri spun wool,
Wove six-thread yarns.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Slide on the linen, long fiber!  
Velcro for the velcro pusher,  
Lint for the lint remover!

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Slide and slide  
A long fiber of flax!  
Flax for the slider,  
Burrs for the fire-pusher,  
Fluff for the leader.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
What are you creaking, our swing,
Or what groans, our swing support?
The swing creaks from the mitten's edge,
The baseboard groans under the support,
The top beam has red ribbons.
Let my brother take a wife,
The second brother brings another wife,
The third brother has been to propose,
The fourth brother has seen a maiden,
The fifth brother has drunk vodka,
The sixth brother has been announced, -
Then the swing creaks from the mitten's edge,
The baseboard groans under the support,
The top beam has red ribbons.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
The house ghost is an old woman,  
the house spirit is an old man,  
accept the Kadris!  
The Kadris have come from far away.  
Kadri's fingers are cold,  
Kadri's toes hurt.  
With Kadri, spinning wool,  
weaving six-thread yarn;  
she doesn’t want a skein  
nor a little angel.  
Pinch wool, pinch wool,  
for Kadri with six-thread yarn...

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
The Kadris came from afar,  
across the bog, over the slippery path.  
Kadris have an ashen foal,  
a slender log hut,  
a slender little whip.  
The Kadris came from far away,  
over five rainbows,  
over this red sky,  
around this curved moon.  
Kadri spins, Kadri weaves,  
Kadri spins the wool,  
weaves on the six-thread loom,  
Kadri's children without a cap,  
Kadri's father without a belt.  
If there is full wool in the shepherd's bag,  
twist the wool, pinch the wool,  
take the wool and tear it into five pieces.  
Mistress, young maiden,  
rise from bed,  
take pillows out of the bedding,  
stretch out the silk clothes,  
look for a piece of bone for Kadri,  
try to find a piece of cake for Kadri!  
House daughter, young maiden,  
blow the fire in the room,  
fan the fire into a blaze!  
If the fire does not reach the house,  
bring the apple into the room,  
light the tail on fire,  
push the candle up to the chandelier.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadris have come from afar,  
over the swamp, through the slippery paths.  
Kadri has a birch horse,  
a wicker sled,  
a sapphire blue little sleigh.  

Kadri has come from afar,  
around five rainbows,  
around the curved moon,  
around the red day.  

Rise up, little old lady!  
Come out of bed,  
straighten out your silk clothes,  
fluff up your pillows!  

Go tiptoe, go stealthily,  
go tiptoe to the cellar,  
go stealthily to the chamber,  
across the yard to the granaries.  

Take the keys, prepare!  
Kadri spins, Kadri weaves,  
Kadri spins wool,  
weaves on six threads.  

Pinch some wool, pinch some wool,  
if you are fully full of wool,  
take the wool in five pieces.  

Housemaid, little maiden,  
rise up from bed,  
blow that fire in the room,  
blow the bellows to the embers.  

If there is no old man in the house,  
take the rafter from behind the wall,  
but hit the wooden shingles from the ceiling,  
the plank from the bench,  
take the ring from a finger,  
another from the other hand,  
a third from a golden finger.  

Who does not cast pearls  
and does not save beads,  
take his father's horse,  
unsaddle the fine horses,  
from the dappled eyes on the stable bed.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadris have come from far away in the countryside,  
over the marsh, over the open field,  
over the long Lake Peipus.  
Kadris have a birch foal,  
a pine sapling rake,  
a willow whip.  
Good evening, lady of the house!  
Good evening, female head of the household!  
Do you want to receive Kadris?  
Get up, young maiden,  
blow this fire inside,  
stoke the fire in the stove!  
If there is no glass in the window,  
take bark and patch it up;  
if there is no bark,  
take boards and nail them;  
if there are no boards,  
pull moss down from the ceiling. —  
Blow this fire inside,  
stoke the fire in the stove!  
Get up, lady of the house,  
get up, female head of the household,  
take these keys in your hands,  
turn these locks with your palm,  
clickety-clack to the chamber,  
across the yard to the granaries,  
search for a little piece of luck for Kadri,  
try to find a little cake for Kadri,  
roll it with a rolling pin like a wool tack.  
Behind Kadri’s back is sheep wool,  
longing for angels,  
Kadri wants sheep’s wool,  
longing for a skin cover; —  
Kadri herself without a belt,  
Kadri’s children without a cap.  
To the front room, old lady’s blessing,  
to the back room, pigeon’s blessing,  
to the front, maiden’s blessing! —  
Thank you, ladies of the house,  
thank you, female heads of the household…  
A hundred sheep in the barn  
and a thousand lambs behind them…  
May there be no more in the barn  
and fewer behind the barn…

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Mistress, little bird,  
let the Kadris go to the cellar!  
Kadri has come from a distant land,  
the edge of the dark world,  
this moon is crooked from the ear.  
Let the Kadris go to the cellar!  

Bring that fire into the room,  
let the bellows blow the fire bright!  
Pull the snake, lift the blanket,  
dance quietly, little Kadri,  
So that this fire does not fade from the earth  
and the sand does not move from the ground.  
The fire ruins the apron’s embroidery,  
the mist is the new moon’s pattern...  
..........

Mistress, little bird,  
search for an offering for Kadri,  
look for a karask for Kadri.  
If you don’t know, I will guide you:  
the oven is on the top of the stove,  
the karask is in the room on top of the cupboard.  
Kadri cries wiping her nose,  
Kadri whines with a woolen thread,  
Kadri wants to pace...

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I heard the village rocking,  
the village whispering,  
I took to my tasks,  
I began to move step by step:  
I carried hay to the mountain,  
into the cellar under the door,  
I opened the lid of the chest,  
the lid of the painted chest;  
I took the whitest from the chest,  
I took the axe from the chest,  
eagerly mounted on my back  
a slender, pointed spear,  
a bucket of water,  
linen stockings up to the toes.  
Overhead came a big night,  
as if a rainy rainbow,  
around it spread a big cloak,  
as if half a reindeer skin.  
I laid out the wide apron,  
as if new barn doors.  
On my chest, I placed a big brooch,  
as if the moon in the sky.  
On my neck, a grandmother’s barley straw,  
like a hen laying straw eggs.  
On my head, I put a big wreath,  
like a large rooster's comb.  
On my finger, I linked a ring,  
like stars in the sky.  
On my foot, I wore woolen socks,  
at the end, shoes tied with straps,  
then I went rocking to the village,  
whispering through the village.  
There they moved boldly around,  
the district was full of mischief,  
bread was gold-like fine,  
I laid my apron on the floor,  
mist swirled over the fir trees.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Now let's go on the swing of the letter,  
on the back of the audio letter,  
between the white buds.  
Now we  
to the heights, to the distance,  
from here I live  
from here I appear.  
Though I do not live myself,  
I live the life of a living soul;  
though I do not appear myself,  
my face shows red;  
though I do not wave myself,  
my flax hair waves.  
Let my beloved look, let her see,  
let my beloved look under the stage—  
is there here someone of that kind,  
that kind, that little bunch,  
that little tree boy,  
who takes the swing of the letter,  
on the back of the audio letter,  
between the white buds.  
If it can’t be, it can’t happen,  
if it’s not possible, I won’t endure.  
Men will come to me from other lands,  
companions will come from afar,  
a groom will come from the Polish border,  
bringing a handful of gold,  
a clear handful of oats...  
someone brings a yoke to the father-in-law,  
someone brings a saddle to the mother-in-law,  
the village brings a basket of oats,  
gifts with spiked shoes for the bride.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Oh my dear swinger,  
whisperer to spring,  
don’t throw angry,  
don’t pull truthfully.  
Here is a swing under a stone,  
here is a spring under a hollow,  
a deep hollow, iron-bottomed,  
in the night a curse, in the cold,  
in the cold a cunning one.  
I am weary of knocking,  
cross to pull,  
to rain a mark down,  
neither father nor mother under the spring,  
who steals from my dear,  
who takes my mark away,  
who pulls my cross away.  
The Russian under the spring,  
here tears my cross from the ground,  
here takes my mark from the ground,  
here steals from my dear.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let Christmas come, let it come,  
let the simple New Year be,  
so the children can eat bread,  
take their own small ox, –  
munch on sausage!

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadris have come from afar,  
over the bog, over the swamps,  
over the long Lake Peipus.  
Kadris have a birch foal,  
a slender little whip,  
a broom-like bundle.  
The furrow was lean, the surface was wooden,  
the furrow-fingers were made of alder wood.  
On the floor, pig luck,  
to the heated room, good luck for the husband,  
in front of the door, luck for the harvest,  
in front of the stove, luck for the cow...  
Quick, go to the cellar,  
across the yard to the granary,  
look for a little bone for me,  
bring me a small cake!

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the Kadris come in,  
the Kadris have come from afar,  
over the swamp, over the rivers.  
Kadri has a birch foal,  
a small spruce reindeer,  
a small pine whip.  
Kadri spins, Kadri weaves,  
Kadri spins wool,  
weaves six-thread yarn.  
If you can't eat whole bread,  
cut the bread in half.  
If you can't eat half the bread,  
then eat a crumb.  
If you can't finish the whole sausage,  
take half the sausage.  
If you can't use the whole fabric,  
cut the fabric in half.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Ten girls to the village,  
eight across the street,  
eight to the herd trail,  
do not come to swing on the swing,  
to sing on the swing board,  
afraid the gold will wear out,  
the bells to straighten,  
the money bags to wear out.  

We have goldsmiths at home,  
at the gate there are coppersmiths,  
down in the yard silversmiths,  
tinsmiths on the road;  
from the bag rises gold smoke,  
from the gate copper smoke,  
down in the yard silver smoke,  
tin smoke on the road.  

I go to the smith with a request:  
Little smith, little fellow,  
take my things and fix them,  
straighten the bells,  
make the money bags hold.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Ellakene, dear mother,  
go to the room,  
across the yard to the barn,  
bring a piece of pork,  
a bowl of white peas.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Oh you golden swing boys,  
Delicate swayers!  
Swing, sway!  
Swing high,  
High towards the distance;  
So that I can see the land of Paide,  
The land of Paide,  
So that I can see the top of the forest,  
The forest beneath my feet,  
The body’s lovely turn in the forest.  
If I do not shine myself,  
The shining cheeks are red.  
If I do not live myself,  
The lively sprigs are beautiful.  
If I do not shine myself,  
The linen fibers shine.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
What is the swing for swinging?  
The swing’s board for hitting?  
The swing swings a bit  
With wide lace ribbons  
Posts with bags at the shops.  
What shall I give the swinger  
I shall give the beginner of the swing?  
Let the merchant come to the village  
The shop boy to the neighbor.  
I’ll buy a little knife with an inscription  
A sheath with a fire inscription  
A small sign or gate sign,  
That is what I will give the swinger  
I shall give the beginner of the swing. -

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Martins come from afar  
Across the bog, across the open land  
Martin has a birch horse  
A whip made of juniper  
A small bird cherry paddle  
A reek made of rosemary  
Martin’s fingers are cold  
His toes are painful  
Bless the grandmother, bless the grandfather  
Let the Martins come in  
Look for Martin’s little bone  
Touch Martin’s little cake  
Martin does not desire the soul  
Nor does he want the lamb  
Martin wants the lamb’s fleece  
The soul’s flesh  
Go to the cellar for cabbage  
Across the yard to the barns  
Take these keys from the rack  
Turn the locks in your hand  
Blow that fire into the room  
Kindle the fire under the bellows  
If there is no mirror  
Take the board from the wall plank  
................................................................  
Spread the linens for luck  
Behind the wall, good fortune to him  
In the corner, good fortune to the young man

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I defiantly went onto the swing.  
What did I find on the swing?  
I found a flea swinging,  
a spider cradling,  
a grasshopper chirping.  
I hit the flea on the side,  
the spider on the tail,  
the grasshopper on the back.  
The flea then yelped in pain,  
the spider was in tail pain,  
the grasshopper was in back pain.  
I then went to Riga to get some grass,  
so I could cure the flea's side,  
the spider's tail,  
the grasshopper's back.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
do not swing me from evil  
nor sway me from wickedness:  
I am an evil one to fall.  
If I fall, who will pay me:  
a pair of oxen pays the palate,  
another palate pays another pair,  
ears for a small horse’s ears,  
eyes for a silk scarf.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come, come little Christmases,  
Niki-näki little New Year's,  
So the children can eat bread,  
Take a piece with their own hand!

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Good evening, dear grandmother of the family!  
Good evening, dear aunt of the family!  
May I sing as well?  
Post the link, dear Leenu,  
Open the door, dear Anne,  
Get the ribbons ready, Madli!  
We will throw seed barley,  
Behind the wall, barley stems,  
In front of the fire, barley seeds  
Dear grandmother of the family,  
Dear aunt of the family!  
Look for a share for Mart,  
Try to find a fish for Mart,  
Six or seven little sausages,  
Two or three little cakes!  
Mart has not been taken from the land,  
Mart has come from heaven,  
Along the edge of the field,  
Along the golden field,  
Along the copper gates.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swingsmiths, dear brothers,  
Do not swing out of malice,  
Do not tease the wicked.  
 I am an angry breaker,  
A leaf of anger rolling.  
If I fall, who will pay me?  
Who will pay me, who will pay me?  
My side will pay a pair of oxen,  
The other side will pay another pair.  
Swingsmiths, dear brothers,  
Do not swing out of malice,  
Do not tease the wicked.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let Kadri's fingers in  
Kadri's nails are cold  
Kadri's toes are aching.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Martin has come from a distant land,  
Martin's toes are freezing,  
Martin's toes are hurting.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Mart did not come to eat,  
Mart did not come to drink,  
Mart came for the beauty of the house.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Linen sliver, long fiber.  
Linen slivers for the flax breaker,  
Locks for the room sitter,  
Fuzz for the front puller.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
Please let me down,  
If you don't let me, don't ask,  
Let me swing until evening,  
Move until early morning,  
Surely the aunt is milking the cow,  
Surely the father is herding the cattle,  
The white brother is watering the calves.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Linen slide, long fiber,  
Linen for the slider,  
Knots for the fire pusher,  
Lint for the leader...

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing-makers, brothers,  
Why do you swing from wickedness,  
Maliciously teasing,  
Frowning with anger?  
Oh, you crazy swing-makers,  
You go to the village chambers,  
Spread the village on the floor,  
Pinch the maidens,  
Nibble on the girls,  
No butter-mothers come to the swing!  
Where are those golden curls of fat,  
Silver buds of fat,  
Brass of the calf,  
Day-fed fat?  
The sheep like that tower,  
Horns like sauna horns,  
Villu carries hay on his back.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Why do you long for our swings,  
Or why for our father-in-law?  
The swing is longed for by the mitten's shed,  
The baseboard will be given away,  
The top layer will be tied with ribbons,  
The post with silk hemp ribbons,  
The shaft with a pure wax coating.  
Wait—wait for the swing, no—no for the swing!  
Let the brother take a wife,  
The second brother another wife,  
The third brother will go courting,  
The fourth demanding a maiden,  
The fifth to carry the flag,  
The sixth to catch a sail,  
Then the swing will be the mitten's shed,  
The baseboard will be given away,  
The top layer will be tied with ribbons,  
The posts with silk hemp ribbons,  
The shaft with a pure wax coating.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadri comes from far away,
Over the swamp, over the bogs.
Kadri has a copper horse,
A whip with a whistle,
A reekene made of sedge.
Kadri’s nails are freezing,
Kadri’s toes are aching.
Give Kadri a fleece jacket,
She does not want a lambskin,
Nor does she want a fleece jacket.
Split the fleece of the plague into two,
Pinch the fleece into five pieces.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
The village dreamer shouts to the swing,  
What is the swing shouting there?  
The swing is shouting from the mitten shed,  
While by the edge, the mother’s leg binders.  
Let my brother take a wife,  
The second brother promises to take one,  
The third brings a bride home.  
Then the swing will have the mitten shed,  
And by the edge, the mother’s leg binders.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing blacksmiths, dear brothers,  
Look where they made the swing,  
They made the swing in the middle of the village,  
Of old women quiet (reserved?).

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Row, swing, reach the swing,  
Row across three forests!  
There were three forests side by side:  
One was a golden spruce forest,  
It was the forest of young men;  
The second was a dear birch forest,  
The third was an orchard forest,  
It was the forest of young girls,  
The beauties with cannon heads.  
Row, swing, reach the swing,  
Row across three fields!  
One was a distant rye field,  
It was the field of young men;  
The second was a field, a barley field,  
It was the field of young women;  
The third was a wheat field,  
It was the field of young girls,  
The little birdies with flaxen heads,  
The beauties with shaved heads.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Row, swing, reach the swing,  
Row across three forests!  
There were three forests side by side:  
One was a golden spruce forest,  
It was the forest of young men;  
The second was a dear birch forest,  
The third was an orchard forest,  
It was the forest of young girls,  
The beauties with cannon heads.  
Row, swing, reach the swing,  
Row across three fields!  
One was a distant rye field,  
It was the field of young men;  
The second was a field, a barley field,  
It was the field of young women;  
The third was a wheat field,  
It was the field of young girls,  
The little birdies with flaxen heads,  
The beauties with shaved heads.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Or what creaks our father-in-law?  
The swing creaks with the glove-maker,  
the base boards with the gift-giver,  
the outer boards with the band maker,  
the moss with the silk ribbon maker.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Oh, you crazy swing-makers  
where have they made the crazy swing  
They made the swing at the village pasture  
At the guests' grazing land  
At the village boys' stick land

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
If only the little Christmas elves would come,  
niki-naki little New Year elves,  
then the children could eat bread,  
the women could drink sweet beer,  
the men could make merry,  
the boys could mess about in the hay.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, my brothers,
come together to swing,
to sing the swing song..
- -
My cheek appears red,
a ring shines on my finger.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Rowing swing, rowing swing  
To the swing, to the swing, village girls  
Lively young maidens of the parish  
Come to try the swing  
Does this swing carry you?  
Bring eggs, bring chickens  
Bring ducks, drakes  
Bring birds with slender legs  
Bring geese from the pond  
But I heard, I replied:  
We come to try the swing  
Does this swing carry us?  
If it doesn’t carry, then let it go  
My brother will make a new swing  
A new swing, a proud swing  
He will make a German swing from hazel wood  
A maiden’s swing from lime wood  
A proud swing from apple wood  
A stronger one from bird cherry wood

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let's go swing on the swing  
The swing board  
Will this swing hold us  
If it doesn't hold, then away with it  
My brother is making a new swing  
He is making a swing with crutches from hazel wood  
He is making a loose swing from linden  
He is making a proper swing from apple wood  
He is making a low swing from berry wood  
Let's go swing on the swing  
The swing board  
Will this swing hold us  
Swing it, rock it  
Sway it over the crossbar  
Don't push me too hard  
Nor slam it too forcefully

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Dear family elder woman  
Dear family widow  
Look for a share for Martin  
Try to give Martin a small cake  
Bring Martin some raw meat  
For beggar Martin a little bag  
Martin has come from afar  
Over the bog, over the plain  
Across the long Lake Peipus  
Along the woolly hill  
Along the golden stubble  
I throw in grain luck  
I hang up door luck  
For the family girl, bride luck  
For the family bride, maiden luck

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
(Appetite appetite, I would soak and go,  
Swing, swing far away –  
I should row to that town,  
Where the girls knit stockings,  
Boys palm ribbons,  
Children shape wide belts.  
Girls pull flax like this,  
Servants weave cloth.)

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths  
Do not confuse me,  
Do not toss me over the axle:  
I am an angry rooster,  
Always vain,  
I fall onto the side stone,  
My cheek onto the thick rock.  
If I fall, who pays?  
My side costs ten rubles,  
The cheek costs a couple of rags,  
The other cheek costs another couple of rags,  
The nose is fresh as a berry,  
The eyes are like silk chicks.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, strong brothers,  
Do not swing me in anger.  
If angry, pull me roughly;  
I am an angry rooster,  
Angry, I am to be pulled.  
If I fall, who will pay?  
The cheek will pay for a pair,  
One cheek for the other pair,  
A nose for a young cannon,  
An eye for a silk speck.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, Ella brothers,  
Take the swing to the cellar,  
Beams to the barn master,  
Posts for the sauna gate—  
Now the busy time is coming,  
The sharp working time is here;  
The rye heads are already visible.  
Barley ears are covered with soil,  
Wheat is coming from the spikelets,  
Peas adorn the field,  
Cabbage is swelling under the sun,  
The sickle is working tirelessly,  
The knife is for the meadow’s edge.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
Why did you make the swing in the pasture,  
Didn't you make the swing in the berry field:  
If I go to herd the cattle,  
The swing will remain unused;  
If I go to pick berries,  
Then I go to try the swing.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, strong brothers,  
Will this swing hold us—  
If it won't hold, let it be lost;  
My brother will make a new swing,  
A new and better swing.  
Why are you creaking, little swing?  
Holes, little baseboard?  
The swing creaks because of the glove mother,  
The baseboard was given by the glove mother.  
(Let) my brother take a wife,  
Uncle takes a new wife,  
Aunt goes to another man—  
Then the swing will creak because of the glove mother,  
The baseboard will be given by her,  
The overhead beam will be tied;  
Let the chickens bring, let them bring eggs,  
Let the geese bring what is woven,  
Let the ducks come in pairs—  
This is the swing smith's wage,  
Given for the baseboard.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Here is a golden swing at the guesthouse,  
At the gate is a brass swing,  
Under the yard is a silverish swing;  
Here are golden swingers,  
Silvery rockers,  
Brassier shakers.  
If we had a golden swing at home,  
A brass swing at our gate, -  
No one would come from the village to beg,  
Or from a neighboring area to stare;  
We have no one at home,  
We have nothing on the land -  
Because from the village they come to beg,  
From the neighboring area to stare.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Let's go test the swing,  
If this swing will carry us  
If it doesn't, let it be lost.  
My brother is making a new swing.  
A new swing, a proud swing  
A white swing in the gate  
Behind the room from bird cherry,  
Under the threshing floor from elm,  
Out in the yard from apple wood.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers.  
Let's go test the swing.  
Will this swing hold us?  
If it doesn't hold, let it go.  
My brother will make a new swing.  
A new swing, a proud swing.  
A white swing at the gate.  
Behind the bog, from the pine.  
Under the thigh, from a splinter.  
In the yard, from the apple tree.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Auntie, granny,  
Grandmother, little uncle.  
Blow this fire into the room,  
Fan the flame beneath the hearth.  
If you have no elder brother in your family,  
Draw the boards of the loft tightly.  
If you have no boards on the loft,  
Draw the hearth's smoke tightly to the room ceiling.  
If there is no smoke to the room ceiling,  
Take twigs from the edge of the roof.  
If there are no twigs at the edge of the roof,  
Bring a horse into the room,  
And set its tail on fire.  
Keep blowing the fire into the room,  
Fan the flame beneath the hearth.  
Auntie, granny,  
Grandmother, little uncle,  
Alone to the barns,  
To the ripe cabbage chamber,  
Look for a little bone on Martin,  
Try Martin's little cake.  
Our Martins, welcome Martins,  
Village Martins, old Martins,  
Martin has come from the earthly world,  
Over the beautiful lands.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Housemaid, young maiden  
Farm girl, sturdy one!  
Blow that fire in the room,  
The fire in the bellows to the bottom.  
If the fire is burning in the fireplace,  
Take a piece from under the eaves.  
If there is nothing under the eaves,  
Take a splinter from the fence.  
If there is no splinter in the fence,  
Hit a sliver from the ceiling.  
Surely I know the ways of servants,  
Servants' ways, herding ways.  
As a servant, one is more diligent,  
In the herd one goes more sparingly,  
I would certainly become a wise mistress.  
What is the wage of a mistress?  
Fire without a key to the room,  
Water disappears without a key.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
On St. Catherine's Day, a birch horse,  
A whip made of juniper,  
A reed-made rod.  
The Catherines have come from far away;  
Catherine's fingers are cold,  
Her toes are aching.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing me high,  
So that I shine over the fields  
Over five grain fields,  
Over six plowing fields,  
Over seven seed fields,  
Eight barley fields,  
Nine rye fields.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadri carding, Kadri spinning  
Kadri cards wool  
Kadri spins oat yarn  
She smooths soap pieces  
She combs flax fibers.  
Master, little master  
Mistress, little mistress  
Is going to the cellar to bake  
Is going to the chamber to peel cabbage  
Bring to Kadri raw food  
Give Kadri fresh food  
Kadri has walked far away  
Kadri has a birch horse  
A twigs-y cart  
A rowan whip.  
Take it, dear Kadri  
Kadri’s hands are freezing  
Her toes are hurting.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
To the Attüma family father  
To the Attüma family mother  
Good gifts to give  
To hand into the box  
What do I wish for the family mother?  
Ten good milking cows  
What do I wish for the family father?  
Ten oxen to the field  
What do I wish for the family son,  
Young maidens, large beds,  
What do I wish for the family daughter?  
For the family daughter to have a dowry  
If there is power in the royal family  
What do I wish for the family girl?  
Fingers full of rings  
Bracelets of money  
What do I wish for the family servant?  
An old horse and a collar around the neck.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I throw in the luck of grain  
On the roof, the luck of chickens  
Into the barn, the luck of horses  
To the stable, the luck of a gray stallion.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the Kadrikeis in  
Kadri rattles in the loft  
From the Sagarista sends off  
Among Kadri the door smiths  
Among them are the sagar smiths  
In the pocket the rattlers.  
Kadri makes the doors from apple wood  
From the twisted horn makes the sagars  
Puts rattles in the elk antler.  
Welcome the Kadrikes  
The family's young maiden  
Rise up from the bed  
Move the sheet aside  
Between the soft pillows  
Blow the fire into the room  
Blow the bellows at the fireplace  
If there’s no bed in the house  
Pull some kindling to the stove  
If there’s no kindling for the stove
