Take some grass from the eaves  
If there’s no grass from the eaves  
Pull the bundle from the ceiling beam.  
If there’s no bundle from the ceiling beam  
Take the bed from the frame  
But do not put out the fire in the room.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Why do you creak our swing  
Why do you groan our cradle  
The swing creaks in the mitten's shed  
Giving the baseboard  
The upper cover rises  
Sway-sway swing, wait-wait swing  
Let my brother take a wife  
The second brother takes another wife  
The third brother then takes a third  
The fourth starts demanding wives  
The fifth starts drinking vodka  
Then the swing will creak in the mitten's shed  
The baseboard will be given  
The upper cover will rise.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Village boys, dear brothers  
Came to swing on the swing  
Let me get down, I would ask  
If you don’t let me, I won’t ask  
Let me swing until evening  
I’ll swing until midday  
I’ll sing until the broad white (day)  
Surely the grandmother is milking the cow  
Surely the grandpa is herding the cattle  
The white-headed one is feeding the calf  
If the grandmother doesn’t milk the cow  
And the grandpa doesn’t herd the cattle  
Then the alder will milk the cow  
Then the birch will herd the cattle  
The white-headed one is feeding the calf.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come, swing, village girls  
Bring eggs, bring chickens  
Bring ducks in pairs  
Waterfowl in ordered rows.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come to the swing, village girls  
Bring chickens, bring eggs  
Bring geese from the barn.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Yesterday Marti waited  
Today Mart has come here  
Mart has not come from the earth  
Mart has come from the sky's year  
Crawling between the clouds  
At the middle of the sixth dawn.  
Door smiths, frame smiths  
Give Mart a share  
If you cannot give bread  
Cut the bread in half  
If you cannot give a full candle  
Cut the candle in half.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Village girls are sisters  
Village boys are half brothers  
Come to Jaan's fire  
Come to keep the fire  
To protect the sparks  
The fire roams around the building  
Bitterness swirls at the doorway  
The spark settles under the eaves  
What man did before  
And slowly God gave.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Old granny from the family
Old man from the family
Let the Kadris in
The Kadris have come from afar
Over the swamp, over the rivers
Over the wet mountains
Over the clear springs
Kadri's fingers are cold
Her toes are causing pain
Old granny from the family
Blow, fire, into the room
Blaze, fire, on the floor
If the fire burns hot in the house
Pull the smoke to the ceiling
If the smoke is in the ceiling
Pull the cinders to the hearth
If the cinders are in the hearth
Pull the ashes to the floor.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
The young moon is dim  
The opposite night is white  
Young men are lazy  
They hit strong walls  
Tapers behind the pole  
You go to the pub to dance  
Slip and slide.  
The opposite night is white  
Sheets for the slider  
Clamps for the push-backer.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come from Jaani to the fire  
Come to keep the fire.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadri has come from afar  
Over the glow of the moon  
Over the redness of the day  
Over five rainbows  
Kadri has come from afar  
Kadri's fingers are cold  
Her toes are in pain  
She asks the mitten's keeper for cold  
She wants winter's shoe keeper  
Where she had put a thorn in her throat  
Covered her throat with a fur coat  
On the wall of Seidl's tavern  
On the beam of Vaida's tavern  
There she had put a thorn in her throat  
She covered her throat with a fur coat  
Where can one get a mitten's keeper in the cold  
Or where can one get winter's shoe keeper  
In the chill weather, felt boots  
From sheep comes mitten's keeper in the cold  
And gets winter's shoe keeper  
In the chill weather, felt boots.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Why are you creaking our swing  
Why are you groaning our cradle  
The swing creaks like a glove-shed  
Base boards give and bend  
Top boards stretch and sway  
Wait, wait, swing, noo, noo swing  
Let my brother take a wife  
The second brother brings another wife  
The third girl is to be served  
The fifth girl is to watch  
The sixth girl is to listen  
Then the swing will creak like a glove-shed  
On the base board give and bend  
On the top board stretch and sway.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Oh, you crazy swing smiths  
Swing smiths, gentle brothers  
Look where they have made the swing.  
Made the swing in the grazing land  
In the grazing land, on the pasture path  
In the boys’ clearing  
In the girls’ shavings land  
They did not make the swing in the hayfield  
Nor in the middle of the coltsfoot  
Nor among the meadowsweet.  
Come to the swing, village girls  
How many girls are in the village?  
Ten girls in the village  
Seven of them nearby  
They are not allowed to come to the swing  
They fear their gold will wear out  
Their silver beads will tarnish  
Their money pouches will empty  
Our two poor children  
Our gold will not wear out  
Our silver beads will not tarnish  
Our money pouches will not empty  
Surely the alder will milk the cow  
Surely the birch will tend the herd.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Row the swing, row the swing  
Row the swing to that land  
Where the logging forest is visible  
Where the tar willow forest rises  
Where the spruce forest grows.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, tender brothers,  
Look where they have made the swing  
To the pasture, to the berry field  
They have made a narrow axle  
A narrow axle, a tall axle  
From below, one cannot swim through  
From above, one cannot crawl through  
Inside there is no silk bird  
Above there is no colorful finch  
May the devil take the swing smiths

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadri spins, Kadri knits  
Kadri spins a wool thread  
Knitting a yarn of six threads  
Kadri has a birch horse  
A spruce fir whisker  
A rowan whip  
Kadri has come from afar  
Kadri's toes are aching  
Let Kadri in.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Come to swing the swing  
To sing songs on the swing board  
The crossbeam was brought from Võhmuta  
The beams were brought from Annu  
Swing boards from Laiuse  
Posts from Sinisalu  
Rods were brought from Puivere  
Large boards from Laiuse  
Large rods from Puivere  
Large posts from Sinisalu  
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Look at where they made the swing

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Mart had come from far away,  
through the marsh step by step,

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing brothers, swing brothers  
Let me down, I would ask,  
If you don’t let me down, don’t ask—  
Then I will swing until evening,  
I will swing until morning,  
I will sing until broad daylight.  
Surely the aunt is milking the cows,  
Surely she is herding the cattle at home,  
The white straw is feeding the calf,  
Drives the herd far away,  
Far away, high away.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the St. Martin's Day visitors in
St. Martin's Day visitors, St. Catherine's Day visitors,
Catherine's fingers are freezing,
Catherine's toes are aching.
The Catherines have come from a far land,
The Martins from deep in the forest.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers!  
I heard the swing swinging,  
In the birch treetop waving,  
In the heath flowers blooming,  
I heard it at home,  
At home, at the homestead,  
Among the long threads of flax,  
Among the broad leaves,  
I wait to hear the wolf howl,  
To dig two wells.  
I do not wish to be called off,  
To dig two wells.  
I went myself, a little one,  
Along the road, a tiny one,  
Along the water, a small one,  
Into the garden, a modest one.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the Kadris enter,  
Kadri’s nails are cold,  
Kadri’s toes are aching.  
The Kadris have come from far away,  
From far away, from high places,  
Over the marsh, over rivers,  
Over wet mountains,  
Over long reeds,  
Over the tall frost wall.  
They were driving the brother’s servants,  
The brother’s servants, the brother’s slaves,  
The brother’s purchased horses,  
Locals pledged for money.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
The mistress of the house, the family matron,
Decided to go to the cellar,
Into the new oven,
To fetch some schnapps,
A piece of raw meat,
A plate of white eggs.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Sisters, darling ones!  
Come and swing on the swing.  
Is this swing made of cherry wood,  
Or from a beautiful apple tree,  
Or from a sweet berry tree?

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come, come maidens  
Come to the solstice fire  
To Peter's brightness  
Come to keep the fire  
To twist the fire's swirl  
The fire is pressing into the building  
A spark into the bedpost  
Into the broad tongues of flame

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
What do you do, our swing  
Or what do you groan, our cradle  
The swing needs the glove's support  
The baseboard needs the gifts  
The top beam wants to be tied  
Wait, wait, swing, no, no, swing  
Let me come tomorrow a second time  
The day after tomorrow a third time  
Let my brother take a wife  
The second brother a second wife  
The third brother goes to listen  
The fourth to catch a girl  
The fifth brother goes to drink alcohol  
The sixth brother goes to the matchmaker  
Then the swing will get the glove's support  
The baseboard will get the gifts  
The top beam will get tied.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadri combs, Kadri carding  
Kadri combs six threads  
Kadri cards the wool  
Little old woman, little old man  
Kadri’s fingers are freezing  
Kadri’s toes are aching.  
Little old women, little old men  
Let the Kadris come inside.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, gentle brothers  
Look, where they have made a swing  
Let's go try the swing  
Will this swing carry us?  
If it doesn’t carry, then let it go  
My brother will make a new swing  
A new swing, a proud swing  
The stone quarry is under the swing  
Wide stones under the table  
I don’t have my own swing  
Nor does my brother under the table  
Who will pick the stones from under the swing  
Wide stones from under the table  
If I fall, who will pay for me?  
My father will pay with a pair of oxen  
One ox for one pair  
The nose of a young horse  
Eyes like little silk drops.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Hello, hello, little family  
Hello new doors of the family  
Hello birches of the market  
Hello branches of the island  
Lift the latch, Leenokene  
Open the door, Annekene  
Mari, get the ribbons ready  
Are you maidens going home?  
Mistress, little maiden  
Let the vagabond Mardis in  
Take the shoes from the room  
Stockings from the big edge  
Step into the barn  
Hurry to the cellar  
Look for a share for Mardi  
Try to get fish for Mardi  
Bring a piece of raw meat  
A plate of white beans  
Two or three pancakes  
Five or six sausage pieces  
The family grandmother is an old lady  
What I promise you  
Fill the doorways with grain  
Threshing floors  
Fill the sheep’s barn  
Flood the pigsty with straw  
Fill the manure pits  
Master, little master  
What I promise you  
Horse stable, braiding sheds  
Close walls fattening  
The hay must be good  
House girl, little maiden  
May six suitors come to her  
Some go, others come  
The third must stay at home  
Servant, little servant  
What I promise you  
Iron gun, forest signal  
Drooping ears of the little dog  
May your pigs die  
Piglets fall ill with cough  
Gray bulls fail.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
But Marti is coming, Marti is staggering  
Marti is walking, Marti is shining  
Marti has buds on his hips  
Marti has lace from head to toe  
Silver buttons on his hips  
Where did Mart get that from?  
Mart has come from afar  
Along the silver edge  
Along the golden shore  
Before me I throw lucky barley  
Behind the wall, strong barley  
Lee forward, sturdy barley  
House girl, maiden  
Rise from under the gray carpet  
From above the precisely folded cloth  
Housekeeper, old lady  
Go to the cellar  
Go to the room  
Across the yard to the granaries  
Look for a share for Mart  
Catch fish for Mart  
Bring a piece of raw meat  
White plates, old maiden  
Two or three baskets  
Housekeeper, old lady  
What I promise you for luck  
May a dark cow rise for you  
May the blood roll red  
May your barn be full of sheep  
May the hayloft be full of piglets  
May the manure heap be full of piglets  
May the chicken coops be full of chicks  
May the duck pens be full of ducks  
Housekeeper, old lady  
This I promise you for luck.  
Put on a silk shirt  
Hands wrapped with knitted gloves  
House girl, maiden  
But you have five drinks to take  
Five drinks, six courtships  
Seven hundred slender (women).

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
Blacksmiths of Kiik, gentle brothers  
Do not rock me out of spite  
Do not taunt me cruelly  
Do not lash me angrily  
Do not rock the world with anger  
Do not taunt it with malice  
Do not lash it with rage  
Or else let me say it again  
I will sing the words back  
I came to seek gold  
I came to ask for silver  
I came to gather melodies  
Oh, my dear old lady  
And my wise old man  
I am afraid to go home  
Afraid to shine afar  
Afraid to creak behind the beach  
Afraid to knock in Paide  
The city walls clattered  
The Paide towers boomed  
Against my good voice  
Against a rougher throat  
Against a more painful tune  
I went to Harju to learn  
To take the tune of Viru  
In Harju I learned from sisters  
In Viru five brothers-in-law  
In Kurus six daughters-in-law  
Dear little sister, little brother.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Do not swing me out of anger  
Do not crank me angrily  
I am an angry swing  
I am a cranky anger leaf  
If I fall, who will pay me  
At my side a pair of oxen pays  
At the other side another pair  
The nose of a young horse  
Ears to the ears of the foal.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swingsmiths, dear brothers  
Do not ask me for eggs  
The hen stayed home to lay eggs  
The hawk stayed to watch.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Do not swing me out of anger  
Do not torment me in anger  
I am an angry cockerel  
I am a leaf of anger to torment  
If I fall, who will pay me  
My neck is paid for by a pair of oxen  
Another neck by another pair  
Eyes like a silk chick  
Nose like a young horse  
Ears like the rune of the ear.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadri carding wool  
Carding with six threads  
Kadri came from afar  
Over the bog, over the ridges  
A little rowan, a little whip  
A little spruce branch, a little twig  
The grandmother was a little old woman  
Going to the cellar  
Across the yard to the granaries  
If you can't fill the wool  
Just take half the wool.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come together, children of Mardi  
Come in front of Mardi's chair  
Thank the father of the family  
Thank the mother of the family  
Give a kiss to the father of the family  
Give a kiss to the mother of the family  
Hurry to go to the cellar  
Across the yard to the barns  
Look for a gift for Mardi  
Try to find fish for Mardi  
Find meat for Mardi.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Look, where they made the swing,  
Made the swing in the pasture  
For guests' playgrounds  
For the maidens' bog land.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swingsmiths, gentle brothers  
Do not swing me angrily  
Do not pull me out of spite  
I am an angry rooster  
I am a wound on a leaf of anger  
I am a dancing oak leaf  
When I fall, cost me  
My side costs a pair of bulls  
The other side another pair  
Eyes of a blue-gray rooster  
Ears of a small horse  
Nose of a young horse.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
Village dear young men  
Take the swings to the cellar  
Hayricks down to the haystacks  
Swing boards to the workshops.  
The haytime is coming  
Already the rye heads are seen  
The barley ears are turning green  
Oat ears cover the ground  
Wheat ears will come soon  
Go to the swing high up  
So I can see over the field  
I can see beyond three fields  
Three fields stood side by side  
One was the rye field of Ruuga  
The second was the beloved oat field  
The third weaves into the wheat.  
What was Ruuga’s rye field?  
That was the young women’s field  
What was the beloved oat field?  
That was the young men’s field  
What weaves into the wheat there?  
That was the young maidens’  
Up, up, little swing  
Up along the apple trees  
Down, down, little swing  
Down along the berry trees  
Go to the swing high up  
So I can see a lot of land  
I can see beyond three forests  
Three forests stood side by side  
One was the golden spruce forest  
The second was the beloved birch forest  
The third was truly the apple tree forest.  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
That was the young men’s forest  
What was the beloved birch forest?  
That was the young women’s forest  
What was truly the apple tree forest?  
That was the young maidens’  
Go to the swing high up  
So I can see over the field  
I see the field, I pay a lot  
Even if I do not see myself  
My wreath shines red  
It sparkles also around my neck.  
A ring shines on the finger too  
Another in the other hand  
A third in a golden hiding place  
Let it end, let it stay again  
Let it end until tomorrow  
Until the coming Friday  
Until the four Whitsuntide days  
Until the dear time of Jaanipäev (Midsummer).

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
Village dear young men  
Take the swings to the cellar  
Hayricks down to the haystacks  
Swing boards to the workshops.  
The haytime is coming  
Already the rye heads are seen  
The barley ears are turning green  
Oat ears cover the ground  
Wheat ears will come soon  
Go to the swing high up  
So I can see over the field  
I can see beyond three fields  
Three fields stood side by side  
One was the rye field of Ruuga  
The second was the beloved oat field  
The third weaves into the wheat.  
What was Ruuga’s rye field?  
That was the young women’s field  
What was the beloved oat field?  
That was the young men’s field  
What weaves into the wheat there?  
That was the young maidens’  
Up, up, little swing  
Up along the apple trees  
Down, down, little swing  
Down along the berry trees  
Go to the swing high up  
So I can see a lot of land  
I can see beyond three forests  
Three forests stood side by side  
One was the golden spruce forest  
The second was the beloved birch forest  
The third was truly the apple tree forest.  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
That was the young men’s forest  
What was the beloved birch forest?  
That was the young women’s forest  
What was truly the apple tree forest?  
That was the young maidens’  
Go to the swing high up  
So I can see over the field  
I see the field, I pay a lot  
Even if I do not see myself  
My wreath shines red  
It sparkles also around my neck.  
A ring shines on the finger too  
Another in the other hand  
A third in a golden hiding place  
Let it end, let it stay again  
Let it end until tomorrow  
Until the coming Friday  
Until the four Whitsuntide days  
Until the dear time of Jaanipäev (Midsummer).

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Look where they have made the swing  
Made it in the village’s meadow  
For the children of the parish  
For the boys to play on.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, gentle brothers  
Do not swing me from evil  
Do not spite me cruelly  
I am an angry swinger  
I am a rolling wrath leaf  
I was not given much time  
Only a little time was given  
The hen stayed at home to lay eggs  
While the rooster crowed at the ear.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the Kadrisants enter  
Kadri’s nails are cold  
Toes are in pain  
Kadri has come from afar  
Over the marsh, over the bogs  
Over the pine forest hills  
The uncle without a family  
The grandmother without a family  
Rise up from the bed  
The family daughter, the maiden  
Take this fire into the house  
Light the fire in the stove  
If there is no bread in the house  
Take some food from the harvest  
If there is no food in the harvest  
Pull the smoke from the ceiling  
Still take the fire into the house  
The grandmother without a family  
Skip off to the cellar  
Go to the pantry  
Bring raw meat  
Put a pig’s head  
If there is not enough whole bread  
Cut the bread in half  
If there is not enough wool to fill  
Take half of the wool  
If there is not enough whole leg of meat  
Pull half of the leg  
Thank you to the family father  
Thank you to the family mother  
Thanks for giving good gifts  
For giving strong gifts.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Look, where they made the swing  
Made the swing on the road  
On the road, to the land of lands  
To the village boys' gathering place  
To the children’s play area.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the St. Catherine's Day visitors in  
Mart is not taken from the ground  
Mart has come for the heavenly year  
Let the St. Catherine's Day visitors in  
On St. Catherine's Day, a birch horse  
A bundle of twigs covered with bark  
A rowan whip  
Catherine plaits, Catherine twists  
Catherine twists the wool  
Plaits a six-thread braid  
Catherine does not want a lambskin  
Catherine wants a woolen coat for the lamb  
If the loaf is not fully whole  
Cut the bread in half  
If not fully half a loaf  
Turn, turn the slices  
If you cannot fill the shuttle  
Cut the shuttle in half  
If you cannot fill the spindle  
Take the spindle in half.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Maidservant, young maiden  
Blow this fire into the stove  
The bellows’ fire on the floor  
If it doesn’t reflect back inside  
Take the firewood from the room ceiling  
If there is no firewood on the room ceiling  
Pull the slats from the window  
Always keep the fire in the stove  
Sweep the room, clear the table  
Sweep under the windows  
Turn near the cellar  
Then you will be praised  
Praised as a good old woman’s daughter  
As a wise old man’s little plant  
A good girl has made you dance  
Made a wise old man dance  
Let the Sandimardis come in  
Mardi’s toes are aching  
Mardi’s nails are freezing  
Master, little master  
Mistress, young lady  
Go dance in the cellar  
Go dance in the chamber  
Find a portion for Mardi  
Bring a piece of raw meat  
A plate of white eggs for the lady.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Hello father-in-law, hello mother-in-law  
Are you girls at home  
The linen heads are in front of the barn  
The tidy heads are at the back of the room  
May I come into the room  
Help with driving in the yard  
Tie the straw to the wall  
Perhaps you have some grey ones indoors  
A linen cloth slide on the floor  
Mart does not care about drowning  
Nor falling into the water  
Does not fear breaking his neck  
Mart goes straight to the stream  
Together to the fishing nets.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Accept, poor Mardis  
The Mardis have come from afar  
Over five grains out  
Over six handfuls out  
Over seven seeds out  
Mardi's nails are freezing  
His toes are hurting  
Accept, poor Mardis  

What I wish for the father of the family:  
Good oats for the fields  
Good rye to turn out  
Good barley for the back  
Good peas for the front  
This I wish for you  

What I wish for the mother of the family:  
Loads filled with sheep's hay  
Doors filled with grain  
Rows of hens to lay eggs  
This I wish for the mother of the family  

What I wish for the daughter of the family:  
A white young man from the parish  

What I wish for the daughters of the family:  
A white sheep with kind wool  
A tail like a broom in the wind  
This I wish for the daughters of the family  

What I wish for the farmhand of the family:  
The dog has six puppies  
From them all will become hunting dogs  
This I wish for the farmhand of the family  

Accept, poor Mardis.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the St. Martin’s visitors in  
The Martins have come from afar  
This Martin is not just taken from the ground  
Martin has come from afar  
Over the swamp step by step  
Through the forest swiftly and lightly.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Why do you creak our swing?  
Why do you rustle our garden?  
Let one brother take a wife,  
Another brother take another wife.  
The third brother goes to courtship,  
The fourth brother has seen a maiden,  
The fifth brother has drunk spirits,  
The sixth brother has been to the courts.  
Then the swing can be fenced securely,  
The base boards can be given,  
The top plank can be fastened with straps,  
The rafters with silk leg straps,  
Sisters, dear little sisters.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Do not swing me from evil  
I am an angry rooster  
I am a leaf of wrath to be tied  
I am a birch leaf to jump  
I am a poplar leaf to fly.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the Martin guests come in  
Do you promise to come inside  
To bustle around the yard  
To drumming under the eaves  
Martin’s nails are getting cold  
Toes are causing pain  
House daughter, maiden  
Pull that fire from the room  
The fire in the bellows smolders  
Master, little master  
Mistress, little mistress  
Get up from the bed  
Move the linen cover  
Jump and go to the cellar  
Across the yard to the building  
Bring those salted meats, bring those tongs  
Two or three turnips  
Meat for Martin and everyone  
What gift do I give you  
House daughter, maiden  
Nice sled to follow  
A bucket in the hand  
Son of the house, little boy  
What gift do I give you  
A fine stallion in the stable  
A nice sleigh to the stallion  
I cast luck of peas to the lady  
I cast luck of barley to the yard  
To the back wall I cast luck of sticks.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
The martens have come from the sky's year  
Along silver branches  
By the bronze gates

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Do not swing me with malice  
Do not spitefully torment me  
I am a cruel rooster  
I am a willow leaf for whipping.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the St. Martin's guests in  
For Martin has not come from the land  
Martin has come from the sky's harvest  
From up high, from the tall place  
Martin's fingers are cold  
Toes are aching  
The old woman was a little old lady  
Look for sausage ends  
Touch the bread crumbs.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Up, up our swing  
Up the swing to the high edge  
To the high edge, to the distant edge  
So that I can be seen far and wide  
Near the town it seems  
Behind Tartu shining  
Even if I do not shine myself  
My red ribbon shines  
My blue silk gleams  
The striped ribbon lies  
Who brought the red ribbon  
Father brought the red ribbon  
Who brought the striped ribbon  
Brother brought the striped ribbon  
Who brought the rose-patterned scarf  
My lover, gentle brother  
He brought the rose-patterned scarf.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I went to test the swings  
To see if the swings were made by my brother  
The swings were not made by my brother  
Why are you fussing about the swings?  
The baseboards are creaking  
The top beams are rattling  
The swing groans in the mitten shed  
The baseboard is in the grain store  
The top beam has red ribbons  
Let my brother take a wife  
Another brother takes another wife  
The third brings me home  
Then the swing will groan in the mitten shed  
The baseboard will be in the grain store  
The top beam will have red ribbons.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Kadris came from far away countryside  
Over wide and open land  
Over the long dark bog  
Kadri’s fingernails are cold  
Kadri’s toes are aching.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the St. Martin's masqueraders in,  
For Martin has not come from the ground,  
Martin has come from the sky,  
Across the marsh, through the rivers,  
Across the marshy bog,  
Over the hill and knoll.  
Martin has a birch horse,  
A spruce sleigh,  
A hazel sledge,  
A willow whip.  
Mistress, little woman,  
Go quickly to the cellar,  
Go quickly to the chamber,  
Across the yard to the granary,  
Bring from there some pork fat,  
Bring a sheepskin bag,  
If the louse does not fill the wool,  
Take half the wool,  
If the louse does not fill the ham,  
Take half the ham,  
Always bring Martin his due.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Family daughter, little maiden  
Family daughter, little plant  
Blow this fire into the room  
Stoke the fire on the floor  
If there is no mirror to the room  
Take the horn from the ceiling  
If there is no horn on the ceiling  
Pull scales from the candle  
If there are no scales on the candle  
Take herbs from the row  
If there are no herbs in the row  
Light the candles to burn  
Keep the fire inside the room  
Fire on the hearth.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the St. Martin’s visitors in  
Martin’s nails are cold  
Martin’s toes hurt.  
Martin has come from a distant land.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Shoot me down, I would ask,  
if they don't let me, I won't ask,  
I swing until eight o'clock,  
I sway until mid-morning;  
the village woman milks the cow,  
the village man herds the cattle.  
If the woman doesn't milk the cow,  
and the man doesn't herd the cattle,  
the village woman from Leppa milks the cow,  
the village boy from Kas´ki herds the cattle.  
The herdsman Muri, the little herdsman boy.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, kind brothers,  
do not ask me for eggs,  
the hen stayed home to lay eggs,  
the rooster kept crowing by the ear.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come to the John’s fire,  
To the bright shine of Peter,  
The fiery John’s fire,  
The burning shine of Peter,

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Oh, you crazy swing-makers  
Look where they have made the swing  
Made the swing on the common pasture  
Where the guests graze  
Where the village boys graze  
Where the village wives gather  
The village cried over the plowed fields  
Village wives over the grazing land  
Village boys over the grazing land.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Do not swing me with malice  
Do not spitefully mock me  
What I promise to the war journey  
To the war journey, to the force journey  
From the room behind to the wide field  
The field was triangular  
In each corner three clusters  
In each cluster three bundles  
In each bundle three sheaves  
In each sheaf three stalks  
In each stalk three heads  
In each head three ears  
I promise this to the war journey  
To the war journey, to the force journey  
To the one who swings himself  
To the one who rolls over the axle  
What I promise to the second  
The fairest young maiden from the parish  
The tallest, the slenderest  
The whitest from the parish too  
The proudest among the crowd  
The straightest of men  
I promise this to the second  
What I blow to the third  
A horse a pile long  
A gray one the height of a garden fence  
From the pile he limits the barn  
From the room he lifts the roof  
From the garden he steps on the back  
What I name to the fourth  
Let a merchant come to the village  
A shop boy comes to the door  
I buy a knife for the border pattern  
A duck pattern behind the head  
A cross of the poultry pen  
I name this to the fourth  
Swing smiths, dear brothers

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
What do you care about our swings
Or what do you groan about our cradle
The swing wants to be woven with gloves
The baseboard wants to be made with gifts
The outer layer wants to be tied with ribbons
Pillars with silky leg ribbons
Wait, wait, swing, noo, noo, swing
Let my brother take a wife
The second brother brings a girl
The third brother goes to propose
The fourth one to demand a maiden
The fifth to patter the fishing rod
The sixth to catch sailcloth
Then the swing can be woven with gloves
The outer layer can be tied with ribbons
Pillars with silky leg ribbons

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let's go to the midsummer bonfire  
Whoever doesn't come to the midsummer bonfire  
Will stay at home sulking  
Molding in front of the stove  
Picking mushrooms by the wall  
Getting angry by the pile  
What do I promise to the war year  
To the war year, to the power year  
From the parish, a fair-haired girl  
Whom I offer to the second  
Or whom I kiss to the third  
A horse the length of a stack  
Puts a saddle on the sled

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come to the bonfire  
Come to keep the fire  
To forbid the rage  
The fire roams into the building  
The rage twists into the roof

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Angrily I throw up my hands  
Maliciously I set the price  
Shoot me down, I would ask  
If you don't shoot, don't ask  
Let me sway until evening  
Swing until early morning  
The village cow milks the alder  
The village spruce strains milk  
The whitehead waters the calf  
The birch indeed drives the herd.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dung smiths
Look where they have made a swing
Made a swing down by the village
To the guest boys' pasture
To the village boys' wedding pasture
So come then to swing on the swing
To sing in the swing bench
Then bring chickens, bring eggs
Bring ducks for hatching
Bring ducks in pairs

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let's go to the midsummer bonfire  
To keep the bonfire  
Not to forbid bitterness  
The fire keeps it in the building  
Bitterness twists into the roof

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Come to the Midsummer bonfire  
To keep the Midsummer fire  
If you don't come, no need to ask  
The flame wanders into the buildings  
A spark jumps onto the roof  
Ashes scatter across the village  
Wait, wait, Jaan, no, no, Jaan  
Why do you come early in Jaan  
Let the hay be cut first  
Rye begins to grow in the fields  
Barley heads bend in the swaths  
Barley ears turn green  
Cover the oats with soil  
The rye heads are already visible  
Wheats come out of the sheaths  
Beans grow their pods  
Peas decorate the clothesline  
Lentils wrap around the legs  
And flax is like a whip around the waist.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Come to the Midsummer bonfire  
To keep the Midsummer fire  
If you don't come, no need to ask  
The flame wanders into the buildings  
A spark jumps onto the roof  
Ashes scatter across the village  
Wait, wait, Jaan, no, no, Jaan  
Why do you come early in Jaan  
Let the hay be cut first  
Rye begins to grow in the fields  
Barley heads bend in the swaths  
Barley ears turn green  
Cover the oats with soil  
The rye heads are already visible  
Wheats come out of the sheaths  
Beans grow their pods  
Peas decorate the clothesline  
Lentils wrap around the legs  
And flax is like a whip around the waist.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Where our swings were brought from  
Where they were brought from, where they were obtained  
The shaft was brought from Võhmutas  
The boards brought from Laiusilta  
The rods brought from Puiveres  
The swing supports from Ammutasta  
The braces brought from the fields of Türi  
The swing pillars from Sargvere  
Who was bringing our swings  
Who was bringing, who was receiving  
Toomas bringing, Sander receiving  
Toomas was bringing a thousand horses  
Receiving was a hundred carts  
Pulling was five grays  
Gathering was six wastelands  
Seven bluish-gray skins  
Eight Kalev grays  
Nine good choirs  
Ten plow handles

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Wait, wait Jaan, well, no Jaan -  
Come Jaan, hurry Jaan  
Why did you come early, Jaan  
Let’s finish making the hay first  
Pigs big, not yet fed  
Sheep to the yard, not yet housed  
Breads still not kneaded  
Grain still not winnowed  
Come Jaan, hurry Jaan  
Pigs big, fed  
Sheep to the barn led  
Breads firmly kneaded  
Grain already winnowed  
Grain stalks tied.  
Barley lying straight  
Oats two by two behind  
Foam over the threshing floor

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Row with the oars, reach with the oars  
You go to the high swing  
To the high one, to the distant one  
So that I shine across the land  
I shine across, I pay across  
My face shines red  
A brooch glints on my chest  
A gold chain around my neck  
A silver ring on my finger  
A third gold one in the chest

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
May these Christmases arrive  
So one could eat pork  
And take a piece of ox with their own hand.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Ten girls in the village  
Nine across the street  
Eight to the herd path  
Seven towards the middle  
They are not allowed to come to the swing  
They fear the gold will wear off  
Silver items to be mended  
Money bags to wither  
We have goldsmiths at home  
At the gate are coppersmiths  
Tinsmiths to the road

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
How many girls to the village?  
Ten girls to the village  
Nine across the street  
The swings won’t come to swing  
They won’t come to try our swings  
They fear the gold will wear out  
The silver ornaments will lose their shine  
The copper jugs will stop flowing  
The clover jugs will stop flowing  
Let our gold wear out  
Let our copper wear down  
Let the silver ornaments lose their shine  
Let the copper jugs stop flowing  
Let the clover jugs stop flowing  
We have goldsmiths at home  
Outside are silversmiths  
At the gate are coppersmiths  
On the manure heap are chainsmiths.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Do not swing me badly  
Do not tease cruelly  
Do not tug harshly  
There is no evil swinging here  
No cruel teasing here  
No angry tugging here  
Here are golden swings  
Silver teases.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
A cradle, a cradle, our swing  
What do you rock, little swing?  
The swing rocks the mitten's yard  
The baseboard gives support  
The shingles tie it on  
Wait, wait, swing, no no swing  
Let my brother take a wife  
The second brother another wife  
The third brings the bride home  
Then the swing will indeed rock the mitten's yard  
The baseboards give support  
The shingles tie it on

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Move the swing to the cellar
For four Pentecost days
For five Midsummer days.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Let's go swing on the swing
Let's sing to the swing board
Will this swing carry us
If it won't carry, let it fade away
Where have they made the swing
Made the swing in the shed
In the visitors' gathering place
In the village boys' meeting place
Village wives, little wives
Young men, lads
Bring chickens, bring eggs
Bring baskets for weaving
Bring twin chickens
Forest roosters to sing
On our dear swing
Our dear young men
Made us a dear swing
Where we young men sing
To our dear maidens
Across forests, across lands
Across distant seas
Across wide fields
Across echoing cliffs
Across deserts, across mountains
Across forests, fields
My brother is making a new swing
A new swing, a splendid swing
Making a German swing from hazel
Making a dear swing from birch
Making a dull swing from pine
Making a long swing from rowan
Making a loose swing from alder
Rowing swing, arriving swing
Rowing swing to the land
Where roosters eat gold
Roosters gold, chickens silver
Baskets green, silver.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Let's go swing on the swing
Let's sing to the swing board
Will this swing carry us
If it won't carry, let it fade away
Where have they made the swing
Made the swing in the shed
In the visitors' gathering place
In the village boys' meeting place
Village wives, little wives
Young men, lads
Bring chickens, bring eggs
Bring baskets for weaving
Bring twin chickens
Forest roosters to sing
On our dear swing
Our dear young men
Made us a dear swing
Where we young men sing
To our dear maidens
Across forests, across lands
Across distant seas
Across wide fields
Across echoing cliffs
Across deserts, across mountains
Across forests, fields
My brother is making a new swing
A new swing, a splendid swing
Making a German swing from hazel
Making a dear swing from birch
Making a dull swing from pine
Making a long swing from rowan
Making a loose swing from alder
Rowing swing, arriving swing
Rowing swing to the land
Where roosters eat gold
Roosters gold, chickens silver
Baskets green, silver.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Open wide the broad doors  
Open wider the gates  
Let the Kadrisants in  
Kadri has come from afar  
Over this deep bog  
Over this wide plain  
Over this nimble field  
Around the whole round world  
Kadri brings luck to the herd  
Brings harvest luck to the yard  
Brings child luck under the table  
Kadri’s fingers are freezing  
Kadri’s toes are aching  
Grandmother was an old lady  
Grandfather was an old man  
Rise up from the bed  
Unfold the broad sheets  
Smooth out the fine pillows  
Take the keys from the hook  
With a crooked hook from the ear  
Quickly go to the cellar  
Quickly go to the chamber  
Look for a share for Kadri  
Try to find something for Kadri  
Find some meat for Kadri  
A plate of white peas  
For the barn’s master  
For the barn’s mistress  
To feed, to water  
To send off well  
May it, may it, I curse  
May your wives die  
May your pigs die  
May the hens lay in the well  
May the horse drown in the river  
One today, two tomorrow  
And the day after tomorrow ten pieces.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Do not ask me for eggs  
The hen stayed home to lay eggs  
The rooster stayed to crow

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, gentle brothers,  
See where they have made the swing,  
Made the swing by the shore,  
On the edge of five waters.  
They wanted to drown the girl,  
Our girls are too clever,  
Too clever, cunning,  
They went to adorn themselves,  
To watch their own shadows,  
Whose ornament is beautiful,  
Whose cheek is red,  
Whose shadow is whiter,  
Leenu’s ornament is beautiful,  
Mari’s shadow is whiter.  
Oh, you crazy swing smiths,  
Oh, you crazy and foolish,  
See where they have made the swing,  
Made the swing between the village,  
On the boys’ stick fields,  
On the guests’ resting places,  
On the village women’s laughing grounds.  
My brother is making a new swing,  
A new swing, a proud swing,  
You can’t get out from below,  
You can’t slip out from above,  
No little silk bird inside,  
No copper ring in between.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I heard swinging on the swing  
Singing in the swing barn  
I was listening at home  
In the room, in front of the room  
Behind the pile, by the rowan tree  
At the gate, by the maple  
I had to come earlier  
Earlier I put the family to sleep  
I brought the old to the beds  
I laid the old in the beds  
The children in wide linen  
In fine pillows  
Then I must come  
My cat is multicolored  
My cat the gravekeeper  
Watch over my grave  
I am going to the village swing  
To demand young men  
Red-headed lads  
Curly-haired handsome boys

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come to Jaan's fire  
Peeter to the sunshine  
Jaan's fire was beautiful  
Peeter's shine was red  
If Jaan could have given more time  
He would have done it earlier  
The rye would have been covered with dew  
The oats put into fine flour  
The barley for beer malt

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Niece, young maiden  
Young maiden, young girl  
Blow the fire in the room  
Bellows, fire on the hearth  
If there is no groom in the family  
Take a dish and line it up  
Again, blow the fire in the room  
Bellows, fire on the hearth  
If there is no groom in the family  
Pull the beam from the room ceiling  
Again, blow the fire in the room  
If there is no beam in the room ceiling  
Take the ring from under the bed  
If there is no ring under the bed  
Pull the beam from the room ceiling  
Bring my horse to the room  
Light its tail on fire  
Keep blowing the fire in the room.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come to the swing, village girls,  
Come to the fire of Jaanipäev  
Come to keep the fire alive  
Bring chickens, bring eggs  
Bring birds with webbed feet  
Bring capercaillies with their wings spread  
Come to keep the fire alive  

The fire roams the buildings  
A spark flies into the walls  
Then the girls came for fun  
The maidens for cheerful singing  
The maidens swung onto the swing  
The maidens swung, their wreaths gleaming  
But the men looked down into the forest  
The boys down by the river  
If there were a gun, they would shoot a maiden  
If there were a net, they would catch a maiden  
If there were a trap, they would seize her  

Then I realized, I answered back  
I am not to be hunted by a gun  
Nor captured by a net  
Nor caught in a trap  
A wolf can be hunted by a gun  
A fish caught with a net  
Caught in the trap.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come to the swing, village girls,  
Come to the fire of Jaanipäev  
Come to keep the fire alive  
Bring chickens, bring eggs  
Bring birds with webbed feet  
Bring capercaillies with their wings spread  
Come to keep the fire alive  

The fire roams the buildings  
A spark flies into the walls  
Then the girls came for fun  
The maidens for cheerful singing  
The maidens swung onto the swing  
The maidens swung, their wreaths gleaming  
But the men looked down into the forest  
The boys down by the river  
If there were a gun, they would shoot a maiden  
If there were a net, they would catch a maiden  
If there were a trap, they would seize her  

Then I realized, I answered back  
I am not to be hunted by a gun  
Nor captured by a net  
Nor caught in a trap  
A wolf can be hunted by a gun  
A fish caught with a net  
Caught in the trap.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let's go from Jaan to the fire
Come to keep the fire
Swing smiths, dung smiths
Tell me, where have they made the swing
Made the swing far away
In the land of guests
In the village boys' stick-fighting land
Come to swing the swing
To sing at the swinging bench
Bring eggs, bring chickens
Bring birds, waterfowls
Bring ducks from the pond
Bring ducks in pairs
The music will come in a dance tune

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Master, little master,  
mistress, little mistress  
Take this, Kadris  
Kadris have come from far away  
Kadri’s nails are freezing  
Kadri’s toes are aching.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Now the time of St. Martin's Day is here  
ai trulla-li trulla-li  
the Martin children are full of joy  
pim pom pom and pim pom pom  
that was our carnival  
that was our carnival, carnival, carnival

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Master, little master,  
Mistress, little mistress,  
Come greet the Kadris,  
The Kadris have come from far away,  
Kadri's fingers are cold,  
Kadri's toes are aching.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let's go to Jaan's fire,  
Come to keep the fire  
The fire casts into the building  
It smokes into the vodka chamber

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Row strength, strength of strength  
So that I shine on the shore,  
I shine on the shore, I pay on the shore  
If I do not even shine  
Then I shine the wreath on my head.  
If the wreath does not shine on my head  
Then I shine the wreath's ribbon  
If the wreath's ribbon does not shine  
Then I have jingling beads on my neck  
If the beads do not jingle on my neck  
Then I row a brooch on my chest.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Kalendrilaulud
translation:
If I were in charge  
And my doing came second  
I would make gentlemen oxen  
Young ladies wear caps on their heads  
I myself would start plowing  
Go plowing to the village  
Furrow under the meadow  
Strong oxen in front  
Plows following behind  
I went under the village swing  
What did I find under the swing?  
I found a flea swinging  
Making the midwife’s voice  
I sat on the flea’s belly  
On the midwife’s lap  
I went back under the swing  
Men were watching from the forest  
Boys were kneeling in the barn  
If these were men in my power  
I’d first put beads on the mother’s neck  
Before putting them on that maiden’s neck.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Kalendrilaulud
translation:
If I were in charge  
And my doing came second  
I would make gentlemen oxen  
Young ladies wear caps on their heads  
I myself would start plowing  
Go plowing to the village  
Furrow under the meadow  
Strong oxen in front  
Plows following behind  
I went under the village swing  
What did I find under the swing?  
I found a flea swinging  
Making the midwife’s voice  
I sat on the flea’s belly  
On the midwife’s lap  
I went back under the swing  
Men were watching from the forest  
Boys were kneeling in the barn

I’d first put beads on the mother’s neck  
Before putting them on that maiden’s neck.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
To the Attüma family father,
To the Attüma family mother,
Here may rise the dark-haired
Aunt’s luck,
Behind the wall the old man’s luck,
The family son’s bride luck,
To the Attüma old man
Who takes the children to the market.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Go look to the high place  
To the high place, to the distant place  
So that I shine over the wide land  
If I do not even shine myself  
My face shines red  
A ring gleams even from the finger  
Another from the other hand  
A third from the golden hairdo.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come to rock, village ladies,  
Bring chickens, bring eggs,  
Bring ducks from the pond.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Why do you creak our swing  
Or why do you groan our cradle  
The swing creaks from the glove-mother  
The cradle groans from the gift-mother  
The outer cover is tied with a ribbon  
Wait-wait swing, no-no swing  
Let my brother take a wife  
Another brother a different wife  
The third is going to the bridal path  
Then the swing will creak from the glove-mother,  
The base boards from the gift-mother  
The outer cover tied with a ribbon  
On moss, silk ribbons.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Why do you creak our swings  
Or why do you groan our father-in-law  
The swing creaks at the glove-maker's  
The base board at the gift-giver's  
The top beam with reddish ribbons  
Wait-wait-wait-wait, no-no-no-no  
Let my brother take a wife  
Another brother take another wife  
The third brother goes to ask for a bride  
The fourth to catch a maiden  
Then the swing will creak at the glove-maker's  
The base boards at the gift-giver's  
The top beam with reddish ribbons

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Take the swing to the cellar  
Carry the swing to the room  
Grain barns to the barnyard gate

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Hello, hello, family,
Hello, family mothers,
Hello, family widows,
Do you allow me to come in,
To roam around the yard?
To set the herring on the pillar,
To spin straw into yarn,
To give a share to Kadri,
To go dancing to the cellar,
To go dancing to the room,
Across the yard to the neighbors,
To the living old mothers,
To the neighbors' cabbage sheds,
To give a little share.
The Kadris have come from afar,
Across the swamp, through the slippery,
Kadri’s servants without jackets,
Kadri’s children without coats,
Kadri herself without a belt,
The wreath was without a ribbon on her head,
The skirt worn without a belt,
The braided ribbons missing from her hand.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation: Mart was on a swamp trip.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing, swing, village girls  
Bring the hens, collect the eggs,  
Bring the ducks with long necks  
Bring the birds with webbed feet

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the little Martins in! -  
May I come inside?  
To ask for oats  
To hang herring by the wall  
To tie a chick to the hen  
Maybe there are gray ones in the room  
Flax and linen on the floor  
Into the room I cast barley luck  
In front of the wall, rye luck  
For the yard, oat luck

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the Kadris in  
Kadri's nails are cold  
Kadri's toes ache  
The Kadris have come from afar,  
Over the marsh, over the slippery path  
Kadri has a birch horse  
A spruce sleigh  
A rowan whip  
Kadri does not want the lamb  
Kadri wants a little fleece for it  
If the louse does not fill the fleece  
Pinch a little bit.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I came to swing on the swing,  
To try the village swing.  
Will this swing carry me  
If it doesn't, let it go. -  
My brother is making a new swing,  
A new swing, a colorful swing. -  
I do know my brother's swing,  
My brother's swing was written  
With a hazel twig,  
Fastened with alder twigs,  
Written on a crane's beak,  
It won’t let a frog crawl underneath,  
Nor a mouse sneak on top,  
Nor a silk bird inside,  
Nor a white finch in between.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Go high on our swing,  
Go high, from the rushing river,  
So that I can be seen from afar,  
Be seen, pay to be seen. –  
My coat shines in Kuuramaa,  
To Kuivastu in Viirand.  
My wreath shines in Pärnumaa,  
The wreath's ribbons in Saksamaa. –  
The swing was brought from afar,  
The swing was brought from Kilvandu,  
The shafts were brought from Võivere,  
The poles from Sinisalu,  
The boards from Laiuse,  
The beams from Ammuta. –  
Pulling were the brother's oxen,  
The brother's oxen, the brother's slaves,  
The brother-purchased steeds. –  
The swing is paid by the spotted ox,  
The shafts paid by the fertile horse,  
The beams all by the good mare,  
The poles a hundred coins.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Ten girls in the village,
Nine across the street,
Eight on the herd paths,
They must not come to the swing,
They fear the goldwearing away.
They fear the copper sinking.
Ivies withering.
Money pots running out. -
Let our gold wear away
Let our copper sink
We have goldsmiths at home
Tinsmiths on the road
Coppersmiths at dawn.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing-makers, dear brothers,  
Do not take offense,  
Nor be upset.  
For I have come under your swing  
To swing your swing,  
To sing the swing plank.  
Your swing swings with glove ropes,  
The posts with silk thread ropes,  
The baseboard wants to be supported,  
The top layer wants to be tied.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Flax slide, long fiber,  
Flax for the slider  
Locks for the fire starter  
Burrs for the leader.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Sisters, little souls,
How many girls are in the village?
Ten girls in the village.
Nine across the street.
They must not come to swing,
They fear gold will wear out,
They fear copper will sink,
Silver buttons will straighten,
Money pots will run out.
Your gold does not wear out here
Nor does your copper sink here
Your silver straightens.
Here the boys' gold wears out
Here the boys' copper sinks
The boys' silver straightens;
We have goldsmiths at home,
We have coppersmiths at the gate,
We have silversmiths in the yard,
We have tinsmiths by the road.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Let me down, I would ask  
If you don't let me, don't ask: -  
Then I will swing until evening  
I swing until mid-morning  
In the village, the old woman milks the cow  
In the village, the woman drives the herd home  
In the village, the spruce draws milk  
In the village, the brother feeds the calf.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
I come to your swing  
To swing your swing.  
Is this a swing from the cherry trees,  
From the sweet berry trees?  
Come to our swing  
To swing our swing.  
Our swing is from the cherry trees,  
From the sweet berry trees,  
From the beautiful apple trees,  
From the blue currant bushes. –  
Our village smells of wallflower,  
The village meadows of sulfurwort,  
The streets of thyme,  
The garden bed of pepperwort.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
I come to your swing  
To swing your swing.  
Is this a swing from the cherry trees,  
From the sweet berry trees?  
Come to our swing  
To swing our swing.  
Our swing is from the cherry trees,  
From the sweet berry trees,  
From the beautiful apple trees,  
From the blue currant bushes. –  
Our village smells of wallflower,  
The village meadows of sulfurwort,  
The streets of thyme,  
The garden bed of pepperwort.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing maker brothers
Do not ask me for eggs
The hen stayed home to lay eggs. -
Row the swing, pull the swing
The swing was brought from afar
The swing was brought from Viljandi
The supports were brought from Ambla
The swing boards from Laiuse
The posts from Sinisalu
The rods brought from Puivere. -
Tuomas had a thousand horses
Suamas had a hundred swift ones
In Vidama were the brother’s oxen
The brother’s oxen, the brother’s servants
The brother’s purchased horses. -

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
What I promise to the warrior  
To the one rolling over the shaft  
Behind the door of this yard  
The field was pentagonal  
In each corner five roots  
In each root five shoots  
In each shoot five heads  
In each head five kernels  
This I promise to the warrior  
To the one rolling over the shaft  
Let me say it again  
Let me sing it back again  
To the second I drive a young maiden  
To the second a taller, finest one  
The youngest among them all  
This I drive to the second

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, kind brothers  
Do not swing me out of anger  
Do not sway me with wickedness  
I am an angry rooster  
I am a show-off who likes to startle  
If I fall, who will pay me  
My count is paid by a pair of oxen  
Another count by another pair  
My brooch is paid by a riding horse  
My sash is paid by Preedi manor.  
I do not have my own under the swing  
My own man is not under the table  
Who will take the stones from under the swing? -  
Mine is far away  
My man is in the seawater  
There he swings in captivity  
Under the keel’s laths. -

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Where has our swing been brought from?  
Where has it been brought from, where has it been obtained?  
Who is bringing our swing,  
Who is bringing it, who is receiving it?  
Toomas is bringing, Sander is receiving.  
The beams were brought from Arjamaa,  
The shaft was brought from Võhmuta,  
The steps from Sinisalu.  
So a new swing was made,  
Will this swing carry us?  
If it does not carry, let it perish.  
Swing smiths, eldest brothers,  
Bring fire, burn the swing,  
Bring water, soak the swing,  
You yourself, hew the swing frame.  
Come, women of the swing village,  
Bring chickens, bring eggs,  
Bring vessels for weaving,  
Bring a pair of ducks;  
The hen stayed at home to lay eggs,  
The rooster stayed at the yard crowing.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Village girls as sisters,  
Village boys, half brothers,  
Come to try the swing;  
Why do you darken the home,  
Swaying by the garden edge,  
Afraid your gold will wear away,  
Afraid your copper will sink,  
Your beauty fade?  
In the city, city maiden,  
Your gold wears out in the city,  
Your copper sinks in the city,  
In the country your beauty fades.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Where has this Jaan come from?  
Already a little rye head is visible  
Barley ears are bending low,  
Oats are covering the ground.  
Wheat would come from the ear.  
Peas to lentils and lupines  
Had been made earlier,  
Rye cut into bundles,  
Barley placed in ricks,  
Oats in sheaf staves,

label: Looduslaulud  ; Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Up to the high hill,  
High hill, distant hill,  
What did I see from the high hill?  
There were three forests nearby:  
One was a golden spruce forest,  
Another a precious birch forest,  
The third a beautiful apple tree;  
The one that was the golden spruce forest  
Is the forest of young men.  
The one that was the precious birch forest  
Is the forest of young women.  
What is the beautiful apple tree?  
That is of the young maidens.  

Up to the high hill,  
High hill, distant hill,  
What did I see from the distant hill?  
From the high hill, from the distant hill?  
There were three lakes nearby:  
One was the lake of strong spirits,  
Another was the red beer,  
The third was of sweet mead.  
What is the lake of strong spirits?  
That is the lake of young men.  
What was the red beer?  
That was the lake of young women.  
What was the sweet mead?  
That was the lake of young maidens.  

Up to the high hill,  
High hill, distant hill!  
What did I see from the high hill  
From the high hill, from the distant hill?  
There were three fields nearby:  
One was an oat field,  
Another was a rye field full of weeds,  
The third was a beautiful wheat field.  
What was the precious oat field?  
That was the field of young men.  
What was the rye field full of weeds?  
That was the field of young women.  
What was the beautiful wheat field?  
That was the field of young maidens.  

Up to the high hill,  
High hill, distant hill.  
What did I see from the high hill?  
There were three swings nearby:  
One was with blue silks,  
Another with red ribbons,  
The third with golden cords.  
What was with the blue silks?  
That is the swing of young women.  
What was with the red ribbons?  
That is the swing of young maidens.  
What was with the golden cords?  
That is the swing of young men.  

Where was the swing brought from?  
The swing was brought from Kilvandu,  
The frame from Ambla,  
The posts from Sinisalu.  
I swing, I praise the swing,  
I sing, I honor the feast:  
Oh my golden swingers,  
Do not swing me angrily,  
Do not frighten me cruelly;  
I am harsh in scolding,  
Like a whip on a nettle leaf,  
Like the birch leaf to be lively,  
Like the oak leaf for dancing,  
Like the aspen leaf to tremble.  

If I fall, who will pay?  
My cheek is worth a pair of oxen,  
Another cheek another pair,  
My ear is worth a horse’s bridle,  
Another ear another bridle,  
My eyes worth silk leaves of wild garlic,  
Another eye another leaf,  
My nose worth a young stallion.  
I do not have a brother under the swing,  
Nor a sister’s child under the table,  
Large stones are under the swing,  
A thick one is under the table.  
I fall onto the stones with my eyes,  
I strike the cheeks of a fellow fighter.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Up to the high hill,  
High hill, distant hill,  
What did I see from the high hill?  
There were three forests nearby:  
One was a golden spruce forest,

The one that was the golden spruce forest

The one that was the precious birch forest

I sing, I honor the feast:  
Oh my golden swingers,  
Do not swing me angrily,  
Do not frighten me cruelly;  
I am harsh in scolding,  
Like a whip on a nettle leaf,  
Like the birch leaf to be lively,  
Like the oak leaf for dancing,  
Like the aspen leaf to tremble.

My cheek is worth a pair of oxen,  
Another cheek another pair,  
My ear is worth a horse’s bridle,  
Another ear another bridle,  
My eyes worth silk leaves of wild garlic,  
Another eye another leaf,  
My nose worth a young stallion.  
I do not have a brother under the swing,  
Nor a sister’s child under the table,  
Large stones are under the swing,  
A thick one is under the table.  
I fall onto the stones with my eyes,  
I strike the cheeks of a fellow fighter.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Who was swinging on the swing for us?  
Kerp  
The lizard was for war time  
The spider was for time.  
I hit the kerbu on the side  
Grasshopper  
I play until evening  
I swing until midday,  
In the village, the alder milks the cow  
The village birch herds the cattle  
The maple tree chases the calf  
The village spruce squeezes milk.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Curly-Kadri maiden  
Let the little Kadris in  
Take in the poor children  
Kadri has come from afar  
Over the marsh through the slippery  
Over long Piibe  
Kadri’s horse is a little birch birch  
Kadri’s whip is rowanberry  
Kadri’s sled is rough twig  
Kadri’s cap is loose-fibered  
Kadri’s fingers are freezing  
Toes causing pain  
Old grandmother of the household  
Little one of the household room  
Blow the fire in the room  
Make the fire blaze fiercely  
If the fire burns in the house  
Pull the smoke to the ceiling  
If the fire burns to the ceiling  
Pull the sparks down from the chimney  
If the sparks go to the chimney  
Pull the smoke from under the eaves  
If the smoke goes to the eaves  
Then bring the apple into the room  
Light the tail on fire  
The fire must be inside the room. -  
Old grandmother of the household  
Get up from the bed  
From under the pillows  
Between the white linens  
Take the key from the hook  
Even from the edge of the golden loaf  
Go to the cabbage chamber  
Go to the cellar  
Step on the way to the granary  
Look for a share for Kadri  
Try to give Kadri a chicken  
Want to give a drum  
To the living old grandmother  
Clatter them in the cabbage shed  
Run them in the sausage shed  
Twist them in the twisted shed  
Old grandmother of the household  
Pluck wool, pluck wool!  
If the louse doesn’t fill the wool  
Cut the wool off the neck too  
Pluck from the brush  
Take the brush in half. -  
What I wish for the housemother:  
Fill the barn with sheep  
Fill the straw house with pigs  
Manure  
What I wish for the housefather:  
You need to fill the horse stable  
What I wish for the household daughters:  
Fill the henhouse with chickens.  
What I wish for the household son:  
A dozen puppies for the dog  
All to be rabbit dogs. -  
Kadri sows millet  
Takes care of barley  
In I cast grain luck  
Good luck for the chickens  
Good luck under the linens. -  
- - - - - - - - -

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swingers, droppers  
Swayers over the edge.  
Do not swing with malice,  
Do not sway with wickedness,  
Do not pull angrily.  
If you swing with malice,  
Then I will pay a wicked wage,  
I will charge the wicked a price,  
I will throw back in anger,  
If I fall, who will pay?  
If I fall on a rocky eyebrow,  
On a wide cheekbone.  
My eyebrow costs six rubles,  
The cheekbone costs a pair of oxen,  
Eyewitness four tacks,  
I myself still pay a lot of money.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Row, row, our swing,  
Row, row, with strength and ease.  
Rowers are slender maidens,  
Men of strength are weaklings.  
What do I give to the rower:  
A young white maiden from the county,  
A fine young maiden,  
The most splendid among others,  
The straightest among others,  
The whitest among others,  
I give her to the rower.  
What do I assign to the second?  
A long apron from the back of the room:  
A wide field behind the barn,  
A triangular field.  
In each corner three stalks,  
In each stalk three sheaves,  
In each sheaf three bundles,  
On each bundle three heads,  
I assign this to the second.  
What do I give to the third?  
A horse as tall as a stack of firewood:  
A saddle as tall as a sauna,  
Hair rising from the top of the room,  
A fence surrounding the stack.  
A saddle put on the sauna top.  
Stepping on the saddle from above,  
I give this to the third.  
What do I name the fourth?  
Let the merchant come from the village,  
The town boy near the gate,  
The shop boy come down to the yard.  
I buy a knife with a meadow pattern:  
A document behind the head,  
A letter with a cross on the sheath.  
This sheath is of various kinds:  
Beautiful on one side,  
Reddish on the other side,  
Rosy from Sweden,  
Violin-like from Estonia,  
Toothed from Harju,  
Notched from Narva,  
Otherwise patterned from Moa,  
I name this for the fourth.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let me try the swing seat,  
Does the swing run on a line?  
Does it run along the horse's track,  
The old oxen's furrow,  
The young ox's teaching,  
The young man's hay cart,  
I praise the swing seat:  
The swing is praised by the keel-rider.  
The keel-rider praises the knife,  
The knife praises the good smith,  
The smith praises the good iron,  
The iron praises the good ship,  
The ship praises the good water,  
The water praises the good wind,  
The wind praises the good time,  
The time praises the guide,  
But I praise the swing seat.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Where were our swings brought from?  
Where were they brought from, where were they obtained:  
Swings were brought from the village of Kei,  
Kei from the household of Pei,  
Boards were brought from Laiuselt,  
Beams were brought from Ammutasta,  
A pole was brought from Võhmutasta,  
Rods were brought from Puiveresta,  
Saddles from Sinisalusta.  
Who was bringing our swings;  
Who was bringing, who was receiving.  
Toomas was bringing, Sander receiving:  
Toomas had a thousand staves,  
Sander had a hundred rods,  
Five were for guidance ahead:  
Six were ears from the ear field,  
Seven were clear blueberry fields,  
Eight were Kalev's ears,  
Nine were good staves,  
Ten were plowing marks.  
Where were then our swings taken:  
Immediately taken to Kubja's yard,  
Put down in Mardi's yard.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
do not ask me for eggs.  
The hen stayed for the nest’s eggs,  
The rooster stayed crowing by the ear,  
Let me come a second time:  
Then I will bring eggs, then I will bring hens.  
Then I will bring geese with gray necks,  
Then I will bring birds with webbed feet,  
Then I will bring ducks in pairs,  
Of course, the eggs will be in a row.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Go swing on the high one.  
On the high, on the distant one.  
So that I can be seen from afar,  
I am very visible, I am very valued.  
If I myself am visible,  
I am seen from the top of the head.  
A ring lives on a finger,  
Another on the other hand,  
The third is gold on the thumb,  
The fourth is on the maiden’s finger,  
The fifth is copper on the little toe.  
My head appears above the forest:  
My feet seem under the forest,  
My body in the middle of the forest,  
The spirit asks the echoes,  
“Is that the maiden under the swing,  
What can be seen above the forest there?”  
I hear and answer back:  
Little spirit boy.  
That is not meant for you,  
That girl belongs to the farmer’s son,  
Her own dear boy.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing-makers, dear brothers,  
Let me touch the swing frame,  
I admire the songboard.  
Who made our swings:  
Was the swing made by a brother?  
Brother made it, brother made it,  
Or was it planted by the father-in-law,  
Or cut by the husband.  
The swing was not made by a brother.  
Brother made it, brother made it,  
It was not planted by the father-in-law,  
Nor cut by the husband.  
Craftsmen made the swing,  
Helpful spirits put the shafts,  
Boys made the sticks.  
Family sons made the soft boards.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
What creaks our swing?  
Or what hollow sounds our voice?  
The swing creaks in the glove shed:  
The upper boards are tied with ribbons.  
Wait, wait, swing, no, no swing,  
Let the first brother take a girl,  
The second brother another girl,  
The third brother courts the lady.  
Then the swing can creak,  
The baseboards can give way.  
The upper boards are tied with ribbons.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I heard swings swinging,  
Singing loudly in the swing shed,  
I went under the village swing.  
What I found under the swing:  
Black skirts from Mundel,  
From Annel two-year ones,  
From Liisu too threadbare,  
From Mari chronic to the hem.  
Alone I stand on the envelope,  
Alone I stand on the skirt,  
I walk alone on the cuffs,  
Just the right half-cloak.  
As long as the coats are crowned,  
The shoulder seams are sewn,  
The brass coats are mended.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Dear gentle mother,  
Give me the garden key,  
Give me the coffin key,  
Lend me the attic key.  
I am going to decorate the west,  
To adorn Kerstu,  
I put on a silk shirt,  
On my head I place a red wreath,  
On my back a blue skirt.  
Sleeves rolled up,  
Silk stockings on my legs,  
New closed shoes on my feet,  
Then I go to church,  
Then the boys' eyes will burn.  
If those wild wolf eyes,  
Spring goat eyes,  
Winter hare eyes,  
Into my golden grove,  
Onto the green field.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
Please lower it down,  
If you do not, I will not ask.  
I will swing until evening,  
Sing until broad daylight,  
Swing until midmorning. -  
The village grandmother milks the cow,  
The village host sends the herd.  
The white brother waters the calves.  
If the grandmother does not milk the cow,  
If the host does not send the herd,  
Then the village grandmother will milk the cow,  
The village spruce tree yields milk,  
The village birch sends the herd,  
The whitehead waters the calf.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I walk along Jüri's river,
Step on Mardi's gray moss.
Jüri did not drink from the river,
Did not taste Mardi's gray moss.
Jüri drinks, Jüri gambles,
Jüri gives to others too,
Gifts to the parish as well.
Jüri has the keys to heaven in his pocket,
Jüri has the keys to hell in his belly.
Jüri opens the gate of heaven,
Throws away the gate of hell.
Jüri wanted to go to heaven,
But had to sneak into hell instead.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I went under the village swing,  
What did I find under the swing:  
I found Kerpu under the swing,  
Hämelik under the cradle,  
The big mosquito under the dock.  
I hit Kerpu on the side,  
Hämelik on the tail,  
Mosquito on the lower leg bone:  
Kerpu, go home to plow,  
Hämelik, go to the threshing floor,  
Mosquito, go set the plow.  
Kerpu went to Sweden to bring grass,  
For his sick side,  
Looked for the path of the list,  
Wanted to run far away,  
Jump over the sea.  
But little brother fell into the water,  
Could not jump over the water! –  
Had to return unwillingly.  
Could not bring grass from Sweden  
For his sick wound.  
Then the little creature promised to repay,  
Cursed in secret words:  
Never forget the person,  
Do not grant power when sleeping,  
Kerpu did not go to plow,  
But the plowman’s hat went,  
The furrow opener’s shirt,  
In place of the field cultivator,  
Fled between the pillows.  
Took the harvest secretly,  
Got silly, even talked.  
Hämelik remained tailless!  
Did not go to threshing with the tail,  
But went to the edge of the threshing floor.  
There made a very clever cradle,  
Started between the windows,  
Tied it to the wall beam,  
In the cradle, then, the tailless master.  
Mosquito, the tailless German:  
Did not go to set the plow,  
But flew sideways to the field,  
To the eyes of the plow setter,  
Looked for a place to rest,  
Stole harvest from the plow setter.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let me try the new swing,  
whether it can run,  
whether it can be made ready,  
whether it runs a horse race  
or goes at a horse's pace,  
drives oxen to haul,  
young men’s haystack,  
farm boys’ barley sowing.  
If it cannot run new,  
cannot be made ready,  
then bring the axe, dear brother,  
then bring the axe to chop the swing,  
then bring the knife to carve the swing,  
then bring the needle to sew the swing.  
I hear, I hear again,  
the swing is not for chopping with an axe,  
nor for sewing with a needle,  
nor for beating in the barn,  
the swing is paid for by a spotted ox,  
the swing is for a steed in the stable,  
posts a hundred without money,  
supports a thousand tucks,  
the swing was brought from far away,  
from far and high away  
around the world, all around,  
behind the red sky  
between five rainbows.  
Where was our swing taken,  
it was immediately taken to the miller’s yard,  
behind the gates of the miller,  
immediately the miller’s wives praised it,  
daughters of the miller praised it.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let's go to the Jaaniste fire,  
To keep the Jaanituli,  
Bright  
The fire feeds on the buildings,  
The brightness creeps onto the roofs.  
Jaani has come early to us,  
Came early, came too soon,  
Do not start haymaking before,  
To make the rye ears close together,  
To roll into a sheaf,  
Do not let the Jaani frost come,  
Frost brings many illnesses.  
Do not let the Jaani with caraway come,  
Caraway brings a heap of grain,  
Come, Jaani with the boat's roof,  
The boat brings better grain,  
Come, Jaani with the polecat,  
The polecat brings strong grain.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Töölaulud  ; Murelaulud  ; Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Family daughter, young maiden,  
Farm daughter, little plant,  
If you care to be alert  
And you want to be wise,  
Sweep the room, the glass, the table,  
Dust under the windows,  
Turn the front of the cellar,  
Wash the jugs in the pantry,  
Bring them to the corner of the room,  
Buckets to the kitchen roof,  
Buckets to the well,  
The man comes to the village bucket boy,  
An old man to the young man,  
He thinks of the moon shining,  
He thinks of the holiday shining,  
Our ladle handles glowed,  
Our shining pewter plates,  
Our white bucket plates,  
Our jugs in the pantry,  
Bring to the corner of the room,  
Buckets to the kitchen roof,  
Family daughter, young maiden,  
Farm daughter, little plant,  
When she hears the holidays coming,  
You start driving the cows,  
Easter sliding,  
The moon pushes the curls,  
The holiday pushes the pearl maiden,  
The year dresses itself,  
Starts asking the maidens,  
Starts asking the men,  
Argues with the elders,  
Where is my cloak, where is my brooch,  
Where are my embroidered handkerchiefs,  
Where is my belt with maple embroidery,  
Where are my stockings with Finnish embroidery,  
Shoes with a cross-stone pattern,  
Mother, dear woman,  
Give me the barn key,  
Give me the chest key,  
I am going to decorate the barn,  
Smooth my head on the kerchief,  
I will put gold underneath, silver on top,  
In between I will put white ribbons,  
A skirt with eyelets on,  
Belt with maple embroidery,  
Stockings with Finnish embroidery,  
Shoes with a cross-stone pattern,  
Then I go to the village swing,  
Village girls, young brothers,  
Guessing, pondering,  
Whose beautiful lady,  
Whose handsome maiden,  
The town's beautiful lady,  
Paide's fair maiden,  
From whom are you asking a servant,  
From whom a poor one disputes,  
You ask a servant,  
Argue with the arm,  
Have you done nails for the village,  
Have the arms worked hard,  
Mother, dear woman,  
Some got a place by the bread oven,  
Others found a place by the bread oven,  
You put the cart on the distant road,  
Distant road for the weaver,  
To the town for the turner,  
To the countryside for the miller,  
Mother, dear woman,  
I did not go to the miller,  
The miller's stone roars,  
I did not go to the weaver,  
The weaver's cloth rustles,  
I did not go to the smith either,  
Blacksmith's children are coal-black,  
Smoky blacksmith's children,  
I want clean wood workers,  
Lovers who read the beginnings,  
A wide splitter of firewood,  
Someone who pees a long fart,  
A little fart for me,  
A little fart to chase away,  
Surely we have fast children,  
Surely we have busy workers,  
Let's set the children on fire,  
To burn the rendered fat.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Where were our swings brought from,  
Where were they brought from, where were they obtained?  
The swing was brought from Kei village,  
From Kei village, from Pai village,  
The rails were brought from Ammutaste,  
The beams were brought from Puiveraste,
