The ropes were brought from Võhmutaste,  
The baseboards from Laiuse,  
The posts from Sinisalu.  

Who was bringing our swing,  
Who was bringing it, who was receiving it?  
Toomas was bringing, Sander was receiving,  
Joining with a marriage knot.  

Toomas was buying a thousand,  
Receiving was a hundred bundles,  
Weaving were the brothers' yarns.  

What do I promise the bringer!  
A cow for milking,  
An ox for working.  

From the hilltop he lifts the hair,  
From the path he steps on the back,  
From the barn he sets the saddles.  

What then do I promise the other?  
A tall maiden from the center of the village,  
A young fair maiden from the parish,  
Young maiden from the other side,  
The tallest, the slenderest,  
The fairest from the parish,  
The plumpest maiden from the raft.  

What do I gather for the third?  
Let the merchant also come to the village,  
Let the shop boy come down to the yard,  
Then I bring a knife with a meadow inscription,  
A head scarf with a forest inscription,  
A scabbard with a cross inscription.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Family daughter, a maiden  
Farm daughter, a little plant  
Blow, fire, into the room  
Fan the embers of the fire  
If you don’t have a mirror in the house  
Pull splinters even from the wall  
If you don’t have splinters in the wall  
Take a chip from the ceiling beam  
If there’s no chip in the ceiling beam  
Take bark from the edge of the eaves  
If there’s no bark at the edge of the eaves  
Bring my apple into the room  
Light the wick to burn  
The fire must be in the room  
The fire must blaze openly  
The fire in the room must not go out  
The spark must reach the bed’s side  
A young woman doesn’t dress up  
She doesn’t prepare her apples for the basket  
I’m looking for a child’s lament  
The sound of weak crying.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Family daughter, a maiden  
Farm daughter, a little plant  
Blow, fire, into the room  
Fan the embers of the fire  
If you don’t have a mirror in the house  
Pull splinters even from the wall  
If you don’t have splinters in the wall  
Take a chip from the ceiling beam  
If there’s no chip in the ceiling beam  
Take bark from the edge of the eaves  
If there’s no bark at the edge of the eaves  
Bring my apple into the room  
Light the wick to burn  
The fire must be in the room  
The fire must blaze openly  
The fire in the room must not go out  
The spark must reach the bed’s side  
A young woman doesn’t dress up  
She doesn’t prepare her apples for the basket  
I’m looking for a child’s lament  
The sound of weak crying.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come to the swing, village girls  
Bring chickens, bring eggs  
Bring geese for hatching  
Bring ducks for brooding  
Ten girls in the village  
Nine across the street  
The tenth, the smith's daughter of the village  
They were allowed to come to the swing  
Afraid the gold would wear out  
The silver beads fade away  
The bright eyes grow pale  
There were many swinging on the swing  
Many swaying on the swing’s beam  
There are no singers on the swing  
No ones singing on the swing plank  
I praise the swing frame  
May the swing-makers be healthy  
Who made the swing to the berry hill  
Among the blue flowers  
In the red alder growing  
Between the beautiful birches  
Kind swing-makers, dear brothers  
Please let it down for me  
If you don’t let it down, I won’t ask  
Then I’ll sway till evening  
Swing till midday  
Surely then the young woman will milk the cow  
Surely then the old man will drive the herd  
If the young woman does not milk the cow  
Nor the old man drives the herd  
Surely then the alder will milk the cow  
Surely then the birch will drive the herd  
The maple tree will give water to the calf  
I hear the village swinging  
Singing on the swing plank  
Waiting for the crowd to call out  
Afraid the crowd will shout  
The crowd does not call me out  
Nor does the crowd shout at me.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Mihkel came from heaven’s year  
Over nine rivers  
Under ten cold springs.  
A household girl, a maiden  
Blow the fire in the stove  
The fire roar from the bellows’ breath.  
When the fire is in the stove  
Take the firewood from the shelf.  
When there is firewood on the shelf  
Pull kindling from the barn.  
When there is kindling in the barn  
Take the straw from the hayloft.  
A household grandmother  
A household auntie  
Hop to go to the cellar  
Skip to go to the chamber  
You bring Mihkel’s share  
Two pieces of cabbage from the cellar  
I bring luck to the yard  
Doors full of chickens  
Barns full of lambs  
Straw full in the pigsty.  
These I wish to the head of the household  
These I wish to the mistress of the household  
Dearest greetings to the maiden  
These I wish for the son of the household.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, noble brothers  
Come to the swing, village women  
Bring chickens, bring eggs  
Bring ducks to the footrest  
Bring scoops for the vat,  
Bring birds with webbed feet!  
Do not ask me for eggs  
The hen stayed at home to lay eggs  
The sow stayed by the ear to watch  
The rooster stayed by the root to crow  
Swing smiths, noble brothers.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swinging brothers,  
Do not swing me angrily.  
I am an angry rooster.  
I am a birch leaf ready to jump.  
I am an aspen leaf ready to blow.  
If I fall, my liver will hurt.  
The cheek will hurt from the stick as well,  
The other cheek from the other stick.  
The nose is running with snot,  
The crown covered in golden drops,  
The shoulders swollen,  
The hips aching,  
The legs with new bruises.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Ten girls in the village  
Nine across the street  
They were not allowed to come on the swing  
They were afraid the gold would wear out  
The ornaments would be damaged  
The money pouches would tear.  
But our gold does not wear out  
The ornaments do not get damaged  
The money pouches do not tear  
We have a goldsmith visiting  
At the gate is a coppersmith  
In the street is a tinsmith.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
What are you rocking the swings for  
On the planks nailed down  
Woot, woot, rocking, rocking  
Let my brother take a wife  
The second brother takes another wife  
The third brother has been courting  
The fifth brother has been drinking spirits  
The sixth brother has been announced (engaged)  
The seventh brother has been married  
Then the swing will seal the fate  
The plank will give the gift  
The red ribbons on the handkerchief  
The swing-makers are the brothers.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
The young maiden, the daughter of the family,  
Went to the farm to get dressed up,  
Dressed in silk fabric,  
Put on ribbons,  
A golden chain around her neck,  
A star-shaped pin on her forehead.  
Then she went to the village swing,  
She appeared as radiant as the day,  
Young men were thinking,  
She is our young lady.  
She does not fit on our swing,  
Nor does she belong in our company.  
You are from a noble family,  
From a higher class of people.  
The daughter went home crying,  
The mother was there, the father was there.  
Why are you crying, young daughter?  
Why doesn’t the old woman cry?  
Why doesn’t the father cry?  
I did not fit on the village swing,  
Nor did I belong in their company.  
I was from a noble family,  
From a higher class of people,  
Because I did not fit on their swing.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I heard swinging in the swing  
Rustling in the birch grove  
Blooming on the heath hill.  
I listened at home  
Behind the fence of the yard  
Long rows of ears  
Under wide leaves.  
I waited for the wolf to howl  
I waited for the bear to call  
Neither the wolf howled for me  
Nor did the bear call me.  
The wolf howled  
The bear called.  
I went myself, little one  
You know, little one  
Little seed for the garden  
Little drop of water  
What did I find at the swinging place?  
I found moss at the swinging place  
Lizard’s spit  
Wasp’s sting.  
I struck the moss on the cheek  
The lizard’s eye on the cheek  
The wasp’s sting on the cheek  
The moss went to Sweden to get medicine  
For itself, a healing herb  
For the lizard, an eye herb  
For the wasp, a healing herb.  
Here I stayed myself, swinging  
Swinging, blooming.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I came to see the new one  
The old year’s end  
Can the new one run  
Will it run the ox’s course  
Will it drive the oxen’s wagon  
Young men’s eagerness  
The herdboy’s barley bundles  
If the new one cannot run  
Does not run the ox’s course  
Does not drive the oxen’s wagon  
Then bring a complete swing of logs  
Bring fire to burn the swing  
Let me tell again  
I will sing back again  
The swing is a complete hewing  
No payment of the axe  
No burning coming  
No carving of the new (year)  
The swing has been brought from far away  
From far away, from high places  
Over swamps, over rivers  
Over wet mountains  
Over high fences  
Over long reed beds  
Pulling were the brother’s oxen  
The brother’s oxen, the brother’s servants  
The swing is paid by the colorful oxen  
With smooth small runes  
By fences with full horns.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Juanituld tehakse  
Kodaraida koputatakse  
Vana märssa mängitakse  
Tõrvatõrssa tõmmatakse  
Come quickly, young men  
Come now to keep the fire  
To forbid the bitterness  
To set sparks  
So the fire does not go to the village  
Bitterness does not jump onto the stove  
Fire causes harm in the village  
Bitterness causes damage to the stove  
Juan has three things on the swing  
A beer anchor under the swing  
A vodka bottle on the table  
The fire burns at the tip of the rod  
He wishes for luck for the coming year  
Oh you, Juani, orc-foot  
Orc-foot, cork-eye  
Today you are Juani of our land  
Tomorrow Juani of Germany’s land.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
The flax fiber, long fiber;
Flaxes for the slider,
Tow fibers for the push-backer,
Fleeces for the leader,
Locks for the picker.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, pampered ones:  
Do not ask me for eggs,  
The hen stayed home to lay eggs,  
The box stayed to keep watch,  
The rooster stayed to crow angrily.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Mart comes, staggers Mart,  
Mart leaves, shines Mart.  
Let the dear Marts come inside:  
Mart brings home grain luck,  
From afar brings oat luck,  
Throws luck-barley into the yard,  
Lucky oats for the stove,  
For the house daughter a bundle of herbs.  
Master, little master;  
Rise, rise from your bed,  
Awake at the dim dawn,  
Find some food for Mart,  
Try to find some grain on the heap,  
Bring a piece of raw meat,  
Pour white cream.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I came to your swing  
To swing your swing  
Is your swing made of cherry wood?  
From beautiful apple wood?  
From sweet berry wood?  
From slender currant wood?  
Or is it from white maple?  
Or from tall rowan?  
Or from wild sallow?  
Or from dear birch?  
It is not made of cherry wood,  
Nor from sweet berry wood,  
Nor from slender currant wood,  
It is not from white maple,  
Nor from tall rowan,  
Nor from wild sallow,  
Nor from dear birch,  
But from dry spruce wood.  
Come to us  
To swing our swing.  
Our swing is made of cherry wood,  
From beautiful apple wood,  
From sweet berry wood,  
From slender currant wood.  
It is from white maple,  
From tall rowan,  
From wild sallow,  
From dear birch.  
The maple is in the harness here,  
The sallow is in the beams here,  
The rowan is in the dowels here,  
The berry wood is in the seat boards,  
The currant wood is smooth at the edges,  
The apple wood is strong for the crosspieces,  
The cherry wood is harder for the wedges,  
The beams are tall and slender,  
The supports are strong.  
Where was this swing brought to you,  
Brought to you, made for you?  
It was brought from Suaremaa,  
From a very distant foreign land,  
Across swamp and wetland,  
Across land and sea,  
Across towns near and far,  
Across lands and mountains.  
Thousands were the carriers,  
Hundreds the path makers,  
A thousand men drove the stakes,  
A hundred men worked the paths,  
Until the swing was fully wedged,  
The wedges fitted tightly to the ends.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Christmas Eve long day:  
I am stuffing sausages,  
I am roasting giblets,  
I am preparing black puddings,  
Preparing them, baking in the oven,  
I am brewing strong beer,  
The proud ale for the feast!

label: Improvisatsioonid  ; Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come to the swing, village women,  
Bring chickens, bring eggs,  
Bring ducks in pairs,  
To attract young men  
And to lift the swinger’s spirits.  
Who is swinging the lovely ones here,  
Swaying the gentler ones,  
Caressing the wreathed heads?

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Boys made a swing:  
They made a narrow swing,  
Swing posts narrow,  
The narrow one pulls the clothes,  
The narrow one breaks the cradle!  

They made the swing in the smithy yard,  
Made it on the big hill,  
So that it could be seen from Riga,  
From Paide – visible,  
So that I myself would be visible,  
My face would blush.  

So that I myself would be a leaf, –  
A leaf, my flaxen hair.  
So that I myself would flow,  
Flow my white cuffs.  

Village boys, dashing lads,  
Villagers, lively men,  
Village women, sweet ladies,  
Flax aprons, little birds.  

If you swing me high up,  
Gently swinging?  
Then I will pay well.  

Let me down if I ask,  
Down somewhere, on the head,  
On the drunk head,  
On the messenger's head:  
A ring wears off from the finger too,  
Another on another's hand.  
A third on a golden thumb,  
If you don't let, you don't ask.  

I swing until midday,  
So the village can't sleep,  
The farm stays breathless.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Village women, strong women,
Farm women, wise women.
Come swing on the swing
Does this swing carry us?
If it doesn't carry, then let it go!
Row the swing, pull the swing -
Row the swing over there
So I will appear much brighter
So my wreath's redness will show.
Who made this swing?
The boys made the swing
Helpers put the axes.
Swing makers, strong brothers,
Do not ask me for eggs.
The hen stayed home to lay eggs:
The rooster started to crow
The bailiff started to prepare.
- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I went under the village swing  
To try the village swing  
Will this swing carry me?  
Is this swing made by brother  
Brother-made, bright with light?  
Or is it made by a young lad?  
I surely know brother’s swing  
Brother’s swing moves firmly  
I surely know young lad’s swing  
Young lad’s swing is inscribed  
This swing was not made by brother  
Brother-made, bright with light  
Nor was it made by a young lad  
Town boys made the swing  
Helpers set the supports  
The swing was brought from afar  
The swing’s supports from Harju County  
Main boards from Põltsamaa County  
The swing brought from Türgu County  
Swing’s rope from Vaivera  
Tuomas has a thousand hooks  
Suama has a hundred riders  
Pulling are brother’s bulls  
Swing blacksmiths are kind brothers  
Take the swing to the cellar  
Carry the swing to the chamber  
Supports to the barn attic  
Throw the rope over the fence  
Now it’s time to go to the harvest  
Go to the harvest with the light rope  
Now comes the good hay season  
Dear time of barley sowing  
Hurried time of rye harvest.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I came to your swing,  
To try your swing,  
Will this swing carry us,  
Will it carry two horses,  
Two horses’ harnesses,  
Four girls’ jewelry,  
Five girls’ felt shoes,  
Six girls’ headscarves?  
If the swing does not carry us,  
Bring an axe to chop the swing,  
Bring fire to burn the swing.  
Wait, let me say it again:  
Let me sing it again.  
The swing was not chopped by an axe,  
Nor burned by fire,  
Nor pierced by a knife.  
The swing was brought from afar,  
The swing was brought from the village of Moe,  
The crossbeam was brought from Viru,  
The posts were brought from Ambla,  
The main planks from Põltsamaa.  
The swing costs a speckled bull,  
The crossbeam costs a bay gelding,  
The posts cost a grey stallion.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Ten girls in the village  
Nine across the street,  
They must not come to the swing,  
They fear the gold will wear away  
The silver beads will tarnish,  
The money pots will rust.  
I sometimes am a warrior child,  
Neither my gold wears away  
Nor the silver beads tarnish  
Nor the money pots rust.  
Perhaps I will rock until evening,  
Swing until midday,  
Sing until broad daylight,  
The village aunt milks the cow,  
The village man drives the herd,  
The white brother waters the calf.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Young men, strong brothers
Mother, where have they made a swing
They must have made a swing in the pasture
In the pasture, in the berry field
They made a swing in the stones
In the stones, in the stumps
If I fall, who will pay for me
The palm pays for a pair of oxen
Another palm for another pair
The nose of the young man's horse
Ears close to the rune mark
Eyes like a drop of silk.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Good evening, dear family grandma  
Good evening, dear family grandpa  
May I also have permission to sing?  
Raise the link, Leenukene  
Open the door, Annekene  
Get the ribbons ready, Madli  
I throw barley seeds  
Behind the wall, two barley grains.  
In front of the barn, barley grains  
Dear family grandma  
Dear family grandpa  
Look for a share for St. Martin  
Try to give St. Martin some fish  
Five or six small sausages  
Two or three little cakes  
For Martin has been taken from the earth  
Martin has come from the sky  
Along the garden of flowers  
Along the golden garden  
Along the copper gates.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
So that I sway until evening,  
I sing until broad daylight,  
I swing until mid-morning.  
The village alder milks the cow,  
The village birch herds the cattle,  
The village spruce strains the milk,  
The maple tree waters the calves.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Ten girls in the village  
Nine across the street  
Eight on the edge of the herd  
Seven on the other side of it  
Six on each of the chimneys  
Five are brothers’ wives  
They were not allowed to come to the swing  
They feared the gold would wear out  
They feared the copper to fade,  
Money pots to become empty,  
Clover pots to dry up  
Silver beads to cease  
We are two poor children  
We go, we walk  
We go under the swing  
Let our gold wear out  
Let our copper fade  
Let the silver beads cease  
We have goldsmiths at home  
Outside are silversmiths  
Tinsmiths on the trail  
Blacksmiths in the field  
In the bag rises gold smoke  
From outside silver smoke  
Copper smoke from the wives  
Tin smoke from the trail  
From under the stack blacksmith’s smoke.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Ten girls in the village  
Nine across the street  
Eight on the edge of the herd  
Seven on the other side of it  
Six on each of the chimneys  
Five are brothers’ wives  
They were not allowed to come to the swing  
They feared the gold would wear out  
They feared the copper to fade,  
Money pots to become empty,  
Clover pots to dry up  
Silver beads to cease  
We are two poor children  
We go, we walk  
We go under the swing  
Let our gold wear out  
Let our copper fade  
Let the silver beads cease  
We have goldsmiths at home  
Outside are silversmiths  
Tinsmiths on the trail  
Blacksmiths in the field  
In the bag rises gold smoke  
From outside silver smoke  
Copper smoke from the wives  
Tin smoke from the trail  
From under the stack blacksmith’s smoke.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Wait, wait, swing, no, no, swing  
What have you swung, what have you walked  
Holes in the baseboard  
Blankets on top  
Swing, swings the glove’s yard  
The baseboard gave  
The blanket tied.  
Let my brother take a wife  
Another brother another wife  
The third walks the bridal path.  
The fourth brother goes to bring a wife  
The fifth brother goes to drink booze  
The sixth brother comes out of the crown  
The brother makes a new swing in the yard  
Behind the room with tomingas (a type of evergreen)  
On the thigh with a strap  
On top of a pile with rowan (mountain ash)  
In the yard with apple trees.  
In front of the garden a bare spruce  
On the fence a maple.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Come, village women to the swing,  
Bring strong eggs, bring strong chickens,  
Bring birds with webbèd feet,  
Bring ducks for the rake,  
Bring geese for hatching;  
Oh, make the crazy swingers,  
Oh, crazy or foolish,  
Where they have made the swing:  
Made the swing on plowed land,  
And children’s playground,  
On tiny little playground.  
The village mourns the plowed land,  
The village children the playground,  
The tiny little playground.  
We have swingers,  
Made the swing on hayfield,  
Among the bluebells,  
Among the meadowsweet,  
In the globe flower’s cluster.  
When I step downward,  
Down I step on berries,  
On bluebell’s eye,  
Among the meadowsweet. -

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, lads and brothers,  
Please let me down,  
If you won’t, I ask again,  
If you still don’t, I ask once more,  
I say let me swing till evening,  
Swing till mid-morning,  
Sing till broad daylight.  
Surely the cow milks the willows,  
Surely the birch tends the herd,  
The maple tree calls the calves. -

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
I came to try your swing,  
To see if the swing will carry me,  
If it will carry two to disappear,  
Two disappear without clothes,  
Four maidens without ornaments,  
Five maidens without woolen caps.  
If it does not carry, then let it disappear,  
Then bring an axe and cut the swing,  
But the swing blacksmiths replied:  
The swing is not for axe cutting,  
It is not for burning,  
The swing is worth a speckled bull,  
And spokes of a gray hen,  
A foal’s foal,  
A hundred rubles.  
The swing has come from afar,  
Swing spokes from Võivere,  
Swing spokes from Ampel,  
Leader poles from Paide.  
Take the swing to the cellar,  
And the spokes to the sauna roof,  
Ahead comes hay time,  
And the time of harvesting oats,  
The rye ears are already seen,  
Barley spikes are greening,  
Oat spikes are covered with mud,  
Peas are decorating the ornament,  
Lentils are set for pigs,  
Cabbage is fermenting on the hill,  
Beans are growing pods.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
What are you doing to our swing  
Why are you groaning at our hall  
The swing needs a glove-holder  
The baseboard already gives support  
The top log is already tied  
Wait, wait, swing, no, no, swing  
Let my brother take a wife  
The second brother brings another wife  
The third brings a daughter-in-law home  
Then the swing will be secure  
The baseboard already gives support  
The top log is already tied  
Swing smiths, gentle smiths  
Don’t ask me for eggs  
The hen stayed home to lay eggs  
The rooster stayed to make the nest  
What are you doing to our swing  
Why are you groaning at our hall.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
My tender little brother  
Bring the sturdy axe swing  
Bring the knife arrow swing  
Bring the battledore battledore swing  
Maybe I'll say it again  
Yes, I'll say it back like this:  
The swing is not sturdy axe work  
Nor knife arrowing  
Nor battledore battling  
Nor peg carving  
The swing costs a speckled horse  
Posts a hundred money  
From the parish a white-headed calf.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Let the little Kadris come in  
Kadri's nails are cold  
Kadri's toes are aching,  
Kadri has come from afar  
Over painted mountains  
Over swamps and bogs  
Kadri has a birch horse  
A spriggy sled  
Kadri has brought luck to the herd  
Harvest luck from Oonurme  
Animal luck from Sasi  
Pig luck from Sigula  
Kadri has come to see  
If the boys have done their work  
If the men have cut the wood.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Grandmother of the household, little grandmother,  
Grandfather of the household, little grandfather,  
Let the St. Martin’s visitors in,  
Martin’s claws are cold,  
Toes are aching.  
For Martin did not come to soil,  
Martin came from the year of heaven,  
Along silver edges,  
Along golden chords.  
Grandmother of the household, little grandmother,  
Grandfather of the household, little grandfather,  
Rise up from the bed,  
Lift your heads from the pillows,  
Spread your legs on the linen,  
Let the St. Martin’s visitors in!  
Grandmother of the household, little grandmother,  
Grandfather of the household, little grandfather,  
Take the keys from the latch,  
Open the county’s spirit cellar,  
Open the county’s spirit chamber.  
Across the yard by the barn,  
Look for a share for Martin:  
Five or six little sausages,  
Two or three heads of cabbage.  
For Martin does not cause harm,  
Martin brings luck to the barnyard,  
Luck to the pigs on the manure,  
Fill the straw for the piglets,  
Fill the barns for the sheep,  
Fill the stalls for the stallions,  
Fill the henhouses for the chickens.  
Let us in, I ask!  
If you do not let us, I will ask again,  
If not, I will knock on the doors of worry.  
From the horn I send to you.  
I have in my throat a locksmith,  
My sons are locksmiths of worry,  
I myself am a locksmith of the soul.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Grandmother of the family,
Grandfather of the household,
Rise up from the bed,
Lift your head from the pillow,
Spread your legs on the linen.
Look at poor Kadri.
Let Kadri in,
Kadri has come from afar,
Across the swamp, through the slippery path.
Kadri has a birch horse,
A hazel sleigh,
A rowan whip.
Let her in, I kindly ask!
There is no harm from Kadri,
Kadri brings luck to the herd outside,
Luck for the cattle at the gate,
Luck for the pigs in the manure,
Luck for the chickens in the yard,
Straw full of fleas,
Barns full of sheep,
Yards full of chickens.
Grandmother of the family,
Grandfather of the household,
Let the Kadri girls in,
Kadri's fingers are freezing,
Toes aching.
Grandmother of the family,
Take the keys from the stranger,
Try to go to the cellar,
Try to go to the chamber,
Across the yard towards the barn.
Bring Kadri some food,
Two or three heads of cabbage,
Five or six sausages,
A plate of white beans.
Grandmother of the family,
Grandfather of the household,
Please let me in!
If you do not let me in, I won't ask,
I will bang on the door,
I will send a herd your way,
I have a blacksmith at the door,
I myself am the smith,
My sons are the apprentices.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Do not ask me for eggs  
The hen stayed home to lay eggs  
The rooster crouched by the ear  
The box stayed to watch.  
To the swing, to the swing, village girls...

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing makers, dear brothers,  
Do not take it badly,  
Do not be upset,  
That I came under your swing!  
I came to swing on the swing,  
To try your swing.  
I did not bring any eggs:  
Let me come tomorrow again,  
The day after tomorrow,  
Then I will bring hens, then I will bring eggs,  
Then I will bring ducks in pairs,  
Then I will bring geese to hatch.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing-set brothers!  
Take the swing to the cellar,  
Carry the swing to the room,  
Barnsheds to the barn floor!  
Haytime is approaching,  
The time to feed the oats to the animals is near,  
Rye looks ready to harvest,  
Barley ears sway gently,  
Oat ears cover the ground,  
Wheats are coming to maturity,  
Peas decorate the clothes,  
Beans are growing pods,  
The peas stretch along the pig row.

label: Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, brothers,
Do not swing with malice,
Do not tease cruelly,
Do not roll in anger!
There are no evil ones swinging here,
No cruel ones teasing here,
No angry ones rolling here;
Here gold ones are swinging,
Silver ones are teasing,
Copper sparks are rolling.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Swallow of Piiri-pääri  
Where is your golden nest?  
"On the dry spruce top in the forest."  
Where did the dry spruce come from?  
"An old man cut it down."  
Where did the old man come from?  
"He crawled into the field garden."  
Where did the field garden come from?  
"Driven oxen plowed it up."  
Where did those driven oxen come from?  
"A German shot them down."  
Where did that German come from?  
"The German went to town."  
Where did that town come from?  
"The town burned down."  
Where did the town ashes go?  
"The wind blew them into the sea."  
Where did the sea come from?  
"A black rooster drank up the sea."  
Where did that black rooster come from?  
"The black rooster flew to the sky."  
Where did the sky come from?  
"The sky fell down."  
Where did the earth come from?  
"The earth sank down."  
Where did the bottom come from?  
"A goose ate up the bottom."  
Where did the goose come from?  
"A pig ate up the goose."  
Where did the pig come from?  
"A knife killed the pig."  
Where did the knife come from?  
"The knife disappeared by itself."

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The mouse, however, walked the ipsu path,  
Ipsu path, apsu path,  
With the crab’s carts behind.  
"Where are you going, little mouse?"  
"To the manor lord to drink,  
From the pantry to eat bread,  
From the cellar to catch fish,  
To lick the frying pan.  
The lady gives me porridge,  
The cook makes cream sauce for me.  
The maid is cooking blood sausage;  
I myself eat pork,  
I try fish bones.  
From the manor I got war news:  
Father went to the hayfield,  
The maid gave a slap,  
Then I got to run away from the town."  
"Where are you going, little mouse?"  
"I'm going to thresh the grain;  
Maybe a tree will fall on my head,  
Then I will run to its roots."  
"Roots will make your teeth rusty,  
Your eye sockets moldy."  
"I will wash with water;  
If the water chills me,  
I will swell with fire.  
If the fire burns me;  
I will smear with butter!"  
"Where does the mouse get butter?"  
"From the mistress’s pot,  
From the free woman’s dish."

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lõõri-lõõri, little bird,
Piiri-pääri, little swallow,
Where did your golden nest get made?
"Behind the oak tree."
Where did the oak get made?
"An old man cut down the oak."
Where did that old man get made?
"He was placed under the field bed."
Where did that field bed get made?
"Hitched oxen plowed the bed."
Where did those hitched oxen get made?
"A bear broke the hitched oxen."
Where did that bear get made?
"The bear went into the tall reed."
Where did that tall reed get made?
"A sickle mowed the reed down."
Where did that sickle get made?
"Hard"
Where did that hard get made?
"A hen scratched the manure."
Where did that hen get made?
"It was taken to the town, eaten away."
Where did that town get made?
"Fire burned the town down."
Where did that fire get made?
"Water put out the fire."
Where did that water get made?
"Water sank beneath the earth."
Where did that earth get made?
"The earth is for us to stand on,
To stand on, to remain on."

label: Lastelaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ille-prille, the sprayer,  
From sprayer became a ripsaw,  
From ripsaw became a thigh cleaver,  
From thigh cleaver became a cider press,  
From cider press became a rainbow,  
From rainbow became a chapel,  
From chapel became a beautiful forest.  

What shall be done in that forest?  
In the forest, honey shall be drunk,  
Brandy splash shall be put,  
We shall attend the wedding.  

There was one Intu, another Antu,  
The third was the suitor Nigula,  
The fourth was a maiden,  
The fifth Viileke the servant,  
The sixth was a little curly-haired boy,  
The seventh was a soldier's son,  
The eighth was a fisherman's son,  
The ninth was Hüide-Tüide,  
The tenth was a blacksmith in the village.  

Then came to hear the news  
The silver-marked little bird,  
Silver-marked, blue-marked,  
With a golden tuft on its head.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ille-prille, the sprayer,  
From sprayer became a ripsaw,  
From ripsaw became a thigh cleaver,  
From thigh cleaver became a cider press,  
From cider press became a rainbow,  
From rainbow became a chapel,  
From chapel became a beautiful forest.  

What shall be done in that forest?  
In the forest, honey shall be drunk,  
Brandy splash shall be put,  
We shall attend the wedding.  

There was one Intu, another Antu,  
The third was the suitor Nigula,  
The fourth was a maiden,  
The fifth Viileke the servant,  
The sixth was a little curly-haired boy,  
The seventh was a soldier's son,  
The eighth was a fisherman's son,  
The ninth was Hüide-Tüide,  
The tenth was a blacksmith in the village.  

Then came to hear the news  
The silver-marked little bird,  
Silver-marked, blue-marked,  
With a golden tuft on its head.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Hello, hello, Tepermann  
From Tepermann I got madara  
From madara I got madlipilli  
From madlipilli I got peburi  
From peburi I got Peteriku  
From Peterik I got Riivaku  
From Riivak I got Riiven salt  
From Riiven salt I got sobina  
From sobina I got salt box  
From salt box I got vadin  
From vadin I got barrel of vodka  
From barrel of vodka I got rainbow  
From rainbow I got whip forest  
From whip forest I got big islands  
Big islands, wide plains  
Under the berry pine forest  
Under the golden spruce  
There the buns were eaten  
Vodka mugs were carried  
Bottled beers were drunk.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Oh, poor little bird of Virbel,
Virbel bird, Virbel bird,
Where shall I make my nest?
In the haystack of the family,
In the wise buildings of the farm.
The family’s daughter, a young maiden,
Went to fetch wood from the haystack.
She knocked down my nest from the haystack,
From the wise buildings of the farm.
Oh, poor little bird of Virbel,
Virbel bird, Virbel bird,
Where shall I make my nest,
To lay my eggs in,
To hatch my chicks under?
On the dry spruce in the forest,
Behind the room, on the oak branch.
Where did that dry spruce come from?
The old man cut down the spruce.
Where did that old man come from?
He was buried on the field of the peasants.
Where did that peasants’ field come from?
The oxen from the village plowed the field.
Where did those village oxen come from?
A bear killed the village oxen.
Where did that bear come from?
The Saxon shot the bear.
Where did that Saxon come from?
The Saxon went to the town.
Where did that town come from?
The town burned down.
Where did the ashes of the town go?
Under the streets of the new town,
Beneath the ramparts of the old town.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Sleep, sleep, tumbleweed,  
The chilly one did a somersault,  
The key to the sausage chamber got lost,  
The butter pot tipped over.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Sleep, come in through the door,
fall like a child,
let your eye rest on the table.
But sleep comes howling,
singing through the groves.
Place a bundle of straw on his shoulder,
a sheaf of barley on the stack;
he seeks to send the dreaming child,
the sleeping little boy.
But sleep says: wander, wander,
sleep: march, march.
Sleep will not give the new cap,
sleeping without a shirt.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Sleep, come in through the door,
fall like a child,
let your eye rest on the table.
But sleep comes howling,
singing through the groves.
Place a bundle of straw on his shoulder,
a sheaf of barley on the stack;
he seeks to send the dreaming child,
the sleeping little boy.
But sleep says: wander, wander,
sleep: march, march.
Sleep will not give the new cap,
sleeping without a shirt.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri-lõõri sparrow,  
piiri-pääri swallow,  
where was your golden nest?  
In the forest on top of a dry spruce.  
Where did that dry spruce come from?  
An old man cut it down.  
Where did that old man’s father come from?  
The old man died.  
Where was that old man’s grave?  
In the long field’s garden.  
Where did the long field’s garden come from?  
Driven oxen plowed it up.  
Where did those driven oxen come from?  
A bear tore the driven oxen apart.  
Where did that bear’s father come from?  
The bear ran into the reeds.  
Where did those reeds come from?  
The sickle whistled down.  
Where did that sickle come from?  
It wore out its blade.  
Where did that blade come from?  
A hen rummaged in the dung.  
Where did that hen come from?  
A kite carried the hen over the sea.  
Where did that kite come from?  
The kite went to town.  
Where did that town come from?  
The town burned down.  
Where did the town’s ashes go?  
The wind blew them away.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Hello, hello, little mouse!  
Oh my, little kitty!  
Where are you going, little mouse?  
I am going to chop wood.  
What if this wood falls on your head?  
I will run to the roots.  
What if you get hungry?  
I will start peeling birch bark.  
What if that bark gets stuck in your throat?  
I will cut it with a knife.  
What if blood comes out?  
I will wash it with water.  
What if those hairs get wet?  
I will dry them with fire.  
What if those hairs get singed?  
I will butter them with butter.  
Where do you get the butter?  
From the old woman's chamber pot.  
Where does the old woman get it?  
From the black blueberry seed.  
Where did the black blueberry get it?  
It drank from our lake water,  
tasted our well water,  
smelled the spring water,  
and sampled our sea water.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri-lõõri song,  
where is your darling’s nest?  
In the forest, on a dry spruce.  
Where did that spruce itself come from?  
An old man cut it down.  
Where did that old man himself come from?  
The old man died.  
Where was he buried?  
In the long vegetable garden.  
Where did that vegetable garden come from?  
Brown oxen plowed it up.  
Where did those brown oxen come from?  
A bear tore the brown oxen apart.  
Where did that bear itself come from?  
The bear tricked the reeds.  
Where did those reeds themselves come from?  
A scythe whistled by the reeds.  
Where did that scythe itself come from?  
The sly one deceived the scythes.  
Where did that sly one itself come from?  
A hen scurried into the sly one’s manure.  
Where did that hen itself come from?  
An eagle tore the hen apart.  
Where did that eagle itself come from?  
A spark burned the eagle down.  
Where did that spark itself come from?  
The spark went out in the ashes.  
Where did those ashes themselves come from?  
The wind blew the ashes under the sky.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Äia-tuia,  
Let the swing go  
Towards the sea, into the forest,  
To the top of a dry spruce.  
Others eat bread rolls  
We eat rye bread  
Bang! bang! they buried  
Gypsy Manni  
The stone bell was struck  
Sauce was eaten with bread.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri, lõõri, little bird  
Where is your golden nest?  
In the forest on a dry spruce tree.  
Where has that dry spruce remained?  
An old man cut it down.  
Where is that old man's gold?  
The old man has died.  
Buried in the garden bed.  
Where is that golden garden bed?  
Draft oxen plowed it up.  
White oxen chased the calves.  
Where have those oxen gone?  
They crawled into the reed thicket.  
Where is that golden reed thicket?  
A sickle mowed it down.  
Where is that little sickle?  
The scythe blade wore it out.  
Where is that golden blade?  
The hen was scratching in the manure.  
Where is that little hen?  
A hawk took it away.  
Where is that little hawk?  
It shot the village tomcat.  
Where is that little village tomcat?  
Sitting at the edge of the tavern table,  
With a shot glass between its fingers,  
And a beer mug under its arm.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
From sõmeru I got fingerless gloves,  
Fingerless gloves into a bundle.  
Bundle and jug.  
From the jug I got the market thumb,  
From the market thumb a curious one.  
From the curious thumb a cross,  
From the cross to the rimal.  
From the rimal I got a clasp for the chest,  
From the clasp for the chest I got sõmeru.  
From sõmeru I got fingerless gloves,  
Fingerless gloves into a bundle,  
Bundle and jug.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri-lõõri, swallow,  
Piiri-pääri, little swallow,  
Where is your golden little nest?  
In the forest, on the dry spruce.  
Where did that dry spruce stay?  
An old man cut the spruce down.  
Where did that old man himself go?  
The old man died.  
Where was the old man buried?  
Under the long garden bed.  
Where did that garden bed remain?  
Spotted bulls plowed it up.  
Where did those spotted bulls stay?  
A bear tore the bulls down.  
Where did that bear itself go?  
The bear jumped into the reeds.  
Where did those reeds remain then?  
The sickle whistled it down.  
Where did that sickle stay again?  
The sled wore it out.  
Where did that sled go?  
The sled splashed into the lake.  
Where did that lake remain again?  
The rainbow drank the lake empty.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Hello, hello Teppelmann.  
Teppelmann became a maraka,  
The maraka became a maanipill,  
The maanipill became a piburi,  
The piburi became a piitsarikki,  
The piitsarikki became a rimakas,  
The rimakas became a breastpin,  
The breastpin became a fleshy one,  
The fleshy one became a salt container,  
The salt container became a formula,  
The formula became a rainbow,  
The rainbow became a kavak,  
From the kavak came dear forests.  
Those forests, honeyed forests.  
From an apple tree came a beer cupboard,  
From rowan came milk bottles,  
From juniper came rock jugs.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Reed warbler, reed warbler, skylark,
Swallow, swallow, swallow,
Where is your golden nest?
In the forest, on the top of a dry spruce.
Where did that spruce come from?
An old man cut down the spruce.
Where did that old man come from?
He crawled under the garden bed.
Where did that garden bed come from?
Oxen yoked to plows dug it up.
Where did those yoked oxen come from?
A Russian bought the yoked oxen.
Where did that Russian come from?
The Russian went to the town.
Where did that town come from?
Fire burned the town down.
Where did that fire come from?
Water extinguished the fire.
Where did that water come from?
The sea is under our yard.
What is in the middle of the sea?
A well in the middle of the sea.
What is in the middle of that well?
A pillar in the middle of the well.
What is on the edge of that pillar?
A ring on the edge of the pillar.
What is on the edge of that ring?
A cuckoo on the edge of the ring.
What is on the cuckoo’s wings?
A child on the cuckoo’s wings.
What is on the child's chest?
A ring on the child's chest.
Where did that ring come from?
From the shins of soldiers,
From the knees of boys.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Reed warbler, reed warbler, skylark,
Swallow, swallow, swallow,
Where is your golden nest?
In the forest, on the top of a dry spruce.
Where did that spruce come from?
An old man cut down the spruce.
Where did that old man come from?
He crawled under the garden bed.
Where did that garden bed come from?
Oxen yoked to plows dug it up.
Where did those yoked oxen come from?
A Russian bought the yoked oxen.
Where did that Russian come from?
The Russian went to the town.
Where did that town come from?
Fire burned the town down.
Where did that fire come from?
Water extinguished the fire.
Where did that water come from?
The sea is under our yard.
What is in the middle of the sea?
A well in the middle of the sea.
What is in the middle of that well?
A pillar in the middle of the well.
What is on the edge of that pillar?
A ring on the edge of the pillar.
What is on the edge of that ring?
A cuckoo on the edge of the ring.
What is on the cuckoo’s wings?
A child on the cuckoo’s wings.
What is on the child's chest?
A ring on the child's chest.
Where did that ring come from?
From the shins of soldiers,
From the knees of boys.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Of fineness and brittleness.
From brittleness came nettle thumb,
From nettle thumb came the mitten,
From the mitten came the thumb-cross,
From the thumb-cross came the ribbed wale,
From the ribbed wale came the ring channel,
From the ring channel came the coarse wale,
From the coarse wale and the mitten.....

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Titmouse-lark lark,  
Where is your golden nest,  
Behind the room in the oak grove,  
Under the elm by the yard,  
Where did that oak itself come from?  
The bear scratched up the stump,  
Where did that bear itself come from?  
The bear went long ago to other lands,  
Left footprints on this land.  
The cat went to see the bear off,  
Fell into the village well,  
From under the belly came tall boots,  
From the nose the girl got boots,  
From the tail came the ox’s saddle.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Border swallow,  
Where is your golden nest?  
On a dry spruce in the forest.  
Where is that golden dry spruce?  
An old man has knocked it down,  
Where is that golden old man?  
He has buried it in the edge of the field,  
Where is that golden field edge?  
The furrows have been plowed up.  
Where are those golden furrows?  
A bear has trampled the furrows,  
Where is that golden little bear?  
The hunter has shot the bear down.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Mamsel cooked jam,  
I tasted the jam.  
Mamsel hit me with a ladle,  
I hit with a fire striker;  
Mamsel fell down,  
I went behind the cupboard.  
The clock had not struck twelve,  
The stallions were not fed in the stable.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri, bird, swallows,  
Piiri, sparrow, swallow,  
Where is your golden nest?  
In the forest, on top of a dry spruce.  
Where did that spruce itself come from?  
An old man cut the spruce down.  
Where did that old man himself come from?  
The old man died.  
Where was he buried?  
At the edge of a long field.  
Where did that field edge come from?  
The farmers plowed it up.  
Where did those farmers come from?  
But a bear broke the farmers.  
Where did that bear itself come from?  
The bear hid in the reeds.  
Where did those reeds come from?  
The sickle whistled through the reeds.  
Where did that sickle itself come from?  
A cheat cheated the sickles.  
Where did that cheat himself come from?  
I put the cheat on the window of the sauna,  
The hen scratched in the dung.  
Where did that hen itself come from?  
The eagle broke the hen.  
Where did that eagle itself come from?  
The eagle flew under the sky.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Sleep came in through the door  
aimed at the child's eye  
fell onto the child's eyebrow  
stared at the child's shadow.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The lark sings on the tile  
The swallow at the edge of the border  
Where is your darling’s nest?  
In the forest on top of a dry spruce.  
Which spruce?  
An old man cut the spruce.  
Which old man?  
The old man died.  
When was he buried?  
In the long field’s garden bed.  
Which garden bed?  
A hen scratched the garden bed.  
Which hen?  
The hen flew into the grass.  
What grass?  
A sickle mowed the grass.  
Which sickle?  
The sickle’s edge is worn.  
Which edge?  
A bear gnawed the edge.  
Which bear?  
The bear swam across the sea.  
Which sea?  
A black bull drank from the sea.  
Wh-  
The black bull went to the barn.  
Which barn?  
The fire burned in the barn.  
Which fire?  
The fire went to the sky,  
With pots, pans,  
Spatulas, spoons.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lyre, bellows, chatter,  
border, brocade, swallow,  
where is your golden nest?  
On the dry spruce tree in the forest.  
Where did the dry spruce come from?  
An old man cut the spruce down.  
Where did the old man himself come from?  
He was buried under the garden patch.  
Where did the garden patch come from?  
The oxen plowed it up.  
Where did the oxen themselves come from?  
The wolf had eaten the oxen.  
Where did the wolf itself come from?  
The wolf had gone into the deep forest.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
A thousand-thousand, a hundred-hundred
You go along the rye bridge
To fight in the Iron City,
From the Iron City to the wooden block,
From the wooden block to the sliding gate,
From the sliding gate to the leather pouch,
From the leather pouch to the manure mound,
From the manure mound to the pig mound.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri, lõõri, Swallow,  
border, Pääri, little swallow,  
tell me, where is your golden nest?  
In the forest, on the branch of a dry spruce.  
Where did that dry spruce come from?  
An old man cut the spruce down.  
Where did that old man come from?  
The old man died himself.  
Where was that old man buried?  
In the bed of a long field.  
Where did the bed of the long field come from?  
Farmers plowed it up.  
Where did those farmers come from?  
A bear tore them apart and ate them.  
Where did that bear come from?  
The bear went into tall reed.  
Where did that reed come from?  
A sickle cut it down.  
Where did that sickle come from?  
A rake wore it down.  
Where did that rake come from?  
It got lost in the sleigh’s dung.  
Where did that sleigh’s dung come from?  
A white hen was running about.  
Where did that white hen come from?  
A hawk ate it.  
Where did that hawk come from?  
A hunter shot it down.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lõõri, lõõri, little lark,  
Piari, piari, little girl,  
Where is my golden peach?  
The peach is on a wet pine tree.  
Where is that wet pine?  
In the forest grew the wet pine.  
Where did the forest grow?  
Fire burned the forest down.  
Where did the fire come from?  
Water extinguished the fire.  
Where did the water come from?  
A black bear drank the water.  
Where is that black bear?  
A bear broke the black bear.  
Where did that bear come from?  
A rifle shot the bear down.  
Where did the rifle come from?  
The rifle is in the reed in the forest.  
Where is that reed?  
A sickle whistled on the reed.  
Where did the sickle come from?  
The whetstone sharpened the sickle.  
Where did that whetstone come from?  
The whetstone was in cow dung.  
Where is that cow dung?  
A hen scratched in cow dung.  
Where did that hen come from?  
An eagle took the hen away.  
Where did that eagle come from?  
The eagle went over the sea and the forest itself.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Monday - oats were brought,  
Tuesday - flax was processed,  
Wednesday - the dog was sent to the store,  
Thursday - the hide was taken,  
Friday - bread was prepared,  
Saturday - it was put in the oven,  
Sunday - yes, today is Sunday!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Our cat has scratched eyes,  
Sat on a stump in the forest.  
A pipe was in its mouth and a stick in its hand,  
Called children to read,  
Those who could not read,  
Were sent off to take a nap.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lõõri-lõõri swallows.  
Piiri-pääri swallows,  
where is your golden nest?  
In the forest, on a dry spruce branch.  
Where did that dry spruce come from?  
An old man cut it down.  
Where did that old man come from?  
The old man died.  
Where was he buried?  
On the bed of a long field.  
Where did that bed of the long field come from?  
Peasant lads plowed it up.  
Where did those peasant lads come from?  
A bear broke them up.  
Where did that bear come from?  
The bear jumped into the reeds.  
Where did those reeds come from?  
A sickle whistled down.  
Where did that sickle come from?  
A scythe wore out.  
Where did that scythe come from?  
A hen pecked in the dung.  
Where did that hen come from?  
The gold broke.  
Where did that gold come from?  
The gold flew to the town.  
Where did that town come from?  
The town burned down.  
Where did the town's ashes go?  
The wind scattered them across the meadow.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Six, six, little darling,  
kitten, spotted little bird,  
lark, lark, skylark,  
where is your golden nest?  
The nest is in the vegetable patch.  
Where did the vegetable patch come from?  
The hired farmers plowed it up.  
Where did the hired farmers come from?  
The bear broke the hired farmers.  
Where did the bear's father come from?  
The bear went to the top of the spruce in the forest.  
Where did that spruce come from?  
An old man cut down the spruce.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Piece of cake, piece of cake,  
slice of raw meat,  
dip it in egg,  
roll, roll, roll - jump into the oven!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Border-border, little swallow,  
lilt-lilt, little swallow,  
where is your golden nest?  
In the forest on a dry spruce.  
Where is that dry spruce left?  
An old man cut down the spruce.  
Where is that old man?  
He went to the garden patch.  
Where are those old garden patches?  
Servants plowed them up.  
Where are those old servants?  
I sent them down to the reeds.  
Where are those old reeds?  
The scythe mowed the reeds.  
Where is that old little scythe?  
The weasel sneaked, alarmed loudly.  
Where is that old little weasel?  
A hen scratched in the manure.  
Where is that old little hen?  
A hawk took it to the forest stump.  
Where is that old little stump?  
A bear scratched the little stump.  
Where is that old little bear?  
He crossed over two forests,  
jumped over eight forests,  
his head showed above the forest,  
his body twisted in the middle of the forest,  
his legs were hidden under the forest.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Come here, pig Jaan,  
shut Kusti’s eyes,  
eyes shut, mouth closed,  
nose to the little piggy,  
ears to Kiki-Koki,  
tail to the little dill-fool.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri-lõõri, skylark,  
piiri-pääri, swallow,  
where is your golden nest?  
In the forest, on the dry spruce.  
Where did the dry spruce come from?  
An old man has cut it down.  
Where did the old man come from?  
He crept into the reed bed in the forest.  
Where did the reed bed come from?  
A sickle has swished.  
Where did the sickle come from?  
A blade has worn out.  
Where did the blade itself come from?  
A hen has scratched in the manure.  
Where did the little hen come from?  
A hawk took the hen away  
to the field’s edge outside,  
to the back of the field in the buckwheat,  
in the middle of the field of boiling oats,  
across the sea, onto a tree stump.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
A goat kid for the herd,  
take the son behind,  
make a fence from there.  
I make a fence for the cow,  
the cow gives me milk.  
I give the milk to the piglet,  
the piglet gives me a mate.  
I give the mate to the mother-in-law,  
the mother-in-law gives me a cake.  
I give the cake to Kalev,  
Kalev gives me iron.  
I give the iron to the reaper,  
the reaper gives me gold.  
I give the gold to the oak,  
the oak gives me a leaf.  
I give the leaf to the sheep,  
the sheep gives me wool.  
I give the wool to the spinner,  
the spinner makes me a new hood.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Sleep comes with a yawn  
through the forests and groves,  
brings a little hay with it,  
two stalks of straw it carries.  
Sleep comes in through the door,  
falls upon the child's cheeks,  
upon the edges of the child's cheeks,  
falls upon the child's eyebrows,  
falls upon the child's eyelids,  
closes the big eyes.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lyre-flute, lark,  
border-edge, swallow,  
until your nest?  
In the forest, on the dry spruce.  
Until the little spruce?  
An old man carved the little spruce.  
Until that old man?  
The old man died.  
Where was he buried?  
By the edge of the long field.  
Where did he remain there?  
The hen’s tail sticks out.  
Where is that little hen?  
The hen flew to the barn.  
Where is that barn?  
The sickle reaped the barn.  
Where is that little sickle?  
The sickle sits in the basket.  
Until that little stump?  
The bear scratched the stump.  
Until that little bear?  
The bear rolled into the water.  
Until that little one?  
The black bull drank water.  
Until that black bull?  
The black bull went to the cowshed.  
Until that little cowshed?  
Fire burned the cowshed.  
Until that little fire?  
The fire went up to the sky.  
Who will go there after?  
With tin, with copper,  
with sledges, with spoons,  
and with pots, with pans.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Lõõri-lõõri, skylark,  
pääri-pääri, swallow  
where is your golden nest?  
In the forest on top of a dry spruce.  
Where did that dry spruce come from?  
An old man cut it down.  
Where did that old man come from?  
The old man died.  
Where was he buried?  
Under a long garden bed.  
Where did that garden bed come from?  
Küidud sons plowed it up.  
Where are those Küidud sons?  
The bear ate them all.  
Where did that bear come from?  
The Saxon shot him.  
Where did that Saxon come from?  
The Saxon went away to the town.  
Where did that town come from?  
The fire burned it down.  
Where did that fire come from?  
The water put it out.  
Where did that water come from?  
The water went away to the sea.  
Where did that sea come from?  
The sea is under our yard.  
What is in the middle of that yard?  
A well is in the middle of the yard.  
What is on the edge of that well?  
A post is on the edge of the well.  
What is on top of that post?  
A cuckoo is on top of the post.  
What is on the cuckoo’s hands?  
A child is on the cuckoo’s hands.  
What is on the child’s chest?  
A brooch is on the child’s chest.  
What is on the eye of that brooch?  
A ring is on the eye of that brooch.  
But what is the ring made of?  
Of a soldier’s finger bone,  
of a hero’s collar bone.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Lõõri-lõõri, skylark,  
pääri-pääri, swallow  
where is your golden nest?  
In the forest on top of a dry spruce.  
Where did that dry spruce come from?  
An old man cut it down.  
Where did that old man come from?  
The old man died.  
Where was he buried?  
Under a long garden bed.  
Where did that garden bed come from?  
Küidud sons plowed it up.  
Where are those Küidud sons?  
The bear ate them all.  
Where did that bear come from?  
The Saxon shot him.  
Where did that Saxon come from?  
The Saxon went away to the town.  
Where did that town come from?  
The fire burned it down.  
Where did that fire come from?  
The water put it out.  
Where did that water come from?  
The water went away to the sea.  
Where did that sea come from?  
The sea is under our yard.  
What is in the middle of that yard?  
A well is in the middle of the yard.  
What is on the edge of that well?  
A post is on the edge of the well.  
What is on top of that post?  
A cuckoo is on top of the post.  
What is on the cuckoo’s hands?  
A child is on the cuckoo’s hands.  
What is on the child’s chest?  
A brooch is on the child’s chest.  
What is on the eye of that brooch?  
A ring is on the eye of that brooch.  
But what is the ring made of?  
Of a soldier’s finger bone,  
of a hero’s collar bone.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Where did the bear itself come from?  
The bear went to the reed.  
Where did the reed itself come from?  
The sickle cut it down.  
Where did the sickle itself come from?  
The scythe wore it out.  
Where did the scythe itself come from?  
It disappeared on the sauna manure heap.  
Where did the sauna manure come from?  
The white hen scattered it.  
Where did the white hen come from?  
The eagle ate it.  
Where did the eagle itself come from?  
The falcon shot it down.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Ride, ride along,  
from there we can eat.  
Ride, ride to Riga,  
a cup to Tallinn,  
beer to Petersburg,  
and a tail to the German shore.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Ott, ott, ott, ott, Otike  
Where is my little neck pouch?  
/:The pouch is on a stump in the forest:/  
Where did the stump itself come from?  
An old man cut down that stump.  
Where did the old man himself go?  
The old man died.  
Where was he buried?  
In the garden of a long field.  
Where did those gardens themselves come from?  
Draft oxen plowed them up.  
Where did those oxen themselves go?  
A bear tore them apart.  
Where did that bear itself go?  
The bear ran to town.  
Where did that town itself go?  
The town burned down.  
The bear disappeared, the town burned down,  
The wind scattered the ashes.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Loori loori loorke,  
pairi pairi swallow  
Where is your golden nest?  
In the dry spruce in the forest.  
Where did the dry spruce come from?  
An old man cut it down.  
Where did the old man come from?  
The old man died.  
Where was he buried?  
In the border of the long field.  
Where did the border of the long field come from?  
The peasants plowed it up.  
Where did those peasants come from?  
A bear broke the peasants.  
- - - - - - - - - - - -  
Fire under the ash sky  
Around Alli Krans’s neck,

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lark, pipe, skylark,  
Border, gate, swallow,  
Where is your darling’s nest?  
In the forest on a dry spruce branch.  
Where is that darling spruce?  
An old man has cut it down.  
Where is that darling old man?  
He crept into a long garden bed.  
Where is that darling garden bed?  
Oxen that have been yoked plowed it up.  
Where are those darling yoked oxen?  
A bear killed the yoked oxen.  
Where is that darling little bear?  
He crept into a long reed bed.  
Where is that darling long reed?  
The sickle whistled as it cut down.  
Where is that darling little sickle?  
It has worn out completely.  
Where is that darling little hard one?  
A chicken scurried into the manure.  
Where is that darling little chicken?  
A hawk has killed the chicken.  
Where is that darling little hawk?  
The hawk has flown over the town.  
Where is that darling little town?  
The town has burned down.  
What is left of that town?  
A pig with seven piglets,  
A horse with its foal,  
A hen with two chicks.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lira, pipe, lark  
Border, pennant, swallow  
Where is your dear little nest?  
In the forest on a dry spruce  
In the meadow on a broad alder.  
Where has that dry spruce gone?  
Where has that broad alder gone?  
The old man has cut it down.  
Where has that old man gone?  
The old man has died.  
Where was he buried?  
In a long garden bed.  
Where has that garden bed gone?  
Oxen that were yoked plowed it up.  
Where have those yoked oxen gone?  
A bear broke the yoked oxen.  
Where has that bear itself gone?  
The bear ran into the reed.  
Where has that reed gone?  
The scythe sang on it —  
It has worn it down hard.  
Where has that hard place gone?  
On a dry stumptop in the forest.  
Where has that dry stump gone?  
Roosters and hens have scurried up.  
Where have those roosters and hens gone?  
The eagle has eaten the roosters and hens.  
Where has that eagle itself gone?  
The eagle has flown under the sky.  
Men, brothers, let's make stairs,  
So we could catch the eagle.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit, tit, the titmouse,  
look, look, the sparrow.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went to the herd company group,  
its tail curled, its leg bent,  
from the herd home it moved along,  
tiptap tiptap tiptap.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Chirp chirp titmouse,  
watch watch sparrow  
jump over the pea field  
leap over the cabbage patch  
whiz through the forest.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went with the herd's gang  
tail tucked, snout in the ditch.  
From the herd to home, swift and nimble.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
A goat in a herd of sheep  
Across the sea, a berry  
Bring me hay  
I will give hay to the cow  
The cow will give me milk  
I will give the milk to the baker  
The baker will give me bread,  
I will give the bread to the shoemaker  
The shoemaker will give me boots  
I will give the boots to the tailor  
The tailor will give me clothes.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit, tit chickadee,  
look, look sparrow.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went to the herd in a group,  
tail drooping, the foot in a rut.  
From the herd home, carefully winding.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri’s flute is a nightingale  
the border is a swallow  
jump over the bean field  
hop over the cabbage patch  
over the forest, whirl.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went to the herd:  
in a group.  
Tail drooping  
head down low,  
from the herd home  
tugging pulling jerking  
shuffling stamping stirring.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri's pipe tune,  
the swallow at the edge of the border  
jump over the oat field fence  
leap over the cabbage patch  
whiz over the forest.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Chirp chirp chickadee,  
look look sparrow  
flew over the pea garden  
jumped over the cabbage patch,  
over the forest a horn.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The lira's warbler,
the border's swallow
flies over the oat field
jumps over the cabbage field
over the forest the horn.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
A swallow flying around the border,  
a meadowlark trilling,  
flew over the oat field.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri, pipe skylark,  
border's edge swallow.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit, tit, titmouse,  
see, see, sparrow,  
fly over the pea field,  
jump over the cabbage patch,  
buzz over the forest.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went to the herd compa-kapa
tail drooping in the hollow.
From the herd home twisting turning twisting,
timpa tampa tirdi.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit, tit chickadee,  
look, look sparrow.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Roll the cake, roll the cake  
put it in the oven, take it out  
eat it all up!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The goat carries the herd,  
over the sea the berry,  
bring me hay,  
I will give the hay to the lamb,  
the lamb gives me wool,  
I give the wool to Kalev,  
Kalev gives me iron,  
I give the iron to the blacksmith.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri, lõõri, skylark,  
border, edge, swallow  
jump over the bean field fence,  
leap over the cabbage patch,  
over the forest vroom!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit tit chickadee,  
look, look sparrow.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went to the herd: quick quick
tail wagging, nose in the valley,
from the herd home twist turn.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went to the herd, the group, the company.  
It came back with a winch, a crank, a whir  
and tipa tapa tirr.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Kurr karr, where are you going,  
purr, I’m going to the corner.  
Pirr, parr to the parsnip.  
Mirr, märr what’s there.  
Nirr norr to catch mice.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri, little skylark of the flute,  
little swallow on the border,  
jump over the oat field fence,  
leap over the cabbage patch,  
over the forest run.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Purr, fool into the corner,  
pirr parr to Parsile.  
Mirr, marr what’s there,  
sirr, sorr to eat mice.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
A goat, a cat, a herd  
across the sea with berries  
bring me hay,  
I bring hay to the cow,  
the cow brings me milk,  
I bring milk to the piglet,  
the piglet brings me meat,  
I bring meat to the chicken,  
the chicken brings me an egg,  
I bring the egg to the buyer,  
the buyer gives me money.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri, little swallow of the pipe,  
swallow of the border edge,  
jump over the oat field fence,  
leap over the cabbage patch,  
over the forest vroom.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Prigadi pragadi where did you go.
Prigadi pragadi I went to the forest.
Prigadi pragadi what did you see.
Prigadi pragadi I saw a boy.
Prigadi pragadi what did he ask,
prigadi pragadi he asked for a kiss,
prigadi pragadi did you give,
prigadi pragadi no I did not give
prigadi pragadi why didn’t you give,
prigadi, pragadi his mouth was scabby.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri, the lark of the pipe,
piiri, pääri, the swallow,
jump over the oat field fence,
leap over the cabbage patch,
over the forest vroom...

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
A goat leads a herd,
over the sea of berries,
bring me hay;
I give hay to the cow,
the cow gives me milk,
I give milk to the piglet;
the piglet gives me meat,
I give meat to the chicken,
the chicken gives me an egg,
I give the egg to the buyer,
the buyer gives me money,
I take the money to the store,
from the store I get goods.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri, pipe, skylark,  
border, end, swallow  
jump over the bean field fence  
hop over the cabbage patch,  
over the forest, dart.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went to the herd: in a group, in a pack,  
tail drooping, nose down  
from the herd home, twisting, turning, stirring  
hustle, bustle, rustle.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri, little swallow,
border swift swallow
jump over the oat field fence
hop over the cabbage patch,
over the forest ridge.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tii, tii titmouse  
look look sparrow  
flew over the pea garden  
jumped over the cabbage garden  
over the forest, off it goes.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The song of the nightingale in the linden tree  
Where is your nest  
nest on the dry spruce top?  
where is that dry spruce  
the old man cut it down  
where is that old man  
the old man died  
where was he buried  
under a long garden bed  
where is that long garden bed  
the serfs plowed it up.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit, tit chickadee  
caw-caw sparrow.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit, tit titmouse  
caw-caw sparrow.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went to the herd kompa-kompa,  
its paw drooped, tail dragged,  
came home, vinta-vanta virrdi,  
timpa tampa tirrdi.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri, lõõri, swallow.
Border chief swallow,
where is your dear nest?

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog goes to the herd: in a group, in a bunch,  
tail tucked, lost on the hill.  
From the herd home: sling, sling the skirting.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit, tit, the titmouse,  
look, look, the sparrow  
flew over the bean field,  
jumped over the cabbage patch,  
over the forest, off it goes!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went to the herd's timpa tompa,  
its tail was in a ditch, its tail was crooked,  
from the herd to home with a twist and turn,  
timpa, tampa, tirdi.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went to the herd: in a group together,  
tail wagging at the riverbank,  
from the herd home: twist, turn, spin,  
tip-tap, tip-tap.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Where are you going, capercaillie?
I am going to the black grouse's feast,
who will kill the black grouse?
A billy goat with a prong,
a sheep with a paw,
a goat with a kid.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit, tit titmouse,  
look, look sparrow  
flew over the pea garden  
jumped over the cabbage patch  
over the forest ran a squirrel.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit, tit, titmouse,  
chick, chick, sparrow,  
fly over the pea garden,  
jump over the cabbage patch,  
over the forest whoosh!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went with the herd, in a group,
tail tucked, horn crooked,
from the herd to home, twist and turn,
swish, swash, twirl.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit, tit, the titmouse,  
vaa, vaa, the sparrow,  
fly over the oat field fence,  
jump over the cabbage garden  
over the forest whistle -.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit, tit titmouse,  
look, look sparrow,  
fly over the bean field;  
jump over the cabbage patch,  
over the forest, whir!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tii tii titmouse  
vaa vaa sparrow  
fly over the fence  
jump over the cabbage patch  
whizz over the forest.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
It was beautiful to skip as a girl  
It was costly to raise a girl  
Even better to marry her off  
But she takes the milkmaid’s cow  
From Laugu stallion’s stable.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Swallow, swallow swallow,  
Swallow, swallow swallow.  
Where is your golden nest?  
In the forest on a dry spruce.  
Did that dry spruce grow?  
An old man cut it down,  
Where did that old man get it?  
Oxen plowed it down.  
Where did those oxen come from?  
A bear tore them down.  
Where did that bear come from?  
A German shot him down.  
Where did that German come from?  
The German went to town for a walk.  
Where did that town come from?  
The town burned to ashes.  
Where are those ashes and burnt soil?  
But the wind whistled through the reeds—  
Where did those reeds come from?  
The sickle whistled through the reeds!  
Where did that sickle come from?  
That is not known.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Oh, poor me, where is my wife?  
In the forest of Kihlevere with the goat herd.  
What is she doing there?  
Sitting on a new chair,  
sewing a new coat,  
patching an old sheepskin coat,  
with silk and thread,  
old woolen yarn.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Swallow on the border edge,  
lark in the field, skylark,  
where is your dear little nest?  
In the forest, on a dry spruce branch.  
Where is that dear dry spruce?  
The old man has cut it down.  
Where is that dear old man?  
He went to the garden patch.  
Where is that dear garden patch?  
Oxen have plowed it up.  
Where are those dear oxen?  
A bear had broken the oxen.  
Where is that dear little bear?  
In the front field is rye,  
in the back field is buckwheat,  
and in the middle field is a swirl of oats.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Sleep, sleep Maalu, weigh:
I have shut the fleas,
counted the bedbugs;
I put the mosquitoes in tongs.
The child can sleep quietly.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Ptruu, go to Riga,  
then back to Tallinn.  
Bring bread for the child:  
horn-shaped loaves,  
double-crust buns,  
flavored honey breads,  
sour breads with holes,  
the better the more adorned.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
:,: Sleep, sleep little heartbeat,  
Sleep little baby bundle. :,:
Mother went to the forest to get the baby,  
Set the cuckoo to rock,  
Set the summer bird to move.  
There the cuckoo fell asleep  
Because the summer bird sang too much.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
Susu, susu sugar egg.  
Mom went to the forest to get mammu.  
Started swinging the cradle,  
Moving the summer bird.  
There the cuckoo called out loud,  
The summer bird sang too much.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Make a cake, dip the bread,  
Lean pork,  
Dip in egg,  
Put a dollop of butter on top,  
Roll, roll, fall – quickly eat the bread!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri, little swallow,  
Where is your darling’s nest?  
In the forest on a wet pine,  
At home on the dry spruce.  
Where did that dry spruce come from?  
An old man cut it down.  
Where did that old man go?  
He crawled under the garden bed.  
Where did that garden bed come from?  
Draft oxen plowed it up.  
Where did those draft oxen come from?  
A bear tore them down.  
Where did that bear come from?  
A German shot it.  
Where did that German come from?  
The German went to the sauna, stuck his head in the bundling of birch twigs,  
The door locked, the cuckoo went cuckoo!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Son, ride to town,  
old man to Tallinn.  
Bring an apple, horse,  
bring a roll, tail.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
A goat goes to the herd,  
over the sea to the berry,  
bring me some hay.  
I give the hay to the sheep:  
the sheep gives me wool.  
I give the wool to my mother,  
mother makes me a warm knitted jacket  
for going to the herd.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Swallow at the edge of the border,  
flute at the end of the hornpipe,  
where is your dear nest  
behind the room on the oak branch?  
Where did that oak remain?  
The old man cut it down.  
Where did that old man remain?  
The old man died.  
Where did that death remain?  
Death went to hell.  
Where did that hell remain?  
Hell burned down.  
Where did that fire remain?  
The fire went out.  
Where did that stump remain?  
The bear tore the stump.  
Where did that bear remain?  
The bear went across the sea to Sweden.  
How can one get there?  
With nails, with nails,  
on old iron wheels,  
with pointed stakes.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Ptruu is going on a trip to Riga  
to the city of Tartu  
to bring tobacco for her father  
and some candy for her mother.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow, the meek little bird  
flew to the city's street  
from there flew to the funeral  
of the innkeeper's mother,  
Called together the wicked birds  
the eagle.  
The eagle was a fierce predator  
tore apart the jaws of the swallow,  
the swallow's wreath ribbon.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow, the meek little bird  
flew to the city's street  
from there flew to the funeral  
of the innkeeper's mother,  
Called together the wicked birds  
the eagle.  
The eagle was a fierce predator  
tore apart the jaws of the swallow,  
the swallow's wreath ribbon.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Hey little bee, sleepy mood,  
sleep long, grow big.  
Aidel as a haycutter,  
Taadi as an oak harvester,  
a hard wood clatterer.  
A long fart puller.  
A wide hem splitter.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit, tit chickadee,  
look, look, sparrow,  
Liri, lõõri, swallow,  
piiri, pääri, swallow,  
jump over the oat field,  
hop over the cabbage patch,  
over the forest - whir.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went to the herd's company group  
tail drooping, head down  
from the herd home, pulling, tugging, twisting  
clumsy, awkward, stumbling.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
A goat brought the village's herd,  
across the sea, berries,  
brought me hay,  
I gave the hay to the cow.  
The cow gave me milk,  
I gave the milk to the piglet,  
The piglet gave me a side (cut of meat),  
I gave the side to the village.  
The village gave me cake,  
I gave the cake to Kalev.  
Kalev gave me iron,  
I gave the iron to the sled.  
The sled gave me gold,  
I gave the gold to the oak.  
The oak gave me leaves,  
I gave the leaves to the sheep.  
The sheep gave me wool,  
I gave the wool to Veerik,  
Veerik gave me a pike (fish),  
Kalev’s neckerchief.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tit tit chickadee,  
Look, look sparrow,  
Lieri looli lark,  
border prince swallow,  
jump over the bean field,  
hop over the cabbage patch,  
over the forest – whizz!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
A goat is a herd of goats,  
berries across the sea.  
Bring me hay,  
I will give wool to the sheep,  
the sheep will give me wool,  
I will give the wool to the little twig  
for the shepherd’s shoes,  
to the servant big socks,  
to the master long trousers,  
to the groom a spruce hat.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The raven, a gentle little bird,  
flew to the town's street.  
From there it flew to the tavern,  
to the innkeeper's funeral.  
The raven made a gathering call,  
called together the sad birds.  
He did not call the eagle, -  
the eagle was a fierce predator.  
The woman made trousers, the old man made trousers,  
he himself already had trousers.  
Then three pairs of trousers came together,  
three new pairs of trousers in total.  
The blue ones were bridge-trousers,  
the red ones were wedding trousers,  
the yellow ones were suitor trousers,  
the white ones were raven trousers.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Ut-tut-utt - come to us!  
Our girl is coming to you tomorrow,  
bringing you bread  
and a tall bottle of milk.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Swing, swing little goat kid,  
Laala, laala little lamb!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Oh, poor me, where is my wife?  
In the goat herd at Kirevere manor!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
A goat leads the herd  
over the sea of berries.  
Bring me some hay,  
I will give it to the sheep,  
the sheep gives me wool,  
I give the wool to the spinner,  
to the shepherd, clogs,  
to the servant, large socks,
