to the master, long trousers,  
to the groom, a spruce hat!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Border-swallow swallow,  
lyre-lark lark,  
where is your darling's nest?  
Behind the room, on the oak tree.  
Where is that oak?  
The old man cut it down!  
Where is that old man?  
The old man died.  
Where is that death?  
Death went to hell.  
Where is that hell?  
Hell burned down.  
Where is that fire?  
The fire went out.  
Where is that stump?  
The bear tore the stump apart.  
Where is that bear?  
The bear went across the sea to Sweden.  
How can you follow there?  
With pegs, with pegs,  
old iron wheels,  
sharp-pointed poles!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Toot, toot toot come to us,  
our girl is coming to you tomorrow.  
Today we have a Swedish dance,  
the two-egg dance.  
The girl dances, the old man dances,  
I with Miku-Mari,  
the sexton in the kitchen with Triin,  
the gentleman with his lady!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lullaby, lullaby, hush-hush  
Hush, little child,  
sleep for a long time, grow up big,  
grow up to be our shepherd.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The man went to the sauna  
put his head in the sauna stove  
The knife went up his ass  
Let out a stinky fart  
The sauna caught fire,  
Where did the sauna ashes go?  
The wind carried them to the sea  
Where did the sea come from?  
A black ox drank it all up  
Where did the black ox come from?  
It ran into the forest reeds  
Where did the reeds come from?  
A sickle whistled down  
Where did the sickle come from?  
The sickle broke into five pieces  
Where did those sickle pieces go?  
The grindstone wore them down  
Where did the grindstone come from?  
A chicken scratched on the manure  
Where did the chicken come from?  
The eagle ate the chicken  
Where did the eagle come from?  
The old man shot it down  
Where did the old man come from?  
The man went to war to fight  
There his head was cut off  
And his blood was shed

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Oh, poor me, where is my wife?  
In the Tärivere forest with the goat herd.  
She sits on a golden chair  
on a silver-framed bucket,  
with silk, with thread,  
with double gold.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Border swallow  
where is your nest?  
Liiri, the lark  
where is your nest?  
Behind the room, on the oak tree  
Where is that oak?  
An old man cut the oak down.  
Where is that old man?  
The old man is under the garden bed.  
Where is that garden bed?  
The oxen plowed the garden bed.  
Where are those oxen?  
Bears broke the oxen.  
Where are those bears?  
Guns knocked the bears down.  
Where are those guns?  
Fire burned the guns.  
Where is that fire?  
Water extinguished the fire.  
Where is that water?  
The water is in the sea now.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri-lõõri, lõuke,  
Pääri-pääri, swallow!  
Where was your golden nest?  
In the forest on a dry spruce.  
Where did that dry spruce come from?  
An old man cut down the spruce.  
Where did that old man come from?  
The old man died.  
Where was he buried?  
In the flower bed of Pikapõllu.  
Where did the flower bed of Pikapõllu come from?  
Cursed swine plowed it up.  
Where did those cursed swine come from?  
They went on a long road.  
Where did that long road come from?  
Grass grew over the clearing.  
Where did the grass on the clearing come from?  
Ani ate the grass down.  
Where did Ani come from?  
Ani flew into the sky  
into the blue cloud,  
and the red cloud’s wedding,  
and the white cloud’s branch.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lõõri-lõõri, lõuke,  
pääri-pääri, swallow,  
where was your golden nest?  
In the forest on a dry spruce.  
Where did that dry spruce come from?  
An old man cut the spruce down.  
Where did that old man come from?  
The old man died.  
Where was he buried?  
In the garden of a long field.  
Where did that field garden come from?  
Servants plowed it up.  
Where did those servants come from?  
A bear broke the servants.  
Where did that bear come from?  
The bear jumped into the reeds.  
Where did those reeds come from?  
A sickle cut the reeds.  
Where did that sickle come from?  
A trickster tricked the sickles.  
Where did that trickster come from?  
A hen stirred in the manure.  
Where did that hen come from?  
A hawk took the hen away.  
Where did that hawk come from?  
The hawk flew to the town,  
to the blue eye,  
to the red wedding,  
to the white post,  
to the black funeral.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lõõri-lõõri, little bird,  
pääri-pääri, little swallow,  
where was your golden nest?  
On a dry spruce in the forest.  
Where did that dry spruce come from?  
An old man cut down the dry spruce.  
Where did that old man come from?  
A bear broke the old man.  
Where did that bear come from?  
The bear chewed the reed.  
Where did that reed come from?  
The sickle whistled it down.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri-lõõri, swallow,  
where is your dear little nest.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lilac, let the lilac be,
Let the cuckoo be.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri-lõõri swallow,  
Piiri-pääri swallow!  
Where is your golden nest?  
- In the forest, at the top of a dry fir.  
Where did that fir come from?  
- The village boy cut it down.  
Where did that village boy come from?  
- A bear tore him apart.  
Where did that bear come from?  
- The bear is in the tall reed.  
Where did that reed come from?  
- The sickle whistled it down.  
Where did that sickle come from?  
- The whetstone wore it out.  
Where did that whetstone come from?  
- The eagle broke it.  
Where did that eagle come from?  
- The eagle has flown to the town.  
Where did that town come from?  
- The town has burned down.  
Where has that town's ash gone?  
- The wind has blown the ash to the sky.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tui, tui, tui, tui, tui, tui, come to us,  
Our lady is coming, our lady is coming to you tomorrow,  
Wearing a skirt made of sackcloth,  
With a headscarf made of corduroy.

label: Lastelaulud
Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri-lõõri swallow,  
Piiri-pääri swallow!  
Where is your golden nest?  
In the forest, on the dry spruce branch.  
Where is that dry little spruce?  
The old man cut it down.  
Where is that old little man?  
Into the piece of garden field.  
Where is that old garden field?  
The yoked oxen plowed it up,  
The gray oxen drove it up,  
The black oxen pulled it up.  
Where are those old yoked oxen?  
I sent them down to the reed.  
Where is that old reed?  
The scythe mowed the reed.  
Where is that old little scythe?  
The (little) salamander crept, crawled hard.  
Where is that old little salamander?  
The hen scurried into the dung.  
Where is that old little hen?  
The hawk took it to the stump in the forest.  
Where is that old little stump?  
The bear scratched the little stump.  
Where is that old little bear?  
He jumped over nine forests,  
He jumped over eight forests.  
His head was visible above the forest,  
His feet were visible beneath the forest,  
His body twisted through the middle of the forest.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Come home, dear father,  
Come home, dear mother.  
Come home from the windy place,  
From the wide field,  
From the church tower.  
Bring me the wide-horned ones,  
The honey bears with honey,  
The best blacksmiths.  
But be quiet, little child,  
It seems they are already coming.  
The head appears above the forest,  
The feet rarely through the forest,  
The middle twisting over the forest.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Sleep, sleep little pulse
Sleep, little berry twig
Breathe gentle little leaf
Rest little lingonberry flower
A child will grow into a little lamb
A little lamb, a herdsman,
Brings me a basket of strawberries
A handful of cranberries
A sieve full of currants
Half a measure of lingonberries
A full measure of raspberries.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Mouth, mouth little child  
Let the mouth grow bigger  
Become stronger than others  
Grow straighter than others  
As heavy as a crumb of bread  
As tall as a straw  
As light as a healthy leg  
As slender as a berry stem  
As tall as a reed  
When will I repay grandmother's effort  
Grandmother's effort, tender milk  
Grandmother's dear growing up  
Sucking at the mouth  
Being carried on the hand  
Quiet by the ear  
How many nights were sleepless  
How many smokes without growth  
How many mornings to know  
The fire did not end from the stove  
The spark was not in the bedpost  
In the fart puddle, from the earth  
The woman from young clothes  
The grey horse from harness  
Looking for a child's talker  
The taker of the young cry  
I promise the lamb to the talker  
The goat to the tongue beater  
The peacock to the stallion knower  
To the taker of the young cry.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Mouth, mouth little child  
Let the mouth grow bigger  
Become stronger than others  
Grow straighter than others  
As heavy as a crumb of bread  
As tall as a straw  
As light as a healthy leg  
As slender as a berry stem  
As tall as a reed  
When will I repay grandmother's effort  
Grandmother's effort, tender milk  
Grandmother's dear growing up  
Sucking at the mouth  
Being carried on the hand  
Quiet by the ear  
How many nights were sleepless  
How many smokes without growth  
How many mornings to know  
The fire did not end from the stove  
The spark was not in the bedpost  
In the fart puddle, from the earth  
The woman from young clothes  
The grey horse from harness  
Looking for a child's talker  
The taker of the young cry  
I promise the lamb to the talker  
The goat to the tongue beater  
The peacock to the stallion knower  
To the taker of the young cry.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lõõri-lõõri little lark  
Piiri-pääri little swallow  
Where is your dear little nest  
In the forest grove, on top of the spruce  
Where did that spruce then go  
An old man chopped it down  
Where did that old man then go  
He crawled into the garden bed  
Where did the garden bed come from  
Oxen yoked plowed it up  
Where did those yoked oxen come from  
The bear yoked ate them up.  
Where did that bear itself come from  
The bear limped into the reed bed  
Where did that reed then come from  
The scythe hissed down  
Where did that scythe itself come from  
It wore itself out  
Where did that itself come from  
The hen scurried into the manure  
Where did that hen itself come from  
The hawk ate the hen.  
Where did that hawk itself come from  
The hawk flew into the sky  
Where did that hawk reach the sky  
Along a blue silk ribbon  
Along a red braided ribbon.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Sleep come in through the door  
Knock on the child's eyebrow  
Let it rest on the child's table  
For the child to take time  
To linger at twilight  
To whistle during the smoke.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Äia-tuia, let the swing go  
Kibi-käbi-nuia  
To cross the sea  
To fly over the forest  
To the forest folk’s wedding  
To old Villu's yard.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri-lõõri little flute  
Piiri-pääri little swallow  
Where is your golden nest?  
In the forest on a dry fir tree  
Where did that dry fir come from?  
An old man cut the fir down  
Where did that old man come from?  
The old man died  
Where was he buried?  
In the long bed of the field  
Where did that field bed come from?  
Oxen plowed it up  
Where did those oxen come from?  
A bear broke them down  
Where did that bear come from?  
The bear went to the long reeds  
Where did those reeds come from?  
A sickle mowed them down  
Where did that sickle come from?  
A hoe chopped it down  
Where did that hoe come from?  
A hen scratched in the manure  
Where did that hen come from?  
A hawk broke it down  
Where did that hawk come from?  
A Saxon shot it down  
Where did that Saxon come from?  
The Saxon went to town  
Where did that town come from?  
The town burned down  
Where did that fire come from?  
Water put it out  
Where did that water come from?  
The water ran into the sea  
Where did that sea come from?  
The sea grew into black land.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Sleep, sleep, father-in-law, garden  
Sleep comes hooting  
Roaring over the mountain  
Come, sleep, through the door  
Step in through the window  
Roll onto the child's bow  
Go onto the child's head.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Sleep, come in through the door  
Step onto the child's eye  
Walk onto the child's nest.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Grandfather, grandfather, let the swing go  
I sing to the child  
Just like a lamb to a lambkin  
To a mother grouse’s tender one  
To a little duckling.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Mood, mood from the wind's ridge  
Let loose wide from the ridge  
Spin from the church's ridge  
You bring horned rolls  
Honeyed breads delightful  
Triangular cakes  
Aspen leaves even better.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Swing, move little cradle  
Endure until autumn for me  
Let me go to Riga city  
From there I will bring you a braided ribbon  
A braided ribbon, a throat ribbon.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Sleep, sleep, little one  
Stump-stump, stick  
Let the cradle rock  
On top of the big stone  
Sleep come through the door  
Cuckoo on the child's eyebrow.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Lastelaulud
translation:
My bride was a little maiden  
Still rocking in the cradle  
Faint on the weak bowstring  
She had three swings  
One was under the home barn  
The second out in the field on the hill  
The third down in the meadow  
What was under the home barn  
Was smoking the pipe  
What was out in the field on the hill  
Was moving the birds  
What was down in the meadow  
 Was swaying the wind  
Oh, oh, my bride  
Let the smoke rise higher  
Grow a little stronger  
Stretch a bit taller  
As tall as a straw stalk  
As long as a drop of milk  
As heavy as a grain of sand.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Lastelaulud
translation:
My bride was a little maiden  
Still rocking in the cradle  
Faint on the weak bowstring  
She had three swings  
One was under the home barn  
The second out in the field on the hill  
The third down in the meadow  
What was under the home barn  
Was smoking the pipe  
What was out in the field on the hill  
Was moving the birds  
What was down in the meadow  
 Was swaying the wind  
Oh, oh, my bride  
Let the smoke rise higher  
Grow a little stronger  
Stretch a bit taller  
As tall as a straw stalk  
As long as a drop of milk  
As heavy as a grain of sand.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
A goat herds the flock,  
Over the sea, the berry.  
Bring me hay,  
I bring hay to the cow,  
The cow gives me milk,  
I give the milk to the piglet,  
The piglet gives me the lice,  
I give the lice to the mother-in-law,  
The mother-in-law gives me wool,  
I give the wool to the calf,  
For myself a good hat,  
For the master long pants,  
For the servant big socks,  
For the shepherd boy clogs.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Where did that old man come from?  
The old man died.  
Where was he buried?  
Between the garden plots.  
What happened to those garden plots?  
Speckled bulls plowed them up.  
Where did those speckled bulls come from?  
Bears broke the bulls.  
Where did those black bears come from?  
The old man killed the bear.  
Where did that old man come from?  
The old man died.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Äia-tuia, kibi-käbi club,  
Let the swing go  
Over the door into the yard  
Out the back end  
A somersault into the ditch.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Father-in-law, mother-in-law, club and stick,  
let the swing go.  
Over the door, out into the yard,  
to the end of the field,  
a somersault into the ditch.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Lastelaulud
translation:
Taverns manners, dirty manners  
No vodka without money is given  
No beer without buying  
No fishing without a permit  
Vodka tells me: it’s time to go  
Beer says: stay here  
What am I, a little beer  
Or what vodka, little vodka  
Others went fishing on Monday  
I will go on Tuesday  
The oxen are in front  
The plows are in the furrows in the lap  
I went behind the big hill  
I started to patch my shoe  
But the manor's black cat came  
Took away a suitable patch  
I chased after the cat  
The cat ran into the master's room  
I myself went into the mistress's room  
The mistress was cooking berry jam  
But I was tasting the jam  
The mistress scolded me  
The boy with a whip scolded me  
The girl chased me  
Then I got an enemy to curse  
The man in long trousers to weave  
Along the field, along the field  
Sometimes along the yards  
Then I got a bad blister on my foot  
Then I kissed the foot  
Hands and feet, hold on  
The capturers are coming  
Big mouths, thick noses  
Beards shaved away

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Lastelaulud
translation:
Taverns manners, dirty manners  
No vodka without money is given  
No beer without buying  
No fishing without a permit  
Vodka tells me: it’s time to go  
Beer says: stay here  
What am I, a little beer  
Or what vodka, little vodka  
Others went fishing on Monday  
I will go on Tuesday  
The oxen are in front  
The plows are in the furrows in the lap  
I went behind the big hill  
I started to patch my shoe  
But the manor's black cat came  
Took away a suitable patch  
I chased after the cat  
The cat ran into the master's room  
I myself went into the mistress's room  
The mistress was cooking berry jam  
But I was tasting the jam  
The mistress scolded me  
The boy with a whip scolded me  
The girl chased me  
Then I got an enemy to curse  
The man in long trousers to weave  
Along the field, along the field  
Sometimes along the yards  
Then I got a bad blister on my foot  
Then I kissed the foot  
Hands and feet, hold on  
The capturers are coming  
Big mouths, thick noses  
Beards shaved away

label: Lastelaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Iidu-Tiidu, I am coming to you  
To the quiet gentleman’s field  
Stockings torn, shoes broken  
I sit down on a stone  
I begin to mend the shoes  
Then out came a black cat  
A speckled cat from the writer  
An old gentleman’s white cat.  
I took my patch of the right size  
To chase after the cat  
The cat ran into the master’s house  
I ran into the mistress’s house  
The mistress cooked berry jam  
Melted sweet honey  
I am going to taste the honey  
Taste the berry jam  
The girl struck with a matchstick  
The housewife with a fart  
The grandma with a jam ladle  
Then I got the enemy to stammer  
The man wearing long pants to plot  
Along the field, along the field  
Sometimes past the garden’s fence  
I reached behind the corner of the garden  
There I looked back once  
Look, where the hell pit shows  
Looks like the devil’s house  
The hell pits are greening  
Ghost towers are standing around

label: Lastelaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Iidu-Tiidu, I am coming to you  
To the quiet gentleman’s field  
Stockings torn, shoes broken  
I sit down on a stone  
I begin to mend the shoes  
Then out came a black cat  
A speckled cat from the writer  
An old gentleman’s white cat.  
I took my patch of the right size  
To chase after the cat  
The cat ran into the master’s house  
I ran into the mistress’s house  
The mistress cooked berry jam  
Melted sweet honey  
I am going to taste the honey  
Taste the berry jam  
The girl struck with a matchstick  
The housewife with a fart  
The grandma with a jam ladle  
Then I got the enemy to stammer  
The man wearing long pants to plot  
Along the field, along the field  
Sometimes past the garden’s fence  
I reached behind the corner of the garden  
There I looked back once  
Look, where the hell pit shows  
Looks like the devil’s house  
The hell pits are greening  
Ghost towers are standing around

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Sleep, sleep, little Matsikene  
Sweet sleep to Mats  
Drink, drink, little Juku  
Juku's decorated little jug  
Play the pipe, little Peedu  
Peedu has delicate little fingers.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Taita  
Come, come from the top of the wind  
Let, let from the top of the flames  
Spin from in front of the church  
Bring hundreds of horned ones  
Honey cakes delightful  
Pies with two crusts.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Häia-tuia,  
Kibi-käbi stick  
Let the swing go  
Over the Kingu forest back, to the edge.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Swing, swing, little goat kid  
Sing, sing, little lamb  
The little goat kid has scratched eyes  
The little lamb has sores on its ears.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Sleep, sleep, little heartbeat  
Sleep, little teddy bear  
Sleep, sleep, little one  
Quickly, quickly, hurry  
Let the swing go  
Out the door and window  
Sleep come in through the door  
Come in through the door and window  
Come into the child's eye  
Go before the child's hands  
Sit on the child's eyebrow  
Let it rest on the child's table.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Hello, hello, Tepermann!  
From Tepermann came a maruna,  
From maruna came Mari's pipe,  
From Mari's pipe came a pibun,  
From pibun came a baker,  
From baker came a salt box,  
From salt box came a tremor,  
From tremor came a rainbow,  
From rainbow came a cab,  
From cab came a beautiful forest.  
In that forest, people drank mead,  
Silver mugs were cherished,  
Tin dishes well respected;  
Wooden utensils, clothèries,  
Inside five kinds of oak,  
Brass on the wall.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lõõri-lõõri little cuckoo  
Piiri-pääri little swallow  
Where is your darling's nest?  
In the field under a big stone  
Where is the stone from the field?  
A black bull plowed that stone  
Where is the black bull from?  
A bear killed the black bull  
Where did that bear come from?  
The bear went across the sea to Sweden  
It won't come out before  
Bridges are built across the Baltic Sea

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, little cuckoo  
Where did you get your golden nest?  
In the forest, on top of a dry spruce  
Where did that dry spruce come from?  
An old man chopped it down  
Where did that old man come from?  
He sits on the garden bed  
Where did that garden bed come from?  
Oxen plowed it up  
Where did those oxen come from?  
A bear killed the oxen  
Where did that bear come from?  
The bear jumped into the reeds  
Where did those reeds come from?  
He whistled down the scythe  
Where did that scythe come from?  
Luisku lied about the scythe  
Where did that Luisku come from?  
A hen scratched in the manure  
Where did that hen come from?  
The cuckoo took the hen to the sky.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Fingerless gloves in a bundle,  
A bundle and a lamp,  
A lamp and a mitten;  
From a mitten came a market thumb,  
From a market thumb came a pebble,  
From a pebble came a cross peider,  
From a peider came rinulad,  
From rinula came a breast pin,  
From a breast pin came porous ones,  
From porous ones came fingerless gloves.  
Fingerless gloves in a bundle,  
A bundle and a lamp,  
A lamp and a mitten.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Let the rye grow taller,
Let it grow stronger,
Let it grow a bit longer,
Higher than a straw,
Heavier than a crumb of bread,
Smarter than a grain of buckwheat,
By sliding, your socks became big,
By swinging, your shoes became new.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Sleep, sleep, little blink  
Sleep, sleep, little mamma.  
Sleep little mamma's child,  
Row the swing, reach the swing,  
Row the swing to the sunny land,  
Where the roosters drink gold,  
Roosters gold, hens silver,  
Ducks green silver,  
Drakes drink river water,  
Goslings drink forest water,  
Lads drink woodland water.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Sleep, sleep, little blink  
Sleep, sleep, little mamma.  
Sleep little mamma's child,  
Row the swing, reach the swing,  
Row the swing to the sunny land,  
Where the roosters drink gold,  
Roosters gold, hens silver,  
Ducks green silver,  
Drakes drink river water,  
Goslings drink forest water,  
Lads drink woodland water.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Loori-loori, little loon  
Piiri-paari, little headband  
Where is your precious little nest?  
In the forest on a dry spruce branch  
Where did that dry spruce come from?  
An old man chopped it down  
Where did that old man go?  
The old man died  
Where was he buried?  
In a long field bed  
Where did that bed come from?  
Oxen plowed it up  
Where did those oxen come from?  
A bear tore the oxen  
Where did that bear come from?  
A gun shot the bear down  
Where did that gun come from?  
Fire burned the gun down  
Where did that fire come from?  
Water extinguished the fire  
Where did that water come from?  
A black bull drank it up  
Where did that bull come from?  
Jumped into the river, plunged into the river  
Where did that river come from?  
Reeds grew on it  
Where did those reeds come from?  
A sickle rang down  
Who sharpened the sickle?  
A glint sharpened the sickle  
Where did that glint come from?  
A hen was scratching in the manure  
Where did that hen come from?  
A hawk ate the hen  
Where did that hawk come from?  
The hawk disappeared.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Breast, breast little child,  
Breast, breast for the older one,  
A drop of milk for fullness,  
For the height of the cream pitcher.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Purr-narr to the corner
Pirr-parr to the duck
Great tit to the gate
Finch to the stake.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Kuissu-kuissu, dear children,  
Häia-häia, good children.  
Suisu-suisu, winter child  
Grow into a winter chicken,  
A hay-season duck,  
A rye-slice little bird.  
I take care of the servant’s child.  
I care for the servant boy.  
I take care of the servant’s child,  
The winter boy at the oven.  
The lazy mistress’s child.  
This is my winter knitting needle,  
This is my winter spindle spun cone,  
Spring carding.  
Autumn yarn bundle.  
Spring’s precious fabric bundle.  
Weekday pastime,  
Sunday’s little toy:  
Here the mitten swings,  
Weakly it sways while being pulled,  
Hugged with two hands,  
Carefully held here.  
So that the wind doesn’t come,  
Nor the sun shine,  
So that the rain showers don’t roll,  
Nor hailstones fall.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lõõri-lõõri little bird,  
Pääri-pääri little swallow,  
Where is your golden nest?  
In the forest on a dry spruce.  
Where did the dry spruce come from?  
An old man cut the dry spruce.  
Where did the old man come from?  
The old man died.  
Where was he buried?  
In a long field garden,  
Where did the field garden come from?  
Plowing bulls plowed it up.  
Where did the plowing bulls come from?  
A bear tore the plowing bulls apart.  
Where did the bear come from?  
The bear crawled into the reeds.  
Where did the reeds come from?  
A sickle mowed the reeds.  
Where did the sickle come from?  
A blade wore down the sickle.  
Where did the blade come from?  
A hen scratched in the manure.  
Where did the hen come from?  
A hawk snatched the hen away.  
Where did the hawk come from?  
The hawk flew over the sea.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Tune, tune, little lark,  
Fly, fly, little swallow,  
Where is the golden nest?  
In the forest on the dry spruce tip  
Where the dry spruce has grown.  
The old man has cleared it away.  
Where has the old man gone?  
He has gone to the garden patch.  
Where is the garden patch?  
The worked oxen.  
Where are the worked oxen?  
The bear has broken the worked oxen.  
Where is the little bear?  
The bear has gone into the reeds.  
Where are the reeds?  
They have been cut down with a sickle.  
Where is the sickle?  
It has been worn out hard.  
Where has it gone?  
The hen has scratched in the dung,  
Where is the little hen?  
The hawk has taken the little hen.  
Where is the little hawk?  
The hawk has flown under the sky.  
How did he get there?  
Along the red store ribbon,  
Along the blue silk strap,  
Along the silver chain,  
Through the magnificent mist,  
Through five rainbows,  
Between white clouds,  
Into the blue cloud.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Make a cake, dip the bread  
Put a lot of butter on it  
Smooth it with pork  
Roll it with an egg  
Put it in the oven, take it out  
Someone? – zap the cake!

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
The dog went to the herd in a group  
Came home quickly.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Wander, wander, little granny  
Come to us, little wanderer  
Bring bread a hundred ways  
Village bread in the dawn’s manner  
Old bread in the grave’s manner  
In the field in the plow’s manner  
Honey bread from the honey maker  
Black bread from the baker  
Blue as the sea’s edge  
Fancy Perch from Peipus  
Praised herrings from Arju  
Big herrings from Finland  
Cowslips from the alder grove  
Stumps with burnt mushrooms  
Raisin-like small mushrooms  
To buy clothes with money in advance  
With a penny and two in advance  
Half a penny and a half in advance  
Ten pennies and a die in advance.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Grandfather, grandfather,  
Fir cone, club,  
Over the door, outside,  
To the edge of the clearing,  
A somersault into the ditch.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
A ride, a ride there:  
From there a loaf of bread,  
A long bottle of milk,  
A wide, flat loaf of bread.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Come, come little sleep,
Fall upon the child's eyelids,
Narrow the eyes little by little.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
One does not make one,  
Two do not attack the sofa,  
Three not at once.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Liiri-lõõri swallow  
Piiri-peari small chick  
Where is the golden little boy?  
In the forest, on the tip of a dry spruce.  
Where has the dry spruce stayed?  
The old man cut down the dry spruce!  
Where has the old man remained?  
The old man has died!  
Where was he buried?  
Under the long field garden!  
Where has the field garden stayed?  
The oxen have plowed it up!  
Where have those oxen gone?  
The bears have eaten the plowed oxen!  
Where have those bears remained?  
The bears have gone to the reeds!  
Where have those reeds gone?  
The sickle has mowed the reeds!  
Where has the sickle itself stayed?  
The scythe was lying near the sickle!  
Where has the scythe itself stayed?  
The black rooster ran to the manure!  
Where has that manure stayed?  
The manure was taken to the field!  
Where has that black rooster stayed?  
The hawk flew to the black rooster!  
Where has that hawk itself stayed?  
The hawk flew under the sky!  
How did he get there?  
Along the red shoelace  
Along the blue thread!  
Where did he go from under the sky?  
From under the sky, he went to Rootsimoa!  
Where has Rootsimoa stayed?  
Rootsimoa has burned down!  
Who put it out?  
The water put it out!  
Where has that water itself gone?  
The water flowed into the sea!  
The sea came under our yard  
A little swamp is in the middle  
What is on this swamp?  
A pillar is on this swamp.  
What is on top of this pillar?  
A sieve is on top of this pillar!  
What is inside the sieve?  
Seven children are inside the sieve:  
They all govern the weather  
They guide the sea wind.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Ride, ride, to town,  
What to go there for?  
To bring bread from there.  
The horse brings the sheep,  
The tail brings the bread,  
The anus brings onions,  
A tuft to the fiery nose,  
A lick for the child’s whip.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Mother-in-law, spruce,
a twig, cone, and club.
Start the swing,
Throw the bow aside.
Horse, go to Riga,
Behind to Tallinn;
The horse brings apples,
The tail brings bread rolls,
The flange brings bread,
The poop hole brings onions.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lyre’s tune, little flame  
Border’s edge, little stone  
Where is your dear little nest?  
In the forest, on top of a dry spruce.  
Where is that dear dry spruce?  
An old man has whistled it down.  
Where is that dear old man?  
The old man has died.  
Where is his dear little grave?  
Outside, under the field’s edge.  
Where is that dear field’s edge?  
Working oxen have plowed it up.  
Where are those dear working oxen?  
A bear has killed the working oxen.  
Where is that dear little bear?  
The bear has hidden in the reed.  
Where is that dear reed?  
The scythe has whistled it down.  
Where is that dear little scythe?  
It has worn itself out thoroughly.  
Where is that dear worn one?  
A hen has shuffled into the manure.  
Where is that dear little hen?  
The gold has killed the hen.  
Out in the field is the pea,  
In the middle are the nice wheats,  
At the back is the barley.  
Where is that dear little gold?  
A hunter has shot the gold down.  
Where is that dear little hunter?  
God has killed the hunter.  
Where is that dear God?  
God is now in heaven.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lõõri lõõri little lark
Where is your dear nest
In the forest on a dry spruce top
Where is that dear dry spruce
The old man cut it down
Where is that dear old man
Resting on the field edge
Where is that dear field edge
The variegated oxen plowed it up
Where are those dear variegated oxen
A bear tore the variegated oxen
Where is that dear bear
In the forest in tall reed grass
Where is that dear reed grass
The sickle whistled it down
Loudly clanged it down
Where is that dear sickle
I placed a lid outside
Where is that dear lid
The hen flew off the lid
Where is that dear hen
The hawk took the hen over the sea
Over the sea into the hands of men
Over the island into the hands of Saxons
Into the hands of small boys.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Grandfather Tuia, stick and club,
Let the hand go!
Spruce and juniper on the edge,
A pile of wood and a bundle of twigs,
Two ells of Robuska,
Five old sheep,
Ten lambs.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lastelaulud
translation:
Oh, poor little titmouse!
Titmouse, little titmouse.
Where should I make my nest?
Family pecks and picks.
On the farm’s wise buildings.
The daughter, a young maiden,
Went to fetch wood from the stack.
She knocked my nest off the stack.
From the farm’s wise buildings.
Oh, poor little titmouse,
Titmouse, little titmouse!
Where should I make my nest?
Lay eggs inside?
Brood the young below?
On the dry spruce tree in the forest,
Or behind the oak tree.
Where did that dry spruce come from?
An old man cut down the spruce.
Where did that old man come from?
The old man died.
Where was he buried?
Buried in Matsi’s field.
Where did Matsi’s field come from?
Shod oxen plowed it up.
Where did those shod oxen come from?
The bear ate the shod oxen.
Where did that bear come from?
The hunter shot the bear.
Where did that hunter come from?
The hunter went to town.
Where did that town come from?
The town burned down.
Where did the town’s ashes go?
To the street of the new town,
Under the rampart of the old town.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lastelaulud
translation:
Oh, poor little titmouse!
Titmouse, little titmouse.
Where should I make my nest?
Family pecks and picks.
On the farm’s wise buildings.
The daughter, a young maiden,
Went to fetch wood from the stack.
She knocked my nest off the stack.
From the farm’s wise buildings.
Oh, poor little titmouse,
Titmouse, little titmouse!
Where should I make my nest?
Lay eggs inside?
Brood the young below?
On the dry spruce tree in the forest,
Or behind the oak tree.
Where did that dry spruce come from?
An old man cut down the spruce.
Where did that old man come from?
The old man died.
Where was he buried?
Buried in Matsi’s field.
Where did Matsi’s field come from?
Shod oxen plowed it up.
Where did those shod oxen come from?
The bear ate the shod oxen.
Where did that bear come from?
The hunter shot the bear.
Where did that hunter come from?
The hunter went to town.
Where did that town come from?
The town burned down.
Where did the town’s ashes go?
To the street of the new town,
Under the rampart of the old town.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Hello, hello Teppermann  
From Teppermann I became a mammoth  
From mammoth I became a string instrument  
From string instrument I became a pipe  
From pipe I became a fiddler  
From fiddler I became Sõmer  
From Sõmer I became a saltbox  
From saltbox I became a hammer  
From hammer I became a winnowing rope  
From winnowing rope I became a hat  
From hat I became a hunter,  
The hunter went to the forest to hunt  
On Sunday morning,  
On weekdays in the evening.  
What did he find in the forest?  
He found a child lying down  
Among the dogwood bushes.  
He took the child on his lap  
Wrapped the child in swaddling clothes  
Took her to the girls’ room  
To the center of the wreath-hedged room  
Asked and listened  
Whose child this was.  
One swore, another swore  
Quarreling, shouting, maiden  
She swore she was the old woman;  
He took the child on his lap  
Took her to the bridegroom’s room  
To the center of the hats.  
Asked and listened  
Whose child this was.  
Hannus, a great German man  
He swore he was the old strong one:  
He must be thrown into the water  
God gave Märta a blue apron.  
He must be thrown into the fire  
God gave Hannus a red hat,  
He must be pushed into the flames  
God loosened the child’s tongue.  
The child began to speak.  
Then they asked and listened  
Who was your father?  
Hannus, a great German man  
He was my father.  
Quarreling, shouting, maiden,  
She was my mother.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lõõri lõõri little swallow  
pääri pääri little swallow  
where is the golden nest  
in the forest on the dry spruce top  
where has the dry spruce gone  
the old man has cut it down  
where has the old man gone  
to the edge of the field  
where has the field’s edge gone  
oxen have plowed the land  
where have the oxen gone  
the bear has broken the oxen’s yoke  
where has the little bear gone  
the bear has crouched in the reed  
where is the reed for the pipe  
the sickle has rusted down  
where is the sickle  
worn out hard  
where have the hard ones gone  
the chicken pecked at the dung  
where has the chicken gone  
when it flew under the sky  
how did you say it  
along silver strings  
along red ribbon shops  
along blue silk straps.

label: Lastelaulud
translation:
Lõõri lõõri little swallow  
pärri pärri sweet little bird  
where is the golden little nest  
In the forest on the dry spruce top  
where did the dry spruce go  
an old man has cut it down  
where did the old man go  
he plowed the field’s fine soil  
where did the field's edge go  
oxen have plowed the field  
where have the oxen gone  
a bear has broken the oxen  
where has the little bear gone  
the bear has eaten the green grass  
where has the green grass gone  
the sickle has been whistled down  
where did the sickle go  
it has worn away sharply  
where did the sharpness go  
a chicken was fluttering in the dung heap  
where did the little chicken go  
it has gone beneath the sky  
where has it gone and how  
along silver paths  
along a red ribbon’s edge  
along a blue silk strap.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before, another partner  
She was plump and red  
She was pale and sickly  
She was healthy and sharp  
I took her to the tender hayfield  
To the dry, golden meadow  
She mowed hay, rustled the forest  
Took the swath, caught in the forest  
Took the bow, took the two  
Slept on the third  
Put her apron under her head;  
The apron strings as a pillow  
The apron straps as bedding  
I went home crying:  
Under the stable yard  
The woman came out to meet me, the old man came out to meet me  
Immediately asking me:  
Why do you cry, young lad?  
Why should I cry, old lady?  
Or why should I cry, old man?  
I answered, I told:  
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before, another partner  
She was plump and red  
She was pale and sickly  
She was healthy and sharp  
I took her to the tender hayfield  
To the dry, golden meadow  
She mowed hay, rustled the forest  
Took the swath, caught in the forest  
Took the bow, took the two  
Slept on the third  
Put her apron under her head  
The apron strings as a pillow  
The apron straps as bedding  
She began to limp!  
From the pig seller to sleep  
From the shoelace to shrivel  
From the sole to fall  
The old woman replied, answering:  
Oh, you crazy young lad!  
Oh, you crazy and foolish  
Throw the city girls away  
Barley rolled directly  
Oats following in pairs  
Do one deed without alcohol  
Half a vat of moonshine;  
Five vats of beer  
Oh, woman is ale’s delight:  
Sharp woman is beer’s joy,  
Plague woman is beer barrel’s pleasure;  
Then the woman will be healthy!  
Like other women. -

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When can I go to my father!
When can I go to my mother?
When will I roll to my brothers;
When will I reach out to my sister?
In autumn the rivers are deep;
In spring the waves are wide.
Night’s glance and darkness
The field is broad and open!
Willow
Thick and full of water.
The apple falls into the brook
Amongst the mossy hummocks;
Into the chariot of the horse,
In the bend of the gray time;
Into the wave of the clear lake!

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When I was growing up at father's home  
When I was growing up at mother's home!  
I used to scold my brother in the yard  
Sometimes I got to eat in the barn  
Even more in the living room  
With a share in the chamber.  
Indeed, I got to eat in the kitchen too  
Secretly eat in the sauna too  
Now I can cry in the corner  
Sometimes cry in the barn too  
Even more in the living room  
With a share in the chamber.  
Indeed, I can cry in the kitchen too  
Secretly cry in the sauna too  
I cry with harsh words  
I shout demanding order  
The den of the bloodhound

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
My fiancé is a musical instrument maker  
Along with a kannel maker  
He takes me to bed with an instrument  
With a kannel to the chamber  
Puts the instrument’s head below  
Between the kannel’s ceiling  
Rises with the morning  
If I don’t hear, he wakes me  
Takes the instrument from under its head  
From between the kannel’s ceiling  
Lets the instrument call to me  
My kannels announce:  
"Rise, bird, milk the cow,  
Rise, hen, send the herd."  
Your fiancé is a whip maker  
Along with a wooden rod maker  
He takes you to bed with a whip  
With a wooden rod to the chamber  
Puts the whip’s head below,  
Between the wooden rod’s ceiling  
Rises with the morning  
If you don’t hear, he wakes you  
Takes the whip from under its head  
From between the wooden rod’s ceiling  
Lets the whip call to you  
Your wooden rods announce:  
"Rise, lazy one, milk the cow!  
Rise, herder, send the herd."

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, my dear God!
Why did you let me sleep?
If this had been followed through
Men would have been mixed without seed.
Other fellows gave donations
Wore pretty neckwear
I was given black and foolish
A tar stump and a sick one.
Where to go away with the black;
To hasten with the sick,
To rush with the foolish?
Where there is a pond, I shake there
Where there is water, I splash there.
A bar of soap on my wall,
A white towel on the rack;
Why do I wash the pergelida
With a scanty rasp of raspberry?!

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
O, God, dear God!  
Why did you leave me sleeping  
When the feast was held  
And the drink was being passed  
Men’s seed was mixed  
The gift of a man's happiness.  
Others got handsome ones, all nice  
I got a black and foolish one  
A charred stump and a sickly one  
Where should I go with the black  
Hurrying with the foolish one  
Facing the sickly one  
Rolling with the angry one.  
Where there is water, there I splash  
Where there is food, there I swim  
Where there is a puddle, I rinse.  
I have a piece of soap on the wall  
A white towel on the rod:  
So I can make the man clean  
Clean, fine  
Fashionable among others  
White among the crowd.  
Now, in spite, they tease me  
Laughing through jokes.  
In spite, the lords tease me  
In spite, the ladies tease me  
The Germans laugh through jokes.  
They do not think to understand  
Do not know the stars  
Do not guess the signs  
I understand, why they do not understand.  
I know the stars in the sky  
Guess the eels in the sea  
Count the burbots in the waves  
So why, in spite, is the teasing  
The laughing through jokes.  
I am not laughed at by a wise woman  
Nor by the diligent young girl  
I am laughed at by a lazy woman  
By a spiteful girl  
Called a dung heap  
Slandered as a sleeper of pawns  
A skater at the pub doors  
A mover of ribbons and ties.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I was at home, paid for gold  
Father’s crooked yard  
The copper brother’s scent  
The tin brother’s path.  
I roll like an egg on the grass  
Twist like a ball on the field  
A German apple in the yard.  
Now Mari got others’ homes  
The goose got others’ springs  
The duck got others’ places  
The grouse other waters.  
Now you shouldn’t pay attention to the land  
That is under the goose’s wing  
The color between the toes  
The feather of a bird’s wing.  
Now a dog boy courts me  
A starving servant bites  
A bear’s keeper washes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Ox, ox, young virgin,  
Ox’s beautiful hay,  
If the hay is not mowed,  
Ox’s beautiful arch,  
If the meadow is not taken.  
Ox’s thin calves,  
If the rake is not used.  
Ox’s bright piles,  
If the cart is not started.  
Ox’s young maiden,  
If the boy is untouched.  
Never touched the dear hand,  
Nor felt the young finger.  
If the dear hand has touched the boy,  
Felt the hand caress:  
It immediately begins to wither,  
With eyes wilting at the sight.  
It starts to suffer in the knees,  
Falls into bad sleep,  
Fumbles around aimlessly.  
“Village old women, little old women,  
Help me understand,  
Give advice for a bit:  
Last year I took a young wife,  
The year before the other half.  
I took her to the tender hayfield,  
Put gold over the arch.  
The hay was mowed, the forest trapped.  
The willow undergrowth cracked.  
It immediately began to wither,  
With eyes wilting at the sight.  
It started to suffer in the knees,  
Fell into bad sleep,  
Fumbled around aimlessly.”  
The village old women answer back:  
“Throw the dirt from the lime kiln,  
Barley lies flat and scattered,  
Rye into the field ditch.  
A great feast will come to you,  
A big feast, a long feast.  
A great pauper will come to your family,  
Will fill your house.”

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
The mistress, the little lady,  
The master, the little master:  
"Look for new servants,  
Try out new herdsmen,  
Find new bread-eaters,  
Ask for new wage-workers:  
My year is up,  
My weeks have been seen,  
The garlanded years have been kept.  
A man's bread is yet untasted,  
His share of the cake untried.  
He should go to taste it,  
To try it for a month or two,  
To show himself for a week.  
To patch it for a few months,  
To seek a head to harvest.  
Then he will be born to return,  
To lay money on the table.  
Old man, old devil,  
Give me my honor in my hand,  
Slip red into my cheek.  
I am going back to Tapa,  
To be a girl in another place."

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
The mistress, the little lady,  
The master, the little master:  
"Look for new servants,

Find new bread-eaters,  
Ask for new wage-workers:

Give me my honor in my hand,  
Slip red into my cheek.

To be a girl in another place."

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I have already been waiting for it,  
I have been watching for it,  
I patiently endured,  
My shoes tore from it,  
My laces begged for it,  
The bull’s hide groaned:  
The last servant of Viru,  
The continued hat of Järva,  
The bad shepherd boy of Harju.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Clean the straw, rinse the straw
Wash the straw finely,
Beat the straw to whiteness
The straw comes from the straw house
It starts knocking on the door
Swearing at the manure
Throws socks onto the manure,
Shoes onto the midden heap.
Surely Maie will take the land
Anne to the oven and the barn,
Kai will carry to the chamber,
If Mai does not take the land,
Anne will not take to the oven or barn,
Kai will not carry to the chamber:
Surely Matar will take the land,
Kansik will carry to the chamber.
I do not look at the calf sheds,
Stopping at the pigsties
There I curse the farmhand,
Scolding the courier.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Village maids, language maids,  
Parish maids, liars:  
They said under the apron is better,  
More honest under the hem.  
I was eager to believe,  
Foolish to take advice,  
Immediately I jumped to test it!  
Let them take the apron’s hem,  
Under the hem of the hem, -  
You can suffer a lot on the apron,  
You can be sick under the hem! -

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Called to be a son-in-law,  
A family daughter’s husband,  
Chosen as the owner of a silver ring,  
Listener to a golden necklace,  
Observer of a copper necklace,  
Time came, the day rolled by,  
The night was spent asleep,  
Two nights were yoked together.  
The father-in-law began to bark,  
The mother-in-law began to bark!  
"What did you have when you came to the fire?  
You didn’t have fire when you came,  
The iron of the sled behind you,  
The little whip of reed,  
The pike tail of the sled."  
I heard, answered back:  
"What did you have to show off with,  
By the name of the maiden;  
"One was a glove in the coffin,  
That too was foolish without fingers,  
Blind without a groom.  
"The father got one sock,  
"The best man got half a ribbon,  
"The bride’s rider got a glove finger—  
That was your boasting,  
By the name of the maiden." -

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
What use is a tall man,  
Too proud of his height:  
The proud one wants to go to the tavern,  
Politely count money,  
Sits at the edge of the tavern table,  
Beer mug on his knee,  
Another tankard in hand,  
Money on the tavern table;  
Comes home in the evening  
In a drunken stupor,  
Comes inside, takes off his shoes,  
Throws the shoes askew,  
Socks near the big door,  
He himself looking sideways  
Whether the woman will take young men  
Or gather the household’s work.  
If the woman does not take young men,  
Nor gather the household’s work,  
He takes a whip off the bench,  
From the ruffian’s pocket too,  
Drags the woman’s skin along,  
Hits the little one hard;  
The woman jumps into the room,  
Across the yard to the storehouse,  
Starts swearing loudly,  
Cursing the scoundrels,  
Where are my beautiful ribbons?  
Or my proper ties?  
Where is my beautiful quilt?  
My aching beautiful carpet?  
Could have made the home shabby,  
Helped father to gray it,  
Made mushrooms grow on the wall edge  
Before wasting on the bad one,  
Murmuring to the cruel one,  
Whispering to the evil one,  
Wandering steamily around.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Called a servant  
To help the father- and mother-in-law  
To endure the blows from the father- and mother-in-law,  
To be the feeder of the father- and mother-in-law.  
I do not want this  
To become a servant.  
This is fastened tightly  
To the sled of a bright horse.  
Not suitable to be a servant.  
Let me buy a new horse  
A bright-horse sled.  
I will nail silver shoes underneath  
I will cover it with a red blanket  
More dear, woven by a weaver.  
I will put ruble pieces on the runners.  
Then I will go to find my own  
To feel my dear one  
To claim my girl.  
Then the room will be free of wind  
The house will be on a good site  
I will find the house in the forest  
I will find the cottage in the woods.  
I went inside to look  
In front were polished walls  
At the back were hewn boards  
Three were gold on the table  
One was the groom, the other the girl  
The third the handsome master's wife  
The fourth the dear companion wife.  
Wait, master's wife  
Be patient, companion wife  
Hold on, young man  
Let me teach the sister  
Admonish the young maiden  
If you found a place for bread  
And you got a place for rolls  
You knew a place for sprouting grain.  
Listen then, dear maiden, to the talks  
Of the young man's teasing  
The man is angry, you be sad  
Soften your heart  
Walk with a wise mind  
Then you will be praised well.  
A good girl has learned well  
A wise girl has been taught to dance  
Has brought the best to her husband.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
They said to me and said  
They thought and thought  
That my father-in-law is quick-tempered  
Mother-in-law is nagging  
Still a bit angry  
The woman speaks loudly  
What is now, later  
That my father-in-law is quick-tempered  
Mother-in-law is nagging  
Still a bit angry  
The woman speaks loudly  
Better, better, little old lady  
Better to the women's village  
And this lack of grudges.  
I am going to plow for the woman  
The father-in-law I am going to harness  
To the brother-in-law’s mill  
Grudges are boiling there  
Seeking to make peace with words  
To exchange the path of enmity

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud abielust
translation:

That my father-in-law is quick-tempered  
Mother-in-law is nagging

Better to the women's village  
And this lack of grudges.

The father-in-law I am going to harness  
To the brother-in-law’s mill  
Grudges are boiling there  
Seeking to make peace with words  
To exchange the path of enmity

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Little sister, darling  
I have a groom, you have a groom  
My groom is a piper  
Dear kannel player  
Takes my piper to sleep  
Takes the kannel player to the chamber  
Helps my piper up  
From the kannel player's chamber  
Your groom is a whip maker  
Dear whip wielder  
Takes your whip to sleep  
Takes the whip to the chamber  
Drives the whip up  
From the whip wielder’s chamber  
Get up, heifer, milk the cow  
Old heifer, feed the calf

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, my dear honey man  
Made from fish bones  
From the other side of the partridge bones  
Does not take away the wool nail  
Does not carry the thistle nail  
Jumps on the hop nail  
Does not beat with one stroke  
Scolds with two strokes  
Let me make six strokes crisp  
A chick eats eight  
Then makes a little whip from thread  
A little stem from oat straw  
Then starts beating the crisp  
Punishing the chick.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Wait, wait, my dear ones  
Be patient, dear clans  
Let autumn come  
Step, lovely little time  
I will lay down a big feather  
I will kill the winter bull  
Valleys flowing with beer  
Mountains playing hide and seek  
When I go to meet my own  
And I reach the sister hill  
I bake moon cakes  
Day-child’s loaves  
I wash the season’s year  
I found my father on the meadow  
My brother on the meadow’s edge  
There my horse neighs  
Father’s harm is a pike (uncertainty in metaphor)  
Opposite me came a dear brother  
Opposite bloom sisters  
In the dark my brother’s servants  
Many my brother’s paid ones.  
One there quarreled on the heart’s bridge  
Another fought, knocked on the bridge  
A third tapped on the shoulder  
The fourth held the reins of oxen  
I came to the Viru brothers  
To the sister hill in Saaremaa  
Mother went quickly to cook food  
Father went quickly to kill the bull  
Sister went quickly to knead sourdough  
Brother went to bring fresh spirits  
Mother dear, darling  
Do not go quickly to cook food  
Do not go quickly to kill the bull  
I am not coming because of food  
I am not coming because of drink  
I came to see my own  
To visit my dear ones  
I came to the Viru brothers  
To the sister hill in Saaremaa.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Neither fieldwork tires me
Nor outdoor work exhausts me
Nor the pain in my thigh.
Break me with a well-digger’s hoe
Tire me with empty talks
Trouble me with false tongues.
Stories ran, I did not run
Speeches happened, I did not go
Stories flowed without drinking
Speeches without ever entering the tavern
Because I happened upon a drinking companion
Right away a tavern-goer
The drinker has no money
The tavern-goer has a few coins.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Brother, little brother  
I am going to Narva to bring a wife  
From across the other side of Petersburg  
Do not go dressed as a man  
With an old ploughman's hat  
Skip down to the cellar  
Step down to the barns  
Take new clothes from the barn  
White shirts from the clothes rod  
Then go to Narva to bring a wife  
From across the other side of Petersburg  
Go through Lihula  
The little tin bell is ticking  
The iron cart is rattling  
The fire sings a tune below  
On the other side of the black grouse's perch  
A cuckoo behind in the sleigh basket  
A bunch of girls came in front of the carriage  
The maiden, however, behind the sleigh  
Whose handsome boy is that  
Whose sweet lad  
Whose fire sings a tune below  
On the other side of the black grouse's perch  
A cuckoo behind in the sleigh basket.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Yesterday the weddings began  
And today the lunch ended.  
My wife very ill  
Very ill in bed  
Old women at the bedside  
I stand and watch  
God knows what to do about it  
War is sad and the mood is bleak  
Old grandmothers argue  
Go outside, young man  
Go outside, kill time  
Sharpening the stick shed  
Breaking the branch shed.  
I went outside, killing time  
I did not sharpen the stick shed  
Nor break the branch shed  
I came back inside  
Found a moth under the apron  
A little under the rag  
A lump under the six edges  
I was very startled  
It is not my fault  
It is the blacksmith apprentice’s fault  
The labor of the tailor boys  
I begin to bark at the woman  
To corner the young woman  
You could have made noise in the forest  
You could have shouted in the grove  
Could have flown in the poplar grove  
That came into our chamber  
Old grandmothers quiet down  
Be quiet, young men  
You are growing up to be herdsmen  
Growing up to be feeder men  
Adding up to broth makers

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took my young wife  
The year before last, the other half  
I took her to a tender hayfield  
Down to the village meadow  
She mowed hay, jingled in the forest  
Took a breath, caught in the forest  
The willow branch cracked  
Mowed a meadow, mowed two  
Started the third mowing place  
She immediately began to wilt  
Her eyes began to wither  
Started to ache in the knees  
To lie down like a sick sow  
Lost her shoelaces  
Complained about her heels  
I went to the old woman's prayer  
Oh, my dear old woman  
The wife became sweetly ill  
Started aching in the knees  
To lie down like a sick sow  
Lost her shoelaces  
So look after, young lad  
Take the barley to the threshing floor  
Carry the oats to the barn  
The rye to the flax field  
Then we will slaughter the lambs  
Then we will fry the ducklings  
We will put the firewood by the stove  
Strike the blows on the thigh  
Between the white horns.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, God, beyond strength,  
Beyond the hill’s summit,  
Who left me sleeping,  
Lulled me in your lap.  
When strength was pursued,  
Man’s seed was mixed,  
Boy’s luck was burned out,  
Others got mules, all got horses,  
The others became strong men.  
I got black and foolish,  
A tar stump and a sickly one.  
Where must I go with the black one,  
Rush with the foolish one,  
Face the sickly one?  
Others do a piece of work,  
A piece of work, on dark soil,  
Weaving coarse fabric,  
I must cultivate grayness,  
Nurture without bathing,  
I hammer on muddy water,  
Wash in muddy puddles,  
Carry the plows without rest,  
Old bones from the ancestral land.  
The plow is before me,  
A man is behind it,  
The broom doesn’t end at the corner,  
The bar of soap doesn’t sit on the wall,  
The white towel on the rod.  
Come now, boo, to the chamber,  
Step up, stench, to your place,  
Secretly come to the bed,  
To the jointless ears,  
Whether from earth or man wooed,  
I will not call you as a companion,  
Nor consider you a dog,  
Yet I will still be a father.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
If I had known, could have known  
If I had seen even in a dream  
Brought to me while dozing  
I would think of heating the stove  
That I got an evil companion  
An evil companion, a loud man  
Of a cheerful other half  
Of a gloomy young man  
If the six-stringed were struck together  
The edges of the carpet in the middle  
Thin slices of bread   
Before wasting on the wicked  
On the cruel to scold  
An angry man wastes six  
A strong man ages his wife  
A bitter man spoils the other half  
Of a worried young woman.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, my dear little grandmother  
Honeyberry granny  
You nursed, you pampered  
You raised, you hugged  
You took me in your lap, gave me food  
Called me to the root, gave me drink  
Put me down, played with me  
You thought you would become a German woman  
The wife of the lord of Hiiumaa  
The wife of the king of Courland  
Time came, the day rolled by  
You did not become a German woman  
The young lad's little lady  
In the end, you became a poor woman  
A lifter of the poor man's bag  
A mender of an old shirt

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, my dear little grandmother  
Honeyberry granny  
You nursed, you pampered  
You raised, you hugged  
You took me in your lap, gave me food  
Called me to the root, gave me drink  
Put me down, played with me  
You thought you would become a German woman  
The wife of the lord of Hiiumaa  
The wife of the king of Courland  
Time came, the day rolled by  
You did not become a German woman  
The young lad's little lady  
In the end, you became a poor woman  
A lifter of the poor man's bag  
A mender of an old shirt

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
You gave me, grandmother  
You gave me a shoemaker's place  
You found me a bread place  
You knew a plowing place  
You gave me, grandmother  
You gave me a sandy place  
You knew a thorn place  
Do not let my fingers die  
Do not let my nails wear out  
When searching for thorns  
When picking crowberries  
Thorns are slave berries  
Crowberries are heath berries.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
How the lazy woman said  
If I had barley in front of the door  
Oats by the stove  
Lentils in the pantry  
Then I could manage to feed  
Answer to take the foster mother  
Set the table for the fields  
The wolf was the Hulja's guard  
The fox was the barnkeeper  
The duck was the proper barn boy  
The lizard was the bed warmer  
The cuckoo was the messenger  
The goldfinch was the one who blows the whistle  
The crow was the receiver  
The magpie was the one on a journey  
When will I go to father  
When will I go to mother  
When will I roll to the brothers  
When will I scold the sister  
Autumn is not born to go  
Spring is not fit to walk  
Autumn rivers are deep  
The horse drowns in this ditch  
The donkey among the mounds  
Make the path crooked

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:

When will I go to father  
When will I go to mother

When will I scold the sister  
Autumn is not born to go  
Spring is not fit to walk  
Autumn rivers are deep  
The horse drowns in this ditch  
The donkey among the mounds  
Make the path crooked

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
How the lazy woman said  
If I had barley in front of the door  
Oats by the stove  
Lentils in the pantry  
Then I could manage to feed  
Answer to take the foster mother  
Set the table for the fields  
The wolf was the Hulja's guard  
The fox was the barnkeeper  
The duck was the proper barn boy  
The lizard was the bed warmer  
The cuckoo was the messenger  
The goldfinch was the one who blows the whistle  
The crow was the receiver  
The magpie was the one on a journey  
When will I go to father  
When will I go to mother  
When will I roll to the brothers  
When will I scold the sister  
Autumn is not born to go  
Spring is not fit to walk  
Autumn rivers are deep  
The horse drowns in this ditch  
The donkey among the mounds  
Make the path crooked

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud abielust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
You ruled over a gentle brother,  
You ruled over polite ones,  
You ruled over the fairest,  
The most beautiful in the world.  
Afterwards, you got the woman  
Who had an elbow on her shoulder,  
Who had arsenic teeth,  
With dull eyes in exchange.  
She does not feel like walking,  
Sitting on the bed without sparks,  
Cooking broth without bitterness.  
Taken along with me:  
I know a dull girl,  
I see that snub-nosed girl,  
A dull girl with long hair,  
A lazy girl with wide locks,  
A belt fluid on her thigh.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud abielust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
An old woman’s skin journey:  
She offers a piece to her daughter,  
Offers a little straw to sleep on;  
Where should I put the straw,  
Where to sit the dear one?  
The straw takes up much space.  
Let me call out again,  
Let me sing back again:  
Dear one, little brother,  
You cannot marry from this land,  
Nor visit maidens from there.  
You go to marry a thief  
From the church to snatch,  
From the chapel to dig,  
From the grave to raise up.  
You bring to the house the schools,  
Bring inside the grumbling,  
Give to the stove the smoking,  
Show the leash to the spinning wheel.  
Father-in-law starts to say,  
Mother-in-law starts to say:  
"Our boys, little boys,  
Bring the ladder to the room,  
Pull near the stove,  
Bring the tobacco bag from outside,  
Steam bag from atop the stove;  
Bring from outside the grumbling,  
From atop the stove the smoking,  
From the spinning wheel the showing of the leash,  
From the bench the cracking of teeth."

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
They were called and listened to,  
Searched for and waited.  
Called to be home guards,  
A family daughter as a wife,  
Promised to give a horse  
To the iron plow handle.  
Silver sleigh harnesses,  
The cuckoo crows at the watermill,  
The grey bird at the trading place,  
The swallow at the headgear,  
The white bird at the harness,  
The iron rack at the stocks.  
I want Anne, I don't want Kaie,  
Anne has few teeth,  
Kaie has broken limbs,  
I want maidens from the farm.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Other men go to the sea,  
Other women go fishing,  
My man dozes at home,  
Sits under the stove,  
With a pipe in his mouth,  
A poker in his hand,  
But reads bread crumbs,  
Thinks about soot in the oven.  
I send my man to the sea,  
I send my wife to fish.  
The man comes home lying:  
Says the fish were caught earlier,  
The pikes have been driven to the end.  
Oh, poor little wife of mine,  
What am I to do?  
I will start heating the oven,  
I will drive my man away.  
The man, upon my prayer,  
I will add steam,  
Close the sauna door,  
So it finally comes to an end.  
Oh, poor little wife of mine,  
Then I dress for bed:  
In the middle, a patchwork quilt,  
On top, a big quilt.  
What was I supposed to do then?  
The man was a drunk:  
He drank bulls and horses dry,  
He drank sheep even from the barn,  
He drank cows dry without milking.  
So I got rid of my drunken man,  
A drunkard and a rake—  
Then I went to the sea myself,  
I went fishing myself,  
To see if fish were caught earlier.  
I went, a little one,  
Along the road, tiny,  
Along the land, small,  
But the road did not tire me,  
I went boldly to find my fortune.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
O little god of God  
Why did you let me sleep  
When it was time for men to get together  
Everyone got girls, everyone got kisses  
I got black and foolish  
A burnt stump and a sickly one  
A birch stump, a miserable one  
A spruce stump, ugly  
Others had beautiful and nicer ones  
I had cold and rough  
They rode in pairs to church  
I was hunched over my man  
The field was outside on the edge  
The man was inside, on the back edge  
The handkerchief stood on the corner  
The soap bar was on the wall  
The white narcissus was on the rod  
Then I took between the white ones  
Among the nicer ones' middles  
Still it was black as a mole  
Still it was gray as a rat.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before, another half  
I took a whole and sound one  
I took a plump and red one  
Very pale and fragile  
I took her to the gentle meadow  
To the golden hay arcs  
She mowed the beautiful hay  
Made a lovely arc  
Did not let the ribbon flutter  
Nor the dewy grass grow  
She began to suffer from her knees  
Her soles became weak  
Her apron strings stretched  
She became sick from under the belt  
I, the man, sought advice  
At the village women’s plea  
The women understood and replied:  
Oh, you foolish young man  
You didn’t want to be without nights  
Could not endure two moons  
Pinched weeks  
Now go quickly home  
Then put your little chickens to bed  
Let the barley lie straight  
Then you’ll have a healthy wife  
Healthy and light  
Like other women.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Little sister, dear one,  
You got a passionate husband,  
A loving spouse,  
Who leads you to bed with skirts,  
And a grain sack to the chamber.  
He rises in the morning:  
"Rise, bird, milk the cow,  
Herd dear, send the herd;  
The other herd is far away,  
Our herd is in the yard."  

Little sister, dear one,  
You got a man, an angry man,  
Who leads you to bed with a whip,  
And a rod to the chamber.  
He rises in the morning:  
"Rise, servant, milk the cow,  
Herd mare, send the herd;  
The other herd is far away,  
Our herd is in the yard."

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Do you know what a family salary is?  
You have a lock in the room,  
You have water in the well without a key,  
The door to the pigs' pen is open,  
The butter churn has a key in front,  
The cream jar has three locks,  
The old bottle has a lid in front.  
But be diligent in the morning,  
Go through the cowsheds,  
Cowsheds, sheep sheds,  
You will find a calf with the cow,  
A lamb in the sheepfold.  
Then go inside to tell  
By the midwife’s bed,  
To cheer up the midwife.  
If the child is in your arms,  
The child is lying in your lap,  
Go on by, do your work,  
Put the child to swing,  
The child is on the stove,  
Or take a buttered bread in your hand,  
Then you will be called bad,  
Called the old woman’s daughter,  
Called an old man’s child.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God, dear God!
Why did you let me sleep,
When everyone else had their time on earth.
Everyone got the good stuff, others got the leftovers,
I got the black and the foolish,
The tar stump and the sickly,
The wounded and the sparse,
Where there was a hole, I filled it,
Where there was a puddle, I washed it.
Ruu-roa and rallal-la.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Senni's calves, Senni's brothers,
Senni I got to keep cattle,
To raise strong oxen.
Well, last year I took a young wife,
The year before last, from the other side,
She began to suffer from knees,
Sleeping with sore shins.
You take Kaie, I take Maie,
Each the daughter of a different old woman.
Mai had a sturdy leg,
Kai had a slender body.
I tell it again anew,
Perhaps with a new turn of events:
Other women otherwise trip up,
Oh my Kärtu, the little sparrow,
She doesn't ruin the cloth,
She doesn't wet the cupboard,
She keeps her new coat
With blue stockings.
I bought a horse from Oti,
A cap from the village tailor,
A saddle from Saare Siim,
I went to Koiki to court.
Koiki's cobbler had three daughters,
One was Anne, the second Kaie,
The third slippery Liisuke.
Anne gave me a gift,
Kaie traded with me,
Liisuke was what my heart longed for.
Eeru Ann doesn't mow hay,
Keeru Kai doesn't roll hay.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Senni's calves, Senni's brothers,
Senni I got to keep cattle,
To raise strong oxen.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God, master of the afterlife,  
remember the master of the afterlife,  
why did you leave me to sleep –  
where the share was divided,  
where the seed of maidens was mixed  
and the happiness of women given.  
Others get young women,  
I got a black and foolish one,  
a tar stump and a sick one.  
Where must I go with the black one,  
face the sick one,  
hurry with the foolish one.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, my former life  
Dear furry knee-cover!  
When I was at the old woman's place,  
At the old woman’s, at the old man’s.  
I was the old woman’s little mouse,  
The two-legged little cat.  
I took some butter without lessening,  
I touched the cake without diminishing.  
The old woman never managed to hit me,  
Nor did the old man manage to hit me,  
To rattle the five brothers,  
The nettle was the brother’s rattle,  
The straw was the sister’s rod,  
The old man’s rod was a clump of moss.  
I ate without care, drank without care,  
Slept without care,  
Put care upon the ridgepole,  
Worry upon the black beard.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my gentle granny,  
Little honey berry granny!  
You nursed, you pampered,  
You raised, you hugged.  
You took me in your lap, gave me food,  
Called me to the root, gave me drink.  
Put me down, played with me.  
You thought you would become a German woman,  
The wife of a lord from Hiiumaa,  
The wife of the king of Courland. -  
Time passed by.  
You did not become a German woman.  
The lady of a Hiiumaa boy.  
In the end, you became a pauper's wife,  
A lifter of the pauper's sack,  
A mender of old shirts.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
How the lazy woman said:
If I had barley at the door,
Oats on the room's floor,
Lentils under the bench.
Then I could manage to feed,
Receive strangers.
Set the table for the guests.
The wolf was Hulja's watchman,
The fox was the barn's priest.
The duck was just the right lad.
The lizard was the bringer inside.
The cuckoo was the giver.
The titmouse was the blower.
The crow was the greeter,
The magpie was the traveler.
When will I go to my father?
When will I go to my mother?
When will I roll to my brothers?
When will I visit my sister?
One cannot be born in autumn.
One cannot walk in spring.
The rivers are deep in autumn.
The horse drowns in the brook,
The sow among the tussocks,
The path is broken in the marsh.
