label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my honey man,  
Hug along, dear comrade,  
In the capercaillie’s feathers from the other side.  
He ate a spoonful at noon.  
He hadn’t coughed a third time.  
He hadn’t nibbled a fourth time.  
He gathered five or six spoonsful,  
Lost seven or eight,  
Then made a little whip from a thread,  
A proper handle from a straw.  
Then struck the whip down,  
Along both forearms.  
Despite me, I screamed,  
Out of anger, I whimpered.  
Immediately he began to test me;  
Looked under my forearm:  
Are there whip strikes on the palm?  
Whip strikes on the top of the neck?  
No red marks on the whip,  
No blue marks on the back.  
Oh my honey man,  
Hug along, dear comrade.  
That helped to accomplish the task,  
To move the household’s laundry,  
To disturb the pig from eating.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Don't you run,  
Don't you run  
Don't you run to the drunkard,  
To the tavern-goer.  
The drunkard has no money,  
The tavern-goer has no coins.  
The sailboys tried to catch me,  
The meime boys wanted me.  
The old woman didn’t give, the old man didn’t put,  
The old woman didn’t give to the good-for-nothing,  
The old man didn’t put to the naked one.  
Some girls are like reindeer,  
Easily flattered and vain sluts.  
Look where the girl is beautiful and plump,  
Like an apple that's very fresh.  
Her hands are golden and beautiful,  
Her fingers soft and slender,  
Her hair is the color of black gold,  
Shining against the stocking needle.  
Her voice is like sunshine,  
Penetrating from the hearts of young boys  
And through their souls,  
So that singing about it is shameful.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Don't you run,  
Don't you run  
Don't you run to the drunkard,  
To the tavern-goer.  
The drunkard has no money,  
The tavern-goer has no coins.  
The sailboys tried to catch me,  
The meime boys wanted me.  
The old woman didn’t give, the old man didn’t put,  
The old woman didn’t give to the good-for-nothing,  
The old man didn’t put to the naked one.  
Some girls are like reindeer,  
Easily flattered and vain sluts.  
Look where the girl is beautiful and plump,  
Like an apple that's very fresh.  
Her hands are golden and beautiful,  
Her fingers soft and slender,  
Her hair is the color of black gold,  
Shining against the stocking needle.  
Her voice is like sunshine,  
Penetrating from the hearts of young boys  
And through their souls,  
So that singing about it is shameful.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before, a partner  
I brought her to the tender hayfield  
To join that precious golden arch  
She mowed the beautiful hay  
Shaped a lovely arch  
The grass bent beneath her skirt  
Bluebells on her back  
Dewy grasses for the path  
But the woman began to moan  
Moan and groan  
She threw her apron into the bush  
Pearls into the hay arch  
I went home crying  
Put on the black coat  
Between the gray old men  
I went to seek help for my wife  
An old woman came to meet me  
The old woman asked me  
Why do you cry, young man?  
Why do I cry, old woman?  
But the old woman understood and replied  
Go home, young man  
Take the oats to the ditch  
Two handfuls into the wind  
Then prepare your wife without oil  
Then the woman will be healed  
Healed and light  
Like other women  
I went home crying  
But the woman sat on the toilet  
Sought support from the chair  
Help from the stove  
Come Jesus, come Jesus  
And Jesus came into the room  
Two hands, two feet  
Twenty fingers and nails.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Is this the man meant for me  
Is this the husband for the household  
He comes home drunk,  
The whole room is filled with sorrow  
The house full of angry quarrels  
He throws stockings into the manure  
Shoes in the middle of the floor  
If you don’t take the berries from the field  
You won’t get a gift from the stove.  
He brought in the devil  
A rowan branch under the bench  
A birch twig under the sleigh  
He started hitting along the waist  
Along the waist and shoulders  
Along the shoulder blades,  
Then the calf felt the devil  
The poor maple felt it on its back  
The waist, however, felt the fine willow  
I surely know, dear woman  
I surely feel the waistband of the underwear  
The taste of frying pans still good  
Even though the oven isn’t lit yet.  
The loom hasn’t been tried yet  
The fist quickly came  
The fierce one clenched his hand.  
The bread peel flew.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
The birds sang with a kind tongue  
with a kind tongue and joyful spirit.  
The flowers are blooming wide open  
The blossoms lift sweetly into the air  
I went to tease the woman  
to love the spouse  
The woman did not want to joke  
the spouse did not want to love  
I went to settle in the forest  
to walk into the birch grove  
to step into the meadow  
to march to the berry land  
to look for love herb  
to gather comfort herb  
From the forest, I found the leaf herb  
From the birch grove, I got the comfort herb  
From the meadow, I got the love herb  
From the berry land, the sweet herb  
I gave the woman to sniff  
The spouse to smell  
The woman began to joke  
The spouse began to love me.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Jaan made a sledge to the forest
Gold to the skull in the hollow,
Silver to the pond outside,
Every noon he struck the chip
Every desire he put into winter,
Every day he added a patch,
Every month he dug a spoke.
Then the sledge was ready
What does this sledge need?
Ice is needed, Ruun is needed
Roma is needed, Looga is needed;
Inside a silk maiden.
From Kalev’s precious one.
Then the sledge goes to father-in-law’s yard
And to mother-in-law’s yard,
Under mother-in-law’s window yard.
Thunder, little mother-in-law,
Come then out to see,
Is there a girl of my liking,
A bride with an apron?
If there is a girl of my liking,
A bride with an apron,
Send her back with the sledge.
Now take my horses,
My ears walk,
My steeds are harnessed.
The household little one was called,
Father-in-law, to help mother-in-law,
To the young lady, to the counselor,
To the head of the family daughter.
I don’t have that service#
Like a lord of the household,
To the young lady, to the counselor
To the head of the family daughter.
Father will never give the service
Brother not from the sledge with the fringe.
I will take my horse
My saddle from the island
A small hide of a boar
A small whip of rowan
I strike the iron on the workbench
A rouble coin to the steed,
Then the maiden responds.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I surely know those slave songs,  
Slave songs, ox songs,  
How a slave should be,  
How a young slave should be,  
A slave must be diligent.  
At the door, one must sleep,  
In the room, one must rest the head,  
Send sleep to the stable,  
Sweep the twisting hall,  
Rake the bad dining table,  
Handle the stingy firewood,  
The most miserable corners.  
A fool does not understand thanks,  
I cried in the corner,  
Secretly I cried in the sauna.  
Over the yard to the granary,  
Even more to the cellar,  
Into the chamber with two doors.  
Oh, my former life,  
Dear foster home.  
In the evening I ate an apple soup,  
At night I ate milk soup,  
At dawn I ate cream cakes.  
In the evening I eat straw soup,  
At dusk water grass,  
At dawn I eat dog food.  
I take flower stalks from the breast,  
From the bluebell alder grove,  
Water to splash in the vessel,  
Still, the red stalks,  
Water to sprinkle power,  
Bluebell’s lushness,  
More famous than dog food.  
It is good to praise a good woman,  
Good is a good companion,  
When one comes home from the tavern,  
Beautifully lifting the door handle,  
From the serious carpet’s edge,  
Gently lifting the edge of the blanket,  
I stubbornly fish for kicks,  
She quietly keeps quiet.  
“Oh, my dear old woman,  
Honeyberry grandma,  
Bring water with a bucket,  
We will take the twigs to whisks,  
To weave the winnowing screen.  
The old woman is frightened,  
But be silent, young son.  
Throw the linseed into the waters,  
Barley straight into the rivers,  
Two oats into the gray field,  
Then the wife will become healthy,  
Healthy while raw,  
Red and plump.”

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
So far, I have toiled at pleated shirts  
Two or three wrinkled ones  
Four or five crumpled ones  
When my old lady had her turn.  
When misfortune befell the old lady  
Then I crawled into the sack  
Felled a young tree  
I carry a nail in the pocket  
Another nail in the sheath  
The third in a small pouch.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
It was said at night  
thought and pondered  
some kind of thought arose  
thinking of those left without a husband  
without a partner, struggling,  
I got a husband from among men  
from beyond the boys  
I got a husband mindful  
caring from afar  
a dear companion beloved  
comes like a wren to the barn,  
creeps like a bird into the nest  
lets go like a gull into the bed,  
calls the morning awake  
rise bird to milk the cow  
rise dear to take the herd  
but I understand again, I answer  
I must have a birch to milk the cow  
I must have an aspen to take the herd  
a maple tree waters the calf  
I must have a spruce to strain the milk,  
good folk, good folk sister  
good folk to praise, good to talk.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When will I rock for the sisters,  
When will I roll for the brothers.  
Not born to go in autumn,  
Not fit to go in spring:  
Rivers are deep in autumn,  
Waves are broad in spring;  
My horse drowns in the brook,  
My stallion in the waves,  
My colt in the high grasses,  
My foal between the tussocks.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
What was under the slave's care  
The domesticated animal had shelter  
The cattle had a place to go  
The fire was without a latch  
The well was uncovered on the hill  
The stable was without a key  
That was under the slave's care  
The domesticated animal had shelter

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Why do you bluster, little wind  
Bang, bad weather  
Today my man is at sea  
Today my companion is fishing  
My boy is on the sail  
Blustering tomorrow, little wind  
Bang, bad weather  
Tomorrow a stranger man at sea  
A stranger companion fishing  
A stranger boy on the sail  
Tomorrow breakers at sea  
Fish slayers fishing

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife,  
The year before the other half,  
I took her to a tender meadow,  
She mowed the hay, the forest snapped,  
The willow bushes crackled.  
Her knees began to ache,  
Her calves to ache as well,  
Her apron strings started to swell,  
The man hurried home,  
Over the fence to the mother-in-law’s,  
- Listen, mother-in-law, dear mother-in-law,  
Understand what is wrong with my wife,  
Her knees began to ache,  
Her calves to ache as well,  
Her apron strings started to swell,  
- Oh you crazy young men,  
Oh you young and foolish.  
You should remember last year,  
Recall the year before last,  
What you did under the blanket,  
Quietly under the carpet,  
You haven't gone a night without it,  
You have it six times once,  
Four times a week,  
Five times at dusk,  
Six times before dawn.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God, dear little god,  
Why did you let me sleep,  
When it was time for hay harvest,  
Everyone became beautiful and smart,  
I became black and stupid,  
I scrubbed my husband,  
The field was in front of the house,  
The man was inside the barn,  
The soap was on the wall,  
The white scarf was on the willows,  
I scrubbed and scrubbed,  
Still was black as a mole,  
Still was gray as a rat.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young woman,  
The year before last, to the other side.  
I brought her to a tender hayfield,  
I placed a golden arch over it...

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Tiidut, tiidut, the daughter is crying,  
The daughter does not cry for nothing,  
The daughter has troubles coming,  
And the logs are being measured,  
The fire does not go out from the rooms,  
No spark from the bedpost,  
No woman from young clothes,  
No gray from the dun horse's mane.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I went to the forest to walk  
and to look for a bird's nest.  
And while looking for the bird's nest  
I found a boy under a thicket.  
His underside was covered in mud  
and his upper side was tanned.  
Then I took the boy on my shoulder  
and took him to the Paide river;  
there I washed him with a brush  
and with soap from the Seven Societies.  
Then I took him on my shoulder again  
and took him to the Vändra fair.  
Then I traded him for a colt  
and a stallion with two ears.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young woman  
The year before last, another half,  
I took her to the other side of the river,  
I carried the dear one.  
Mowed a bow, mowed two,  
Started limping on the knees,  
Bled from the shoelace,  
Tied from the shoelace of a pig’s ear.  
The fire did not end here,  
A spark in the bedpost.  
I gave the woman an incantation,  
To the young one to make her cry...  
If there is no dung in the yard,  
Take fodder from the forest,  
If there is no fodder in the forest,  
Take the bran from the threshing floor,  
If there is no bran on the threshing floor,  
Bring an apple from the house,  
Light its tail on fire.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
You know what the prime minister says:
You have a locked room,
your water is in a well without a key,
the pigsty door is open,
you have the butter churn key with you,
the cream container has three locks,
the old container has a lid in front.
But be diligent with your work,
go through the cowsheds,
the cowsheds, the sheep sheds.
You will find a calf with the cow,
a lamb in the pen,
then go inside and tell grandma,
by the edge of grandma’s bed,
but cheer up grandma’s spirit.
If a child goes into the spider,
the child will come as a destroyer.
You go along, do your work,
push the spider to swing,
the child goes on top of the oven,
butter and bread are to be secured,
then you will be called,
the good old lady's daughter,
the good old man's daughter.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife,  
the year before last, to the other side,  
I took her to Einamaa.  
Einamaa reaped, grazed the forest,  
took the meadow, the grove rustled,  
then began to limp on the knee,  
to sleep in stocking feet,  
to swell from the apron straps.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God, dear God,  
Why did you let me sleep,  
When it was time for manhood,  
Everyone got prizes, some got treats,  
I got black and foolish,  
A pitch pine and sickly,  
A willow stump and pitiful,  
Everyone else went properly to church,  
But I am stooping with my man,  
The plow was in the front on the ridge,  
The man was behind with the pack,  
The pipe was standing in the corner,  
The soapdish was on the wall,  
A white cloth hung on the rod.  
Still I was black as a mole,  
Still I was gray as a rat.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
What do I fear here while singing
Declaring my voice
I have no fear of the bride
Nor am I afraid of the groom
Nor ashamed of father- or mother-in-law.
My bride from afar
Wears twisted socks to Viljandi
She drinks from the lake's pitcher
Before the lake's mouse-gray
Behind the lake's golden ears
Sometimes blood-red.
But I went to woo a young woman
I took a wife for fun
Across for another's sake
Others got fools and all got rogues
I got the black and the foolish
From Tõrva's corner, sickly
With a squinting eye and angry
Where I had to go with the black one
Confront the sick one
Hurry with the foolish one.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before last, across the river  
I took her to a tender hayfield  
Turned her onto a golden arc  
She mowed beautiful hay  
Did it for the joy of the song.  
She made a pretty arc.  
Then she started moaning  
Moaning and wailing  
And digging into her trouble  
From her shoelace to wither  
From her ankle to sleep  
From her stocking band to die.  
From her apron string to swell.  
Neither the doctors of Harju  
Haunted doctors, nor wise women helped there.  
But the porridge pitchers helped there  
Porridge pitchers with cake bags  
Watching over an old woman  
A daughter is needed for the farm  
A child is needed for the plough  
One for the guardian of another  
A third is needed for the herdsman.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Dear mother, oh my dear mother!  
Why did you raise me then?  
You could have thrown me into the river,  
And left me on the riverbank.  
The water would have carried me along,  
The riverbank would have held me,  
A doe would have nursed me,  
And raised me up strong.  
Oh, poor me, why did I go  
To the drunkard man!  
The drunkard will know hunger,  
He cannot go back anymore.  
When he comes home from the tavern,  
He grabs a big stick,  
And beats me,  
Beat me badly.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Dear mother, oh my dear mother!  
Why did you raise me then?  
You could have thrown me into the river,  
And left me on the riverbank.  
The water would have carried me along,  
The riverbank would have held me,  
A doe would have nursed me,  
And raised me up strong.  
Oh, poor me, why did I go  
To the drunkard man!  
The drunkard will know hunger,  
He cannot go back anymore.  
When he comes home from the tavern,  
He grabs a big stick,  
And beats me,  
Beat me badly.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife,  
The year before the other half.  
I took her down to the meadow,  
Put a gold ring on her finger.  
She mowed beautiful hay for me,  
Mowed the lovely arch as well.  
Then my knee began to ache,  
My ankle to ache too.  
I went to see the doctor.  
No help from Pärnu doctors,  
Pärnu doctors, Paide doctors.  
Trouble went into the ground,  
Mercy went into the grave,  
The smoker died,  
The cuddler disappeared.  
One cannot die at death’s ear,  
Go to the grave at mercy’s ear.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When will I go to my father,  
When will I go to my mother,  
When will I roll to my brothers,  
When will I walk to my sisters?  
Autumn is not yet born to go,  
Spring does not yet go to the field.  
The autumn streams are deep,  
Spring has wide waves.  
The apple sinks into my stream  
To the waves of the field’s shore,  
Into the gravel of the timothy grass  
Among the mossy clumps.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh god behind the power!
Why did you let me fall asleep,
when those girls were watched?
Everyone got high,
others got stumps,
I got black and stupid.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife,  
the year before took another half.  
I led her to a tender hayfield,  
placed gold over the arch,  
She mowed beautiful hay for me,  
mowed the lovely arch as well,  
She took to resting as if she were suffocating.  
She began to ache in the knees,  
fell asleep on the shin,  
complained about the heel.  
I tried to treat her,  
tried giving doctoring,  
But no Pärnu doctors helped me,  
Pärnu doctors, Paide healers,  
the worry bearer went to the ground,  
the giver of grace went to the grave,  
the one who soothed died away,  
the one who embraced disappeared.  
One cannot die by the ear of the dead,  
nor go to the grave by the ear of grace.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Tiidut-tiidut I found a coin,  
found a coin, put it in my pocket,  
with the coin I bought a horse,  
with the horse I harvested oats,  
with the oats I took a wife,  
with the wife I kept a family.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife,  
the year before last, another half,  
I brought her to a tender hayfield,  
placed a golden arch above her.  
She mowed the low meadow,  
cut the smooth stalks.  
She remained limping on her knees,  
fell asleep with aching calves,  
started limping with her shoelace,  
and limped barefoot.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, you beautiful rakes,  
Oh, you slender fellows,  
Young gentlemen of Hõbenukk,  
Ladies of the golden lace,—  
Rise up from your chairs,  
Get up into the hall,  
Step onto the windowsill,  
Look upon the poor parish,  
How this parish is tormented,  
How the little ones are tormented,  
How the size of a finger is scolded,  
How the size of a finger is pointed at.  
If we had shoulders like alder trees,  
Forearms like maple trees,  
Fingers like cudweed twigs,—  
They would not fear harsh treatment,  
Forearms would not fear torment.  
Oh God, dear God,  
Be an aid to the poor,  
Be a shadow to the poor child.  
Why did you let my little sleep,  
My pretty little sleep,  
When you divided it out,  
You shared it with men,  
You granted the luck of boys,—  
The rest went to rascals,  
For me remained black and ugly,  
A tar stump and a sickly one.  
Where must I go with the black,  
Face the sickly,  
Rush with the ugly?  
Where there is water, there I splash,  
Where there is a puddle, there I wash,  
Where there is a roof, I pour again—  
So that I may sleep a little,  
A tiny little angel.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, you beautiful rakes,  
Oh, you slender fellows,  
Young gentlemen of Hõbenukk,  
Ladies of the golden lace,—  
Rise up from your chairs,  
Get up into the hall,  
Step onto the windowsill,  
Look upon the poor parish,  
How this parish is tormented,  
How the little ones are tormented,  
How the size of a finger is scolded,  
How the size of a finger is pointed at.  
If we had shoulders like alder trees,  
Forearms like maple trees,  
Fingers like cudweed twigs,—  
They would not fear harsh treatment,  
Forearms would not fear torment.  
Oh God, dear God,  
Be an aid to the poor,  
Be a shadow to the poor child.  
Why did you let my little sleep,  
My pretty little sleep,  
When you divided it out,  
You shared it with men,  
You granted the luck of boys,—  
The rest went to rascals,  
For me remained black and ugly,  
A tar stump and a sickly one.  
Where must I go with the black,  
Face the sickly,  
Rush with the ugly?  
Where there is water, there I splash,  
Where there is a puddle, there I wash,  
Where there is a roof, I pour again—  
So that I may sleep a little,  
A tiny little angel.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
If I were a man of age,
I would buy an apron with pockets,
A shawl with rye ears;
Because the wicked little wolf -
When he rode to courtship,
He promised to bring a silk hat,
A silk hat, a calico apron,
A dear calico apron;
It came to be, it came to live -
Now the fist is checkered,
The palm is grim,
On the back of the hand is cross-shaped.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, God, dear God,
A little worry in my mind,
Why did you let me sleep
When it was harvest time.
Everyone went to church
I was scrubbing my man,
The field was in front with soil,
The man was inside with a sack.
The soap was on the wall
A white towel on the pole.
It was always as black as a mole
Always as gray as a rat.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I already knew what was coming to me!  
It lifts the coal sack,  
Lifts my kettle.  
If only I had known this before,  
Known it before, heard it long ago,  
I would have tied my hands to a stump,  
Rather than to this Jaan.  
Oh, my dear gray blanket,  
Thin foggy shawl!  
I could have kept silent,  
Lurked beneath the moon,  
Before I gave in to the fool.  
He got to sleep in the sheepfold;  
The sheepfold might have become tangled,  
The rider might have circled the shoulders.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Good was praised a good man.
Good was to live with the good.
He comes like a quiet guest,
Steps like a feather to the nest.
Gently he lifts the edge of the blanket,
Carefully the edge of the carpet:
Fearing to startle the dear one,
So as not to jump out of the bed:
Rise, dear, milk the cow,
Drive our herd afar.
Bad was praised a bad man,
Bad was to live with the bad.
He comes like the devil home,
Steps like a kick to the nest.
From bad he lifts the edge of the stove,
From noise he lifts the edge of the carpet,
Fiercely the edge of the blanket.
Rise, though, fool, milk the cow,
Other calves to the pasture,
Our calves to the mud.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, my little blue coat,  
And my dear gray blanket,  
A thin layer of fog.  
You could have lain down  
To brood under the cover,  
Before you had to lie down in the sapard,  
You could roam on the rafter.  
In the sapard, you had to lie down quickly,  
On the rafter, you could roam around.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
The old woman came out to the gate  
The old man came out to the street.  
I turn around, step inside.  
Then an old lady came out to meet me.  
She was tiny under the blanket,  
A little bit under the bed,  
A beautiful piece was under the rag,  
A little under the carpets.  
Oh, you foolish young men  
Throw the barley into the mud,  
The oats straight into the sea.  
That's how to treat a woman without oil.  
Then the woman will stay whole  
Like other women.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Others took black heads,  
The handsome took white heads.  
I was left with the black and the foolish,  
A tar stump and a sickly one.  
Where there is a stream, there I swim,  
I wash the patch of shavings,  
So that I might become as fair  
As once were others' heads.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When shall I borrow from my father,
When shall I go to my mother,
When shall I run to my sisters,
When shall I roll to my brothers.
Autumn is not for going out;
Autumn has deep rivers.
Spring is not for roaming;
Spring has wide waves.
The road is rocky.
Do not saddle the horse;
It wears out its hooves.
The horse drowns in the creek,
In the boat among the willow shrubs,
In the turf with stone fragments.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When will I go to my father,  
When will I go to my mother,  
When will I roll to my brothers.  
Autumn does not want to come,  
Spring does not want to go;  
Autumn’s rivers are deep,  
Spring’s waves were wide.  
I take hold and get angry.  
But I put on an apron as a sail,  
And a winnowing basket as a wise barrier,  
Then I go to my father,  
Then I go to my mother.  
Then I roll to my brothers.  
My sister-in-law had daughters under the yard,  
Brother’s daughters at the gate.  
Brother took a horse from the harness,  
Brother’s wife took off her gloves.  
Nadu gave a rocking chair.  
Then the brother began to talk,  
To speak with his wife:  
“Look for food for my sister.”  
The wife took some sips,  
Beloved Käli scolded:  
“What will I give my sister to eat?  
The water is cloudy and gone,  
The scales are cold in the cellar.”

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my honey woman  
Rise, dear, milk the cow  
Rise, darling, send the herd.  
Other bulls to the grass,  
Our bulls to the mud  
Other cows to the alder grove,  
Other herds far away,  
Our cows on the sand.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When do I sway to the sisters
When do I roll to the brothers.
I was late to go to father
I was late to go to mother.
At last I roll to the brothers,
And I sway to the sisters.
I was not born to go in autumn,
Spring was not suitable to walk;
The autumn rivers are deep,
The spring tides were wide.
My horse drowned in the ditch,
In the wide wave of the meadow,
In the broken stones of the boat,
In the gray turn of age,
In specks of stone.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I was once at grandmother's home,  
I grew up once at grandfather's home;  
I was like a thistle in bloom  
I grew like a hemp vine,  
Flax fiber, clover,  
Granny was gentle with me like a cat,  
Grandpa handled me with care.  
I don’t know how I walked,  
Head first, feet last.  
A stranger looks at a stranger  
Heart sideways into it.  
Started rattling at the door,  
Beating back to the wall,  
Click-clack on the latch  
There was surely banging on the floor.  
Everyone is angry at me  
The lower garden twigs,  
The upper linden barks.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I was always miserable,  
So many times my mood was sad.  
Because I was bad, because I was sad,  
Because it was a bad time,  
To the autumn frost,  
To the spring snow cover,  
It began to pour snow,  
To shake hail,  
But snow with a thick layer to the roof,  
Snowdrifts to the ridge roof.  
Autumn does not come to pass,  
Autumn rivers are deep...

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I took the mistress from Võnnu,  
Then from Narva the young maiden.  
I led the tender haymaker,  
Carried the gold on her back.  
She began to swell at the knees,  
To limp on her feet,  
To sleep on her calf muscle.  
She did not mow the tender hay,  
Nor did she take the gold on her back.  
I feared the weeping of the young,  
The bow would be drawn,  
Suddenly there was trouble in the room,  
The bow aimed at the eye.  
It is not he who aims at the father-in-law’s eye,  
The father-in-law’s eye, the mother-in-law’s eye,  
The hunchback’s shepherd’s eye,  
The jesters’ eye,  
The five brothers’ maiden’s eye.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young woman,
The year before I took another.
I took her to the tender hayfield
And placed a golden arch above.
She mowed the beautiful hay,
She mowed the lovely arch;
She cut the creeping grass,
The dewy grass in the clearing,
The bluebells in the clearing.
She began to suffer from her knees,
Complaining of her heels.
I sought help.
The gray horses went to the doctor,
The linen carts to the town road.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, my honey man,  
Little bird from the bone.  
Grant me the life of a lady.  
Lady, place me on your lap.  
May the life of the master be yours.  
I have no riffraff among me,  
No scoundrel in my hand.  
My lap is empty for a burden,  
My hands empty for walking.  
Dear sister, gentle one.  
Oh, you poor child captive,  
Child captive, broken bed:  
May your lap be full even for a burden,  
Your hands full for walking.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Now permission is granted to spy
It is nice to go to the sauna
Beautiful to shout with a turnip
Now the reins are held by the old woman
The bridle is in the brother’s sleeve
The reins are held by my own.
When the reins can be held by the fool
The bridle is held by the madman
The bridle is in the man's sleeve,
Then I ask permission from the cap
To the wise one from six
Whether the cap allows me to the village
Thanks to another household
An apron to another room
A clog to the building
The cap does not allow to the village
Thanks not to another household
An apron not to another room
A clog not to the building
I sneak away like a pig to sleep
Go like a lamb to bed
Pull my knees up to my chest
Elbows to my sides
Fists near the heart
Warmth flows from the knees to the belly
Warmth from the elbows to the sides
Warmth from the fists to the heart
I get up in the morning
Elbow water was under the side,
There was a knob of water on top
Jelly water under the heart
Rolling tear water
Eyebrows in a drop of water
When I grow up at home
I sat in father's room
I sat in mother's room
I looked into the brother's room
I grow like hemp in the ditch
I rise like a strawberry in the house
Long pipe in the corner of the room
Neither the old woman has beaten me enough
Nor the old man beaten me enough
To frolic with the mild brother
A straw was the old woman's rod
A cattail was the old man's rooster
A willow leaf was the brother's twig
I got to a bad man
To a wicked companion
Oh, my unruly hair
Or my honeycomb
Dear golden-haired one
I am beaten with a fist
My bottom is pinched
My palm is clipped.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I went to Narva to fetch my wife,  
The wife died on the road to Narva,  
I threw my apron into the ditch,  
The shawl into the cattails,  
Beads onto the hayrack,  
The braids onto the pile,  
Rings onto the thicket.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took my young wife  
The year before last, across  
She was plump and red  
She was pale and painful.  
Mowed hay, gleamed forest  
Took a trap in the bottom forest  
The willow branches cracked  
Gathered on the haystack  
Put down meadowsweet  
So it wouldn't mildew underneath  
Put in rush stems  
So it wouldn't rot with fungi  
On top, golden buttercup leaves  
So it wouldn't yellow the gold  
Now the knee began to ache  
Leg muscles to sleep  
From the sole to the ankle  
I myself go running to the village  
To the old woman next door  
The village old women, little old women  
Help to understand  
What is wrong with my young wife  
Empty, I don’t know, something on the other side  
The old woman hears, answers back  
Oh, you crazy young men  
You surely know what you've done  
You haven’t been without her a night  
You have had six times  
Four times a week  
Five times at twilight  
Six times at dawn  
The old woman hears, answers back  
Go running home then  
Put the linseed to soak  
Barley laid straight  
Oats in pairs into the spring  
Make a deed without oil,  
Then call the relatives together  
Then give the intestines without oil.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
A doll is not praised for its veil  
A son’s wife is not told off on her knee.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I took my young wife,  
A delicate silver bead,  
Took her to a gentle meadow,  
Carried her onto a golden arch.  
Cut the arch, cut twice,  
Began to ache from the knees,  
From the calf to the thigh,  
From the shoulder blade to the groan.  
Oh, my passionate husband,  
Also full of compassion,  
Take a tidy stallion from the stable,  
Pull up the bony saddle,  
Ride quickly to Harjumaa,  
And then travel to Finland.  
From Harju, bring me a doctor’s sister,  
From Finland, bring me a nurse,  
Who would help me in my misery.  
The fire cuts the swift shoulders,  
Sparks struck the slender calves,  
Lightning struck the foal’s hooves.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
It was before the old woman's time,  
Before the old man's time.  
There was plenty to eat, plenty to drink,  
Plenty to eat in the kitchen,  
Even to eat in the barn,  
Secretly to eat in the sauna,  
Even more in the living room.  
Now is the time of scarcity,  
Now you can cry in the corner,  
Even cry in the barn,  
Secretly cry in the sauna,  
Even more in the living room,  
Twice as much in the chamber.  
Now you can cry in the corner,  
Even cry in the barn.  
A tear runs from the eye,  
Runs down to the chest,  
From the chest down to the waist,  
From the waist down to the toes,  
From the toes it falls off,  
Here the rivers started to flow,  
The rivers began to bend,  
The springs began to flood,  
The oats dried in the village.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
It was before the old woman's time,  
Before the old man's time.  
There was plenty to eat, plenty to drink,  
Plenty to eat in the kitchen,  
Even to eat in the barn,  
Secretly to eat in the sauna,  
Even more in the living room.  
Now is the time of scarcity,  
Now you can cry in the corner,  
Even cry in the barn,  
Secretly cry in the sauna,  
Even more in the living room,  
Twice as much in the chamber.  
Now you can cry in the corner,  
Even cry in the barn.  
A tear runs from the eye,  
Runs down to the chest,  
From the chest down to the waist,  
From the waist down to the toes,  
From the toes it falls off,  
Here the rivers started to flow,  
The rivers began to bend,  
The springs began to flood,  
The oats dried in the village.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
God, God beyond the river  
Why did you let me sleep  
When these shares were distributed  
Others were given moose fawns  
To me, the black and the foolish  
The tar-coated and the sickly  
Where must I go with the black one  
Face the sickly one  
Hurry with the foolish one  
Where there was water, I splashed  
Where there was a cure, I soaked  
Now the man has the black one, I have the wise one  
The honey of the bee is in the head  
Special wisdom in the pocket.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God up above  
Remember up above  
Why did you let me sleep  
When the seed of laughter was mixed  
Oh, Mari, low grass  
Smelly hay  
You ate in the barn too  
Secretly you ate in the bed too  
You ate the breads, you ate the cakes  
The best pastries.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
If only my heart could feel  
That we were courted with evil  
Viitane was given a song  
Sopardi was fed bread  
Lakardi was given vodka to drink  
If only my heart could know,  
Could know, could feel  
I would throw the vodka bottles to the ashes  
The beer mugs to the street  
I would give the bread to the beggars.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Little horse, little brother  
Is this the mare that you took  
From the wooden fire, bare behind  
Takes a step after the stallion  
Water is available below five mills  
Where to get six ears to drink  
To make the stallion trot around  
To pull the wet mare there  
Look at the mare that I took  
I took it from the fields of Harju  
From the swing of Viru sickle  
From the leftovers of Järva saw  
See where the mare works  
Milks the cows, carries the foals  
Marries the daughters off  
The sons to take wives.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Little Hellakene, little brother  
Maybe you pecked my gold  
Silver flakes  
Remember the past  
Recall the former times  
When you went to the tavern road  
You had a crowd of whores ahead of you  
A prostitute’s flag behind you  
You bought beer for the whore  
And red vodka for the slut  
For the prostitute, pork skin  
Your woman, a virgin  
Must not be awake  
If she hears you coming home  
Making noise outside  
Swearing at the gate  
Then the room fills with pain  
The chamber glowing with candles  
She herself goes to the barns  
Soon hides in the stables  
Oh, keep the doors closed  
Hide, old dear ones  
The heart will not be calm  
Then she goes to look inside  
The evil one fell into the bed  
Into the whore’s wide sheet  
Banished between the pillows  
Then she begins to cry  
Ah, my little cloud patch  
Smooth silk blanket  
Pretty colorful little rug  
Could have yellowed at home  
Father helps to gray it  
Before I gave the mad garden  
I started to throw a tantrum.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young bride  
The year before, the other half  
She was plump and red  
She was white and tender  
Mowed the hay, cleared the forest  
Took the hayrack, trapped the forest  
The willow bushes crackled  
Gathered into a pile on the stage  
Put down meadowsweet underneath  
So it wouldn’t mold underneath  
Put rush stems inside  
So it wouldn’t rot or develop fungus  
On top, placed precious cobweb leaves  
So it wouldn’t yellow the gold  
Now the knees began to ache  
He fell asleep from calf pain  
Descending from the instep  
Digging into the soles  
Down below the belt, digging the meadow  
I’m running toward Juuru  
Leaping toward Kabala  
There met an old woman  
Why do you weep, young lad?  
Last year I took a young bride  
The year before, the other half  
She was plump and red  
She was white and tender  
Mowed the hay, cleared the forest  
Took the hayrack, trapped the forest  
The willow bushes crackled  
Gathered into a pile on the stage  
Put down meadowsweet underneath  
So it wouldn’t mold underneath  
Put rush stems inside  
So it wouldn’t rot or develop fungus  
On top, placed precious cobweb leaves  
So it wouldn’t yellow the gold  
Now the knees began to ache  
He fell asleep from calf pain  
Descending from the instep  
Oh, you clumsy young man  
Put the linnets close  
Barley scattered straight around  
Oats followed in pairs.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Little Hellake, little brother  
You were looking for a sleepless woman  
An unwinking maiden  
You looked in Orina parish  
You tried in Karina parish  
Otherwise, your black one could run away  
Your gray one could wander without a handle  
You could pull the hammer on the anvil  
The boat could circle the mooring  
Now you got that woman  
The clock was sharp at the tooth edge  
Two spans long was the tongue  
An elbow long was the sidebone  
She heats the stove on one side  
On the other side she bakes bread  
She washes the pot with her ass  
She does everything with skill  
With a cake she calls you to sleep.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
My husband, dear husband  
Takes me to bed with a fiddle  
Takes the kannel to the chamber  
Puts the instrument’s head down  
The kannel hangs from the room’s rod  
Rises up in the morning  
Takes the instrument’s head down  
The kannel from the chamber, from the rod’s end  
Rise up, flower, and milk the cow  
Rise up, dear, and give food  
Rise up, beloved, and send the herd  
The child still screams in defiance  
And murmurs with a closed mouth  
Your husband, sea demon  
Takes you to bed with a whip  
Takes the stick to the chamber  
Rises up in the morning  
Takes the whip from the head down  
The stick from the chamber, from the rod’s end  
Rise up, mares, milk the cows  
Rise up, servant, give food  
Karimära, send the herd  
The village’s herd is far away  
Our herd is in the pasture  
The village’s bulls are in the meadow  
Our bulls are in the stable  
The village’s stallions are in the rye field  
Our stallions are in the harness house.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my honey man  
Made from fishbone rind  
Before, I made nine sins  
And caused eight harms  
Then made a small whip from thread  
A little handle from a straw  
Then took it to the building  
Carried it to the chamber  
Where the young ones are scolded  
Beaten at the border  
Whipped on the head with a birch rod.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
What is under a slave’s care  
No little ones to take  
There are paths for the feet  
The fire is in the room without a lock  
The well is on the hill without a cover  
The pigsty is without a door  
The pigsty fence without a latch  
The slave must be cared for  
The maid must hold her head  
At the door, one must sleep  
If that door is locked  
If the underpants are snapped  
Then the slave is going to wander  
Like a poor vagrant  
Why do you sigh, whore?  
Knife in the pine, on the hill  
Take the sparkle from the eye to the ear

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Grandmother, dear little grandmother  
When evil comes home  
I roll the words into a ball  
I put the bad ones into a braid  
Let the water come to our land  
Water under our yard  
Then I toss the ball to the stream  
I let the braid go to the loan  
The water informs the brother  
The stream informs the sisters  
The loan knocks on the father's door  
The loan knocks on the mother's door  
Then the evil grandmother heard it  
Then the evil grandfather heard it  
The sick sister suffers  
The enemy tries to roll the brother away  
Then came the bigger kin  
Then came the people with stronger iron  
My brothers do not bleed  
They came to listen to gold  
To look at the material  
Are the golden eyes dry  
Is the duck's skin hardening  
Look, look, dear little grandmother  
Look how the spruce roots  
Rot the pine roots  
Harden the oak roots  
Look how dry the golden eyes  
And the duck's skin hardens.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, poor me,  
I am in trouble,  
Because I cannot get a wife,  
What am I to do.  
Today one, and tomorrow another,  
The third stayed behind yesterday.  
- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -  
I went to Viru to court,  
I got that woman then.  
She was mowing hay, gleaming in the forest,  
She took a spoon, rattled the pine grove.  
Then she started limping,  
And fell asleep from juniper berries.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
My own is not here,  
My partner does not grow here.  
My own is in Holland,  
My partner far away.  
May the wind carry health,  
May the clouds lengthen with longing,  
Hail falling on the books.  
Little sisters, dear little ones.  
Let me say again  
When will I roll to the brothers?  
In autumn, the swamps are deep,  
In spring, the waves are wide.  
The apple sinks into the ditches,  
Between the wet hummocks,  
I myself depart into the waves.  
But the brother watched at the door  
"From there come big eaters,  
Big eaters, heavy drinkers,  
Many who settle down.  
They eat even the dry branches  
And drink the juice of the vineyard."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
My own is not here,  
My partner does not grow here.  
My own is in Holland,  
My partner far away.  
May the wind carry health,  
May the clouds lengthen with longing,  
Hail falling on the books.  
Little sisters, dear little ones.  
Let me say again  
When will I roll to the brothers?  
In autumn, the swamps are deep,  
In spring, the waves are wide.  
The apple sinks into the ditches,  
Between the wet hummocks,  
I myself depart into the waves.  
But the brother watched at the door  
"From there come big eaters,  
Big eaters, heavy drinkers,  
Many who settle down.  
They eat even the dry branches  
And drink the juice of the vineyard."

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I thought you were a manor man,
I thought you were an official,
About to wear a fog hat.
Now came a tobacco root,
Became a horn knocker,
Mover of straw rods,
Keeper of the straw benches.
Comes home, beats me,
Puts the whip under his head,
Against the wall of the chamber.
Gets up in the morning,
Takes the whip from under his head,
From the wall of the chamber.
Get up, Litsi, milk the cow,
Oh cowherd, drive the herd!
The man comes home,
Puts the pipe under his head,
The kannel against the chamber wall.
Gets up in the morning,
Takes the pipe from under his head,
From the wall of the chamber.
Get up bird, milk the cow,
Get up dear, drive the herd,
To a distant field, to a high field,
Calves to Varesmäe,
Goats to the Kiltsi fold.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When I was a maiden before,  
When I was a priest's daughter before,  
Then I wore slit shirts  
With laces and two looped fastenings.  
Now I am married and in foreign lands  
Now I mutter in a sack,  
I shiver in the corn stalks,  
I faint in the needles.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took the wife’s youth,  
The year before last, the other half,  
She was plump and red,  
She was healthy and lively.  
Then I took her down to the eating place  
Put gold on the ladle,  
She mowed hay, grazed in the forest,  
Took hay rides, clattered in the woods.  
Now she started limping with her knees,  
Sleeping on her calves.  
Constantly: Bring a doctor and carry the physicians.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, bless this life  
Dear birch bark  
Where I once denied  
And I dear grew up  
I grow behind the birches  
In the midst of apple blossoms  
In the shelter of autumn winds.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my manly man  
Fish-bone companion  
He was strong in lifting  
He took away the wool nail  
At home, he has a tack nail  
He carried a hop nail in his hand.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Before Mari paid with berries  
The Home Daughter paid with gold  
Father with silver in the yard  
Brother with copper for wine  
Now Mari went to other lands  
The hen went to other pastures  
Now the land does not pay with grass  
Neither with grass from the land, nor leaves from the tree  
Now the father-in-law walks stealthily  
And the mother-in-law walks stealthily  
Look, between the toes  
And between the middle of the nails  
If I had ten heels  
I would get a hundred spankings  
The heel walks on tavern roads  
The spanker pecks inside the room  
Looking for a fight while traveling  
To exchange oaths  
I hear, I hear again  
Do not quarrel, sisters  
Do not argue, brothers  
Fight, cross brothers  
The quarrel of the sisters is to blame  
The oath of the brothers is evil  
The noise of the quarrels is jealous  
Ai-joi-juppi, our boys  
Our boys, our girls.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
My brother brought a bride from Viisu  
My cousin brought a bride from Oisu  
My uncle’s son brought one from Tännassilma  
I will bring one from the Tori route  
But I will carry out from Vändra.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Tiiut-tiiut I found a he-goat  
With the he-goat I bought a horse,  
With the horse I made barley,  
From the barley I made beer,  
With the beer I took a wife,  
With the wife I kept a family,  
The family said to beat the wife,  
The farm said to kill the wife,  
But she went to the swamp rowan grove.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Today I am my husband's sister-in-law
Luring young men
Seductively inviting the young one
So that he hasn't learned to drink beer
Or to take vodka.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
A man who takes a wife from Narva  
A woman who buys a cow from the town  
The Narva wife did not have a son  
The town cow did not give milk.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young bride  
The year before the other half  
This year it became a year  
I took her to the tender hayfield  
To turn the gold cart  
She began to suffer from her knees  
Fell asleep from the calf of her leg  
She fell ill below her navel  
Dear one, little brother,  
Look for a cure, try the medicine  
Midwives' warnings don’t help here,  
Midwives' warnings, doctors' medicines  
Only thick barley porridge helps.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Led me to sleep with a whip  
The bailiff led me to the chamber  
Valge led me to sleep on the instrument  
On the kannel led me to the chamber  
Helped me make a bed  
Fed the pig  
Moved the household goods.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
If only I had seen in my dream  
While dozing, I came to know  
That I got a drunkard husband  
A tavern-goer as my partner.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Oh, poor me, why did I go  
To a drunken man as a husband?  
A drunkard may face hunger,  
He cannot turn back anymore.  
He goes to the tavern, eats and drinks,  
Comes home and beats me.  
He takes a whip, gives it to me,  
Along my waistline.  
Oh, my father and my mother,  
Why did you raise me?  
You could have taken me to the river,  
Or at least to the riverbank.  
There the water would have rolled,  
The riverbank would have raised me.  
There I could have found a man in the forest,  
A companion near the jar.  
A golden girl for a maiden,  
She lives without worries.  
Oh, poor me, why did I go  
To a drunken man as a husband?

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Oh, poor me, why did I go  
To a drunken man as a husband?  
A drunkard may face hunger,  
He cannot turn back anymore.  
He goes to the tavern, eats and drinks,  
Comes home and beats me.  
He takes a whip, gives it to me,  
Along my waistline.  
Oh, my father and my mother,  
Why did you raise me?  
You could have taken me to the river,  
Or at least to the riverbank.  
There the water would have rolled,  
The riverbank would have raised me.  
There I could have found a man in the forest,  
A companion near the jar.  
A golden girl for a maiden,  
She lives without worries.  
Oh, poor me, why did I go  
To a drunken man as a husband?

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
A little bird went to the town  
A sweetheart to the small borough  
A companion to the fishing shore  
A boy to Lake Peipus  
Will he come home or not  
Will he bring bread or not  
The first cares even about bread  
The second about the town’s bread  
The third about Narva’s pastry  
Even if he would come home  
He would bring a horse home  
So that the town wouldn’t remain a ruin  
Nor the small borough a desolation  
To the market square, a scamp  
The town’s birds eat the meat  
The shore’s crabs eat the fat  
The Viru crows drink the blood  
The town’s birds became meaty  
The shore’s crabs became fatty  
The Viru crows became mighty.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Old man is old and frail  
Alli with a beard, damn it  
When you get up, you grumble  
When you wake, you growl  
I come there, poke your eye  
Set your head on fire  
I take the old grudge with me  
Come after with a fireplace poker  
Then you will finally meet your end.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God, dear little god  
Do you remember the year before last  
Great God from long ago  
Unfortunately under the stove  
Why did you let me sleep  
When the chase was being chased  
And the party was held  
The men’s happiness was given  
The women’s harsh bearing  
Others got darlings, all got beautiful  
I got black and foolish  
A tar stump and a sickly one  
Where must I go with the black one  
Where must I go driving with the gray one  
Hurrying with the fool  
Rushing with the sickly one  
Where there was water, there I swirled,  
Where there was a granary, there I hummed  
I struck on the fifth water  
I spent on the sixth mischief  
On the seventh soap stump  
So that the old white one would be lost.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my former life  
Dear fur knee pad  
When I was at grandmother’s  
And I was at grandfather’s  
I took butter, it didn’t lessen  
I touched the cake, it didn’t shrink  
I went to the shelter to dress up  
To Kerstut to adorn  
My head touched the pork  
Knees in a half-beer  
Shoulders in sprat quarter  
Feet in flour trays  
Now the reins have gotten into crazy hands  
The bridles in a wild grip  
I go to the shelter to dress up  
To Kerstut to adorn  
My head does not touch the pork  
Knees are not in flour trays  
Shoulders are not in sprat quarter.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
But the brothers are tender
But I saw a girl growing
A strawberry berry rising
Her head appears above the forest
Her feet, however, are below the forest
Her body in the middle is badly in the forest
I fell asleep to sleep
When those girls were distributed
Others got the mukid, everyone got the kekid
I got the black and the stupid
Tar in the jug and trouble
I started going with the black
Hurrying with the stupid
Rushing with the tar
Where I saw water, there I splashed
Where I saw a well, there I watered
I washed five times with water
Six times with cucumber juice
Seven times on a piece of soap
Then I put it on the edge of the sled
From there his evil fell on the hearth
It burned both knees
His knees began to ail
I warmed the calf with сolar
I slapped the leg with a blade
Then I went to look for a doctor
To listen to the old woman of Kupu
To wait for the midwife
Then an old woman came to me
Then an old man came to me
They began to ask me
Where are you going, young man?
I am going to find a doctor
To listen to the old woman of Kupu
To wait for the midwife
My girl got a navel illness
Oh you foolish young man
You are going to look for a doctor
To listen to the old woman of Kupu
To wait for the midwife
Now go home quickly
Now put the oats in the trough
And two buckets of barley.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
When I begin to compose poetry  
To sing poetry  
The whole village stays to listen  
The whole parish stays to watch  
My weary words  
A child's bad song  
Where did this child get those words  
From which words did they start  
She went to Harju to study  
To take a melody from Viru  
I heard, I answered back  
She did not go to Harju to study  
She did not go to Viru to take a melody  
In Harju studied the sisters  
In Viru the brother's wife  
They took, they took, the dear sisters  
They took me to a Viru man  
They made me beat my thigh  
Between five brothers  
At the hand of the sixth  
There my field became ruined  
My linen sack became sooty  
I went to the village to plow  
To borrow an ox from my father-in-law  
To steal some gold  
The villager took the ox to the village  
The father-in-law took the ox to the cellar  
The stolen goods to the chamber  
I took it and got angry  
I throw away my father-in-law’s shirt  
I throw away my mother-in-law’s shirt  
Immediately even my socks  
The villager brought the ox to the village  
The father-in-law brought it to the cellar  
The stolen goods to the chamber  
Wash well, maiden  
Wash well, fine one  
Make yourself white and clean.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife,  
the year before that, the other half,  
brought her to a tender hayfield,  
to the honey chicken's bow;  
mowed the hay gently,  
turned the rows lightly.  
Began to ache from the knee,  
to sleep from the calf;  
then the young wife went home,  
the young wife, the other half,  
brought me a tiny one home,  
a small nugget to the corner cupboard.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before last on the other side  
She was plump, she was red  
She was pale and in pain  
I took her to the tender hayfield  
I will soon take her to the golden arch  
He mowed the arch, he mowed the bushes  
He began to suffer from his knees  
From the shinbone to sleep  
From the foot to the ankle, pressing  
But I started to look for a doctor  
Looking one day, looking two  
Then I began to look a third time  
But the old woman came in return  
She asked me  
What are you looking for, young lad?  
What am I looking for, old woman?  
Or what am I looking for, old man?  
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before last on the other side  
She was plump, she was red  
She was pale and in pain  
I took her to the tender hayfield  
I will soon take her to the golden arch  
He mowed the arch, he mowed the bushes  
He began to suffer from his knees  
From the shinbone to sleep  
From the foot to the ankle pressing.  
You go home, young lad  
You put the city folk to shame  
The barley is lying flat  
You make half a barrel of beer  
Then the wife will get well  
Well, completely well.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh those proud women of Harju  
Near the rich city  
They cook porridge, they sell flour  
Lentils are going towards the city  
But the beans follow by hopping  
We drink it without beer  
Where there was a thick foam on top  
The elbow porridge in middle of the night  
When will I go to my father  
When will I go to my mother  
When will I roll to my brothers  
I fall to six sisters  
I understand, I go to my father  
I understand, I go to my mother  
I understand, I roll to my brothers  
It did not happen to go in autumn  
Nor did it happen to go in spring  
Rivers are deep in autumn  
In spring, roads were wide  
Still, the sisters made holes  
The brother’s wives nodded  
See where the big eater comes from  
Big eater, big drinker  
The keeper of the threshing floor  
The ox eats even what he eats  
The vat drinks even what he drinks  
Wait, let me say it again  
Let me sing the words back  
What harm is there in eating the ox  
Or what harm is there in drinking from the vat  
The yeast was beneath and the foam was on top  
A drop of thick beer  
Or what harm is there in eating the ox  
The bone was beneath and the skin was on top  
A piece of lean meat

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I took a young girl  
A slender letter to her  
A delicate silver flower  
I brought her to a tender meadow  
Placed a golden arch above her  
She began to suffer from her knees  
Swelling from the apron string  
From the calf to the shin

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Everyone else got mugs, others got makis  
I got the black and the stupid  
A tar stump and a sickly one  
A willow stump and a pitiful one  
I went in order to the church  
But I was bending over my man  
The man was a furrow clod  
The soap cake was on a mushroom  
The rod was on the corner of the furrow  
The white daffodil was on the rod  
Still, it was black like a mole  
Still, it was gray like a wolf.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh you foolish young men  
Throw away the city boys  
Follow the oats directly  
Make the action on the cheek without oil  
So that the woman becomes healthy  
Healthy, light  
By the way, like women  
By the way, as tall as a pine tree

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Mari does not pay for the barn  
Father still has silver outside  
If Mari went to other lands  
The chicken to other pastures  
Then Mari does not pay for the barn  
Does not pay for that mud  
That came from under the father-in-law's foot  
Between the mother-in-law's toes.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my former lifeless one
Dear fleece kneecap
When it could squeal at the shirt
It could flutter on the linen
Whirling without a belt.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Is this a man for me
Is this a partner for the channel
Comes to lean on the home
Home to all evil scribbles
Room to all wind pains
Throws stockings on the manure
Shoes to the middle of the chaos
If you don't take the berries from the ground
Neither to the oven nor to the barn
Something was brought into the room
Rowan under the bench
A twig under the rack
Took and gave along the spine
Along the spine, along the edges of the back
Through the barn with a broomstick
Then felt the somethings
On the back of the poor maple
The spine but a slender willow
I do feel, dear little sister
I do understand to take the skin
I taste the pancakes’ sweetness
But came quickly a fist
Harshly turned a hand
The paddle for the bread flew
And the oven remained unstarted
The loom beams are untouched

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When do I go to father
When do I go to mother
When do I roll to the brothers
The autumn rivers are deep
In spring, the loans were wide
My horse fell into a ditch
A bull into the swamp
A mare between the tussocks.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I waved, I waved  
I waved to the young woman  
I waved to the old woman  
With my sandy linen  
With my sooty tobacco  
With my straw apron  
I went from place to place asking  
From the mother-in-law for the stove  
From the father-in-law for the golden-headed club  
The father took the sod to the village  
The mother-in-law took the sod to the cellar  
The mother took the sod to the chamber

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Oh, poor me, why did I go
To a drunkard man
A drunkard may suffer hunger
No way back anymore
Dear mother, gentle woman
You could have taken me to the water
To grow up by the riverbank

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Oh, poor me, why did I go
To a drunkard man
A drunkard may suffer hunger
No way back anymore
Dear mother, gentle woman
You could have taken me to the water
To grow up by the riverbank

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before last, to the other side  
I took her to the dear hayfield  
Carried her to the golden arch  
To the edge of the golden arch  
She mowed the beautiful hay  
Took joy from the toil  
She got a couple of bruises  
Started groaning in pain  
Complaining about her trouble  
Started aching in her knees  
Fell asleep from calf pain  
Fell down from the ridge  
Then an old wife came to you  
She was the wife from Kõnnu village  
Why do you cry, young men?  
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before last, to the other side  
I took her to the dear hayfield  
Carried her to the golden arch  
To the edge of the golden arch  
She mowed the beautiful hay  
Took joy from the toil  
She got a couple of bruises  
Started groaning in pain  
Complaining about her trouble  
Started aching in her knees  
Fell asleep from calf pain  
Fell down from the ridge  
Oh, you foolish young men  
Buy barley, water the oats  
Then the wife will be healthy  
Healthy and strong  
Like other women.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Listen, my dear son,  
The farm needs a worker,  
Young feet to run,  
Quick hands to leap,  
A pipe was brought for resting,  
Poor one to whine here,  
Started to ache from the knees,  
Went numb from the shin,  
Started to fall from the foot.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
What is under a little control  
What kind of hand follows the hand  
Came to the locked room  
There is no key in the barn  
This is what is according to my mind  
This kind of hand follows the hand.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young bride  
The year before last, on the other side  
I took her to the tender hayfield  
To the bend of Keekaela  
She mowed hay, flicked through the forest  
Took the season, caught in the forest trap  
She mowed the beautiful meadow  
Made a lovely bend  
Soon she began to wither  
Her eyes grew dull and droopy  
Her knees started to ache  
Her shins started to hurt  
Her apron strings began to swell  
But I ran home  
To the village girls, little old ladies  
The village elders  
Last year I took a young bride  
The year before last, on the other side  
She was tender and beautiful  
She was lively and strong  
I took her to the tender hayfield  
Carried her to the golden bend  
She mowed tenderly, flicked through the forest  
Took the season, kindled the forest  
She mowed the beautiful meadow  
Made a lovely bend  
Soon she began to wither  
Her eyes grew dull and droopy  
Her knees started to ache  
Her shins started to hurt  
Oh, you foolish young lad  
Young lad, little boy  
Go brew some beer for the neighbors  
Barley water for the brothers  
Then the bride will be healthy  
Healthy and strong.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Where do we go against the night  
Against the night, against the darkness  
Against the gentle frost  
Against the high moon  
Maybe we get lost in the frost  
Maybe we fall into the dew  
Who is looking for us in the frost  
Who is digging in the dew  
Mother is looking for us in the frost  
The devil is digging in the dew  
It was beautiful for father to leave  
It was beautiful for mother to leave  
The rivers of autumn are deep  
The rivers of spring are wide  
The horse drowned in the stream  
The water-grey went into the bog  
The flaxen-haired went to the waves  
To this birthplace the bridges are made  
Bridges are made, rafts are set  
Who will bring the horse from the stream  
The water-grey from the bog  
The flaxen-haired from the waves

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my dear little old woman  
You nursed me, pampered me  
You raised me, carried me  
You kept me at the party  
You did not know what I would become  
I have become worldly  
Worldly, a prostitute  
My head wears a lace cap  
My hips are like a fancy skirt  
My breasts like a Riga scarf  
My shoulders sewn with chains  
My knees in a silk apron  
My feet in tight shoes  
I grow like hemp in the village  
Behind the door of the old woman’s room  
Now the little old woman is dead  
Now the old man is dead  
Oh, that beautiful fur knee-cover from long ago  
When I got to wave my shirt  
I fluttered in linen  
I was made to dance at the tavern.  
The little old woman worry  
The black spruces worry  
The gray quilts worry  
The linen shirts mourn.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Alright, alright young daughter  
Who ordered you to endure  
Who ordered you to endure the strike  
Who ordered you to confirm the betrothal  
The sickle forced me to endure  
The rake forced me to confirm the betrothal  
Oh God, dear God  
Why did you leave me to sleep  
When they gave those gifts  
To others they shared  
I got the bad and foolish one  
A tar stump and a sick one  
Where must I go with the black one  
Hurried with the foolish one  
Hastened with the sick one  
Where there was food, I dipped  
Where there was water, I drank  
Then I went to a charming husband  
A madman into marriage  
The man started insane work  
The wife started weaving fabric  
Weave, woman, fabric for the shed  
For yourself, jewelry shelter  
For the husband, the clothes for the mind  
If you don’t weave fabric for the shed  
For yourself, jewelry shelter  
For the husband, the clothes for the mind  
Then you will feel punishments  
Tastes of gooseberries  
Blows on the shoulder from the wicked  
Blows on the neck from the sexy  
Blows on the back from the whip.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before last, another half  
She was lively and fresh  
She was tender and beautiful  
She was plump and red  
I took the tender one to the hayfield  
I carried her over the golden arch  
Then her knees began to ache  
Her calves to fall asleep  
She limped with a bent head  
I went home crying  
The old woman answered, the old man answered  
Both those old ones answered  
Why do you cry, young son?  
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before last, another half  
She was lively and fresh  
She was tender and beautiful  
I took the tender one to the hayfield  
I carried her over the golden arch  
She mowed hay, cropped the forest  
She took a broom, hit the forest  
Then her knees began to ache  
Her calves to fall asleep  
She limped with a bent head  
She swelled from the apron strings  
Dear one, brother dear  
Go quickly home  
Throw the oats into the water  
The two barley into the gray field  
Hurry up to become a town  
Then the wife will be healthy again

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I grew up like a chicken at home  
I sat in my father's room  
I could eat in the kitchen  
Sometimes I could eat in the shed  
I could secretly eat as well  
My head touched the meat  
My toes touched the wine barrels  
My shoulders touched the beer anchor  
Now I can cry in the kitchen  
Sometimes cry in the shed  
I can secretly cry as well  
Even more in the living room

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
It is good to praise a good man
When he comes home from the road
The blanket rises at the edge
Modestly at the edge of the carpet
Afraid to startle the dear one
Afraid to alarm the beloved
Lest he might receive a fatal blow
The poor wife a blow of suffering
I make many apologies
Pity the beautiful little home
Still he strokes her back
Still he caresses her face
It is bad to speak of evil
He comes home from the tavern
Begins grumbling at the door
Swearing at the gate
Swears by body, swears by soul
Swears by every vein of the heart
There is no loving time
Nor a long day
If he does not have a bloody head
Nor a blue back
I have whip strokes on my back
Blows of a stick on my neck

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Virgins, young girls  
Perhaps you stay alive  
Perhaps you stay after me  
Do not run to the drunkard  
Put it on the card game  
The drunkard has no money  
From the tavern-goer, a penny  
Money stays on the trails  
A penny on the tavern table

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God behind the gates  
Great God behind the bushes  
Under the herd's pale one  
Who left me to sleep  
When the portion was being shared  
Piia was caught by the flock  
Everyone got heights, some got lumps  
I got the black and the foolish  
The tar stump and the sick one  
Where there is a bay, I fish there  
Where there is a puddle, I wash there  
Where there is a well, I water it.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God Almighty  
Why did you leave me to sleep  
When those traps were set  
I became black and foolish  
A tar stump and sickly  
Where must I go with the black one  
Where must I come with the tar stump  
Where there is fodder, there I sway  
Where I see a well, I water in.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I rode a little to see
I went to demand a wife
To catch from another side
That girl by my name
Who had twisted shoelaces
Jet-black leg straps
Snow-white footsteps.
I took the young woman
Small and lively
With a bull's big hoof
And a prominent breast bone
She began to limp from the knees
To sleep from the calf
And to sprain her hip.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my God, dear God  
Why did you let me sleep  
When those girls were being handed out  
I got the black and the foolish  
The tar stump and the sickly one.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God, dear God  
Why did you leave me to sleep  
When it was time for the maiden's harvest  
I ended up black and foolish  
From the charred stump and the sickly one  
From the rat's herb bundle inside  
And from the molehill mound inside.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
What was under the care of the slave  
At the mercy of the master  
To be walked on the command of the master  
The fire was in the room without a lock  
The well on the hill was without a cover  
The cow barn was without a gate  
The peat stacks without protective cover  
These were under the care of the slave  
At the mercy of the masters  
To be walked on the command of the master.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Can herd the cattle  
Drive the father-in-law’s oxen  
Pinch a nail on the shoulder  
The master interrupts himself  
Even in my anger, without delay  
Even when putting on a halter  
I can indeed hold back my anger  
And I put them in the halter.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I was once a young man
I wore patched shirts
With two or three patches
Now I have taken a young wife,
Now I can have a dowry settlement
Still my body appears healthy
My body looks beautiful
The fire in the stove does not go out
The spark does not cease
The young wife does not get dressed
The gray horse is not harnessed
She seeks to wean the child
She takes the weak crying.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I was once a young man
I wore patched shirts
With two or three patches
Now I have taken a young wife,
Now I can have a dowry settlement
Still my body appears healthy
My body looks beautiful
The fire in the stove does not go out
The spark does not cease
The young wife does not get dressed
The gray horse is not harnessed
She seeks to wean the child
She takes the weak crying.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I was taken to be a servant
Made to wash the pot
To scrub the kettle gold
To polish the spindle shaft
The field became a little dusty
The scarf a little flaky
The handle got sooty
I went from house to house to ask for plowing
From mother-in-law to mother-in-law for polishing the kettle
From daughter-in-law for polishing gold
No one gave plowing
No one polished the kettle
No one polished gold for the club
I went to plow in the village
Went to mother-in-law's shed
Went to gold chamber
When I take it and get angry
I separate the linen threads
I do not put the man on my lap
I myself run to the alder grove
Jump into the birch grove
There I melt fresh cheese
Separating golden patches
Golden stuff in hand
Borderboards in the doorway
Who came to call me home
Mother-in-law came to call me home
Mother-in-law barked sharply
Who came to call me home
Mother came to call me home
Mother barked sharply
Who came to call me home
The man came to call me home
The man's senses were disturbed
The springs of his tongue were twisted.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When I was at my father's home
I rolled down the yard with my brother
I was like a beautiful apple
I walked like a nice pine cone
Now I have grown into a man
Close to the rich man in the town
Now that water is no longer worth anything
That runs under the side
Father-in-law carries the rolling
Between the toes of the foot
Now the boys of the dogs beat me
The boys of the pigs stretch me
A hungry magpie pecks at me.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When do I go to my father  
When do I go to my mother  
When do I roll to my brothers  
I fall to six sisters  
The autumn rivers are deep  
The spring floods are wide  
Our horse drowns in the ditch  
To the spike in the wave  
The goat between the tufts  
The uniform has stripes, the uniform has fields  
I have no field nor land  
I throw barley into the river ditch  
Oats to the riverbanks  
Rye to the wilderness spruce forest  
The horse eats barley from the ditch  
The ox eats rye from the spruce forest  
The bear eats oats from the banks

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Töölaulud  ; Laulud abielust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Ann was a young heifer  
Keedi, a little beauty  
She went to jump in the barn  
Tried to make a fuss  
Between the stones on the threshold  
Anne’s beads broke  
Anne’s ribbon broke  
Ann went to her brother's plea  
My dear little brother  
Take my ribbons and fix them  
Sew the money pouches shut  
Renew the edges of the clasp  
Ann didn’t take it, Ann didn’t go  
She went on her own carefully  
Along the road, very slowly  
Across the land, very quietly  
Ann went to the blacksmith’s plea  
Little blacksmith, little fellow  
Take my ribbons and fix them  
Sew the money pouches shut  
Renew the edges of the clasp  
The blacksmith wished it, Ann was ready  
The blacksmith’s children were sly  
Blacksmith himself was without blemish  
Blacksmith’s wife was the darkest  
I thought I'd become the manor lord  
I thought I'd become the aid man  
Black becomes the free servant woman  
The other half of the stable hand.  
Who was under the care of the slave?  
Under the will of the servants  
The fire was in the room without a lock  
The well was without a cover on the slope  
The pigsty was without a fence  
It was under the care of the slave  
Under the will of the servants  
May this not be  
May this not be born  
Who will bear the crown’s burden?  
The stable boy may well be cloaked  
Where was the crown man's home?  
A stable boy’s lodging  
Where was the fire made in the room?  
Hundreds of walls covered with moss  
Water rolled over the logs  
Mist hung over the door  
Dew tied onto the roof.

translation:

Keedi, a little beauty  
She went to jump in the barn  
Tried to make a fuss  
Between the stones on the threshold  
Anne’s beads broke  
Anne’s ribbon broke

Take my ribbons and fix them

She went on her own carefully  
Along the road, very slowly  
Across the land, very quietly  
Ann went to the blacksmith’s plea

Take my ribbons and fix them  
Sew the money pouches shut

The blacksmith wished it, Ann was ready
