label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Hiiut-tiiut I found flax  
With the flax I bought a horse  
With the horse I made barley  
From the barley I made beer  
With the beer I took a wife,  
With the wife I started a family  
But the family said: beat the wife  
The farm said: kill the wife  
I will not beat my wife  
My wife is my dear wife  
Who knows how to weave fabric  
And to twist silver thread  
And to frolic in the saddle barn.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
The golden life of a girl  
She lives without worries  
When she has dear health at hand  
Her time runs out happily  
Oh, poor me, why did I go  
To a drunkard man  
A drunkard can see hunger  
No longer can go back  
Goes to the pub, takes vodka  
Comes home, beats me  
He took the strap, gave me  
Strikes along my side.  
Oh my fathers and my mothers  
What did you raise me for  
Should you have thrown me straight into the river  
And also by the riverbank  
There the water would have rolled me  
The riverbank would have raised me  
A cow would have nursed me  
And raised me up tall.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When will I visit my father,  
When will I visit my mother,  
I will turn to my brothers,  
Turn to my brothers’ wives;  
Yet the brothers bark,  
The sisters-in-law nod:  
Look where this great eater comes from,  
Great eater, great drinker,  
One who puts much into place.  
He eats the bulls at the feeding trough,  
And drinks the barrel at the drinking trough.  
But I hear, I answer back:  
What good is there in eating that bull,  
Bones beneath, skin on top,  
A piece of lean meat—  
What good is there in drinking from that barrel:  
Yeast beneath, foam on top,  
A bit of red beer.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When will I go to my father,  
When will I go to my mother;  
In autumn the rivers are deep  
In spring there were wide waves;  
My horse into a shallow ditch  
A colt into the waves  
A heifer among the tussocks.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife,
The year before last, another half,
This year has passed.
But I took her to the tender hayfield,
Carried her over the golden arc;
She mowed the beautiful hay,
Made a lovely arc.
Below, she mowed the aspen leaves,
Above, she mowed the lily of the valley,
Inside, the blue stems.
Then her knees began to ache,
To sleep from calf cramps,
But to itch beneath the navel.
I am going to look for a doctor.
Then an old woman came out to meet me.
I greeted her.
She asked me:
"Where are you going, young man?"
I again heard, responded:
"Last year I took a young wife,
The year before last, another half,
This year has passed.
But I took her to the tender hayfield,
Carried her over the golden arc,
She mowed the beautiful hay;
Made a lovely arc.
Below, she mowed the aspen leaves,
Above, she mowed the lily of the valley,
Inside, the blue stems.
Then her knees began to ache,
To sleep from calf cramps,
But to itch beneath the navel."
The old woman hears and replies:
"Oh you foolish young man,
You must speak of last year,
Recall the year before last.
When you touched her belly,
And pinched beneath the navel.
Go home then, young man.
Take the little birds to the water,
Barley straight to the river,
Oats twofold after them.
Then bring the old woman soon
Look, there will be a little one in the bed,
A water bath in front of the bed;
And a tiny baby in the bath."

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Sister, little Anne,  
Listen, dear little cousin,  
My husband, the sea devil,  
Eats the flesh off my back,  
Sucks the fat from my ass,  
Gnaws between the sea creatures.  
When he comes home from the tavern:  
The room fumes, the chamber crackles,  
The room cries into the four corners,  
The chamber screams into two corners.  
He sits in the middle of the floor,  
Begins to take off his feet;  
Throws his stockings on the dung,  
Shoes on the floor;  
He himself looks from the corner,  
To see if the berry would fall into the grass,  
Cleans the house to the dog.  
If the berry doesn’t fall into the grass:  
Then the stocking stood in the room  
Between many pillows,  
The miska demands the woman's mind,  
My husband, the sea devil. -  
Sister, little Anne,  
Listen, dear little cousin,  
Made by my husband's hands,  
Poured from honey wax;  
When he comes home from the tavern  
Late he pulls the worn doors,  
Quietly turns around and stomps.  
But he fears to startle the dear  
Along with the feared one;  
Made by my husband's hands,  
Poured from honey wax.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I nursed a young girl,  
I nursed and raised her. -  
A wonder, your beautiful life,  
Grow, your humble one grow,  
Perhaps from you a German woman will arise  
A merchant’s lady will grow,  
A shop boy’s other half. -  
I hear I answer back:  
"Ah, from you will arise a poor woman  
Grow to be my shepherdess."

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Look, man, what I have,
Try to understand what I can:
I got a man like the ruins of Viru,
A broken-minded fool,
He doesn’t know how to do chores,
Can’t milk the cow.
Can’t even put on a cover.
He would take it to Viru, bring it to the market,
Plant it on the pig’s field.
Where must I go with the black one:
Maybe to the old spring,
Hurrying with the rumal,
Rushing with the sick one,
Where there is a hole, I drain it,
Where there is a puddle, I wash it,
The broom stands in the corner,
An old broom is hanging on the hook,
Why do I wash the hearth,
A creak from the old spring.
Poor little kannel-vaarik.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my honey man,  
From beyond the briar bush,  
From under the duck's wing,  
From the fish's scale.  
You come from the straw to home,  
Gently lift the coverlet,  
Carefully to the mat,  
So as not to startle Ella  
Nor to scare Kulla.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
If I grow at home,  
I grow like hemp in the willow.  
I roll like an egg on the grass,  
A delicate one outside,  
No quarrel was heard in the village,  
No fight in another farmstead,  
From us came an echo of quarrel,  
A strike on the wide table’s sound.  
Now there is a quarrel in the village,  
A fight in another farmstead.  
The daughter of the house pays with berries.  
Father in the yard with a disease.  
The copper brother is a spirit,  
The gold brother in the chest.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my honey husband,
A fish-and-bone little scoundrel,
He makes the beds, joins the tables,
Puts me to sleep on my pillow.
The head doesn’t peek out from the pillows,
The feet don’t go under the gray carpet,
The body doesn’t get cold in the middle.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud abielust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
I went to dig in the furrow,  
To cast a wide plowshare,  
I myself ride along the ridge,  
Dogs ran along the marsh,  
Horses along the road.  

I heard the horse harness clatter,  
I heard the duck splashing.  
I took the duck from the horse’s harness,  
The hound from the horse’s bridle.  

I took the duck to Paide,  
There I handed over the hound.  
From Paide, I got money for the duck.  
I bought a new little instrument.  
I started to play the instrument.  

Father heard the sound of the instrument,  
Father started to make butter.  
Mother heard the sound of the instrument,  
Mother started to churn cream,  
To play the butter churn.  

Brothers heard the sound of the instrument,  
Brothers went to draw water.  
Sister heard the sound of the instrument,  
Sister went to get salt.  

The young woman heard the sound of the instrument,  
The young woman smiled kindly:  
“Here comes the instrument home,  
Takes my instrument to sleep,  
Carries it to the room,  
Calls in the morning.  

Takes the instrument and starts to play,  
The instrument at the door of the room.  
Rise, maiden, milk the cow,  
Rise, take care of the herd.  

My herd goes to the fields,  
Our herd to the pasture,  
My steers graze on the grass,  
Our steers on the mud.  
My bulls to the meadow,  
Our bulls to the barn.  
My mares to the rye field,  
Our mares to the granary.  

Grandmother hears, answers again:  
“Oh my charming daughter-in-law,  
My daughter-in-law with the apron!  
Don't feed the instrument without a family,  
The kannel cannot live alone.  
The instrument walks on dry straw,  
Sings on the edge of the straw board;  
The kannel in the straw chamber.  

There it guards prostitutes,  
Drags scandalous women,  
Takes the gate to the market,  
Sells too much at the tavern.”

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I sing, I praise otherwise  
I do myself, I do otherwise  
I chase the horse after  
I drive the slaves ahead.  
Be a slave, be a slave  
I have indeed been a slave before  
I have indeed walked by orders  
I know the care of a slave  
The care of a slave, the ox's knee  
How one becomes a slave  
How I became mine  
How I walked to Käliste  
What was under the care of a slave  
To where I was to go  
To where I walked to Käliste.  
A fire came to the yard  
The well was deep in the hill  
The barn was without a door.  
That was under the care of a slave  
To where I was to go  
I did the old woman's work at the hearth  
I did the young woman's work at the hearth  
I think of giving help  
To give the herd into someone's care.  
The time came, a joyful day  
The barn was given  
The herd was given into care  
I was given the aid  
The herd was given to Käliste  
I was the first to be given  
Shown by others with a pole  
This stays with the daughter at home  
Poor from the hard work seen.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I sing, I praise otherwise  
I do myself, I do otherwise  
I chase the horse after  
I drive the slaves ahead.  
Be a slave, be a slave

The care of a slave, the ox's knee  
How one becomes a slave  
How I became mine  
How I walked to Käliste

I did the young woman's work at the hearth

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young maiden  
The year before the last, on the other side  
But I took her to the hayfield  
To the hay mowing meadow  
To turn the precious cart  
She mowed the hay, no hesitation  
She took the scythe, no faltering  
Mowed one cart, mowed two  
Started the third mowing  
Suddenly began to moan  
From the apron strap she suffered  
From the harness she slept poorly  
From the shoelace she kept twitching. -  
I went quickly to fetch medicine.  
Then the old woman appeared  
Then the old matron appeared  
I immediately began to dig out  
To tell my trouble:  
Last year I took a young maiden  
The year before the last, on the other side  
But I took her to the hayfield  
Carried her onto the precious cart  
She mowed the hay, no hesitation  
She took the scythe, no faltering  
Mowed one cart, mowed two  
Started the third mowing  
Suddenly began to moan  
From the apron strap she suffered  
From the harness she slept poorly  
From the shoelace she kept twitching. -  
The woman hears the voice from the hut:  
"Throw the barley into the ditch  
Oats afterward twice  
Wheat after that, thrice  
Blow the maiden into the fire  
Whip the maiden into a blaze  
Then the maiden will get well  
And be like other maidens." -

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Maybe I was crazy last year
The year before that, crazy too
I didn’t understand to get married
When the German from Sõmeru rode
Wearing a grey coat
To Pikevere's fenced-off barn
Where I stayed waiting foolishly
I kept twisting keriking
I kept staring at the painted leg
Finnish sunti, suare santi
Alle alpi herder boys
My shoes were worn out
My laces were torn
The oxhide ones groaned

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God Almighty  
Remember, Almighty  
What made me fall asleep  
When that part was watched  
And the seed of the bride was mixed  
I became black and foolish  
A tar stump and a sick one  
Where must I go with the black  
Hurrying with the foolish one  
Facing the sick one  
I take [it] to Viru, bring [it] to the market  
I drive to Harju Street  
I trade a colt for a foal there  
I give a gift in exchange for a spotted dog.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, I alone am the old woman's son,  
Alone the old man's son.  
I was not filled by the old woman's lash,  
The old woman's lash, the old man's lash,  
To painfully shake my brother.  
The bramble was a thorn to the old woman,  
A thorny piece was a thorn to the old man,  
A reed was my brother's whip.  
So far I was tender, so far I was brothers,  
So far I was Ella, the old woman's son,  
Until the woman was taken,  
The little sail was caught.  
I held torn shirts,  
Wore two-colored ones.  
So far I sway on the cuckoo's bed,  
Rock gently on silver reins,  
Under the plank, tied with ribbons,  
Into weak, weak waters.  
The girl was weak, the groom was weak,  
The girl was weak for the chores,  
For catching the little sail,  
For the will of the blue-and-grass.  
They must not, must not, dear brothers,  
Must not go to the farm,  
Drive under the eaves,  
Maybe there are pikes under the doors,  
Bloody trees under the gate,  
Maybe I will ruin new clothes,  
Drench my pockets.  
I will not ruin new clothes,  
Nor drench my pockets.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
What was at my place to go to  
To walk on small stones  
There was fire in the room without a lock  
Water was in the well without a bucket  
The back shed was without a window  
That was at my place to go to  
To walk on small stones

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Long ago I found a mare  
With the mare I bought a horse  
With the horse I harvested oats  
With the oats I took a wife  
With the wife I lived my life  
With a pipe I supported the family  
With tobacco I fed the children.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Long ago I found a mare  
With the mare I bought a horse  
With the horse I harvested oats  
With the oats I took a wife  
With the wife I lived my life  
With a pipe I supported the family  
With tobacco I fed the children.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I drank a little cup at the tavern  
To Alevisse spring  
To Linalaka town road  
Sometimes I go to my father  
Sometimes I go to my mother  
Sometimes I roll to my brothers  
Sometimes I quarrel with my sister  
In autumn, there is no need to go  
In spring, it’s not suitable to walk  
In autumn, the rivers are deep  
In spring, the waves are broad  
Apples fall into the stream  
Flax falls into the waves  
Tavern horse into the heather  
Ali’s time into the winds  
My brother is blood-red  
He could cross the stream  
Wade through the mud  
Tread through the muck  
If only I had wings to fly  
If only I had wings like a bird  
The bird’s wings would fly  
But the fish only swim in the stream

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
One indeed said: Take a woman,
Another said: Take a woman;
I took a poor one, a woman from the manor.
In the manor, there are certain types,
Some who sleep in beds,
Some who lie on straw,
Some who lie on chaff.
One even had a shirt on,
Made by the rural nobility;
Two walking sticks in hand,
Even those woven by the weaver.
The linens wore out, the flax was exhausted,
The fabric of the sack was pecked by a rooster.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Ask, ask little village,  
Chase away the little winter,  
Where the goose had put its neck,  
Where the throat had drunk the coat:  
At the Jõepere tavern by the pole,  
Under five mugs of vodka  
Under six mugs of beer

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God, dear God,  
Why did you let me sleep  
When this inheritance was pursued,  
Mixed with manhood seed,  
Nurtured a woman’s happiness.  
Others got riches, all got high status,  
I got black and foolish,  
A tar bucket and a sickly one;  
A broomstick stands in the corner,  
A white scarf on the pole,  
A scrap on the wall,  
A small fire under the spring,  
A barren comb-thorn,  
So that I become a beloved of Alli,  
Like a black coat.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Who is going to the ploughed village?  
Mihkel is going to the ploughed village,  
Harnessing down to the steppe.  
Who took him there to eat?  
Miina took him there to eat.  
Go home, little Miina,  
My ploughs are not set,  
The cranes have not pecked the stubble,  
The pillows are worn out,  
I come tired from work  
And come weary from the field,  
Gently I lift the edge of the blanket,  
Edge of the woven carpet,  
I fear my darling falling,  
I fear to startle her.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I was nicely on the church road  
At the center of the white parish  
Neither the old woman’s rod fulfilled me  
Nor the old man’s rod fulfilled me  
The woolen rod was the old woman’s switch  
The fir branch was the old man’s switch  
The brother’s rod was even harsher.  
Let this empty generation take it too  
The stall beneath the stable  
Not to wear gold from cheese  
Not to age from blessèdness  
Not to endure under the head  
My little old woman

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Women praised the apron knee,
Respect the hearth base.
I was foolish to believe!
I was weak to take advice.
Take, please, the apron knee,
Preserve the hearth base.
The field brings the knee down without worry,
The hearth took care at the edge.
Oh, you dear wreath knee,
Beautiful hair knee.
Where I want, there I dance,
Where I can, there I roll,
Where I reach, there I run.
Gold has no language barrier,
Life has no riser.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I was called a house crab,
A man to the family girl,
A relative of the buttercup,
A neighbor from Kuldanoasto.
"I am a house crab,
A man to the family girl,
A relative of the buttercup,
A neighbor from Kuldanoasto.
Five was a blister on the toe!
Six was a bump on the cheek,
Seven knots were on the nose.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
When shall I go to my father,
When shall I go to my mother!
The autumn rivers are deep,
The spring waves are wide,
A horse into a muddy ditch,
A stallion into a wave,
Into the gray bend,
A mare among the tussocks.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife,  
The year before that, from across the border.  
I took her to a tender meadow,  
Carried her onto a golden stack.  
She mowed the beautiful hay,  
Drove the lovely stack together.  
There was joy in the barn, joy in the house,  
It was a beautiful sight to see.  
But then her knees began to ache!  
Her shins grew swollen,  
Her heels began to dig,  
Her apron strings started to swell.  
I went home, even crying!  
The old woman answered in the room,  
Those two old ones answered.  
The old woman asked:  
"Why do you cry, young man?"  
"What should I cry for, old woman:  
Last year I took a young wife,  
The year before that, from across the border.  
Her knees began to ache,  
Her shins grew swollen."  
The old woman’s reply could be heard:  
"No Harju doctors can help here,  
Harju doctors, Toompeh’s healers,  
Paide agreement-makers,  
Narva belly-lifters,  
You go, malt brewer,  
Barley crusher to the river.  
Two handfuls of oats afterwards,  
Then the woman will be healthy again."

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God, dear little God!  
You let me sleep for a long time.  
When this residue was left,  
When this seed was mixed.  
Mixed with men's seed,  
Ruined women's luck.  
Everyone else got something, everyone got high:  
I got black and foolish  
A tar stump and sickly.  
Where should I go with the black?  
Then I will start tending my beloved  
To grow chamber raspberries.  
The snuffbox stands on the corner  
A bar of soap on the wall,  
A white headscarf on the end of a rod.  
If I find the way, I will wave,  
If I find the puddle, I will wash,  
If I find the box, I will take again.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
What drives a horse crazy?  
What drives a rifle mad,  
What makes a woman foul?  
A fool is a flirt,  
A home daughter a scamp,  
A family daughter a deceiver,  
A farm daughter a little brat.  

A madman throws a horse into a ditch,  
Chases a greyhound under the hill,  
Lets loose a fox in the forehead,  
Dashes a doe into a stone,  
On top of a tall cliff,  
Into a red tree root,  
Into black mud like a serpent.  

A madman with a rifle bangs:  
Shoots into the soles of the feet,  
Aims between the eyes.  

A naughty woman has no enjoyment:  
With whom can she show tender love,  
Who feeds the domestic bird.  

A slut is naked:  
No rags on her body,  
Nothing on her skin.  

A home daughter is a flirt:  
Does not spare her mouth afterward.  

A deceiver beats the family daughter,  
Hits hard without mercy.  

A bratty farm daughter:  
Swears with crazy words.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my dear home of gold,  
Love my father’s hearth,  
Berry from grandmother’s land,  
Better place in the room.  
I did not see days of hunger,  
Did not hear harsh words,  
Malicious barking,  
Angry swearing.  
I went to a sensible man,  
A pitiful marriage,  
With an immature partner.  
A partner does not grow,  
A man is not willing to take.  
Now I see days of hunger,  
Now I hear harsh words,  
Father-in-law’s mother’s barking,  
Hiding angry swearing.  
Better to go to the servant’s village,  
Than to endure the master’s mockery,  
The servant goes plowing the field,  
The father-in-law’s son to help,  
The husband’s brother goes to the mill.  
The master snaps at the room,  
Seeks to fight with words,  
Seeks to quarrel with clothes,  
Trim-trim with nonsense.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
A mute little bird  
My sweetheart’s fiddler  
Together the kannel player  
Takes me to sleep with the fiddle  
To the kannel’s chamber  
Morning rises  
Takes the fiddle under the piano  
From the kannel’s chamber to the end of the yard  
Rise, flower-milking cow  
Rise, dear Anna, to give milk  
Rise, beautiful one, to drive the herd  
Other herds in the cattle yard  
Our herd in the distant field  
Other bulls in the mud  
Our bulls on the grass  
Other cows unmilked  
Our cows in the grove.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Family daughter, young maiden  
Farm daughter, little plant  
I ask for your hand in visit  
I do not seek advice from another woman  
I take your hands into mine  
Fingers from mittens  
Eyes from silk scarf  
Why do I knead father-in-law’s breads  
Why do I knead mother-in-law’s breads  
Rolling family cloths  
Making new white ones  
I throw old ones to the wool  
Foam jumped into the iron  
Clumps in the comb  
Tears to another family.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my honey man,  
Or my fishing companion!  
Every Monday has gone fishing.  
Monday brings eels,  
Tuesday brings smelts,  
Wednesday nice fish,  
Thursday mother’s eels,  
Friday Riga roaches,  
Saturday broad burbots,  
Sunday pikeperch.  
Surely the fish will come home,  
Roes are falling by the bed,  
Trouts into the oven to fry,  
Pikes to the smokehouse to cure.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Prayer birds, sorrowful birds,  
Forest cranes, sad birds,  
Forest ducks, humble birds,  
Go through my homes,  
Pass through my homes,  
Bring word to my father,  
Bring word to my mother,  
Inform the old man:  
How boring life is for me,  
The place of the maiden.  
When I cry for my brother’s face,  
When I cry for my brother’s wreath,  
The whole year passes old position.  
Where I sit, the seat is wet,  
Where I stand, the walls are slippery,  
I desire, but my place is empty,  
I cannot find understanding in the night,  
Home daughters together.  
The home daughter paid with gold,  
In father’s house paid with berries,  
To the copper brother’s widow.  
He demanded, pay the water,  
That flows from under the nail to the nail bed,  
Between the toes.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I send regards to my father,  
I send regards to my mother,  
I send regards to my brothers,  
Yet the sisters sulk,  
The brothers’ wives nod:  
It is not born to go in autumn,  
It is not allowed to walk in spring:  
The autumn rivers are deep,  
The spring waves were wide,  
The apple fell into the stream,  
The ewe stayed between the tussocks,  
The bullock into the waves.  
I send regards to my brothers,  
Yet the sisters sulk,  
The brothers’ wives nod,  
They say where the great eater comes from,  
The great eater, the great drinker,  
The one who places much,  
He drinks up the food even while eating,  
He drinks from the keg even while drinking.  
I understand, I answer again:  
What can be eaten from that growler,  
What can be drunk from that keg,  
Bones are underneath, skin on top,  
A piece of tough meat;  
Dregs underneath, foam on top,  
A drop of clear beer.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I send regards to my father,  
I send regards to my mother,  
I send regards to my brothers,  
Yet the sisters sulk,  
The brothers’ wives nod:  
It is not born to go in autumn,  
It is not allowed to walk in spring:  
The autumn rivers are deep,  
The spring waves were wide,  
The apple fell into the stream,  
The ewe stayed between the tussocks,  
The bullock into the waves.  
I send regards to my brothers,  
Yet the sisters sulk,  
The brothers’ wives nod,  
They say where the great eater comes from,  
The great eater, the great drinker,  
The one who places much,  
He drinks up the food even while eating,  
He drinks from the keg even while drinking.  
I understand, I answer again:  
What can be eaten from that growler,  
What can be drunk from that keg,  
Bones are underneath, skin on top,  
A piece of tough meat;  
Dregs underneath, foam on top,  
A drop of clear beer.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I am a drunkard, an old man from Nurme  
A tavern-goer, a little boy  
I still drink at the tavern  
I sing songs on the tavern table  
Alone at night for two  
For four weeks  
For two to three months  
For five quarters  
I drank bulls out of the stable  
I drank stallions out of the harness room  
Cows from the milking place at home  
Still left to grumble  
Hooves left to rustle  
Scraps left to listen  
The nape left to laugh  
Talks remain to tell tales  
I still drink at the tavern  
I sing songs on the tavern table  
I don’t know where my sweetheart is  
Whether she is outside or in the yard  
A young maiden  
Go outside to check  
Look into the tavern yard  
Whether she is outside or in the yard  
Or has been locked in  
Or is in the grasp of the blizzard wind  
A wide stable for the pilot  
Hundreds of big walls  
The maiden understood and replied  
She is neither outside nor in the yard  
Nor is she in the grasp of the blizzard wind  
A wide stable for the pilot  
Hundreds of big walls  
She stands here by the wall  
Hiding among the crows  
A young maiden  
You bring a starry blanket  
Wipe my sweetheart’s dew  
Swish, spread the dust.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Iiut-tiiut I bought a plough  
With the plough I bought an ox  
With the ox I took a wife  
With the wife I lived life  
With a pipe I kept a family  
With tobacco I fed the children.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Iiut-tiiut I bought a plough  
With the plough I bought an ox  
With the ox I took a wife  
With the wife I lived life  
With a pipe I kept a family  
With tobacco I fed the children.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before last to the other side  
I took her to the gentle hayfield  
And under that golden arch  
She mowed the beautiful hay  
Made a lovely arch  
The grass grew a creeping veil  
Bluebells a strand veil  
Dew grasses a side veil  
But the wife began to groan  
To cry out, to moan  
She threw her apron into the bushes  
Pearls into the hay arch  
I went home even crying  
Crying even out of fear  
I put her in a black coat  
Between gray shoulders  
I went to seek help for my wife  
An old woman came out  
Old woman, old man  
Why do you cry for your young husband  
Or what do you lament for the townsmen  
Why should I cry, little woman  
Or why should I lament, little old man  
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before last to the other side  
I took her to the gentle hayfield  
And under that golden arch  
She mowed the beautiful hay  
Made a lovely arch  
The grass grew a creeping veil  
Bluebells a strand veil  
Dew grasses a side veil  
But the wife began to groan  
To cry out, to moan  
She threw her apron into the bushes  
Pearls into the hay arch  
But the woman understood, the man replied  
Go home, young man  
Take the barley to the ditch  
The oats two by two to the haystack  
Then make the wife oil  
Then the wife will get well  
Quite well, even fully well  
Like other wives  
I went home even crying  
The wife sat by the stove  
Searched for support on the chair  
Ah, from the little oven help  
Come Jesus, come Jesus  
That Jesus had come into the room  
Twenty hands, twenty legs  
Twenty fingers, nails

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I could have been a girl  
A girl, just a girl  
I could have worked as a girl  
Being a servant to my father  
Being a servant to my mother.  
I could have gone out for my brother  
I could have been in the manor for my sister  
If I ended up with a bad man  
A scoundrel of a corpse  
A horse changer  
A hat maker  
A dog collar cutter.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Village old women, little old women  
Village houses, little rooms  
Help to understand  
Give a little advice  
I took a young wife  
Yesterday the wedding started  
Today is the proper wedding day  
My wife is so sick  
God knows what will happen to her  
Old women by the bed  
But they send me outside  
I turn outside  
But then turn back inside  
A woman came to the door  
Tiny in an apron  
A little in a scarf  
A bit in a kerchief  
But the woman immediately understood and said  
Oh you foolish young man  
You have pinched the wife  
You have stroked under her stomach  
Throw the malt aside  
Barley straight into the river  
Oats after two  
Rye carry quickly to the mill  
Make the wife without oil  
Give the wife some sour whey to drink  
Then the wife will become healthy  
Healthy and strong  
Like other wives  
Oh, if I say it differently again  
I will turn the words upside down  
Other wives make a mess  
My Kärt's little stool  
She does not spoil clothes  
Nor waters the cupboard.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
The woman said, "Take a wife."
The man said, "Be without one."
I took a young wife last year,
The year before last, across the way.
She was plump and red,
She was pale and painful,
She was healthy and sharp.
She began to suffer from her knees,
To die from her garter,
To wither from her shoelace.
I went searching for a doctor,
I rode to the five corners of Viru,
I rode to the twelve corners of Kuura,
I searched thoroughly through Luane.
An old woman came to meet me.
"Go home, young man!
Take some of this whiskey,
And some of this cheap beer!"
Then the woman became healthy,
Healthy and sharp,
Plump and red,
Pale and strong.
Then the old woman came back,
She had a small apron on,
A little rag on,
And a nicely fitted petticoat.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I went to Veigu’s brother  
And to Veigu’s sister-in-law too  
The indifferent brothers do not know me  
Nor the indifferent sisters-in-law.  
For an hour I stood unknown  
For another hour unrecognized  
I heard I stood by the hut sailing  
During the day a long towel was wiped  
Respect your shoulder  
There is no cloth tearing  
There are no bitter debaters  
By the wreath on the head of the wiper  
Comes my own Ella’s brother  
Comes the sister to talk  
To offer a seat  
For me to sit down  
Veigu’s sister-in-law  
Asks me riddlingly  
Where have you come from  
Have you come from Suaramaa  
Or from across the Russian border?  
Veigu’s sister-in-law  
Asks me puzzlingly  
What will I eat  
What will I drink  
The rat’s intestines without food  
The moor’s juice untaken  
The cat’s fur without scratching  
I will give yesterday’s eastern lient  
Today’s eastern lient  
Three-day-old female lient  
A week’s needle lient  
A bitter ink lient  
A mild broth lient.  
I hear, I answer again  
I understand the verse, I reply again.  
Veigu’s sister-in-law  
I did not come to eat  
I did not come to drink  
I came to talk to my brother  
To greet my sister-in-law.  
At home I ate to my heart’s content  
At home I drank to my fill  
At home I sipped the broth  
I left home with three dishes  
The oven had duckling broth left  
The firepit had bird broth left  
Behind the chamber was sheep broth left  
At home I ate to my heart’s content  
At home I drank to my fill  
Veigu’s sister-in-law  
Let the servant go home  
To the field under the sun  
Surely your inks will be tipped  
The broths will be tasted  
The meat broths tested  
The needle broths sniffed.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
What is a tall man made of
From proud beans,
From white foam;
A man is loud with a white fist.
A head strong with a fist,
Quickly comes the fist's strength,
With harsh fists loud.
A man has a wise mind,
A flour sack whiter,
A grain barn clearer,
A wallet heavier;
It won't hit me to the bone
Nor hit me twice,
If I do the fire dance,
Burn a little fire.
Seven flax seeds to burn -
Then he makes a little whip from thread,
A little handle from straw -
Then he snaps at the woman,
Still he jumps to try
If there is a whip in his hand.
Because I praise my man
As a strong lifter,
A hard carrier of bags;
Look, how strong a lifter,
He lifts a nail like a stick,
Throws a nail like a woolen pin,
A nail on the shoulder like a lump. -
Because I praise my man
As a strong lifter,
A hard carrier of bags. -

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
My gender was unwanted,  
The sisters were jealous of the flowers;  
They left me to sleep,  
When there was a men's game with us,  
On the pine forest clearing was a groom’s flock,  
Then the boys got other drinks,  
Others got tough men—  
I got a fool and a fool,  
Pine stump and sickly;  
Where must I go with a black one  
To confront the sickly one,  
To rush with the fool,  
Where I hit water with a bottle,  
Where there is feed, there I diverge,  
Where there is a puddle I wash,  
The switch stands on the corner,  
A white scarf is on the stick,  
A piece of soap is on the wall—  
That’s why I wash the groom,  
Care about my beloved;  
I do not complain nor fear  
That I have become a slave to a servant,  
I have passed the dawn of a servant,  
Above was the luck of a slave,  
The poor child’s foal luck,  
The family son’s pipe luck,  
Pipe luck, whip luck—  
So I do not complain nor fear  
That I have become a slave to a servant.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my former life  
Beautiful childhood  
When I was growing up at home  
I sat in father's yard  
I grew like hemp fibers  
I rolled like an egg in the grass  
Across the yard, little sister  
Across the fence, little fence  
I did not grow without fish  
Did not grow without pork  
I did get to eat in the kitchen  
Early to eat in the barn  
Secretly ate in the boots  
Even more in lifelessness  
Now is the time of bitter childhood  
Now I must cry in the corner  
Or cry in the barn  
Secretly I can cry  
In a double room  
Even more in lifelessness  
Where I stand, the walls are wet  
Where I cry, the corners are wet  
I do not sleep in the evening  
I rise in the morning  
The floor was covered with water  
An ell of water under the bed!

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I was once my father-in-law’s daughter-in-law  
Father-in-law’s daughter-in-law, mother-in-law’s daughter-in-law  
When I managed to make the bed  
Made the bed, walked softly  
Always did chores  
Then I was left behind in my father-in-law’s mind  
Then I was left behind in my mother-in-law’s mind  
I became ugly in my brother-in-law’s mind  
I became cold in the servants’ mind  
I became a fool in my husband's relatives’ mind  
I started washing clothes  
The servants didn’t allow me to thresh  
Father-in-law didn’t want to help with the kitchen  
Relatives were full of criticism  
Mother-in-law didn’t want to speak with me

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Corner woman, a little weak  
Looks for help from the stove  
Seeks support from the winding  
Cannot get help from the stove  
Seeks support from the winding  
Come inside, little Jesus  
Place your hand on her chest  
Then it will get a little better  
After an hour, stronger.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before last on the other side  
I took her to the tender hayfield  
I placed gold upon her head  
He mowed the hay in the nook of the forest  
He took the glade in the trap of the forest  
Then her knees began to ail  
Her soft eyes to sleep  
Her shoelaces to untie  
Her apron strings to ache  
I asked the woman  
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before last on the other side  
I took her to the tender hayfield  
I placed gold upon her head  
He mowed the hay in the nook of the forest  
He took the glade in the trap of the forest  
Then her knees began to ail  
Her soft eyes to sleep  
Her shoelaces to untie  
Her apron strings to ache.  
The woman heard and answered back  
Young men, like her brothers  
Throw city boys into mud  
Barley was lying straight  
Oats into two middens  
Then make good beer  
Taste the sweet mead  
Make a fine brandy  
Knead bread to be tough  
Then the wife will be healthy  
Healthy and strong  
Then the time came for the child to roll  
Then the son came home  
Prepared for his time.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Village girls, tongue-tied,  
Parish girls, deceitful!  
They said the apron is better,  
More honest under the hem.  
I was diligent in believing,  
Foolish in taking advice!  
Let them take the hem under the apron,  
Under the hem of the apron.  
One can suffer a lot on the apron,  
Under the hem one can be sick!

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God, beyond power,
Great God, beyond the swamp!
Who left me sleeping:
When those women were given,
Necklaces were twisted.
Others got gems, all got heights,
I got black and foolish
A diseased tar stump,
A pea stump: just gray.
Where should I go with the black?
Face the sick?
Where there was water: there I splashed,
Where there was a puddle: there I flapped!
Where the moss was not wet,
No need to flap the puddle:
There I was born to give mouth
Immediately to change into a worm.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Hello, hello Tedre Rein!  
Did you see Kadaka Rein?  
Asking Rein from Rein:  
"Are you women healthy?"  
The women’s health is unknown,  
Some are healthy during the day, others are sick!  
Mother-in-law is healthy, mother-in-law is sick,  
The sugar mint is dying,  
In the support hut, there is a cold spell.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Three on the daughter's knee  
The first was Ella’s knee  
That was Ella grandmother’s knee  
The middle one was dear husband’s knee  
That was dear husband’s knee  
The last was the wretched knee  
That was evil husband’s knee  
Sudden father-in-law’s knee  
Sudden mother-in-law’s knee  
Künastu servant’s knee  
Nurjatu midwife’s knee  
The mad man’s knee  
When it was Ella grandmother’s knee  
Then you could run around in a shirt  
You could flutter in linen  
When it was dear husband’s knee  
The middle one was dear husband’s knee  
That was dear husband’s knee  
You could eat in the shed  
Secretly you could eat in the sauna  
Surely you could eat in the kitchen too  
In the double-layered blanket  
Even more in the living room  
Finally, that was evil husband’s knee  
Sudden father-in-law’s knee  
Sudden servant’s knee  
Nurjatu midwife’s knee  
The mad man’s knee  
You could cry in the shed  
You could cry in the sauna  
Surely you could cry in the kitchen too  
In the double-layered blanket  
Even more in the living room  
Everything comes upon  
The pig’s eye falls upon  
Father-in-law came upon, mother-in-law came upon  
Father-in-law came with a bull’s yoke  
Mother-in-law came with a cow-milker  
The midwife on the stove porch roof  
Her husband was a stone fist  
I got from the door to the yard  
Through the gate I got out  
Then I got more on the back  
I didn’t wait, I came out  
I went back quickly  
Then I go back inside  
I throw the chips on the bench  
Already the father-in-law spoke of goodness  
Already the mother-in-law spoke of goodness  
The servant spoke of gold  
Her husband had gone astray  
Already I was at home  
Who went from the door to the yard  
Went out through the gate  
Then began to argue back  
Began to scold the father-in-law  
To speak against misfortune  
To beg my husband  
Be well father-in-law  
Be well mother-in-law  
Teach the son goodness  
Don’t put him to sleep on the patch  
Don’t send him to the stove  
Forced to do heavier work  
Commanded to work faster  
Don’t put him to drink vodka  
Don’t make him want sour wine  
Don’t make him taste bitter beer  
Teach him to drink beer  
Wait, wait, come on, mother-in-law  
Let him go to the tavern a second time  
I dig a hole under the door  
Put chains on the hole  
I throw branches on the floor  
When he comes home from the tavern  
He immediately steps into the hole  
Breaks his neck on the branches.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Dear bride, do you hear me,
When I come to woo you,
Will you accept me;
Will you accept me with honor,
Or drive me away with shame:
You are black and hairy
And like a furry wreath.
If you accept me with honor,
Then I will give a betrothal gift,
Give a silver ring,
I will bring you a measure of vodka.
Come to me, my darling,
My little sweetheart,
I will set work for the bride:
I will make you give kisses,
Look for a head for the day,
Patch coats for the month,
Make a place for the year.
I will bring a thousand slippers,
A hundred pairs of socks,
I got them as presents for you,
Again, meant for you,
Even a pair of blushed shoes were given,
Those between the bands:
The nose was rubbed and worn,
Tilting and trembling.
There she cries with young eyes,
Her curly hair disheveled:
"This is my lace cap,
This is my silk eye kerchief,
A brightest neck scarf,
It flew here, it flew there,
It flew straight to the chamber,
Legs followed after."
Old man, old devil,
Give me back my honor,
And put on my red robe,
I am going to a new husband,
To be a young bride again.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Hour, hour little rain,  
Let it fall kindly,  
Maybe the hail will melt,  
Let the rain fall from the rod,  
There must be a serf,  
There must be a serf working,  
The serf is thoughtful,  
The farmer's wife is discontent,  
The serf is praised,  
The farmer's wife is scolded,  
The tomcat wants, it will kill me,  
The tomcat bothers, it strikes,  
The tomcat thinks, it hits!

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Hour, hour little rain,  
Let it fall kindly,  
Maybe the hail will melt,  
Let the rain fall from the rod,  
There must be a serf,  
There must be a serf working,  
The serf is thoughtful,  
The farmer's wife is discontent,  
The serf is praised,  
The farmer's wife is scolded,  
The tomcat wants, it will kill me,  
The tomcat bothers, it strikes,  
The tomcat thinks, it hits!

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Hour, hour little rain,  
Let it fall kindly,  
Maybe the hail will melt,  
Let the rain fall from the rod,  
There must be a serf,  
There must be a serf working,  
The serf is thoughtful,  
The farmer's wife is discontent,  
The serf is praised,  
The farmer's wife is scolded,  
The tomcat wants, it will kill me,  
The tomcat bothers, it strikes,  
The tomcat thinks, it hits!

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God, dear God,  
Great God above!  
Why did you let me sleep,  
When it was time to hunt?  
Others got the hares, all got the high ones,  
I got the black and foolish one,  
The tar stump and the sick one,  
The dead stump and fierce one;  
The birch stump and the hairy one!  
Others here wash their hare,  
Nurturing their lovers -  
I got the gray one,  
The poor furred one!  
Field to the side,  
Hare behind the back,  
Nose at the field corner,  
White scarf on the stick,  
A piece of soap on the wall.  
So I could be like others,  
Like the men of others.  
Then came the blessed evening,  
Dear month-play time.  
Hard then to call grandma,  
It’s sad to call to be a man,  
Come to bed, little toad,  
Step into the gray bed.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh Jüri behind the river  
Oh Mari under the earth  
Why did you leave me sleeping?  
When the maidens were being distributed?  
Others got the fair ones, everyone got the tall ones  
I got the dark and the foolish.  
A tar stump and a sickly one  
Where am I supposed to go with the dark one.  
To come with a tar stump?  
I found a stream, I splashed it  
I found a brook, I splashed it  
I found a pond, I washed in it.  
So that the dark one would have a different color,  
The tar stump a different color.  
Then I got a wife with a hooked nose  
Small and upright.  
Who knew how to handle fire  
To milk a cow without a chandelier  
Surely she kneaded bread clumsily  
And spilled a little flour.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife,  
The year before last – a different one.  
I took a healthy and strong one.  
I took a plump and red one.  
Very white and tender.  
I took her to the hay meadow,  
To the precious hay barn,  
Mowed beautiful hay there,  
Drove the little barn’s beauty.  
Did not leave a ribbon fluttering.  
The dew-hay began to grow,  
Suddenly started to weaken,  
Started to thin from the sole of the foot,  
Swelling from the apron strings,  
Fell ill from under the belt.  
I, the man, was left to ponder:  
To the village women’s prayer.  
The women understood and answered:  
Oh, you foolish young man,  
The night cannot be without weather,  
The moon cannot bear two -  
Weeks cannot be pinched,  
Now go quickly home,  
Put the little birds in the barn,  
Put the oats straight into the water.  
Then your wife will be healthy,  
Healthy – light –  
Like other women.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Hello farm, hello room  
Hello new doorframes  
Hello little noisy sparrows!  
May I come into the room  
Take care of the yard  
Ride outside on horseback?  
Perhaps you have springs inside  
Secret wells in the chamber  
Linen boards under the bench?  
Perhaps I will spoil the clothes’ edge  
My bride’s ornament edge  
In blue silk stockings?  
Let the speakers come out,  
Old fingers from in front of the stove  
Zippers from in front of the linen bench  
Make room in front of the stove ceiling  
In the hiding place above the bench  
Behind the back wall  
In front of the doorframe?  
A standing place in front of the wall.  
The drum is brought into the room  
A chair is offered on the floor  
Now our midday bread runs out  
Pieces of bread are aging  
Fur mittens are lasting  
Let me make a big bag from a sock  
Make a thief’s bag from a sleeve  
Make a carrying bag from a cupboard  
Where shall I find the pieces  
To carry fur mittens?  
Where shall I find the pieces  
To carry fur mittens?  
The fire is locked inside  
The well is dry at the hill  
The hayloft has no key  
Bread crumbs are in the book  
Milk pails with a lid!

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Among us is a proud girl  
She was a white girl in the parish  
Mareta from Keskküla, the nice one  
She was the village's pride  
Taken out in full bridal attire  
To her mother's wedding.  
Celebrations were held - for the couple  
Then illness began to come  
Hard days started ahead  
Joosep also told Kaie  
Oh, my poor young girl!  
Where should I lay you?!  
You must not be taken before the elders  
You must not be brought before the wise ones  
Not to your father Marjus's house  
Nor to your mother's beloved chamber  
I will send you to the pigsty  
In your clean wedding clothes.  
Joosep asked Kaie,  
Whose own is this?  
Kaie laid her head on the pillow  
Eyes inside the clothes  
Joosep asked a second time  
Oh, my young wife  
Tell your father  
Whose own is this?  
Kaie struck her fist on her chest  
Like a sturdy oak cup  
Nine are your fathers  
The tenth swings Kaie.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Called a member of the household,  
A son-in-law,  
A husband to the little ring finger,  
A listener to the gold necklace,  
A watcher to the copper necklace.  
Time came, a day rolled by,  
Had slept through the night,  
Endured two nights,  
The father-in-law began to growl,  
The mother-in-law began to growl.  
What was brought into this?  
There was no concern for the newcomer.  
From the iron horse’s back,  
From the whip on the reed horse,  
From the sash of the spotted tail,  
I heard and responded:  
What did you gain in wealth  
By the name of the maiden?  
One had a glove for a mitten,  
Even that one silly without a finger,  
Blind without a veil,  
The father got one stocking,  
The page boy got half a ribbon,  
The bride rider got a glove finger—  
That was what you gained in wealth  
By the name of the maiden.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I wanted to plant a seedling  
I wanted to plant a seedling  
I plant it in hard soil  
I put it in the cracks of the limestone  
Where no shoot grows  
No head grows.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Yesterday the wedding began  
And today the lunch ended  
My wife is very sick  
Very sick in bed  
Old women by the bedside.  
I stand and watch  
God knows what will come of it  
The heart is sad and the mind is weary  
The old ladies whisper.  
Go outside, man, young men  
Go outside to pass the time  
To sharpen a staff in the shed  
To break a twig in the shed.  
I went outside to pass the time  
I did not sharpen a staff in the shed  
Nor did I break a twig in the shed  
I came back inside  
Found a blemish beneath my apron  
And a lump under my sixpence,  
I was very startled  
This is not my fault  
This is the blacksmith's fault  
The work of the crippled boys.  
I will start harassing the woman  
Cornering the young woman.  
Surely you could make noise in the forest  
Surely you could shout in the grove  
Fly through the alder thicket.  
Why did you come into our room?  
The old ladies silence them  
Just leave the young men be  
They will grow to be herders  
They will grow to be stable boys  
They will become soup cooks.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before, another partner  
I led her to a tender hayfield  
Under the darling into the woods  
She mowed hay, clattered in the forest  
Took the forest trap for hay  
The willow bush snapped  
She mowed a bow, mowed two  
Started the third mowing meadow.  
Immediately she began to wither  
Seeing her eyes, began to wilt  
Started to suffer from knees  
To sleep from lung disease  
To knit from shoelaces  
To complain about the soles of her feet  
I went to ask the old woman.  
Oh, my dear old woman  
The wife remained dear and ill  
Started to suffer from knees  
To sleep from lung disease  
To lose the shoelace.  
And beyond, watch the young son.  
Take the oats scattered  
Carry the barley to the spring  
The rye to the linen  
We will kill the lambs here  
Fry the ducklings here  
We will beat the fibers upon the threshing floor  
A slap to the thigh  
Between the white horns.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh God, beyond the mountain
Beyond the mountain ridge
Who left me to sleep
Lulled me in your lap.
When this ridge was followed.
Men’s seed was mixed
Boy’s happiness was denied
Others got muffs, all got high,
Others became sharp men
I got black and stupid
A tar stump and sickly.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
What is under the care of a slave,  
At the mercy of minnows?  
A fire in an unlocked room,  
A fart in a parson without a net,  
A barn without a key,  
A well without a cover on the hill.  
This is under the care of a slave,  
At the mercy of minnows.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Other men go to the sea,  
Other husbands go fishing,  
My man rummages at home.  
I sent my man to the sea.  
The man replied deceitfully:  
He said the sea is cold,  
The shore is frozen.  
I went myself, my dear!  
The sea was not cold,  
The shore was not frozen.  
I brought fish, ate fish,  
Counted fish bones for the man.  
The man eats deceitfully.  
I began to heat the stove:  
A warm sauna for myself,  
A hot sauna for the man;  
A leafy whisk for myself,  
A juniper whisk for the man.  
Then I took the man in my arms,  
I took the man to be whisked.  
Then the man began to say a prayer:  
Oh, my humble wife!  
I am born along the heart,  
I go after your hand,  
I let my bare feet follow;  
I give my foot strength,  
Strength to your big toe.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
How should I go home!
When home is angry,
Words begin to disappear.
Who else but me,
To the daughter, to troubles,
To the more fatherless child,
To the more motherless child.
I roll the words into a ball,
Put the bad into a braid.
Let the water come to our land,
Water to our yard,
Then I throw the ball aside,
Let the braid loosen.
The water informs the brother,
The breeze tells the sisters,
The braid knocks on the father,
The braid knocks on the mother.
Then the evil old woman heard,
Then the evil old man heard,
The sick heard the sister’s house,
The anger made the brother roll.
Then came the kin with a great sword,
Then came the people with an iron sword,
My brothers bleed from the sword —
They came to listen for gold,
To look at the ainekest,
Whether the golden eyes are dry,
Whether the duck's face is hard.
Before, before, little old woman!
Before the spruce roots dry,
Before the oak roots harden,
Before the pine roots rot,
Before the golden eyes dry,
Before the duck's face hardens.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
How should I go home!
When home is angry,
Words begin to disappear.
Who else but me,
To the daughter, to troubles,
To the more fatherless child,
To the more motherless child.
I roll the words into a ball,
Put the bad into a braid.
Let the water come to our land,
Water to our yard,
Then I throw the ball aside,
Let the braid loosen.
The water informs the brother,
The breeze tells the sisters,
The braid knocks on the father,
The braid knocks on the mother.
Then the evil old woman heard,
Then the evil old man heard,
The sick heard the sister’s house,
The anger made the brother roll.
Then came the kin with a great sword,
Then came the people with an iron sword,
My brothers bleed from the sword —
They came to listen for gold,
To look at the ainekest,
Whether the golden eyes are dry,
Whether the duck's face is hard.
Before, before, little old woman!
Before the spruce roots dry,
Before the oak roots harden,
Before the pine roots rot,
Before the golden eyes dry,
Before the duck's face hardens.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Two are offered to me:
One is lazy, the other a widow.
Which one do I leave, which one do I take?
I leave the lazy one, I take the widow.
How I go to bed with the old man:
I give my hand like to a stump,
A kiss like to a dead pig,
Shame to call a father-in-law,
Ugly to call a companion,
Frightening to call a father.
Still, I say:
Come to bed, old man!
How do I give the old man my hand?
A hand like to a dry roll,
A kiss like to melting butter.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I used to be a young man  
I wore pleated shirts,  
I carried rolled-up sleeves. –  
Now I rustle in a sack,  
I wither in the barn.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Until now, I was Ella Eide's son,
When she had no husband,
I wore slit shirts,
I wore two-hooked ones.
Last year I took a young wife,
The year before last, the other side,
Then I crawled into the sack,
I live from the old horn,
Now I bend at the needle—

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I mowed hay that shimmered in the forest  
I took the loosening sound in the grove  
Started to ache in the knees  
Fell asleep from calf pain  
From the soles of the feet to the thigh  
Then I took it to the tender hayfield  
I called my dear to the arch's end  
Our little brave boys  
Haymakers, wood carriers  
Shakers of the tall forest  
I fell cutting down wet pines  
Wet pines, dry spruces  
I leave the ashes to grow  
Birch branches to be straight  
Branches groan like a father  
Leaves mourn like a mother  
But the trees like sons and daughters

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
There was a rye bread to live on
One was a mitten, probably a towel
Also this one was foolish to touch
Blind without a turn
They nursed a girl child
They nursed, raised life
They grew, embraced
A miracle to a righteous, beautiful soul
Grow to a righteous, humble soul
You become the firstborn to the grandmother
A step to the gravedigger
A defender against fire
A water carrier for Virve
The time came, the day arrived
Support was given to one unknown
Help was given to one disregarded
Did not understand to thank angrily
Did not give the beginning to help
So ended up a slave without searching
A bread eater without being found
Who is under the care of a slave
Under the will of daughters-in-law
There came a locked room
Farted in the guest room without a curtain
The well is without a lid
The cowshed is without a lock
These are under the care of a slave
Under the will of daughters-in-law
Under the control of the commander

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Village women are cold-hearted
Parish women are deceitful
They said I would remain without a husband
Live without a spouse
I got a faithful husband
A loving spouse
When he comes home from the tavern
He takes a carpet from the closet
A slippery door handle.
He rises early in the morning
The wife gets up to milk the cow
Dear wife, take the herd.
She makes a small whip from thread
A handle from a straw stalk
She strikes him once, she strikes him twice
Immediately he jumps off the bed
Are there rods on the belt
Tools on the arm.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
A Naughty Youngster  
When you go to the farmhand  
The farmhand goes to sleep  
The grain grows in the field  
This puts the flute to sleep  
The kantele takes it to the chamber  
The morning rises  
Rise, bird, milk the cow  
White bird, give milk to the calf  
Dear bird, join the herd  
The village herd goes to the distant one  
Our herd is to the herd-grass  
When you go to the official  
The official goes to sleep  
The official stands by the wall  
This puts the whip to sleep  
The kantele takes it to the chamber  
The morning rises  
Rise, bitch, milk the cow  
Old bitch, give milk to the calf  
Herd-bitch joins the herd  
Munde’s herd goes to the distant one  
Our herd is to the herd-grass.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
A young corner woman  
Does the farm's stable work  
From morning till evening.  
She goes to bed in the evening.  
She does not feel that hour,  
Nor thinks about the time  
When long troubles  
Start to trouble her.  

The poor woman gets up,  
Already heavy with age  
The old mother's long troubles.  
Pain runs down her back,  
Bites along the hip bone.  
What should I do?  

She rises from the bed,  
Lets herself kneel on the floor,  
Lies on her back against the wall.  
Leans her back against a pillar,  
Seeks support from a chair,  
From the corner cupboard for support.  
Asks for stability from the wall—  
Cannot lean on the chair,  
Cannot get support from the corner cupboard,  
Cannot get stability from the wall.  

Jesus waits in the chamber,  
Mary sleeps in the corner,  
When will help be called?  
What is the poor woman doing now?  
She asks little Jesus for help.  
Come to help, little Jesus.  

Jesus steps in to help now.  
Jesus had two hands,  
One big, one small.  
The big hand for visiting women,  
The small hand for the young corner woman.  

Jesus brings the boy to the room.  
The companion waits in the chamber.  
What do I pay Jesus?  
Jesus does not want money.  
Jesus wants kind hearts.  

What is the poor woman doing now?  
Come to Mary's help!  
Mary steps in to help.  
Mary had two hands,  
One heavy, one light.  

The heavy hand for visiting women,  
The light one for the young corner woman.  
Mary holds her daughter with her scissors,  
The daughter was brought to the room.  
Mary thinks in the chamber,  
What do I pay Mary?  
Mary does not want money,  
She wants kind hearts.  

The young corner woman  
Now you see the long weeks  
Of the old mother's long troubles.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
The birds sang with gentle tongues  
With gentle tongues and joyful spirits  
The flowers just began to bloom  
The blossoms lifted sweetly into the air.  
The wife began to joke  
To love her husband  
The woman did not want to smile  
The husband did not want to love  
I went to the forest to settle things  
To walk into the grove  
To step into the meadow  
To march to the berry fields  
To look for love herb  
To gather affection herb  
From the forest I found nettle herb  
From the grove I got affection herb  
From the meadow I got love herb  
From the berry fields sweet herb  
I gave the woman to sniff  
The husband to smell.  
The woman began to joke  
The husband began to love me.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Old man with an old devil’s beard  
Hell with the beard  
The old man caught me  
In his gray love  
From under the moonlit day,  
Through the wooden gate.  
Over the yard with apple trees  
Over a pile of rowan trees.  
I stoked the stove with kindling  
Set the sauna for the horse’s tail (sauna whisk)  
I took the whisk poorly  
On a cowardly donkey upwards.  
With a broom twig, I lit the stove.  
Then I added steam  
More miserably I endured  
The old man begged me:  
“Come and help, dear woman.”  
But I blocked the doors  
I loved the windows.  
Then my own ale came  
I took it in a shirt’s embrace.  
With a fur coat to the stable  
I took it to an anthill.  
Before the lice,  
Until the core of the lice,  
To the bees’ dominion,  
Special ones ate it themselves,  
The lice ate its tongue,  
The bees ate its mind.  
Then I was free from the carcass,  
From the gray-bearded devil.  
Now I am going to Latvia  
For half a year to the Cranberry land,  
For life, far to the Western island.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud abielust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Maiden, young girl,  
You go to the dear home,  
To the silver yard,  
To the copper gate.  
You had bread without crust,  
Given without fat.  
I go to the evil home,  
To the wicked yard,  
To the angry gate.  
My home is sad, my home is bad,  
My home has evil strangers,  
Bad words given.  
I wind this evil thread,  
Lay it on a birch bark piece,  
Tie it into shirt thorns,  
Press it between my belt.  
When I go to bring water from the well,  
I throw this thread into the water,  
Onto the birch bark piece.  
The water carries knowledge to my brother,  
The water carries knowledge to my sister-in-law,  
Feathers carry the news to relatives,  
Straw carries the news to my sister.  
To whom do I complain? To whom do I grumble?  
To whom do I cause pain?  
To the church stone on the road,  
To the limestone outcrop!  
The stone was stubborn, does not speak,  
The limestone was thick, does not tell.  
The stone does not understand the maiden’s words,  
The limestone does not grant the maiden’s request.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud abielust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Maiden, young girl,  
You go to the dear home,  
To the silver yard,  
To the copper gate.

I go to the evil home,

My home is sad, my home is bad,

To the church stone on the road,

The stone was stubborn, does not speak,  
The limestone was thick, does not tell.  
The stone does not understand the maiden’s words,  
The limestone does not grant the maiden’s request.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
My wife into the water hole  
The night wants river fish  
Big pike in the pike's den  
I plow whatever buzzes  
Sea shores rumble  
Father desires fish  
Brother catches burbot  
Madam wants big cod  
Cod's salmon's salmonberry was gone  
Oh, you cod's salmon-like  
Yesterday you swam to the sea  
Down south you swam to the spring  
Now you dance on the millet stalk  
Strike the cod with a spoon.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
My wife into the water hole  
The night wants river fish  
Big pike in the pike's den  
I plow whatever buzzes  
Sea shores rumble  
Father desires fish  
Brother catches burbot  
Madam wants big cod  
Cod's salmon's salmonberry was gone  
Oh, you cod's salmon-like  
Yesterday you swam to the sea  
Down south you swam to the spring  
Now you dance on the millet stalk  
Strike the cod with a spoon.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before, on the other side  
Sometimes she was plump and red  
Sometimes pale and in pain  
It was beautiful to go to the hayfield  
It was nice to walk under the arch  
From the beautiful one, he mowed hay  
From the nice one, hay on the cart  
He mowed hay, rustled in the forest  
Took a bundle, snapped in the forest  
She started to wither immediately  
Seeing the eyes, blossoming  
From the apron strings, swelling

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife  
The year before that another half  
She was healthy and sharp  
She was fair and nimble  
I took her to a tender meadow  
Carried her over a golden arch  
She began to suffer from knees  
To sleep under the rosehip bush  
To disappear from the edge of a basket  
To swell from the apron string

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Hans was from Harju, Jaak was from Järva  
Hans was a man from Harju, humble  
A strong man from Tinatupp  
A powerful man from Vaskiväitsa  
He went from Harju to bring a wife  
There were three girls there  
One was the daughter of a wealthy farmer  
The second was the daughter of poor people  
The third was a slave's daughter  
In between I took the poor girl  
Starting with the slave's daughter  
Then began to come home  
Along the water's edge  
Then the church bells rang out  
And the Tallinn bells chimed  
The Toompea bells struck the hour  
The woman died on the road to Narva  
She threw her apron into the bushes  
Threw it into the willow thicket  
The handle hung drops of dew below  
Hans came home crying  
He went down outside slowly  
The woman was there, the old man was there  
Both the old ones were there  
They started asking me:  
Why are you crying, young son?  
Why do I cry, old woman?  
Why do I cry, old man?  
I went from Harju to bring a wife  
There were three girls there  
One was the daughter of a wealthy farmer  
The second was the daughter of poor people  
The third was a slave's daughter  
In between I took the poor girl  
Starting with the slave's daughter  
I began to come home  
Along the water's edge  
Then the church bells rang out  
The Tallinn bells chimed  
The Toompea bells struck the hour  
The woman died on the road to Narva  
She threw her apron into the bushes  
Into the poor willow thicket  
The handle hung drops of dew below  
For this I cry, old woman  
For this I cry, old man

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Hello, hello, Tedre Rein!
Did you see Kadaka Rein;
I would ask Rein from Rein:
Are you all well?
The empty one knows the illness of those who have eny
One is sick today, the other is healthy.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Rise up, young nation  
Wake up, household children  
Rise, gold smith’s apprentice  
Among silver-haired ones  
Rise, milk the cow  
Rise, Atu, gather the herd  
Other bulls to the pasture.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Glasses, glasses, I found a Vitu  
Curls, curls, where did you find them  
On the mountain, but among the tussocks  
Iidu tiidu, I found a Tinga  
With the Tinga, I bought a horse  
With the horse, I made barley  
From the barley, I made beer  
With the beer, I took a wife  
With the wife, I kept a family  
The family says: beat the wife  
The farm says: kill the wife  
I certainly hear, I answer back  
What of the family beating  
What of the farm killing  
The family should beat with a rowan stick  
The farm should kill with a tominga stick.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I see a woman,
Proud and vain,
With foam-white hair,
Fed on chaff, fed on chaff,
Wash the chaff with fine water,
Boil the chaff with white water.
The chaff was pretty in the straw,
Proud among the lads.
But she bought raw, without oil,
Pork for a prostitute,
Eggs for a scoundrel.
Her own little woman
Had her dog at home.
Barking down outside.
When she came home from the straw,
She began to knock at the door,
Shook her head at the gate,
Looked for a quarrel in her clothes,
A fight with the soles of her feet,
From the wretched, nasty brats.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Other men go to the sea,  
Other husbands go fishing.  
My husband dozes inside.  
Sits under the stove bench  
A little pipe in his hand,  
A little hearth tool in hand,  
But he reads the people's promises,  
And thinks of putting out the fire.  
I went  
I went fishing with my husband.  
The man comes home lying:  
Says the fish were caught beforehand,  
The holes were fished out completely.  
Oh, poor little wife,  
What am I supposed to do?  
I will start stoking the stove,  
But the husband will go whisking.  
A man is going to church,  
I added steam to the sauna.  
I shut the sauna door,  
So it would finally get over.  
Oh, poor little wife!  
What was I supposed to do!  
The oxen and horses ate it all,  
The sheep ate it inside the barn,  
The cows ate it from the milking pail.  
Then it was finally over,  
Then I got rid of the worthless man,  
The drunk featherbrain.  
Then I myself decided to go to the sea,  
Then I myself will go and see  
If the fish were caught beforehand.  
I will go myself late,  
Along the thin road  
Along the shallow shallow.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife
The year before last, from across the way
That one was stout and red
That one was white and slender
I took her down to the hayfield:
She mowed hay—sparkled in the forest
Took the bedsheet—slapped the grove.
She began to suffer from her knees
Fell asleep on her calves
I began to tease my wife
Tickling under her belly
Pinching below her navel.......

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
I went to my brother’s to thresh:
When I stood in the granary,
In the sunlight,
With two hands in mittens,
In front of the cart horse.
My brother went to ask the woman:
Where did this threshing come from,
Did it come from Turkey,
Or did it fall from Germany,
Or roll in from the Russian border?
I hear and answer:
I was here myself,
When this room was built,
When this place was settled,
When the house was newly knocked together.
I twist a strong whip,
I dragged moss from the swamp,
I gathered stones from the field.
I will not leave here,
Until I split the firewood!
I take the firewood on my back,
I carry the thin branches on my shoulder.
My brother went to the woman to ask:
Put the pot on the fire,
Try to heat the kettle,
Bring in the pork!
Try a chicken egg,
Take some butter to rub,
Give some food to my sister,
Give some bread to my mother!

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Others got muck, everyone got keks,  
I got the black and the foolish,  
The tar stump and the sickly.  
Where to go with the black,  
Where to rush with the fool,  
Where to face with the sickly?  
Where there was food, I ate,  
Where there were waves, I splashed,  
Here I put the spruce to dry,  
The oak to harden,  
The pine to fade.  
The spruce dried golden,  
The oak silver,  
The pine sun-colored.

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Vaila aila, vaila wooden:
Where did the cucumber put the hook,
Pour the throat with a coat?
Out in the tavern on the pole,
On the bench of Pikkovi tavern,
On top of the cupboard in Kõpu tavern;
Under five jars of vodka,
Under two pitchers of beer.
Big toes itching,
Heels itching partially,
Right next to Kaasik tavern:
There I tip with a tip
Carrying a sack with honey
Drinking so that he would keep drinking.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I found a note, a note on the tinge  
With the note I bought a horse  
With the horse I took a wife  
With the wife I lived life  
With the pipe I maintained the family  
With tobacco I fed the children  
There was not a crumb of bread.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I found a note, a note on the tinge  
With the note I bought a horse  
With the horse I took a wife  
With the wife I lived life  
With the pipe I maintained the family  
With tobacco I fed the children  
There was not a crumb of bread.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
I even told the old woman  
Gave notice to the old man as well  
The woman, look for me  
Old man, listen along with me  
If the woman doesn’t want to come along  
If the old man doesn’t want to listen or marry  
Then I will wander to the village  
To the village’s cold warehouse  
To the unheated building  
To the uncleaned chamber  
Here a woman was taken last year  
This year another was brought  
Now the bed remained for weeping  
Sent me to look for a doctor  
To listen to the doctor’s words  
Old women came to meet me  
Where are you going, young man?  
But the man understood and answered again  
Here a woman was taken last year  
This year another was brought  
Now the bed remained for weeping  
For crying out loud on the bed  
Sent me to look for a doctor  
To listen to the doctor’s words  
Both the old ones responded  
Oh you foolish young man  
Go home and look for work  
You put the rye in the river  
The barley straight into the river  
The oats follow in pairs  
Make oil for your woman  
Then the woman will be well.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud abielust
translation:

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Others got soft, everyone got tall  
I got black and foolish  
Tar stump and grumpy  
Where must I go with the black  
Rush with the foolish  
Face the sickly  
Where I find a stream there I dip  
Where I find a puddle there I wash  
So that I wouldn’t be the only white one  
Like the other girls

label: Laulud abielust
translation:
Tiiu, with a rope I found  
With the rope, I bought a horse  
With the horse, I took a wife  
With the wife, I lived a life  
With instruments, I supported a family  
With tobacco, I fed the children

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I was the father’s albino child,  
I was the mother’s albino child.  
Driven away by the sisters,  
Subject to the brothers' anger.  
Father called me a monster,  
Mother called me premature,  
Sister called me a bad luck,  
Brother called me a millstone.  
Mother despised me from her bosom,  
Sisters shunned me from the yard,  
Brothers even from the grain fields,  
The parish from the church.  
Where should I soon go?  
Where should I soon rest?  
Shall my legs soon give out?  
Shall my body soon turn?  
I will go to become a mole in the soil,  
A rat in the grass nest;  
I will become a mole’s nut,  
The iris’s beauty attendant;  
The mole made me a black cap,  
The rat made me a grass hat.  
The iris adorned me,  
The hedgehog clothed me in silk.  
Then I went under the village swing.  
All those people puzzled,  
The lords marveled:  
Whose beautiful lady is she?  
Whose lovely maiden is she?

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When do I pay the old woman’s trouble!  
The old woman’s trouble, the old man’s trouble?  
Taking care of the old woman;  
Feeding her from the mouth:  
Biting from the forehead,  
Carrying the burden.  
The woman saw a great death,  
A great death, loud trouble;  
She endured hard bitterness,  
Now the soul chains are removed!  
The tried neck chains;  
They weighed heavily on the soul!  
On the dear neck veins.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Aunt called me a witch
Grandfather premature
Aunt despised from the chest
Grandfather despised from the knees
Did not consider kin as kin
Sisters not truly as own
Brother’s wife as a nuisance
Nephews as prisoners
Wait-wait, kin, no-no, kin
Let me make luck-barley in the yard
Rye from the ashes of the oven
Buckwheat behind the room
Lentils to the stew pot
I cut luck-barley from the yard
But rye from the ashes of the oven
Buckwheat from behind the room
Lentils from under the stew pot
Barley is for the house
Then I can support the kin
Battle against the brushwood
Here kin accepts as kin
Sisters truly as own
Brother’s wife as a nuisance
Nephews as prisoners
Then I plow with ten pairs
Horses harness nine
Mow with five sickles
Rake with eight
Cut with seven little scythes

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Oh, my Ella little grandmother  
And my wise little grandfather  
If you had known, you could have known  
If you had seen in a dream  
While sleeping it came to mind  
That I was born a sinner  
Grew up to be a wrongdoer.  
Rolled like a spinning top  
Could have been lying on an apron  
Carried a kettle to the shore  
Before carrying bundles inside  
Carried warm sauna steam  
The sea would have fed me, the sea would have watered me  
The sea would have given me to another man  
The holes would have given me gifts  
The burbot would have made a slippery track  
The fish with cat’s paws.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Now it is past midnight
Half without speaking.
The fire does not go out
The cold on the bedpost
The horse from the gray harness
The woman from the young clothes
Looked for a child's soother
Taker of the young's crying
Binder of the tongue
A little screecher
Despite, despite, little old woman.
Despite, I shouted
Tapped the dry mouth
A rod is for the child's soother
For the taker of the young's crying –

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Little Ella, little brother!
We are both lads.
Together comrades.
One calls the other by name,
One calls the other by nickname.
The dear one called tenderly,
The tender one called warmly:
Called the dear one curly-haired,
Called him the wheat washer, -
Dug up the oat plant.
I am the old woman’s bad child;
I am the old man’s bad child:
The enemy of brothers,
The great scold of sisters!
Father despised me from the knees,
Mother despised me from the chest,
Sisters burned me out of the yard,
Brother scolded me between the villages,
Threw me into the mossy mound,
Into the rat's grass nest!
There the mole got uniform,
The frog became a lung carrier,
So let me tell it again,
Let me sing it back again:
I had no father,
No mother in the world,
God knows who made me,
Whether the black grouse made me,
Or the black birds laid me,
Or if the shy little devils
Put me together.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
