label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
All my kin are hoop-earrings,  
All my brothers have vests on their backs:  
Brothers-in-law wear straw hats!  
I, poor one, have a five-foot walk,  
Five-foot walk, turn the shoe!  
Wait-wait, kin, no-no, kin;  
Let me go begging!  
There is a house where I get food;  
From the second house, I get food;  
From the third, I got a box full.  
Then I went to the mill:  
I set the mill rolling;  
To the miller, I paid food:  
To the second sack lifter;  
To the third grain drawer  
I brought food home from the mill.  
Then I made your beer,  
I call you a neighbor:  
Come, kin, hoop-earrings!  
All my brothers have vests on their backs,  
Sisters-in-law have silver hats.  
Come, neighbor, to the feast,  
With meat on the trough.  
I, poor one, have a five-foot walk;  
Five-foot walk, turn the shoe!  
Wait-wait, kin, no-no, kin!  
Let me grow stronger,  
Let me be ready for the feeding time of the pig.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Oh, poor me, child captive,  
Under the hearth’s embrace!  
There were three hiding places there:  
One was beside the door,  
Another in the middle by the floor;  
The third in the back corner.  
The fire never ended there  
A spark from the bedpost’s pillar  
A bonfire from under the kettle.  
What is there beside the door?  
Poor people were rocking it.  
What is there in the middle by the floor?  
That is mine to rock.  
What is there in the back corner?  
The village girls were rocking it.  
One was rocking softly,  
Another was gently swaying,  
The third was whispering at dawn.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Ten girls in the village!  
Nine across the street  
Eight by the herd:  
Seven on that side  
Six on the barn’s yard;  
Five on the brother’s meadow.  
They were not allowed to come to the swing!  
They feared the gold would wear out,  
Feared the copper would exhaust,  
The cash vessels would become empty;  
The clover vessels would empty,  
The silver beads would disappear  
From our two poor children!  
We are going, we are walking  
We are going under the swing  
Let our gold wear out,  
Let our copper shine;  
The silver beads to straighten out  
We have goldsmiths at home  
Outside are silversmiths:  
Tinsmiths by the road;  
Blacksmiths in the timber yard  
Gold smoke rises in our bag  
Silver smoke from outside  
Copper smoke from the meadow  
Tin smoke by the road  
Blacksmith smoke from under the timber pile

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Ten girls in the village!  
Nine across the street  
Eight by the herd:  
Seven on that side  
Six on the barn’s yard;  
Five on the brother’s meadow.  
They were not allowed to come to the swing!  
They feared the gold would wear out,  
Feared the copper would exhaust,  
The cash vessels would become empty;  
The clover vessels would empty,  
The silver beads would disappear  
From our two poor children!  
We are going, we are walking  
We are going under the swing  
Let our gold wear out,  
Let our copper shine;  
The silver beads to straighten out  
We have goldsmiths at home  
Outside are silversmiths:  
Tinsmiths by the road;  
Blacksmiths in the timber yard  
Gold smoke rises in our bag  
Silver smoke from outside  
Copper smoke from the meadow  
Tin smoke by the road  
Blacksmith smoke from under the timber pile

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Who has water nearby?  
Whose rowan is red?  
The butterfly has water nearby,  
Villem's rowan is red.  
Whose goat has scratched eyes?  
Whose piglets have dry pox?  
Tidri's goat has scratched eyes.  
Sahka's piglets have dry pox.  
Who has those bluish pigs?  
Who has those copper gates?  
Vilgut's bluish pigs.  
Tooma's copper gates.  
Who is that on the low grass?  
Who has the wobbly garden?  
Juhan's low grass,  
Jaagu's wobbly garden.  
Who has new barn doors?  
Who has fierce dogs in the yard?  
Mardi has new barn doors,  
Kangru has fierce dogs in the yard.  
Whose apple tree is beautiful?  
Whose garden is branchy?  
Niidu's apple tree is beautiful,  
Otsa's garden is branchy.  
Whose cows have bell collars?  
Who has those grey bulls?  
Kiik's cows have bell collars,  
Hans Jüri has grey bulls.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
What's wrong with living on an island  
It's beautiful to live on an island  
Nearby is the birds' song  
Welders, potters  
All came home to the eggs  
To incubate in the oven cupboard

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
My gentle old granny  
The wise old man  
Hours you grow plants  
There’s no time to plant them.  
You planted on hard ground  
On hard stony ground;  
Where shoots cannot sprout  
Leaves spread wide apart  
Heads not rolling on  
Into the mix of stone chips  
Into the thick limestone fragment.  
Shoes break against the quarry stone  
Shoelaces break on the limestone,  
The fragment gnaws the heart  
Scratches the skin of the hair  
Presses the honey wax  
Bleaches the milk-colored hairs.  
If only you had known, old granny  
If only you had known, could have known  
You might have even seen it in a dream,  
Thought about it while sleeping,  
Brought that knowledge in your drowse:  
That black was born guilty  
It grew under misfortune.  
You could have thrown it in the swamp  
Before you threw the bows.  
You could have turned the stump aside  
Before you turned into hiding.  
You could have wrapped in a moss clump  
Before you wrapped in a swaddle.  
Surely the rain would have nurtured it  
The sea would have played black.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Where must I go
Where must I lay my head?
Giving way beneath my feet
Turning my body at the center.
It is deep to go into the sea
Pitiful to go into the gray,
The shore calls to the well
The sea is black and deep
The well is long and dark
Oh, my boring life
And my dark feast!
A weeping young maiden
My old grandmother
Others found a place for bread
For me, they found a place of mourning.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
From the edge of the village to the edge of the forest,  
Set and loved,  
The places are very fitting.  
Planted for beauty,  
Placed for the sun to shine.  
The sacred grove is below, the stone is above,  
The sacred grove is below, hay to be mowed,  
The stone is above, the bed of grain,  
The village smells of caraway,  
Village fields with water lilies,  
Stream banks with wild vinegar,  
Streets with starry pear,  
Garden twigs are curled.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Young men, tender brothers,  
Why do you praise emptiness?  
You strike excessively,  
What will it enrich you?  
One is the barn courtyard,  
That barn linen.  
What else is in that barn?  
One is the chest in the barn,  
What was in that chest?  
Old trousers in the chest.  
How many buttons in front of the trousers?  
One button in front of the trousers.  
Even that is a wooden rattle,  
That will enrich you.  
Come to us, little brother,  
Come to us to see,  
What we have for the girls.  
Three are in the barn courtyard,  
One was a golden comb in the barn,  
Another a silver comb in the barn,  
The third a brass comb in the barn.  
What is the golden comb in the barn?  
It belongs to the young maidens.  
What is the silver comb in the barn?  
It belongs to the young women’s barn.  
What is the brass comb in the barn?  
It belongs to the young men’s barn.  
What is in our girl’s barn?  
The barn is full of silken boxes,  
Boxes full of silk,  
Box corners full of thalers.  
What is in our women’s barn?  
The barn is full of chests,  
Chests full of cloth,  
Pegboards full of fabric.  
What is in our men’s barn?  
The barn is full of chests,  
Chests full of grain,  
Chest corners bursting with money.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I worked despite others,
For my own honor.
I worked, was not thanked,
I struggled, was sworn at,
I thought to give a barn,
To carry the herd.
Who gives the barn to the goose?
Who keeps the herd for the hen?
The sister was given the barn,
The niece kept the herd,
To the brother’s wife, some small things.
To me, an empty barn was given;
An empty barn, a bare stable.
The barn was empty of clothes,
The stable was bare of sheep,
The room was poor of wool,
Wait, be patient!
Wait, great kin,
Be patient, broad fields:
Let me grow barley for the yard,
Barley for the street.
Buckwheat for the peasants,
Buckwheat for the Tartumaa path.
Then I can feed the kin,
Take the grain,
Cover the stables with the fields.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When will I pay grandmother’s toil,  
Grandmother’s toil, tender milk,  
And mother’s nursing toil?  
The sucking at the mouth,  
The carrying on the hip.  
How many nights were sleepless,  
How many days without lunch.  
How many mornings without rest,  
How many smoking sessions without joy.  
The fire never ended,  
The spark didn’t fade from the hearth,  
The blaze roared.  
The woman quarrels with the young,  
The horse with the gray harness.  
She seeks children who speak charms,  
Charmers, dancers,  
Those who take away young tears,  
Promised to the lamb’s charmer,  
The goat’s tongue-teaser,  
The foal’s playful colt,  
The gray bull’s healer,  
The kimmel tongue-beater,  
The taker of young tears.  
Now I would come to thank by word  
And ask on a tongue string.  
To praise little Jesus with my tongue,  
To give a thank offering.  
Wear the scissors in Mary’s hands,  
The knees on the Creator’s lap.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I breastfed, grandmother  
I breastfed the girl,  
I breastfed, cherished her  
I raised her, hugged her.  
A wonder, a gentle young daughter,  
A wonder, grow dear  
From you comes the grandmother’s hall  
At the grave they tread solemnly  
The whirlpool is the water bearer,  
The firewood is the firewood carrier,  
The chip, the decorator  
The maker of the bows’ shine  
The illuminator of the trays  
The washer of the milk cans.  
The time came, the end rose  
Help came to the one not counted,  
Support to the one not recognized,  
The stove remained grandmother’s helper,  
The room remained grandmother’s support,  
The log remained the neck breaker,  
The sled remained the hand warmer,  
The cradle’s daughter.  
Help came to the other tooth  
Support came to the other shoulder,  
Help came to the garden tool  
Support came to the wooden staff. -

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Be proper, our girls  
Be proper, walk tall,  
Surely proper behavior will show  
When gathering the tall ones.  
Properness was taken because of manners,  
Tallness because of beautiful singing,  
Daughters were taken because of work,  
Visits for marriage were done because of skills,  
Engagement was arranged because of the finger,  
Not because of the father's scolding,  
Nor because of the brother's cattle,  
Nor because of the mother-in-law's good looks,  
Nor because of the father-in-law's good lands.  
When I was once asked for marriage,  
The horses stayed in the father's yard,  
The calves stayed in the brother's yard,  
The herd stayed in the brother's pasture.  
I began to leave,  
From the yard I took sheaves of straw,  
From the gate I secretly took twigs,  
Even that I took secretly,  
To surprise over the fence,  
To pass through the fence.  
Father called me stingy,  
Mother called me premature,  
The servant called me a meltaway,  
The children called me a table sweeper.  
I got to live, I got to be,  
Wait, wait, kin, bad kin,  
Let my room be made,  
New rooms built,  
New houses painted,  
Then father will come with decorations,  
Mother with anticipation,  
Then I will be able to feed my kin,  
To take back to the family,  
To support relatives,  
To comfort downfalls.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:

To take back to the family,  
To support relatives,  
To comfort downfalls.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When do I repay grandma’s effort  
Grandma’s effort, tender milk  
Good mother’s breastfeeding  
Rocking by the mouth  
Carrying on the hand,  
How many nights were sleepless,  
How many mornings were to be endured,  
How many smoke without size,  
How many days without lunch.  
The fire in the hearth did not end  
The spark from the bedpost  
The fire from the fireplace  
From the young maiden’s clothing  
From the gray horse’s harness.  
The child sought a storyteller  
The young one a weeper  
Promised the sheep storyteller  
The dung-runner of the dung-heap  
The goat tongue-beater.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
My dear little grandmother,  
A gentle, wise little early snow,  
You brought plants to grow,  
You did not know how to plant them.  
You planted on hard ground,  
On hard rocky land,  
Mixed with stones,  
In thick chalky soil  
Where no shoot can sprout.  
Leaves spread wide open,  
But you did not curl them inwards,  
Quarry stone, little grandmother,  
Your shoes break the quarry stone,  
Lime breaks the ties,  
The shards eat the heart,  
The hair grows coarse and rough,  
It presses down the wax of the bees,  
Fades the milky-haired ones.  
If only you had known, little grandmother,  
If only you had known, could have known,  
You might have seen it even in your dreams,  
Thought of it even in your sleep,  
Brought it to knowledge during naps,  
Given it as knowledge while dozing,  
That black was born guilty,  
Grew among losses,  
You could have thrown it away,  
Before you threw away the bow,  
You could have turned it into yarn,  
Before you snapped the thread,  
You could have wrapped it in a bundle,  
Before you wrapped it in a diaper.  
Surely the rain would have nurtured it,  
The sea would have played with the black one.  
Oh, my life is dull,  
And my celebration dark,  
A weeping forest of youth,  
My dear little grandmother,  
You found me a place of bread,  
You found me a place of mourning,  
Where must I go?  
Where must I lay my head?  
Give way under my feet,  
Turn from the center of my body,  
Deep is to go into the sea,  
Harsh to go to the spring,  
To call to the dear well,  
The sea black and deep,  
The well long and dark,  
Grayish, gray and muddy.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I worked, but received no thanks,  
I toiled, but was sworn at;  
I worked despite others.  
After my own honor,  
I thought to help donate  
And carry to the herd.  
Time came, days passed,  
Who gave the goose a barn,  
Who carried the herd to the hen!  
The younger sister was given a barn,  
The older brother a valuable herd,  
The sister-in-law some calves.  
To me was given an empty barn,  
An empty barn, an empty stable,  
The barn was empty of clothes,  
The stable empty of sheep,  
The room poor in chests.  
When I began to leave,  
I got a hay band from the yard,  
From the gate I got some withered twigs,  
Even that I got secretly,  
Without the old woman's knowledge,  
Without the old man's knowledge.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Father called me long ago  
Father called me a thief  
Mother called me untimely  
Brother called me a little guard,  
Where must I go?  
Where must I lay my head?  
I will go to the mole as a mole  
To the frog as a pot boiler  
To the hedgehog as a feeder  
The mole will bring me a black apron  
The hedgehog will bring seven linen aprons  
The frog will bring a three-curled one  
The lynx will bring beautiful silver  
Then the pretty one began to live  
To do quiet work  
The guard began to walk outside  
Then father appeared to the pretty one  
Mother came to the untimely one  
Brother came to the little guard.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I had a home, I paid with gold  
Father with silver in the yard  
Brother with copper in the fields  
Brother with tin on the road  
Now this land is not paid for  
That lies under the goose’s foot  
Between the toes of the little branch  
In the middle of the claws  
Father called me a thorn  
And mother, before time  
Wait, wait, aunt, no, no, uncle  
Let a little one live  
Do steady work  
Maybe I plow with ten pairs  
A horse pulls nine  
Mows with five sickles  
Cuts with seven little scythes  
Rakes with eight

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I am the grandmother’s bad child  
I am the grandfather’s bad child  
The grandmother despised me down to my knees  
The grandfather despised me from my face  
My brother despised me from my hair  
My husband despised me from my eyebrows  
Where must I go  
Where must I lay my head  
Give way from under my feet  
I will go as a mouse to a hole  
I will go as a mole to a mound  
As a rat to a grass nest  
I began to be, I began to live  
From a mouse I became a lady  
From a mole I became a mole-rider  
From a rat I became a Swedish German  
Then I went as a stranger to grandmother  
I went as a stranger to grandfather  
The little grandmother did not recognize me  
The little grandfather did not recognize me  
Where has this stranger come from  
Is she from Finland or from an island  
Or is she a maiden from Riga  
Or is she a German from an island  
Or is she the king of Courland

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Where I gilded gold  
I served a silver wreath  
I cherished a blue ribbon  
I went to the forest among men  
Among boys bringing wood  
Behind Narva among women  
I have a field of flax  
And a cartload of butter  
I myself lifted the hazel branch  
I myself, poor, won the cart  
There I gilded gold  
I served a silver wreath  
I cherished a blue ribbon  
What I earned as a girl working  
A poor servant struggling  
From the yard I got a hay band  
Early in the morning I got a twisted twig  
That too I hid secretly  
Cutting through the fence  
Let me say it again  
Let me sing the words back  
The master gave me a press  
The mistress gave me a pin  
I myself bought a bead string  
I put a string of pearls on my head  
That I earned as a girl working  
A poor servant struggling.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When I pay grandmother’s trouble  
Grandmother’s trouble, tender milk,  
Grandmother’s good nursing trouble;  
How many nights were sleepless,  
How many mornings were waitless,  
How many smokes without a fire,  
The fire did not end in the room,  
No spark in the bedpost.  
The horse does not neigh at the gray patch,  
The young woman does not quarrel,  
She sought for the child who would speak,  
For the husky stallion’s paces,  
Let it be, let it be, dear grandmother,  
Let summer come, and autumn follow.  
I will bring mat of strawberries  
Half a measure of lingonberries,  
A measure of old cranberries.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When Do I Pay for Grandma’s Trouble  
Grandma’s trouble, tender milk  
Work by the mouth, troublesome  
Carrying gold in the hand  
How many nights were sleepless,  
How many days without lunch,  
How many smokes without luxury  
The fire did not go out back  
The spark not from the bed’s post  
The campfire not from the logs  
The woman not from young clothes  
The horse not from gray harness  
Searching for the child’s speaker  
Taking away the young one’s cry  
Promised to the lamb’s speaker  
To the rump’s stallion to the bringer  
To the goat’s tongue tester

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I was like pieces in a river,  
Grew like hemp in time,  
I would sleep on clover hay  
I couldn’t fall asleep,  
I couldn’t see daylight  
Rolled like a rain pearl  
Hid behind a snow cloud  
If I had known, could have known  
I would have taken a nap on the lap,  
Planted in the frostbox  
I was tender even to the old woman  
I thought to grow into two,  
Into nine.  
I grow strong alone,  
Rolled under a seesaw  
Got a whip on my back,  
Suffered the manor’s whip.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Oh, little Jüri
In my mind, a little worry
In Mundi's mind, a piece of earth
In my mind, a piece of gold
Take me or leave me.
Beauty does not leave anywhere
Pleasure does not bring joy to anyone
I was the old woman's bad child
And I was the old man's bad child
I went underground as a mole
As a rat in the grass bush
As a frog under a burdock leaf
The mole brought me a black hat
The frog brought me a tavern apron
The rat brought green silk
Then I hit the old woman’s stepfather
And I went to the old man's stepfather
Where did that lady come from
She probably came from Germany
Rolled from the Russian trail
I heard, I responded
She has not come from Germany
Rolled from the Russian trail
I am the old woman’s bad child
I am the old man’s bad child
The mole brought me a black hat
The frog brought me a tavern apron
The rat brought green silk.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
How the Lazy Woman Said  
If I had barley in front of the door  
Oats for the room maid  
Lentils for the dairy maid  
Then I could gather fodder  
Take the cow to the mother  
Set the table for the barn doors  
The wolf was a watchdog of Hulja  
The fox was the barn keeper  
The duck was the proper barn boy  
The lizard was the bed maker  
The cuckoo was the hander over  
The sparrowhawk was the herder  
The crow was the receiver  
The magpie was on a long journey  
When will I go to my father  
When will I go to my mother  
When will I roll to my brothers  
When will I scold my sister  
It’s no time to go in autumn  
Spring is no good for walking  
Autumn rivers run deep  
The horse drowns in that brook  
The donkey among the tussocks  
The rune goes to the manor

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
How the Lazy Woman Said  
If I had barley in front of the door  
Oats for the room maid  
Lentils for the dairy maid  
Then I could gather fodder  
Take the cow to the mother  
Set the table for the barn doors  
The wolf was a watchdog of Hulja  
The fox was the barn keeper  
The duck was the proper barn boy  
The lizard was the bed maker  
The cuckoo was the hander over  
The sparrowhawk was the herder  
The crow was the receiver  
The magpie was on a long journey  
When will I go to my father  
When will I go to my mother  
When will I roll to my brothers  
When will I scold my sister  
It’s no time to go in autumn  
Spring is no good for walking  
Autumn rivers run deep  
The horse drowns in that brook  
The donkey among the tussocks  
The rune goes to the manor

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
How the Lazy Woman Said  
If I had barley in front of the door  
Oats for the room maid  
Lentils for the dairy maid  
Then I could gather fodder  
Take the cow to the mother  
Set the table for the barn doors  
The wolf was a watchdog of Hulja  
The fox was the barn keeper  
The duck was the proper barn boy  
The lizard was the bed maker  
The cuckoo was the hander over  
The sparrowhawk was the herder  
The crow was the receiver  
The magpie was on a long journey  
When will I go to my father  
When will I go to my mother  
When will I roll to my brothers  
When will I scold my sister  
It’s no time to go in autumn  
Spring is no good for walking  
Autumn rivers run deep  
The horse drowns in that brook  
The donkey among the tussocks  
The rune goes to the manor

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Therefore I am beautiful  
The girl ate beautiful berries  
Make the berry bushes beautiful  

Therefore I am harsh  
The girl ate harsh berries  
Make the berry bushes harsh  

Therefore I am painful  
The girl ate painful berries  
Make the berry bushes painful  

Therefore I am sharp  
The girl ate sharp berries  
Make the berry bushes sharp  

Therefore I am red  
The girl ate red apples  
Make them red  

One was a blossom of the apple tree  
One was the apple on the branch  
That too was many-haired  
Beautiful on the evening side  
Red on the sunrise side  
Cross-shaped on the Riga side  
Knobby on the Narva side  

That was my little girl  
Who was kept in the building  
Kept in the milk chamber  
She was not taken into the rain  
Nor put in the sunshine  
Afraid of smoke inhalation  
Afraid of bleaching by the sun  
Afraid of the pulling of the wind.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
My old grandmother  
Give me a key to the shed  
Give me a key to the chest  
Lend me the key to the cabinet  
I am going to decorate the shed  
To fix the chest  
I put on a silk shirt  
Wearing twisted cuffs.  
Send me away secretly  
Before I lack wood  
There is a case of nettle  
Nettle has big roots  
Burdock has clever children  
The tree root has a large family.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I had a rich godmother  
I had a rich godfather  
They took me to Riga to be baptized,  
To give me the name Tallinn  
Now they gave me a golden locket  
As an old taler’s light.  
I ate gold up to my throat  
Into the old taler’s belly  
Because I got a golden little throat  
A silvery little voice.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When I endure the grandmother’s toil,  
The grandmother’s toil and tender milk,  
The dear woman’s carrying,  
The dozing by the mouth.  
The maiden guarded me with her soul,  
Redeemed the frail flesh.  
The old man bought me oats,  
Also got me a salve for wounds.  
Many nights were sleepless,  
Many mornings without smoke,  
Partly missed.  
The fire did not end there,  
The spark did not lie in the bedpost.  
The woman argued with the young,  
The horse was in the gray harness:  
Sought those who soothe a child,  
Those who take away a young cry,  
Promised a sheep to the soother,  
A steed to the tail-holder,  
A couple of oxen to the prayer-giver.  
I screamed in spite,  
Tasted the mouth without a bride.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
What the daughter gets to work on,  
The enemy child gets with difficulty:  
The son gets happiness, the son gets a yard,  
The son gets plenty of buildings,  
The son gets ruins to look after,  
The son gets a house to maintain.  
What the daughter gets to support,  
The enemy child gets with difficulty,  
From the gate comes the distaff.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When I repay grandmother's trouble,
Good mother's milk-giving trouble,
Carrying with both hands!?
You raised daughters,
Led them to the field with aprons,
Put them between the plough oxen,
In the middle of the haystack:
So that no winds would come,
No foolish rain would fall,
Nor a sudden downpour of rain.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
My dear little grandmother!  
You breastfed, caressed,  
Held and raised me.  
You hummed by my mouth,  
Whispered in my ear,  
Carried me on your apron;  
Placed me between the piglets;  
Made the leaf move.  
You made the alder leaf move:  
You made the birch leaf sway,  
You made the aspen leaf hold on!

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
My daughter still so tiny,  
Swings just in a cradle,  
Sways in a gentle movement,  
Underneath the ribbons of a bonnet.  
The village lifted their hats,  
The boys raised their heads.  
Once I got to drink vodka,  
A hundred times I got to eat bread,  
Sometimes to drink and chat.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Why did God create me,
Created a fool and an idiot, -
Couldn't He have created a fart with a story -
A fart for the fool's brother with a story
A bull's crotch for the father,
Maybe a villain's fart for the sister...

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
The female capercaillie struts  
Turns her head towards the male.  
"Where should I make the nest  
Where should I hatch the chicks  
In the branches, in the twigs  
In the swamp on top of a big spruce  
On the wide forest linden  
Behind, on top of an oak  
At the gate, in the maple.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When will I repay grandma's toil,  
Good mother’s milk-giving work,  
Carrying with both hands?  
How many nights were sleepless,  
How many days without rest.  
There was no fire in the stove,  
No spark in the hearth,  
No grey horse in the harness,  
No new clothes for the woman:  
This made the child a babbler  
While I was deprived of tears.  
Despite myself, I screamed,  
Otherwise, I was holding back my strength.  
The wife gave a lamb to the babbler,  
And a goat to the tongue-tester.  
When will I repay grandma's toil,  
Good mother’s milk-giving work,  
Carrying with both hands?

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
How many guests do we have in the village?
We have three guests in the village:
One is a guest of the household,
Another is a guest of the cattle,
The third is a guest, a young maiden.
When the household guest comes,
Then comes the ringing of bells;
When the cattle guest comes,
Then comes the rattling of the rakes;
When the maid guests come,
Then comes the rustling of brooches,
The ringing of silver beads,
The creaking of striped shoes.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
How many guests do we have in the village?  
We have three guests in the village:  
One guest is a cart,  
Another guest is cattle,  
The third is a young maiden.  
When the cart comes as a guest,  
There is the ringing of bells.  
When the cattle come as guests,  
There is the scraping of hooves.  
When the young maiden comes as a guest,  
There is the rustling of brooches,  
The tinkling of silver beads.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When do I repay grandmother's trouble,  
Good mother's milk trouble,  
The sucking at the mouth,  
The carrying on the hand,  
How many sleepless nights there were,  
How many days without lunch,  
How many mornings without greeting,  
How many cigars without reward.  
The fire did not go out from the room,  
No spark from the bedpost,  
The horse was never untied from the harness,  
The woman never changed clothes.  
They sought the child's speech,  
They took away young crying.  
But I screamed in defiance,  
I tasted words unwillingly. –  
I would understand to thank with the mouth,  
To give gratitude with the tongue.  
To God the mother's soul,  
Heart to the bosom of Jesus,  
Hands to the hands of Mary,  
Knees to the Creator's footstool.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I have no father, I have no mother,  
I have no proper teacher.  
The tavern door and the card table,  
That is my father, that is my mother,  
That is my true teacher.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Mother, grandmother,  
Why did you raise me?  
You could have put me on a hill  
Before you placed me in a cradle;  
You could have put me on a hummock  
Before you wrapped me in a swaddle.  
The fire would not have gone out,  
The spark would not have ceased to flicker,  
The blaze would not have faltered,  
The candle would not have been covered by a cradle,  
A woman would dress the young in clothes,  
A horse would be yoked to a grey harness.  
The greys rode to the doctor,  
The black-and-white to the church,  
Boats—big boats to Paide,  
Sheets to the town.  
The child had a plain face,  
Liia was known for her tears.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I have no father, I have no mother,
I have no true teacher,
I have no dear educator.
A straw door and a gaming table -
that is my father, that is my mother,
that is my true teacher,
that is my dear educator.
I was left alone to cry,
a chick left to crow,
a flaxseed left to buzz...

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Where must I go,  
where must I be?  
Shall I go down as a mole,  
a rat into a grass tuft,  
a hedgehog into a moss patch?  
I will not go among the giants,  
will not fit into the rich man's house;  
I sit apart,  
among other sufferers,  
among the wretched in the middle of life,  
soon I speak with the distressed,  
immediately I converse with the wretched.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
An angry man wears out a woman,  
A harsh man ages a woman.  
He comes to the porch to peep.  
He starts to pry from the door,  
To watch from the gate.  
I run to the village cabbage garden.  
It is cold there, I am freezing,  
I am freezing my hands,  
My toe hurts.  
My father should know this!  
My mother should know this!  
Let the horses run fast,  
The stallions completely escape.  
They would look for all the new moons,  
And wear the old fur coats.  
They would bring warmth to my fingers:  
Driving away the pain in my toes,  
Taking away the cold from my nails.  
Maiden girls, little birds!  
Take care of your maiden knees  
As you would a precious chronicle.  
Oh my father, oh my mother!  
A cat has raised me.  
It could have taken me to the river,  
To the riverbank.  
The water could have rolled me,  
The water waves rejoiced,  
A doe has nursed me,  
And raised me big.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When do I bear the grandmother's pain,  
The grandmother's pain, tender torment,  
The grandmother's good miraculous pain?  
How many nights I was sleepless,  
How many mornings waitless.  
The fire never ended in the room,  
No spark in the bedpost,  
The horse not in the grey harness.  
The young woman not in clothes.  
She searched for the child's speaker,  
The grey stallion's wanderer.  
Let me, let me, little grandmother,  
Let summer come, autumn will follow,  
I will bring berries from the ground,  
Half a measure of cranberries,  
A measure of old blueberries.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Oh, Jüri, little stubborn one,  
In my mind, a little troublemaker.  
To Munda, a piece of soil.  
To me, a piece of gold.  
Take me or leave me.  
Beauty will not remain anywhere,  
Joy nor happiness to anyone.  
I was an old woman's bad child,  
And an old man's bad child,  
I went underground as a mole,  
A rat into the grass nest,  
A frog under a burdock leaf.  
The mole brought me a black hat.  
The frog brought me a tavern apron,  
The rat brought green silk.  
Then I went to the old woman's as a stranger,  
And I went to the old man's as a stranger,  
Where has the lady come from?  
She probably came from Germany,  
Rolled from the Russian road.  
But I heard and replied:  
She did not come from Germany,  
Nor rolled from the Russian road.  
I am the old woman's bad child,  
I am the old man's bad child.  
The mole brought me a black hat.  
The frog brought me a tavern apron,  
The rat brought green silk.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Because I am beautiful,  
The maiden ate beautiful berries.  
Make the berry tree beautiful.  
Because I am rough,  
The maiden ate rough berries.  
Make the berry tree rough.  
Because I am painful,  
The maiden ate painful berries,  
Make the berry tree painful.  
Because I am steel,  
The maiden ate steel berries,  
Make the berry tree steel.  
Because I am red,  
The maiden ate red berries,  
Make the apple tree red.  
There was one blossom on the apple tree.  
One was even on the apple blossom,  
Also many-branched:  
On the evening side quite beautiful,  
On the sunny side reddish,  
On the Riga side with a cross pattern,  
On the Narva side prickly.  
That was my grandmother,  
Who was kept in the barn,  
Kept in the milk chamber,  
She was not taken out into the rain,  
Nor exposed to the sun,  
Fearing smoke exposure,  
Fearing sun bleaching,  
Fearing the wind's draft.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When do I repay grandmother’s trouble  
Grandmother’s trouble, elder’s trouble, golden trouble  
When you raised me  
Lifted me up, helped me up  
Danced me against the sky  
Swung me on three arms  
One was down in the hayfield  
Another out in the field on the farmland  
The third under the home’s doorstep  
One was moved by a bird  
Another swayed by the wind  
The third soothed by the smoke.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
The grays traveled on the doctor's road  
The thick boats on the Paide road  
Sheets on the city road  
Cremiums on the church roads  
A child is looking for speakers  
Liia for a taker of tears  
Who can be a speaker for the child  
Liia for a taker of tears  
God for the child's speaker  
Liia for a taker of tears.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Me alone under grandmother’s protection  
Under grandmother’s protection, under grandfather’s protection  
Under the hatred of brothers  
Father despised me since I was a child  
Mother despised me even in the cradle  
Alone, alone live the brothers  
Alone I was left to mourn  
To make the hen chicks crow  
To make the alder bird mourn  
To make the barley stirring  
To make the oat cawing  
To make the flaxseed move.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Because of that, those are brothers  
Because now the family sways  
Father’s brothers are smirking  
That they can’t feed the family  
To feed the family, to give the family drink  
To hold a great feast  
Five brothers at the pier  
The sister, the table-setter, arranges  
The sister’s children at the table  
Children, children are brothers  
Children’s happiness in the yard  
In the street – barley grains  
At the gate – grain outside  
Beneficial oats on the roof  
Potatoes piled in the center  
Then I manage to feed the family  
To feed the family, to give the family drink  
To hold a great feast  
Five brothers at the pier  
The sister, the table-setter, arranges  
The sister’s children at the table.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
The berry bringer to me is a Marion
The berry bringer is a herdsman
From the field, the ox driver
From the swamp, the swamp dweller
Mari brought berries from another land
Lingonberries from across the river
The crane then stayed to listen
The crow then stayed to watch
The fool stayed to understand
If only God would grant daughters
The daughters are taken to be married
Then the ox is slaughtered
The cow is taken to the field
Then the crane gets a full six
The old crow a bellyful
The fool gets a handful.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
The berry bringer to me is a Marion
The berry bringer is a herdsman
From the field, the ox driver
From the swamp, the swamp dweller
Mari brought berries from another land
Lingonberries from across the river
The crane then stayed to listen
The crow then stayed to watch
The fool stayed to understand
If only God would grant daughters
The daughters are taken to be married
Then the ox is slaughtered
The cow is taken to the field
Then the crane gets a full six
The old crow a bellyful
The fool gets a handful.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When will I repay grandma’s toil,  
Good mother’s milk toil,  
The carrying with two hands?  
Oh my dear little old woman,  
How did you raise me,  
Raised me, hugged me,  
Lifted me, soothed me,  
Put me down, played with me,  
Soothed me at the mouth,  
Rocked me with two hands,  
Took me on the field in an apron,  
Put me between the straw stacks,  
A handful to the middle,  
So no wind would blow on me,  
No foolish rain would fall,  
Nor hail showers.  
When will I repay grandma’s toil,  
Good mother’s milk toil,  
The carrying with two hands?

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Grandmother, dear grandmother,  
You raised a daughter,  
You raised her, embraced her,  
Laid her down, played with her:  
Lifted her up, jumped with her.  
You thought to help,  
You thought to support,  
Help went to others, who were not expected.  
Support went to others, who were not known.  
The daughter is taken away young,  
The stove remains as grandmother’s help,  
Support remains as grandmother’s pillar;  
A spinning wheel is put into the hand.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Where my nightingale sings  
There I break into the forest.  
The tall trees lean to the pine,  
The elders gather for a meeting.  
Without a young man, without a husband,  
Without a hat, without height,  
Without a copper belt, without white,  
Without a silver belt, without correctness,  
He cannot step straight,  
Without spurs, without height.  
Very, very young men,  
In our village and many villages,  
There is bustle in the meadow village,  
There the bells ring,  
On the open field, a cattle village,  
There is the crackle of carts.  
Below the village is a maiden's gold,  
There is the crack of gunfire,  
There the beads ring,  
The clinking of gold coins,  
The crackling of silver money.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I was thought to be a warrior's friend,  
Considered the companion of a fisherman,  
A sleeper at the manor master's,  
The coachmen's dear gold.  
A little horse groom's plant,  
A handful for the scabs.  
I am not a warrior's friend,  
Nor a companion of a fisherman,  
Nor a sleeper at the manor master's,  
Nor the coachmen's dear gold,  
Nor a little horse groom's plant,  
Nor a handful for the scabs.  
I am the grandmother's praise daughter,  
I am the grandfather's praise daughter.  
I fed the child, plucked its eyes,  
Heated the stove, burned the room.  
I walk, spinning the threads,  
Dancing on the butler's shoulder.  
When a boy comes to touch me,  
A ladies' man comes to mock me:  
I strike the boy on the head,  
And the woman's face into two pieces,  
So that he hears nothing for six days,  
And sees nothing for seven weeks.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Father called me a lynx,  
Mother again called me premature,  
wait-wait father no-no mother,  
let me just grow bigger  
and let me grow stronger.  
The village lynx has horses,  
the premature ones have animals,  
let me make happy barley in the yard,  
frost barley on the roof,  
tassel barley behind the room,  
then I will manage to feed the family,  
to feed the family and water the family,  
to hold a big gathering,  
brothers in the barn,  
sisters standing ear to ear,  
brother’s children at the table.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Little Ella, little brother  
Both of us lads  
Two comrades  
One named the other  
One greeted the other!  
She greeted Ella, called her dear  
Greeted Ella the bright one  
Called her dear curly-haired  
Named her wheat ear  
Dug for oats plant  
I am the old woman's child,  
I am the old man's child,  
Under the brothers' hatred  
The sister’s great scolding.  
Father despised me even at my knees,  
Mother despised me even from her bosom,  
Sisters drove me out of the yard  
Brother drove me between the village  
Meadow into a molehill  
Into a rat’s grass nest,  
There the mole got a coat  
The frog became a lung carrier  
So let me tell it again  
Let me sing it back again:  
"I didn’t have a father  
When I was born on earth  
God knows who made me!  
Were the black grouse that made me  
Or were the black birds that laid me  
Or were the scatterbrains  
That put me together."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Murelaulud
translation:

Both of us lads  
Two comrades  
One named the other  
One greeted the other!  
She greeted Ella, called her dear  
Greeted Ella the bright one

Dug for oats plant

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Dear gentle mother,  
You raised me,  
Raised me, hugged me,  
Nursed me, soothed me.  
You took me in your arms and soothed me,  
Put me down and played with me,  
Protected me from horses' hooves,  
Protected me from the village herd,  
So I wouldn't get a scar on my face,  
Or a smack full in the face.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Dear mother, dear mother,
What have you made of me?
Tears of sorrow,
You forbade me to nurse,
From wetting my eyes while swaddled.
Take me to my Viru river,
Carry me to the Harju spring,
From there the water rolled,
Lost on the Harju shore.
To my dear mother,
How I brought my blood to the water,
Let the flesh be in the waves,
I wrapped you in a swaddle,
In linen and canvas,
In white wool,
In soft fleece.
I held you in my arms,
In a bundle I cut clothes,
To weigh to send the herd.
Now they take me to Russia,
Sent to Germany,
To clean Russian rifles,
Russian rifles, long rifles,
Swedish iron, heavy iron,
They press against me.
The hollow of my shoulder,
Presses on my forearms,
If only rowan wood were against me,
Shoulder wood like apple wood,
Forearm wood of maple.
Let it be, let it be, dear brothers,
Let it be under the town of Narva,
There we will make many feats.
We will make the market storm,
The street misty with rain,
Eaves rattling with hail,
Setting the shop door on fire.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Dear mother, dear mother,
What have you made of me?
Tears of sorrow,
You forbade me to nurse,
From wetting my eyes while swaddled.
Take me to my Viru river,
Carry me to the Harju spring,
From there the water rolled,
Lost on the Harju shore.
To my dear mother,
How I brought my blood to the water,
Let the flesh be in the waves,
I wrapped you in a swaddle,

In white wool,
In soft fleece.
I held you in my arms,

To clean Russian rifles,
Russian rifles, long rifles,

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Our child had three swings:  
One was tied under the duck,  
another down at the meadow,  
the third out in the field.  
What was down at the meadow,  
was to move the birds;  
what was out in the field,  
was to sway the winds;  
what was tied under the duck,  
was to rock the mother.

label: other
translation:
It is tall, it is tall, the reed,  
taller still, taller still than my dear brother.  
It is bright, it is bright, the hay oak,  
brighter still, brighter still than my dear brother.  
It is white, it is white, the sea foam,  
whiter still is she, whiter still is she, my dear bride.  
Who is she, who is she, my dear bride?  
Malle  
If she is beloved, if she is beloved, seize her neck.  
Take silk and velvet into your arms.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Mother, dear mother,  
how you raised me,  
raised me, endured me,  
lifted me up, nursed me,  
put me down, played with me;  
you took me out into the fields in an apron,  
placed me among the bushes,  
so that no wind would come,  
nor rolling rain.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Perhaps my girl could feed the ox,  
could raise a bear,  
before she gave me to eat,  
to eat, to drink;  
she thought I would be help,  
felt support was coming to me. –  
Help went down to the altar,  
support to the Toompea doctor.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
A cruel man wastes his wife,  
a harsh man ages his wife,  
he comes to the house stomping,  
starts banging on the door,  
watching from the gate.  
I must not be in the house,  
I run to the village cabbage field,  
it is cold there, I freeze,  
I freeze with my hands,  
I ache in my toes.  
If only my father knew this,  
if only my mother knew this,  
they would look for all the new moons,  
they would wear those old coats,  
then let the steeds run fast,  
the stallions completely escape,  
........  
would bring warmth to my fingers,  
chase away the pain in my toes,  
carry the cold away from the fingers.  
Oh my father and my mother,  
you raised me so kindly!  
You could have taken me to the river,  
to the riverbank,  
you could have let the water flow,  
and nurture the riverbanks!

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Three barns in the yard:  
One is a rye barn,  
The second a dear oat barn,  
The third stores the wheat.  
Three barns in your yard:  
One for mice to jump,  
The second for cats to cry,  
The third for dogs to sniff.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Our grandmother had two sons  
I waited for them for a long time  
I wrapped them, played with them  
I raised them, endured them  
I built, I mended  
I hoped to have two good ones  
But did not get a single good one  
Instead, I got two stubborn ones.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
My existence would be  
My making into reality  
If I knew what I would do  
I would build a stable under the sky  
Inside the stable a ladder  
On the ladder a colt of a stallion  
For the colt of the stallion a saddle  
For the saddle a German boy.  
For the German boy a hat  
For the hat a golden tassel  
For the golden tassel a knitter.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Yesterday Monday  
I went to draw lots  
The first number was two  
And the second time number one  
Then I, a poor ready soldier  
A poor ready Russian.  
Then I was taken to Laadi tavern  
At Laadi tavern I was fed  
And they gave me beer to drink  
From there I was taken to the town of Pärnu  
Before the Pärnu commission  
There, once again, was the manner of Russians  
And every Russian had five borders  
They circled my head  
And fed me green grass.  
Now I, a poor ready soldier  
A poor ready Russian.  
In Lihula they baked bread for me  
In Haapsalu they shaved the beard  
Now I, a poor ready soldier  
A poor ready Russian.  
From there I was taken to the town of Musk  
In Musk they gave me a uniform  
And put gloves on my hands  
There I became a rifle cleaner  
A wide-breeched sergeant  
A green sword holder.  
Now I, a poor ready soldier  
A poor ready Russian. -  
A father might have fed the wolf  
A mother raised a bear  
Before she has fed me  
She fed me, she gave me drink  
Fed me to become a straight man  
Gave me drink to become a brave man  
Before she could have wrapped me under the hill  
Before she wrapped me in a diaper  
Before she could have buried me under a stump  
Before she folded me inside the grass  
Now I must go  
And leave others behind  
How miserable, how distressing  
To step away miserably  
To leave suffering behind again  
I must still go  
Let the sky fall down  
Let the rain pour down in torrents  
I must still go  
I must still be ahead.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Yesterday Monday  
I went to draw lots  
The first number was two  
And the second time number one  
Then I, a poor ready soldier  
A poor ready Russian.

From there I was taken to the town of Pärnu

There, once again, was the manner of Russians

And leave others behind

To step away miserably  
To leave suffering behind again  
I must still go  
Let the sky fall down  
Let the rain pour down in torrents  
I must still go  
I must still be ahead.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Oh, poor me, what did I do  
Going to a drunken man  
You can sometimes see a drunkard  
No turning back anymore  
He goes to the tavern, takes vodka  
Comes home, takes a whip,  
Beats me all over, lengthwise  
Oh, my mother, oh, my mother  
Why did they raise me  
They could have thrown me into the river  
Perhaps even onto the riverbank  
The riverbank raised me  
A laughing cow nursed me  
And raised me up.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Oh, poor me, what did I do  
Going to a drunken man  
You can sometimes see a drunkard  
No turning back anymore  
He goes to the tavern, takes vodka  
Comes home, takes a whip,  
Beats me all over, lengthwise  
Oh, my mother, oh, my mother  
Why did they raise me  
They could have thrown me into the river  
Perhaps even onto the riverbank  
The riverbank raised me  
A laughing cow nursed me  
And raised me up.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Mother, dear mother!  
Why did you raise me?  
You could have put me in a clod of earth,  
Before you put me in a diaper!  
You could have put me on a hill,  
Before you put me in a cradle!  
The fire did not go out,  
The spark did not extinguish in the stove,  
The candle did not burn on the bedside table,  
The woman did not wear young clothes,  
The gray horse was not harnessed.  
The gray ones went to the doctor,  
The dark ones went to the church,  
The white ones went to the city,  
The boats  
Searched for the path of the child’s speech,  
For Liia’s crying voice.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Mother, grandmother!  
But why did you raise me?  
You could have put me in the river,  
And left me by the riverbank?  
The water would have carried me away,  
The riverbank would have held me,  
A deer-cow would have nursed me,  
And raised me to grow up.  
Oh, poor me, why did I go  
To a drunken man!  
A drunkard will go hungry,  
There is no way back.  
When he comes home from the tavern  
He takes a big stick in hand,  
And beats me thoroughly,  
Beats me thoroughly, lying down.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Mother, grandmother!  
But why did you raise me?  
You could have put me in the river,  
And left me by the riverbank?  
The water would have carried me away,  
The riverbank would have held me,  
A deer-cow would have nursed me,  
And raised me to grow up.  
Oh, poor me, why did I go  
To a drunken man!  
A drunkard will go hungry,  
There is no way back.  
When he comes home from the tavern  
He takes a big stick in hand,  
And beats me thoroughly,  
Beats me thoroughly, lying down.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
For me, for me, dear brothers,  
For they troubled my kin,  
Father’s brothers sneered,  
That I did not reach to feed the kin,  
To feed the kin, to give the kin drink,  
To honor the kin greatly,  
But to raise the sister’s children,  
The brother’s children at the table.  
Wait wait wait oh, dear brother,  
Let me make barley for the kettle,  
And street beer’s barley.  
Then I will reach to feed the kin,  
To feed the kin, to give the kin drink,  
To honor the kin greatly,  
But to raise the sister’s children,  
The brother’s children at the table.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When do I repay grandma's trouble,
Or mother's trouble with nursing?
When you rocked me,
Rocked me on three hands.
One was beneath the cradle,
Another out in the field on the fence,
The third hung on the home crossbeam.
What was beneath the cradle,
Was to be moved by the bird.
What was out in the field on the fence,
Was swayed by the wind,
What hung on the home crossbeam,
Was swung by the smoke.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Thank you, lady, thank you, sir,  
Thank you for raising the daughter!  
That lady truly toiled  
While raising her daughter.  
Many nights were sleepless  
And many noons without bread.  
Grey she stood between the barns,  
One fire came from her and another went,  
Looking for the child's storyteller,  
The young one's weeper.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When do I repay grandma's trouble,  
good mother's milk's trouble,  
who carried me on the apron in the field,  
but put me among the midges,  
a handful at middle age,  
where no wind came,  
nor hillside water.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When do I repay grandma's trouble,  
good granny's milk trouble,  
cradling in granny's lap,  
carrying in both arms?  
She took me from the apron to the field,  
placed me among the best ones,  
so that I would not be exposed to the sun,  
nor come out into the wind,  
nor be drenched by the rain.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Mother called me an illudeks,  
father a premature one,  
sister called me a straw doll,  
brother called me a mill vault.  
I heard it, I answered:  
"Wait-wait father, wait-wait mother,  
let my home be crowned,  
the house painted up,  
then father will become an illudeks,  
mother a premature one,  
sister will become a straw doll,  
brother will become a mill vault."

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Our bulls on the lawn,  
your bulls in the mud:  
our bulls to the herd,  
your bulls to the pasture.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
How many guests did we have in the village?
We had three guests in the village.
One was from Hooste village,
Another from Veiste village,
The third from the young maiden’s village.
When the guest came from Hooste,
Then came the ringing of bells.
When the guest came from Veiste,
Then came the clattering of hooves
And the rattling of spurs.
When the young maiden came,
Then came the tinkling of brooches,
The jingling of silver beads,
The clinking of money containers.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When I pay the grandmother's toil,  
When I pay the grandfather's toil,  
The great journey of the backbone,  
The horse’s quick running.  
The greys rode to the doctor,  
The linen sheets to the town path,  
The thick boats to the Paide road.  
The fire did not end in the room,  
The spark did not step on the bed,  
The woman dressed the young ones.  
They sought a song for the child,  
A cry-taker for the young one.  
They thought to plant two,  
Five or six rolling;  
One grew stubborn,  
The rolling one under the whip,  
Became one with a sugar back.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh, you Mari, bringer of berries,  
Bringer of berries, herdsman,  
From the swamp, also bearer of redcurrants.  
Mari brought berries from other lands,  
Cranberries from across the rivers.  
Then the crane began to listen,  
The magpie started to reason.  
God grant daughters:  
A daughter is taken to a husband.  
Then the bull is slaughtered,  
The field cow is butchered.  
Then the crane got a mouthful,  
The magpie got a bite,  
The old crow got a bellyful.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh, you Mari, bringer of berries,  
Bringer of berries, herdsman,  
From the swamp, also bearer of redcurrants.  
Mari brought berries from other lands,  
Cranberries from across the rivers.  
Then the crane began to listen,  
The magpie started to reason.  
God grant daughters:  
A daughter is taken to a husband.  
Then the bull is slaughtered,  
The field cow is butchered.  
Then the crane got a mouthful,  
The magpie got a bite,  
The old crow got a bellyful.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I worked, but was not thanked,
I toiled, yet was sworn at.
All my fingers were scolded,
My soles were beaten.
I did the grandmother’s work,
The grandmother’s work, the house’s work,
Up to the shoulders, the sisters’ work,
Up to the arms and torso, the brother’s work.
What did I get from my father,
What did I get from my mother?
I thought to give a helper,
Hand over the herd.
They gave me a garden path.
They twisted me to the gate path,
To the cattle market path.
From my father I got a straw band,
From my brother I got a shepherd’s staff.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Father called me illuke,
Mother prematurely
Then illuke began to live,
And had to pay the penalty,
Then I make luck-barley for the yard,
Barley stalks for behind the room,
Bran oats for the chamber,
Strong linen for the hall.
Then I manage to feed the livestock,
Feed the livestock, water the livestock,
Maintain the livestock well,
Provide them with fodder,
Take care of siblings’ children.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Why did God create me  
To this rich ruckus,  
For peasants to quarrel,  
For daughters to fight!  
He could have made me a tree trunk,  
My father a bull yoke,  
My mother a dairy cow.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
The old woman went to the hayfield yesterday,  
The young woman went to the oak grove today.  
She locked the children in the barn,  
Gave them a handful of straw.  
She gave a little bit that was wet with milk,  
And a little bit that was sour with kvass.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I have no father, I have no mother,  
I have no true teacher.  
The tavern door and the card table.  
/: This is my father, this is my mother, :/  
This is my true teacher.  
Oh, to let this poor slave,  
Cut the reed from the bush,  
/: But he licks water from the water pots :/  
The edges of his ears are trembling.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
What’s wrong with living with us!  
There were three loaves in the household.  
One was to dip in broth,  
Another to knock with a jar,  
The third to swallow dry.  
There were three in the barnyard too.  
One of us racks the rye barn,  
Another breaks the wheat,  
The third gathers the oats.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
The daughter is given in marriage  
What will be done with the daughter?  
A deed is done without oil  
An ovenful is made of sourdough bread,  
Seven round loaves of bread.  
This is done for the daughter  
The son begins to take a wife  
Weddings are made for the son  
The top of the broom is twisted,  
The head is placed on the pot.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
You must not sleep in the room,  
Do not place your head on the pillow,  
Your foot under the white carpet,  
Your hand must not hang under the blanket.  
I fear a gentle fright,  
I fear a sudden scare,  
The cuckoo brought into the room.  
The cuckoo in our room quarrels,  
The bow becomes a thorn in the eye,  
Because it pricks the father-in-law’s eye,  
The father-in-law’s eye, the mother-in-law’s eye,  
The hunchback’s eye, the shepherd’s eye,  
The funny relative’s eye,  
And the homeowner’s eye.  
When you raised me,  
Raised and bore me,  
Wrapped and played with me.  
You rocked me on three cuckoos.  
One was under the home in the ducks:  
It was to smoke the smoke.  
A bond on the field on a straw:  
It was to sway the straw.  
The third under the meadow:  
It was to swing the wind.  
You thought I would become  
A good child in one place.  
But one grew stubborn,  
Rolled under the spinning top,  
Gained a black big hunchback.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I have no father, I have no mother  
I have no proper teacher.  
The tavern bench and the card table,  
These are my father, these are my mother,  
These are my true teachers,  
And my dear caretakers.  
My little heart wants to eat,  
My little throat calls for a drink.  
I have no one to give me food,  
No one to bring me drink.  
Those who gave food are dead,  
Those who gave food, those who brought drink,  
Gone to the grave, the ones who showed mercy,  
Laid in the earth, the bearers of sorrow.  
I was left alone to cry out,  
Like a chicken left to crow,  
Like a flax seed left to chirp.  
I remained like a hare on the grass,  
A magpie left on the yard fence,  
A doll upon the armrest. -  
The room is dark, the people sad  
Oh, I am the only joyful man.  
I alone am the joyful man  
Among this great company.  
Why do the dear ones, why do the brothers  
Why do the kin reproach me,  
The father-brothers hover?  
Because the dear ones, because the brothers  
Because the kin reproach me,  
The father-brothers hover:  
Because I cannot feed the kin,  
Feed the kin, give the kin drink,  
To hold great company,  
To set the brothers at the table,  
To have nephews at the table.  
I say again anew:  
"Wait-wait, brothers, no-no, brothers,  
I want to bark to the kin.  
Let me make lucky barley in the yard,  
On the roof let me make sturdy oats,  
Strong linens for the yard.  
Then I will be able to feed the kin,  
Feed the kin, give the kin drink,  
To hold great company,  
To set the brothers at the table,  
To have nephews at the table."

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
If only my mother had seen in a dream  
While dozing I remembered  
That a black one was born a soldier  
And was put to carry a gun.  
I pull the bowstring to my head  
I also pull the knot to my neck.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Mouth, mouth, little child  
Grow your mouth bigger  
Become stronger than others  
Grow straighter than others  
To the weight of a bread crumb  
To the height of a straw stalk  
To the lightness of a healthy leg  
To the thickness of a berry stalk  
To the length of a reed  
When will I repay grandmother’s toil  
Grandmother’s toil, tender milk  
Grandmother’s dear nurturing  
The sucking at the breast  
Carrying on the hand  
Sucking on the ear  
How many nights were sleepless  
How many smokes without a break  
How many mornings must be endured  
The fire in the stove never ends  
The spark does not go out in the fireplace  
From a little fart under the floor  
A woman has no new clothes  
A gray horse is not hitched  
Searching for the child’s babbling  
For the taking of a young child’s cry  
I promise a sheep to the babbler  
A goat to the tongue-beater  
A stallion to the one who knows the tail  
To the one who takes the young child’s cry.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
My brother Ilmarikas  
Built a stable under the sky  
Near the little corner of dawn.  
What did he put in there?  
He put in three stallions.  
One was gray, the other a sorrel,  
The third blood red.  
It went to Finland on a trip  
To see the Finnish stallion.  
Knocking on the Finnish bridge,  
Pounding on the foundation beams,  
The Finnish stallions ran out  
To buy his horse,  
To want his stallions.  
They offered a lot of money:  
A hundred sacks of rye,  
A thousand bushels of wheat,  
Countless ruble notes.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:

He put in three stallions.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When do I repay grandmother’s effort
Grandmother’s effort, tender milk
Effort by the mouth’s warmth
Carried on the hands
How many nights were sleepless
How many days without lunch
How many smokes without a long piece
The fire in the stove never ended
The spark in the bedpost never went out
The candle hidden in the corners
The horse in the grey tack,
The woman in young clothes
Seeking those who speak for the child
Those who take away the young child’s crying.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I had, I had tender brothers  
I had three hiding places  
One was on the distant hill  
Another down in the hayfield  
The third at home down by the barn  
Even that smoked smoke  
What was on the distant hill  
That was blowing wind and snow  
What was down in the hayfield  
That was still rolling water  
In my lap I was even burning  
On the shoulder when going outside  
Hand in hand when walking.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When the daughter was taken from home  
Then the beauty was taken from the father  
Then the beauty was taken from the mother  
When you grew up at home  
Then there was beauty for the father  
Then there was beauty for the mother  
You grew up as a birch in the chamber  
As a golden spruce at the edge of the room  
When the daughter was taken from home  
Then the beauty was taken from the father,  
Then the beauty was taken from the mother  
The crook was just right on the field  
The scythe was quickly taken away from the field  
Then the corners were left to crying  
The benches longing  
The corners cried for the young girl  
The benches cried for the head of the house.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Mother, little mother  
Why did you raise me.  
You raised me, hugged me  
Nursed me, pampered me  
Put me down, played with me,  
Took me in your arms, soothed me  
But why did you raise me  
Answer, danced from heaven.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
My sisters, do not worry  
We surely will find bread from a leaf of a tree  
A leaf on a tree, grass on the ground  
Revives the hay in the meadow  
Break branches, maiden  
Break branches, diseased birch  
Leave the tender treetop  
Leave the treetops for the birds to fly  
Birds to fly, wind to come  
Nightjar to descend at night  
To ruffle other black grouses  
The male black grouse ruffled  
The female black grouse ruffled  
The hawk birds ruffled  
The young ducks chattered  
Where must I go  
Where must I make a nest,  
To the dry ridge, to the scrubland  
To the thick coast forest  
Where the German dogs can’t reach  
Nor the lord’s hounds  
Nor the sisters’ fiercer hounds  
Nor the brother’s small hounds.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Kossi-kossi, äia-äia,  
I had three things to hide  
One was for the room at home  
The second for the field, the hill  
The third down in the hay meadow  
What was for the room at home  
That was to puff smoke  
What was for the field, the hill  
That was to sway the wind  
What was down in the hay meadow  
That was to move the bird.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When I take care of grandmother’s needs  
Grandmother’s needs, tender milk  
Tender care for grandmother  
Carrying her in my arms  
If grandmother were to pass away  
I will make a good grave for grandmother  
Carving iron crosses inside  
Iron crosses, golden crowns  
From silver I make seats  
From old copper I make a gate.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
The daughter does not go even to the third home  
She does not thresh, she does not husk  
She does not milk the cow  
Nor feed the lamb  
She desires the donkey  
She drags a halter over its head  
What use is the daughter in the house  
What use is the poor woman in the work  
Straw bundles come from the yard  
Birch rods come from the gate  
The little one was given the barn  
The calves were grazed on the hill.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Dear little brothers,
Here has plowed a bent back,
Harrowed and nurtured,
Your fields have grown dewy grass.
Risen a fiery red,
Dear little one, little brother,
Here has plowed a dear brother,
Harrowed the foot of the field,
Your beautiful grain has grown,
Risen on the barn’s rack.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
A little eel, a little herring  
Could have stayed, could have known  
Born dark from the spawn  
Growing from the spawn  
The pike sends me to the deep sea  
To the fishing nets up to the neck  
Like Elmi's Mother river  
Surely the shore would have grown  
The sea would have enticed darkly  
The rudd would have knitted gloves  
The pike would have shared the bait  
The roach would have set up the fishermen's lines  
The zander would have laid the stripes.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Eidekene, hellakene  
You know how to grow a plant  
But you don’t understand how to plant it  
You planted it on a hard hill  
In a narrow garden bed  
Where no shoots can grow  
Where heads roll off  
Leaves scattered all over the barn.  
It should have stony shoes  
And ribbons made of limestone  
Shoes break the rock quarry  
Ribbons break the limestone quarry.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Little Ogres, Little Darlings  
Where does that poor one get the carpet  
Innocent patchwork  
The sheep disappeared from the barn  
Another disappeared from the flock  
The third became part of the wolf  
You cannot make a glove from stone  
A sock does not warm with moss  
Flax does not move in the hayfield  
The bull does not dig in the riverbanks  
What became of the daughter from work  
The enemy child got some trouble too  
Could not get a piece of work  
Could not get a carpet from trouble either  
Flax does not run easily  
Not much walking on the pillow  
Brother got bulls, brother got horses  
The stable full of stallions  
The barn full of sheepfold  
A stack got the household tools  
The granary full of oats  
Five crops in there  
Six piled standing  
Barns stacked with hay  
Straw piles in five corners  
Wood stacks along the fence  
Brother got the wagon gilded  
The yard bright  
Brother got wide, long fields  
Brother got big, broad meadows  
Brother got five plow boys  
Six hired servants  
Seven girls spinning  
Eight weaving  
Ten cows to milk  
Brother got the mother-in-law's room  
Who has a lot of money  
A lot of silver goods  
Who is even famous for gold  
Stands in silk clothes  
Wears a cloak of narrow flax  
Wears German boots on feet  
On the head she wears lace  
No fault in living well there.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Tiiu had a baby and Kai had a child,  
From a chair he became strong,  
His beard remained thick,  
The bow was smoked to a thin strip  
There was a trap in the middle of the floors.  
It was for the slave to rock;  
What was smoked to a thin strip,  
That was to rock on its own.  
The baby's mother herself.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
If only I were alive  
Secondly, my doing  
I would make stables under the sky

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I was a young man before,  
A wooden plug was in my pants front.  
I build stables under the sky,  
Barns close to the roof.  
Wide stumps stick out,  
EÜS VII 761 (14) < Järva-Jaani parish, Einmann village, Kerguta village - O. Lõvi & Joh. Lauri & O. Lalli < Jüri Toll, 87 years old (1910)  
Beer  
¤  
Oh, you beautiful little beer,  
Little brother vodka bottle,  
A light, nice drink,  
You took the minds from men's heads,  
Half the minds from boys' heads,  
Temples from women's foreheads,  
Garlands from daughters' heads.  
Men dance in boots,  
Boys in half boots.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Why are we dear ones sad,  
Dear sad ones, poor and silent,  
We live in bad times,  
We have loud parents,  
Harsh caregivers,  
Bread-eaters too cruel.  
My father was cruel,  
My mother was cruel,  
Go to the hill of sorrow  
And carry it to the caregiver.  
Butter-butter, I’ll take a tune,  
Riila-raila, raila-la.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
The fire did not go out,
The spark did not fall from the column,
The woman dressed the young ones,
The apple shed its skin,
Help for the child in need,
Tears for the young one in sorrow.
But I screamed in defiance.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Oh, poor despised creature that I am  
Father despised me, mother despised me  
All the housemaids, the good girls  
They were angry with me  
I was taken under the manor's levy  
That levy fell upon me  
I was sent to the city barracks  
There my head was shaved  
There my hair was cut off  
Iron clothes were put on me  
A copper helmet was placed on my head  
I became a soldier under the crown  
With the crown's white gloves on my hands  
Proud straps across my chest  
As a soldier I got boots on my feet  
An honest gray uniform on my back  
There my head was shaved,  
My hair was cut off  
I sent my hair to my father  
I sent my hair to my mother  
My father cried for me, my mother cried for me  
My sisters cried for me, my brothers too  
Oh, poor stranger in a foreign land  
Under a great heavy cross  
I have no relatives here  
Here my stomach remains empty  
My belt nips in hunger  
My old woman should know  
My old man should know  
My young women should know  
Young women, another half  
They should come back with a carriage  
Let the horse run swiftly  
Playing the fiddle, Eeli  
Take me, poor one, from a foreign land  
From under the great heavy cross.
