I have wise stable boys  
Diligent, careful stable boys  
They made stables in the sky  
Stable doors in the clouds  
Lanterns near the open field.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Answer, twilight and dawn,  
Answer, dawn and dusk,  
We are looking for a night's lodging,  
then we got a night's lodging.  
Where should I lay my head,  
Where should my hair root.  
I laid my head on a stump,  
My hair on a spruce root.  
Who came up to the night-pie?  
Mine, but to my own brother.  
Rise up, little sister:  
You have been sold away,  
Traded far away,  
Given away to someone else.  
Father got an old plough,  
Mother got old milking cows,  
Brother got three plough oxen.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When will I repay grandma's toil,  
mother's milk toil,  
being carried on two arms?  
Oh my dear little grandmother!  
How did you raise me,  
raised me, hugged me,  
lifted me up, soothed me,  
put me down, played with me,  
shushed me by my mouth,  
rocked me on two arms!  
You took me in the apron to the field,  
put me between the pigs,  
a handful on the middle of the belly  
so that no wind would come,  
no foolish rain would fall,  
nor a quarter's downpour.  
When will I repay grandma's toil,  
mother's milk toil,  
being carried on two arms?

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Old woman, lively old woman  
You raised a daughter.  
You raised the young ones,  
You put them down, you played with them,  
Lifted them up, you jumped,  
You thought about their benefit, making profit,  
You thought about coming to their aid.  
Help was given to others who were not expected,  
Support was given to others who were not known.  
Young daughters are taken away,  
The stove remains to help the old woman,  
Support remains to be the old woman's aid.  
In the mouth, it will be paid later.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
When I go through the villages
I sow caraway in the village
I pick blackberries in the family
The village flourishes for caraway
The farm flourishes for buckwheat.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Little Sisters, Little Souls  
Whose village is this, it seems  
Whose ridges are green  
Whose roofs shine  
Whose rows sparkle  
Whose village is this, it seems  
Whose ridges are green  
Whose roofs shine  
Whose rows sparkle  
There the plants are blossoming  
The daughters are serious  
Without a rod, they are submissive  
Without a birch tip, they are timid  
Without a willow hollow, they are diligent  
A little hole in the village  
This made on the rivers  
Placed on the edge of the hill  
Set in the sunlight  
Sister is beautiful, isn't it right  
Sibling is dear, isn't it clear

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Once I had the time of an old woman  
The honey-sweet time of a granny  
Now comes Virve's brother's time  
Virve's brother-in-law's time  
Before, I could eat a little in the barn  
Secretly, I could eat a little in the sauna  
Even more in the living room  
In the chamber with two beds  
Now comes Virve's brother's time  
Virve's brother-in-law's time  
Now I can't eat in the barn  
Secretly, I can't eat in the sauna  
Even more, not in the living room  
Not in the chamber with two beds

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Tiny little lowland.  
Old times were wagon times,  
Chinese times were long.  
Thus was born a story,  
That here sings itself:  
Father called me late,  
Mother called me too early;  
Brothers as storytellers,  
Sisters as well-wishers.  
I began to go from there  
Along the road, tiny one  
Along the land, little lowland.  
I went into a molehill,  
Into a hedgehog’s anthill.  
The mole made me a black hat,  
The ant brought a silk dress.  
The rat gave me a Swedish shirt.  
I went to the grandma’s forest;  
I went to the grandpa’s forest.  
“Hello, hello, little granny!  
Hello, hello, little grandpa!”  
The granny heard me, answered,  
The grandpa heard me, answered:  
“Whose is this beautiful lady,  
Whose is this silk German maiden?” –  
“Not a beautiful lady,  
Not a silk German maiden.  
I am the despised daughter of the granny.”  
Father began to slaughter an ox  
And mother to cook soup.  
“Come eat, daughter dear!” –  
“I do not want to, little granny,  
I do not want, little grandpa;  
I left three courses at home.”

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I watch over grandma's cow,  
I protect grandpa's large herd.  
Grandma went to the forest to gather berries,  
To bring me some blueberries.  
Blue ones, blue ones,  
Red ones, lingonberries.  
With a rowanberry basket,  
An oak wood bucket,  
And a juniper staff.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Oh my dear little grandmother!  
Sweet little old lady;  
When you nursed me,  
You nursed and nourished me.  
You raised and hugged me,  
Thought of making me grow,  
Expected three to come,  
One will be a tobacco smoker,  
One will be a horn tapper.  
When will I repay grandmother’s effort?  
Grandmother’s effort, dear effort?  
Grandfather’s effort, precious effort?  
When will I repay mother’s nursing effort?  
Sucking at the breast?  
Being carried in the hand?  
How many nights were sleepless,  
How many noons were without lunch.  
The fire never went out in the stove,  
No spark in the bedpost,  
A grey horse to the harness,  
A woman with new clothes.  
She searches for child talkers,  
For those who touch the tongue’s edge.  
Promises to the lamb talker,  
To the goat tongue toucher,  
To the ruffian steed teaser,  
To the young steed wanderer.  
I screamed out loud,  
I tasted the mouth of the beloved.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Mother, dear mother!  
Why do the broad slanderers blame me,  
The tin hats mock me.  
That I have a few little flaws,  
A frugal collar?  
Wait, wait, wait, wait; no-no-no!  
Let me grow two brothers,  
Above them one brother alone,  
Then a third brother will grow,  
A young girl has four brothers.  
They go  
Go in pairs of two,  
They bring cunning charms,  
They bring shining gold,  
They bring suitable blows.  
Then indeed, indeed, lively brothers,  
I will adorn myself with jewelry,  
I will dress myself with blows.  
Then I will whirl around the church,  
Spinning around the sanctuary.  
The boys quietly watch:  
"If that were a bean for oneself,  
If that were a cone in the hand,  
That walks along the roads,  
Tramp along the streets."  
I understand, answer I give:  
The bean is theirs,  
The cone is theirs,  
Who brought the cunning charms,  
Who brought the shining gold,  
Who brought the suitable blows,  
Who brought the twisting one.  
Brother brought a red wreath,  
Andres brought cranberry red,  
Toomas brought fiery red,  
Mart brought liver hairy,  
My man brought sweet,  
My dear brought a beloved,  
A green little rye,  
An apron with a Swedish pattern.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Mother, dear mother!
Then you saw great deaths,
Great deaths, loud sufferings,
When you raised me,
Lifted me up, soothed me,
Put me down, played with me:
You waited for support to come,
For help and kindness to arrive,
Support came to them, dear mother,
Support came to those who did not feel,
Help to those who did not count.
Support came to them - to the numb ones,
Help to them - to the weak ones,
To the corpse - as a money collector,
To the faint - as a water carrier,
To the numb - as a worker,
To the lazy as a cradle-rocker,
To the naughty as a child-petter.
Mother, dear mother!
You sat now wondering at me,
You came to give mercy;
You heard from the cruel guardian,
From the troubled man,
Words loud from the stranger.
Mother, dear mother!
Now I begin to understand,
What I owe to the old woman’s toil,
The old woman’s toil, her milk,
The age,
The coaxing by the mouth,
The carrying on the hip?
How many nights were sleepless,
How many mornings to endure,
How many days without afternoon;
The fire never went out again,
The spark from the bedpost,
The woman dressing the child,
Took away the loud crying,
Took away the noisy crying.
Mother, dear mother!
I did not cry to complain,
I cried with a big heart,
I wept for my own mischief.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Young maidens,  
Do not plan weddings,  
Do not prepare sleeves,  
Do men's work at the breast,  
Women’s work breaking the neck,  
I serve my father with praise work,  
I serve my mother with praise work,  
To my brother, a home,  
I think to help by donating,  
To carry the herd,  
Who gave the barn to me,  
Turn the herd to the daughters,  
From the yard I got the straw band,  
From the gate I got a twisting twig,  
Even this I got secretly without pay,  
To pass through the fence.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
A little dear granny  
When you raised me  
You bore with me  
Lifted me up, jumped with me  
Took me in your arms, fed me  
Put me down, played with me  
Put me to bed, rocked me  
Hoped for sons to grow  
Nine of them growing  
I grew black but stubborn  
Rolled under the seesaw  
Thrashed with a stick of bird cherry  
Bent stick of elderberry  
Screamed with a poplar stick  
Along my fine backsides  
And along my straight back  
If only my yards were rowan-lined  
Shoulder blades like apple trees  
Forearms like maples  
Fingers like currants  
Then I should endure whip blows  
Endure the blows of the cane  
Before the harsh scoldings  
The more relentless nods of disapproval  
Those went right through the heart

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I was grandmother's dear child  
Grandmother fed me with bread  
Gave me fresh milk to drink  
Hoped for me to grow strong  
One grew up wild and unruly  
I immediately went to work for the dog  
Father, help against the thief  
Mother to pull (me) into the coffin

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Father calls me a bastard sex
Mother calls me premature sex
The rest of the family calls me testicle tearing sex
The people call me rusted iron sex
My own man calls me wild sex

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Our father kept an oak branch in the sacred grove  
Behind the room an oak branch  
Hoping for sons as heirs  
Daughters as workers  
When a son was taken from his father  
Taken as support from the room  
When a daughter was taken from her mother  
Taken like a ladle from the kettle  
From the blue seed  
Right from the patch of field

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I worked but was not thanked  
I struggled but was sworn at  
All my words were scolded  
The barefoot were slandered  
I did bitter work  
Yet I tended a madwoman's house  
I cleaned a crazy room  
Yet I kept a frugal comb  
It is not bitter to understand gratitude  
Madness understood pride  
My father called me shameless  
My mother called me premature

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
They called me a Turkish girl  
And laughed at me as the sauna woman of Narva  
I am not a Turkish girl  
I am not the sauna woman of Narva  
I am the daughter of a farmer  
Daughter of a father, daughter of a mother  
A child of a broad oak  
Often fed by oak branches  
Oak branches, animal leaves  
Grown juniper berries  
I was not fed by oak branches  
Oak branches, animal leaves  
Grown juniper berries  
I was fed by young bulls  
Father's kept horses  
Brother's iron steeds  
They rattled and clattered  
Pounded on the boats  
I ate rye and drank red  
Tasted like liver hair.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Let the caller be healthy  
To the caller, to the digger  
Who calls the sad bird  
Drives the bad horse  
Sent to the worker in the ditch  
He did not call like a dog  
Nor like a checker digging a hole  
He called like a king  
Drove the horse like a lord  
Sent the sled like a German  
The caller also had a coat on his back  
The digger also had a fur coat  
Now the soup is full to the mouth  
Up to the brim with pork  
Up to the neck with eggs

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
My apple tree iron!
It stands by the tavern wall
Watching by the tavern pole
Tail rolling into a curl
Ears weaving threads
Eyes writing silk
Its mane on the forehead like a goad

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Oh, that madwoman!  
What I saw last year  
The year before last, I saw another madwoman  
Nelli's women were fighting  
Splashing across the yard  
One was Anne with a yellow tail  
The other Kaie with a twisted shoe  
The third was sailor Marikene  
The fourth was ribboned Liisukene  
Anne had a bow, Kaie had a cake  
Mai had a pear tree on her shoulder  
Liisu had a small ladle  
Their breasts moved with a snap-snap  
Udder-like over their shoulders.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Whose lint, whose lint paid for the cow,
Whose boat :,: a pair of oxen?
Whose buckle :,: of the large parish
Whose hometown :,: the town of Pärnu?
Mari's lint, Mari's lint paid for the cow
Mari's boat :,: a pair of oxen
Mari's buckle :,: of the large parish
Mari's hometown :,: the town of Pärnu
Mari herself :,: without everything.
Take silk, take silk on your lap
Take darling, take darling, start turning
Silk velvet, silk velvet, take it on your lap
Mari dear, Mari dear, start turning
Mari's silk, Mari's silk, take it on your lap
Mari's dear, Mari's dear, jump on the neck
Exchange, exchange good men.
July dear, July dear, start turning
July dear, July dear, jump on the neck
July's silk, July's silk, take it on your lap...

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
O, our golden king!  
Rich gentleman of Riga,  
True boy of Pärnu.  
Why didn’t you come to us last year  
When the rivers ran without oil?  
You stirred from the deep spring,  
You twisted from the shallow pond,  
Now you have come at just the right time!  
At the light spring,  
At the dry autumn,  
At the snowy winter,  
Now you tear off your clothes,  
Break off your neck chains,  
Take the wreath from your head!  
“O, our golden king!  
Rich gentleman of Riga,  
True boy of Pärnu,  
Give me poor gold;  
Throw away the troublesome silver.  
My master bought a press:  
My mistress the prong,  
I myself got a bit of shawl,  
Got a press from making leg ribbons,  
Got some broth from cooking soup,  
A buckle from kneading bread.”

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Cross dance, cross dance let's dance  
Our dance, our dance, let's keep it  
Järva dance :,: let's leave it  
Viru dance :,: let's carry it  
Dance, dear :,: dance, darling  
Dance, tallermaa :,: king  
Roll, little one, roll, little manor owner  
Blue rises :,: sprouts from the swamp  
Sprouts from the swamp :,: from Margu land  
Rolling of neighbors :,: from the middle  
Twisting shoe :,: from the snakes (snakes)

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Belly of the flute, belly of the flute, roomja  
Over the twig, over the twig, hay hook  
Who went yesterday :,: to the hayfield  
It was Mari :,: the maiden  
That she :,: cut her finger  
Poor little finger :,: on the saw  
Who jumped up :,: to touch it  
It was her fiancé :,: the young lad,  
Tied up :,: that finger;  
Wrapped up :,: in a mossy cloth!  
Where did she get :,: that scarf?  
Took from the neck :,: a neck scarf  
Where did she get :,: that ribbon?  
Took from the belt :,: a belt ribbon.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row across three forests  
There were three forests side by side:  
One was the golden spruce forest  
The second was the dear birch forest,  
The third was the true apple orchard!  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
It was the forest of young men  
What was the dear birch forest?  
It was the forest of young women:  
What was the true apple orchard?  
It was for the young maidens.  
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row the swing to the land of words  
Where those roosters drink gold  
Roosters gold, hens silver  
Ducks with silver feathers  
Surely it will also come to us two young ones:  
Rightly so to the young couple.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row across three forests  
There were three forests side by side:  
One was the golden spruce forest  
The second was the dear birch forest,  
The third was the true apple orchard!  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
It was the forest of young men  
What was the dear birch forest?  
It was the forest of young women:  
What was the true apple orchard?  
It was for the young maidens.  
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row the swing to the land of words  
Where those roosters drink gold  
Roosters gold, hens silver  
Ducks with silver feathers  
Surely it will also come to us two young ones:  
Rightly so to the young couple.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row across three forests  
There were three forests side by side:

The second was the dear birch forest,  
The third was the true apple orchard!

It was the forest of young women:  
What was the true apple orchard?  
It was for the young maidens.  
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row the swing to the land of words  
Where those roosters drink gold  
Roosters gold, hens silver  
Ducks with silver feathers  
Surely it will also come to us two young ones:  
Rightly so to the young couple.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Oh, king, little king!  
Rich gentleman of Riga;  
Pure boy of Pärnu:  
The king could have come last year  
If the rivers had flowed with oil?  
The springs ran with vodka:  
The village folk to pick ginger  
The outskirts to gather sulfur.  
Now times have turned bad  
Now I strip my chest  
Take the wreath from my head.  
Oh, king, little king!  
Rich gentleman of Riga;  
Pure boy of Pärnu,  
Give me some poor gold  
A bit of tin-silver.  
That was not given to you  
That was given to me myself,  
That was brought by my fine suitor;  
That was brought by my dear companion.  
The master brought me a frock  
The mistress brought me a clasp:  
I bought a string of beads myself;  
My companion brought a necklace  
I got a clasp from kneading dough  
A frock from beating thighs  
Beads from hay arguing  
A chain from bending the arc  
Oh, king, little king  
Give me some poor gold.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The squirrel wanders on the mountain,  
The cone crackles in the fireplace;  
Bring young men here:  
Take me to the hut's home  
Carry me to the farmhouse;  
Put me on the edge of the beam  
From there I will roll into the corner  
I will dress in half a beer  
I will drown in the corner with a murmur!  
Beers will rise on the ridge.  
Then I will take the senses from the men's heads.  
Squirrels go to the women’s end,  
Half of the senses are in the boys' heads.  
Then men will shout with hats off,  
Women will dance without crutches,  
Boys in half boots,  
Girls with wreaths on their heads!

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Let's go, darlings, to Kundelasse  
Sisters, dears!  
Young maidens, youths.  
We'll gather the three of us together  
Consult the four of us,  
Cross the strait with five of us.  
Let's go, darlings, to Kundelasse  
To the dear Kalju manor!  
We'll make a stone church there.  
We'll carry a kettle to the chapel.  
No weary sticks of labor there;  
The water spider does not tire,  
The thick fog does not strike:  
There on the instrument, legs are beaten,  
At the dance, the thick fog is stomped on,  
In song, the boards are struck.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
At Tont
At Irus
At Kärmu
At Pajula
At Kostivere
At Liivakandi
At All
At Sookõrts
Then Totti came home.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I sing, why don’t you sing?  
I sing by the little barn,  
Behind the barn by the little stable,  
Inside the stable by the lantern;  
Inside the lantern by the flock’s shelter,  
At the shelter by the saddle:  
On the saddle sits a German boy,  
On the German boy a hat;  
On the hat a golden crown,  
On the golden crown a weaver;  
To the weaver a golden hammer,  
To the golden hammer a maker,  
To the maker another half.  
I sing, why don’t you sing?  
I sing a song about a horse.  
Its head capped with a nutshell,  
Eyes like heavenly stars;  
Ears like cabbage leaves.  
Its body like a haystack,  
Legs like chopped wood below;  
Shoes from whip twigs,  
Tails behind from footprints.  
Then I go on a journey to Finland!  
I tap the Finnish bridge,  
Pound the foundation beams.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let's leave this land  
Let's go to Finland for the summer  
For half the summer to the Cranberry land  
For the winter too, to Alutahja.  
When a girl comes to the countryside hungry  
A girl hungry, lacking food;  
When it comes to searching for a horse  
Riding on the shank of the leg:  
The flanks strike with sparks  
The shanks of the legs struck sparks  
The foal's hooves flash lightning  
When a girl goes to the road  
Far to catch the herd.  
Young maidens, young girls  
When you leave  
Who will do the work then?  
"Girls do the work  
Young maidens cut the hay  
The stacks appeared in Kuramaa  
Large flocks in Saaremaa  
Hands busy in Alutahja.  
These are made by our girls  
The stacks gathered by the daughters  
Busy are the maidens at work.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let's leave this land  
Let's go to Finland for the summer  
For half the summer to the Cranberry land  
For the winter too, to Alutahja.  
When a girl comes to the countryside hungry  
A girl hungry, lacking food;  
When it comes to searching for a horse  
Riding on the shank of the leg:  
The flanks strike with sparks  
The shanks of the legs struck sparks  
The foal's hooves flash lightning  
When a girl goes to the road  
Far to catch the herd.  
Young maidens, young girls  
When you leave  
Who will do the work then?  
"Girls do the work  
Young maidens cut the hay  
The stacks appeared in Kuramaa  
Large flocks in Saaremaa  
Hands busy in Alutahja.  
These are made by our girls  
The stacks gathered by the daughters  
Busy are the maidens at work.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The hook, the churchwarden  
The Virve-colored basket.  
Where do I put the hook,  
The hook, the churchwarden,  
The Virve-colored basket?  
Behind the room, in the slaughteryard,  
Among the bundle of long sticks,  
Between the wide leaves.  
There it stands, unplanted,  
Untouched by the lovely,  
Without young fingers needing it—  
Where do we put the hook to sleep?  
Right next to the stove in the wool box,  
A nice boy with an ear, wearing a colorful coat.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row the boat, reach the boat  
Row the boat to that land  
Where roosters eat gold  
Roosters gold, hens silver,  
Geese green silver,  
Fine birds of money,  
Crows old money.  
Ship captain, little boy  
Come to hold the boat.  
So it doesn’t bump against the rock  
Roll into the water streams,  
Pour onto the rocky cliff.  
Silk ships would flow in  
Kalev's ships would pour in  
My dear little lady  
I came to ask for a favor  
I have a lot of grocery:  
Two came with men from afar  
Two were cats in the yard  
Three were Baltic herrings in the towel  
The cats want to boil  
The goose meat to bake  
The duck meat to fry.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Little sisters, dear ones,  
Where are we going this night?  
This night, this evening,  
We go to listen to the moon,  
To watch the day,  
To touch the gentle evening.  
Does this moon serve without oil,  
The day ripples meaning,  
The evening rolls a hymn.  
No, this moon does not serve without oil,  
The day does not ripple meaning,  
The day plays with pearls,  
Plays sieve with rings,  
The evening with silver beads,  
The moon with golden rings.  
"I had three suitors.  
One was the moon, another the day,  
The third a man full of stars.  
I will neither go to the moon,  
Nor to the day.  
The moon has three duties:  
Sometimes it rises early,  
Once it rises at dawn's gold,  
Once in the middle of dawn.  
Let the man full of stars come,  
Horses full of stars,  
Clothes full of stars.  
Stars sparkle and rattle,  
Golden glitter chimes.  
"Bring me my own harp,  
Send me my verses to the world."

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:
Blow the pipe, little Peebu,  
Play the horn, Saxon.  
I am coming to dance the oak,  
To make young spruces sway.  
I dance, the fence rumbles,  
The spruce room resounds,  
The flagstone floor clatters,  
Feet want to dance,  
The room wants new clearing;  
The floor wants stomping,  
The wallsides want stepping.  
Dance, dance, Lutsu Mari.  
Do your feet work right away,  
Do handfuls of steps go through.  
Is the envelope round,  
Is the fabric half-length,  
Or are the little ones under wings,  
The rascals between belts.  
Slowly, slowly, wisely, wisely,  
Gently dance, young lady,  
Step beautifully.  
So the apron strings don’t snap  
Nor the too tight shoe straps.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:

Nor the too tight shoe straps.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Glass to the bottle, glass to the bottle.  
Pick a berry, pick a berry.  
Cold twig, cold twig.  
Meadow parsley, meadow parsley.  
Who was it yesterday, who was it yesterday  
That cut the finger, that cut the finger?  
It was Else Mari, Else Mari was a young maiden.  
Is that it yesterday, is that it yesterday that cut the finger,  
Who jumped, who jumped into the swaddling cloth?  
A village boy, a dear brother.  
Is that it that jumped, is that it that jumped into the swaddling cloth?  
Where did he take from, where did he take the finger candy?  
From the neck, from the neck a neckerchief.  
Where did he take from, where did he take from the ribbon end?  
From the belt, from the belt, a belt ribbon.  
Snatch tight, snatch tight the maiden’s finger  
Tie tight, tie tight the silk finger  
He did not give, he did not give to others’ hands  
He gave his own, he gave his own love to her hand  
Who walks, who walks to the shoe?  
Who steps, who steps to the boot?  
Whose blanket, whose blanket with golden embroidery  
Blue silk, a little blue silk bird  
Jingling, jingling shoelaces  
Thin, thin trouser ribbons.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
New, little bullfinch  
A little bullfinch’s beak.  
“What is the bullfinch looking for?”  
“The bullfinch is looking for a ewe,  
Through straw to poke,  
Through manure to scavenge,  
Through the yard’s henhouse.”  
“How is the bullfinch’s beak?”  
“Like a village well’s spout.”  
“How is the bullfinch’s wing?”  
“Like a broom on the meadow.”  
“How are the bullfinch’s claws?”  
“Like the sticks of a rake!”  
“Bullfinch, bullfinch, chicken thief,  
I won’t give you a single chick.  
In summer I fed it to grow big,  
In winter I keep it for the logs.”

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Jump to the doll, leap to the doll,  
Make the doll’s feet sharp.  
The doll has rocky shoes.  
Sandal straps made of limestone.  
The doll is invited to the village.  
The doll has no time to go,  
The doll weaves, weaves on the loom  
A thin border on the edge.  
Linen close to the open field.  
"I weave lattice, I weave lattice,  
Five cubits behind the thread.  
After Niidu Tiina’s mows  
Kassi Anne’s loom beams  
Tammemää Leenu’s foot beams.  
The doll went to Udri’s bean field  
Leapt into Padrik’s willow grove,  
The stepboy’s barley field.  
From there it brings a bundle of knees,  
From the sheaf it brings a bundle of barley.  
Feeds the father-in-law’s pair of oxen,  
Feeds the mother-in-law’s milking cow,  
Its brother’s suitor rune."  
"Let it be, let it be, I curse:  
Mother-in-law goes to milk the cow,  
One udder watery, another bloody,  
The third milky red,  
The fourth butter white.  
Father goes out to plow,  
May the oxen not get tired,  
May the plow handles not break!  
Brother goes to the suitor’s ride,  
May his horse not disappear,  
May the reins stay in hand,  
May the bridle be red on the head."

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
O, dear little king,  
Why didn’t you come to us last year  
When the commands were delivered  
Five books were carried,  
Six letters were written.  
Where rivers ran without oil,  
Forests echoed with lament,  
Beers fermented in the chamber,  
Ale anchors in the vats.  
Loaves of bread were abjectly made  
For your consumption!  
Now you want to own,  
Now you strip off clothes  
You try on neck collars,  
You search for silver beads.  
You take the maiden’s wreath from her head.  

“Throw the beads away, maiden,  
Throw the beads away from your neck too,  
Remove the brooch from your chest too,  
Take off the wreath from your head.”  

“Whose beads are being thrown away?”  
“Anne’s beads are being thrown away!”  

“Whose brooch is being removed?”  
“Maie’s brooch is being removed!”  

“Whose wreath is being taken off the head?”  
“Mari’s wreath is being taken off!”  

“Throw the beads away, Anne,  
Throw the beads away from your neck too!  
Remove the brooch, Maie,  
Remove the brooch from your chest too!  
Take off the wreath, Mari,  
Take off the wreath from your head!”

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
"Borrow the sieve, little bit of rain.  
The woman sifts the peas for us,  
The old man sifts the buckwheat for us.  
I myself will knead the enemy's wheat flour."

"I will not lend it, little bit of rain,  
You have tall servants,  
Like-minded flatterers,  
Stubborn upbringing.  
They take the sieve to the glove,  
The scavenger to the village road.  
The pig ruins the bottom of the sieve,  
The foal ruins the bottom of the cart,  
The horse ruins the bottom of the scavenger."

"Just lend it, little bit of rain.  
The war stands on the threshing floor,  
The force lurks on the watch floor;  
War loaves are unkneaded,  
Enemy's fowls unculled."

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Risti dance, little dance  
Start the Harju dance,  
Yes, the Järva dance.  
Every Friday the Risti dance,  
Every Saturday children’s dance,  
Every holiday boys’ dance,  
Every month the king’s dance,  
Risti dance, little dance,  
Risti dance, little dance.  
Your players are coming,  
Your player beats the drum,  
Your moor’s steward,  
Dear moor’s servant.  
"What can I give the player?  
I have no gold, no silver,  
No copper, old money,  
No fine coins."  
"I don’t want gold, nor silver,  
Nor copper, old money,  
Nor fine coins."  
I want a young maiden,  
Silk finger, thread finger,  
A hard fabric weaver,  
A canvas stitcher,  
A paper shaker,  
A fine script writer  
Who makes letters into gloves,  
Slit letters into canvas,  
Bright patterns into shirts,  
Grouse patterns into a quilt.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
"Is Siim, and Siim,
From here, Siim's servant, and servant,
An honorable master, and master,
Among all the others, and among them."

"But here becomes Siim, and Siim,
Here Siim's servant, servant,
An honorable master, and master,
Among all the others, and among them."

"Who once can rise, rise?"
"It is time to rise, rise,"
"Who is without a pledge, and without?"
"Leenu is without a pledge, and without."
"Put out the pledge, and out."
"Is Siim, and Siim, from here?"
"Hello, hello, Siim, and Siim."
"Dear man, what do you want?"
"I came for my boat!"
"When were the pledges placed, and placed?"
"In the poor time of spring, and time.
In the warm time of summer, and time."
"What did you take from here, and take?"
"Countless eggs, and eggs,
Mati's oats, and oats."
"What will you do for it, and do?"
"What is to be pledged, and pledged."
"Then bring lies, lies."
"Oh, wonder, or wonder,
What I saw was a wonder;
What I saw at Näu's visit
At Näu, beaten by hunger.
In front of the door in a row,
At the gate in the chamber,
Behind the room in the bird cherry.
One door had a chip,
The other's stalls had broken planks,
The third kitchen had side branches,
The fourth cellar had long branches,
The fifth gate was damaged."

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The old woman despised me from the bosom,  
The man from the other side of the forest.  
My father called me a lynx,  
My mother called me premature,  
My sisters dragged me to the garden,  
My brothers as thieves at the mill:  
Because I resembled a black old woman,  
Dark-skinned, from the other side of the forest.  
I was a boat to my brother’s face.  
I took matters into my own hands and got angry:  
I went as a mole into the clump,  
As a rat into the grass field,  
As a mouse down to the hay meadow,  
As a hedgehog into the ant nest,  
As a frog under a burdock leaf,  
As a lizard between the wall.  
As a stump-dweller on the tree stump.  
The mole brought me a black hat,  
The rat brought a rose-colored apron,  
The lizard brought a silk scarf,  
The mouse brought new anklets,  
The hedgehog brought calico suits,  
The frog brought crumpled boots,  
The stump-dweller brought a handkerchief.  
Then I went to the bad old woman,  
Unfortunate, I went to the sisters,  
Poor and miserable, I went to the brother.  
My brother was chopping wood for the sauna:  
“Come home, dear brother,  
Come home to bring firewood,  
Bring firewood, haul the load.  
Start chopping wood for the sauna,  
Then we will make a stone church  
And a chapel of birch leaves.  
We will drive in iron crosses,  
Iron crosses, golden inscriptions,  
Silver nails on the corners.  
We will lay a floor on the ridge beam,  
With strips of pork,  
Doors of chicken eggs.”  
Passing by were the stonemasons,  
Along came the weavers,  
The lords of Iru sneered and mocked:  
“Look, here is the town of Lihula,  
Look, there is the foolish tower,  
Here is the Keidela church.”  
I understood and replied,  
I answered firmly:  
“Oh, you crazy German townsmen,  
And you foolish Germans of Narva,  
Mocking lords of Iru!  
This is not the town of Lihula,  
Nor the tower of Tohela,  
Nor the church of Kaidela.  
This is my brother’s sauna.  
You should see it from the inside  
As you see it from the outside:  
Inside it’s striped,  
Outside it’s golden-haired,  
Striped by some miracle.”

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:

I answered firmly:  
“Oh, you crazy German townsmen,

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Tiit, Tiit, let's make oil,  
There are three things missing:  
Hop bines, malt bags,  
Yeast brewer's tools,  
Barley head calls to the plow,  
Cone rustles in the pantry.  
Come, young ones, to pick,  
Small ones, to catch,  
To look at the white heads.  
The hop began to go wild,  
Moving feet in the town,  
Cone rustling in the pantry.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The cold chills me  
I shiver in my hands  
My toes hurt.  
Wait-wait, cold, no-no, cold:  
Let me make the room warm  
The room is being renovated,  
The house is being painted new:  
Then you will howl at the door  
You will beg behind our hut,  
You will shout at the threshold of the chamber.  
You seek warm buildings,  
Unheated rooms,  
Closed windows,  
Ask, ask, little cold,  
Drive away the little gray one.  
Where is your summer warmth,  
Where is your external effort?  
Haymaking time and beautiful days,  
Daylight and heat.  
The warm days of rye harvest.  
Where have you put your goose's neck,  
Where have you put your fur coat?  
Behind the tavern at Tapa, in a chamber.  
On top of the beer barrel,  
On the twig of the vodka barrel.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The cold chills me  
I shiver in my hands  
My toes hurt.  
Wait-wait, cold, no-no, cold:  
Let me make the room warm  
The room is being renovated,  
The house is being painted new:  
Then you will howl at the door  
You will beg behind our hut,  
You will shout at the threshold of the chamber.  
You seek warm buildings,  
Unheated rooms,  
Closed windows,  
Ask, ask, little cold,  
Drive away the little gray one.  
Where is your summer warmth,  
Where is your external effort?  
Haymaking time and beautiful days,  
Daylight and heat.  
The warm days of rye harvest.  
Where have you put your goose's neck,  
Where have you put your fur coat?  
Behind the tavern at Tapa, in a chamber.  
On top of the beer barrel,  
On the twig of the vodka barrel.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Village boys, scythes at their belts,  
Young men, knives at their belts.  
My fiancé, buckle at his belt,  
My husband at Kalev's belt.  
Drinks a sip from the trough,  
Pulls toward the village's end,  
Down to the village mill.  
Throws the reins on the apple tree,  
The bridle in the linden tree.  
The riders in the blue cloak.  
Then he goes to look for the horse.  
Comes toward the maiden’s group,  
Driving the cattle at a distance.  
"Maiden, young man,  
Did you see my horse?"  
"Sweetheart, little brother,  
What color is your horse?  
Maybe I saw it, but I don’t know."  
"Maiden, young man,  
My horse is very beautiful:  
Golden-colored coat,  
Flaxen mane, leaf-shaped spots.  
Shackles on its feet,  
Golden bell on its neck,  
Silver bridle on its head,  
Copper bit in its mouth."  
"Sweetheart, little brother,  
That was your horse."  
Passed through our yard,  
Drank our river water,  
Tasted our sea water,  
Touched the village wells."  
"Come, come, young men,  
Come to the horse market,  
To listen to the golden bells."

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I guard grandmother's sheep,  
I protect old man's herd  
In front of Mary's room  
Between the Creator's barns  
"Come along, little wolf!  
Grab some cream for the woman,  
Take the little lamb into the forest.  
Leave grandmother's sheep alone.  
Grandmother makes me socks,  
Red patches.  
Blue barrows,  
White wooly mittens."  
The wolf ate the goat, the kid stayed with me  
I put the kid around the woman's neck  
The woman was loud in snoring  
The kid was loud in bleating.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Give a drink to the poet,  
To the poet, to the singer.  
Let the singer's ceiling burn,  
Let the drinker’s throat be raw.  
Let me also drink enough  
To drive out my anger with vodka,  
To soothe my mind with honey.  
Drink down the throat, bear it to the neck,  
Accept the copper chest.  
My throat has dried up,  
My vocal cords are rusted.  
Surely the feet will lead home,  
The sores set the bed,  
The knees fall to rest,  
The soles to the bed.  
I drink beers, I clink the jars.  
I toss the rods into the whip,  
The boards into the grove.  
There grow big islands,  
Big islands, wide spaces,  
Notice the pine-covered meadow.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Neither here the sound of uniforms,  
Nor the singing voice of uniforms.  
If we two sisters,  
Two growing chickens,  
Five sparkling maidens.  
We make the path dance,  
Dance the path, enjoy the land.  
The tall tree of the forest thunders,  
The broad field sings a song.  
If we had a third one:  
Then we would fill the rooms,  
Then we would unite the towns  
And carve a new thigh.  
What of the room to be filled,  
Of the new thigh to be carved?  
A little heart fills the room,  
A little bird unites the town,  
A little bug carves the thigh.  
Dear sister, listen, darling,  
Why don’t you sing with me?  
Speak with a golden voice?  
You must open your mouth wider,  
Make your tongue dearer than gold.  
Your mouth, my mouth,  
Tongues are golden for each.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
If I had control over my mind,  
Control over my mind and tongue,  
Control over my speech and heart.  
Then I would know what I would do:  
I would set the room in the wind,  
On the cobwebs in the corner.  
The house on the berry branch,  
A party on the reed.  
Life on the haystack,  
Benefit on the birch grove.  
I would ride to Tõnismägi,  
There I would eat currants,  
I would crack hazelnuts.  
I would bring bird cherries home,  
For hungry children to nibble,  
For boys to have a feast.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
If I had control over my mind,  
Control over my mind and tongue,  
Control over my speech and heart.  
Then I would know what I would do:  
I would set the room in the wind,  
On the cobwebs in the corner.  
The house on the berry branch,  
A party on the reed.  
Life on the haystack,  
Benefit on the birch grove.  
I would ride to Tõnismägi,  
There I would eat currants,  
I would crack hazelnuts.  
I would bring bird cherries home,  
For hungry children to nibble,  
For boys to have a feast.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Now the meals have been eaten  
And the drinking sessions have been drunk;  
The sleeping times slept;  
The singing times without songs,  
The good cook unpraised,  
The giver unhonored:  
Do not blame him for killing this ox  
Who has never before worked,  
Plowed the field on his knees,  
Seen a plowshare in a hundred years.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Last year I fed on blueberries  
The year before, on polecats.  
I brought an ass in front of the barn,  
Rushed up the hill;  
Moved toward the town,  
Made my way toward Narva.  
Danced in Tapa tavern,  
Played in Mää.  
From there I marched to Matte,  
From Matte I detoured to Parik.  
In Kukeveri I listened,  
In Pirsu tavern, tall girls,  
In Raudla tavern, lazy women.  
I fell asleep in Jäneda.  
Spent time in Aegviidu,  
Roosters sang in Kosenõmme,  
Mowed hay in Nikerjärv.  
Paused on the bridge,  
On the new lake I drank beer without foam.  
Others go to Mustjõgi,  
I crawl to Reite.  
I hear the frogs in Kuusiküll,  
In Pähusti there's a lot of land.  
On a log I tied a ribbon,  
Between the swamps, big hounds,  
Wide lords on the open grounds.  
In Iru manor I breathed,  
Moved to the sandy shore,  
Lit a fire with a pipe on the ghost,  
Set bags in Koogi tavern,  
From there I went to Lasnamäe,  
Rolled to the Russian market,  
Brought an ass in front of the harbor barn,  
Carried bags on the scales,  
Gave grain to the barn helper.  
Then I got a whip from the city,  
From the small town I got a driver.  
Toompea clocks struck the hour,  
Trilli-tralli, other clocks as well,  
In the small town the party died down,  
The girls in the meadow started to cry,  
Kärmu Kaie regretted,  
Harju girls lamented.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The man did not want a wife for me  
The lord did not give a good dowry,  
The brother did not forge a copper ring,  
The sister did not knit a mitten.  
My family wanted me to be a soldier,  
Brothers to become Russian men.  
I do not want to be a soldier,  
Nor do I want to become a Russian man.  
I am a bad drudge,  
Chosen to bark against,  
A black hay mower,  
A hunchbacked ploughman:  
I leave the hills unploughed,  
The flower beds uncultivated.  
It’s harsh to walk the road,  
Misery is the lord’s legacy.  
The road is shown with a stick,  
Given a piece of garden weed.  
In the manor, fields are measured,  
Additional fields are rented,  
Many fields are offered.  
Today I drink in the tavern, tomorrow in the tavern,  
On Sunday I drink without pants.  
Monday is for sleeping,  
Tuesday is to go to the road.  
Wednesday to carry stones,  
Thursday to make wheat,  
Friday to cut sticks,  
Saturday to saw beams.  
Hooray, hurrah! we shout,  
We break men down  
We catch girls!  
In the tavern I drink a little,  
To the small town, to the gray one.  
To the linen town road,  
To the market, red-faced,  
To the fair a little bit,  
Always money for barn planks.  
I have no money at home!  
With me is walking money,  
In my vest pocket is vodka money,  
In my boot shaft is bread money,  
By the wall is standing money,  
In the barn is gray gelding money,  
In the stable the money for a spotted cow.  
In the sack is pocket money,  
From last year the money for a black bull,  
From the year before the money for a polecat.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I had to go soon,
Long ago to start the journey,
Before the day had begun.
Time came, the day rolled by.
Sleep overcame the madman,
The day hid the slender one,
Pressed the woolen carpet.
Let's go, let's go, let's walk, let's walk
Let's go along the made road,
Along the carved paths.
Where once the slaves walked,
Slaves walked, oxen walked
Late wanderers wandered late,
Early travelers marched early
Early walked poor children
Let's begin, men,
Let's light the pipes,
Let the smoking stems smoke.
Our home far away,
Far away, high away,
Five are outside in the field
Six to dry by the river
Seven soaked with soda
Eight with fish baskets,
Nine with good rivers,
Ten with cold springs.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Hops started to go wild,  
Moving legs in the town,  
Took the senses of men's heads,  
Half the senses of boys' heads,  
Ruined the daughters' steadiness.  
Men shouted without hats,  
Women danced without steadiness,  
Boys halfway in their trousers! -

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Oh wonder, or wonder  
What I saw in Näu village:  
Dogs were plowing, oxen were barking,  
Spinning wheels were drawing water,  
Spinning wheels were boiling the broth.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Wandering about aimlessly,  
I have a coat of patchwork,  
A jacket made of fish skin,  
A shirt made of fish scales,  
Pants of rough cloth,  
My hat is like a miller's,  
A cap like a church sexton's,  
That neither goes into town,  
Nor takes a lord's place,  
At the gathering court.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Lace of a ribbon, wild strawberry  
A bright twig, hayberry.  
Who cut the finger yesterday?  
Anne, the maiden, cut it.  
Who jumped to bind it?  
The suitor boy.  
Where did he get the ribbons?  
He brought the ribbons from the town of Narva.  
Where did he get the bands?  
He brought the bands from the town of Paide.  
He tied the sore finger  
The maiden carefully wrapped it up.  
Take care of your finger, Anne  
Take good care yourself  
Protect it with heartfelt concern.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Oh, wonder, or wonder,  
What I saw, oh wonder,  
I went visiting, walking along,  
Taking care of some matters,  
I did not see that wonder  
Which I saw in Näu village,  
At the very end, in the house:  
Two maidens were fighting,  
One was young and the other old.  
Then the young one growled at the old:  
"What do you offer to a man?  
"You're bony underneath, wooden on top,  
"A tar stump in the middle.  
"Let me offer to a man,  
"I'm fat underneath, soft on top,  
"I'm full of manly play in the middle.  
When I go to sleep  
The sheets slide, the pillows crack,  
All night the straw rustles  
Beneath two young ones."

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
It is beautiful to live in Tapa,  
Oats were down, barley was on top,  
Oats were down to be threshed,  
Barley was on top to carry the grain,  
The village smells of cumin,  
The village fields of sulfur,  
The village yards of apple trees,  
The village rooms of thyme,  
The village roofs of rowan,  
The garden edges of vinegar.  
When you travel through the village,  
The barley street booms,  
Through the new lanes,  
Through the brass gate,  
Through the oak street,  
A shining pearly horse gleams,  
The clattering horseshoes sound sharp,  
The gate begins to tremble,  
The oak street starts shaking.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Let's go shoulder to shoulder towards Riga  
Quietly towards Tallinn  
Let's go shoulder to shoulder, step by step.  
Let's go to Riga to spoil things  
To burn Põltsamaa;  
Viru maiden, little virgins,  
Harju girls, slender ones,  
Do not spoil Riga,  
Do not kill little Tallinn,  
Let Riga bring salt  
Tallinn to pound money  
Bring ribbons to Leenu  
Bring a butter churn to Anne  
Bring an earring to Krõõt.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sisters, dear ones,  
Let's join together as three,  
Let's leave this land,  
Let's go to Finland for the summer,  
For the winter to Tähkvere,  
We will make a stone church there,  
A chapel of birch leaves,  
We will put in iron crosses,  
Silver little windows.  
Let me tell it again:  
When we leave,  
Who will do the work,  
The girls will do the work,  
The Elm-necked ones will mow the hay,  
The Curled-necked ones will make stacks,  
The stacks will be seen in Courland,  
Large herds to Germany,  
Prepared ones to Alutaguse.  
We will leave this land  
If a maiden remains hungry in the land,  
A hungry maiden, a missing girl.  
Then a horse will be sought,  
Ridden on the hollow limb,  
Heard on golden hooves,  
Seen on copper hooves,  
Then sparks fly from the chests of the mares,  
Sparks from the hollow limbs,  
Lightning strikes from the hooves of the steed  
To the girl in the gathering woods,  
On the way to courtship.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sisters, dear ones,  
Let's join together as three,  
Let's leave this land,  
Let's go to Finland for the summer,  
For the winter to Tähkvere,  
We will make a stone church there,  
A chapel of birch leaves,  
We will put in iron crosses,  
Silver little windows.  
Let me tell it again:  
When we leave,  
Who will do the work,  
The girls will do the work,  
The Elm-necked ones will mow the hay,  
The Curled-necked ones will make stacks,  
The stacks will be seen in Courland,  
Large herds to Germany,  
Prepared ones to Alutaguse.  
We will leave this land  
If a maiden remains hungry in the land,  
A hungry maiden, a missing girl.  
Then a horse will be sought,  
Ridden on the hollow limb,  
Heard on golden hooves,  
Seen on copper hooves,  
Then sparks fly from the chests of the mares,  
Sparks from the hollow limbs,  
Lightning strikes from the hooves of the steed  
To the girl in the gathering woods,  
On the way to courtship.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I happened to go on a drinking binge  
Poor, miserable vodka binge  
I ended up among good men  
The beer tells me: wait, wait!  
The vodka tells me: waste time!  
What do I waste the little vodka for  
Or what do I wait for the little beer,  
My fields remained unplowed  
Plowing mistakes unmade.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Others drink through a straw  
I run through the ear  
On Monday I drank a new jacket  
On Tuesday I drank a whole shirt  
Wednesday a nice coat  
On Thursday I drank among them  
Friday I drank my clothes off  
Saturday I drank a wide belt  
On Sunday my pants off.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I drank beers, cleaned the kettles  
I toss the twigs into the twig pile  
The cover boards into the grove  
From there grew large islands,  
Large islands, wide plains  
At each end is a squirrel  
At each top is a titmouse  
In each treetop a songbird

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The roosters of the city of Riga crowed  
The shrill sea gulls called out,  
The black bull roared in the sea:  
"Lock the city gates,  
"Fasten the day's gates,  
"The maidens of Viru are coming,  
"The maidens of Harju are chasing,  
"The boys from Järva are smiths in the forge."  
Viru maiden, a young virgin,  
Harju girl, slender one,  
Do not spoil Riga,  
Kill Tallinn,  
Let the city bring salt,  
Let Tallinn strike money,  
Let the boys bring port wine,  
Let Leena bring ribbon bands,  
Let Krõõt bring earrings,  
Let the women bring linen and wool,  
Let the daughters bring blue stones.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The doll was called to the village  
Over the field to the oats  
The doll had no time to go  
The doll was tangled in fabric.  
Bow down, bow down, doll  
Bow down alone, to the clever one  
Your father is already dying  
A grave is being dug for your father  
The grave has a cunning iron bottom  
Nine fathoms deep  
Eight feet cunning  
Five but clever planks  
Rise up, rise up, doll  
Your father is still alive  
Sitting in a silk bed  
A golden doll in hand  
Silver flowers around the doll.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Now we three shall unite  
We shall counsel as four  
Then let us leave this land  
Let us go to Riga to plunder  
To kill in Tallinn  
To burn Põhamaa  
To shed blood in Russia.  
Then the guard shouts from the rampart  
From the boys at the gate of the store  
A townsman near the gate.  
Young maidens, lovely girls  
Fine scribes in herds  
Combed beauties.  
Let us go to Riga without plundering  
Not killing in Tallinn  
Not burning Põhamaa  
Not shedding blood in Russia  
Let it be to bring salt to Riga  
To shake money in Tallinn  
For the boys to bring piglets  
For the women to bring a grain sieve  
For the girl a blue stone.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Oh, master, little master  
Of the manor with the silver sword  
Older one with the golden wreath head  
Why didn't you come to us last year  
When rivers ran without oil  
When river mouths were muddy  
When springs ran with vodka  
When the edges ran with vinegar  
Now you come with an empty foot  
To the light spring  
To the drab autumn  
Now you pluck clothes,  
But take them to the fragment-crates  
You try the neckbands  
Throw the beads around your neck,  
But don't break the neckbands.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Now it's starting to get chilly  
The snow begins to swirl  
The ponds to make big holes.  
Ask, ask, little frost  
Drive away the winter.  
Where have you put the throat  
Where the neck's coat  
Taken to the tavern for vodka  
Cans for the beer jug  
Under five shots of vodka  
Under two beer jugs  
Wait, wait, cold, no, no, cold  
Cold makes me shiver  
I am freezing my hands  
My toe was hurting  
Let my room be fixed  
A new stove is crowned  
A new house is painted  
I go into the warm room in the evening  
You stomp at the doorstep  
You shout on the porch  
You look for a warm room  
For knitted building clothes  
I sit in the warm room  
Sitting by the stove  
With the family club in hand.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Here is the translation of the text into English:

When I took the pacifier out of the mouth  
From behind the teeth of the pike  
From there I became a child singer  
From there I became a word setter  
I was at the wedding with an old woman  
I was at the wedding with an old man  
With an elder on the sleeve  
I drank from the singer’s hand  
From the piper’s flask  
From the maidens' jugs  
Then I became a child singer  
And became a word setter  
I used to study in Harju  
To learn the virus tune  
Sisters studied in Harju  
Viruses like brothers-in-law  
Let the brewers be healthy  
Brewers of beer, brewers of kvass  
The keepers of anger and groans  
The sufferers of great smoke  
Who combined these three  
First water, liquid  
Second coarse flour  
Third crazy hops  
Fourth fine junipers  
Then the hops started to go crazy  
Moving their legs in the city  
That took away men’s wits  
Half of the boys’ wits  
Men rumbled without hats  
Women danced without tanuta  
Boys without soap  
Alone, without hats  
Two jugs without lids  
Five vats to whip  
Then I sang, I shook hands  
I struck my hair on my back  
To the broad long table  
Beer was made from mikid  
From mikid, from makid  
Miki-maki from the meadow of sohust  
From the outer yard’s barley  
From the middle yard’s fine wheat  
From the front yard’s peas.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Our two poor lads  
Let's go along this road  
Where slaves have walked before  
Slaves have walked, oxen have walked  
Many a proud maiden has gone  
To the parish's rich maiden  
The district has gone to the white maiden  
No one has beautified the road  
Beautify the road, go to the field  
Resound on the high tree in the forest  
Our two poor lads  
We beautify the road  
Beautify the road, go to the field  
Resound on the high tree in the forest.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
So I dance in the Tapa rye field,  
Kun's heels are wearing out,  
Big holes in the stocking.  
Then I go to mend the shoe.  
I cut patches of the right size.  
But the village black cat  
Took the best patch.  
Then it ran to the village well.  
Then I go to look for ropes.  
Then I go to look for men.  
From Hans in the sauna, a hundred men,  
From Jüri, a thousand men to bring.  
Then I go to look for ropes,  
From Hans in the sauna, a hundred ropes,  
From Jüri, a thousand ropes to bring.  
Then I go to pull the cat.  
The rye ropes stretch,  
The hemp ropes strain,  
The tarred ropes pulled out.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Master, little master  
Mistress, little mistress,  
Said to eat a lot,  
Eat a lot, drink a lot,  
Put a lot in place.  
When I go to the village, I also eat;  
When I go to the village, I also drink,  
When I go to the village, I also put in place.  
The ox eats too much,  
The barrel drinks too much,  
What can I eat from that ox?  
Skin is on and bone is underneath.  
What can I drink from that barrel?  
Yeast is at the bottom and foam is on top.  
A drop of red beer. -  
Don’t go west, don’t go north;  
I leave this place here.  
I give the place to others,  
I move to another room.  
Better come to the place,  
More honest instead,  
More famous near the door.  
I will come to see tomorrow,  
I will come back from the room.  
The shirt was the place where I left,  
The chair was the place where I sat,  
The bench was the place where I stayed.  
To cover the room with linen,  
To split firewood for the stove,  
The straw cracked like a whip,  
Slippers asked for cleaning,  
Spoons burned in the fire,  
Ladle handles warmed up.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I traveled a bit in Turkey  
A hundred versts in Germany,  
A little on the road to Narva  
Half a verst in Finland.  
I did not see the wonder  
That I saw in Näo village.  
Bulls were barking, dogs were plowing,  
Cats were weaving fabric.  
The pig went to work, the sickle was on the belt,  
The fox traveled with reins around its neck.  
The horse brought a young bull calf,  
The cow brought a clump of grass,  
The sheep went to the table to lay eggs.  
The hen brought two lambs,  
Girls made sledges,  
Women spun spinning wheels,  
Men walked the path of straws.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Go to the corner, go to the corner, young men,  
Under the bench, under the bench, pipe smokers,  
Under the table, under the table, broad jaws,  
Stay still, stay still, soot noses.  
My brother is coming, my brother is coming to watch,  
Clog-shoe is coming, clog-shoe is coming to wander,  
Boot-heel is coming, boot-heel is coming to roam,  
Nice heel is coming, nice heel is coming to turn,  
Twisting sock is coming, twisting sock is coming to roll.  
My brother is coming, my brother is coming to see the bride,  
Where does she stand, where does she stand, my brother's bride?  
She stands here, among the group here.  
How proud, how proud is my brother's bride.  
Even prouder, even prouder is my brother.  
How tall is the reed, how tall is the reed,  
Even taller, even taller is my brother.  
How white is the sea foam, how white is the sea foam,  
Even whiter, even whiter is my brother.  
How pink is the cornflower, how pink is the cornflower,  
Even pinker, even pinker is my brother's bride.  
Look for your own, look for your own brother,  
Look for your own, look among the group,  
Watch your own, watch beyond the parish.  
Young cook, come to us and us.  
We have a lot of fine rye,  
We have a lot, we have a lot of bread rye,  
A lot of pounding, a lot of pounding-doers,  
More to guard the beads, more to guard the beads.  
Take the silk cobweb on your lap and on your lap,  
Throw the silk cobweb out and out.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sister, little Anne  
Listen, dear little Mari  
Let's leave this land  
Let's go to Finland for the summer  
For half the summer to the land of cranberries  
For the winter to the shores of Tallinn  
When a girl comes here hungry  
Hungry girl, missing maiden,  
There’s truly nothing of girls.  
When they search for me on horseback  
Riding the rough paths  
Looking on the tips of foals' hooves  
Hooves strike sparks with lightning flashes  
Sparks fly from the hooves of foals  
When searching for a maiden's group  
Gathering a crowd of maidens.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust

Gathering a crowd of maidens.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The herd is sent out  
A switch is lit in the hand  
A crow is put in the pocket  
I keep the old woman's goat  
I protect the lady's herd  
The woman makes me new stockings  
The man makes me new shoes  
A snow-white coat  
Go home, little herd  
Go home, little herd  
Take home the little wool  
The lady's little milk

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:

A crow is put in the pocket  
I keep the old woman's goat  
I protect the lady's herd  
The woman makes me new stockings  
The man makes me new shoes  
A snow-white coat  
Go home, little herd  
Go home, little herd  
Take home the little wool  
The lady's little milk

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Tiidu-tiidu, the road is crooked  
The edges of the field are crooked  
The straw woman is the most crooked  
Pouring crookedly in the vineyard  
A scoop of beer jug  
Because I have become poor  
Emptiness arises, weakness creeps in  
I will show it from the strength still  
I’m already farting loudly  
Let the emptiness go to hell  
Let it go to the grinding mill.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Let's go, let's go, let's walk, let's walk  
Let's go along the made roads  
Along the carved paths  
Where slaves had walked before  
Early walked poor children  
Late wandered old women,  
After saved by the day laborers  
Before dawn were orderly  
Let's go towards the waving town  
Packed towards Tondi tavern  
Heading towards the small borough  
Let's go to take the town’s beverage  
Under the shade of Mardu manor  
Greetings to the town's beverage, gentlemen  
Gentlemen of Mardu manor  
Here is the man who stands before you  
The man at the front number  
This man fits before the shade  
This man is suitable for the court room  
To sit with gentlemen  
To drink punch with ladies  
I was left with a dear shepherdess  
Beloved was left lamenting  
The girls wiping their eyes  
Young maidens crying  
Kai cries at the shepherd's meadow  
Anne cries before the granary  
Leenu in the hayloft  
Mari under the berry tree.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:

Let's go to take the town’s beverage  
Under the shade of Mardu manor  
Greetings to the town's beverage, gentlemen  
Gentlemen of Mardu manor

The man at the front number

To sit with gentlemen  
To drink punch with ladies  
I was left with a dear shepherdess  
Beloved was left lamenting  
The girls wiping their eyes  
Young maidens crying  
Kai cries at the shepherd's meadow  
Anne cries before the granary  
Leenu in the hayloft  
Mari under the berry tree.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The shepherd drove the flock
Drove me to search
In the foggy morning
In the early light
Along the made paths
Along the carved trails
Where slaves had walked before
Slaves walked, oxen walked
Early walked poor children
Late wandered the latecomers
Before dawn the orderly ones
After light the little lads

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I started to fix the patches,  
To mend the torn gift,  
Then came the manor’s black cat,  
It took my better patch away.  
I went after the cat to the well,  
The cat ran to the well's narrow space,  
Its tail remained long in the narrow space.  
Pity the cat, pity the well,  
Pity the village’s cold water.  
I went to the village to get a rope,  
The rope from the village, the man stopped me,  
Two ropes from the junk dealer,  
From Nigul I brought a linden rope,  
From Tooma Jaan I brought a tarred string,  
From Handrek I kept bringing new ones,  
But I couldn’t get the cat out of the well.  
I made five lashing straps,  
Then I got the cat out of the well.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Hans was from Harju  
Jaan was from Järva  
From the locality was the cook’s boy  
Hans was a man from Harju, a lowlander  
Tina Tupest was strong  
Bought a horse from Harju  
A saddle from the Saare German  
From his own heap a little whip  
Then he went to Riga on a journey  
The Germans of Riga thought  
They thought, they watched  
Look who rides without a master  
Without a master, the king of the land  
I heard, I answered back  
I replied with a stallion  
I am not without a master  
Without a master, the king of the land  
I am another man’s servant  
In summer I walk with sleds on my back  
In winter a tapper with bridle on

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Now the weakness was young men  
Weakness was young boys  
That there was tinder, taken cold  
The garden was fenced with fences  
The field was written with lightning  
Now I had to leave  
Long ago to start walking  
I thought to make the road tick  
To make the trails crack  
The road does not tick, it is tin  
The trail does not crack, it is low  
I have a tin sleigh ticking  
A iron sleigh cracking  
Clinking golden nails on the bend  
Bang inside the street  
A silver horse neighs  
The reins snapped  
The cuckoo fell on the haystack  
A gray bird on the hayrack  
A small bird on the threshing floor  
A singing bird on the threshing platform  
A swallow on the head.

The reins snapped  
The cuckoo fell on the haystack  
A gray bird on the hayrack  
A small bird on the threshing floor  
A singing bird on the threshing platform  
A swallow on the head.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Here is a snipe bird  
In our lord’s garden  
It eats all the currants  
The lord’s straight onions  
Whoever has a gun, shoots the bird  
Whoever has a fork, takes the bird  
Whoever has time, chases after  
Hello, hello, needle maker  
My God, what are you looking for  
I came to look for a needle  
Where did it disappear  
By the door, in the hedgehog’s hole  
Where were you yourself  
In the field making a garden  
What was behind the needle  
A piece of blue, another red  
A third one golden-haired  
Bend, bend, little needle  
Bend, little needle eye  
Needle for the heath's small home  
For the thicket’s sprout  
The needle hasn’t disappeared from the cloth  
The needle hasn’t disappeared from the work  
Sewing a sheer cloth  
Cutting a fine shirt  
Stitching a child’s shirt  
Mending a child’s shirt

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Essu-lessu leaves were wide  
Our parish boys were tall  
They went to the heathland rides  
Rides, victorious rides  
Under the horse like under an oven  
Headless like the day itself  
Hat like a king’s crown  
Belt on the waist like a city ribbon  
Boots shining on the feet.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Go, go men,  
To the homeland, to the homeland, boys,  
Wandering, wandering new sixes  
Marching, the landless.  
Let us go, men,  
Let us light the pipes,  
Smoky noses smoking:  
Now the trouble is below the town,  
The power is under our rule.  
Now is the time for war,  
The Russian is brandishing,  
Far from our home:  
Five to exchange swords,  
Six to dry the rivers,  
Seven the tough swamp,  
Eight the fish nets,  
Nine good rivers,  
Ten dry hills.  
When will we get there  
Where the wind has made a room,  
Water has rolled the logs,  
Frost has mossed the walls.  
There our log forest shines,  
Tall spruces shimmer,  
Straight aspens bluish,  
Broad birches green.  
There west along the great island,  
Along the wide shore of the island.  
There the woodpecker pecks wood.  
Blue feathers for the head,  
Old copper for the line.  
They build new houses,  
Create dear chambers,  
New storehouses are thatched,  
The starling makes the roof,  
The woodpecker smooths the walls,  
The ptarmigan makes the lintel,  
The sparrow the bare ceilings,  
The magpie makes the stove,  
The cranes make the beams,  
The duck pats the lintel.  
They nail in iron crosses,  
Silver buttons on the corners.  
Line them with meatskin,  
Hinges made of eggshells.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
This is how it is in our country,  
This happens in our farm:  
A pig was plowing, a dog was biting the stake,  
A sheep was biting a wolf,  
A chicken was pecking a golden ring,  
A pig was wearing a brooch on its chest,  
A dog was walking with a crown on its head,  
A goat had bindings on its legs,  
A sheep had wide hoofs.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Now I go singing to the threshing floor  
Head twisted to the father-in-law  
Sister boiled apple broth,  
Father-in-law made fresh Baltic herring broth.  
Mother-in-law baked fish,  
Brother-in-law drank brandy.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I watch over the old woman’s sheep,  
I protect the old man’s herd.  
The old woman makes a new cozy hut for me,  
From blue wool,  
From blue she makes a standing hut,  
From white wool a warm hut,  
From gray wool a rough hut,  
From black wool another hut.  
From red she makes a wedding hut.  
A little wolf comes,  
Takes the child and the sheep to the forest,  
Pulls the grandmother into trouble.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Oh my little dog of Krants  
With a little collar over your head:  
In summer you brought feathers home,  
In winter you brought big hares  
And brought a couple of ducks,  
From between the village of Kuksema,  
From the forest of Varivere.  
I will take the ducks to Paide  
From Paide I will get a lot of money  
I will buy my own little pipe,  
I will start playing the pipe,  
Blowing the German horn:  
All the beautiful village girls  
Will then run after me  
And sing their song.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Oh my little dog of Krants  
With a little collar over your head:

And brought a couple of ducks,

From the forest of Varivere.  
I will take the ducks to Paide  
From Paide I will get a lot of money  
I will buy my own little pipe,  
I will start playing the pipe,  
Blowing the German horn:  
All the beautiful village girls  
Will then run after me  
And sing their song.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Upon the great clans,  
Proud of the fathers,  
Proud of the mothers,  
Dignified of the five brothers,  
Noble of the six sisters,  
On whom does the poor child rely?  
I am proud of myself,  
Dignified on the five fingers,  
Noble on the two hands.  
I have no clan here on this land,  
No sister in my village,  
No aunt in this people’s place;  
All my kin are in Finland,  
Father’s brothers in Hiiumaa,  
Half the brothers in Pohja Land,  
Sisters at the edge of Laiuse.  
Kin speaks Finnish language,  
Father’s brothers speak Hiiumaa language,  
Half the brothers speak Pohja language,  
Sisters speak Laiuse dialect.  
All my kin in foreign places,  
Sisters in closed shoes,  
But I alone am five-legged,  
Five-legged, wearing a buckskin shoe,  
Keeper of the willow slippers;  
God grant willow offshoots,  
So all the birch calves could grow,  
From which poor children get shoes,  
Footwear for the poor child.  
My father the miracle worker:  
Made me a wondrous coat,  
Made a spotted tipi,  
A goatskin on a birch,  
I am not born among the great,  
Nor fit in the house of the rich,  
Noble among the ear-seaters.  
I sit particularly  
Among other weary ones,  
In the midst of beggars.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
The herd has gone to the proud ones,  
A row of rich stalks,  
A bunch of pipe sweepers,  
Once of chimney scrapers.  
They did not come to greet,  
God help us.  
They made you wander the roads,  
Wander the roads and enjoy the land.  
The dry tree in the forest,  
The wide song of the grove.  
Tender little sister,  
We once were many,  
Many a beautiful herd.  
Now the herd has ended,  
Beautiful order has disappeared,  
Now I am left alone to wail,  
To move the flax seed,  
To pulsate the oat seed,  
To watch the barley seed,  
Among the other seeds.  
Do not hit the poor one,  
The poor cries without being hit,  
Howls without a whip,  
Gets up in the morning,  
To the elbow of the water below,  
To the bottom of the bucket,  
Under the striped blanket,  
Under the hairy coat.
