They did not come to greet,  
God help us.  
They made you wander the roads,  
Wander the roads and enjoy the land.  
The dry tree in the forest,  
The wide song of the grove.  
Tender little sister,  
We once were many,  
Many a beautiful herd.  
Now the herd has ended,  
Beautiful order has disappeared,  
Now I am left alone to wail,  
To move the flax seed,  
To pulsate the oat seed,  
To watch the barley seed,  
Among the other seeds.  
Do not hit the poor one,  
The poor cries without being hit,  
Howls without a whip,  
Gets up in the morning,  
To the elbow of the water below,  
To the bottom of the bucket,  
Under the striped blanket,  
Under the hairy coat.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Let's leave this countryside,  
Let's go to Finland for the summer,  
For half the summer to the land of cranberries,  
For a little while to the town of Narva.  
There is no hay cutting,  
Nor meadow mowing,  
But there someone mows the hay poorly,  
The scythe takes the meadow,  
The cuckoo calls from the hills,  
Who brought here  
Who brought the red ribbon?  
Who brought the rose-colored cloth?  
Father brought the blue here,  
Mother brought the red ribbon,  
Brother brought the rose-colored cloth.  
I myself am weaving a Kalev's belt,  
With big, wide heddles,  
With big heddles made by my father-in-law,  
Wide heddles made by my mother-in-law,  
With loom feet made along.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
If we had a whole yard full of us  
Oh, if we had a whole sled full of us,  
A brother’s lap full of warmth,  
A valley full of white boys,  
Then we would know what to do:  
We would build a barn on the wind,  
At the other edge of the barn –  
Behind the barn, a small stable,  
Inside the stable, a gray mare,  
A good saddle for the mare,  
A German boy for the saddle –  
A golden belt for the German boy,  
A weaver for the golden belt...

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Oh you little hop madman  
A tiny drop of poison  
Took the minds from men's heads,  
Half the minds from boys' heads,  
The temples in front of women's faces.  
Men roar without hats  
Women dance without skirts  
What else do we drink beer for  
If not to glorify beer,  
Little hop bear in a new cap  
I drink beer, rattling the jug  
I cast the whips into the whip bag  
Legboards from the brushwood.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I guard grandma’s ewe,  
Protect grandpa’s large herd;  
From the swamp came great wolves,  
From the forest behind, wide paws,  
Broke down the black ewe,  
Tore away the speckled bull.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Let the brewers be healthy,  
Brewers, pourers,  
Who have put together six.  
First, water, which is liquid,  
Second, fire full of strength,  
Third, dear junipers,  
Fourth, malt sweet,  
Fifth, bitter hops,  
Sixth, those hot stones,  
Seventh, yeast added on top.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Morning squirrel fur  
Daytime raccoon fur  
Noon skylark fur  
Evening rye flower fur  
"He went under our yard  
Ate a heap of our hay  
Reduced our rye in the field."

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Six little gold pieces  
A colorful little bird  
I will not give my hen  
I feed it with sugar  
In winter with buckwheat groats.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
If I were alive,
If I were made second,
Then I would know what I would do:
I would put the room out to the wind,
The sauna on the edge of the island,
The house on the slope of the hill.
Who rolled the wages?
The water rolled the wages.
Who mossed the walls?
The rain mossed the walls.
Who made the doors new,
The fog made the doors new.
They come into the room and ask:
"Do you have a town, don’t you have a flag?
We have Finnish salt ships,
We have Riga money ships.
We don’t have a town, because we don’t have a flag.
We have a famous little place
On the slope of the hill
On the edge of the island sauna
And the room is out to the wind.
If I were alive,
If I were made second:
I would make the Germans peasants,
The gentleman in front oxen,
The ladies behind cranes.
Then the gentleman would shout ai-ai-ai.
And I would shout trai-rai-rai behind!

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
If I were alive,
If I were made second,
Then I would know what I would do:
I would put the room out to the wind,
The sauna on the edge of the island,
The house on the slope of the hill.
Who rolled the wages?
The water rolled the wages.
Who mossed the walls?
The rain mossed the walls.
Who made the doors new,
The fog made the doors new.
They come into the room and ask:
"Do you have a town, don’t you have a flag?
We have Finnish salt ships,
We have Riga money ships.
We don’t have a town, because we don’t have a flag.
We have a famous little place
On the slope of the hill
On the edge of the island sauna
And the room is out to the wind.
If I were alive,
If I were made second:
I would make the Germans peasants,
The gentleman in front oxen,
The ladies behind cranes.
Then the gentleman would shout ai-ai-ai.
And I would shout trai-rai-rai behind!

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Lastelaulud
translation:
I had a wealthy godmother  
She gave me a little goat,  
A little bell on the goat’s neck.  
The goat went into the forest bleating,  
The bell on its neck tinkling;  
The goat went to the village herd,  
Across the sea and forest.  
The goat brings me hay,  
I give the hay to the cow;  
The cow gives me milk,  
I give the milk to the piglet;  
The piglet gives me a son-in-law,  
I give the son-in-law to my mother-in-law,  
The mother-in-law gives me a cake.  
I give the cake to my father-in-law,  
The father-in-law gives me iron.  
I give the iron to the smith,  
The smith gives me a horse collar,  
I give the horse collar to the oak tree.  
The oak tree gives me leaves,  
I give the leaves to the sheep  
The sheep gives me a lamb,  
The lamb gives me wool,  
I give the wool to the spinner.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Sisters, dear ones,  
did you see my horse  
wandering in the street,  
dancing in the street?  
What color is your horse?  
Morning squirrel color,  
noon flame color,  
and midday gosling color.  
It passed through our yard,  
its tail was tied with silk,  
its mane was braided with thread,  
its head was placed in a braid. -  
It tasted our seawater,  
tried our well water,  
pulled tar from the tar bucket. -

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Young men of Nurme village  
are going to a winning ride,  
to take winning women,  
to hold a winning wedding.  
They have a horse as fast as a stove,  
themselves as bright as the day,  
a hat like a church bell,  
a belt like a rain rainbow.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Young men of Nurme village  
are going to a winning ride,  
to take winning women,  
to hold a winning wedding.  
They have a horse as fast as a stove,  
themselves as bright as the day,  
a hat like a church bell,  
a belt like a rain rainbow.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row the swing, row the swing,  
row the swing to the sunny land,  
where the spruces yellow,  
the firs bow wisely,  
the straight alders turn blue,  
the snakes are long and red;  
where the roosters eat gold,  
the hens drink fear,  
the geese cluck silver,  
the crows old copper,  
the small birds  
the tits tin pieces...

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Neigh, neigh, grey stallion  
Try to jump out into the yard.  
If you get out, you will get barley;  
if you stay inside, you will go without.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I make a tua (thatched roof) for the wind,  
a branch from the saana tree.  
I make a roof from feathers of a grebe,  
a room from a hen's egg.  
Inside I make the walls blue,  
on top I put red beams.  
Then the fire came to see,  
the parish came to listen.  
Every head went to Pärnu's Saxons,  
the Palve Fridays to Paide Saxons,  
every month kings would come.  
They wondered about the town of Lihula,  
was it Rahula's shore,  
or was it the emperor's church?  
But I listen and answer:  
"This is Halva Anne's sauna."

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Sleepy, sleepy little one.  
The wolf pulls the old woman's beard.  
The old woman makes me a new hat,  
The old man makes a winter coat for her,  
from flags and patches  
and the fur of an old bear.  
"Have you seen my sheep?  
They passed by here, I threw leaves in front of them.  
But why is your mouth red?  
I ate strawberries.  
What is behind your back?  
Stones, stumps, brooms, shovels.  
May I come out to play?  
Certainly.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I traveled a bit in Turkey,  
A hundred versts in Germany,  
A little on the Narva road,  
Half a verst in Poland.  
I have never seen the miracle  
That I saw in Näuküla:  
Bulls were barking, dogs were plowing,  
Cats were weaving cloth,  
The pig went to work, the sickle was at its waist,  
The fox stood with reins around its neck.  
The horse brought a bull-calf,  
The cow brought a little washbasin,  
The sheep went to lay eggs in the barn,  
The hen brought two lambs.  
Girls were busy spinning,  
Women were carding wool,  
Men were walking the tavern road.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Hans was from Harju,  
Jaan was from Järva,  
There was a cook's boy from Kohala.  
Hans was a man from Harju, lowborn,  
Strong from Tinatupe.  
He bought a horse from Harju,  
A saddle from a Saare German,  
A little whip from his own cooper,  
Then he set off on a journey to Riga.  
The Germans in Riga were thinking.  
They thought, they watched:  
Look, there he rides without a master,  
Without a master, the king of the land.  
I heard this and responded,  
Answering the verses, I said:  
I am not without a master,  
Without a master, the king of the land.  
I am another man's servant.  
In summer, I walk carrying sacks,  
In winter, a tapper in the blacksmith's shop.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The shepherd herded the flock;
Drove me out to search.
In the misty morning,
In the early dawn,
Along the made paths,
Along the carved trails,
Where before the slaves had walked,
Slaves had walked, oxen had walked.
Early went the poor children.
Late wandered the latecomers.
Before dawn, the orderly ones.
After the day, the day workers.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I began to patch the pastels,  
To mend the gift’s binding.  
The manor’s black cat came,  
It took away the patch from my right pastel.  
I went after the cat,  
The cat also went into the well’s shelter.  
Its tail remained as a long escape.  
A pity for the cat, a pity for the well.  
A pity for the village’s cold water.  
I went to the village to get rope.  
Rope from the village, man from the household.  
Two ropes from the thief.  
From Nigul I brought linden rope.  
From Tooma-Jaan, tar string,  
From Andres, I brought only new;  
Couldn’t get the cat out of the well,  
I made five ribbons of five stripes,  
Then I got the cat out of the well.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Who here rides along the road,  
Gallops along the paths?  
The horse is silver in front.  
The wagon is copper behind.  
The wheel shakes coins,  
The rims clink ruble pieces,  
The spokes chime pennies,  
The horse in front like the stove,  
The head itself like the day,  
The hat like the moon’s servant,  
The belt like a rain rainbow.  
Master, little master!  
Here someone rides along the road,  
Gallops along the paths.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Now there were weak young men  
Weak young boys.  
Because the firewood was cold  
The fences were driven by a living being  
The field was written by lightning.  
Now I had to leave my head  
Long ago to walk the road.  
I thought the road would tick.  
The paths would crack.  
The road doesn’t tick with tin  
The path doesn’t crack with mud.  
My tin sleigh ticks,  
My iron sleigh beats,  
The golden claws snap with a flick.  
The gray street cracks.  
The horned horse with a bell.  
The peaceful bars cracked.  
The cuckoo fell into the hollow,  
The gray bird to the shed,  
The woodpecker to the barn,  
The songbird to the cradle,  
The swallow to the roof.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Now there were weak young men  
Weak young boys.  
Because the firewood was cold  
The fences were driven by a living being  
The field was written by lightning.  
Now I had to leave my head  
Long ago to walk the road.  
I thought the road would tick.  
The paths would crack.  
The road doesn’t tick with tin  
The path doesn’t crack with mud.  
My tin sleigh ticks,  
My iron sleigh beats,  
The golden claws snap with a flick.  
The gray street cracks.  
The horned horse with a bell.  
The peaceful bars cracked.  
The cuckoo fell into the hollow,  
The gray bird to the shed,  
The woodpecker to the barn,  
The songbird to the cradle,  
The swallow to the roof.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
A little beer, a little beauty,  
A little ale, a little darling,  
From your face, you are the handsome one,  
From your stomach, you are the sweet one.  
You walk along my stomach,  
March along my belly,  
Stretch along the spine.  
Be healthy, brewers!  
Who made good beer,  
Often went to the tavern,  
Sometimes went to the well.  
But I sip along with my mind,  
I try along the edge of the pitcher.  
The hop calls in the stream,  
The cone crackles in the bush:  
Come, come young men,  
Pick me into your sack,  
Take me out for the work in the field,  
Put me on the bench edge,  
Carry me to the kitchen stove!  
Then I rise onto the loft,  
I swell along the long gutter.  
Then I take the minds of men,  
Half the minds of the boys' heads,  
The temples from the women's foreheads.  
Men make noise without hats,  
Women dance without shoes,  
Boys half kneeling.  
The beer was wild and the bottle was smart,  
Ale was very sly.  
Oh, you proud barley water,  
Oh, you wrathful grain water,  
Though you surely bring me down,  
Still, I take you inside me.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Crazy are those hops,  
mighty are the killer vines!  
They take the minds from men's heads,  
have stolen them from wise women's heads!

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The song ended, the child went to the barn.  
I quickly went out the door.  
There I saw seven jokes.  
The girl milked an ox by the shoulders,  
The pig sang on a penny.  
The hen brought two lambs,  
The cow hit the leaf of the field with a stallion.  
The horse is a Dutch calf.  
Huhuhu and hahahaa  
The child went to the barn with the song.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Tiidut-tiidut, the road was crooked  
The road was crooked, the ground was crooked.  
The wrinkled leg was crooked  
The distiller poured crooked brandy  
The slope brought ointments.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Let's get going, men,  
Light the pipes,  
Little ones rushing  
Smoke pipes smoking,  
And steam pipes steaming.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I am a drunkard, an old man from Nurme  
A tavern boy  
I still drink in the tavern  
I also sing tavern songs  
Alone I waited for two heads  
Weeks in groups of four  
Months in twos and threes  
Years in groups of five  
I drank away the yoke from the oxen  
The cows from the milking pail  
Still there was grumbling  
The sawdust kept buzzing  
The pinecone remained listening  
The navel stalk kept laughing  
The youth kept talking endlessly  
I still drank in the tavern  
I sang tavern songs  
I don't know where my horse is  
Whether it is outside or in the yard  
A young maiden  
Go outside to check  
See for yourself  
Whether it is outside or in the yard  
Or has it been let loose  
Or caught in a snowstorm wind  
To a wide plank floor  
Rain leans against the big wall  
Stands here by the wall  
Gets caught between the toes  
A young maiden  
Bring a blanket to the bed  
Wipe my beard from the mist  
Dirty the steps of the mother-in-law.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Little Jüri, a little rascal  
But a rich boy with money  
Jüri made a room out in the wind  
A sauna on the big island  
He made doors from wolf bones  
And covers from bear bones  
Antler clusters from moose antlers  
Jüri, God's servant  
The Creator’s storyteller  
But Jüri cruelly killed a cat  
Made a coat for Kaie  
A base cloak for Anne  
A full cloak for Maie  
Because Anne loved  
Kaie dearly  
Maie was her love  
Liisu long ago was her torment.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
But I went on the wedding path  
On the wedding path of Black Mats  
An ox was slaughtered at Mats'  
But I was called a cook  
I put the meat to cook  
And the roast to stew  
Then came the cat with quick eyes  
Ate the meat before it was cooked  
Before the roast was stewed  
I went to catch the cat  
The cat went into a crack in the wall  
I went to find a rope  
Tõnu brought a tarred rope  
From the linden tree I brought a hemp rope  
From the birch tree a hemp rope  
Then I went to catch the cat  
The hemp ropes got tangled  
The linden rope pulled out  
The mice laughed in the corner  
Ast ast ast ast little kitty  
Where is my golden son of the spruce  
Everything is up the cat's ass  
In Black Mats' stomach.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Liisakene little ribbon  
Snap as soon as you see the eye  
Tap as soon as you hear the ear  
An old sneaker in the shadow  
Liisu heard the sound of an instrument  
She went outside, flashing  
The floor beneath was beating  
Tapping on the boy's ear  
Liisu heard the sound of a shoe  
Clicking sounds against the codes  
Immediately as the store falls  
Click, run away.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I rode, I rode, little brother  
I rode through a piece of Turkey  
Half of the world, the land of the bog  
A bit of Narva land  
I didn’t see the miracle  
That I saw inside the house  
Oxen yoked, dogs plowed.  
Girls made sledges  
Women chopped wheelwood  
Father chopped oxwood  
Old wise man cut timber  
They knocked on spoke wood,  
Men walked on straw roads  
Brandy flasks inside  
Beer jugs at the door.  
They bloomed on straw beer  
Sang on the straw table.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The family daughter, a young maiden  
When she hears the fiddle player  
The fiddle player, the fine player  
Then she lifts her forehead  
Steps out from the thicket  
Her chest swells openly  
Now to the oak to dance  
The young spruces to bend  
To the apple trees to sway  
The tumpet to beat  
When I hear the drum player  
The drum player, loud and clear  
Then I head towards the bed  
Step towards the thicket  
Hopping towards the table  
There I sleep, my darling  
My beautiful blossom  
Resting, a bride at the wedding.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Uiu Tuiu, a hunter’s bundle  
A ghost’s bundle behind the room  
An evil lord’s gold bundle  
A poor people’s pain bundle  
The laborers have much work  
In the large hall are many lazy ones  
On the family platform much farting  
In the room’s corner much shouting.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Be healthy, brewers  
Who put these three together  
First, water is liquid  
Second, that malt is sweet  
Third, you hate fools.  
This took the minds from men's heads  
Half the minds from boys' heads  
Thanks to the wise women's heads.  
Men break into smoking  
Women dance without thanks  
Boys halfway to drunkenness.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I had the thought to go on a journey,  
To go on a journey, to travel to the countryside.  
When I set off, it started drizzling,  
When I reached the countryside, it began to hail.  
The shutters were struck by sleet,  
The beams were rattled and shaken.  
Then more wind came, raising a storm,  
It lifted my grey oars upright.  
So I went against the night,  
Against the night, against the moon,  
Against the white dawn,  
Against the dark clouds.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
In the meadow, a skate was skating inside me,  
The claw pointed into the bush:  
"Bring here some young men,  
Take me home with a sack,  
Take me into the house with a mat,  
Put me on the edge of the board,  
Boil me in the kettle,  
Lift me onto the edge of the threshing floor,  
From there I sneak into the barrel,  
Into the half-full beer,  
Between the sticks of the cask."  
There was beer for the wild, and the bottle was wise,  
It took the senses from the heads of men,  
The sense from the heads of women.  
Men made noise without hats,  
Women danced without senses,  
Boys halfway on their knees,  
Girls on all fours,  
Dancing on their heels and toes.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Vaida, the Vaida factory is visible,  
The citizen's chimney is smoking.  
The city brooms are turning green,  
The Toompea towers are gleaming.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Let us drink, let us drink, dear brothers!
Let us drink, dear good fellows.
We drink vodka when we can,
We live the master's way,
We work the wise way.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The horse runs because of the reins  
It beats its head because of the whip  
It leaps because of the herd’s whip.  
It steps because of the dry hay  
Then the horse curls up in the shed  
In the grey garden corner.  
Milla comes to the drunkard’s doorstep and  
To the ear of the stalk-walker  
To raise the slanted cloak  
The family’s maiden  
The farm girl, the little plant  
You bring the striped blanket  
The woolen, patterned rug.  
Wipe my horse from the mist.  
Wipe the rain from the hooves  
From the wide-lipped stable floor.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Whose litter, whose litter, paid for a cow,  
Whose boat :,: a pair of oxen?  
Whose buckle :,: of the large parish,  
Whose wreath for the town of Pärnu.  
Mari’s litter :,: paid for a cow  
Mari’s boat Mari’s boat a pair of oxen  
Mari’s buckle :,: of the large parish  
Mari’s wreath :,: for the town of Pärnu  
Mari herself :,: without all  
Kis Siidi :,: take on your lap  
Kis Armas :,: start turning  
Siidisammet :,: take on your lap  
Mari Armas :,: start turning  
Mari Siidi :,: take on your lap  
Mari dear :,: jump on the neck  
Exchange :,: a good husband  
Juuli Armas :,: start turning  
Juuli dear :,: jump on the neck  
Juuli Siidi :,: take on your lap,

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Oh, our golden king!  
Rich young gentleman of Riga,  
A true boy from Pärnu.  
Why didn’t you come to us last year,  
When the rivers ran without oil,  
And the springs bubbled below;  
When the surface swirled in whirlpools,  
Now you have come at the beginning of time,  
At the light springtime;  
At the rustling autumn,  
At the snowy snowfall of winter.  
Now you strip off your clothes:  
Rip off your collars,  
Take off your wreath from your head!  
Oh, our golden king!  
Rich young gentleman of Riga,  
A true boy from Pärnu,  
Give me some poor gold;  
Cast away the troublesome silver!  
Last year the master bought a press.  
The mistress got a little burden;  
I myself got a shawl’s worth;  
The press I got from beating the thigh,  
The broth I got from boiling the soup,  
From kneading the bread with a clasp.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Cross dance, cross dance, let’s dance  
Our dance, our dance, let’s keep  
Järva dance:,: let’s leave  
Viru dance:,: let’s lead  
Dear dance:,: beloved dance  
Dance of the threshing floor:,: the king  
Roll, little one:,: from the manor  
Blue rises:,: grows from the swamp  
Grows from the swamp:,: from the borders of the land  
Circle of dancers:,: between  
Turning of the shoe:,: from the snakes.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Belly of the drum, belly of the drum, reed berry!
Over the twig, over the twig, clover stem.
Who went yesterday :,: to the hayfield?
It was Mari, :,: the young maiden;
That she :,: cut her finger!
Poor toe :,: in the haystack.
Who jumped up :,: to check?
It was her fiancé :,: the young man,
Tied up :,: that finger;
Wrapped it up, a wet bandage.
Where did she get :,: the scarf?
She took it from her neck :,: the neckerchief.
Where did she get :,: the ribbon?
She took it from the belt :,: the belt ribbon.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row, swing, reach the swing!  
Row across three forests;  
There were three forests side by side;  
One was a golden spruce forest,  
The second a dear birch forest;  
The third a true apple tree forest.  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
That was the forest of young men.  
What was the dear birch forest?  
That was the forest of young women.  
What was the true apple tree forest?  
That was the forest of young maidens.  
Row, swing, reach the swing!  
Row the swing to the spoken land.  
Where the roosters drink gold,  
Roosters gold, hens curry:  
Show the silver chest!  
Surely it will come to us two young ones;  
Just right for a couple of young ones.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row, swing, reach the swing!  
Row across three forests;  
There were three forests side by side;  
One was a golden spruce forest,  
The second a dear birch forest;  
The third a true apple tree forest.  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
That was the forest of young men.  
What was the dear birch forest?  
That was the forest of young women.  
What was the true apple tree forest?  
That was the forest of young maidens.  
Row, swing, reach the swing!  
Row the swing to the spoken land.  
Where the roosters drink gold,  
Roosters gold, hens curry:  
Show the silver chest!  
Surely it will come to us two young ones;  
Just right for a couple of young ones.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Hops called me into the ditch,  
Cone creaked into the corner stove  
Come here, young men,  
Take me to the hut of the bear,  
Make me a steed for the stag,  
Put me to dry on the railing.  
From there I go to the barn,  
Slip into the half-pint of beer,  
Roll between the twigs;  
Then I take the senses from men's heads,  
Half the senses from boys' heads,  
Thanks from wise women's heads;  
Men roar without hats,  
Women dance without the ground,  
Boys half without boots,  
Girls without belts.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, poor child of a slave,  
I am always gloomy,  
Every day my days are long,  
Every month was sad for me,  
Where I plant dullness,  
Where I step gloomily.  
On the stone to the church road,  
On the stone to the priest’s field,  
The stone was hard, it did not speak,  
The slab was thick, it did not whisper.  
The stone did not understand the maiden’s language,  
The slab did not heed the maiden’s prayer.  
“Sisters, darlings,  
Let us come together as three,  
And hold counsel as four.  
Let us leave this land,  
Let us go to Finland,  
For a few days to Pärnu County,  
For a year to Alutaguse.  
There we will build a stone church,  
There we will make a wooden chapel.  
From onions we will build a bridge,  
From wild garlic we will lay it.  
If there comes maiden hunger to the land,  
Maiden hunger, a curse,  
Surely they will look for us carefully,  
Ride along the herring’s leg,  
Test the foal’s hoof.  
They cut fire with the horse’s chest,  
Sparks flew from the herring’s leg,  
The foal’s hooves clattered,  
Searching for the maiden flock,  
Catching the curse flock.”
