Riding on the shin of a slim person:  
The shoulders of the hurser strike fire  
The shins of the slim one threw sparks  
The hooves of the colt strike lightning  
When a girl goes on the road  
Far to catch the herd.  
Young maidens, young girls  
When you leave  
Who will then do the work?  
"The girls do the work  
Young girls mowing hay  
Heaps appeared in Kuuramaa  
Big flocks in Saaremaa  
Young flails in Alutaguse.  
These are made by our girls  
Heaps gathered by daughters  
Flails started by maidens.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I am Maie, I am a berry  
I am a strawberry on the mountain  
In the bluebell grove  
In the hawfinch thicket,  
In the red willow bushes  
In the meadowsweet fields:  
"Come, boys, to weave me  
To find the bluebell  
To gather the hawfinch  
Let us look at the meadowsweet  
To weave the red willow  
I slept, Mari, on the mountain  
Among the meadowsweet  
In the bluebell grove  
In the red willow bushes  
I saw a dream while sleeping:  
A fig tree in front of the room  
A golden tree behind the room  
A downy birch in the chamber,  
A sapling inside the sauna  
An apple tree in the yard.  
"Village grandmothers, old ladies  
Understand this dream  
That I saw while sleeping."  
"You drink spirits from the fig tree  
Suitors come from the golden tree  
The downy birch is a companion,  
The sapling is a matchmaker  
The apple tree is a wedding guest."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
What is a nap for
For a man from the western forest
From a tree root for a boy,
From a stump root for a spouse.
I drink without sister's beer
Half a glass from brother's side
An aunt tries to fill up.
Who made me without beer?
The titmouse made me without beer.
Who brought two loaves of bread?
The bear brought two loaves of bread.
Who brought slices of meat?
The wolf brought slices of meat.
The trees were tall wedding guests
The birches broad bridesmaids
The field a wide dining table.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
What is a nap for
For a man from the western forest
From a tree root for a boy,
From a stump root for a spouse.
I drink without sister's beer
Half a glass from brother's side
An aunt tries to fill up.
Who made me without beer?
The titmouse made me without beer.
Who brought two loaves of bread?
The bear brought two loaves of bread.
Who brought slices of meat?
The wolf brought slices of meat.
The trees were tall wedding guests
The birches broad bridesmaids
The field a wide dining table.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Be plump, our girls  
Step softly, step softly, go lightly.  
Young laughers seem to be  
Girls’ critics are coming  
“Where do they come from, where do they seem to come from?  
From the ravine side of the pine grove,  
From the bog side of the pine forest,  
From the clearing side of the open land.”  
Surely from there they come wandering,  
Surely from there they rain in groups,  
Surely from there the crooked-jawed one rolls,  
Among our elders,  
Into the midst of decent people,  
They poke their nose askew.  
What are you searching around for,  
Stuffing your belly with them?  
This is not yours to have.  
Here is yours to have—  
Your dear little child.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My father scolded me for the rich man's daughter  
My mother for the granary beginner,  
Sisters for the silver thread picker,  
Brothers for the copper blade wielder,  
The cooper for the gold bearer.  
I will not go to the rich man,  
The rich dress in words.  
I will not go to the gold bearer!  
My gold will not be spent.  
I don't want the copper blade wielder.  
The coppers mine my old ones.  
I don't want the miller.  
The miller's stone grinds loudly.  
Nor do I want the weaver,  
The weaver's spool rattles.  
I don't want the coachman,  
The coachman's hands are shaky.  
I want to be a ploughman,  
A ploughman, a sower.  
I do not want to be a tailor.  
The tailor ruins clothes.  
I will still go to the ploughman,  
The German officer's broom sweeper,  
The lord of the granary,  
The turner of black soil.  
My husband sits in the room,  
Lying on a broom straw mattress.  
He peeks into the oven speck,  
Lies on his back inside the bed,  
Grabs my waist,  
Presses it against his chest,  
When he's done with his work,  
The affairs managed.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Other times I would pass the time otherwise  
Other horses I should otherwise take.  
When Saturday evening comes,  
Sunday’s small holiday.  
There are no root diggers,  
No mouth closers,  
No waist binders.  
Father goes with my mother,  
Sister goes with her husband,  
Brother goes with a young lass,  
I go crying towards the corner,  
I drift toward the empty bed;  
I turn my eyes, I find the wall,  
I turn on my side, I find the cold.  
I put my socks against my mouth,  
The coat against my neck,  
The old skirt against my back.  
Socks give warmth to the mouth,  
The coat warmth to the neck.  
The old boot warmth to the back.  
With whom do I talk?  
I talk with scabies,  
I strike the receiver with lice,  
I stomp the dance of fleas.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Tiidu-tiidu, along the road,  
I drive oxen through the garden;  
Horses through the field.  
I go from village to village,  
Tread through farmyards,  
Turn the family gates.  
I roam past pastures,  
Trouble the poor girl,  
Torment the maiden of the household.  
I tease the farm girls,  
Call up the suitors.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What are you blowing, little wind  
Bursts of bad weather  
Today my man is at sea,  
My partner by the fish river.  
Let the little wind come tomorrow  
Bursts of bad weather:  
Tomorrow the murderers at sea  
The Cossacks by the fish river.  
Push the murderers into the sea  
The Cossacks into the fish river.  
I heard men being thrown  
Into the sea during punishment.  
I thought about my own fate  
I ran running, went walking  
My legs started to burn  
Sparks scalded my calves.  
There was a stranger with a twisted neck,  
I helped to tie his hands  
To put a hoop on his leg,  
To fasten the collar.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What are you blowing, little wind  
Bursts of bad weather  
Today my man is at sea,  
My partner by the fish river.  
Let the little wind come tomorrow  
Bursts of bad weather:  
Tomorrow the murderers at sea  
The Cossacks by the fish river.  
Push the murderers into the sea  
The Cossacks into the fish river.  
I heard men being thrown  
Into the sea during punishment.  
I thought about my own fate  
I ran running, went walking  
My legs started to burn  
Sparks scalded my calves.  
There was a stranger with a twisted neck,  
I helped to tie his hands  
To put a hoop on his leg,  
To fasten the collar.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Ox, ox, little virgin ox  
Ox’s beautiful hay,  
If the hay is not mowed,  
Ox’s beautiful arch  
If the meadow is not taken.  
Ox’s fat calves,  
If the rake is not used to smooth.  
Ox’s bright haystacks,  
If the threshing is not started.  
Ox’s young maiden,  
If the boy has not touched her.  
A dear hand not tested,  
A young finger not demanded.  
When the boy’s hand  
Has touched the dear one:  
It immediately starts to yellow,  
Seeing the eyes wither.  
The knee begins to ail,  
Sleep plagued by sores,  
Dragging on with pain.  
"Village women, dear old women,  
Help understand,  
Give some advice:  
Last year I took a young wife,  
The year before, another half.  
I led her to the tender hayfield,  
Placed gold on the arch.  
She mowed hay, trapped in the forest.  
The willow bush snapped.  
It immediately started to yellow,  
Seeing the eyes wither.  
The knee begins to ail,  
Sleep plagued by sores,  
Dragging on with pain."  
The village women answer back:  
"Throw loosened seeds in the mire,  
Barley will lie straight,  
Rye in the threshing floor heap.  
A great feast awaits you,  
A great feast, a long feast—  
A long pauper comes to your household,  
And fills your house."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud abielust

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Why should a man let me sleep,  
Or even eat bread for me,  
Buy wild apples for me,  
Carry sugar sticks for me?  
Still, I cannot give to you,  
My little head does not turn its head.  
When my lover comes,  
Immediately I give to you,  
My little head turns its head,  
Without buying beer,  
Without eating bread,  
Without carrying sugar sticks,  
I will hold the beloved tightly.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Our temples, our neighbors,
Both our brothers.
Calls tenderly, calls dear.
Calls tenderly a boy,
Calls dear a girl.
Names (him) a wheat ear,
Complains of a barley seed.
Together we eat, together we drink,
Together we sleep at night;
We divide the fish between two,
Together we pinch a girl,
Together we pinch a maiden.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What do I hear in the poem  
In the poem, in the song;  
I have no fire to warm me,  
Here there are no comrades,  
Here no pairs in a flock.  
I have no father-in-law for anger  
I have no mother-in-law for anger;  
There is no husband for fear,  
My fiancé is in Peibes,  
My spouse far away,  
My father over the seas,  
Servants in Pohlamaa,  
Maids in Germany.  
May the wind quickly bring health  
May hail swiftly deliver messages.  
A shower of warm words.  
So many greetings to him,  
So many kisses between us,  
So many trees between us.  
True apple trees,  
White maples,  
Tall curly trees.  
There he swings in the hut,  
Bends in the corner with a bow,  
Under the ducks in a ribbon.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little boy, little lad,  
Why did you leave me behind  
Without blame, did you despise me?  
I was certainly beautiful  
I certainly looked nice  
I did not wear expensive clothes  
I did not tolerate a German boy,  
I did not fancy a nobleman  
My mind did not think of a lord  
My heart did not call for a coachman  
But I wanted a peasants’ son  
A peasants’ son is wise at work,  
Wise at work, a good match for the land,  
The ox is clever for plowing  
The mare for walking the harness.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The whore will come before me,  
The thief will look before me,  
The bitch laughs too much.  
I heard, I answered:  
A whore will get the wolf first,  
The bear will get two puppies,  
A bitch will suddenly come from a rabbit  
Before anything comes from me,  
Before anyone comes from a chicken.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If a girl dies,  
What grows on the maiden's grave?  
Marsh grasses, bog roots,  
German straight thistles,  
Garlic, little nails.  
These plants grow on the maiden's grave.  

If a boy dies,  
Where is he buried?  
In Kuuramaa under the spruce,  
In Pärnumaa under the fir,  
In Läänemaa under the alder.  
What grows on the boy's grave?  
Thistles and bindweeds,  
Wedlocks and heather branches,  
Junipers and shepherd's staffs.  
These grow on the boy's grave.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ann was a dark maiden.  
She sat on the Harju granary,  
On top of the Viru distillery.  
Silk ribbons in her lap,  
Shop ribbons at her waist,  
Precious ribbons on her shoulders,  
Weaving ribbons for the shop,  
Tying silk ribbons.  
She sews udulinas (a type of linen) there.  
Boys kneel and beg.  
Bowing without hats:  
Fall down, little Anne;  
Fall on my hat,  
Fall on my wide brimmed hat.  
Fall down, little darling  
On the grass, little picture  
To feed little onion  
On Hansu’s gray carpet,  
On Peeter’s long apron,  
Into Siim’s silk bed.  
Anne hears and answers:  
I won’t fall, little darling  
To feed little onion,  
Not on the earth, little picture.  
I sew udulinas,  
Weaving shop ribbons,  
Tying silk ribbons.  
Dear one, little brother,  
Take the gray one below,  
Kiss the ear, little ear:  
Then bring the reed rods,  
The grain racks with golden hair.  
Miska, take my golden chests,  
Carefully stacked gift boxes.  
Then carry the bundles to the cellar,  
Gift boxes to the granaries.  
Now I will fall, little darling  
To feed little onion  
On the earth, little picture.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
This maiden is in my name,  
A garland head in my favor.  
Who has twisted shoelaces,  
Pitch-black shoelaces.  
She walks past the field.  
Between the garden beds  
A bear in a barley twist.  
Head bowed, hair braided,  
Shoe heels inscribed.  
This suitor is in my name:  
Who plows down into the valley  
With gray oxen,  
Powerful verses,  
Red bulls,  
Blue bulls.  
Head bowed, hair braided,  
Shoe heels inscribed.  
The vest is striped on the back,  
The belt like a rainbow.  
This is my young man.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Come to me, my kisser,  
To the servant, the kisser.  
What is wrong with the servant woman,  
To eat bread like the sauna woman:  
Sits like a lady in the room,  
Creeps like a mistress into the pillows.  
Autumn does not beat the thigh,  
Spring does not take debt.  
What was after the marriage,  
I did not swim to the slave,  
I crawled to the hired boy,  
I ran straight to the servant.  
I belong to the slave,  
Suitable for the hired boy,  
A woman created for the servant.  
The slave bought me with barley grain,  
The foster son with oats,  
The servant with summer salary.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went to walk in the forest
On a Sunday holiday
During everyday times.
I found girls even in the forest,
I found girls in a group of four.
One there knitted a golden belt,
Another spun a twining thread,
The third one spun a cat’s paw pattern,
The fourth one cries for a young man.
This girl is called mine,
The wreath bearer is from me,
Who there cries for a young man.
Girls with delicate feet,
Delicate feet, little buttercups,
Beautiful like rosebuds,
German bluebells and onions.
They are created because of boys,
Because of boys, because of men,
Because of half-hearted dreams about boys,
Because of men’s melancholy.
That men cannot sleep,
Boys cannot lull themselves to sleep.
I saw the girls growing,
The wreath bearers bowing over,
Their hair rising like tin.
I saw the girls growing,
I took care of them,
Fed them pitch-black
Cured their iron sickness.
I fed, I watered
I carried water for the foal.
On the feast day I put hay on the table,
On the bundled sheaf I carried oats,
Then I gained what I was longing for,
On the Sunday to be awaited,
Waiting for everyday times.
I gained a bright visit,
From the wood grouse from the spruce forest;
The daughter’s mother spat.
I gained what I went for,
On Sundays, what I tried to get.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
All the gold goes home,  
All the berries fall asleep.  
Every man with his own,  
Every companion with his own hen.  
The brother goes with a young maiden,  
I go crying toward the corner,  
Toward the empty bed.  
I take to talking to the bedbug,  
Hitting the receiver with the louse.  
I turn over, find coldness;  
I turn my back, find a wall.  
The neighbor says: take a wife,  
But mine says: be without!  
If I get angry, where can I put it?  
You cannot sell the ox,  
Nor take the cow to the town.  
You cannot put the sheep into the barn,  
Nor give the goat away as a gift,  
You cannot trade the horse.  
My wife is too lazy,  
She hasn’t bothered to shear the wool,  
Only wanted to pick at the ticks.  
The wool decays in the basket,  
The ticks crawl on the fleece.  
The wool went to the villagers,  
The ticks ended up with the single women.  
That’s where my savings melt away,  
That’s where my assets vanish.  
Pieces of meat disappear,  
Loaves of bread run out,  
The serving trays paid off their debts,  
The flour sacks paid their dues,  
The oats' tails rattled.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
All the gold goes home,  
All the berries fall asleep.  
Every man with his own,  
Every companion with his own hen.  
The brother goes with a young maiden,  
I go crying toward the corner,  
Toward the empty bed.  
I take to talking to the bedbug,  
Hitting the receiver with the louse.  
I turn over, find coldness;  
I turn my back, find a wall.  
The neighbor says: take a wife,  
But mine says: be without!  
If I get angry, where can I put it?  
You cannot sell the ox,  
Nor take the cow to the town.  
You cannot put the sheep into the barn,  
Nor give the goat away as a gift,  
You cannot trade the horse.  
My wife is too lazy,  
She hasn’t bothered to shear the wool,  
Only wanted to pick at the ticks.  
The wool decays in the basket,  
The ticks crawl on the fleece.  
The wool went to the villagers,  
The ticks ended up with the single women.  
That’s where my savings melt away,  
That’s where my assets vanish.  
Pieces of meat disappear,  
Loaves of bread run out,  
The serving trays paid off their debts,  
The flour sacks paid their dues,  
The oats' tails rattled.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
We are like tender brothers,  
We are like sisters.  
We go like cheerful young men,  
We roll like nephews.  
Together we eat, together we drink,  
Together we buy without beer,  
We pay Margal with money,  
For the blanket, half a measure,  
But we do not take the same woman,  
One takes another woman.  
One takes another old woman's daughter,  
Another takes another old man's daughter.  
You get Kaie, I get Maie,  
Kai went to gather leaves,  
She threw her apron at the bakery bread,  
Tanu [threw] the beaten stick.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Ui, ui, ui, ui, in the town of Juuru
Today I visited the town of Tänn,
Tomorrow I will go to the Uni church.
The day after tomorrow to Toome town.
To see the maidens,
To listen to the braided necks,
To row the brooch chests.
This maiden by my name
My garland head from my wreath:
Who has twisted shoelaces
Snow-white walks.
Deep black around.
Wade-wade, boys of Toome,
Oh, you boys of Udeva!
The herding boys of Käru village,
Young men of Norra parish,
Boys from Udeva manor!
Big ones from Sõidla, stout ones from Paide,
Ahula straw horses.
Viru lads, Harju lads,
Kolu end’s bag boys:
Red boys from Järva,
Thin girls from Järva.
Järva women with small bumps.
Viru fleece, Harju locks;
The fine linen from Laijuse:
Bring it to our Kärtu
Kärtu swift to comb it,
Quick with the comb to churn the wool.
Summer makes the fleece, winter makes the locks,
Spring the beautiful hemp.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Ui, ui, ui, ui, in the town of Juuru
Today I visited the town of Tänn,
Tomorrow I will go to the Uni church.
The day after tomorrow to Toome town.
To see the maidens,
To listen to the braided necks,
To row the brooch chests.
This maiden by my name
My garland head from my wreath:
Who has twisted shoelaces
Snow-white walks.
Deep black around.
Wade-wade, boys of Toome,
Oh, you boys of Udeva!
The herding boys of Käru village,
Young men of Norra parish,
Boys from Udeva manor!
Big ones from Sõidla, stout ones from Paide,
Ahula straw horses.
Viru lads, Harju lads,
Kolu end’s bag boys:
Red boys from Järva,
Thin girls from Järva.
Järva women with small bumps.
Viru fleece, Harju locks;
The fine linen from Laijuse:
Bring it to our Kärtu
Kärtu swift to comb it,
Quick with the comb to churn the wool.
Summer makes the fleece, winter makes the locks,
Spring the beautiful hemp.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ena girls, from the village  
Which is also in Tapa parish  
Walk on the church road,  
Birds made of aspen leaves  
Spruces on their backs made of spruce bark  
Scarves made of birch bark,  
Rowan berry headscarves.  
Farewell, brides of Tapa!  
I have Muskus brides  
Their dresses rustle on their backs,  
Coins jingle on their necks.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Girls, little birds,  
Cornflowers, beauties,  
Maidens with tin hair:  
Do you know where you will go,  
Where you will be taken in the end?  
Some will end up among pigs,  
Some will go among horses,  
Some will roll among cattle,  
Some will find a place with bread,  
Some will get a place with rolls,  
Some will go for the sprouts,  
Some will go to the mountains,  
Finding a place with bread,  
But getting a place with rolls; –  
Some got under a dry spruce,  
Some got under a wide alder,  
Some got under a dear birch,  
Some got under a grey aspen,  
Some got under a wet pine.  
Who got under a dear birch,  
Got a young man;  
Who got under a wide alder,  
Got a widower;  
Who got under a dry spruce,  
Got a hermit;  
Who got under a grey aspen,  
Got an old man;  
Who got under a wet pine,  
Got a drunkard.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
We men like forest bulls,  
Wise Jaani’s gray bulls,  
The famous threshing oxen of the wilderness:  
These were the tough ones to fight,  
To scatter the manor’s boys,  
To shake up the parish thieves!

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What is a nap, therefore,
That I sing near the village,
Near the moon I hum,
Against the white little towers.
There listens the king's daughter,
Watches the old lord's daughter,
Hears the golden cuckoo
Of the silver little bird:
When came here to crow,
To shine in our forest,
To sing behind the grove,
To crow in our forest,
The meadow's bell,
The field's thunder.
The village said: cuckoo, cuckoo,
The village children: sing, sing.
One who cares, two who fears:
One who cares for singing
Two who fears for crying out,
I have no moon to hear,
No shelter to hide.
My moon far away,
A good man over the seas,
There he swings in his cradle,
Bends in the corner with his bow.
May the wind carry greetings to him,
May the rains send messages.
So many greetings to him:
As many as footsteps in the snow,
As many as trees in the bush,
Of crooked ones, the true maid,
Of hazel, the straight maid.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I dreamed on Mari Hill  
In the grove of Linden trees,  
Among the Meadowsweet bushes,  
In the middle of the Buttercup flowers,  
I dreamed as I was sleeping:  
Before the fig tree,  
Behind the golden tree,  
The downy birch was in front of the barn,  
The spruce stood in tall thickets,  
The apple tree was large in the yard,  
The rowan tree was large in front of the sauna.  
The village old women, the little grandmothers,  
The old ladies of the parish  
Understood my dream;  
In the fig tree, you have young daughters,  
In the fig tree, you drank spirits,  
In the golden tree, you went courting,  
Your spruce has big spruce curls,  
Your apple tree has great luck,  
Your rowan tree has a large sauna mistress. -

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
When I went to courtship,
I went before the gray barn
Before the ash stove
Before the flax barn,
I went inside to look:
The wind was missing a whistle
Fleas walked with straws in their mouths
The louse went to the corner, the cap was on,
The stove was split into logs,
The room swept into corners,
The water bucket was shiny
The cellar ears were swept.
The girl dozed on a chair
The old woman by the stove.
There I was offered food,
I was offered a small piece,
A crumb of bread was given.
The mouth ate, the heart worried:
Where can I find those maidens
Those good-crowned girls
With smooth hair ends.
I go outside to look,
I chase before the gray barn
Before the ash stove
Before the flax barn
I go to another household.
I chase before the gray barn
Before the ash stove
Before the flax barn
I go inside to look;
The wind was ready to whistle,
The stove was already heated
The room was cleanly swept
The bucket was well cleaned
The cellar ears were swept
The girl spins, the spindle hums
The old woman spins, the spindle buzzes
There I was given food
I was offered a small piece
A crumb of bread was given
The mouth ate, the eyes watched:
Here I can find those maidens
And those good-crowned girls
With smooth hair ends
With neat flax bundles.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The young men of our village  
Go to the midsummer church  
A horse underneath like a star  
Themselves on top like the sun,  
A hat like a moonhouse  
A belt like a rainbow.  
The young men of another village  
Go to the midsummer church:  
A horse underneath has turned gray  
The tail bitten by sawflies  
Themselves on top weathered.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Girls, little birds,  
Locks of hair, little tufts,  
Little bald heads, smooth ones,  
Little wreathed heads, beautiful ones,  
Do you know where you will end up  
Where you will be taken at last?  
Some will get a dry spruce forest  
Some a rare aspen forest  
Some a wet pine forest  
Some a dear birch forest  
Some a wise oak forest  
Some a flying alder forest.  
Whoever gets a dry spruce forest  
Will end up with a bad man,  
Whoever gets a wet pine forest  
Will end up with a drinking man,  
Whoever gets a dear birch forest  
Will end up with dear waistbands,  
Whoever gets a wise oak forest  
Will end up managing a farm,  
Whoever gets a rare aspen forest  
Will end up with a bad man,  
Whoever gets a flying alder forest  
Will end up begging for bread.  
Girls, little birds,  
Locks of hair, little tufts,  
Pray to your God  
That you don’t run to a drinker,  
Don’t go to a tavern-goer,  
The drinker drinks to the last drop,  
The spruce drinker drinks at the Spruce tavern,  
The birch drinker drinks at the Birch tavern.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Be proper, our girls  
Be proper, go high,  
Surely they were led proper  
When gathering the heights  
They were led proper because of the way  
The heights because of the beautiful song  
The daughters were led because of work  
They went to the suitors because of their hands  
They were taken for marriage because of their finger,  
Not because of the father’s stallion  
Not because of the brother’s cattle  
Because of the mother-in-law’s good beauty  
Because of the father-in-law’s good lands  
When I was once taken to the suitors  
The horses stayed in father’s yard  
The calves stayed in brother’s yard  
The herd stayed in brother’s pasture  
I began to leave  
I got straw bands from the yard  
I got small switches from the gate  
I even got these secretly  
To sneak over the fence  
To slip through the fence  
Father called me stingy  
Mother called me premature  
The servant called me buttery  
The children called me table sweeper  
I got to live, I got to be,  
Wait, wait, kin, bad kin  
Let my room be made  
A new cabin is being knocked down  
New house being painted  
Then father comes with adornments  
Mother with premature ones  
Then I will be able to support the family  
To take a wife, to have children  
To encourage acquaintances  
To sweep away misfortunes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:

To take a wife, to have children  
To encourage acquaintances  
To sweep away misfortunes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If I had a man,  
If I had a husband with a chicken,  
Riding along the road,  
Galloping along the path,  
A horse beneath like the oven,  
A man above like the day,  
A hat like a church bell,  
A belt like a city’s flag ribbon.  
If that man does not want me:  
Let it be, let it be, I curse,  
Let his horse die  
And the man with it to fade away,  
The hat on his head to wither,  
The belt on his waist to rust.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
There are men by me,
There are boys in the village,
Walking between the village:
They keep socks at their waist,
A piece of rope on their hat,
Pipes smoking in their mouth,
Wall pillars inside,
They don’t know how to make tobacco,
Nor to hit the milking pail.
Look here, look here, the boys of Kuru:
Walking between the village,
A purple ribbon on their hat
And beautiful belts at their waist.
They all know how to make tobacco
And to hit the milking pail.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Father argued with the rich,
Mother with the gold carrier,
Sisters with the silver harvester,
Brothers with the copper scale bearer.
Neither did it go to the rich,
Nor to the gold carrier,
Nor to the silver harvester,
Nor to the copper scale bearer.
The rich argue with words:
"Don’t wear me out with gold,
Don’t exhaust me with silver,
Don’t burden me with copper."
To the poor I adorned myself,
I pulled sleeves for the farm boy,
For the plowman, the sower,
Lord, help the beginner,
The one who sweeps the German hearth,
The one who turns the black soil.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ille-prille the sprayer,  
From sprayer became a thong wearer,  
From thong wearer became a thigh-warden,  
From thigh-warden became a caretaker,  
From caretaker became a rainbow,  
From rainbow became a chapel,  
From chapel became a beautiful forest.  
What is done in that forest?  
In the forest honey is drunk,  
Moonshine is poured,  
We hold a wedding feast.  
One was Intu, another Antu,  
The third was suitor Nigula,  
The fourth unworthy of a girl,  
The fifth was Viileke the servant,  
The sixth a rough boy,  
The seventh a soldier's son,  
The eighth a fisherman's son,  
The ninth was Hüide-Tüide,  
The tenth was a cooper in the village.  
Then came to listen for gold,  
A silver-marked little bird,  
Silver-marked, blue-striped,  
With a golden tuft on its head.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ille-prille the sprayer,  
From sprayer became a thong wearer,  
From thong wearer became a thigh-warden,

One was Intu, another Antu,  
The third was suitor Nigula,  
The fourth unworthy of a girl,  
The fifth was Viileke the servant,  
The sixth a rough boy,  
The seventh a soldier's son,  
The eighth a fisherman's son,  
The ninth was Hüide-Tüide,  
The tenth was a cooper in the village.  
Then came to listen for gold,  
A silver-marked little bird,  
Silver-marked, blue-striped,  
With a golden tuft on its head.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What am I to be,
Or what do I fear to shout,
Mine is not here,
My spouse does not grow here,
Mine is in Holland,
Truly mine in Pärnumaa,
Let the wind carry greetings to him,
Let the cloud be long and faithful to him,
If he is healthy, let him work,
If he is sick, may God help.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went from village to village,  
And from farm to farm,  
To watch the maiden,  
Whether her beauty would give a sign  
Or her beloved would carry the chair;  
Neither did her beauty give a sign  
Nor did her beloved carry the chair.  
Then I watched from the shadows,  
Standing by the wall,  
How she madly kneaded the bread,  
Blindly throwing flour;  
Her hands went clump-clump,  
Her hair strands here and there,  
Her eyes went all over the place.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Give me a beautiful young maiden,  
Give me a beautiful neck scarf,  
Sitting on Harju's granary,  
On Viru's distillery,  
On the roof of the cattle shed,  
Silk ribbons in her lap,  
Precious ribbons around my neck,  
Pink ribbons on my blouse.  
Boys kneel and beg,  
Bow without hats:  
"Fall down, little darling  
To the silk, little onion,  
To the earth, little painting.  
Fall on my hat,  
On the gray shoulder of the sleeve,  
On Peter's long beauty  
On the silk bed of the harness.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My dear little brother,
If you are going to take a wife,
You should try to get a crown,
You should row a little boat,
Do not take a wife from the manor,
In the manor there are some
Beautiful ones below the windows,
Red ones on the roof,
They praise the stone chimney,
The master's wedding chambers,
Do not know how to come to the fire.
Set the bed without sparks.
Milk the cow without a chandelier,
Cook the broth without bitterness.
Do not take a wife from the tavern,
All are proud in the tavern;
All are pretty on the church road.
Look at a woman at the yarn wheel,
Aim at a woman at the sickle,
Try a woman at the weaving bench,
The sickle woman is smooth,
The yarn woman is nimble,
The one at the weaving bench is beautiful.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sisters, dear ones,  
Let’s join together, the three of us,  
Let’s leave this land,  
Let’s go to Finland for the summer,  
For the winter to Tähkvere,  
We’ll build a stone church there,  
A chapel made of birch leaves,  
We’ll set iron crosses inside,  
Delicate silver grilles.  
Let me say it again:  
When we leave,  
Who will then do the work?  
The girls will do the work,  
Elm-necked girls will mow the hay,  
Flax-necked girls will make stacks,  
The stacks will be seen in Courland,  
Large flocks to Germany,  
Half-grown ones to Alutaguse.  
We will still leave this land,  
If the maiden is left hungry at home,  
The maiden hungry, the maid missing.  
Then they will look for the horse,  
Ride on the slopes,  
Listen with golden-hooved ears,  
Look with copper-hooved eyes,  
Then the fences on the hillsides will spark fire,  
Sparks fly on the slopes,  
The foal’s hooves flash lightning,  
The maiden to the forest of the herd,  
To the path of courtship.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sisters, dear ones,

Let’s leave this land,

Sparks fly on the slopes,  
The foal’s hooves flash lightning,  
The maiden to the forest of the herd,  
To the path of courtship.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My dear little brother,
If you are to take a wife,
Take me with you,
I do feel a dull girl,
And I see a sharp-nosed girl.
I feel the dull one even in sleep,
I feel the lazy one even in the cleaning,
The hazel belt is tight.
The dull girl has long hair,
The lazy girl has wide brush,
The hazel belt is loose.
Do not look at the fair ones,
Do not seek the proud mothers,
Do not aim for the upright ones,
The fair one watches for shadows,
The upright one smooths the finger band,
The proud one wanders the streets,
Take from the top slave children,
From the top the slave line follows.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
No snapping, no cracking,  
No pulling from the heart,  
But ashes are lifted from the fire,  
Made from leaf-lists,  
The bridegroom’s foot is washed,  
White shirts are chosen,  
The bridegroom’s jewelry is kept,  
Tomorrow to go home,  
Some men watch closely,  
Some look out the window,  
Some scrub the wickets,  
Some smooth the boot doors,  
For that young maiden,  
For that big journey.  
Wait, let me say it again,  
I’ll say it correctly;  
It’s not possible for my black horse to run otherwise,  
To load up on rides,  
To carry stones,  
Before those maidens,  
Half of the country’s brides,  
What is kept halfway,  
Loved prematurely,  
Overplayed,  
Dragged away from the lock by the lock-keeper.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
It was said and said,
It was thought and thought,
I was called a whore to the gentleman,
A sleeper at the young lord's,
To be toyed with by the manor lords,
To be teased by the farmboys.
I have never been a whore to the gentleman,
A sleeper at the young lord's,
To be toyed with by the manor lords,
To be teased by the farmboys.
I have not had a son as long as my knee.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
They said to me and said  
They thought and thought  
That my father-in-law is quick-tempered  
My mother-in-law is sharp-tongued  
A bit angry sometimes  
Speaks with a harsh voice  
What is it with that, and why  
That my father-in-law is quick-tempered  
My mother-in-law is sharp-tongued  
A bit angry sometimes  
Speaks with a harsh voice  
Better, better, little old woman  
Better to go to the neighbors’ village  
And that little anger.  
I will plow for the neighbors  
Father-in-law will harrow  
Brother-in-law will grind at the mill  
Anger sticks there  
Looking for a fight to argue  
To change the path of enmity

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
They said to me and said  
They thought and thought  
That my father-in-law is quick-tempered  
My mother-in-law is sharp-tongued  
A bit angry sometimes  
Speaks with a harsh voice  
What is it with that, and why  
That my father-in-law is quick-tempered  
My mother-in-law is sharp-tongued  
A bit angry sometimes  
Speaks with a harsh voice  
Better, better, little old woman  
Better to go to the neighbors’ village  
And that little anger.  
I will plow for the neighbors  
Father-in-law will harrow  
Brother-in-law will grind at the mill  
Anger sticks there  
Looking for a fight to argue  
To change the path of enmity

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Young men, tender brothers  
Why do you laugh at the maiden  
And why do you disdain the bride  
Here you choose white heads  
Pipes in your mouths and stooped backs  
Beards like oven jaws  
You go to Virus to quarrel  
In the district to see the doctor.  
In Järva to fix things  
A piece of rope on the hat  
A piece of rein over the shoulder  
Rooster’s dung in your purse  
Chicken dung in your wallet  
Goose dung under your shoulder  
Duck dung on top of your stomach  
If I had ten daughters at home  
There would be work for the daughters  
I would put five to card wool  
Two to weave fabric  
Three to spin flax  
There is work indeed for the daughters.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little boy, young lad,  
When you go to courtship  
To meet the maiden,  
Put on a silk shirt,  
A silk shirt, a golden hood,  
Blue ribbons on your shoulder,  
Invite me to the sheepfold.  
Go to the church road,  
All the high ones go on the church road,  
The fools go among other people,  
Follow the path behind the scythe's blade,  
Go to the hay arch,  
Watch from the corner of the barn,  
Then you will get a wife who is broad-shouldered,  
With a cross on her chest,  
She knows how to manage the household,  
She makes the bed without sparks,  
She cooks broth without bitterness,  
She milks the cow without a chandelier,  
But she knows how to handle  
To hit the children softly,  
To make the family neat,  
She weaves patterns into fabrics,  
Diamond shapes into rings,  
Ram horns on the outer edges,  
Calf horns in the middle,  
Goat horns in the center,  
Hoof horns at the back end.  
If you don’t go to the church road,  
She doesn’t know how to manage,  
She just goes to bed,  
Places her feet against the wall,  
Her bare feet on the table.  
Oh, my dear mutual companion,  
Help me to the oven,  
Lift me onto the stove.  
From there I roll like a log,  
A half-filled bottle of beer.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What is the poor child’s wage?  
From the forest, he is taken as a husband  
From the stump, he takes roots with him  
Who are his wedding guests?  
White birches as bridesmaids.  
Linden leaves as broth ladles,  
The fox as the chowder maker,  
The hare as the fast bridegroom,  
The fodder is large dining tables,  
The garden bed a bench to sit on,  
The wolf is the wedding mayor,  
The bear the dear carriage driver.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I must not go away  
I must not leave the people’s place  
From better places  
There I leave this place  
There I forget this burden  
Let better ones come to the place  
Honest ones instead  
More rightful to the spot  
I will come back tomorrow  
Maybe I will come to see  
If there is a better one for the place  
Instead of an honest one  
Still worse for the place  
Instead of a better one  
Next is the place where I left  
The bench was there where I paused  
The stove wants to be chopped  
The room wants to be swept  
The bucket wants to be scrubbed  
The cauldron wants to be scrubbed clean  
The broom shakes the dust off  
The room remained empty after the daughter left  
The barn became sparse after the clothes left  
The chamber decorated with woven fabrics  
Surely she thought of some men  
Surely she wanted some boys  
There is no money to count  
Afraid of the money purse wearing out  
The wallet falling apart  
I do not lack fooling around  
Done with linen thread  
Ravens to be sewn in  
Crows to be shooed away  
The kerchief tied tight  
Village women laughed at me  
Village girls mocked  
Said I sleep in the barn  
Lying among the boys  
They themselves slept in the barn  
Lying among the boys  
No woman laughed at me beautiful  
The girl teased playfully  
But the lazy woman laughed at me  
The girl teased badly  
They said I would remain without a husband  
Living without a partner  
I got a husband like a honey tree  
Got another like a German from Saaremaa  
I took him like a master's young man  
My groom was from Lake Peipus  
Dear man from Alutaguse  
Drinks from the lake's shore  
Drowns across the lake  
Dances beyond the lake  
Rode over the Finnish bridge  
Finnish bridge creaked  
The bottom beam cracked  
The hat flapped gently  
Village maidens watched  
If only that man were mine  
If only he were my partner far away  
Then he would ride along the road  
Gallop along the paths  
Horse below like the stove  
Head above like the moon  
Hat like the church bell  
Belt like the city flag ribbon  
Then he passed by, turned his head  
Lifted a nice hat  
Farewell, little maiden

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, you proud young men  
Pride on a new face  
Height on your hat  
Early on the pipe stem  
What are you aiming at, little Siim  
This is not yours to have  
Here in the herd is your Canada  
Here is yours to have  
In the herd is your little companion  
The sheep sings the other half

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I would like to marry a man  
Whom I would marry madly  
Without growing attachments  
A wife does not make one grow  
A man does not lose his mind  
A wife asks for cloth  
A man demands fine clothes  
Every month he needs new trousers  
Each week he needs four shirts  
Two neckerchiefs  
Morning shirts fuzzy  
Afternoon shirts canvas-like  
Evening ones thin as gauze  
If there is no man's clothing  
Nor wife's cloth  
Then you will feel the belt around your waist  
And the taste of fat on the pan.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, you deceitful little boy  
You are cunning, dear companion  
Come to me and hurry  
Come to me, little maiden  
I will put Hõlbu to work  
For a long time I will put Kuube to mend  
For days I will seek the head  
For a year I will take his place  
I won’t go to make a fire  
Nor cut down willows  
To tie the cold cubu (bundle)  
Come to me, little maiden  
Come to me and hurry  
I have a villager as a servant  
Such is the village maiden  
Come to me, little maiden  
Come to me and hurry  
In our yard grows an apple tree  
The stack is long from Linus (flax)  
From Karjalaadad (market fairs) from Kangaste (cloth)  
Let the Saturday evening come  
The Sunday holiday  
Then take the ladder and set it straight  
Go outside to the apple tree  
To the sweet berry tree  
Come to me, little maiden  
Come to me and hurry  
I have a villager as a servant  
And such is the village maiden  
From there you can eat, from there you can sell  
From there you can send to Saaremaa  
To Lahedaste and Läänemaa  
To take to the Russian road  
There are surely caretakers enough  
There are surely guards of wealth  
Küdi sits on the village  
And Nad on the salve (shelf) of the lake  
The fire without a lock  
Water in the well without a key  
That is food for me in the village  
That is drink for me in the village.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, you boys, button eyes  
Button eyes, ring collars  
Go to the wall to sleep  
To hang on the stove  
Put their pants under their heads  
Pant straps as pillows  
Pant belts as clothes  
Pant buttons as a young man  
Oh men, what do we have  
Oh boys, what in the village  
Men like Viru crows  
Just like Harju magpies  
They go to Viru to thresh  
To Harju to treat navels  
In Järva to set things right  
A bit of a rein for their belt  
A piece of rope on their hat  
Handcuffs as a neckerchief

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Now the horses start playing,
By the rose to steal,
Came four young men,
One said, fear, fear,
Another said, love, love,
The third offered rings,
Offered rings from finger to finger,
Others had other hands,
The third had a golden thumb,
The fourth a copper toe.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Young men, tender brothers  
Mother, where did they make the swing  
They made the swing at Karjasmaa  
At Karjasmaa, Marjamäe  
And they made the swing narrow  
The goose can’t swim underneath  
The baby can't crawl over the top  
The little silkworm can't get through inside  
The Swedish sprat can't get between  
Girls rocked on the swing  
Girls rocked, the wreaths gleamed  
Men watched from under the swing  
From under the swing, from above the swing  
If there were guns, they would catch the girls  
If there were nets, they would take the girls  
If there were seines, they would scoop the girls up  
The girls heard and immediately called out  
The wolf is caught by the gun  
Fish are taken by the net  
Crayfish are scooped by the seine  
A thousand is the price of a girl’s troop  
A hundred is the price of a girl’s ale

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, we two paupers  
Set up a wedding room for ourselves  
We will have the wedding out in the field  
By the garden's edge, on the snowdrift  
The feeding corner is the dining table  
The field bed is the bench to sit on  
The joy straw is my spoon  
The village street is the dance circle  
The sky's edge is my bagpipe  
The rainbow is my violin playing.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Our men like wild bulls  
Aru Jüri's gray bulls  
Kure Jüri's curved horns  
Men like the village's worn-out oxen  
The mountain manor's black oxen  
The brown manor's red oxen  
Vearuka's blood-red  
They come bellowing and lowing  
Mooing down into the swamp

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Before me, I trust the worm's tongue  
The worm's tongue, the worm's mind  
Before the young boy's tongue  
The boy's tongue, the boy's mind  
The boy is born a deceiver  
A deceiver and a catcher  
Fingers weak to touch  
He deceived the orphan  
Deceived the young girl of the family  
Promised to buy a new hat  
A new hat, a proud lace  
Proud lace, a beautiful ribbon  
She lived for a year  
A couple of days short of a year  
The swamp brought those warm rods  
The willow brought those burning rods  
He beat her back blue  
And bent her neck twice  
This is for your new hat  
New hat, proud lace  
Proud lace, beautiful ribbon.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
We share one meal  
We eat the meal, we drink the drink  
We buy one horse  
Margu pays the money  
One cannot take the other's wife  
The other takes another wife  
Then we are in-laws  
The in-law eats and the in-law drinks  
Behind the sleeve of a coat  
Under a hundred-year-old tail  
We are each other’s kin.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What do I care about the flower cluster  
Or what do I fear by shouting  
Mine is not here  
My companion does not grow here  
Mine is in Holland  
My companion is in dear Viljandi.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If only I hadn’t turned black, I could have run  
If only I hadn’t turned gray, I could have wandered through the garden  
The boat became the mast’s leaner.  
Before sailing the maidens  
Who were half-kept  
Loved too early  
Overplayed  
Danced beyond measure  
Beneath, I broke gray iron  
I shattered black hooves  
I wore down bridles on the ears  
I bought oil for the whore  
Too much white for the slut  
And also redness for the prostitute  
Now I cry for young men  
I turn my side, find coldness  
I turn the sail, find my back.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:
Be healthy, young maiden  
Be healthy growing up  
Protecting your honor  
Now you may ride the sled.  
Men would leap about  
If a man had gone into the forest  
From the tree top to the boys too  
From the stump to the companions.  
The trees would be bridesmaids  
The white birches as bridesmaids  
A bite for your feeding table  
Heather for your haystack  
A garden bed for a bench.  
Now you have been courted as a companion.  
Now the sled has ridden  
Whip lashes, tail lashes  
Rode under father-in-law’s yard  
Rode under mother-in-law’s yard  
Come mother-in-law, bring from the fur coat  
Come perhaps to look  
Take away the angry one  
Take away the quarrelsome one  
Bring him as the devil to the house  
In mother-in-law’s eye, in mother-in-law’s ear  
In the keyhole, in the dog's ear  
Crooked foot before the mother-in-law  
Move now move, little flame  
Give space, little oven  
Step back, back wall  
Now comes the drummer to the house  
A clatter crashing down,  
When I storm and get angry  
I take the ladle from the dog’s possession  
Bend the broth bowl flat  
I give the ladle to the old woman’s hand  
The broth bowl to the old woman’s lap.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went to the Koiki suitor's gathering  
To the daughter of the Koigi bailiff  
In front of Aasa Hall's barn  
In front of the flax market  
In front of the red door.  
I went inside to have a look.  
A chair was brought for me.  
There were five at the table  
Three were doing bad work  
The daughter was dozing on the chair  
The old woman was watching the stove  
The old man was wandering by the oven  
Lice were crawling, straws in their mouths  
Fleas were in the corner, swatting with a broom  
Here there would be scooping with a bucket  
Here there would be working on the roof  
Here there would be raking with a rake.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sisters, dear ones  
Let's join together as three  
Let's hold counsel as four  
Let's leave this land  
Let's go to Saaremaa for the summer  
For half the summer to Pohla land  
For the winter behind the seas  
There is no hay to be mown  
The flame catches the flame  
The cuckoo calls on the heap  
Let's make a tin city there  
Pour a copper chapel  
Put a ceiling made of burbot  
A tall tower of rowan  
Ourselves a doll on the corner  
Ourselves a flag on the town  
Ourselves a bell at midday  
Boys down to beg  
Without hats, without gloves  
Without a silver belt, without a brooch  
Now fall, from the apple, from the apple tree  
Fall, berry, from the berry bush  
Fall down, golden doll  
Let down, singing swan  
Don't fall on the stone  
On the stone, on the stump  
Fall on my hat  
Roll onto my vest  
Let it fall on my wide ribbon  
I will take the hood into the room  
In my lap on a silk bed  
You will grow in the room  
Grow up on the silk bed  
Swell between the pillows.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, my dear little grandmother  
Little honeyberry granny  
Buy a golden dog for the yard  
A copper kettle for the gate  
Let the men pass by  
Suitors come by  
My daughter, a little dill  
A plant of a wise farm  
She is not suitable to come as a bride.  
A donkey in front of the gray barn  
A donkey in front of the shed  
A polecat in front of the room  
They call me inside  
I was given a chair  
I was called to the barn  
I was given food  
Hello father-in-law and hello mother-in-law  
Is she suitable to come as a bride here?  
She is not suitable to come as a bride here.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Dear little brother,  
Understand my dream,  
What did I see in the dream?  
The fig tree in our yard,  
The yellow tree in front of the house,  
The willow was on the sauna path,  
The birch was in the chamber room,  
The alder was on the wide, barn path.  
I understand the dream  
On the fig tree they would drink brandy,  
Suitors are walking on the yellow tree,  
On the willow they would eat bread,  
The birch is a lover,  
The alder is a child reconciler.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Who is riding along the road here  
Trotting along the path  
A horse with a silver harness in front  
A wagon made of copper behind  
The wheel shakes coins  
The spokes clink ruble pieces  
Coins on the rim.  
The horse in front is like the oven  
Itself on top like the day  
The hat like the moon's servant  
The belt like a rain rainbow  
The master is the little master  
That one is riding along the road  
Trotting along the paths.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little sister, Annekene  
Listen, dear Marikene  
Let's leave this land  
Let's go to Finland for the summer  
For half the summer to the land of berries  
For the winter to the streets of Tallinn  
When a girl comes here hungry  
Hungry girl, missing a maiden,  
No one among the girls to be found.  
If they look for me on horseback  
Riding on a windswept path  
Watching with foal’s hoofsteps  
Hooves strike the fire with sparks on their backs  
Sparks fly from the hooves of the foal  
When searching for a group of girls  
When gathering a flock of maidens.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little sister, Annekene  
Listen, dear Marikene  
Let's leave this land  
Let's go to Finland for the summer  
For half the summer to the land of berries  
For the winter to the streets of Tallinn  
When a girl comes here hungry  
Hungry girl, missing a maiden,  
No one among the girls to be found.  
If they look for me on horseback  
Riding on a windswept path  
Watching with foal’s hoofsteps  
Hooves strike the fire with sparks on their backs  
Sparks fly from the hooves of the foal  
When searching for a group of girls  
When gathering a flock of maidens.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My daughter-in-law is recognized  
By coming through the threshing floor  
By greening through time  
By wandering through the fenced pasture  
Wearing slippers on her toes  
Her hair was tied behind the neck  
Let the mute not come to us  
Let the rascals not come to us  
Let the fools not rush  
Let the sick not hurry  
Let not come as my daughter-in-law  
Someone who pulls stones from dung  
Brews weak beer  
Cooks broth without bitterness  
Milks the cow without a churn.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I lull the nights, hide the birds  
I wander the lifeless waters  
The birds are in the cherry chamber  
The Sabbath evening comes  
The Sunday little feast  
I have no girls to take  
Nor companions to hold.  
The old man goes to sleep with the old woman  
The sister with her husband  
The brother goes with a young maiden.  
With whom, poor me, should I go?  
I go crying toward the corner  
Toward the empty bed  
I rattle old fur coats  
I put pants under my head  
Pants buttons become young girls  
Pants strings become pillows  
Pants straps become clothes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Last year I took a young wife
I fear young weeping mothers,
Beautiful longing mothers.
I try to bring her inside,
Putting a bow on the goose
Hands as a hair drawer
A bow as an eye shooter
I put the mother-in-law’s eyes away
I put the father-in-law’s eyes away
Now the unrest walks
The fire sits on the gold
The throat on the edge of the scabbard:
Thought it out even for the parish,
For the district as well.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I fed the gray, I gave drink to the gray  
I went to the gray riding grounds  
Night with pain, moon with pain  
Under the sky full of stars  
A girl came towards me  
Towards the girl, a maiden in a group  
I asked the maidens:  
Are these maidens for sale?  
Are they traded far away?  
The maiden heard and answered:  
Oh, you foolish man  
Oh, you crazy young man  
Outside there is horse merchandise  
In the street there is rooster merchandise  
Foal merchandise by the neighbor  
Maid merchandise on the bench  
Under the elm there is whore merchandise  
Prostitute merchandise in the barn as well.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
May was a sweet young maiden  
She called the boys to the spruce grove  
Down to the marsh  
Down to the pine forest  
The porridge pot was under the apron  
The flagon in the beer house  
She said in the forest to the honey pot  
Under the hornbeam, a cake  
Under the root is drinkable vodka  
She went to the forest, made deeds  
She raised her white tail  
Showed her black navel  
Can you run straight  
At my black mark?

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ellakene, little brother,  
Peiukene, little boy,  
If you want to marry a woman  
To catch a sparrow  
To catch a little bird  
Then come and tell me.  
But I know a quiet girl,  
A tall girl I know,  
The quiet girl has long hair,  
The lazy girl has a wide braid,  
The virsakal (plump girl) has a loose belt,  
The närsak (thin girl) has a crooked nose.  
Don’t take a farm daughter,  
A slender farm daughter,  
She wants to take butter  
And wants to mess with eggs,  
She won’t even cook pork  
She doesn’t bother to make butter,  
She won’t tap cream into a bowl,  
The witches said to do magic,  
And those sprites would carry the cream,  
The witches never did magic,  
Nor did those sprites carry the cream.  
The throat was like a churn, the tongue like pine,  
The pan was expensive for roasting.  
She herself is fluent in song  
And steady in dance,  
Good for my work,  
When she hears the sound of an instrument,  
Then she wants to dance,  
When she hears the sound of spinning,  
Then she lets the spindle fly.  
To live in a hay cellar,  
To rest in a boy’s lap,  
It’s good to sleep on a mattress,  
Good to live in a hay cellar,  
Good to rest in a boy’s lap.  
When the girl goes to the hayfield,  
She puts her foot on the wall,  
The village boy comes clucking-clucking,  
Kükakille, küürakille,  
Kükakille from the village street,  
From Perseli farm’s gate,  
To the edge of the road,  
Always toward that farm daughter.  
Take enemy servants from the parish,  
Trace them down one by one,  
They will manage the work,  
They will weave the fabric.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Call in response, little sacred grove  
Sing in response, little grove  
To my good voice in response  
To the deeper throat in response  
If before it greeted well  
It did not hide the instrument sounds  
Concealed the German horn sounds  
The buzz of the kannel was muffled  
We also had a wolf  
We also had a flock  
It moved like a village herd  
It wandered like a swarm of gnats  
Rolled like a herd of cattle  
Now the herd has ended  
And the flock has disappeared  
Have then taken the boys of Viru  
Loved the Hanses of Harju  
Courted the carriage drivers of Kuie  
If taken, take further  
If gotten, get further  
We still have the Kaids growing  
The Leenus gliding over them  
We still have the Maids' berry wreaths  
We still have the Kaids' juniper wreaths  
The gifts remain in the gray spruces  
The Leenus remain in the flax locks.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little boy, little lad  
Why did you come to us last year  
When you were fetching orders  
Mouth messages were sent  
Five books were carried  
Six letters were written  
Now you come to an empty chamber  
To a light hallway  
Spiky mountain barleys  
Oat grains covered in dew  
Rye has grown through the weeds  
Wheat is full of pests  
Now you comb the chaff from the clothes  
You touch the collar folds  
Here no one offers food  
No piece is offered  
No crumb is spared  
No mouthful is desired  
Do not be the bringer of death  
The lost fishers  
The bread messengers who have gone away  
If dead, let them die  
If lost, let them be lost  
One cannot die along with death  
Disappear along with disappearance  
No one follows where no one goes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If I were a man  
If I were a husband to a henhouse  
That drives along the road  
Clattering along the track  
Like a horse under the oven  
Like a man over the fire  
A hat like a church bell  
A belt like the town flag ribbon  
Stepping down, stumbling  
Boot irons clattering  
If I were Olev  
I would wear the Kalev  
I would carry a long sword  
I would fear touching heels  
Reaching the door  
Getting to the flock.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, you little deceitful boy,  
You deceived the fine girl of the family,  
The village's cold word,  
Into the house of carelessness.  
They don’t say he is a father,  
They don’t say she is a mother,  
They don’t say there is a brother nearby,  
What is yours is lost there,  
Left there as your own loss.  
There was a father, there was a mother,  
There was a guiding little brother,  
Two pretty sisters as well.  
Küdi like a crafty fox,  
Nadu like a cunning little goblin,  
Küdi goes out to plow the field,  
Nadu is tinkering indoors,  
Looking for quarrels to start,  
From petty quarrels.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
I slept in Mari's meadow  
Among the bluebells  
Near the red willow by the store  
I saw a dream while sleeping  
I went home crying  
Grandmother came to meet me, grandfather came to meet me  
Why are you crying, young daughter?  
Why not cry, dear grandmother?  
Why not cry, dear grandfather?  
I slept in Mari's meadow  
Among the bluebells  
Near the red willow by the store  
I saw a dream while sleeping  
The fig tree was in front of the room  
The yellow tree was behind the room  
The cypress tree was even in the sauna  
The apple tree was also in the yard  
The birch was in the chamber  
The alder was by the hearth  
Don’t cry, young daughter  
This dream is already meant  
For the middle of the night  
The fig tree represents spirits  
The yellow tree represents a suitor’s visit  
The cypress tree represents eating cake  
The apple tree represents a wedding  
The birch represents a co-wife  
The alder represents a soup stirrer

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Here there is no such man,  
No such man in the shire either,  
Not even in the parish:  
Who feeds a large herd,  
Who waters a large herd,  
Pays the great stable boy,  
On the eve lays large lanterns,  
Puts boats in the dock,  
Gray oxen in the yoke,  
Ears into earrings,  
Runes into ruble coins,  
Stallions into full money,  
Horse eyes into rings,  
Glitter into shields,  
Only I am the strong man!  
The other replied in return:  
Why do you boast in vain?  
I went to you yesterday,  
I came to you the day before yesterday:  
You had mice in the barn,  
Cats strong in the cellar,  
Rats creeping in between.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
We sing of this autumn  
Of this light spring  
Who knows, maybe another year  
Will bring us showers of rain  
Snow calls for new doors  
Great rains reach the ground  
Let Hantsu come, the father horse  
Let Peeter come, sealing with a song  
The cuckoo calls in the hollow  
The gray bird on the plank  
Then the sleds got ready to ride  
Reetallad to dance  
The spokes to rattle  
And the fire strikes on the strong backs  
Sparks flew from every leg  
The horse neighing in front  
The stallions neighing in the field too

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What do I care about shouting  
Or fearing when screaming  
Mine is not here  
My companion does not grow here  
Mine is in Holland  
The companion is in the fish shores  
A good man across the sea  
A handsome man beyond the fish  
My little bird went to the city  
The town's beloved  
A little spark to the market  
Brings me horned bread;  
An empty one cares even for bread  
Another for city bread  
There my little bird stayed  
The beach cranes eat the fat  
The Viru crows drank the blood  
There my little bird stayed  
There my beloved disappeared too

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The old maiden, the young maiden  
Marry this daughter  
Who walks along the yards  
From a foggy head to foggy hair edges  
With nice steps tied up  
I make a beer drain outside  
A rock drain in the room  
A vodka drain at the door  
I make a new barn outside  
When it's time to decorate the barn  
Then you aim along the bridge  
Walk along the edge of the yard  
"If you don’t put her, don’t ask  
Let her stay home sulking  
Molding at the garden’s edge  
Mushrooming at the wall’s edge  
A birch grows on her neck  
A linden tree on her temples  
An apple tree on her shoulders  
A juniper on her hair  
Reed grass between her thighs."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
In our village ten men  
A couple dozen boys  
Went to the gray taproom  
To wet their whistle  
They hopped over the lake  
Dancing at the back of the lake  
Then came a group of girls  
In herds from afar  
The boys began to ask the girls.  
Are these girls for sale  
Are the goods traded afar  
Crazy buyers, young men  
Crazy buyers and fools  
Outside there are hoes for sale  
On the street there are baskets for sale  
A maid’s goods in the family room.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Girls, little birds,  
Low-necked beauties,  
Curly-necked darlings,  
Beaded necklaces, dear ones,  
Wreath-crowned low-neck:  
Now you are going south,  
Pattering to the shop,  
Squeaking in city shoes.  
Mother builds you tenderly,  
Father raises you dearly.  
Do you know where you will go?  
Where you will finally be taken,  
And where you will be put afterward?  
To which flock will you swim,  
To which herd will you stray,  
To which domain will you fall,  
To which group will you be assigned?  
Some will get wet pine forest,  
Some will get harsh spruce forest,  
Some rarely get aspen forest,  
Some get leafy alder forest,  
Some get dear birch forest,  
Some the hazel thicket,  
Some get a beautiful apple tree.  
Whoever gets harsh spruce forest,  
Gets a bad man,  
Whoever gets wet pine forest,  
Gets a more reckless man,  
Whoever gets rarely aspen forest,  
Gets a bad man.  
Whoever gets leafy alder forest,  
Gets a widower.  
Whoever gets the hazel thicket,  
Runs off to the drinking hole.  
Whoever gets dear birch forest,  
Gets a dear companion,  
Whoever gets the beautiful apple tree,  
Gets an apple blossom.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
You are a deceitful young lad,  
You are a cunning companion:  
You deceive your father-in-law, you deceive your mother-in-law,  
You deceive the fine lady of the family,  
The white-headed girl of the parish,  
Among the village's fair Mareta.  
You promised to take oaths truly,  
To give strong engagements,  
To give your hand in love.  
You promised to bring three pairs of shoes:  
One pair slippery, another wooden,  
The third pair stony shoes.  
The slippery ones to walk on the track,  
The wooden ones to walk on the boards,  
The stony ones on the church road.  
Time came, the day rolled by,  
The night was spent at a mill,  
Two nights in a smithy:  
Turned your back, mocked the eyes,  
Laughed at another woman,  
Flirted with the girl.  
This is not worthy of a foolish woman,  
A sharp eye is not worthy of a fool,  
A snotty nose is not worthy of horse hooves.  
No carcass is worth money,  
A fool is not worth rings,  
A weak one is not worth an apron string.  
The angry man does not drink vodka,  
I leave behind what I slept for,  
I leave behind what I courted at home.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Be quiet, buy black ones,  
Be tall, buy tall ones:  
Put the black ones into the clothes,  
The tall ones into golden cases,  
The gray into silver beads,  
Bright-eyed ones into rings,  
Garlic bulbs into clasps.  
With gray beneath the meadows,  
With tall ones on top of the meadows,  
Between five rainbows  
In the middle of three dawns.  
Make marks in the tufts,  
Scars on the garden tape,  
Crosses in the Riga row,  
Crackling-crackling in the clouds.  
Then go for dawn to Courland,  
For sunrise to Pärnu County,  
Courland as a bridegroom,  
Courland girls as young maidens,  
Pärnu girls slender:  
Do you promise to come to me,  
To come to me, to be mine?  
Come to be my keeper,  
My property’s ruler:  
Then I will carry you in a belt,  
Hold you in my lap.  
In summer, I feed you sugared syrup,  
In winter, buckwheat syrup,  
The whole year goose fat.  
At night, I feed you sugared syrup,  
At dawn, walnut kernels.  
“Go ahead, stitch straps,  
Beds with golden stripes,  
Bring me to my decorated chest,  
To the honored place of the gift,  
You will be carried into the dear chamber.”  
Life was lived, life was:  
A fist was checkered,  
A striped sash.  
Salt box was for syrup,  
A jug for goose fat.  
I was once a grandmother’s home,  
Before that, an old man’s home,  
No silk or bread went inside;  
Now, malt broth tastes good,  
The oven rake is a lover,  
The dugout cake a dear one,  
Juniper is a rocky cape,  
The joint hole is a salt box.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Wandering,hooting,hoot, bring bundles  
Hoot, hoot, hoot, bring rods  
From the thatch, bring rods,  
And from the garden, garden bundles.  
Let's make a garden on the street  
Whose cabbages are torn and broken?  
Kangru's cabbages are broken,  
But who trampled them down?  
The shop boy, a dear brother,  
How did he trample them down?  
He went wandering in the evening,  
Peeking in the dark,  
Brandishing at twilight,  
Sneaking on the housemaid,  
Embracing village Kaie,  
Loving village Anne.  
Krants started barking,  
Muri started tearing at him:  
Immediately he jumped into the cabbage garden,  
Across the yard into the apple garden.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The scythe comes to the gentleman's field  
I will soon tell the gentleman too  
I dig for the dear lady as well  
The gentleman brings nice beads  
The lady brings a red wreath  
The young lady brings a green scarf  
Then the scythe goes through the villages  
All the village dogs bark  
All the old women stare  
Swish swish, slip slip  
A little wreath for Pärtel  
The wolf gets a taste from the mouth  
The wolf’s mouth will be sweet  
For the wild ones in good spirits  
Boys can use the rest  
The manor men’s little game  
The little boys’ little ones  
The herd children’s little heels  
The grass season’s little rose  
The apple tree’s little blossom  
Then the scythe goes through the villages  
All the village dogs bark  
All the old women stare  
A little neat one for the smith  
A little wreath for Pärtel

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
When the boy died,  
Where was he buried?  
In Kuuramaa under a spruce,  
In Läänemaa under a alder.  
Who visited the boy's grave?  
Every Sunday pagans came,  
Every Friday quarrelsome ones,  
Every Saturday lantern bearers.  
What grew on the boy's grave?  
Thistles and bindweed,  
Junipers and shepherd's staffs.  

When the girl died,  
Where was she buried?  
Under the church on Gold Hill,  
Under the silver tower.  
What grew on the girl's grave?  
Coltsfoot and marsh marigold.  
Who visited the girl's grave?  
Every Sunday lords came,  
Every month kings came,  
Every Friday the Germans from Riga,  
Every Sunday the gentlemen from Pärnu.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud abielust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
You ruled a gentle brother,  
You ruled polite ones,  
You ruled the white ones,  
The most beautiful across the world.  
Afterwards, you got that woman,  
Who had an elbow to lean on,  
Who had many teeth  
And blind eyes to exchange.  
Doesn't feel like walking,  
Lies down without a spark,  
Brews soup without spice.  
Taken along until now:  
I know a dull girl,  
I see that snub-nosed girl  
A dull girl with long hair,  
A lazy girl with wide flax,  
A thin belt on the thigh.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Certainly, the plant sprouts wanted me  
And the burdock boys caught me,  
The coarse coachmen courted,  
The honey-loving lads delighted,  
The mother did not give, the father did not agree,  
And I myself did not go.  
Mother did not give an unkind kiss,  
Father did not give a hard slap,  
Seeing my eyes were hungry,  
Hearing from my ears about school,  
That I was an advanced haymaker,  
A slender messenger,  
A skilled stacker of hay,  
I made large stacks in the marsh,  
Slender stacks in the clearing,  
Mighty stacks in the field.  
The stacks were visible wherever the secret place was,  
With legs sprawling behind the meadow,  
The stacks seemed to reach Narva,  
This was laughed at by the Germans of Narva,  
The lords of Viru were amazed  
That the stacks were made by women,  
The stacks were gathered by daughters,  
The pile of six maidens,  
The envy of five lazy boys.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The maiden goes to the field  
Walks along the edge of the field,  
Behind the corner of the field.  
Her head adorned, hair tied,  
Shoe heels marked.  
The woman goes after the daughter.  
“Come home, young daughter,  
Suitors visited home.”  
“Where did the suitors come from?”  
“The suitors came from Latvia.”  
“No, I will go to Latvia,  
To eat Latvian bread,  
To drink Latvian beer,  
To live in Latvia.”  
The maiden goes to the field,  
Walks along the edge of the field,  
The woman goes after the daughter.  
“Come home, young daughter,  
Suitors visited home.”  
“Where did the suitors come from?”  
“They came from Viisu.”  
“Then I will go to Viisu.  
In Viisu, eat Viisu bread,  
In Viisu, drink Viisu beer,  
In Viisu, live politely.”

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Understand, understand my sisters  
Know, know wise women  
What is under our yard?  
The sea is under our yard.  
What is in the middle of the sea?  
A well is in the middle of the sea.  
What is under the edge of the well?  
A pillar is under the edge of the well.  
What is on top of the pillar?  
A sieve is on top of the pillar.  
What is at the bottom of the sieve?  
An egg is at the bottom of the sieve.  
What is on top of the egg?  
A cuckoo is on top of the egg.  
What is on the cuckoo’s head?  
A brooch is on the cuckoo’s head.  
Where was that brooch brought from?  
From the finger bones of warriors,  
From the knee bones of foot soldiers,  
From the pockets of daughters,  
From the bracelets of women.
