label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Be proud, young men,  
Be dashing, buy black ones,  
Wear high ones, buy earrings;  
Buy lace for the whip,  
Silver locks with a locket,  
Copper between the straps.  
Ride to the girls’ homes,  
Visit the maids' households,  
There you will be given food,  
Chairs will be pulled out,  
A seat will be made for you!  

I was a proud young man,  
I was dashing, bought black ones,  
Wore high ones, bought earrings,  
Bought lace for the whip,  
Silver locks with a locket,  
Copper between the straps.  
I rode to the girls’ homes,  
Visited the maids’ households,  
There I was given food,  
Chairs were pulled out for me,  
A seat was made for me,  
I was taken to the chamber to sleep.  

I slept half the night,  
The other half I was thinking,  
The rooster crowed, they didn’t hear it,  
The rooster crowed a second time,  
Neither of us heard it,  
The rooster crowed a third time,  
The girl turned to another lad.  

Then I begin to understand:  
I put on my earrings,  
I put on my coat,  
I shake hands with the gray horseman,  
I speak to the earring horseman,  
I chat with the whip horseman:  
Farewell, maiden,  
I will not come back to you,  
You will be left behind,  
When this pea plant blossoms,  
And puts purple leaves on!  

The girl begins to cry:  
When you walk through Riga town,  
Always stand before me,  
So that no leaf will rustle alone,  
And no straw stalk will whisper.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I neither complain nor fear:  
That I was born a beggar's daughter,  
A ragged little child,  
What of the swamp so vast,  
Of five or six brothers even,  
No kin at all to feed me,  
No sisters in the evening hours,  
Still I was chased by rogue boys,  
They wanted me as a servant in Tallinn,  
Some among them manor boys.  

I did not want to be a dancer  
Nor did I want to be a coachman's lass,  
The coachman's tail is dirty,  
I did not want a stable boy,  
Stable boys are clumsy.  

I wanted a peasant lad,  
A peasant lad is wise at work;  
An official lies down to sleep  
An official stands by the wall,  
The farmer lies down to sleep  
Grain grows without him;  
Because I wanted a peasant lad,  
A ploughman, a sower,  
A tiller of black soil.  

What is there in being a fancy man  
Dressed in new clothes and proud:  
A man is pale, his fist is loud.  
His head, big, his fist loud.  
Quick comes the blow of the fist,  
From the harsh man comes a loud punch.  

My man is dark, his mind is wise,  
His flour sack is whiter,  
His oat sack taller,  
His purse heavier.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Girls are little birds,  
Snow-necklaces are beauties,  
Let's get together, the three of us,  
We'll hold a council, the four of us:  
Let's leave from this land  
Let's go quietly to Finland,  
For a month or two to Courland.  
There we will build a stone church,  
We'll carry a chapel made of carrageen,  
We'll make a bridge from onions,  
A fence from garlic.  
If there comes to the land a shortage of girls,  
A shortage of maids,  
They will surely search for us on horseback,  
Testing on foal hooves,  
The boys under our prayers:  
Come to the land, maidens!  
We will soon answer,  
Soon respond:  
Is there wild strawberry on the land,  
Blue gooseberries,  
Red hazelnuts?  
If there is no wild strawberry on the land,  
No blue gooseberries,  
No red hazelnuts,  
Then I am a Swedish maiden  
Walking with a elbow in the ditch,  
Marching with a marker on the shoulder,  
Sowing peas in the parish,  
In the district, knitting.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
A little brother,
I will not come to you,
To your garden fire,
To the horse’s dream,
To the dog’s snout on the face.
Send ships to Germany,
Empty ships to Turkey,
Bring out from there fiercely,
Pull out from the tar vat,
Bring out the arrow noses;
Those who understand drinking but not eating,
Do not really understand how to devour.
I will, of course, become a man,
The son of the barley field,
I have men to the sea,
To Peibu, to the hiding places.
Let time roll the days,
Then Hans will come from Harjumaa,
Kaarel dear from afar,
Peeter from Peibu,
Mart comes from the sea, from the sea lands,
Hans takes Anne to Harjumaa,
Kaarel takes Kaie to the distant place,
Peeter takes Liisu to Peipus,
Mart takes the sea to the sea lands.
Why do you laugh at me so,
With little eyes, you mock me!

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
What do I care about shouting,  
Or do I fear screaming,  
Mine is not here,  
My companion does not grow here,  
Mine is in Holland,  
My companion is among the fish on the shores,  
My fiancé is on the shore of Peipus,  
Now let the mouth just run,  
Let the tongue bark to the limit.  
So let me say it again,  
Let me sing it back again,  
I tilt the words cleverly,  
I roll the words skillfully:  
I have a home with cruel brothers,  
Wise brothers in the back room,  
Words are noticed,  
Whisked and dashed,  
Put on paper.  
Tomorrow they will be laid before me  
When I bring the thought home.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I was once a little boy;  
I worked on the manor estate days,  
I did the servant's helper days.  
I was not given food,  
Better things were not offered.  
When the estate days were done;  
The servant's helper days followed.  
Akkan  
Then I put on a gray cap  
With ears hanging high like rings  
Songbirds sang below,  
One on the other's yoke pole.  
Then I went through the villages.  
Rustling man, rustling horse;  
Rustling on the golden hoof!  
Then I went under the gray yard  
Took from the gray yoke-poles;  
From the ears hanging high like rings.  
Songbirds sang below,  
One on the other's yoke pole, too.  
I sat inside on the bench!  
Waiting to be given food,  
Better things were certainly offered.  
I was not given food!  
Better things were not offered.  
Now  
I put my rods through the door  
Girls smiled from the corner:  
“Is this the tail of a mad wolf?  
Or is it the paw of a forest bear?  
I hear and answer,  
""That  
""That is not the tail of a mad wolf;  
""Nor is it the paw of a forest bear.  
""These are clean boys’ rods.  
Now I am going to woo Koiki  
Koiki's bailiff's three daughters:  
One is dear Annekene,  
The second beloved Kaieke,  
The third sweet Mallekene!  
Anne was given to me,  
Kaie’s dowry was bargained.  
I wanted Malleke.  
Malle pays the city of Maali  
Anne’s rare shirt remnants,  
Kaie’s cowbell holes!

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
The flock has gone to the proud ones,  
A row of rich stalks,  
A group of pipe-sweepers,  
Once chimney scrapers,  
They did not come to greet,  
God, help us.  
They have made a winding road,  
A winding road of earthly pleasures.  
The crackle of dry trees in the forest,  
The wide singing of the woods.  
Dear little sister,  
There used to be many of us,  
Many of us, a beautiful flock.  
Now the flock has ended,  
The beautiful order has disappeared,  
Now I am left to howl alone,  
Linen seed stirring,  
Flax seed flickering,  
Rye seed watching,  
Among other seeds.  
Do not strike the poor child,  
The poor one cries without being struck,  
Howls without a whip,  
Rises in the morning  
To the elbow beneath the water,  
Under the blanket of rippling water,  
Under the striped quilt,  
Under the furry coat.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Dear gentle mother  
Make me a five-wool carpet  
Knit me six new coats  
Seven linen sheets  
Eight pillowcases.  
I am going to a man far away  
To a rich son-in-law  
Where ten pairs plow  
Nine take care of the horse  
Seven wield sickles.  
Be quiet, young daughter  
Yesterday I came from there  
There were mice in the threshing barn  
Rats in the Swedish barn  
Seven poles stand in the field  
There were peas in the granary  
There were sheaves in the barn  
Sheaves at the edge of the stack.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
A niece of the family praised her sewing trick,
That her trick was from a linden tree,
The tip was from an apple tree.
She had dozens of suitors,
One was Intu from Hiiumaa,
Another Antu from Harjumaa,
The third was the village's cooper's son,
The fourth from Neeruti meadow,
The fifth Villem, the scoundrel,
The seventh a blacksmith's apprentice,
The eighth a fisherman’s son,
The ninth Upper Ants,
The tenth the king of Kuurem.
I have golden keg cakes,
Silver barn doors,
I build bridges over swamps,
Barriers over rivers,
Where no woman’s foot treads,
Woman’s foot like an ox’s ring,
A maiden’s silk boot treasury,
A horse’s golden bridle case.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
A niece of the family praised her sewing trick,
That her trick was from a linden tree,
The tip was from an apple tree.
She had dozens of suitors,
One was Intu from Hiiumaa,
Another Antu from Harjumaa,
The third was the village's cooper's son,
The fourth from Neeruti meadow,
The fifth Villem, the scoundrel,
The seventh a blacksmith's apprentice,
The eighth a fisherman’s son,
The ninth Upper Ants,
The tenth the king of Kuurem.
I have golden keg cakes,
Silver barn doors,
I build bridges over swamps,
Barriers over rivers,
Where no woman’s foot treads,
Woman’s foot like an ox’s ring,
A maiden’s silk boot treasury,
A horse’s golden bridle case.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ui, ui, ui, ui, I plowed the valleys,  
I plowed the valleys, rye grew,  
I plowed the hills, pines grew,  
I plowed the swamps, bluebells grew,  
Then the girls went to pick flowers,  
To weave red ribbons.  
White heads to rule;  
Breasts full of baptismal bread,  
Necks full of hearth bread,  
Wrists full of money,  
I went to watch the girls,  
Bent under the spruce,  
By the edge of the alder,  
Is my own among them?  
They are not among my own!  
Girls, girls, do not cry,  
With twisted necks do not complain:  
Mine is not here,  
My match does not grow here.  
My beloved from far away  
A good bride from beyond the rivers,  
May the wind bring him health,  
May the rain send messages.  
As many greetings to him  
As there are sticks in a stick bundle,  
Many-wood good wife,  
Maple white wife,  
Bird cherry strong wife,  
Apple tree good wife.  
From the apple tree, I get a true wife,  
From the maple, a white wife,  
From the bird cherry, a strong wife,  
From the rowan, a tall wife,  
From the hazel, a straight wife,  
From the alder, the shortest.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sister dear Viiukene  
Listen, dear little maiden  
You cannot save the suspender  
Or break the rag string  
Before the man has put on a pair  
The vicar has written

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Otherwise, would live, otherwise would be
Otherwise, other days would be lived -
Let the evening come on Saturday:
The old woman goes to bed with the old man,
The father goes with his mother,
The brother goes with the young girl,
The sister with her husband,
But I go crying towards the corner,
Towards the empty bed
Where I turn my cheek, I find cold,
I turn my back, I find a wall,
All the boards are cold cheeks,
The edge boards are frosted,
The logs of the wall are mouldy...

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
So far, the meadow is beautiful,  
When the hay has not been mowed,  
So far, the little bow is lovely,  
When the tune has not been taken.  
Young woman, young girl,  
A little girl, a small child,  
Whim, whim of the sock maker,  
Little blue silk bird.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If this girl were to die  
where would she be buried?  
In the churchyard under the bell,  
under the silver tower.  
Who visited the girl's grave?  
Every month the kings came,  
every Friday the gentlemen of Riga.  
The prayer was in the middle led by the Germans of Paide,  
every Saturday the gentlemen of Laupa.  

If this boy were to die  
where would the boy be buried?  
In the swamp under a large spruce,  
in the forest under a wide alder.  
Who then visited the boy's grave?  
Every month the devils came,  
every Friday the dogs of Riga,  
every Saturday the dogs of Laupa,  
the prayer was in the middle led by the dogs of Paide.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What are you old women thinking about?
Old women are arguing
I cannot bring a woman here.
This parish will not age
This place will not gather
I will bring a woman to Narva road
A slippery woman to the city road
A suitable woman to the Paide road
I will bring Hans from Harju County
And Jaan will come from Järva County
Hans brings money for the horse
And a ring for the finger on the edge of the forest
Jaan brings shoes with high heels
The moon will shine on their heels
The sun will shine under their soles
Hans made juniper wooden containers
Rowanwood milk pails
And made spruce butter churns.
Juniper wooden containers,
They were good for greening.
Rowanwood milk pails,
They were swift for churning
Spruce butter churns,
They were easy to rinse.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What are you staring at, old boy?  
Why don’t you sometimes take a wife?  
Take an old girl from town.  
Chasing after a servant from the start  
It wears you out sometimes  
Turns the dew-soaked hay.  
Father goes to bed with his mother  
Brother goes with a young maiden  
I go crying to the corner  
Towards the empty bed:  
All the sideboards were cold,  
The wall logs were covered in fungus,  
I fumble for the old coat  
Oh, you old coat.  
It was good to walk along with the ox’s catch,  
Beautiful to walk with the herd’s catch,  
Even better to sleep on the barn floor.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Paleness comes because of mercy,  
Weeping comes because of the young maiden,  
Tonight must be endured,  
There is no maiden to hold,  
With whom I speak:  
We have fun with the bedbug,  
I speak with the louse;  
I turn my side, find coldness  
I turn my eyes, find the wall.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin girl, young maiden,  
Do not go to a young man as a husband,  
To an ungrown mate.  
You think he will help you grow,  
You think he will help you grow,  
But he gives only a man’s mind?  
A mate does not help growth,  
But a man takes away your mind;  
A mate asks for cloth from a weaver,  
But a man asks for clothes from a tailor:  
Every month he wants a new hat,  
Every week he wants four shirts,  
Every day he wants something fine,  
Every noon he wants linen.  
If you do not give it to him,  
Then you shall feel the whip,  
And you shall taste the rod:  
The man warns with his arm –  
You respond with your leg,  
Your mouth to your big toe:  
"Oh, legs, keep moving, legs,  
Keep me from beating,  
Keep me from the lash of the whip!"

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
"Oh, you deceitful boy,  
Clever to walk as a companion,  
You deceived the father-in-law, deceived the mother-in-law,  
You deceived the whole household, the fine young maiden:  
'Maiden, young girl,  
Darling, come to me,  
I will make you three pairs of shoes:  
One slippery, another wooden,  
The third a bitter pair of shoes.  
I will make slippery ones from snow shoes,  
Wooden ones from wedding shoes,  
Bitter ones from church shoes.'  
He became your servant,  
You again became his supporter—  
One pair of shoes was donated,  
Their toes made of soft leather,  
Heels of cat leather,  
In the middle patchy dog leather—  
You let the servant carry burdens,  
The hired boy to tie ribbons:  
The servant made big burdens,  
The hired boy thick ribbons—  
Those were then my three pairs of shoes."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
A young virgin girl  
A slender figure  
Do not go to a young man  
Who has no grown-up bride  
You think the bride grows up with him  
But he only gives the man's mind  
A bride does not grow up  
But a man takes the mind  
The bride asks for cloth  
The man for garments  
Every month he wants new jackets  
Every week he wants four shirts  
Every day he wants a fine one  
Every noon a meal  
If you cannot give this to her  
Then you will feel the whip’s blow  
Then you will taste the rod’s sting  
The man informs his boss  
You inform your foot  
With your mouth to the big toe  
Oh legs, move swiftly, legs  
Oh toes, wag your toes  
Hold me, but stop it  
Save me from the whip’s lash.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
King, little king,  
Rich lord of Riga:  
But why didn't you come to us last year,  
When the orders were carried out  
Five or six jugs of spirits,  
Seven or eight jugs of beer.  
Beer could be drunk from the trough,  
Ale could be drunk by all the livestock.  
The squire grabbed by the hair,  
I grabbed the squire by the chest,  
But I struck the squire against the walls.  
The pigs got to eat dung,  
While all the livestock could drink.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What’s wrong with becoming a prostitute,
Then you can go to the city as a barmaid.
My head wears a lace cap,
My feet new clog shoes,
Legs silk stockings,
Hips a shop apron.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I heard, I heard, crazy brothers,  
I heard the clatter of rascals,  
the silver raindrop’s pattering,  
the tin roof’s clinking.  
Bring that fire to the room,  
the bellows’ blaze to the floor;  
bring me silk stockings,  
silk stockings, thread stockings,  
thread stockings, high shoes.  
So I can go out to the dance,  
and wrap my arms around his neck.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My sister, a beautiful girl,
sits on a linden leaf,
dances on an oak branch,
boys kneel below.
Fall down, golden doll,
a hundred German apples fall down.
Fall onto my pillar,
let a wide hat fall on it,
on the edge of the wide hat.
I will not fall onto the pillar,
let a wide hat fall on it,
on the edge of the wide hat.
The devil falls onto the pillar,
I send him tall in a silk bed,
the devil swells in the pillowcase.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I just wanted to elope  
couldn't go secretly  
I watched through the keyhole  
the girl was dozing against the guard  
the old lady again leaning against the post  
the clean lines were burning  
there was nowhere a fire extinguisher

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Girls like snowdrops,  
Beautiful as rosebuds,  
They would sneak into boys' hearts,  
Slip under their skirts,  
Without chasing a boy,  
Without asking for a promise,  
Without trying a loving hand.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The evening comes, twilight arrives:  
the young woman goes to bed with the old man,  
the brother goes with the young maiden,  
the sister goes with her husband.  
Oh, poor me, always alone!  
I took the lice to talk to,  
the bedbug to amuse myself.  
The louse was shy, did not talk,  
the bedbug was talkative, but said nothing.  
My edges froze,  
my sideboards chilled,  
my pillows got moldy.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If I were Olev,
I would wear the cloak of Kalev,
I would carry a long saber:
I fear touching heels,
stretching out on the street,
getting caught by the crowd.
I would take a young wife:
I fear her young weeping,
bringing secrets into the room,
putting a bow by the edge.
Secrets are troublesome in a room,
a bow is used to poke the eye.
She did not poke the father-in-law's eye,
she did not poke the mother-in-law's eye.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
... I had a home with three sheds:  
one for cracking flax,  
another for storing goods,  
the third . . . .  
You had a home with three sheds:  
one for mice to jump,  
another for cats to roar,  
the third for dogs to roam ...

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
There was no broom on the table,  
no timid one tending the fire.  
Maggots crawled with stalks in their mouths,  
lice went to the corner with bundles on their backs,  
bedbugs dragged the trough.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The young men of Nurme village  
set out to ride for victory,  
to take women for victory,  
to hold a victorious wedding.  
They have a horse as dark as the oven,  
themselves as bright as the day,  
a hat like a church bell,  
a belt like a rain rainbow.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The young men of Nurme village  
set out to ride for victory,  
to take women for victory,  
to hold a victorious wedding.  
They have a horse as dark as the oven,  
themselves as bright as the day,  
a hat like a church bell,  
a belt like a rain rainbow.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I still go where it is created,  
Where the buildings might be dilapidated,  
And the cattle barns on a slope,  
A room supported by beams,  
And the stove resting on brackets,  
Long ago with a half-faced cow,  
In a yard full of fences,  
A woman would milk beside her hip,  
In an earthen floor barn,  
I still go where it is created.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Aidu, raiu, with wheels,  
before me I play with tussocks,  
I jump with birch stumps,  
before with those maidens,  
who are half-promised,  
loved beforehand,  
brought to the beer table,  
tasted on a bread roll.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mari granny, Mari grandma,  
Mari strawberry on the hill,  
called the boys to the spruce forest,  
tricked them into the long-leaved forest.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Beautiful girl, beautiful girl,  
white maiden,  
do you promise, do you promise  
to come to me.  
To come to me, to come to me,  
to be mine.  
I have no wealth, I have no wealth;  
I have no lack of bread, I have no lack of bread.  
I will make a granary, I will make a granary  
from goose meat,  
a roof on it, a roof on it  
from chicken meat;  
I will make walls, I will make walls  
from pig meat,  
a floor below, a floor below  
from piglet meat,  
a house in front, a house in front  
from frog meat.  
Inside I will put, inside I will put  
a silk bed,  
a silk bed, a silk bed,  
a silk bed.  
At the foot I will put, at the foot I will put  
junkru boy...

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The bachelor’s miserable fate,  
Always must he wander,  
Sleep on a haystack  
And fall down from the roof.  
He sits under the haystack,  
An old pipe in hand,  
Coal inside it,  
Waiting to blow luck.  
If only I had a woman’s embrace,  
Surely it would be dear to me.  
There would be no wandering at night,  
No sleeping on the haystack,  
No falling down from the roof.  
I will go sleep on the loft.  
I rummage in the old sheepskin.  
Even better to sleep on the loft.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I sing, why not sing,
I sing a song about a horse.
His legs are made of horseshoes,
His tail from gathered hay,
His body from hay stalks,
His ears from birch leaves,
His forelock from fireweed,
His eyes from stars in the sky,
His hair from dew grass,
His bits from branches.
Then I go to Sõrve journey
To see the maidens of Sõrve.
The maiden is gazing through the plank wall,
Through the plank gate.
If only I had that man
Who rides past on the road,
On the road, over the land,
Riding along the paths.
His horse is like a furnace,
He himself is like the day,
His belt like a rainbow in the rain,
His hat like a king’s crown.
Then the maiden begins to sing:
Go past me, man,
Go past the clever boy,
Take care of this road,
Record the paths in a book,
Write the road signs in gloves,
Come back in autumn!
Lead me from the downpours,
Redeem me from snowy weather,
Stop the winter winds,
From the strike of the Viru sickle,
From the plough legs of Harju.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I sing, why not sing,
I sing a song about a horse.
His legs are made of horseshoes,
His tail from gathered hay,
His body from hay stalks,
His ears from birch leaves,

His eyes from stars in the sky,
His hair from dew grass,
His bits from branches.
Then I go to Sõrve journey
To see the maidens of Sõrve.
The maiden is gazing through the plank wall,
Through the plank gate.
If only I had that man
Who rides past on the road,
On the road, over the land,
Riding along the paths.
His horse is like a furnace,
He himself is like the day,
His belt like a rainbow in the rain,
His hat like a king’s crown.
Then the maiden begins to sing:
Go past me, man,
Go past the clever boy,

From the strike of the Viru sickle,
From the plough legs of Harju.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I drove a grey horse, I gave water to the grey horse.  
I went riding on the grey horse.  
The night is full of pain, the moon is full of pain,  
The sky is covered with stars.  
A girl came towards me,  
A maiden from the group of girls.  
I asked the girls:  
Are these girls for sale?  
Are the hens being traded?  
From the girls came the answer:  
Oh, you foolish man!  
Oh, you crazy young man!  
Outside there is plenty of merchandise,  
On the street there is all kinds of goods.  
There are foals for sale by the aunt.  
Maiden goods on the bench,  
Under the hill there is whore merchandise,  
Prostitute goods even in the barn.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Call back, little sacred grove,  
Sing back, little forest clearing.  
To my good voice,  
To the deeper throat.  
If it used to call well before,  
Deceive not with false sounds,  
Hide the German horn sounds.  
The kannel’s sound was heard.  
There were also many of us,  
We also had many groups.  
They wandered like herds of horses,  
Rolled like herds of cattle.  
Now the herds have ended.  
And the flock has disappeared.  
From here the boys of Viru have gone,  
Beloved by the Hans of Harju,  
The coachmen of Kuie courted (them).  
If they have gone, let them go,  
If they have been taken, let them be taken.  
We have Kaia staying to grow,  
Leena to slide on top.  
We have Mai with berry heads,  
We have Kaia with cardigans.  
Ann remains a gray spruce,  
Leena remains with flaxen locks.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little Hellak, little brother!  
Wake me up from my sleep.  
What I saw in the dream;  
The fig tree was in our yard,  
The yellow tree was in front of the hut.  
The shawl tree was on the sauna road,  
The birch was in the room.  
The alder was on the wide market road.  
I understand the dream:  
From the fig tree drinks are drunk,  
From the yellow tree come marriages.  
From the shawl tree are eaten breads.  
The birch is a lover,  
The alder is a child's reconciler.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little boy, little lad!  
Why are you standing at the edge of the table,  
And why are you looking through the window?  
Is there no ear of rye nearby,  
Or no white field opposite,  
Is there not a beautiful body,  
Or a face not red,  
Or lips not more shapely,  
Ears more elevated.  
Where must I go?  
Where must I lay my head?  
I myself will lie down in the mouse’s box,  
My head I will lay on the bed of bites.  
My legs I will save for the mosquito bed.  
Girls, little birds,  
Lovely with slender necks!  
Do you know where you will go,  
Where you will finally be taken,  
Where you will be placed afterward,  
Where you drift in the crowd,  
Where you sink in the territory.  
To the beggar, to the scavenger,  
To the buyer of barley sacks,  
To the filler of oats sacks,  
To the city’s floaters,  
To the wearer of hats,  
To the destroyer of new huts.

label: Töölaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Here is the translation:

There was a healthy little bull,  
And another dear little cow.  
Who had made horns in the field  
And had a wedding in the meadow.  
A horn killed this bull,  
Who had never walked in the world,  
Nor worked the field in a hundred years.  
Ten cubits of side length,  
A hundred spans of horn length.  
A feeding piece was a feeding table,  
The field bed was a bench.  
The alder was broad-leaved,  
The birch was a dear companion.  
The willow was a long pipe player.

label: Töölaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Here is the translation:

There was a healthy little bull,  
And another dear little cow.

A hundred spans of horn length.

The field bed was a bench.  
The alder was broad-leaved,  
The birch was a dear companion.  
The willow was a long pipe player.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, you little deceitful boy!  
You deceived the fine girl of the family.  
In the village cold in the barn,  
In the heated building.  
Neither told the father was there.  
Nor told the mother was there.  
Nor told the brother was around.  
It became your own doing,  
It stayed your own doing.  
There was a father, there was a mother,  
There was a wise little brother,  
Also two beautiful sisters.  
Like a hunchbacked fox,  
Pecking in the stove.  
Looking for torn clothes,  
Wretchedly shabby rags.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sister, darling, first I told!
Naughty, young, first I scolded!
I am not a young man,
Nor an unripe wife!
A wife does not make one grow,
A man has no desire to take one,
The wife asks for the linen store,
The man has no desire for clothes.
If he could not have her,
Then there was brandishing of a whip,
And noisy scolding hand in hand.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Don't you run,  
don't you run  
Don't you run to a drunkard,  
to a tavern-goer  
A drunkard has no money,  
a tavern-goer has only coins.  
Surely I was chased  
by sailboys, they wanted  
to catch me, those boys.  
The woman didn't give, the old man didn't allow  
The woman didn't give to the scoundrel.  
The old man didn't allow to the naked one.  
Some girls are like a moose,  
easily faint and a vain slut.  
Look at that girl, beautiful and plump,  
like an apple fresh and ripe.  
Her hands are golden beauties,  
her fingers soft and slender,  
her hair the color of black gold,  
shining against the stocking rod.  
Her voice is like sunshine,  
piercing through the hearts and souls of young boys,  
so that singing about it makes you blush.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Don't you run,  
don't you run  
Don't you run to a drunkard,  
to a tavern-goer  
A drunkard has no money,  
a tavern-goer has only coins.  
Surely I was chased  
by sailboys, they wanted  
to catch me, those boys.  
The woman didn't give, the old man didn't allow  
The woman didn't give to the scoundrel.  
The old man didn't allow to the naked one.  
Some girls are like a moose,  
easily faint and a vain slut.  
Look at that girl, beautiful and plump,  
like an apple fresh and ripe.  
Her hands are golden beauties,

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I have, I have a little brother,  
he is a weather-wise one,  
he makes a bonfire into waves,  
he turns ropes into aganasta,  
throws a spear into the sky,  
to the other Jordan river,  
pulls rocks out of the cliff,  
ties knots into eggs;  
he makes gusts from the winds,  
prepares a cart from snow,  
rides across the Finnish bridge,  
along the arcs of the rainbow,  
the sky arches get tangled,  
the pillars of the world creak,  
when my little brother rides,  
the weather-wise one rushes on the path.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
We eat from one, we drink from one,  
Together we sleep through the night,  
We wear one embrace;  
One silk shirt on the back.  
But we do not take one wife,  
One takes another's wife,  
Another takes another old woman's daughter.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mother made five twisted socks  
I knitted six wedding socks  
Then I went to Kolka to ask for a bride  
To the daughter of the Kolka blacksmith.  
One was naked on the stove  
Another in bed without a shirt  
The third had a bare buttocks by the door.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Tiidu, Tiidu, Tiiu's thighs  
The wolf had torn Kaie’s thighs,  
The wolf had bitten Anne,  
The bear had scratched Kaie.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Before I throw a stone to the stone  
To the stone, to the stump  
Before the crazy boy  
The stone does not pull, the stump does not try  
The boy does not leave untouched  
The girl, however, is not undressed at the chest  
The hand cunningly untried  
Before I play with turf  
But joke with women  
Before those girls  
Who are engaged  
On the outside a fine young lady  
Underneath long since a woman.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Our Maied, our Kaied  
They were lively girls  
They wanted to share from the sky  
To scatter little clouds  
But then Maie would disappear without Kaie  
Then the clatter from the barnyard would vanish  
The laughter rolling from the alder grove  
The boys’ shouting from the spruce stand  
The boys’ jokes from the fields  
Mai was a fond young maiden  
She tended to give gifts to her man  
But she left the gifts on top of the coffin  
The stockings on the big table  
The shoes on the echoing path.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Put beads around Heide's neck  
Pearls on Pärts's neck  
Money but wide coins around Laugu's neck  
Rubles on the steed  
Before this girl's neck  
Who has been half-promised  
Loved too early  
Else my black mare could run  
Could neigh under the saddle  
The boat could circle the quay  
The chimney could smoke  
Before it would quiver and twitch  
Through the town's brothel  
Across the islands' crossroads  
Behind Paide's palm tree.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Dear little brother, dear little one,  
Do not take a woman from the manor.  
In the manor there are certain ones,  
Beautiful beneath the windows,  
Delicate and slender above the sill.  
How many men have they had,  
How many boys have they slept with,  
A hundred Martis they have slept with,  
A thousand known Thomases.  
Yet they remain innocent,  
Standing upright,  
Growing mold on the hill.  
They do not know how to weave fabric,  
How to stretch the fine thread,  
How to weave on the loom,  
How to twist the linen thread,  
How to flash the copper combs.  
Dear little woolen one, dear little one,  
Take instead the village outcasts,  
The servants from the end of the village.  
They know how to weave fabric,  
How to stretch the fine thread,  
How to weave on the loom,  
How to twist the linen thread,  
How to flash copper combs.  
They may go to the fire,  
Light the bed without sparks,  
Milk the cow without a lamp,  
By moonlight strain the milk,  
In the village buzz of beauty,  
On the forehead of the horned ox under the sun.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Boy, what wandering are you looking for here?  
Or are you watching aimlessly here?  
There are no strays of wandering here,  
Or lazy ones worthy of it.  
Here are sorrowful dark girls,  
The poor little children of the county.  
Go along the beaten paths,  
Walk through the shaggy bushes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Who went down to the meadow?  
Jaan went down to the meadow.  
Kaare lets the other mow.  
What is there under Kaare's side?  
An eagle under Kaare's side.  
What is there under the eagle's wing?  
An axe under the eagle's wing.  
What is there under the axe's eye?  
Shavings under the axe's eye.  
What are those shavings made into?  
Jaan's house.  
What should be put inside the room?  
Three young maidens.  
One of them weaves golden thread,  
Another braids silk ribbons—  
The third weeps for a young brother.  
Why do you weep, maiden?  
Why do you weep for the young brother?  
He is already seen coming:  
His head appears above the forest,  
His legs below the forest,  
His body from the middle through the forest.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The young men of our village,  
Leaving for Midsummer at the church,  
Under the horse like the oven,  
On top like the day?  
A hat like the moon’s servant,  
A belt like the rainbow of rain.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If I were the present  
If I wore the helmets  
If I had a long spear  
I would fear touching the heels  
Stabbing the toes  
Marking the calves  
I do not know how to hold a rifle  
Nor how to handle a wise spear.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mistress, little woman,  
Do not laugh at women’s children,  
Do not let others’ children wander.  
You have a herd growing,  
A roomful is rising.  
A scabby head is in a diaper,  
A crusty head is hidden inside,  
A bloody one rolls in the cradle.  
You do not know what will become of them,  
Whether they will be able to saw wood,  
To saw wood, to carry a load,  
For fathers’ wives to sew,  
For wives to spin.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
We, the beautiful girls, walk,
We make noise as we walk,
The empty one knows it a second time.
Will the rain drive us away,
Or will the dew carry us,
Snow orders new gates,
Hail orders new power.
Let me say, I will respond immediately:
Neither will the rain drive us away
Nor will the dew carry us,
Snow orders new gates
Hail orders new power,
But we are driven by the little brother,
Carried by the farm boy,
Warming the earth in the melting season.
I belong to the servant
To the cunning farm boy,
A woman created for the servant.
The servant buys me with barley,
The farm boy with oats,
A servant in the summer melt.
A lady, a woman,
Immediately hears and answers back:
"Where is the servant’s barley field,
The farm boy’s oat field?"
I hear and answer back:
"In the swamp is the servant’s barley field,
The farm boy’s oat field.
In the swamp roams the servant’s wife,
In the heath the farm boy’s wife."
A lady, a woman,
Immediately hears and answers back:
"The wolf eats the servant’s wife,
The bear eats the farm boy’s wife."
I hear and answer back:
"The wolf eats the bought geldings,
The bear the combed horses.
Linen bundles, wild garlic.
Mouse fur, greyish,
Barn hair ears.
White, well sheared,
Fat iron-gray horses."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The old woman lied  
Said my rune-carved one  
I have been a stallion until today  
Bachelor tomorrow until morning.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where shall the three of us go  
out together, the five of us  
will go  
to Maria’s hayfield,  
I’ll take a bow, I’ll take two  
I’ll start taking a third  
what I found under the bow  
an eagle I found under the bow  
what is under the eagle’s wing,  
an axe under the eagle’s wing,  
what is in the axe’s blade,  
a shaving in the axe’s blade,  
what will be made from that shaving,  
John’s barn will be built  
what goes inside that barn  
a bed inside that barn  
what goes inside that bed,  
straw inside that bed,  
what goes on top of that straw,  
flax on top of that straw  
what goes on top of that flax  
pillows on top of that flax  
what goes on top of those pillows  
four young maidens  
one goes home with a golden belt  
the second  
the third is a catpaw louse,  
the fourth cries for a young man.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where shall the three of us go  
out together, the five of us  
will go  
to Maria’s hayfield,  
I’ll take a bow, I’ll take two  
I’ll start taking a third  
what I found under the bow  
an eagle I found under the bow  
what is under the eagle’s wing,  
an axe under the eagle’s wing,  
what is in the axe’s blade,  
a shaving in the axe’s blade,  
what will be made from that shaving,  
John’s barn will be built  
what goes inside that barn  
a bed inside that barn  
what goes inside that bed,  
straw inside that bed,  
what goes on top of that straw,  
flax on top of that straw  
what goes on top of that flax  
pillows on top of that flax  
what goes on top of those pillows  
four young maidens  
one goes home with a golden belt  
the second  
the third is a catpaw louse,  
the fourth cries for a young man.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mari grandma, Mari granny,  
Mari strawberry on the hill!  
The bullfinch in the spruce forest;  
The bluebell in the alder grove.  
Surely you were caught by the boys in breeches,  
Loved by the boys of Harju.  
You were not caught with a gun;  
Nor taken with a net,  
You were happy to wander.  
Exploring the shore.  
Ants listened in the wasteland;  
Jaan Jaak in the hayfield  
Peeter in the long rowan;  
He was fit to be a smallholder;  
A husband for a farmer's daughter!  
For a fingered glove,  
A neighbor like Kuldanaast.  
Some kind of man  
Some man keeps close watch;  
Some chooses the barn,  
Some works in the kiln;  
Because of that young maiden  
Because of that long whip:  
Because of that wide strike.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Dear sister, dear brother  
We, the two lads  
Companion pair  
One named the other  
One called the other by a nickname!  
Called "ella," called "kulla"  
Called "ella" as "erne'eksi"  
Called "kulla" as "kudruseksi"  
Called "nisi" as "ivaksi"  
Dug as an oat plant  
I am the grandmother's simple child,  
I am the grandfather's simple child,  
Between brothers there is hostility  
Between sisters great scolding.  
Father despised me from his knees,  
Mother despised me from her bosom,  
Sisters chased me from the yard  
Brother drove me through the village street.  
They put me as a mole in the mound,  
As a rat in the grass nest,  
There I became a mole in uniform,  
The frog became a lung carrier.  
So let me tell it again  
Let me sing it again:  
"I had no father while alive  
No mother when I came to the world  
God knows who made me!  
Was I made by the black grouses  
Or was I laid by black birds  
Or were I stolen by the frogs  
Put together by the joiners."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Dear sister, dear brother  
We, the two lads  
Companion pair  
One named the other  
One called the other by a nickname!  
Called "ella," called "kulla"  
Called "ella" as "erne'eksi"  
Called "kulla" as "kudruseksi"  
Called "nisi" as "ivaksi"  
Dug as an oat plant  
I am the grandmother's simple child,  
I am the grandfather's simple child,  
Between brothers there is hostility  
Between sisters great scolding.  
Father despised me from his knees,  
Mother despised me from her bosom,  
Sisters chased me from the yard  
Brother drove me through the village street.  
They put me as a mole in the mound,

So let me tell it again  
Let me sing it again:

No mother when I came to the world  
God knows who made me!  
Was I made by the black grouses  
Or was I laid by black birds  
Or were I stolen by the frogs  
Put together by the joiners."

Brother drove me through the village street.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The sister went to bed with the man,  
The brother went with the young maiden.  
With whom shall I go?  
I go crying to the corner,  
Striking the wall with my face.  
I fumble in the old fur coat.  
I turn around, I find coldness,  
I turn my back, I find the wall.  
All were cold sideboards,  
Frost-covered were the edgeboards.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Iiu-tiiu Tiiu’s thighs,  
The bear had bitten Kaie’s thighs,  
The wolf had bitten Anne.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Maiden, young girl,  
Do not marry a young man,  
An immature husband:  
A husband does not make you grow,  
A man does not want to take a wife;  
A wife asks for cloth,  
A man desires fine clothes;  
Every month he wants a new coat,  
Every week he wants four shirts,  
Every quarter he wants tailored pants,  
Every year two fur coats.  
If he does not earn them,  
He cannot have a new coat each month,  
Four shirts each week,  
Tailored pants every quarter,  
Two fur coats each year,  
Then he starts flailing around,  
Clutching and growling.  
Your fist is square-shaped,  
The material is overfurnished;  
You have a scold,  
A hen to pester.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin girl, young maid,
Come to me, I have a visit;
Come to my visit, a shelter,
Let’s look after the early crops.
I will put the work to the willow:
I will put a yoke to your mouth,
To find the head for the day,
I will make the breads ready,
I will make the oats for the roof,
The barley under its own courtyard.
But the maiden understands, replies:
I can be yours to be,
Again I remain yours,
You make the oats far away,
The linens halfway to town,
The barley down in the valleys.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Girls, do you hear,  
What do you think of yourselves,  
That you despise boys,  
Leave men behind?!  
Later you come to the boy’s request,  
The sheet flutters in the hand,  
Pillows shifted to the side:  
Dear one, brother,  
Come to my arms to sleep;  
I will lay down sweet fern  
Under your head parsley,  
Under your feet St. John's wort. -  
The boy understands again, answers:  
- Before I throw a stone into a deep place,  
A stone into the depths, a stump into the depths,  
Before this girl’s depths,  
Who is half engaged,  
Excessively loved,  
Immoderately played with;  
Before I drag (her) to the peach cottage,  
Through Narva to the wells’ cottage,  
Before those girls,  
Who had too many loose ways,  
Who had half the hangman’s ways  
And yet had whore’s ways.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
With sparrow-bird wheels,  
Before I play with mosses,  
I jump with birch stumps,  
Before those girls,  
Who were half-promised,  
Loved too early,  
Boys' hands had fondled,  
Boys' penises had fondled.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Which of us swung in the cherry tree,  
And which of us rocked in the apple tree?  
The mill watch Maija maiden,  
She was the one who swung in the cherry tree,  
And rocked in the apple tree.  
Sass from Mäeküla was a boy,  
He went down to the prayer place,  
Spread the sheets loosely,  
Beat the pillows soft:  
Fall down, little Maija,  
Step onto my shoulders,  
So you don’t break your leg,  
You'll surely break your skull.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
A girl is trapped, a girl is screaming,  
Trapped in custody,  
Screaming in the town hall.  
There was her town hall, standing still,  
She turned her mouth towards the boy,  
Began to ask the boy:  
"What is buzzing in your pants?  
Trembling below the waistband?  
Can I give the girl into your hand,  
And place the girl in your palm?"  
Then the boy understands, replies:  
"You cannot give the girl into my hand,  
Let him put a couple of coins,  
Sign the wide-waisted certificate,  
Let the parish priest announce,  
And the blue coat persuade,  
Then you can give the girl into my hand  
And place the girl in my palm."

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, poor child of a slave,  
I always have sorrow,  
Every day my days are long,  
Every month was sad for me,  
For whom I plant boredom,  
For whom I set sorrow.  
On the stone to the church path,  
On the rope to the priest’s field,  
The stone was hard, did not speak,  
The stone was thick, did not tell tales.  
The stone did not understand the maiden’s language,  
The stone did not answer the maiden’s prayers.  
“Sisters, dear ones,  
Let us join together in three,  
And hold counsel as four.  
Let us leave this land,  
Let us go to Finland,  
For a few days to Pärnu County,  
For a year to Alutaguse.  
There we will make a stone church,  
There we will make a chicken chapel.  
From onion stems we will make a bridge,  
From wild chives we will lay it down.  
If maiden hunger comes to the land,  
Maiden hunger for grain,  
Surely someone will look for us,  
Riding along the body of a pike,  
Testing the foal’s hoof.  
They cut fire with storms’ flanks,  
Sparks flew from the pike’s body,  
The foal’s hooves cut iron,  
Searching for the maiden’s flock,  
For catching the flock of grain.”

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, poor child of a slave,  
I always have sorrow,  
Every day my days are long,  
Every month was sad for me,  
For whom I plant boredom,  
For whom I set sorrow.  
On the stone to the church path,  
On the rope to the priest’s field,  
The stone was hard, did not speak,  
The stone was thick, did not tell tales.  
The stone did not understand the maiden’s language,  
The stone did not answer the maiden’s prayers.  
“Sisters, dear ones,  
Let us join together in three,  
And hold counsel as four.  
Let us leave this land,  
Let us go to Finland,  
For a few days to Pärnu County,  
For a year to Alutaguse.  
There we will make a stone church,  
There we will make a chicken chapel.  
From onion stems we will make a bridge,  
From wild chives we will lay it down.  
If maiden hunger comes to the land,  
Maiden hunger for grain,  
Surely someone will look for us,  
Riding along the body of a pike,  
Testing the foal’s hoof.  
They cut fire with storms’ flanks,  
Sparks flew from the pike’s body,  
The foal’s hooves cut iron,  
Searching for the maiden’s flock,  
For catching the flock of grain.”

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, poor child of a slave,  
I always have sorrow,  
Every day my days are long,

For whom I set sorrow.

The stone was hard, did not speak,

Let us join together in three,  
And hold counsel as four.
