label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin girl, young maiden,  
Do not go to the young man,  
Unmature companions  
Do not make a companion mature,  
A man does not intend to take a wife  
If he must ask a fabric shopkeeper for a wife.  
Every month he demands six new coats,  
Every week he demands four shirts,  
Every quarter he demands felted trousers,  
Every year two fur coats.  
If not, if not, virgin girl,  
If he does not get six coats a month,  
Four shirts a week,  
Felted trousers every quarter,  
Two fur coats a year,  
Then the drummer will come to strike,  
Hand in hand to quarrel.  
Your fist is checkered,  
Your jacket woolly,  
The drummer is your water carrier,  
The calf is the wife carrier.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin girl, young maiden,  
Come to me, I have a visit,  
Come to my home to keep watch,  
To watch after the evening hours.  
I will set the flax to work.  
I will put the mouth to give,  
To seek the head for the day;  
For the year to set the place.  
I will make barley on the roof,  
I will lay flax nearby.  
I will place oats under the yard.  
The maiden answers to the visitor:  
"I can be yours, I have gotten,  
Again I will remain yours,  
You make barley far away  
Flax halfway to the town,  
Oats in the mountain valleys.  
I will go when it is time.  
If his buildings were hills,  
Cowsheds on slopes,  
A room leaning on supports.  
If I get a willing husband;  
A beloved spouse,  
I will make a big mess,  
Would cause a neck-breaking harm,  
Still he strokes my head,  
Tries on my necklace."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ann went down to the hayfield,
Mowed the swaths for the third time,
What is under the swaths there?
An eagle under the swaths.
What is under the eagle's wing?
An axe under the eagle's wing,
What is under the axe's eye?
A wooden shaving under the axe's eye.
What is carved from that shaving?
Jaan's barn is made.
What is put inside there?
Beds are put inside,
What is put on the beds?
Boards are put on,
What is put on the boards?
Straw is put on,
What is put on the straw?
Planks are put on,
What is put on those planks,
Four young maidens.
One wove gold
Another cast copper.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Do not rush to get married  
or become a young widow.  
What use is a crazy husband,  
to ungrown wives?  
A man does not like liking,  
a wife does not make grown.  
A man wants clothes to the ground,  
a wife asks for fine fabric,  
every month wants new skirts,  
every week wants four shirts,  
two fur coats a year.  
If he does not get this from you,  
then he feels the whip,  
knocks on the household goods,  
tastes the sweetness of plates.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
A little scoundrel,
when you deceived me,
You lingered in the room and at the door,
In the room and behind the door.
On your knees and in the pantry,
Without hat, without gloves,
Without a copper belt, without saddlebags.
You promised to keep me at the instrument,
promised to raise me on the kannel.
I go to sleep in the evening,
Put the instrument by the window,
The kannel by the chamber ceiling.
I wake you up in the morning,
Take the instrument from the window,
The kannel from the chamber ceiling.
You get up to milk the cow, Lilli,
White head, nurse the calf,
Then I became yours,
I remained your remnant.
You take the whip to keep me,
To raise me in the chamber.
You get up in the morning,
Take the whip from the window,
From the chamber ceiling, —
"Get up, bitch, milk the cow,
Old whore, nurse the calf,
I'll bring you an apron red
To the ground, with wolf fur,
Probably bringing striped ones,
Quickly bringing checkered ones,
With mustard’s paw prints.
The whip is red aprons,
The quiver is striped,
The comb has cat’s paw prints.
I have quilts with capercaillie patterns,
The blankets have duck patterns,
I have pillows with border patterns,
Pillow shams with lace.
These could have rotted
And become moldy under the cover.
If you spend badly,
The evil man scolds.
The evil could have endured living,
The bad could have slept in the willow.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
There was cold, there was hunger  
There was drought, there was heat  
But still not that hunger  
That was the hunger of the girls  
And there was emptiness of the girls  
The boys kneeled down  
Bowed without hats  
In the village street in Külleli  
At the Persel family gate  
Juniper berries in the hand  
Rowan berries in the palm  
Let’s bring down the tulips to the shoulder  
Pastry jugs on the bench  
Come to me, maiden  
Come to me and pull  
Get to me and ask  
The maiden heard and answered  
I still won’t come to you  
Your yard is muddy  
The cattle pen is full of holes  
The suitor heard and answered  
I have bulls to thresh  
Fat cows are sleeping  
Surely you’ll walk past the barn  
You’ll step along the edge of the garden  
Along the fences and reeds  
Until the bridge is made  
The foundation board is laid  
Bridges are made here from onions  
Logs are laid from carrots

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The goat came  
The mother goat came from Hiiumaa  
Then I grabbed the goat by the horns,  
Grabbed the mother goat by the ears.  
Then I took it to the smith in Viru  
The smith in Viru, my dear brothers,  
Made a horse from the horns,  
From the tips of the horns I got a saddle.  
Then I rode to the town  
Where the streetlamps shine  
Silver brushes turn green  
There the girls are on their haunches  
Crows behind the stove  
In front was a pile of pearl necklaces,  
Behind was a pile of firewood  
Sometimes there was a crowd of poor children.  
Still, I will not take you  
I will not take you, I will not leave you.  
I am going courting to Kooku,  
Koogu's farmer has three daughters,  
One is Anne with the crooked leg  
The second Kaie with the stubby hand,  
The third Maie with the forked mouth.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The goat came  
The mother goat came from Hiiumaa  
Then I grabbed the goat by the horns,  
Grabbed the mother goat by the ears.  
Then I took it to the smith in Viru  
The smith in Viru, my dear brothers,  
Made a horse from the horns,  
From the tips of the horns I got a saddle.  
Then I rode to the town  
Where the streetlamps shine  
Silver brushes turn green  
There the girls are on their haunches  
Crows behind the stove  
In front was a pile of pearl necklaces,  
Behind was a pile of firewood  
Sometimes there was a crowd of poor children.  
Still, I will not take you  
I will not take you, I will not leave you.  
I am going courting to Kooku,  
Koogu's farmer has three daughters,  
One is Anne with the crooked leg  
The second Kaie with the stubby hand,  
The third Maie with the forked mouth.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went to walk in the forest  
And look for bird nests  
I found a boy under a bush.  
His underside was moldy  
And his top side was sunburned.  
I threw him over my shoulder  
And went back for a piece  
There I scrubbed him with sand  
And dried him with a sack.  
Then I threw him over my shoulder  
And took him to the Viitna fair  
There I traded him for a rooster  
And a mottled sack.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Once I went wandering in the forest  
There I found a boy under a bush.  
His underside was moldy  
And his upper side had sunblotch.  
I threw him over my shoulder  
And carried him to the river.  
There I scrubbed and rubbed  
And dried him with a sack.  
Then I threw him over my shoulder  
And took him to Paide fair.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Other days I would live as usual,  
I would spend the peak days,  
When a blissful little evening comes,  
A little wandering one rolls by:  
The maiden goes to bed with the old man,  
The sister goes with her husband,  
The brother goes with a young maiden,  
With whom, poor me,  
With whom, little plain one!  
But I, crying, towards the corner,  
Towards the empty bed,  
Old coat on the hanger,  
Old sixpence in hand,  
The flea was proud, did not buzz,  
The louse did not bring joy,  
I turned my back – I found the wall,  
I turned to my side – I found cold.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Here I have boys, six boys,  
Their horse has turned gray in front,  
The man has sunburned on top,  
A thin strip of leather belt,  
Hat on the head like a mushroom,  
Look at the boys, our boys,  
The horse in front like an oven,  
The man on top like the sun,  
Belt around the waist like a rainbow,  
Hat on the head like the moon.  
Look where the boy is across the county,  
Across Koeru parish.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little maiden, thin and slender,  
If you want to come to me,  
To me, a young man,  
A red bull?  
If you don't come – I won't want you.  
Stay home to stew,  
By the stream to mildew,  
By the wall to gather mushrooms,  
The maiden responded with a refusal:  
I will not come to you,  
To you, the sow,  
To the horse’s dream,  
To the dog’s clumsy paw,  
The boy then replied,  
But I do not want a lazy one,  
You wash the pot with your backside,  
You crouch to heat the stove,  
While lurking you look for a broom,  
When napping you sweep the room.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went to Kadi's village,  
Got lost on Emu hill.  
From there I found Salla's trap,  
Rushed to Ruuna's house.  
There I had a woman to bid farewell,  
At Kilimitu, at Riga's rack,  
There were three ashes’ daughters.  
Mouse-gray was on the oven,  
Old silver was on the distaff,  
One was dozing with a safety pin;  
A piece of trousers was under the head,  
A window plug was over the hole.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Before, little mother,  
Put this daughter to a husband,  
Who walks around the yard,  
Foghead, foghair,  
Nice sleeves on her hands.  
If you don’t put her - don’t ask.  
Let her stay home knocking,  
By the fence picking mushrooms,  
By the garden wilting,  
A birch grows on her neck,  
A spruce grows over her eyebrow,  
A bird cherry on her eyelashes,  
A juniper among the hairs.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The evening comes through happiness,  
Through the fruit of twilight,  
The evening brings straw to the barn,  
Puts the straw on the threshing floor,  
Straws side by side,  
Each partner by the neck,  
Whoever has a partner, take the cat,  
The rat from the tuft of grass,  
The mole from the molehill.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Liiva-Liis's little bird,  
Kalmu-Hans's little companion,  
Come to me, you to me;  
I will build a barnyard,  
Make the walls from pork,  
The floor from pig fat,  
Shelves from bread rolls,  
The ceiling from pike fish,  
My cattle pen from fabric.  
If you go wandering outside,  
Bring pike fish from the ceiling,  
Pig fat from the floor,  
Pork from the walls.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin girl, young maiden  
Cutting in the field, on the hill  
Between the garden beds.  
Brother called out:  
"Come home, my sister,  
You have suitors at home!"  
"Where did these suitors come from?"  
"Suitors came from Kuur."  
Did not want to go to Kuur  
To eat the bitter bread of Kuur.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
We are three sisters,  
In the field, three stalks,  
Our home has three suitors.  
One was a clever one from Viljandi,  
The second a wise one from Tammiku,  
The third from our own village.  
From our village's high ground,  
Our parish's fair area,  
Our family's fine estate.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If this man had me,  
This man would have to take me.  
Who rides along the road,  
Rides along the paths,  
Chases along the edge of the fence  
Under the horse like to the oven,  
On the man like to the day,  
A hat like the church bell,  
A belt like a rainy rainbow.  
If this man does not take me,  
Let the horse under him grow moldy,  
The man still gets suntanned,  
The hat turns yellow on the head,  
The belt rots on the waist.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I can’t find the girl, where has she gone,  
Her footprints have vanished from here,  
She had tied the sandal straps.  
Now I have caught the girl again.  
Why do you hesitate when coming to us,  
In our yard, the apple trees,  
In the grass garden, the little roses,  
By our gate, the tall birches.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little Toomas from Turu  
Brother from Järva-Jaani  
Went to Koigi to woo,  
To Koigi cobbler's daughters.  
One was slippery little Liisuke,  
The other dear little Annake.  
I used to be a young man  
I jumped over a horse  
Over a creaking log boat  
Over a hazelwood sled.  
Now I have grown old and weakened  
I cannot anymore  
Over a creaking log boat  
Over a hazelwood sled  
Over a little piglet’s sow.  
Why didn’t you come to me last year?  
Last year I could not come to you.  
I would have long ago built barns  
Barns built even from eggs,  
Roofs from chicken skin,  
Stone bridges to the church,  
Priest’s streets from limestone.  
Otherwise my black could have run  
Otherwise my stallion could have ridden empty  
Kimme could have hauled stones  
Boat  
Before I grew old,  
I played on a rotten chest.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
I saw a hawk growing  
Rising from the ground like a berry  
I took to hold the horse  
I took the spark in the fire.  
Horse, you are beautiful  
May your mane be smooth  
I ride to see the girl  
To look at the girl  
Whether she is healthy or sick  
Or has long been ill.  
I rode to father-in-law’s doorstep  
Greeted the father-in-law:  
Is your girl at home?  
The girls went to fetch water.  
May I ride to the well’s path?  
Certainly, you may ride to the well’s path.  
I greeted the girls:  
“Hello famous home hens.”  
“Are you a suitor?”  
No, I am not a suitor  
I am a poor traveler.  
A messenger of German affairs.”  
“If you are not a suitor,  
Why is your horse groomed,  
And the horse’s tail tied?”

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Iiu, tiiu, Tiiu’s thighs,  
The bear had bitten Kaie’s thighs,  
The wolf had long ago taken Anne,  
And the snake had tasted Marit.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Beautiful girl, beautiful girl,  
white maiden,  
Do you promise, do you promise  
to come to me?  
To come to me, to come to me,  
to be mine?  
I have no wealth, no wealth,  
I do not have,  
I will not suffer from lack of bread,  
I will not suffer from lack of bread.  
I made a granary, I made a granary  
of elk meat,  
A roof over it, a roof over it  
of chicken meat.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, you deceitful young lad,  
a companion for playing cards,  
you deceived your father-in-law, you deceived your mother-in-law,  
you deceived the young maiden in the household.  
"Young maiden, young girl,  
darling, come to me!  
I will make you three pairs of shoes:  
one pair slippery, another wooden,  
the third pair of stone shoes.  
The slippery ones I make as snow shoes,  
the wooden ones as wedding shoes,  
the stone ones as church shoes."  
I received for you dried peas,  
and again for you dried peas,  
one pair of shoes I donated,  
even those with scarce stitching,  
their tips made of thin leather,  
heels at the back of cat leather,  
and the middle part of patchy dog leather.  
I gave the servant to braid,  
and the hired boy to lace.  
My servant made big braids,  
the hired boy made thick laces.  
Those were my three pairs of shoes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin girl, young maiden,  
delicate to the writing board!  
Do not go to a young man,  
to unripe companions.  
You think that they will nurture you together,  
but they only please the man.  
The companion does not cause growth,  
but the man takes away the mind.  
The companion only asks for fabric,  
the man for garments,  
every month wants a new coat,  
every week wants four shirts,  
every day wants something delicate,  
every noon wants linen.  
If you do not give this to him,  
then you will feel the whip,  
and you will taste the rod.  
The man advises the arm,  
you advise the leg,  
but the mouth to the big toe:  
Oh legs, go forth, legs!  
Oh toes, move, toes!  
Keep me off the hooks,  
save me from the lash’s thongs.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I came from work, tired,  
I came from the pub, weary,  
I refused to rest on the porch;  
my tools were left hanging,  
left visible through the window.  
The mistress came from the chamber,  
happened to see this:  
Could it be the tail of a wolf  
or perhaps the foot of a hare?  
It is not the tail of a wolf,  
nor the foot of a hare;  
these are the boy's genitals,  
fully grown man's testicles.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mother, dear mother,  
give your daughter in marriage here!  
If you do not give her, I will not ask.  
Let her stay at home knocking,  
mushroom picking by the wall,  
tending the garden's edge.  
A birch will grow on her neck,  
a spruce over her eyebrow,  
a willow over her breast,  
a juniper among her hairs!

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where did you get the hedgehog's eyes,
to come to our land,
to our great family,
to the dear people,
to the family of money coins?
We have the sea at our doorstep,
golden fish inside,
silvery little roaches,
and little bream in the rushes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I am mowing on a mountain meadow,
between the garden beds,
in the middle of six fields.
Who came home calling?
My own brother:
"Come home, my sister,
suitors have visited the home."
I then asked my brother:
"Dear brother!
Where have the suitors been?"
The suitors came from Latvia.
If they have come, let them come on!
I will not go to Latvia,
to eat Latvian bread,
to drink Latvian beer,
to live in Latvia.
- -
I am mowing on a mountain meadow,
between the garden beds,
in the middle of six fields.
Who came home calling?
My brother came home calling:
"Come home, my sister!
Suitors have visited the home."
Where have the suitors been?
The suitors came from Viisu.
Maybe I will go to Viisu,
to eat Viisu bread,
to live in Viisu.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I sing, why wouldn’t I sing.  
I sing a song about an apple,  
put a walnut on its top,  
ears made of oak leaves,  
eyes made of heavenly stars,  
legs made of birch,  
irons made of branches,  
tail made of foodstuffs,  
body made of food-stuffs.  
Then I go to Sõrve’s land,  
to see the maidens of Sõrve,  
a maiden gazes through the fence,  
through the gate of the fence.  
If only that man were mine,  
the one riding along the road,  
along the road, along the land,  
riding along the paths.  
Apple to him like the oven,  
head like the day,  
belt like the rainbow,  
hat like a king’s crown.  
Go past me, man,  
go through, cunning boy,  
pay attention to this road,  
mark the paths in the book,  
mark the writings on the gloves!  
Come back in autumn,  
take me from the rainflows,  
redeem me from the snowfall,  
stop the winter weather,  
from Viru’s scythe blow,  
from Arju’s plough cranes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If you want to go to the sauna,  
invite me to be a listener,  
choose me to be a viewer;  
I know a dull girl,  
I guess a naive girl.  
Dull with tangled hair,  
naive with rough hands,  
lazy with wide shirt sleeves,  
the tall one goes to sleep on the bench.  
Take a very tiny one,  
a tiny one, a little grain,  
a little wild one, a little diligent one.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Anne has been a horn for a long time,  
Kaie has been a bear's horn.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I came home from work tired,  
weary from the manor’s courtyard.  
I went into the hot parlor,  
life’s tools left hanging,  
through the parlor’s window.  
Maids were smiling in the corner.  
The housekeeper came out of the room.  
“Girls, why are you laughing?  
That is the tail of a dumb wolf,  
the leg of a hare from Jändrik,  
or the paw of a bear from the wilderness.”  
The housekeeper then replied:  
“That is not the tail of a dumb wolf  
nor the leg of a hare from Jändrik  
nor the paw of a bear from the wilderness.  
That is the tip of a teopoiss (tea boy)  
and the finger of a spirit boy.”  
- - -  
From there he went to the shirt hedgehogs,  
but disappeared into the sixfold folds.  
From there he traveled along the journey,  
reached the source of life,  
there he warmed himself in the cozy spot.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The dull girl’s bangs were long,  
the lazy girl’s braid was long,  
the cradle’s belt was wet,  
the slender heart was watery.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Iiut-tiiut Tiina’s thighs,  
the bear had torn Kaie’s thighs,  
the snake had bitten Maie’s thighs,  
the wolf had gnawed Anne’s.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mother dear, granny dear,  
granny dear, mother dear,  
Put this daughter to a husband,  
who walks along the streets,  
fog-headed, fog-haired,  
handsome little hands in hers.  
If you don't put her, don’t plead!  
Let her stay knocking at home,  
picking mushrooms by the wall,  
let  
a birch grow on her neck,  
a willow on her forehead,  
a spruce on her brow,  
a juniper among her hairs.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
/Beautiful girl, beautiful girl, white young lady:/  
/Do you promise, do you promise to come to me:/  
/To come to me, to come to me, to be with me:/  
/I will build a barn, I will build a barn out of meat:/  
/A roof on top, a roof on top made from a hen’s egg:/  
/I will put inside, I will put inside a silk bed:/  
/Into the bed, into the bed a silk cover:/  
/Around it, around it a garland of bride’s roses:/  
/At the root, at the root I will put a young man:/  
/Sit down, sit down on the bench:/  
/The boy’s hand, the boy’s hand on your neck:/  
/As soft as, as soft as a pillow:/

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Taiut, taiut, to Tammik,
Taiut to the Tammik path,
There was bad boy merchandise there:
Five received an old song in return,
Six received a dry shoe in return,
Seven received a boot leg in return,
A hundred received an old whip in return.
They were used to fence the herd yard,
To spread in the barn.
From them a bridge was made in the swamp,
A fence was made in the meadow.
Yet the crooks did not end,
The old boys from Tammik either.
Their horses are gray in front,
The men are sunburned on top,
Pea stalks as small ropes,
Bean stalks as cigar ribbons.
Look where the Moe boys are then:
The horse in front is like an oven;
The man on top is like the day,
They have silver small ropes,
Cigar ribbons made of fox tail.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Aiut, to Taiut in Tammik
Taiut to the village of Tammik
From there comes
Five to the old visa
Six to the dry shoe
Seven to the wall log
Eight to the coat
Nine to the horse
Ten to the plow ox.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where are the three of us going,  
Over the field with five!  
To the meadow under St. John's Day.  
I took the bow, left the other,  
Started to take the third one,  
What did I find there then?  
I found an eagle under the bow.  
What does the eagle have under its wing?  
An axe under the eagle’s wing.  
What is at the end of the axe?  
A shaving at the end of the axe.  
What is being chopped from that shaving?  
Base logs are being chopped.  
What is being chopped from that log?  
St. John's Day's barn is being chopped.  
What is inside that barn?  
A lock is inside the barn.  
What is inside the lock?  
A bed is inside the lock.  
What is inside the bed?  
Straw below, hay above.  
What is above the hay?  
A plank above the hay.  
What is above the plank?  
A pillow above the plank.  
What is above the pillow?  
Four young maidens.  
One is leaning on a golden belt,  
The second wears green,  
The third beats a hamstring,  
The fourth weeps for a young man.  
Don't you cry, little maiden,  
He is already seen coming,  
His head peeks above the forest,  
His feet rarely below the forest,  
His body twists through the middle of the forest.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Come to me, be mine,  
I will put you to easy work –  
To learn how to be strong,  
To seek a head for the day,  
To make a place for the year.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ship of lips, ship of rags  
Ship of lips' bird feathers  
Ship of rags' wood feathers  
Place the ship’s deck feathers  
Put covers on with bear feathers  
The floor with moose bones  
Doors in front with wolf feathers  
Oars with duck feathers.  

So the ship was finished  
Roll the ship into the water  
The water carried it down to the city of Riga  
There were girls quarreling  
In front were quarrels of noble lads  
Behind were quarrels of common lads  
In between were quarrels of poor children.  

Cast down the noble lads  
Stamp down the common lads  
Beat down the tufted chest  
Sometimes take enemy children  
From poor one becomes a thief  
To a rich village becomes a keeper.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ship of lips, ship of rags  
Ship of lips' bird feathers  
Ship of rags' wood feathers  
Place the ship’s deck feathers  
Put covers on with bear feathers  
The floor with moose bones  
Doors in front with wolf feathers  
Oars with duck feathers.  

So the ship was finished  
Roll the ship into the water  
The water carried it down to the city of Riga  
There were girls quarreling  
In front were quarrels of noble lads  
Behind were quarrels of common lads  
In between were quarrels of poor children.  

Cast down the noble lads  
Stamp down the common lads  
Beat down the tufted chest  
Sometimes take enemy children  
From poor one becomes a thief  
To a rich village becomes a keeper.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little Riinukene sister  
Who told you to walk so prettily  
Across the beautiful yard  
Over the straight bridge  
You looked at our shy lad  
If only that man were mine  
I would stand without eating  
Winter without fuel.  
Oh, you frost devils  
Half the rascals of Põltsamaa  
What do you have against my sister?  
Put a wreath on her head  
A slave's leaf in front of her forehead  
A singing leaf on her bald head—

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, you deceitful shepherd boy  
You loved your wife  
Promised to bring three pairs of shoes  
One pair smooth, gray wooden  
The third pair rocky shoes  
Smooth shoes for having fun  
Wooden shoes for carrying wood  
Rocky shoes for going to church.  
I got there,  
And I stayed there  
They gave me a blow on the crown of my head  
It was at the doorstep of the cellar  
They struck my knees  
They broke my money pouch  
That was the pay of the deceitful shepherd  
The wife’s lover.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went out wandering in the evening,  
Looking for a girl.  
The girls did not want to go,  
Because I didn’t have a silk vest on,  
A silk vest, calico shirt.  
On my feet were German boots,  
At night no store bags,  
Around my neck a kerchief.  
Then I went home with a sad mind,  
With a sad mind, with mourning on my lips.  
The maiden came to meet me at the gate,  
The old man came to meet me on the street,  
They began to ask me.  
Why do you weep, young boy?  
Why do I weep, old lady?  
And why do I mourn, old man?  
The girls did not want to go,  
Because I didn’t have a silk vest on,  
A silk vest, calico shirt.  
On my feet were German boots,  
At night no store bags,  
Around my neck a kerchief.  
"No, no, no, no, young boy,  
Tomorrow we’ll go to Huus’s store,  
From there I’ll bring you a silk vest,  
A silk vest, calico shirt.  
On your feet German boots,  
At night store bags,  
Around your neck a kerchief."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Hooves, hooves on Tiiu's thighs  
The bear had attacked Kaie's thighs  
The wolf had bitten Anne  
The bear had chased Kaie away.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Who went down to the hayfield?  
Jaan went down to the hayfield,  
Kaare stayed behind, mowing the other,  
what is there under Kaare's side?  
An eagle under Kaare's side,  
What is there under the eagle's wing?  
An axe under the eagle's wing.  
What is there under the axe's blade?  
Shavings under the axe's blade.  
What are those shavings made into?  
A house is built for Jaan,  
What is put inside the chamber?  
A bed is put inside the chamber?  
What is put inside the bed?  
Straw below, hay above.  
What is placed on top of the hay?  
A pillow below, a coverlet above.  
What is put on top of the coverlet?  
Three young maidens.  
One weaves golden thread,  
the second binds a silk ribbon,  
the third cries for the young brother.  
Why are you crying, maiden,  
why are you crying for the young brother?  
He is already coming:  
his head appears above the forest,  
his feet beneath the forest,  
his body turning in the forest.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I had only one son alone,  
he vowed to live without a wife,  
to live without a partner.  
He started living, he existed,  
he could neither live without a wife  
nor thrive without a partner.  
But he went from home to seek a bride,  
for a little while on a maiden’s path.  
He returned from the bride-seeking,  
then went to the room behind,  
placed his hat on the table,  
gloves on the edge of the hat,  
while himself began to cry;  
the father then asked the son:  
"Why are you crying, young son?"  
"Why should I not cry, dear father?  
I cannot live without a wife,  
cannot thrive without a partner.  
I went just briefly to seek a bride,  
a little while on a maiden’s path;  
the girls did not like me,  
started to bark at me.  
You don’t have a chintz shirt on,  
a chintz shirt, a silk vest,  
lace ribbons around your neck,  
German boots on your feet."  
The father then understood, responded:  
"Don’t cry, young son,  
let’s go to Riga to buy clothes,  
we will buy a chintz shirt to wear,  
a chintz shirt, a silk vest,  
lace ribbons for your neck,  
German boots for your feet."  
I went again to seek a bride,  
for a little while on the maiden’s path,  
then the girls loved me,  
they kissed me sweetly,  
embraced me dearly.  
Then I came from bride-seeking back home,  
brought a bride with me,  
from across the gate,  
her chest shone with rubies,  
apron with splendid lace.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Swamp I plowed – the piglets  
Field I plowed – the crows I watered  
Then I let the girls go picking  
Who was quick to pick  
Quick to throw the sheaf  
That girl for my name  
And a wreath on my tongue  
Then I started feeding the horse  
So I could ride the horse up close  
In a pile, I carried oats  
In the shade, I fetched water  
Flour in my pocket, I endured  
Then I rode straight to my father-in-law’s house  
Then I rode straight to my mother-in-law’s house  
Hello father-in-law, boot tops  
Hello mother-in-law, boiled neck  
Are the girls at home,  
Our girl by the well  
At the well, watering the herd  
I rode again to the well  
Hello girl, boiled neck  
Bring water to the calves,  
Bring water to my horses  
Let me pour water down  
Mouth black and rough  
Ear to ear linked  
I am a poor traveler  
Traveler, land measurer  
Messenger of kings  
Handler of German affairs  
Writer of big letters  
The girl heard again, the voice said  
You are not a poor traveler  
Traveler, land measurer  
Messenger of kings  
Handler of German affairs  
Writer of big letters  
You are a cheating lad  
Why do you have spurs on your whip  
And silver buckles on your strap  
And a horse’s tail in a braid.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went to Laar for a song,  
Down to the meadow below Aru.  
I began to gather the song,  
To pick the hay stalks.  
I found an eagle under a bow.  
What is under the eagle's wing?  
An axe under the eagle's wing.  
What is on the axe's blade?  
A shaving on the axe's blade.  
What is made from that shaving?  
Jaan’s barn is built.  
What is inside Jaan’s barn?  
Four young maidens.  
One of them wears a golden belt,  
Another has a tail of alder,  
The third wears a green dress,  
The fourth weeps for a young man.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
We are clean bachelors  
and demand to go courting.  
And none of us has a man's wisdom  
nor any desire for marriage.  
We are going to court Koigi.  
Koigi's steward has three daughters:  
One is Anne, the second Kaie,  
the third is Litter-Liisukene.  
Anne was naked like a stove,  
Kai was in rags,  
Liisu was at the pot,  
a little pipe in her fist  
and a small ladle in hand.  
He searches but completely ignorantly:  
whatever was bigger, he put in his mouth,  
whatever was smaller, he threw away,  
whatever was just right, he put in the pot.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mari was fond of strawberries,  
called the boys to the spruce grove,  
behind the room to the oak grove.  
The linden tree, how beautiful it was,  
Mari already knew that.  
On top of it went her swing,  
letting the linden branches move.  
Then the boys' eyes turned red  
as if they were seeing different worlds.  
Fall down, little Mari,  
Fall on my six-string,  
lay on my wide ribbon.  
If you don’t fall, I won’t call you.  
Pull your back like a cat,  
Mari’s bed is at the top of the linden.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I rode to my father-in-law, I rode to my mother-in-law.  
I rode to my father-in-law's doorstep.  
Under the meadow near the pond,  
Under the table by the door,  
Under the thick post of the boat.  
I myself was watching the doorstep,  
The hole of the doorstep's stick.  
The daughter was dozing on the chair,  
The old woman under the stove,  
The old man was watching the end of the rod.  
The fire began to die down,  
The stoves began to go out;  
The old woman said to the daughter,  
"Go sleep in the stable,"  
Then the daughter took a wild mood,  
And went to sleep in the stable.  
.......... .......... ......... .......  
Then grumbling behind the backside.....  
I took the gray one out from under the pond,  
Under the table by the door,  
Under the thick post of the boat.  
Then I rode out from under the pond,  
I rode down to Andrekse.  
There were marten daughters,  
And vesihall sisters.  
There I was invited inside,  
And a chair was given to me.  
A chair was given to me,  
And a table was set before me.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh dear, the rat’s wedding  
the mouse’s daughter got married,  
to the young son of the mole.  
There were proud wedding guests:  
the pig was in silk, the crow was in copper,  
the rooster had golden spurs.  
A piece of fodder was for the dining table,  
a garden bed for the bench.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh dear, the rat’s wedding  
the mouse’s daughter got married,  
to the young son of the mole.  
There were proud wedding guests:  
the pig was in silk, the crow was in copper,  
the rooster had golden spurs.  
A piece of fodder was for the dining table,  
a garden bed for the bench.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Aim for the woman behind the sickle,  
watch from behind the rod,  
take the stroke of the scythe.  
Do not lurk on the church path,  
there all are proud and beautiful.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ants was a man from Harju, Joak was a man from Järva.  
Ants was a humble man from Harju,  
Joak was a stout man from Järva.  
Ants bought a horse from Harju  
and a saddle from a Saare German,  
rode across Saare bridge,  
the bridge planks creaked.  
The girls of Saare smiled,  
secretly they watched.  
If only that suitor were mine,  
the one who rides across the bridge there.  
His horse is as strong as a stove,  
he himself is as bright as the day,  
a hat like a church bell,  
a belt like the flag of Riga city.  
Then I heard, I answered back:  
Beautiful girls are seen from afar,  
lovely girls are seen from a distance,  
only an empty one knows a girl’s heart,  
maybe there is a bug inside.  
A special one under the teeth,  
a bee on the mind.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Girls, little birds,  
Little doves in braids,  
Linen cloth little birds,  
Beautiful wreath-wearers!  
Do you know where you will go?  
In what group will you swim,  
Where will you stop in company?  
Some will end up in the dry spruce forest,  
Some in the wet pine forest,  
Some rarely in the aspen forest,  
Some flying into the alder forest,  
Some in the wise oak forest.  
Who ends up in the dry spruce forest,  
Will get a bad man.  
Who ends up in the wet pine forest,  
Will get a drinking man.  
Who gets the dear birch forest,  
Will get a dear companion.  
Who gets the wise oak forest,  
Will manage a farm.  
Who flies into the alder forest,  
Will worry about bread.  
Who gets the bad aspen forest,  
Will get a bad fate.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went to the village barn roof,  
The girls did not want to go,  
They started barking at me.  
You don’t have a chintz shirt on your back,  
A chintz shirt, a silk vest,  
Around your neck a calico scarf,  
On your feet German boots.  
I went home crying,  
Home to the cabin.  
Then I put a hat on the table,  
Gloves on the edge of the hat.  
Then an old woman came into the room,  
And an old man came into the room.  
Why are you crying, young boy?  
Why am I crying, old woman?  
Why am I crying, old man?  
I went to the village barn roof,  
The girls did not want to go,  
There I got bad treatment,  
Jeering from the girls.  
Don’t cry, young boy,  
Tomorrow we’ll go to the new store,  
From there I’ll bring you a chintz shirt,  
A chintz shirt, a silk vest,  
Around your neck a calico scarf,  
On your feet German boots,  
On your back a German tailcoat.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let's go down to the meadow  
I mowed a swath, I mowed two  
I cut a third for the threshing floor  
I found an eagle under the swath  
A whole feather under the eagle's wing  
What's whole there?  
A splinter is stuck right in the nose.  
What is cut from that splinter?  
They cut a St. John's Day hay rake  
What to put into that rake?  
Four young maidens.  
One is shaping a golden belt  
The second ties a silk ribbon  
The third is weaving a wave pattern  
The fourth is crying for a young man.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh you gentle little brother,
Do not take a tall wife!
The tall one goes to sleep on the bench,
But the shins stick up against the wall,
The knee bones against the doorframe,
And the head against the beam’s edge.
Take a short wife:
The short one milks the cow,
Drives the herd far away,
Far away to the hayfield,
The bulls to the golden barn,
The calves to the rook’s hill.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
We are three sisters,
Three ears of grain in the field,
Three suitors for the home:
One was clever from Viljandi,
The second wise from Tammiku,
The third from our own village.
From our village's high heart,
From our parish's fair heart,
From our family's fine heart.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I can't find the girl, where she has gone.  
From here the tracks have gone straight,  
Having tied the shoestrings.  
Now I have found the girl again.  
Why did you resist coming to us,  
In our yard beneath the apple trees,  
In the grassy garden with little roses,  
At the gate we have tall birch trees.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
A young maiden, a young girl,  
Went to the meadow on the hill,  
Between the garden patches,  
"Come," called her little brother,  
"Come home, my sister,  
You have suitors at home!"  
Where did these suitors come from?  
Suitors came from Kuura,  
I do not want to go to Kuura,  
In Kuura, one eats harsh bread.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If I were Olev  
I would wear the helmets,  
I would carry the long sword.  
I am afraid to wear the helmet,  
To fall into the comb,  
To reach the street.  
I would take a young wife  
I am afraid of young weeping,  
Suddenly  
To put a bow on the duck.  
Suddenly it becomes troublesome,  
To put an arrow in the eye.  
Do not put it in father-in-law’s eye,  
Do not put it in mother-in-law’s eye.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went into the forest, fell asleep,  
Under the porch to rest.  
The wind came, raised a storm,  
Carried my oars away,  
Filled my bucket with water.  
I hung the bucket to dry,  
To the top of a birch tree to chirp.  
Came the cruel claws of the goldfinch,  
Took it away from drying,  
From chirping on the birch tree.  
The village boy was left crying,  
Crying, longing.  
Oh, that wicked little goldfinch,  
Where he took my little bucket,  
A pretty little toy.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went to the forest for hazelnuts,  
But up the hill for strawberries.  
I went to the forest, fell asleep,  
Resting under the bush.  
The wind came and stirred a storm,  
And broke my oars in two.  
One took one oar,  
Another took the other oar,  
The third one took everything away.  
The village boy got free from wandering,  
From roaming on a dark night.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I wanted to go to the sauna.  
I looked through the keyhole:  
My daughter was dozing with a soft face,  
The peers were burning cleanly.  
I took up a brave spirit,  
I stepped boldly into the room.  
Anne was given to me,  
Kaie was traded to me.  
Father-in-law started scolding me,  
Mother-in-law began reproaching me:  
"How could you dare come to the sauna,  
You have nothing of your own!  
You have a vest from other brothers,  
A knife from other young men,  
A sheath from other acquaintances,  
An apple from the village heard about,  
A gift sent from the village."  
But I understood, I answered again:  
"Oh, oh, oh, dear father-in-law,  
Oh, oh, oh, dear mother-in-law!  
Surely here in the sauna there would be no sketching,  
And on the new one, no crawling,  
First, I should come to the sauna for you.  
The mites are dancing on the floor,  
The lice go to the corner with a broom strapped on.  
Who made your breads?  
The cows made your breads.  
The oxen drove the fire to the oven."  
"Where is the sign then?"  
"The ox's horns are doughy,  
The cow's hooves are ashes."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I had a little blackbird,  
A speck of flax cloth.  
I fed it, I gave it water,  
I was tending it for myself.  
With it, I go to the western Kõpu peninsula,  
With it, I take the sleeper from the room,  
From under the blood sausage bed,  
I take the sleeper from on top of the stove.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Dear brother,
Do not take a wife from the manor.
In the manor, there are certain women,
Beautiful from below the windows,
Finer from above the sill.
Take the enemy's children from the parish,
Next to a slave from the village,
Who knows how to work with fire,
Milk the cow without a pail.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
A blissful little evening comes,  
a miserable little twilight,  
then those loud quarrels arise,  
the barnyard ducks argue.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Listen, dear little daughter,  
don't believe the wolf's story.  
The wolf has those crazy tales,  
the bear has cunning words.  
But he promises to bring slippers,  
a bunch of clog shoes;  
he will live, he will be,  
then there will be spinning,  
the mitten immediately yapping.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If you start to take a woman,  
then invite me as the matchmaker;  
I know a dull maiden,  
I consider bad maidens.  
Dull with tangled hair,  
angry, watery eyes,  
angry faces red.  
Do not take a big woman,  
a big one will make your throat silent,  
a tall one will sleep on the bench,  
take a quite small one,  
small and sharp.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mother, dear little mother,  
Marry that daughter off,  
The one who walks along the yard,  
Foghead, fog-haired,  
Nice, strong sleeves.  
If you don't marry her off, don't ask,  
Let her stay at home chopping wood,  
Picking mushrooms by the garden edge.  
A birch tree around her neck,  
An alder on the rye,  
A spruce on the eyebrow corner.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Boys, boys, bright-eyed,
Boys, boys, ring-collared,
Do not laugh at the maidens,
Do not tease the girls.
You will surely suffer a bride shortage,
A lack of brides, a shortage of women.
You squat on the village street,
You loiter at the farmyard gate,
Looking for a girl there,
Begging for a bride there:
"Dear master of the household,
Kind mistress of the family!
Give me that daughter,
The one swung on the swing,
Rocked softly in the cradle."
The mistress listens and replies:
"I will not give you my daughter.
She may stay at home with me,
Sit by the stove,
Pick mushrooms by the wall,
Before I give her to you."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Why didn’t you come to us last year,  
The year before that you didn’t come either,  
When rivers ran without oil,  
Springs flowed with vodka,  
And the garden bed was peppery?  
Now you have come to the sour eel,  
To this sad month,  
Now there are no more peers in the family,  
No village comes to the sauna.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I set out walking,  
When a maiden came towards me,  
A maiden, like a flock of sheep.  
In front were quarrelsome lads,  
Behind were quarrelsome young men,  
In the middle, lovely maidens.  
I carefully watched,  
I am neither in a crowd,  
Nor in a row,  
Nor among companions.  
My sweetheart,  
A good bride beyond the seas,  
The wind carries her health,  
Willows bow in prayer,  
Clouds stretch long and wide;  
So many greetings to her,  
As many trees are in the grove,  
Straight and tall maples,  
White birch trees;  
In every treetop a titmouse,  
On every branch a squirrel,  
In every crown singing birds,  
All branches filled with squirrels,  
All leaves filled with birds,  
So many greetings to her.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I am not allowed to prove singing,  
Not allowed to proclaim loudly –  
There are listeners in the spruce woods;  
Speakers in the young forest,  
All the forests are full of men,  
Wastelands full of walkers,  
Groves full of underbrush breakers,  
Sharpeners of spears;  
Some are perhaps looking to buy,  
Some are watching to sell,  
Some are aiming for maidens:  
If I had a bow, I would ask for a maiden,  
If I had a gun, I would try to shoot,  
If I had a net, I would catch.  
But I hear, I answer back:  
Hear, dear young men,  
A bird is caught by a gun,  
A fish is caught with a bow,  
A fish is caught in a net,  
A maiden is caught with an arrow,  
Send her with bread,  
Bring her with beer,  
Test her with a bun.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Listen, dear young man,  
If you are going to take a wife in the west,  
Then take me with you:  
I am a dull girl to you,  
The last angry girl –  
A dull girl with long hair,  
A lazy girl with long braids,  
A slender waist.  
You surely rode to the marriage proposals,  
You surely wanted to have one –  
You rode to Viru's farm,  
You visited Harju crosses,  
You sent for West’s bride,  
But you did not find one to have.  
Look at the women along the church roads:  
All you see are churchways,  
Girls among other peoples;  
Aim for a woman even without dowry,  
Look at women from other villages,  
Try even from places unknown.  
Do not look for a wife from the manor;  
There are all sorts at the manor,  
The beautiful ones beneath the windows;  
But seek from the orphans –  
There you will find maidens in the granary,  
At the front of the granary are lively ones,  
At the back of the granary serious ones;  
There stand poor children,  
Find one from there for yourself:  
She knows how to walk around the fire,  
Sets the bed without sparks,  
Cooks the broth without scum,  
Warms the room without smoke.  
If you take a farm girl –  
She will tease you out of spite,  
Torture you out of anger;  
When she returns to the child’s home,  
If she teases you out of spite,  
She will pretend to be fond of a groom –  
The fire will not end in the house,  
No spark in the bedpost,  
Fruits will not ripen on the tree:  
But seek a woman from the dangers,  
A taker of young tears.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mari was a girl, Mari was a woman,  
Mari was a strawberry on the hill  
(To the blue-lily grove)

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Old-but-boys, loud boys,  
Frowning eyes, angry boys.  
Go to sleep by the wall,  
Until the wagon is tarred,  
The old wheel is greased,  
Then you will be pushed onto the wagon.  
You will be rolled into the carriage.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Indeed, the pot smiths tried to catch me,  
Pot smiths, cauldron smiths.  
They wanted me, an iron smelter.  
In the mouth, I melt the iron,  
On the tongue, I boil it shut.  
My tooth against the forge,  
My jaws like bellows.  
The woman didn’t give it, didn’t put the cauldron,  
And I didn’t want it myself either.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Indeed, the pot smiths tried to catch me,  
Pot smiths, cauldron smiths.  
They wanted me, an iron smelter.  
In the mouth, I melt the iron,  
On the tongue, I boil it shut.  
My tooth against the forge,  
My jaws like bellows.  
The woman didn’t give it, didn’t put the cauldron,  
And I didn’t want it myself either.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The hayloft is being cut down –  
What to put inside the loft.  
Four young maidens.  
One is weaving a golden belt,  
Another ties a silk ribbon,  
The third creates a lace pattern,  
The fourth weeps for a young man.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin girl, young maiden,  
Let us go to the young man,  
Unmatured companion.  
You think of nurturing,  
Giving manly mind.  
A companion does not make one mature,  
A man does not take the mind.  
But the companion asks for cloth,  
The man asks for fine clothes.  
Every month wants a new coat,  
Every week wants four shirts,  
Every day wants something delicate,  
Every noon wants linen.  
If you do not give this to her,  
Then you will feel torment,  
And you will taste bitterness.  
The man informs the flame.  
I inform the foot,  
The mouth to the big toe.  
But hold me back from it,  
From the whip's straps on my head.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little Hellakene, little old lady,  
Marry that daughter off,  
Who walks along the outdoor paths,  
Foggy head, foggy hair,  
Nice hands in hands.  
But I understand, I hear,  
Soon I will answer her:  
Before I sell a piece of poison,  
I will trade for a piece of steel  
Before I give the daughter away.  
Our pigs are sleek,  
The sheep's wool is loose,  
Like a daughter at home,  
A smooth sickle hangs on our wall,  
A beautiful curve in our hayfield,  
A fitting pile on the field.  
When our daughter returns home,  
Beauty will remain in our house,  
A beautiful curve will be in the hayfield,  
A smooth sickle will be on the wall,  
A fitting pile on the field.  
Then the pigs will die in the house,  
The sheep’s wool will rot.  
Like a daughter at home,  
Like beauty in our house.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
A virgin, a young girl,  
A little spark, come to me,  
I will make you three pairs of shoes,  
One slippery, another wooden,  
The third pair bitter shoes.  
I will make the slippery ones snow shoes,  
The wooden ones wedding shoes,  
The bitter ones church shoes.  
Oh, you little deceitful boy,  
A partner in playing cards.  
I became your possessor,  
And you remained mine.  
One pair of shoes was given away,  
Those too were cut hastily,  
They had pointed toes of soft leather,  
Heels made of cat leather,  
In the middle, the fur of a spotted dog.  
You gave the servant to moan,  
You made the wage boy tie ribbons.  
The servant made large moans,  
The wage boy thick ribbons—  
Those were then my three pairs of shoes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Quickly, quickly, quickly,  
Quickly, young maiden,  
So you can go to the sawmill.  
When you went to the sawmill,  
The room was left empty of the young daughter,  
The barn was left without stored clothes,  
The stable was left lacking sheep.  
The seats were left yearning,  
The corners of the room were weeping.  
When the daughter was taken from home,  
Then beauty disappeared from the house.  
Dear one, little mother,  
Do not wish the daughter near;  
Wish the daughter far away,  
Seek for her from far away.  
If the daughter is near,  
Then the evil mothers will be heard,  
Then there will be sorrow at home.  
There the threshers are threshed,  
The fat wheels roll,  
The meat slicers slide.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
But I, the low one, pay a lot,  
The tiny one stands upright.  
I strike down with low legs  
I slam against the floor  
I thresh the fine grain in the threshing barn  
The threshing barn walls are like seeds.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let's go to the meadow,
To Maria's hayfield.
I take a bow, I took arrows,
To harvest the third,
To name the fourth.
What did I find under the bow?
I found an eagle under the bow.
What is under the eagle's wing?
A whole eagle's wing.
What is whole in the nose?
A bone is whole in the nose.
What is carved from that bone?
John's barn is carved.
What is inside the barn?
Four young maidens.
One wears a golden belt,
Another rattles an iron belt,
The third has a cat's tail,
The fourth cries for a young man.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let's go to the meadow,
To Maria's hayfield.
I take a bow, I took arrows,
To harvest the third,
To name the fourth.
What did I find under the bow?
I found an eagle under the bow.
What is under the eagle's wing?
A whole eagle's wing.
What is whole in the nose?
A bone is whole in the nose.
What is carved from that bone?
John's barn is carved.
What is inside the barn?
Four young maidens.
One wears a golden belt,
Another rattles an iron belt,
The third has a cat's tail,
The fourth cries for a young man.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
We are cousins,  
We are full maternal cousins.  
One of our women is not taken.  
One takes another woman,  
Another takes another eidetütar,  
Another takes another tuaditütar.  
Eidetütars are beautiful,  
Tuaditütars are gentle,  
Memmetütars are spirited.  
Toomas goes to visit Tori,  
I am going out to Vändra,  
From there I will bring the daughter of Rein Seajala.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, poor me, poor all alone.  
I have no father as I am,  
No mother when I come.  
I have been made a grouse,  
Turned into a gray bird,  
Laid as a black bird.  
But I went crying to the corner,  
Wanted to talk to the flea,  
To play hitting with the bedbug,  
The flea was proud, did not speak,  
The bedbug was wide, did not bring enjoyment.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
I saw the young maiden
Growing like a bluebell,
Swelling like a red ribbon.
I began to take care of the harness,
Adjusting the fetlocks,
Watching the foal's hooves.
When the horse was harnessed,
The foal's hooves adjusted,
The fetlocks set,
I put on the silver beads.
I rode up to the father-in-law's door,
Greeted in a courtly manner:
Hello, my dear father-in-law,
Hello, my dear mother-in-law!
Are your daughters at home?
Our daughters are not at home:
The maiden went down to the gray slope,
To fetch water for the cattle,
So they could drink as a herd.
I went after the gray slope,
Greeted in a courtly manner:
Hello, my dear little maiden.
Will you promise to come to me,
To come to me, to be with me?
Do not be afraid to come to me,
I will not give you hard work.
You can knit socks,
Spin yarn in winter.
I will prepare the bed with wheels.
I will roll you
By the winter stove.
I will feed you sugar
In winter with buckwheat porridge.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Otherwise I would live, otherwise I would be!  
That blessed evening time will come,  
Dear embrace,  
Hand on neck.  
Father goes with my mother,  
Brother goes with a young woman,  
Sister goes with a village boy.  
I go to the corner crying,  
I turn my back, find a wall,  
I turn my stomach, find hardness,  
I turn my side, find cold,  
I turn my shoulder, find outside.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Old boy, wagon iron  
Sat on the leelõuk,  
Asked for a spark of fire  
To atone for sin.  
Old boy, wagon iron  
Was driven to the garden nails.  
Placed in the sun,  
Words were tarred like a cart  
And the wheels were greased,  
So that hell could be ridden immediately.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I do not care even about Oesus,  
Nor do I respect the boy,  
From Dirty Mikus  
Or from Kelg Aadus anyone,  
I pass over the best boys,  
I pass between the chosen boys,  
Among the fine boys in the middle,  
But I go past, turn my head,  
I wave flaxen locks,  
I lift the wreath higher.  
The Germans looked from the window,  
The officers from their room:  
"If only that maiden were mine,  
I would offer ribbons for money,  
A thousand bushels of wheat,  
A hundred sheaves of rye bread."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Do not take a rich woman,
Try for a farmer's daughter.
She goes out to the field to harvest,
Cuts a sheaf, cuts another,
Throws one sheaf to the side,
Another sheaf under her head.
She falls asleep for an hour,
Then begins to rot.
There birds disturb her,
Worms make her different.
Hedgehogs nibble her eyes,
Moles kiss her lips.
But then comes an evil eagle-hawk,
It is a slippery bird,
Quick to give orders,
It flies over the village yard,
Lands on the market roof.
It calls the young men of the village:
"Go out and see,
Walk through the rye field.
What will you find in the field?
You will find a young girl.
She has begun to rot underneath,
Out of her mouth rises the stench of death,
From her nose a fourfold spiral."
Then the young men wept:
"Why did you come to me last year,
I would have sent you to be cut,
To be plucked for the linen.
Now worms renew you,
Worms make you different.
Moles kiss your lips,
Hedgehogs nibble your eyes."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Our noblemen both,  
Two companions together.  
We eat together, we drink together,  
We sleep together at night,  
We rest during the long days,  
We do not take the same woman.  
One takes the other’s woman,  
The other takes the other’s maiden daughter,  
The other takes the other’s old man’s daughter.  
Maiden daughters are beautiful,  
Old man’s daughters are calm.  
You take Kaie, I take Maie.  
Kai was clumsy at work,  
Mai had a gentle step,  
Eeva beautiful with pearls.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The girl got stuck, the girl shouted,  
Got stuck while being caught,  
Shouted while being taken to the barn.  
Arrived at the barn, didn’t say a word,  
Gave kisses and gave more,  
Allowed her belly to be pinched,  
Allowed her navel to be tweaked.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Old boys, loud boys,  
Were taken to sleep on the wall,  
A word was tarred like a wagon,  
The wheel of the cart was oiled,  
Then they were taken before the goods,  
Five got the old song before them,  
Six got a dry shoe before them,  
A hundred got the heel of a boot against them,  
A thousand got winds of creation before them.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Come to me, Viru girl,
I will give you an easy job.
I make oats for the roof.
Flax on top of the beam,
Lentils under the beam,
Cabbage on the cellar shelves.
Come to me, Viru girl,
I will give you an easy job.
I boil a pot from the chain,
I make a key from scraps.
Come to me, Viru girl,
I will give you an easy job.
From the chain I make a cellar cover,
From scraps an outbuilding door.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where do the three of us go,  
Over the field with five!  
We go to the sunset story  
To Marialla's hayfield.  
I took the bow, left the other,  
I began to take the third,  
Started the fourth,  
Took the fifth tune.  
What did I find under the bow?  
I found an eagle under the bow.  
What is under the eagle's wing?  
An intact eagle's wing.  
What is tucked into that intact wing?  
A splinter tucked into the intact wing.  
What is carved from that splinter?  
John's barn is carved.  
They are pounding the foundation.  
What is inside John's barn?  
Salve inside John's barn.  
What is on the edge of the salve?  
A bed on the edge of the salve.  
What is inside the bed?  
Straw inside the bed.  
What is on the straw?  
A patch on the straw,  
What is on the patch?  
Pillows on the patch.  
What is on the pillows?  
Four young maidens.  
One is wearing a golden belt,  
The second wears a green skirt,  
The third wears a patterned skirt,  
The fourth is crying for the young man.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where do the three of us go,  
Over the field with five!  
We go to the sunset story  
To Marialla's hayfield.  
I took the bow, left the other,  
I began to take the third,  
Started the fourth,  
Took the fifth tune.  
What did I find under the bow?  
I found an eagle under the bow.  
What is under the eagle's wing?  
An intact eagle's wing.  
What is tucked into that intact wing?  
A splinter tucked into the intact wing.  
What is carved from that splinter?  
John's barn is carved.  
They are pounding the foundation.  
What is inside John's barn?  
Salve inside John's barn.  
What is on the edge of the salve?  
A bed on the edge of the salve.  
What is inside the bed?  
Straw inside the bed.  
What is on the straw?  
A patch on the straw,  
What is on the patch?  
Pillows on the patch.  
What is on the pillows?  
Four young maidens.  
One is wearing a golden belt,  
The second wears a green skirt,  
The third wears a patterned skirt,  
The fourth is crying for the young man.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, why do I sing of bad children  
Or why do I become a sad bird.  
Oh, my own is not here  
And my companion does not grow here.  
Oh, my own to Holland  
And my companion far away.  
May the wind bring him a thousand greetings  
In the love of the eternal sky.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mari went down to the haystack,  
Mowed the curved grass, left two.  
What is under the edge of the curve?  
An eagle under the edge of the curve.  
What is under the eagle's wing?  
A twig under the eagle's wing.  
What is on top of the twig?  
A chip on top of the twig.  
What is carved from that chip?  
Jaan's barn is carved.  
What would be put inside that barn?  
Beds would be put inside the barn.  
What would be put inside the beds?  
Boards would be put inside the beds.  
What would be put on the boards?  
Straw would be put on the boards.  
What would be put on the straw?  
Pillows would be put on the straw.  
What would be put on the pillows?  
Quilts would be put on the pillows.  
What would be put on the quilts?  
Four young maidens.  
One imagines a golden night,  
The second reaps a field plot,  
The third works a different task,  
The fourth weeps for a young brother.  
When will the brother Jaak come from Harjumaa, from Järvamaa?  
His head was visible above the forest,  
His feet rarely below the forest,  
The middle part through the winding forest.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Who was dozing on the oak branch there?  
Liisu was dozing on the oak branch there.  
Who took Liisu down?  
Hans came and took Liisu down.  
Where was Liisu taken from there?  
Onto Kirsi's new barn  
Onto the mill's old barn.  
There the bed was long prepared.  
Pillows under the head as a daily record  
Sheets beneath as a bird script  
Quilts on top as a capercaillie pattern.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I saw the girls walking by  
The girl’s hair rising  
The girl’s braid falling.  
The girls from the four villages  
The girls looking out loudly  
Behind the garden hedge, behind the snowdrift,  
Behind the stone, crouching  
At the gate of Perseli’s farmstead  
On the humpbacked village street:  
“Look, here comes my groom  
He had a shining horse,  
A rowan-wood whip,  
A juniper-wood shaft  
A tamarisk tassel  
“Why are you girls smiling,  
Why are you laughing across the fence,  
That I have a shining horse,  
A rowan-wood whip,  
A juniper-wood shaft,  
A tamarisk tassel?  
Wait, wait, girls, well, well, girls,  
I’ll pay the girl a smiling wage,  
To the maidens a working wage.”

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ten girls did come to the village  
Praised the song, criticized me  
Saying I didn’t wear a proper necklace  
Nor did I wear German boots on my feet.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let darkness not come to me,  
Let not the fool rush;  
Let wealth come to my chest,  
Wealth to the chest, stalks to the field.  
Who brought money to the horse?  
The horse tramples ruble pieces,  
The wheel spins kopecks,  
But the fingers have only thin money.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The evening comes, the day rolls by,
The evening takes the straw to the stove,
The twilight takes to sleep.
I slept, I slept gently, brother
I bring the girl to the cradle,
One to one hand,
The third to the middle hand.
I turn my back, I find the wall,
I turn my side, I find the cold.
All were cold sideboards,
The edgeboards were frostbitten.
I will surely plunder the clothes chest,
I will rummage the old coats,
I search for new jackets.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Don't drink, sir bulls,  
Your manor's black bulls.  
Bring against me the parish bulls,  
The parish's famous bulls,  
Your manor's black bulls.  
I won't get against the parish bulls,  
The parish's famous bulls,  
Your manor's black bulls.  
I won't come to quarrel  
Loudly with you.  
I am not afraid of the hairy-headed,  
The thieves of Viru grain.  
I don't care about one man,  
I am not afraid of two men.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Dear darling, dear grandfather,  
I am going to take a wife. -  
I have three homes:  
One was gray, another had ears,  
The third had wide-leafed meadows.  
The gray one was beautifully trotted,  
The one with ears was good at walking kabala,  
The meadow was a smooth cart path.  
I will ride the gray one to the courtship.  
Mother said to her son:  
"Listen, son, dear son,  
If you go to take a wife,  
Take your sister under your wing;  
Your sister knows the shy girl:  
Shy ones have tangled hair,  
The lazy ones' braid was on the back."  
The son then said: "Dear darling,  
I will listen to work for nothing,  
If she has beautiful breathing,  
Precious embraces,  
Sweet lying down.  
If this papa matches us,  
Then I will unbutton the wide button.  
Twice kisses, and three times other things."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I had a deceitful little boy,  
A cunning little companion;  
The boy deceived me until midnight,  
But lied until dawn.  
He promised to take true ones  
But promised to bring slippers,  
To buy new clog shoes,  
To put red ribbons on them,  
To put beautiful heels underneath,  
To put little silk straps inside.  
At night while sleeping  
He talks evil about others,  
Speaks against the unmarried children:  
"This poor one doesn’t have a rug  
Nor a pitiful undercloth."  
Oh, I say, I answer again:  
"Where would I, poor one, get a rug,  
A pitiful undercloth?  
I don't even have a sheep in the sheepfold,  
The linen doesn’t stretch out.  
Let me at least have a sheep in the sheepfold,  
The linen stretched out.  
Then I will make a quilt with black grouse pattern,  
An undercloth with duck pattern,  
Linen with bird pattern underneath."

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My wife is a little fragile,  
the fabric seems delicate:  
one elbow, two heads,  
an elbow at ten weeks of pregnancy!  
Her stitches don’t end at the hem,  
nor do the wool threads end at the hem,  
the pouch fabric comes from the rooster!  
They creak, they squeak,  
the fabric squeals and rattles!  
The stitches are large, the seams are wide!  
The stitches are large - received from the father-in-law,  
the seams are wide - made by the mother-in-law,  
fabric legs were given as well,  
the seat bench was received from the groom.  
I will go out from the groom’s gate,  
along with the dowry;  
I will go to look inside,  
how many beds they have in the room.  
They have seven beds in the room:  
father’s bed was a bench,  
mother’s bed was on a mattress,  
groom’s bed was on a plank,  
kitchen bed was on a peg,  
house daughter’s bed was on spikes,  
I do not know,  
boys’ bed was on poles!

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My wife is a little fragile,  
the fabric seems delicate:  
one elbow, two heads,  
an elbow at ten weeks of pregnancy!  
Her stitches don’t end at the hem,  
nor do the wool threads end at the hem,  
the pouch fabric comes from the rooster!  
They creak, they squeak,  
the fabric squeals and rattles!  
The stitches are large, the seams are wide!  
The stitches are large - received from the father-in-law,  
the seams are wide - made by the mother-in-law,  
fabric legs were given as well,  
the seat bench was received from the groom.  
I will go out from the groom’s gate,  
along with the dowry;  
I will go to look inside,  
how many beds they have in the room.  
They have seven beds in the room:  
father’s bed was a bench,  
mother’s bed was on a mattress,  
groom’s bed was on a plank,  
kitchen bed was on a peg,  
house daughter’s bed was on spikes,  
I do not know,  
boys’ bed was on poles!

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Why didn't you come to me  
When you went to get the orders  
Five or six vodka mugs  
A hundred pastries  
A thousand beers  
Still, I don't want you  
If you didn't come to me first.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let it come to Mr. Väila too  
I will tell the gentleman right away  
I complain to the dear lady too  
The gentleman brings beautiful beads  
The lady brings a red wreath  
The young man brings pink shawls.  
Then I go through the villages  
All village dogs bark  
All old women stare  
U'st u'st Little Tuisu  
The smith's little Viksikene  
Dear Krantsikene of Pärtli  
Then the wolf gets a share  
The beasts are disheartened  
Then I got the boys' little bride  
The manor men's little playmate  
The young lads' little darling  
The herdsmen's little bone  
The meadow's little rose  
The apple tree's little blossom.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Girls are little birds  
Necklaces are pretty  
Wreath heads are beautiful,  
Do you know where you go  
Where you head to  
Where you arrive  
Where you slide your flag?  
Some get a wet pine forest  
Some a dry spruce forest  
Some get a bad aspen forest  
Some get a dear birch forest  
Some then find a alder forest  
Some get a willow forest  
Whoever gets a wet pine forest  
Gets a drinking husband  
Whoever gets a dry spruce forest  
Gets a harsh companion,  
Whoever gets a bad aspen forest  
Gets a bad life  
Whoever finds an alder forest  
Finds a place to get bread  
Whoever gets a willow forest  
Gets a place to bake bread  
Oh, my crazy kin  
Sisters truly foolish  
Forced me straight to the swamp  
Running to the bear's cub  
To the forest breaker they liked  
I had a little carpet  
A light, beautiful blanket  
A tiny carpet  
I went to sleep with the bear  
To breathe with the she-bear  
The bear’s claws stuck out  
Bear’s claws, bear’s legs  
Bear’s toes under the carpet.  
Oh, the golden king’s son  
Nollernoat had a daughter-in-law  
Began making a woman of wood  
Drying a woman from spruce  
Chiseling from an oak block  
Making peace with a woman from alder  
Taking care of a woman from aspen  
He put gold on her cheeks  
Silver for beauty  
He went to sleep with the gold  
To breathe with the silver  
What was attracted to gold  
Was frozen to it  
What was fond of silver,  
Was fond of it dearly  
What was under the old woman's carpet  
Was warm to her.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Maiden, young girl  
What are you afraid of when coming to me,  
Coming to me, arriving to me  
I will not force you to hard work  
I will not put you to much work  
To make a place for a year  
To search for a head for a day  
To patch clothes for a month  
I will not take you into the rain  
I will not send you before storms  
Between snow showers  
I will make oats into the chamber  
Linen into the flax-cradle  
Barley directly to the oven  
Oh, you trickster little lad  
You were cunning, little companion  
I managed to have, I managed to live  
You put me in a few debts  
You made oats for the distant one  
Made linen for the town road  
Made barley for the valleys  
Then he took me into the rain  
Then sent me into storms  
Between snow showers  
Where there was severe cold weather  
Hawkberry against storms  
Alder against wide frost  
The girl is even tender  
The child is delicate from the top  
Who marries young  
To unripe spouses  
Thinks to please the man  
To raise the precious spouse  
The man does not want to like  
The spouse does not want to grow  
The spouse asks the fabric mother  
The man wants the clothing mother  
Every day wants finery  
Every noon a plate of food  
In a week he gets four shirts  
Every month new clothes.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where we go as three  
Across the field as five  
We go to Looja for a story  
To Maria’s hayfield  
Looja to take a story  
Mari to mow the hay.  
What is given to us there,  
Six golden belts  
Seven silver belts  
Eight neckerchiefs  
Nine heart beads  
Ten plough symbols  
Where we weave the gold  
Where we cast the copper  
We place it on the hat  
We place it on the gate  
On grandmother’s pillowcase  
On father-in-law’s granary door  
The pillow caught fire  
Whom do I call to put it out,  
I call the moon, I ask the day,  
The moon did not come when called  
Nor did the day when asked  
Came a little cloud boy  
He brought clouds with him  
Take the clouds when you go too  
From the cloud comes a drop of water  
From the drop came a long horn  
From the horn came a red berry  
From the berry came a blue tail  
Under the tail was a German bed  
What was in the bed  
Four young maidens  
One knitted a golden belt  
The second tied a silk belt  
The third knitted a thread belt  
The fourth cried in the corner  
They had been preparing gifts for a long time.
