label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Who is allowed to be dull to me  
Who may be loud to me  
I am an eternal slave  
A lady’s lifelong servant.  
Let the gentleman also come outside  
I will immediately complain to the gentleman too  
The gentleman brings beautiful pearls  
The lady brings a red wreath  
Then I go through the villages.  
All village dogs bark  
All village girls stare.  
I make the dogs bark  
Door, door, Tuisu Tuksikene  
The blacksmith’s little Viksikene  
Dear Krantsikene of Pärtli  
You will be dealt to the wolf  
To the despair of the wild ones.  
You will become the boys’ bride  
The plaything of manor men  
The little rose of the haymaking time  
The blossom of the apple trees.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Here go the straight tracks  
Straight tracks, winding tracks  
Through the willow, the duck's tracks  
Immediately after, the squirrel's tracks  
Here the straight one paused  
Had to locate the duck  
At home stayed the greyhound  
I forgot both greyhounds  
I myself went to look for the greyhound  
After the greyhound in pursuit.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I put barley into the ditch  
Oats by the riverbank  
Rye at Kurve’s spruce grove  
The horse ate the barley from the ditch  
The ox ate the rye from the spruce grove  
The bear ate oats from under the bank  
I was going to sell the horse  
I got a cheap hide from the horse  
I was going to slaughter the ox  
I got strong meat from the ox  
I was going to skin the bear  
I got a full-length coat from the bear  
Then I went to travel to town  
To stroll around Paide  
The gentlemen watched in wonder  
The German landlords stared  
Where did this German come from?  
Is he some manor owner  
Or the son of a nobleman?  
He is not some manor owner  
Nor the son of a nobleman.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I put barley into the ditch  
Oats by the riverbank  
Rye at Kurve’s spruce grove  
The horse ate the barley from the ditch  
The ox ate the rye from the spruce grove  
The bear ate oats from under the bank  
I was going to sell the horse  
I got a cheap hide from the horse  
I was going to slaughter the ox  
I got strong meat from the ox  
I was going to skin the bear  
I got a full-length coat from the bear  
Then I went to travel to town  
To stroll around Paide  
The gentlemen watched in wonder  
The German landlords stared  
Where did this German come from?  
Is he some manor owner  
Or the son of a nobleman?  
He is not some manor owner  
Nor the son of a nobleman.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
You wanted to come to propose  
To the big farm  
To the proud building  
To the tall roof  
Don't you dare take the gentleman's daughter  
The golden king's daughter  
Before you took the humble daughter  
The humble daughter, just a little.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ai-rui-ralla  
Sunday I’m going to a wedding  
To the Koigi manor  
To the three daughters of the Koigi farmhand  
One was Anne, the second Kaie  
The third Mareti Maie  
There I was spinning with a distaff  
With the young lord's whip  
With the chamberlain's chair  
With the lady’s long bench.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Who is allowed to be dull to me  
Who is allowed to be loud to me  
I am the eternal slave of a gentleman  
The lifelong servant of a lady  
Let the gentleman come outside too  
I will immediately complain to the gentleman as well

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Why are you laughing, laughing at me  
With mocking eyes you scorn me  
With tin eyes you glare at me  
With woodpecker eyes you speak to me  
You cannot marry while laughing  
Mocking eyes cannot endure  
Tin eyes do not court  
A sharp nose does not seek a girl  
Perhaps you would seek with fire  
You would go to the golden stilt  
Squatting on the village street  
Behind the back door of the household  
You would ring the beads  
You would make the bangs  
I am like a little boy from the room  
A little boy, a small lad  
You take the beads to the old woman  
The old woman will put them on the dog’s neck  
In front of the cowshed on the mother’s neck  
On the farm shed on the mother’s neck  
Maybe she will put them on the calf’s neck  
Still, I will not come to you  
To you, the handsome one  
To the horse’s dream  
To Mihkel, the dog companion.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The heart calls to go courting  
The father does not approve  
Mother forbids the jewelry  
Sister the silver rings  
Threaded the string of beads  
Wiped the handkerchief  
I put my hat on the table  
Gloves and a cap on the ice  
I myself inside, near the fireplace  
Then father gave a horse  
Mother gave good jewelry  
Sister gave silver rings  
And the threaded strings of beads  
I rode to the father-in-law’s door  
I rode to the mother-in-law’s door  
Hello father-in-law, hello mother-in-law  
Hello to both of you old ones  
Do you have daughters at home  
A girl fetching water from the well  
Ride to that well’s ice edge  
Where the well cake is frozen  
The cake is frozen, the twig is sharp  
Sharper still is the water carrier  
The girl with the twig  
Carries water for the cattle to drink  
Give them all water to drink  
Give water to my horse  
Otherwise the gray one would stall  
Ears would dangle on the yoke  
Come to me, girl  
Come to me, be mine  
I will bring you three pairs of shoes  
One pair made of wood, another of leather  
The third pair stone-soled shoes  
Wood shoes for carrying timber  
Leather shoes for stacking straw  
Stone shoes for going to church  
The girl hears, answers back  
When the father has given a dowry  
Wearing a black jacket  
Singing in a wide jacket  
Then you will get shoes to one pair  
Not two pairs from Kalev  
Young girl, maiden  
Come to me, be mine  
I have three barns outside  
One for chaffing the wheat  
Another for threshing rye  
The third for cleaning barley  
The girl hears, answers back  
One is for mice to jump on  
Another for cats to scratch  
The third for beating rats in the barn  
If you were my guest  
You would surely be cared for  
You would surely be noticed early.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
There are so many in the field without owners  
There are so many strong ones in the field  
Besides the meadow maidens  
There are no singers in the field  
No gentle glimmers  
The meadow’s golden droppers  
Those droppers are worn out  
The field singers are tired  
Some have been taken by rain showers  
Or killed by winter cold  
Burned by summer drought  
Snow has lulled new ones to sleep  
Dew has bent the cold ones  
They have been taken by Viru boys  
Järva boys have come to fetch them  
Harju boys have rotted them  
With their bad money  
With worn-out coins  
With rotten cents  
With worthless pennies.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Wait, boys of Ori  
Endure, boys of Kapu  
Hold on, boys of Preedi  
Be silent, boys of Varangu  
I shall grow into a maiden  
A girl will seize youth  
Who knows whose luck it will be  
Is it the luck of a son of the family  
Or the luck of a poor child  
Or the luck of a great servant.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Otherwise, I would take the young woman  
Afraid of the young woman’s crying  
Putting the bow on the boat  
But the bow poked the father-in-law’s eye  
The father-in-law’s eye, the mother-in-law’s eye.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I don't tell anyone  
Nor boast to anyone  
Where I have my beloved  
Raising my partner  
My beloved in the clouds  
My partner in a distant land  
Under the moon is the other side  
On the star is the other side  
My partner is beneath the moon’s ice.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let's go, darlings, let's go, sweethearts  
Let's go, darlings, quietly along  
Let's go, sweethearts, along the pines  
Secretly along the groves  
So that neither roosters hear  
So that neither dogs hear  
The young men of the grove heard  
They stood by the gates  
In the barracks, on top of the sauna broom  
Clinging to the door of the room  
One came to call me  
Come to us, to the wind shelter  
Roll to us, to the rain shelter  
The horse is taken out of the stable  
Quietly as I lead it to the stall  
Hay is given in front  
October mowed  
As I lead myself inside  
Given under the chair  
Thrown on the pillow  
Raised in front of the barn  
There I’m given food  
There I’m given drink  
Sweet beer and mead  
Then the groom started talking to me  
Young maiden, young girl  
Come to me, won’t you?  
I have three barns at home  
One stores barley  
Another holds money  
The third keeps rye grain  
But I understand, I immediately answer  
Young lad, little boy  
Whom do you praise in your village?  
To me or to some other girl  
You have three barns at home  
One is a playground for mice  
Another for cats to howl in  
The third a rat’s pleasure house.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
They are waiting for me at home  
They are watching me at the gate  
Immediately bringing water home  
Father is warming up by the stove  
Grandfather is going to cut oak  
The oak had grown to the sky  
Branches had reached the clouds  
From there, ship planks are made  
Ship planks, sail wood  
Floating wood on the water  
Trumpets over the marshes  
What was left over  
From there comes the sauna for my little brother  
Where my brother goes to take steam baths  
The grandmothers go to weave  
Along come the Germans from Mündi  
Through come the Germans from Kaalepi  
Directly come the Germans from Arina  
They think of the town of Lihula  
They imagine Toompea’s tower  
This is my little brother’s sauna  
Where my brother goes to take steam baths  
The grandmothers go to weave  
One who cares about the flowers  
Two who fear the shouting  
Speaking a little out loud  
It’s not my own fault  
Nor my wife’s fault to grow  
My own to the valleys  
My wife’s to the distant  
Keeps watch over the lake  
Comes to the market bright red  
In the mud drinks from the blueberry  
The pike drinks from the gray  
On the stone drinks the chub  
Will he come home or not  
Will he bring bread or not  
The empty one cares even for bread  
The other about city bread  
If he would even come home  
He would bring the horse home  
I left a little bird in the town  
A lover in the small borough  
A companion by the fishing shore  
City sluts who have eaten meat  
Marsh scoundrels who have eaten fat  
Space crows who have drunk blood.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where do we go against the night  
Against the night, against the garden  
Against the well to build  
Against the golden moon  
Maybe I will drown in the creek  
Maybe I will wander to the shores  
Who is searching for me in the creek  
God is searching for me in the creek  
God is touching the shores  
His golden roof of the hut  
Silver roof of the barn  
Where do we go against the night  
Against the night, against the garden  
We are going to the place  
Where the sickle mows hay for me  
Where the scythe turns in an arc  
There we stay overnight  
Where the alder milks the cow for us  
Where the birch sends the herd  
The whitehead waters the calf  
Sends the herd far away  
Drives our geese to the marsh  
Turns the hens to the roost  
White-footed to the meadow  
Fleck-legged to the alder grove  
Surely we will milk the spruce  
The whitehead waters the calf  
Why didn’t you come to us last year  
When the rivers ran without beer  
The springs, the meadows ran with vodka  
Boiling holes boiled up  
The day had intended to linger  
The evening had rolled the mist  
Now you have come to us  
Now the rivers do not run beer  
The springs do not flow vodka  
Boiling holes are boiled up  
My horse is clumsy  
My aunt has spotted ears  
My brother’s gray one is grumpy.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mai fed me for dinner  
Maie kept watch until midnight  
When I fell asleep  
She killed the singing bird from the barn  
Killed the black rooster from the meadow  
From the street, a little lamb  
From the market, a white-headed calf  
Morning came out white  
Mother-in-law goes upstairs shouting  
Rise, Maie, my dear one  
Maie immediately understands, hears  
Cannot rise, cannot wake  
The misty blankets are lost  
The covers are torn differently  
Mother-in-law immediately understands, hears  
Why did you kill the cruel companion  
The cruel companion, the dreamed man  
I killed the singing bird from the barn  
From the street, a little lamb  
From the market, a white-headed calf  
Maie comes up to dress  
Puts on a blue silk  
Around the waist a rose-patterned belt  
On the head, a red wreath  
Mai puts a mark in the tuft of grass  
A scar in the garden stick  
A cross in the rings of Riga  
Beloved, dear one  
Sweetheart, companion  
If you come looking for me  
Take the marks from the tuft of grass  
The scars from the garden stick  
The crosses from the rings of Riga  
Then come looking for me  
Give strength to the gray horse  
Give the boat appropriate power  
Then come looking for me  
Sparks struck the shad fish’s legs  
Kicks struck the foal’s thighs  
The maiden, entering the stable  
The maiden rushing into the stall  
Row to that city below,  
Where there are three maidens  
The first has pearls on her neck  
The last has a tag on her neck  
In between, there are poor children  
At the top, there are slave children  
They see the fire coming  
Shouting by the chandelier  
Spinning without nails  
To the pain of the night, to the pain of the moon  
The night’s pain milks the cow  
The moon’s pain strains the milk  
The pain of the bull’s head in the barn  
The beauty of the colorful gloves

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I had, I had dear brothers  
I had three good ones to care for  
One was gray, another desert-colored  
The third a fine flaxen-haired one  
I went with the three to courtship  
To the gray one's barn before  
To the flaxen-haired one's yard before  
To the hearth's room before  
I myself stepped before the door  
Peered inside through the bars  
The girl was dozing on the chair  
The old woman was watching the spindle  
The old man was lounging by the stove.  
Then I sat down below  
The table was set before me  
Food was offered to me  
My mouth ate and my eyes watched  
My eyes watched the music before me  
The music was to be taken home  
Lice walked in the straw in the mouth  
A flea went to the corner, the pouch was on  
There would be raking with the rake  
Sweeping on the new floor  
Putting together on the threshold.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
Oh, Mari, the low grass  
Why didn’t you come to me last year  
When you came to deliver the orders  
Five vodkas, six courting visits  
Thousands of beer crates  
Hundreds of bread rolls  
I would have built a barn long ago  
Made the barn from boards  
From the red stone of the chamber  
Inside I would have made silk beds  
Silk beds, thread games  
For a young woman to sleep on  
To lull a new husband to sleep  
To quiet an old husband.  
Laala, laala, little mouth  
Mumbling, mumbling, little berry  
You can still be a home  
To be a home, to hold court  
Between the white boards  
Pretty Kerstu in the middle.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Liisukene, little bow  
A little snap when heard by the ear  
A little pinch when seen by the eye  
In the shadow, an old little tuft.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
There are indeed men in our parish  
A plump red-haired woman  
A fair-haired woman to choose  
Not to my liking  
Neither a hen with feathers  
Nor a beard with flaws  
Mistakes don’t come in error  
I’ll bring a dear one from afar  
A beauty from the holy land  
A red-haired one from Põltsamaa  
A gentle one from Tagandi land  
She will bring six measures of gold  
Silver she will bring in handfuls  
Half a chest of files  
An old row of trunks  
Countless keys  
A handful of rings.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Jüri was Jüramu's follower  
Soo Sanger, Sarapuu's rogue  
Jüri would buy a horse  
Fears the Saxons’ sickness  
Jüri should have a long mind  
Fears the fashion’s flash  
Jüri wears Kalev’s coat  
Fears the rolling fur  
Jüri would take a young woman  
Fears the woman’s snapping  
Navel strings snapping  
Jüri is behind the men’s forest  
A handsome boy from Illeveri  
A nice boy from Keika village  
Jüri puts horses into the rake  
Old girls before the cart  
Before the two-wheeled cart  
Then takes a long whip in hand  
Teases old girls  
From Illevere meadow  
From Varivere grove.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Old-old Tiiu’s thighs  
The bear had torn Kaie’s thighs  
The snake had stung Maie,  
The wolf had bitten Anne  
The bear had licked Kaie’s heel.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I walk past the bridegroom's gate  
Alongside the buildings  
I go inside to see  
How many beds they have in the room  
They have seven beds in the room  
Father's bed was wooden  
Mother's bed was beaded  
The bridegroom's bed was iron  
The maiden's was made of linen.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Do not be afraid, do not be afraid when coming to us  
To our young man  
There are hayfields, oats,  
Plenty of rye  
For you to eat to your liking.  
In your room, there is tobacco smoke  
In front of the fire, there is alder smoke  
In your bed, a feather mattress  
Under the eaves, sheaves hanging  
We have three barns outside  
One waters the wheat  
Another cracks the rye  
The third threshes the rye  
Young boy, little boy,  
You have three barns outside  
One for the dogs to jump in  
Another for the cats to run in  
The third for the dogs to sniff.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Think, think, girls  
Think a little about me  
I will put the flax to work  
Well to the flax’s task  
To beat the rye on the thigh  
To tread the oats into groats  
Think, think, girls  
Think a little about me  
I will put the flax to work  
Well to the flax’s task  
I will make the stocking knit  
For a year to lay the nest  
Think, think, girls  
Think a little about me  
I will put the flax to work  
Well to the flax’s task  
For a year to lay the nest  
To sleep beside me  
Think, think, girls  
Think a little about me  
I will put the flax to work  
Well to the flax’s task  
For a month to patch coats  
For a day to seek heads.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What kind of mischief are you looking for with us  
You came to fulfill yourself with us  
Are you looking for horse sausage  
Or do you want stallion sausage  
You have run out of horse sausages  
There were stallion sausages in the street  
If you want, dance along  
If you feel like it, roll along.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Father made a sacred grove yard  
At the gate a wide forest  
Ordered me to live in the grove  
To sing in the forest  
To howl at the tavern's edge  
It was beautiful to live in the grove  
It was joyful to sing in the forest  
To howl at the tavern's edge  
Don't go to the forest to break it.  
To the back forest to kill  
Don't break the calf  
Don't kill the farm's lambs  
Otherwise, break the maidens  
Kill the farm's daughters.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If I were Olev  
I would carry Kalev  
I would have a long mind  
I would buy a new horse  
I would take a young woman  
I fear a young weeper  
The young want to eat little bits and pieces  
The beloved wants to eat fish  
For the little ones, fine bread  
There are no fish in the sea for our land  
Sea trout in the village barn  
Where could the beloved get fish  
For the young to eat little bits and pieces  
For the little ones, fine bread.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
One thing I care about in blooming  
I resist lifting my feet  
One thing I care about in blooming  
Two, I fear when shouting  
When said very clearly  
Mine is not here  
My companion does not grow here  
Mine is in Holland  
My fiancé on the Peipsi trails.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I seem to have a lot of land  
A lot of land, I pay a lot  
A pair of oxen seem to be on my fence  
The nose of a younger horse  
A thousand for a girl’s ponytail  
A hundred for a girl’s belt  
Fifty for a hat  
Before, a girl paid a mark  
Now, a girl cannot pay forty  
Not fifty for a little whip  
Not sixty for a little cap  
A thousand for a girl’s ponytail  
A hundred for a girl’s belt  
Fifty for a hat.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The drunken lads tried to catch me
The town wheelwrights wanted me
Mother wouldn’t give, father wouldn’t put
Mother wouldn’t give to the scoundrel
Father wouldn’t put on the beam
Mother put me on bread
For the plowman, the sower
The one who mixes the seeds
Who has plowed long fields
And has plowed wide meadows
Mother wouldn’t put me to the hungry one.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If only that man were mine too
Who rides along the road there
A horse in front, when it’s a stove
A man behind, when it’s a log
A hat on his head, when it’s a bell
A belt like a rainbow in the rain.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Maidens, young girls  
What are you building for yourselves  
Winding wreaths on your heads  
Still, I will not take you  
I will not come to courtship at all  
I have three girls at home  
Who pull at my stockings  
Slip through my fingers shining  
The apple jumps to the blackberry  
Foam leaps into the pine tree  
Tears go to another household.  

Maiden, young girl  
Come now and drive  
Come to my village to guard  
To manage my property  
To milk one cow  
To nibble on rye bread  
To lock away the draft of bread  
Do not tire my hands  
To milk one cow  
Do not dull my knife  
While the bread is warming.  

Maiden, young girl  
Come indeed and drive  
I will bring a patterned apron  
One I will bring in blue silk  
Another in red apron  
A third in checkered  
The fourth hairy and ragged  
The fifth golden-striped  
Ellakene little brother  
You bring a checkered fist  
Matar the hairy one  
The water feminine and veined  
Kämmel with golden patterns  
Ellakene, young girl  
Come now and drive  
I have three barns outside  
One is nibbling on rye  
Another is rattling money  
The third is threshing rye grain  

Ellakene, little brother  
You have three barns outside  
One for mice to jump in  
Another for rats to crawl in  
The third to be scolded by cats.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went to propose with three
And from Saturday evening with joy
To the grey barn of the meadow
To the room of the ash stove
I myself go inside to look
The daughter is dozing on the chair
The old woman is weaving with a spindle
The mother-in-law is by the hearth's edge
The louse goes to the corner, with a cap on
The little kids walked with wheat in their mouths
To sweep the room into the corners
To start the fire with kindling
The thicket weeps like a rag
Here would be raking with a rake
Here would be stirring with a shovel
On the dull shovel scrubbing.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin girl, young maiden  
Come to me and push  
If you do not come, at least ask  
You will be left entirely at home  
As a path on father’s hayfield  
As a furrow on brother’s field  
As a hump on the threshing floor  
As a child’s pimples on the face  
If you do not come, do not ask  
You will be left standing as fodder  
Growing among heather  
Damp and rotting in the grain.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If you were a man to me  
If that partner were gone  
Who rides along the road there  
Rides along the path  
Horse under, if it’s the fire  
On oneself, if it’s the day  
Hat like a king’s crown  
Belt like a rainbow in the rain  
If that man does not take me  
Then I will start to complain  
Horse under, let it mold  
On oneself, let it cycle  
Hat on the head, let it get cold.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I do not disdain the little children  
I leave behind the little old men  
The plow legs of my parish  
A beauty is coming from afar  
A lovely one from a distance.  
A little bird near the town  
A little picture from other lands  
It brings money for the rider  
Rings on the edge of the sieve  
An old coin on the beam  
Turns on the cold stove.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
That blissful little evening arrives  
That miserable little twilight  
Everyone goes with their own  
The brother goes with his sister-in-law  
The boy goes with the girl  
But who will I go with?  
I go crying towards the corner  
Towards the cold, chilly bed  
The sideboards have chilled  
The edgeboards have frosted  
The flea was proud, did not speak  
The bug did not make a sound  
Straw below, straw above  
Straw around the bedposts  
There we must keep the marital peace  
And also maintain harmony after the wedding

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Tiiut-tiiut Tiiu’s thighs,  
The bear had clawed Kaie’s thighs,  
The wolf had bitten Anne,  
The matchmaker had put a wax  
From below near the navel  
And a few fingers from above near the buttocks.  
I surely heard, when it was done to me,  
Behind the corner, they were having sex,  
By the wall, they were making love.  
The woman did it quickly, the man did it quickly,  
The old woman did it quickly for me,  
But ordered the sacks to be quickly full of moths,  
The old penises to shake.  
The moth hits were terrible,  
The sex hits were more messed up.  
The old woman’s toes were trembling,  
The old man’s sacks were rattling.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
I would go to a man  
To whom would I go  
I do not want to be a miller  
The miller's stone grinds  
I do not want to be a tinker  
The tinker's pot rattles  
I do not want to be a blacksmith  
The blacksmith himself was black  
The blacksmith's son was pitch black  
I want to be a proper farmer  
Turner of black soil  
From there you can get rye bread  
Every day you can have your own bread  
On Sunday you get special bread.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
I would go to a man  
To whom would I go  
I do not want to be a miller  
The miller's stone grinds

The tinker's pot rattles  
I do not want to be a blacksmith  
The blacksmith himself was black  
The blacksmith's son was pitch black  
I want to be a proper farmer  
Turner of black soil  
From there you can get rye bread  
Every day you can have your own bread  
On Sunday you get special bread.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My daughter is a virgin  
She has many suitors  
When I start to guess  
One was Hantsu, with a sparse beard  
The second Lauri, with a wide mouth  
The third Mihkel, with a small mouth  
The fourth Marti, with swollen feet  
The fifth a servant of Virviku  
The sixth the six-ruble Jaan  
The seventh better than these  
The eighth a fisherman's son  
The ninth the commander's son  
The tenth the village smith's son.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The evening comes unexpectedly  
Without the sign of twilight  
The evening brings straw into the house  
Straw stalks together in the ear  
People in pairs  
Each partner by the neck  
Who has no partner, take a cat  
Who has no cat, take a rat  
Rat to the grassy bush  
Mole to the molehill  
I have none of my own here  
My partner does not grow here  
Mine is in Holland  
My partner lives far away  
Five was exchanged outside  
Six were bad rivers  
Seven was a strong old woman  
Eight fish of the sea  
Nine good rivers  
Ten cold greyness  
Give a drink to the son  
He drinks black mud from the puddle  
He drinks grey from the grey hill  
From rocks broken on the boat  
That brings money to the horseman  
On the horse market to buy  
To sift the ear of grain  
May the wind bring health  
May the cloud send desire  
Heaven for a wise spirit  
I, poor one, always alone  
An abandoned little creature  
pag, 1753 I have no father  
I have no mother  
No flower sister  
Nor a young brother  
The wolf has scared me off  
The bear has raised me  
The forest boar has played with me  
When the gold coins go home  
Each gold coin with a gold partner  
I go home fatherless  
I go home motherless  
I step home without a helper  
I roll home without brothers  
To the stone, to the stump  
To the willow bush, to the hazel bush  
Into the blacksmith's straight lap  
The stone does not understand maiden’s words  
The rock does not heed maiden’s prayers.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What are you guarding, old bachelor?  
You should sometimes take a wife.  
Your beard has already turned gray,  
The edges of your eyes covered in mold,  
The edges of your ears flaking,  
Your mouth runs deadly water,  
Grave water of the gray beard,  
Your nose drips with tin.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What is this about, because of this  
That I became a slave  
I fell to a poor child  
I bowed my head to a servant  
I am a slave to my own  
A proper wage to a boy  
Where is the slave’s barley field  
The foster boy’s rye field  
In the swamp plays the servant's wife  
The willow, the wage boy’s wife  
The wolf eats the servant’s wife  
The bear, the foster boy’s wife  
So let me say again  
The wolf eats the stolen geldings  
The bear, the combed horses.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My sister called me a beer drunkard  
My brother called me a wine drunkard  
Käli called me a peasant from Kal’la  
I called the bird too clever  
The hen cunning until the last drop  
I sow barley in the valley  
Oats by the river bank  
Rye ears in the spruce forest  
I went to sell a horse  
I got money for the horse skillfully  
I went to sell an ox  
I got strong meat from the ox  
I went to shoot a bear  
I got a full-length bear coat  
Then I go to the town for confirmation  
To get a haircut in Paide  
Asking and guessing  
Are you the king’s son  
Or are you the councilman of Riga  
I hear, I answer right away  
But I am Trill, the drummer boy  
The old gentleman’s spirit boy.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little maiden, young girl
Throw a floral wreath into the swamp
A cedar wreath to the doorstep
A patchwork one for the ceiling
Why are you hesitant to come to us
We have apple trees outside
We have berry bushes in the house
We have plank ladders
To climb up into the apple tree
To come down from the berry bush.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh, my dear old grandmother  
My precious dear old man  
Why did you sell your daughter  
Barter the home to Canada  
Was it anger at the drunkard  
Or was it right for the beer drunkard  
You also traded the fabric shop  
You also softened the lime workers  
You wore out the sleeves of the clothes  
Before you sold your daughter  
Barter the home to Canada  
For that bad money  
Half a crown and a penny  
Oh, my dear old grandmother  
When you go to fetch water from the well  
The well bucket falls on your neck  
When you go to fetch wood from the pile  
The wood stick falls on your chest  
If your daughter were home  
She would grab by the neck  
By the wood stick on the chest  
Rescue your death from your mouth  
Defend you from your enemy’s power  
Now you've given her to a bad man  
Sent her to announce evil  
To pound the bad deed  
Leena, dear young maiden  
It’s no longer ten or more  
When there is only the only daughter  
When she goes out to plow the field  
The midwife’s son goes to harrow  
The only daughter frets in the field  
Speaks ill words  
Accuses the oath woman.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What was wrong before to be  
Before, that girl cost many  
Faraway two marks  
Darlings three marks  
Maiden four marks  
Little Virves five marks.  
Girls six marks  
Silk flowers seven marks  
Now you cannot get a girl for four marks  
Nor a little Virve for five marks  
Nor a girl’s neck for six marks  
Nor an eye for seven marks  
The spirited ones strike fire  
The fiery sparks from sunken legs  
Lightning strikes from the legs of a foal  
Looking for a girl  
Listening to the girl’s neck  
Now put a thousand on the crown  
Now put a hundred on the braid  
Fifty on the hat  
Big brooch on the chest  
Gold necklace on the neck  
Silver beads for decoration  
A ruble coin for a pendant  
Wear the necklace on the neck  
Build the girl up  
Then only go to propose marriage  
Then go to ask the father  
To show respect to the gentleman  
Then the village will hear and rejoice  
The women will know and be envious  
The wicked women will hear  
Then the malicious ones come barking  
Ruining the bad matter  
Some are loud and throwing insults  
Sending away empty-handed  
Try to hold them gently  
Try to entice them with liquor  
Tempt with a bun  
Then you’ll get the maiden  
As a companion in the household.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
From the village, I brought quite a gray one
From the market, I brought a bright red one
From the devil, I brought golden ears
From under the skirt, I give hay
In a bundle, I carry oats
Did not see the old woman’s gentle eyes
Nor hear the old man’s golden ears
Five of the brother without knowledge
Six of the sister without hearing
This horse became beautiful
Then I put it in a gray harness
Then I put it in a black uniform
Ears adorned with rings
I rode under the father-in-law’s door
I rode under the mother-in-law’s door
Hello, father-in-law, do you recognize me?
Hello, mother-in-law, do you recognize me?
Give me your daughter
Father-in-law hears, answers back
Mother-in-law hears, answers back
You cannot give your daughter
If I give your daughter away
The room will be empty of the daughter
The barn will be poor of sheep
The chamber empty of fabric
What are you afraid of when giving to me
Giving to me, giving to me
I have a home with three barns
One is a famous grain barn
Another a silver barn
The third a white copper barn
I put her to live in the room
To walk in the herb garden
Between glass windows
On a purple floor
At our marble dining table
With velvet wall beams
With pearl doorposts
With necklace wells
Gray under our door
A river behind our room
Fish wide in the ponds
Large roaches by the shores
Guards by our water’s edge
Waterfowl moving
Duck flocks swimming
Swans flying luxuriously
Heard by father-in-law, answered by father-in-law
Take that daughter away
Heard by mother-in-law, answered by mother-in-law
I will give you an honor
Set before you a beauty
The brother said five ways
Keep that sister well
Dear sister-in-law warm
If you come to us as a stranger
The horse neighs in the hay barn
The stallion shouts in the oats barn
Hay is offered before you
Oats are carried before you
October’s spinning will be done
If you keep the sister angry
To the young maiden’s tears
A lover looks at you
A pitcher is carried to you
You would be pushed out of the yard
From the gate by the lone old woman
From the street to the grazing path
Then go travel to Riga
Roam Harjumaa
To feed the Finnish pigs
In Riga, you will sleep in a barn
In Finland, in the pig pasture
In Harju between garden fences
Housewives, tall women
Farm women, wise women
Neat women, sharp women
Drink cream, give milk
Take butter to the threshers
When the serf comes home
They pick those weeds
They look for thistles
To put in front of the serf
The serf eats, eyes watery
Cheeks wide on the fields
Eyebrows dripping gold
Let the day of death come once
Let us go to Mary’s land
Surely there judgment will be made
Then you will be thrown to hell
There your bones will be dried
Surely then you want to pay
You walk to the prison tree
In fabric in two pieces
A ball of yarn in your fingers
Come down, little serf
I will start giving wages
To a longer plowman
To a wider weaver
I hear, I answer
What do I do in the berry fields
If I didn’t give in to the land
Where I broke ribs
Snapped neck bones
Doing big work
Walking around the manor
Under the evil bailiff’s stick
Out in the yard of the cruel lord
There were five exorcists
Six behind the midwife
Seven back thinkers
Eight there were cowherds
Nine there were callers
A lover even behind the back
Two pitchers in hand
Pulled along the belly down
Along the wide robe
Then my envelope leapt
Half a coat put in place
A strike along the wide robe
The serf cries in the room
Complains to the dear maiden
That he could go home
