Beds inside the barns.  
What is under the beds?  
Pillows under the beds!  
What is on the pillows?  
Four young maidens,  
One is crying for a young man,  
Another is weaving a silver belt,  
The third is making a golden wreath;  
The fourth is crying for her father and mother.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
What is there below our yard?  
The sea is below our yard.  
What is in the middle of the sea?  
There was a well in the middle of the sea.  
What was under the edge of the well?  
A pillar was under the edge of the well.  
What was on top of the pillar?  
A sieve was on top of the pillar.  
What was on the bottom of the sieve?  
A brooch was on the bottom of the sieve.  
What was on the edge of the brooch?  
A ring was on the edge of the brooch.  
Where was this ring brought from?  
Brought from there, made there.  
From the finger bone of a soldier  
From the knee bone of a boy  
Kapu

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
What is there below our yard?  
The sea is below our yard.  
What is in the middle of the sea?  
There was a well in the middle of the sea.  
What was under the edge of the well?  
A pillar was under the edge of the well.  
What was on top of the pillar?  
A sieve was on top of the pillar.  
What was on the bottom of the sieve?  
A brooch was on the bottom of the sieve.  
What was on the edge of the brooch?  
A ring was on the edge of the brooch.  
Where was this ring brought from?  
Brought from there, made there.  
From the finger bone of a soldier  
From the knee bone of a boy  
Kapu

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Wait, wait, old woman, no, no old man!  
If you don’t give me a wife,  
Then I will wander to the village,  
Along the village barn roofs,  
Along the barn roof, across the barn roof,  
Sometimes along the spaces in between.  
I will make little paths on the roads  
For the scoundrels under the gate.  
I will do it all for the old woman’s soul,  
I will do it all for the old man’s soul,  
Upon the necks of two brothers.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Our beautiful haymower  
Nice curved twister  
Slender arranger of haystacks,  
In front was the hay to be gathered  
At the back to be taken to the stack,  
In the middle to be twisted into hay  
To gather the arches together. —  
Until the hayfield was beautiful  
Until the hay was mown  
Until the arches were more beautiful  
Until the stacks were taken  
Until the sheaves were slender,  
Until the pile was under the animals  
Until the little heap  
Was taken home.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Father, this little light  
Buy a golden dog for the yard  
A rusted lock for the gate  
So that the men can pass by  
So there is time to make a deal  
To roll the little ring  
To write wheel gloves  
Let the wolf eat the smith’s dog  
Break the smith’s black dog  
Let the gray dog bite  
Let the smith’s speckled dog tear  
So that I could secretly go  
Among the smith’s daughters  
Among the smith’s women's neighbors.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Young girls, little doves  
Keep yourselves in the buildings  
Protect yourselves in the chambers  
Hide in the pile of grain  
Go to the shadows of the hedge. -  
Now the wolves are roaming  
With bristly tails moving  
Wide paws going down  
If you don't keep to the buildings  
Protect yourselves in the chambers. -  
Then the rooks will carry you in their arms  
The farm boys in the kerchief  
The owls on their shoulders. -

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Young men of Kuksema parish  
Go to church on St. John’s Day  
Under the horse as if it were a cart  
On themselves as if it were a bench  
Wore a coat, hair uncovered  
Pants on, bare behind  
A short rein as a belt  
A piece of rope on the hat  
They ask the beggar for money  
From the beggar a coin. –  
Young men of Metsataha parish  
Go to church on St. John’s Day  
Under the horse as if it were an oven  
The man was on as if it were a day  
Coat like the moon’s servant  
Belt like a rain rainbow –  
They give the beggar money  
To the beggar a coin.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
When I went to courtship
To the suitor with three horses
A gray donkey in front of the barn
A Lauk gelding in front of the stable
In front of the ashwood room.
I peered through the doorframe
I peered through the stick hole. -
The daughter was dozing on the chair nail
The old lady was watching the carriage nail. -
There would be timid stepping
Sneaking on the old stove. -
Bugs dance with straws in their mouths
The louse went to the corner carrying a noose on its back. -

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went to the lake to fetch water with a pail
What did I find in the lake?
Liisuke with a linen headscarf
She had beads around her neck
Pearls were shining on her necklace
Come here to me, Liisuke
I will put a comb in your hair
I will braid a crown into your hair. -
Liisu understood, answered back
Dear one, brother
Put the comb in the birch
Braid a crown into the spruce
Before you braid it into my hair.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin, young maiden  
Slender in figure  
Come to me and mine  
I will put you to work on the field  
I will not take you into the rain  
Nor bring you into the wind  
Place yourself in the sunshine  
I will grow barley to the yard edges  
Oats to the chambers  
Flax to the threshing floor  
You may live, you may exist  
You grow barley in the valleys  
Oats by the lake shores  
Rye in the pine forest  
Flax along half the town's road  
Then you will bring me into the rain  
Then you will bring me into the wind  
Place yourself in the sunshine  
Bring the evil dew with you.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My heart called me to go courting  
My navel wanted to take a wife  
Father would not allow  
Mother did not forbid the ornament  
Brother did not forbid the brass comb  
Sister did not forbid the silver ring  
Aunt did not forbid the handkerchief  
Sister did not forbid the mitten  
Mother did not forbid the thread spool  
I put my hat on the table  
Then I began to cry  
Then father gave me a horse  
Mother did give the ornament  
Brother gave the brass comb  
Sister gave the silver ring  
Aunt gave the handkerchief  
Sister gave the mitten  
Mother gave the thread spool. –  
Then I went to Narva to fetch a wife  
There were three girls there  
One was the daughter of a rich family  
Another was the daughter of poor people  
The third was the slave’s daughter. –  
I took the poor child in between  
Starting with the slave’s daughter. –  
She was a girl who had worked  
A poor child who had seen hardship. –

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went to Koiki's suitors  
To Koigi's three farm daughters  
One was the daughter of a spindle  
The second was the daughter of a yarn wreath  
The third was the daughter who worked.  
What was the daughter of the spindle  
She was naked on the stove  
What was the daughter of the yarn wreath  
She was bare-assed on the bench  
What was the daughter who worked  
She was in the corner tied up.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
We patch up ships, we patch up ships  
We make other new ships  
We put the ships to row  
The ships row, the ships reach  
They go down to the city of Riga  
There were maidens in the city  
In the front row, they wore elm necklaces  
In the back row, they wore thaler necklaces  
In the middle row, they wore twisted necklaces  
Ellakene, vellakene  
If you go to take a wife  
Break those chain necklaces  
Trample down the thaler necklaces  
Let’s knock down the twisted necklaces  
Sometimes let’s take a poor girl  
Right from the slave’s yoke  
A diligent girl comes out of slavery  
A meek girl comes out of poverty.  
She then knows how to manage  
Knows the measures of work  
Her eyes peer inward  
Her eyes glance outward  
The center has a bear’s paws  
Between are the raven’s feet  
She then knows how to manage  
She knows how to walk firmly  
A barefoot girl without a ladder  
A cow without a chandelier for milking.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The billy goat came from Turkey  
The nanny goat came from Hiiumaa  
I grabbed the billy goat by the horn  
The nanny goat by the ear  
I took them to the smith from Viru  
The smith made a horse from the horn  
A saddle from the tip of the horn  
I'm going to the town  
Where the streets shimmer  
There the girls squat  
Squatting, standing  
In front was an elm leaf  
Behind was a linden leaf  
Between them were poor children.  
Still, I won't take you  
Won't take you, won't leave the other  
I will go to Kooku to court  
To the three daughters of Kooku’s bailiff  
One is Anne with a pointed foot  
The second Kaie with a crooked hand  
The third Maie with a puckered mouth.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The billy goat came from Turkey  
The nanny goat came from Hiiumaa  
I grabbed the billy goat by the horn  
The nanny goat by the ear  
I took them to the smith from Viru  
The smith made a horse from the horn  
A saddle from the tip of the horn  
I'm going to the town  
Where the streets shimmer  
There the girls squat  
Squatting, standing  
In front was an elm leaf  
Behind was a linden leaf  
Between them were poor children.  
Still, I won't take you  
Won't take you, won't leave the other  
I will go to Kooku to court  
To the three daughters of Kooku’s bailiff  
One is Anne with a pointed foot  
The second Kaie with a crooked hand  
The third Maie with a puckered mouth.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My little bird went to the town  
My sweetheart went to the borough  
To the fish shore, a little hut  
Young men from Petersburg’s groom  
God knows this  
Dear Jesus thinks  
Will he come home or not  
Will he bring bread or not  
The town was empty even of bread  
The other town even of loaf  
Even if he came home  
So he wouldn’t remain a burden to the town  
A raw one on the market. -  
Town birds eat the meat  
Crabs on the shore eat the fat  
Viru crows drank the blood. -  
The town birds became meaty  
The crabs on the shore became fat  
When eating a man’s meat  
When drinking a man’s blood.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
All the women I had had field breasts  
The breasts of the tested women were nipples  
My wife had field nipples  
But I went to the garden  
Picked two heads of cabbage  
From them I got nipples for my wife  
All the women had thighs  
My wife had field thighs  
I took the thighs of an old stallion  
From them I got thighs for my wife.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
But then they were brought inside
And given a seat
And asked and listened
Whether the servant was a house boy
The family daughter had a fiancé
The horse was down like the oven
The man was above like the day
The hat like the church bell
The belt like the flag of the city of Riga.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
So that mamma would just be the same  
Why don't you give me your daughter  
I will bring half a mat of strawberries  
Half a mat of lingonberries  
Oh oh, Oisu men  
No no, young men  
You will see what you get  
You will surely have a girl hungry  
A girl hungry, lacking drink  
You will surely visit the whole family  
At the family gate of the Persel  
Give that daughter to me  
Who swings in the arms  
Sways in silver bridles  
Under the bell straps  
Falls into weak carrying  
The girl was weak, the groom was weak  
The girl was weak for the dishes  
For the little boat’s catching

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh our dear brothers or we brothers-in-law  
Together we are stepbrothers  
We eat the same food  
We drink the same drink  
We do not take the same wife  
One takes another wife  
One takes another old woman's daughter  
One takes another old man's daughter  
You take Maie, I take Kaie  
Kaie was quick with work  
Mai was clumsy with her foot  
Anne was assigned to me  
And Kaie was traded to me  
I do not take Anne as a wife  
Nor do I take Kaie as a wife  
Anne was given torn shirts  
Kaie had broken sleeves  
I flirted around Liisu  
Had to go to Pilla  
I ended up sleeping with Malle  
Malle was sweeter to me  
Than those Pilla's milk cakes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Do not make other men
Who have made our Mart
Act crazy
Swap old horses
Take a widow woman
The widow was a woman wasted on bread
Old horse to the hay door
Buy a horse foal
Take a woman from the maidens
Then you will be a fashionable gentleman
But you do things another way
Pay taxes in other ways
Still live in the landlord's way
Just like the Riga aldermen
I ride on the road to Tipula
At the Riga road crossroads
Let me go down the Riga track
From there I bring a woman with studs
A tiny little pistol
Tin-and-chest crossed on the neck
Wants to weave cloth
Tin-and-thread to twist
Wagon to roll earlier
Silver-and-suds to rub
They, however, go nika naka
Sole boards to the stall floor
Salt fence to the fence post.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If I were to exist  
Secondly, my doing  
I would surely know what I would do  
I would open the window to the wind  
Saunas by the edge of the meadow  
I would heat the room with firewood  
Sauna with the beginnings of the island  
Still, I did not get the warmth  
That I got from Anne's love  
From Kaie the dear kindness  
From Liisu, Ella’s companions  
I had to go to Pille  
I fell asleep at Malle’s  
Malle is sweeter to me  
Than those Pille’s cream cakes

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If I were to exist  
Secondly, my doing  
I would surely know what I would do  
I would open the window to the wind  
Saunas by the edge of the meadow  
I would heat the room with firewood  
Sauna with the beginnings of the island  
Still, I did not get the warmth  
That I got from Anne's love  
From Kaie the dear kindness  
From Liisu, Ella’s companions  
I had to go to Pille  
I fell asleep at Malle’s  
Malle is sweeter to me  
Than those Pille’s cream cakes

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let's go to the Koiki bride's meadow
Daughters of the Koigi smith
The smith of Koigi had three daughters
One was Anne - the second Kaie
The third slippery Liisukene
Anne was naked by the stove
Kaie was bare-legged by the ironing board
Liisu was under the flax bundle
Anne was meant for me
And Kaie was also bargained for me
I will not take Anne as a wife
Nor do I want to marry Kaie
Anne had torn shirt sleeves
Kaie had broken stockings
I crawled around Liisu
Liisu had beautiful linen.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What is here beneath our yard  
The sea beneath our yard  
What is there in the middle of the sea  
A well in the middle of the sea  
What is there on the edge of the well  
A post on the edge of the well  
What is on top of the post  
A sieve on top of the post  
What is on the edge of the sieve  
A cuckoo on the edge of the sieve  
What is on the cuckoo's hands  
A child was on the cuckoo's hands  
What is on the child's chest  
A brooch on the child's chest  
What is on the edge of the brooch  
Even this is a soldier's ring  
How did it get to this land  
From the kneecaps of stone boys  
Iron shafts from German calves

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
For I have a servant  
That my bride walks with a wreath,  
Under the window of the manor,  
Digging a stone ditch  
Turning muddy soil.  
May the wind bring her health,  
May the hail carry away her books,  
May the rain warm her words,  
May the clouds long mourn  
In the sky of wise understanding.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Old maid in misery,  
Iron in mouth and collars on neck,  
She herself in front of the cart,  
I have a small whip in my hand,  
I keep hitting the girl.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ants was from Arju, Jaan was from Järva,  
Koka Miku was from Koala,  
He bought a casque from Arjusta,  
A saddle from Saare's German.  
Then he went on a trip to Finland,  
With the daughter of a Finnish merchant.  
One was Anne, another Maie,  
The third Kaie from Kierukanda.  
Anne was naked by the stove,  
Mai was bare-bottomed on the pig pen,  
Kai was lying face down on the roof,  
Leenu was under the stake,  
Holding a soup ladle;  
Listen, dear young man:  
Why are you traveling on the road?  
I am looking for my girl,  
A redhead from Piiakesta.  
Listen, dear young man,  
Is there an iron wagon,  
A bream-scaled sleigh,  
A little red one,  
A small grayish one,  
Why do you come to Kurna to the wedding,  
Do you come to Nagala to take a wife?  
Come, you dear one, to listen to gold,  
To look for a bride,  
To bargain for a tin chest,  
To look at a copper chest.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Come to me, Marikene,  
Come to me and promise,  
I will put you to easy work,  
To thresh the rye,  
To separate the bundles,  
To straighten the ties,  
To trample down the chaff.  
Mari heard the answer clearly,  
Soon replied in turn:  
Dear brother,  
Boy, young lad,  
Is there easy work?  
There is easy work:  
To make a bed in the hut,  
To mend socks for the summer,  
To patch coats for the month,  
To search for heads by day.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Nõnna, nõnna dear souls,  
Nõnna, nõnna dear brothers,  
Nõnna was a poor widow,  
As the roof was without shingles;  
All the snowstorms come upon her,  
All the rains fall upon her.  
I turn my side, find cold,  
I turn my back, find a wall;  
All the boards on the side are cold,  
The edge boards are warped,  
The wall boards are warped.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Nõnna, nõnna dear souls,  
Nõnna, nõnna dear brothers,  
Nõnna was a poor widow,  
As the roof was without shingles;  
All the snowstorms come upon her,  
All the rains fall upon her.  
I turn my side, find cold,  
I turn my back, find a wall;  
All the boards on the side are cold,  
The edge boards are warped,  
The wall boards are warped.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mother, oh dear granny,  
Put this daughter into a husband,  
Whoever rocks the cradle,  
Under the ribbons of the skirt;  
If you don’t put her, don’t ask,  
Let her end up wandering at home,  
Idle by the fence’s edge,  
Picking mushrooms by the wall.  
May a spruce grow over her eyebrows,  
May a birch grow over her neck,  
A linden over the rye,  
A juniper among the hairs.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What I fear in the land of poems,  
In the land of poems, in the land of songs,  
Is that my own is not here,  
That my companion does not grow here.  
My own is in Holland,  
My companion far away,  
My beloved is at Peipus,  
A fisherman across the sea,  
Trying to catch dear fish  
Lovely perch.  
Monday brings smelt,  
Tuesday brings young cod,  
Wednesday brings nice fish,  
Thursday perch,  
Friday Riga crayfish,  
Saturday broad ruffe,  
Sunday fishing fish.  
May the wind bring health,  
Riga paths bring books,  
Clouds may be long and endless.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Before I go to the thief,  
To the predator in the barn;  
Before I go to the herdsman;  
The herdsman loses the herd,  
But then they come looking for me  
In the fog, in the morning,  
To the thief in the white,  
Fog spoils my new coat,  
Rain spoils the sleigh cover,  
Dew on the white coat  
Is the father’s punishment.  
Bring me the cover, take that carpet,  
Wipe my apples clean,  
Even the saddle of the father is filthy.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ellakene, little brother,  
When you go to take a wife,  
Take a sister under your wing;  
The sister knows a dull girl,  
From the barn knows a lazy girl,  
The dull girl has long hair,  
The lazy girl has wide hair,  
A harlot has tangled hair.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little boy, little rascal,  
When you go to take a wife,  
Come ask me for permission;  
I will give you a new coat from the barn,  
Bring a stick from the lantern,  
A wooden spoon from the totter.  
Then go to Koigi to court.  
On the mound of Koigi are three daughters;  
One was Anne, the second Kaie,  
The third slippery little Liisuke.  
Anne was given to me,  
Kaie was traded to me,  
Liisuke I infused myself.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What do you complain to me about when you come,  
When you come to me, when you come to me,  
To me, a young man,  
With a high hat on your head?  
I will put the work on my shoulders,  
To find a day for you,  
To make an arrangement for a year.  
Why do you praise empty things,  
Waste your time,  
I didn't come to you yesterday,  
I came to you the day before yesterday.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Three women in our parish:
Liisu Lints, Mai Mats,
Anu Otsa from Oriküla.
They went in front of Kukk's tavern,
Bought together a dozen bottles of vodka,
Boys drank themselves drunk,
Went to the family gate,
Deceiving the family boys,
Making the neighbors laugh:
Help us to the barn,
Carry us to the cattle shed,
Take us to the distillery room,
There our place is ready long ago.
The sheet is under the bird pattern,
The floorboard has a duck pattern,
The quilt on top has a grouse pattern.
Take my belt from my waist,
Pull my gloves off my hands,
Light my candle up,
Dress into my chest to rest.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Why do you complain to me when you come  
When you come to me, when you reach me,  
I bring a cross to you from Riga,  
A cross from the city of Riga,  
A necklace from Kuressaare,  
From the store I bring you a long apron,  
A forester’s cap from Tontisaare.  
Let me say again around,  
New words again,  
Again back to the old order,  
You strike my chest with your fist,  
That cross of yours from Riga;  
You twist your neck twice,  
That necklace of yours from Kuressaare;  
You beat your knees red,  
That long apron of yours from the store.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I would like to sleep  
In Mardi’s lap,  
Under Hans’s rug,  
In Kaarli’s warm cloak,  
In Peeter’s long linen,  
In Tõnu’s tanner’s clothes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, my life is boring  
Or my celebration is dark  
Tearful young knees  
Father went to bed with mother  
Brother went with a young girl  
Sister went with her husband  
I went crying towards the corner  
Quietly towards the wall  
Slipping towards the oven  
I fumble with an old fur coat,  
Oh, you old fur coat!  
You are too old to walk around  
Better to sleep on the backside  
If only I had a husband  
If only I had a rooster hen  
What rides along the road there  
Grain falling down like in the oven  
The man is on top like the day  
The cup like a church bell  
The belt like the city’s flag ribbon  
The cuckoo fell to his claws  
The Alli bird to her husband  
Little head on top of the head  
Living elk are grain  
The street crackled in the yard  
Clutched with the lock of golden nails  
Rode over the Finnish bridge  
Rubbed the Finnish bridge  
Pounding the floorboards  
Water ran from the maiden’s eyes  
Water, those maiden’s eyes  
On top of that beauty  
On top of a grouse-feather blanket  
On top of a perch-tail lake.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Village women are gossips  
Old women are deceitful  
They wanted to disgrace me  
Prematurely careless  
I would have known to work diligently  
Nor do they respect diligence  
You told me to sleep in the barn  
Under the barn roof  
Among the coachmen and drivers  
Under the stable boys' covers  
Under the farmhands' shoulders  
Then I would have half men as sons  
The child would be as tall as the table.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Some men are of a certain kind,
Some men hold the whip,
Some wash the harness,
Some choose the oxen,
Against this evil suitor.
Now the riders are mad,
The drivers more miserable,
The whip bearers blind.
They do not see the maidens,
Beautiful as apple blossoms,
Red as lingonberries,
Straight as wild rose flowers,
White as maple tree blossoms.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I surely have ten willing ones,
Nine admirers,
If I am to count:
One was Hantsu’s boatman,
Second Mart’s mule driver,
Third was Jaan’s small spruce,
Fourth was a worthy maiden,
Fifth was a servant of Virvik,
Sixth was a famous horn blower,
Seventh was worthy of a smith,
Eighth was Kalevipoeg,
Ninth was across the sea,
Tenth was a visitor from Kubjas.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin maidens, young girls,  
Timid little flowers.  
Green little roses.  
Think a little of me,  
Of me, the son of a man,  
The ploughman, the sower,  
The explainer of seeds,  
The thrower down of grain.  
Come to me, I have a visit,  
Come visit me,  
To see a treasure,  
The maiden hears and answers:  
"You have no guardian for your visit,  
No early watcher.  
A shepherd sits at your head during the visit,  
The father-in-law by the edge of the bed.  
It must be in the corner of the room."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mother said on her deathbed,  
Father breathed his last,  
You will stay behind,  
A little head to the end.  
"Do not go to the village,  
Do not roll to the cemetery.  
Let the maidens go to the village,  
The white ones roll to the cemetery.  
Koeo is coming to propose,  
To the gate to the colorful arch,  
Behind the heap to the willow branch.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Bride boy:
Do not laugh at the maiden,
Do not scorn the girl maiden,
She is still a daughter.
Let the servant children be,
Let the girls do work,
The servant child is unbroad,
The girl is unworked,
The lineage unscolded,
The old mother unvowed.
You may get a German wife,
Court a king's daughter.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Girls, little birds,  
Lovely marguerites.  
I will weave if you want to come to me,  
Come to me, I am having guests:  
The barn has a clever horse:  
The rooster crows with much money,  
Don't plow with ten pairs,  
The harrow hums nine.  
The rake scratches eight,  
The sickle cuts seven,  
The scythe falls six,  
Mows five with a sickle.  
The girl hears a voice answering:  
"What are you talking about for nothing?  
I was at your place yesterday.  
You had mice under the yoke.  
Cats were outside the barn.  
Rats between the cracks,  
The rabbits were in the barn harvest.  
You put (them) by the edge of the gate,  
You have no money for chopping,  
Not a penny for anyone,  
Money remained on the paths,  
Pennies on the straw table."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Girls, little birds,
Come to me a little.
I will set the work on the shoulder;
Then I will make socks for the needle:
In winter, I will sift the tango,
Then I will feed the sugar:
In winter, to the buckwheat tangos.
If you don't come, don't ask! -
Let the house get moldy,
Let the oven slacken behind.
Behind the wall, let it get moldy,
Boys farting on top.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sons are rightful men,  
Nephews full men,  
They take debt but do not steal,  
Nor break down barn doors,  
Nor rip off coffin lids,  
Nor break chests and boards.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oval brother,  
Buy a horse from the foal,  
Take a wife from a maiden,  
Raise a dog from a puppy;  
When you come home from work,  
The woman laughs from the maiden,  
The dog barks from the puppy,  
The horse neighs from the foal.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Take greetings to the distant one,  
The man beyond the sea.  
Let the wind carry health to him,  
Let the bad weather offer prayers.  
Let the hail accompany the books,  
Let the clouds stretch with longing,  
Let the sky have a wise spirit.  
So many greetings to him,  
As many as there are forests in between.  
So many greetings to him,  
As many as there are pine trees in the forest.  
So many greetings to him,  
As many as there are twigs in the pine forest.  
So many greetings to him,  
As many as there are branches in the twigs.  
So many greetings to him,  
As many as there are leaves on the branches.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Before, there were many of us in the herd,  
A large number in the herd of Heagi,  
A large number of beautiful cattle.  
It grew like a herd of cattle,  
Moved like a village herd,  
Scattered like a flock of geese.  
Now the herd has come to an end!  
The group has disappeared,  
The brood has been scattered.  
No one knows where they have gone,  
They have been taken to Venje,  
Or put on a raft,  
Sent to the Western Sea.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I set out on the road in the evening,  
Went to the village threshing floor,  
The girls didn’t want to go,  
They said I wasn’t worth much,  
They started barking at me:  
You don’t have a calico shirt on your back  
Nor a pipe in your pocket,  
You don’t have German boots,  
Nor a kerchief around your neck,  
They slandered me with wide tongues,  
I came home with a sad mind,  
Laid my cap on the table,  
Gloves on the stove,  
Sat sadly behind the table,  
And a girl tried to talk to me.  
“Why do you complain, young boy?  
Tell me your wish.”  
Last evening I set out on the road,  
Went to the village threshing floor.  
The girls didn’t want me,  
They said I wasn’t worth much,  
They started barking at me,  
Barking and nodding,  
That you don’t have a calico shirt on your back  
Nor a pipe in your pocket,  
Nor German boots  
Nor a kerchief around your neck.  
“Don’t cry, young boy,  
Tomorrow I’m going to the Paide fair,  
From there I’ll bring you a calico shirt,  
Bring a pipe for your pocket,  
I’ll bring German boots for you,  
Then you can go courting again.”  
Then I set out again.  
Went to the village threshing floor,  
All the girls wanted to go with me,  
They said I was worth a lot,  
Thought I was a manor master,  
Considered me a steward,  
Declared me a chamberlain,  
Appointed me bailiff.  
I am Mihkel, son of grandma,  
Tõnis, son of another woman.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My sister, the beautiful one,  
The little strawberry,  
Tiny as a tin ridge,  
A charm of the copper fence.  
She went to the meadow hill,  
Between fields and farmsteads,  
With her head bowed, hair adorned.  
Who came calling to the house,  
Calling across the meadow:  
That to her dear brother,  
"Come home, my sister:  
Suitors came to your home,  
Four or five with bottles of vodka,  
Two or three with beer jugs,  
Hundreds of seven-fold messages."  
The sister asked her brother:  
Where were these suitors from?  
"From Finland, there were great Germans,  
From the bog lands, red robes,  
From Pärnu county, a local gentleman,  
I will not come, dear brother:  
Let them go if they have come,  
The gatepost will not tire,  
The ring of the gate will not creak,  
While holding the feast,  
Carrying the burden along."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Footmen away from the master,  
Feet off the road, clumsy feet.  
Bent feet to the side.  
The daughters of the household are coming!  
Heads white, wreaths red,  
Hands white, cuffs white,  
Silk shirts rise on their backs.  
Barking dogs under the wide fence.  
Their belts have shining embroidery,  
Half ball patchwork.  
Envelope with five-pointed star.  
Necks boiled with lice,  
Shoulders soaked in sweat,  
Breasts crossed and sore.  
Little hands with pearls.  
They do not want stable boys,  
Hearts do not call for coachmen,  
Minds do not think of the manor lord.  
Some are manor lords,  
Some do not want flax,  
Nor do they want cabbage linen,  
Some do not eat pork,  
Nor take cow butter,  
Nor touch chicken eggs.  
The pig has slept in filth,  
The cow has been scented by the ox,  
The hen has been spurred by the rooster.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Whose village are these young men from?  
They are from Kalitsa village.  
They are the hungry young men,  
Rough in manner.  
The young men of that village!  
They were the poor boys of the parish,  
Tormented by the parish,  
Forced by the stronger ones.  
They are going to Jaanipäev church:  
Hoboes underneath like that rag,  
Saddle on the back like that rascal.  
Themselves on top like a ghost.  
Hat placed on the head,  
Stockings patched on the foot.  
They had nails in their shoes,  
Two nails in the rags,  
Not to mention the tatters.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Whose village are these?
They are from Visusti village.
That is the golden village,
Green and flourishing.
The young men of our village:
Those chosen boys of the parish,
Praised by the district,
Are going to the midsummer church:
Cap down like a stove,
Saddle on like that German.
Confident as a master.
Hat like the moon's servant,
Belt like a rainbow,
Multicolored gloves, white stockings,
Which the grandmothers knitted,
Made by the old mothers.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Both of us are manor men,  
Comrades together.  
Both have swords at their belts,  
Sword tips pointing to the ground,  
The spur rattles from the boot,  
I am with a young maiden.  
We eat plenty, we drink plenty,  
We buy plenty without beer,  
We pay marks in cash.  
We go to the town road in formation,  
In a troop to the German camp.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The nightingale has a mino characteristic,  
The harsh one has a companion,  
Viiso had a little brother,  
Koordi had a little treasure,  
Today the family has a second half.  
It is difficult to live in the nightingale,  
It is beautiful to grow in the harsh one.  
Whoever goes to live in the nightingale,  
To grow in the harsh one.  
Let them bring stony shoes,  
Laces inside the shoes afterward.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little lads, little girls,  
You shouldn't be sleeping.  
Before going to the root (field),  
Feed the piece of bread.  
Why else would you be sleeping?  
You are making secret love  
If you don’t let there be a wedding,  
If you don’t have a black robe announced,  
Nor put money in a pair (for the bride).  
The courted maiden stays at home,  
The slept-with maiden is left behind,  
Sung about at the end of the table.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The men were taken because of their manners,  
The chiefs because of the large forest.  
The daughter was taken because of work,  
Because of their hands to the bridegroom.  
Neither the father because of worry,  
Nor the brother because of cattle.  
At home stayed the family hearth,  
In the yard stayed the father's horses,  
At the gate stayed the brother's oxen.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I was said to be the friend of a soldier,  
I was dug up as the companion of a fisherman,  
Sleeping near the Junkers,  
The fiancée of the outlaws.  
I am not the friend of a soldier,  
Nor the companion of a fisherman,  
Sleeping near the Junkers,  
Nor the fiancée of the outlaws.  
My friend is my sister’s husband,  
The neighbor is a virgin.  
I was said to sleep with men,  
I was said to keep boys,  
To love the husband.  
If I had slept with men,  
Perhaps I would have kept boys,  
Loved the husband.  
Then I would have a son half a man,  
A daughter a worker,  
A child grown tall enough for the table.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let's go, let's go, let's walk,  
Let's go, let's go, young maidens,  
Along the path of the sacred grove,  
Secretly behind the forest,  
To make the workdays,  
For the sighing of the servants!  
The younger son of the grove heard,  
Dear Kalev spoke:  
Come in, young maidens,  
Come to arrange the bed,  
To soften the pillow,  
To weave the golden fabric!  
"Annel has no time to come,  
She has business to do everywhere:  
In the manor, a striker awaits us,  
Behind the stable, a fighter,  
In the wine cellar, a stirrer."  
Come still, young maidens,  
Come for cheer,  
For joy to the heart.  
"The dove cannot come,  
Dear Kalev, little bird.  
Who then will make the workdays?  
The herd boys are walking,  
The barnyard lads are drowsing,  
The blacksmith's apprentice is bent over at his foot!"  
Let the servitude end,  
Let the workdays be done,  
Let the herd boys be looked after:  
Then, dear one, I will hear you,  
Longing for you, son of the grove.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What suits a wild man,
Is a miserable marriage,
With unkempt wives.
A wife does not make one grow,
A man does not like to take (one).
The wife asks for clothes,
The man for leather goods,
Everyone wants fine cloth,
Every head wants linen.
Everyone wants six new coats a month,
A week needs four shirts.
If one cannot get six new coats,
In a week not four shirts.
Then men know the belt!
They taste the sweetness of the pan,
Missing the fine cloth,
Missing the thin shoulder!

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Village old women grannies,  
Village old men grandfathers,  
The old mothers of the parish,  
The old men of Allipart:  
Do not take offense at this,  
Do not get angry at this,  
Do not quarrel with words,  
Do not scold me.  
For I sing near the village,  
Near the red town,  
Answering the white-walled woman.  
Here listens the king's son,  
Thinking about the bride,  
Watching with a copper gaze,  
Standing by in boots.  
"This maiden is named for me,  
A wreath on my head,  
I take her into my arms and embrace,  
Take her hand in mine,  
On my shoulder when going outside,  
In my embrace when going to sleep.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Uniformed women, uniformed jokes,
I have neither a wife nor jokes.
So I began to think,
To seek a little advice,
To get a wife somehow.
I took a rotten stump,
Then I got my wife's body,
Then she became like other women.
My wife lacked a belly,
I took an old woolen bundle,
Made my wife a belly,
Then she became like other women.
My wife lacked thighs,
I took old rune thighs,
Made thighs for my wife,
Then she became like other women.
My wife lacked legs,
I took old plow legs,
Made legs for my wife,
Then she became like other women.
There were no big toes on the feet,
I took old spindle handles,
Made big toes for my wife,
Then she became like other women.
My wife lacked arms,
I took an old cranking handle,
Made arms for my wife,
Then she became like other women.
My wife had no breasts,
I took two turnip roots,
Made breasts for my wife,
Then she became like other women.
My wife lacked a head,
I took two cabbage halves,
Made a head for my wife,
Then she became like other women.
My wife lacked ears,
I took old squirrel ears,
Made ears for my wife,
Then she became like other women.
My wife lacked eyes,
I took old hedgehog eyes,
From those I made eyes for my wife,
Then she became like other women.
My wife lacked a nose,
I took a well-dipper's hook,
Made a nose for my wife,
Then she became like other women.
My wife lacked a tongue,
I took an old goat's tongue,
Then I got a tongue for my wife,
Then she became like other women.
My wife lacked a throat,
I took an old frog's throat,
Made a throat for my wife,
Then she became like other women.
My wife lacked a soul,
I took an old sparrow's soul,
Then I got a soul for my wife,
Then she became like other women.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sister, little fool,  
Found a lover in the poplar grove,  
Found a companion in the birch grove,  
A husband in the forest thicket.  
I told the old woman too:  
My sister is a fool!  
Found a lover in the poplar grove,  
Found a companion in the birch grove,  
A husband in the forest thicket.  
The old woman said, let it be:  
"You must have wandered, sister,  
Said to the deceitful one:  
A trickster in the poplar grove,  
A jealous companion in the birch grove,  
A madman in the forest thicket."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Peeter was a tall, straight boy,  
Peeter had curly hair,  
Peeter was the proud son of Uku,  
Peeter was the wise son of Taara.  
When Peeter walked to the village,  
when Peeter came to the farm,  
[he was] tailoring new coats,  
making new blankets,  
tearing clothes into pieces,  
cutting fabric to shreds.  
Then the maidens started staring,  
the young women's eyes fixed on him,  
the girls peeking—  
the housewives feeling disappointed,  
persuading him to become a son-in-law,  
inviting him to be the daughter's companion,  
to give a grey stallion as a bride gift,  
a strong stallion,  
a beautiful grey bull,  
a pleasant black cow,  
golden pearls for wedding gifts,  
plenty of money,  
a coffin full of colorful clothes,  
a large trunk of carpets—  
But Peeter did not take the farmer's daughter,  
Peeter did not want the farm girl.  
He took a poor little spirit,  
a slave child, a little bird.  
He took the young and beautiful,  
he took the fashionable one,  
but he could not bring anyone as a bride,  
he was not given anything in return.  
He himself bought a new coat,  
a new golden coat,  
silk skirts to wear,  
many pearls on his head,  
silver jewelry around his neck,  
white shoes for his feet as a shelter.  
When the neighbors held a wedding,  
when the wedding was being prepared:  
then the maidens began to cry,  
and the housewives to grieve.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Maiden, young girl,  
why do you refuse to come to me?  
To me, a young man,  
(buttoning my jacket,  
putting on my hat,  
tying a leather belt) –  
I have apple trees outside,  
I have berry bushes in the house,  
I have sturdy ladders,  
and sharp little steps.  
Pick apples from my apple trees,  
pick berries from my bushes.  
Maiden, young girl,  
do not complain, do not be afraid,  
if I lead you to my barn,  
there is no shortage of meat there,  
shoulders in the beer half-glass,  
hands in the bread buns,  
stomachs.  
Ellakene, little brother,  
why do you boast emptily,  
why do you brag so much?  
I visited you yesterday,  
came to you the day before yesterday.  
Ellakene, little brother,  
you said there are three barns outside,  
one to store rye,  
another to store oats,  
the third to pile up wheat.  
One was for dogs to urinate,  
another for cats to defecate,  
the third for mice to jump.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let me sing back again  
I guess I was at your place yesterday  
The day before yesterday I came to you  
You had mice in the house  
Cats chasing them away  
Rats crawling about  
Surely there will be tears in the kitchen harvest  
Tears in the barn harvest  
Secret tears in the sauna harvest  
Even more in the living room  
Dear little brother  
Young boy, little boy  
Maybe you would have a pile of gold  
A pile of gold, a basket of copper  
A hundred earnings of silver  
Still, I will not come to you  
I will go where I am meant to go  
Maybe to the Tõllakиль garden  
To the gate closed with a whip  
To milk a cow to the hip  
To stick a hook in the trap  
I will go where I am meant to go  
Even if there are buildings in Uurakille  
Barns in Karjalaukille  
Rooms nailed with boards  
A stove under the arch  
Still, I will go where I am meant to go  
Still, I will not come to you.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went to Latvia to court
To see Latvian girls
Latvian maids smiling
My small horse
A tiny suitor
Wait, wait girls, well well girls
Let me bring a courtship rune from Koigi
From Laimets to the wide lake
Then I go to court in Latvia
To see the Latvian maids
Then I go through the village
Through the golden gate
Through the stone street
To see the Latvian girls
To tell myself
If there would be a man for me
If there would be a spouse for me
Who goes through the village
Through the stone street
The man stands out like this day
Hat like a servant of the moon
A horse in front of him like a stove
Belt like a ship's flag scarf
He has locks on top of the braid
Locks on top of the braid end
He has lace on top of the whip
Lace on the whip handle.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Dear sister Marikene  
Or rather, gentle Liisuke  
Otherwise, you would marry  
Having no shirt to put on  
Nor a sock to wear  
Dear sister Marikene  
Or rather, kind Liisuke  
Don’t go wild marrying  
To a stump of a husband  
A man doesn’t care for liking  
A wife doesn’t help growing  
A man wants clothes to please him  
A wife is asked to make gloves  
Every month wants a new coat  
Every day wants fine shirts  
Every noon from linen.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Töölaulud  ; Murelaulud  ; Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Family daughter, a maiden,  
Farm daughter, a little plant  
If you bother to be awake  
And you want to be wise  
Clean the room, the glass, the table  
Around the window sills  
Turn near the cellar  
Wash the glass lamps in the attic  
Bring them to the corner of the room  
Flip the baskets on the kitchen roof  
Buckets to the well edge  
The mother-in-law comes to the village  
To the old man, Vaini Jüle  
He thinks of the glowing moon  
He thinks of the shining Easter  
Our ladle handles gleamed  
Our copper trays shone  
Our wooden buckets rumbled  
Our glass lamps in the attic  
Bring them to the corner of the room  
Flip the baskets on the kitchen roof  
Family daughter, a maiden  
Farm daughter, a little plant  
When she hears the holidays are coming  
You get the firewood ready  
Easter sliding  
The moon pushes the curls  
Easter pushes the pearl maid  
The year builds itself  
Starts asking the elders  
Begins asking the old men  
Consulting the elders  
Where is my sash, where is my buckle  
Where are my embroidered cuffs  
Where is my belt with maple embroidery  
Where are my socks with Finnish patterns  
Shoes with the stone cross pattern  
Mother Ella, dear old lady  
Give me the barn key  
Give me the coffin key  
I’m going to decorate the barn  
Smoothing the head on the chest  
Under I put gold, on top silver  
Between I put white ribbons  
Skirt on with eyelets  
Belt on with maple embroidery  
Socks on with Finnish patterns  
Shoes with the stone cross pattern  
Then I will go to the village under the swing  
The village young men  
Ponder, think  
Whose beauty is the midwife  
Whose is the pretty maiden  
The city’s beauty midwife  
Just the right maiden from Paide  
From whom will he ask a servant  
From whom the poor beg  
The servant will ask  
The worker will beg  
Have your hands made for the village  
Have your arms worked hard  
Mother Ella, dear old lady  
Some got a place to bake bread  
Others found a place to bake  
You put me far away  
Far away to the weaver  
To the city road, to the turner  
To other lands, to the miller  
Mother Ella, dear old lady  
No, I didn’t go to the miller  
The miller's stones are rumbling  
No, I didn’t go to the weaver  
The weaver’s fabric is rustling  
No, I didn’t go to the smith either  
The smith’s children are coal black  
Children of the soot-smith  
I want pure woodworkers  
Lovers of the beginnings  
The wide splitter  
The long fart-burrower  
A little for us fart bag  
A little for the fart snatcher  
Surely there are farmers for us  
Surely there are makers of farmers  
Make the flame glow bright  
Let the fat burn clear

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgins, virgins  
There were many of us before the herd  
A thick herd before the herd  
Rolled like a cattle herd  
Damp like a spring flood  
Fell apart like a scattered flock  
Now the herd has ended  
The group has disappeared  
The brood has been scattered  
Oh, that beauty of sisterhood  
Dear little birch forest  
Here the plants disappear, scarce  
Grandmother's daughters are beautiful  
Grandfather's daughters are even-tempered  
Grandmother's children are thoughtful  
Neither grandmother's whip fills them  
Nor grandfather's rod fills them  
To stir up the brother  
A straw was the sister's rod  
A bark piece was grandfather's switch  
A willow leaf was brother's lash  
They were on my wing like titmice  
And under my arm like chickens  
On the shoulder, little happy birds  
Neither pests to put in a bad mood  
To sell to dark-haired men  
Peter and Paul  
Yuri and Yurika.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Girls are little birds  
Beautiful fringed necklaces  
Ask your gods  
For a beautiful evening  
For a bright dawn  
That you do not get the man  
Who before had a wife  
Had a woman, a raised wife  
Drills oneself lifeless  
One's wife's jewelry  
Old woman's ailments  
It cannot be so  
If it was blessed before  
Had walked a crooked path before  
To feed a pig, to kill another  
Or to poke the nest  
To boil a hen in a cauldron.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
In the forest, I become a man  
From the tops of trees to the boys  
The trees are my wedding guests  
Firs are the golden noblewomen  
Birches are the white companions  
Alders are the straight sisters  
There was a wolf as the wedding officer  
A piece of fodder on our dining table  
A fox, the robber  
A hare, a lively groom.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If the maiden dies  
Where then shall I bury the maiden?  
Under the golden church  
Under the slim tower  
Who visits the maiden’s grave there?  
Every month the kings come  
Every Friday the Germans from Riga  
The Germans from Paide ask for prayer  
Let me tell it again,  
Let me sing it back again  
If the boy dies  
Where then shall I bury the boy?  
In the swamp under the big spruce  
Under the broad meadow’s alder  
In the street on the farmstead  
In the gate, in the manure pit  
Who visits the boy’s grave there?  
Every month the devils come  
Every Friday the servants from Riga  
The servants from Paide ask for prayer  
Old boys, loud boys  
Are taken to the mill to sleep  
To make a nest in the threshing barn  
There where the tar road stretches  
The old wheel’s grease trail  
You will stay, you will stay  
Stay feeding the bait  
Look into the water  
Be plump, our maidens  
Be loud, old girls  
The mockers will come  
The maiden’s laugher scorner  
Their mouths big and jaws wide  
Tongues like church doors  
Tines like gate latches  
The dangers driven away  
I understand, answer again  
My dear, little brother,  
If you care for me  
I will care for you  
I care for your paths  
I slander the wide field paths  
I care for the tin belt  
I slander the broad face  
I mock the long dresses  
You have five blisters under your toes  
Six scars on your face  
Seven  
Elbows bent  
Knock your teeth out.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I did a bitter job,  
To grind the flax straw,  
To endure the stingy rogue.  
The angry one knows no thanks,  
The wicked shows no gratitude,  
The evil one gives two pennies.  
What says  
The slobbering red stone?  
I take from the poor child,  
From the poor servant child.  
It knows how to walk in the fire,  
To sing without a chandelier,  
To write without a candle:  
It milks the cow without a chandelier,  
It strains the milk without a candle  
In the pain of night, in the pain of the moon,  
In the pain of splitting the day’s head.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
How should I go home,
Home to angry words,
To the deep hole.
At home, all the spruce trees were angry,
At home, the tunes were furious,
At home, everything was confused.
The sideboards were cold,
The edge boards were thawed.
Who am I talking to?
I am talking to a louse.
Who shall I have fun with?
I have fun with a bedbug.
I shake the old fur coat:
Oh, you old fur coat!
It was nice to go to the awakening,
Nice to go to the herd,
To joyfully howl for pleasure.
I turn to the side, I find cold,
I turn back, I find a wall.
Fleas were crawling in my mouth,
Lice went to the corner, biting in the lap.
Bedbugs had lice on their backs.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The bumblebee, the little fan,
Is throwing a big wedding in the swamp,
Playing beautifully on the tussock,
Even inviting the cuckoo as a cook;
The cuckoo boiled the anger stew,
Put in a lot of salt,
A handful of pepper,
But the meadow went off sawing,
Quarreling among the wedding crowd:
The tit gets on the dill,
The crow on the hammer,
The raven on the shovel.
The capercaillie's tuft is pulled,
The sparrow's tail is twisted,
The swallow's head is shut off.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Along the west from the bridegroom's gate,  
Through the beds of sparrows.  
Let's go inside to see,  
How many beds they have in the room?  
Seven beds they have in the room,  
Father's bed was for sitting,  
Mother's bed was for lying,  
Grandfather's bed was a slatted one,  
Grandmother's bed was a peaked one,  
The bridegroom's was striped lengthwise,  
Mine was made of beeswax,  
Children's bed was made of planks.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Go to the corner young men,  
To the dark pipe men,  
Lazy men under the table.  
From my brother’s sword path,  
My brother is coming to roll,  
Blue stocking is coming to sew.  
There is a tall reed,  
Taller than my poor brother.  
There is white sea foam;  
Whiter than my poor brother,  
Threw a bundle onto the grassland,  
Onto the meadow of the flute player,  
Who cut their finger yesterday?  
It was that young lad.  
Who jumped into the swaddling?  
It was that young maiden.  
Where did she get the scarf?  
Took a neckerchief from the neck,  
Tore apart the jawkerchief,  
Tore the headscarf to bits.  
Where did she get the ribbon then?  
Took a leg ribbon from the calf;  
That ribbon will disappear,  
The village lad will get a new one!  
That scarf will disappear,  
The village lad will buy a new one:  
Russian boy brings a striped one,  
German boy brings a velvet one,  
Country boy brings a mottled one,  
Swedish boy brings a checkered one,  
Makes Finnish patterns inside,  
Fills it with journals,  
Throws in logical patterns.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I did not intend to understand
The little star does not know
The little duck does not think.
I am wise to understand
I am a star to notice
A little duck to think.
I come along the swan’s snowdrift
Barefoot along the water
I come along the edges of the flock
Cranberry along the river
Strawberry along the mountain.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What was Mihkel thinking  
That Mihkel will not take me  
I am not to Mihkel’s liking  
Nor at Mihkel’s beck and call  
Mihkel’s whip was flexible  
The stick was skinny and pointed  
The apple was with a protruding stem  
The bend was sinewy, the surface was woody  
The ox’s knees were made of firewood.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mother dear  
Marry off that daughter  
What goes along the edge  
Foolish head, untidy hair  
Pretty strutters, flashy  
If you don’t marry her off, don’t ask  
Let her stay at home picking  
Mold by the fence  
Mushrooms by the wall  
Birch on the birch neck  
Spruce on the eyebrow  
Juniper between the ears  
Rowan on the temples.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Flaxen darling  
Don’t you take a wife from the manor  
There are certain kinds in the manor  
Beautiful ones below the windows  
Delicate ones above the lintels  
How many men have they had  
How many boys have they slept with  
They have slept with a hundred Marts  
They were familiar with a thousand Toomases  
Yet they remained standing innocent  
Messed up their understanding  
Decayed on the hill  
Cannot weave cloth  
Cannot work the fine loom  
Cannot spin thread  
Cannot twine linen yarn  
Cannot flash like copper  
Flaxen darling  
Take instead captive children from the parish  
Or orphan children from the orphanage  
They know how to weave cloth  
How to work the fine loom  
How to spin thread  
How to twine linen yarn  
How to flash like copper  
They may walk to the fire  
Without sparks, stir the bed  
Milk the cow with the chandelier  
Milk the cow by nightlight  
Squeeze milk by moonlight  
The village is the beauty of candles  
In the light of the bright ox’s head.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Before I throw a stone by the stone,  
By the stone, by the stump,  
Before the crazy boy,  
The stone does not pull, the stump does not tug,  
The boy does not leave untouched,  
The hand does not test cunningly.  
Before I play with the tussocks,  
I joke with the girls,  
Before those girls  
Who are half-promised,  
On the outside a fine young girl,  
Underneath long since a woman,  
She has slept with a device,  
She has had piglets tumbling in her womb.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Our Maies, our Kaies  
They were some lively girls  
Wanted to share from the sky  
To scatter little clouds  
But if Kaie disappeared, Maie would end  
Then the clatter from the yard would vanish  
The laughter and noise from the alder grove  
The boys' shouting from the spruce forest  
The boys' jokes from the meadows  
Mai was a fond little maiden  
She tended to the man without a gift  
But left her shoes on the coffin  
Stockings on the big table  
Slippers on the echoing path.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Brother dear, little darling  
When you are about to marry  
Take a sister under your arm  
But the sister senses dull girls  
The dozing one senses dull girls  
The lazy one senses lazy girls  
The dull one has tangled hair  
The lazy one did nothing about it  
The fluid one is in the night’s embrace  
The diligent one is all new.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Young maiden
Why didn’t you come to the mill last year?
I will put you to work picking bilberries
I will make you knit stockings
You must search for days
You must make places for the year
The maiden understands again and replies
Be quiet, you young boy
I will not come to you
To your pigsty
To the black earth’s turner
To the hat maker
To the horse changer
To the tavern drinker and rascal -
I understand again and reply
Come, young maiden
Come to me, my sweetheart
To the melted, sweet one
I will bring chest scarves from Riga
From Amla, I will bring underwear
From the market, I will bring a deer hat
From the store, I will bring a calico apron
The maiden understands again and replies
Be quiet, you young boy
The trumpeter brings a bloody apron
Matar, with fur like liver
The fist one with a checkered pattern
In the garden sheaves, an old one
The Tomingas is a shoulder dog
In the garden sheaves, a ring wreath
The bog myrtle is a thigh dog.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Swing, drop  
Swing, brothers, swing  
Swing higher  
Sway more properly  
So that I appear much grounded  
So my back would show red  
Silver ring off the finger  
Another from the other hand  
A third from the golden little finger  
So I would appear in the groom's family  
In the spouse's chamber.  
This urge came to me  
I began to tell Hans  
Complain to Kaarel  
Come now to my room  
Step to my place  
Grope me in the waist  
Pinch me, pinch me  
Pinch my grain now  
Just like a leather glove.  
Touch my fur coat  
Look at the white thigh  
Take the key from my pants  
Hold my body well  
Protect my fur coat.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Swing, drop  
Swing, brothers, swing  
Swing higher  
Sway more properly  
So that I appear much grounded  
So my back would show red  
Silver ring off the finger  
Another from the other hand  
A third from the golden little finger  
So I would appear in the groom's family  
In the spouse's chamber.  
This urge came to me  
I began to tell Hans  
Complain to Kaarel  
Come now to my room  
Step to my place  
Grope me in the waist  
Pinch me, pinch me  
Pinch my grain now  
Just like a leather glove.  
Touch my fur coat  
Look at the white thigh  
Take the key from my pants  
Hold my body well  
Protect my fur coat.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Annekene, a young maiden  
You have gone from the forest to a man  
From the top of the tree to the boy  
From the birch hut to the cattle shed  
You waited for father without oil  
At mother’s wide table  
Half-brother half-divided  
Now you can ride the saw  
On the long spindle of the spinning wheel  
On the bagpipe you will play  
The trumpet was given to the man.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The dusk is coming on Saturday evening  
The holy evening of Sunday  
The girl goes to bed with the child  
The sister goes with her husband  
The brother goes with a young maiden  
I go to the empty corner  
Towards the empty bed  
All the tables are cold  
The benches were cold  
The window sills were misty  
The wall planks were covered with fungi  
I turn to my side, find coldness  
I turn my back, find the wall.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
A young maiden  
Struck by the riverbank  
A golden club in hand  
A silver striker  
The boys' eyes ran with water  
Over that gold’s six  
Over blue silk  
Over a redder bull  
Kessi brought blue silk  
Mother brought blue silk  
Kessi brought a red wreath  
Sister brought a red wreath  
Kessi brought a striped ribbon  
Brother brought a striped ribbon.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The bonfire is coming, singing  
I am going west, the herd girl makes a cuckoo  
Where should I lead the herd  
I would lead the herd to Kaunissaare  
I myself would go to Iilissaare  
I would make a fire on Tuulissaare  
The cat came to my fire  
The boy came to my fire  
I had a stone in my mitten  
An iron rod in my pocket  
I hit the boy on the head  
I knocked on both eyebrows  
The boy went to the manor court  
Which court will judge  
The court would judge me  
The boy will get beaten on the backside.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sister Ella, as I said  
Relative, as I wished  
Don't try to please the man  
With an unripe wife  
A wife does not make one grow  
A man does not want to take one  
But the wife asks for a cloth shed  
The man wants a tailor's shop  
Every day wants something fine  
Every noon wants a full plate  
Every evening something light  
Every month wants a new coat  
A week wants four shirts  
A year, two fur coats  
If you can't wear a shirt properly  
A light one on your shoulders properly  
A fine one on your chest properly  
Then you will feel the frost  
Frost must not be ignored  
The fist is checkered  
The pea is hairy like a hedgehog.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
We drink in the tavern, the errand boys  
One on the other's money  
If yours doesn’t pay  
Then mine will pay  
If mine doesn’t pay  
Then madam will pay from the cupboard  
If madam doesn’t pay from the cupboard  
Then sir will pay from the barn  
Our master has big barns  
Big barns with new doors  
New doors with tight locks  
No keys can open them  
I brought a skeleton key from the city  
From the town, I brought a green iron  
From the market, I brought strong iron  
I opened the tight locks  
The bailiff ran to the squire  
Our barns are already robbed  
The squire is at Mr. Joan’s room  
Our barns are already robbed  
At Mr. Joan’s room, upstairs  
Our barns are already robbed  
The master came down with ropes in hand  
Mr. Joan has shackles on his feet  
They came to catch me  
To arrest me  
I fell down, the gray-haired one  
Old man with a brittle ear  
Ear to ear, a blueberry mark  
I rode down to the city of Riga  
There are three girls in the barn  
In front, a barn with a beaded necklace  
Behind, a barn with a tin necklace  
In the middle, lined up, a hemp necklace  
I took the beaded necklace from the front  
Did not take the tin necklace from the back  
Nor the hemp necklace from the middle  
I took the poor child from between  
One slave by one at the head  
Who can weave cloth  
Nikutate the tin thread  
Silver-colored sew silver  
These go on, but nika-naka  
Silver on top but solla-salla  
Floorboards trill-trall.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:

One on the other's money
