Then mine will pay  
If mine doesn’t pay  
Then madam will pay from the cupboard  
If madam doesn’t pay from the cupboard  
Then sir will pay from the barn  
Our master has big barns  
Big barns with new doors  
New doors with tight locks  
No keys can open them  
I brought a skeleton key from the city  
From the town, I brought a green iron  
From the market, I brought strong iron  
I opened the tight locks  
The bailiff ran to the squire  
Our barns are already robbed  
The squire is at Mr. Joan’s room  
Our barns are already robbed  
At Mr. Joan’s room, upstairs  
Our barns are already robbed  
The master came down with ropes in hand  
Mr. Joan has shackles on his feet  
They came to catch me  
To arrest me  
I fell down, the gray-haired one  
Old man with a brittle ear  
Ear to ear, a blueberry mark  
I rode down to the city of Riga  
There are three girls in the barn  
In front, a barn with a beaded necklace  
Behind, a barn with a tin necklace  
In the middle, lined up, a hemp necklace  
I took the beaded necklace from the front  
Did not take the tin necklace from the back  
Nor the hemp necklace from the middle  
I took the poor child from between  
One slave by one at the head  
Who can weave cloth  
Nikutate the tin thread  
Silver-colored sew silver  
These go on, but nika-naka  
Silver on top but solla-salla  
Floorboards trill-trall.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I bought a horse from Oti  
A hat from the village huntsman  
A saddle from Saare Saksas  
I rode through Fenno, I rode through the islands  
I rode onto the Finnish bridge  
But the Finnish bridge was a rickety path  
The foundation beam was a noisy path  
My hat was a light path  
Pushed onto the ear.  
A maiden is watching from the shadows  
A crouching drop, a visiting drop  
Through time, a wandering drop  
If only that man were mine  
Who is on the Saare bridge  
The horse was underneath like the oven  
The man was above like the day  
The hat like a church bell  
The belt like a rain rainbow  
Whose is that handsome master  
Take me, lovely mistress.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I bought a horse from Oti  
A hat from the village huntsman  
A saddle from Saare Saksas  
I rode through Fenno, I rode through the islands  
I rode onto the Finnish bridge  
But the Finnish bridge was a rickety path  
The foundation beam was a noisy path  
My hat was a light path  
Pushed onto the ear.  
A maiden is watching from the shadows  
A crouching drop, a visiting drop  
Through time, a wandering drop  
If only that man were mine  
Who is on the Saare bridge  
The horse was underneath like the oven  
The man was above like the day  
The hat like a church bell  
The belt like a rain rainbow  
Whose is that handsome master  
Take me, lovely mistress.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
It was said at night,  
Thought and pondered,  
Thought about some kinds  
Some suitor milk drinker  
Some widow bread eater  
I, a pure boy.  
Some girl sleeper  
Mouth was lovely, untouched  
Hand dear, untried,  
Pig girl sleeper  
Piglet suitor milk drinker  
Sheep widow bread eater.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Who thinks Hansu a man  
Thinks pike a fish  
Burbot a bread crust  
Bleak a big bite.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
For I thank my aunt  
She gave soap to make clearer.  
To make foam whiter  
The day gave time to wash the head  
The moon to dry the hair  
I put the spruce to dry  
To sway in the apple tree  
To shine in the pines  
The spruce dried with golden hair  
The apple tree with silver hair  
The pine with gray hair  
The boys of Albu barked  
The boys of Sõidla rode around  
They thought the moon would shine  
The day gently shine  
The stars sparkle in the sky  
Those were my white hairs  
They wanted to crop my head  
To shorten the flaxen hair  
I gave to crop the head  
To shorten the flaxen hair  
Now Hans comes from Harju too  
Now Jaak from Järva too  
Peeter from long Piibe too  
They have scissors in hand  
Shears in the pocket too  
But they crop my head  
Shorten the flaxen hair  
Hans brings shoes with hooks  
Jaan brings shoes with leather straps  
Siim brings shoes with spikes  
I throw Hans’s shoes into the oven  
I throw Siim’s shoes in the shit  
I put Jaan’s shoes on my feet  
Then westward with shoes to the church  
Shoes click underfoot  
Laces snap behind  
Hair stands on the head  
Goes up to the armpits  
Sways over the shoulders  
I pass through the men’s men  
I slip also through the boys  
Albu boys are kind brothers  
Don’t think  
That I will become a bad woman  
Become one bad, stay one bad  
Become a kind one or maybe old  
Not even a whole parish  
I will become a woman on the Narva road  
A slippery woman on the city road  
A suitable woman in Paide  
My husband over the seas  
Drinks there at river’s edge  
Under the bluish-gray bridge  
Under the watery-gray mill  
Under the church of lavender  
I cannot ride to say  
To make a place for him  
To turn at the pillowcase  
The wind takes him entirely away  
The cloud pulls him  
What a life I have  
The life of a mistress I have  
The daughter of a lady’s lap  
She comes with a carriage after me  
Rides a sledge at seven  
Drives the horse at nine  
Already from that we get by three  
That we started well  
Let us finish comfortably

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
This girl is even crazy  
Foolish in her head  
Who marries young  
Without gaining skill,  
Thinks to charm the man  
Thinks to raise skill.  
The man puts in mad work  
The skill weaves the fabric.  
If you don’t tend the fabric storeroom,  
The husband’s clothes storeroom,  
And your own jewelry storeroom,  
Then you will face the whip of the hearth;  
Then you will soon get fists  
Swift on your face.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What was it for  
That I walk near the villages  
My own is not here  
My companion does not grow here  
My own is on their own path  
My companion is far away  
A good man across the seas  
A dear companion across the waters  
There he swings in the hidden forest  
Bends the flexible bow  
Sways under the net  
Ties under the strap  
Now I want to talk to him  
Nights do not pass sleeping together  
Nor whispering close to the ear  
May the wind carry greetings to him  
May the rains send messages  
May the clouds linger and watch.  
So many greetings to him  
As there are wood bushes  
Straight hazel  
Fine rowan  
Proper apple tree,  
White maple.  
Proper apple trees in the grove  
My fiancé's true mother  
Maples white on the mountain  
My fiancé's white mother  
There was cunning fish in the sea  
My fiancé more cunning.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
These are the men we have  
Try to stay away if you are in Järvamaa  
They don’t hesitate to resist  
To chatter like a milker  
To lie down on the bench  
To give a good whip to the bucket.  
Into the peat bog grows a forest  
A row grows by the alder grove  
Tall trees go into stacks  
The foundations of the barns  
Without young men and men  
Without sharpening the scythe.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
We used to have a whole bunch
A big amount of goods.
There was quite a flock of maidens.
Now there are many gone
The herd has disappeared
The brood has been scattered
The stock has been separated.
Now I am left alone to tend the goods,
To call the chicks,
To pick flax seeds,
To separate the seeds of the clothes,
Among the beans,
In the middle of the nice seeds.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I cut the meadow hill  
I turn the center of the fields  
I cry at the edge of the meadow.  
Who is coming home to call?  
For my own brother.  
Come home, my sister,  
Your suitors have come to the house  
Five vodkas, six suitors.  
I hear it, I respond again:  
Dear brother  
If they have come, let them come  
The gate post does not tire  
Break the room's digging cake,  
The workbench is not for the landowner  
Holding the suitor’s horse,  
Carrying the field's aid,  
Surely the pressure on the ring's aid  
To stab behind the wall  
Who must keep the suitor’s horses  
Carries the suitor’s field aid.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Taurus  
When the hay was not mowed  
Taurus  
When the song was not sung  
Taurus  
When the berry was not picked  
Taurus  
When the boy was untouched.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The evening of Saturday is coming  
A little Sunday day.  
The girl goes to bed with the child,  
The brother goes with the young sapling,  
I go crying to the corner  
I poke towards the empty bed.  
I turned my feet and found Juan,  
I turned the sail and found the wall  
I turned to the side and found cold  
Where I stood the walls were wet  
Where I cried the corners were wet.  
The cold water was flowing down the side  
The dark water under the carpet.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I know a girl with a pointed hat  
A girl with a black lock of hair  
Then came a widower in response  
With widow’s gloves on hand  
With widow’s letters in gloves  
Immediately asking me:  
Young maiden, young girl  
Will you come to me  
To be mine, my own  
To look after early  
To milk five cows  
To feed five pigs  
To do the work under the stove  
To lay out the carpet?  
I surely hear and answer back  
I will not come to you  
To you, the widower  
To your dream of a horse  
Better I know a girl with a pointed hat  
A girl with a black lock of hair.  

Now came a young man in response  
A young man, a plump boy  
Immediately asking me:  
Hello, young maiden  
Beautiful, plump and petite  
Will you perhaps come to me?  
To be mine, my own  
To look after early  
To milk two cows  
To feed two pigs  
To weave my fabric  
To sew the shirt border  
To do the work under the stove  
To lay out the carpet. –  
I surely hear and answer back  
Yes, I will come to you  
To be yours, my own  
To look after early  
To milk two cows  
To feed two pigs  
To weave your fabric  
To sew the shirt border  
To do the work under the stove  
To lay out the carpet.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
In our village several young men  
About forty lads  
Went to the drinking place  
To wet their throats  
Swimming across the lake  
Dancing behind the lake.  
Then came from the maiden group  
Two from a distant herd.  
Some of you were young men  
Some of you were young and foolish  
Outside is market goods  
In the street are seller's goods  
Maiden goods in the chamber  
Maidservant goods in the household  
Foal goods in the pasture.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The moon shines from the Kuura river,
The day from the Pärnu spring.
But I am among the girls,
Between two girls,
Under the shoulders of five girls,
Under the blanket of six girls.
The moon shone along my side,
The day wheel hit along,
The stars stood at the edges.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh my little brother Ella  
If you want to marry a woman  
If you want a whip  
If you want a blue flower  
Do not look at the shadows!  
Do not look at the thorns.  
Look at those with braided hair  
Know those with wild hair  
Look at those with combed hair.  
The braided-haired one goes to cut  
The combed one goes to weave cloth  
The wild-haired one goes to work.  
Oh my little brother Ella  
Let me sing the words back,  
Take the enemy's patch  
Cut off the slave’s tail  
They know how to walk in fire  
They lie without sparks  
They milk the cow without a chandelier  
They strain milk in the moonlight  
They stew soup without herbs.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I am not a bully out of spite  
Out of spite bullying the shores  
But my mouth is for cursing inside  
Backside for slandering  
The fool laughs at me, the rogue praises me  
My own man always takes me.  
My own is not here  
My barley does not grow here.  
My own is in Holland  
Where there were fishing shores  
My own man across the seas  
A handsome man across the waves.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virulasi five-six  
Arjakaid seven-eight  
Still a Järva boy alone  
And also a Järva herdsman boy  
One ribbon to this ribbon  
I put one ribbon on the ribbon  
I throw it into the pear tree.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, oh, oh, oh, lover’s scarf  
Early I took the weary scarf  
Early I confirmed the engagement  
Early I exchanged the rings  
Without the old woman’s knowledge  
Without the mother’s knowledge  
Without the elder’s consent  
Without the dear one’s hearing.  
They caught me, sharp Jüris  
They wanted the children of the house  
The girl neither gave nor allowed  
Nor did I even go  
The girl gave no kiss  
Nor allowed any touch  
Seeing the hunger in the eye  
The girl gave not a single one  
Nor allowed any caress  
I myself did not go, little flower.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Waves-waves weddings on the beach  
For some men weddings in the forest  
The trees are tall wedding guests  
The alders wide beggars  
The feeding piece is a feeding table  
The field edge is a bench.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Before I play with the hills  
Before those girls  
Who are half-used  
Overplayed  
I do not seek a proud mother  
Nor do I watch a white mother.  
From the market I take a servant child  
From the village a poor child  
Let the meadow girls then cry for me  
Always the Arju girls  
Afterwards let the Järva girls mourn  
Let the Viru girls shed tears.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Before I play with the hills  
Before those girls  
Who are half-used  
Overplayed  
I do not seek a proud mother  
Nor do I watch a white mother.  
From the market I take a servant child  
From the village a poor child  
Let the meadow girls then cry for me  
Always the Arju girls  
Afterwards let the Järva girls mourn  
Let the Viru girls shed tears.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Juotme  
The women went to the meadow.  
They made a wedding in the garden.  
Who did they invite to the wedding?  
The crane with the golden spur  
The crow with the copper sabers  
The titmouse angrily went without oil  
The sparrow tried to pull  
The dung beetle put in the fools.  
Who went to try fishing?  
The hen went to try fishing  
The rooster went to run to the threshing floor.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let the young girls have fun,  
Let the wise women frolic –  
God commands to enjoy pleasure,  
The Heavenly Father to celebrate.  
Pleasure is to eat, pleasure is to drink,  
Pleasure is to live for pleasure;  
There is no pleasure in doing work,  
Pleasure is to go on a ramble,  
With dear crutches.  
The soldier does not tie this knot,  
Nor does the warrior paint it,  
Nor do they put it on a raft.  
The soldier ties the knots whole,  
The warrior paints them white,  
The well-fed were put on the raft.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Be proud, young men,  
Be proud, go to the hills,  
Be bold, buy black ones,  
Go to the hills, buy ears;  
Buy laces for the whip's handle,  
Lovely bows for the whip's handle,  
Copper between the joints.  
Ride to the maidens' homes,  
To the families of the eel girls –  
There you will have food on the table,  
There you will be lifted down,  
Placed below the seat;  
Taken to the chamber to sleep  
Half the night by the brother,  
Half the night you slept,  
The other half you thought –  
The rooster crowed, the maiden did not hear,  
The rooster crowed a second time,  
The maiden heard neither;  
The rooster crowed a third time,  
The maiden turned to the other side.  
The suitor began to think –  
To think – to speak:  
I sit by my road’s edge,  
I jump by my ear’s edge,  
I let my song run along;  
I offer my hand from the road’s edge,  
I speak from the ear’s edge –  
I tell tales from the clearing’s edge:  
Farewell, maiden,  
You will fall behind me,  
I won’t leave you,  
As long as the flower blooms,  
The purpling leaves flourish.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Listen, young ladies!  
What can you actually do?  
You don't have places to sit -  
And your skirts are at your knees!  
The boys from Võivere won't take you,  
Nor do the boys from Tapa want you,  
The boys from Kärda won't hold your hand,  
Nor the diligent boys from Orina,  
You will see this time,  
You bring it upon yourselves,  
Sometimes you bring spirit troubles;  
You regularly go in an orderly manner  
To Lähte crying towards the manor,  
Through filth and mud;  
You have bread crumbs on your skirts,  
A rag in your armpit!  
You go to the manor yard,  
Under the windows -  
You beat your chest with your fist—  
This is my little cross—  
The Kubja stick is upon my neck,  
This is my dear necklace.  
Now there are weak young men,  
Round, new moons,  
Girls are left uncaught;  
Now the oven door is closed,  
Driven under the ale vats—  
The field is written in the field. -  
The ribbons belong in town,  
The maids belong in the store.  
The Kalevs stayed in the German chamber.  
Because there are weak young men,  
Girls are left uncaught.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Listen, young ladies!  
What can you actually do?  
You don't have places to sit -  
And your skirts are at your knees!  
The boys from Võivere won't take you,  
Nor do the boys from Tapa want you,  
The boys from Kärda won't hold your hand,  
Nor the diligent boys from Orina,  
You will see this time,  
You bring it upon yourselves,  
Sometimes you bring spirit troubles;  
You regularly go in an orderly manner  
To Lähte crying towards the manor,  
Through filth and mud;  
You have bread crumbs on your skirts,  
A rag in your armpit!  
You go to the manor yard,  
Under the windows -  
You beat your chest with your fist—  
This is my little cross—  
The Kubja stick is upon my neck,  
This is my dear necklace.  
Now there are weak young men,  
Round, new moons,  
Girls are left uncaught;  
Now the oven door is closed,  
Driven under the ale vats—  
The field is written in the field. -  
The ribbons belong in town,  
The maids belong in the store.  
The Kalevs stayed in the German chamber.  
Because there are weak young men,  
Girls are left uncaught.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The girls were milking on the hill  
I am milking behind the hill  
Golden scissors in hand  
Bright border rails  
Why does the girl limit me?  
I pay with a branch base  
I throw the bundles to the bottom of the pond  
Golden buckles on the skull cap  
But I take the bundles to Germany  
Golden buckles to Courland

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My dear little brother  
You've been to see a girl from this land  
From these villages  
Between the parishes  
From the center of the district  
Ships sailed, ships sailed  
Ships went to Germany  
Half of the ships to Finland  
Push the little boats into the sea  
Send the tar boats after them  
Row down to the city of Riga  
There are three rows of girls  
One is the row of rich ladies  
The second row of poor ladies  
The third row of servants  
The row of rich ladies  
Have fields to tend  
The row of servants  
Have fields to tend  
I took in the poor child in between  
The prettiest in the middle  
She started coming home  
A big gate was hung at Olevi  
At Nigula it clanked sharply  
The bell of Tuompia beat a big drum  
The girls' ribbons stayed in the town  
The girl's things stayed in the small borough  
The brooch stayed on the friend's table  
The daughter stayed in the stone oven  
The knives stayed in the German box.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What I was doing at the fair  
I refused to lift the hay  
I have no time to listen  
The bridegroom is hiding his eyes  
My time is far away  
My bridegroom is in Peipus  
He is across the sea  
A white sail in the Viru bay  
A wavy sail in Kadi village  
The wind will bring greetings  
The rain will carry the messages  
So many greetings to him  
So many words in between  
So many good words  
Straight as hazelwood  
Small as rowanberries  
True as apple trees  
White as maples.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
A young virgin maiden  
Do you want to come to me  
If I come to woo you  
Will you accept my answer with pride  
Or reject me angrily  
If you reject me angrily  
Then out of spite I will sing  
Spitefully praise myself  
Even criticize you  
I still have many brides in Muskus  
I still have another bride in Tartu  
A beautiful flock of stepsisters  
Rings in their ears, skirts flowing  
The rings in their ears are jingling  
The skirts flowing are rustling  
Idut-tiidut, I have wings  
If those wings take me there  
From here I will take a young wife  
A kind-hearted wife  
A passionate one from the other side.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Beautiful Senni maiden  
Fair Senni beloved  
When the meadow is unmowed  
Slim Senni cows  
When the summer pasture is not yet begun  
Wretched Senni pile  
When there is no livestock in the pile  
Young Senni maiden  
When untouched by a boy  
Untouched by a dear hand  
Yet has touched a boy  
Has felt the dear hand  
Soon begins to wither  
To bear the maiden’s name.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Girls are dill tips  
Dill tips are coltsfoot buds  
Beautiful as rosebuds  
Straight German sigurs  
They are made because of boys  
Halfheartedly worried for boys  
To please the men  
Then men didn’t go to sleep  
Boys were lulled to sleep  
Men slept in the forest  
Under the alder grove  
Under the spruce they bowed.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I slept by Mary’s meadow  
Among the bluebells  
Between the meadowsweet  
In the middle of the globeflowers  
And even while sleeping I saw a dream  
Fig trees behind the fence  
Rowan trees behind the fence  
An apple tree was in the middle of the yard  
A big hazel tree by the path  
My grandmother’s life  
Understand this dream  
I slept by Mary’s meadow  
Among the bluebells  
Between the meadowsweet  
In the middle of the globeflowers  
And even while sleeping I saw a dream  
Fig trees behind the fence  
Rowan trees behind the fence  
An apple tree was in the middle of the yard  
A big hazel tree by the path  
Grandmother immediately heard and said  
What are you trying to riddle about?  
This has already been figured out before  
Told half the world  
You drank vodka under the fig tree  
Suitors came by the rowan tree  
The apple tree was the beer keg  
The big hazel tree is the matchmaker.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
A young maiden, a young man  
Let’s leave this place  
Let’s go to Finland for the summer  
For half a year to Pohla land  
For the winter already to Alutaguse  
Let’s build a stone church  
Let’s put a floor of boards  
Let’s drive in iron crosses  
Iron crosses with golden crowns  
A little altar in the corner  
Golden locks on the skull. -  
Then comes here the young maiden  
The young maiden without a girl  
Then comes the search for a groom  
Traveling on the seat plank  
Checking the foal’s hooves  
The fire cuts the groom’s shoulders  
Sparks fly from the seat plank  
The foal’s hooves flash like lightning  
The suitor’s shoes cut sparks  
Looking for a young maiden  
Trying to touch the sweetheart  
Come to me, young maiden  
Come to me and call  
The maiden hears and answers  
I will not come to you  
I will neither come nor call  
Come, indeed, young maiden  
Come to me, my blooming one  
Come to watch my wealth  
Come to tend my money  
Come to be my pig feeder  
Come to beat my pillow  
Come to separate my linen. -  
I will neither come nor call  
The suitor hears and answers  
Come, indeed, young maiden  
The maiden hears and answers again  
I will not come nor call  
The suitor hears and answers  
Why don’t you come to me?  
Because you do not come to me  
Because the yard is muddy  
The cattle pen full of holes  
Young maiden, young man  
There is much stirring on the seat plank  
The cows have slept thickly  
The bulls have fouled the haylofts  
The stallions have circled the cows  
The maiden hears and answers  
Would if only the yard were planked  
The cattle pen woven from cloth  
The bundles from fine flax  
The bridges from silk thread  
Still I will not come to you  
To you, the vague desire  
The groom’s dream  
To the face of Kuera’s hut  
There I go where created  
Where my creator has made a place  
Where Mary has given a home  
Where my blood has drawn  
Where my luck has been counted;  
Where my strength has been followed  
Would if only there were a pillar in the yard too  
A hundred bundles of pillars  
Still I go where created  
Where my blood has drawn  
Where my color has entered.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sisters, dear ones  
Now let's gather as three  
We must consult as four  
Let's go away from here, elsewhere  
Let's go to Finland for the summer  
And to Hiiumaa for the autumn  
For the winter beyond the seas  
There is no hay to mow there  
Nor meadow to take  
But there the girl mows the nirki  
The little mare takes the load  
The cuckoo sings on the pile  
We will not return from there  
Until a girl’s hunger comes to us  
A girl’s hunger, lack of cloth  
A void among the girls  
When we are sought at the orchard  
And driven from the meadow  
The foal trots on its hoof  
So that fire strikes the doorways  
Sparks fly around the cabins  
The mare’s harnesses are bloody  
You seek maidens  
Listening at the braided neck  
Sitting in the brooch’s embrace.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ann was a shy maiden  
Sitting on the Arju barn roof  
On top of Viru distillery  
Holding a golden stick  
The boys below hold their knees  
Without hats, without gloves  
Without a teacher's belt, without a hairpin  
Without a tin belt, without a drop  
Fall down, golden little one  
Let the wide ribbon fall down  
Fall like an apple from an apple tree  
Drop like a berry from a berry bush  
Don't fall on the stone  
On the stone, on the pot  
Make your beloved stay true  
Fall on my forehead  
Roll onto my vest  
Fall onto my hat  
I will take you to the chamber  
Into a bed of silk  
I will place many pillows  
You will grow in the chamber  
You will straighten in the silk bed  
You will swell among the pillows.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, my life is boring  
Oh, my party is dark  
Weepy youth  
It is worn by sorrow  
It is sunk by noise  
From wicked life into the evening  
From darkness into clouds  
Sunk down low  
At other times I would live differently  
At other hours they would come differently  
Comes that blessed little evening  
Miserable little twilight  
Father goes with mother  
Brother goes with a young girl  
I go crying to the corner  
Warming by the stove  
Regretting in the room  
I hug the old fur coat  
Oh, you old fur coat  
You are fit for wandering  
Fit for sleeping on a patchwork  
Not suitable for going to church.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Brother, little brother,  
I am going to Narva to fetch a wife  
From beyond Petersburg.  
Do not go dressed in men's clothes  
Or with an old plowing hat.  
Go down under the plaster  
Step down into the cellar.  
Take new clothes from the granary  
White shirts hanging on the rod.  
Then go to Narva to fetch a wife,  
From beyond Petersburg,  
Go through Lihula  
The tin whistle is ticking,  
The iron cart is creaking,  
The little songbird sings under the sunset,  
On the other side of the capercaillie’s perch,  
The cuckoo is behind in the sleigh basket.  
A girl’s group came ahead of the carriage,  
The maiden’s shadow is behind the sleigh.  
Whose handsome boy is that?  
And whose good-looking son  
Is singing a little song under the sunset  
On the other side of the capercaillie’s perch?  
The cuckoo is behind in the sleigh basket.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Brother, little brother,  
I am going to Narva to fetch a wife  
From beyond Petersburg.  
Do not go dressed in men's clothes  
Or with an old plowing hat.  
Go down under the plaster  
Step down into the cellar.  
Take new clothes from the granary  
White shirts hanging on the rod.  
Then go to Narva to fetch a wife,  
From beyond Petersburg,  
Go through Lihula  
The tin whistle is ticking,  
The iron cart is creaking,  
The little songbird sings under the sunset,  
On the other side of the capercaillie’s perch,  
The cuckoo is behind in the sleigh basket.  
A girl’s group came ahead of the carriage,  
The maiden’s shadow is behind the sleigh.  
Whose handsome boy is that?  
And whose good-looking son  
Is singing a little song under the sunset  
On the other side of the capercaillie’s perch?  
The cuckoo is behind in the sleigh basket.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Before I believe a snake's tongue  
A snake's tongue, a snake's mind  
Before a young boy's tongue,  
A boy's tongue, a boy's mind.  
A boy is born a deceiver  
A deceiver and a tricker.  
He deceived the family mother  
And the poor family mother,  
Deceived the family's fine girl.  
But he promised to buy a new hat,  
A new hat, new lace,  
Proud lace with a beautiful ribbon.  
He got his punishment  
A couple of days, then a year.  
Then from the swamp he brought warm rods,  
From the willow he brought those burning rods.  
He beat the back blue,  
And twisted the neck twice.  
"That is for your new hat,  
New hat's proud lace,  
Proud lace with a beautiful ribbon!"

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
One of us eats to eat,  
One of us drinks to drink,  
One of us buys items,  
Margu pays the money.  
You cannot take one wife,  
Another takes another wife,  
Another takes another sister.  
Then we are all linked together.  
One eats and one drinks,  
Somewhere under the oil,  
The bridegroom drinks under the bench,  
Like a hundred-year-old tail,  
One of us helps the other.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The daughter is crying in the courtyard!  
And the daughter is not crying for nothing  
The daughter has a quarrel coming  
And weighing the stakes.  
The fire in the rooms does not go out  
Nor the spark from the bed posts  
Nor the woman from young clothes  
Nor the horse from the gray harness  
Nor the spark from the bed posts.  
Illu-tillu, pregnant gray  
We have several barns in our yard  
In each barn seven mounds  
They snarl and make trouble  
They all wanted payments  
Settlements on a bear’s ridge  
Wages from a golden comb  
Gentler possessions  
I could neither pay them  
Nor settle on a bear's ridge  
Nor earn with a golden comb  
Nor care for them more gently.  
Then I was beaten with a whip  
Pulled with a tarred rope  
But I, poor, got away  
And wandered across the field  
Then they caught me with a rope  
And chased me relentlessly  
The rope was a thousand fathoms long  
And a hundred knots were in the rope.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The daughter is crying in the courtyard!  
And the daughter is not crying for nothing  
The daughter has a quarrel coming  
And weighing the stakes.  
The fire in the rooms does not go out  
Nor the spark from the bed posts  
Nor the woman from young clothes  
Nor the horse from the gray harness  
Nor the spark from the bed posts.  
Illu-tillu, pregnant gray  
We have several barns in our yard  
In each barn seven mounds  
They snarl and make trouble  
They all wanted payments  
Settlements on a bear’s ridge  
Wages from a golden comb  
Gentler possessions  
I could neither pay them  
Nor settle on a bear's ridge  
Nor earn with a golden comb  
Nor care for them more gently.  
Then I was beaten with a whip  
Pulled with a tarred rope  
But I, poor, got away  
And wandered across the field  
Then they caught me with a rope  
And chased me relentlessly  
The rope was a thousand fathoms long  
And a hundred knots were in the rope.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The daughter is crying in the courtyard!  
And the daughter is not crying for nothing  
The daughter has a quarrel coming  
And weighing the stakes.  
The fire in the rooms does not go out  
Nor the spark from the bed posts  
Nor the woman from young clothes  
Nor the horse from the gray harness  
Nor the spark from the bed posts.  
Illu-tillu, pregnant gray  
We have several barns in our yard  
In each barn seven mounds  
They snarl and make trouble  
They all wanted payments  
Settlements on a bear’s ridge  
Wages from a golden comb  
Gentler possessions  
I could neither pay them  
Nor settle on a bear's ridge  
Nor earn with a golden comb  
Nor care for them more gently.  
Then I was beaten with a whip  
Pulled with a tarred rope  
But I, poor, got away  
And wandered across the field  
Then they caught me with a rope  
And chased me relentlessly  
The rope was a thousand fathoms long  
And a hundred knots were in the rope.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The young maiden daughter,  
Be diligent in the morning,  
Earlier than the white dawn;  
Clean the room, set the table,  
Sweep under the windows;  
Silk ships are coming in,  
Merchant ships are sailing by,  
Passing by proud coaches,  
Suitors are coming in,  
Young men, lads.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What is the reason for the nap,  
That my groom is a little weak?  
At the village, he also grows thin,  
Sprouts in a silk bed,  
Swells among pillows.  
He does not grow without a heel,  
Does not grow without leg meat,  
Swelling does not thicken without milk!

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Family boys are deceitful,  
The father-in-law deceives, the mother-in-law deceives,  
The fine young lady of the family deceives!  
In the cold village house,  
In the ugly building,  
He promised to bring a silk hat,  
He promised to bring a calico apron,  
Green with little roses,  
Down to the waist, with chestnut-colored hair.  
He will be, he will live!  
His fist is checkered,  
With faint stripes,  
Mattar with chestnut-colored hair.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What suits a frantic man,
Ungrown beans?
Beans do not grow,
A man is not willing to take.
The bean asks the fabric woman,
The man desires the cloth woman;
Every day wants fine things,
Morning silk clothes,
Every noon linen,
Evening silk bedding.
If that cannot be afforded;
Then the torso feels rowan berries;
Shoulder blades of the apple tree,
Fingers of the currant bush branches.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Our men like wild bulls,  
The gray oxen of Harju Jaan:  
They were fierce to fight,  
To scatter the manor boys,  
To shake the village bulls.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh stupid and even more stupid,  
What I saw last year:  
I went to the family as a suitor,  
The family gate was a latch,  
The door below was ajar;  
The daughter of the family was in the chamber,  
In the chamber and in the cupboard,  
The cupboard door was locked,  
The key was in the old woman's pocket.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Girls, little birds,  
Lovely pearl necklaces,  
Maiden with a tin curl:  
Do you know where you are flying?  
Where will you finally be taken?  
Some fly among the pigs,  
Some fly among the barnyards,  
Some roll among the cattle,  
Some find a place of bread,  
Some fly to the place of bread rolls,  
Some know to the spring shoots,  
Some notice the mountains,  
Find the place of bread,  
But fly to the place of bread rolls —  
Some got under a dry spruce,  
Some got under a wide alder,  
Some got under a dear birch,  
Some got under a slim pine,  
Some got under a wet pine.  
Who got under a dry spruce:  
That one got a bachelor man,  
Who got under a wide alder:  
That one got a widower man,  
The widow’s widow grieving,  
Choosing poor children,  
Who got under a dear birch:  
That one got a young man,  
Who got under a slim pine:  
That one got an old man,  
Who got under a wet pine:  
That one got a drunkard man.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I saw the girl growing,  
Rising from the cradle,  
Immediately I went to guard,  
Feeding the pitch-black one,  
Giving rye to the gray one.  
Under the oil, I gave meals,  
Carrying rye in heaps,  
In the shade, I carried water.  
I fed the gray one for Anne,  
For Linalakk Liisu,  
For Kõrv Krõeda,  
I fed the black one to run otherwise.  
Neither the old woman’s soul knew this,  
Nor the room’s soul knew this.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virulasi five-six,  
Harjakaid seven-eight:  
From them alone come Järva men –  
Järva herdsman boy!

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin pretty young girl  
Slender scribe  
Come to me and hasten  
I will set the sleeve to work  
No, I will not take you into the rain  
Nor send you into the storms.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin young maiden  
Slender at her writing desk  
Why do you hesitate when coming to me  
Coming to me; coming to me  
To me, a young man  
To me, proud of my good genes  
To the copper belt, white dawn  
Because you hesitate when coming to me  
Coming to me; being with us  
To me, a young man  
To me, proud of my good genes  
To the copper belt, white dawn  
That our yard is muddy  
The cowsheds full of holes  
Oxen treading in our yard  
The cows have slept heavily  
Foals playfully running around  
The maiden walks with the ox  
The maiden walks on the dry heath  
Walk along the edge of the garden  
Until you come to our yard  
Because you hesitate when coming to me  
Coming to me; being with us  
To me, a young man  
To me, proud of my good genes  
To the copper belt, white dawn  
That our wells are far away  
Water five versts (3.3 km each) away  
Do not complain, maiden  
The oxen will bring the water  
The reins will pull the cart  
Servants will bring the tub  
Will put the kettle on the fire  
Because you hesitate when coming to me  
Coming to me; being with us  
To me, a young man  
To me, proud of my good genes  
To the copper belt, white dawn  
That you have many debts  
More than pearl beads  
You think to pawn them  
To borrow bread  
To waste money  
To take grains  
To send salt  
You will not pawn those  
No bread will be borrowed  
No salt will be sent  
We will be thirsty at the foot  
When the harvest is poor  
The man’s gun will bring catch  
Do not hesitate when coming to me  
Coming to me; being with us  
We have large fields in the fields  
And wide strips in the forest  
Edges run down to the brook  
Sides reach the sea  
Corners to the gray meadow  
If you are, if you are a maiden  
If you are quick to pick  
Swift to take by hands  
Then your crown will carry gold  
Your head will carry a lace cap  
Your neck will carry a golden necklace  
Crown with golden ribbons  
Shoulders with pearl beads  
Hips with a half skirt  
Shins with silk stockings  
Feet with new shoes  
If you are, if you are a maiden  
If you are quick to pick  
Swift to take by hands  
Then you will feel the thimbleberries  
Your side will feel the rowans  
Shoulder blades the apple trees  
Forearms the maples  
Thighs like rowan bushes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Why are you laughing at me,  
With your narrow eyes mocking me,  
You are not worth a smile,  
Nor is the girl with the narrow eyes.  
The boy went to the manor court,  
The girl went to eavesdrop,  
Whose right would prevail,  
Whose right would rise.  
The maiden’s right prevailed,  
The boy’s right declined.  
Where did that maiden herself end up?  
The maiden herself died.  
Where was that maiden buried?  
In the churchyard of Kullamaa.  
Who buried that maiden?  
There were five clever men from the manor,  
Five had gray scarves,  
Six wore golden earrings,  
Seven had star-shaped marks on their shins.  
What grew on that maiden’s grave?  
Peony reeds, lily leaves,  
Straighter German onions.  
Where is that boy now?  
The boy himself died.  
Where was that boy buried?  
Behind the room in the slaughter yard,  
Above the urine buckets,  
Between the pig baths.  
Who buried that boy?  
There were six Krants dogs from the village.  
What grew on that boy’s grave?  
Dog thistles, club leaves.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Boys, young lads  
Do not make the girls laugh  
Girls should not be untamed  
Surely you sometimes crave girls  
Crave a bride  
Surely you go from village to village  
And visit house to house  
You ask from the family mother  
And ask from the family housekeeper  
Give me the daughter  
Who swings in your arms  
And sways on a silver rein  
Faints into weak water  
And under the ribbons of the skirt.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let other men not do  
What I myself have done  
With my foolish drinking,  
With my silly thinking.  
I took an old woman,  
I bought an old horse,  
I took an old little dog.  
When I come home from the tavern,  
Neither does the old dog bark,  
Nor does the old horse neigh.  
Boys, lads,  
Take wives from the maidens,  
Buy horses from the foals.  
Take dogs from the puppies.  
When you come home from the tavern,  
The foal neighs,  
The woman laughs from the maidens,  
Your mother made you warm socks,  
Your mother made you wise shoes,  
She told you to walk nicely,  
Across the yard properly,  
Across the bridge straight,  
Across the field clearly,  
Across the yard prettily.  
So the man leads our maiden,  
Like the shoots on the walnut tree,  
Hazel in the blue forest,  
Roses in the herb garden.  
But then comes the trickster boy,  
The cunning rogue,  
He takes the shoots from the walnut tree,  
The hazel from the blue forest,  
The roses from the herb garden.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Awake on the pine forest trail  
The rooster crowed like a young girl  
Five met an old tune  
Six met a dried shoe  
Seven met a shoemaker's apprentice  
A hundred met an old series  
A thousand met the wind vane.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Our men are beer drinkers  
Beer drinkers, fishing neighbors.  
They hear the horse urinating  
The foal throwing water  
They think of running to drink vodka  
Rising beer barrels.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Why do men hate me  
Why did boys despise me  
The blue-collars dug at me  
The sailors cursed me  
The black-collars proclaimed?  
I did according to the men's wishes  
Following the boys' lead.  
I did according to the maiden's wishes  
Following the shepherd's lead.  
Then I was dear in the maiden's eyes  
Then I was dear in the shepherd's eyes  
I was dear to two sisters  
I was well-behaved in the eyes of five brothers.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I go through the villages,  
Through new streets,  
A pearly horse gleams,  
The pavement street clatters.  
Girls watched from the shadows,  
From under the village the firewood,  
From under the time the asparagus,  
One said to the other:  
"If only that man were mine  
Who rides along the road,  
Gallops along the path."  

I passed by the miller,  
Also went by the weaver;  
The miller’s stone rumbled,  
The weaver’s spindles creaked;  
I went inside Siimul’s—  
At Siimul’s a pig was slaughtered,  
In the black bull’s store,  
At the gentleman’s ox-selling place,  
Then sausages began to sizzle,  
Pig intestines to smoke,  
Fatballs to swell—  
But then came the cat, the evil bird,  
Took the sizzling sausages  
And the swelling fatballs away;  
I chased the cat,  
The cat went scratching to the well,  
I went to bring cords from the village,  
From Nigul I brought linden cord,  
From the weaver hemp cord,  
I twisted the linden cords,  
Treated the tarred rope with pitch,  
The hemp rope broke,  
Then the mice laughed from under the wall:  
“Just right, just right, little cat,  
Where are my nine brothers,  
Nine good sisters,  
Seven straight cousins;  
All are taken by the cat,  
Into the cat’s twisted paws!”

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My grandmother was a pearl necklace:
She got daughters married,
Sons took wives.
My grandmother was a horn necklace,
An old, crafty woman’s daughter;
She brought a handsome young man,
Brought a capable one from elsewhere.
My dear gold twisted necklace:
She had three suitors.
All were from craftsmen’s families,
All were from wise households,
From the richest of the rich,
From families with gold coins,
From households with copper coins.
One was a ploughman,
A wielder of a broad axe.
He was skilled at measuring,
Had seven sickles,
Nine planes at home.
The second was a blacksmith's apprentice;
He was a smelter of iron.
A maker of copper wagons.
An old hitter of nails,
A tapper of small coins.
The third was a weaver:
He made blankets with capercaillie patterns,
Pillowcases with duck patterns,
Sheets with atlas patterns,
Tablecloths with ribbons,
Handkerchiefs with woodpecker patterns.
The old rune was a strict necklace:
She brought sour cream with butter,
She poured five vats of vodka.
The old cow was a cud-chewer necklace:
Because she got Võru butter.
The old pig was a silk necklace:
Because she got lard even for the table,
She could eat on a saw horse.
The old sheep was a fleece necklace:
Because she could knit cubes,
Make a baby’s cradle,
Weave leg straps.
The old dog was a snarling necklace,
She was the evening herald outside,
Announcing the arrival of the suitors.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh dear fellows, young lads,  
Maidens, young girls,  
Why do you girls smile,  
Why do you sneer at me?  
Indeed, the maidens laughed in the corner,  
That I might slip  
Like a duck-wing  
From the dewy grass to the straw bundle.  
Let me come a second time,  
A second time and in autumn!  
My horse is below like this stove,  
The man was with the feast like this day,  
The hat is like the moon’s servant.  
A wreath like the day’s young boy.  
The girls fled to the shade,  
Behind the bush, behind the stump,  
Behind the bush a hunchback,  
Behind the stump a limping one,  
On the fourth a little finger.  
“If only that man were mine,  
If only he were to be with me,  
Who rides through the village  
Before him rides the grey spirit,  
In his ear flows golden ears,  
Behind flows a dancing step  
Riding over the Finnish bridge,  
Along the golden shore of Kudrun.  
On the Finnish bridge tapping,  
The man’s hat swinging,  
The foundation beams cracking,  
The cuckoo fell from the roof beam,  
Another from the second roof beam,  
The third from the third beam,  
The fourth with the maiden in her arms.”

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Grandma kindly told me to go,  
proudly across the yard.  
When coming, bring firewood to the stove,  
when leaving, take food to the cattle...

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
This girl is my namesake,  
This wreath is my crown  
Whose coat is gold-decorated  
But whose wreath is made of fur.  
I do not want Anneke,  
Nor do I want Kaieke.  
Anne has torn shirt tails,  
Kaie has worn-out sleeves.  
But I will take Mari, the berry-bringer,  
The berry-bringer, the herder.  
Mari is plump, Mari is sweet,  
Mari has a fashionable mouth.  
She misleads boys into the spruce forest,  
To the edge of the ravine,  
Young men to the meadow,  
Old men to the hunter’s hut.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I don’t have German women by birth,  
But I do have German women by birth,  
Poepoiss’s little bride.  
Poepoiss calls me inside,  
Into the red yard,  
"From whom do you buy silver?  
From whom do you exchange copper?  
From whom do you listen to the little gold chain?"

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I had to go to Pilli  
To tease Pinni Pilli girl  
To charm Maidu Anne  
To pull at Kõrtsi Kaie’s neck  
To hustle Suigu Salme;  
But I ended up falling asleep with Malle  
Malle’s kisses were sweeter,  
Than Pilli’s milk cakes  
Than Anne’s fragrant breads  
Than Kaie’s chicken eggs  
And Salme’s warm milk.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My sister was taken to a place of fortune
Where there are long milk cans
Where there are tall cream tops
And also wide bread tables
The room was made of turf dust
The stove made of goose jawbones
The hearth from chicken eggshells
The roof from pea pods
The eaves from meat
When the wind blows, it rattles
When the sun shines, it roars
When it rains, it clatters against it.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Father said a death reaction in the ground,  
Mother a soul's annex:  
Girls are little birds,  
If you start to walk away,  
Start to walk to the meadow:  
Then take sleeves into your fists.  
Handkerchiefs down the shoulder.  
If you meet a crowd of men,  
A crowd of men, a gang of boys.  
Pull the boy’s head down.  
A married woman pulls her face!  
Knock both eyebrows.  
Do you want a man from me?  
Go through Kalevipoeg,  
What is your concern with me?  
Let me be a little donkey,  
Grow into a little herdboy,  
Rise into an acorn.  
There will be plenty of seekers on that donkey,  
Wanting on the acorn—  
Demanding on the heath girl.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Go away, family boy.  
The family boy is deceitful!  
He deceives a lot, beats a lot,  
Steals and lies a lot.  
He quarrels a lot to bring:  
Many worthless gifts,  
A hundred useless slippers.  
When I had to deal with madness,  
And had to live with life,  
I didn't finally get five gifts.  
I didn't sneak through the bark of the pine!  
Like here, before six months,  
Old then five woolen carpets.  
Now you want to grow big.  
Into the purse of the people's treasure.  
To the family of the schoolmaster -  
To the farm of the old tanner.  
Where there are big dining tables  
And long benches to sit on.  
Wide bread paddles;  
Very tall birch trunks.  
Then go under the mother-in-law's foot,  
Then go under the father-in-law's foot.  
Stir the milk churn,  
Then take the golden club,  
The schoolmaster's whip.  
Warm the father-in-law's sides with a stick,  
Warm the mother-in-law's sides with a stick.  
From the cheeks hit the mark,  
Sell the vodka skin.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin maiden, young man,
Why do you resist when I come to you?
To me, a young man,
With a flushed, rosy cheek?
Tomorrow I am going to Germany.
From there I will bring three crowns,
Green - braided ones.
I got to live, I got to be: -
A fist brings a braided one,
A whip brought blood-red, -
A farmer with a matted head.
Then I brought rods to lash
Willow rods - to whip.
I brought rods for spanking
These go along the skin of a woman.
Thin twigs along
Delicate shoulders along.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, if only I had seen it in a dream  
If only I had thought of it while sleeping.  
Dozing, it came to my mind:  
That I had received an angry sweetheart  
A quarrelsome second wife -  
A sorrowful young woman!  
Then I would have built a new barn here  
A barn with a lintel,  
Walls made of pork.  
A roof made of chicken eggs.  
Inside I would have placed a silk bed  
A silk bed, a thread game  
For the young son to sleep  
To lull the new sweetheart to sleep  
To quiet the old sweetheart.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Why do you blow, little wind  
Bang, bad little weather?  
Today my man is at sea  
Along the fish river.  
Blow tomorrow, little wind  
Bang, bad little weather!  
Tomorrow the brides are at sea  
The kittens – along the fish river.  
The brides went to the sea  
The kittens – along the fish river.  
I heard a boy banging  
Throwing men into the sea:  
I thought I should be there  
I ran running, walked walking  
My legs burned  
Spark fires – scalded  
There was a stranger, a scoundrel  
I helped to tie his hand  
Put the ring on his foot –  
Fasten the lock.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I am Maie, I am a berry  
I am a strawberry on the hill.  
In the bluebell grove  
In the wood pigeon copse  
In the red willow grove  
In the meadowsweet thicket  
Let the boys come to pick me  
To find the bluebells  
To gather the wood pigeons  
To harvest the meadowsweet  
I slept, Mari, on the hill  
Among the meadowsweets  
In the bluebell grove  
In the red willow grove  
And I saw a dream while sleeping:  
"In front of the fig tree  
Behind the gold tree  
In the chamber of the alder  
The bog myrtle was inside the sauna  
The apple tree was in the yard.  
Village girls, little old women!  
Understand this dream  
That I saw while sleeping:  
'In front of the fig tree  
Behind the gold tree  
In the chamber of the alder  
The bog myrtle was inside the sauna  
The apple tree was also in the yard.  
You drink spirits under the fig tree  
Suitors come from the gold tree  
The alder is a female companion  
The bog myrtle is a lady of the sauna  
The apple tree is the one at the wedding.'"

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
What is a nap for  
So I go to the man from the forest  
From the tree root - even for the boy  
From the stump root to the suitor?  
I send the sister without oil  
Brother halfway with a crescent  
Aunt filling the trough  
Did the kettle make it without oil?  
The titmouse made it without oil  
Who brought the bread roll for the kettle?  
The bear brought the bread roll for the kettle  
Who brought the pieces of meat?  
The wolf brought the pieces of meat  
The trees were tall wedding guests  
The birches broad as a stack of ladies.  
The field a wide dining table.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:

The wolf brought the pieces of meat

The birches broad as a stack of ladies.  
The field a wide dining table.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My dear little brother!  
If you are going to take a wife,  
If you are going to get a wreath,  
If you are going to row a boat:  
Do not take a wife from the manor.  
In the manor, they are somewhat  
Beautiful under the windows,  
With red balconies above,  
Polished stone cellars,  
The master’s wedding chambers.  
They do not know how to walk with fire,  
How to set a shirt without sparks,  
How to milk a cow without a chandelier.  
How to cook broth without bitterness.  
Watch the woman at the distaff,  
Aim the woman with the sickle,  
Try her on the loom.  
The woman at the sickle is smooth,  
The woman at the distaff is nimble,  
The woman at the loom is patient,  
The one in the hay barn is tender.  
Do not take a wife from the tavern.  
All are above even the stalks,  
Fine for going to church.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Girls, little birds  
Cheese locks, neat ones  
Sweethearts, smooth ones  
Wreath heads, pretty ones!  
Do you know where you will go?  
Where will you be finally taken?  
Some will go to the sad spruce forest,  
Some to the bright open forest,  
Some to the wet pine forest,  
Some to the dear birch forest,  
Some to the wise oak forest,  
Some flew to the alder forest.  
Who goes to the bad spruce forest  
Goes to a bad man.  
Who goes to the wet pine forest  
Goes to a man who drinks heavily.  
Who goes to the dear birch forest  
Goes to a dear embrace.  
Who goes to the wise oak forest?  
Goes to tend the farm.  
Who goes to the bright open forest?  
Goes to the white plain.  
Who flew to the alder forest?  
Goes to beg for bread.  
Girls, little birds,  
Sweethearts, smooth ones!  
Ask your Gods!  
That they do not drink heavily  
Nor become tavern-goers.  
A drunkard drank to the tavern's end  
To Kuie's tavern in Kuie,  
To Kase's tavern in Kasuka.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Be well-mannered, our girls  
Be well-mannered, walk tall  
The well-mannered are taken to the village  
The tall are picked up  
The well-mannered were taken because of good behavior  
The tall because of their strong walk  
Because of work, daughters were taken.  
Because of hands, marriage proposals were made  
Because of fingers, engagements happened  
Not because of father's scolding  
Not because of brother’s cattle  
Because of father-in-law’s good bulls  
Father’s horses stayed in the yard  
Brother’s cattle stayed in the yard  
The herd stayed poor in the enclosure  
When I was once courted  
I began to leave  
I got straw bundles from the yard  
I got birch twigs from the gate  
This too I had to take secretly  
To surprise across the meadow.  
To tread through time  
Father called me stingy  
Mother called me untimely  
A servant as a maid’s helper,  
Children as table sweepers...  
I got to live, I got to be  
Wait, wait, lineage, Alba lineage  
Let my room be made  
New homes are being knocked on  
New houses painted  
Then father will come to make it nice  
Mother will come untimely  
Then I can feed the lineage  
And scar the pastures—

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:

The well-mannered are taken to the village

I began to leave
