label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I would go to the man, the man would not take me,  
I would stay home, the boy would not stay.  
Neither would he leave me  
Nor would the little mad one forget.  
If I get angry, where can I lay it down?  
If I go mad, where do I forget?  
I'm not going to be a bull to be killed,  
Nor a horse to be exchanged,  
Nor a mule to be abandoned,  
Nor food to be fed.  
When I come home from the threshing floor,  
I may not live indoors,  
In the room, by the door.  
I jumped into the barnyard,  
I may not be there either.  
From there I step into the barn,  
I may not be there either.  
I went back inside after all,  
Watching secretly myself,  
Head poked through a crack,  
Feet beneath the window.  
He was angry, fallen into bed,  
Badly sunk onto the pillow,  
I sit under the cradle  
Beginning to cry softly,  
Calling for my foster parents.  
Mother-in-law came to ask me,  
Mother-in-law came to ask me,  
Speaking despite the stove,  
Laughing near the window.  
I heard, soon I answer:  
Oh you father-in-law, with sparse beard,  
You stoke the stove without tongs.  
Oh you mother-in-law, with a crooked jaw,  
You boil broth with a ladle.  
Or you stove, with a curved back,  
You carry ashes without a bag,  
Or you window, with a tattered curtain,  
You sweep the room without a broom. -  
What can a woman do with an empty pocket,  
A person with an empty bag,  
A farm girl with a runny nose,  
A house girl with a sore mouth. -  
If only I had seen in a dream,  
While dozing the thought came to me,  
While sleeping I imagined,  
That I am being courted by the bad one,  
Given to a cruel one,  
Drunken with angry spirits,  
Fed with bitter bread:  
If at least the six-stripy ones were gathered,  
Between the edges of the carpet,  
Join these two linen pieces,  
Let these men go past,  
Let the suitors walk by.  
There would be time to be with my beloved,  
To grow with gentian,  
To prepare in the copper age.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I rode, I rode, little brother  
I rode over the Finnish bridge  
The Finnish bridge swayed  
The foundation beams creaked  
I would look at Finnish girls  
From under the fence, from above the pile  
If these were my men  
A horse in front like an oven  
Men on top like the sun  
A belt on the waist like a rainbow  
A hat like a church bell.  
(This appeals to men's minds  
To boys' white heads  
To the girl's untamed hair.)

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I rode, I rode, little brother  
I rode over the Finnish bridge  
The Finnish bridge swayed  
The foundation beams creaked  
I would look at Finnish girls  
From under the fence, from above the pile  
If these were my men  
A horse in front like an oven  
Men on top like the sun  
A belt on the waist like a rainbow  
A hat like a church bell.  
(This appeals to men's minds  
To boys' white heads  
To the girl's untamed hair.)

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What is beneath our yard?  
The sea is beneath our yard.  
What is in the middle of the sea?  
A well is in the middle of the sea.  
What is in the middle of the well?  
A pillar is in the middle of the well.  
What is on the top of the pillar?  
A sieve is on the top of the pillar.  
What is at the bottom of the sieve?  
Soot is at the bottom of the sieve.  
What is on the hands of the soot?  
A child is on the hands of the soot.  
What is on the child’s chest?  
A brooch is on the child’s chest.  
What is on the edge of the brooch?  
A ring is on the edge of the brooch.  
Where did you get that ring?  
From the finger bones of soldiers,  
From the collarbones of merchants,  
From the knee bones of shop boys.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If the boy died,  
Where was he buried?  
In Kuuramaa under a spruce,  
In Pärnumaa under a poplar,  
In Läänemaa under a alder.  
What plants grow on the boy’s grave?  
Groundsel, burdock leaves,  
Garden-edge nettles.  
Who visited the boy’s grave?  
Every month the devils came,  
Every Friday the quarrelsome ones.  

If the girl died,  
Where was she buried?  
In Kullamäe churchyard,  
Under the slender tower.  
What plants grow on the girl’s grave?  
Coltsfoot, cranesbills,  
Long-necked foxgloves,  
German edible onions.  
Who visited the girl’s grave?  
Every month the kings came,  
Every Friday the lords of Riga.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
I plowed the swamp, the bluebells grew,  
I plowed the ridges, the heathers grew.  
Then I sent the girls to pick hay.  
Who was quick to pick hay,  
Swift to toss the bundle,  
That girl was mine in name,  
The wreath-bride at my side.  
Here I began feeding the horse,  
So it could grow strong for riding.  
I carried bundles of oats,  
Drew water from the shade,  
Carried flour in my pocket.  
Then I rode straight to my father-in-law’s house,  
To my father-in-law’s house, to mother-in-law’s house.  
Hello father-in-law, boot top!  
Hello mother-in-law, cooking neck!  
Are the girls at home?  
Our girl is by the well,  
At the well to water the herd.  
I rode straight to the well.  
Hello girl, cooking neck!  
To the one who makes calves drink,  
To the one who makes my horses drink,  
Give the grey one a saddle,  
Let the black one nibble,  
And let the ears hang one over the other.  
I am a poor traveler,  
A traveler, a land measurer,  
A messenger of kings,  
A handler of German affairs,  
A writer of large letters.  
The girl heard and answered:  
You are not a poor traveler,  
A traveler, a land measurer,  
A messenger of kings,  
A handler of German affairs,  
A writer of large letters;  
You are a scoundrel, young man!  
Why do you have kicks on your whip,  
Braided straps on your jacket,  
And a braided tail on your horse!

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I went to visit under the village barn,  
The girls didn’t want to go,  
They started barking at me:  
You don’t have a calico shirt on your back,  
A calico shirt, a silk vest,  
Beads around your neck,  
German boots on your feet.  

I cried by the stream,  
I put my cap on the table,  
And the cap on the bottom.  

A girl came to ask me:  
Why are you crying, young lad?  
I replied back:  
Why should I cry, young lad?  

I went to visit under the village barn,  
The girls didn’t want to go,  
They started barking at me:  
You don’t have a calico shirt on your back,  
A calico shirt, a silk vest,  
Beads around your neck,  
German boots on your feet.  

The girl heard and replied back:  
Wait, wait young lad!  
Tomorrow we’ll go to the town of Paide,  
Paide town, to Ohmann’s shop,  
There you can get a calico shirt,  
A calico shirt, a silk vest,  
Beads around your neck,  
German boots on your feet.  

Then go under the village barn:  
The girls will want you then.  
Some will think you are the manor’s son,  
Or a farm worker,  
Or a steward,  
Or a groom following behind.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Maiden, young maiden,  
Come to me and drive!  
I will not take you into the rain,  
Nor carry you into the dew —  
I will bring the oats into the chamber,  
The barley to the edge of the oven,  
The flax to the threshing floor.  
Maiden, young maiden,  
Come now and drive!  

Come to my village, caretaker,  
To manage my property,  
To milk a single cow.  

We have three barns outside:  
One is for threshing wheat,  
Another for threshing rye,  
The third for threshing barley.  

Be true, do not lie!  
I was at your place yesterday,  
I did come to you.  

You have three barns outside:  
One for mice to jump on,  
Another for cats to roar,  
The third for rats to crawl.  

Maiden, young maiden,  
Come now and drive!  

We have dun mares,  
We have bays in the harness,  
Chestnuts with spotted coats.  

Be true, do not lie!  
You had mice in the dun mares,  
You had rats in the bays,  
Cats in the chestnuts’ harness.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgin girl, young maiden,  
Come to me and hasten!  
I will bring tunes in the apron basket:  
One I bring is silk blue,  
Another is apron red,  
The third is checkered,  
The fourth is mare’s hair,  
The fifth is golden-striped.  
Ellakene, little brother,  
I have become yours,  
I have remained yours,  
Then you bring the checkered fist,  
The mare’s hair headscarf,  
The striped coat,  
The golden-buttoned jacket.  
Ellakene, little brother!  
First I will sit in the river,  
In the running river water,  
In the cold village spring—  
Before I get into your carriage,  
Your poor wagon,  
The shabby man’s coach.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Virgins, young girls!  
What are you building for yourselves,  
Dressing up finely,  
Twisting wreaths on your heads!  
Yet I will not take you,  
I will not come as a suitor to the gathering.  
I have three girls at home,  
Who dangle stockings for me,  
Slip through my hands:  
The marten jumps into the hole,  
Foam jumps into the bowl,  
Tears to another household.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Father is going with his wife  
Brother is going with a young girl  
I go crying towards the corner  
I wander towards the empty bed  
I grope in the old fur coat.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If only my grandmother knew  
If only my grandfather knew  
If only my other half knew  
If only my spouse knew  
If only my young men understood  
If only they truly knew my sorrow  
Yearning in this world  
Worrying elsewhere  
They would run with ears pricked up  
Let the stallions run freely  
They would come after me  
Take my beloved home  
Take my berries to the house  
Take flowers to the porch  
A pretty jug to the chamber  
Take gifts to my father-in-law  
Take gifts to my mother-in-law  
Bring joy to my husband  
And comfort to my neighbors

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Village girls, little old women;  
Village men, little old men,  
Help me understand  
Give a little advice  
Should I leave soon, or should I stay soon  
Or should I soon give a promise? -  
Rings on the autumn finger;  
Clasp on the table,  
I am a girl today! -  
A maiden until tomorrow morning,  
I keep my honor and grow for a long time,  
And will soon have a good wreath of years;  
A wreath of straight hair  
A wreath of white sleeves  
I have ten suitors;  
Nine admirers  
When I begin to consider:  
One was: Ants, rarely near the boat;  
Second: Lauri with a broad jaw,  
Third: Mihkel with a thin mustache  
Fourth: a worthy maiden;  
Fifth: a servant of a torch,  
Sixth: Jaan from the spruce forest;  
Seventh: that one, better  
Eighth: a fisherman’s son  
Ninth: beyond the seas  
Tenth: a groom from the village

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
If that man were mine  
Who rides past you  
Rides along the road  
Does he have a rainbow’s belt  
A hat on his head like a wreath?  
Riding past the Finnish bridge  
Over Curonian mountains  
Near the soft Finnish bridge.  
Curonian mountains roaring  
Fallen under the water  
He struck my brother in the water!  
My brother went under the water  
My brother’s wreath remained on top of the water  
I am going to look for my brother;  
Who will come back to me?  
The moon came from Curonia.  
I greet the moon:  
Hello moon from Curonia!  
You walk high above,  
You aim straight,  
You watch from the light.  
Did you see my brother?  
Rowing on the river  
Rowing in the sea bay  
In the small pilot boat there  
With a white sail in the middle  
The moon answered me:  
I did not see your brother,  
Rowing on the river:  
Rowing in the sea bay.  
In the small pilot boat there  
With a white sail in the middle.  
I am going to look for my brother,  
Who will come back to me?  
He came from Pärnu region.  
I greet the one from Pärnu  
Hello from the Pärnu region.  
You walk high above,  
You aim straight,  
You watch from the light  
Did you see my brother  
Rowing on the river  
Rowing in the sea bay  
In the small pilot boat there  
With a white sail in the middle?  
The one from Pärnu answered me:  
I did not see your brother  
Rowing on the river  
Rowing in the sea bay  
In the small pilot boat there  
With a white sail in the middle.  
I am going to look for my brother  
Who will come back to me?  
A second man came from the stars:  
I greet the man from the stars  
Hello man from the stars  
You aim straight  
You watch from the light  
You walk high above,  
Did you see my brother  
Rowing on the river  
Rowing in the sea bay  
In the small pilot boat there  
With a white sail in the middle?  
The star answered me:  
I did not see your brother  
Rowing on the river  
Rowing in the sea bay  
In the small pilot boat there  
With a white sail in the middle.  
I will start to cry for my brother  
The moon, I cried for my brother  
The one from Pärnu, I cried for my brother’s wreath  
The year will bury my brother.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Essu lessu leaves were wide  
The boys of our parish were tall  
They went to the heath to drive  
The apple was under the stove  
They themselves were bright as the day  
Their hat like a king's crown  
A belt on their waist like a city ribbon  
Boots shining on their feet  
Gloves colorful on their hands  
Essu lessu leaves were wide  
The boys of the other parish were tall  
They went to the heath to drive  
The apple had fallen down  
They themselves were bright as the day  
Their hat had wilted  
The belt on their waist had rusted  
Boots muddy on their feet  
Gloves worn on their hands

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
If there were a man for me,
Who rides along the road there,
Rides along the trail,
A rainbow belt around his waist,
A hat on his head like a wreath,
Rides along the Finnish bridge,
Along the Curonian mountain.
Along the Finnish bridge he bent,
The Curonian mountain roared,
Fell under the horse:
He struck my brother down.
My brother went under the water,
My brother's wreath stayed above the water.
I go to look for my brother.
Who will answer me?
The moon came from Curonia.
I greeted the moon:
"Hello, moon from Curonia!
You walk high,
You aim straight,
You watch over the light:
Have you seen my brother
By the river idling,
Rowing on a sea island,
In Luisa's boat,
In the middle of Puise's sail?"
The moon answered me:
"I did not see your brother
Idle by the river,
Rowing on a sea island,
In Luisa's boat,
In the middle of Puise's sail."
I go to look for my brother,
Who will answer me?
The sun came from Pärnu land.
I greeted the sun:
"Hello, sun from Pärnu land!
You walk high,
You aim straight,
You watch over the light:
Have you seen my brother
By the river idling,
Rowing on a sea island,
In Luisa's boat,
In the middle of Puise's sail?"
The sun answered me:
"I did not see your brother
Idle by the river,
Rowing on a sea island,
In Luisa's boat,
In the middle of Puise's sail."
I go to look for my brother,
Who will answer me?
The stars, the other men, came.
I greeted the stars:
"Hello, stars, the other men!
You aim straight,
You walk high:
Have you seen my brother
Idle by the river,
Rowing on a sea island,
In Luisa's boat,
In the middle of Puise's sail?"
The stars answered me:
"I did not see your brother
Idle by the river,
Rowing on a sea island,
In Luisa's boat,
In the middle of Puise's sail."
I begin to weep for my brother,
The moon I wept for my brother's death,
The sun I wept for my brother's wreath,
The year for my brother's resting place.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The leaves of the Essu-Lessu were broad,  
The boys of our parish were tall,  
They went to the heath to ride:  
The horse was harnessed beneath,  
The rider himself in the daylight,  
A hat like a king's crown,  
A belt on their waist like a city ribbon,  
Boots shining on their feet,  
Gloves brightly patterned on their hands.  

The leaves of the Essu-Lessu were broad,  
The boys of another parish were tall,  
They went to the heath to ride:  
The horse had become moldy beneath,  
The rider himself had aged in the daylight,  
The hat on his head had grown moldy,  
His belt had rusted at the buckle,  
His boots muddy on his feet,  
His gloves worn on his hands.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If only I had seen it in a dream.
Came to mind even while dozing,
I would have long ago built the barns
Barns built from rye straw,
Walls made from pig meat,
Roof from chicken eggs.
Doors out of eyelids
Locks from the jawbone.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mine is not here.  
My companion does not grow here.  
I am mine in Oandi  
My companion far away,  
The man across the sea,  
The wind may carry health,  
To ask in prayer for bad weather,  
May hail not destroy the crops,  
May clouds not last long.  
Heaven, grant wise mind.  
If healthy, let him work,  
If sick, may God help!

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My fiancé Peibesessa,  
Accompanies the young far away,  
Neither moonlight is heard by me,  
Nor the week is seen by me,  
Nor is it proper to offer a hand.  
In summer, it is not appropriate to talk,  
When coming to visit home  
He then brings money for the horse.  
At the edge of the sieve, rings  
And old talents are secured.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Before I play with the tufts  
Around the wheels,  
Before with those girls,  
What was half-finished at Kis,  
Loved too soon.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What the mud horse does
The woman annoys the youth
The madman goes wandering to the village
The woman goes crying towards the corner
In the barns towards the stove
Pecking towards the empty bed
Rustling old coats
Oh you old coat
It was hard to walk
Even better to sleep on the ridge pole
Look, the famous woman of our farmstead
A rich woman with thick thighs
They went to the spruce trees to relieve themselves
To the chaff to fart
The floor stuck in the butt
A needle at the edge of the hole
The stove pulled even the claws
The nest on all four paws
Her own man in the crotch.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I sing, why don’t I sing
I sing a song about a horse
Its head covered with nutshells
Its body made of haystacks
Its tail made of hay remnants
Its hooves made of birch bark
Its iron made from twigs
Its nails made from pot shards
I am a man, the son of a sailor
A dear boy, the son of a fisherman
I step to the edge of the boat
I row with a big ship
I push another man’s sea
I hunt another new moon
I wear another sheepskin coat
I am a man of the forest
A decent boy from the boys’ field
A dear boy from Kapu manor
A famous boy from Kauska parish
Wait, wait, dear boys
Endure, boys of Kapu
Hold on, boys of Preedi
The boys of Karina are coming
Big mouths, thick noses
Upper lip was long
Lower teeth were broad
Even this broth is for drinking
To scratch and to cleanse.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I sing, why don’t I sing
I sing a song about a horse
Its head covered with nutshells
Its body made of haystacks
Its tail made of hay remnants
Its hooves made of birch bark
Its iron made from twigs
Its nails made from pot shards
I am a man, the son of a sailor
A dear boy, the son of a fisherman
I step to the edge of the boat
I row with a big ship
I push another man’s sea
I hunt another new moon
I wear another sheepskin coat
I am a man of the forest
A decent boy from the boys’ field
A dear boy from Kapu manor
A famous boy from Kauska parish
Wait, wait, dear boys
Endure, boys of Kapu
Hold on, boys of Preedi
The boys of Karina are coming
Big mouths, thick noses
Upper lip was long
Lower teeth were broad
Even this broth is for drinking
To scratch and to cleanse.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Oh those good Kusna boys  
March like those Germans  
We eat the same food  
We drink the same drinks  
We sleep together at night  
We wear the same fur coats  
We have the same new clothes  
We buy many horses  
One takes another's wife  
Another takes from another's farm  
We go to the midsummer church  
I hang a rope on the hat's brim  
A rein on the belt  
You go to grandma's grave  
I go to grandpa's grave  
Rise up, little grandpa  
Rise up, little grandma  
Cannot rise, cannot wake  
Grass has grown over the ground  
Hay over the grave  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Buttercups over the eyebrows  
Dew herbs around the neck

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Oh those good Kusna boys  
March like those Germans  
We eat the same food  
We drink the same drinks  
We sleep together at night  
We wear the same fur coats  
We have the same new clothes  
We buy many horses  
One takes another's wife  
Another takes from another's farm  
We go to the midsummer church  
I hang a rope on the hat's brim  
A rein on the belt  
You go to grandma's grave  
I go to grandpa's grave  
Rise up, little grandpa  
Rise up, little grandma  
Cannot rise, cannot wake  
Grass has grown over the ground  
Hay over the grave  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Buttercups over the eyebrows  
Dew herbs around the neck

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
And those strong Raua boys  
Will go to the church on Midsummer  
Seven pigs in front of the sleigh  
There’s pig dung in the pipe  
Tatirida in front of the line  
Wooden turn in front of the pants

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
My mother made woven stockings  
My father made clever shoes  
Daughter, daughter, walk nicely  
Across the yard right steps  
Across the garden white steps  
Across the field light steps  
Men peeked from the shadows  
From under the fence, quietly  
If only that girl were mine  
If only she were my wife from the farm  
Who is walking along the road  
Across the field nicely  
I do not have the courage to approach  
I do not have the heart to take her into my arms  
If I had a purse of gold  
If I had a rich dowry  
Then I would dare to approach  
And take her into my heart

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Little Juan, little squeeze,  
Make me a little wheelbarrow.  
In summer I roll on the grass,  
In winter by the stove’s glow.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Village old women, little old women.  
Help me understand,  
Give me a little advice.  
I had three suitors:  
One was a wolf, another a bear,  
The third was a beautiful lynx,  
The fourth was a handsome fox.  
The wolf brought me great boars,  
The bear brought two necklaces.  
The lynx brought silver without end,  
The fox brought broad crosses.  
Help me understand,  
Give me a little advice,  
How should I sleep with the wolf,  
Breathe with the lynx.  
I have a small little rug,  
A tiny little patch,  
My feet peeked out from under the rug,  
My legs from the wolf’s bed,  
The stirrups from the bedposts.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
In the forest, I got a husband  
Up in the tree, I held a wedding  
In the top of the birch, a veil fluttered  
In the willow bush, a ring was placed  
The trees were long wedding guests  
The birches were white bridesmaids  
A bite was at the feeding table  
The field's edge was a bench  
Stubborn, stubborn old woman  
Stubborn to eat foreign bread  
Ale to eat in holes  
Ugly to eat crusts  
Hard is to eat, sworn old woman  
Oh, my hardship, slave child  
Like pushing and pulling  
A village well's bucket  
Water was drawn out.  
The farmer's daughter born lazy  
Born lazy, become lazy  
Sleeping without a belt in bed  
Much footwork  
I, poor slave child  
Rise up from the mud  
Wash piles of dishes  
Work is put in the box  
The old sprightly spirits  
Then I go plowing to the village  
A furrow goes to the feast  
You hold the harness straps  
The oxen are harnessed at the yoke  
The father is following behind.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let other girls not become  
Accidental third parties  
I feared boys would disappear  
Men drown at sea  
I went to the forest to get a husband  
I went to the barn to herd  
I got a husband, a sea devil  
A strangler at the barn door  
But he beat me with his fist  
And also beat the thumb flower  
And also scratched the knuckle flower  
He put me to sleep on the floor  
Under the stove to warm me up  
To burn the junipers.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Four maiden girls,
Five of them livelier,
Six of them taller,
Went out into the field to reap,
Reaped two rows of rye,
Laid down for a nap,
To rest a little,
Oh, an eagle came from the forest,
Shot onto our barn,
Flew onto the granary roof,
Then began to proclaim there,
Dear boys, dear brothers,
Go out to the field,
See what is done in the field,
I found the girls sleeping,
They had gone underneath to rest,
They had gone on top to brighten,
Ants had eaten their eyes away,
Different souls inside,
Moles had nibbled their lips,
I took the girls to the sauna,
Brought them home to the market house,
I myself lay on the hay,
Placed my head on a pillow,
Put my arm around the girls’ necks,
Got up in the morning;
Thought about leaving the girls behind,
The girls drive away in a cart,
With a small whip,
To the blacksmith's shop in the meadow,
From there she buys syrups,
Ten pounds of cake dough,
Makes the boys fry it,
Boys turn the roast,
Gives it to the young maiden to eat
Whose face blushed
Because she had been touched by the boys,
The boys’ hands had fiddled with her.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I bought a horse from Otti,  
A hat from the village hunter,  
A saddle from the cedar of Saare,  
I rode to Finland, I rode to the islands,  
I rode onto the Finnish bridge,  
The Finnish bridge was a shaky road,  
The main beam was a noisy road,  
My hat was a light road,  
Above the ear was a cut road,  
A girl looks from the shadows,  
From the crouching hill and the coming hill,  
Through time, a fallen hill,  
If only this man were mine,  
Who is on that Finnish bridge,  
The horse was beneath like that stove,  
The man was above like the day,  
The hat like a church bell,  
The belt like a rain rainbow,  
Whose is this handsome master,  
Take me, handsome master.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I bought a horse from Otti,  
A hat from the village hunter,  
A saddle from the cedar of Saare,  
I rode to Finland, I rode to the islands,  
I rode onto the Finnish bridge,  
The Finnish bridge was a shaky road,  
The main beam was a noisy road,  
My hat was a light road,  
Above the ear was a cut road,  
A girl looks from the shadows,  
From the crouching hill and the coming hill,  
Through time, a fallen hill,  
If only this man were mine,  
Who is on that Finnish bridge,  
The horse was beneath like that stove,  
The man was above like the day,  
The hat like a church bell,  
The belt like a rain rainbow,  
Whose is this handsome master,  
Take me, handsome master.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The wind comes on Saturday evening,  
Sunday’s little day,  
The girl goes to sleep with the old man,  
The sister goes with her husband,  
The brother goes with a young maiden,  
I go crying towards the corner,  
Turning towards the empty bed,  
All were cold sideboards,  
The benches were edge tables,  
The end boards were misty,  
The wall logs were like mushrooms,  
I turn to the side, find coldness,  
I turn my back, find the wall.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The fire comes, the bonfire sings,
I drive the herd, the girl makes a cake,
Where I would drive the herd,
Drive the herd to Kaunissaare,
I myself went to Hülissaare,
Made a fire at Tulissaare,
The cat came to my fire,
The boy came to my fire,
I had a stone in my glove,
An iron rod in my hand,
I hit the boy on the head,
Knocked on both eyebrows,
The boy went to the manor court,
Which court will judge,
The court would judge in my favor,
The boy would be beaten on the behind.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If I were Olevik  
If I wore the Kalev shoes  
I would have a wise mind.  
I fear stepping on thorn bushes  
Pricking my toes bloody  
Marking my shins  
I do not know how to hold a gun  
To trample a wise blade.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mother dear,  
Marry this daughter off.  
If you do not marry her off, do not ask either,  
Let her stay home to comb wool,  
To thresh by the riverbank,  
To gather mushrooms by the wall.  
Let a birch tree grow on her neck,  
A buckthorn on her head,  
A juniper among her hair.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mother Ella granny,
Spin me long threads,
Long threads, fine threads,
From which I will make three scarves.
One I will make with a long striped pattern,
The second wide and patchy,
The third with a cat's paw pattern.
Maybe for a lame man,
For a poor unmarried man.
I will lend the first one as bread,
Send the second one with salt,
Wear the third one around my neck.
The one with the long stripes,
I will lend as bread.
The one wide and patchy,
I will send with salt.
The one with the cat's paw pattern,
I will wear around my neck.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Mother, oh dear granny,  
Give that girl to me,  
Who rocks in the cradle,  
Sways in the silver reins,  
Faints in the weak carriage.  
She is very tiny.  
Come back another time,  
Then the young girl will be ready,  
The modest young maiden will be ready.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I saw the girl growing,  
Rising from the ground like a berry.  
I began to take care of the hay.  
Under my arm, I carried the hay,  
In a bundle, I carried the oats.  
Then I gained full strength,  
I went to the father-in-law’s door.  
The father-in-law came outside to look,  
The mother-in-law came outside to look.  
Hello, father-in-law, hello, mother-in-law.  
The horse was driven into the yard,  
Hay was given in front,  
Oats were carried in front,  
I was invited inside,  
They gave me food.  
I did not come to eat afterward,  
Nor did I come to drink,  
I came for the sake of the house,  
I came to see the girl.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
King, little king,  
Why didn’t you come to us last year?  
When rivers ran without oil,  
Springs flowed with vodka.  
From the yard ran beer veins,  
From the gate ran vodka veins,  
From the well path ran rock veins.  
Now you come to a bad swamp,  
The year after year begins.  
Now I strip off the clothes,  
Cut off the plague necklace,  
Save the wreath head from misery.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Kessi's swing - rowan tree  
Girl  
Who went down to the prayer place  
Was Jüri's little boy  
He went down to the prayer place  
Don't fall, maiden  
On a stone, on a stump  
Fall on my hat  
Put a cap on it too  
Then I will give you a gift  
A big brooch

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
A little old woman, a little auntie
Buys a golden dog for the yard
To the iron rack at the gate!
Who lets the men pass by
Without a dowry, going far away.
Her head wears a lace cap
Her belly wears a straw apron
Her shoulders sewn, she would walk,
Wearing silk stockings
Her hips with store skirts.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
When the old bachelor dies  
Then the boy's bell is rung  
The pig cries its eyes out  
Both his bastards are his own.  
Both his bastards are his own  
Old Kulti of the hills.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Other days I should otherwise  
Other times I otherwise would drive  
Comes that blessed little evening  
Wretched little twilight  
All the children go to sleep  
Father goes with his mother  
Brother goes with the young girl  
Sister goes with her husband  
The guy goes with a girl  
I stay alone  
I go crying to the corner  
Toward the empty bed  
I take the crab to talk  
To hold a flea's gratitude  
To the louse a little fun  
The crab is an easy jumper  
The flea is a wise dancer  
The louse slides flying

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What is there below our yard?  
The sea is below our yard.  
What is there in the middle of the sea?  
There is a well in the middle of the sea.  
What is there under the well's shore?  
A pillar is under the well's shore.  
What is on top of the pillar?  
A sieve is on top of the pillar.  
What is at the bottom of the sieve?  
A cuckoo is at the bottom of the sieve.  
What is on the cuckoo's hands?  
A child is on the cuckoo's hands.  
What is on the child's chest?  
A brooch is on the child's chest.  
What is on the eye of the brooch?  
A ring is on the eye of the brooch.  
Where did this ring come from?  
What is this ring made of?  
From the finger bone of a soldier,  
From the neck bone of a merchant,  
From the tail bone of a sailor,  
That is where this ring comes from.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What use is your empty boasting  
Wasting your own time  
There are few of you, many of us  
We are still more  
Outside we have a group of boys  
At the gate a crowd of men  
If you want to fight  
Or pull a billy club  
Or wrestle bear-style  
Then come inside, rope-pullers  
And all you potato hurlers  
With big thick noses  
And sparse teeth, thick upper lips  
With noses like worms that pull  
And gather birch bark.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
A young maiden  
Why do you resist coming to us  
Coming to us, arriving to us  
We will not put you to hard work  
The wolf tears at our clothes  
The bear scratches the stove  
The fox beats our threshing floor  
We have gutters under our thighs  
Beer gutters in the chamber.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
A young maiden  
Why do you resist coming to us  
Coming to us, arriving to us  
We will not put you to hard work  
The wolf tears at our clothes  
The bear scratches the stove  
The fox beats our threshing floor  
We have gutters under our thighs  
Beer gutters in the chamber.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I plowed the valleys, the oats grew,
I plowed the hills, the pines grew,
I plowed the swamps, the bluebells grew.
Then I sent the girls to pick
And to weave red aprons.-
I watched from the shade myself:
Is he plowing well.-
But my bride is tiny,
Tiny and upright,
Crossing thin twigs,
She never finishes the southern bread,
No difference in the little counts lost
Among the stone oaks.-
The horse I bought from there,
The girl I took from there.
The horse from Obliqa field
The girl from Strawberry hill,
I took the girl, I buried the other.
I paid honest grave money,
Beautiful soul money,
Handsome clock money,
Pleasant man money.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
There were many of us before,  
A beautiful herd grew up,  
Wandering like a flock of geese,  
Stretching like a herd of cattle,  
Smooth like that flight of wild geese.  
Now the flock has come to an end,  
The herd has disappeared,  
The flocks of wild geese are scattered widely,  
The flock of twite.  
Now the wanderers have become a crowd,  
And became a row of travelers,  
The troop has become an army from the tower,  
Separating the flock of wild geese,  
Scattering the flock of twite.  
Now I am left alone to hoe,  
Left to gather chicken chicks,  
To sow flaxseed.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, you little deceiver  
Clever little companion!  
You deceived the father-in-law, you deceived the mother-in-law,  
You deceived the family's fine young lady,  
You said you were coming to visit to eat,  
To visit to eat, to visit to drink,  
To visit to eat in the kitchen,  
To eat downstairs in the cellar,  
To eat fish in the chamber.  
Even more so in the living room.  
I got to live, I got to be,  
There was plenty of crying in the kitchen,  
Crying all the way to the cellar,  
Twice as much in the chamber,  
Even more in the living room.  
Oh, you little deceiver  
Clever little companion!

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Voiu and Mari the short one  
Loans but little Liisukene  
When the orders were delivered  
Five vodkas, six mugs  
What do you fear, what do you grieve  
When coming to us, when arriving to us  
To me, a young man  
To the top of the hat  
To the leather belt, to the white one  
That we have far Karelia stores  
Water five versts away  
Village boys carry water  
Runes push carts  
I make gutters under the horse’s belly  
And make new granaries in the yard  
I make walls from pork fat  
Floors from pork lard  
Roof from cured meat  
Rafters from dried meat.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ghostly mist, little breath  
Take me a wife soon,  
Whisper to me a golden moon!  
If you don't take, don't ask.  
I go heating the village,  
To the cold village granary,  
To the unheated building.  
There lie little houses on the road,  
Malformed ones on the yard,  
Children on the granary floor.  
In the rustling swamps,  
In the gray woods,  
In the broken gate,  
Upon father and mother's souls,  
Upon the black cat's neck.  
Daughters are like little houses,  
Sons are like pot makers.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Ellakene, little brother  
If you go to ask for a bride  
To court the home hen;  
Invite me also as a suitor  
I know the maidens.  
From the tuft know the dull maiden  
From the lock know the lazy maiden.  
The dull tuft was unrecognizable  
The lazy lock was not combed.  
Angry eyes watery  
Angry black eyebrows.  
Fierce cheeks red.  
Quick hands were scabbed.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
I saw the maiden growing;
By the blue fence
Swelling with a red wreath;
Rising in a tin crown.
I, from the stables;
From the coal-black haven,
From the window I gave barley,
In the shadows I carried water;
Without the old woman's knowledge,
Without the old man's knowledge,
That I gained the horse's strength.
In front I put sturdy irons;
Behind irons from the threshing floor.
Then I harnessed the horse.
After that I put on the bridle,
On top I put a playful bow,
Eyes into silk reins,
Eyebrows into golden rings,
Tail into a braid,
Brushed the mane to one side.
I rode to the father-in-law's door,
I rode to the mother-in-law's door:
"Hello, father-in-law! Boot-tops,
Hello, mother-in-law! Neck of the kettle,
Are your maidens at home?"
The mother-in-law understood, answered:
"Our daughters are at the well,
Drawing water at the well."
I ride to the well road:
"Hello, dear maiden!
Drawing water with the bucket,
To water all the village's herd;
To water my horse at the well.
Let my black one paw,
Let my gray one sway,
Let my dappled one tug,
Move on the linen cloth.
I, a poor wayfarer,
Wayfarer, knower of lands;
Lord of matters,
Messenger of kings,
Keeper of the town council's book,
A postman from St. Petersburg."
The maiden hears, answers again:
"You are not a poor wayfarer,
Wayfarer, knower of lands,
Lord of matters,
Messenger of kings,
Keeper of the town council's book,
Postman from St. Petersburg.
You are an evil suitor,
And a deceitful groom:
Why is your horse harnessed?
Brushed mane and swayed tail?
Why do you have locks on the bridle?
Why do you have an eagle's skull?
A headgear in the harness?
A sparrow in the reins?"
I heard, answered again,
"I have at home an old father
Who makes the locks on the bridle.
I have at home a little brother
Who is my horse's groom,
Brushed mane and swayed tail.
He puts the tail in a braid,
Eyes in silk reins,
Ears in golden rings.
I have at home a little sister
Who makes the letters on gloves
Who makes lace on the whip..."

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Marikene little berry  
Mari berry little maiden  
Mari brought berries from other lands  
Purple ones she brought from the city street  
Mari brought Matt's strawberries  
Half a cup of lingonberries  
Then she went up to Harju's granary  
To the Viru distillery  
The boys down to the prayer room  
Down fell a little doll  
Fell on my hat.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I go crying towards the corner  
I head towards the empty bed  
I turn over and find cold  
I turn my back and find the wall  
I fumble with an old fur coat  
A furry little coat  
It was good to go to the slave's hall  
To go among others  
Sometimes among the hunt people.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I made a wedding feast in the swamp  
Games on the hillock  
Who was invited to the wedding?  
The bear was invited to the wedding  
The wolf came with a gray fur coat  
The titmouse with a tin whistle  
The crow came with copper boots  
Who was invited as the cook?  
The cuckoo was invited as the cook  
The cuckoo boiled boiling broth  
Added angelica for flavor  
Added bile for herbs  
The meadow went to the feast  
The roosters went crowing.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Indeed, the stable boys wanted me,  
The lords of Viru enticed me,  
The riders of Riga quarrelled,  
The Hans of Harju pursued me long ago.  
I did not want to be a stable boy,  
Nor be enticed by the lords of Viru.  
My heart did not call to the coachman,  
My mind did not long for the manor’s mood.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
One is from one family  
Another is from another family  
One does not want to name the other  
One does not call the other by name  
Calls tenderly, calls darling  
Calls tenderly her beloved  
Calls her golden curls  
Names her wheat-colored  
Complains about hairy seeds  
I already understand, I respond  
Calls tenderly, calls darling  
That we are not sisters  
That we are not sisters-in-law  
Let our hands be sisters-in-law  
That we are not brothers  
Let our toes be brothers  
That we are not siblings  
Let our fingers be siblings.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
What do I care about the flower,
Or what do I fear the scream.
Mine is not here,
My companion is not useful here.
Mine is in Holland,
My companion is in the fish nets,
My fiancé is from the shores of Peipus.
Now let the mouth run its course.
I cannot bear to shout the tongue.
Maybe if I say it roughly again
Let me sing the words back.
I favor cunning words.
I knock down low feet,
I have angry brothers at home,
Wise brothers in the back room
Words are taken seriously.
With a scratch and a flutter,
They are put on paper.
Tomorrow they will be presented
When I come into the room.
Still, onto the dining table,
Onto unwashed hands.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sit on the edge of the fence.  
Nicely onto the side of the boat,  
Give the order to the sailor:  
Row the feathers, fly to the leaves,  
Sail, boy, to that land  
Where my dear father-in-law is,  
Dear father-in-law, dear mother-in-law,  
Silver maiden of youth.  
My father-in-law used to drink a flask of vodka,  
My mother-in-law used to drink another flask,  
I with the maiden, the fourth.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If this maiden dies  
Who will accompany the maiden?  
Germans accompany the maiden  
Ladies build  
Kings are listening  
What grows on the maiden’s grave?  
Buttercups, golden tops –  
If this boy dies  
Who will accompany the boy?  
A pig accompanies the boy  
An old boar is listening  
What grows on the boy’s grave?  
Dog fennel, burdock leaves  
Old sparse meadowsweet  
Old twisted nettles

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Why should I care about the shouts  
Why should I fear the cries  
For I have no one here of my own  
My own is Harju County  
The groom’s five versts away  
From there I searched, from there I found  
When I went to stroll there  
Into the grassy forest  
There was joy for both of us  
When our hearts felt joy  
The bride wondered if she should leave him behind  
The groom looked with a sorrowful eye  
They gave each other their hands  
With a kiss.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Aiti raiti with wheels  
Before I play with tufts of grass  
I strike with bursts of laughter  
I jump with birch stumps  
But when it comes to those girls.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
A little virgin, a young girl  
A slender figure in writing  
Why didn’t you come to me last year  
When the orders were being carried out?  
The fifth were the vodka carriers  
The sixth were the betrothed  
The hundredth were the bread makers? -  
Still, I will not come to you  
Maybe you would have a precious face  
Bluer eye sockets  
Golden eyebrow hairs  
You surely walked in a silk dress  
White stockings, black shoes  
When you walked along the road  
Then the crowd stared at you  
You were beautiful like a painting there on the highway  
You kept going and said goodbye to everyone  
A large spruce gate came opposite  
There he sat under the spruce  
There tears began to drop from his eyes  
The young man went to him  
Why do you sit here with your pale face?  
I have lost my way on the roads.....

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Old boy, why are you still watching  
If you don’t take a young woman?  
Your mouth has been burned,  
Your beard has turned gray.  
Your mouth runs smoke water,  
Your beard runs gray healing water.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sister Ella Annekene,  
Your shoes hurt,  
Your laces begged,  
The bullhides groaned,  
The goat hides led;  
Women waited to die,  
Men to remain widowers,  
Children to remain orphans;  
So you could become a mother to the children,  
A leader over the family,  
Hands to carry the ladle,  
Mouth to taste the soup.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
If only you knew my other half,  
My poor husband would think,  
If only my deed existed,  
It would bring fire to me,  
It would carry my old furs,  
It would take me from the rain streams,  
It would redeem me from the snowdrifts,  
Behind the Harju plows,  
From the strike of the Viru scythe.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let other girls not do
What I foolishly did
I went to a young man
Without having a dowry.
I thought he was a considerate man,
A loving husband.
The man was like a sea devil,
A servant of coastal demons,
He ate the flesh off my back,
Sucked the blood from my chest.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Old boy, old devil  
Sits on the leelõuk  
Pipe stick in his mouth  
Asks forgiveness for sin  
How many young ones have been made to cry  
How many old ones silenced.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Before, I throw a stone by the edge  
By the edge of a stone, by the edge of a stump,  
Before going to the crazy boy.  
The stone does not pull, the stump does not touch,  
The boy does not leave untouched  
The skin hair untested.  
A good boy sleeps with good ones  
But puts his clothes on his thigh  
His shirt on his shin.  
A bad boy sleeps with the bad ones  
But pulls his clothes off his thigh  
His shirt off his shin.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where do I know the dull girl from?  
From Tukas, I know the dull girl,  
From Laka, I know the lazy girl,  
The lazy girl from Laka is tall,  
The dull one has tangled hair,  
And a low curvy waist.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
May was a sweet maiden  
She called the boys to the spruce forest  
She made peace with them in the alder grove  
She annoyed them in the meadow  
She had seven sprats in her purse  
Eight herring in her scarf.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where do the three of us go  
Out beyond, the five of us  
We go to Looja's story  
To Maarja's meadow  
I take a bow, I take two.  
I will take the third  
What did I find under the bow?  
I found an eagle under the bow.  
What is under the eagle's wing?  
An axe is under the eagle's wing.  
What is under the handle and nose?  
A shaving under the handle and nose.  
What will be made from the shaving?  
John's barn will be cut.  
What else goes inside the barn?  
A bed inside the barn.  
Straw goes into the bed.  
What goes over the straw?  
A plank over the straw.  
What goes on the plank?  
Pillows on the plank.  
What goes on the pillows?  
Four young maidens.  
One returns with a golden belt  
Another cracks an iron belt.  
The third has a cat's belt  
The fourth cries for her young husband.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where do the three of us go  
Out beyond, the five of us  
We go to Looja's story  
To Maarja's meadow  
I take a bow, I take two.  
I will take the third  
What did I find under the bow?  
I found an eagle under the bow.  
What is under the eagle's wing?  
An axe is under the eagle's wing.  
What is under the handle and nose?  
A shaving under the handle and nose.  
What will be made from the shaving?  
John's barn will be cut.  
What else goes inside the barn?  
A bed inside the barn.  
Straw goes into the bed.  
What goes over the straw?  
A plank over the straw.  
What goes on the plank?  
Pillows on the plank.  
What goes on the pillows?  
Four young maidens.  
One returns with a golden belt  
Another cracks an iron belt.  
The third has a cat's belt  
The fourth cries for her young husband.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh you boys, budding eyes  
Budding eyes, ring-necked  
Don’t make the girls laugh  
Don’t insult the girls with dimples  
Don’t slander the money-chests  
You will surely suffer from a lack of girls  
A lack of girls, a shortage of brides  
You will surely go from village to village  
Also from farm to farm  
Squatting in the village street  
Sitting bare-bottomed at the family gate  
I have a grandmother, a little old lady  
I have a grandfather, a little old man  
Give me the daughter  
Who swings in your arms  
Sways with a gentle pull  
With ribbons under the skirt  
While the girls watch secretly  
If only that fiancé were mine

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
What is there beneath our yard  
The sea beneath our yard  
What is in the middle of the sea  
A pillar in the middle of the sea  
What is at the end of the pillar  
A sieve at the end of the pillar  
What is inside the sieve  
A ring inside the sieve  
What is inside the ring  
A cuckoo inside the ring  
What does the cuckoo bring us  
It brings news of war  
Whose turn is it to go to war  
Will father go, or will mother go  
Or will the one who is swinging hidden go  
Sister went to teach brother  
Ride to the middle of the war  
The first are thrown out  
The last are killed  
The middle ones come home

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I was a young man before  
The wooden pin was in my pants  
I saw berries growing  
Blue legs straightening  
I start tasting the pokes  
I rode onto the Finnish bridge  
To the Finnish bridge, the snaps  
The base beams to the crackles  
Where has it come to here  
It has come from Finland  
Or from the Russian road slope

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I was a young man before  
The wooden pin was in my pants  
I saw berries growing  
Blue legs straightening  
I start tasting the pokes  
I rode onto the Finnish bridge  
To the Finnish bridge, the snaps  
The base beams to the crackles  
Where has it come to here  
It has come from Finland  
Or from the Russian road slope

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
One of me was shouting  
Two of me were afraid while screaming  
A little when speaking out  
My own is not here  
My companion does not grow here  
My own is in Holland  
My companion far away  
A good man over the seas  
A good companion over the fish  
Sings by the lake shore  
Where he jumps by the shore  
There he leaps by the building  
Where he dances by the shore  
There he dances in the stable  
Where he spins by the shore  
There the church spins  
I go through the masculine forest  
Through the boyish street  
Do not stick your head out anywhere  
Do not put your hand in a glove  
Do not press your hair down into a shadow  
I am going home to the old lady’s yard -  
If the good lady does not stop me  
Then the wise old man will stop me  
If the wise old man does not stop me  
Then my sister will stop me  
If my little sister does not stop me  
Then the young brother will stop me  
If the young brother does not stop me  
Then the secret man will stop me  
If the secret man does not stop me  
Then the dear companion will stop me  
If the dear companion does not stop me  
Let the hair be washed to the cup  
Let the night be slept until dawn

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Hello there, little brother  
There were quite a few of us before  
There were quite a few who went with the herd  
It was like a group from Hoosta  
Moved like a village gang of rascals  
Now the group has come to an end  
The flock has been driven to a place  
Where did they go – where did they stay  
Did rainstorms carry them away  
Or did windstorms bring them  
Or was there hail here  
Rainstorms did not carry them away  
Nor did windstorms bring them  
Nor was there hail here  
From here, boys of Viru have taken them  
Boys of Harju have loved them  
Viru "kimmelid" have led them  
Swedish stallions have trotted around  
Horse backs were struck with fire  
Sparks from their rascals’ shins  
The foal’s hooves cast lightning  
Even when walking on maiden paths  
Or going on a bride-seeking errand

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Young men are gentle brothers  
Let's harness the steeds to the yoke  
Gray in silver beads  
Blue eyes in rings  
Then we go through the villages  
Through the trodden street  
The golden nails click in the harness  
Behind it rattles the iron plow  
A maiden watches from the side  
The chamberlain calls along  
If only this man were mine  
Who goes through the villages  
Through the trodden street  
The horse shines with beads  
The stone-paved street thunders  
The golden nails click in the harness  
Behind it rattles the iron plow  
I would wash his head  
And comb his neck  
I would rinse the stockings and gloves  
I would drip them through the waters  
Öivo, Võivo, victorious shoes  
Mother-in-law’s shoes without grease  
Edges sharp without tearing  
Soles without fat

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Sister dear, did I say  
Many young ones, did I scold  
Dear wife, did I say  
Friend dear, did I ride  

Oh, go to the young man  
Unmature wife  
The wife does not grow (mature)  
The man is unwilling to marry  
The wife, however, inherits the linen shed  
The man, however, prefers clothes  

Every noon a meal  
Every month wants a new cap  
Every day wants a white shirt.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
I even told the old woman,  
And informed the old man as well.  
The old woman, find me a wife,  
Old man, listen along with me.  
If the woman does not want to come,  
If the old man does not want to listen to marry,  
I will drift into the village,  
Into the cold village warehouse,  
Into an unheated building,  
Into an unmade chamber.  
Last year a wife was taken here,  
This year another was brought,  
Now the bed remains for weeping,  
Driving me to look for a doctor,  
To listen to the doctor’s advice.  
The old women came to meet me,  
“Where are you going, young man?”  
The man understood again and replied:  
“Last year a wife was taken here,  
This year another was brought,  
Now the bed remains for weeping,  
The bed is crying out,  
Driving me to look for a doctor,  
To listen to the doctor’s advice.”  
The old women both answered:  
“Oh, you silly young man,  
Go home and look for work,  
You should sow rye in the field,  
Barley straight into the field,  
Oats in pairs after that,  
Make the woman oil-free,  
Then the woman will become healthy.”

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
I even told the old woman,  
And informed the old man as well.  
The old woman, find me a wife,  
Old man, listen along with me.  
If the woman does not want to come,  
If the old man does not want to listen to marry,  
I will drift into the village,  
Into the cold village warehouse,  
Into an unheated building,  
Into an unmade chamber.  
Last year a wife was taken here,  
This year another was brought,  
Now the bed remains for weeping,  
Driving me to look for a doctor,  
To listen to the doctor’s advice.  
The old women came to meet me,

The old women both answered:

You should sow rye in the field,

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let's go to the Creator for a story  
Out into the yard, to the meadow  
I take one arch, leave the other  
I begin to gather the third  
What do I find under the arch?  
I find an eagle under the arch  
What is under the eagle's wing?  
An axe under the eagle's wing  
What is on the axe's blade?  
A shaving on the axe's blade  
What is carved from that shaving?  
A stove shed is carved  
What is put in that shed?  
A bed is put in that shed  
What is inside the bed?  
Straw is inside the bed  
What is laid on the straw?  
A mattress is laid on the straw  
What is laid on the mattress?  
A pillow is laid on the mattress  
What is laid on the pillow?  
Two young girls are laid in pairs  
One weaves a meadow ribbon  
The other ties a silk ribbon  
The third weaves a golden belt  
The fourth weaves a wavy braid  
The fifth sets a narrow braid  
The sixth a cat’s paw braid

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let's go take Maie as a wife  
The second takes another wife  
The second takes another old woman's daughter  
You get Kaie, I get Maie  
Kai was stout for work  
Mai was chubby for the leg

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, our one little sister  
Oh, our two little hands  
The sparkles of one room  
The little birds of one bread  
Together we eat, together we drink  
Together we sleep through the nights  
Together the head on the pillow  
Together the feet under the carpet

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My brothers are like these spires  
Cousins on my uncle’s side are like these parts  
Cousins on my aunt’s side are grown men  
They did not take debt, nor steal  
Did no harm to anyone  
Not more than anyone  
The German did not send me to the same place  
The parish did not send me to court  
Other brothers, unlike these frogs  
Took on debt and stole  
The German already sent them to the same place  
The parish already sent them to court

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What is here beneath our yard  
The sea beneath our yard  
What is in the middle of the sea  
A well in the middle of the sea  
What is on the edge of the well  
A post on the edge of the well  
What is at the very end of the post  
A sieve at the very end of the post  
What is on the edge of the sieve  
A cuckoo on the edge of the sieve  
What is on the cuckoo's hands  
A child was on the cuckoo's hands  
What is on the child's chest  
A brooch on the child's chest  
What is on the edge of the brooch  
A ring on the edge of the brooch  
Even this is a soldier's ring  
How did it come to this land  
From the knees of a chimney sweep boy  
From the shin bones of a German soldier

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
In the corner, in the corner, young men  
In the dark tobacco pouches  
Lake maidens are coming  
Their mouths sealed shut  
Their heads crowned with feathers  
Their necks curled with lice  
Snow-white gloves on  
Blue-gray mustache  
Dark black six-button jacket  
Fish-scaled stockings on their legs  
Cherry leather shoes  
Cork soles made from spiny oak  
Silver glass patches underneath  
When he turns, he glances  
When he steps, he shines.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, but the harvest festival in Tammiku,  
There was bad girl merchandise there,  
And there was a broad girl without permission.  
They all came together there  
Probably from Harjumaa,  
Undivided from Järvamaa.  
They stand there on Siim's hill,  
The sails were against Siim's garden,  
Their bellies faced the sexton's yard.  
Then they were counted off—  
Some went to Siim,  
The bowlegged one went to the sexton;  
Some mad one went to the new tavern.  
When the night had now passed,  
A letter came from Kehras,  
The third book from Raasik:  
Girls needed for work,  
Some old ones to go as spirits,  
Proper cattle herds,  
Whores to milk cows,  
Harlots to pick apples.  
What were they given to eat there?  
Kaalileent and cabbage stew!  
The whores ate apple soup,  
The women ate sweet soup.  
When the night had now passed,  
Then when they took them to town,  
To be pricked by the town doctor,  
To be rubbed with kolostrohv,  
To have syrup put in the wound.  
The syrup dripped under the bridge,  
The kolostrohv in the home room,  
The syrup dripped from wet eyes,  
Kolostrohv on wet eyebrows,  
At Rotermann the jaws wide open.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I fed the stallion, I gave the stallion to drink...  
I went to the German's matchmaker,  
I sent the Germans to Saaremaa,  
The lords to Hiiumaa,  
Half the gentlemen to Poland.  
I started coming home,  
I found a tavern down a lane.  
I went to take a drink,  
I took one drink, I took two,  
I went to take a third drink,  
A thief came from the grove,  
Another from outside the yard,  
They took my good horses,  
A ten-ruble hat,  
A hundred-ruble saddle.  
I came home crying,  
Weeping outside in the yard;  
The woman was there, the man was there,  
Both the old ones answered.  
"Why do you cry, young son?  
We have three stallions at home,  
One is a gold-maned stallion,  
The second is silver-maned,  
The third bronze maned."  
"Well, we will give them for you,  
Redeem you from this trouble."  
...  
I fed the stallion, I gave the stallion to drink,  
The stallion gained strength indeed.  
I went to Koigi to court,  
To the smith Koigi’s daughters  
Were quite famous maidens.  
One was Anne, another Kaie,  
The third a sweet Maiekene.  
Anne was given to me,  
Kaie was bargained for me.  
Ann was naked by the stove,  
Kai was bare bottomed by the rack,  
Mai was on the threshing floor,  
A sack underneath her, and a sack on top,  
A piece of sack by her side,  
No way to bring her home.  
I came home crying,  
The woman was there, the man was there,  
Both the old ones answered.  
"Why do you cry, young son?  
- Why not cry, old woman!  
Why not cry, old man!  
I fed the stallion, I gave it to drink,  
The stallion gained strength and stamina.  
I went to Koigi to court,  
To the smith Koigi’s daughters  
Cannot bring them home,  
I left them out as wild ones,  
The garden barn as helpers!"  
...  
The woman was there, the man was there,  
Both the old ones answered.  
"Why do you cry, young son?  
Bring home the announced wife!  
Set her on the table!  
If not, she will take the lower place,  
She will manage the household affairs,  
She will rinse the spoon handle."

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Now our men were in the forest,
Boys in the thicket;
When it was time to take the draft
To find the hussars:
The picking of young men
Then I was the first!
Number one man.
I went to the town, took the draft
Went to Paide, cried a lot
Other fathers were crying in front
Other mothers were crying behind
Father was crying, mother was crying;
I was being mourned by Harju Annas:
Harju Annas, Järva Kaies,
Virumaa Maie the lowly;
Lääne's little Liisuke
Finally, the dear ones, finally the brothers,
Finally the drum beating
The whistling of pipe!
Chopping with an iron sword
The cracking of a long gun
The booming of a cannon
The popping of a firebomb
Then the young men came home;
Then the young girls came to meet them,
Ann no longer recognizes Antsuke;
Kai no longer recognizes Kaarlike
Mari goes to bed with Mart
Kai goes to Kaarli's army camp
Ann goes under Antsu's wing.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Little eides, little old men  
Village old women,  
Family girls, little boys  
Neighbor's young wives:  
Girls, acquaintances,  
I saw in the corner crying,  
Crying, sobbing,  
Tearing joy from the heart.  
Do not shame me,  
Girls, by your tears.  
Do not humiliate me shyly  
Among men, condemn me.  
A boy is not made to stagger  
Or stumble in the yard,  
To love stove-pokes,  
To sulk in the corner of the room.  
A man is made for work  
To overcome the enemy and win.  
I am going west to take a chance in the city,  
To take a chance, to rush to war,  
There I will become like a German,  
I will train myself like a gentleman.  
I sing songs, is there harm in that,  
By the black hot six stove?  
From a soldier I become a warrior,  
An iron helmet on my head.  
If then I leave you with God,  
I do so with a joyful heart.  
Won’t you girls then cheer  
And sing me war songs?  
Is it not right, sisters,  
My brave little brothers?  
Raise your bright voices  
High, my precious voices,  
Sing so that the ceiling creaks,  
The ceiling creaks, the floor cracks.  
If God grants strength,  
Surely my breast will bear the steam.  
I take a rifle with a brave grasp,  
Put a sword at my hip,  
Proud straps across my chest,  
White gloves on my hands.  
Won’t you girls then cheer  
And sing me war songs?  
What I have won from Russia  
I found through battles.  
Pride alone does not shout then,  
Honour of a man does not seek war:  
But so that I could kill the enemy,  
I tear myself from my mother’s embrace,  
From my father’s grace, from my bride’s bosom,  
From the wings of my parents' courtyard.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
My dear little grandmother.  
You nursed, nurtured,  
Raised, embraced.  
You held me in your lap, kissed me,  
Put me down, played with me.  
You nursed, stroked my head,  
Thought to be a help,  
To come as a support to me.  
Now my family wants me as a soldier,  
Brothers to be Russian men,  
To be a piece of tin in the rye,  
To carry a long rifle.  
Tin is thick, iron is heavy,  
Copper is noisy to polish,  
This falls on the hands,  
Burdens the poor soul.  
Put the pack on to march,  
Put the pack on the right leg,  
Put the pack on the left leg,  
Now I am taken to the Russians,  
To be taken to the Black Sea.  
Learn to handle the rifle,  
Learn the rifle, learn the sword.  
The rifle is a long stick,  
The sword is a wide plank.  
I will start firing the rifle,  
Swinging the wide sword.  
Making big bangs.  
It echoed at home, in grandmother’s hands,  
It echoed at home, in grandfather’s hands,  
It rolled to my little brother’s hands.  
Grandmother started to cry,  
Grandfather jumped up,  
Sisters wiped their eyes;  
Listen, where is our brother,  
Listen, where is the one thundering in war,  
Firing the gun in battle?  
Fallen under the Russian.  
May the wind bring him back whole,  
May the hail quickly pass the book,  
Thick clouds bring prayers,  
Smoke bring warm words.  
So many greetings to him,  
If he is safe somewhere,  
And if he has firewood somewhere.  
Lilacs straight,  
Maples white,  
Apple trees true,  
Birches curly.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Hussar without a mustache
Grenadier without a mustache.
Majors without a mustache.
Let summer come, then autumn
Will arrive towards Christmas,
After Mart's day on the second,
Then comes the time for men's games,
The time for boys' music,
The picking of young men.
Then I am a Hussar without a mustache,
A Major without painting,
A Grenadier untried.
Then one can sleep in the forest,
Lie under the frost-covered alder,
Bow to the frost-covered spruce.
I went home to the pen,
Home to the yard in the pen,
The captors came home.
They made us sit on the bench,
Made us step into chains,
Put one shack on the left foot,
Put one shack on the right foot.
Then taken to the Russian market,
To the barracks by the herd gate.
There my head was shaved,
Hair taken by the roots.
Uniform was crushed on me,
Greatcoat put on.
There my stripes were scraped off,
Epaulettes were taken off.
There the company drove us,
Made us stand in ranks.
The officers were on one side,
The sergeant stood behind.
The officer shouted, the slaps sounded,
My eyes filled with tears.
“Take the sticks, give them to those
Who do not understand how to march,
To lift their feet lightly.”
How can I understand marching,
To lift the foot lightly?
My stomach is empty, my leg is weak,
As soon as the moose gets tired,
Lead is heavy, iron is hard,
Copper is too hard to ring.
This applies to the hands,
Weighs upon the poor soul.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
One was like a little brother to me  
This too was sealed by wars  
The color guard in Russian service  
May the sail strings break  
May the ship masts fall apart  
When taking my brother  
He grew like in a fish trap  
Into a grayish blue  
He was searched for from there  
Always enlisted for war.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Let's go, let's go, let's walk, let's walk  
Let's go along the beaten paths  
Along the carved trails  
Where slaves had walked before  
Where poor children walked early  
Where the old wanderers roamed late,  
Later gone astray on holy days  
Well-behaved before dawn  
Let's go towards the city in the flag  
Crowded towards the Tondi tavern  
Heading towards the borough  
Let's go to take a lisu in the city  
Under the estate of Mardu manor  
Greetings to the city lisu, gentlemen  
Gentlemen of Mardu manor  
Here is the man who stands in front  
The man of the first number  
This man fits before the manor  
This man is good for the courthouse  
To sit with the gentlemen  
To drink punch with the ladies  
I left behind a dear herding-place  
Beloved left in the wailing-place  
The girls wiping their eyes  
Young maidens crying  
Because my herd is crying in the pasture  
Anne cries in front of the barn  
Leenu at the louse chamber  
Mari at the berry tree hill.

Young maidens crying

Anne cries in front of the barn  
Leenu at the louse chamber  
Mari at the berry tree hill.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Little old grandfathers  
Village old grannies  
Neighbor’s young ladies  
Family little boys  
Don’t shame me  
Maidens, through your crying  
Make me shy, humble me  
Drown me among men  
A man is made for work  
For victory against the enemy  
I’m going to the western town to draw lots  
To draw lots, to hurry to war  
I became a soldier, boots on  
Gray coat on the back  
I became a soldier, a warrior  
Proud straps across my chest  
Won’t the cousins protect me then  
Village brothers, family spirits  
Won’t you sisters cheer then  
Sing me a war song  
That was right, little sisters  
My brave brothers  
Raise your bright voices  
From your high, precious tongues  
Sing so the ceiling creaks  
The ceiling creaks, the floor thuds.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
My sisters, do not worry,
Surely you have a mourning birch!
And the garden grew green with sorrow
At the edge of the great swamp
Among wide waves
Break the branches, maiden
Break the branches, the plague's branches
Leave the tops for the birds to sing
For the birds to sing, for the winds to come
For the nightingale to tell a story
For the lark to trill
For the titmouse to descend
For the nightingale to tell a story.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Sex, sex, dear brothers,  
Sex wished me to be a soldier,  
Brothers wished me to be a Russian man,  
Others, others wished me to be a madruke,  
I was not born to be a soldier  
Nor fit to be a madruke:  
A soldier needed big boots for me  
A madruke, a wide coat for me.  
Farewell, dear brothers,  
Those men have already left us  
The rifle bearers are gone.  
The crooked flail had become dented,  
Rusty from Swedish summer weather,  
The canons had disappeared:  
Pieces at the border of Turkey.  
Oh you beautiful Estonia,  
Precious sound of the kannel,  
Bright whistle of the flute!  
Oh my good little flame,  
The five-note tune,  
The darkening trachea!  
It certainly cried long ago!  
When this Swedish reed pipe  
The appropriate trumpet of Paide  
The dark pipe of Tallinn,  
Bright whistle of the flute.  
It did not win the instrument voices  
It hid the German horn tones.  
If only I still had my voice  
Old mouth made of brass,  
Throat like golden honey!  
I could with the voice of an ox  
The song range of a stallion,  
The throat of a golden horse,  
A word as a traveling vehicle.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
When I begin to leave,  
Then sorrow will begin to come  
And water will roll from the eyes,  
From the eyes it flows down the chest,  
From the chest to the toes,  
From the toes to the village cemetery.  
There the village gave the herder to drink,  
The parish to enlighten the poor.  
Old women, old men,  
family elders, little boys,  
Girls, acquaintances,  
I see them crying in the corner  
And mourning  
And tearing joy from my chest.  
Do not cry, young girls,  
Do not grieve too much.  
When I then come out of there,  
When I defeat the enemy,  
Then a star will shine on my chest.  
Won’t those girls cheer for me,  
Won’t they sing me a war song.  
They will sing so that the ceiling creaks,  
The ceiling creaks, the floor cracks.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist

family elders, little boys,

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Oh, I, the poor despised child!
My father despised me, my mother despised me,
The manor elders despised me.
All the serfs, the overseers, the barn keepers
They were angry at me.
I was assigned to take the draft.
That draft fell upon me.
I was taken to the town barracks.
There my head was shaved,
The hair cut to the roots,
A gray coat was given,
A bayonet and rifle were placed in my hand.
Yet I became an officer
And elder of that parish.
All the serfs, overseers, barn keepers,
They are in hell before all others,
In the fire and sulfur smoke.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Our road is unknown,
the bridges unmeasured,
the milestones unplaced,
Our road goes toward the sea,
to the great harbor,
where crown ships meet,
crown ships, merchant ships,
and also large warships,
and also long sailing ships.
We put up the masts,
just like tree trunks in the forest,
we put up the sails
like women’s aprons;
we pull them with ropes
like kantele strings...

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist  ; Töölaulud
translation:

the bridges unmeasured,

Our road goes toward the sea,
to the great harbor,
where crown ships meet,
crown ships, merchant ships,
and also large warships,

We put up the masts,

we put up the sails
like women’s aprons;

like kantele strings...

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
The whole world was sighing  
The whole people were resting  
We poor ones were walking  
Our roads were unknown  
Our lands were unmeasured  
Ditches not yet dug  
Bridge pillars unbuilt  
The boy’s path leads to the girl  
Our path leads to the sea  
There to the big harbor  
And onto that broken ship  
Mending the ship, patching the ship  
But making the ship whole  
I put sails on the ship  
Like a bride puts her apron on  
When I start to leave  
The storm begins to come  
Tears begin to roll  
While the waves break on the shore  
Eyebrows like drops of gold  
Then the young girls began to cry  
Regretting their broad shirts  
Mourning their linen shirts  
And the young girls cried for Sõnn  
Sõnn when the ship’s sails appeared  
At last the ship’s flag flickers  
The ship’s canvas shines.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Old women, loud women  
Black eyebrows, angry women  
They forced me to become a soldier  
Made me carry a rifle  
They walked on Ella's soul  
Carried on the veins of the neck.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
I am going to town to enlist,  
To enlist, to hurry to war;  
Then I will set myself in the German way,  
Train myself in the gentleman’s way;  
I will cast lots, what harm in that? –  
To the black six-hot ovens.  
From a soldier, I will get boots on my feet,  
From a soldier, I will become a warrior.  
I will put on an iron helmet.  
I went to town to the castle.  
There, my head was shaved,  
Hair taken out from the roots.  
Then I began to be tearful,  
Tearful, complaining:  
Oh my Juhan, where is my hair,  
Dear Juhan, where are my locks!  
I see in the corner tearful  
Old women, old men,  
Village elderly women,  
Tearful, complaining,  
Plucking joy from the chest,  
That I am about to exchange all  
For war weapons.  
I begin to scold them:  
A man is not made to be a bungler,  
Or a stumbler in the home yard,  
A lover of the stove stick;  
A man is made for others,  
To go out against the enemy.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
I go to town to enlist,  
To enlist, to rush to war.  
Then I’ll dress myself in the German way,  
Groom myself like a gentleman.  
I’ll throw my songs away, what’s the loss,  
From a soldier I’ll get boots on my feet,  
An honorable gray uniform on my back,  
From a soldier I became a warrior,  
An iron helmet on my head.  
I went to town to the castle.  
There my head was shaved,  
Hair taken from the roots,  
Then I began to cry:  
"Oh my Juhan, where are my hairs,  
Dear Juhan, where are my locks,  
Where is my beautiful white head,  
Old grandmothers, old grandfathers  
I see in the corner crying,  
Crying, sniffing,  
Taking joy from the heart.  
Because I’m about to wholeheartedly  
Exchange for war gear.  
A man is not made for stumbling,  
For stumbling in the home yard,  
For being a pike lover of the brain.  
A man is made for work;  
To go out against the enemy."

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
I am going to town to take a bet  
To take a bet and rush to war  
I am not afraid of the town's bet  
The town's bet and the manor's staff  
I came away from the lords  
And then from the doctor's assistance  
Under the town lord's bet  
The town lords began to quarrel  
The Saxons of the county to argue  
The Toompea doctors to grumble  
The betting table kept moving  
The manor's club kept swaying  
Surely that man could stay with us  
The whitehead among us to support  
The flax house could not be shaky  
Then the poor man got to thresh  
The long pants man to weave  
The broad boot man to hurry  
Then the captors come  
Ten poods of rope in hand  
Ten knots in the rope

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Mother dear, dear one  
What have you received from me.  
Tears of sorrow  
Ike stopped nursing  
From wrapping a wet eye.  
Take me to the Viru river  
Carry me to the Harju spring  
There the water was rolling  
Lost on the Harju shore  
To my dear mother  
How I brought blood to the water  
Let the flesh go to the waves  
I swaddled you in swaddling clothes  
On linen and canvas  
On white wool  
On soft little scarves  
I held you in my arms close  
I played in the grove  
I weighed the herd for sending  
Now take me to Russia  
I am being sent to Germany  
To clean Russian guns  
Russian guns, long guns  
Swedish iron, heavy iron  
They press against me.  
Hollow at the neck of the shoulder  
Pressing against the forearms  
If only my shoulder were rowanwood  
The neck of the shoulder applewood  
The forearm maplewood  
Let it be, let it be, dear brothers  
Let it be under the city of Narva  
There we will do many feats.  
We will make the market bustle  
The street will fog with harbor mist  
The eaves will shake with hail  
The store porch will burn.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Mother dear, dear one  
What have you received from me.  
Tears of sorrow  
Ike stopped nursing  
From wrapping a wet eye.  
Take me to the Viru river  
Carry me to the Harju spring  
There the water was rolling  
Lost on the Harju shore  
To my dear mother  
How I brought blood to the water  
Let the flesh go to the waves  
I swaddled you in swaddling clothes  
On linen and canvas  
On white wool  
On soft little scarves  
I held you in my arms close  
I played in the grove  
I weighed the herd for sending
