Now take me to Russia  
I am being sent to Germany  
To clean Russian guns  
Russian guns, long guns  
Swedish iron, heavy iron  
They press against me.  
Hollow at the neck of the shoulder  
Pressing against the forearms  
If only my shoulder were rowanwood  
The neck of the shoulder applewood  
The forearm maplewood  
Let it be, let it be, dear brothers  
Let it be under the city of Narva  
There we will do many feats.  
We will make the market bustle  
The street will fog with harbor mist  
The eaves will shake with hail  
The store porch will burn.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Be healthy, grandmothers, grandfathers,  
be healthy, my workers,  
so that no rib is broken,  
no kneecap injured,  
no skin bone, no angel bone,  
no mouth bone, no heart bone.  
I was born a soldier,  
but ended up a sailor.  
As a soldier I got big boots,  
a noble coat with six buttons,  
white gloves with a crown.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Mother, dear mother,  
what will you get from me?  
Tears, sorrow of the soul, —  
you never nursed me,  
never wrapped me in wet cloths,  
never carried me with cold hands.  
I will carry you to the Viru river,  
I will carry you to the Arju spring,  
I will let the child  
roll the water from there,  
the Arju shore losing  
against mother’s will:  
How do I carry blood to the water,  
let flesh float on the waves?  
I wrapped you in a diaper,  
linen cloth on flax,  
woolen blanket on wool;  
soft packcloth,  
I soothed you in my lap,  
cut the rags for wrapping you,  
led the herd afar;  
how many sweet breads I missed eating,  
how many sips I missed drinking,  
how many nights I lay awake.  
I thought of coming to help,  
I thought of stepping into aid,  
now they came,  
stepped into the world to help;  
now you will be taken to Russia,  
sent to Germany,  
to wipe the Russian rifle,  
to handle Swedish iron.  
Russian rifle, long rifle,  
Swedish iron, heavy iron,  
so your  
shoulder blade shakes,  
your arm weights down.  
If only your chest were rowanwood,  
shoulder blade applewood,  
arms maple wood...  
Let it be, let it be, brothers of life,  
let it be under the town of Narva,  
there we will make many a trick:  
we will make the market bustle,  
the street fog settle,  
hail shake from the eaves,  
the storehouse burn to ashes.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Before me washed the grandmother’s hand,  
the grandmother’s hand, warm water,  
Now the crown’s hand washes me,  
the crown’s hand, seawater.  
Before I got a hundred in money,  
Now I don’t get bread money,  
Two or three kopeks.  
Others had long weddings,  
Wide rods were prepared,  
But I had long roads made,  
Wide highways prepared.  
My road went long to Petersburg,  
Wide highway onto the ship.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Yesterday, on Monday  
I went to take the draft  
The first number two  
And the second time number one  
Then I, poor ready soldier  
Poor ready Russian.  

Then I was taken to Laadi tavern  
In Laadi tavern I was fed  
And given beer to drink  
From there I was taken to Pärnu town  
Before the Pärnu commission  
There was again a tune in Russian  
And every Russian had five borders  
They bordered around my head  
And pulled my green locks.  

Now I, poor ready soldier  
Poor ready Russian.  
In Lihula I was given bread  
In Haapsalu my beard was shaved  
Now I, poor ready soldier  
Poor ready Russian.  

From there I was taken to Musku town  
In Musku a uniform was given  
And leather gloves were put on my hands  
There I became a rifle cleaner  
A wide buckle fitter  
A handler of a green sword.  

Now I, poor ready soldier  
Poor ready Russian. -  
Father could have fed me to a wolf  
Mother could have raised a bear  
Before they had fed me  
They had fed me, they had given me to drink  
Fed me to become a straight man  
Given me drink to become a brave man  
Before they could have buried me in a mound  
Before they wrapped me in a shroud  
Before they could have laid me under a stump  
Before they wrapped me in a bundle  
Now I must go  
And leave others behind  
How miserable, how troubling  
So miserable to step away  
Trouble again to be left behind  
Still I must go  
Let fire come from the sky  
Let rain pour down in sheets  
Still I must go  
Still I must be ahead.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Yesterday, on Monday  
I went to take the draft  
The first number two  
And the second time number one  
Then I, poor ready soldier  
Poor ready Russian.  

Then I was taken to Laadi tavern  
In Laadi tavern I was fed  
And given beer to drink  
From there I was taken to Pärnu town  
Before the Pärnu commission  
There was again a tune in Russian  
And every Russian had five borders  
They bordered around my head  
And pulled my green locks.  

Now I, poor ready soldier  
Poor ready Russian.  
In Lihula I was given bread  
In Haapsalu my beard was shaved  
Now I, poor ready soldier  
Poor ready Russian.  

From there I was taken to Musku town  
In Musku a uniform was given  
And leather gloves were put on my hands  
There I became a rifle cleaner  
A wide buckle fitter  
A handler of a green sword.  

Now I, poor ready soldier  
Poor ready Russian. -  
Father could have fed me to a wolf  
Mother could have raised a bear  
Before they had fed me  
They had fed me, they had given me to drink  
Fed me to become a straight man  
Given me drink to become a brave man  
Before they could have buried me in a mound  
Before they wrapped me in a shroud  
Before they could have laid me under a stump  
Before they wrapped me in a bundle  
Now I must go  
And leave others behind  
How miserable, how troubling  
So miserable to step away  
Trouble again to be left behind  
Still I must go  
Let fire come from the sky  
Let rain pour down in sheets  
Still I must go  
Still I must be ahead.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
I have no wife who bakes the bread,  
nor a child who pats my cheek,  
I have no one to give me a seat,  
no one to bring cabbage soup,  
nor one to cut the clean bread.  
Better I go down to the crown:  
there are those who give seats,  
and those who bring cabbage soup,  
and those who give clean soup.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
I am not afraid of the town's mischief,  
The town's mischief, the manor's nonsense,  
I came out from among the guards,  
From the center of Kinderall.  
But the guards keep swearing,  
The Kinderall people keep cursing,  
The doctors keep grumbling.  
The Tuompea bells struck the hour,  
In Lihula, they struck with crack and clash.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Ancient conscription  
No fortune for men.  
Alva—men’s sons were captured,  
Secretly wherever they were found.  
They were caught, taken to the town,  
Where they served for a long time.  
Some fathers never saw their sons.  
Now there is a better story about this,  
About the conscripted men’s kind:  
Be who you are—  
A ranger, a stableman, a barn helper,  
An old field guard,  
Taking the crown’s duty at old age,  
Respect and fear your elders.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
When I start to go,  
then sorrow begins to come,  
water runs down from the eyes;  
it flows from the eyes to the chest,  
from the chest down to the toes,  
from the toes to the entire village.  
From there the village got milk to drink,  
to enlighten the poor of the parish.  
Old women, old men,  
little ones, boys!  
Girls, daughters  
I see weeping in the corner.  
They cry and grieve,  
taking joy away from my chest.  
"Do not cry, young girls,  
do not grieve too much!  
If I defeat the enemy,  
then my chest will bear stars.  
When I come out from there,  
won’t those girls cheer for me,  
won’t those girls sing for me?  
Songs so loud they crack the ceiling,  
the ceiling cracks, the floor claps?"

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
In the old days, there was no conscription,  
nor did men try their luck.  
The sons of bad men were secretly caught  
wherever they were found,  
taken prisoner, brought to town;  
there they immediately became soldiers.  
Now the story is better:  
under conscription are all men’s kin.  
Those without a fault in the register  
are heavily sought after.  
Before, there were men  
who were not listed for conscription.  
They were hidden somehow,  
poor children were brought out.  
Let’s go to town to take the conscription.  
Was Kissi the first?  
I, father, was the first.  
They took me to the Russian market,  
brought me under Toompea Tower.  
Russian barber-surgeons appeared;  
Scissors go snipping,  
silver-headed knife in the palm,  
on my head, a shaver,  
cutting hair down to the bone.  
Cut bangs, cut another,  
Started cutting the third.  
But I immediately collapsed  
and began begging for mercy:  
My wife was left to weep,  
the chicken chick was left to cry,  
the flax seed left to rustle;  
I didn’t come here by choice,  
I came to the doctor’s place,  
from the Russian barber-surgeon’s hands.  
The scythes kept buzzing,  
the doctors kept complaining,  
the Russian barber-surgeon kept brandishing.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Then I will soon be a poor soldier,  
watching over the white town,  
so that no war rides in from here,  
so that the enemy force does not defeat us,  
our king will not fall.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Oh, I, poor beggar boy
Aunt sent me begging.
I did not resist anywhere,
I went begging immediately,
Visited farm, visited two,
Went to tailor Jüri,
Jüri sent me to work.
I did not resist anywhere
I went to work immediately.
I plowed out in the manor field.
The manor lord said to me:
"Listen, young man;
You will be put to draft duty."
I did not resist anywhere,
I went to take the draft immediately
I was number one.
Then I move towards the town
Between Russian guards.
One guard in front and another behind.
There my head was surrounded,
There my hair was torn out.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Now came the tying of the war,
The searching for the hussars,
The trying of the commander,
The painting of the white army.
The battle was tied straightforwardly,
The white was painted in strength.
I was given a green saber,
A green saber, a green rifle
(I was given a training rifle,
A training rifle, a training saber)
The rifle was like a small log,
The saber like a saw blade.
A drum was put on the shoulder.
The tin was big and the iron was heavy
It affected the tender soul
It rested on the shoulders.
They made me shoot the rifle
To roar the big gun
The bangs were heard by grandma,
By grandma, by grandpa,
By my elder sisters.
I had no wife in the war, dear,
Dear wife, beloved companion;
I had a dear green saber,
My beloved steadfast horse.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Our poor lads
When these little oak stumps
In summer we carry scythes on our shoulders
In winter a stout stick in hand
Then came the autumn pay day
That’s when we get paid
Money handed over on paper
We go towards the city in rows
In a group we head towards Paide
In a troop towards Toompea Castle
There my head is measured
At the wide cutting place
My hair was surely taken to the roots
Then I was given grey clothes
Put on a shroud
A mourner’s scarf was placed around my neck
A worry cap put on my head
And a veil pulled over my face.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Widows, old men  
Village old women  
Family’s scolding boys  
Neighbor’s young women  
Girl’s acquaintances  
I saw in the corner crying  
Crying, sobbing  
Joy tearing from the chest.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Some had long weddings  
Wide poles were made  
But for me, a long road was made  
A wide highway was built  
The road was long, it went to Peterborough  
The wide highway leads onto the ship  
Where our long weddings were  
There was a large lively town  
A proud highway towards Narva  
The middle of the road was gravel and soil  
Along the fence, apple trees  
Spruces, birches mixed together  
Where there was a village, there was a church  
Village children play  
Plant apple trees  
Grow cucumbers.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Oh, you poor boys  
Some are among the manor boys  
Behind are still the stable boys  
When we were taken as soldiers  
Taken along Mustla road  
Along the flat road to Tallinn  
Along the road to Paide  
We were called into the courtroom  
I went inside crunching  
Boots put on with a snap  
There my clothes were torn off  
Health was searched behind  
Hair was sharply gripped from my head  
Pants were pulled on  
Boots sent to be put on  
Uniform was buttoned on  
A belt was buckled on me  
Overcoat was laid on  
Helmet hammered on the head  
Then I was a poor ready soldier  
I am standing on the city wall  
My five fingers are on pennies  
Waiting for food to be brought  
Roast to be set before me  
A janitor came to ask me  
Hello, my dear little crown man  
What is wrong with you to live  
I hear, I answer in reply  
One is empty, another is hungry  
The third girl to suffer  
The fourth has itching desires  
I was left with a little bride at home  
I can't get off the sled  
I can't get up from inside the carriage  
The guards were angry to silence me  
I left her with goodbye  
Goodbye, little bride  
Now you see me leaving  
Maybe your eyes first.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Listen, listen, girls  
What do you think of yourselves  
Why do you dance in the tavern  
You are pulling your father's shoes off  
This is not the right time  
Now is the time for marking men  
For entwining boys  
Men were taken, men were brought  
Men were carried in both directions  
Those big culprits  
Beginners of the vodka cellar  
Gentlemen who drag to the cellar  
If you don't believe, you will surely see  
You still go to the manor  
Sand is even in your back  
Salt pots are in your pockets  
Digging ditches and trenches  
The gentleman strikes his chest with a fist  
The pimp strikes on the neck with a stick  
That poor cross on your chest  
That poor shadow on your neck  
If you don't believe, you will surely see.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Look at the man standing in front,  
Inside the first number.  
I am not afraid of a manor-minded person,  
A manor-minded person, a city scoundrel,  
To treat the wise doctor of Toompea,  
The fat German from Paide,  
I will not come from the hand of the gentleman,  
To the doctor of Toompea I will go.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Oh, we poor workboys,  
Some were howling manor boys,  
At the back edge, stable boys.  
When we were taken as soldiers  
Taken along Mustla Road,  
Along Narva Road to Mastulist,  
Along Lasna Road to Laastulist,  
Along the flat road to Tallinn.  
The bells of Tõnismäe toll the hour.  
The city clock was moved,  
The Paide clock was struck,  
Olevi was turned into a garden,  
Niguliste into a tinker’s workshop.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Oh, we poor chimney sweeps,  
Some howled like manor boys,  
Others like stable boys at the back,  
When we were taken to be soldiers,  
To defend the homeland.  
The Germans went in clumsily,  
The magistrate fumbled about,  
The stable boy followed behind.  
We were called to court.  
There our clothes were torn off,  
And our health was checked,  
Our hair was roughly shaved from our heads,  
A greatcoat was clearly cut,  
Pants were put on,  
Boots were set on our feet,  
Uniforms were put on,  
A rifle was put in my hand,  
A helmet was fitted on my head,  
A belt was strapped around me.  
The poor recruit was ready.  
I kept watch on the town rampart,  
My five fingers on the pan.  
I waited for food to be brought,  
Set in front of the stove.  
A janitor came to me asking.  
"Hello, dear crown man."  
I heard and answered:  
"One piece is empty, another hungry,  
The third very miserable,  
The fourth pining for girls.  
I left my little bride at home.  
I can’t write a letter,  
Nor send greetings from here  
To my bride at home.  
I must bear all the pain  
And give my love in return."

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Oh, we poor crown men  
The Germans wanted us as soldiers  
Made us carry packs

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
For others, the wedding was long  
Wide spars were prepared  
For me, the road was made long  
The road long, the highway wide  
The wide highway onto the ship  
To that large harbor  
There my head was border-cut  
Hair was cut off  
Tomorrow come to the streets  
Day after tomorrow to the market  
Long pants pinch  
Gray clothes smell  
Boots are tight on the feet.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Went to take the town license  
And I go under the manor’s control  
I do not fear the town license  
Town license, manor’s control  
I came from the gentlemen’s room  
From the chamber of kings  
The gentlemen remained silent  
The license table kept moving  
The dressmaking tree kept swaying  
Why did this man remain there  
There carrying his gun  
I did not come from the gentlemen’s room  
From the doctor’s room in Toompea  
I left Town Leena  
To the fish shore Kaie  
To the Alutaha Anne  
The meadow girls are left to cry  
At Alatsema Arju Anne  
In Kahats Järva Kaie  
In Virumaa Mari Madal  
In Lääne little Leena  
I lick water from the borrowed  
I try from the village wells  
Still I am red in the hut  
In the watermill I am strong  
In the fire hay I am powerful  
In the hunger hay I am handsome  
May water last as long as milk  
May kvass last from the well  
May fire hay last as tobacco  
I am beautiful for the hay  
Handsome for the watermill  
Strong for the fire hay  
Handsome for the hunger hay  
I push from the hut’s shop  
From the watermill’s water  
Warrior, war horse  
I come but under my father’s door  
I come but under my mother’s door  
Father did not recognize his son  
Mother did not recognize her son  
Sister recognized the young brother  
From his worn smocks  
From his set shirts  
From his patterned gloves  
Warrior, war horse  
War whip on the head.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Liisake and Leenake  
Liisa complained to the gentleman  
The gentleman said to me  
Oh, Jaanike and little brother  
What have you done now  
You have sold yourself out  
You, Liisa, have to leave  
The guards came and arrested me  
Took me to Tõnismäe  
A guard in front and another behind  
And me between the guards  
Between two guards in the middle  
There I was called by the underofficer  
The sergeant stood behind me  
Jaanike and little brother  
Jaan, you have to march  
I cannot march  
My legs hurt from digging  
My knees are covered with soil  
From there they took me to the tin can  
On Tont, I drank beer without top  
At the foot of the hill, I drank wet beer  
In Lasnamäe, I took flight  
Tallinn girls ran after me  
Oh, Jaanike and little brother  
Proclaim your song well  
I look askance back  
The Tallinn girls stayed behind  
I have girls in Riga myself  
Other girls in the tin can  
Narva girls with tattered necklaces  
In misty stockings, with clogged shoes,  
Earrings jingling in ears  
Dresses rustling on them  
They go to the city swing  
Pull the swing until it creaks  
Stamp feet until it clacks on the floor  
City hill little bird  
And Lasnamäe children’s game.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Oh, those city conscripts  
Pitiful matters  
The young generation crying  
I took the draft, I went to the city  
I took a number, cried a lot  
I feel miserable leaving  
Some dear people  
A friend with a sad heart  
There is no aunt, no old man  
Who quickly cheers us up  
And entices the warriors  
May the black sea entice us  
May the gray sky support us  
Black sea, my horse  
Gray sky, my saddle  
Oh, I poor soldier  
I stand straight in my greatcoat  
I step inside the gray hall  
My belt was long and tight  
My rifle green on my shoulder  
Black straps across my chest  
Helmet high on my head  
But as I lift my helmet  
And touch my head with my hand  
Then I felt sad  
When my beautiful hair  
And my fine long topknot  
Now my hair is shaved  
And my long topknot trimmed  
Then I remembered many times  
Where my dear parents are  
And my own sisters and brothers  
Where I lived before  
And where I grew up  
Oh, I poor soldier  
By day I am alert  
And at night I watch over the rampart  
I stand straight on the bridge  
And watch from the rampart ready  
So that this war does not come in  
No foreign army will defeat us  
No enemy will hide from us.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Let's go to town to draw lots  
To draw lots, to rush to war  
The lot did not fall on us  
The lot fell on the boys of the town  
Then, then, dear brothers  
Then, crying young maidens  
The girls of Harju are grieving  
When those masts appeared on the land  
Masts on the land, tents on the road  
Flags can be seen towards the town  
Towers in front of the Tondi tavern  
When the masts remained visible  
Then the maidens remained to weep  
The girls of Harju endlessly  
Go away, go away little brother  
Our fellows were taken away  
Our companions were taken far  
Those who went to fight were taken to war  
This is the great Finnish road  
To the flat road of Tallinn  
To the crossroads of Riga  
To the spot of Pärnu  
To the tired road of Russia  
Along this Moscow highway  
My beloved left  
I was left behind, the poor girl  
She went to foreign lands  
I was left under the cruel German  
Under a loud old gentleman  
To dig stone trenches  
To turn over black soil  
Oh, if tomorrow would be nice weather  
Nice weather and not cold  
I would go after her  
With a big, splendid carriage  
With a broad Russian sleigh.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Oh, I, a poor despised child  
Father despised me, mother despised me  
The manor elders despised me  
All the bailiffs, stewards, and granary keepers  
Were angry with me  
I was taken to town to pay the fine  
There my head was shaved  
And my hair was cut off.  
Then my brothers came to see me  
I sent my hair home to my mother  
Letters to my dear bride  
Then the bride wept sadly  
Oh, my beloved spouse  
This bread was made for you  
You eat it with great sorrow.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Oh, I, a poor despised child  
Father despised me, mother despised me  
The manor elders despised me  
All the bailiffs, stewards, and granary keepers  
Were angry with me  
I was taken to town to pay the fine  
There my head was shaved  
And my hair was cut off.  
Then my brothers came to see me  
I sent my hair home to my mother  
Letters to my dear bride  
Then the bride wept sadly  
Oh, my beloved spouse  
This bread was made for you  
You eat it with great sorrow.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
I had one little brother  
He was also caught in wars  
He was also painted forcibly  
Put under a heavy burden.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
My dear little brother  
Mundel had a long wedding  
Wide branches were prepared  
For me, a long road was made  
Wide highways were built  
When I start to go  
Then sorrow begins to come  
Tears roll from my eyes  
Afterwards, the girls of Järva cried  
The wreaths were laid aside  
The girls of Harju bowed  
The flaxen locks mourned  
Until the masts were visible on land  
The ship’s topsides turned green  
Then I looked back  
The girls of Tallinn stared down  
I have new girls in Muskus  
In Tinaburg, there are other girls.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
I took the number, cried a lot  
Sparks were made, eyes were washed  
Taken to Toompea Castle  
There my head was shaved  
Hair was singed away  
Prison irons were prepared  
Chains for the soul were sought  
Chains for the neck  
Chains for the broken heart  
There I was lifted onto the cart  
Bound with silk cords  
Moved towards the town.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Let St. Martin's Day come to the countryside  
Then comes men's holiday  
The picking of young men  
Then you can sleep in the forest  
Creep from the bush into the shed  
Freeze in the hay barn  
A bundle on the worse foot.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Who is taken from us to Russia
Neither father went, nor mother went
Neither sons-in-law went
The youngest brother is taken from us
The tallest, the finest
The straightest of all
The loved ones went along the road
I myself walk along the garden
The ox stayed out in the yard mooing
The horse stayed banging on the iron
I myself went along the road
The loved ones went along the garden
Farewell, girls of this land
I have new girls from Muskus
On the head wears a lace cap
Breasts covered with Riga scarves
Hips adorned with a festive skirt
If I were a soldier friend
A bride of the shop boys
My head would wear a lace cap
Hips adorned with a festive skirt
Breasts covered with Riga scarves

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Now we are plowing with ten pairs  
The horse pulls with nine  
Seven are reaping with sickles  
Five mowing with scythes  
Six raking with rakes  
Then I go to see my sister  
But to hear news of my sister  
Whether she has received a baking place  
Or has found a place for bread  
Or a place of dark troubles  
The sister understood, answered back  
Clearly, clearly, brother mine  
Clearly, a place of dark troubles  
Clearly, a place of bread and sorrow  
The brother went to the black sea  
To clean the crown's gun  
To scrub the iron's rust  
The horse kindly cutting the greenery  
The tin was dense, the iron was heavy  
The copper was strong to cut the greenery  
The brother writes letters here on the land  
The whole world is breathing  
The pure people resting  
Our poor ones marching  
Our work was unknown  
Signposts unset  
Milestones unplaced  
The boy's path goes to the maiden  
Our path goes to the sea  
There, to the large harbor  
This touched the soul tenderly  
The brother's tender blood.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
The Russian general wrote:
Men needed for the militia
Youth needed for the regiment
Where I was finally taken
I was taken to the Russian path
Made to fire the rifle
The rifle was long, the iron was heavy
The brass was loud and shining
It pressed on the shoulders
Goes on the hands of men
Rolls over five fingers.
The commander shouted, the corporal yelled
The sergeant ordered to stand straight
Ordered to raise the rifles
To poke into sheaths
Oh, you old Peasant Marie
Gave the Crown bad food
The spear has lost its mind
What was brought here to kill was given
That I think afterwards
A salary was given to me
That I think back on
Let it be on me
Such a great sinner
I did not sin greatly
That I am bound
Put under guard
Then I am bound
And sent to Finland
I rode to the five corners of Viru
I rode to the seven corners of Kolga
I crossed Harju
Laid through Lääne.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Little old ladies, little old men  
Village old grannies  
Neighbor's young wives  
Girls and acquaintances  
I saw in the corner, crying  
Crying, sighing  
Tearing joy from the chest  
Because I am eager, whole-hearted  
To exchange the wargear  
Do not shame me  
Girls, through your crying  
Do not humiliate me for being shy  
Knock among the men  
A man is not made to stumble  
Not to stumble in the home yard  
Not to love the stove's corner  
Not to grumble in the room's corner  
A man was made for roads  
For roads, for work  
To fight against the enemy for victory  
I am going to the city to draw lots  
To draw lots, to hurry to war  
Then I will polish myself in the German way  
Dress myself in the gentleman's way  
I will sling tunes, will it hurt?  
Into the black six-hot stove  
I will become a soldier, a warrior  
I will become a soldier with boots on  
Proud belt in front of my chest  
White crown gloves on my hands  
Will you then not scold, enemies  
Village brothers, family spirits  
Will you not then shout, sisters  
We will not slur the war song  
Sing so that the ceiling rattles  
The ceiling rattles, the floor claps  
Because God gives me strength  
That my chest bears steam  
What I conquered under the Russian army  
Passed through battles  
Not only pride calls me  
To bring my life to war  
But to be able to kill the enemy  
I tear myself from mother's neck  
From father's grace, from bride's bosom  
From the rut of parents' yard

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
When I go to run errands  
To run errands, to rush to war  
Number one or number two  
I was sent to Finland  
I was driven to Harju County  
To see the Russian ways  
To understand Russia  
I traveled to Finland, I traveled to the Islands  
I took a rifle, I took a gun  
I marched forward  
I informed my feet  
I gave my big toe a push  
Legs, hurry up, row your shins  
Let your bare feet go  
The captors are coming  
Through Viru’s torchlight  
Through Harju’s wilderness  
No leaf fluttered  
No birch rustled  
Why did that woman leave me  
Despised me without guilt  
Because of the lies of this parish  
Because of the tongues of the county  
Let that woman go if she wants  
With all the keys to my cellar  
And the wide lock bar of the granary

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
My father guarded the sacred grove forest  
My mother cherished the birch forest  
She waited for her sons to grow  
For her daughters to become wise  
The sons grew, the sons became strong  
The sons were taken to Russia  
The sons were sent to war

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
A long road was indeed made  
A wide highway was constructed  
The long road went to Petersburg  
The wide highway went onto the ship  
Tomorrow by noon we will arrive there  
To the proud city of Petersburg  
There, however, several kitchens are smoking  
They are all cooking our food  
Several thousand bread eggs  
Some fresh, some old  
Some old rotten eggs

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
My father wanted me to be a soldier  
My mother wanted me to be a sailor  
My sisters wanted me to be a handmaiden  
My brothers wanted me to be a Russian man  
Then the decision was made and I went to the city  
It happened once and I stayed there  
They took me to the Tallinn castle  
There my head was shaved  
And my sideburns were cut off  
My hair was shorn off  
Then I put on a soldier’s shirt  
I put on the king’s coat  
On my head, the leader’s hat  
I put the nobleman’s sword at my belt  
Then an old woman came to see  
Will you stay in this city?  
No, I will not stay in this city  
I am going down to Pärnu city  
Long ago, Pärnu had bulls  
The black ox of the sea roared  
No, I will not stay in this city  
I am going down to Riga city  
The rich rooster of Riga crowed  
The gray stallion of the forest neighed  
And I will stay in this city  
There I will make the markets bustle  
I will make the towns steam  
The streets cover with mist  
There the market women recognized me  
The city’s whores recognized me  
Look, here comes the brawler  
The pincher of the Riga maiden

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Then it was an era for our land  
When Kadrina had a king  
Liisabet was the money maker  
Soon Aleksander began  
Pandi Peeter Paulovits  
Then the era changed  
It began to gain power over us  
Squeezing the boys  
Then came Liisu’s sending away  
The hiding of the family son  
The search for hussars  
The testing of the grenadier.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
But I am alone on the soil,  
Like a little flower out there.  
I had no father,  
Nor did my mother come into the world. –  
But God knows who made me.  
Many men have made me,  
Many Germans have sought me.  
I was made by country boys.  
Germans have sought me.  
Then I grew bigger,  
I grew a little stronger;  
Once I went to my aunt,  
My aunt sent me to beg.  
I did not resist anywhere,  
So I went to beg.  
I visited farms, visited two,  
Went to a third to beg.  
The third farmer said to me:  
“Do you hear, you poor boy?  
You will be made to serve!”  
I did not resist anywhere,  
So I went to serve.  
I served one year, served two,  
Began to serve a third:  
Then I went to a manor worker,  
To the manor lord’s place.  
The manor lord saw me.  
And the manor lord said to me:  
“Do you hear, young man,  
You will be put under a conscript.”  
I did not resist anywhere,  
So I went under conscription.  
I took number one,  
Number one, and number two.  
Now I am moving towards the town,  
Swaying towards the wilderness tavern,  
Already Toompää is in sight,  
Our Patarei is visible.  
The town’s chimneys smoke,  
Silver tips glisten.  
Russian barbers are visible.  
Golden scissors in hand,  
Ready to trim my hair,  
To crop a lock of hair.  
Then I fall to my knees:  
Oh my God, where are my hairs?  
Dear God, where are my locks?  
Where is my white head?  
Hairs pulled from the roots,  
Locks held in the hand to be trimmed—  
Now I am ready to be a Russian.  
Now I was enlisted as a soldier,  
Painted in Russian troops,  
Spirit beads were torn off,  
Necklaces were taken,  
Blood sacrifices were ready;  
For my tender soul,  
And for my dear neck.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
War churns in our yard,  
Plague tramples our street!  
They prepare weapons:  
They clean their gunpowder,  
Sharpen their blades,  
Feed themselves well.  
Against the minds of our men,  
Against the sides of boys,  
Against the braids of maidens.  
They go with fierce breasts,  
Fierce breasts toward Riga,  
Marching toward Tallinn.  
They go to plunder Riga,  
To kill in Tallinn,  
To burn Põltsamaa.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
War churns in our yard,  
Plague tramples our street!  
They prepare weapons:  
They clean their gunpowder,  
Sharpen their blades,  
Feed themselves well.  
Against the minds of our men,  
Against the sides of boys,  
Against the braids of maidens.  
They go with fierce breasts,  
Fierce breasts toward Riga,  
Marching toward Tallinn.  
They go to plunder Riga,  
To kill in Tallinn,  
To burn Põltsamaa.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
My father tended the sacred forest,  
Growing the birch forest.  
He surrounded the forest with rowan trees,  
Waiting for his son to grow,  
The son grew into a handsome one,  
Grew into a tall man.  
The beautiful sacred forest was left behind,  
The dear birch forest was left behind,  
The rowan forest was left behind.  
The son was enlisted for wars!  
The son was painted with strength,  
Made to carry a pike.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Sometimes the old man whips me,  
Sometimes the old man whips me,  
Then I have to clean the gun,  
Remove rust from the iron,  
Sharpen the bare sword;  
Then the room remains for the old man,  
The house remains for the old woman,  
The yards, the markets remain for the sister,  
The small herd remains for the brother.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Sõni was once a force in our land,  
Sõni like the king of Kadrina;  
Peeter Pavlovits was placed,  
He began to take power,  
Picking young men,  
From here, however, from the larger gatherings,  
From the rougher parts of the parish,  
Longer ones, finer ones.  
Then comes the Russian takeover,  
The will of the peasantry.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Do not shame him
Girls wail with their tears
Do not humiliate me,
A man is made for work
To fight against the enemy for victory
Let's go to town to take a chance
I take the chance, hastening to war
There I polish myself in the German way
I groom myself in the gentleman's way
I throw my clothes fully into the stove
Black coat into the hot stove
I become a soldier, a warrior
I get an iron helmet on my head
Then I say farewell
I take you with a joyful heart
I take the rifle firmly in hand
I put a strange saber on my belt
Neither kin nor playful family spirits will protect me then
Nor will the girls cheer
Singing battle songs to me
Come on mama, give me a leader
Why don’t you give me your daughter?

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
Shame be upon the lord  
And the other upon his lady  
They put me under the town's cross  
And under the manor's domain  
I do not fear the town's cross  
The town's cross – the manor's domain  
The doctor of Toompää will treat me  
I will not come from the lords' hands  
From the Toompää doctor's chamber  
Among the Russian guards  
The lords remain sighing  
The doctors grumbling  
The Russian guards cursing  
The Sander men shouting

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
I went to the meadow,
Singing across the wide clearing.
A herdsman came from Kuura's side,
Helpers from Arude's side,
A young man running from the manor's side,
A youth from Kiima's barn side.
He gave me a large piece:
A large piece, a wide piece,
Swamp from the bog, swampy land,
Stream from the river, white land,
Marsh from the lake, wet land;
I plowed until my horses were tired,
Blades worn out from hard work.
I let the horses rest at noon,
Under the hayloft to graze,
Under the heathland to roam.
A wolf came from the great forest,
With wide paws even from the hayloft,
It broke my black horse,
Tore my gray horse,
Ate both foolishly.
I wept bitterly:
An old woman was there, an old lady was there.
"Why do you cry, young one?"
"Why do I cry, old lady,
Or why do I cry, little lady?
Be quiet, young one,
Let the wolf come, the wolf's kin:
He lets the wolf from the great forest,
Wide-pawed from the hayloft forest."

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I do not belong among the great;
Nor fit in the house of the rich!
To sit on a high ear.
I sit especially.
Among other burdensome troubles;
Among the common people.
I speak plainly with the lowly!
Soon I will speak with the weary.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The frog went with three districts  
The flea went with two parts,  
But I went with the chief.  
I went to Harju street,  
There were two gray ones in front.  
There stood the court lords opposite,  
Court lords, governors.  
But the lord shouted from the stairs,  
"On whom is this hornbag?"  
On me is this hornbag!  
"How did this grain get wet?  
The grain got wet from rain,  
It was covered with fog,  
Wet barns were struck.  
It is not my fault,  
Nor is it the fault of the poor parish.  
It is the fault of the evil commissioner,  
It is the fault of the club servant."

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Maiden, young girl!  
The fish darted from under the stone  
At the crab's bright mountain:  
This girl is my namesake  
My wreath-head beside me  
Who has knots in her shoelaces  
No young woman laughed at me!  
The girl fluttered strongly.  
Lazy woman laughed at me;  
The girl fluttered fiercely.  
Hello, brother!  
Take a whip from your pants;  
Pay the women laughter wages  
The girl’s flutter wages.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Widows, I would become a widower,  
Would broad aprons fall?  
But I say to the widows:  
O widows, go away from me,  
Let broad aprons fall.  
But I will take Epuke,  
At Epuke’s place, pearl-like beads.  
A maiden, a young girl,  
So let me say once again:  
The usual one is already drowsy,  
A hand that works, a foot that walks;  
Who will buy me out of slavery?  
Redeem me from the snowfall?  
Take me away from the rain showers,  
From the Viru sickle’s blade,  
Behind the Järva plows,  
From Harju’s ard cranes.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, we poor gentlemen  
Ladies with golden crowns  
Let us rise up from our seats  
Let us go up to the hall  
Let us step onto the window frame  
Look at the poor parish  
How this parish is tormented  
The small ones are tormented  
The bigger ones are killed  
Oh, our poor manorial spirits  
Late spirits of the lord  
The most unfortunate servants  
Oh, we poor road walkers  
Road walkers, land measurers  
Managers of German affairs  
Parish letter bearers  
Readers of the municipal book.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
My dear old grandmother!  
She heard me coming home,  
Beyond Väila,  
Beyond the forest's edge,  
She immediately said to her daughter:  
Jump, Anne, leap, Kaie;  
Then take the gate from the fence,  
Close the doors tightly;  
Home is coming a golden bird,  
Coming in a silk bird,  
Then light the stove;  
Heat the stove for the cumin;  
Honor to the whisk of branches,  
Then the branch goes to the whisk:  
The goose is going to the ditch,  
The duck is going to wash knees,  
The pig is going to wash the piglets!  
In her fist are whip strokes;  
On her neck are rod strokes,  
Whip marks between the belt.  
Who had struck the cruel servant,  
Set the strong village chief,  
Knocked on the wicked Kilter;  
Kilter had a stone stick,  
The Junker had a five-striped,  
The servant a six-twisted,  
The barn had a toothed one.  
This went straight through;  
Along the thin shoulder,  
Along the small arm!

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
May the harvest come in the year of heaven!  
May it rain in the year of the rod;  
May hail fall like iron,  
There must always be a slave,  
A servant to do the work,  
To keep things running properly.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Mother, dear little woman!  
Stoke the stove with kitchen firewood.  
Honor the whisk for Virve,  
I will take the whisk to Virve,  
To sew the hem of Aniluida:  
Whip strikes from the hip;  
Whip strikes from the neck,  
Blue marks on the back!  
Oh, this poor Esna parish,  
Among other parishes,  
In the middle of the district;  
Whip lashes were heard from Laiuse  
Whip lashes to Paide!  
Torment from Alutaguse;  
The maiden's crying in the meadow!  
I cried before, I cried yesterday,  
Again I cry today!  
*  
Why did the golden eyebrows dry up,  
Why did the eyes straighten?  
Into the rough village,  
To the border of Esna parish.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, God, dear God!  
Take away the poor children  
Gather up the immoral ones;  
Take into your arms the guilty  
Put in your bosom the despised beasts  
Pollute the rest who are rich  
Shoot the daughters of the family  
Swear at the free women.  
The rich curse me as a farmer  
As a money-driven taxi  
As a gold-plated carriage.  
Let me live without a necklace  
Without a growing collar  
Without rings to row:  
While living, I get a bead  
While growing, I get a neck-bead  
Take the rich landowner away  
Not the money-driven taxi  
Not the gold-plated carriage  
Not the free woman's carriage.  
Let the poor go to hell  
The golden ones to the devil  
The paupers to the demon  
The great rye of the swamp  
I will stay living without a necklace  
Without a growing collar.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Our noble little gold one  
The boldly patterned little bird  
The helper man with a silver cap.  
The silver bell waver  
The gold stick swinger  
Let the children go to lunch  
Sisters in the evening!  
The children ask for lunch  
Sisters in the evening.  
Surely comes from the black manor field  
From the blue grove inside.  
Between gray gardens.  
Now comes the nodder  
Now appears the pincher  
The complainer of five girls,  
The dropper of six girls.  
I will put the gentlemen to the oxen  
The lads into the words  
The bold ones into the tassels  
Ladies with headscarves;  
Myself behind the plowman.  
Plow the barn, pour into the field  
The furrow’s track, old red  
Lift to the land, the dark ox.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I was a slave, I tended the herd  
I was in the Dutch service  
In the fields of Lehila's sheep  
Holland made a new dome  
Lehila land a wide coat.  
Oh, poor me, a prisoner of children  
Complained from the hiding place!  
Nights I am imprisoned, nights I am saved.  
Nights I keep watch over my father’s herd  
I protect the herd of my brother  
At the river mouth, at the lake mouth  
In the middle of the river’s whirlpool  
Between the river’s currents.  
Two merchants came  
Two merchant servants  
Both had swords at their belts  
Both had spurs on their feet.  
"Maiden, young girl  
In embroidered clothing, slender,  
Sell me father’s horse  
Trade for brother’s saddle."  
"I will not sell father’s herd  
I will not trade brother’s saddle  
Father has a good horse,  
Brother has a dear saddle."  
"Sell me the sword at your belt  
Trade for the spurs on your feet."  
"I will not sell the sword at my belt  
I will not trade the spurs on my feet  
Maiden, young girl  
Allow me to come to you,  
To come to you until death  
To be with you until the end of life.  
I will put you to easy work:  
Beating rye stalks  
Treading on oat husks.  
In summer I keep smoke away  
In winter I lift dust away.  
In summer I feed you sugar rushes  
In winter buckwheat tangles,  
A year's goose meat.  
I protect the herd with walnut cores."  
"I hear, I answer:  
Salt cellars you have for sugar  
Pumpkins for goose meat."  
"Allow me to come  
To come to you, to stay with you."  
"Maybe you would bake me in gold  
Spin silk threads  
Sew with silver thread.  
Maybe there would be a silver yard  
An iron-forged gate  
Stone in the corral,  
Still I will not come to you.  
For you a pigsty  
A horse’s nightmare,  
A dog’s snout with a stick in it  
A mouth like a poor man’s hole  
Face like a paper sack  
Teeth like cattle gates  
Jaws like that of a tongs.  
I will go where it is created  
Where the Creator has made a place  
Mary has given a home.  
Where my blood has carried  
Where my color has taken me  
Where my little luck shares in.  
Maybe there would be an oily yard  
Maybe a fenced garden  
Gates forged from twigs,  
Maybe there would be a pillar in the yard  
In the corral stone stakes  
Still I go where it is created,  
Where the Creator has made a place,  
Mary has given a home.  
Where my blood has carried  
Where my color has taken me  
Where my little luck shares in."

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, our handsome gentlemen  
And our slender Germans  
Young gentlemen with golden hair  
Ladies in silver lace.  
Rise up onto the chair  
Step onto the windowsill;  
Look upon the poor parish  
Upon the whole district,  
When this parish is afflicted  
The district is troubled.  
The bulls still eat  
The poor horses are confined  
Men dressed in their jackets.  
The bulls groan with their horns  
The horses toss their chests around.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
When I have to go to Tapa
To the dust of Tapa, from the soil of Tapa
From the evil place of Tapa.
From under the German scoundrel
From under the loud old gentleman
From under the bitter soldier
From under the cruel master.
I would look back sorrowfully,
The dog would turn its head from the load,
Before ruin to the home
Before berries to the house,
Under the beloved man's yard.
Then I would look back a little
Turn my head a bit:
"Where the hell is the place,
Where the damn spot.
The windows of hell are burning
The towers of ghosts are foolish
Heating up the evil spirit
Where I was beaten with a whip
Where my arms were tormented,
Where the finger bones were scolded.
If only the wrists were rowan-berry red
The forearms maple-colored
I had thin wrists
Thin shoulder buds."

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, I am a poor purchased slave!  
Purchased slave, crowned with a wreath,  
A pledged servant.  
Who will buy me, and crown me?  
Who will buy me from slavery  
And crown me with a wreath,  
Redeem me from the snowfall?  
Take us away from the rain showers.  
Viru Peter, a young lad,  
He will buy us an ox yoke,  
A tall apron for the belly,  
A wide ribbon for the head,  
Ruffled cuffs for the hands,  
New buttoned shoes for the feet.  
He will buy us from slavery  
And crown us with days  
Redeem us from the snowfall,  
Take us away from the rain showers.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Who waits for me at home?  
The dog waits for me at home.  
Who urges me into the house?  
The cat urges me into the house.  
The dog waits for crusts,  
The cat for crumbs of bread.  
I have no other waiters,  
The dog is dear, the cat is precious.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, this poor Tapa parish,  
The hungry Näu village,  
Where I don’t dare to walk in the manor,  
I don’t flash before the lord:  
I don’t even have a barley cake in my pocket,  
Nor any oat porridge in my pan.  
Salt is scarce inside the box,  
And beer is thin in the neck.  
Women walk the manor roads  
Wearing egg mittens.  
Pairs of chickens in the stall,  
Pairs of geese under the hem.  
Thread scarves in their hands,  
The court’s sheep on the hilltop  
Are led out into the manor yard  
To the old lord on the rampart.  
From there the lord comes grunting:  
“Look where the poor sheep stands.  
Take it home to the dogs,  
Give it to the hound!  
Bring me a fatty from the barn,  
A pretty one from the herd  
To carry to the manor’s sheepfold.”  
When the people go to work:  
The coachman goes like a devil,  
The young lord like Judas behind;  
The parish like a prisoner in the middle.  
Then you hear the coachman’s voice,  
The old lord’s cursing.  
There the rowan struggles,  
And then the fir tree moves  
And there the bird cherry staggers  
Over the shoulder of the apple tree.  
But it strikes past the fine coat,  
Past the thin shoulders,  
Past the long sleeves.  
The tenant sighs in slave bondage,  
A tear in his eye.  
The coachman is called hell,  
The young lord is sent after,  
The old lord to watch.  
Tables are set in place,  
Chairs are brought to the table,  
Fire and tar also brought:  
Eat, coachman, drink, steward,  
This poor parish’s toil,  
Its tiny pain-blood.  
If you tormented the parish,  
If you tormented the little ones.  
Oh, this golden Kuruda,  
Silver Roosnaida,  
When the people went to work:  
The coachman like the moon,  
The young lord like God behind,  
The parish like copper in the middle.  
No fir moves there,  
Nor does the rowan struggle.  
No bird cherry staggers there,  
Over the shoulder of the apple tree.  
No sound of the coachman’s voice,  
Nor the steward’s whining,  
Nor the old lord’s cursing.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, we poor spirits of the parish,
The demons, the other spirits,
The proper third ones,
The auxiliary spirits the fourth,
The drunkards the fifth:
The pain of the night, the pain of the moon,
The pain of God's fire
Golden rakes in hand
Silver plow handles:
I hunted a hundred head for the Saxon,
Six piles for the king.
The piles appeared in Courland,
The herds appeared in Germany,
The flocks seemed to Narva!

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Master, little master,  
Mistress, little lady,  
Pay the servant's wage,  
Reward the poor child's toil!  
If you do not pay the servant's wage,  
Nor reward the poor child's toil,  
Then you will pay in the afterlife,  
Pay to Maria's house:  
You will walk to the gallows tree,  
Clothes rolled in bundles,  
You will seek your little servant,  
Beg for someone to pay wages:  
"Come servant, take your due,  
Come poor one, take your toil!"  
Then I will understand, reply in return:  
Dear miss and little sir,  
Surely you have paid there, in the land,  
Where my path is one of torment,  
An aching road of hardship;  
I do not want to go to the afterlife,  
Among the blue clouds,  
Between the white clouds,  
At the midpoint of the three dawns  
To accept the poor one's toil!

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Our parish, captive parish,  
Coming parish, tar parish,  
Parish of white huts,  
Bulls are eating into the yoke,  
Runes have withered into branches,  
Young men went into the tree,  
Poor spirits went into the poles,  
Rogues were beaten into the pillar,  
The poor into the prison house.  
Women wept in front of the door,  
Wearing egg mittens,  
A fat wheel under the apron,  
Went to the gentleman's petition,  
Went to the lady's petition:  
"Oh, my pea gentleman,  
Golden crown lady,  
Silver button young gentlemen,  
Copper button old gentlemen,  
Rise up onto the chair,  
Sit on the edge of the sofa,  
Look at the poor parish,  
How this parish is tormented,  
The bigger ones are killed,  
The stronger ones are carried away,  
The bigger ones are killed,  
The stronger ones are carried away,  
Do not walk to the bog."  
The lady turned her head.  
"Go home, poor woman,  
From the swamp I will bring those warm switches,  
From the willow I will bring proper switches,  
From the aspen I will bring softer ones:  
Beat the leaves, beating men.  
Lift up the oak roots,  
Switches rough in the hand,  
The ends of the switch will blister,  
The ends of the stick will splinter."

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I went home wandering,
To see the old woman,
I heard the old woman crying,
Mourning the deceased.
I asked the old woman:
"Why are you crying, old woman?"
The old woman heard, replied:
"Why do you cry, young daughter?
You still wander the manor,
You rock before the swing,
You shout in the middle of the courtyard,
Roaming among spirits!
I am afraid, I am afraid, young daughter,
Afraid of the fine shirts betraying me,
Of being buried beneath the husband's coat,
Covered by the worn fur coat!"
I heard, replied:
"Not me, not me, old woman,
The fine shirts do not betray me,
The husband's coat
Covers the worn fur coat;
But me, but me, old woman,
I am hidden by fine sand
And buried by black soil,
Covered by the worn church,
Concealed by the white little tower."

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, we poor tiumehed  
When we come from the wind  
And we are wet  
Where do we dry ourselves  
Let's go to Rein's thigh,  
What do we put in our oven?  
Kubja's shanks, the constable's gloves  
We both help the fields  
We put these in our oven  
And then dry ourselves.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
If I were in charge  
If I were in control  
I would turn the oxen into horses  
The ladies into sticks  
The braids on the ears into earrings  
The lads into companions for the girls

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Listen, dear old granny  
Listen, dear old grandpa  
Throw a bundle of twigs into the stove  
An honor birch whisk for whipping  
I go to whip the miserable  
I go to chase the geese  
That had beaten the angry groom  
The angry groom, the brave young man  
Even louder the manor lords  
Would be my existence  
Would be my doing  
I would know what I would do  
I would turn the gentlemen into bulls  
The young men into stories  
The grooms behind bars  
And the maidens into bald heads  
I myself would sit on the saddle  
A long whip in my hand  
Plowing the yard, turning the field  
Wow, old red one  
The plow plows, the back bends  
Plowing the field, the tail swishes  
Our day blazes into noon  
Turns to the middle of the day  
We have nothing to eat  
Neither given nor offered without asking  
If I get angry  
I leave the bulls right there  
I myself go where the devils  
Then I go to the forest as a breaker  
Then I break the bulls  
I kill the lambs in the barn.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The master was angry with me  
The mistress was loud with me  
I am good to the oxen  
Good matches for them  
I tended the oxen well  
Gently harnessed the horses  
I plowed the sod land  
Did not spoil the horn roots  
Threw willow without a family  
The housewife was angry with me  
Sprinkled salt among the coarse salt  
Added straw to the hay  
Poured water into the milk.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Slave labor is sought  
A poor child is seen  
If not changed, forced to carry mud  
To beat clods in the field  
To carry stones together.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Brother invited to the wedding  
I don't have time to go  
I’m going to town tomorrow  
With the church gentleman  
With the old gentleman of the parish  
I will become the parish carpenter  
The trial bag will be on me  
I, the poor stable boy  
The gentleman of the parish’s spiritual boy  
When they go through Kiu  
I go through Tond’s straw  
But here comes a German  
Hello, hello, parish carpenter  
Who has the trial bag on  
Open the trial bag.  
Listen, you parish carpenter  
Why is the rye wet?  
That’s not my fault  
Nor the poor parish’s fault  
It is the evil carpenter’s fault  
Grain was wind-sifted in the blizzard  
Grain was carried in the rain  
Grain was caught in the hail  
The barn roof keeps breaking  
Oh, I, the poor stable boy  
The gentleman of the parish’s servant boy.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
In the ancient times, the daughter cries  
It is not for nothing that the daughter cries  
The daughter has a quarrel coming  
And is weighing the rods  
The fire does not extinguish  
The woman does not argue with the youth  
The horse does not belong to the gray harness  
There is no spark in the bedding stakes  
Illu-lillu pregnant gray  
In our yard several stalls  
In each stall seven gray ones  
They neigh and make a ruckus  
All of them wanted payments  
Settlements with silver manes  
Rates with golden combs  
Tender caresses  
I could not pay them  
Could not settle with silver manes  
Could not rate with golden combs  
Could not caress them more tenderly  
Then had to be beaten with a whip  
Pulled by a tarred rope  
But I managed to get away poor  
Skirting along the field.  
Then I was caught with a rope  
And chased mercilessly  
See, the rope was a thousand fathoms long  
And within the rope were a hundred knots.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The landlady tricked me  
She promised to cook cat soup.  
She cooked soup from an old shoe  
Threw in a piece of song.  
The master thought: a piece of bacon  
The servant thought: a piece of bone  
The children: a piece of lamb meat  
The old man: a heel.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The devil dug in the spruce forest,  
The pagan hammered on the sapwood,  
Made the wheelwright’s barn  
An old gentleman’s carriage.  
The rims rusted from the iron,  
The spokes from wolf bones,  
The side boards from forest trees.  
The hub was made of pine,  
The hoops cast from brass,  
Boards broken from logs,  
End pieces from teeth,  
The breasts from ladders,  
Legs considered as shafts,  
A plug rod made of dill,  
The back beam of a pine tree.  
Tail base for the tail end.  
The horse was placed in front;  
The gentleman between the shafts,  
The coachman’s ears, the bell on the tilt,  
The gentleman’s ears by the shafts. –  
The lady with a whip on the bench,  
The young lord with a running foot.  
Misses tarred the carriage skin,  
Nailing the coach’s hide.  
Then the Germans were sent to hell,  
The peasants to heaven.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Investigate, investigate, what are you investigating  
Investigate a new thigh  
Boundary on a long border  
Let it be on a wide board  
There is pitch water of reeds  
Five buckets of cracked water  
Six buckets of devil’s urine  
There the investigators swim  
Washing their heads with borders.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I was a slave, I tended the herd  
I was a Dutch servant,  
A shepherd boy from the land of souls,  
What was my wage for the dyke path?  
An old coat, an old fur coat,  
Are you satisfied with that?  
And are you satisfied with us?  
I do not agree to that  
Nor am I satisfied with you.  
If I serve, I will serve still  
A rich man and the well-mannered.  

I was a slave, I tended the herd  
I was a Dutch servant,  
A shepherd boy from the land of souls,  
What was my wage for the dyke path?  
An old rag on a shriveled branch,  
An old bundle at the edge of the forest,  
Do you agree to that?  
And are you satisfied with us?  
I do not agree to that  
Nor am I satisfied with you.  
If I serve, I will serve still  
A rich man and the well-mannered.  

I was a slave, I tended the herd  
I was a Dutch servant,  
A shepherd boy from the land of souls,  
What was my wage for the dyke path?  
A sheep with a lamb,  
A pig with a hundred piglets,  
Do you agree to that?  
And are you satisfied with us?  
I am not satisfied with that  
Nor do I agree with you.  
If I serve, I will serve still  
A rich man and the well-mannered.  

I was a slave, I tended the herd  
I was a Dutch servant,  
A shepherd boy from the land of souls,  
What was my wage for the dyke path?  
A horse with the largest bridle,  
A barn with a hundred horned heads,  
Do you agree, do you agree with that?  
And are you satisfied with us?  
I do not agree to that  
Nor am I satisfied with you.  
If I serve, I will serve still  
A rich man and the well-mannered.  

I was a slave, I tended the herd  
I was a Dutch servant,  
A shepherd boy from the land of souls,  
What was my wage for the dyke path?  
From Tallinn to the roads of Tartu,  
The big cities of the land of Finland,  
Do you agree to that?  
And are you satisfied with us?  
I do not agree to that  
Nor am I satisfied with you.  
If I serve, I will serve still  
A rich man and the well-mannered.  

I was a slave, I tended the herd  
I was a Dutch servant,  
A shepherd boy from the land of souls,  
What was my wage for the dyke path?  
A virgin's bed of gold,  
Supply all the house tools,  
Do you agree to that?  
And are you satisfied with us?  
Yes, I agree to that  
And I am satisfied with you.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Loori-loori chant  
The chief bird, the little swallow  
Sang a song from the eaves, little bird  
The rich man killed a great bull  
I am the enemy, a crow  
Meat was eaten, meat was sold  
The hide went to Narva to the tanners  
The head went to the chief of Pärnu  
From the beak I got a blowhorn  
From the tuft I got a snow cap  
From the tail I got a woolen hat  
Then I went to the town to roam  
To Paide to steal ribbons  
The manor lords were baffled  
The town masters wondered  
Where has this man come from?  
He has come from afar  
He is a rich man from Riga  
Or is he the real lord of Pärnu  
Or is he the king of Courland  
I heard, I answered in turn:  
I am not a rich man from Riga  
Nor the real lord of Pärnu  
Nor the king of Courland  
I am a poor servant boy  
A spirit boy from the parish of Hallik

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Now beautiful Viru's water  
Lovely to sing on the shores  
Viru rolls the words  
Dear beloved to speak  
Dew carries the voice box  
The aal carries the voice to Harjumaa  
Dew carries the voice far away  
Harju women to bark  
Viru women to roll  
To follow the daughters of Järva.  
Harju woman to bite the crust  
Viru woman with a fierce back  
Western woman with a lame leg  
Do not eat my heart  
Drink the good voice box  
I am great to set the pot  
To spread widely on the table  
Easy to cook the kettle  
White to set on the platter  
To place the dish on the board.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Mari from Magas  
Among the bluebells  
Beneath the buttercups  
Between the meadowsweet  
The bluebells are seeding  
The buttercups are yellowing  
The meadowsweet is turning gray  
The grass weakens the young ones  
I weep for the young grass  
I was once a gentleman's boy  
A stick-bearer’s servant  
Now I am the servant’s slave  
Of the thirty dog boys  
The overseers had large runes  
Wide clearings, thick boats  
They wanted to work in the summer  
To cultivate the iron furrow  
To tend the golden comb  
If I had been able to work in the summer  
To tend the golden comb  
Then they would have beaten me with a whip  
Driven me по the dog rose  
Led me по the bull’s path.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
To wander the leg for an hour  
To stumble slightly on the leg  
I had a wide belt woven  
To twist it from coarse yarn  
To lay it from fine yarn  
To fasten my stomach  
To pull from the belly’s hardship.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
We had a damn blacksmith  
The bitter shit was a constable  
The thief was the parish leader  
This meadow was for spirits  
Timber men for whips  
Young men for blades  
The old gentleman for hell's tobacco  
Was from a crueler blacksmith  
Of a stronger parish leader  
Pulled together, pulled the constable  
Imprisoned the parish leader  
"Listen, blacksmith, darling  
Manor lord, little calf  
Constable, spotted little bird  
Let the sisters go to the evening  
The sisters are diligent in the morning  
Before dawn, the crows  
Before the rooster crows  
Before the second capercaillies  
Before the day’s swallows"

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Our groom, the golden one  
The manor lord, the lowland one  
You have surely felt the feeling  
You have surely guessed the time  
No need to discuss further  
Already has Virus eaten lunch  
Of course, from Alutaguse  
A thousand times from Turkey  
A hundred times from Germany  
Our servants without pride  
Geese always without a meal  
Blueberries without lunch  
Cuckoos without a morning

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
I myself am a little man  
A little man, very small  
A knee-high little boy  
Yet with a thousand tasks  
Not counting the official ones:  
At night I beat the manor's sheds  
By day I plowed the manor's land,  
Where I stood, the walls were wet  
Where I cried, the corners were wet  
Where I slept, the earth was muddy.  
Then a little pond appeared  
Grew into a fish pond  
Born from drops of tears  
Poured from the shores of water  
The village had enough fish to eat  
Even the poor of the parish were appeased  
The weak even helped.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
I myself am a little man  
A little man, very small  
A knee-high little boy  
Yet with a thousand tasks  
Not counting the official ones:  
At night I beat the manor's sheds  
By day I plowed the manor's land,  
Where I stood, the walls were wet  
Where I cried, the corners were wet  
Where I slept, the earth was muddy.  
Then a little pond appeared  
Grew into a fish pond  
Born from drops of tears  
Poured from the shores of water  
The village had enough fish to eat  
Even the poor of the parish were appeased  
The weak even helped.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
What are you scurrying for, little Siim  
Are you watching the crow, little Jaagu  
Are you guarding the pigsty, little Hans  
The garden is not yours to have  
Nor the valuable land  
Among the pigs it is yours to have  
In the flock your companion  
In the barn your little child.  
Among the pigs it is growing up  
In the flock it is growing  
In the barn the child is being taken care of  
At home they are waiting for me  
They are watching at the gate  
To see if the bringer of fire is coming  
Or the water carrier is rolling by  
The kitty is waiting for me at home  
Or maybe watching me at the gate  
The dog is waiting for me at home  
The cat is watching at the gate  
The dog is waiting for scraps  
The hen for fish heads  
The sheep for bread crusts.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Our captain, little gold one,  
Dainty, colorful little bird,  
Let the children go to lunch,  
Sisters to evening,  
Boys to mid-afternoon.  
Now it is children's lunchtime,  
Sisters' evening time,  
Boys' mid-afternoon time.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Is Kessi waiting for me at home?  
The dog is waiting for me at home.  
Is Kessi coaxing at home?  
The cat is coaxing at home.  
The dog is waiting with little barking dogs  
The cat with little bread crumbs.  
That slave is waiting at home.  
The slave is looking for work  
The channel is being tested for work  
If nothing else, to carry mud  
To gather firewood.  
A bit of these days  
Thinking of those times  
Even if the slave is praised  
As a son-in-law in jest.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Come together, young children  
Let's go see the manor  
What is understood in the manor  
In the manor, people get beaten  
Meat is soaked in bones  
Bloodletting is done  
"Dear hunchback, stop, hunchback  
Don't hit me, I'm short  
Don't beat me, I'm small  
Shoes worn out on the barn floor  
Brother's worn shoe soles  
Sister's sewn shoes.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, I, a bought slave,  
A bought slave, head-dressed with a wreath,  
A pledged servant,  
Who will buy me from slavery  
Who will free me from bondage  
But carries me away from the rain showers  
Redeems me from the snowfall  
A brotherly Russian  
A sailor, a lowly boy  
He will buy me from slavery  
He will free me from bondage  
Redeems me from the snowfall  
He will gather me under the spruce  
Hide me under a woolen blanket  
Under a striped cover.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh God, oh little God  
Throw down the ropes of scum,  
Push down the cords of tar  
Pull me into heaven  
Among the rich of others,  
Among the poor of others;  
I am not born among the great  
Nor fit into the house of the rich  
Nor to sit on a high ear.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Iitus-tiitus, a pregnant Hall,  
In our yard several stables,  
Seven Halls in each stable,  
Neighing and making fiends.  
They all wanted payments,  
Settlements on silver ridge,  
Yardings on golden comb,  
Tender caresses more delicate.  
I could not manage to pay them,  
Nor to settle on silver ridge,  
Nor to yard on golden comb,  
Nor to caress more delicately.  
Then they had to whip me with a whip,  
Pull me with a tarred rope.  
Then I, poor man, had to shed  
Socks in my mouth and rags under my arm,  
A skirt between my teeth.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The village calls me to visit,
To visit and eat,
To visit and drink.
Oh, how I understand, I immediately respond,
I would answer with a verse:
"For how long would I eat at the village,
Or why would I run to the village:
I eat the ox without eating,
I drink the barrel without drinking.
What is there to eat from the ox?
The bone is inside, the skin is on top —
A chunk of lean meat.
What is there to drink from the barrel?
The yeast is down, the foam is on top —
A drop of dark beer."

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, I, poor wife of a free man,  
A wretched little sauna woman!  
I pace around the mistress,  
I walk around the maid,  
I ask her for a little pot,  
I beg for the corner of a linen cloth.  
I have to carry the broth,  
I have to cook the beans,  
I have to walk on tiptoe,  
I have to scoop ashes with a ladle,  
I myself get beaten,  
The wretched little one is so scolded,  
I ask for a strip of the field,  
Where I may sow cabbage,  
But meanwhile I look around,  
To see how they dig for gold.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Now it is time for the court to go home,  
the right to go to the evening gathering;  
Now it is time to milk the cow,  
the calves' drinking time,  
the young noblemen's drinking time.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
If it were tomorrow,
The coming Friday,
Then I would know what I would do:
I would turn socks into trousers,
Gentlemen into oxen,
Caps into storks,
Kilts into wedges,
Young ladies into calves.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Our little golden cub,
A jaunty, colorful little bird,
Lets the children go to lunch,
The right time to the evening.
The children are diligent in the morning,
Early before dawn,
Before the sun has risen,
Before the dawn, they have gathered.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Before I was little,  
A knee-high boy,  
At night I beat the manor walls,  
By day I plowed the measured land,  
Where I stand the walls are wet,  
Where I cry the corners are wet,  
A small pond dripped water,  
A fish trap grew.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Before I was little,  
A knee-high boy,  
At night I beat the manor walls,  
By day I plowed the measured land,  
Where I stand the walls are wet,  
Where I cry the corners are wet,  
A small pond dripped water,  
A fish trap grew.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The village calls me to visit  
They eat in the village, they drink in the village  
When I understand, I immediately reply  
Soon I will respond to them  
How many villages would I eat at  
Or at which village would I run  
I eat the ox altogether  
And drink beer from the barrel altogether  
What is there to eat from that ox  
Or to drink from that beer barrel  
The ox has skin on  
Skin on, bone underneath  
A chunk of beef  
Or to drink from that beer barrel  
Yeast settles, foam stays on top  
A drop of dark beer.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I was diligent, I tended the flock,  
I was Hollard's servant,  
Lieberland's shepherd boy.  
What was I paid?  
A sheep with two lambs.  
I did not agree to that,  
I was not satisfied with that lamb.  
I was diligent, I tended the flock,  
I was Hollard's servant,  
Lieberland's shepherd boy.  
What was I paid?  
A horse colt.  
I did not agree to that,  
I was not satisfied with that colt.  
I was diligent, I tended the flock,  
I was Hollard's servant,  
Lieberland's shepherd boy.  
What was I paid?  
A fur coat and a six-year-old (horse).  
I did not agree to that,  
I was not satisfied with the six-year-old.  
I was diligent, I tended the flock,  
I was Hollard's servant,  
Lieberland's shepherd boy.  
What was I paid?  
A pig with seven piglets.  
I did not agree to that,  
I was not satisfied with the piglets.  
I was diligent, I tended the flock,  
I was Hollard's servant,  
Lieberland's shepherd boy.  
What was I paid?  
The landowner's daughter with a farm,  
With that I am satisfied.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
If I were alive,
If I were doing something,
I would surely know what I would do:
I would put the room out in the wind,
The sauna by the island’s edge,
The house on the hillside.
Who rolled the bark?
The water rolled the bark.
Who covered the walls with moss?
The rain covered the walls with moss.
Who made the new doors,
The mist made the new doors.
They come into the room and ask us:
“Do you have a town, do you have a flag?
We have Finnish salt ships,
We have Riga money ships.
No, we don’t have a town, because we have no flag.
We have a famous little place
On the hillside of the house,
By the island’s edge of the sauna,
The room is there in the wind.
If I were alive,
If I were doing something:
I would make the Germans digging,
The master before the oxen,
The ladies behind like cranes.
Then the master would shout ai-ai-ai.
I would make a rach-rachi-rach behind!

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
If I were alive,
If I were doing something,
I would surely know what I would do:
I would put the room out in the wind,
The sauna by the island’s edge,
The house on the hillside.
Who rolled the bark?
The water rolled the bark.
Who covered the walls with moss?
The rain covered the walls with moss.
Who made the new doors,
The mist made the new doors.
They come into the room and ask us:
“Do you have a town, do you have a flag?
We have Finnish salt ships,
We have Riga money ships.
No, we don’t have a town, because we have no flag.
We have a famous little place
On the hillside of the house,
By the island’s edge of the sauna,
The room is there in the wind.
If I were alive,
If I were doing something:
I would make the Germans digging,
The master before the oxen,
The ladies behind like cranes.
Then the master would shout ai-ai-ai.
I would make a rach-rachi-rach behind!

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Master, little master,  
Mistress, little mistress:  
Pay the servant well,  
Reward the poor child's toil.  
If you do not pay in this world,  
Surely you will pay in heaven,  
You will pay before Mary.  
Surely you will walk to the stocks,  
March to the whipping post,  
The fabric to the broad loom.  
Come, then, pay the servant wages,  
The servant’s summer wages,  
A day’s full day’s wages.  
Then I understand, I reply:  
I do not want to go to heaven  
If I was not paid in that world,  
Where the breastbone was broken,  
The neckbone snapped,  
And many other bones broken.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, that poor Väätse parish:
The parish is sluggish ahead,
Humpbacked like the devil from behind.
Oh, that good Kerna parish:
The parish is strong as copper ahead,
Humpbacked golden from behind.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Hello, dear gentleman,  
Lady of the Golden Crown,  
Rise up onto the chair,  
Step onto the window frame,  
Look upon the poor parish,  
Upon the wicked deanery,  
How the parish is tormented,  
The little ones are punished,  
The bigger ones are killed,  
The deanery is harassed.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
It would be my being,  
for another my doing.  
If only I knew what I would do,  
I would turn gentlemen into oxen,  
young ladies into headscarves,  
old ladies into plough handles,  
myself at the back calling out:  
"Plow the furrow, pour the seed,  
A'a furrow the old red one,  
lift the earth a little,  
turn the earth a little."

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Master, little master,  
Mistress, little lady,  
Pay well the servant’s wage,  
Compensate the poor child’s toil.  
If you do not pay in this world,  
Then you will pay in heaven,  
(You pay before Mary)  
You will be at the mercy of the elbow tree,  
You will march to the shoulder of the whip tree,  
The cloth to wide tension.  

"Come now, servant, take your pay,  
Servant—winter pay,  
Day laborer—daily wage."  

Then I understand, I answer:  
"I do not want to go to heaven,  
If I was not paid in that world,  
Where the ribs were broken,  
Where the neck bones were fractured,  
And many other bones were broken too."

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, that poor Väätsa parish:  
the parish is miserable,  
hunched like the devil afterwards.  
Oh, that good Kerna  
parish is like a copper entity,  
hunched golden afterwards.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Mother, dear little mother,  
stoke the stove with kitchen herbs,  
honor will be bound to Virve.  
I am coming, Virve, to whisk the birch branches,  
I am coming, goose, to break twigs,  
swallow, to wash your head.  
I have on my back whip blows,  
on my neck rod blows,  
wooden rod blows under my skirt.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
If it were for my being,  
for my doing,  
I would know what I would do.  
I would make the skirts into petticoats,  
the carts into wagons,  
the huts into cranes,  
the ditches into wedges.  
Then the plow would come to the village,  
furrow breaking the fields.  
I myself am muddy to the crown,  
my body wet to the clock.
