I beat the threshing floor, that I forget  
I sing a song, I worry about something else.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The hostess deceived me  
She put water into the milk  
Hagan mixed bread in the granary  
The meadow made me plow the field  
But I deceive the hostess  
I leave the stubble to another  
We let the plow share go  
The sled can slide to the clay.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Our steward, dear golden one  
Kilter Ella, dear brother  
Helper, fair one  
Let the children go to lunch  
To the nice midday meal  
To the sisters in the evening  
Our servants without rank  
Ellas without a meal  
The boss goes to lunch  
Turns to the midday meal  
To eat, dear heart  
To drink, dear throat  
I wait for the food to be brought  
To run after the drink  
To carry after the fish  
To send the bread  
I received news from Tuli Tuul  
That the food bringer has died  
The fish carrier is gone  
The bread sender has left  
If the steward is dead  
If the messenger is gone  
The socks stay in the grave  
The shoes went with Kersto  
The skirt stayed on another woman  
The belt stayed on another woman’s waist.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, this good Aavere parish  
Went to town with a bit of liquor  
The old gentleman with vodka.  
Hunchbacked like the moon in front  
Kilter danced in the middle  
Young man like God behind.  
Oh, this bad Kuie parish  
Went to town with a bit of liquor  
The old gentleman with vodka:  
Hunchbacked like the devil in front  
Kilter [a term for a rogue or trickster] in the middle  
Young man like Judas behind.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Our trooper little gold,
Helper honest one,
Quirky speckled little bird.
You start to guess,
To feel the hours.
That in Harju it has long been noon,
The virus last eaten.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I was the one who acted, I broke the planks,  
I did not break the ox’s horns,  
The sturdy horses are down. -

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Is the cat waiting for me at home?  
The dog is waiting for me at home.  
Is the cat guarding my house?  
The cat is guarding my house,  
The dog is waiting for crusts,  
The cat for bread crumbs.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
What era in hell it is,  
That era in Liigvala,  
Liigvala the captive land,  
That land the Siberian land,  
With the whip the threshing is done,  
With the bagpipe the wind is played,  
With the whip the grain is carried,  
On the kantik the bars are rung.  
Oh, that good Hallikuda  
Or that famous Kuksimäe,  
Mesista beyond the forest,  
With the fiddle the threshing is done,  
With the bagpipe the wind is played,  
With the violin the grain was carried,  
On the kannel the bars are rung.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh my noble sir  
Lady with a golden crown  
Young gentlemen with silver buttons  
Old gentlemen with brass buttons  
Rise up onto the chair  
Get up into the hall  
Step onto the window frame  
Look upon the poor parish  
How this parish is afflicted  
Some are killed more  
Some are afflicted less  
Some are tormented the least  
Every Friday they are scourged  
Every Saturday they are lashed  
Blood is shed in the procession

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I never walk on the field  
I never linger in the manor  
Under the spear of the five-tined harrow  
Under the stick of the six-pronged fork  
When it cuts along the side  
Along the side, against the edge  
Across the broad shoulder  
It strikes so that the bone is visible  
Down to the skin’s edge  
For the sake of the blood to the toes. -

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Our strong little gold one  
A colorful little bird  
Let the children have lunch  
At the right time in the evening  
Others eat for the second time  
The householders for the third time  
Us without a meal  
Without cutting the fish  
Our salmon unbroken. -

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Why Väetsa became poor,
Kurjavere disappeared,
Because there was a manor in the village,
A parasite in the parish,
He made the village pay tenfold.
He put a barrel on the girl,
A vat on the old women,
Half a vat on the boy,
The pig had seven piglets,
They were taken to the German as pork,
The sheep brought a lamb,
It was taken to the German's table,
Because Väetsa became poor,
Kurjavere disappeared.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Let the rain roll down from the sky,  
Let the storm come from the heavens:  
Still I must go,  
Still I must not be a slave,  
Still I must not wander in poverty.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, we poor manor spirits  
The second ones are poor servants  
The third ones are regular workers  
The fourth are assistant spirits  
The fifth are the liquor men.  
They were driven to howl at night  
To march along the roads  
To walk through the wilderness  
The pain of the night, the pain of the moon  
The pain of God's fire  
We struck the pain of the night  
We struck the pile of the moon's pain  
The pain of God's fire.  
Golden rakes in hand  
Sharp sickle shafts.  
When then shall I leave the manor  
Leave the manor, leave for hell  
Shall I leave from your mouth  
Between the jaws of the lion  
Between the pipes of the monkey.  
I cannot turn away  
With a load of dogwood, not turn my head  
Before the home gate  
Before father's granary door  
On brother's chamber threshold.  
Then I look over the edge  
From the worse side.  
See how hell’s place shines  
Evil flames blaze  
The windows of hell are burning.  
The towers of ghosts stand tall.  
Life seems to smile. -  
There are large German estates  
Bottomless lady’s fields  
There we will melt our feathers  
There we will catch our fish  
True fellow servants will wander  
Money pitchers will break  
On that large estate  
On the bottomless field  
On the cornered meadow  
Now I say again  
To sing the Danish song again  
I turn words upside down.  
There the Germans perish on their backs  
May the lady’s neck break  
May the lady’s hip bones crumble  
May the maiden’s ribs be dislocated  
May our backs stay whole  
May our scythes be quick  
May our necks be strong. -

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Perhaps the father is wealthy,
The mother wrapped in silk,
The brother kept as a darling,
The sister with a hundred coins,
But if he were to die,
The land would remain the wealthy father,
The mother wrapped in silk,
The brother kept as a darling,
The sister with a hundred coins.
No riches are laid in money,
No king’s gold is buried.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Where is it as it is for us  
When it is at our manor's estate  
The whip cracks, the fiddle plays. -  
The fiddle is round  
The whip crackles like rowan  
The stick beats like an oak.  
The stick moans like a maple.  
It strikes the girl along the way  
Along the young maiden's skin  
Along the sister's shoulders.  
The whip cracks against the waist  
It snaps against the maiden's skin,  
From the sorrow then comes crying  
From the bitterness then comes screaming,  
Blood flows out  
Then ran home quickly  
Playing to the father's farmhouse  
Give mercy, old lady  
Give mercy, old man.  
You cannot give young daughters  
You cannot give mercy to yourself.  
You must be a serf at the manor  
And pray to the Lord of Heaven.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The Hellish Mistress  
Boiled shoe lye for me  
Put the stove to burn  
To the chimney to boil  
Under the fire to simmer  
Shields wash their behinds  
Pigs washed the pigsty  
The servant ate a large amount  
The mottled one stays long  
I, poor, around the bean  
The mistress turned her neck  
Tied a string to the dog's neck  
To the other end, to another dog's neck  
Made me hole out in the yard  
To watch the gate.  
A hole dog on two legs,  
Milk the girl with a scabbed paw.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The evening comes, dusk arrives  
The low sun lies beside the day  
Evening brings happiness along  
Whose hen am I  
Whose wings am I, titmouse  
Whose shoulder am I, little apple  
Evening brings straw to the hut  
Twilight leads to sleep  
Dawn wakes us up  
Scatters us around  
To some manor, to some home  
I linger on the manor’s side  
I howl on the wilderness side  
I swing on the cradle’s edge  
I sway on the smith’s bench  
I went home howling  
I heard the maiden crying  
Entreating and imploring  
I asked the maiden  
Why do you cry, little maiden  
The maiden hears and replies  
Why do you cry, young daughter  
I fear deceiving little shirts  
Burial mantle underlays  
Covering furred coats  
I heard and responded  
Answering the colt  
Not me, not me, little maiden  
Don’t deceive me, little shirts  
Burial mantle underlays  
Covering furred coats  
The earth will hide me with little sand  
And the soil will bury me black  
Covering with furred church  
Concealing white little tower.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The evening comes, dusk arrives  
The low sun lies beside the day  
Evening brings happiness along  
Whose hen am I  
Whose wings am I, titmouse  
Whose shoulder am I, little apple  
Evening brings straw to the hut  
Twilight leads to sleep  
Dawn wakes us up  
Scatters us around  
To some manor, to some home  
I linger on the manor’s side  
I howl on the wilderness side  
I swing on the cradle’s edge  
I sway on the smith’s bench  
I went home howling  
I heard the maiden crying  
Entreating and imploring  
I asked the maiden  
Why do you cry, little maiden  
The maiden hears and replies  
Why do you cry, young daughter  
I fear deceiving little shirts  
Burial mantle underlays  
Covering furred coats  
I heard and responded  
Answering the colt  
Not me, not me, little maiden  
Don’t deceive me, little shirts  
Burial mantle underlays  
Covering furred coats  
The earth will hide me with little sand  
And the soil will bury me black  
Covering with furred church  
Concealing white little tower.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
God be born, great Saxons,  
Let the hungry lords be seen:  
Who were sent to wander at night,  
To walk in the moonlight,  
The nights are long and dark,  
The roads are black and muddy.  
The nights are indeed long, I thresh the threshing floors,  
By day I cut the sheaves.  
At night the body is alive,  
By day, the back is beaten.  
Some complain of the sad day,  
Some lament the evening.  
I lament at home,  
Sensing pain in my bed,  
My father helps me sigh,  
My mother cares with love.  
Where can I complain, where grumble!  
Before me all are noble lords,  
Around me all are hungry scoundrels;  
But I, a poor spirit child,  
A small walking boy.  
I have no court anywhere,  
No righteous judges—  
But I must suffer on my knees,  
Sleep on my calves.  
I would cry big lakes,  
Perhaps deep rivers from my eyes!  
No one hears the lament,  
Nor the cries of distress!  
Grandfather is merciful,  
Taarataat, a wise man:  
Repay the proud lord,  
Repay the cruel bailiff,  
The tormentor, the tormentor.  
Repay before nightfall,  
Before going to the grave.  
In the earth, change is born;  
In the earth, no crushing is seen,  
In the grave no mercy is seen,  
Where could one find true testimony,  
Where could Taarataat be known  
And give justice.  
The lord is skilled in words,  
The bailiff evil in arguing.  
But I, a poor servant child,  
A small lost boy.  
I have no pleasant thought,  
No skillful word.  
For I remain without justice,  
Until then sighing,  
Lamenting with love:  
Until Kalev comes home  
To see his tired brother,  
But Kalev breaks the iron,  
While I break my chest straps,  
My back blue from stripes.  
He wears a shield on his neck,  
Green swords on his hips.  
Why do the tormentors talk nonsense,  
The long tormentors flee.  
"Then life will be ours in the front,  
We will live on Taaramäe  
In service to the steel."

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Master is the little master,
Mistress is the little mistress,
Give the servant good food.
The servant feeds the ox,
The ox takes the straw to the field barn.
The field will become strong to the bottom,
The barn will suffice for a while.
How many barns are under the yard?
There were three barns under the yard!
One waters the wheat,
The second threshes the rye,
The third rakes the barley.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Our little golden chief,  
Lanky, sharp little brother,  
Look out for the day for a moment.  
The day had long since gone down.  
Let us into the evening of Saturday.  
Already the moon is in front of the hut,  
Already the sieve stands on its edge. -

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Now the eating days are over  
Now the drinking days are over  
We ate the bull in the eating place  
We drank the barrel in the drinking place  
What could be drunk from that beer  
The yeast settled down, the foam was on top  
Beer itself for the mid-tongue  
What could be eaten from that bull  
What was left behind  
The tail remained and the horns remain  
The horns remained as new ornaments  
The tail remained for the sheath to burst

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh my dear young gentleman  
Young men with silver buttons  
Young ladies with silver lace  
Stand up on the chair  
Look at the poor parish  
How this parish is troubled  
The parish is being harassed.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I don’t care even because of this  
That a widow laughed at me  
The girl from Teuta was shaky  
Cursed with foul words  
Keeper of straw benches  
Stacker of straw roofs

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Little Sisters, Little Birds  
Where are we going, the six of us?  
Where will we swim that crowd?  
Where will we place that flock?  
In front is the long peat forest  
Behind is the wide plank forest  
In the middle is the beautiful spruce forest  
Sometimes the old deciduous forest.  

Let’s go over five fields  
Let’s go over four meadows  
There sits a lovely room  
And a white wall watches from the chamber  
And a white wall watches from the chamber  
The wind sweeps through the rooms.  

There were many Saxons there  
They laid down in the beds  
Placed in a pot to sleep  
Still, I managed to come secretly  
I gave the door some oil  
And sent some beer to the forks  
So that the new doors wouldn’t creak.  

The creaking forks shouted  
I set off on the road to walk,  
What met me on the road?  
Old Valga Saxons came against me  
Old gentlemen of Valga came against me  
In front of them were iron fences  
Behind iron shackles  
Among them the old hares  
In the middle the beautiful foxes.  

I took a twig to strike my dog  
To shake the harness straps  
I took a long rowan twig  
And a nice little stick.  

In front of me I struck wild dogs  
Behind iron shackles  
In the middle the beautiful foxes  
Between them the old foxes.  

I myself ran homeward  
I was not allowed to sleep indoors  
That night I was in the barn  
Two nights in the oat field.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I was a slave, I herded sheep,  
I was a servant in Holland,  
A lamb's shepherd in Leerland.  
Holland made me a new coat,  
Leerland a wide jacket  
Made of flags and patches,  
Colorful goat skins. -  
On Monday I ate herrings,  
On Tuesday I ate smelts,  
On Wednesday good pikes,  
On Thursday large perches,  
On Friday Riga flounders,  
On Saturday broad breams,  
On Sunday pike-perches.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The man was angry with me,
The woman of the house was loud with me;
They made me plow early in the season,
But early I was made to take the yoke,
The head of the plow, however, was the cooking head.
By dawn I plowed three furrows,
By sunrise a long field,
By noon I spread out a wide field:
I wait for food to be brought
Carried after the plow.
What food have I produced
That is carried after the plow?
Dead nettles, burdock leaves,
Old cursed nettles,
Sprat shins, small seeds,
Moss those more suitable thighs.
Those are the foods I have produced
Carried after the plow.
Then I swore in anger
With a big mouth I scolded...
Plow the head, step into the furrow,
Make the old red furrow straight!

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I do not serve a rich man  
A rich man and a wise man.  
What will be my wage, the road toll?  
A horse with all its foals.  
I did not take, I did not want to.  
I do not serve a rich man.  
A rich man and a wise man.  
What will be my wage, the road toll?  
A cow with all its calves.  
I did not take, I did not want to.  
I do not serve a rich man.  
A rich man and a wise man.  
What will be my wage, the road toll?  
A pig with seven piglets.  
I did not take, I did not want to.  
I do not serve a rich man,  
A rich man and a wise man.  
What will be my wage, the road toll?  
A hen with eight chicks.  
I did not take, I did not want to.  
I do not serve a rich man,  
A rich man and a wise man.  
What will be my wage, the road toll?  
The village’s fair-haired girl.  
This is what my thighs rejoice in,  
This is what my customs love.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh our honorable sir  
Lady of the golden crown  
Young gentlemen with silver buttons  
Let us rise onto the chair  
Let us step onto the windowsill  
Let us look upon the poor parish  
How this parish is tormented  
The smaller ones are persecuted  
The bigger ones are put to death  
Two are pillars to the manor  
Three are rings on the pillar  
Where women are taken away  
Little boys are sold  
The beauty of the golden ones was chased away.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Is a cat waiting for me at home?  
A dog is waiting for me at home.  
The cat is coaxed inside the house.  
The dog is waiting for crumbs,  
The cat for crumbs of bread.  
It waits for the servant to come home.  
Work is sought for the servant.  
The henhouse is called to work.  
If nothing else, to carry mud.  
To gather manure...  
When the servant is given an end  
To be put on the head of a day laborer.  
To be taken a little during the day,  
To think about a maiden during the day,  
If only the servant is praised.  
A daughter-in-law never.  
The son's wife is always joking.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
If only my existence  
Were someone else’s doing  
I would make gentlemen into oxen  
Ladies into sticks  
Spades into wedges  
Hoods into cranes  
Brooms into shovels.  
Now the gentleman goes ai-ai-aaa  
Behind me there’s trai-rai-raaa

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Here, our masters,  
Lady of the golden crown,  
Young scissors with silver knobs.  
Let us rise onto the chair,  
Step onto the window frame,  
Look upon the poor parish:  
How this parish is tormented,  
The smaller ones are tortured,  
The greater ones are killed.  
There are two pillars in the manor,  
Three are the rings on the pillar:  
Where those women are snatched,  
The boys are pawned,  
The daughters' beauty is taken away. -

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
The others went on Monday  
I will go after on Tuesday.  
Oh, then I have oxen unshod,  
Oxen unshod, oxen unshod.  
I shod the oxen, but shod them wrong,  
I knocked on the crane's beak,  
Then I go outside the manor to the beak.  
There others in the mid-morning  
Say one, say two  
And start to count the third;  
There came a cooper from the manor side,  
From the northern stone yard side,  
The old master from the parish side.  
I go to the cooper's request:  
Dear cooper, good cooper!  
Measure for me a small piece,  
Please.  
The cooper took the measure  
Measured a big piece for me,  
A big piece, a wide piece,  
A big piece for summer,  
A wide piece for spring,  
A little mound from the edge,  
A gravelly, rocky bit.  
I plow all day,  
Autumn's fodder,  
Long spring day;  
All  
I bent the oxen's horns crooked.  
The cooper shouts: Work on, Jüri,  
"Don't you know, master,  
What about the poor ox boy!?  
I have a piece of bread in my pouch,  
Two fishes in the box.  
There came a cooper from the manor side,  
From the northern stone yard side,  
The old master from the parish side;  
A bundle was in the stick pile,  
A bunch was of red willow twigs;  
I stare along the eyes,  
Along the eyes, wide ears:  
Who else but me.  
I go to the cooper's request:  
Dear cooper, good cooper!  
Don't be too harsh,  
Don't show too much anger, -  
I became a poor child early,  
Early for a stranger's servant,  
Early left to strangers' care:  
You hear now coming home,  
Outside  
Behind, singing a lament -  
Then: - "Up Elts, jump Kaie,  
Turn the turn at the gates,  
Close the doors with rings:  
Hear where the wicked bird falls,  
Waves the evil bird!  
Hear, there comes to the village a feeder,  
A feeder to the village, a drinker to the village,  
Placing hay at the place."  
My girl was a little old woman,  
My stove was a little heap, -  
She heard him coming home,  
Outside  
Behind singing a lament:  
"Hear where the golden bird falls,  
Waves the aunt's bird!  
He comes tired from work,  
Comes from outside  
Oh! Work has tired him,  
The field has worn him out."

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
The others went on Monday  
I will go after on Tuesday.  
Oh, then I have oxen unshod,  
Oxen unshod, oxen unshod.  
I shod the oxen, but shod them wrong,  
I knocked on the crane's beak,  
Then I go outside the manor to the beak.  
There others in the mid-morning  
Say one, say two  
And start to count the third;  
There came a cooper from the manor side,  
From the northern stone yard side,  
The old master from the parish side.  
I go to the cooper's request:  
Dear cooper, good cooper!  
Measure for me a small piece,  
Please.  
The cooper took the measure  
Measured a big piece for me,  
A big piece, a wide piece,  
A big piece for summer,  
A wide piece for spring,  
A little mound from the edge,  
A gravelly, rocky bit.  
I plow all day,  
Autumn's fodder,  
Long spring day;  
All  
I bent the oxen's horns crooked.  
The cooper shouts: Work on, Jüri,  
"Don't you know, master,  
What about the poor ox boy!?  
I have a piece of bread in my pouch,  
Two fishes in the box.  
There came a cooper from the manor side,  
From the northern stone yard side,  
The old master from the parish side;  
A bundle was in the stick pile,  
A bunch was of red willow twigs;  
I stare along the eyes,  
Along the eyes, wide ears:  
Who else but me.  
I go to the cooper's request:  
Dear cooper, good cooper!  
Don't be too harsh,  
Don't show too much anger, -  
I became a poor child early,  
Early for a stranger's servant,  
Early left to strangers' care:  
You hear now coming home,  
Outside  
Behind, singing a lament -  
Then: - "Up Elts, jump Kaie,  
Turn the turn at the gates,  
Close the doors with rings:  
Hear where the wicked bird falls,  
Waves the evil bird!  
Hear, there comes to the village a feeder,  
A feeder to the village, a drinker to the village,  
Placing hay at the place."  
My girl was a little old woman,  
My stove was a little heap, -  
She heard him coming home,  
Outside  
Behind singing a lament:  
"Hear where the golden bird falls,  
Waves the aunt's bird!  
He comes tired from work,  
Comes from outside  
Oh! Work has tired him,  
The field has worn him out."

label: Laulud laulust  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
If it were my being,  
And secondly my doing,  
I would surely know what I would know:  
I would make the oxen into oxen,  
The horses into horses,  
The helpers into fathers,  
The boys into proud cranes,  
The girls into blossoms...  
I myself would be in the field,  
Plowing the furrow in the meadow,  
Letting the songs ring out,  
And the words be lovely to hear.  
Then the village would listen,  
The parish would watch closely,  
The forest would roar back,  
The grove would respond,  
Young women would smile,  
Men would ponder their thoughts,  
Children would quietly spy,  
Squatting by the village gate,  
Near the backyard of the household.  
Oh! If only it were my being,  
And secondly my doing;  
Surely I would know what I would know.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The Mistress, a bad woman  
Woke me up early,  
To do the household chores.  
I answered, begging:  
- Please, my mistress,  
Delicate mistress!  
Let me laze a little,  
Doze by the door of sleep,  
Stretch my back:  
My sides are burning from plowing,  
My palms ache from the harvest.  
The mistress scorned my plea.  
Kept sending me to the chores.  
When I cunningly understood,  
The lady of the house’s intentions,  
I set a trap under the door latch,  
Laid ribbons on the floor.  
The baking pan got caught in the ribbons.  
The mistress came to beg,  
To soften her mood:  
"Good boy, dear boy,  
Undo the ribbons from my pan,  
I’ll let you lie down till noon,  
I’ll give you beer to drink."  
The fair maiden,  
The household daughter,  
When she went to feel the latch,  
It caught her chin.  
She begged fiercely,  
Trying to coax me:  
"Good boy, dear boy,  
Release my chin from the latch,  
Undo the ribbons:  
I’ll be a better servant below the stove,  
I’ll bring you softer blankets,  
I’ll obey, I’ll hold you in my arms."

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh, we poor chimney sweeps  
When we get ourselves wet  
Where do we dry ourselves  
Down there in the barn sauna  
There we dry ourselves  
What then in the oven chamber  
The blacksmith's legs, the miller's thighs  
The farmhand's shoulders both  
The barn priest's thigh bones  
There we then dry them in the oven  
There we dry ourselves indeed

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Don't kill the gentleman  
Don't kill the lady  
I got a little lamb  
What I fed her was  
Sheep's marrow and rooster gizzard  
Grandma's broth and swan meat  
This is my poor possession

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I was a slave, I tended the flock  
I was a servant of the Hoolender  
A shepherd boy of Liiderland  
The Hoolender made a new jacket  
A wide coat of Liiderland  
Made from flags and patches  
And from doghide legs

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
If it were tomorrow  
If it were next Friday  
I would know what I would do  
I would put boots on my feet  
Gentlemen in front as horses  
Wedges inside the saddlebags  
The young lady behind as a swan.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The hedgehog came from the manor,  
The weevil from the new thigh,  
Gathered sticks in a bunch,  
A baton shaft in the bundle,  
Brought me a measuring tape.  
Measured me a large piece,  
A bog but hollow inside,  
Under the mountain, a clump of moss,  
I plow and worry myself,  
I watch the stake myself,  
Plowing hills into furrows,  
Clumps between the furrows.  
The hedgehog came to plow the furrow,  
Found hills inside the furrow,  
Clumps between the furrows.  
They beat my blue sail,  
My sides scraped and scratched,  
The backside pure filth.  
Wait, wait, little hedgehog!  
Let Saturday evening come,  
The hedgehog will come to the village!  
Saturday evening came,  
And a message followed:  
"Come, come, young men!"  
And the hedgehog came to the village.  
I took rowan branch from the heap,  
Juniper from the cart,  
Bird cherry from behind the house.  
Juniper walked along the road,  
Bird cherry moved past the back.  
Then I escaped from the hedgehog’s grip,  
At last it was over!  
What became of that hedgehog’s body?  
It ended up as the devil’s nutshell!

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
There are those Harju ridges,  
Near the city of the wealthy mother,  
They sit on Irumäe,  
Pipes smoking in their mouths,  
Mosses on the wall inside,  
Waiting for a mare’s urine  
To be thrown into the water.  
Virumaa has five or six,  
Harjumaa has seven or eight ridges.  
Those will be over the sedge,  
I will put a ribbon on one,  
Threw it at a margapuu (a kind of fence),  
Then there will be a Järva boy alone,  
Still a pig boy,  
Alone a Järva herdsman.  
A man from Virumaa was a grain bull,  
A man from Harjumaa was a chaff bull,  
A little bull boy from Järva.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Rice, I spoil you!
God, that Lord
“In the name of God, the Son,
and the Holy Spirit.”
Shit into the swamp, wicked into the bush;
my child healthy

label: Loitsud
translation:
Jesus was on top of a high mountain;  
Three roses in hand!  
Three roses in hand!  
He strikes with one, heals with the second.  
With the third, he takes away completely!  
With the third, he takes away completely!

label: Loitsud
translation:
Saint George's little boy!  
Let this flock go far away.  
Drive the sheep to the meadow  
I myself will lie down to rest!  
I will lay my head on a stone;  
My feet on the top of a alder.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Snake under the black peat  
under the gray gardens  
under the twisting stone  
crawling through the waters.  
But you, root of the birch tree  
eat the core of the willow  
pluck at the branches of the birch.  
Do not eat my core  
do not drink my juice  
do not trouble the little spirit.  
Eat the moss from the swamp's edge  
the tuft of grass from the rotten lake  
the fish's bread from the fish lake.

label: Loitsud
translation:
The rice has come from Riga Land,  
To eat meat, to suck blood.  
Go where I lose,  
Go where I send.  
Where those mothers give birth to sons,  
Where tar stumps sprout leaves.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Oh, snake, little snake!  
Why do you secretly bite me,  
why do you sting me in the dark,  
pinching without being seen.  
Fly to the alder, jump to the birch.  
Gnaw on tree roots,  
on the small branches of the meadow willow.  
Rustle the hornbeam,  
gnaw, nibble on the lime trees.  
Wrap yourself in the moss,  
tangle in the swamp's moss.  
Do not pinch the maidens,  
do not tease the women,  
stay away and do not touch,  
let the men be forgotten,  
leave the children alone!  
The snake has red lips,  
the lizard has black eyes.  
How many hairs on the mite;  
on the mite, on the moss mite,  
swamp hair, blue hair;  
earth hair, moss hair,  
time hair a bit gray,  
water hair a little bit.  
Hornbeam with hundreds of hairs.  
Some have a black uniform,  
some blaze golden hair.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Disease to the magpie  
Pain to the jackdaw  
Other sickness to the black bird.  
Let the disease come, let another go  
May there be no change in the rook.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Clay soil, clay subsoil  
Willow soil, willow subsoil  
Black snake, common snake  
Hornbeam sandy snake  
Why do you secretly carve me  
Stealing unseen  
To go to the hollow in the hill  
To wrap around the hummock.

label: Loitsud
translation:
The forest wolf, the forest old man  
The beautiful master of the forest  
The forest's hairy coat  
If you hear me on the ice  
And on the ice of my dear herd  
Put your head into the hollow  
Your nose into the moss  
Your teeth into the hay  
Look for my sheepfold elsewhere  
Try my pastures elsewhere  
Break my heifers elsewhere.

label: Loitsud
translation:
I have already grown old  
No longer an ear hears  
No eye explains  
No tooth starts  
No foot reaches anymore.  
If a hunter came and shot me down  
I have done much wrong  
Sixty shovels of feet  
A hundred horned heads  
A thousand tufted heads  
Fifty bow noses  
The lad shoots bam-bam-poum!  
Dear Jesus, how sudden that was.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Earth, earth, keep the snake’s head down,  
until the alder is soaked.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Snake of black underground  
Tone, hairy breed  
Hair of earth, black hair  
Hair of water, hair of rain  
Hazel, hazel hair  
Precious dew, hay hair  
Weaving without tree roots  
Crunching birch bark  
Bee, milk bird  
Brings butter to melt in the mouth  
On the tongue, bitter grass  
That I can blend with butter  
We heal the swelling  
We humble the sick mother.

label: Loitsud
translation:
A few flashes of bone tooth  
give me an iron tooth.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Come on, damn, hurry damn,
Over nine churches
Over eight chapels,
Bring me wool on the end of a switch,
Tin pieces on the ass.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Little snake, little crazy one,  
Little fashionista, little snake,  
Why did you bite me,  
Secretly pinch me?  
Let it go to Maarja Hill,  
There let it twist around,  
Stretch the blood vessels.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Disease to the magpie,  
pain to the crow,  
other ailments to the blackbird,  
bitterness to the finch.  
Our child's hand  
before that of a young woman.

label: Loitsud
translation:
At the altar, pure wood,  
gnaw on finger wood,  
bite the aspen,  
eat the marsh moss,  
do not pinch the woman  
nor strike the girl!

label: Loitsud
translation:
Eliting red  
eliting - blue,  
eliting - yellow,  
eliting - green,  
eliting - black,  
eliting - white,  
eliting - multicolored,  
eliting - gray  
eliting - sky-colored.

label: Loitsud
translation:
"Sprat, bone horse,  
give me an iron horse!"

label: Loitsud
translation:
Shad, quick, bonefish,  
give me an ironfish!

label: Loitsud
translation:
Sprat, give me a bone,  
Give me an iron bone!

label: Loitsud
translation:
Sprat, give me a bone,
give me an iron bone!

label: Loitsud
translation: Sprat, give me an iron nail, I will give you a bone nail.

label: Loitsud
translation: Herring, come on, I'll give you a bone tooth, give me an iron tooth.

label: Loitsud
translation:
To the eagle, illness,  
to the raven, pain,  
to the blackbird, another disease!  
Let one illness come, let another go,  
may there be no change for the better,  
a pig’s ass surface –  
may my daughter’s hand be healed!

label: Loitsud
translation: Tuluk, give me the other sheep...

label: Loitsud
translation: Sprat, come on, bone tooth, give me an iron tooth!

label: Loitsud
translation: Sprat, swoosh, old ewe, give me a new ewe.

label: Loitsud
translation: Smelt, smelt, give me a ling, I will give you a codling.

label: Loitsud
translation: Kilk, come on bone, give me an iron bone!

label: Loitsud
translation: Kilk, quick bone, give me an iron tooth!..

label: Loitsud
translation:
Saki, saki, little spear,
Saki, like a spear!
To whip with a wolf's tail,
to beat with a fox's tail.
May the stove tenders be healthy,
the trench makers, the water carriers,
and the trench walkers too!
They twisted these stones,
carved the cornerstones,
and put the rocks in place!

label: Loitsud
translation:
To the oak’s bitterness,  
to the crow’s pain,  
to the tit’s bitterness,  
to the blackbird’s other illness—  
our child’s... (hand) healthy again!

label: Loitsud
translation:
Quick pain, sharp pain,  
a pain in the crow,  
another disease in the black bird,  
bitterness in the speckled bird,  
may our ?? child’s stomach be healthy!  
Healthy! Healthy! Speckled cow,  
you have received salt and vodka!

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain to the crow,  
disease to the timid one,  
any other ailment to the black bird,  
to the pig’s ass the surface—  
our child's finger to be whole!

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain to the crow,
soreness to the shrew,
other disease to the blackbird,
a child's hand to be healed!

label: Loitsud
translation: Sprat, give me an iron bone quickly.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Disease to the magpie  
Pain to the crow  
Bitterness to the tit  
Other illness to the blackbird  
Let the disease come, let another one go  
Let there be no change in the patch  
A lump on the stone

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain for the crow,  
disease for the magpie,  
another illness  
for the black bird.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain for the crow  
disease for the partridge  
another illness for the blackbird.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain to the crow,  
disease to the magpie,  
another illness to the black bird,  
let the disease come and go,  
but may it never be healed in this world.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain to the crow,  
illness to the magpie,  
to the black bird  
some other illness.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain to the crow,
another illness to the black bird,
may another illness come,
let it not be an old one.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Early pain  
disease of the magpie  
another disease of the blackbird  
the sick spot to health.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain for the crow,  
disease for the magpie.  
other ailments for the black bird.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain to the crow,
disease to the magpie,
another illness to the blackbird,
let one disease come, let another go,
there should be no battle between them.

label: Loitsud
translation:
The crow has pain,  
the black bird has another illness,  
and our little boy will get well.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain to the crow,
disease to the magpie,
another illness to the black bird.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain for the crow,  
disease for the magpie,  
another bird another illness,  
our child will be healthy again.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain to the crow,  
disease to the magpie,  
a different illness to the black bird.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain to the crow,  
disease to the magpie,  
another ailment to the blackbird.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain to the crow, disease to the magpie,  
another illness to the black bird.  
Let one illness come, let another go,  
may there be no change in the jackdaw.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain for the crow,
disease for the magpie,
no other illness for the black bird.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain for the crow
pain for the black bird.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain for the crow
is another disease for the black bird.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain to the crow,  
disease to the magpie,  
bitterness to the jay.  
No other ailment to the black bird.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain to the crow,  
illness to the coward,  
a different disease to the blackbird.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Pain to the crow,
disease to the magpie,
other ailments to the blackbird.

label: Loitsud
translation:
"Sill, quickly bonefish,  
give me an iron fish!"

label: Loitsud
translation:
Little worm, little bug!
Curl yourself into a hill,
Wrap yourself into a tuft.
The worm has swamp dirt fur,
The worm has water sparkle fur.
Swamp fur, rust fur,
Often hazel fur.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Lock, lock your hair,  
Lock, lock your eyes,  
Lock, lock your nose

label: Loitsud
translation:
Disease to the magpie, pain to the crow,
any other illness to the black bird!
Let one disease come, let another go,
let there be no difference in the risk,
in through the door, out through the window,
may no one be touched!

label: Loitsud
translation:
Disease to the magpie, pain to the crow,
another illness to the black bird!
Begging the ale to vanish,
a sore on the pig’s behind!

label: Loitsud
translation:
May disease come to a magpie, pain to a crow,
any other illness to a black bird.
Let one illness come, let another go,
there should be no change in fate.

label: Loitsud
translation:
I swear, I swear I curse,  
I swear I will die a woman,  
To fill the bed  
Today alone, tomorrow two  
The day after tomorrow five or six.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Loitsud
translation:
This is how they react to me  
They react, they laugh  
Empty talk is spread  
False talk taken to the garden  
Like fire comes from wood  
Water from a boiling kettle  
So it goes, the talk about me  
Empty talk about a girl  
False talk about a poor child  
Stories ran, but I did not run  
Noise happened, but I did not go  
Let the stories run without drinking  
Let the noise happen without visiting the tavern  
What do I promise to the reactor  
To the reactor, to the laugher  
To the spreader of empty talk  
To the one who tricks themselves.  
Let it be, let me curse  
Let it be as I curse  
Let worms crawl on the tongue  
Let worms sit on the lips  
The way to the heart is through food  
This I promise to the reactor  
To the reactor, to the laugher  
To the one who tricks themselves.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Loitsud
translation:
This is how they react to me  
They react, they laugh  
Empty talk is spread  
False talk taken to the garden  
Like fire comes from wood  
Water from a boiling kettle  
So it goes, the talk about me  
Empty talk about a girl  
False talk about a poor child  
Stories ran, but I did not run  
Noise happened, but I did not go  
Let the stories run without drinking  
Let the noise happen without visiting the tavern  
What do I promise to the reactor  
To the reactor, to the laugher  
To the spreader of empty talk  
To the one who tricks themselves.  
Let it be, let me curse  
Let it be as I curse  
Let worms crawl on the tongue  
Let worms sit on the lips  
The way to the heart is through food  
This I promise to the reactor  
To the reactor, to the laugher  
To the one who tricks themselves.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Saga, let there be, I curse  
Wolves on the back of the flock  
Bears on the back of the herd

label: Loitsud
translation:
"Whips whips, wool wool,  
Wool tufts on the end of the whip,  
Perhaps the whips for the complainers,  
Whip pain to the grumbler,  
Bitterly go to the stone,  
Strike the Latvians and put on the pot."

label: Loitsud
translation:
Oh, snake’s shelter,
Behind the patchwork stone,
Poppy-colored, chickpea-colored,
Grass-colored, rust-colored,
Eat-colored, mustard-colored,
Gray, like a time-worn twig,
Like the dew-grass itself,
Do not secretly strike me,
Do not stealthily pinch,
Do not poke me long:
I have with me
Wild garlic by my side,
Under my tail, horsetail herbs.
Curl yourself up,
Bend your head,
Let your tail hang down,
Crawl under the tree root,
Eat there, the swamp moss,
Chew there, willow root.
Do not appear to humans.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Forest elf, forest ally,  
Golden king of the forest,  
Living mistress of the forest,  
Young maiden of the heath:  
Protect my little herd,  
Let the sheep roam widely.  
The crowberry came from other lands,  
A distant herding rhyme,  
May it come through the path,  
Iron bridles on heads,  
Iron rings on noses.  
The wolf was not appeased,  
The white one was not struck down:  
Protect my lambs,  
Guard the little herd lambs!

label: Loitsud
translation:
Dog forward, dog backward,  
Dog both ways,  
Look at the crow, hole of the raven,  
Kitten of the he-goat and the stump.

label: Loitsud
translation:
"Araka’s disease, woodpecker’s goiter,  
Black bird’s ringworm,  
Let one disease come, let another go away,  
Let there be no change in the disease."

label: Loitsud
translation:
Jesus was on a high mountain.  
Three roses in hand,  
Three roses in hand!  
Three roses in hand!  
With one he strikes; with another he heals,  
With the third he takes away completely.  
With the third he takes away completely.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Saint George, little boy!
Let my flock graze far away
Let the sheep gather in the meadow,
I myself will lie down to rest,
I will lay my head on a pillow
My feet on a leafy branch.

label: Loitsud
translation:
The wood under the forest, the fool of the forest  
The golden king of the forest  
The generous lady of the forest.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Oven, oven, our oven,  
Honeycakes in our oven,  
Buns in Sandi’s oven,  
Hot stones in Kubja’s oven,  
Ropes knots in the stoker’s oven,  
Limestone stones in Papi’s oven.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Forest grandmother, forest grandfather,  
Golden king of the forest,  
Little boy of the rock bush!  
Go away from our herd shelter. -  
Let our herd roam freely  
Cows indeed in the alder grove  
Sheep to be free in the meadow,  
Pigs to play in the mud.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Do not make me cold,  
Cold to touch, cold to eat,  
Cold to sip, cold to gnaw.  
From the yard over the fence,  
To the pigsty over the bridge,  
From the digging path on the hill.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Sore on the magpie's wing  
Another disease on the black bird.  
If another disease comes,  
Let there be no change at all.

label: Loitsud
translation:
The snake under the black peat,  
The garlic under the gardens,  
Under the colorful stone,  
Creeper through the waters,  
But you, burrow under the tree roots,  
You eat the heart of the hornbeam,  
You nibble on the birch branches,  
The open sea branches,  
Do not eat my heart,  
Do not drink my spirit,  
Do not trouble the little soul!  
You eat the mossy swamp hummock,  
The rotten lake's mound,  
The fish lake's shore.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Hatred as butter  
For the sauna steam ointment  
So dear to the minds of men  
So dear to the minds of women  
When a sheep has ended according to the minds of dogs  
Bloom in sorrow, happiness in sorrow  
Flock’s sorrow, benefit sorrow  
Saki saki little sauna  
Saki sauna steam ointment  
Irmukakku sweetheart  
Through the thigh Reinu’s cake  
To whip with a wolf’s tail!  
To win with a fox’s tail.  
Jesus Christ Jesus Christ  
Holy Mary  
Amen Amen.

label: Loitsud
translation:
Field, field, little bird  
Marsh-colored, blue-colored  
Earth-colored, soil-colored  
Field, field, little bird.

label: Loitsud
translation:
O black snake,  
Head underground,  
In the swamp moss’s grasp,  
Fire-colored and tuft-haired,  
Living-haired and hay-haired,  
You do not need to stretch your head,  
You do not need to burst out,  
You do not need to cause pain.  
Come here, touch the heart of Jesus in Elbur,  
Come here and taste the honey drop on your tongue.  
Good wine, snake mama.  
Do not take the midge's tooth,  
Do not bite the bone eater.  
O snake, earth snake,  
Curl yourself into a coil,  
Smear yourself in the moss.  
Curl under the stone.  
You do not need to stretch your head,  
You do not need to burst out,  
You do not need to cause pain,  
Here is your honey drop on the tongue.

label: Loitsud
translation:
To the grey of the forest, to the madman of the forest!  
Forest mamka, little maiden  
If you hear my voice  
Or the voice of my dear herd.  
Put your head under the stove!  
Put your teeth under the pillow!

label: Loitsud
translation:
The snake was a piece of dull fur  
The snake was water reed fur  
The snake was gray fence fur  
The snake was swamp pit fur  
The snake was bog valley fur  
Horned fir fur  
The snake was white foam fur  
I wrapped myself in a tussock  
I rolled myself onto a hill  
Through the fence to the shiny one  
On the stone to the one who twists

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little rooster, little hen.  
They are rushing off in a race.  
The rooster went under the rich man's leg,  
The hen went to the poor barn.  
The rooster got one barley seed,  
The hen got two oat grains.  
They put the money on the table,  
Rolled it to the miller,  
Paid master miller,  
And to another village man.  
They started making beer.  
Got a horn full of beer,  
A womb full of earwax.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, little cuckoo.  
The cuckoo will not cuckoo to me,  
The cuckoo cuckoos to the herdsman.  
The cuckoo cuckooed in the spruce forest.  
I play in the pine forest,  
I keep daughters in the house,  
And boys under my heart.  
The cuckoo came to ask me:  
Give me my children!  
Tomorrow I will go toward the town,  
From there I will bring you three pairs of shoes:  
One pair will be loose and floppy,  
Another pair will be firm and sturdy,  
The third pair will be stony shoes.  
The loose ones are your snow shoes,  
The sturdy ones are your wedding shoes,  
The stony ones are church shoes.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Surely I know the father-in-law's yard  
Surely I know the mother-in-law's yard  
A miserable cow mooing  
An ugly dog barking  
A pig whining with a fierce eye  
A rooster crowing with a dry tail  
Wait-wait, pig; no-no, pig;  
In summer you were grand  
Surely you will give it back in winter.  
Surely you howl at the door  
Surely you beg behind the sauna:  
Master, little master  
Mistress, little mistress  
Cook me some barley porridge  
Give me some bran porridge  
Then you’ll get meat in the broth  
Wedding meat in halves  
Shoulder meat on a dish  
Then the young men got to eat  
The boys got to marry  
The old people got the shoulders.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What is under our yard?  
The sea is under our yard.  
What is in the middle of the sea?  
A lake is in the middle of the sea.  
What is under the lake's shore?  
A well is under the lake's shore.  
What is under the well's shore?  
A post is under the well's shore.  
What is on the edge of the post?  
A bundle is on the edge of the post.  
What is on the bottom of the bundle?  
A sieve is on the bottom of the bundle.  
What is on the edge of the sieve?  
A cuckoo is on the edge of the sieve.  
What is on the hands of the cuckoo?  
A child is on the hands of the cuckoo.  
What is on the child's chest?  
A brooch is on the child's chest.  
What is in the eye of the brooch?  
A ring is in the eye of the brooch.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Ellakene, little brother!  
I am cutting down dry spruces,  
Dry spruces, wet pines:  
I leave the birches to grow;  
The little branches to moan  
The little leaves to mourn!  
Branches moan for their father  
Flowers mourn for their mother,  
On every top there is a titmouse  
In every treetop a songbird.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row the boat, reach the shore  
Row across three forests  
There were three forests side by side:  
One was the golden spruce forest  
The second was the dear birch forest,  
The third was the true apple tree forest!  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
It was the forest of young men  
What was the dear birch forest?  
It was the forest of young women:  
What was the true apple tree forest?  
It was the forest of young maidens.  
Row the boat, reach the shore  
Row the boat to the sunny land  
Where roosters drink gold  
Roosters golden, hens fearful  
Ducks green silver  
Surely it will come to us two young ones:  
Properly to the couple of young ones.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row the boat, reach the shore  
Row across three forests  
There were three forests side by side:  
One was the golden spruce forest  
The second was the dear birch forest,  
The third was the true apple tree forest!  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
It was the forest of young men  
What was the dear birch forest?  
It was the forest of young women:  
What was the true apple tree forest?  
It was the forest of young maidens.  
Row the boat, reach the shore  
Row the boat to the sunny land  
Where roosters drink gold  
Roosters golden, hens fearful  
Ducks green silver  
Surely it will come to us two young ones:  
Properly to the couple of young ones.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row the boat, reach the shore  
Row across three forests  
There were three forests side by side:

The second was the dear birch forest,  
The third was the true apple tree forest!

It was the forest of young men

It was the forest of young women:  
What was the true apple tree forest?  
It was the forest of young maidens.  
Row the boat, reach the shore  
Row the boat to the sunny land  
Where roosters drink gold  
Roosters golden, hens fearful  
Ducks green silver  
Surely it will come to us two young ones:  
Properly to the couple of young ones.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Ride, pig, stud, piglet  
Tiny, red piglet  
Do not go to the rye field  
One comes carrying rye on the shoulder  
Another comes carrying a staff on the shoulder  
The third comes, followed by a dog.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What are you neighing, Hiiumaa grey,  
Or what are the Swedes shouting at the ears?  
The horse answered:  
I neigh from the bubbling spring,  
From the old dance house. –  
When I went to seek a bride!  
My body was under a colorful blanket;  
My tail in a German buckle  
My head in a copper pan!  
My eyebrows in a golden ring.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Nõnna’p sang our men!  
Our men and Merja’s men:  
The strong men of Aruküla parish;  
The spirited boys of Väinjärve parish.  
When these men raised their heads,  
They sang the Laglas under the sky:  
Plowing fields in the barn,  
Walking among the stumps,  
Shouting seals into the sea,  
Herding seagulls by the sea;  
They sang away the destruction.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Itut-tiitut, the son of Tika  
Tika's son killed a woman  
The son of the bog ravens  
To the youngest maiden  
The youngest lads mowed hay  
The reapers took the swath  
Flies settle in place  
Gnats trapped in a net  
Over the sea to the destination  
To Saaremaa on a journey  
There it was wonderful to live  
Goats ate sprats and fish  
Sheep grazed on wide clover fields  
Squirrels broke horses  
Grasshoppers ate the sheepfold.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Rain, rain, little rain  
Rain, rain, soak the rye  
The new hauler to haul the grain  
The handsome jackets of Kilter  
Don’t leave me unwatered  
I have no home where I dry  
I have no barn where I lay  
I have no room where I harden  
I have no sauna where I warm up

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
What a nap is for
To the man from the western forest
From a tree root to the boy,
From a stump root to the bridegroom.
I drink without sister's oil
Half without brother's help
Trying to fill aunt's pipe.
Who made me without oil?
The titmouse made without oil.
Who brought two loaves of bread?
The bear brought two loaves of bread.
Who brought pieces of meat?
The wolf brought pieces of meat.
The trees were tall grooms
The birches broad bridesmaids
The field a wide dining table.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:

The birches broad bridesmaids
The field a wide dining table.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, young men  
Know, know, wise women  
Guess, heads of families:  
Who takes the hay from the meadow?  
Who takes the peat from the moor?  
Who takes the wide birch from the swamp?  
Who takes the horse from the hill?  
Who takes the stallion from the mine?  
Who takes the maiden to visit?  
"The sickle takes the hay from the meadow,  
The knife takes the peat from the moor,  
The scythe takes the wide birch from the swamp.  
The wolf takes the horse from the hill,  
The bear the stallion from the mine.  
The village boy, a tender brother—  
He takes the maiden to visit."  
The corners of the room begin to weep,  
The barn walls begin to wail,  
The chamber walls begin to regret:  
Now the beautiful one was taken to visit,  
The proud one was taken from the street.  
The bright sickle from the hayfield,  
The swift scythe from the meadow;  
The full sheaf from the field,  
The right kind from the haystack.  
"Take, take what you take,  
Burn, burn what you burn.  
Good ones you take, better ones remain:  
We kept the poles growing,  
The May flowers rising from the ground,  
The gifts remained lying in the field,  
The ribbons sliding on top.  
The gifts remained gray cuffs,  
The lace remained flax locks,  
The poles golden necklaces,  
The May flowers little berry stems,  
The ribbons slippery tongues."

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I am chilled by the cold  
I am cold in my hands  
My toes are aching.  
Wait-wait, cold, no-no, cold:  
Let me make the room warm  
The house is being plastered anew,  
The house is being painted anew:  
Then the wolves will howl outside the door  
The wolves will ask behind our hut,  
They will shout at the chamber’s threshold.  
You seek warm buildings,  
Rooms without heating,  
Windows without creditor,  
Ask, ask, little cold one,  
Drive away the gray one.  
Where is your summer warmth,  
Where is your outer toil?  
Haymaking time and beauty,  
Sunshine and heat.  
The warm weather of rye harvest.  
Where did you put the throat of the spruce,  
Where did you put the throat of the coat?  
Behind the tavern in Tapa, in the chamber,  
On top of the beer barrel,  
On the branch of the vodka barrel.

On the branch of the vodka barrel.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What are you rustling, little wind,
Bangings, bad little weather,
Heavy little wind pellet.
You blow redness from me.
You draw blood from the little one.
The red stayed on the manor’s boards,
The lord’s blood on the outside.
Blow winds on the manor’s boards,
Drive winds through the manor’s yards,
The bellows' thighs shaking,
The new thigh sleeping,
Covering the cow shed,
Touching the stable stall.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, little cuckoo,  
Helgi, little haybird,  
Won’t you cuckoo for me?  
You cuckoo for the herders in the forest,  
Helgis for the haycutters,  
For the trap setters,  
For the breechcloth fishermen.  
The cuckoo cuckoos, the spruce bends,  
I sing, the valley echoes,  
Come home, little gull.  
Come home uncalled,  
Without shouting across the fields.  
Come from three places:  
Peek from the ridge, visit,  
From under the swing at Alu village,  
From the parsonage at Vahakulmu.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, young men
Know this, wise women.
"Who is the house hen?
Who is the gate guardian?
Who is the tasty little bird?
Who is the road wanderer?
Who is the lady of the aspen?
Who is the little one of the pine grove?
Who is the king of the spruce forest?
Who is the slippery one on the sand?
Who is the agile one in the field?"
"I understand, though others may not,
I know, though others may not:
The cat is the house hen,
The dog is the gate guardian,
The rooster is the tasty little bird,
The horse is the road wanderer,
The hare is the lady of the aspen,
The wolf is the little one of the pine grove,
The cuckoo is the king of the spruce forest,
The bear in the wilderness is the herd boy,
The water on the sand is slippery,
The wind is agile in the field."

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
If it were up to me,  
Up to my will, up to my language skills,  
Up to my speech, up to my heart.  
Then I would know what I would do:  
I would set the room to the wind,  
Set the hut to the alder leaf.  
The house on the berry stem,  
A feast on the reed.  
Life on the haystack,  
Benefit on the birch stump.  
I would ride to Tõnismäe,  
There I would eat currants,  
Firecracker on the walnut branch.  
I would bring bird cherries home,  
For the children to nibble on hunger,  
For the boys to hold feasts.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
If it were up to me,  
Up to my will, up to my language skills,  
Up to my speech, up to my heart.

The house on the berry stem,

Life on the haystack,

I would ride to Tõnismäe,  
There I would eat currants,  
Firecracker on the walnut branch.

For the children to nibble on hunger,  
For the boys to hold feasts.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Come, come, little wind,  
Shine upon us, little sun,  
Carry water from the grain fields,  
Dew from the oat stalks,  
Rust from the grass meadows,  
Frost from the fields,  
The grain sprouts from rain,  
The grass rots in wetness,  
The barley grows with dew.  
Rust spoils the grass meadows,  
Dew spoils the oat stalks,  
Frost is good for the fields.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Now it is born to be proud as a slave  
Proud as a slave, proud as a bull  
The nobleman said to the serf:  
That now the rye heads would appear,  
The barley ears turning green,  
The oat ears covering the ground,  
The wheat in the sheath turning yellow,  
The peas sprouting leaves,  
The row of flax reaching out,  
The beans with new pods.  
The flax waves in the field,  
Copper and gold in the clouds.  
Dear little brother:  
Take in your hands the golden spindles,  
The silver scythes.  
Come to cut the flax,  
To harvest copper and gold.  
I will put the flax into the bundle,  
Copper and gold also in the field.  
Then I will take it home to grandmother,  
Grandmother will set the box to sink,  
To grow beneath the lid.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Why is the weather foggy,  
The sky smudged with soot?  
Because the weather is foggy,  
The sky smudged with soot:  
So that frost stays on the field,  
So the fodder wilts.  
Seli, seli, little weather,  
Clear up the weather,  
Make the sky bright,  
Blow the weather clean.  
Come, come, little wind,  
Wave, Creator’s little servant,  
Take water from the crops,  
Dew from the oat straw,  
Rust from the grass stalks.  
Neither will the rainseed sprout,  
Nor will the wetland rot,  
Nor will the dew grow.  
The grain sprouts in the rain,  
The grass rots in the wet  
The oats are long in the dew.  
I grow on the edge of a canyon,  
I grow on tender flesh.  
I swell with thick cream,  
I stretch with a butter slice.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The pig rummages a lot in the yard  
Cannot go to Christmas without oil.  
The woodpecker pecks a lot of wood  
Cannot build a pile.  
Tidu, Liidu, would do without oil:  
There are three missing things,  
Hops, linnets,  
Junipers, dear woods.  
"Cuckoo, little hen  
Let's run a race,  
Under the rich man's threshing floor,  
In the poor man's barn,  
The rooster gets one measure of barley,  
The hen two measures of oats.  
Let's do without oil.  
Herding birds brought junipers,  
The fox brought bread crumbs;  
The birds carried the meat.  
Ui, ui, ui, ui, let's drink,  
Surely others will bring more.  
If others do not bring more,  
Then the tavern token will pay,  
If the tavern token does not pay,  
Then the master will pay the stable.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
A shower of rain over the hill,  
Over the hill I wait,  
Enduring a shower of dew.  
Bring rain from Vihula's heights,  
Drive the shower to Harjumaa,  
Thick clouds to Paide,  
Hailstorms toward the coast.  
In our land, men are fair,  
Men are fair, women are fair,  
Half the boys are Germans,  
The girls are very beautiful.  
In Harjumaa men are dark,  
Men are dark, women are lazy,  
Half the boys are dark-skinned,  
The girls are always clever.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I had two good cats  
One was a siugu, the other a näugu,  
Siug was a great singumaie,  
Näug was a great näugumaie.  
Let’s get hilli, liirespill,  
Let’s get the gray laadermann.  
I have three good dogs  
One was gray, the other krants,  
The third was kind of blackish.  
The gray was a greedy bark-maie,  
Krants was a brisk hunter-maie,  
The black is kind of a worker.  
Let’s get hilli, liirespill,  
Let’s get the gray laadermann.  
I have three good horses,  
One is gray, the other desert,  
The third kind of blackish.  
The gray is a greedy step-maie,  
The desert is a brisk walker-maie,  
The black is kind of a worker.  
Plowing fields, carrying foals,  
Daughters ride to their husbands,  
Sons go to take wives.  
Let’s get hilli, liirespill,  
Let’s get the gray laadermann.  
I had three suitors:  
Hans was from Harju, Jaan was from Järva,  
Osmi was from his own village.  
Hans was a man from low Harju,  
Jaan was a man from thick Järva,  
Osmi harnesses horses,  
Kalevine drives the sled.  
A stripe struck Osmi’s chest,  
At Kalevine’s neck.  
Osmi slept a hundred summers,  
Ten cold winters.  
When he dies,  
Where will he be buried?  
In Kuuramaa, under a spruce,  
In Läänemaa, under an alder,  
In Pärnumaa, under a birch.  
In the marsh, in rotten earth,  
By the shore of a rotten lake.  
Kessi cries on the grave,  
Kessi on the grave bank?  
A wolf howls on the grave,  
A bear on the grave bank.  
What plants grow on Osmi’s grave?  
Thistles and slave twigs,  
Nettles and heather twigs.  
When the maiden dies,  
Where will she be buried?  
In Kullamäe churchyard.  
Who cries on the grave?  
Peigu cries on the grave,  
Peigu by the grave bank.  
What grows on the maiden’s grave?  
Beet slices, beetroot,  
Blood root, root of Moor,  
German bulb onions.  
Peigu cries on the grave,  
Peigu by the grave bank:  
“Come out, little one,  
Step out, my darling!  
Hear my plaintive sobbing,  
My fervent prayers.”  
The maiden hears and replies:  
“I can’t come out, little one,  
I can’t step out, my love.  
Leave the weeping unwept,  
Tears unfallen.”  
“The earth crushes my lips,  
The stones press my face.  
The sand sticks to my chest.  
But one day we shall meet,  
In another land we shall greet.  
Where there are no curses,  
No tongues that beat with malice.”

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, little cuckoo,  
Helgi gentle little bird!  
Won't you cuckoo for me,  
Cuckoo for the village herdsmen,  
Traps for the poachers,  
Shots for the bushwhackers!

label: Looduslaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
I am a sailor man’s son,
Dear man, the fisherman’s son,
I sit on the edge of the boat:
I pick herrings from the seaweed,
The bigger ones I put in my mouth,
The smaller ones I throw aside;
There they grow among the fish,
They rise from below, —
Here the large ones, the dark sea bass,
Broad salmon, flounder at the end.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
I am a sailor man’s son,
Dear man, the fisherman’s son,
I sit on the edge of the boat:
I pick herrings from the seaweed,
The bigger ones I put in my mouth,
The smaller ones I throw aside;
There they grow among the fish,
They rise from below, —
Here the large ones, the dark sea bass,
Broad salmon, flounder at the end.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, little cuckoo  
Helgi, little haybird.  
The cuckoo does not cuckoo to me,  
The cuckoo cuckoos to the herdsman  
To Helgi, the hay cutter  
To the stonecutter.  
To the trapper.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
You might remember from last year  
Recall from the year before last  
Where last year we sowed rye  
Sowed rye, drank honey mead,  
There I bent the sickle  
Wielded the golden scythe  
Forgot the silver knife.  
There grew a buttery forest  
Buttery forest, golden birch grove  
Silver little clearing.  
Young men, dear brothers  
Sharpen your blades  
Go to the forest to cut,  
Cut down that buttery forest  
Buttery forest, golden birch grove  
Silver little clearing  
Plow that field up  
Sow buttery barleys there  
Buttery barleys, golden oats  
Silver little rye ears.  
Take yourself a buttery woman  
Buttery woman, golden bride  
Silver second half,  
She will cut the buttery barleys  
Buttery barleys, golden oats  
Silver little rye ears.  
If she doesn’t cut the buttery barleys  
Buttery barleys, golden oats  
Silver little rye ears:  
Take a rowan branch from the stack  
A beam from the barn roof  
Pull it along the slender shaft,  
Then she will cut the buttery barleys  
Buttery barleys, golden oats  
Silver little rye ears.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I, a poor little bird,  
Where should I build my nest:  
Should I make it in a spruce or a bog pine,  
Or should I make it in a pine or on a hill,  
Or should I make it on the mossy patch of a swamp.  
On the heath, on the top of a hill,  
There I will make a nest for my son, my grandson.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, young men,
Listen, wise women,
What is seen from there on the hill?
An apple tree is seen on the hill!
How many branches does the apple tree have?
The apple tree had three branches.
How many blossoms on the branch?
There were three blossoms on the branch,
How many apples on the blossom?
There was one apple on the blossom.
From the water side it was streaked,
From the moon side it was crowned,
From the side of the pasture it was reddish,
From the side of the earth it was sweet.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh, that beautiful time  
Oh, that lovely spring,  
The leaf was on the tree, the grass was on the ground,  
A clear voice comes to the girls,  
The heath’s little hill bends,  
A little birch swings in the swamp,  
The horse neighs in the cabin,  
The cuckoo falls into the spruce forest,  
A girl sings from the swing.  
Consider, young men,  
Consider, wise women,  
Who takes the hay from the meadow?  
The sickle fiercely strikes the iron,  
The knife from the heath’s little hill,  
The axe peels bark from the birch,  
The wolf takes the horse from the cabin,  
The bear takes the stallion from the mine,  
The village ghost, the dear brother,  
He takes the girl from the swing.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Now a slave may have pride,  
A slave’s pride, a proud one’s pride  
The serf even has courage:  
Already the rye ear seems  
The barley ear is greening  
The oat ear covers the ground  
Wheats come from the husk,  
Flax stretches its fibers,  
Beans show new pods,  
Peas adorn the stalk,  
Cabbage twists here and there,  
Cauliflower’s leaves unfold.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
How many apple trees on the hill  
There are five apple trees on the hill  
How many branches do the apple trees have  
There are five branches of the apple trees  
How many blossoms on the branch  
There are five blossoms on the branch  
How many apples on the blossom  
There are five apples on the blossom.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Now the young men are weak  
The hat of the young man is weak,  
Because the cold has taken hold  
Ale has been driven away by troubles  
The crab has written letters  
Hail has torn the rye  
Now the woman remains unclaimed  
The clasped breast remains untraveled  
The curly neck remains unheard  
Now the town girl's ribbons remained  
And the girl's bundles remained in the shop  
The girl's things stayed in the borough  
The German box kept the Kalevs.  
The hat stayed between the walls  
The necklace fell down to the cellar  
The clasp stayed on the friend's table  
The boat ended up in the lady of Paide's hands.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Now it starts to get chilly  
The snow begins to fly  
Big ditches start to dig holes.  
Ask, ask, little cold one  
Drive away the little winter.  
Where did you put your throat  
Where your neck is in your coat  
Took the tavern for vodka  
Jugs for beer jugs  
Under five shots of vodka  
Under two beer jugs  
Wait, wait, cold, no, no, cold  
Cold makes one freeze  
I am freezing my hands  
My toe ached  
Let my room be heated  
New crowns for the house  
New paint for the walls  
I am going in the evening to a warm room  
You wander by the doorway  
Yell near the room's threshold  
Looking for a warm room  
A cozy shelter  
I am in a warm room  
Sitting by the stove  
Holding the family distaff.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, young men  
Know, know, wise women  
What is there beneath our yard  
The sea beneath our yard  
What is there in the middle of the sea  
A well in the middle of the sea  
What is on the edge of the well  
A pillar on the edge of the well  
What is at the top of the pillar  
A garland at the top of the pillar  
What is on the edge of the garland  
An eagle on the edge of the garland  
What is under the eagle’s wing  
A trapper under the eagle’s wing  
What is on the trapper’s eye  
A shard on the trapper’s eye  
Where was that shard cut from  
Cut from Jaan’s granary  
What is in Jaan’s granary too  
Four young maidens  
One is knitting a golden belt  
The second is weaving a threshold band  
The third is sewing a silk belt  
The fourth is weeping, the little girl.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Up, up, our swing  
Down, down, our swing  
What I saw going up  
Going up, coming down  
There were three lakes nearby  
One was the lake of angry spirits  
It was the lake of young men  
The second was red beer  
It was the lake of young women  
The third was sweet mead  
It was the lake of young maidens  
Up, up, our swing  
Down, down, our swing  
What I saw going up  
Going up, coming down  
There were three forests nearby  
What is at the center of the forest  
There were three mountains in the forest as well  
What is at the center of the mountains  
There are three apple trees in the meadow  
How many branches does an apple tree have  
There are three branches on the apple tree  
How many blossoms are on the branch  
There are three blossoms on the branch  
How many apples are on the blossom  
There are three apples on the blossom  
Up, up, our swing  
Down, down, our swing  
What I saw going up  
What I saw coming down  
There were three fields nearby  
One was a ripe rye field  
It was the field of young women  
The second was a moist wheat field  
It was the field of young maidens  
The third was a precious oat field  
It was the field of young men

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Understand, understand, people of the manor  
Help to comprehend  
What is under our yard  
An apple tree there under the yard  
A cuckoo is calling in the apple tree  
Announcing the message of war  
Kessi is going to war from us  
Is the father going or is the mother going  
Or is the youngest brother going  
The youngest, the weakest  
The plumpest and reddest  
The palest and the most painful  
But this is going to war  
They paint the Russian troops  
They make them carry a bludgeon  
Make them carry a piece of lead.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Come home, little head,  
Your brother has already taken a wife,  
The other brother went to get a wife,  
The tit made it without beer,  
Put water in the bucket,  
The willow bird placed hops on it,  
Called the colors in the harvest,  
The color was angry at the clothes moth,  
Clothes moth, talk moth:  
Pull the color’s hair,  
The head was struck bleeding.  
They took it to the doctor,  
Brought together to heal,  
Put together prayers,  
No more tender words.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I made the marsh warm beers,  
The willow’s beers are burning hot.  
The wolf went to make beer,  
The bear, in a stump at the edge of the forest,  
The titmouse with a tin pipe,  
The duck went with a wig on its head.  
The wolf’s straw hat was taken,  
The bear’s fur was pulled off,  
The titmouse’s pipe was broken.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, young men,  
Inform wise women:  
The cat is a cozy kitten,  
The dog is lively at the gate,  
Who is the proud little bird?  
The horse on the road goes clip-clop,  
Who is the mistress of the desert?  
The hare is the pine forest’s daughter-in-law?  
Who is the king in the spruce forest?  
Who is slippery on the sand,  
The wind is agile in the field?  
I understand why others do not understand,  
I know why others do not know:  
The cat is a cozy kitten,  
The dog is lively at the gate,  
The rooster is the proud little bird.  
The horse on the road goes clip-clop,  
The hare is the mistress of the desert,  
The wolf is the pine forest’s daughter-in-law,  
The cuckoo is king in the spruce forest,  
The bear is a shepherd boy in the wilderness.  
Water is slippery on the sand,  
The wind is agile in the field.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Bring rain towards Vigala  
Drive the pace towards Harju  
To water Vigala's cattle  
To wash the hands of Harju women  
To wash hands, to wash towels  
In Vigala the men are dark  
Men are dark, women are dark  
Girls are fiery  
Boys have knotted hair  
Do not curse us  
In our land the men are white  
Men are white, women are white  
Girls are full of life  
Boys have golden hair.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, little cuckoo  
Helgi, little hay bird  
Cuckoo, cuckoo, why are you cuckooing  
Are you not cuckooing for me?  
You cuckoo for the forest shepherds  
Helgi, for the hay mowers  
For the barn woodcutters  
For the trap setters  
The sickle mows the oats  
The wind howls in the morning  
The rake hums on the field edge  
The iron shouts at the dew.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
How the lazy woman said  
If I had barley in front of the door  
Oats for the stove  
Lentils for the kitchen  
Then I could manage to feed  
Take the neighbor’s milk  
Set the table on the slopes  
The wolf was the Hulja watchman  
The fox was the barn keeper  
The duck was the proper barn boy  
The lizard was the housekeeper  
The cuckoo was the messenger  
The bittern was the shepherd  
The crow was the receiver  
The magpie was on the sour trip  
When can I go to my father  
When can I go to my mother  
When will I roll to my brothers  
When will I argue with my sister  
Autumn is not fit to go  
Spring is no good to walk  
Autumn rivers are deep  
The horse drowns in that stream  
The donkey among the tussocks  
The rune makes the road muddy

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Now the weakness was young men  
Weakness young boys  
For it was cold, taken cold  
The garden was fenced for the living  
The field was written with lightning  
Now I had to go away  
Long ago to set out on the road  
I thought about the road ticking  
Roads cracking  
The tin road does not tick  
The low path does not crack  
My tin sled ticks  
My iron sled cracks  
Golden claws click on the bend  
Inside the yard the street bangs  
The horse clinks with bells  
The reins cracked sharply  
The cuckoo fell to the valley plain  
The gray bird to the barn  
The cicada to the kitchen  
The songbird to the shore  
The swallow to the head.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Mari was sleeping in the meadow  
Among the bluebells  
By the red willow in the shop  
I saw a dream while sleeping  
I went home crying  
Grandmother came to meet me, grandfather came to meet me  
Why are you crying, young daughter?  
Why don't you cry, grandmother?  
Why don't you cry, grandfather?  
Mari was sleeping in the meadow  
Among the bluebells  
By the red willow in the shop  
I saw a dream while sleeping  
The fig tree was in front of the room  
The yellow tree was behind the room  
The willow tree was even in the sauna  
The apple tree was even in the yard  
The silver birch was in the chamber  
The alder was in the smoke sauna  
Don't cry, young daughter  
This dream has already been thought of  
Past midnight  
The fig tree is for drinking alcohol  
The yellow tree is for marriage proposals  
The willow tree is for eating bread  
The apple tree is for weddings  
The silver birch is for the wife  
The alder is for moving the broth
