label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Why were the young men weak
Weak young boys?
Because the young men were weak,
The young boys were weak:
For the firewood was taken by the cold,
Driven by frost,
The field was written by lightning.
Why was the firewood taken by the cold,
Driven by frost,
The field was written by lightning
The sky is soot-haired;
Because the atmosphere is foggy
The sky is soot-haired;
Because frost stays on the crops,
It is bad for the feed.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The spruce is large in Kuuramaa  
The alder is wide in Läänemaa  
All branches float together  
The tops fall together  
The roots grow together  
The river runs beneath the roots,  
Beneath the roots, above the trees  
There are golden fish in the river,  
Large whitefish, black backs  
Wide salmon, with large scales  
From there I catch sacred fish,  
Common perch of an ordinary boy.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
The servant of the month of March  
The little boy in the pillow  
We slept in our meadow  
In the middle of our forest.  
In the middle of our forest clearing  
By the birch stump clearing.  
Boys were brought from Paide,  
Sacristans over the church,  
Millers over the seas:  
To baptize our forest,  
To baptize and to sprinkle  
The long fence of the grove.  
Then the grove began to grow blue,  
To grow on the stump in the grove.  
Men thought about forest smoke,  
Boys about smoke from trees,  
Girls about the smoke from rags,  
Women about the smoke of Riga town,  
Then the smoke of great men  
On the gray steppes of the field.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Oh my little bundle of fur  
With a small bundle on your head:  
In summer you brought feathers home,  
In winter you brought big hares  
And a couple of ducks,  
From the village of Kuks,  
From the Varivere manor.  
I will take the ducks to Paide,  
From Paide I will get a lot of money,  
I will buy myself a little instrument,  
I will start playing the instrument,  
Blowing the German horn:  
Then all the pretty village girls  
Will run after me  
And sing their own song.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:

I will start playing the instrument,  
Blowing the German horn:  
Then all the pretty village girls  
Will run after me  
And sing their own song.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Understand, understand, my sisters  
Know, know, wise women  
What is there under our yard?  
The sea is under our yard.  
What is in the middle of the sea?  
There is a well in the middle of the sea.  
What is under the shore of the well?  
There is a pillar under the shore of the well.  
What is on top of the pillar?  
There is a sieve on top of the pillar.  
What is at the bottom of the sieve?  
There is an egg at the bottom of the sieve.  
What is at the end of the egg?  
There is a cuckoo at the end of the egg.  
What is on the cuckoo's crest?  
There is a brooch on the cuckoo's crest.  
Where was the brooch brought from?  
From the finger bone of a soldier,  
From the knee bone of a boy,  
From the palm of daughters,  
From the bracelet of women.

translation:

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, little cuckoo,  
Gentle bird Helgi!  
Won't you cuckoo for me—  
You cuckoo for village herders,  
For trappers setting snares,  
You rattle for the willow wood gatherer!

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little rooster, little hen!  
Let's scurry to win,  
The rooster goes under the rich thigh  
The hen to the poor hayloft  
The rooster finds one barley ear  
The hen two oat grains  
Let's put a flatbread on top  
Roll it under the mill  
Then we'll make thin oil  
Call the cranes for a feast  
We'll leave the eagle uninvited  
That was a fierce struggle.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Our little woodcutters,  
Like little oak stumps;  
Haymakers, tree fellers,  
Shakers of the whole forest.  
Down I fell the wet pines.  
Wet pines, dry spruces,  
We leave the birches to grow  
Birch branches to sway,  
Birch leaves to mourn.  
The branches sway in sorrow,  
The leaves mourn the mother,  
The buds, the sons, the daughters.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little Red Pig  
Lied to the old sow:  
You need to graze and feed;  
Don’t go to the neighbor’s grain field,  
The neighbor is coming, carrying straw on his back,  
The other neighbor carrying a staff on his back,  
Turning your weaving loom along,  
Will bump you on the belly.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
There were indeed many children in the field.
Rye had rough hair,
Barley had a spiky crown,
Oats had long trousers on,
Beans were somewhat elongated.
Peas were quite round,
Lentils were wide and flat.
Cabbage had seven skirts
On the scale

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow cawed, the sack fell  
The eggs fell into the hole  
The young woman picked a handful of apron  
The old woman a handful of basket  
Boys' caps full  
I (picked) a hatful of hay.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The little red pig  
Lied to the old pig:  
“Don’t go into the neighbor’s grain field,  
A stranger is on the stubble with a sickle,  
Another stranger is on the stubble with a scythe,  
The third one is even knocking it down.”

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Who was naked,  
who wore a silk shirt on their back,  
who wore a yellow cap,  
who had coats on their shoulders,  
who was quite round,  
who was somewhat elongated,  
who was wide and flat,  
who was triangular?  
The wheat was naked,  
the rye wore a silk shirt on its back,  
the barley wore a yellow cap,  
the oats had coats on their shoulders,  
the pea was quite round,  
the bean somewhat elongated,  
the lentil was wide and flat,  
the buckwheat triangular.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The rooster and the hen together  
went to the barn to peck,  
to peck at the neck of the barn.  
The rooster found three barley grains,  
the hen two oats grains.  
- Let's roll to the mill,  
let's make oilless soot;  
we call together the sad birds,  
we gather the bad birds.  
We do not call the little hawk,  
nor the hawk's wife,  
The hawk was angry at the rooster,  
the hawk's wife at the hen;  
they tore the crane's new nest,  
the swallow's headgear,  
the woodpecker's jaws apart.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow rolled up its sleeves,  
called together the sad birds,  
gathered the bad birds.  
It did not call the goldfinch;  
the goldfinch was a fierce predator,  
ripped off the fur of the partridge,  
and the little jaw scarf,  
and even the thin strip from the duck.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What was under our yard?  
The sea was under our yard,  
What was in the middle of the sea?  
A post there in the middle of the sea.  
What was on top of the post?  
A sieve on top of the post.  
What was at the bottom of the sieve?  
A cuckoo at the bottom of the sieve.  
What messages does the cuckoo bring?  
The cuckoo brings war news,  
Will it go from family to family,  
Or will it go from family to aunt?  
It will not go from family to family,  
Nor will it go from family to aunt,  
But from the family a younger brother will go.  
The brother goes to decorate the barn,  
The sister to listen by the ear.  
Will you put on a shroud,  
Take the gloves in hand?  
Do not go before the war,  
Nor go after the war,  
Go correctly to the middle of it.  
The first are thrown away,  
The last are killed,  
The middle ones come home.  
Then the younger brother comes home —  
The sister goes out to look:  
Are my letters in the glove,  
Are my roses in the scarf.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
What’s down there under our yard?  
The sea is down there under our yard.  
What’s in the middle of the sea?  
There is a post in the middle of the sea,  
A cuckoo crows on top of the post.  
The cuckoo does not crow for no reason,  
The cuckoo crows announcements,  
Nonsense opinions.  
Whose turn is it to go to war?  
The woman is old, the old man is old,  
The brother is still a young lad,  
Still rocking in the cradle,  
Sighing in silver reins,  
Bending to weak labor.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little rooster, little hen!
You went off running in a race.
The rooster went under the rich man's thigh,
The hen to the poor man's barn.
The rooster got one barley ear.
The hen got two oat grains.
They put a plank on top,
Rolled it to the mill.
They paid Matt the miller,
And the other to the trash heap owner.
They started making beer.
Got a pig's horn full,
A mother's goat's ear full.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Up, up goes our swing!  
Down, down goes our swing!  
What did I see while going up?  
Going up, coming down.  
There were three lakes side by side.  
One was the lake of anger and spirits.  
It was the lake of young men.  
The second was red beer.  
It was the lake of young women.  
The third was sweet mead.  
It was the lake of young maidens.  
Up, up goes our swing!  
Down, down goes our swing!  
What did I see while going up?  
Going up, coming down.  
There were three forests side by side.  
What’s in the middle of the forest?  
There were three mountains in the forest.  
What’s in the middle of the mountain?  
There were three apple trees on the mountain.  
How many branches on the apple tree?  
There are three branches on the apple tree.  
How many blossoms on the branch?  
There are three blossoms on the branch.  
How many apples on the blossom?  
There are three apples on the blossom.  
Up, up goes our swing!  
Down, down goes our swing!  
What did I see while going up?  
Going up, coming down?  
There were three fields side by side.  
One was a rye field of bruised rye.  
It was the field of young women.  
The second was a damp wheat field,  
It was the field of young maidens.  
The third was a dear oat field,  
It was the field of young men.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Bring the rain towards Vigala.  
Drive the momentum towards Harju.  
To water the cattle of Vigala,  
To wash the hands of the women of Harju.  
To wash hands, to wash scarves.  
The men in Vigala are dark.  
The men are dark, the women are dark.  
The girls are fiery,  
The boys have stumpy hair.  
Don’t rain on us!  
In our land the men are fair,  
The men are fair, the women are fair.  
The girls are beautifully fiery.  
The boys have golden hair.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo little cuckoo,
Helgi the haybird!
Cuckoo, cuckoo, why do you call?
You are not calling to me,
You call to the forest herdsman,
Helgi the hay cutter,
The flint striker,
The scythe reaper of grain.
The rake rattles in the morning dew,
The iron clinks on the harness.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
How the lazy woman said:
If I had barley in front of the door,
Oats for covering the room,
Lentils under the bed.
Then I could manage to feed,
To receive strangers.
To set the table with leeks.
The wolf was Hulja’s guard,
The fox was the barn keeper.
The duck was a proper lad.
The lizard was the bringer inside.
The cuckoo was the deliverer.
The titmouse was the caller.
The crow was the receiver,
The magpie was the fishing companion.
When will I see my father?
When will I see my mother?
When will I roll to my brothers?
When will I lean on my sister?
Autumn is not fit to be born.
Spring is no good to go.
Autumn’s rivers are deep.
The horse drowns in the ditch,
The sow among the tussocks,
The mare on the stony road.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Now there were weak young men  
Weak young boys.  
For the firewood taken was cold  
The yards were driven by a living creature  
The field was written by lightning.  
Now I had to go away  
Long ago to walk the road.  
I thought the road would tick.  
The paths would crack.  
The road does not tick in tin  
The path does not crack shallow.  
I have a tin sled ticking,  
It thumps, this iron sled,  
Clinks the golden claws of the harness.  
The sandstone street bangs.  
The horse gleams with pearls.  
The harsh bits cracked.  
The cuckoo fell into a hollow,  
The grey bird into the barn,  
The woodpecker into the yard,  
The songbird into the barn,  
The swallow onto the roof.

translation:

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The little red pig  
lied to the old pig:  
"Don't go to the stranger's grain field.  
A stranger is coming on a carriage,  
another stranger on a cart,  
a third a dog behind."

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh you little, red piglet,  
Dear gray-banded one,  
Trot the fields and fill your belly,  
Don't go into a stranger's fruit.  
A stranger comes with a whip on his back,  
Another comes with a club on his back,  
He beats your hips with a scythe-swish,  
Your ribs with a swing-swash.  
Tuka-tuka, tillilli.  
The potty tipped over on its side!

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The rat's wedding  
The mouse's daughter got married  
To young Villem Nirki  
He had proud wedding guests  
The wolf was the proud groom  
The bear was the strong carriage driver  
The fox was the troublemaker  
The hare was the lively best man.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
The berry bringer for me is a berry bringer  
The berry bringer is a herd herder  
From a meadow a cattle driver  
From a swamp a swamp dweller  
Mari brought berries from another land  
Cranberries from across the river  
The crane then stayed to listen  
The crow then stayed to watch  
The fool to reason  
God grant daughters  
Daughters are taken to husband  
Then the ox is slaughtered  
A cow is separated in the barn  
Then the crane is fully satisfied  
The old crow is fully sated  
The fool is fully content.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:

The fool to reason  
God grant daughters  
Daughters are taken to husband  
Then the ox is slaughtered  
A cow is separated in the barn  
Then the crane is fully satisfied  
The old crow is fully sated  
The fool is fully content.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Roll, roll, little day,  
Starting a little time.  
Roll to where your brother is,  
Row to where your sister is,  
Nor will the day roll away,  
Nor will the time begin.  
There was much work in the day,  
Great work on the snow bridge.  
The day passes over the servants’ heads,  
It passes over the herdsmen’s heads.  
A golden comb in hand,  
Approach the silver barn.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Why do you worry for nothing  
The rye head is already visible  
Barley spikes are green  
Oat spikes are covering the ground  
Wheat is coming from the ear  
Peas decorate the clothing  
Lentils run down to the legs  
Cabbage arches towards the sun  
Turnip is thriving in the leaf  
Rutabaga is spreading in the leaf.

label: Improvisatsioonid  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Forget the days of fear  
Ancient days of worry  
Old days of pain  
Days of torment in the slave’s era  
Already the rye head is visible  
Wheat heads can also be seen from the stalk  
Barley ears are turning green  
Oat ears cover the ground  
Lentils stand near the legs  
Peas adorn the garment  
Carrying pods in their place  
Cabbage beautifully ripens its head  
Cabbages grow leaves  
Turnips spread their leaves.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Drink, drink little throat  
And scratch your throat by the jar  
Throw the branches into the twig basket,  
There big islands were born  
Big islands, you think of oaks  
All the branches full of squirrels.  
The leaves were full of birds  
But the river ran under the roots  
And the fish swam along the shore.  
The whitefish were big, their backs black  
The char were wide by the cove’s end;  
They stood by the sea’s edge  
Drifting here and there.  
The fisherman caught the fish  
The fish caught the catcher.  
But the water took the young brother  
Into the deep graves  
To a higher shore  
Stem, stem, stem,  
Kämm, kämm, kämm,

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oiu-roiu rat's wedding  
The mouse's daughter got married  
To Nirgi's younger son  
Who was invited to the wedding?  
There were proud wedding guests:  
The wolf came with a new violin  
The bear with two kantele (Finnish zithers)  
The crane with golden spurs  
The crow with copper boots  
The titmouse with a tin hat  
The woodpecker wore iron clothes  
The titmouse brewed beer  
The swallow broke wood  
The weasel twisted a whip  
The poor hen carried water  
The rooster followed on crutches  
The sparrow cooked soup  
The moorhen brewed sour beer  
The magpie's beard was shaved  
The crow's pain was taken away  
The duck's rear was patched  
And the bells were cut off from the jaws.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The spruce was tall in Courland,  
The birch was on the Harju pasture,  
The alder was on the city street,  
The willow on the Vinni meadow.  
Their roots ran into one,  
Their branches intertwined.  
Their leaves fluttered together,  
Their tops intertwined.  
The river ran beneath the roots,  
The spring beneath the fields,  
Golden fish inside.  
Make nets, young men,  
Cast your nets, lads;  
Let’s go fishing,  
To catch the sacred fish,  
The desired fish for father,  
The desired fish for mother;  
Fresh smelt for brother.  
I brought the fish home,  
I began to cook the fish,  
To cut the fish apart,  
The fish began to speak,  
To speak of sorrow;  
I was not brought to be eaten raw,  
Nor to be served fresh,  
I was brought to be the ploughman,  
The ploughman, the sower,  
The turner of black soil.  
Below I turn the hay side,  
Above the black soil side,  
Into these go the seeds.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The lord praised the horse
The gentleman praised the stallion
I praise the dense forest
The old white one to be wet.
Plows the pieces, carries the foals
Marries off the daughters
The sons take wives.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh, poor traveler of the roads,
a reader of the great book,
I got lost in Elimäe,
ended up in Käbiküla,
then a young girl came to meet me,
a young girl from a bride’s company,
I asked the girl,
if she knew where to find,
who goes to the alder forest,
the saw goes to the alder man,
who goes to the dear birch forest,
that one gets the dear man,
who goes to the bad spruce forest,
that one gets the bad man,
who goes to the sorrowful alder forest,
that one surely finds a place to bread.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Dear little brother!  
I will cut down the dry spruces;  
Dry spruces, wet pines:  
I will leave the birches to grow;  
Twigs to mourn,  
Leaves to grieve,  
Branches groan like a father,  
Leaves mourn like a mother.  
At every top is a titmouse,  
In every crown a songbird.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Row, row the swing, row the swing!  
Row across three forests;  
There were three forests nearby;  
One was the golden spruce forest,  
The second was the dear birch forest;  
The third was the true apple tree forest.  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
It was the forest of young men.  
What was the dear birch forest?  
It was the forest of young women.  
What was the true apple tree forest?  
It was of the young maidens:  
Row, row the swing, row the swing!  
Row the swing to the sunny land.  
Where the roosters drink gold,  
Roosters gold, hens carrd:  
The geese strip silver!  
Surely it will come to us both young ones;  
Suitably will come to the pair of young ones.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What is there under our yard?  
The sea was under our yard.  
What is there in the middle of the sea?  
A well is there in the middle of the sea.  
What is there in the middle of the well?  
A post is there in the middle of the well.  
What is on top of the post?  
A cuckoo is on top of the post.  
What did that cuckoo bring to us?  
It brought war messages.  
Whose turn was it to go to war?  
It was Ansu's turn to go to war.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Thus sang the chatterboys,  
The assistants of the new manor,  
When the cuckoos called,  
The night and dawn cranes cried out.  
The seals barked, the gulls sang,  
The sea tit chirped.  
The forest wren sang,  
Along with the seaweed wave,  
And the coastal plants to be eaten.  
The water in the sea was mighty,  
The coastal grass was lush.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
When I begin to go,
Then sorrow begins to come,
And water rolls from the eyes.
From the eyes it ran down the chest,
From the chest down to the belt,
From the belt to the colored leather strap;
From the colored leather strap to the deceased.
The city had springs,
The city’s rivers flowed,
Fish grew inside.
The graves were large, gray necks,
Large catfish, black backs,
Oh you cunning fish,
And you pike, too clever.
Rivers flowed into the sea,
The south made a splash in the bay,
And the danger boiled in the kettle,
And drops fell onto the tin plate,
From there they went onto the German table.
The Germans eat "niuh" and "näuh",
Forks went "kiuh" and "käuh".

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh dear, the rat's wedding:
The mouse's daughter got married
To Nirgi's youngest son.
There were splendid wedding guests:
The hedgehog wore a colorful dress
And the louse had a white coat,
The rooster had golden spurs,
The crow with copper leg rings,
The tit with tin bells.
The hare was a lively bridegroom,
The fox was the matchmaker.
The wolf was the master of ceremonies
And the bear was the dear sled stallion.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
But the lord praised to shine,  
The master praised to work.  
I praise the stone pile,  
The track from the wagon to get wet.  
It bears the pieces and bears the calves  
And gives the daughters in marriage  
And takes wives for the sons.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
For the young men are weak  
Weak are the young boys  
For the frost had taken the cold  
Ale was driven down  
The field was struck by lightning  
The boys could not hold weddings  
Young men as young as they were  
Old men as if shrunken  
Then came the horned German on a cart from the mill  
Devoured their barley ears  
From the hilltop, the curled rye  
They took it up and struck  
Struck on the cart’s side  
The cart immediately started to howl  
Blood began to flow out

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Our little workboys  
If these little oak stumps  
Haymakers, wood-cutters,  
Shakers of all the forest.  
I cut down dry spruces,  
Dry spruces, wet pines.  
I leave the birches to grow,  
Birch branches to sway,  
Birch leaves to mourn.  
Branches sway belonging,  
Leaves mourn their mother,  
Buds to bear offspring.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The buck came  
The mother goat came from Hiiumaa  
Then I took hold of the buck's horns,  
Held the mother goat by the ears.  
Then I took them to the Viru smith  
The Viru smith is my dear brother  
He made a horse from the horns,  
From the tips of the horns I got a saddle.  
Then I rode to the town  
Where the streets shine  
Silver combs gleam  
There the girls wear petticoats  
Behind are feathered skirts  
In front were rows of pearl necklaces,  
At the back were rows of fiery necklaces  
Sometimes there were poor children in the crowd.  
Still, I do not take you  
Do not take you, do not leave you.  
I am going courting Kooku,  
The cobbler of Koogu has three daughters,  
One is Anne with lanky legs  
The second Kaie with chubby hands,  
The third Maie with sharp lips.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Flute, flute, lark,  
Swallow, swallow, swallow,  
Where is your golden nest?  
In the forest on the dry spruce.  
Where did that spruce come from?  
An old man cut the spruce down.  
Where did that old man come from?  
He crawled under the garden bed.  
Where did that garden bed come from?  
Oxen plowed it up.  
Where did those oxen come from?  
A Russian bought the oxen.  
Where did that Russian come from?  
The Russian went to the town.  
Where did that town come from?  
A fire burned the town down.  
Where did that fire come from?  
Water put out the fire.  
Where did that water come from?  
The sea is beneath our yard.  
What is in the middle of the sea?  
A well in the middle of the sea.  
What is in the middle of that well?  
A post in the middle of the well.  
What is on the edge of that post?  
A ring on the edge of the post.  
What is on the edge of that ring?  
A cuckoo on the edge of the ring.  
What is on the hands of that cuckoo?  
A child on the hands of the cuckoo.  
What is on the child's chest?  
A ring on the child's chest.  
Where did that ring come from?  
From a soldier's shinbone,  
From a boy's kneecap.
