label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Silent, silent, it was white outside,
Accidental dawn was outside.
I went to bring fire from the dawn.
What was done there at the dawn?
The tit made beer,
The sparrow carried water,
The dawn-maker gathered wood,
The swallow broke wood,
The bald head planted hops.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Was the cat beautifully blossomed?  
The pea was beautifully blossomed.  
Was the cat somewhat elongated?  
The bean was somewhat elongated.  
Was the cat liter-larger?  
The lentil was liter-larger.  
Who had a silk shirt on their back?  
The rye had a silk shirt on its back.  
Was the cat naked?  
The wheat was naked.  
Who had long rough hair?  
The barley had long rough hair.  
Who had long pants on?  
The oats had long pants on.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The cat was on a beautiful flower
And had a flower in its vagina?
Ernes was on a beautiful flower
And had a flower in its vagina.
The cat was a bit tallish?
The bean was a bit tallish.
The cat was liter-lattergune?
The lentil was liter-lattergune.
Who had a silk shirt on their back?
The rye had a silk shirt on its back.
The cat was naked?
The wheat was naked.
Who had long curly hair?
The barley had long curly hair.
Who had long pants on their legs?
The oats had long pants on.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Roidu, roidu, rat's wedding,  
the Irish girl got married,  
to the youngest son of Nirgi.  
What proud wedding guests they were!  
The pig wore a silk shirt,  
the crow wore a copper hat,  
the titmouse with a tin tail.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What is beneath our yard?  
The sea beneath our yard.  
What is in the middle of the sea?  
A well in the middle of the sea.  
What is on the edge of the well?  
A sieve on the edge of the well.  
What is at the bottom of the sieve?  
A ring at the bottom of the sieve.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The wedding of Roiu-roiu the rat  
Irish Anna got a husband,  
To young Kustas Nirgi.  
There were splendid wedding guests  
The fox with his instrument,  
The bear with two drums,  
The hare with five violins,  
The pig with silk boots,  
The grouse in a second outfit,  
The rooster with golden spurs.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
What is under our yard?
The sea is under our yard.
What is in the middle of the sea?
An oak was in the middle of the sea.
I take the oak, I carry the oak,
Carry the oak to the old woman's yard;
I sit
And grow it for the old woman's benefit.
The oak does not grow, the oak does not thrive,
The oak does not reach the sky,
Its branches do not touch the clouds,
Its leaves do not bow to the moon.
I take the oak, I carry the oak,
Carry the oak to the wolf's yard,
Plant it for the wolf's beauty,
Grow it for the wolf's benefit.
The oak does not grow, the oak does not thrive,
The oak does not reach the sky,
Its branches do not touch the clouds,
Its leaves do not bow to the moon.
I take the oak, I carry the oak,
Carry the oak to the brother's yard;
Plant the oak for the brother's beauty,
Grow it for the brother's benefit.
The oak does not grow, the oak does not thrive,
The oak does not reach the sky,
Its branches do not touch the clouds,
Its leaves do not bow to the moon.
I take the oak, I carry the oak,
Carry the oak to the sister's yard;
Plant the oak for the sister's beauty,
Grow it for the sister's benefit.
The oak does not grow, the oak does not thrive,
The oak does not reach the sky,
Its branches do not touch the clouds,
Its leaves do not bow to the moon.
I take the oak, I carry the oak,
Carry the oak to the maiden's yard,
Plant the oak for the maiden's beauty,
Grow it for the maiden's benefit.
Then the oak grew, then the oak thrived,
Then the oak reached the sky,
Its branches climbed into the clouds,
Its leaves then bowed to the moon.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Rooster-rooster, rat’s wedding!  
The Irish daughter got married  
To the son of a young poor mother.  
What proud wedding guests they were!  
The rooster was proud, wearing a crown,  
The hen wore a coarse apron,  
The duck had a new beak,  
The bear had two drums;  
The fox came with a sled,  
The crow with copper shoes,  
The titmouse with a tin sled...  
The poor fool was driven away...

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Roiu, roiu, rat's wedding!  
Irish Anna got a husband,  
He had splendid wedding guests:  
The bullfinch with golden spurs,  
The titmouse with a tin bell,  
The crow with copper boots,  
The sow with lace ribbons,  
The bull was fierce and savage,  
He tore the jaw of the gate apart,  
The swallow's wreath ribbons.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
What is under our yard?  
The sea is under our yard.  
What is in the middle of the sea?  
A post is in the middle of the sea.  
What is on top of the post?  
A sieve is on top of the post.  
What is at the bottom of the sieve?  
A cuckoo is at the bottom of the sieve.  
What messages does the cuckoo bring?  
The cuckoo brings war messages.  
Who among us is going to war?  
Neither the young woman, nor the old man,  
nor the prime minister is going...  
It is one of us who goes to war...  
You go ahead to the war,  
go to the middle of the war.  
The first are betrothed,  
the last are killed,  
the middle ones come home.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I am a poor free man's wife,  
a little pauper sauna woman,  
my bean soup was ruined,  
my pea soup was spoiled,  
I scooped up ashes with a ladle.  
I would like a strip of field,  
where I can sow seeds.  
But I look around,  
to see how the grains have grown.  
Who had a beautiful flower,  
with a fruitful blossom?  
The pea had a beautiful flower,  
with a fruitful blossom.  
Who was alone and round?  
The pea was alone and round.  
Who is slightly elongated?  
The bean is slightly elongated.  
Who is loose and irregular?  
The lentil is loose and irregular.  
Who was naked?  
The wheat was naked.  
Who had a silk shirt on their back?  
The rye had a silk shirt on its back.  
Who had red hair?  
The barley had red hair.  
Who had long trousers on their legs?  
The oat had long trousers on its legs.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
The cuckoo does not simply call,
the cuckoo calls to the herd,
the cuckoo brings war messages.
What is under our yard?
The sea is under our yard.
What is in the middle of the sea?
The sea is in the middle of the sea.
What is in the middle of the sea?
A post is in the middle of the sea.
What is on top of the post?
The cuckoo is on top of the post.
The cuckoo does not simply call,
the cuckoo brings war messages.
Whose turn is it to go to war?
The old woman is old, the old man is old,
the son is still young,
still swinging in the hideaway,
rocking in the rein-harness,
weak for the light load.
Rise up, little old man,
get ready to go to war!
Rise up, little brother!
. . . . . . . . .
Do not go before the war
nor go behind the war,
go to the middle of the war.
The first are despised,
the last are killed,
the middle ones come home.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Roiu, roiu, rat's wedding!  
The Irish daughter got married  
To Nirgi’s younger son.  
Who was the tall bridegroom?  
Unt was the tall bridegroom,  
Bear with his little kannel...

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh roi roi roi the rat's wedding!
Iire's daughter got married
to the worthless young son,
to the scrawny young lad.
The scrawny one had a gun in his hand,
the squirrel a spear as a weapon...

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Where I spread the song,  
forests broke without words,  
groves fell without words.  
Pine, pine, alder brothers!  
The trees were full of insects,  
the branches full of squirrels,  
the canopies full of songbirds.  
The river flowed from beneath the roots,  
fish were like gold along the river.  
The perch had colorful scales,  
the eels were very long,  
the bullheads were black, the bullheads thick,  
the roaches had red eyes,  
the perch had a noble back.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little rooster, little hen,  
went scurrying in a race.  
The rooster went under the rich man’s leg,  
the hen to the poor fellow’s barn.  
The rooster got a handful of barley,  
the hen got two grains of oats,  
they put them on a plank,  
rolled them to the mill.  
They paid Master Miller,  
the other to the farm bailiff,  
started making beer.  
Got a handful of pig’s ear,  
a handful of mother goat’s ear.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The little red pig
spoke to the big pig,
lied to her white face,
how there was barley growing there,
how there was oats growing there,
how I was eating rye bread.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Roiu-roiu, the rats' wedding,
the youngest daughter got married
to the youngest son of Nirgi.
There were proud wedding guests:
the rooster with golden spurs,
the crow with copper boots,
the tit with a tin bell,
the nightingale with a steel bow,
the pig was in chintz clothes...

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Damn! Damn! Damn! Damn! for opening,
for pulling the spotted mare!
Go to the dead man,
there are three kinds of tails;
some black, others white,
the third bright red,
wonder at those scoundrels,
gnaw at those rascals!

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The goat went bleating boastfully,  
the wolf followed growling.  
“Come to me, little goat!  
I’ll make you new nailed shoes,  
I’ll put red taps underneath,  
and I’ll tie silk ribbons to your feet.”  
But the bird sang in the alder grove:  
“Don’t believe the wolf’s words!  
The wolf tells foolish stories,  
the bear speaks cunningly.  
His own skin—nailed shoes,  
underneath the blood are the taps,  
on the feet are silk ribbons.”

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Up, up, lively brothers  
Let's go to Sõõrumäe  
There we eat strawberries  
We crack nuts  
There the spirits are stirring  
The fires are glowing.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow had a proud wedding  
Luukas danced with Liisu  
And Veo Villem with Viiu.  
The rooster had golden spurs  
The crow had copper boots  
The titmouse had a tin leg.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The thieves are on the move, the rats' wedding
Iire’s daughter got married
To Nirgi’s youngest son
What proud wedding guests there were
The father’s brothers from Iiumaa
Half the brothers from Pohlamaa
The sisters had oiled hats
The crow wore brass boots
The rooster had golden spurs
The titmouse had tin shoes.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little red piglet,  
Lied to the old sow:  
Do not go to the stranger's grain field,  
A stranger comes with a whip on his back,  
Another comes with a pole on his back,  
A third comes with dogs behind,  
A fourth comes with a knife in hand,  
To kill the pig,  
To take the sow's life.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I have three geldings at home  
One was gray, the other black  
The third one light-eared  
I went to the forest to bring hay  
To bring rocks from the swamp and bog  
To bring dry wood, hay  
I brought gray irons from the meadow below  
I put bells on the ears  
With the black one, I brought branches from the forest  
A large number for the sledges.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
I fall over three forests.  
There were three forests side by side:  
one was a golden spruce forest,  
the second a dear birch forest,  
the third a beautiful apple tree.  
Listen, golden spruce forest!  
Can I get sailwood from you,  
sailwood, ship planks?  
No, I cannot get sailwood from you,  
sailwood, ship planks,  
I will get mast wood from you.  
Listen, dear little birch!  
Can I get sailwood from you,  
sailwood, ship planks?  
No, I cannot get sailwood from you,  
sailwood, ship planks,  
I will get wheel wood from you.  
Listen, beautiful apple tree!  
Can I get sailwood from you,  
sailwood, ship planks?  
Yes, I can get sailwood from you,  
sailwood, ship planks.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, little cuckoo.  
The cuckoo will not cuckoo to me,  
the cuckoo cuckoos to the shepherd.  
The cuckoo cuckooed in the spruce forest,  
I play in the pine forest,  
I hold daughters in my palm,  
cuckoo chicks in my womb.  
The cuckoo made a request to me:  
give me my children,  
I will go towards the town tomorrow,  
from there I will bring you three pairs of shoes:  
one pair of slippery shoes,  
another pair of wooden shoes,  
the third pair are stony shoes.  
The slippery ones are your snow shoes,  
the wooden ones are your wedding shoes,  
the stony ones are church shoes.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The wedding of the Oidu-roidu rat  
The mouse's daughter got married  
To the youngest son of Nirgi  
They had a grand wedding party  
The rooster had golden spurs  
The pig wore silk boots  
The crow had copper toes  
The mole came in a black carriage  
The titmouse was with a tin sled.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Rowing boat and reaching boat  
Reach beneath our yard:  
What is there beneath our yard?  
The sea is there beneath our yard.  
What is there in the middle of the sea?  
A post is there in the middle of the sea.  
What is on top of the post?  
A ring is on top of the post.  
What is inside the ring?  
Cuckoo’s feet are inside the ring.  
What messages does the cuckoo bring?  
The cuckoo brings war messages.  
Who among the three of us goes to war?  
Does the father go, or does the mother go,  
Or does the eldest son go?  
Neither the father goes, nor the mother,  
Nor does the eldest son go.  
The youngest brother goes to war.  
The sister went to teach the brother:  
“Don’t always ride ahead of the war  
Nor ride behind the war.  
Ride into the middle of the war!  
The first are thrown away,  
The last are killed,  
The middle ones return.”  
---------------  
I row beneath father’s door.  
“Hello, father, know your son,  
Know your son’s gray horse!”  
“Oh my God, I cannot know  
The son nor the gray horse.”  
I row beneath mother’s door.  
“Hello, mother, know your son,  
Know your son’s gray horse!”  
“Oh my God, I cannot know  
The son nor the gray horse!”  
I row beneath brother’s door.  
“Hello, brother, know me,  
Know my gray horse.”  
“Oh my God, I cannot know  
The brother nor the gray horse!”  
I row beneath sister’s door.  
“Hello, sister, know your brother,  
Know your brother’s gray horse.”  
“Oh my God, yes I can know!  
Come inside, sit down,  
Tell me stories about the war!”

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oiu roiu rat's wedding:  
the swift daughter was married  
to the youngest son of Nirgi.  
The bear was the dear groom,  
and the hare was the lively bridegroom,  
the wolf was the wedding elder,  
and the fox was the toastmaster.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Who is walking there along the ridge?
Stepping along the edge of the time?
Turning along the reed?
- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -
The little bee-bird
That is walking there along the ridge,
Stepping along the edge of the time,
Turning along the reed.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, dear, the rat's wedding
the mouse's daughter got married,
to the young son of a weasel.
There were proud wedding guests:
the pig was in silk, the crow was in copper,
the rooster had golden spurs.
A piece of fodder was a dining table,
a garden bed was a bench.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, dear, the rat's wedding
the mouse's daughter got married,
to the young son of a weasel.
There were proud wedding guests:
the pig was in silk, the crow was in copper,
the rooster had golden spurs.
A piece of fodder was a dining table,
a garden bed was a bench.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow, the humble little bird  
flew to the town street  
from there it flew to the test  
to the innkeeper's mother's funeral,  
Called together the angry birds  
the hawk  
The hawk was an angry predator  
tore apart the jaws of the swallow  
the swallow's wreath ribbon.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow, the humble little bird  
flew to the town street  
from there it flew to the test  
to the innkeeper's mother's funeral,  
Called together the angry birds  
the hawk  
The hawk was an angry predator  
tore apart the jaws of the swallow  
the swallow's wreath ribbon.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow had Jews on the branch.  
He called together the sad birds.  
He did not invite the eagle.  
The eagle was angry and tearing.  
It tore apart the swallow's jaws.  
The swallow's wreath ribbons.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The wedding of the roadside rat  
the mouse's daughter married  
the youngest son of the weasel.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Rain, go to Russia,  
Sun, come to our land.  
Rain is awaited in Russia,  
Sun is desired in our land.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Mists and fogs, the rat's wedding,
The mouse's daughter got married,
To the youngest son of Nirk,
The wedding guests were proud,
All were golden-haired!
The pig went in a spotted dress,
The crow with copper boots,
The magpie with a gray bull,
I came in a little sled.
I came from behind, I went ahead.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh, I, the poor rampart soldier,  
Sit on the edge of the rampart,  
My head is bare, I cry there.  
I pick dill from the full patch,  
I twist it from the thick bunch,  
I put the bigger ones into my mouth,  
The smaller ones I throw into the sea.  
There they grow into fish,  
Multiply into whitefish,  
Decay into bream.  
The whitefish were big, their backs black,  
Holes big, ponds wide,  
The bream had humps on their backs.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I will fell the pine forest,  
Leave the birches to grow,  
Birch branches to groan,  
Birch leaves to mourn.  
The leaves mourn the father,  
The branches groan the mother,  
The shingles grieve the children.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The cuckoo does not sing for me,  
The cuckoo sings for the herdsman,  
For the haymaker who swathes the hay,  
For the one who rakes up the loose hay.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
A hundred rains, water the rye,  
leave me unwetted,  
home dryers of the Germans,  
washers of fine shirts,  
ironers of white shirts,  
I have no home dryers...

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Rain, rain, little rain,  
don’t rain on us,  
rain, rain on the Germans.  
We have nowhere to dry,  
the Germans have home dryers,  
washers of fine shirts,  
whiters of white shirts,  
Let’s go down to the threshing room.  
What do we have then for the oven?  
The quartermaster’s thighs, the barn keeper’s thighs,  
we’ll help with both flails.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Rain, rain, little rain,  
don’t rain on us,  
rain, rain on the Germans.  
We have nowhere to dry,  
the Germans have home dryers,  
washers of fine shirts,  
whiters of white shirts,  
Let’s go down to the threshing room.  
What do we have then for the oven?  
The quartermaster’s thighs, the barn keeper’s thighs,  
we’ll help with both flails.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Row, swing, row, swing,  
Row across three forests!  
There were three forests side by side:  
One was a golden spruce forest,  
It was the forest of young men;  
The second was a dear birch forest,  
The third was an orchard forest,  
It was the forest of young maidens,  
The beauties with cannonheads.  
Row, swing, row, swing,  
Row across three fields!  
One was a faraway rye field,  
It was the field of young men;  
The second was a field, a barley field,  
It was the field of young women;  
The third was a wheat field,  
It was the field of young maidens,  
The little birds with linen heads,  
The beauties with cannonheads.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Row, swing, row, swing,  
Row across three forests!  
There were three forests side by side:  
One was a golden spruce forest,  
It was the forest of young men;  
The second was a dear birch forest,  
The third was an orchard forest,  
It was the forest of young maidens,  
The beauties with cannonheads.  
Row, swing, row, swing,  
Row across three fields!  
One was a faraway rye field,  
It was the field of young men;  
The second was a field, a barley field,  
It was the field of young women;  
The third was a wheat field,  
It was the field of young maidens,  
The little birds with linen heads,  
The beauties with cannonheads.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh, I poor ditch soldier
sit on the edge of the ditch,
my head is bare, I cry there.
I twist lice off the scab;
the bigger ones I put in my mouth,
the smaller ones I throw into the sea.
There they grow into fish,
breed into pigs,
decay into bream.
The pigs are big, the sails black,
the holes big, the ponds wide,
the bream have hunched backs.
Tin was for making matches,
iron was hard to shake,
copper was loud to drain.
My soul armor is pierced,
my neck armor bruised,
my blood shedders are ready. -
These upon my tender soul
these upon my golden neck.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The fox had a proud wedding:
The rooster wore golden spurs,
The crow wore copper boots,
The titmouse wore tin shoes.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Sots pig, sow, boar,  
Little red piglet,  
Spotted golden piglet  
Lied to the old sow:  
Off to Tuusti, you must feed,  
Don’t go to the neighbor’s grain field,  
One neighbor is coming with a sheaf on his back,  
Another neighbor with a pole on his back,  
A third one is coming with a dog behind.  
Jaan, come to tend the feet,  
Kusta to tend the throat,  
Mart, come to take down,  
Women to move the blood.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
A hundred drops of rain  
A hundred years of rain  
Years of fire and tar in the sky  
Still, I must go  
Still, I must not be a slave  
Still, I must not walk as a servant  
May it rain a hundredfold and water the meadow  
Bring rain towards Vigula  
Drive the storm to Harju County  
Send the rain to Germany  
Let it rain upon the German back  
To the German with a wide umbrella  
But the fog would spoil the new moon  
The rain would spoil the haymaking moon

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
A hundred drops of rain  
A hundred years of rain  
Years of fire and tar in the sky  
Still, I must go  
Still, I must not be a slave  
Still, I must not walk as a servant  
May it rain a hundredfold and water the meadow  
Bring rain towards Vigula  
Drive the storm to Harju County  
Send the rain to Germany  
Let it rain upon the German back  
To the German with a wide umbrella  
But the fog would spoil the new moon  
The rain would spoil the haymaking moon

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
But we are both woodmen,
Above all
We cut down wet pines,
Wet pines, dry spruces,
We carry the beans.
From the rowan tree I get a long pile,
From the bird cherry I get whip twigs,
From the hazel I get a forked sled.
The birches remain growing,
Birch branches do not groan,
Birch leaves do not mourn.
Branches groan for the father,
Leaves mourn for the mother,
Shavings for the sons, daughters.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Wait, wait, pig with silk eyes,  
Young golden yellow moon,  
You have grown in summer,  
Surely you will fight back in winter,  
Surely you howl behind the door,  
Surely you beg behind the stove,  
Surely you stand behind the wall:  
Master, little master,  
Mistress, little mistress,  
Cook me kie  
Honor to me a porridge of chaff,  
Knead the eating time of dung;  
From me comes meat into the broth,  
Wedding meat goes into the sack,  
Shoulder meat onto the plate,  
From there  
Daughters can go marry.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Rooster, hen.  
You go to sift the grain,  
One under the other's granary.  
The rooster found a grain of sand,  
The hen two oats.  
I put a plate on top  
And roll it to the mill,  
Still I make your bread without oil,  
I call together the sad birds,  
I gather the noisy birds,  
I call the lords of Hiiumaa,  
The young Saxons from behind Narva,  
I leave the hawk uninvited –  
The hawk is a cruel predator,  
It tore the fur coat,  
The alder bird’s wide bow,  
The sacred bird’s long pants.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The mouse's daughter married  
Nirgi's young son;  
They had proud bridesmaids;  
The rooster drank from a golden cup,  
The hen from a golden bowl,  
The pig from a silk soy cup,  
The crow from a copper chariot,  
The titmouse from a tin teapot.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, little cuckoo,  
Sing, little eavesbird!  
The cuckoo does not cuckoo for us -  
The cuckoo cuckoos for the herdsman,  
Clucks for the milker,  
Sleeps for the thread-spinner,  
Knocks for the willow basket maker -  
Little cuckoo, little treasure.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The cat rises blue from the bog,  
Blue from the bog, red from the ground,  
Round over the forest,  
Through the forest.  
The moon rises blue from the bog,  
Blue from the bog, red from the ground,  
Round over the forest,  
Through the grove.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Over, over, rain showers!  
Over the shower, I wait.  
I endure the dew shower.  
Take the rain to Vigala land,  
Send the rain to Harju land,  
For the cattle of Vigala to drink,  
For the women of Harju to wash their hands,  
To wash their hands, to wash their scarves.  
In Harju land the men are dark,  
Men are dark, women are dark,  
Girls fiery,  
Boys with curly hair,  
Children like little flames.  
In our land the men are fair,  
Men are fair, women are fair,  
Girls very beautiful,  
Boys with golden hair,  
Children like cowslips.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
A hundred rainfalls on the Germans,  
But don’t rain on us.  
We don’t have a home dryer,  
Nor a white shirt bleacher.  
German home dryers,  
White shirt bleachers,  
Fine robe washers.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, little cuckoo,  
Helgi, gentle little bird.  
The cuckoo does not cuckoo to others,  
The cuckoo cuckoos to the herdsman,  
To Helgi, the hayfield worker,  
To the one who takes the cattle,  
To the one who cuts the grass,  
To the one who digs ditches.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The cuckoo calls on the top of the spruce  
Sings on the top of the broadleaf aspen  
I play on the top of the pine  
The titmouse dances on the top of the oak.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Who was beautifully blooming?
The pea was beautifully blooming,
The flower blossomed beautifully.
Who was round alone?
The pea was round alone.
Who was a bit elongated?
The bean was a bit elongated.
Who was lens-shaped?
The lentil was lens-shaped.
Who wore long trousers?
The oat wore long trousers.
Who had reddish hair?
The barley had reddish hair.
Who was naked?
The wheat was naked.
Who wore a silk shirt on their back?
The rye wore a silk shirt on its back.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh, you Mari berry-bringer,  
Berry-bringer, cattle herder,  
From the swamp, the one who carries gooseberries.  
Mari brought berries from other lands,  
Cranberries from across the rivers.  
Then the crane began to listen,  
The magpie started to deliberate.  
God grant daughters:  
A daughter is taken to a husband.  
Then the ox is slaughtered,  
The cow is butchered in the barn.  
Then the crane got a mouthful,  
The magpie got a bite,  
The old crow got a bellyful.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh, you Mari berry-bringer,  
Berry-bringer, cattle herder,  
From the swamp, the one who carries gooseberries.  
Mari brought berries from other lands,  
Cranberries from across the rivers.  
Then the crane began to listen,  
The magpie started to deliberate.  
God grant daughters:  
A daughter is taken to a husband.  
Then the ox is slaughtered,  
The cow is butchered in the barn.  
Then the crane got a mouthful,  
The magpie got a bite,  
The old crow got a bellyful.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Rui, rui, rui, rui Ruti's wedding  
The mouse's daughter got married,  
To Nirgi's youngest son.  
The wedding guests were so proud!  
The rooster had golden spurs,  
The crow had copper boots,  
The titmouse had a tin tail,  
The magpies were grey birds,  
The crows were poor birds.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What are you worried about for nothing? -  
Rye heads are already visible,  
Barley ears are turning green,  
Oat ears cover the ground,  
Peas decorate the clothesline,  
Beans bloom proudly on the clothesline,  
Cabbage grows on bright sunny days.  
Turnip spreads its leaves.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Mother Eltse, daughter Kaie,  
Come outside to look!  
The sky is dancing, the earth is rumbling,  
A rainbow strikes the violin,  
The stars play dice,  
The moon is a golden ring.

label: Töölaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
I wanted to go to the communal work  
I wanted to go without the communal meal  
There were ladles to wash the dogs  
The broth was too weak to move.  
Here I leave this place as well,  
I give you the meadow instead,  
Pastures to scare away the bear,  
Fields to harass the moose,  
Hedges for the hares to run through.  
I took a rifle from the peening bench  
Amur rifles from the window,  
Bear rifles from the chamber;  
I went to hunt the moose,  
To shoot the bear from the oats,  
To catch the wool from the grain.  
I shot the moose in the backside,  
The bear between the fur,  
The moose went to the forest in scattered places,  
The bear went to the forest in various directions,  
The wool flashed behind.

label: Töölaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
I wanted to go to the communal work  
I wanted to go without the communal meal  
There were ladles to wash the dogs  
The broth was too weak to move.  
Here I leave this place as well,  
I give you the meadow instead,  
Pastures to scare away the bear,  
Fields to harass the moose,  
Hedges for the hares to run through.  
I took a rifle from the peening bench  
Amur rifles from the window,  
Bear rifles from the chamber;  
I went to hunt the moose,  
To shoot the bear from the oats,  
To catch the wool from the grain.  
I shot the moose in the backside,  
The bear between the fur,  
The moose went to the forest in scattered places,  
The bear went to the forest in various directions,  
The wool flashed behind.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
In the forest, the birch grows well green  
Among the pine twigs.  
From there a rich man gets firewood,  
A bailiff gets a cartload,  
A poor man gets a wagonload.  
I cut down the wet pines,  
I knock on the spruces,  
I leave the birches to grow,  
The aspens to mourn the serf,  
The straight alders to the blue.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Is the cat plowing in Võõbu meadow?  
The pig is plowing, the wind is rushing,  
the crow is ready to sow oil,  
the crane is fiercely harvesting oil,  
the curlew is gathering,  
the wagtail is stacking the hay,  
the nightingale is raking around the rice field;  
the swallow put the dishes on the table,  
the bug is pulling inside,  
the hare is a huntsman;  
the duck is eager, the young drake,  
the fox is eager, the old fox,  
the otter was an experienced helper in the barn,  
the bear was a quick writer!

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The cuckoo calls, the spruce bends  
I sing, the treetop moves  
The boys walk in drought  
The girls walk in hand  
The cuckoo, go with a prayer  
Dear daughter, young maiden,  
Farm daughter, little plant  
Give my boys strength  
Bring my daughters home  
Four children to the table  
I will go to the city road tomorrow  
I will bring you three pairs of clogs  
One pair for carrying bones, another for stacking wood,  
The third pair for going to church.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The little red pig  
Said to the big pig  
He lied to the white one  
How there was barley bread  
How there was oats in the bag.  
Let's go rummaging in the garden  
We rummage the spruces sideways  
Then the junipers on the slope.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Why are our boys sad  
Why are the girls of our parish silent  
Mourning linen hair  
Because, because there are dear brothers  
That have caught cold from the frost  
Driven by the hail.  
The field is written with lightning  
Because the bride was not taken  
The dear horse remained unbought.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Let's go to the sheaf, foxes  
In the grain sheaf, squirrels  
The fox is in the sheaf bag  
The magpie was a trading fellow  
The duck was a ware packet  
The lizard pushed us in  
The wolf was the fast runner  
The bear was strong at writing  
Understood the estate's crops  
Sent big crops to the granary.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The river flowed beneath the roots  
Goldfish in the river  
The pike had large spots on the tip  
The bullheads had colorful sides.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The lord praised the sacred grove  
The king, indeed, the golden summer  
I praise the fertile soil  
The rotten thicket becomes moist.  
He plows the pieces, carries the stems  
This leads the girls to become wives  
Carries the girls to be young brides  
The elders indeed become leaders.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
If only I existed  
If only I were made  
I would know what I would do  
I would make a wooden horse  
That does not neigh at hayfields  
Nor shout at grainfields  
It neighs at riders on its back  
It shouts at those who lean on its back.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Hüpelik is my horse  
Täpelik was a little mare  
Hüpelik is the gray one of the sacred grove  
My path was time-bound  
Time-bound, full of obstacles  
Rocky, hilly  
Mountainous, tufted with moss.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The landlady beat the cat  
The maid cursed the cat  
What bad thing did the cat do  
The cat was pulled by another cat.  
Between two rooms  
In front of the new barn door.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The wolf was Uila's stable boy  
The bear was Kõrve's herdsman  
The fox was Riia's leg servant.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Neigh, neigh, O sacred grove  
Bellow, bellow, O merchant beetle  
Why do you neigh at the sacred grove  
Or why do you bellow at the market heap  
You neigh at those who sit on your back  
You bellow at those who jump on your back  
You bellow loudly from the granary  
Did you see my foal?  
What color is your horse  
In the morning squirrel-colored  
At sunrise dark-colored  
At midday salmon-colored  
In the evening my own color.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Why are the young men weak  
Silence, the boys of our parish  
Sad, tall hats  
Because, because, little brother  
Because the young men are weak  
Silence, the boys of our parish  
Sad, tall hats  
Because the firewood was taken cold  
Hay was driven away by frost  
The field was written by lightning  
Because the horse remained unbought  
The young woman remained unmarried  
Ribbons remain in the town’s maiden’s hair  
The girl’s gates remain in the shop  
Kalevs remained in the German cupboard  
The brooch remained on the friend's table  
The kettles remained in the stone cellar  
The knife remained on the corner of the shop  
Clog shoes remained on the door.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
What is there under our window?  
The sea under our window.  
What is there in the middle of the sea?  
A well in the middle of the sea.  
A post on the edge of the well.  
What is on top of the post?  
A sieve on top of the post.  
What is on the bottom of the sieve?  
A cuckoo on the bottom of the sieve.  
What is on the cuckoo’s hand?  
A child is on the cuckoo’s hand.  
What is on the child's chest?  
A brooch on the child's chest.  
What is in the middle of the brooch?  
A ring in the middle of the brooch.  
Where did this ring come from?  
From the fingers of soldiers,  
From the necks of merchants,  
From the ears of shop boys.  
I am going to cut an oak tree,  
From the crown will come a singing board,  
From the middle will come a spinning stool,  
From the root will come after God.  
What will the shavings be used for?  
They will become the sister’s praise chest.  
It fastens even while leaving,  
Sings children while making too,  
Fastens like a church bell,  
Sings like the only one on the ship.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo little cuckoo  
Since we got the beach money  
My throat can't take it  
The Murga children are singing  
Planting apple trees  
Growing a vegetable garden.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Feed the pig, the sow, the piglet  
Dear gray-banded one  
Golden-maned little male pig  
Iron-maned little working pig  
Go to the forest to walk  
Walk the hills, walk the pines  
Walk the spruces on the ridge  
Booted ones on the backside  
Come home for Christmas  
Quickly bring the white meat  
A cubit of lard to the backside.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Sisters bloom on the hill  
Already a leaf waves in the tree  
A leaf in the tree, grass in the ground  
Green hay ripples  
Already a rye head is visible  
Wheat comes from the ear  
Oat spikelet covers the ground  
Barley spikelet turns green  
Beans are flooding with flowers  
Peas nod on the stalk  
Tails turn blue for the pig  
A horse neighs in the grove  
A foal calls to the market  
A deer bends in the heath  
A birch foot swings in the bog  
Let time pass, let the day roll  
The horse from the grove sleeps deeply  
A bear from the market’s foal  
A knife from the heath’s deer  
An axe from the bog’s birch foot.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The cuckoo calls, the spruce bends  
I play, the pine bends  
I have cuckoo chicks in my hand  
Cuckoo daughters on my lap  
The cuckoo comes to my prayer  
Give me my children on my hands  
I am going to town tomorrow  
I will bring three pairs of shoes from town  
One pair will be new ankle boots  
The others afterwards.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Virgins, young girls
What are you thinking about for so long
How long will you make noise
The rye head already appears
The barley ear is greening
The wheat ear is coming out of the sheath
The oat ear covers the ground
The pea is growing pods
The bean blooms proudly
The cabbage grows heads
The lentils are growing pods.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
My sisters, don't worry  
Surely we will find bread in the leaves of the tree  
In the leaves of the tree, in the grass on the ground  
The pasture encourages the meadow hay  
Break the branches, maiden  
Break the branches, withering birch  
Leave the tender treetop  
Leave the treetops for the bird to fly  
The bird to fly, the wind to come  
At nightfall below the night  
Tochering other black grouse  
The male black grouse cooed  
The female black grouse cooed  
The golden eagles cooed  
The young ducks chattered  
Where must I go  
Where must I build my nest,  
To the clearing, to the ravine  
To the thick beach grove  
Where the German dogs cannot reach  
The lord’s hounds  
The sister’s meaner jesters  
The brother’s little dogs in harness.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
My sisters, don't worry  
Surely we will find bread in the leaves of the tree  
In the leaves of the tree, in the grass on the ground  
The pasture encourages the meadow hay  
Break the branches, maiden  
Break the branches, withering birch  
Leave the tender treetop  
Leave the treetops for the bird to fly  
The bird to fly, the wind to come  
At nightfall below the night  
Tochering other black grouse  
The male black grouse cooed  
The female black grouse cooed  
The golden eagles cooed  
The young ducks chattered  
Where must I go  
Where must I build my nest,  
To the clearing, to the ravine  
To the thick beach grove  
Where the German dogs cannot reach  
The lord’s hounds  
The sister’s meaner jesters  
The brother’s little dogs in harness.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Bring the rain, water the rye
Bring the rain towards Vigala
Drive the speed towards Harju
To water the cattle of Vigala
To wash the hands of the women of Harju
To wash the hands, to wash the kerchiefs
Women of Harju, gray women
Gray women, black men
The girls became angry
The boys, half pagans
In our land, men are stupid
Men are stupid, women are stubborn
The girls became beautiful,
The boys are half Germans.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The mouse faced the bottom of the wall  
Started threatening the cat  
Wait-wait, cat, well-well cat  
Where is my little spruce boy  
My five little nephews  
The cat heard, an answer sounded  
From the black cat’s hole in the mouth  
From the gray cat’s hole in the mouth  
Between the speckled bells.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Owl owl little boy  
Who was running along the sand  
The hare was running along the sand  
Owl owl little boy  
Who was sneaking through the swamp  
The wolf was sneaking through the swamp  
Owl owl little boy  
Who was stumbling along the road  
The horse was stumbling along the road  
Owl owl little boy  
Who flew into the alder grove  
The foal flew into the alder grove.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Do you remember last year  
Where last year we ate food  
Ate food, drank water  
With a knife, a silver plate  
With a gold fork  
In the town I forget the knife  
I use the gold fork  
There grew a sleepy forest  
A golden fly in the sleepy forest  
You have a brother, I have another  
I put my brother to cut wood  
You put your brother to plow  
He cuts  
He plows the middle fields  
We fed pieces to the pigs  
Then we tenderly come together  
Tenderly to sleep together  
To weave golden belts together.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I will make a honey drink  
I will make a spirit jar  
Then I will put it in the garden shed  
The crooked one thought the moon was shining  
The old man blushed during the day  
My ladles were glowing  
My honey boards were shining  
Then I go to Miku's room  
What will be done in that room  
Bear baked fish  
The little mill broke wood  
The miller moaned in his head.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Our little workboys,  
If these are little oak stumps.  
Wall beaters, tree cutters,  
Shakers of the whole forest,  
We cut down wet pines,  
Wet pines, dry spruces;  
We leave the birches growing,  
Birch branches groaning  
Branches groan like a father  
Leaves mourn like a mother.  
Break the branches, maiden,  
Break the branches, plague two,  
Then leave the tops for the birds to fly,  
For the birds to fly, for the pigeons to come,  
For the swallow to fly over,  
For the capercaillie to make its nest early.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The horse runs because of the reins,
Because of the thin whip,
Because of the broad lash.
Let me say it again,
Let me sing it back again:
The horse does not run because of the reins,
Nor because of the thin whip,
Nor because of the broad lash—
The horse runs because of the hay,
It leaps because of the barley,
Because of the young man's spirit.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I am a sailor's son,  
A dear man, a fisherman's son,  
I look for dill under the cabbage,  
Under the shirt of the seasoned ones.  
I put the bigger one in my mouth,  
And throw the smaller one into the water.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Row the swing, pull the swing,  
Row across three forests;  
There were three forests side by side:  
One was the golden spruce forest,  
The second the precious birch forest,  
The third the forest with apple trees.  
The one that was the golden spruce forest  
Was the forest of young men.  
The one that was the precious birch forest  
Was the forest of young women.  
The one that was the forest with apple trees  
Was the forest of young girls.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Row the swing, row the swing,
Row beyond the three fields;
There were three fields in a row:
One was the rye field with ergot,
The second was the dear oat field,
The third was the beautiful wheat.
The one with the rye field with ergot
Was the field of young women.
The one with the dear oat field
Was the field of young men.
The one with the beautiful wheat
Was the field of young girls.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, my little sisters,  
What can be seen from the hill?  
An apple tree can be seen from the hill.  
How many branches does the apple tree have?  
The apple tree had three branches.  
How many blossoms does the top branch have?  
The top branch had three blossoms.  
How many apples does the blossom have?  
The blossom had three apples.  
One was the apple of young men,  
The second the apple of young women,  
The third my own apple.  
With this I deceive the hidden ones,  
I pick the young men,  
I try to follow customs  
Whether there is a hint of whisky,  
Or a slender plate of bread,  
A cunning beer jug.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, brothers alive,  
Where have they made the swing?  
They made the swing below the village,  
By the guests’ resting places,  
By the village boys’ stick places.  
Where was this swing brought from?  
Swing brought from Kei  
Kei village, Pei homestead.  
Posts brought from Ammutasta  
Beams from Sinisalusta  
Axles brought from Võhmutalta.  
Swing smiths, brothers alive,  
Little sisters, little ladies.  
Pearl earrings glistening.  
Let’s go to swing,  
The boy comes to row the swing,  
To sing the swing song,  
Rows from the high place, reaches the swift,  
So that I would be noticed for a long time  
Be talked about a lot, be remembered long,  
So that I would be noticed across the forest:  
Across three spruce forests.  
Three forests were side by side:  
One was an apple tree forest  
It belonged to the young youths,  
The second a dear birch forest  
It belonged to the young maiden,  
The third a precious spruce forest  
It belonged to the young men.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow is a gentle little bird  
Under our soil,  
Under the wall of Aganik.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What is there under our yard?  
What is there under the seashore?  
A column under the seashore!  
What is on the end of the column?  
A brooch was on the end of the column!  
What is on the edge of the brooch?  
A ring on the edge of the brooch!  
Where did this ring come from?  
The ring came from the war,  
From the finger of a soldier.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Jüri told me  
That he had been in the moose field,  
And the bear was at the haystack.  
I took the rifle from the attic,  
The horn from under the eaves,  
I went to watch for the moose.  
The moose went into the forest here and there,  
The bear followed in pairs.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The sky danced, the earth thudded,  
Clouds whistled and blew.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Sisters, darlings,  
Young maidens,  
Where last year we sowed flax,  
Sowed flax, drank honey,  
There grew a buttery forest,  
A buttery forest, golden spruce,  
Silver birches.  
Listen, brothers, listen, kinsmen,  
In healthy bodies, a sharp blade,  
Make a strike sharp as fire,  
Cut down the buttery forest,  
The buttery forest, golden spruce,  
Silver birches.  
Then make buttery plows,  
Buttery plows, golden oats,  
Silver rye ears.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The fox was a thigh-stabber  
The wren was a wind-chaser  
The sun was a sweeper  
The ghost was a sack-stuffer.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The flax wears a silk shirt  
Oats wear long pants  
Barley has a prickly crown on its head  
Rye has rust-colored hair  
Pea is busy sowing  
Lentils tie around the leg  
Cabbage swirls on top of the hill  
Let's go to steal the flax  
To pull gold from the sack  
We steal linseed in clusters  
Burdens in six embraces  
We take it home to the grandmother  
The woman makes the box sink  
To grow under the lid  
It grew for one month, it grew for two  
Three quarters of a month  
A quarter of five months.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Here had gone the partridge tracks  
Partridge tracks, black grouse tracks  
Through the willow, the duck tracks  
Straight the squirrel tracks  
Here the partridge halted  
The root of the lily of the valley, the rat’s wedding  
The mouse’s daughter got married  
To the younger son of the black grouse  
They had splendid wedding guests  
The crow wore copper shoes  
The rooster had golden spurs  
The titmouse with a tin bell.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Here had gone the partridge tracks  
Partridge tracks, black grouse tracks  
Through the willow, the duck tracks  
Straight the squirrel tracks  
Here the partridge halted  
The root of the lily of the valley, the rat’s wedding  
The mouse’s daughter got married  
To the younger son of the black grouse  
They had splendid wedding guests  
The crow wore copper shoes  
The rooster had golden spurs  
The titmouse with a tin bell.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The cold chills me  
I am cold in my hand  
My toe hurts  
Wait-wait, cold, no-no, cold  
Let my room be made  
But the home is still being knocked on  
And the house is still being painted  
Then I sit in the sauna  
You howl outside the door  
You shout between the rooms.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Row the swing, swing the rowboat  
Row over three forests  
One was a fir forest  
It was the forest of young men  
The second was a birch forest  
It was the forest of young women  
The third was an apple forest  
It was the forest of young maidens.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What are you mooing, old cow  
You are mooing, you are a pregnant cow  
You are talking, old red one  
You are mooing like crazy bullocks  
From the blue pea manure  
From the red pullet's dung heap.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Crow, quiet little bird  
Laid eggs in the hollow below  
Called the maiden to pick them  
The maiden picked a full sip  
Piret a full long apron  
Maret a full Mardi's shirt  
Villager a full hat.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, little cuckoo  
Helgi's hay bird  
My cuckoo does not cuckoo  
The cuckoo cuckoos to the shepherd boy  
To Helgi, the hay cutter  
To the willow branch cracker  
To the one who takes the meadow  
Why do you chirp, little bug  
Filthy fly, so dirty  
You are servant of the bug  
Son of the filthy fly  
From your mouth drips urine  
Below is the row of teeth  
Above is the long tongue  
Let me sing the flax song  
From my mouth drips gold  
Below is silver of teeth  
Above is the fine skin of the tongue  
Where did this singer come from  
Came from here, got from here  
Came from the pigsty  
From the pig’s barn with straw  
Where did the child get these songs,  
I took the stories from the suckerfish’s mouth  
From between the teeth of the pike.  
Tunes from a crooked tail  
A girl went to some wedding  
Rode to the seventh (village)  
Carried me under the belt  
Kept the apron strings  
He drank from the cupboard, drank from the pitcher  
From the bottom of the flute  
From the pipe-maker’s cup  
He drank himself, gave me some  
Because I became a singer  
Stories from the girl closing the door  
Words tangled in knots  
I was told to close the reading  
The singer was my father  
The singer was my mother  
Both singer sisters.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What is beneath our yard  
The sea is beneath our yard  
What is in the middle of the sea  
There was a well in the middle of the sea  
What is in the middle of the well  
A pillar in the middle of the well  
What swayed on the top of the pillar  
A cuckoo swayed on the top of the pillar.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow, a meek little bird  
Laid an egg under our yard  
I pick a full sip  
The village boy a full hat  
The village wife a small full.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Row the swing, row the swing  
Row the swing to that land  
Where the log forest shines  
Where the alder shrubby forest rises  
Where the pine board forest grows  
There are many fish in the river  
Big graylings, black-backed  
Wide salmon, with broken tails.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The heads were bulls, big bulls  
They go with sledges to the village  
The sledge carries bundles of firewood  
The heads were bulls, big bulls,  
Even more bulls pulled  
A hundred fathoms of fir wood  
A thousand fathoms of pine wood.  
Even more bulls pulled.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Our little woodcutters  
These little oak stumps  
Haymakers, wood carriers  
The greatest forest swingers  
We cut down the wet pines  
Wet pines, sad spruces  
We leave the birches growing  
Birch branches crooning  
Branches croon to the father  
Leaves mourn the mother  
Break the branches, maiden  
Then leave the treetops for the birds to fly  
For the birds to fly, for the dove to come  
For the swallow to rest  
For the capercaillie to make a home.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
My horse runs because of the rein  
Because of the thin whip  
Because of the wide lash  
Let me say it again  
Let me sing it back again:  
The horse does not run because of the rein  
Nor because of the thin whip  
Nor because of the wide lash  
The horse runs because of care  
It leaps because of the oat crib  
Because of the young man's spirit.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I am the son of a sailor man  
Dear man, son of a fisherman  
I search inside a full dill  
Perch under the shirt  
I put the bigger ones in my mouth  
The smaller ones, I throw away.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Row the boat, swing the boat  
Row across three forests  
There were three forests side by side  
One was a precious spruce forest  
The second a dear birch forest  
The third an orchard forest  
Which was the precious spruce forest  
That was the forest of young men  
Which was the dear birch forest  
That was the forest of young women  
Which was the orchard forest  
That was the forest of young maidens  
Row the boat, swing the boat  
Row across three fields  
There were three fields side by side  
One was the rye field of Ruuge  
The second a dear oat field  
The third was a beautiful wheat field  
Which was the rye field of Ruuge  
That was the field of young women  
Which was the dear oat field  
That was the field of young men  
Which was the beautiful wheat field  
That was the field of young maidens.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The little blue-tailed bird  
The hunter went to the forest with a gun  
The hunter shot the bird with the gun  
The bird fell straight down  
Its blood ran profusely  
The hunter went and took the bird from the ground  
The hunter gave the bird to the gentleman.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Look where they have made a swing  
Made a swing below the village  
To the guests' gathering places  
To the village boys' stick fields  
From where the swings were brought  
Swings brought from the Kei village  
Kei village, Pei farmstead  
Fathers brought from long ago  
Moss from the blue pastures  
Straw brought from the meadows  
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
Little sisters, dear ones  
Silver beads shining  
Let's go to swing on the swings  
The boy comes to swing on the swings  
To sing the swing song  
He comes from afar, rows eagerly  
So that I shine wider than the field  
Shine wider, cover the land  
So that I shine over the forest  
Over three spruce forests  
There were three forests nearby  
One was an apple orchard forest  
That belonged to the young women  
Another a dear birch forest  
That was the young girls'  
The third a precious spruce forest  
That was the young men’s forest.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand my little sister  
What can be seen from the hill  
An apple tree can be seen from the hill  
How many branches are there on the apple tree  
There were three branches on the apple tree  
How many blossoms were on the branch  
There were three blossoms on the branch  
How many apples on the blossom  
There were three apples on the blossom  
One was the apple of young men  
The other the apple of young women  
The third my own apple  
With this I deceive the hidden ones  
I pick young men  
I try to take the customs along  
Is there a whistle of wine  
Or is there a slender loaf  
A clever beer jug.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow, the modest little bird  
In the nest under our hill  
Under the Akanik wall.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
The village's gentle young men  
Take the swings to the cellar  
The aisles down to the barns  
The swing boards to the barns.  
Haytime is coming  
The rye head is already visible  
The barley ear is greening  
The oat ear covers the field  
The wheat will be ripe soon  
Go swing high  
So that I can see a lot of land  
I can see over three fields  
Three fields side by side  
One was the rye field of Ruuge  
The second was the dear oat field  
The third is working on wheat.  
What was the rye field of Ruuge?  
That was the young women's field  
What was the dear oat field?  
That was the young men's field  
What is working on the wheat?  
That was the young maidens'.  
Up, up, little swing  
Up past the apple trees  
Down, down, little swing  
Down past the berry bushes  
Go swing high  
So that I can see a lot of land  
I can see over three forests  
Three forests side by side  
One was the golden spruce forest  
The second was the dear birch forest  
The third was the true apple tree forest  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
That was the young men's forest  
What was the dear birch forest?  
That was the young women's forest  
What was the true apple tree forest?  
That was the young maidens'  
Go swing high  
So that I can see a lot of land  
I see the field, I value a lot  
If I don't even see  
My wreath shines red  
And it glimmers on my neck.  
A ring shines also on my finger  
Another on the other hand  
The third in a golden secret  
Let it end, let it remain  
Let it end until tomorrow  
Until next Friday  
Until Pentecost  
Until dear Jaanipäev (Midsummer).

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
The village's gentle young men  
Take the swings to the cellar  
The aisles down to the barns  
The swing boards to the barns.  
Haytime is coming  
The rye head is already visible  
The barley ear is greening  
The oat ear covers the field  
The wheat will be ripe soon  
Go swing high  
So that I can see a lot of land  
I can see over three fields  
Three fields side by side  
One was the rye field of Ruuge  
The second was the dear oat field  
The third is working on wheat.  
What was the rye field of Ruuge?  
That was the young women's field  
What was the dear oat field?  
That was the young men's field  
What is working on the wheat?  
That was the young maidens'.  
Up, up, little swing  
Up past the apple trees  
Down, down, little swing  
Down past the berry bushes  
Go swing high  
So that I can see a lot of land  
I can see over three forests  
Three forests side by side  
One was the golden spruce forest  
The second was the dear birch forest  
The third was the true apple tree forest  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
That was the young men's forest  
What was the dear birch forest?  
That was the young women's forest  
What was the true apple tree forest?  
That was the young maidens'  
Go swing high  
So that I can see a lot of land  
I see the field, I value a lot  
If I don't even see  
My wreath shines red  
And it glimmers on my neck.  
A ring shines also on my finger  
Another on the other hand  
The third in a golden secret  
Let it end, let it remain  
Let it end until tomorrow  
Until next Friday  
Until Pentecost  
Until dear Jaanipäev (Midsummer).

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
A leaf already lies on the tree  
A leaf on the tree, grass on the ground  
The meadow's hay is greening  
A birch swings by the swampy foot  
The heather bends on the heath  
The cat takes hay from the meadow  
The sickle takes hay from the meadow  
The cat from the swampy birch foot  
The axe from the swampy birch foot  
The cat from the heath's heather  
The knife from the heath's heather  
The sickle has taken the beauty of the carcass  
The scythe has caressed the meadows.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
A hundred rains, pour German rain,  
Leave me unwatered by dew.  
I have no home dryer,  
To whiten a white shirt,  
To wash a fine shirt,  
Who will whiten my white clothes?  
Who will bleach the fine clothes?  
If only it were under my control,  
Control of mind, control of language,  
Control of mouth, control of heart,  
Control over white hairs,  
Then I would know what I would do:  
I would make gentlemen into oxen,  
Ladies onto ragged clothes,  
And I myself would become a laborer.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
A hundred rains, pour German rain,  
Leave me unwatered by dew.  
I have no home dryer,  
To whiten a white shirt,  
To wash a fine shirt,  
Who will whiten my white clothes?  
Who will bleach the fine clothes?  
If only it were under my control,  
Control of mind, control of language,  
Control of mouth, control of heart,  
Control over white hairs,  
Then I would know what I would do:  
I would make gentlemen into oxen,  
Ladies onto ragged clothes,  
And I myself would become a laborer.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Our little woodcutters  
These little oak stumps  
Haymakers, bundle carriers  
The most forest shakers  
I myself cut down wet pines  
I tapped dry spruces  
I left the aspens growing  
Straight alders blueing  
Green birches turning green  
Leaves mourned the father  
Branches groaned for the mother  
Roots for the sons, for the daughters.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, manor lords
Guess, village elders
What was under our yard?
The sea was under our yard
What was in the middle of the sea?
A well was in the middle of the sea
What was on the edge of the well?
A post was on the edge of the well
What was at the top of the post?
A wine vat was at the top of the post
What was on the wine vat?
A cask was on the wine vat
What was on the cask?
A sieve was on the cask
What was at the bottom of the sieve?
A pitcher was at the bottom of the sieve
What was under the pitcher’s lid?
An eagle was under the pitcher’s lid
What was under the eagle’s wing?
A cuckoo was under the eagle’s wing
What was on the cuckoo’s crest?
A silk moth was on the cuckoo’s crest
Where did that silk moth come from?
From the finger bones of soldiers
From the neck bones of ushers
From the kneecaps of boys.
We trill and we frolic,
We dedicate it to Kai
To Anne, sick with the field
Soon to everyone, to Kai
Finally to Vilbu Mai
To Trixie-Traxie Triinu.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little red piglet  
Lied to the big pig  
That there was barley there  
And oats there  
And rye bread to eat.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What is visible on the hill  
An apple tree is visible on the hill  
How many branches does the apple tree have  
The apple tree had three branches  
How many blossoms are on the branches  
There were three blossoms on the branches  
How many apples are on the blossoms  
There were three apples on the blossoms  
One was honeyed with mead  
The second was waxed with beeswax  
The third was written in gold  
The one honeyed with mead  
That was my own apple  
The one waxed with beeswax  
That was the apple of the young men  
The one written in gold  
With that I deceive the elders  
I pick the young men.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little rooster, little hen  
Rushed off to scurry in a race  
The rooster went under a rich thigh  
The hen to a poor hut in the barn  
The rooster got one spike of barley  
The hen got two oat grains  
They put a plank on top  
Rolled it to the mill  
Paid Matt the miller  
Paid the owner of the other stuff  
Started making beer  
Got a pig’s horn full  
A goat’s ear full.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Roll, roll, little day  
An hour full of gold  
May you reach God's time  
Because of the kings' gold  
Because of our poor labor  
Because of the rich people's money  
The king had plenty of gold  
I, the poor one, had plenty of labor  
The rich had plenty of money  
Roll, roll, little day.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, little cuckoo  
Helgi, gentle little bird  
Won't you cuckoo for me  
You cuckoo for the shepherd  
Helgis for the hay cutter  
You thump for the ploughman  
You bounce for the reap harvester  
You bang for the willow washer.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Heads were bulls, wide horns  
They came clattering into the village  
Biting, they came down to the yard  
Shouting on the furrow   
A golden yoke on the head  
An iron hail inside  
They brought a big spruce from Finland  
From the forest, a broad-headed pedant  
From there came a nice little bundle  
A chronic little cover  
Clattering shut in the pan  
Singing open and closed  
Clattering like a church bell  
Crooning like ship doors.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I went to walk in the forest  
On a Sunday morning  
On a weekday evening  
What I found there in the forest  
I found a crane plowing  
An Araka plowing  
A capercaillie raising a field  
Then I struck the crane on its side  
On the Araka’s shoulder  
The crane fell down awkwardly  
I fell down on my knees  
Make me a couple of wheel guards  
Make another pair of warp sticks  
What can bring the crane home  
Ann went to milk the cranes  
Milking the crane a full pail  
Pounded a full basket  
Dipped a full tin  
But then came the lone enemy child  
Stuck a finger in the milk jar  
Ann hit the child’s finger  
The child went crying to the corner  
Screaming, went to the threshold  
Ann went to quiet the child  
You be quiet, enemy child  
Your father will come without a lid  
Your mother without a center  
In the sieve brings a fingerful  
In the box brings a tufty handful.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Quietly I ride through Hiie  
Secretly through Salum  
The Hiie roosters did not hear me  
Hiie roosters, Hiie dogs  
Hiie’s younger brother heard  
He quickly snapped to touch me  
He glanced quickly  
Golden kettles in hand  
Silver cloth on the kettle  
Set the cloth on fire  
Silver threads blazing  
Who was quick to put it out  
Kalev's young lad  
He was quick to put it out  
He brought clouds when coming  
Other clouds when leaving  
The cloud has water tears  
The tear had long feathers  
The feather has a blue tail  
On the tail was a silk bed  
Silk bed, thread play  
There slept the major’s lady  
Breathed the Hiie maiden  
Rested the wedding bride.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers  
Do not swing me out of anger  
Do not spitefully tease me  
I am the angry rooster  
I am the leaf of anger rolling  
I am the birch leaf shouting  
If I fall, who will pay for me?  
My side will pay a pair of oxen  
The other side, another pair  
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row the swing to the land  
Where our timber forest shines  
The tall spruces shimmer  
The straight alders turn blue  
What was the dear spruce forest?  
The third forest with apple trees  
What was the cherished birch forest?  
What was the forest with apple trees?  
What was the dear spruce forest?  
It was the forest of our men  
What was the cherished birch forest?  
That was the forest of our women  
What was the forest with apple trees?  
That was the forest of young maidens  
Row the swing, reach the swing  
Row over three lakes  
One was the lake of beer  
The second lake of mead  
The third lake of vodka  
What was the lake of beer?  
That was the lake of our women  
What was the lake of mead?  
That was the lake of young maidens  
What was the lake of vodka?  
That was the lake of our men  
Now let me down, I would ask  
If you won't let me, I won't ask  
So that I may sway until evening  
Swing until midnight  
Sing until the wide dawn  
The village alder milks the cow  
The village spruce strains the milk  
The village birch sends the herd.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The bee, the little bird  
The horsefly was the second little boy  
Crawls along the grass  
Flies along the forest leaves  
Walks along the straw stalk.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The rabbit sighed in the pasture  
Could not fill its belly  
Nor enjoy any fun or joy  
It went to the manor fields  
The grey wolves came out of the forest  
Tore apart the tender shoots  
The meat went to the town to the falconer  
The tail to the salve maker  
The skin to the Narva conscript  
The head to the manor’s cook.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Up, up, little swing  
Up along the apple trees  
Down along the apple trees  
What I saw going up  
What I saw coming down  
There were three fields side by side  
One was a field of rye  
The second a precious field of oats  
The third a handsome field of wheat  
The fourth was a field of barley  
What was the rye field  
It was the masters' field  
What was the precious oats field  
It was the masters' field  
What was the handsome wheat field  
It was the young maidens' field  
What was the beautiful barley field  
It was the young men's field  
Up, up, little swing  
Up along the apple trees  
Down along the apple trees  
What I saw going up  
What I saw coming down  
There were three forests side by side  
One was a precious spruce forest  
The second a precious birch forest  
The third a precious pine forest  
The fourth was a forest of apple trees  
What was the spruce forest  
It was the masters' forest  
What was the birch forest  
It was the young men's forest  
What was the pine forest  
It was the young maidens' forest  
What was the apple tree forest  
It was the young men's forest  
Up, up, little swing  
Up along the apple trees  
Down along the apple trees  
What I saw going up  
What I saw coming down  
There were three lakes side by side  
One was a lake of beer  
The second a lake of kvass  
The third a lake of mead  
The fourth a lake of vodka  
What was the lake of beer  
It was the masters' lake  
What was the lake of kvass  
It was the masters' lake  
What was the lake of mead  
It was the young maidens' lake  
What was the lake of vodka  
It was the young men's lake

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Jüri told me  
He said he had been in the elk field  
The bear was resting in the forest shade  
I took the gun from the shelf  
From under the row of grass horns  
Then I go to hunt elk  
To shoot the bear in the forest  
I shot the elk in the backside  
Between the bear's fur  
The elk went trotting into the swamp  
The bear resting in the forest.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Shine, shine little sun  
Brightly, little world  
Shine always on us  
Do not shine on the shore  
Shore men eat fish  
We poor eat water bread  
Tiny milk bread  
Thin beer bread  
Flat kvass bread.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Shine, shine little sun  
Brightly, little world  
Shine always on us  
Do not shine on the shore  
Shore men eat fish  
We poor eat water bread  
Tiny milk bread  
Thin beer bread  
Flat kvass bread.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Rustle, rustle, bird  
Three were from the ear field  
One was the rye field with rust  
The second was the dear oat field  
The third was the beautiful wheat  
Which was the rye field with rust  
That was the field of young men  
Which was the dear oat field  
That was the field of young married women  
Which was the beautiful wheat  
That was the field of young maidens.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh, that beautiful time  
Or that lovely spring  
The leaf is on the tree, the grass is on the ground  
The hay is being mowed in the meadow  
The juniper bends in the heath  
The birch swings wide in the swamp  
The maiden sings on the swing  
Who takes the hay from the meadow?  
The scythe takes the hay from the meadow  
Who takes the juniper from the heath?  
The scythe takes the juniper from the heath  
Who takes the birch from the wide swamp?  
The knife takes the birch from the wide swamp  
Who takes the maiden from the swing?  
The tender lad, the dear brother  
He takes the maiden from the swing.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, little cuckoo  
Helgi, gentle little bird  
The cuckoo does not cuckoo to me  
The cuckoo cuckoos to the herdsman  
To Helgi, the hay cutter  
Clacking to the woodpecker  
I am in the forest like a bear cub  
At the top of the spruce like a titmouse.  
In the birch top a songbird  
In the aspen top a leaf bird.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, little cuckoo  
Helgi, gentle little bird  
The cuckoo does not cuckoo to me  
The cuckoo cuckoos to the herdsman  
To Helgi, the hay cutter  
To the wood chopper  
I am in the forest like a moose  
At the top of the spruce like a titmouse  
In the birch crown a songbird  
In the aspen crown a leaf bird.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Hiiu, hiiu, the weather is cold  
Hiiu, hiiu, the hay is black.  
The war horse did not eat it  
The wedding rune did not carry it away  
Let the spider come to the pillar  
It eats hay, moves its lips  
Waits for new growth  
At the edge of the field turning green.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Roll, roll, little day  
Roll because of the evening  
Because of the money of the rich  
Because of the toil of us poor  
Kings have much gold  
The rich have much money  
But we poor have much toil  
There is no time to roll during the day  
The day burns on the master's head  
Restricts the tall maiden's head  
But Andres, the poor boy,  
Go, bring a burden from the sea  
Andres did not accept the words  
I went slowly  
Along the small road  
Along the low ground  
Along the small waves of water  
Along the small pools of flame  
The lazy one waits for evening  
The slow day rolling  
I wait for the morning  
For another day to rise  
For the third dawn to become yellow  
Evening comes unexpectedly  
Twilight comes quietly  
Evening brings straw to the house  
Twilight puts to sleep  
The night spirit wakes up  
Dawn sends us to the distant evening  
White spreads apart  
The day scatters us haphazardly.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Roll, roll, little day  
Roll because of the evening  
Because of the money of the rich  
Because of the toil of us poor  
Kings have much gold  
The rich have much money

The day burns on the master's head

Andres did not accept the words  
I went slowly  
Along the small road  
Along the low ground  
Along the small waves of water  
Along the small pools of flame

The slow day rolling  
I wait for the morning
