label: Looduslaulud
translation:
A tiny little red piglet  
A golden-haired little piglet  
Spoke to the big pig  
But lied to the white pig  
Run a bit and feed your head  
Don’t go to the straw grain  
One comes with straw on his back  
Another comes with a staff on his back  
The third comes after the dog  
They went to catch the pig  
The pig had added excessive weight  
And had swollen fat.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
We have men, we have forest
We have logs, we have wood
We have lumberjacks
Cutting down pine forest
Gathering spruce forest
Leaving birches to grow
Birch branches to protect
Birch leaves to mourn
Branches protect the father
Leaves mourn the mother
The buds, however, weep for poor children.

label: Töölaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Rain for a hundred days, so one could sleep  
If the storm came, I would doze off  
The slave can sleep a little  
But the gap lulls the sleep away  
Still, leaning against the walls  
An axe on a crow's perch  
A prayer on the foundation log.  
A hundred rains to Harjumaa  
Take the rain towards Vigala  
Do not rain on our land  
In Harjumaa, the men were black  
The boys were candle flames  
The children were sweet little oxen  
In our land, the men were white  
The men white, the women white  
The boys golden-haired  
The children cowslips  
The girls fire-beautiful.

label: Töölaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Rain for a hundred days, so one could sleep  
If the storm came, I would doze off  
The slave can sleep a little  
But the gap lulls the sleep away  
Still, leaning against the walls  
An axe on a crow's perch  
A prayer on the foundation log.  
A hundred rains to Harjumaa  
Take the rain towards Vigala  
Do not rain on our land  
In Harjumaa, the men were black  
The boys were candle flames  
The children were sweet little oxen  
In our land, the men were white  
The men white, the women white  
The boys golden-haired  
The children cowslips  
The girls fire-beautiful.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
A hundred, a hundred little rains  
Don’t rain on us  
Rain on the German children  
We don’t have a home dryer  
A washer of new clothes  
A bleacher of white shirts  
A stitcher of handkerchiefs.  
German home dryers  
A washer of new clothes  
A bleacher of white shirts  
A washer of fine shirts  
A stitcher of handkerchiefs.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh, me poor white sheep,  
I crouch in the village street.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh dear, oh dear, the rats' wedding  
The mouse's daughter got married  
To the youngest son of Nirgi  
What a splendid wedding it was.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The rye head already appears,  
Barley ear is turning green,  
Wheat is just beginning to come out of the husk,  
Peas are setting their tendrils,  
Vetch is intertwining its vines,  
Beans are growing their pods,

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, my sisters  
Know, know, wise women  
What can be understood  
Or even counted  
Who was the western shepherd boy  
Who was the forest herdsman  
The bear was the forest herdsman  
Who was the desert midwife  
The hare was the desert midwife.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The rye head already seems
The barley ear is greening
The oat ear covers the ground
The wheat is coming out of the sheath
The pea sets pods
The bean grows pods
The vetch puts out leaves
It must be June now
So that the hay has been made
The rye ears have filled
The barleys have been put into the barns

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
What can be seen from the hill  
An apple tree can be seen from the hill  
How many branches does the apple tree have  
The apple tree had one branch  
How many flowers are on the branch  
There were three flowers on the branch  
How many apples are on the flower  
There was one apple on the flower  
That apple is beautiful  
Beautiful on the evening side  
Red on the daylight side  
Cross-shaped on the Riga side  
Knobby on the Narva side  
I went to look for apples  
What would touch my knees  
Or what would crack on my chest  
Maybe it touches my knees  
Cracking all over my chest  
Knife shining and sparkling  
I took the knife to the courtyard  
I put it on the lord’s table  
I asked, I guessed  
Who brought this knife here  
The knife is worth a lot of money  
A thousand barrels of wheat  
A hundred sacks of rye.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What can be seen from the hill  
An apple tree can be seen from the hill  
How many branches does the apple tree have?  
The apple tree had three branches  
How many flowers on the branches?  
There were three flowers on the branches  
How many apples on the flowers?  
There were three apples on the flowers  
One was a honeyed apple  
The second was a waxed apple  
The third was made golden and variegated  
What was the honeyed apple?  
That was my own apple  
What was the waxed apple?  
That was my dear spouse’s apple  
What was the golden and variegated one?  
With it I cheat the fiend  
I pick young men  
Is it for vile vodka  
Or for lean bread  
Or for a sly beer jug?  
If it’s not for vile vodka  
Nor for lean bread  
I thank my aunt, I thank my uncle  
For raising a good man.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, young men  
Guess, eager boys  
What can be seen from the hill  
What is sprouting green on the field  
What to understand, young girls  
An apple tree can be seen from the hill  
A spike is sprouting green on the field  
How many branches does the apple tree have  
The apple tree had three branches  
Three branches had the apple tree  
How many blossoms on the branches  
There were three blossoms on the branches  
How many apples on the blossoms  
There were three apples on the blossoms  
One was bitten by a worm  
Another was coated with wax  
The third was written on with gold  
What was bitten by the worm  
That is my own apple

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh wonder, what a wonder
What a wonder I saw
The ox said to the horse
Oh you horse, pebreperse
Pebreperse, ringneck
The horse said to the ox
Oh you ox, evening drover
Evening drover, club neck
Low under the matarde
Birds eat its meat
Citizens eat its bones
When the horse dies
It had pointed dog ears
And it had wolf gums
Horse meat is eaten
and its fat too.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Who was rich in chest outside  
Who was fiery quick inside  
Or who was free in the chamber  
Ait was rich in chest outside  
Cat was fiery quick inside  
Rat was free in the chamber  
But the cat sat by the road  
Who knew the cat’s thoughts  
How precious was the cat’s soul  
What became of that cat for us  
The girl got shoes on her nose  
The boy got shoes on the nape  
The tail became saddle straps  
The penis became a whistle  
The penis sheath became a wedding trumpet  
From the old pike, a lively dance.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Who was rich in chest outside  
Who was fiery quick inside  
Or who was free in the chamber  
Ait was rich in chest outside  
Cat was fiery quick inside  
Rat was free in the chamber  
But the cat sat by the road  
Who knew the cat’s thoughts  
How precious was the cat’s soul  
What became of that cat for us  
The girl got shoes on her nose  
The boy got shoes on the nape  
The tail became saddle straps  
The penis became a whistle  
The penis sheath became a wedding trumpet  
From the old pike, a lively dance.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The Rabbit, the Dog, and the Naughty One  
He does not listen to his father's words  
Nor does he listen to his mother's words  
He went to the mountain to play  
To jump on the haystack  
The hunters came from the manor  
Jägers, dogs following them  
Rifles, scythes on their shoulders  
The jäger immediately caught him  
Then I was taken to the manor  
One got a piece, another a tuft  
Torches get hornflowers  
A colorful sack got a campanula  
The girl got the chambermaid  
Kalda Mart got a neck piece

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The forest goat was strange  
The hole was below, the tail was above  
Half the butt was untrimmed  
The asshole was untrimmed on the edge  
Where is the goat's wedge hole  
The mother sheep's life hole  
If it were a ram, I would surely be a moth  
Mother sheep, I would not give.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The goat was a nice little maiden  
She stubbed her foot on a stone  
She flashed the wolf a wink  
Come here, little goat  
I will make new clog shoes  
I will put in silk straps  
I will put golden ribbons on top

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Let the girls take the empty ones  
To go eat berries from the bush  
Cranberries from the riverbed  
So the crane got to gulp  
The baby crane to cough  
But the crow heard, cawed again  
God grant daughters  
Girls go to marry  
Immediately the ram is butted  
The sheep in the field is killed  
The guts go to the crow

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What is beneath our yard  
The sea beneath our yard  
What is there in the middle of the sea  
There was a well in the middle of the sea  
What is there in the middle of the well  
A pillar in the middle of the well  
What is at the top of the pillar  
A cuckoo at the top of the pillar  
What is under the cuckoo's throat  
A shingle under the cuckoo's throat  
What is made from the shingle  
John's barn is made  
Who brought the war news  
The cuckoo brought the war news  
Whose turn is it to go to war  
John's turn is to go to war  
But John heard and answered back  
It is Grandma's turn to go to war  
Who has ever seen before  
That women have gone to war  
John's turn is to go to war  
Is there a dear woman in the war  
There is no dear woman in the war  
In the war, dear is a green blade  
A beautiful strong-headed horse  
Who beheads a man from the war  
Are there men's fingers in the war  
In the war, there are such men's fingers  
Just like reed stems are broken  
Are there men's heads in the war  
In the war, there are such men's heads  
If there is a bog with a peat stack  
Is there men's blood in the war  
In the war, there is such men's blood  
If there is widely water in the sea  
Mixed water, clear water.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
We have men, we have forest  
We have woodcutters  
Those who deliver the land  
Fires burning in the forest  
Shots fired in the glade  
We cut down wet pines  
Wet pines, dry spruces  
We leave the birches to grow  
Birch branches to protect  
Birch leaves to mourn  
Branches protect the father  
Leaves mourn the mother  
Nests for sons and daughters  
Swallows nod their heads  
The cuckoo calls loudly  
The cuckoo may fall to the eagle  
But at night and far away, it sings well

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The Blue Stripe Little Bird  
Flew over the bog, glided over the bog  
Flew over three straits  
Three are side by side  
One is the rye field of Rye  
The second is the dear oat field  
The third is the wheat field  
Which is the rye field of Rye  
That is our men's field  
Which is the dear oat field  
That is our women's field  
Which is the one named wheat  
That was our maiden's field  
The Blue Stripe Little Bird  
Flew over the bog, glided over the bog  
Flew over three lakes  
Three lakes were side by side  
One is the lake of fierce vodka  
The second is the lake of blessed beer  
The third is the lake of sweet mead  
Which is the lake of fierce vodka  
That is our men's lake  
Which is the lake of blessed beer  
That is the lake of young women  
Which is the lake of sweet mead  
That is for the young maidens.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Already a rye head is visible  
Barley spikes turn green  
Oat ears bend down  
Wheat is ready to harvest.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Our little woodcutters  
Just like oak stumps  
Haymakers, wood haulers  
The ones who rock the whole forest  
Fir forest shooters  
I cut down wet pines  
Wet pines, dry spruces  
I leave the birches to grow  
Birch branches to keep  
Birch leaves to mourn  
Branches keep the father  
Leaves mourn the mother  
Nests the sons and daughters.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The cuckoo calls, the spruce bends  
The hawthorn's core crackles  
The roots of the alder blaze

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The lords of Viru drank vodka  
The keepers of the forest ate bread  
The forest maidens drank honey  
Understand, understand young men  
Who is the heather midwife  
The hazel heather midwife  
What is to be guessed from the riddle  
Before midnight  
Come here, young men  
Guess, eager boys  
Who is the heather maiden?  
The hare is the heather maiden.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The lords of Viru drank vodka  
The keepers of the forest ate bread  
The forest maidens drank honey  
Understand, understand young men  
Who is the heather midwife  
The hazel heather midwife  
What is to be guessed from the riddle  
Before midnight  
Come here, young men  
Guess, eager boys  
Who is the heather maiden?  
The hare is the heather maiden.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
I was given other tasks  
I was given ducks to keep  
To keep ducks, to protect chickens  
To pacify the woodlice  
I went to the village under the swing  
I drowned the ducks in the ditch  
I turned the chickens into the forest  
Came a kite, an angry bird  
A titmouse second, bitter  
A seagull third, envious  
Chased my ducks out of the ditch  
Turned my chickens out of the forest  
Woodlice from the grove  
Springtails from the meadow  
Then I went home crying  
Answered by the old woman, answered by the old man  
Both the elders replied  
The crow made a feeble sound  
He did not call the kite  
The kite was an angry predator  
Pulled the coarse coat  
The hazel grouse the striped blanket  
The lynx the linen shirt  
The duck’s fine feathers  
The crane’s crooked neck feathers

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Come to the St. John's bonfire  
To keep the St. John's fire  
If you don’t come, don’t ask  
The fire wanders into buildings  
Sparks jump onto the roof  
Embers scatter between the village  
Wait, wait, Jaan, oh, oh, Jaan  
Why do you come early, Jaan?  
Let the hay be mown first  
The rye grows thick in the fields  
Barley puts on ear coverings  
The barley ears turn green  
Cover the fields with oat ears  
The rye heads are already seen  
The wheats are coming from the doves  
The beans grow pods  
The peas dress with clothes  
The lentils widen the legs  
And the flax piece lies on the forehead.

label: Kalendrilaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Come to the St. John's bonfire  
To keep the St. John's fire  
If you don’t come, don’t ask  
The fire wanders into buildings  
Sparks jump onto the roof  
Embers scatter between the village  
Wait, wait, Jaan, oh, oh, Jaan  
Why do you come early, Jaan?  
Let the hay be mown first  
The rye grows thick in the fields  
Barley puts on ear coverings  
The barley ears turn green  
Cover the fields with oat ears  
The rye heads are already seen  
The wheats are coming from the doves  
The beans grow pods  
The peas dress with clothes  
The lentils widen the legs  
And the flax piece lies on the forehead.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
Break the branches, little maiden  
Break the branches, blistery birch  
Let the leaves fly to the bird  
Fly to the bird, come to the turtledove  
Attack the swallow  
Bellow at the woodpecker  
Knock the hawk off the falcon  
Answer, Helgi, little voice  
Answer, Laala, little sound  
Answer my good one well  
Answer with a whistling tune  
Answer with a deeper throat sound  
The cuckoo calls, the spruce bends  
I sing, the forest rustles

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud laulust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
How deserted was the field,
How barren the meadow,
Master, little master,
Mistress, little mistress,
Give the servant good food,
Food for the servant, food for the ox,
The servant feeds the ox,
I will kill the bull for the wedding,
Verses for the meadow's stakes.
There were no wanderers among the people,
Wanderers, singers.
The room was dark, the people weak,
I alone a beautifier,
Joking around the ladies.
Already the rye heads seem to
Come out of the wheat awns,
The barley ears are greening,
The oat ears cover the ground.
Why do you babble if you don’t understand,
You sing the pig’s messages,
The servant’s dreams,
The old golden custom-mother.
I sing upon a bare head,
The tuft was on the other ear.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud laulust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
How deserted was the field,
How barren the meadow,
Master, little master,
Mistress, little mistress,
Give the servant good food,
Food for the servant, food for the ox,
The servant feeds the ox,
I will kill the bull for the wedding,
Verses for the meadow's stakes.
There were no wanderers among the people,
Wanderers, singers.
The room was dark, the people weak,
I alone a beautifier,
Joking around the ladies.
Already the rye heads seem to
Come out of the wheat awns,
The barley ears are greening,
The oat ears cover the ground.
Why do you babble if you don’t understand,
You sing the pig’s messages,
The servant’s dreams,
The old golden custom-mother.
I sing upon a bare head,
The tuft was on the other ear.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Evening came unexpectedly  
The day set without hope  
The village boy came uninvited  
He put his hand on me without asking  
Uiu uiu, sleep came over  
Sleep came over, Jüri came closer  
Jüri told me  
That there is a moose in the field  
The bear was in the oat field  
The bear was biting Kaie's thighs  
Anne had bitten Unt  
The fox ate Tiiu's thighs  
There was a high bump on the stomach  
Below the navel there was a little

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Evening came unexpectedly  
The day set without hope  
The village boy came uninvited  
He put his hand on me without asking  
Uiu uiu, sleep came over  
Sleep came over, Jüri came closer  
Jüri told me  
That there is a moose in the field  
The bear was in the oat field  
The bear was biting Kaie's thighs  
Anne had bitten Unt  
The fox ate Tiiu's thighs  
There was a high bump on the stomach  
Below the navel there was a little

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Do you want to come to me  
No, I am coming to you  
I had two little dogs  
One black, the other brindle  
The black one barked in the yard too  
The brindle one was with me in the herd too

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Do you want to come to me  
No, I am coming to you  
I had two little dogs  
One black, the other brindle  
The black one barked in the yard too  
The brindle one was with me in the herd too

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What is there under our yard  
The sea under our yard  
What is there in the middle of the sea  
A well in the middle of the sea  
What is there in the middle of the well  
A pillar in the middle of the well  
What is there on top of the pillar  
A sieve on top of the pillar  
What is there at the bottom of the sieve  
A brooch at the bottom of the sieve  
What is there on the eye of the brooch  
A ring on the eye of the brooch  
Where was that ring brought from  
Brought from there, received from there.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
What is there under our yard  
The sea under our yard  
What is there in the middle of the sea  
A well in the middle of the sea  
What is there in the middle of the well  
A pillar in the middle of the well  
What is there on top of the pillar  
A sieve on top of the pillar  
What is there at the bottom of the sieve  
A brooch at the bottom of the sieve  
What is there on the eye of the brooch  
A ring on the eye of the brooch  
Where was that ring brought from  
Brought from there, received from there.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The moon shone from the Kuura river  
The day from the grayness of Pärnu  
The star from Taalingi hill  
The moon like a cloth of golden glow  
The day like a cloth of linden glow  
Dew glow, divine glow

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Why are our darlings sad  
Why are our young men weak  
Why are the boys in our parish loud  
Because our darlings are sad  
Our young men are weak  
Because they have been caught in cold lure  
A field written by lightning  
The girl’s ornaments remain in the shop  
The swords remained in the German cupboard  
The girl’s ribbons remain in the town

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Blue stripe, golden stripe  
Flew over three lakes  
One was Ruuge Rye Lake  
The second dear Kallisa Kalja Lake  
The third true Beer Lake  
Which was Ruuge Rye Lake  
That was the lake of our men  
Which was dear Kallisa Kalja Lake  
That was the lake of our boys  
Which was true Beer Lake  
That was the lake of our women.  
Blue stripe, golden stripe  
Flew over three fields  
One was Ruuge Rye Field  
The second was dear Oat Field  
The third true Barley Field  
Which was Ruuge Rye Field  
That was the field of our men  
Which was dear Oat Field  
That was the field of our boys  
Which was true Barley Field  
That was the field of our women.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Rain showers towards Harju  
To be washed into the hands of Harju women  
To be washed in hands, to be washed with a cloth  
In Harju County, men are dirty  
Half of the boys are Mischievous  
like young devils!

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Up, up, little swing,  
Up along the apple tree,  
What did I see going up,  
Going up, coming down?  
There were three at the edge of the forest,  
One was in the spruce forest,  
The second in the birch forest,  
The third in the apple tree forest.  
What was in the spruce forest?  
It was the forest of young men.  
What was in the birch forest?  
It was the forest of young women.  
What was in the apple tree forest?  
It was the forest of young maidens.  

Up, up, little swing,  
Up along the apple tree,  
What did I see going up,  
Going up, coming down?  
There were three at the edge of the field,  
One was in the rye field,  
The second in the oat field,  
The third in the wheat field.  
What was in the rye field?  
It was the field of young men.  
What was in the oat field?  
It was the field of young women.  
What was in the wheat field?  
It was the field of young maidens.  

Up, up, little swing,  
Up along the apple tree,  
What did I see going up,  
Going up, coming down?  
There were three at the edge of the lake,  
One was in the beer lake,  
The second in the vodka lake,  
The third in the mead lake.  
What was in the beer lake?  
It was the lake of young men,  
What was in the vodka lake?  
It was the lake of young women,  
What was in the mead lake?  
It was the lake of young maidens.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, little cuckoo  
Helgi, gentle little bird  
Won’t you cuckoo for me?  
The cuckoo cuckoos to the herdsman  
To Helgi the haymaker  
To the one who gathers the harvest  
To the one who plows the field  
Won’t you cuckoo for me?  
I have paw boys even in the drought  
Cuckoo’s daughters in my hand.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What are you wandering, little wind
Booms, bad little world
Don't go, little wind, to the sea
My brother to the seaman
My companion to the fisherman
Monday brings herring
Tuesday brings squids
Wednesday a nice catch of fish
Thursday brings pike fish
Friday brings Baltic sprats
Saturday brings bream fish.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Who was gray unwashed?  
The wolf was gray unwashed.  
Who was white unwounded?  
The hare was white unwounded.  
Who was quite round?  
The pea was quite round.  
Who was broadly oval?  
The lentil was broadly oval.  
Who was slightly elongated?  
The bean was slightly elongated.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Blue series, golden series  
Flew over three meadows  
One was the wheat meadow  
It was the meadow of good women  
The second was the rye meadow  
It was the meadow of young men  
The third was the precious oat meadow  
Precious oat field.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little rooster, little hen  
Let's go chatter in the barn  
The rooster found a barley grain  
The hen pecked at two oat grains  
Let's put a flatbread on top  
Roll the mill to the grain bin  
Then we'll make broth without oil  
We call together the sad birds  
We gather the grey birds  
We leave the goshawk uninvited  
The goshawk was angry and hunting  
Tore apart the crane's nest  
Tore apart the capercaillie's cover  
Sew the colorful coat

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Rain, rain, little rain  
Don’t rain on me  
We don’t have a home dryer  
Rain, rain on the German  
The Germans have home dryers  
They wash fine clothes  
They bleach white clothes.  
We don’t have a home dryer  
To wash fine clothes  
To bleach white clothes  
An attic shirt, a bright shirt.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Ring, ring, through the forest  
Tinkle, tinkle, through the forest  
In response to our good voice  
In response to a hoarser throat  
Break branches, little maiden  
Cut down the birch, dear one  
Leave the leaves for the birds  
For the singers of joyful songs.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I sowed barleys in the valleys  
Oats on the rocky shores  
Then the goat came, with wide horns  
Ate my barleys from the valleys  
Oats from the rocky shores  
I grabbed the goat by the horns  
I struck the goat to the ground  
Its blood spurted onto the ground  
Far away, further away  
Away to Hiiumaa  
Further away to Kaljumaa.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
How many girls in the village  
Ten girls in the village  
Nine across the street  
Seven on this side  
Row, row, on our swing  
Row, row, gain strength  
Cross over three forests  
Three were near the forest  
One was the spruce forest  
Another was the birch forest  
The third was a beautiful apple tree  
That was the spruce forest  
It was the forest of young men  
That was the birch forest  
It was the forest of young women  
That was the beautiful apple tree  
It was the forest of young maidens  
Row, row, on our swing  
Row, row, gain strength  
Row over three fields,  
Three were near the fields  
One was a rye field  
Another was an oat field  
The third was a beautiful wheat  
That was the rye field  
It was the field of young men  
That was the oat field  
It was the field of young women  
That was the beautiful wheat  
It was the field of young maidens,  
Two-headed beauties.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Äivere bulls, Puivere bulls  
Kodasema steers  
Siisiku spike hangers  
Paluküla pole dancers,  
Went roaring to the hill  
Lowing down to the meadow  
Galloping to the well ridge  
Mossy tufts on the horn tips

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Our girls were checkered  
The boys were pinstriped  
They had long song tunes  
They had wide dance circles  
They wore out three pairs of boots  
They went to the village to bring shoes  
The village bull was not slaughtered  
The sheep in the barn not sheared  
The squirrel was not sought from the forest  
The nirgi's skin was not taken  
The tit made without oil  
The swallow washed the bedding  
Called the frogs to the wedding  
The willow bird got a panful  
Some birds got a measureful  
The crow got a bellyful

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
What is beneath our yard?  
The sea beneath our yard.  
What is in the middle of the sea?  
A pillar in the middle of the sea.  
What is on top of the pillar?  
A brooch on top of the pillar.  
What is on the edge of the brooch?  
A ring on the edge of the brooch.  
I went to wash Piibe's head  
At the long river Piibe,  
In cold gray Harju.  
I put beads on the hay,  
A necklace on the arch once,  
A ring on the sturdy one,  
A pin on the fine sand,  
On the big stump of the brooch.  
Then water came from under the hole,  
A swallow from above the water,  
It took my beads from the hay,  
From the sturdy one, the ring,  
From the fine sand, the pin,  
From the big stump of the brooch.  
Then it went to tell the maid,  
Then it went to tell the old man.  
I went to wash Piibe's head  
At the long river Piibe,  
In cold gray Harju.  
I put beads on the hay,  
A necklace on the arch once,  
A ring on the sturdy one,  
A pin on the fine sand,  
On the big stump of the brooch.  
Then water came from under the pike,  
A swallow from above the water,  
It took my beads from the hay,  
From the sturdy one, the ring,  
From the fine sand, the pin,  
On the sturdy one, the brooch.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The raven made weak twigs  
Called together sad birds  
Gathered together bad birds  
He did not call the golden little one  
The gold was a fierce predator  
Tore the shawl  
The capercaillie's striped blanket  
The fine linen shirt  
The duck's delicate boards  
The crane's hoarse croak.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Swing smiths, dear brothers,  
What I promise to the rower  
Rolling over the beam  
From the manor a white maiden  
The most splendid among the crowd  
This I promise to the rower  
To the rower, to the pusher  
Rolling over the beam  
Rowing over three forests  
There were three forests nearby  
One was a spruce forest  
Another a dear birch forest  
The third a beautiful apple tree  
This I promise to the rower  
To the one who swings me  
Come, village girls, to the swing  
Bring eggs, bring chickens  
Bring ducks in pairs.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Listen, how the girl is singing  
The summer night is short  
The boy is very silly  
The village boy, my dear brother  
The seam boy, my other brother  
Come to me boldly  
Hold me close lovingly  
Try to touch my nose  
Come to touch my box  
To taste the honey pot  
Come to mow my hay  
Come to ride my carriage  
I have a heap of land to spend  
A hundred and eight bundles of hay  
The hay is good, otherwise black  
The war horses wouldn’t eat it  
The wedding runes wouldn’t last  
Let your bull come with its calf  
It will certainly feed, eyes wet  
Eyebrows dripping with gold  
The table will surely roll water

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo little cuckoo  
Helgi, gentle little bird  
Won't you cuckoo for me  
You cuckoo for the village herder  
Helgis for the hay cutter  
You knock for the thief  
You squawk for the daylight maker  
You chirp for the sawyer.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The cuckoo calls into the spruce forest  
Leeni plays in the pine forest  
Cuckoo chicks in Leeni's womb  
The cuckoo to Leeni's prayer:  
Dear Leeni, pet Leeni  
Give my children back to me  
I will make you little sweet buns  
Little sweet buns, cuddly little bundles  
I will tie in silk ribbons  
I will put on red ribbons  
Wear the curled ribbons behind.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The village's golden young men  
The parish's white little boys  
Haytime is approaching  
Midplowing time is near  
The rye harvesting time rushes in  
Already the ear is greening  
Oats cover the ground in the ear  
Wheats come from the sheath  
The bran blooms like cloth  
Beans show new pods  
Peas turn blue in rows

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Flowered piglet from Orikas  
Golden-maned little gold  
Go to the hill to root around  
Root the hills, root the pines  
Root the spruces on the knolls  
Straight alders along the ridge  
White birches standing against the ground  
Then come home for Christmas  
On the evening of St. Martin's Day  
Then you will be strong in your back  
And you will have a full face  
The village’s golden young men  
The parish’s fair boys  
Come to slaughter the pig  
Hold the harness tight  
Stick the knife in the throat  
Brothers, to shed blood  
To wipe the lineage and kin.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The pig ruined the city of Riga  
The piglet burned Põltsamaa  
The sheep parted Laiuse  
The goat's scribbles on the foot  
The sheep have wide feet.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Let us guess, let us guess, dear brothers,  
Let us reckon, eager women:  
"Who has those rust-colored hairs?"  
I hear again, I respond:  
"What is wrong with guessing,  
It was said earlier halfway through:  
"The rye has those rust-colored hairs."  

Let us guess, let us guess, dear brothers,  
Let us reckon, eager women:  
"Who had a Moor's hat on their head?"  
I hear again, I respond:  
"What is wrong with guessing,  
It was said earlier halfway through:  
"The barley had a Moor's hat on its head."  

Let us guess, let us guess, dear brothers,  
Let us reckon, eager women:  
"Who had long trousers on their legs?"  
I hear again, I respond:  
"What is wrong with guessing,  
It was said earlier halfway through:  
"The oats had long trousers on its legs."  

Let us guess, let us guess, dear brothers,  
Let us reckon, eager women:  
"Who was rarely pike-like?"  
I hear again, I respond:  
"What is wrong with guessing,  
It was said earlier halfway through:  
"The potato was rarely pike-like."  

Let us guess, let us guess, dear brothers,  
Let us reckon, eager women:  
"Who was a bit elongated?"  
I hear again, I respond:  
"What is wrong with guessing,  
It was said earlier halfway through:  
"The bean was a bit elongated."  

Let us guess, let us guess, dear brothers,  
Let us reckon, eager women:  
"Who was quite round?"  
I hear again, I respond:  
"What is wrong with guessing,  
It was said earlier halfway through:  
"The pea was quite round."  

Let us guess, let us guess, dear brothers,  
Let us reckon, eager women:  
"Who was broad and flat?"  
I hear again, I respond:  
"What is wrong with guessing,  
It was said earlier halfway through:  
"The lentil was broad and flat."  

Let us guess, let us guess, dear brothers,  
Let us reckon, eager women:  
"Who was the king of the spruce forest?"  
I hear again, I respond:  
"What is wrong with guessing,  
It was said earlier halfway through:  
"The cuckoo was the king of the spruce forest."  

Let us guess, let us guess, dear brothers,  
Let us reckon, eager women:  
"Who was the forest maiden?"  
I hear again, I respond:  
"What is wrong with guessing,  
It was said earlier halfway through:  
"The capercaillie was the forest maiden."  

Let us guess, let us guess, dear brothers,  
Let us reckon, eager women:  
"Who was the lady of the willow thicket?"  
I hear again, I respond:  
"What is wrong with guessing,  
It was said earlier halfway through:  
"The hare was the lady of the willow thicket."

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The weather in Hiiumaa is cold,  
The weather is cold, the hay is black  
Neither war horses ate it  
Nor did the wedding rune bark boats,  
An old field mouse came  
Ate the hay and crunched the stalks  
Smirked with sweet lips.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Rain, rain, little rain,  
Don't rain on me,  
I don't have home dryers,  
Nor white shirt rollers,  
Rain on that poor German instead.  
He had home dryers,  
White shirt rollers,  
Fine shirt washers.  
If it were my existence,  
If it were my doing,  
I would make the gentlemen plow,  
The ladies pick berries,  
The masters make hay,  
The mistresses mow the grass.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:

The masters make hay,  
The mistresses mow the grass.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The apple belonged to Al’s younger brother:  
The apple had bright leaves.  
The hooves were golden-colored.  
The tail was braided with silk.  
The head was adorned with a ribbon.  
The ears with tall earrings.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little rooster, little hen,
Let's go outside to scurry,
One under one barn,
But the rooster found a barley shaft,
The hen found a barley shaft too, -
We put a patch on it
We roll it to the millstone,
From there we make oilless fat
We call cranes "butter ears",
The storks as "pilferers",
The crane came with golden spurs
The crow with copper boots,
The titmouse with a tin hoe,
The sparrow with a cart,
The magpie came to boil the broth,
The crow came to boil the broth,
It put pepper on the pail,
It drives the saw to the tape.
They knocked on the crane,
The stork’s helper was driven away,
The crow's pain was taken away,
The titmouse’s tuft was pulled,
The duck’s bottom was slapped,
The sparrow’s blood was spilled.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
You probably remember from last year  
You recall from the year before  
Guess that time as well  
Keep in mind that day as well  
When we won the race  
Won the race, drank honey  
A golden spruce grew there,  
A golden spruce, a prickly birch  
A trembling alder forest.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, little men,  
Guess, eager boys;  
Know, wise women.  
What was our grazing land,  
In the meadow of our pasture,  
A hundred hares were frolicking. –  
The head was big, the tail was big,  
Eyes the size of fists.  
Horns were a foot long. –  
When walking, as big as a sow.  
When standing, as tall as a heap?  
"Perhaps it was the lord of Iiumaa,  
Or a swift messenger,  
A carrier of German writings,  
A rider of messages." –  
"Go to the forest, more insane ones,  
More miserable corners,  
That was not the lord of Iiumaa,  
Nor the swift messenger,  
Carrier of German writings,  
Rider of messages,  
That was our land's hare,  
Our long-eared rabbit,  
Our meadow boss."

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, my sisters,  
Take notice, wise women.  
What can be seen from the hill?  
An apple tree can be seen from the hill.  
How many branches does the apple tree have?  
The apple tree had three branches.  
How many blossoms on the branches?  
There were three blossoms on the branches.  
How many apples on the branches?  
There were three apples on the branches.  
One was engraved on the bark,  
Another sealed with wax,  
The third was written in gold  
What was engraved on the bark,  
That is my own apple.  
What was sealed with wax,  
That is my dear bride’s apple,  
What was written in gold,  
That is a deceitful peacock,  
Picking the young men,  
I am taking the man’s path,  
Trying the bride’s customs,  
Whether there is a wretched wine drink,  
Or a slender beer drink.  
Therefore, thanks to the man’s sister,  
The man’s sister, the man’s room,  
For not raising a bride for me,  
Nor attracting a young man,  
There was no wretched wine drink,  
Nor a slender beer drink.  
No beer is given without money,  
No rage without a barrel of wine,  
No German coin without a guest room.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
What is there under our yard?  
The sea is under our yard.  
What is in the middle of the sea?  
There was a well in the middle of the sea.  
What was under the well’s shore?  
A pillar was under the well’s shore.  
What is on top of the pillar?  
A sieve was on top of the pillar.  
What is on the bottom of the sieve?  
A brooch was on the bottom of the sieve.  
What is on the edge of the brooch?  
A ring was on the edge of the brooch.  
Where was this ring brought from?  
Brought from there, made there.  
On a soldier’s fingerbone  
On a young boy’s kneebone  
Kapu

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
What is there under our yard?  
The sea is under our yard.

Brought from there, made there.  
On a soldier’s fingerbone  
On a young boy’s kneebone  
Kapu

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
My sisters, do not worry,  
Surely we have leaves on the tree,  
Leaves on the tree, grass in the ground  
Greening the meadow hay,  
Bending the birch leg in the swamp.  
Break branches, young maiden,  
Break branches, break the birch  
Leave a tender treetop.  
Let the treetops remain for the bird’s flight,  
For the birds to fly, for the turtledoves to come.  
At night the nightingale will be there,  
The capercaillie coos to others.  
The male capercaillie cooed,  
The female capercaillie cooed,  
The lovely birds cooed,  
The young ducks chatted,  
Where must I go,  
Where to build a nest?  
Into the blackberry bush, into the willow bush,  
Into the thick willow by the shore.  
Where there are no German dogs barking,  
Where the lord’s hounds cannot reach,  
The wicked ones of my sister’s sister,  
The scoundrels of my brother’s little rivers.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I, a little boy with a son,
Like a little oak stump,
A tail like a sweet little bird.
Haymaker, wood stacker,
Shaker of all the forest,
Poplar forest's thunderer.
I fell wet pines,
Wet pines, dry spruces,
I leave the birches to grow,
Birch branches to sigh,
Branches sigh for the father,
Leaves mourn the mother,
Tops the rows of sons and daughters.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, my sisters,  
Communicate, wise women,  
What rises from the swamp is blue,  
Blue from the swamp, red from the land,  
Round over the forest,  
Glowing through the forest.  
The moon rises from the blue swamp,  
Blue from the swamp, red from the land,  
Round over the forest,  
Glowing through the bog.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud abielust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
I went to the harrow ride,  
To let loose the broad team,  
I myself ride along the heath,  
The dogs rode along the marsh,  
The sledges along the road tracks.  
I heard the sledge clattering,  
I heard the ducks splashing.  
I took the duck from the sledge's mouth,  
The hound from the sledge's teeth.  
I took the duck to Paide,  
The hound I gave away there.  
From Paide I got money for a pail,  
Bought a new little instrument.  
I began to play the instrument.  
Father heard the player,  
Father began to oil.  
Mother heard the player,  
Mother began to knock on the bread crust,  
Started to play the spinning wheel.  
Brothers listened to the player,  
Brothers went to fetch water.  
Sister heard the player,  
Sister went to fetch salt.  
The woman heard the player,  
The woman smiled at the young men:  
Here comes the player home,  
Takes my instrument to sleep,  
Leads it to the kannel room,  
Calls up the morning.  
Takes the instrument to start,  
The kannel at the room's threshold.  
Rise, woman, milk the cow,  
Rise, man, take the herd.  
Another's herd to a distant place,  
Our herd to the herd's yard,  
Another's calves in the meadow,  
Our calves in the mud.  
Another's oxen to the hayfield,  
Our oxen to the barn.  
Another's mares to the rye field,  
Our mares to the sledge.  
Grandmother hears, again sounds:  
Oh my little darling,  
My son’s daughter in apron!  
The instrument does not feed the household,  
The kannel cannot live alone,  
The instrument walks the straw road,  
Sings on the edge of the straw board;  
The kannel in the straw room.  
There it keeps the harlots,  
Drags away the wicked women,  
Takes a scolding at the door,  
Scolds the whore excessively.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Swing brothers,
Take the swing to the cellar,
To the chamber in the cellar.
Already the rye head is visible,
The barley ear is greening,
The oat ear is covered by husk,
Wheat is coming from the stalk,
Beans are growing pods,
Peas are growing little leaves.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Some man a certain kind  
Some quite round  
Some with a wide lantern  
Some had long pants on their legs  
Some wore a cap down to their chin  
I understand why Ernes does not understand  
Ernes quite round  
Leats with a wide lantern  
Kaar had long pants on his legs  
Odra wore a cap down to his chin,  
Nisu had a silk shirt on  
Rukkil had frizzy hair.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
The crow, gentle little bird  
In the quiet of our maple grove  
With a tail as long as rowan berries  
Waited for the horse to die  
The old ear to nod  
I had wise stable boys  
Diligent were the stable helpers  
They made a stable in the sky  
Lanterns near the ceiling  
Where German dogs could not reach  
Where gentlemen’s hounds could not reach  
The crow waited a long time  
But eventually went flying away.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
The crow, gentle little bird

Where gentlemen’s hounds could not reach  
The crow waited a long time  
But eventually went flying away.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Long ago I was a manor lord,
Anchovy breads stood before me,
Now I am a poor farmer,
Plowing inside the great forest,
Socks, shoes, ribbons all torn,
All the cuffs are worn out.
I sit down on a stump
Start darning socks,
Patching shoes.
Then came the manor’s black cat,
Took my sock, needle, thread,
And the string of my shoe patch.
I chased after the cat,
The cat ran into the master’s room,
I ran after it.
From there it ran into the lady’s room,
I jumped after it.
The lady was making berry jam,
Thinking - now I will get some jam
I lick with a tongue,
Afterwards I test with a paw.
The girl ran with a mop,
The lady with a kitchen shovel.
I quickly fled,
Leaving them to catch their breath.
I did not catch the cat,
Shoes and socks stayed torn.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Why were the young men weak?  
Our village boys were silent.  
Our new six were bustling  
That wood had been taken cold  
Ale had been driven under the bowl  
The field was written by lightning.  
A town girl had stayed with the ribbon  
A girl had stayed at the shop gate  
German boxes, leather straps  
Shoes had stayed in the stone hall  
There was a taking of troops  
Searching for hussars  
Wanting dragoons  
Testing grenadiers.  
Because the young men were weak  
Our village boys were silent.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Row the swing, reach the swing,  
Row over three lakes.  
One was the lake of vodka,  
The second the lake of beer,  
The third the lake of meads.  
The lake of vodka  
Was the lake of young men.  
The lake of beer  
Was the lake of young women.  
The lake of meads  
Was for young maidens.  
Row the swing, reach the swing,  
Row over three forests.  
One was a spruce forest,  
The second a birch forest,  
The third an apple orchard.  
The spruce forest  
Was the forest of young men.  
The birch forest  
Was the forest of young women.  
The apple orchard  
Was of young maidens.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I am a man, son of a Russian man  
A handsome man, son of a fisherman  
I sit by the edge of the boat  
Picking full from the basket.  
The bigger ones I put in my mouth  
The smaller ones I throw away  
There they grew into fish  
They multiply into whitefish  
They decay into bream  
Into the big fish of the sea.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
The buck came from Turkey  
The mother goat came from Hiiumaa  
I grabbed the buck by the horn  
The mother goat by the ear  
I took them to the blacksmith in Viru  
The blacksmith made a horse from the horn  
From the tip of the horn, a saddle  
From there I ride to the town  
Where the streets shine  
There the girls squat  
Squatting, standing  
In front was a stack of elm bark  
Behind was a stack of rye stalks  
In between were stacks of poor children.  
Still, I will not take you  
I won’t take you, nor leave another  
I will go to Kooku to propose  
To the Kubja’s three daughters  
One is Anne with the ark’s leg  
The second Kaie with the kitchen paw  
The third Maie with the chatter mouth.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust

The third Maie with the chatter mouth.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo, my dear gold,  
Cuckoo, golden girl,  
Cuckoo on the six-pointed star,  
Hang a wide ribbon on it.  
Cuckoo, bow to the gold,  
Honor the silver things.  
Let the gold remain under the six points,  
The silver between the points.  
When you walk along the moonlit night,  
Moonlit night, all alone,  
Then a little bird will fly to you,  
Then you will have a sweetheart. –  
The bird brings messages from the town,  
The thrush brings greetings from the market.  
You shall have a slender groom,  
You shall have a loving husband,  
A fortunate man with a silver cap,  
A fisherman with a horned cap,  
A famous smith from Kulmua,  
A farmhand from Harjumaa,  
They all try to come courting,  
Want to be fine gentlemen,  
Looking for a diligent maiden,  
Wanting to bring a sweetheart.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Laala, laala little mouth  
Move like a little bird’s tongue  
Sway like a little berry mind  
You can still be the village fool  
When I am buried in the ground  
Covered beneath the sand  
Stones placed on top  
I sing, why don’t you sing  
I fall, why don’t you fall  
I do sing, so it rattles  
I do fall, so it thuds  
So it thuds right into the heart  
And it gleams gently ahead  
It cracks on the carpenter’s door  
-----  
Cuckoo, cuckoo little cuckoo  
Gleam softly little bird  
Don’t you fall for me  
You fall for the forest herder  
Gleam for the hay cutter  
Crack for the bark peeler  
The cuckoo falls on the spruce that bends  
The cuckoo falls by the river flow  
To the grey bird by the threshing floor  
To the titmouse by the haystack  
On my dear man’s shoulder  
Gleams a living horse  
Cracks on the crowded street  
Clicks on the winding golden nails.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo cuckoo cuckoo  
Let the little bird live  
The cuckoo will not cuckoo to me  
The cuckoo cuckoos to the herdsman  
It lives with the mower  
It traps the hay taker

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The swallow brings spring warmth  
The nightingale brings night warmth  
The cuckoo brings the warmest warmth  
The lark brings southern warmth.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Young men are calling out
Calling out beyond the three forests
One was in the spruce forest
The second was in the pine forest
The third was in the birch forest
There grow the crooked birches
From there I cut wheel wood
For father, ox yoke wood
For brother, strong horse wood
For sister, club wood
Why aren’t you sisters calling out here?
Often I do not slip up either
Sing so that the ceiling rattles
The ceiling rattles, the rafters clap
I toss the verses, or is it pity
For the black spruce in the hot stove.
Sisters, you hear
And brothers, pay attention
What a dreadful story in hell
How the rich burn inside hell
The poor watch from the shadows
As the rich ride recklessly
And the wealthy recklessly
The poor watch from the shadows.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh you mighty crab  
And you fish of the kabala  
Morning streams into the sea  
Noon shots into the waves  
In the evening you boil in the kettle  
But you fry, sir, on the pan  
And old sir in the bath.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little Goat Maiden  
The kid was a little plant  
The goat ate leaves from the alder grove  
Branch hay from the aspen grove  
Golden daisies from the spruce forest  
But the wolf waited in the valley  
Come here, little goat  
I will bring you a red apron  
I will bring you a dear necklace  
I will bring new clog shoes  
The fox shouted from the brushwood  
Little Goat Maiden  
Don't be silly to believe  
The lip is ready to lie  
Blood is the red apron  
Lungs and liver are the neck ornament  
The skin is your dear necklace  
New clogs for hooves  
Intestines like silk ribbons.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow flew into the maple,  
Its tail touched the rowan,  
I asked my apple to die  
On the linen field,  
On the oat field.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
If it were up to me,  
If it were up to my actions,  
I would set sail for the wind,  
For the troubles of life,  
For the berry vine of the house,  
For the clump of leaves in the hut.  
Wait, wait, cold, no, no, cold,  
So then you scream inside the door,  
Shouting on the chamber's threshold,  
Looking for the shelter of a room,  
To warm the little room.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
If it were up to me,  
If it were up to my actions,  
I would set sail for the wind,  
For the troubles of life,  
For the berry vine of the house,  
For the clump of leaves in the hut.  
Wait, wait, cold, no, no, cold,  
So then you scream inside the door,  
Shouting on the chamber's threshold,  
Looking for the shelter of a room,  
To warm the little room.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Chill, chill, cold, cold,  
Cold, I’m chilled through,  
Chill makes me complain;  
If only our manor folk understood,  
They would surely bring after me  
Old fur coats to wear,  
To warm my fingers,  
To shield my toes.  
So let me tell once more,  
Dear ones, brothers,  
Where did the frost settle in the throat,  
Where did the fur coat cover the throat?

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Chill, chill, cold, cold,  
Cold, I’m chilled through,  
Chill makes me complain;  
If only our manor folk understood,  
They would surely bring after me  
Old fur coats to wear,  
To warm my fingers,  
To shield my toes.  
So let me tell once more,  
Dear ones, brothers,  
Where did the frost settle in the throat,  
Where did the fur coat cover the throat?

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The Master was at the wedding  
At the wedding and the wedding,  
The Mistress was on guard,  
On guard and on guard;  
A pig is wallowing in the garden  
In the garden and in the garden,  
Three forks on its back,  
On its back and on its back,  
The hen is screeching in the wall,  
In the wall and in the wall,  
The yoke is staring at the raven,  
At the raven and at the raven,  
The mare is neighing in the stable  
In the stable and in the stable,  
Wants to go riding  
Go and go;  
Why did you neigh so early,  
Or why are you shouting into the wolf’s ear?  
Did you neigh like a lone one,  
Or are you shouting like a curved one?  
I did not neigh like a lone one,  
Nor do I shout like a curved one,  
I neigh from a dying age,  
I shout from a dying sorrow.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Kalendrilaulud
translation:
Up high on the swing,
High up, far away,
What did I see up high?
There were three at the forest’s edge:
One was a golden spruce forest,
The second a precious birch forest,
The third a beautiful apple tree;
The one that is the golden spruce forest
Is the forest of young men.
The one that is the precious birch forest
Is the forest of young women.
What is the beautiful apple tree?
It is the orchard of young maidens.
Up high on the swing,
High up, far away,
What did I see far away?
Far away, far away?
There were three by the lakeside:
One was a lake of strong spirit,
The second was a lake of red beer,
The third is a lake of sweet mead.
What is the lake of strong spirit?
It is the lake of young men.
What was the lake of red beer?
It was the lake of young women.
What was the lake of sweet mead?
It was the lake of young maidens.
Up high on the swing,
High up, far away,
What did I see up high,
Up high, far away?
There were three by the fields:
One was an oat field,
The second was a rye field full of grain,
The third was a beautiful wheat field.
What was the precious oat field?
It was the field of young men.
What was the rye field full of grain?
It was the field of young women.
What was the beautiful wheat field?
It was the field of young maidens.
Up high on the swing,
High up, far away,
What did I see up high?
There were three swings side by side:
One with blue silk,
The second with red ribbons,
The third with golden cords.
What was the one with blue silk?
It is the swing of young women.
What was the one with red ribbons?
It is the swing of young maidens.
What is the one with golden cords?
It is the swing of young men.
Where was this swing brought from?
The swing was brought from Kilvandu,
Its posts from Ambela,
The supports from Sinisalu.
I swing, I praise the swing,
I sing, I honor the table:
Oh, my golden swingers,
Do not swing me with malice,
Do not harm me with spite;
I am the one who can scold,
I am the willow leaf that whips,
The birch leaf that bounces,
The oak leaf that dances,
The aspen leaf that quivers.
If I fall, who will pay?
My cheek will pay with two oxen,
The other cheek with another pair,
My ear will pay with an ear gelding,
The other ear with the other ear,
My eyes with silky garlic chives,
The other eye with the other leaf,
My nose with a young stallion.
I have no brother under the swing,
Nor a sister’s child under the table,
There are big stones under the swing,
There is thick smoke under the table.
I fall and my eyes hit the stone,
Cheeks trample in the smoke.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The goat was a young maiden  
The mare was a wise little plant  
Danced on the hill of the oak grove  
The wolf called out from the meadow:  
"Come here, little goat!  
Little scratched goat  
I will bring new shoes  
I will put on red ribbons  
Inside I will tie silk straps  
Underneath there are beautiful hooves."  
The bird sang in the alder grove:  
"Little goat, little scratched one!  
Do not be foolish to believe  
Do not be weak to take advice  
The new shoes are made of skin  
Inside are silk straps  
Blood on the red ribbons  
Hooves are beautiful."

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Blue-eye golden inscription,  
Blue-eye little bird.  
Flew a lot, glided a lot,  
Flew over three fields.  
There were three fields in a row.  
One was the rye field of Ruuge:  
The second, the dear barley field,  
The third, the field named Niso.  
The rye field of Ruuge  
Was the field of our men.  
The dear barley field  
Was the field of our women,  
The field named Niso  
Was of the young maidens,  
The beauties with curly hair.  
The flax-headed little birds,  
The tan-headed little plants,  
The beautiful ones with hair.  
Blue-eye golden inscription,  
Blue-eye little bird.  
Flew a lot, glided a lot,  
Flew over three forests,  
There were three forests in a row.  
One was the golden spruce forest,  
The second, the dear birch forest,  
The third, the beautiful apple tree.  
The golden spruce forest  
Was the forest of our men.  
The dear birch forest  
Was the forest of our women.  
The beautiful apple tree  
Was of the young maidens,  
The beauties with curly hair,  
The flax-headed little birds,  
The tan-headed little plants,  
The beautiful ones with hair.  
Blue-eye golden inscription,  
Blue-eye little bird.  
Flew a lot, glided a lot,  
Flew over three lakes,  
There were three lakes in a row.  
One was the lake of fierce Viha Viina,  
It was the lake of our men,  
The second the true beer lake,  
It was the lake of our women.  
The third was the sweet mead,  
It was of the young maidens:  
The beauties with curly hair,  
The flax-headed little birds,  
The tan-headed little plants,  
The beautiful ones with hair.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Why are you without mist?  
The sky is soot-haired,  
The people are rusted iron.  
Because the weather was misty  
The sky is soot-haired  
The people are rusted iron  
The godchildren's quarrels are large:  
The fathers' oaths are broad,  
The listeners' minds are sad,  
The viewers' minds are painful.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I am a man, the son of a sailor,  
A handsome man, the son of a fisherman,  
I sit by the sea shore,  
Picking full from the eelgrass.  
The bigger ones I put in my mouth,  
The smaller ones I throw away.  
From there come the big plants,  
Big plants, dark backs,  
Leaves wide at the blade ends.  
They stood at the edges into the sea,  
Rustling in the waves.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Who has a walled-up sore on the butt?
Barley has a walled-up sore on the butt.
Who has arched feet?
Oats have arched feet.
Who is tall and slender?
Rye is tall and slender.
Who was naked?
Wheat was naked.
Who wears a silk shirt?
Flax wears a silk shirt.
Who has a golden hat?
Spruce has a golden hat.
Who has a fur coat?
Birch has a fur coat.
Whose head is red?
Rowan’s head is red.
Who wears a mourning dress?
Alder wears a mourning dress.
Who was the shepherd of the wilderness?
Broom was the shepherd of the wilderness.
Who was the lamb of the forest?
The wolf was the lamb of the forest.
Who is the desert ruler?
The hare is the desert ruler.
Who is the black rooster of the forest?
The capercaillie is the black rooster of the forest.
Who is the king of the spruce grove?
The cuckoo is the king of the spruce grove.

label: Murelaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Who is early in the morning for us?  
The crow is early in the morning.  
Before the swallow flies.  
Even earlier are the enemies,  
Before the forehead is touched,  
The forehead is touched, the eyes are washed,  
The feet were already trained to walk.  
They sent to find the plow,  
To wake the ox.  
What answer came?  
The plowman answered.  
I asked the plowman,  
Called out to the driver,  
Did you see my plow!  
You woke the oxen.  
Neither the plowman spoke,  
Nor did the driver answer.  
I took the plowman’s hat,  
The driver’s good horse.  
Then the plowman will speak,  
The driver will answer,  
He went through our yard,  
Drank water from our river,  
Tasted water from our well.

label: Murelaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Who is early in the morning for us?

Before the swallow flies.

The forehead is touched, the eyes are washed,

The plowman answered.

Called out to the driver,  
Did you see my plow!  
You woke the oxen.  
Neither the plowman spoke,  
Nor did the driver answer.  
I took the plowman’s hat,  
The driver’s good horse.  
Then the plowman will speak,  
The driver will answer,  
He went through our yard,  
Drank water from our river,  
Tasted water from our well.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Who was slightly lanky?  
Who was broad and lumpy?  
Who was round alone?  
Who was triangular?  
Who wore a silk shirt on their back?  
Who had long pants on their legs?  
Who had a Moorish hat on their head?  
Who had curly hair?  
Let me say it again,  
Sing it back once more,  
Soon in the old order again.  
Ernes was round alone,  
Uba was slightly lanky,  
Leats was broad and lumpy,  
Tatar was triangular,  
Nisu wore a silk shirt on their back,  
Kaer had long pants on their legs,  
Oder had a Moorish hat on their head,  
Rukkil had curly hair.

label: Töölaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Sisters, little souls,  
Where do we go, the six of us,  
Across the field, five of us?  
We go to the sunset’s tale,  
To Oleva’s hayfield,  
To disturb Kalev’s turf.  
On the beautiful grove of flowers,  
On the lovely flowered turf.  
We open wide arches,  
Turn thick arches around,  
We rake the edges.  
On golden stalks we dry  
Hay for the proud horse,  
For the rider of the strong man.  
Kalev himself carries it all,  
Oleva gathers the hay,  
They make a pile in the spruce grove,  
The harvest goes into forest clusters.  
The pile shows in the spruce grove,  
The harvest passes over the great forest.  
A thousand stallions cannot haul the pile,  
A hundred steeds cannot carry it.  
The ropes will break,  
Yoke irons will crack,  
Chest straps will snap.  
Then the fire will come,  
It will set the great pile ablaze,  
The high heap will burn,  
The large shed will smoke—  
Where then will we find a queller,  
A wide extinguisher of the flame?  
We then call the moon and the day,  
Call upon Kalev the mightier,  
The long young lad Pääva.  
He brings clouds over the head,  
A rainbow bow in his hand.  
Within the cloud falls long rain,  
On the rainbow streams of rain,  
Many tears in the clouds,  
Raindrops on the rainbow,  
Who will put out the pile,  
Make the harvest wet with rain?  
Little young sister,  
Fire is not strong against us,  
Nor is water powerless,  
Iron is hard, but we are stronger,  
The sky is our wise man.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, young men,  
Know, know, wise women,  
Guess, eager boys,  
Why don’t I understand the youth?  
What walks along the fence?  
Steps along the edge of time,  
A little bee, a little bird.  
Understand, understand, young men,  
Know, know, wise women,  
What draws water from the river?  
Touches the village wells,  
Why don’t I understand the youth:  
A rainbow draws from the river.  
Touches the village wells,  
Understand, understand, young men,  
Know, know, wise women,  
Guess, eager boys.  
What comes mooing from the meadow?  
Stepping through the blue grass,  
Rain comes mooing from the meadow,  
Stepping through the blue grass,  
Rustling from the grove.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The woodpecker pecked a piece of wood,  
It couldn't split a chip,  
It couldn't make it bigger,  
So that it would have been enough to heat the stove.  
The pig ate a lot of food.  
There was no Christmas oil,  
No holiday rye bread,  
No expensive beer to make,  
To sleep for Martinmas evening.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The dog was hungry, tail down,  
No meat was good for anything.  
The pig was foolish,  
The sheep was blindly silly.  
Yet some sweet part  
Is born to be eaten, born to be sold,  
Good for everyone's stomach.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Rise, rise, little wind,
Shine, shine, little sun.
Carry water from the grain field,
Dew from the oat stalk,
Rust from the grass blade,
Frost from the fence.
Grain sprouts with rain,
Rust spoils the grass blade,
Dew spoils the oat stalk,
Frost spoils the fence.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Go over the rain showers,  
Over the showers, I wait,  
Carrying dew to the ankles.  
Over these diligent times,  
Over these frequent rains,  
Over dark clouds,  
Over harsh heavy burdens.  
Take the rain toward Vigala,  
Scatter the rain toward Harju,  
Carry the rain to the distant places.  
For the cattle of Vigala to drink,  
For the women of Harju to wash their hands,  
To wash their hands, to wash the kerchief.  
In Harju County the men were dark,  
Men were dark, women dark,  
Boys half were dark-skinned.  
In our land the men were white,  
Men were white, women white,  
Boys half were Saxons.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What do you blow, little wind?  
Thunderstorms bring bad weather.  
The wind is not tame,  
There is no place for bad weather.  
The wind is made to blow,  
Bad weather is made to thunder.  
Why do you blow the north wind?  
You give strength to the morning.  
Why do you belch the south wind?  
You channel the evening wind.  
You blow redness on me,  
You belch blood on my face.  
With redness I cut wood,  
With beauty I mow hay,  
Blow on those who are red,  
Belch on those who are blazing.  
A little redness is on me,  
A little blood on my face.  
Redness remains on the wood,  
Beauty on the haystack,  
Blood on the manor's courtyard.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I went to the swamp for cranberries,  
To the riverbank for lingonberries,  
To the sandy meadow for purple flowers,  
To the berry meadow for strawberries.  
The buckets filled up with water,  
The stockings dampened in the swamp;  
I put the spruce to dry,  
The oak to harden,  
The willow to bleach.  
The golden spruce dries in the village,  
The beautiful oak hardens,  
The bright white willow bleaches.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Why were our lands low?  
Why was the thatch on the roof sparse?  
Why were the reeds at the eaves poor?  
Because our lands were low,  
The thatch on the roof sparse,  
The reeds at the eaves poor:  
Because cold had set in the stove,  
The ale had been poured into the troughs,  
The field had been marked by lightning.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
There are three by the edge of the forest;  
One is the dear spruce forest,  
The second is the precious birch forest,  
The third is the beautiful apple tree.  
What is the dear spruce forest  
It is the forest of young men.  
What is the precious birch forest  
It is the forest of young women,  
Of the flax-headed little birds,  
Of the braids’ tousled ones,  
Of the curly-headed beauties.  

There are three by the edge of the lake;  
One is the fierce vodka lake,  
The second is the precious kvass lake,  
The third is the sweet mead.  
What is the fierce vodka lake,  
It is the lake of young men,  
What is the precious kvass lake  
It is the lake of young women.  
What is the sweet mead,  
It is the lake of young virgins,  
Of the flax-headed little birds,  
Of the braids’ tousled ones,  
Of the curly-headed beauties.  

There are three by the edge of the field,  
One is the ripe rye field,  
The second is the precious oat field,  
The third nods from the wheat.  
What is the ripe rye field  
It is the field of young men,  
What is the precious oat field  
It is the field of young women,  
What nods from the wheat  
It is of the young virgins,  
Of the flax-headed little birds,  
Of the braids’ tousled ones,  
Of the curly-headed beauties.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What can be seen from the hill  
An apple tree can be seen from the hill  
How many branches does the apple tree have  
The apple tree had three branches  
How many apples does the apple tree have  
The apple tree had three apples  
On each branch an apple  
One was a honeyed apple  
The second was a waxed apple  
The third was written in gold  
Which was the honeyed apple  
That was my own apple  
Which was the waxed apple  
That was the dear spouse's apple  
Which was written in gold  
With it I try to deceive the father-in-law  
I try to test the spouse.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
You sleep, I am awake,
You lie down in bed,
Without stockings even in bed,-
I have a dozen fellows,
A stack of milk pails,
A heap of crow spirit boxes,
A task of busy helpers.
Down I fell the wet pines,
Wet pines, dry spruces,
I leave the birches growing,
Birch leaves to mourn,
Birch branches to groan;
Branches groan for their father,
Leaves mourn their mother,
Roots for their sons and daughters.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Over, over the rain stream,  
Over the ditch I wait,  
Enduring the dewdrop stream.  
Bring the rain towards Vigala,  
Drive the rain towards Arju,  
Bring Vigala cattle to drink,  
And Ugala to drink as well,  
To wash the hands of Arju women,  
To wash the hands, to wash the dirt.  
In Arju land the men are dark,  
Men are dark, women are rude,  
Boys like burnt stumps,  
Girls like tar stumps; -  
In our land the men were fine,  
Men were fine, boys were strong,  
Girls were fire-beautiful.  
Over, over the rain stream,  
Over the ditch I wait,  
Enduring the dewdrop stream.  
Why rain should come short,  
What use do we have without dew?  
I do not sow in the rain,  
Nor rot in the wet,  
I do not grow without dew.  
Grain sprouts with rain,  
Grass rots in the wet,  
Oats grow with dew.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh-joy rat's wedding  
The mouse's daughter got married  
To young Villem Nirki,  
He had proud wedding guests  
The wolf was a proud wedding guest  
The bear was an esteemed best man  
The fox was the ring bearer  
The hare was a lively bridesmaid.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Marist becomes the berry bringer  
The berry bringer's herdsman  
From Aru the ox driver  
From the bog the bog-dweller  
Mari brought berries from another land  
Cranberries from across the river  
But the crane stayed to listen  
The poor raven to watch  
Arakas to understand  
If only God would grant daughters  
The daughters are taken to a husband  
Then the ox is slaughtered  
A cow is milked in the field  
Then the crane gets a handful  
Arakas gets a handful of girls  
The old raven a bellyful.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Marist becomes the berry bringer  
The berry bringer's herdsman  
From Aru the ox driver  
From the bog the bog-dweller  
Mari brought berries from another land  
Cranberries from across the river  
But the crane stayed to listen  
The poor raven to watch  
Arakas to understand  
If only God would grant daughters  
The daughters are taken to a husband  
Then the ox is slaughtered  
A cow is milked in the field  
Then the crane gets a handful  
Arakas gets a handful of girls  
The old raven a bellyful.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Our village is like a small town  
A little back from Tallinn  
There is no sea down by the yard  
At the Kalajärve excavation road  
The fish swim home to us  
The peat comes into the house  
The breams go to the stove's warmth  
Roaches under the bed's edge  
Pikes under the bench  
Big pikes at the edges of the lakes  
The bullheads have colorful sides  
The breams have big broad backs  
The pikes have big broad jaws

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Our village is like a small town  
A little back from Tallinn  
There is no sea down by the yard  
At the Kalajärve excavation road  
The fish swim home to us  
The peat comes into the house  
The breams go to the stove's warmth  
Roaches under the bed's edge  
Pikes under the bench  
Big pikes at the edges of the lakes  
The bullheads have colorful sides  
The breams have big broad backs  
The pikes have big broad jaws

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Break the branches, maiden  
Break the branches, forest disease  
Leave the tender little treetop  
Leave the treetops for the birds to fly  
Leave the treetops for the wagtails to come  
The nightjar is awake at night,  
The black grouses are calling to each other.  
The male black grouse calls,  
The female black grouse calls,  
The honey buzzards call.  
The young ducks whispered:  
Where should I go,  
Where should I make my nest?  
To the blueberry bushes, to the bog  
To the thick shore bushes  
To the long reeds by the sea.  
Where those German dogs cannot reach,  
Where the master's hunters cannot reach,  
Where the sister's sharper hounds  
Cannot reach the brother's weak little voice.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The Little Red Pig  
The old sow lied:  
Let's go to the forest, grandmother!  
Sit down and suckle me  
Dig the earth and feed the piglets  
Let's go home for Christmas  
In a few days it will be Christmas.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The man goes to the beach to the sea  
Puts a shroud on his back  
Wears grave gloves on his hands  
Picks full from the Turkish mantle  
Puts the bigger ones in his mouth  
Throws the smaller ones aside  
They then grow into fish  
Climbed over into big ones  
Multiplied into herring.  
They stood along the sea  
Carried cold from village to village  
Waving in the waves.  
If only, if only, our dear brothers  
If only the sea would give fish  
Deep to catch perch  
Big to shroud over it  
Up, up, oarsmen brothers.  
Up, let's plow the sea  
Deep, let's dig a hole  
Save the edges of the sea  
Cut big nets.  
Then the sea will give fish  
Deep to catch perch  
Big to shroud over it.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Four, four little days  
Towards the evening of the fourth day  
The slave awaits the evening  
The sunlit daytime  
Master the sunrise  
The slave is tired of work  
Tired from the sunlit day.  
If only my yards were rowan trees  
Shoulder-high apple trees  
Fingers breaking currant bushes  
Forearms from maple trees.  
Then I would not tire of work  
Then I would be content with a long pole  
And when a pole of great size is born  
I will fit among the rich.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The Little Red Pig  
Said to the big pig.  
Go root in the forest  
Root among the humpbacked spruces  
Those damned bastards!  
You’ll find gold under the spruce,  
A money box under the stump  
Money from the damned ones.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Crooked weather, fierce winds  
Harsh weather, frostier  
They pass through the heart  
Through my beautiful body  
Through my red face  
Through the tangled hair  
Through the straight fingertips

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh, we poor singles  
Other poor wanderers  
Let's go out into the night  
The fox was our leg cuckold  
The wolf was the grain carrier  
The hare was the enemy sleeper  
The recruit was the thrower inside  
The duck was the courtyard boy  
The snipe was the sweeper  
The teal was the chaser  
The crow was the basket thrower  
The white bird was the sack filler  
The rooster was the sack closer  
The lynx was the honest storekeeper  
Walking with a nice stick in his hand  
The bear was eager to write  
Sat by the store door threshold  
His inkwell stood by the threshold.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo cuckoo, little cuckoo  
Yesterday you were a little wall bird  
Someday you will cuckoo for me  
But you will not cuckoo for me yesterday  
From the treetops a bang for me.  
Cuckoos for the forest herd  
Elks for the hay gatherer  
Bangs for the woodcutter.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What are you, holes beneath the cliff  
You squint, dark-faced scoundrels  
What are you watching by the bath  
Between Ravila village  
Holes on the piglet's head  
Sprats on the goat herd  
Eyes on the sow's back  
Watching the cattle herd.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Clear, clear little weather  
Clear weather to become clear  
White weather to become white  
Carry over the rain drops  
Pour over the dew drops  
Take the rain towards Vigala  
Drive the drops towards Arju  
To water the cattle in Vigala  
To wash the hands of the women in Arju  
In Vigala, the men are dark  
The boys are half dark-skinned  
The girls are brave as fire.  
On our side, the men are clean  
The boys are half clean-skinned  
The girls are as beautiful as fire.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little blue-striped bird  
Little blue-striped with golden writing  
Lendaleksi luogeleksi  
Fly over three forests  
There were three forests nearby  
One was the golden spruce forest  
The second was the precious birch forest  
The third was the beautiful apple tree.  
What was the golden spruce forest?  
It was the forest of young men.  
What was the precious birch forest?  
It was the forest of young women.  
What was the beautiful apple tree?  
It was the little young maiden.  

Little blue-striped bird  
Little blue-striped with golden writing  
Lendaleksi luogeleksi  
Fly over three fields  
There were three fields nearby  
One was the rye field of Ruuge  
The second was the precious oat field  
The third was the handsome wheat.  
What was the rye field of Ruuge?  
It was the field of young women  
What was the precious oat field?  
It was the field of young men  
What was the handsome wheat?  
It was the field of young maidens.  

Little blue-striped bird  
Little blue-striped with golden writing  
Lendaleksi luogeleksi  
Fly over three lakes  
There were three lakes nearby  
One was the lake of wrathful spirits  
The second was the lake of lamenting fishermen  
The third was the lake of sweet mead  
What was the lake of wrathful spirits?  
It was the lake of young men  
What was the lake of lamenting fishermen?  
It was the lake of young women  
What was the lake of sweet mead?  
It was the lake of the little young maiden.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Listen, you young girls!  
What can you really do?  
You have no charms at all –  
And your skirts are knee-length!  
The boys from Võivere won’t take you,  
Nor do the boys from Tapa want you.  
The boys from Kärda don’t offer their hands,  
Nor do the boys from Orina,  
You will see that time well,  
You make foolish mistakes yourselves,  
Sometimes you make stupid blunders;  
Usually, you go regularly  
To the manor crying,  
Through filth and mud;  
You have no bread dough at all,  
And a bundle of hay under your arm!  
You go to the manor yard,  
Lean against the window sills –  
You beat your chest with your fists,  
"This is my little cross" –  
The stick from Kubja is near my neck,  
That is my dear necklace.  
Now, young men are weak,  
Round and new fellows,  
Girls are free to go;  
Now the stove has been taken cold,  
Ale driven under covers –  
Written in the summer fields. –  
The girl’s ribbons remain in the town,  
The girl’s aprons remain in the shop.  
The gentlemen have stayed in the German room.  
Because young men are weak,  
Girls are free to go.
