label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Why do you sleep, little wind,  
Southern hawk,  
Do you blow red on me,  
Hawk, blood on the cheek?  
There is a little red on me,  
A little blood on the cheek.  
The red remains on the trees,  
On the face of beauty.  
Wait, wait, wind, no, no wind,  
Let summer go, let autumn come,  
Before Christmas, time will come,  
After Martin’s day or two.  
You howl at the door,  
You shout on the room's threshold,  
I want to warm you inside,  
Warm by the oven fire.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh my dear brother Ella  
Do you remember from last year  
Recall from the year before  
Where last year we lived in the meadow  
We lived in the meadow, drank honey  
There in the buttery forest  
Buttery forest, golden spruce  
Elegant butterfly  
In the healthy body, a gentle spirit  
The axe is sharp and bright  
Cut down the buttery forest  
Buttery forest, golden spruce  
Elegant butterfly  
There are buttery oats  
Buttery oats, golden barley  
Elegant rye ears  
Then take a buttery woman  
Buttery woman, golden bride  
Elegant handmaiden  
The cat plays with buttery oats  
Buttery oats, golden barley  
Elegant rye ears

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Sisters, dear little sisters  
The spruce was in dry Kuuramaa  
The alder was in wide Lianemaa  
The birch was in dear Harjumaa  
The oak was on the path to Tartumaa  
Their roots grew together  
Their branches grew together  
Their leaves flew together  
Rivers ran beneath the roots  
Coils beneath the alder  
Fish swam in the river  
They twisted and turned  
Large perch in the bays  
Wide flounders in the shallows  
Big, black seals were there  
Broad trout at the shallow ends  
Small sprats had bright eyes.  
Young men in the village  
Make a net, young men  
Võnnu girls, weave your nets  
Be quick at the net weaving  
Catch the sacred fish  
Especially the perch.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Row, swing, come, swing  
Row over three forests  
What was the spruce forest  
That was the forest of our men  
What was the pine forest  
That was the forest of our boys  
What was the birch forest  
That was the forest of our women  
Row, swing, come, swing  
Row over three fields  
What was the rye field  
That was the field of our men  
What was the barley field  
That was the field of our women  
What was the oat field  
That was the field of the foster boys  
What was the wheat field  
That was the field of the young maidens.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand young men  
Know, know wise women  
Guess, village elders.  
What rises blue in the swamp here  
Blue in the swamp, red elsewhere  
Round over the forest  
Through the moor glowing  
What is to be pondered from this  
The day rises blue in the swamp  
Blue in the swamp, red elsewhere  
Round over the forest still  
Through the moor glowing.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What flows from the river,  
Touches the village wells,  
Among the gravel stones?  
What comes while drinking from the field,  
In the bends of the blue grass?  
"The rainbow flows from the river,  
Touches the village wells,  
Among the gravel stones!  
Rain comes while drinking from the field,  
In the bends of the blue grass."

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I am a man, a sailor’s son,  
A dear man, a fisherman’s son.  
I sit on the side of the boat;  
I pick bullheads from the peat,  
The bigger ones I put in my mouth,  
The smaller ones I throw away,  
There they grow in the fish,  
They rise to the surface.  
Here are big whitefish, black vendace,  
Salmon are strong, and burbot at the end.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
One was an apple tree on the hill  
Two were branches on the apple tree,  
Five were branches full of blossoms,  
Ten apples among the blossoms;  
My bride is a maiden:  
She went to pick apples,  
A silver basket in her hand,  
A golden handle on the basket,  
Picking apples, picking berries.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo little cuckoo,  
Helgi gentle little bird!  
You won’t cuckoo for me,  
You cuckoo for the village shepherds,  
For traps for the wood robber,  
For shots at the firewood thief.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow is a quiet little bird,  
It flew to the streets of the town  
From there it flew to the tavern,  
To the innkeeper's funeral.  
The crow made the branch drunk,  
Called together the sad birds  
Left out the hawk uninvited,  
The hawk was angry and destructive  
Tore apart the lark's jaw,  
The swallow's wreath ribbon  
Pushed the cuckoo aside,  
Siu säu seilali  
Whose wheel is spinning in the distaff?  
The old grandmother's spinning wheel  
Wool in the basket, burrs in the shed,  
Bagcloth in the rooster's beak

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Who was a bit elongated?  
Oat a bit elongated.  
Who was quite round?  
Pea quite round.  
Who was broad-shouldered?  
Lentil broad-shouldered.  
Who wore long pants?  
Barley wore long pants.  
Who had curly hair?  
Rye had curly hair.  
Who wore a silk shirt?  
Wheat wore a silk shirt.  
Who wore a Moorish hat?  
Spelt wore a Moorish hat.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The shark raids on the rat's wedding,
The mouse's daughter got married,
To the younger son of Nirgi.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Now it is born to be proud as a slave  
Proud as a servant  
Even a smelt looks splendid  
Already a rye ear seems to appear  
Barley ears are greening  
Oat ears are wetting the ground  
Lentils stretch without stems  
Beans new pods  
Wheat should be husky.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The golden script of Blue-row  
The little bird of Blue-row  
Flying, fluttering  
Flew over three forests.  
Three forests stood side by side  
One was the golden spruce forest  
The second the dear birch forest  
The third a beautiful apple tree  
Which was the golden spruce forest  
That was the forest of young men  
Which was the dear birch forest  
That was the forest of young women.  
Which was the beautiful apple tree  
That was the forest of young maidens.  

The golden script of Blue-row  
The little bird of Blue-row  
Flying, fluttering  
Flew over three lakes.  
Three lakes stood side by side  
One was the lake of bitter liquor  
The second the lake of true beer  
The third was sweet mead  
Which was the lake of bitter liquor  
That was the lake of young men  
Which was the lake of true beer  
That was the lake of young women  
Which was the sweet mead  
That was the lake of young maidens.  

The golden script of Blue-row  
The little bird of Blue-row  
Flying, fluttering  
Flew over three fields.  
Three fields stood side by side  
One was a rye field  
The second a dear oat field  
The third a beautiful wheat field.  
Which was the rye field  
That was the field of young women  
Which was the dear oat field  
That was the field of young men  
Which was the beautiful wheat field  
That was the field of young maidens.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The coastal man goes to the sea  
Puts on his death shirt  
Death mittens on his hands  
Death stockings on his feet  
He picks fish even from the market  
Bigger ones he puts in his mouth  
Smaller ones he throws away  
These then grow into fish  
Rise up to become sprats  
Multiply into smelts  
Descend into burbots  
They stand there in the sea  
In the cold water  
Like rippling waves  
If, if, dear brothers  
If the sea does not give fish  
Big perch to the sprats  
The sea will not show its shores  
Deep like a hole with perch  
The sea will not show its waves like pike  
Up, up, brothers  
Up we plow the sea  
Let us save the shores of the sea  
Let us cut down big islands  
Then the sea will give fish  
Deep like a hole with perch  
Big perch to the sprats.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Knowledge-gains in the oak forest,  
We cut down the pine forest,  
We leave the birches to grow,  
Birch branches to moan,  
Birch leaves to mourn:  
Branches moan father,  
Leaves mourn mother,  
Shoots weep poor children.  
We go along this path,  
Along low-lying land,  
Where the road had a summit,  
The ground was thick with moss,  
Where before slaves had walked,  
Slaves had walked, bulls had passed,  
Early had walked the poor children,  
Late came the masters.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Knowledge-gains in the oak forest,  
We cut down the pine forest,  
We leave the birches to grow,  
Birch branches to moan,  
Birch leaves to mourn:  
Branches moan father,  
Leaves mourn mother,  
Shoots weep poor children.  
We go along this path,  
Along low-lying land,  
Where the road had a summit,  
The ground was thick with moss,  
Where before slaves had walked,  
Slaves had walked, bulls had passed,  
Early had walked the poor children,  
Late came the masters.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little Sister, Little Old Lady  
Why are we, dear ones, sorrowful?  
Already withering is the leafy tree,  
The leafy tree, the grass in the ground.  
The barley ear is greening,  
The oat ear covers the ground,  
The lentils shine up to the shin,  
The beans grow beautifully.  
Bean pod is a good pod,  
Lentil pod is a dull pod.  
The lentil was liter-shaped,  
The bean a bit elongated,  
The pea quite round.  
I keep the old woman’s goats,  
And also protect the house’s herd.  
The old woman made me a new kerchief,  
More reddish ribbons.  
Oh, a wolf came into the herd,  
Took a lamb while going to the forest,  
And tore the old woman’s patchwork apart.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Oh dear, a rat's wedding -  
The mouse's daughter got married,  
To the youngest son of Nirgi.  
There were splendid wedding guests,  
Wedding guests, visitors; -  
There were strong men with zithers,  
A Swedish green rattle,  
Stronger sounds than far away;  
The crane came with a golden spur,  
The finch came with felt boots,  
The fox came with a linden branch sled,  
The hen came with a birch whip,  
The wolf came with a new horn,  
The bear came with two crutches,  
The cat came in a two-wheeled carriage, -  
So the guests gathered together.  
A coat was sewn for the crane,  
The magpie was helped out,  
The owl was pinched on the backside.  
A black bird came chasing eggs,  
A variegated bird came chasing skeins.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I am forced to go  
I am bullied against my will  
They shout over my mind.  
They do not think they know more  
Nor do they think to understand more  
To consider me wise.  
I am always clever  
Clever as a cunning hen  
I know the stars in the sky  
I read the waves in the sea  
Ten eels into the sea  
They stood at the shore of the sea  
In the rippling waves  
In the village's cold bay  
In the cold spring’s water  
Deep in the swamp  
Low in the mud  
On the cunning shore.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I went to town to spy,  
To Paide to braid,  
Horse reins in hand,  
Leading a foal with a halter;  
I went near the sandy town,  
Met a group of girls,  
Ran into a single girl:  
"Have you seen my horse?"  
"What color is your horse?"  
"Morning squirrel-colored,  
At noon boar-colored,  
After midday chief-colored."

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Hour, hour little rain,  
Let it fall kindly,  
Maybe snow will melt,  
Rain will pour down from the rod,  
A serf must be,  
A serf must work,  
The serf is considered diligent,  
The mistress is pleased,  
The serf is praised,  
The mistress is blamed,  
The cat wants, it will kill me,  
The cat is willing, it strikes,  
The cat thinks, it hits!

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Hour, hour little rain,  
Let it fall kindly,  
Maybe snow will melt,  
Rain will pour down from the rod,  
A serf must be,  
A serf must work,  
The serf is considered diligent,  
The mistress is pleased,  
The serf is praised,  
The mistress is blamed,  
The cat wants, it will kill me,  
The cat is willing, it strikes,  
The cat thinks, it hits!

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Hour, hour little rain,  
Let it fall kindly,  
Maybe snow will melt,  
Rain will pour down from the rod,  
A serf must be,  
A serf must work,  
The serf is considered diligent,  
The mistress is pleased,  
The serf is praised,  
The mistress is blamed,  
The cat wants, it will kill me,  
The cat is willing, it strikes,  
The cat thinks, it hits!

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Come home, dear little head,  
Your brother has already taken a wife.  
The other brother went to fetch herbs,  
The titmouse made it without oil,  
The water poured into the vat.  
The willow bird placed knots.  
Called the colors to the row:  
"The color was angry to quarrel,  
To quarrel - to argue.  
They pulled the hair of the color,  
The little head was struck with blood.  
They sent for a doctor there—  
Brought together to treat,  
Placed together a line of prayers,  
No more words to live by."

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I made warm beers for the swamp  
Hot beers for the willow.  
The wolf went to do without beer  
The bear to the thick forest trunks.  
The titmouse with tin wings  
The duck went in a wig on its head  
The wolf’s straw hat was taken –  
The bear’s fur to the rangeland  
The titmouse’s whip was broken!

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Why do you blow, little wind,
Bang, bad weather?
Today my man is at sea,
Together at the fishing river.
Blow tomorrow, little wind,
Bang, bad weather!
Tomorrow the brides at sea,
The kittiwakes – at the fishing river.
The brides went to the sea,
The kittiwakes – at the fishing river.
I heard the boys banging,
Throwing men into the sea:
I thought it was my fate,
I ran running, I walked walking,
My feet started burning,
Spark scars – scorching,
There was a stranger, a roughneck,
I helped to bind his hands,
To lead a ring on his foot –
To fasten the cuff.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Why do you blow, little wind,
Bang, bad weather?
Today my man is at sea,
Together at the fishing river.
Blow tomorrow, little wind,
Bang, bad weather!
Tomorrow the brides at sea,
The kittiwakes – at the fishing river.
The brides went to the sea,
The kittiwakes – at the fishing river.
I heard the boys banging,
Throwing men into the sea:
I thought it was my fate,
I ran running, I walked walking,
My feet started burning,
Spark scars – scorching,
There was a stranger, a roughneck,
I helped to bind his hands,
To lead a ring on his foot –
To fasten the cuff.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The cold makes me freeze,  
I endure the cold,  
I bear the pain.  
Wait, wait, cold no, no, cold,  
Let my room be made,  
The home's door is knocked upon,  
The house's door is beaten on,  
Wolves howl outside the door,  
Snares behind our hut,  
You shout at the chamber's threshold.  
You seek the warmth of the building,  
A heated room,  
A glazed window.  
Ask, ask little cold,  
Go away down,  
Surely the warm summer will come someday,  
Haytime and beautiful,  
Days sunny and hot,  
Warm days of the rye field.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
The crow, the gentle little bird  
Wearing a blue shirt  
Below is an unlined quilt  
Flew over three forests  
Three are the forests of Kõrvistik  
One is the golden spruce forest  
The second is the dear birch forest  
The third is the beautiful apple tree forest  
What is the golden spruce forest?  
It is the forest of young men.  
What is the dear birch forest?  
It is the forest of young women.  
What is the beautiful apple tree forest?  
It is of the young maidens.  
Flew over three fields  
Three were the fields of Kõrvistik:  
One is the dear oat field  
The second is the rye field of Ruuge  
The third is the beautiful wheat field  
What is the dear oat field?  
It is the field of young men  
What is the rye field of Ruuge?  
It is the field of young women  
What is the beautiful wheat field?  
It is of the young maidens.  
Flew over three lakes,  
Three were the lakes of Kõrvistik  
One was the lake of wrath, Viinajärv  
It is the lake of young men  
The second is the dear rocky lake  
It is the lake of young women  
The third is the lake of sweet honey  
It is of the young maidens  
Flew into our cage  
We have three nests in the cage  
One was a blue nest  
The second was a red nest  
The third was golden-haired  
He despised the blue nest,  
He despised the red nest  
He began to love gold  
He began making a nest:  
"Made one, made two  
Made three at the top  
Four in the week  
Then he began to lay eggs:  
Laid one, laid two,  
Laid three at the top  
Four in the week  
Then he began making chicks:  
Made one, made two  
Made three at the top  
Four in the week.  
A quarter, five in number  
The chicks became bloodthirsty  
Then he began counting the chicks:  
"The first was the handsome father  
The second was the quiet mother  
The third became the dog boy  
The fourth became the watchman  
The fifth became the playboy  
The playboy above all  
He went from Narva to bring a bride  
From Peterborough wedding guests  
At Säsnamäe he sang tales  
At the market he beat the drum  
So that those walls trembled  
The foundations cracked  
Kettles broke on the fire  
Down went Major’s house  
Pots and pans shattered  
Wall clocks off the walls.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
What are you milking, a dairy cow?  
Are you milking a pregnant cow?  
You are milking an old red one.  
You are milking as if crazy.  
From the red bun’s hiding place,  
From the blue ox’s dung,  
Your stomach runs along the ground,  
Your belly runs along the straw.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The wedding of the runaway rat  
The mouse's daughter got married  
To Nirgi's younger son.  
They had magnificent wedding guests:  
The rooster had golden spurs,  
The pig wore silk boots,  
The crow had copper toes,  
The mole had a black carriage,  
The titmouse was with a tin cart.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cold weather, chilling winds,  
Big autumn winds,  
Cold spring winds.  
I am chilled by the cold,  
I chill my hands,  
My toes are aching.  
My dear old woman!  
Bring me snowflakes,  
Send me from the raincoat,  
Roll me a rain blanket.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Raven, gentle little bird,  
Laid eggs under our hill.  
In the nest came out five chicks.  
One became a handsome father,  
Another became a calm mother,  
The third became a puppy boy,  
The fourth became a wheat guard,  
The fifth a playful fellow.  
The playful fellow was the cleverest.  
He went to town to bring a wife:  
In the small town, he played a pipe,  
In the market square, he beat a drum,  
So that the walls shook,  
The floor beams rattled,  
Pots broke on the fire,  
Pots broke in the shop.  
Then the poor man began to stumble,  
The man with tight pants began to weave.  
The captors came,  
The reins in the seeker’s hand,  
The rope with the captor.  
I would tear apart the rope’s threads,  
Tear the reins to the end.  
Then the man with tight pants began to weave,  
Across the field, across the field,  
Sometimes along the fence in the yard.  
What will come against me?  
Then came to the golden spruce forest,  
Then to the low pine forest,  
Then to the rare open forest,  
Then I went through the alder forest.  
Then I gave power to my hands,  
Then I gave power to my feet,  
Power, power to my toes:  
Row your legs, reach your hands.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
If I had my own horse,
I would ride to Sõerumäe,
There I would eat gooseberries,
I would crack hazelnuts;
I would bring bird cherry home
For the hungry children to nibble,
The family ate fish roe,
Slaves ate spruce twig,
Children ate pine tops,
I myself chewed cow parsnip roots.
I am a red cow parsnip,
Handsome on the haystack,
Strong on the firewood,
Mighty in the water.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Shine, shine little sun!  
Shine your day upon us,  
Do not shine upon the shore!  
The fishermen eat fish,  
The coastal children eat pike bread –  
We poor ones [eat] water bread,  
The older ones [eat] salt bread,  
The smaller ones [eat] milk bread,  
The stingiest [eat] beer bread.  
Keep shining, little sun,  
Keep shining upon us,  
Do not shine upon the shore!

label: Looduslaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Shine, shine little sun!  
Shine your day upon us,  
Do not shine upon the shore!  
The fishermen eat fish,  
The coastal children eat pike bread –  
We poor ones [eat] water bread,  
The older ones [eat] salt bread,  
The smaller ones [eat] milk bread,  
The stingiest [eat] beer bread.  
Keep shining, little sun,  
Keep shining upon us,  
Do not shine upon the shore!

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The mouse's daughter got married
To Nirgi's youngest son.
They had splendid wedding guests:
The crane with golden spurs,
The crow with copper toes,
The titmouse with a tin whistle,
The magpie with a silver sled.
And what proud musicians:
The wren struck the violins with its wing,
The partridge played the willow pipe.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, my sisters,
Inquire, wise women,
What is seen from the apple tree hill?
The apple tree is seen from the hill.
How many branches does the apple tree have?
The apple tree has three branches.
How many flowers does the branch have?
The branch has three flowers.
How many apples does the flower have?
The flower has three apples:
One is dipped in honey,
Another is coated in wax,
The third is written in gold.
What is dipped in honey -
That is my own apple;
What is coated in wax -
That is my dear wife’s apple;
What is written in gold -
With that I deceive the suitors,
I pick the young men.
I tempt the man with songs,
I test my wife with sweets,
Whether there is a pipe for spirits,
Or a slim loaf of bread.
No beer is given without money,
No anger is stirred into the spirits,
No German coin without a thaler.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Raven, gentle little bird,  
You laid eggs under Saare's hill.  
My maiden got several pieces,  
The village maiden got ten pieces,  
The plowman got a hatful,  
The harrower got a bucketful,  
The guard got a measureful.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Sa'a, sa'a little rain,
Sa'a rain to water the Saxons,
Leave us unwatered!
We have no home dryers,
The Saxons have home dryers.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The men of Nõnna sang  
Our men and Merja's men  
The laborers of Aruküla parish  
The spirited boys of Väinjärve parish  
When the roosters crowed  
The ladies sang under the sky  
Scattering the field birds  
Walking among the stumps  
Calling the seals to the sea  
Shouting to the seagulls  
Singing away into the wilderness.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The pig rides on the sow;
A tiny red piglet
Hey, go to Võera's grain field
One comes carrying a bundle on their back
Another comes carrying a pole on their back
The third comes with a dog following.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The ride of the pig, boar, piglet,  
Little red piglet,  
Go graze in the rye field:  
One comes carrying a bundle on the shoulder,  
Another comes carrying a pole on the shoulder,  
The third follows like a dog.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
The crow caws in the maple  
The ass grunts in the rowan  
Waiting for my horse to die  
The earless one to be lame  
The napkin to fall  
Crow, crow, little brother  
My horse will not die  
My earless one will not be lame  
Let my napkin fall  
I have wise stable boys  
Dutiful groom boys  
They made stables in the sky  
Stable doors in the clouds  
Lanterns near the open field.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
The crow caws in the maple  
The ass grunts in the rowan  
Waiting for my horse to die  
The earless one to be lame  
The napkin to fall  
Crow, crow, little brother  
My horse will not die  
My earless one will not be lame  
Let my napkin fall  
I have wise stable boys  
Dutiful groom boys  
They made stables in the sky  
Stable doors in the clouds  
Lanterns near the open field.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little rooster, little hen  
Went scurrying in a race  
The rooster went under the rich man's thigh  
The hen to the poor man's hut roof  
The rooster ate one barley grain  
The hen two oat grains  
They put the fish on the platter  
Rolled it down to the mill  
Rolled the hand millstone  
Paid the bride price to both  
The second to the house owner  
The third to the one who set the platter  
They started brewing beer  
From there they got a hornful of swill  
A hornful to the mother goat’s ear.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Looduslaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I sing, why don’t I sing  
I sing a song about a horse  
Its head is a walnut  
Its body made of haystacks  
Its tail from hay remnants  
Its hooves from birch bark  
Its shoes from antlers  
Nails from fence posts  
I am a man, son of a sailor  
Dear boy, son of a fisherman  
I step to the edge of the boat  
I row with a big ship  
Push someone else’s sea  
Drown someone else’s new moon  
I dunk someone else’s coat  
I, man, go to the forest  
A fine boy, but from the boys' field  
Dear boy, but from Kapu manor  
Famous boy, but from Kauska parish  
Wait, wait, dear boys  
Bear with it, boys of Kapu  
Hold on, boys of Preedi  
The boys of Karina are coming  
Mouths big, noses thick  
Upper lip was long  
Lower teeth wide  
This broth too is for drinking  
Rushing to harvest.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Let the girls take the empty ones,  
The empty ways of the girls,  
They picked berries even from the forest,  
Cranberries from the riverbank,  
They made the cranes walk,  
The toes to shuffle,  
The capercaillies to stumble,  
Then we will see  
What you did to us yesterday.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I always plowed,
I pranced for fun,
The bulls of the manor did not manage to respond,
The pride bulls of Kihelkonna,
The wide-horned bulls of Laupa manor,
The basket bulls of Kolu manor,
But I, a man with a Michaelmas head,
Jüssike with a St. George’s Day head.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little sister,
Why do we grieve, dear,
Already the leaves are lying on the tree,
On the tree leaves, on the grass on the ground,
The barley ear is turning green,
The oat ear is covering the field,
Lentils are blue up to the calf,
Beans are growing beautifully,
Bean pod, good pod,
Lentil pod, slender pod,
The lentil was small and shriveled,
The bean slightly elongated,
The pea quite round.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Wedding of the Oju-roju rats.  
Iire's daughter got married  
To Nirgi's younger son.  
There were splendid wedding guests  
There were strong players  
There were bagpipe blowers  
There were accordion players,  
A crane came with golden spurs  
A finch came with felt boots  
A fox came with a linden bark sled  
A hen came with a birch bark cradle  
A wolf came with a new six-shooter  
A bear came with two crutches,  
Coats were sewn for the crane  
Help was given to the hawk  
The magpie was pinched on the butt.  
A black bird came chasing eggs  
A spotted bird came chasing yarn balls

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand the riddle, my little sisters,  
Know it, know it, young maidens:  
"What is the fiery flame indoors,  
What is the bright bird outdoors,  
What is the gatekeeper outdoors,  
What is the tall walker outdoors,  
What is the rich one in the chest outdoors;  
What is the tipa-töpa on the road,  
What is the mita-mäta in the forest?  
What is the meaning of the riddle,  
What is the answer to the question?"  

The cat is the fiery flame indoors,  
The rooster is the bright bird outdoors,  
The dog is the gatekeeper outdoors,  
The gate is the tall walker outdoors,  
The barn is the rich one in the chest outdoors,  
The apple is the tipa-töpa on the road,  
The bear is the mita-mäta in the forest.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Whose legs have long pants?  
Oat has long pants on its legs.  
What is quite round?  
Pea is quite round.  
What is slightly elongated?  
Bean is slightly elongated.  
What is broadly elongated?  
Lentil is broadly elongated.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Who tastes the sea water
Drinks, however, the river water on the meadow,
Takes water from the spring
Tries the village wells
Amidst the stones and gravel?
A colorful little brother rainbow
He tastes the sea water
Drinks, however, the river water on the meadow
Tries the village wells
Takes water from the spring
Amidst the stones and gravel.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, my little sister  
Who has beautiful flowers?  
I understand, why don’t you understand  
The bean has beautiful flowers.  
Understand, understand, my little sister  
Who is full to the brim?  
I understand, why don’t you understand  
The lentil is full to the brim.

label: Improvisatsioonid  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Forget the dark days  
Ancient days of worry,  
Days of toil and pain,  
Days of slavery’s torment.  
Already the rye heads seem,  
Wheat heads are just visible above the sheath,  
Barley ears are greening,  
Oat ears cover the ground.  
Lentils shine on the legs,  
Peas adorn the clothes,  
Beans also grow in their places.  
Cabbage beautifully sways its head,  
Cauliflowers grow leaves,  
Turnips grow leaves in the barn.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Why are our boys sad
Silent are the boys of our parish
Sad are the new hats,
Because, because, dear brothers,
Because our darlings are sad
Silent are the boys of our parish
Sad are the new hats.
Because the cold has gripped the stove
Because there is ale
Written with a field whip
Because our birds stayed in the city
Our carts remain at the shop
German swords in the cupboard
New ankle boots stayed in the yard
The brooch stayed on the friend's table
The ring stayed in the friend's chamber
The kettle stayed in the angry cellar
Because the wife remained untaken
The sister remained unlooked at
The tin chest urgently
The silver-necklace unworn
The braids untested.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Sirisirje little bird  
Sirisirje, golden writing  
Lendaliksi, loogeleksi  
Flew over three forests  
One is the dear spruce forest  
The second is the beloved birch forest  
The third is the beautiful apple tree  
Blue writing, golden writing  
Sirisirje little bird  
Lendaleksi, loogeleksi  
Flew over three fields  
One is the rye field of Ruuge  
The second is the beloved oat field  
The third is the beautiful wheat  
Sirisirje little bird  
Sirisirje, golden writing  
Lendaleksi, loogeleksi  
Flew over three lakes  
One is the lake of youthful drink  
The second the true beer lake  
The third is the lake of sweet mead  
Sirisirje little bird  
Sirisirje, golden writing  
Lendaleksi, loogeleksi  
Flew over three ships  
One is the dear bread ship  
The second the beloved trading ship  
The third is the dear money ship. -  
What is the dear spruce forest?  
That is the forest of young men.  
What is the beloved birch forest?  
That is the forest of women and children.  
What is the beautiful apple tree?  
That is the young maiden.  
What is the rye field of Ruuge?  
That is the bread field of women.  
What is the beloved oat field?  
That is the field of our men.  
What is the beautiful wheat?  
That is the young maiden.  
What is the lake of youthful drink?  
That is the lake of young men.  
What is the true beer lake?  
That is the lake of young women.  
What is the lake of sweet mead?  
That is the young maiden.  
What is the bread ship?  
That is the ship of women and children.  
What is the beloved trading ship?  
That is the young maiden's ship.  
What is the dear money ship?  
That is the ship of young men.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
A young virgin maiden  
Why do you resist coming to us  
Coming to us, joining us  
We won’t put you to hard work  
The wolf will wash our clothes  
The bear will scratch the hearth fire  
The fox will beat the knives for us  
We have gutters under our thighs  
Fish gutters into the chamber.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
A young virgin maiden

Fish gutters into the chamber.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
My wife goes to the river to fish
The night wants river fish
Big pike in the pike trap
I bustle as it rumbles
The seashores thunder
Father desires to fish
Brother good bream fishing
For the lady, burbot's slippery ones
The burbot's loins, the salmon run out
Oh you slippery burbot
Yesterday you swam into the sea
The southern loosestrifes to the spring
Now you dance on the spikes
You strike the burbot with a spoon.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:

The night wants river fish
Big pike in the pike trap
I bustle as it rumbles
The seashores thunder
Father desires to fish
Brother good bream fishing
For the lady, burbot's slippery ones
The burbot's loins, the salmon run out
Oh you slippery burbot
Yesterday you swam into the sea
The southern loosestrifes to the spring
Now you dance on the spikes
You strike the burbot with a spoon.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Ui-tui-tui-tui, in the market
Tomorrow I will forge an iron oak
From the heart I ate at the dining table
From the treetop came the ship's anchor rope
From the shoot came the ship's barrel
From the shingles came the lantern
From the branches came the horses' stable
The ship was then finished
Then I climbed onto the ship's bow
And picked a handful of mushrooms
I took the bigger ones into my mouth
The smaller ones I threw into the sea.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Because my brother was a little weakling  
A weak little finger  
He made a room facing the wind  
A sauna by the island's edge  
On top of purple flags  
Half a tower made of cranberry leaves  
How those oxen  
Work well for him  
Both pull the furrow  
He plows the field  
Carries the hay on his back  
The white head trembles.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Because my brother was a little weakling  
A weak little finger  
He made a room facing the wind  
A sauna by the island's edge  
On top of purple flags  
Half a tower made of cranberry leaves  
How those oxen  
Work well for him  
Both pull the furrow  
He plows the field  
Carries the hay on his back  
The white head trembles.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Who is plowing a furrow in the field?  
The pig is plowing a furrow in the field.  
The crow went to sow seeds,  
The hare followed to cut,  
The squirrel to gather,  
The mink to make nests,  
The nightjar to clean the field,  
The flea to gather inside,  
The beetle was placed on the parsnip,  
The magpie began to lift.  
A special little lady  
Started to prepare.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lastelaulud
translation:
Oh, poor little virbelane!
Virbelane, värbelane.
Where should I build my nest?
A family piles up a stack.
On the farm's wise buildings.
The family's maiden girl,
She went to bring wood from the stack.
She threw my nest from the stack.
From the farm's wise buildings.
Oh, poor little virbelane,
Virbelane, värbelane!
Where should I build my nest?
To lay eggs inside?
To hatch young below?
On the dry spruce tree in the forest,
Behind the oak tree.
Where did that dry spruce come from?
An old man cut the spruce down.
Where did that old man come from?
The old man died.
Where was he buried?
He was buried in Matsi's field.
Where did that Matsi field come from?
Working bulls plowed it up.
Where did those working bulls come from?
A bear ate the working bulls.
Where did that bear come from?
A German shot the bear.
Where did that German come from?
The German went to town.
Where did that town come from?
The town burned down.
Where did the town’s ashes go?
To the new town's street,
Under the old town's rampart.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lastelaulud
translation:
Oh, poor little virbelane!
Virbelane, värbelane.
Where should I build my nest?
A family piles up a stack.
On the farm's wise buildings.
The family's maiden girl,
She went to bring wood from the stack.
She threw my nest from the stack.
From the farm's wise buildings.
Oh, poor little virbelane,
Virbelane, värbelane!
Where should I build my nest?
To lay eggs inside?
To hatch young below?
On the dry spruce tree in the forest,
Behind the oak tree.
Where did that dry spruce come from?
An old man cut the spruce down.
Where did that old man come from?
The old man died.
Where was he buried?
He was buried in Matsi's field.
Where did that Matsi field come from?
Working bulls plowed it up.
Where did those working bulls come from?
A bear ate the working bulls.
Where did that bear come from?
A German shot the bear.
Where did that German come from?
The German went to town.
Where did that town come from?
The town burned down.
Where did the town’s ashes go?
To the new town's street,
Under the old town's rampart.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Row, swing, reach, swing,  
Row over three forests.  
There are three forests alongside:  
One is the golden spruce forest,  
That is the forest of our men.  
The second is the precious birch forest,  
That is the forest of our women.  
The third is the forest of apple trees,  
That is the forest of young maidens.  

Row, swing, reach, swing,  
Row over three meadows.  
There are three meadows alongside:  
One is the rye meadow of Ruuga,  
That is the meadow of our men,  
The second is the precious oat meadow,  
That is the meadow of our women.  
The third is sown with wheat,  
That is the meadow of our maidens.  

Row, swing, reach, swing,  
Row over three lakes.  
There are three lakes alongside:  
One is the anger of the moonshine lake,  
That is the lake of our men.  
The second is the precious rock lake,  
That is the lake of our women.  
The third is illuminated by a light,  
That is the lake of our maidens.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The lord has mouse gray  
The king has gold ears  
My brother has blood red.  
. . . . . . . . . . . . . .

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Young girls, young maidens,  
Do you still want to sing?  
If you want, then you are able.  
The apple tree stands on the hill,  
How many branches does the apple tree have?  
There was one branch on the apple tree.  
How many blossoms on the branch?  
There was one blossom on the branch.  
How many apples on the blossom?  
There was one apple on the blossom,  
This apple was of a certain kind:  
From the Russian side it was streaked,  
From the ground side it was wavy,  
From the German side it was velvety.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Why are the young men weak,
Silent are the boys of our parish?
Because the young men are weak.
Silent are the boys of our parish:
For frost had taken the crops,
Ale was driven away;
Lightning had marked the fields,
Hail had damaged the rye.
I sowed barley in the valley,
Oats below the slope;
The horse ate the barley in the valley.
The bear ate the oats by the slope.
Could not brew clear beer,
Could not get red wheat;
Could not get a bit of rye
Nor a young woman.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
I made little marriage cakes to the swamp,  
Little playthings on the top of a hummock.  
But who made them without oil?  
The titmouse made them without oil.  
But who carried beer water?  
The heron carried water.  
But who put the grubs?  
The willow warbler put the grubs.  
Who was then called the cook?  
The cuckoo was called the cook.  
The cuckoo boiled the barley broth,  
Added a little ginger,  
Often added bileweed:  
But set the saw to work,  
The saw women to peck at the duck.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The cuckoo falls into the spruce forest,  
I play in the pine forest,  
Cuckoo’s sons in the lap,  
Cuckoo’s daughters in the hand.  
The cuckoo to my prayer:  
The family daughter, a young maiden,  
Give my sons to the hand!  
Tomorrow I will go to a new town  
Buy new leather shoes  
I will lace them with silk ribbons  
On top, red ribbons  
Underneath, beautiful anklets.  
Let me say again:  
The cuckoo does not fall to me,  
The cuckoo falls to the herdsman,  
Brightens the haymaker,  
Traps the hunter,  
Knocks on the woodcutter.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The wedding of the rats of Huidu, Ruidu  
The mouse’s daughter got married,  
To Nirgi the younger’s son,  
To Kärbi the second servant.  
The rat had a metal boat,  
The crow had copper toes,  
The wolf was a mad captain,  
The bear had a golden hammer.  
The crow had five sons  
Who hatched five sons:  
From one came a late father,  
From another a calm mother,  
From the third a dog boy,  
From the fourth a barn guard,  
From the fifth a player.  
He went from the town to bring a bride,  
The city walls rattled.  
The bottom beams cracked,  
The mother lifted her hat.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Some men were like this:
Some were quite round,
Some were a bit elongated,
Some wide and awkward,
Some had curly hair,
Some wore long pants.
But let me start anew,
I trample these words back:
Ernes was quite round,
Uba a bit elongated,
Lääts was wide and awkward,
Rukkil had curly hair,
Kaera wore long pants,
Lina had a death cap on her head.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The male black grouse curled  
Lowered its head towards the female  
Where should I gather the nest,  
Under the spruce or in the marsh,  
Under the aspen or in the grove,  
Under the pine or on the hill.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Capercaillie in the tree, buds in the mouth  
Me in the ground, berry in the mouth  
Capercaillie in the tree, cook the porridge  
Me underneath or I melt.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Jüri told me,  
I tell Jüri.  
He shot a moose on the field,  
A bear on the barley corner.  
He took the rifle from the bench,  
From the stable of the loigu iron,  
From the window of the ammu iron.  
He shot the moose in the spleen,  
The bear in the fur of the back.  
The moose went to the forest patch by patch,  
The bear went to the grove step by step,  
Dripping behind.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
The crow made a fuss.  
Gave an order to the lads.  
Left the goldfinch uninvited.  
The goldfinch was angry and started to pull.  
Pulled off the hat of the hatter.  
Broke the woodpecker’s iron cap.  
The titmouse made beer.  
The poor hen carried water.  
The bald head set a trap.  
The bald head broke wood.

label: Töölaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
If only it would rain, one could sleep,  
If autumn came, one could nap,  
Take the rain to Vigalas,  
Send the rain to Arjumaa.  
In Arjumaa, the men were dark,  
Dark men, lazy women.  
The girls were stubborn,  
The boys somewhat more sober.  
They played music without worry,  
Carried their kantele without noise.  
There was no beginning to light the stove,  
No way to throw in rye stalks.  
In the room, a bog forest was visible,  
A fire burning in the forest,  
Behind the stove, a wilderness.

label: Töölaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
If only it would rain, one could sleep,  
If autumn came, one could nap,  
Take the rain to Vigalas,  
Send the rain to Arjumaa.  
In Arjumaa, the men were dark,  
Dark men, lazy women.  
The girls were stubborn,  
The boys somewhat more sober.  
They played music without worry,  
Carried their kantele without noise.  
There was no beginning to light the stove,  
No way to throw in rye stalks.  
In the room, a bog forest was visible,  
A fire burning in the forest,  
Behind the stove, a wilderness.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
I do not sing, why do I not sing  
But I sing – barn to the yard –  
Barn to the yard,  
Behind the barn to the stable yard  
Into the stable a lantern  
In the lantern a blank stallion  
To the blank stallion a saddle  
To the saddle a German boy  
To the German boy a hat  
To the hat a golden ribbon  
To the golden ribbon a knitter  
To the knitter a golden knife  
To the golden knife a caster  
To the caster a copper mold  
---------------------------  
The magpie barked at me  
The old crow cursed at me  
Sat on the stable door  
Wanted to eat raw meat  
Waited for my horse to die  
For the tall stallion to fall  
For the blank stallion to fall  
I had wise stable boys

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Let the girls take the empty (basket)  
May they disappear without growing  
Kessi picks berries from the ground  
Berries from the ground, lingonberries from the tree  
Cranberries from the river’s end  
She lets the cranes walk  
Walk on their toes  
Move on their heels

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Rain, rain little rain  
Don't rain on us  
Rain on the Germans  
We don't have a home to dry  
The one who washes the new suit  
The one who sews the handkerchief  
The German's home dryer  
The one who washes the new suit  
The one who irons the white shirt  
The one who washes the fine shirt  
The one who sews the handkerchief

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Little goat, little maiden  
The goat's foal was a little plant  
The goat ate leaves from the alder grove  
Hay from the willow grove  
Golden-headed from the spruce grove  
But the wolf waited in the valley  
Come here, little goat  
I will bring you a red apron  
I will bring a dear neckerchief  
I will bring new closed shoes  
The fox shouted from the brushwood  
Little goat, little maiden  
Don't be foolish to trust  
The angry wolf to lie  
Blood on the red apron  
Lungs and liver as a necklace  
Skin for your dear neckerchief  
Hooves as new closed shoes  
Intestines as a silk ribbon

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Roll, roll, little day  
Roll because of the day's evening  
Because of the kings' gold  
Because of the rich men's money  
The kings have much gold  
The rich have much money  
But we poor have much toil  
There is no time to roll during the day  
The day falls on the servants' heads  
Combs the herdsmen's heads  
Presses on the tall maiden's head  
The brush soiled into the sea  
Andres the poor boy  
Go and bring the brush from the sea  
But Andres did not speak  
I went myself slowly  
Along narrow roads  
Along low lands  
Along small water ways  
Along patches of waves  
The lazy waits for evening  
The sluggish day rolling  
I wait for morning  
For the other day rising  
The third dawn glowing  
Evening comes unexpectedly  
Twilight comes silently  
Evening brings straw to the house  
Twilight takes to sleep  
Dusk awakens  
Dawn sends us far away  
The white scatters wide  
The day sends us scattered.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Roll, roll, little day  
Roll because of the day's evening  
Because of the kings' gold  
Because of the rich men's money  
The kings have much gold  
The rich have much money  
But we poor have much toil  
There is no time to roll during the day  
The day falls on the servants' heads  
Combs the herdsmen's heads  
Presses on the tall maiden's head  
The brush soiled into the sea  
Andres the poor boy  
Go and bring the brush from the sea  
But Andres did not speak  
I went myself slowly  
Along narrow roads  
Along low lands  
Along small water ways  
Along patches of waves  
The lazy waits for evening  
The sluggish day rolling  
I wait for morning  
For the other day rising  
The third dawn glowing  
Evening comes unexpectedly  
Twilight comes silently  
Evening brings straw to the house  
Twilight takes to sleep  
Dusk awakens  
Dawn sends us far away  
The white scatters wide  
The day sends us scattered.

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Cuckoo, cuckoo little cuckoo  
Helgi gentle little bird,  
The cuckoo won't cuckoo for me  
The cuckoo cuckoos to the herder  
To Helgi the hay cutter  
It claps for the woodcutter  
I am in the forest like a moose  
At the top of the spruce like a titmouse  
A songbird in the top of the birch  
A leaf bird in the top of the aspen

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Hiiumaa, Hiiumaa cold weather  
cold weather, black hay  
The war horse did not eat  
The wedding rune did not burst  
Let it come home to the beetle’s pillar  
It eats hay, moves its lips  
Waiting for new growth  
Understanding the edges of the declining spoilage

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
We have men, we have forest  
We have boys, we have wood  
We have a wood raft  
On the pine forest ridge  
I gather from the spruce forest  
I leave the birches to grow  
Birch branches to scream  
Birch leaves to mourn  
Branches scream for the father  
Leaves mourn for the mother  
Broods, however, cry for the poor children

label: Looduslaulud
translation:
Tiny red piglet  
Golden-haired little one  
Spoke to the big pig  
But lied to the white she-goat  
A little land and a feeding head  
Don't go out to the foreign field  
A stranger comes with a scythe on his back  
Another comes with a billhook on his back  
A third comes with a dog after him  
They went to catch the pig  
The pig had grown too big  
And had swollen fat

label: Töölaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
To sleep until a hundred rains fall  
I would take a nap with energy  
The serf can sleep a little  
But only a little to lull sleep  
Still leaning against the walls  
Against the handle of an axe  
On the prayer of a foundation log  
A hundred rains to Harju County  
Take the rain toward Vigala  
Do not rain on our land  
In Harju County, the men were dark  
The men dark - the women dark  
The boys like little flames  
The children like charcoal pieces  
In our land, the men were fair  
The men fair - the women fair  
The boys golden-haired  
The children like cowslips  
The girls fiery beautiful

label: Töölaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
To sleep until a hundred rains fall  
I would take a nap with energy  
The serf can sleep a little  
But only a little to lull sleep  
Still leaning against the walls  
Against the handle of an axe  
On the prayer of a foundation log  
A hundred rains to Harju County  
Take the rain toward Vigala  
Do not rain on our land  
In Harju County, the men were dark  
The men dark - the women dark  
The boys like little flames  
The children like charcoal pieces  
In our land, the men were fair  
The men fair - the women fair  
The boys golden-haired  
The children like cowslips  
The girls fiery beautiful

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Ellakene, little brother!  
Both of us men.  
Comrades together.  
One to name the other  
One to greet the other  
A gentle greeting, called a sweetheart  
A gentle greeting to the younger brother:  
Called a sweetheart of curly hair,  
Named a wheat ear, –  
Dug up a rye plant.  
I am the bad child of the grandmother;  
I am the bad child of the grandfather:  
Enemy of the brothers,  
Greatly scolded by the sister!  
Father despised me from the knees down  
Mother despised me from the chest down  
Sisters chased me out of the yard  
Brother drove me between the village  
Once I was sunk into a mole’s hole  
A rat into a nest of grass!  
There I became a mole in a uniform  
A frog became a lung carrier  
Let me tell it over again  
Let me sing it back again:  
I had no father while I was alive  
No mother when I came into the world  
God knows, who made me  
Made me little grouses  
Or the black birds laid me  
Or maybe the gray bats  
Put together by cobblers

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Ellakene, little brother!  
Both of us men.  
Comrades together.  
One to name the other  
One to greet the other  
A gentle greeting, called a sweetheart  
A gentle greeting to the younger brother:  
Called a sweetheart of curly hair,  
Named a wheat ear, –  
Dug up a rye plant.  
I am the bad child of the grandmother;  
I am the bad child of the grandfather:  
Enemy of the brothers,  
Greatly scolded by the sister!  
Father despised me from the knees down  
Mother despised me from the chest down  
Sisters chased me out of the yard  
Brother drove me between the village  
Once I was sunk into a mole’s hole  
A rat into a nest of grass!  
There I became a mole in a uniform  
A frog became a lung carrier  
Let me tell it over again  
Let me sing it back again:  
I had no father while I was alive  
No mother when I came into the world  
God knows, who made me  
Made me little grouses  
Or the black birds laid me  
Or maybe the gray bats  
Put together by cobblers

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Mistress, woman  
Hears me coming home  
To the edge of the meadow,  
Singing behind the grove,  
Calling the evil to listen,  
The wicked to watch:  
“Hear, where the evil bird falls  
The wicked bird incites.  
Jump, Anne, rise, Kaie  
Roll a little, Mareta  
Turn the gates askew  
Close the doors tight with rings.  
Home comes a great eater  
A great eater, a great drinker  
One who puts many things in place.”  

My grandmother, old woman  
When she hears me coming home  
To the edge of the meadow  
Singing behind the grove,  
Calls the golds to listen  
The silvers to watch:  
“Hear, where the golden bird falls  
The silver bird incites.  
Jump, Anne, rise, Kaie  
Roll a little, Mareta:  
Turn the gates askew  
Close the doors tight with rings  
Handles and locks as well.  
Set the barn in order  
Bring bread to the table  
Make the bed up  
Beat the pillows soft  
He comes from the field, tired  
From work on the chest, weary  
From the haystack, breathing hard.”

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The big spruce did not die  
A broad alder fell down  
Left the branches to creak  
Leaves to mourn.  
The branches creak like a father  
The leaves mourn like a mother  
Left early a hazel rod  
To serve early the hazel rod.  
Poor to eat holes  
Mean to eat bark bits  
Bitter sour sap.  
The bulls went to the master  
The horses went to the German  
The cows went to the young women  
The goats went to stewardess Liisu.  
The sheep to Lalli Leenu.  
The old man’s bulls groaned  
Plowing the master’s field.  
The father’s horses neighed  
Going in front of the German  
The old woman’s cows moaned  
Milked by the young woman  
The old woman’s goats yelled  
By stewardess Liisa’s tugs.  
Our sheep bleated  
When Lalli Leenu mowed.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I am bored  
I am always restless  
Many times my spirits are sad  
Whom should I complain to, whom should I lament?  
Whom should I pity for pain?  
Whom should I blame for faults?  
To the stone on the church road  
On the rope of the priest's path  
I cry for young money.  
The stone on the ridge does not speak  
The limestone is thick, does not chatter  
Young grass does not weaken  
The stone does not understand the language of men  
The limestone does not speak the boys' prayers.  
I complain to the coltsfoot  
I live for the meadowsweet  
I lament to the bluebells  
The coltsfoots turn yellow  
The meadowsweets droop  
The bluebells wilt;  
At my sorrow to my dear  
And my restlessness to my friend  
Wiping my delicate eyes  
Because I am bored  
Always restless  
Many times my spirits are sad  
I had a father when I was young  
A mother when I was born  
I am the son of a horse  
The little child of a wild stallion.  
The horse nursed me  
As I licked the wild stallion.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two mourners  
Let's go to the midsummer church  
Handkerchiefs in hand  
Mourning cloths on the head  
One cries, the other cries  
One wipes the other's eyes  
One strokes the other's head  
Let's go to grandmother's grave  
Let's go to grandfather's grave  
"Rise up, little grandmother,  
Rise to comb my head,  
To prepare the gift basket,  
To fasten the kerchief."  
"You must not rise, young daughter,  
You must not rise, do not awaken:  
Grass has grown over the soil,  
Hay grown over the grave;  
Apple trees over the straw,  
Bluebells over the eyes,  
Coltsfoot over the eyebrows,  
Linden trees over the hair.  
Feed the grass to the young bullocks,  
The hay to the oxen,  
The bluebells to the cows."  
"Still rise, little grandmother;  
I will bring a sand sieve from the town,  
From the market, a fine sieve,  
From the borough, a coarse sieve,  
I would sift the sieve with fine grains,  
Roll it with fine sand."  
The grandmother hears, replies:  
"You must not rise, do not awaken,  
May God comb your head,  
May the Creator fasten your kerchief,  
Prepare the gift basket."  
"Then I go home crying:  
A speckled bird was on the kerchief,  
A white bird was on the basket.  
I would like to thank:  
To God for grandmother's soul,  
To God for grandfather's soul,  
Hands into Mary's hands,  
Heart into Jesus' lap,  
Knees into the Creator's earth."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I want to speak with death  
I want to bark at disease:  
From me you took the younger ones,  
The children chopped off and hidden away;  
From me you took the head of the family,  
The table caretaker of the children.  
You left the crowd to wander,  
A room full of pain,  
A corner full of weeping,  
You left the little one to twiddle,  
The poor child to wander.  
Where must I go?  
I cannot die alongside death,  
Disappear with the disappearing,  
Go after the one who has gone.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Sisters, dear ones,  
Our two poor little children,  
You without a father, I without a mother, -  
Let's go on Midsummer's Day to the church:  
Let's go to the grandmother's grave,  
Let's go to the grandfather's grave.  
Rise up, little grandmother,  
Rise up, little grandfather!  
Rise to comb my hair,  
To prepare the ossuary,  
To fasten the coffin lid!  
"Cannot rise, cannot wake!  
The grass has grown over the soil,  
The hay over the grave,  
The apple tree is at the shoulder,  
The bird cherry tree at the knee,  
The juniper on the other thigh,  
The birch has grown on the neck,  
The spruce has grown on the crown;  
May God comb your head,  
May He prepare the ossuary!"

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Strange old woman, vile old woman,  
Strange house, vile house,  
Comes calling me up,  
Calling me to the barn,  
Lining me up to drive the herd down:  
"Rise, cow, milk the cow,  
"Rise, calf, send the herd,  
"Blackhead, feed the calves.  
"The others' herd to the distance  
"Our herd to the barn  
"The others' heifers on the grass  
"Our heifers on the mud."  
My old woman, little old woman,  
My house, little house,  
Comes calling me up,  
Calling me to the barn,  
Lining me up to drive the herd down:  
"'Rise, bird, milk the cow,  
"Rise, chicken, send the herd,  
"White head, feed the calves.  
"The others' herd to the barn  
"Our herd to the distance  
"The others' calves on the mud  
"Our calves on the grass."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor people,  
Our two like ducklings,  
Poor like little ducklings,  
We go to the midsummer church.  
Mourning scarves in hand,  
Mourning letters in the scarf,  
One wipes the other's eyes,  
The other strokes the other's head,  
You go to your father's grave,  
I go to my mother's grave:  
"Rise up, old woman,  
Rise up, little crane,  
Rise to sew my head,  
Rise to count my money,  
To prepare the spinning wheel,  
To fasten the coffin lid."  
The old woman heard, the little crane heard,  
Soon answered back:  
"You cannot rise, young daughter,  
You cannot rise, you cannot wake,  
Grass has grown over the soil,  
Hay has grown over the soil,  
Bluebells over your eyes,  
Buttercups over your brows.  
Feed the grass to the bull,  
The hay to the oxen,  
The bluebells to the cows,  
The buttercups to the dogs.  
In the grass, the bulls are strong,  
In the hay, the oxen are large,  
On the bluebells, the cows are wide,  
On the buttercups, the dogs are plump."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I heard the cuckoos calling,
Mund's children singing,
Mund's sons shooting,
Daughters adorned,
I am bored sitting,
I am miserable walking,
Standing still, evil thoughts,
Some have boredom when sitting,
Some have misery when walking,
Standing, evil thoughts,
Complaining, sad thoughts?
I complain to the coltsfoot,
I lament to the meadowsweet,
I weep for the young grass,
I grumble to the spotted stone,
The coltsfoot turns yellow,
The meadowsweet withers,
The young grass weakens,
The spotted stone crumbles,
To the lament of my dear,
To my constant misery,
To the young one's weeping.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
If I were to sing, I would be criticized  
If I were silent, I would be sworn at,  
If I sat, it would be boring,  
If I stepped, it would be pitiful.  
To those whose minds are sad:  
I complain under the goldfinch,  
I grow like the bluebells,  
On the stones, on the church path,  
On the altar of the priest.  
The stone does not understand the maiden’s mind,  
The limestone does not heed the maiden’s prayer.  
The stone does not speak loudly,  
The thick limestone does not tell tales,  
The stone does not talk to anyone.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
My grandmother is an old woman  
My grandfather is an old man  
Heard me coming home  
To the edge of the meadow  
Hear where a piece of gold falls  
Spilling a piece of silver  
Hear where a piece of gold sings  
Rings a piece of silver  
The old woman puts the pan on the fire  
The pan’s legs spread wide  
Brings in pork  
Beats on it with a chicken egg  
The daughter comes from work, tired  
And comes from the field, weary  
Now eat, daughter, now drink, daughter  
Lie down, daughter, to breathe  
Rest for a while  
I won’t lie down to breathe  
Rest for a while  
Let me put on the sow's rope  
Weave the old woman’s apron string  
But I strike a striped belt  
I pat the cat’s fur  
A strange woman is a monstrous woman  
A strange man is a monstrous man  
Heard me coming home  
To the edge of the meadow  
Going to the forest to sing  
Hear where an evil bird falls  
Spills an evil bird  
Shrieks like a seagull  
Then puts the pot on the fire  
The pot’s legs spread wide  
Snatches in the laughing girl  
Steals cabbage and turnip  
Now eat, fool, now drink, fool  
Hurry now, fool, to the well road  
Bring water then for the cattle to drink  
Carry water for the whole herd  
Give the gray gelding a drink  
Give the black horse a sniff  
Don’t dunk the kimli.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two peasants  
Let's go to Jaanipää church  
Let's go to grandmother's grave  
Let's go to grandfather's grave  
Grass scarves on the head  
Grass letters on the scarf  
Rise up, little grandfather  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up to comb my head  
To fasten a kerchief  
To prepare an anniversary chest  
You cannot rise, young daughter  
May God comb your head  
May He fasten the kerchief  
May He prepare the anniversary chest.  
You cannot rise, do not wake up  
The grass has grown above the soil  
Above the hay on the grave  
Above the apple tree’s straw  
Above the lime tree’s ears of grain  
The willow rod above the thigh  
Rise up, little grandmother, rise up, little grandfather  
I bring golden scissors from the city  
I cut grass for the calf  
Hay for the oxen  
Blue flowers for the cows  
You cannot rise, young daughter  
You cannot rise, do not wake up.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Always I have a sorrowful  
How many times I am sad  
To whom do I plant boredom  
To whom do I complain my sorrowful mind  
On a stone I plant boredom  
On a stump I complain my sorrowful mind  
The stone does not understand a girl’s refusal  
The slab does not heed a girl’s prayer  
The stone does not utter a sound  
The slab does not tell a story  
To whom do I plant boredom  
To whom do I step sadly  
To whom do I drop sorrowfully  
My young man  
My gentle fiancé.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our couple of peasants  
Let's go to Jaanipää church  
Let's go to grandmother's grave  
Next to each other  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up, little grandfather  
Rise to weave my head  
And make a flaxen bag  
To fasten the kerchief  
Grandmother hears, it sounds again.  
The young daughter cannot rise  
Grass has grown on the soil  
Motherwort on the grave  
Bluebells on the eyes  
Marsh marigolds on the eyebrows  
Motherwort goes to the oxen  
Grass goes to the heifer  
Bluebells to the cows  
Marsh marigolds to the roosters.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Don’t die, milkman  
The caretaker disappeared,  
I buried the painter in the ground.  
Now there is no mercy-giver,  
No head-stroker.  
Who will comb my white shirt?  
Who will tie the apron straps for me?  
Who will fasten the boot laces,  
And stroke my head?  
The fireplace poker is a lover,  
The trench cake is a caretaker.  
A twig washes my fine shirt,  
Ties the boot laces.  
The cat washes the broth bowl,  
The cat rinses the spoons,  
Lice starch the shirt,  
Mites wash my body.  
Don’t eat, I am short,  
Don’t beat, I am little!  
Don’t curse, poor soul:  
My milkman is dead  
My caretaker is gone,  
The reaper on the burial hill  
Left the straw walking  
Wandering the barley field  
Shadow’s scent wandering  
Now there is no mercy-giver,  
No head-stroker.  
The wind comes, it gives mercy,  
The sun shines, strokes the head  
Winds of mercy for hours,  
Mercies for a day or two,  
Mercies for a year or more,  
God’s mercy for life;  
Grandmother’s mercies are dearest,  
Grandfather’s knees are better.  
Let me grow bigger,  
Grow a little taller:  
Then I will bother to bring water  
But for now, I go to the well skipping.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Strange maiden, dreadful maiden  
Strange old man, dreadful old man,  
Hears me coming home,  
To be out there in the clearing,  
Singing behind in the forest.  
Hear where the evil bird falls  
And the cruel bird glimmers,  
That spiteful bird is tapping.  
Jump, Anne, leap, Kaie,  
Roll, little Marikene.  
Close that gate with a net,  
Seal the doors with a ring.  
Now comes home a guest to eat,  
A guest to eat, a guest to drink,  
And to lead to the village.  
My maiden is a little old woman,  
My old man is a little old man,  
Hears me coming home,  
To be out there in the clearing,  
Singing behind in the forest.  
Hear where the golden bird falls  
And the silver bird glimmers.  
Jump, Anne, leap, Kaie,  
Roll, little Marikene.  
Make that gate out of a net,  
Seal the doors tight with a ring.  
Now comes home the golden bird,  
The silver bird comes outside.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I never have boredom  
I never have misery  
How many times a sorrowful mind  
To whom do I complain my sorrowful mind  
To whom do I lament my misery  
To whom do I cast my anger  
I complain to the golden cuckoo  
I think to the meadowsweet  
The meadowsweet lamented  
My youthful crying  
In beautiful longing  
Longing breaks my mind  
Regretting I turn my neck  
I never have boredom  
How many times a sorrowful mind.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
I myself am a little man  
A little man, a tiny one  
A knee-high little boy  
Yet a thousand tasks  
Not counting the duties:  
At night I beat the manor’s granaries  
By day I plowed the manor’s land,  
Where I stood wet walls  
Where I cried wet corners  
Where I slept in the muddy ground.  
Then a little pond appeared  
Grew into a little fish lake  
Born from drops of tears  
Poured from the edge of water  
The village had enough fish to eat  
Even the poor in the parish were quieted  
Even the weak helped.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
I myself am a little man  
A little man, a tiny one  
A knee-high little boy  
Yet a thousand tasks  
Not counting the duties:  
At night I beat the manor’s granaries  
By day I plowed the manor’s land,  
Where I stood wet walls  
Where I cried wet corners  
Where I slept in the muddy ground.  
Then a little pond appeared  
Grew into a little fish lake  
Born from drops of tears  
Poured from the edge of water  
The village had enough fish to eat  
Even the poor in the parish were quieted  
Even the weak helped.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
My grandmother Ella  
Wise old grandfather Sula  
We quickly walk to church  
We go to grandmother’s grave  
We go to grandfather’s grave  
Rise up, grandmother  
Rise up, grandfather  
Rise up to wash your head  
To prepare Anni's basket  
To fasten Kerstu's lids  
Young girls are not allowed to rise  
Not allowed to rise, not allowed to wake up  
Grass grows on the soil  
Hay on the grave  
Blue flowers on the eyes  
Daisies on the eyebrows  
Willow catkins on the ears of grain,  
So that I may say again  
God, wash your head  
Mary, prepare the basket  
Kersti, fasten the lids  
May you, young girls,  
Feed the calf with grass  
Feed the rakes with hay  
Feed the cows with blue flowers.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our Two Poor Girls  
Let's go to the Jaanipää church.  
The mourning scarves folded.  
One wipes the other's eyes,  
The other strokes the other's head.  
Let's go to the grandmother's grave:  
Rise up, little grandmother,  
Rise up to weave my head,  
Rise up to prepare the flax,  
To fasten the flax cover.  
Oh, my stony flax,  
A lock of deer bone,  
A splinter of elk bone,  
A cover made of fish bone,  
From the antler of a small horn  
"You cannot rise, little grandmother,  
You cannot rise, young daughter:  
Grass has grown over the soil,  
Meadow hay over the grave.  
Then bring a sand sieve from the town,  
A sieve from the small town,  
A strong sieve from the market.  
Sift the sieve finely,  
Sift over the wide grave,  
Then you will see grandmother and grandfather,  
Whether grandmother's body is fair,  
Whether grandfather's face is red."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I became a poor child early on  
Early on, a stranger's prey.  
Stranger is harsh, stranger is loud:  
It strikes along my side,  
Along the ribs on my back,  
Straight down my shoulder.  
I have no one to show mercy,  
No one to caress me.  
The wind comes, it gives mercy,  
The sun strokes my head.  
For hours, there is the wind’s mercy.  
For a lifetime, the old woman’s mercy.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I always have boredom,  
Always I have misery,  
How many times my mind is sad,  
Whom do I complain to, whom do I fear,  
Whom do I pity as sick?  
I weep on the young grass,  
I complain to the coltsfoots,  
I grieve to the meadowsweet.  
The coltsfoots are turning yellow,  
The meadowsweet is turning gray.  
Where is shelter for the poor child?  
Under the shadow of a stone, under the shadow of a stump,  
Under the shadow of a tree, under the shadow of a clump of grass,  
In the blue embrace of the alder,  
On the white shoulder of the birch,  
In the gray cradle of the aspen,  
On the long flaxen wing of the willow.  
The birch did not take me on its shoulder,  
The alder did not take me in its embrace,  
The aspen did not take me into its cradle,  
The willow did not take me on its long wing.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
A crowd has gone to the proud ones,  
A row of rich stalks,  
A troop of pipe sweepers,  
Once chimney scratchers.  
They did not come to greet,  
God help us.  
They made paths for the roads,  
Roads to enjoy the land.  
Dry wood in the forest,  
Wide song of the forest.  
Dear little sister,  
We used to be many before,  
Many in a beautiful herd.  
Now the crowd has ended,  
The beautiful order has disappeared,  
Now I am left alone to wail,  
Flax seed to be sown,  
Oat seed to be stirred,  
Barley seed to be watched.  
Do not strike the poor child,  
The poor one cries without being hit,  
Howls without a push,  
Rises in the morning,  
An elbow under the water,  
A leg under the water mat,  
Under the striped blanket,  
Under the furry coat.

Under the furry coat.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
We, two poor children,
Are going to the midsummer churchyard.
I will go to father's grave,
You go to mother's grave.
Rise up, my father,
Come to set the saw,
To turn the saw blade!
Rise up, my mother,
Rise to comb my hair,
To prepare the annivakka,
To fasten the kersti cover.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
When I begin to leave,  
Then sorrow begins to come  
And water starts to roll from the eyes,  
From the eyes it flows over the chest,  
From the chest down to the toes,  
From the toes to the village of Vainu.  
From there, the village could drink from the herd,  
And the parish could enlighten the poor.  
Old women, old men,  
Families, boys,  
Girls, acquaintances  
I see weeping in the corner  
And nodding,  
And tearing joy from my chest.  
Do not cry, young girls,  
Do not grieve excessively.  
When I then come out of there,  
When I have defeated the enemy,  
Then my chest will bear stars.  
Won't those girls cheer for me,  
Won't they sing a war song for me?  
They will sing so that the ceiling rattles,  
The ceiling rattles, the floor cracks.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
When I begin to leave,  
Then sorrow begins to come  
And water starts to roll from the eyes,

From there, the village could drink from the herd,  
And the parish could enlighten the poor.  
Old women, old men,  
Families, boys,  
Girls, acquaintances  
I see weeping in the corner  
And nodding,  
And tearing joy from my chest.

Do not grieve excessively.

When I have defeated the enemy,
