Won't they sing a war song for me?  
They will sing so that the ceiling rattles,  
The ceiling rattles, the floor cracks.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Before I was little,  
A knee-high boy,  
At night I beat the manor's yard,  
By day I plowed the measured lands,  
Where I stand the walls are wet,  
Where I cry the corners are wet,  
A little pond dripped down,  
A fishnet grew.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Before I was little,  
A knee-high boy,  
At night I beat the manor's yard,  
By day I plowed the measured lands,  
Where I stand the walls are wet,  
Where I cry the corners are wet,  
A little pond dripped down,  
A fishnet grew.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children  
Let's go to the Midsummer church,  
You fatherless, I motherless,  
You to your father's grave,  
I to my mother's grave:  
"Rise up, my dear mother,  
Rise up to comb my hair,  
To fix the kerchief,  
To prepare the annivakka (a feast)!"  
Mother hears and responds:  
"Cannot rise, cannot wake:  
Grass has grown over the soil,  
Peace hay over the grave,  
Bluebells over the eyes,  
Buttercups over the eyebrows.  
Mow the grass for the calves,  
Peace hay for the oxen,  
Bluebells for the cows,  
Buttercups for the dogs.  
We are two poor children,  
With sorrowful minds,  
One is like a stone to others,  
A stone on the church road,  
A rock on the priest's outside.  
The stone does not speak,  
The rock is thick and silent,  
The son of the blacksmith is the stone,  
The rock is the rockmaster.  
The fox goes to the threshing floor,  
The squirrel to the barley threshing floor,  
The hen to the oat threshing floor.  
The sieve already stands on the edge,  
The rods are firmly white.  
Already at the door of the house,  
Who waits for me at home?  
The dog waits for me at home.  
Who watches me at the gate?  
The cat watches me at the gate:  
The dog waits for the little rooster,  
The cat waits for the bread crusts."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I went to the old woman's grave:
"Rise up, little old woman,
Rise to comb my hair,
To prepare the Annivakka,
To fasten the Kerstuka cover."
But the old woman understands, replies:
"Cannot rise, no cure.
Cannot rise, young daughter,
Soil is beneath, gold is above,
Grass has grown over the soil,
Meadow herbs upon the grave,
Forget-me-nots on the eyes,
Buttercups on the eyebrows..."
I feed the grass to the calves,
Meadow herbs to the bulls,
Forget-me-nots to the cows,
Buttercups to the roosters.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Mother, little mother,  
Rise up even from the grave,  
Smoothing from day to day,  
Granting mercy from year to year.  
Daughter, little young bird,  
Cannot rise nor awaken,  
I have the earth’s scent of the dead,  
Death’s scent near my mouth,  
Scab’s scent near my hand.  

Mother, little mother,  
I will heat a warm sauna for you,  
Bring water from the Viru spring,  
Firewood from the Polish border,  
I will wash the earth’s scent of the dead,  
Death’s scent near my mouth,  
Scab’s scent near my hand.  

Daughter, little young bird,  
Cannot rise nor awaken,  
Grass has grown over me on the soil,  
Clover over my chest.  

Mother, little mother,  
I will send the herd here to graze,  
I feed grass to the bull,  
Hay to Ellika,  
Clover to Ristik.  

Daughter, little young bird,  
I have two guards with me,  
The Creator’s dog at my feet,  
Mary’s cat on my head.  
If only I could move a foot  
When the Creator’s dog barks,  
If only I could move my head,  
When Mary’s cat puts on shoes.  

Daughter, little bird,  
Cannot rise nor awaken.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two ancestors:  
You as father, I as mother  
We go to Midsummer Day church,  
One to another’s grave,  
Rise up my father,  
Rise up my mother!  
Rise to comb your hair,  
To make a burial basket,  
To fasten the coffin lid.  
The grandfather listened and answered  
He cannot rise nor wake:  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Willow on the grave  
Apple tree on the hay  
Primrose on the eyebrows  
Juniper on the burial mound.  
May God comb your hair,  
Make the burial basket,  
Fasten the coffin lid.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Strange girl, ugly girl.  
Strange old man, ugly old man.  
Heard coming to my home,  
Going to search outside.  
Singing behind the forest.  
Hear where the evil bird falls,  
That bitter bird is tapping.  
Jump, Anne, leap, Kaie.  
Roll, little Marikene!  
Close the gate in Võru,  
Shut the doors tightly in the ring.  
Now comes a guest to the house,  
A guest to eat, a guest to drink,  
And a guest to bring to the village.  
My girl is a little maiden,  
My old man is a little old man.  
Heard coming to my home,  
Going to search outside.  
Singing behind the forest.  
Hear where the golden bird falls,  
Surely it flashes, the silver bird.  
Jump, Anne, leap, Kaie!  
Roll, little Marike!  
Make the gate in Võru,  
Close the doors tightly in the ring.  
Now comes the golden bird to the house,  
The silver bird comes outside.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor ones  
Let's go to the church on Midsummer's Day  
You without a father, I without a mother  
Holding withered branches in hand  
A withered number on the corner  
Let's go to father's grave  
Let's go to mother's grave  
Rise up, grandmother Ella  
Rise up to sew my head  
To prepare a small container  
To fasten the coffin lid  
Because my hiding place  
Cannot rise nor be helped  
Soil is below, soil is above  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Hay on the meadow  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Marigolds by the feet  
Buttercups over the eyebrows  
I feed the grass to the calves  
Hay to the heifers  
Now the calves are big in sorrow  
The cows are wide under the bluebell.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Father died, mother died  
Father was taken out the door  
Thorns went out the window  
Mother was taken along the road  
Thorns went along the garden  
Father and mother were put in the grave  
Thorns by the edge of the grave  
Rise up, dear father  
Rise up, dear mother  
Rise up to fasten my coffin  
To prepare the carrying box  
But the woman understood, the old man replied  
May God fasten your coffin  
May He prepare the carrying box  
The young daughter cannot rise  
The soil is underneath and the soil is above  
Grass has grown on the soil  
Hay for the grave  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Coltsfoot on the forehead  
Mallow on the navel  
Rise indeed, dear father  
Rise indeed, dear mother  
I will feed grass to the bulls  
Hay for the oxen  
Bluebells for the pigs  
Mallows for the sheep  
Coltsfoot to the pasture edge.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I have had no father  
Nor a mother born to me  
I was made by a capercaillie  
Laid by a black little bird  
Fed by a little grain.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I had no father, I had no mother  
I had no true teacher  
Nor any dear nurturers.  
I was left like a hare in the grass  
Like a chick left in the nest  
Like a chicken left to crow  
Like a flaxseed left to wither.  
The weather was my father, the weather was my mother,  
The weather was my true teacher  
The weather was my dear nurturer  
Yet I grew into a man,  
I grew into a handsome young boy.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Listen, where does the big eater come from  
The big eater, the big drinker  
The one who puts a lot in place,  
Jump, Anne, jump Kaie,  
Roll, little Mareta  
Bring the bread to the table.  
Listen, where does the big eater come from  
The big eater, the big drinker  
The one who puts a lot in place.  
He eats like an ox to eat  
He drinks like drinking from a barrel  
He uses up piles of hay.  
What good is eating the ox  
There was bone underneath and skin on top  
What good is using up the pile  
Mattresses underneath and sticks on top,  
A handful of hay in between.  
His own girl is an old woman  
His own man is an old man  
Hears me coming home  
Singing behind in the forest  
Being out in the field  
Sparkling like a silver bead.  
She comes tired from work  
She comes tired from outside  
Jump, Anne, jump Kaie  
Roll, little Mareta  
Put kindling on the fire  
Bring the bread to the table.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Father said upon dying,  
Mother breathing her last,  
Leading to the gate,  
Closing the coffin.  
"Dear son, dear son,  
Leave behind the drinking habits,  
Drinking habits, tavern visits,  
Forget those crazy ways,  
Behave like a dog.  
Then a woman will come to you smiling,  
Longing for a pearl necklace,  
Rejoicing with a calm heart.  
Do not visit the neighbors,  
Do not roll the wine barrel.  
Noise comes from a visit,  
White talk from the wine barrel.  
Stand by your home's sad children,  
By the stove, poor children."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
My brothers, when they are these fellows,  
cousins when they are these parts,  
male cousins grown men,  
built a room in the wind,  
a sauna on the edge of an island,  
on the roof of the hut,  
on the branch of the house.  
Then the Münni Germans passed by,  
every day came the gentlemen of Pärnu,  
every month the queens,  
they asked me:  
a young maiden, a young girl,  
is there a town or a ship,  
is there a town bird-ship,  
or a Pärnu bridal ship?  
But I understand and answer:  
there is no town nor is there a ship,  
not a town bird-ship,  
nor a Pärnu bridal ship,  
nor a Münni hat-  
Tallinn windsock-ship.  
That was my brother’s sauna,  
the walls inside were covered with silk,  
the roof was covered with shingles,  
the stove was made from perch,  
the jaw was made from a salmon jaw.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Little Ella, little brother  
Both of us brave lads  
Two comrades together  
One named the other  
One greeted the other!  
She greeted Ella, called her dear  
Greeted Ella as a diligent one  
Called her a curly-haired beauty  
Named her a wheat sprout  
Dug up an oat plant for her  
I am the old woman’s child,  
I am the old man’s child,  
Under the hatred of brothers  
Being scolded harshly by the sister.  
Father despised me from his knees,  
Mother despised me from her breast,  
Sisters drove me out of the yard,  
Brother drove me out of the village.  
They turned me into a mole under the mound,  
A rat in the grassy nest,  
There I became a mole in a coat,  
A frog became a bearer of lungs.  
So let me tell it again  
Let me sing it back again:  
"I never had a father  
Nor a mother since birth  
God knows who made me!  
Was I made by the black grouse  
Or was I hatched by black birds  
Or was I stitched together by tinker folk."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Gone are the food givers,  
Food givers, drink bearers;  
Gone to the grave are the gift givers,  
Gone to the soil are the sorrow bearers.  
My poor father has died,  
Poor father, poor mother,  
They left me, their property, a poor child,  
I was left alone to lament,  
A chick left to cry;  
I remained like a hare on the grass,  
A magpie left on the garden gatepost,  
A beetle on the room’s broom,  
A flaxseed left to chirp.  
Who was I bored with?  
I was bored with my father.  
Who caused much sorrow?  
My mother caused much sorrow,  
My sister caused much sorrow.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The mistress deceived me,  
Gave me sour fish,  
From Haganas put bread among,  
Oats among the chaff;  
To make me plow the field.  
But I deceived the mistress:  
I plowed the stubble, left the straw,  
Gave space for grass to grow,  
For thistles to rise.  
The master looked through the window,  
His heart inside was miserable,  
That he didn't feel like working.  
I have no field to change clothes in;  
My clothes on me were tattered,  
The hems hanging down;  
I have no father, I have no mother,  
Who would give me a pair of socks,  
As a gift a pair of mittens.  
The master stood, whip in hand:  
"If you don't go, I'll give you a beating."  
I have no one to give mercy,  
Nor to hear my complaints;  
I must go, the enemy’s child,  
And wander barefoot.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Oh we two poor ones,  
Let's go to the Jaanipäev church.  
I without a father, I without a mother  
I will go to grandmother's grave,  
I will go to grandfather's grave:  
Rise up, little grandmother,  
Rise up, little grandfather,  
Rise to comb my head,  
Rise to comb my neck,  
To fasten the coffin lid,  
To prepare the gift box.  
Cannot rise, young daughter;  
There was down in the forehead, there was up in the forehead,  
The limestone lump was on the chest,  
May God comb your head,  
May God comb your neck,  
May He fasten your coffin lid,  
May He prepare your gift box.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh, poor child of a slave,
Always I am sorrowful,
Every day my days are long,
Every month was sad for me,
For whom I plant dullness,
For whom I sow sorrow.
On the stone to the church path,
On the rope to the priest's field,
The stone was hard, it did not speak,
The rock was thick, it did not tell.
The stone did not understand the maiden's tongue,
The rock did not heed the maiden's prayer.
“Dear sisters, sweet ones,
Let’s unite the three of us,
And deliberate as four.
Let’s leave this land,
Let’s go to Finland,
For a few days to Pärnu county,
For a year to Alutaguse.
There we will build a stone church,
There we will build a chapel of reeds.
We will make a bridge of onions,
We will lay [it] with wild garlic.
If maiden hunger comes to the land,
The maiden’s sorrow burns,
Surely the guardian will search for us,
Will ride on the hock of a salmon,
Will test on the foal’s hoof.
The fire cuts the hips of the hosts,
The sparks flew on the hock of the salmon,
Foal’s hooves cut the fence,
Seeking maiden's flock,
Catching the maiden’s herd.”

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Murelaulud
translation:
My young maiden  
If you want to go to the farm  
To the farm, to the farmer,  
To the household, to become a daughter-in-law.  
You go to sleep in the evenings,  
You lay the care on the rack,  
The worries on the black beam.  
You get up in the morning,  
You take the care from the rack,  
The worries from the black beam.  
Then you go to the oxen barns,  
Then you go to the farm barns:  
You find a calf from the cow,  
A lamb from the sheep,  
A kid from the goat.  
Then you place them on the floor in your apron:  
The mother-in-law then speaks kindly,  
Everyone laughs heartily,  
The housewife talks sufficiently.  
The daughter-in-law starts to worry:  
What shall I put in the pot?  
What shall I cook in the kettle?  
The mother-in-law then answers what she knows:  
"What you brought from your father’s home,  
What you brought from your mother’s home,  
Put that in the pot,  
Cook that in the kettle."  
- "I brought a kerchief from my father’s home,  
I brought beads from my father’s home,  
I brought a brooch from my brother’s home" -  
- "The kerchief won’t cook like porridge,  
The beads won’t cook like peas,  
The brooch won’t melt like salt,  
Take the kerchief to the town to be cooked,  
Take the beads to the town to be made into peas,  
Take the brooch to the town to be salted."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children,
We are going to the Midsummer church.
Rise up, dear father,
rise up, dear mother,
Rise to braid on my head,
To prepare the anniversary box,
To fasten the coffin lid,
- You cannot rise, young daughter,
May God braid your head,
Prepare the anniversary box,
Fasten the coffin lid.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Now I go to mother’s grave  
I lay on my chest across  
My knee at the edge of the grave  
Across the chest on the breeze  
I looked, I looked  
Was the cross rusted  
Was the cross horn mossed over.  
At the base of the cross it was bound  
Indeed the cross was rusted  
The cross horn was mossed over  
At the base of the cross it was bound.  
I looked, I looked  
What is there at mother’s grave  
Mother was swaying the rye sacks  
Mother was moving the linen cloth  
Mother was wrapping the firewood  
Dear mother, little mother  
Rise up even from the grave  
Rise up for me to the six little golds  
To a week-old seer  
To a day hair-spinner.  
You cannot rise, daughter, young one  
You cannot rise, no rising promised  
Grass has grown over the earth  
Clover on the chest  
Dear mother, little mother  
I will bring here a little herd  
Feed on the grass of the earth’s crust  
Green grass for the lambs  
Clover for the clover crust  
For the harebell triple.  
You cannot rise, daughter, young one  
You cannot rise, no rising promised.  
I have a stiffness of earth smell  
My mouth has the smell of death  
My hands have the smell of scabs  
Dear mother, little mother  
Rise up to whip me  
I will fire the sauna from the stove  
I will whip the wool with the whisk  
I will make soap from pig fat  
I will make slipperiness from bee wax  
I will cook porridge from sour milk  
Rise up to whip me.  
You cannot rise, no rising promised  
Such a wonder has never been before  
That one has risen from the earthless ground  
From the place of death’s grave.  
Go call to the village  
Call the village man to the uncle  
The village woman to the aunt  
The village boy to the half-brother  
The village girl to the sister.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
My being would exist,  
my doing would be for another:  
I would build a sauna on the island,  
a place without an end or edge.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
If it were up to me,  
If it were my doing,  
I would send you to the wind,  
To the house on the berry stalk.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
If my being  
were my doing:  
I would drive the cows to the wind,  
to the shore of Saare.  
..................  
I would turn horses into spirited steeds,  
put oxen in front as plowmen  
and Germans behind as harrows.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
If my being  
were my doing:  
I would drive the cows to the wind,  
to the shore of Saare.  
..................  
I would turn horses into spirited steeds,  
put oxen in front as plowmen  
and Germans behind as harrows.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Rise up, little old woman,  
Rise up, little old man,  
Rise to weave my head,  
To prepare the Annivakka,  
To fasten the kerchief.  
The young daughter cannot rise:  
Grass has grown over the soil,  
Rue on the field,  
Bluebells on the eye,  
Buttercups on the eyebrow...

label: Murelaulud
translation:
When are we going to the church?
On St. John's Day we go to the church.
I to my mother's grave,
You to your father's grave.
Rise up, dear mother,
Rise above my head,
To prepare the offering dish,
To fix the wreath cover!
You cannot rise, young daughter,
You cannot rise, there is no remedy.
Grass has grown on the soil,
Field walls on the grave...
I feed grass to the calves,
Field walls to the eagles.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I went to the old woman's grave:
Rise up, little old woman,
rise up, little old man,
rise to comb my hair,
to prepare the anvil box,
to fasten the kerchief.
Cannot rise, no remedy:
soil is below, soil is above,
I am in the middle of the soil.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Mother, before dawn!  
Rise up, do not delay  
to the saving sun of the day,  
to the forgiving grace of the year.  
Maiden, little bird!  
You cannot rise, you do not promise:  
Grass grows over the soil,  
crops over the field,  
clover over the chest. –  
Mother, before dawn!  
Tomorrow I will bring the herd here,  
I will feed the grass to the calf,  
the clover to the little clover.  
Maiden, little bird!  
You cannot rise, you do not promise:  
I have the earth of manna,  
My mouth has the death of manna. –  
Mother, before dawn!  
I will heat for you the village stove;  
bring water from the spring of Viru,  
bring wood from the border of Poland.  
I also have your earth of manna,  
your mouth has the chief death of manna.  
Maiden, little bird!  
You cannot rise, you do not promise:  
I have the watch of manna’s scab:  
I walk the Creator’s herbs  
and pick Mary’s buds.  
To the head I go to those who move –  
Mary’s buds to those who scream;  
to the foot I go to those who move –  
the Creator’s herbs to the holes.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Rise up, Ella Maiden!  
Rise up, Ella Girl!  
The little maiden could not rise,  
the little lad could not rise:  
There was soil underneath and soil on top,  
grass had grown on the soil above.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two peasants,  
you are the father, I am the mother,  
we are going to the midsummer church,  
you will go to the father's grave,  
I will go to the mother's grave.  
Rise up, dear father,  
rise up, dear mother!  
Rise and comb my hair,  
prepare the bread box,  
fasten the chest cover!  
You cannot rise, young daughter,  
you cannot rise, do not wake up,  
grass has grown on the ground,  
on the father's grave,  
bluebells on the eyes,  
buttercups on the eyebrows.  
I fed the grass to the calves,  
the bluebells to the heifers,  
the bluebells to the cows,  
the buttercups to the roosters.  
....  
May God comb your hair,  
prepare the bread box,  
fasten the chest cover!

label: Murelaulud
translation:
If it were up to me,
if it depended on my doing —
I would turn the room into wind,
and the sauna onto a big island.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children
Let's go to Jaanipää keriku.
You go to father's side
I go to mother's side.
Rise up, Ella old woman,
Rise up, Ella old man,
Rise to milk my goats,
Rise to count my money,
Prepare the grain box,
Fasten the kerchief.
Cannot rise, dear son;
Cannot rise, dear daughter,
The grass has grown over the ground,
The fields are on the grave.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
I was a slave, I tended the herd  
A knee-high boy  
At night I beat the threshing floor  
By day I ploughed the manor land  
Where I stood, the walls were wet,  
Where I cried, the corners were wet.  
With dung, a small pond trickled  
A fish pond grew  
From there, every guest could drink  
Enlightenment for the townsfolk.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Village Girls, Little Birds  
Let's go to throw the net.  
Where did the net fall?  
The net fell into the churchyard!  
Then the grave doors opened  
Grave doors, cemetery doors.  
I went inside to look  
To look, to search.  
What did I find inside?  
I found my father's grave inside!  
What did my father do?  
Father made a golden coffin.  
I asked my father -  
For whom do you make the golden coffin?  
For you, for you young sons!  
You were left an orphan too early  
Under the cruel cobbler's stick.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I did not have a father,
nor a mother when I came into the world,
I was made by a capercaillie,
hatched by a black little bird,
brooded by a little goose.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I had no father, I had no mother,  
I had no true teacher,  
nor a dear caretaker –  
I was left like a rabbit on the grass,  
a young bird left in the nest,  
a chick left in the henhouse,  
a flaxseed left on the field.  
The weather is my father, the weather is my mother,  
the weather is my true teacher,  
the weather is my dear caretaker.  
Still, I grew up into a man,  
I grew into a handsome boy.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Let's go to Midsummer at the church  
Let's go to grandmother's grave  
Let's go to grandfather's grave  
Rise up, dear grandmother  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up to braid my hair  
To prepare Annivakka  
But mother understood and replied  
You cannot rise, young daughter.  
The grass has grown on the soil  
The clover on the grave  
But I understood and replied  
I will feed the grass to the calf  
The clover to the oxen.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
One wipes the other's eyes  
The other strokes the other's head  
They go to the midsummer church  
Let's go to father's grave  
Let's go to mother's grave  
Rise up, dear father  
Rise up, dear mother  
Rise up, my dear!  
To prepare the anniversary basket  
To fasten the coffin lid  
You must not rise, young daughter!  
You must not rise or awaken.  
May God bless your head  
May He prepare the anniversary basket  
May He fasten the coffin lid!  
Still rise up, dear father  
Still rise up, dear mother  
I bring a sand sieve from the town  
A sieve from the borough  
From the market, I bring a strong sieve  
I sieved moss from under the grass  
From under the grass, from on the grass  
From the grass, in the middle of the soil.  
Still rise up, dear father  
Still rise up, dear mother!  
I feed the moss to the calves  
The meadow hay to the oxen  
The bluebells to the cows  
The coltsfoot to the roosters.  
Midsummer pitchers to the Joadretell.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I have a home with a stranger old woman  
a stranger old woman, a vile old woman;  
she heard me coming home,  
being at the edge of the meadow...  
"Jump, Eltse, hop, Kaie,  
roll, little Mareta.  
Now comes the plague for the chickens,  
now comes the breaking of the eggs,  
now comes the drought for the piglets."  
I heard and answered:  
"My dear old woman,  
I am not the plague for the chickens,  
I am not the breaking of the eggs,  
I am not the drought for the piglets,  
may your chickens’ plague be cut,  
your eggs’ breaking destroyed,  
your piglets’ drought scattered."  
So let me tell anew  
and say the words back.  
I have a home with my own old woman,  
my own old woman, a good old woman  
she heard me coming home,  
being at the edge of the meadow...  
"Here comes a weary daughter,  
here comes a tired girl!  
jump, Eltse, hop, Kaie,  
roll, little Mareta,  
turn the gates around,  
close the doors tightly,  
put the stew on to boil,  
let the fresh milk be boiled,  
sing along yourself nearby:  
boiling a little broth,  
boiling fresh milk,  
a ripe child’s little cake."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Where I cried – corners wet,  
where I stood – walls wet,  
there dripped a little pond,  
a fish lake grew.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Oh, we are two poor children:  
You without a father, I without a mother.  
Let's go to the Midsummer church.  
One strokes the other's head,  
Sprinkles apples on the cloth,  
Holds the cloth in hand.  
You go to your father's grave,  
I go to my mother's grave:  
"Rise up, my father,  
Rise up, my mother.  
Rise to comb my hair,  
To prepare the sudarium,  
To fasten the kerchief."  
"You cannot rise, no use,  
May God comb your hair,  
Prepare the sudarium,  
Fasten the kerchief.  
Grass has grown over the soil,  
Meadow herbs over the grave,  
Bluebells over the eyes,  
Buttercups over the eyebrows.  
Wait, wait, wait, wait, young men!  
Let's bring grain brought from the village,  
Let's plow the grass from my head,  
The bluebells from the eyes,  
The buttercups from the eyebrows.  
Give grass to the calves,  
Meadow herbs to the young cattle,  
Bluebells to the pigs,  
Buttercups to the chickens!"

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
I am still a youngster,  
A knee-high little boy.  
At night I swept the manor’s yards,  
By day I plowed the manor’s fields.  
Where I stood, the walls were wet;  
Where I cried, the corners were wet.  
From that dripped a little pond,  
Grew into a fishing lake.  
From there the village got water for the herd,  
And grandma’s fabrics were soaked.

From there the village got water for the herd,  
And grandma’s fabrics were soaked.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
A lazy niece,
She did not bother to make butter,
To scrape together the cream,
To strike the pine, muta-muta;
She threw the pine in the pine grove,
The cream lump in the enclosure.
I came in the evening wind,
Took the pine from the pine grove,
The cream lump from the enclosure,
Started making butter,
Striking the pine, muta-muta,
Scraping together the cream,
Around the pine, muta-muta:
Together, together, little cream lump,
So that you can have butter and eat porridge,
And also eat yourself;
When melted, there would be a large amount,
For the herdsman’s child in the corner of the box.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Wait, wait, death, well, well, death.  
I want to gouge out death.  
It killed my gentle grandmother,  
It killed my wise grandfather.  
It left me a poor child  
Too early an orphan's child.  
I remained like a hare on the grass,  
Like a white bird on the grave.  
A musket on the edge of the room,  
Hatless, glove-less,  
Without a belt, without boots.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Oh, dear Rõa village
Where curly birches grew!
Eidet girls beautiful
Tuadi girls gentle
Memme daughters affectionate.
Pila cries for her little boy,
Meelitu cries for her man,
The mad one screams for her companion.
But I cry for my lady,
I pity the grave-digger,
The clean porridge cook,
The clear-smith maker.
We are two poor children!
Let's go to the Jaanipäev church
One wipes the other's eyes,
One kisses the other's neck,
One strokes the other's head.
"Rise up, little mother,
Rise up to comb my hair,
Prepare the tray
To fasten the coffin lid."
"You cannot rise, it is impossible;
Grass has grown over the soil,
Hay has grown over the grave."
"Still rise, little mother
Rise to comb my hair,
Prepare the tray
To fasten the coffin lid."
"You cannot rise, it is impossible;
Grass has grown over the soil,
Hay has grown over the grave,
Dandelions over the eyebrows,
Bluebells over the eyes,
Linden trees over the ears."
"Still rise, little mother;
I will bring a sand sieve from the town,
From the borough I'll rarely bring a sieve;
I will sieve this coarse sand,
I will crush this harsh soil."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The rain had mossed the walls  
The water had rolled the logs  
The fog had locked the doors.  
The rain ruined my sled cover,  
The fog ruined my new hood  
The dew ruined my beloved's coat.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
My grandma is an old lady,  
my grandpa is an old man,  
they hear my home coming,  
to the edge of the field,  
singing behind the forest,  
calling the girls to listen;  
jump Anne, skip Kaie,  
come here little Mareta!  
Put the kettles on the fire,  
place ladles into the kettles:  
home is coming with big fires,  
big fires, big drinks,  
bringing many things to the place.  
She comes tired from the fire,  
and comes weary from the field,  
a little one comes from under the whip,  
a little rose from under the rod!

label: Murelaulud
translation:
She comes powerfully from outside,  
from under the whip comes a long lazy one!  
Up Anne, leap Kaie,  
beside you little Mareta,  
take the pots off the fire,  
take the ladles from the pots,  
so that you can no longer eat!

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I am always miserable
So many times my mind is sad
Where I sit, the seat is wet
Where I step, the step is wet
Where I stand, the walls are wet
I sweep the snow from the yard
I trample the drifts on the street
When I shed tears
When thick eyebrows fall
There, a little pond
Grew into a fish lake.
To some I plant boredom
To some I step misery
To some like to a stone
To stumps in the pasture
To a stone on the church road.
The stone was proud and did not speak
The slab was thick and did not tell
The stone did not understand the maiden's refusal
The slab did not respond to the maiden's plea.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
When I was a teenager  
A knee-high little boy  
At night I beat the barns in the manor  
By day I plowed the manor’s land  
Where I cried, the corners were wet  
Where I stood, the walls were red  
There a small pond appeared,  
A fish pond grew  
From there the parish got boiling water  
And the old bachelor gained enlightenment

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
When I was a teenager  
A knee-high little boy  
At night I beat the barns in the manor  
By day I plowed the manor’s land  
Where I cried, the corners were wet  
Where I stood, the walls were red  
There a small pond appeared,  
A fish pond grew  
From there the parish got boiling water  
And the old bachelor gained enlightenment

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Hellish landlady  
Led me to wander at night  
To walk at midnight  
A stone turns under my foot  
A toe rolls in blood  
Stone inscriptions underfoot  
Blood-stained toes  
Hellish landlady  
Heard me coming home  
Was about to get out  
She started growling from the door  
Fighting from the back wall  
Pecking from the hearth edge:  
Look where a great eater comes from  
A great eater, a great drinker  
It eats even bull’s fodder  
It drinks beer from the barrel  
I hear, I answer:  
What does he eat for food?  
There is skin on top and bone beneath  
A little bit of lean meat in between  
What does he drink from the barrel of beer?  
There is foam on top and yeast beneath  
Sometimes a drop of good beer.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
There were three misfortunes under heaven  
The most unfortunate after the day  
One was a daughter without a mother  
Another was a son without a father  
The third was a poor widow  
Who cries, the daughter without a mother  
Cries for a new belt for her waist  
A new skirt for her back  
A new scarf for her neck  
Hands roughened by work  
She cries, the daughter without a mother.  
Who cries, the son without a father?  
Cries the plowmen  
The plow team drivers  
The ones hauling the plow  
The ironwood healers  
The ones beating after winter  
They cry, the son without a father.  
What about the poor widow?  
She cries as she sets the bed  
Folds the bed together  
Lays down to sleep in the evening  
Turns her back, finds a wall  
Turns her side, finds coldness  
There was a board under the water  
An arm's length under the water  
So was the poor widow  
As if the house had no roof  
The barn had no ridgepole  
All winds come in  
All rains pour down  
Rain flows and rolls  
Hailstones fall and batter  
So was the poor widow.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Mother, dear one  
Do not come up from the grave.  
You must not rise, you must not stir  
I have the smell of the homeland's soil  
The smell of the dead of the homeland in my mouth  
The smell of scabs on my hands.  
Mother, dear one  
Do not come up from the grave  
I will heat the sauna with lime  
I will make anger from woolen threads  
I will put a patch from paper.  
You must not rise, my daughter  
You must not rise, do not stir.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The niece is too long,  
Too long, made lazy,  
She sleeps on the ground when this land  
Is ready for colt-making.  
She didn’t bother to make butter,  
Nor to gather the cream.  
To strike the pine with a tuft-hat,  
Threw the pine into the pine forest,  
The bark clump into the spruce grove.  
I came home from the wind,  
Took the pines from the pine forest,  
The bark clumps from the spruce grove.  
I will start making butter,  
Gathering the cream,  
Striking the pine with a tuft-hat.  
Butter was eaten, butter was sold,  
For the farmer to add to the spirit,  
For the shepherd boy to put in the corner of the box,  
The niece must be beaten.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The infernal landlady  
Heard me coming home  
Out by the field edge  
Singing in the back woods  
Then she searched for those thistles  
To set fire to the peat  
She said herself, be gone  
Strangers either leave or be lost.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Ike'p is boring for me  
Always I am miserable  
Who complains of sad feelings  
Who falls ill  
Who is like a stone to others  
A stone on the church path  
Paper laid out on the stone  
The girl did not understand the stone's language  
The girl did not hear the paper's prayer  
The stone spun towards the church  
The paper went out onto the stone  
The sexton understood the stone's language  
The priest heard the paper's prayer.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The mules with tangled saddles passed by  
The crooked-backed pack animals  
With sides scorched askew  
Did not provide help from God  
Help is not forsaken.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
It was before the old woman’s time  
Before the old man’s time.  
There was plenty to eat, plenty to drink  
Plenty to eat in the kitchen too  
Sometimes to eat in the barn too  
Secretly to eat in the sauna too  
Even more so in the living room.  
Now is the time of poverty and misery  
Now you can cry in the corner too  
Sometimes cry in the barn too  
Secretly cry in the sauna too  
Even more in the living room.  
Twice as much in the chamber  
Now you can cry in the corner too  
Sometimes cry in the barn too  
A drop runs from the eye  
Runs from the eye to the chest  
Runs from the chest to the waistband  
From the waistband to the toes  
From the toes down in a little trickle.  
Here the rivers began to flow  
Turn their bends  
Flood the springs  
Dry the rye in the village.

label: Laulud abielust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
It was before the old woman’s time  
Before the old man’s time.  
There was plenty to eat, plenty to drink  
Plenty to eat in the kitchen too  
Sometimes to eat in the barn too  
Secretly to eat in the sauna too  
Even more so in the living room.  
Now is the time of poverty and misery  
Now you can cry in the corner too  
Sometimes cry in the barn too  
Secretly cry in the sauna too  
Even more in the living room.  
Twice as much in the chamber  
Now you can cry in the corner too  
Sometimes cry in the barn too  
A drop runs from the eye  
Runs from the eye to the chest  
Runs from the chest to the waistband  
From the waistband to the toes  
From the toes down in a little trickle.  
Here the rivers began to flow  
Turn their bends  
Flood the springs  
Dry the rye in the village.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Where the sad ones' home is  
And the poor's dwelling place  
The rain shelter of the poor children  
There is the home of the sad ones  
The poor's dwelling place  
The rain shelter of the poor children  
Where the wind has made a hole in the room  
Water has rolled the logs  
Rain has mossed the walls  
Fog has set the doors in front  
There is the home of the sad ones  
The poor's dwelling place  
The rain shelter of the poor children  

There are three bad things under the sky  
More persistent than the day  
One is a son without a father  
Another a daughter without a mother  
The third a poor widow wife  
These are bad things under the sky  
More persistent than the day  

Who cries for the fatherless son  
Cries for the buckles  
For the winter behind, for the opponent of winter  
What about the poor girl?  
She cries for the kerchief fastener  
For the person who makes the stockings  
But who cries for the widow wife?  
She cries for her husband  
Her home's repairer  
The room is her support  
The stove is her helper  
The knee serves as an extension of her hands.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Do not beat the poor child  
The poor cries even without being beaten  
Whines without a whip.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Mother, dear mother  
Rise up, my mother  
Rise to braid my hair  
To prepare the flax basket  
To fasten the kerchief.  
You may not rise, young daughter  
You may not rise, do not awaken  
Grass grows over the soil  
Hay over the grave  
I feed grass to the heifer  
Hay to the oxen  
I yoked the oxen properly  
Gently harnessed the mares  
Ploughed the land low.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children  
We go to Midsummer at the church  
You go to grandmother’s grave  
I go to grandfather’s grave  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up, little grandfather  
To make Annivakka (a traditional dish)  
To lift the little kerchief  
Cannot rise, cannot wake up  
A spruce grows above my eyebrow  
Hepatica on the eye  
A bird cherry tree on the temples  
A maple tree on the white thighs.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Let's go, let's go, let's walk, let's walk  
I walked the path of the tip-tops  
Of the mossy liverworts  
The green roseworts  
When will we reach the land  
Where we have long been awaited  
Where they have long been watching  
There is no sound of a rooster’s crow  
The rooster’s crow, the hen’s coop  
The weak child's crying voice  
The widow women's mourning  
Let's go, let's go, let's walk, let's walk  
Let's walk the path of the speckles  
The green ones, the rose ones  
When will we reach the land  
Where the wind has made a room for us  
The rain has mossed the walls  
The fog has made new doors.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Let's go, let's go, let's walk, let's walk  
I walked the path of the tip-tops  
Of the mossy liverworts  
The green roseworts  
When will we reach the land  
Where we have long been awaited  
Where they have long been watching  
There is no sound of a rooster’s crow  
The rooster’s crow, the hen’s coop  
The weak child's crying voice  
The widow women's mourning  
Let's go, let's go, let's walk, let's walk  
Let's walk the path of the speckles  
The green ones, the rose ones  
When will we reach the land  
Where the wind has made a room for us  
The rain has mossed the walls  
The fog has made new doors.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children  
From our one village  
The other from another family  
We have gates opposite each other  
Door handles close to each other  
Wells near each other  
We go to the midsummer festival at church  
You go to your father's grave  
I go to my mother's grave  
Rise up, my father  
Rise up, my mother  
Rise up to smooth my head  
To prepare the anniversary loaf  
To attach the honey crust  
You must not rise, young daughter  
You must not rise, do not wake up  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Field herbs over the grave  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Buttercups over the eyebrows  
Horsetail on the toes  
I bring a sand sieve from the town  
I bring a red sieve from the store  
I sift grass from the soil  
Field herbs from the grave  
I feed grass to the heifer  
Field herbs to the oxen  
Bluebells to the cows.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Father said while there,
Mother while they go,
To the young bird girl,
Berry necklaces as bracelets
Do not go to the village,
Do not roll to the hayfield,
Let them go to the village,
White wheels roll to the hayfield,
Straight to the bridge they rush.
Surely, surely, young daughter
Surely you are sought by the suitors,
Ridden through the valley grass,
Looked upon on the horse's hoof
Horseshoes cut the fire,
Horse hooves cut the lightning,
Sparks flew on the valley grass,
The maiden is being sought,
Woodpeckers are being touched,
Young bird girls,
Beautifully adorned with berry necklaces.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Father said even when lying down,  
Mother's soul when passing away,  
When the coffin is closed,  
When taken through the gate,  
The grandmother was taken through the door,  
Loved ones went out through the window,  
The grandmother was taken along the road,  
Loved ones went along the garden.  
The grandmother into the grave,  
Loved ones under the sod.  
Rise up, dear grandmother,  
Rise up, dear uncle,  
You cannot rise, poor daughter,  
You cannot rise, do not awaken,  
Grass has grown over the soil,  
Moss has grown over the grave,  
Bluebells over the eyes,  
Buttercups over the eyebrows.  
Rise up, dear grandmother,  
Rise up to comb my hair,  
To lift the kerchief,  
To prepare the little gift box,  
You cannot rise, young daughter,  
You cannot rise, do not awaken,  
May God comb your hair,  
Prepare the little gift box,  
Lift the kerchief.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The big spruces did not die,
The curly-headed birch did not disappear,
It left the branches groaning,
It left the leaves grieving;
The branches groaned for the father,
The leaves mourned for the mother,
The tops for the sons and daughters,
The old grandmother did not die,
The old uncle did not die,
They left the dill to tremble,
They left the poor to wander.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I knew the life where I lived,  
I knew the house where I slept,  
That place no longer exists!  
Where I made fires in the rooms,  
Placed the logs in their place.  
Rain mossed the walls,  
Dew settled on the roof,  
Fog covered the doors,  
Hail damaged the windows.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I have no father at all,  
My mother came into the world,  
I do not know who made me?  
Were the capercaillies that made me,  
The timid heroes under the hill,  
Or were the forest dwellers?  
Or am I a squire's son?  
The sons made the boy rune,  
The white mare made the foals,  
The red stallion made the weddings.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I am going to Midsummer’s church  
Going to mother’s grave  
Going to father’s grave  
Rise up, little mother  
Rise up, little father  
Rise and let me weave  
To prepare a basket  
To fasten the basket cover  
Mother hears, answers sound  
Cannot rise, cannot wake  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Wild herbs over the grave  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Buttercups over the eyebrows  
Marigolds over the neck  
Daughter hears, answers sound  
Feed the grass to the calf  
Wild herbs to the lamb  
Bluebells to the cows  
Marigolds to the hens  
Buttercups to the roosters  
One glove is in the basket cover  
But that one fingerless  
Blind without a veil.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Sister lifted onto the shoulder  
Brother's grave is being dug  
The grave was seven feet deep  
Eight cubits cleverly  
Ten cubits from the barn  
Grass grew on the soil  
Hay of peace on the grave  
Bluebell on the eye  
Buttercups on the eyebrow  
St. John's wort on the neck  
You cannot rise, young daughter  
Soil was underneath, soil was on top  
I am in the middle of the soil  
Between white boards  
Pretty Kersti in the middle.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Oh, you poor miserable man  
Lives in the life of misfortune  
Where he sits, the seat is wet  
Where he stands, the walls are wet  
Where he weeps, the corners are wet  
How cold are the side tables  
The edges of the tables are bright  
I turn my side, I find it cold  
I turn the sail, I find a wall  
He sought a beautiful woman  
He wanted a funny woman  
He encountered a crazy one in bed  
Who could not bring a small stove  
Nor lift a chair up  
Nor help on top of the stove  
Nor place on the beam’s edge  
Oh, I, a poor unfortunate man  
Always have a load  
How many times are there sorrowful minds  
On whom I plant boredom  
On whom I trample the pitiful  
On whom I complain about sad minds  
On whom the signs falter  
To the stone on the church road  
To the lime on the plaster outside  
The stone was proud and did not speak  
The lime was thick and did not tell.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Oh, you poor miserable man

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children  
You fatherless, I motherless  
Let's go to Midsummer at the church  
To the Friday of prayer and fasting  
Let's go to grandmother's grave  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up to comb my head  
To prepare the hair mat  
Grandmother heard, an answer came  
From grandfather's grave hill  
One wipes the other's eyes  
With mourning cloths in hand  
Golden writings on the cloth  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up to wash my head  
To fix the hair mat cover  
Cannot rise, young daughter  
The soil is beneath, the soil is above  
I am in the middle of the soil  
Between white boards  
Rise still, little grandmother  
May God comb your head  
May He prepare the hair mat  
May He fix the hair mat cover  
Grandmother was taken along the road  
Loved ones went along the garden  
Grandmother was put to the grave  
Loved ones stayed at the grave hill  
There is grace for the little children  
By the stone, grace of the wind  
Between the stone ruins  
For an hour, grace of the wind  
For a day, grace of the day  
For life, grace of the grandmother  
Winds come, give grace  
The sun shines, strokes the head.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Oh, we two poor children  
You fatherless, I motherless  
Neither of us has a guardian  
No one standing before life for us  
Who would give us love from sorrow  
Love from sorrow, love from fear  
To stroke the head behind the day  
To give love in a garden shed  
Reply row upon row in a cluster  
Gave a kiss at the gatepost  
The neck began to lean on the well-digger  
Sister dear, tender one  
Let’s go to church on Midsummer’s Day  
White scarves on our heads  
White scarves in our hands  
Letters of weeping in the corner  
One wipes the other's eyes  
The other the other's cheeks  
Let’s go to grandmother’s grave  
Let’s go to grandfather’s grave  
Rise up, dear grandmother  
Rise up, dear grandfather  
Rise to comb my hair  
To prepare the annivakka (anniversary box)  
To fasten the kerstukaan (a traditional cloth)  
You cannot rise, young daughter  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Herb hay on the grave  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Coltsfoot above the eyebrows  
Thick forest beneath the feet  
Linden leaf for the feast  
Willow tree for the nest.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I have black birds of ill omen  
Shabby young magpies.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor ones  
Let's go to the midsummer church  
Let's go to grandmother's grave  
Let's go to grandfather's grave  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up, little grandfather  
Come, braid my hair  
Make a wreath  
Fasten the kerchief  
But grandmother understood, grandfather understood  
You cannot rise, daughter-in-law  
Grass grows on the soil  
Nettle on the grave  
Bluebells on the eyes  
Coltsfoot on the eyebrows  
Guelder rose on the ears  
Apple tree on the shoulders  
May God braid your hair  
Make a wreath  
A colorful bird on your kerchief  
A white bird on your wreath.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Oh, I am a little man  
Knee-high little boy  
At night I beat the manor’s threshing floor  
By day I plowed the manor’s fields  
Where I stood, the walls were wet  
Where I cried, the corners were wet  
From there the rivers started to flow  
The gray pools began to spread  
Mother’s rivers began to flow  
Into my growing eyes' water.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Oh, I am a little man  
Knee-high little boy  
At night I beat the manor’s threshing floor  
By day I plowed the manor’s fields  
Where I stood, the walls were wet  
Where I cried, the corners were wet  
From there the rivers started to flow  
The gray pools began to spread  
Mother’s rivers began to flow  
Into my growing eyes' water.

label: Murelaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
The instrument calls out beautifully  
The maiden dances gently  
The enemy behaves kindly  
I speak softly  
I have no ears for gold  
No compassion for a tender-hearted Estonian  
No mercy for a beginner  
If the wind would come and grant mercy  
If the sun would come, stroke my head  
Grandmother's mercies, grandfather's mercies  
For hours they were wind mercies  
For days they were day mercies  
For a lifetime, God's mercies  
Grandmother was buried  
Mercies remained by the edge of the grave.  
Where must I go  
I must go to the forest  
Crawling into a willow bush  
Rustling in the leaves of a maple  
There was water in the swamp, plenty of water  
Plenty of willow bush shelters  
Rotten shelters, moss shelters  
Young girls, young maidens  
They played with gray waves  
Ducks before the sunrise  
Curly-necked in the meadow  
Beaded necklaces in the hayfield  
I have no courage to go closer  
No heart to call out into someone's arms.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our Child, Two Poor Children  
We go to the Midsummer Day church  
You go to the grandmother’s grave  
I go to the grandfather’s grave  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up, little grandfather  
Rise up to make hay  
To prepare the hay basket  
You cannot rise, young daughter,  
You cannot rise, do not awaken  
Grass has grown on the soil  
Wild herbs on the grave  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Marsh marigolds over the eyebrows  
Midsummer flowers on the feet  
Still rise, little grandmother  
Still rise, little grandfather  
I will feed grass to the oxen  
Wild herbs to the bulls  
Bluebells to the pigs  
Midsummer flowers to the cats.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
But I play with the tufts  
Joke with the laughter  
And also with those young women  
Whom I half-kept company with  
All the summer whispered to  
All the winter danced with  
I would like to build without a building  
Who will cut the logs for me  
But the wind cut down the logs  
The water rolled the logs  
The rain mossed the walls  
The fog put the doors in front  
Now the lathes are on the door  
The wall clocks on the wall  
They were zigzagging  
All around round.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
But I play with the tufts  
Joke with the laughter  
And also with those young women  
Whom I half-kept company with  
All the summer whispered to  
All the winter danced with  
I would like to build without a building  
Who will cut the logs for me  
But the wind cut down the logs  
The water rolled the logs  
The rain mossed the walls  
The fog put the doors in front  
Now the lathes are on the door  
The wall clocks on the wall  
They were zigzagging  
All around round.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
A woman without a man went into the forest  
A horse without reins to hold  
Who has no one to hold the reins  
Tied up with a rope  
The reins hold the horse  
The tender brothers hold the ties  
A woman without a man went into the forest  
The boys' eyes filled with tears  
On the widow's flower  
On the green apron  
On the blue cloth.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children
We go to the church on Midsummer's Day
You go, etc.
Let me stand as a young daughter
Until the very last day.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Oh, poor child of a slave,  
Driven from Aganik,  
Taken from Vahelik,  
Woven from oat husks,  
I truly feel the fire walking,  
Making a bed without sparks,  
Writing without bitterness,  
A family daughter, a little maiden,  
A farm daughter, dough without yeast,  
Does not feel the fire walking,  
Elbow to elbow in front,  
Two candles on the candlestick,  
A candle between the shoulders.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two strangers  
We go to the Midsummer church  
You go to father's grave  
I go to mother's grave  
Rise up, dear mother  
Rise up to comb my hair  
To prepare an offering box  
You cannot rise, poor daughter  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Weeds over the grave  
Rise up anyway, old lady  
I feed the grass to the young bull  
The weeds to the oxen  
The grass makes the calves strong  
The weeds make the oxen big.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Always I am wretched  
How many times has my mind been sad  
To whom do I complain my sorrow  
To whom do I plant boredom  
To whom do I place wretchedness  
On the stone on the church path  
On the rope on the priest’s field  
The stone does not understand the maiden’s denial  
The rock does not heed the maiden’s prayer  
The proud stone did not speak  
But the thick rock did not tell.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our little neighbors  
Let's go to the midsummer church  
Let's go to father's grave  
Let's go to mother's grave.  
Rise up, dear father  
Rise up, dear mother  
Come weave my wreath  
Prepare the anniversary basket  
Attach the cover of the basket.  
Cannot rise, cannot call out  
Grass presses on the soil  
Dewy grass on the grave.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
The mouth sings, the heart worries  
The eyes shed tears  
Eyebrows like golden drops  
Cheeks let go without lending  
From the eyes it rolls onto the face  
From the face it rolls onto the chest  
From the chest it rolls onto the stomach  
From the stomach it would fall down  
Then the rivers would start running  
The gray ones would start thinking  
From my poor eye water  
From the golden eyebrow drops.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
I was a little child  
And a boy taller than my knees  
Vat luli, vat luuli  
A boy taller than my knees  
At night I beat the manor’s barns  
During the day I mowed pieces of hay  
Where I sat, there I cried  
Where I stood, the walls were wet  
There appeared a small pond  
A little lake grew fish.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
I was a little child  
And a boy taller than my knees  
Vat luli, vat luuli  
A boy taller than my knees  
At night I beat the manor’s barns  
During the day I mowed pieces of hay  
Where I sat, there I cried  
Where I stood, the walls were wet  
There appeared a small pond  
A little lake grew fish.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children  
Let's go to the Midsummer church  
You go to grandmother's grave  
I go to grandfather's grave  
Rise up, dear grandmother  
Rise up, dear grandfather  
To fix the cover on the cask  
Anni to prepare the grain measure  
I am going to get married  
The young daughter must not rise  
Must not rise, do not wake up  
A mug falls on the eyebrow  
Sand presses on the chest.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor folk  
We go to the Midsummer church  
You go to father's grave  
I go to mother's grave  
Rise up, little old woman  
Rise to comb my hair  
To prepare a gift basket  
You cannot rise, daughter young  
The grass is pressing on the soil  
Dewy grass on your neck  
Set hay on the grave.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I did not have a father while he was alive  
Nor a mother when she passed away  
God knows who made me  
Whether it was a capercaillie  
Or a little duck he placed  
Or laid a black bird  
Or hatched a little magpie  
Father called me wild  
Mother called me premature  
Brothers called me a Russian man  
Sisters called me a straw stalk  
Fate said I must die  
Brothers to drown in the water  
Sisters to fall into a well  
The great ones do not die  
The dear ones do not wander far  
But those like me do not die  
Nor are they beneath ruin.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
I did not have a father while he was alive  
Nor a mother when she passed away  
God knows who made me  
Whether it was a capercaillie  
Or a little duck he placed  
Or laid a black bird  
Or hatched a little magpie  
Father called me wild  
Mother called me premature  
Brothers called me a Russian man  
Sisters called me a straw stalk  
Fate said I must die  
Brothers to drown in the water  
Sisters to fall into a well  
The great ones do not die  
The dear ones do not wander far  
But those like me do not die  
Nor are they beneath ruin.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The owner, with a gray chin  
Was heard coming to my home  
To be out in the yard  
Singing in the backyard  
Jump, Anne, leap, Kaie  
Roll, little Mareta  
Now comes home the big eater  
The one who sets the cauldron in place  
Turn the gates towards the turning  
Close the doors tightly with a ring.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor ones  
Our two like Canadians  
A pair like ducklings  
Let's go to the Midsummer church  
With mourning scarves on our heads  
With crying scarves in our hands  
Let's go to grandmother's grave  
Let's go to grandfather's grave  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up, little grandfather  
Rise to braid my hair  
To prepare the annivakka  
To fasten the Kerstu cover  
Grandmother heard, answered back  
Cannot rise, young daughter  
Cannot rise, do not wake  
Grass has grown on the ground  
Hay on the grave  
Grass is for the bulls  
Hay is for the oxen  
The cows are for the gentians  
The grass has big bulls  
The hay has tall oxen  
The gentians have broad cows  
Father said when going to Olle  
Mother's soul going  
To the land of spirits  
To fastening the Kerstu  
Daughter, daughter, walk prettily  
Enemy's child, walk purely  
Then you will be called good  
And praised as beautiful  
A good grandmother's daughter  
A wise grandfather's child  
A good woman had jumped  
Had danced the wise grandfather.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Family daughter, young maiden  
Farm daughter, little oak  
Family daughter too tall  
Too tall, born lazy  
Stands nailed to the walls  
Pokes around the chests  
Swings like an old scarecrow  
Floats like a bundle of flax  
You can't ride a saw on her  
Jump on the masters  
Roll on the suitors  
Lift with the companions  
I, poor slave child  
And poor free child  
Tiny I stand upright  
Small I pay the tax  
From me the saw drips to ride  
Jump on the masters  
Roll on the suitors  
Lift with the companions.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children  
Let's go to the Midsummer Day church  
Let's go to grandmother's grave  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Come to braid my hair  
To prepare Annivakka.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor people  
We go to Midsummer church  
One wipes the other's eyes  
One strokes the other's head  
Handkerchiefs on the head  
Numbered corners on the ground  
Notes in the middle  
Knitted gloves in hand  
Notes inside the gloves  
Songs in the nature  
One goes to the old woman's grave  
One goes to the old man's grave  
Rise up, old woman  
Rise up, old man  
Rise up to comb my hair  
To prepare the container  
To fasten the lid  
You cannot rise, poor daughter  
You cannot rise, do not wake up  
Grass grows over the soil  
Dry hay over the grave  
Bluebells over the eye  
Larkspur over the eyebrow  
Feed the grass to the heifer  
Dry hay to the oxen  
Bluebells to the cows  
Larkspur to the roosters.  
Then I can rise and wake up  
Rise up to comb your hair.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
When my father was dying  
When my mother was going to the grave  
Do not go visiting  
Do not wander the streets  
Do not roll to the fields  
Let the handsome ones go visiting  
The proud ones wander the streets  
The white ones roll to the fields  
Daughter, work steadily  
Daughter, strive diligently  
Because of work, you will prosper  
Because of effort, you will grow  
Because of hands, you will move.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor peasants  
Let's go to Midsummer at the church  
Let's go to the grandmother's grave  
Let's go to the grandfather's grave  
Put the sorrow handkerchiefs on  
Put the sorrow apples in the handkerchief  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up, little grandfather  
Rise up to braid my hair  
To prepare the grain container  
To fasten the coffin lid.  
But the woman answered, the man replied  
You cannot rise, young daughter  
You cannot rise, do not wake up  
Grass had grown over the soil  
Branch hay over the grave  
Coltsfoot on the eyebrows  
Bluebells on the eyes  
Marigolds at the feet.  
Still rise, little grandmother  
Still rise, little grandfather  
Rise up to braid my hair.  
I feed grass to the calves  
Hay to the oxen  
Coltsfoot to the roosters  
Bluebells to the pigs  
Marigolds to the calves.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Oh, my poor despised child
My father despised me, my mother despised me
The manor elders despised me
All the bailiffs, stewards, and farmhands
They were all angry with me
I had no father
Nor a mother to come to me
Black birds have pecked at me
Old crows have haunted me
The wolf has bitten me
The bear has carried me into captivity.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Oh, my poor despised child
My father despised me, my mother despised me
The manor elders despised me
All the bailiffs, stewards, and farmhands
They were all angry with me
I had no father
Nor a mother to come to me
Black birds have pecked at me
Old crows have haunted me
The wolf has bitten me
The bear has carried me into captivity.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I had no father while being,
No mother when coming to the world,
Black birds have incubated me,
Magpies have nurtured me,
Crows have prepared me,
Seagulls have raised me.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The family boy was washed in milk  
Washed in milk, taken from the meadow  
Knocked on the young bark  
Oh, wretched enemy child am I  
What was under my control  
The fire was a locked room  
The well was lidless on the hill  
The barn was without a door  
It was under the care of a slave  
To whom I complain with a sad mind  
To whom do I vent my sickness  
To others like to a stone  
To the stone on the church path  
To the rope outside the priest.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children  
We go to the church on Midsummer's Day  
You go to father's grave  
I go to mother's grave  
Rise up, my little mother  
Rise up to comb my hair  
To prepare Annivakka.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Murelaulud  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
Now we shall go  
To our home far away  
Far away, high away  
There were five tunes played out  
Six were rivers drying up  
Seven were tough sodas  
Eight were fish banks  
Nine were good rivers  
Ten were cold grays  
When shall we get there  
Where fire has warmed the room  
Water has rolled the logs  
Fog has closed the doors  
Snow has locked the latches  
Dear ones, brothers  
Do you want to sing more  
If you want, then you may  
At home I left bags full  
On the rod's end baskets full  
When I begin to compose  
To compose, to sing  
Then the village will come to listen  
The parish will come to watch  
Where did the child get these songs  
A crazy one, able to make stories  
I understand, I hear again  
Dear ones, brothers  
I have been in Harju to study  
Not to catch a virus  
To continue speaking in Järva  
I studied at home  
At home, in the home room  
Weaving foggy fabric  
Twisting tin thread  
With silver wool into the lining  
Wide enough for a mother-in-law  
The fur was made large for father-in-law  
Legs of the fabric made along  
Benches built by the lads  
From the village, someone will come to visit us  
Just the right few young ones  
When we Estonians began  
To roll after the tune  
To spin after the time  
Dear one, brother  
I do not sing for no money  
I do not value the ringing voice  
Give money to the singer  
To the tongue-beater of Keedi

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Let's go to Midsummer at the church  
You will go to your father's grave  
I will go to your mother's grave  
Rise up, little old woman  
Rise up to comb my hair  
To fasten the kerchief  
To prepare the anniversary basket  
You cannot rise, young daughter  
You cannot rise, do not wake up  
The grass has grown on the soil  
Meadow hay on the grave  
Bluebells on the eyes  
I feed the grass to the heifer  
Meadow hay to the bulls  
Bluebells to the cows  
On the grass, the heifers are mighty  
On the meadow hay, the bulls are fat  
On the bluebells, the cows are big.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children  
Let's go to the midsummer church  
Rise up, dear mother  
Rise to braid my hair  
To prepare the grain-measure  
You cannot rise, young daughter,  
You cannot rise, do not wake up  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Marsh marigolds over the feet  
Juniper among the fields

label: Murelaulud
translation:
When I begin to go  
Then sorrow begins to come  
Water rolling from the eyes  
I do not forget beauty  
I do not fear dear joy  
I take beauty with me  
Joy between the clothes  
Fun in six folds  
Jokes in the scarlet flax  
Then the corners remained wet from crying  
Father’s horses neighing  
Cattle bellowing  
Grandmother’s punished cows crying  
Good feeders crying  
Feeders, waterers  
Those who give hay before  
Those who bring oats by hand  
Those who raise rye flowers  
I serve bitter work  
I till the evil fields  
Frugal, neat chamber  
Work had to be done, toil endured  
Water twisted from the sleeve  
Turned from six courses  
I do not understand bitter thanks  
The wicked did not give warning  
All my fingers were scolded  
Forearms bruised  
I understand, I answer back  
If only my yards were rowans  
Shoulders like orchards  
Forearms like maples  
Fingers in currant leaves  
Then I should accept punishment  
Would take the beating sticks  
Would endure the strike blows  
Before the harsh scoldings  
The most stern nods  
Pass through my heart  
Through my beautiful body  
Through my red face  
Through my tangled hair  
Whom I show dullness  
Or to whom I complain of sad mind  
To the stone on the church path  
To the lime on the lime wall outside  
The stone was stubborn, would not speak  
The lime was thick, would not chat  
The stone did not understand the girl’s refusal  
The lime did not grant the girl’s request  
Little stone, little rock  
Little lime, plump one  
You could have lived in the sea  
Vanished on the seashore  
Before you came to our land  
To torment me  
To trouble my hands

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
When will we get there
To that big island
To the endless land
Where fire had made a room
Water rolled logs
Rain covered the walls with moss
Fog slammed doors shut
Dew fell on the doorstep
There hay is mowed with a scythe
The calves take a break
There children grew fluent
Daughters grew serious
Mother’s children kinder

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
When will we get there
To that big island
To the endless land
Where fire had made a room
Water rolled logs
Rain covered the walls with moss
Fog slammed doors shut
Dew fell on the doorstep
There hay is mowed with a scythe
The calves take a break
There children grew fluent
Daughters grew serious
Mother’s children kinder

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I was bad, I stood sad  
To whom do I complain of sadness  
To whom do I plant boredom  
To whom do I burrow kindness  
To whom do I complain of a sad mind  
To whom but to the stone  
The stone on the church road  
On the chalk, on the fabric path  
There I plant boredom  
There I complain of a sad mind  
A little stone, a little shard  
A little chalk, a little thick one  
You could have raged in the sea  
Shouted on the sea shores  
Before you came to our land  
To torment us  
To trouble the poor cattle  
If our fields had rowan trees  
Handfuls of maple stems  
Shoulder-knots of apple trees  
Fingers of raspberry bushes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
A little Hellak, little brother  
When you called me  
Called, urged  
You shouted over the fence  
I won’t take you to the hayfield  
Nor carry you on the arch  
Villagers are cutting hay  
Farm wagons are paying  
I mow in five ways with a scythe  
Rake bends in eights  
I put work on the sheath  
I quietly put mouth to feed  
To make a place for the year  
To find a head for the day  
I got you a suitor  
I got you left again  
Quietly you put soda to soak  
For a day to thresh  
For a year to split firewood  
Come to me, little maiden  
Come to me and run  
I’ll bring you a blue apron  
Blue, red apron  
I’ll bring you a cardboard apron for your neck  
One as black as coal  
Likely I’ll bring a striped one  
Come to me, little maiden  
Come to me and run  
I’ll bring buns while sending you off  
I’ll bring pies while courting you  
Apples while coming outside  
Honey breads while leaving  
I got you a suitor  
I got you left again  
You brought hay tenderly to the meadow  
Carried gold onto the arch  
Quietly you put soda to soak  
For a day to thresh  
For a year to split firewood  
The fist was a checkered apron  
Palm-size dear cardboard apron  
Coal-black  
A striped twig bundle  
Oh, I poor, or I poor  
If I had known, could have known  
If I had spilled ash on beer barrels  
Or vodka jugs were broken  
Because I was courted badly  
Wrong suitor given  
I thought to find a place to settle  
To find a big place for bread  
To master the place for barley  
I got a big pauper’s place  
Found a big mourning place  
I mastered the thistle’s place  
Little maiden, young one  
You were made for hardy men  
Needed for farm wagons  
No hay is cooked there  
Farm wagons are not wanted  
Thistles are searched for there  
Nettles are picked there  
Medicinal herbs are brewed  
Food is given to you  
No salt is added inside  
No drop of milk on top  
You eat until your heart cries  
Tears stream from your eyes  
Run from eyes to chest  
From chest to the waist  
From the waist to the feet  
From the feet into the furrow  
From the furrow to the faint field  
From there geese will wander  
Old woman watering the cloth barn  
Your young tear water  
Your poor weary water  
You think of man’s honey cake  
Also the kalewipag[?] along  
Man became a troublemaker  
Also a neck breaker for the wife  
The apron became knee rags  
The harness became a burden  
You could have been alone  
Been alone, walked alone  
Girl turned into work  
Put her head in a braid  
Tied her hair with silk  
If only the child had come to the wig  
Used the rake to the bottom of the can  
Oh that golden maidenhood  
Hair smooth, unstroked  
Dear brush not lowered  
Now you have grown old  
Now your braids face the wind  
Rows now face the rain  
Women praised apron knees  
Honored the harness base  
You were quick to trust  
You jumped quickly to try  
Let them take apron knees  
Begin with harness base  
Because I thought the apron better  
More honest under the harness  
So that no whore’s tales  
Nor harlot’s stories would flow  
I got well, I got to live  
I heard whore’s morning song  
Whore wide and white  
Heard harlot’s clean line  
I hear, I answer back  
The whore had a beautiful life  
Whores walked proudly  
Harlots too wide and white  
Harlots walked purely

You shouted over the fence
