No drop of milk on top  
You eat until your heart cries  
Tears stream from your eyes  
Run from eyes to chest  
From chest to the waist  
From the waist to the feet  
From the feet into the furrow  
From the furrow to the faint field  
From there geese will wander  
Old woman watering the cloth barn  
Your young tear water  
Your poor weary water  
You think of man’s honey cake  
Also the kalewipag[?] along  
Man became a troublemaker  
Also a neck breaker for the wife  
The apron became knee rags  
The harness became a burden  
You could have been alone  
Been alone, walked alone  
Girl turned into work  
Put her head in a braid  
Tied her hair with silk  
If only the child had come to the wig  
Used the rake to the bottom of the can  
Oh that golden maidenhood  
Hair smooth, unstroked  
Dear brush not lowered  
Now you have grown old  
Now your braids face the wind  
Rows now face the rain  
Women praised apron knees  
Honored the harness base  
You were quick to trust  
You jumped quickly to try  
Let them take apron knees  
Begin with harness base  
Because I thought the apron better  
More honest under the harness  
So that no whore’s tales  
Nor harlot’s stories would flow  
I got well, I got to live  
I heard whore’s morning song  
Whore wide and white  
Heard harlot’s clean line  
I hear, I answer back  
The whore had a beautiful life  
Whores walked proudly  
Harlots too wide and white  
Harlots walked purely

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children  
We go to Midsummer Day church  
I go to my father's grave  
You go to your mother's grave  
Rise up, little mother  
Rise up to comb my hair  
Rise up, dear aunt  
Rise up, dear uncle  
Rise up to count my money.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Long ago, people also lived
When they ate hawthorn shoots
Linden leaves as a beverage
Birch sap as a sauce
And horse bean gruel

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
When I was just a teenager  
A knee-high little boy  
At night I thrashed the manor's meadow  
By day I plowed the poor land  
Surely I cried until my eyes stung  
I cried in the barn  
Where I cried, the corners were wet  
And where I stood, the walls were wet  
From there dripped a little pond  
A growing fish pond  
Where the village cattle drank  
From the lights of the poor in the parish

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
When I was just a teenager  
A knee-high little boy  
At night I thrashed the manor's meadow  
By day I plowed the poor land  
Surely I cried until my eyes stung  
I cried in the barn  
Where I cried, the corners were wet  
And where I stood, the walls were wet  
From there dripped a little pond  
A growing fish pond  
Where the village cattle drank  
From the lights of the poor in the parish

label: Murelaulud
translation:
In ancient times,  
When people ate rose hip porridge,  
Boiled linden leaf soup,  
Birch sap sauce,  
One could live well.  
But now we eat rye bread,  
Dip it in fish oil,  
And drink cow’s milk.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
When I was a young boy  
A knee-high little lad  
At night I thrashed the manor's barn  
By day I mowed the hay patches  
Where I cried, the corners were wet  
Where I stood, the walls were wet  
There a little pond formed  
A fish pond grew  
From there village cattle could drink  
Carried by bailiffs from the manor  
My life was very hard  
My work was big and demanding  
The manor lord was very angry,  
Meal times were very bitter  
Bringing home the sack was heavy  
The housewife was very proud  
She put fish in the bread  
The cabbage jar was half empty

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
When I was a young boy  
A knee-high little lad  
At night I thrashed the manor's barn  
By day I mowed the hay patches  
Where I cried, the corners were wet  
Where I stood, the walls were wet  
There a little pond formed  
A fish pond grew  
From there village cattle could drink  
Carried by bailiffs from the manor  
My life was very hard  
My work was big and demanding  
The manor lord was very angry,  
Meal times were very bitter  
Bringing home the sack was heavy  
The housewife was very proud  
She put fish in the bread  
The cabbage jar was half empty

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Until now, life was good
Until now, it was beautiful to live
When there was an old woman in life
And there was an old man on the farm
One could eat in the kitchen too
Or eat in the storage too
Secretly eat in the sauna too
Even more in the living room
Then the old woman died
Then the old man died
One could cry in the kitchen too
Or cry in the storage too
Secretly cry in the sauna too
Even more in the living room

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Võera maiden woman, wicked maiden woman  
Heard me coming home  
To be at the edge of the field  
Behind the forest singing  
Jump, Anne, leap, Kaie  
Throw that gate into Võru  
Close the door tightly  
The titmouse pecks closely  
The cuckoo calls out  
His maiden was a little old woman  
Heard me coming home  
To be at the edge of the field  
Behind the forest singing  
Jump, Anne, leap, Kaie  
Stop that gate from Võru  
Listen, here comes the golden bird  
The cuckoo calls out

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children  
Both from the same village  
One from one farm  
Handkerchiefs in hand  
One wipes the other's eye  
Let's go to church on Midsummer's Day  
You go to father's grave  
I go to mother's grave  
Rise up, old lady  
Rise to comb my hair  
Rise to tend to my feet  
To prepare the annivakka (a traditional dish)  
The old lady answered from beneath the earth  
You may not rise, young daughter,  
You may not rise, do not wake up  
Grass is growing over the earth  
Clever hills over the grave  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Buttercups over the eyebrows  
I heard it, I heard it again:  
I water the grass with dew  
The oxen with fodder herbs  
Bluebells for the calves  
Buttercups for the sheep  
Rise up, old lady  
Rise up, lift your head  
You left many to wander  
A room full of fireflies  
A table full of little children  
Early to the barren field  
Early to the barren fields  
Pulling the eyes of rye  
Water-nymphs playing tricks  
The old lady heard, she heard again:  
Who has heard this before  
Who has heard, who has seen  
That a ghost has visited home  
I heard it, I heard it again  
Who laments with a sorrowful heart  
Who bewails in distress  
I lamented with a sorrowful heart  
I lamented with sobs out loud  
On the road to the stone church.  
The old lady heard, she heard again  
My dear young daughter  
You may not rise, you may not wake  
I hear it, I hear it again:  
I have no giver of grace  
I have no one to stroke my head  
The wind comes, gives grace  
The day comes, strokes the head  
For an hour, grace of the wind  
For a day, grace of the day  
For a lifetime, grace of the old lady.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two girls from Vaestala
We go to church weeping
One wipes the other's eyes
One the other's cheek
One strokes the other's head
I go to the old woman's grave
I go to the Toadi grave
"Rise up, little old woman,
Rise up, little Toadi woman.
Rise to prepare the Annivakka cradle
To fasten the kerstu dress!"
"You cannot rise, young daughter,
You cannot rise, do not awaken.
Grass has grown on the soil
Wild hay on the grave
Bluebells over the eyes
Buttercups over the eyebrows
St. John's-wort over the neck.
May God comb your head
Prepare the Annivakka cradle
Fasten the kerstu dress
A white ribbon on your coffin
A patterned ribbon on your kerstu."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Thus was the poor widow,
When the building had no roof;
All winds blow in,
All rains pour down,
Showers run in.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Daughter, daughter, walk well  
Enemy child, walk purely Estonian  
So that your chick will bow  
The copper fence will turn white  
So that your girls shine  
So that your sparks sparkle  
Our spoon handles shimmered  
Our lip plates gleamed  
Tomorrow the bailiff will come to the village  
Old gentleman to the spirit thief

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Whom shall I plant as a border  
Whom shall I step gently  
Whom else but a stone  
A stone for the churchyard  
On the plaster, on the paper  
The stone is silent  
The slab is thick, does not speak  
The stone does not hear the maiden's tongue  
The slab does not heed the maiden's prayer.  
What do I care for the basin  
Fingers...  
Then I will begin to cry  
Cry, lament:  
Little stone, dear one  
Why did you come to our land  
Couldn’t you roar in the sea  
Shout from the sea shore  
Before you came to our land  
To bother the maiden  
To torment her arm  
I have no milk of the old woman in my side  
No old woman's torment in my arms  
I have no rowan trees in my side  
Maple trees in my arms  
If only I had rowan trees in my side  
Maple trees in my arms.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The lazy one created by the family daughter  
Ready to make foals  
He did not bother to be a winner maker  
Did not bother to churn cream  
Threw the pine into the pine forest  
The cream butter into the churn  
I came from you home  
Took butter from the churn  
Or from the pine forest  
Made butter on the butterboard  
For the herdsman's little corner.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Who is waiting for me at home
Who is calling me into the house
The cat is waiting for me at home
The cat is calling me into the house
Who is watching me at the gate
The dog is watching me at the gate
The dog is waiting for scraps
In the corner of the cat’s basket
The mistress, the mutt
Heard me coming home
Was at the path’s end
Singing behind the forest
The tune from her bread table
The knife from the corner of the table
Carried the cake bowls to the cupboard
My dear gentle mother
Brought me a bowl of cake
You came from work, tired
You come from the path, weary
What’s the use of being tired for nothing
What’s the use of being weary for no reason.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Who is waiting for me at home
Who is calling me into the house
The cat is waiting for me at home
The cat is calling me into the house
Who is watching me at the gate
The dog is watching me at the gate
The dog is waiting for scraps
In the corner of the cat’s basket
The mistress, the mutt
Heard me coming home
Was at the path’s end
Singing behind the forest
The tune from her bread table
The knife from the corner of the table
Carried the cake bowls to the cupboard
My dear gentle mother
Brought me a bowl of cake
You came from work, tired
You come from the path, weary
What’s the use of being tired for nothing
What’s the use of being weary for no reason.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our kaksik vaestalasta  
We go to the Midsummer church  
You go to father’s grave  
I go to mother’s grave  
Rise up, my mother  
Rise to comb my hair  
To prepare anniversary porridge  
You cannot rise, young daughter  
You cannot rise, do not wake up  
Grass has grown over me on the soil  
Hay over the grave  
Let me feed grass to the young bull  
Hay to the oxen.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor people  
We are going to the Midsummer church  
You are going to your father's grave  
I am going to my mother's grave  
Rise up, little mother  
Rise up to comb my hair  
You cannot rise, young daughter  
You cannot rise, you cannot awaken  
Ferns have grown on the grave  
Grass canisters have grown on the soil  
Midsummer cans at the feet.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our Two Poor Ones  
When shall we go to church  
On Midsummer Day we shall go to church  
You will go to your father's grave  
I will go to my mother's grave  
Rise up, tender maiden  
Rise up and row  
Rise up to comb my hair  
Rise up to count my money  
To prepare the storage box  
The maiden understands, responds aloud  
I cannot rise, daughter, I am young  
The grass has grown over the soil  
Hay for peace over the grave  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Daylilies over the feet  
Buttercups over the eyebrows  
I understand, I respond:  
I will bring a sand sieve from the town  
I will bring a strong sieve from the market  
I will bring a proper sieve from Paide  
I will sieve sand towards the town  
Grass towards the black tower  
Rise up and row  
Cannot rise, cannot row  
May God comb your hair  
May God count your money  
May He prepare the storage box.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
We are two poor children,  
You without a father, I without a mother,  
We are going to Midsummer Day church.  
You are going to your father's grave,  
I am going to my mother's grave,  
There we will have a cry of victory.  
Handkerchiefs in hand,  
One wipes the other's eyes,  
One strokes the other's head.  
Rise up, my dear father,  
Rise up, my dear mother,  
Rise up to comb my hair,  
To prepare the woolen bag,  
To lift the kerchief cover.  
Young daughters cannot rise,  
Young sons cannot rise,  
Grass has grown on my grave,  
Hay on the grave,  
Daisy on my eyebrow,  
May God comb your head,  
Prepare the woolen bag,  
Lift the kerchief cover.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
A strange maiden, a vile maiden  
A strange old man, a vile old man  
Heard me coming home  
To be at the edge of the clearing  
Singing in the back forest  
Hear where the bad bird falls  
A fierce titmouse pecks  
Run, boy, reach the boy  
You bring bread to the table  
Grab a ladle from the kettle  
My maiden was a little maiden  
My old man was a little old man  
Heard me coming home  
To be at the edge of the clearing,  
Hear where the golden bird falls  
A good titmouse pecks  
Run, boy, reach the boy  
Bring bread to the table  
Put the knife on the corner of the table  
Carry the ladle also to the kettle  
He comes tired from work  
He comes exhausted from outside,  
From under the whip comes a slender one  
From under the mat a low one

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children  
We go to Midsummer at the church  
You go to father’s grave  
I go to mother’s grave  
Rise up, dear father  
Rise up, dear mother  
Rise to braid my hair,  
To prepare the anniversary basket  
To fasten the kerchief  
Mother heard and replied:  
You cannot rise, my young daughter,  
You cannot rise, do not awaken  
The grass that has grown on the earth,  
The hay on the grave  
Midsummer lilies on your neck  
The daughter heard, again she replied:  
I will bring a sieve of sand from the town  
A sieve of ashes from Harju  
I sieve sands towards the town  
Hay towards Harju.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our pair of mourners  
We go to Midsummer at the church,  
You go to father's grave,  
I go to mother's grave.  
Rise up, my dear mother,  
Rise to tend to my head,  
To prepare the Annivak,  
To fasten the kerstu cover.  
A colorful ribbon on my coffin,  
A white ribbon on my grave.  
The daughter cannot rise to the young men,  
Cannot rise, cannot awaken.  
The grass is crushing me into the soil,  
The bluebell was over my eyes.  
Still rise up and raise your head,  
I feed the grass to the young calf,  
The bluebells to the piglets,  
The buttercups to the roosters.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Töölaulud  ; Laulud abielust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Ann was a heifer maiden  
Keedi a darling around her neck  
She went to the barn to jump  
She offered to rush  
Between the stones on the threshold  
Anne’s beads broke  
Anne’s ribbons broke  
Ann went to her brother’s request  
My dear little brother  
Take my ribbons and fix them  
Sew the coin purses shut  
Renew the edges of the buckle  
Ann did not take it, Ann did not go  
She went herself to the blacksmith  
Walking on the road very little  
Across the land very low  
Ann went to the blacksmith’s request  
Little blacksmith, little man  
Take my ribbons and fix them  
Sew the coin purses shut  
Renew the edges of the buckle  
The blacksmith wanted it, Ann was ready  
The blacksmith’s children were lively  
The blacksmith himself without black  
The blacksmith’s wife the blackest of all  
I thought I would become the manor’s husband  
I thought I would become the barn helper  
A black woman becomes the free mistress of the manor  
The other half of the farmhand.  
What was under the care of the serf?  
Under the whims of the little masters  
The fire was in the room without a lock  
The well was covered without a lid  
The pigsty was without a gate  
That was under the care of the serf  
Under the whims of the little masters  
May it not exist  
May it not be born  
Who carries the crown’s debt?  
The servant will surely cloak it  
Where was the crown’s household?  
The servant’s lodging  
Where was the fire that made the room?  
Hundreds of walls covered with moss  
Water rolling the logs  
Mist casting doors before them  
Dew tying the roof over.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children,  
Our two like little chicks,  
A pair like ducklings,  
I have no father, I have no mother,  
I have no proper teacher  
Nor a wise dancer.  
I go to the church on Midsummer's Day,  
You go to your father's grave,  
I go to my mother's grave,  
With mourning scarves in hand,  
Mourning letters in gloves.  
Rise up, dear father,  
Rise up, dear mother,  
Rise to comb my head,  
To make an anniversary bowl,  
To attach a kerchief cover.  
You cannot rise, young daughter.  
Cannot rise, cannot awaken,  
Grass has grown over the earth,  
Pasture grass over the grave,  
Over the eye, the blue flower,  
Over the eyebrow, the marsh marigold.  
May God comb your head,  
Make the anniversary bowl,  
Attach the kerchief cover,  
Feed the pasture grass to the calves,  
The pasture hay to the oxen,  
The blue flowers to the cows,  
Then I will rise up  
To make the anniversary bowl,  
To attach the kerchief cover.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor people  
Let's go to the midsummer church  
With mourning cloths in hand  
Mourning flowers in the cloth  
One wipes the other's eyes  
One wipes the other's cheeks  
One strokes the other's head  
Let's go to the grandmother's grave  
Let's go to the grandfather's grave  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up, little grandfather  
Rise to comb my hair  
To comb the hair, to smooth the hair  
To prepare the wool carder  
To fasten the kerchief  
The poor daughter may not rise  
Grass has grown on the soil  
Hay for the grave  
A bluebell on the eye  
Buttercups on the eyebrows  
I feed the grass to the little bull  
Hay for the oxen  
Bluebells for the cows.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor men  
Two little farm boys  
We go to Midsummer at the church  
You go to grandmother's grave  
I go to grandfather's grave  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up, little grandfather  
Rise to wash my head  
Rise to wash my eyes  
Wash me a fine shirt  
Choose me a white shirt  
Cannot rise, do not wake  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Field hay over the grave  
Over the eyes of the liverleaf  
Horseshoes over the toes  
Grass I feed to the calf  
Liverleafs for the pigs  
Horseshoes for the foals  
Liverleafs, dung flowers  
Coltsfoot, golden flowers.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Oh my dear old grandmother  
You left me to live without a home,  
To grow without a necklace.  
To run a family without support.  
I would rather live homeless  
Grow without a necklace  
Than live without a grandmother  
Helped by the world without a roof.  
When I walk to the church path,  
I have a linden leaf on my chest,  
A birch leaf on my neck,  
A willow bark on my head.  
The linden leaf breaks my chest.  
The birch leaf breaks my neck.  
The willow bark scratches my hair,  
Aging the honey wax.  
It breaks the golden-haired.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
The Creator made poor white women,  
The gold bearers the most slender,  
The enemy child was a maiden;  
She ate berries from the shop,  
Drank water from the waves,  
The berry was always red,  
With the strength of a water maiden.  
So do not beat the poor child,  
The poor one whimpers for a switch,  
Crying, cheeks red.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
The Creator made poor white women,  
The gold bearers the most slender,  
The enemy child was a maiden;  
She ate berries from the shop,  
Drank water from the waves,  
The berry was always red,  
With the strength of a water maiden.  
So do not beat the poor child,  
The poor one whimpers for a switch,  
Crying, cheeks red.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Three were under the elm tree,
Wandering after the day. -
One was a daughter without a mother,
The second a son without a father,
The third a poor widow woman. -
Why does the daughter without a mother cry?
"I cry for the wedding ornament,
I cry for the wreath on my head,
That belongs to the family daughter,
For the skirt on my back,
That belongs to the family daughter,
For the knitted mittens on my hands,
That belong to the family daughter."
Why does the son without a father cry?
"I cry for the plow driver,
For hauling the plow's load,
For beating it before winter,
For handing the spear. -
If I go to plow the swamp,
To harrow the field,
I have no plow driver,
No one to haul the plow's load,
No one to beat it before winter. -
If I go to go to war,
To tread the road of blood,
To travel the war path,
To march the enemy's road,
I have no one to wield the sword,
No one to tend to the wounded,
No one to hand me the spear."
Why then does the widow woman cry,
Complaining half a summer long?
"I cry for my former life,
I cry for my dear husband,
As a poor widow woman:
Like a house without a roof,
A yard without a fence,
A bedroom without a curtain,
A heap without a crown,
Rolling in the rain,
Treading through wind and storm."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two paupers,  
Let's go on Midsummer to the church  
You without a father, I without a mother,  
One on the other's grave.  
Rise up, little father.  
Rise up, little mother!  
Rise up to dress me,  
To put clothes on me,  
To prepare the food bowl,  
To fasten the kerstuk (headband).  
The old woman hears, the father replies:  
You cannot rise, cannot wake,  
Grass has grown over the soil,  
Weeds over the grave,  
Bluebells over the eyes,  
Coltsfoot over the eyebrows,  
Linden trees over the rye.  
I hear and reply:  
I feed the grass to the calf,  
Weeds to the oxen,  
Bluebells to the cows.  
Coltsfoot to the roosters.  
The old woman probably did not hear,  
Did not understand the words,  
She remains to breathe on the grave,  
To mourn our sorrow.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
When I was still young,
A knee-high boy.
At night I beat the manor’s thigh,
During the day I plowed small fields.
Where I stood, the walls were wet,
Where I cried, the corners were wet.
There dripped a small pond,
A little fish lake grew.
From there the village herds drank,
Village herds, big bulls,
The spirits of the parish enlightened.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
When I was still young,
A knee-high boy.

During the day I plowed small fields.
Where I stood, the walls were wet,
Where I cried, the corners were wet.
There dripped a small pond,
A little fish lake grew.
From there the village herds drank,
Village herds, big bulls,
The spirits of the parish enlightened.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The mind is always sad  
The heart always wretched.  
Some complain of sorrowful minds  
Some of ailing misery  
Some of hatred’s rolling tales  
Even to stone, even to stump.  
The stone does not understand the maiden’s refusal  
The poor maiden’s plea  
The stone responds in Riga languages  
Whispers in Pärnu tongues.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The strange old woman was an old lady  
The strange old man was an old man  
Heard coming to my home  
From the edge of the field  
Singing back and forth in the forest  
Hear where the sad bird falls  
And he still sings for the young children  
He has no feathers in his mouth  
No bindings on his tongue.  
He has no father, he has no mother  
He has no true teachers  
A tavern bench and a card table  
That is my father, that is my mother  
That is my true teacher  
The father's toil has died away  
The mother's toil has died away  
I was left alone to call  
Like a gosling crying  
Like flaxseed rustling  
I stayed like a rabbit on the grass  
Like a magpie I stayed on the fishing rod  
Stamping on the room's floorboard

label: Murelaulud
translation:
My grandmother was an old lady  
My grandfather was an old man  
I heard my home coming  
Out in the village  
But behind, in the forest, singing  
Hear where gold falls from the mouth  
And there sings a dear child  
He has feathered lips  
On the tongue are locked words  
He has a father, he has a mother  
He has a true teacher  
A tavern bench and a card table  
That is not my father to me  
That is not my mother to me  
That is not the true teacher  
My father died in trouble  
My mother died in trouble  
I was left alone to cry  
Like a chick to call  
Not shrieking like flax seed  
Not like a hare to the grass  
Not like a magpie on the roof  
To bang on the roof of the room

label: Murelaulud
translation:
How should I go home  
Heart inside the rib cage  
In front of a foreign stranger  
The door began to creak  
From behind the wall to fight  
To snap from the door shutter  
Boards to creak against  
Do not scold in front of young men  
Force your mouth to loosen  
Tongue behind to be soft

label: Murelaulud
translation:
But then feelings came into the room  
And I was made to sit down  
And they asked and listened  
If you are a child of the home  
When I start to leave  
Then the storm will begin to come  
And water will start to flow from the eyes  
It flows from the eyes to the cheeks  
From the cheeks to the chest  
From the chest to the knees  
From the knees to the toes  
From the toes to the meadow  
There lie the springs  
Where deep rivers are born  
From my poor tears  
To the gold-loving shelter  
To the cheek’s rolling wave.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I had worries, there was no other way,  
I had sorrow, there was no herd.  
My worries were like a black cloud,  
My sorrow like an entire herd,  
My concern like a swelling.  
Who do I complain to with my sad thoughts,  
Who do I tell my troubles?  
To the stone, to the stump,  
To the stone on the church path,  
To the willow outside the fence,  
The stone was silent, it did not speak,  
The rock was thick, it did not chatter,  
The stump was numb, it did not feel me.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children,  
Our two like little ducklings,  
A pair like ducklings,  
Let's go to Juanipää cemetery,  
Handkerchiefs in hand,  
Letters of mourning in the handkerchief,  
I will go to father's grave,  
You will go to mother's grave,  
Rise up, dear father,  
Rise up, dear mother,  
Rise and weave your head,  
Prepare the feeding trough,  
Fasten the coffin lid.  
The son cannot rise,  
The daughter cannot rise;  
Grass has grown over the soil,  
The field is down in the grave,  
Bluebells for the eyes,  
Coltsfoot for the eyebrows.  
I heard the answer,  
I told father the answer:  
Grass I feed to the heifers,  
To the awake fields,  
Bluebells for the cows,  
Coltsfoot for the calves.  
But father understood, answered back:  
The son cannot rise,  
Cannot rise, cannot awaken.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I would die, death does not take me,  
I would want, disease does not kill me,  
I am a relative of death,  
And I am the stable boy of disease.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Murelaulud  ; Töölaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I would sing as I am laid down,  
Be silent as I am sworn,  
Otherwise I would sit, I am bored,  
Otherwise I would walk, I am dull.  
To whom I plant boredom,  
To whom I step dullness,  
To whom I complain of sad minds,  
To whom do I humble my spirit?  
To others, as to stone,  
To stone for the church,  
Papier-mâché on stone.  
The stone does not understand the maiden's language,  
The flagstone does not hear the maiden's prayer.  
Little stone, little gold,  
Little flagstone, little thick one,  
You might be able to sleep in the sea,  
Shout on the seashore,  
Move in the sea sand.  
Before you came to our land,  
Our land, our island,  
To torment us,  
To trouble our hands and arms;  
If we had rowanbushes in the courtyard,  
Maple arms,  
Fingers like currants,  
Then we would beat with whips,  
We would endure blows of a club.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
Foster daughter, vile daughter,
Hears me coming home,
To be out in the yard,
Singing in the forest behind;
Hear where the evil bird falls
The fierce titmouse taps,
The little forest snake sings;
Takes a knife from the table.
Afraid to cut bread,
Takes a ladle from the kettle,
Afraid to touch the fire.
Her girl was a little old woman,
Her father was a little old man,
Hears me coming home,
To be out in the yard,
Singing in the forest behind,
Hear where the golden bird falls,
The lively titmouse taps,
The little forest bird sings;
Mouth sings, heart worries,
Cheeks flushing, wave-lady,
Eyebrows golden drops,
The river runs between the belt,
From there the village cows drink,
To the lively Äivere,
The stormy Puivere,
The bulls of Sõerevere.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Nõnna, nõnna little lives,  
Nõnna, nõnna little brothers,  
Nõnna was a poor widow,  
When there is no roof;  
All blizzards come upon her,  
All rains fall upon her.  
I turn my side, I find cold,  
I turn my back, I find a wall;  
All are cold sideboards,  
The window boards are at the edge,  
The mushroom boards are on the wall.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Nõnna, nõnna little lives,  
Nõnna, nõnna little brothers,  
Nõnna was a poor widow,  
When there is no roof;  
All blizzards come upon her,  
All rains fall upon her.  
I turn my side, I find cold,  
I turn my back, I find a wall;  
All are cold sideboards,  
The window boards are at the edge,  
The mushroom boards are on the wall.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Row the boat, row the boat  
Row the boat to that land,  
Where is the home of the sad ones  
The dwelling of the poor  
The umbrella of the poor children,  
Where the wind has blown dust  
Water has rolled the logs  
Rain has mossed the hundreds  
Fog has closed the doors;  
There is the home of the sad ones  
The dwelling of the poor  
The umbrella of the poor children.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
I sing through worries  
Through all hardships  
I worry, I lose hope  
I regret, my neck aches  
Tangled, scruffy hair  
The mouth sings, the heart worries  
The face sheds tears  
The eyes roll water  
The eyebrows drip gold tears  
From the eyes they roll down to the chest  
From the chest to the rowan berries  
From the rowan berries to the heels  
From the heels they sink down  
Because rivers started to run  
Springs to flow.  
But my poor watery eye  
Does not drip gold tears.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
How should one go home,  
To gnaw the crust at home  
To fuss over the dog’s troubles  
To pluck the eyelashes of the swallow?  
The home is angry in front  
Rustling in the bushes, in the shrubbery  
A knife in the corner in hand  
The heart trembles inside.  
Who waits for me at home?  
The dog waits for me at home,  
The dog waits for the little crusts,  
The cat waits for fish broth bits. -

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I always have boredom  
I always have sorrow  
To whom should I complain my sad thoughts  
To whom should I tell my troubles  
A stone on the church path  
A rope on the priest’s board  
The stone is stiff and does not speak  
The rope is thick and does not tell  
The stone did not understand the maiden’s words  
The rope did not heed the maiden’s prayers  
The stone rolled quickly onto another stone  
The rope rolled onto the priest’s board.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Why do we, gold ones, grieve:
We live among the fields,
The poor stand silently.
Will another generation rise for us?
Or will new times begin for us:
Will a sick generation rise for us:
A time beneath gifts.
Sickness will rise in the land,
Plague will strike the land,
Scabies will roll into the land,
Picking the younger ones to serve!
Cutting off children in the cradle!
Weakening young people at work,
Sucking from the mother's saliva,
Men's scabies is in Viljandi,
Women's disease is on the road to Narva,
Girls' plague is from afar.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
What worries should I worry about,  
What harm should I regret,  
Worrying breaks the mind,  
Regretting strangles the neck.  
I ruin my curly hair,  
Strangle my golden locks.  
If only the worry was known to the boys,  
The harm endured by the boys,  
What is known to our men,  
The suffering of the church lads.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Remembering granny,
Remembering granny's dear love.
When granny passes away,
Then love also disappears.
Granny was taken from the door,
Love went out the window.
Granny was taken along the road,
Love went along the garden!
A hole was made for granny,
Love by the edge of the hole.
Granny was sent into the hole,
Love fell down below!
So remember granny,
Remember granny's dear love,
When you wear granny's shirt,
On your arm granny's sleeves,
Around your waist granny's wide belt,
Around granny’s round shawl.
When granny’s shirt is off,
Granny’s sleeves from your arms,
Granny’s wide belt from your waist,
Then granny will be forgotten,
Granny’s dear love forgotten.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two little seedlings.  
Our two like little chickens.  
A pair like ducklings.  
One we eat:  
One we drink,  
One we sleep through.  
You are fatherless, I am motherless,  
Let's go to the Midsummer hill church.  
You go to father's grave,  
I go to mother's grave.  
Rise up, little old woman!  
Lift your head to weave,  
To prepare the annivakka (grain container).  
To fasten the coffin bracket.  
Rise up, little old man:  
Come help me set up the saw,  
Help twist the saw handle.  
From the distance comes a reply:  
"You must not rise, you must not wake,  
You must not rise, young daughter.  
Earth is beneath, earth is above,  
Grass grows over the earth,  
Clover on the grave,  
Bluebell over the eye,  
Buttercup over the edge.  
Midsummer willows on the face.  
Still rise up, little old woman!  
I will bring a sand sieve from the town,  
From the borough I will bring a hemp sieve,  
From the market I will bring a strong sieve,  
From other lands a soil sieve,  
I sieve sand towards the town,  
Black soil towards the barn.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Do not kill my food owners,  
Food owners and drink owners.  
Godparents went to the meadow,  
The soil went to the mourner!  
The plow stayed in the brother's hands.  
The scythe stayed in the nephew's hands,  
The cow stayed with the widow,  
The dog stayed with the village bailiff,  
The goat stayed with the shepherd's mother,  
The horse stayed with Olli Anso.  
The little oxen whined  
At the brother’s harsh plowing,  
The little horse neighed  
Looking for Olli Anso,  
The old little cow then  
At the widow’s milking.  
The little dog barked  
When the bailiff’s wife called,  
The little goat squawked  
When the shepherd’s mother pulled.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
When I go home.
Ahead are fears, ahead are loves!
Fear will sit in the room,
With a twig bundle in the ear!
I go to the fear's prayer,
Little fear, little love:
Forgive the fault,
Leave those tricks behind.
I will bring a basket of tomingas,
I will bring a basket of strawberries,
A sieve full of currants,
A cloth of sour cherries,
Half a cup of lingonberries,
I will bring cranberries for Christmas,
Nares for Christmas Eve,
I would bring many nuts,
Nuts I bring with pearly shells,
Shells with golden color,
I bring some honey sweetness,
I bring pomerance blossoms.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I have boredom,  
I always have misery,  
Every month my moods are sad.  
Whom do I plant boredom for?  
Whom do I cultivate misery for,  
Whom do I complain my sad moods to:  
To the stone on the church path,  
To the plaster on the paper wall.  
The stone is silent, it does not speak,  
The plaster is hard, it does not chatter.  
The stone does not understand these languages,  
The plaster does not heed these pleas.

label: Murelaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
Who among us is early in the morning?  
The crow is early in the morning.  
Even before the swallow arrives.  
Earlier still are hostile creatures,  
Before the head is set,  
The head set and the eyes washed,  
The feet were already trained below.  
They were sent to look for the ploughshare,  
To wake up the ox.  
What answer did they receive?  
The answer came from the ploughmen.  
I asked the ploughman,  
Called out to the helper,  
Did you see my ploughshare!  
You awoke the oxen.  
The ploughman did not speak,  
Nor did the helper reply.  
I took the ploughman’s hat,  
The helper’s good horse.  
Then the ploughman spoke,  
The helper replied,  
He went through our yard,  
Drank water from our river,  
Tasted water from our well.

label: Murelaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:

I asked the ploughman,  
Called out to the helper,  
Did you see my ploughshare!  
You awoke the oxen.

Nor did the helper reply.  
I took the ploughman’s hat,  
The helper’s good horse.  
Then the ploughman spoke,  
The helper replied,  
He went through our yard,  
Drank water from our river,  
Tasted water from our well.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Let's start going.  
When will we go there today?  
Where winds have made a room,  
Water has rolled the logs.  
Hundreds of walls have mossed over.  
Snow has piled the beams on top,  
Frost has put order to the eaves.  
Severe cold has made a door.  
There is the home of the sad,  
There is the place for the bad,  
The shelter for the poor children.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The woman in the hut was an old woman,  
In the hut was a little old lady.  
She heard me coming home,  
Being at the edge of the meadow,  
Singing behind in the forest.  
She calls the others to listen:  
"Listen where the evil bird falls,  
The fierce creature is ticking,  
The seagull is cawing!  
Listen where it has come to fall,  
To rage in our forest,  
To crow in the heather."  
When I come home from the forest,  
At the gate of the hut,  
Heart fluttering inside,  
Spear directly in the sheath,  
Knife tucked in the hand.  
Seeks to fight words,  
Seeks to clothe the quarrel,  
Growls and tries to corner me.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I was poor to support the parish!  
To care for the peasants,  
To trick the masters,  
To tease the housewives,  
To quarrel with the girls,  
To mock the boys.  
The village invited me to eat at their home,  
The village invited me to drink at their home,  
To set the patch in place.  
I heard, I answered back:  
"One eats as much as is fed,  
One drinks as much as is given:  
The taker eats more.  
When I grew bigger,  
I became stronger:  
Then I made the room face the wind,  
Life without limits.  
The room was strong against the wind,  
Life in the world was indeed beautiful.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I was poor to support the parish!  
To care for the peasants,

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
My dear old grandmother,  
when will I repay my grandmother’s trouble,  
grandmother  
closing at the mouth,  
turning at the back,  
an angel bought me  
an oat cart bought me,  
saved me from scrubbing the tobacco pipe.  
Grandmother is meaty in the town,  
there is cold well cake in the house,  
brother is fierce like the bottomless wind,  
sister is warm like the sauna steam.  
My dear old grandmother,  
how many nights have been sleepless,  
how many meals uneaten,  
how many lunches without lunch,  
how much sauce without fish.  
When you were watching over me  
the fire in the room did not go out,  
the woman dressed the young ones,  
the horse was harnessed to the gray wagon;  
I, despite everything, to the shaving bench  
smiled through my tears.

label: Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
My dear old grandmother,  
when will I repay my grandmother’s trouble,  
grandmother  
closing at the mouth,  
turning at the back,  
an angel bought me  
an oat cart bought me,  
saved me from scrubbing the tobacco pipe.  
Grandmother is meaty in the town,  
there is cold well cake in the house,  
brother is fierce like the bottomless wind,  
sister is warm like the sauna steam.  
My dear old grandmother,  
how many nights have been sleepless,  
how many meals uneaten,  
how many lunches without lunch,

the fire in the room did not go out,  
the woman dressed the young ones,  
the horse was harnessed to the gray wagon;  
I, despite everything, to the shaving bench  
smiled through my tears.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our old grandmother and grandfather,  
You without a grandmother, I without a grandfather,  
We went to the midsummer church,  
You to the grandmother’s grave,  
I to the grandfather’s grave.  
Rise up, little grandmother,  
Rise up, little grandfather.  
The grandmother responds,  
The grandfather responds,  
The poor daughter cannot rise,  
The poor son cannot rise.  
Grass grows over the soil,  
Field stones over the grave.  
Bluebells for the eyes,  
Dandelions for the eyebrows.  
My dear little grandmother,  
I feed grass to the calves,  
Field hay to the oxen.  
Still rise up and lift your head,  
Wave the flaxen sheaves,  
Rise and comb your head,  
Make a flax wick,  
Attach the Christmas gate.  
The grandmother hears and responds.  
May God make your comb,  
May He attach the Christmas gate,  
A colorful bird on the comb,  
A white bird on the wick.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Do not strike a poor person  
Do not strike: they are fatherless  
Do not put it away  
There is no listener for the gold,  
No defender for the dear,  
No mercy for the wicked  
If a cat wants, it kills  
If a cat is willing, it strikes  
Whoever has time will hit.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
How many are under the sky?  
Three were under the sky,  
Unrequested after the day.  
Who were under the sky,  
Unrequested after the sky?  
One was a daughter without a mother,  
Another a son without a father.  
The third a poor widow.  
Who cries for a son without a father?  
She cries for the haymakers,  
The haystack turners.  
Who cries for a daughter without a mother?  
She cries for the coffin makers,  
The coffin lid fixers.  
And what about the poor widow?  
She cries for the root pullers,  
The nettle pullers.  
So the poor widow:  
All blizzards come upon her,  
All rains pour down,  
Rolling rain clouds,  
When the hayloft had no roof,  
The granary had no doors,  
The barn had no wide eaves.  
Mother was a fine old woman,  
Cast down your anger,  
Pull down the tar ropes,  
Stretch the blue thread,  
Drop the red ribbon,  
Pull me up to heaven:  
There is no room for you here,  
No room for your breath;  
All hate the poor child:  
The trees hate her, the lands hate her,  
They hate the roots of the alder,  
They scold the hazel roots,  
They sting with nettles.  
All hate the poor child.  
We are two poor children:  
You fatherless, I motherless.  
Let's go to the Midsummer church:  
You go to the father's grave,  
I will go to the mother's grave.  
Rise up, my mother,  
Raise my head to comb,  
Prepare the honey box!  
Rise up, my father,  
Rise to comb my head!  
- The daughter cannot rise young,  
Cannot rise, cannot awaken,  
May God comb your head,  
Prepare the honey box,  
Fasten the coffin lid,  
Grass now grows over the earth,  
The meadow is for the grave,  
Buttercups over the eyebrows,  
Liverleaf over the eyes,  
St. John's wort at the feet.  
- I feed the grass to the young bull,  
The meadow to the spring lamb,  
The blue flowers to the pigs,  
The buttercups to the roosters,  
The St. John's wort to the hens.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Aigs go to Annelag,  
Two aigs  
What aigs go to me?  
They are my father's wrath aigs,  
Or are they my mother's love aigs,  
Or Mari's sleeping breads,  
Or are they eating-drinking aigs?  
They are not my father's wrath aigs,  
They are not my mother's love aigs,  
They are not Mari's sleeping breads,  
I would like to sleep,  
To sleep in Mari's lap,  
Anne under the carpet,  
Tõnu in the carriage blankets,  
Peeter in the long linen.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor people  
Let's go to the church on Midsummer's Day  
You without a father, me without a mother  
Holding mourning ribbons in hand  
Placing the mourning number in the corner  
Let's go to father's grave  
Let's go to mother's grave  
Rise up, Ella Eite  
Rise up to comb my hair  
To prepare the gift box  
To fasten the coffin lid  
Because my hideout home  
Cannot rise, there is no cure  
Soil is underneath, soil is on top  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Hay in the meadow on top  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Marigolds at the feet  
Buttercups over the eyebrows  
I feed the grass to the calves  
Hay to the oxen  
The calves are now big on the grass  
The cows are wide by the bluebells.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Father died, mother died  
Father was taken out the door  
The darlings went out the window  
Father was taken along the road  
Mother was taken along the road  
The darlings went along the garden  
Father and mother were buried  
The darlings were placed by the edge of the grave  
Rise up, dear father,  
Rise up, dear mother  
Rise up to fasten my coffin  
To prepare the bier  
But the old woman understood, the old man replied  
May God fasten your coffin  
May He prepare the bier  
You cannot rise, young daughter  
The soil is beneath, and the soil is above  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Hay is on the grave  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Pussy willows over the eyebrows  
Darnel grass over the navel  
Rise up still, dear father  
Rise up still, dear mother  
I will feed the grass to the cattle  
Hay to the oxen  
Bluebells to the pigs  
Darnel grass to the sheep  
Pussy willows to the beeswax.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Oh, dear Väetsa village  
Where birch trees grew in curls  
Grandmother’s daughters are beautiful  
Father’s daughters are calm  
Grandma’s daughters are cheerful  
The boy’s daughter is crying  
The girl is crying for her husband  
The madman shouts for the groom  
I am crying for grandma’s daughter  
I pity the plowmen  
The pure porridge cookers  
The clear blacksmiths -  
Mother was taken from the door  
Lovers left through the window  
Mother was taken along the road  
Lovers went through the gardens  
Mother was laid in the grave  
Lovers went down the hill  
We, two orphans  
Go to the John’s Day church  
Black scarves in hand  
We go to mother’s grave  
One wipes the other’s eyes  
One kisses the other’s neck  
One strokes the other’s head -  
Rise up, dear mother  
Rise up to groom my head  
To prepare the gift box  
To fix the coffin lid -  
You cannot rise, daughter, no  
You cannot rise, no use  
Never before has it been so  
That a schoolteacher is at home  
Give the village to the plowman  
May God comb your head  
The Creator grant you mercy  
Mary protect you in heaven -  
Rise up, dear mother -  
You cannot rise, no use  
Grass had grown over the earth  
Hay over the grave  
Cowslips over the brow  
Alder tree catkins over the ears -  
Rise up, dear mother  
I bring a sand sieve from the town  
From the borough I bring a sieve  
I sift that coarse sand  
I knead that rough soil -  
I feed grass to the calves  
Hay to the oxen  
Bluebells to the cows  
Cowslips to the roosters  
Henbane to the hens. -

label: Laulud laulust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Some think of singing for joy  
To sing a beautiful song  
The mouth sings, the heart worries  
The eyes shed tears  
Foreheads frown with wrinkles  
Eyebrows like drops of gold  
Tears roll from the eyes down the chest  
From the chest down to the knees  
From the knees down to the toes  
From the toes to the village street  
There the village drinks from the herd  
My brother drinks from the cattle  
The foals swim in the meadow  
Among German pigs stirring  
The tears of my poor eyes  
Forehead covered with wrinkles  
Eyebrows like drops of gold.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Away to the manly forest  
From the Taiju woman to the street  
The family daughters are coming  
Striped skirt on their backs  
The gate was down, the ribbon was on top.  
There were buns under the arm  
Honey shines in the jar  
One’s own girl was an old woman  
One’s own room was a small room  
She heard me coming home  
Being at the exit  
Singing behind the barn.  
"Jump Anne, leap Kaie  
Roll little Liisuke  
Take the bread to the table too  
Carry the ladle to the kettle  
Come to your hearing  
Being at the exit  
Singing behind the barn."  
Then the gold listened:  
"Hear whether the golden bird falls  
The silver tit is ticking."  
In a foreign place, girl, in a foreign place  
In a foreign room, girl, in a foreign room,  
She heard me coming home  
Being at the exit  
Singing behind the barn  
Then the evil ones listened  
The wicked ones looked out:  
"Hear where the evil bird falls  
The angry tit ticks."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Did not die a dresser  
Did not disappear a nurturer  
The one who kept worries went to the soil  
The one who made decisions went to the grave.  
I bring a sand sieve from the town  
From the borough I bring a sieve of judgment;  
I sift sand towards the town  
Towards the black soil tower.  
My Ella old lady  
Always rise, lift your head  
Rise, weave my head  
To prepare the gift box.  
"Cannot rise nor be awakened  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Peace hay over the grave."  
"I feed the grass to the calf  
Peace hay to the oxen  
Bluebells to the sheep  
Coltsfoot to the pigs."

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Every day is boring for me  
Every day is dull for me  
So many times my mind is sad.  
To whom shall I complain my sad thoughts  
To whom shall I speak openly  
To whom shall I reveal my hatred?  
I complain to the cuckoo  
I think to the meadowsweet.  
The meadowsweet calmed  
My youthful crying  
Longing beautifully.  
Longing breaks my mind  
Regretting I bear the burden.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I was left alone, I was left alone  
I was left by the motherland  
Left to cry like a chick  
To chirp like a bird seed.  
She calls, calls her dear.  
Calls her dear to the woods  
Calls her dear to the home  
Names her like a wheat grain  
Scolds her like bread dough.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Where I hear talking back  
Talking back, laughing  
There I walk tall  
Quickly, upright  
But here, as if with a log  
Feet like clumsy planks  
Straight as a proud colt  
I tread my steps more wisely.  
Where I hear praising  
Praising, talking back  
There I howl in a dream  
I walk with bristly hair.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Grandfather's mother  
Granddaughter of the family  
Hears me coming home  
At the edge of the yard  
The girls are singing behind.  
Hear where the big eater comes from  
The one who sets the baking trays. –  
Up jumps Eltse Kaie  
Next to her little Liisuke  
Take the bread to the table too.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The village talks about me as a village matter -  
The household tells about me as a family matter,  
The people talk harshly of me.  
They talk about me in gossip  
As if boiling water,  
Throwing it into the oven's bottom;  
What kind of fire comes from water?  
Water boils in a kettle -  
So they talk about me,  
Empty words for empty-headed girls,  
False stories about a poor child.  
Where I hear gossiping,  
Gossiping and laughing,  
There I walk taller,  
Soon standing upright,  
Treading more wisely;  
I strike both feet down -  
When these sticks are sharp.  
Where I hear boasting,  
Boasting and bragging,  
There I howl, confused in a dream -  
A sleepy, hairy doll,  
I did not want the village talk,  
I did not want the parish lies,  
Girls' empty talk,  
Boys' spiteful anger,  
Men's ill-will -  
I am proud on the tongues of gossip.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our Two Widows  
Our two like canaries  
We go to church on Sunday  
Handkerchiefs in hands  
Crying letters in handkerchiefs  
One wipes the other's eyes  
One strokes the other's head  
We go to father's grave  
We go to mother's grave  
Rise up, our father  
Rise up, our mother  
Rise to weave our heads  
To prepare the annivakka (a traditional woven belt)  
To fasten the kerstukuanta (a traditional buckle)  
The young daughter may not rise  
May not rise, may not awaken  
May God let you weave  
Prepare the annivakka  
Fasten the kerstukuanta  
Rise also, old grandmother  
Rise also, old grandfather  
May not rise, may not awaken  
Grass grows over the soil  
Hay on the grave  
I feed grass to the calves  
Hay to the cattle  
Gentian to the cows  
Buttercups to the sheep  
The young daughter may not rise.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Oh dear, little brothers!
We are two poor children, -
You without a father, I without a mother,
Let's go to the church on Midsummer's Day:
Let's go to grandmother's grave,
Let's go to father’s grave:
Rise up, little father,
Rise up, little grandmother!
Rise up to comb my hair,
To prepare the anniversary bowl,
To fix the kerchief! -
But grandmother answers back,
She speaks from father’s grave:
"You cannot rise up, cannot awaken!
Grass has grown over the earth,
Hay over the grave,
Apple tree on the shoulder,
Willow on the side,
Juniper on the other thigh,
Birch has grown on the neck,
Spruce has grown in the hair bun; -
God help you to comb,
To prepare the anniversary bowls.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
In the grass, there is evil; in the grass, there is a herd  
A stranger watches from the grass  
There are spoonfuls in the lot  
Bread crumbs in the book,  
Branches between the sticks  
Sparks in the bread's baking  
He hears me singing  
Hear where the evil bird falls  
The damn bird thunders  
Takes a knife from near the bread  
Afraid to cut the bread  
Takes a ladle from the pot  
Afraid to touch the broth  
His girl is a little girl  
His man is a little man  
He hears me coming home  
Singing behind in the grove  
Hear where the golden bird falls  
The strong bird thunders  
Puts the axe by the bread’s ear  
Puts the ladle in the pot:  
Come inside, young daughter  
Taste the broth too, young daughter  
You come tired from work  
You come weary from the field.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children,  
You without a father, I without a mother,  
We go to Midsummer at the church,  
You go to father's grave,  
I go to mother's grave:  
Rise up, little mother,  
Rise up, little father,  
Rise to braid my hair,  
Rise to wash my hair,  
Rise to prepare my loaf,  
To write the kerstukоant.  
From mother's grave comes the answer:  
No young girl can rise,  
On the ground where grass has grown,  
On the burial mound's grass,  
On the hawthorn's eyebrows,  
On the bluebells' eyes,  
On the Midsummer lilies at the feet.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Where the home of the sad is  
And the shelter for the poor,  
There is the home of the sad  
And the shelter for the poor,  
Where the wind has blown it down,  
Water has rolled the logs.  
Rain has mossed the walls,  
Fog has covered the doors,  
Daytime is shut outside,  
The moon is before the windows.

label: Improvisatsioonid  ; Murelaulud
translation:
At mother's grave, a dear place,  
At uncle's tomb, a more beautiful spot,  
Here I weep on the meadow expanse,  
Mourning the dear grave,  
Of my beloved mother,  
Of my beloved father,  
Who tenderly nursed me,  
And lovingly raised me,  
Kept me safe through the night,  
Gently rocked me in the cradle,  
Come, mother, comfort me,  
Come, father, help me,  
Help me through the hardships of life,  
Along strong paths,  
Where there is no falling down,  
No rolling off the paths,  
Send me wandering through deserts,  
Send me through the swamps,  
Through the hills and meadows,  
Through thick dark thickets,  
Send me there,  
So that I may live lovingly,  
Grow beautifully,  
Recover strongly,  
Where my foot will not stumble,  
Where my step will not slip.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I will not leave here before  
I lay out the room,  
I take the walls in my arms,  
The wall beams and even the back,  
I lay the ceiling beams on the shoulder,  
I seal the stove in its corner,  
Then I visit the western heating place,  
Walk through the aisle from the entrance,  
I must not turn back from the threshold,  
Do not turn back from the load on the bench,  
Then I make the room’s wind against the weather,  
Life without frost against the cold.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor children,  
Let's go to Jaanipää church.  
Grave cloths in hand,  
Grave cloths on the chest,  
One wipes the other's eyes,  
One strokes the other's face,  
Will you go to your father's grave?  
Shall I go to my mother's grave?  
Rise up, little old lady,  
Rise up, little clumsy one,  
Rise now to start pounding,  
To prepare the Annivakka,  
To fasten the kerstukont.  
Rise to lead the roads,  
To build the paths.  
You must not rise, young daughter,  
You must not rise, young son.  
Already grass on the grave,  
Peaceful hay on the grave.  
Sand is beneath, sand is on top,  
I am in the middle of the sand,  
A cup on my eyebrows,  
Gravel on my eyes,  
Lime presses my chest!  
Rise nonetheless, little old lady,  
I will bring a sand sieve from town,  
Maybe I will sieve the burial soil!  
I will bring limestone from Paide,  
I will hammer thick limestone!

label: Murelaulud
translation:
The big spruce did not die,  
The wide alder fell down.  
It left the branches groaning,  
The leaves mourning.  
The branches groan like a father,  
The leaves mourn like a mother.  
It left the branch’s shade,  
Early to serve the shade.  
The horse to eat beetles,  
The dog to eat worms,  
The bread to the soup bowls.  
The bulls went to the master’s hand,  
The horses went to the German’s side.  
The cows went to the dairy woman’s hand,  
The goats to farm hand Liisu’s hand,  
The sheep to Lelli Leenu’s hand.  
The bulls moaned in the stable,  
The bulls went to plow the field,  
The horses neighed,  
To go in front of the German.  
The cows groaned,  
To the dairy woman’s milking.  
The goats screamed,  
To farm hand Liisu’s care.  
Our sheep bleated,  
To Lolli Leenu’s meadow.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I am never bored  
I am always miserable.  
Many times my mind is sad  
To whom do I complain, to whom do I tell?  
To the sick do I seek comfort  
To whom do I shift the blame?  
To the stones on the church path  
To the tiles on the priest’s window sill  
I cry on the young grass.  
The stone does not speak,  
The tile of the church does not tell,  
The young grass does not weaken,  
The stone does not understand the language of men,  
The tile does not heed the boys’ prayers.  
I complain to the coltsfoot,  
I begin with the meadowsweet —  
I complain to the bluebells:  
“The coltsfoots are yellowing,  
The meadowsweet is wilting,  
The bluebells are decaying  
In the valley of my dear beloved.  
And in the place of comfort  
Where I wipe away my delicate eyes —  
Because I am never bored,  
I am always miserable,  
Many times my mind is sad:  
“I have had no father,  
Nor a mother coming after—  
I am the foal of a horse,  
The child of the meadow stallion.  
The horse nursed me,  
The meadow stallion licked me.”

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Stranger girl, strange girl  
Stranger rooster, strange rooster  
Comes to wake me up  
To cry on the roof  
To gather under the row  
Get up quickly to milk the cow  
Get up quickly to bring the herd  
Crow’s head to fetch the calves  
Others’ herd is far away  
Our herd is in the herd enclosure  
Our young bulls on the pasture.  
Others’ pigs are inside the pen  
Our pigs are inside the stall  
My girl, little old woman  
My rooster, little rooster  
Comes to wake me up  
To gather under the row  
Get up quickly to milk the cow  
Get up quickly to milk the cow  
Get up chicken to bring the herd  
White head to feed the calves  
Others’ herd is in the herd enclosure  
Our herd is far away  
Others’ calves on the pasture  
Our calves on the meadow.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two little Alands.
Let's go to the Midsummer church.
You go to father's grave,
I go to mother's grave,
One to the other grave.
One cries, the other sobs,
One wipes the other's eyes.
Rise up, my mother,
Rise up, my father,
Rise and start weaving,
Prepare the anniversary basket,
Fasten the bonnet.
The daughter cannot rise,
Grass has grown over the soil,
Meadow hay over the grave,
Buttercups on the brow,
Lungworts over the eyes.
May God weave your head,
Prepare the anniversary basket,
Fasten the bonnet.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I cut to the field a lively  
To the haystack a bright  
To the sunshine red  
Brother came home calling  
Calling across the field  
Come home, sister, lowly  
Suitors came to the house  
Five, six vodka mugs  
Seven or eight beer jugs  
Brother cannot come  
Let's go see our mother  
Rise up, mother  
Rise to braid my head  
To prepare the annivakk  
To fasten the kerstukaan  
You cannot rise, young daughter  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Meadow herbs over the grave  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Coltsfoot over the neck  
Cowslips over the eyebrows  
I feed grass to the bull calf  
Meadow herbs to the oxen  
Bluebells to the cows  
Coltsfoot to the chickens  
Cowslips to the roosters.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I was only little,  
A boy knee-high!  
At night I would beat the manor's thighs,  
By day I plowed the lord's land.  
Where I cried – corners wet,  
Where I stood – walls wet,  
There a small pond formed,  
A little fishpond grew.  
If it were up to me,  
If I could change things:  
I would turn the gentlemen into oxen,  
The maidens into headdresses,  
Old ladies into kitchen brooms,  
Old gentlemen into bone rattlers;  
The steward, the bailiff, the storekeeper  
I would put at the very front of hell.  
The old prison guard,  
He is at the very front of hell  
Inside the foremost kettle.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I was only little,  
A boy knee-high!  
At night I would beat the manor's thighs,  
By day I plowed the lord's land.  
Where I cried – corners wet,  
Where I stood – walls wet,  
There a small pond formed,  
A little fishpond grew.  
If it were up to me,  
If I could change things:  
I would turn the gentlemen into oxen,  
The maidens into headdresses,  
Old ladies into kitchen brooms,  
Old gentlemen into bone rattlers;  
The steward, the bailiff, the storekeeper  
I would put at the very front of hell.  
The old prison guard,  
He is at the very front of hell  
Inside the foremost kettle.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Three were for bad weather  
More unsuitable days  
One was a daughter without a mother  
Another a son without a father  
The third a poor widow  
Who weeps, a daughter without a mother  
Weeping for the belt buckle  
Weeping for skirts to wear  
Hands rough and calloused  
Weeping under a wreath on her head  
She weeps, a daughter without a mother  
Who weeps for a son without a father  
Weeping for the edge of the plow  
For the twisted yoke  
For the winter beatings behind  
She weeps for a son without a father  
Who then weeps, the widow  
Weeping for the edge of the bed  
For the damaged household items  
For the staples of the kitchen table  
For under the floor boards  
She weeps, the poor widow  
So it is, so it is, dear brothers  
So it is, the poor widow  
Like a building without a roof  
A thigh without a rib  
A barn without a ridgepole  
All the blizzards come upon her  
All the rains fall on her  
Rain showers roll down  
Where is the home of the sorrowful  
Or the place of the unfortunate  
The refuge of poor children  
The bread table of the widow

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Oh those good Kusna boys  
They march like those Germans  
We speak the same words  
We drink the same drinks  
We sleep in the same nights  
We wear the same coats  
We own the same new kitchens  
We buy horses in large numbers  
One takes another's wife  
Another from the other's farm  
We go to the midsummer church  
I put a rope for the hat  
The reins on the belt  
You go to the grandmother's grave  
I go to the grandfather's grave  
Rise up, little grandpa  
Rise up, little grandma  
Cannot rise, cannot wake up  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Hay over the grave  
Bluebells over the eye  
Buttercups over the eyebrow  
Dewgrass over the neck

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Oh those good Kusna boys  
They march like those Germans  
We speak the same words  
We drink the same drinks  
We sleep in the same nights  
We wear the same coats  
We own the same new kitchens  
We buy horses in large numbers  
One takes another's wife  
Another from the other's farm  
We go to the midsummer church  
I put a rope for the hat  
The reins on the belt  
You go to the grandmother's grave  
I go to the grandfather's grave  
Rise up, little grandpa  
Rise up, little grandma  
Cannot rise, cannot wake up  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Hay over the grave  
Bluebells over the eye  
Buttercups over the eyebrow  
Dewgrass over the neck

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I heard the falling houses  
The children of Munde singing  
For me, it would be sad to sit  
For me, it would be pitiable to walk  
For some, it is sad to sit  
For some, it is pitiable to walk  
To whom do I complain my sorrowful feelings  
On the stone of the church path  
On the lime of the priest's apron  
The stone standing upright does not speak  
The lime is thick, it does not chatter  
The stone does not understand a maiden's language  
The priest does not understand the maiden's prayers.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I, a poor child without a father  
I, a poor child without a mother  
Without a loom, a bad creature  
I went to church on Sunday  
I went to grandmother's grave  
I went to Toadi's grave  
Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up, little Toadi  
Rise up to weave a cap  
To make an annivakka (small traditional bag)  
To fasten the coffin lid  
Grandmother heard and replied  
Cannot rise, cannot wake  
The earth is beneath, the earth is above  
I am in the middle of the earth  
God, may you weave a cap  
Make an annivakka  
Fasten the coffin lid  
Then I came home crying  
When I lay down to sleep in the evening  
I rose up without calling out  
Washed my hands without being told  
Opened my eyes without force  
Put a skirt on  
Rolled a belt onto my waist  
Dipped the smoking time  
Twirled the twilight  
Wove belts at dawn.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Always I am miserable!  
Every day I am bored,  
So let me say it again  
Let me sing it back again.  
Who finds me boring,  
Or who finds me miserable?  
To whom do I complain sad moods,  
To whom do I lower my sickness?  
To whom am I like a stone;  
A stone on the church path,  
On the rope of the priest outside.  
A stone does not speak loudly,  
A harelip does not chatter.  
A stone does not understand a maiden's language,  
A harelip does not hear a maiden's prayer.  
I rushed on foot,  
I dash through the swamp:  
Better that this stone cracks,  
The harelip bursts loudly  
Before I, poor one,  
Feed the chicken raised in the room,  
The father’s found little bird,  
The mother’s turtledove.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
We two orphans,  
went to the Midsummer church.  
I will stay by father's grave,  
you will stay by mother's grave.  

At father's grave I ask,  
at mother's grave I ask:  
who steps on the grave  
who stomps on the toe.  

Here we two orphans,  
here we younger daughters.  
Why did you leave us behind,  
not to take foreign bread,  
not to taste foreign food.  

Rise up, uncle,  
wake up, mother,  
take us with you also in the coffin,  
take us to the grave to sleep,  
there it is sweet to sleep,  
here it is hard to travel.  

At father's grave they answer,  
at mother's grave they answer:  
you may not take young daughters,  
you may not take nor help them.  

The grass has grown over the soil,  
dust has been laid over the eyes.  

Go to the fields to sing,  
go to the forest to play,  
that wipes your tears from your cheeks,  
that erases from you...

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I alone am a poor child  
For whom I plant boredom  
For whom I sigh sorrowfully.  
For whom I complain with a sad spirit  
For whom, other than the stalk  
On the church path  
On the rope, on the paper edge  
The stalk's husk does not speak  
In the room it does not tell tales  
The stalk is not a maiden with a braid  
In the room, a girl does not pray.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
My sister cleaned my bones,
Tied them in a silk scarf,
But took me to the stump in the forest.
There I was made a handle,
Heated to be a pot hook.

label: Murelaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Three are under the dawn without weather
The most forlorn after the weather.
One poor daughter without a mother
Another poor son without a father
The third is a woman without a husband!
So is a woman without a husband
When the roof is without cover
The barn is dear, unsecured
The cattle shed on the slope
The hay poles hanging loosely!
Why does the woman without a husband cry?
All winds come upon her
All rains fall on her back
Rain clouds roll over me.
Why does the daughter without a mother cry?
When she goes to her husband.
The groom complains once:
“Adorn the girl, don’t you manage
To be ready for the wedding?
Adorn the wife of our men
Prepare before the elder’s house
On the way to God’s house.
Little groom, little boy
Life, little brother
You were given time to grow up
Now give time to adorn,
How long shall I adorn motherless
Older girls without a mother
I have no one to adorn me!
I will dress myself
I will dress my own Kerstu.
God has combed my head
Made a floor for my bed.
The Creator has locked the door.
Why does the son without a father cry
When he goes out to plough the fields
Or goes walking in the wilderness?
When he goes out to plough the fields?
There are no arable land drivers
No teams to harness horses
No one to pull the hay wagon
No one to thresh before winter,
No one to give seed in spring
No one to carry the oats in autumn,
This is why the son without a father cries.
When he comes to the ploughing village
The wagon in the yard is bare
There are no working horses in front
No ploughs put on the shoulders
He himself is worn out to the end
From the neck of a chicken, muddy
His head is wet to the top
Then he stands before the forge
His heart stirred inside
The knife held in his fist
The spear straight in his hand.
Then I drive the whip over the cattle
I hit them with a leafy willow branch
I cut them with rowan branches,
This is why the son without a father cries.
Oh, that dear son of mine
Sleeps without a belt in bed
Between dear cloths
Soft quilts laid on top.
Then he rises in the morning
Right away there is butter and bread for him
And given a portion of cream
They laid out roast duck.
“Eat now, son, drink now, son
Then dress yourself for the adornment
Into Kalev's shirts
Into straight boots
Into a white copper belt
Into tall leather cuffs.
Then go, son, towards the straw,
Buy oil for the doors
Wax the doors
Burn brandy for the drunkards
So that they don’t scream when you shut them
Nor wail when you open them
When you come home from the straw
Knock on the door to check
Read words from the manure
Swear at the gate
Fight at the back wall.
Then you must not be in the room
Don’t stir the fire in front of the stove
Don’t tie a belt at the bed’s end
Don’t put an apron on the dirty floor.
Then the room will become smoky, the chamber pops.
The whole room will be full of soot
The chamber turns into bad luck
The girl then flies to the alder grove
The man then jumps into the birch grove.
The son runs after.
“Be a young bride then
Be a young little maid
I bring home a young woman
A slender daughter-in-law
She wears a lace garland on her head
Her shoulders tied with silk strings
Her bun with golden ribbons
Her breasts crossed with a Riga lace
Her hips with a short skirt
Her belly wears a high apron
Her feet in new ankle boots
Scarves in silk stockings.
She knows how to go to the fire
Milking cows without a lintel
Straining milk by the moonlight.”

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Sisters Ellakesed  
Brothers Vellakesed  
Let's go to Jaanipää church  
With mourning scarves on our heads  
With a mourning letter in our gloves  
Let's go to grandmother's grave  
Let's go to uncle's grave:  
Rise up, little grandmother!  
Rise up to comb my hair  
To make a gift box  
To fasten the kerchief  
Little grandmother spoke from the ground  
Quietly spoke to her daughter:  
Grass has grown on the soil  
On the grave of the sensible one  
Bluebells on the eyes  
Buttercups on the eyebrows  
Marigolds at the feet:  
May God comb your hair  
Make a gift box  
Fasten the kerchief.  
Rise up, little uncle  
Rise up to show me the way  
To guide the young son:  
Little uncle spoke from the ground  
Quietly spoke to his son:  
You cannot rise, little son  
The grave has grown with wisdom  
With boulders on the chest.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Where is it, where is it, dear brothers  
Where is the home of the sorrowful  
Where is the place of the poor  
The refuge of the poor children  
The mourning place of widows?  
There is, there is, gentle brothers  
Where the wind has made a room  
Water has rolled the logs  
Rain has mossed the walls  
Fog has braved the roof  
The moon, however, looks like a chimney sweep  
The sun before the window’s porch  
Stars scattered as dust  
There is the home of the sorrowful  
There is the place of the poor  
The refuge of the poor children  
The mourning place of widows.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our grass is poor  
Let's go to the Jaanipää church  
Put the grass cloth in a bundle  
Put the grass roses in the cloth  
Let's go to grandmother's grave  
Let's go to grandfather's grave  
"Rise up, little grandmother  
Rise up, little grandfather  
Rise to comb my hair  
To prepare Annivakka  
To lift the Christmas wreath."  
"You may not rise, young daughter  
Nor may rise, young son  
The grass has grown over the soil  
Over Aruein's grave  
Over the cowslip's brow  
Over the bluebell's eyes."  
"I will bring black herbs from Mustla  
I will plow the grass over the soil  
Over Aruein's grave  
Over the cowslip's brow  
Over the St. John's wort's foot."

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud abielust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Maiden, young one,  
You go for the home’s treasure,  
For the silver yard,  
For the copper gate.  
You had victories won bread,  
Given the brood of ducklings.  
I go for the home’s trouble,  
For the wicked yard,  
For the angry gate.  
My home is sad, my home is bad,  
My home has angry hardships,  
Has bad words given.  
This trouble I wind into a ball,  
I lay it on a wooden splinter,  
I tie it into shirt spikes,  
I press it between my belt.  
When I go to fetch water from the well,  
I throw this ball into the water,  
On the wooden splinter loan.  
The water carries knowledge to my brother,  
The water carries knowledge to my sister’s husband,  
The feathers carry news to relatives,  
The straw carries news to the sister.  
To whom do I complain? To whom do I tell?  
Whom do I hurt?  
On the stone by the church road,  
On the limestone over the planks!  
The stone was stiff, it did not speak,  
The limestone was thick, it did not tell.  
The stone does not understand the maiden’s refusal,  
The limestone does not heed the maiden’s plea.
