label: Murelaulud  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:
A manless mare went by  
An unbridled horse  
Strike the hoof and hold the mare  
Keep the whip, do not worry  
Wear care with the heel of the shoe

label: Murelaulud  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:
A manless mare went by  
An unbridled horse  
Strike the hoof and hold the mare  
Keep the whip, do not worry  
Wear care with the heel of the shoe

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Understand, understand, young men,
Guess, eager boys.
Know again, wise women,
Linen aprons, little birds,
Heads with smooth brows.
How many apple trees in the village,
How many branches on the apple tree,
How many branches with blossoms,
How many apples on the blossom,
Understand, understand why you don’t understand.
There were three apple trees in the village,
Three branches on the apple tree,
One was a plum of the apple blossom,
Three were a plum of the apple branch.
From the north rose great winds,
Rolling an apple into the sea.
What came out of the sea?
A little blue sparrow,
Blue sparrow, with a mottled neck,
With a low mottled neck.
It came to the shore to explain.
Understand, understand, young men,
Guess, eager boys.
Know again, wise women.
How many blossoms in the courtyard,
How many nests in the linden tree,
How many flowers on the sand hill?
From the nest came a golden flock,
Then the boys began to speak:
Give me a golden button,
I’ll go play on the hill,
And roll apples on the table,
I went to flip the sieve,
Roll a silver apple.
The sieve fell in the churchyard,
Fell on my father’s grave,
It opened the grave doors,
Grave doors, tomb lids,
Tomb lids, sandy drifts.
Look inside, mother:
What did I find father doing?
What did I find mother doing?
I found father counting money,
Who do you count money for, dear father?
“For no one else but for my daughter,
Since you became a poor orphan early,
To quiet the parish early,
To rock the parish cradle early,
You were left to the care of strangers,
You were left to nurse at the breast,
You were left to soothe the widow.
Widow’s milk soothes the alder,
Breast milk is bitter.”
What did I find mother doing?
I found her weaving cloth.
Who do you weave cloth for, mother?
For no one else but for my daughter,
Since you became a poor orphan early,
To quiet the parish early,
To rock the parish cradle.
You were left to the care of strangers.
What did I find brother doing?
I found him fastening the coffin.
What did I find sister doing?
Sister was knitting stockings,
Knitting stockings of seven threads,
So I would have an axe in my hand,
To chop the broken cart shaft,
Cart shafts, cart poles,
To take father’s grey spruce,
Mother’s calming jester,
The gilded hat,
Sister’s blue scrubbing brush,
Brother’s worn coat.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Oh, we two poor souls  
Let's go to the Midsummer church  
Let's go to grandmother's grave  
Let's go to grandfather's grave  
Rise up, wise grandfather  
Rise up, wise grandmother  
Rise to weave my head  
To prepare a basket  
To fasten a kerchief  
Cannot rise, no use  
The grass has grown over the soil  
Hay of peace over the grave  
Bluebells over the eyes  
Buttercups over the eyebrows  
Horseshoe vetch at the feet.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I am always bored,  
I am always wretched,  
Many times sorrowful.  
To whom do I complain, to whom do I grumble?  
To whom do I lament my pain?  
To whom do I cast my faults?  
I cry to the young grass,  
I complain to the coltsfoot flowers.  
Alan  
The coltsfoot flowers turn yellow,  
The meadowsweet withers.  
In whose shadow shall I lie my children?  
In the shadow of a stone, in the shadow of a stump,  
In the shadow of a tree, in the shadow of the ground,  
Into the embracing arms of the alder,  
Into the white cover of the birch,  
Into the gray bedding of the aspen,  
To the long twig of the willow.  
The birch did not take me into its cover.  
The aspen did not take me into its bedding,  
The meadowsweet did not accept me under its cloak.  
The willow did not take me to its long twig.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Oh my old grandmother,  
And my wise old grandfather  
Who went to sleep young.  
To rest in the red one!  
You left the children to laugh with others,  
To feed a handful of dogs,  
To bark at the dogs,  
To whip the little dogs.  
Indeed, the sisters barked at me  
And the brothers whipped me,  
Grandmothers twirled a stick.  
If only the old grandmother were alive!  
And then the old grandfather on the farm!  
Neither Thomas should hit me,  
Hans should not poke roughly,  
Peeter should not bang my head,  
Jaan should not stomp on my foot.  
The old grandmother is dead  
And the old grandfather is dead.  
Now the dog boy bites me,  
The servant always pecks,  
Washes the millet harvester.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two poor ones,  
Let's go to the Midsummer church.  
Let's go to the grandmother's grave,  
Let's go to Toadi's grave.  
Rise up, little grandmother!  
Rise to comb my hair,  
To prepare the basket,  
To fasten the wreath.  
The young daughter cannot rise,  
Cannot rise, cannot wake up.  
Buttercups have grown on the eyebrows,  
Bluebells on the eyes.  
May God comb your hair,  
Prepare the basket,  
Fasten the wreath!

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our two orphans  
Let's go to the church on Midsummer's Day  
Black scarves on our heads.  
You go to mother's grave  
I go to father's grave;  
Rise up, dear father  
Rise up, dear mother;  
Rise up to comb my hair  
Rise up to count my money,  
To prepare grains of barley  
To fasten the coffin lid.  
The young daughter may not rise,  
Grass has grown over the soil  
Peaceful hay over the grave,  
Buttercups over the brow,  
Bluebells over the eye,  
Horseshoes over the toes.

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Wait wait death, no no death  
I want to bark at death  
I want to nod to death  
It took my dear old woman.  
It killed my wise old man.  
I was left an early poor child  
Left to be cared for by my uncles.  
Left to be looked after by my aunts.  
The uncle could not take care of me  
The aunts could not fully support me.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Others left on Monday  
I will go on Tuesday.  
Oh, then I had no troubles,  
No troubles or mistakes.  
I put the plow here, mended the carts,  
I knocked on the stork’s nest,  
Then I went outside the manor.  
There others in the middle of the morning  
Called one, called two  
And began to drive three;  
Here came the smith from the manor side,  
From the crooked stone leg side,  
From the old master's estate side.  
I go to the smith's workshop:  
Dear smith, dear smith!  
Measure me a little piece,  
Piece.  
The smith took a look,  
Measured me a big piece,  
A big piece, a wide piece,  
A big piece of dry wood,  
A wide piece of green wood,  
With moss on one side,  
With stones and rubble.  
I plow every day,  
During the autumn feeding break,  
During the long spring day;  
Also  
I bent the oxen’s horns.  
The smith shouts: “Come on, Jüri,  
Don’t you know that a poor young lad  
Has a piece of bread in his pouch,  
Two slices in the box?”  
Here came the smith from the manor side,  
From the crooked stone leg side,  
From the old master’s estate side;  
The bundle was sticks in the stable,  
A bunch was of red willow twigs;  
I looked along the eyes,  
Along the eyes, wide ears:  
Who else but me.  
I go to the smith's workshop:  
Dear smith, dear smith!  
Don’t be too harsh,  
Don’t seem too hot-tempered, -  
I became a poor child early,  
Early left to a stranger’s care,  
Early remained a stranger’s property:  
Now hears about coming home,  
Outside  
Behind the covering singing -  
Then: - “Up Eltsa, run Kaie,  
Turn the turn at the gates,  
Close the doors with rings:  
Hear, where the evil bird falls,  
The wicked bird flaps!  
Hear, here comes a guest to eat,  
A guest to eat, a guest to drink,  
To the place on the hill.”  
My girl was a little old woman,  
My house was a little hut, -  
She heard about coming home,  
Outside  
Behind the covering singing:  
“Hear, where the golden bird falls,  
The wicked bird flaps!  
He comes tired from work,  
Comes from the fields,  
Oh! How working tires us,  
The fields exhaust us.”

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Murelaulud
translation:

I will go on Tuesday.

She heard about coming home,

Comes from the fields,  
Oh! How working tires us,

label: Murelaulud
translation:
When I begin to leave  
A sorrow begins to come  
Water rolls from the eyes  
Eyebrows let waves flow  
Water rolls from the cheekbone  
From the cheekbone to the chest  
From the chest to the knees  
From the knees to the toes  
From there a little drop dripped  
It grew into a small fish pond  
From there the brother's cattle drank  
The brother's cattle and the sister's geese  
From there the roof birds drank  
From my poor tears

label: Murelaulud
translation:
Our Two Poor Children  
Let's go to the Midsummer Day church  
Let's go to grandmother's grave;  
Let's go to grandfather's grave.  
Rise up, dear grandmother,  
Rise up, dear grandfather,  
Rise to comb my hair,  
To prepare Annivakka,  
To fasten the coffin lid.  

You must not rise, young daughter,  
You must not rise, do not awaken.  
Grass has grown over the soil,  
Field herbs upon the grave,  
Bluebells on the eyes,  
Coltsfoot on the eyebrows,  
Marsh marigold at the feet,  
Wild berries on the toes.  

You must not rise, young daughter,  
You must not rise, do not awaken.  
May God comb your hair,  
May He prepare Annivakka.  
Still rise, dear grandmother,  
I will feed the grass to the calves,  
Field herbs to the oxen,  
Bluebells to the cows.  

Oh, our two poor children,  
Let's go to the Midsummer Day church.  
I am going to grandmother's grave,  
You are going to grandfather's grave.  

Rise up, dear grandfather,  
Rise up, dear grandmother,  
Rise to comb my hair,  
To prepare Annivakka,  
To fasten the coffin lid.  

But grandmother understood and grandfather answered:  
Grass has grown over the soil,  
Field herbs upon the grave,  
Coltsfoot on the eyebrows,  
Bluebells on the eyes,  
Marsh marigolds at the feet.  

Still rise, dear grandfather,  
Still rise, dear grandmother.  
I will feed the grass to the calves,  
Field herbs to the oxen,  
Coltsfoot to the roosters,  
Bluebells to the pigs,  
Marsh marigold to the calves.

label: Töölaulud  ; Murelaulud
translation:
The shepherd had a hard life  
Water was under and over  
Water between the toes  
The elbow was underwater by the side  
The elbow above water by the side  
Where is the dry home  
Water had churned white  
Rain had mossed the walls  
Fog had struck the doors closed

label: Töölaulud  ; Murelaulud
translation:

The elbow above water by the side

label: Murelaulud
translation:
I am the grandmother's bad child  
Grandmother made me a bad shirt  
From the worst fabric  
It was cut from the very end  
I stayed behind like a hare  
Like a chick, I kept crying  
Calling out for my mother  
When they took my last brother away  
The ox stayed outside bellowing  
The stallion stayed dancing in the stable  
The horse stayed banging the iron  
They had their fields to mow  
And oats to carry in front of them

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:
Play the instrument, little Peebu,  
Blow the horn, Saxon.  
I am coming to dance the oak,  
Young spruces to sway.  
I dance, the fence rumbles,  
The six-sided room echoes,  
The stone floor rattles,  
Feet want to dance,  
The room wants to be swept anew;  
The floor to be struck through,  
The edges of the walls to be stepped on.  
Dance, dance, Mari from Lutsu.  
Do your carts go well,  
Are your feet filled with joy?  
Is the envelope round,  
Is the cloth half folded,  
Or are the little ones under the wing,  
The scoundrels between the belt?  
Slowly, slowly, cleverly, cleverly,  
Slowly dance, girls,  
Dazzle with beauty.  
So the apron strings don’t snap  
Nor the ribbons on the skirts fly loose.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
I don’t have to go away
Don’t curse the people
From better places
I leave this place here
Here I forget this place
Let better ones come to this place
In place, more honest ones
To the place, more worthy ones
I will come back tonight
Maybe I will come to see tomorrow
If there are better ones in this place
In place, more honest ones
Still worse ones in this place
In place, more respected ones
Next is where I left off
The bench was there where I lingered
The stove needs chopping firewood
The room needs sweeping
The bucket needs carrying water
The cauldron needs tending the fire
The twig breaks on the handle
The room remains empty and dark
The barn grew sparse of clothes
The chamber faded with storms
Surely she thought of some men
Surely she wanted some boys
There is no money to count
Afraid to spend the purse
The money pouch falling out
I do not lack cunning
I ended the flax-spinning
The shuttles are sewn full
The crows are taken away
The kerchief is tied on
The village women laughed
The village girls mocked
They said I sleep in the barn
Dozing near the boys
They themselves slept in the barn
Dozing near the boys
No beautiful woman laughed at me
The lazy woman teased me
The girl mocked badly
They said I would remain without a husband
Live without a partner
I got a husband like honeycomb
Another like the island’s German
I took him as a young master
My groom was from Peipus
A dear man from Alutaguse
He drinks from the lake shore
Drowns over the lake
Dances behind the lake
He rode along the Finnish bridge
The Finnish bridge, a cracking road
The base board, a creaking road
The hat, a delicate road
The village maidens watched
If only that man were mine
If only he were my long-time partner
Then he rides along the road
Rides along the paths
Horse under him like the stove
Head up like the day
Hat like a church bell
Belt like the city’s flag belt
Then he passed, turned his head
Lifted his fine hat
With God, young maiden

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Rise up, young people  
Mother-in-law has long been heating the stoves  
The smoke had gone out through the big door  
The soot had gone through the vent hole  
The steam had gone out the window  
Young girl, young maiden  
If you want to be a mistress  
Then you must sleep a little  
Under the threshold of the barn door  
Put your head on the threshold  
When you hear the rooster crowing  
And the hen clucking to the rooster  
Then jump up without hesitation  
Leap up without shouting  
Go limping through the yard  
Then run into the pen  
See what you find there  
You will find seven piglets there  
Put those piglets in your apron  
Then take them to show your father-in-law  
To show your father-in-law, to show the mother-in-law  
To show your young man  
Then you will pay in your father-in-law’s eyes  
In your father-in-law’s eyes, in the mother-in-law’s eyes  
In your young man’s eyes.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:
Be healthy, little maiden  
Be healthy growing up  
Keeping your honor  
Now you can go for a ride on the saw.  
Jumping for the men  
Would have gone from the forest to the husband  
From the tree top to the boys  
From the stump to the companions.  
The trees would have been bridesmaids  
The white birches as companions  
A piece of feed to your feeding table  
Heather to your hoof rack  
A garden plot for a bench.  
Now you have been asked as a bride.  
Now the little saw has ridden  
Whip lashes, pen lashes  
Rode to the father-in-law’s yard  
Rode to the mother-in-law’s yard  
Come mother-in-law, bring from the fur coat  
Come maybe to watch  
Take that anger away  
Take that grudge away  
Bring it as a ghost into the room  
Into the mother-in-law’s eye, mother-in-law’s ear  
Into the corner eye, into the dog’s ear  
A crooked foot before the mother-in-law  
Move now, move, little flame  
Give room, little stove  
Step back, back wall  
Now comes the ankle into the room  
A bundle in the heart,  
When I get stormy and angry  
I take a ladle in front of the dog  
A bowl of broth into the crook  
I give the ladle to the old woman’s hand  
A bowl of broth to the old woman’s lap.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Oh my dear little old woman,  
Little honey grandmother,  
Buy new dogs for the yard,  
Loud dogs for the gate,  
Let the men pass by  
And those who court from afar.  
I am a very young girl,  
I don’t wear a shawl, old woman,  
My hips don’t bear a bustle skirt,  
My belly doesn’t carry a high apron,  
On my legs are silk stockings,  
New anklets on my feet,  
My fingers young ring fingers.  
The suitor hears, answers back:  
Young maiden, young girl,  
She will surely grow to be a working woman,  
Straighten up into a silk bed,  
Swell between the pillows—

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud abielust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
You courted a gentle sister,  
You courted a polite one,  
You courted a fair one,  
The most beautiful in the world.  
Later you got the woman  
Who had elbow a bit bent,  
Who had crooked teeth  
And dull eyes.  
She does not know how to walk,  
Does not sleep without spark,  
Does not cook soup with bitterness.  
Taken away from me until now:  
I know a dull girl,  
I see that snub-nosed girl,  
A dull girl with long hair,  
A lazy girl with wide braids,  
A loose belt on her waist.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud abielust  ; Laulud noorrahva elust

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
If the daughter were taken from the house,  
taken away like thunder from the sky,  
just like a piglet from the field.  
Then the corners would remain weeping,  
the cattle barns would be bawling,  
the seats would be longing,  
Corners weep for the young maiden,  
cattle barns for the beloved,  
seats for the fair one.  
The milking cows weep,  
the bulls bellow,  
the father’s horses neigh.  
They weep for good fodder,  
for having oats before them,  
for being given meadow hay.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Shut up, shut up the dog from barking,  
the Lake-krants from growling,  
the Kirjut-krants from tearing,  
the barking to the field with rook crows,  
the piglets to the field. -

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Eat dips, drink dips  
Don’t hide dips in your pocket  
I am careful in seeking  
Clever in feeling pockets  
I feel under your fur coat  
I look for a new coat inside.  
Dress up, dress up, young girl,  
Dress quickly, hasten quickly  
Hasten to the hundred ride  
You will get to dance down  
Spokes clattering  
The sled is ready, the groom is ready  
Dress quickly, young girl  
The horse is already shod with iron  
The bridles’ heads are braided  
The reins’ breasts are already braided  
The reins’ necks are already braided  
The reins’ ears are already jingling  
The reins’ eyes are already silky  
The reins’ eyebrows are golden.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Murelaulud
translation:
My young maiden,
If you want to go to the farm,
To the farm to the peasant,
Into the household as a daughter-in-law.
You go to bed in the evenings,
You lay your care on the loft,
Your worries on the black door.
You get up in the morning,
You take your care from the loft,
Your worries from the black door.
Then you go to the ox stalls,
Then you go to the farm barns:
You find a calf from the cow,
A lamb from the sheep,
A kid from the goat.
You put them down on the floor with a cloth:
Then the mother-in-law speaks kindly,
The sister-in-law laughs pleasantly,
The grandmother chats sufficiently.
The daughter-in-law begins to worry:
What should I put in the pot?
What should I boil in the kettle?
Then the mother-in-law answers with understanding:
"What you brought from your father's house,
What you brought from your mother's house,
Put that in the pot,
Boil that in the kettle."
- "I brought a kerchief from my father's house,
I brought beads from my father's house,
I brought a brooch from my brother's house" -
- "The kerchief won't cook like porridge,
The beads won't cook like peas,
The brooch won't melt like salt,
Take the kerchief to town to be cooked,
Take the beads to town to be boiled with peas,
Take the brooch to town to be salted."

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Where did you take the hedgehog from your eye
The Adrakonn made parts
Into our great and noble line,
Into the wealthy people.
If you were a king's daughter
Then you also married a king's son
If you were a Riga burgher’s child
Then you married a Riga burgher’s son as well.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
What kind of vagabond are you looking for us
You came to us to fill up,
Are you looking for horse sausage
Or do you want stallion sausage.
Behind the barn, you have stallion sausages
Behind the hill, ween sausages.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Where did you know to come to us from?  
Did you know over the hills,  
Did you know over the mountains?  
From our great swamp,  
From the land of the people of Rahakaid,  
From the old farm of the taalders,  
From the old family of the penings.  
- Where did we know to come to you,  
We knew over the hills,  
We knew over the mountains,  
We heard the rustle of legs,  
The rustling of skins,  
At the smell of sourness.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
What are you wandering around looking for from us,  
You came to us hungry.  
Are you looking for horse sausage  
Or do you want stallion sausage?  
We are out of horse sausages,  
We have stallion sausages in the street.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Dear little sister,  
May I come into the rooms,  
Down from under the eaves?  
Or are the springs in the room,  
The linen sliding under the floor?  
Where have you stayed so long?  
Was the horse tired,  
Or was the sled broken,  
Or was the driver foolish,  
Or was the groom blind?  
Dear little sister,  
The horse was not tired,  
Nor was the sled broken,  
There was no foolish driver,  
Nor a blind groom.  
The whip touched the rowan tree,  
The sledge got stuck in the island roots.  
I pulled the whip from the rowan tree,  
From the island roots of the sledge.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Where did you get pig’s eyes from,  
Orikas road parts?  
..................  
I heard the clatter of jugs,  
The rustling of leather horns,  
The donkey’s thirst for blood...

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Little boy, little lad,  
How did you know to come to us,  
To us, the great family,  
Dear to wealth, dear to riches?  
Because I knew to come to you,  
To you, the great family,  
Dear to wealth, dear to riches:  
I heard the rustling of cups,  
The tapping of leather horns,  
I heard cups coughing,  
Leather horns snapping.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Is it allowed to come inside the house,
to go under the eaves?
It is not allowed to come inside the house,
under our eaves, from the towel rack,
the roof fears love.
Can you go to the street,
wolves eat from the street,
or can you go to the meadow,
thieves take from the meadow.
Is it allowed to come inside the house?
Maybe you have a "tukiving" in the room,
a "leelõukal" shingle,
or maybe you have a hole under the door,
where I can water the potato apron?
Surely it is allowed to come inside the house:
we do not have a hole under the door,
we have cleared rooms,
where you can put gold coins,
you stretch the branch of the sow.
We have plenty of space in the attic,
to store the barn's harvest.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
...Katen axe in the coffin,
under the lid, a kaleve axe.
When did they make these axes,
how long ago did the farm give them?
When did the little axe get made,
when did the little one weave the cloth?
The little axe struck the willow grove,
the little cloth struck the bucket’s handle,
by the village boys nearby...
Let a woman come against a woman,
another woman against another!
I throw the rake into the willow grove,
I bear the straw into the valley. -

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Eat, fall, drink, fall,  
Do not fall, put it in your pocket.  
I am here to search,  
to skillfully test the trail.  
I search for a new pocket,  
I dig into the pocket of the coat.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Oh you piglets’ bread,  
The family’s laundry cupboard,  
Father, mother’s handkerchief,  
Begin now with the gift basket,  
Pull up the praise wreath,  
That you praised last year  
Under our village swing,  
At our parish’s outhouse:  
"If only I had a hundred cow-dung cakes,  
If only I had ten bushels!"  
Women, companions,  
Then let me ask patiently.  
Will the father-in-law give a bull?  
Will the mother-in-law give a cow?  
Will the household give a bushel?  
A white-headed calf?  
Then the father-in-law will get a shirt,  
Then the mother-in-law will get a shirt,  
The household will sow the bushel’s sowing ribbons,  
The calf its close-fitting straps.  
Gently dance the girl  
Roll with the melody,  
Spin with order,  
So that the apron strings don’t loosen,  
Nor the hat ribbons move.  
Perhaps children under the wing,  
Scoundrels between the belts,  
Stepchildren also joining in.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Speaking again about the new ones,  
Soon the old will follow,  
Ask, ask around,  
Enjoy, enjoy in the corner,  
Old women bark  
That there is a little mischief,  
Scanty gifts,  
What was done, what was there,  
What kind of patchwork was put together,  
There were servants' slaves,  
Then the sides were folded.  
In winter there was a maiden of women,  
Then the knots stood for sobriety.  
What was done, what was there,  
What kind of patchwork was put together,  
The time of smoke made stockings,  
At twilight made mischief.  
Worked the work of girls,  
Endured hardship like enemies,  
Swept the end of the road,  
Turned water from the hand,  
Others swung glove shafts,  
But she swung an axe handle,  
Others had iron rods as rulers,  
But she rattled an iron beam.  
Others had beautiful nights,  
She had huge herds,  
Others had better ribbons,  
She had better boards,  
Worked and endured hardship,  
Waited to make a room,  
Waited for help to bring,  
To make a room with the lads,  
The barn was given to the little one,  
The herd was turned to the pasture,  
I was given a hearth broom,  
Offered a pot cake.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Speaking again about the new ones,  
Soon the old will follow,

Offered a pot cake.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
How it felt to come to us,  
to our big family,  
to a golden penny in the household,  
to the farm of old Taalder.  
So I felt coming to you,  
to your big family,  
to a golden penny in the household,  
to the farm of old Taalder,  
so I felt coming to you:  
when I heard the rattling of the cups  
and the jingling of the leather pouches.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Lingering songs, little birds,  
What helps you to stay safe,  
To stand against the edge?  
Give us the maiden’s hands,  
Send us back home with her.  
Did we give the bride a price  
And did we put a pledge on the bride:  
Gave a price for the gray coat,  
Put a pledge for the leather coat,  
Here the curls will be worn out.  
When can we go away,  
Go away, stay away,  
Where the wind has made a room,  
Water has rolled the logs,  
Fog has blocked the doors,  
Rain has mossed the walls,  
The sun is in front of the window,  
The moon was above the roof?  
When can we go away,  
Where the spruces grow yellow,  
Birches green and flourishing,  
The straight alders turn blue.  
(The spruces cast golden light at the top.)

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Be diligent in the morning,
Early before dawn.
Go around the cattle market,
Walk around the sheep market.
What do you find at the cattle market?
You find a white-headed calf.
What do you find at the sheep market?
You find lambs at the market.
What do you find at the pig market?
You find seven piglets at the market.
Take those piglets into your apron,
Take them to show your father-in-law,
To show your father-in-law, to show your mother-in-law.
Then you will please the mother-in-law,
Please the mother-in-law, please the father-in-law,
Please your young husband.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Hello links, hello falls,  
hello new doorposts,  
hello beam-like branches,  
hello little twig,  
hello thigh-dragging sedge,  
hello stove, give your hand,  
hello room, know me!  
May I come into the room,  
cause stirring at the threshold,  
tie the foal’s knots,  
make the stallion dance in the stable,  
perhaps you have springs here,  
linen cloths on the floor!  
Perhaps I water the cupboard’s corners,  
disturb the barn’s clothes,  
smooth ones of the linen folk!  
So let me say again,  
singing back again:  
If you have springs here,  
linen cloths on the floor,  
perhaps I water the cupboard’s corners,  
smooth ones of the linen folk,  
turn around the mill-wheel,  
rolling through the ears (of grain).

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
How dare you come to us
We have bleating shepherd boys
Bluer pig boys.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Going, going men  
Packing, packing boys  
The beer barrel is empty  
The vodka anchor is loose  
The sausage chamber key is lost  
The cat devil ate the fish  
The bottom of the butter tub is bare.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Son-in-law Rühi, mother-in-law Rühi  
Take the pot off the fire  
From the broth spoon  
Bring the drum to the stove  
A rag on the floor  
Crooked claw to the chamber.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Carry, carry, I whistle  
Come, come, I curse  
But the old woman does not cry  
Nor does the old man cry  
The pig cries with wet eyes  
The old fellow Kulti's eyebrows are wet  
The pig cries a shit-thrower  
A slacker who lets things slide.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
I must not go  
I must not, my dear, disappear  
Do not swear by the people's year  
By better days  
I leave this place to you  
I give my seat to others  
I push the door to another  
Let me come tomorrow to try  
Let me come to see  
The patch is where I left  
The chair is where I lifted  
The bench is where I held  
The room to sweep with brooms  
The stove to start with sticks  
The chip cries like a whisker.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Rise up, young people  
The firewood has long been burning in the stoves  
The smoke has gone out the big door  
The steam has gone out the vent  
The vapor has gone out the window  
Jump up without shouting  
Spring up without yelling  
Then go jumping across the yard  
Then running across the pen  
See what you find there ahead  
There you find seven piglets  
Put those piglets in your apron  
Take them to show your father-in-law  
To show your father-in-law, to show your mother-in-law  
To show your own young man.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Oh my gentle old woman  
Dear golden auntie  
Why did you sell the little daughter  
Bartered the home to Canada  
Was it anger from the drunken man  
Or was it the right beer drunkard  
You also traded the cloth merchant  
You also made the tailor drunk  
You ruined the sleeve garments  
Before you sold the little daughter  
Bartered the home to Canada  
For that bad money  
Half a penny, a kopek  
Oh, my gentle old woman  
When you go to the well to fetch water  
The well cover falls on your neck  
When you go to the pine forest to bring wood  
The wood pile falls on your chest  
If your daughter were home  
She would take everything from the neck  
From the pile on the chest  
Would save your life from death  
Would strengthen your weak body  
Now you gave her to a bad man  
Sent her to announce evil  
To cause bad things to happen  
Leena, gentle girl  
It's no longer worth going ten times  
When there's only a single poison  
When you go to plow the field  
The midwife goes to harrow  
The poison worm grows in the ground  
Offers bad words  
Instead, she swears an oath.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Little eides, little darlings  
Where does that poor one get the carpet  
Amless little base cloth  
The sheep disappeared from the barn  
Another disappeared from the herd  
The third became a wolf’s share  
Gloves aren’t made of stone  
Socks don’t warm moss  
Linen doesn’t move to the field  
A plow doesn’t dig in the banks  
What happened to the daughter’s work, too  
The enemy child got hardship too  
Couldn’t get a piece from the work either  
Couldn’t get the carpet from the hardship either  
Linen isn’t from too much running either  
A pillow isn’t from too much going either  
Brother got oxen, brother got horses  
The stable full of stallions  
The barn full of sheep pens  
The stack got the household tools  
The granary full of seed  
Five fruits are there inside  
Six are standing in a heap  
Barns are heaped with hay  
Haystacks in five corners  
Wood piles along the fence  
Brother got the gilded badge  
The bright courtyard  
Brother got large, long fields  
Brother got wide, large meadows  
Brother got five plowmen  
Six well-known servants  
Seven girls for churning  
Eight for weaving cloth  
Ten cows for milking  
Brother got the mother’s chamber  
Who has a lot of money  
A lot of silver objects  
Who is famous even for gold  
Stands in silk clothes  
Wears a fleece coat  
Wears German boots on their feet  
On their head they wear a lace cap  
There is health to be lived there well.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Why are you standing by the edge, girl?  
Or why are you twisting your head,  
Is there a whistle in your ear,  
Or isn’t there a white one opposite,  
Beautiful like the lord’s son,  
Slim like a German from Finland.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Hello saw, hello axes,  
Hello from the road of coming.  
May I come into the room,  
To sit down under the eaves?  
Perhaps you have chimney smoke in the room,  
Smoke from the linden tree in front of you,  
Smoke from the oven in the open field,  
Smoke of juniper in the chamber.  
Chimney smoke makes the eyes water,  
Linden smoke causes sneezing,  
Rowan smoke even causes farting.  
May I come into the room,  
To buy a piece of flax,  
To hang it out on the clothesline of the barn?  
Do you have a blossoming pear tree,  
To dry the ears of grain,  
When my brother throws the carpet,  
Sister, blow  
My brother’s arrow is wet  
From sister’s blowing.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Little boy, little lad  
How did you know to come to us?  
You knew to cross the reeds  
You noticed beyond the mountains  
You even had torches when coming  
And language dogs with you too.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
What are you, vagabond, looking for from us  
You, wretched moth, come inside  
Are you looking for horse sausage  
Or are you looking for poor foal sausage  
The horse sausage was finished  
Only the foal sausage remained  
What are you, vagabond, looking for from us  
You, wretched moth, came inside.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
The bride at the feast was a maiden  
Come here, show me  
What you knitted until dawn  
Before the white cock crowed  
Did you knit mittens for the girl  
Slender, delicate mittens  
For father-in-law socks, for mother-in-law stockings  
Let me say it again  
I sing the words back  
The sheep disappeared from the market  
Another disappeared from the grazing field  
The third was handed over to the wolf  
Did not knit mittens for the girl to wear  
Slender, delicate mittens  
For father-in-law socks, for mother-in-law stockings.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Maiden, young girl  
When you start to go  
Then sorrow begins to come  
Tears rolling from the eyes  
Farewell, old lady  
Farewell, old man  
Farewell to the old lady’s neck  
Politely to the brother’s neck  
Properly to the sisters’ necks  
Saying goodbye without God  
Without bidding the old lady farewell  
Maiden, young girl  
You strike the old lady’s cross in front  
You strike the old man’s cross behind  
The sisters’ crosses on the shoulder  
Maiden, young girl  
Let the father-in-law come with gifts  
Let the mother-in-law come with gifts  
Then the father-in-law will give a bull  
Or the mother-in-law will give a cow  
Or a white-headed calf  
Or a little goat  
Then later it can be seen  
Then the father-in-law will repair the shirt  
Then the mother-in-law will repair the shirt  
Or the delicate little hands  
Or the little worn-out pork straps  
I was given gifts at the beginning  
The worse ribbons were thrown  
Where should I put the gifts?  
Should I hide them under the bench  
Or should I hide them under the table  
I put the belt to swing on the shoulder  
The stockings to hang on the wall.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
In the swamp go big corners  
To the forest go wide paths  
Meadow fields in the forest beautiful  
You are not, you are not, boy  
You are not the one plowing the field  
Not the one cultivating in Alemaa  
Not the one mowing hay in the meadow  
Not the one gathering in the forest  
Boy, young boy  
You traveled from Viru six ways  
You crossed Harju  
You patched the fields of Lääne  
In Järva you traveled crisscross  
You looked for a courteous person from Viru  
A proud mother from Harju  
A kind mother from Lääne  
A rich mother from Järva  
You did not find a courteous one from Viru  
A proud mother from Harju  
A kind mother from Lääne  
A rich mother from Järva  
You made plans at Pikva  
At two places in Kambi parish  
You got a bundle of firewood from one.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Hello mother-in-law, hello father-in-law  
Come outside to see  
If the daughter-in-law is to my liking  
If the daughter-in-law is not to my liking  
Then I will send the bride back  
Let the wolf eat that girl  
Let the bear shake off her fat  
Whoever is not suitable for the father-in-law's yard  
The father-in-law's yard, the mother-in-law's yard.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
You begin giving the offerings  
Do I have to put them under the bench  
Or do I have to let them fall under the table  
I stretch the stockings smoked  
I hang the ribbons on the parchment  
I put the belt to hang on the edge  
The family finds them under the bench  
And the children find them under the table.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Give me bad doses  
White doses, gray ribbons  
I have slave children at home  
Slave children, herd children  
Who has to take the bad doses  
White doses, gray ribbons.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Dance, dance, little maiden  
Dance, dance, I am watching  
Is your apron fitting  
Or is the headdress flat  
Maybe there are babies under your wing  
Dung flies under the apron  
But I am dancing, why don't you dance  
Over the crossbeam, over the table  
Over six devil’s heads  
Over seven log walls  
The midwife has babies under her wing  
Dung flies under the apron.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Where did you know to come to us  
We, the people of the great kin  
From the people's garden of Rahakaia  
If you are from the great family  
From the people's garden of Rahakaia  
Put the yards into the canvas  
Gather the streams into the cloth  
Stacked into long linen.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Little boy, little lad  
Yesterday you went like a servant  
Today you come like a king  
But bring something like a lady  
Bread eater, in a sock too  
Coffee drinker, into the ear too.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Slowly slowly, wisely wisely,  
Slowly dance, young lady,  
Jump gracefully  
So that the apron strings do not snap  
Nor the ribbons on the dance shoes move  
Nor the apron strings flutter  
Perhaps the little ones are under the wings.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Otherwise, my black one could run,  
Kimmel could pull a sled,  
Raudjas could weave,  
Before that lazy woman,  
The lazy woman’s little child.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Swing, move, little flame  
Give space, little oven  
Step back, back wall  
The drum is brought into the room  
Barabant to the tambourine  
Now a bunny pot will be made inside  
Before the oven, a pot of malt mash  
Before the flame, a meat stew pot.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Imagine a woman, what you got  
Her belly like a sack of flour  
Her head like a Pärnu wool box  
Her teeth like uneven bolts  
Her jawbones like bellows jaws.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Are these the gifts I received  
Or are they presents that were given  
The pig had turned blue  
The horse had burst red  
Should I put it under the bench  
Or should I let it go under the table  
Or should I take it to the pig feeder  
I am taking it home for the shouting.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
A lively girl was taken from us  
A clumsy girl was brought to us  
The room cries for a new broom  
The chamber screams for two hands  
There is no room sweeper  
Nor is there a chamber cleaner.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
They took away a lively girl from us  
They brought us a scoundrel  
The girls praised the apron bib  
They respected the belt buckle  
I was diligent in believing  
And I was clever in testing  
Because a field is not better under the apron  
You can get sick under the belt  
You can get headaches under the hat

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Maybe my horse could run otherwise  
Could carry a basket in the wagon  
The boat could circle the raft  
Before it pulls peaches  
Across the swamp to Ducks  
To Old Man's house to Grosbeaks  
To Grandma’s in front of the hut

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Töölaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Our master organizes a communal work party  
Despite the great heat  
In more beautiful weather  
On a hotter day  
Full loads are being brought  
We were given wide carts  
Everyone drank out of desire  
The fiddle calls here with a dance tune  
Lift your foot, white stockings  
Wait here, as a dance custom  
The dust will not ruin us  
If you are willing to be lively  
And want to walk wisely  
Before sunset harshly  
Clean the room, wash the tables  
Dust under the windows  
Wash the pewter dishes  
Bring the loads in a row  
Jugs to the kitchen porch  
Windows to the fence post  
Tomorrow the bailiff comes to the village  
The old German comes to Vainiu  
He thinks about the moon shining  
Softening the evening star  
Yellowing the morning star  
Our pewter dishes  
Bring them to the room's eaves  
Jugs to the kitchen roof  
Windows to the fence post  
I went to a wedding once  
Traveled seven saes (distance)  
I cannot tell you  
What I got from that bride  
A belt with five buckles  
Gloves with rough palms  
Should I put them under the bench  
Or should I let them fall under the table  
Or take the bag home  
What the girl forgot at home  
Father-in-law’s shirts, mother-in-law’s shirts  
Every glove was tied  
Those shoes and boots  
The girl forgot at home.  
Brother, little brother  
Drive home to the front of the barn  
Try the key in the porch  
Right at the doorframe  
Hold on, boy  
Give time, green porch  
The horse will eventually move  
The lad beats the whip on a horse’s head  
Father-in-law’s mare hobbles away  
Go, go, men  
The moon is already at home  
The star is above the barn ridge  
Poles lean by the fence  
Go, go, men  
There are uncles and dozens of them  
Aunts fill the barn  
They flew to the crowd  
Now the crowd goes wandering  
The brood is scattered  
The company disappears  
Let's go to Finland  
For a year, let's go to Harjumaa  
For winter, not back to Russia.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Head a hide-and-seek, stand by the saw  
Tie up every horse  
Surround the threshing boy  
Read the bride's story aloud  
Guess about the bride's father  
Are there heifers nearby?  
Saw the buds of the mayweed  
What the maiden forgot  
The father-in-law’s shirt, the mother-in-law’s shirt  
Every glove, every mitten  
The worn-out mittens  
That the maiden forgot.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Downward, downward the bundle  
Upward, upward the eaves  
The stove certainly yields  
The flame certainly leans that way  
A maiden is brought to the room  
A bread apron for the mayor

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Give me the nests as a donation  
The stockings were recommended to me  
I don't know where I should put them  
Should I put them under the table  
Or should I slip them under the bench  
Or should I take them home to the children.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Wait, wolves of Urve  
Endure, shepherd dogs  
You will get it, Finnish mercenaries  
Big socks, thick heads  
Wide belts, neat patterns  
Gloves with three patterns.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Walls, step back in front,  
Walls, step back behind.  
Oven, get away from your place.  
The drum is brought to the room,  
The snare drum to the floor.  
The old bellows to the hearth.  
The cup was snatched from the grandmother’s hand.  
I will not leave here  
Before I cover the room’s floor  
Before I take the wall logs on my back.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
I take the mini to dance
Does the mini dance over the sod
Over the sod, over the shovel
Over the devil’s spruce top;
Over seven sauna logs
I dance, why don’t you dance
Over the sod, over the table
Over the table, over the shovel
Over the devil’s spruce top
Over seven wall logs.
Mother-in-law has made a mess
So many twins
I haven’t done anything
Bring a bucket, I would pee
I rustle the salt box
I’ll make the mother-in-law stink
I’ll make the father-in-law sniff
Have I been trampled by bulls
Or tussled by bulls
Driven by burning bulls

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Maiden, young one  
Praise festively  
Begin the giving basket  
What you praised last year  
The year before, you boasted  
You praised the church road  
You boasted about your family  
You said to the cluster glove-mother  
Preserving the leg ties  
Can one get here the father-in-law’s shirt  
Can one get here the mother-in-law’s shirt  
Many brittle paths  
Counted counted the glove mittens  
Could have informed long ago  
That you have a bountiful abundance  
Systems of delays  
Then the community would have collected the patches  
Would have managed to gather the brushes  
Reserved all the old artifacts  
Where would have come the father-in-law’s shirt  
Where would have come the mother-in-law’s shirt  
Nailed brittle paths  
Counted counted the mittens  
Surely you shall see, it shall appear  
Is the father-in-law giving an ox here  
The father-in-law's ox, the mother-in-law’s cow  
Counted the counted glove of a young lad.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
If you bother to be diligent  
And if you want to be wise  
Be the early riser in the morning  
Before the break of dawn  
More honorable before daybreak  
Clean the room, the stable aisle  
Around the windowsills  
Turn the front of the cellar  
Wash the wallboards thoroughly  
Bring the firewood to the corner of the room  
Put the caps on the kitchen stove  
Buckets to the well hill  
Tomorrow the chamberlain comes to the village  
The old gentleman to the fiefdom

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:
My father is in the grave, my mother is in the grave,
My younger brother was also in the barn.
The sisters were singing by the grave's edge,
They came inside quietly,
Swaying over the yard,
Watching the gate,
Sweeping the yard well.
Then a groom came inside,
A groom, Piia's suitor.
They started discussing,
Give me some advice,
I should then get a long spear,
A long spear, a fine rifle.
The spear was as wide as a small table,
The rifle was about the size of a log.
It rested on the shoulders,
On the hands of men,
Rolled on five fingers,
Rested on the shoulders.
I rode past the miller,
Passed the blacksmith.
At the mill, the stones grind,
At the smithy, the bellows roar.
I thought the moon was shining,
I thought the day was reddening,
I thought the evening was awakening,
The dawn glowing golden.
I am good as a country girl,
A household daughter's husband,
Owner of a silver ring,
A neighbor to golden oats.
I buy a hat for every girl,
I buy a vest for every brother,
I buy nails for every stud,
Then I am invited in,
And painted for the feast.
Silky ones come inside,
Strong ones go away outside.
I am the miller's daughter,
The wife of Jaak the grinder.
Underneath, a valley without,
Beyond, the sky is red,
Then I am good as a country girl,
A household daughter's husband.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
When I was invited to a wedding,  
I was soon there,  
the clock was already ten.  
I greeted the wedding guests,  
they received me honestly,  
a rose was immediately pinned to my chest,  
a glass and a bottle were given to me.  
It was shameful to carry a crown,  
to give others beer and vodka.  
Then I sent away all worries,  
looked out through the window.  
Who is that bride’s brother,  
let him prepare himself soon!  
It is time to go to the church,  
to overtake the other riders.  
Hold on, young man,  
give time, green dagger!  
What did the girl leave at home?  
Father-in-law’s shirts, mother-in-law’s shirts,  
the groom’s fine sheets,  
and stiff cuffs.  
Drive to the house in front of the barn,  
take the key from the roof!  
Answered the aunt, answered the uncle,  
answered both the old ones:  
what did the girl leave at home?  
Father-in-law’s shirts, mother-in-law’s shirts.  
Hold on, young man,  
give time, green dagger!  
A handsome gentleman,  
a rosy young man,  
take the dagger, chop the door,  
ring the room's front –  
silk will come in!  
May I come to the room,  
under the eaves?  
Maybe there is tobacco smoke in the room,  
linden smoke in front of the stove,  
aspen smoke by the fireplace?  
Where is my brother tossing the blanket,  
where is my sister spreading the flax,  
throwing the handkerchief,  
drying the mitten gloves?  
Eat, fellows, drink, fellows,  
do not, fellows, put it in your belly!  
I am diligent at searching,  
cunning at testing:  
I seek new six bellies,  
I rattle sheepskin bellies.  
Get going, men –  
already the moon is in front of the house,  
the star is on the barn’s ridge,  
the rods against the white wall!  
Be healthy, eat well,  
eat well, drink well,  
cover the wide table!  
I am full eater, I am full drinker,  
I am full of sugar in my mouth.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
I do not have to go,  
I do not have to bid farewell to the people's meeting,  
To better places.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Little sister, dear one,  
Maiden, young girl  
If you are willing to be lively  
And you want to be wise  
Be diligent in the morning  
Watch before dawn  
Wash the glasses into the sink  
Put the buckets by the well's edge  
The ladles at the kitchen door  
The gentlemen of Münt passed by  
The gentlemen of Kapu passed through  
They thought the moon was shining  
For us, ladle handles were shining  
The kitchen tables gleamed  
The bucket branches gleamed

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Little sister, dear one,  
Maiden, young girl  
If you are willing to be lively  
And you want to be wise  
Be diligent in the morning  
Watch before dawn  
Wash the glasses into the sink  
Put the buckets by the well's edge  
The ladles at the kitchen door  
The gentlemen of Münt passed by  
The gentlemen of Kapu passed through  
They thought the moon was shining  
For us, ladle handles were shining  
The kitchen tables gleamed  
The bucket branches gleamed

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Sister, dear little darling  
Leave your home, the three or five ways  
Ways of sleep, ways of crying  
Sleep is best on the patch  
Bring us silk threads  
Silk threads, thread threads  
Canvas-like yarn threads  
Kalev's weaving threads  
We have silky servants  
Kalev's herdsmen boys  
Proudly goes the handsome boy  
Silk goes the pig boys  
Dear life, little brother  
Surely you have long ago told  
Brought news from long ago  
That you have many debts  
No more filthy debts  
I would have woven small bags  
Gathered together the patches  
Would have woven woolen caps  
Would have stolen a shirt onto the face  
For myself some jewelry gifts  
Also some rye bundles for the partner

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
If you are willing to be diligent  
And you want to be wise  
Be a busy ant  
Early before the dawn  
Underneath the windows of the barn  
Bring water while coming  
Wash the windows clean  
Move the barrels to the room’s eaves  
Pots to the kitchen doorway  
Windows to the garden’s edge  
Tomorrow the bailiff will come to the village  
The old man will come to Vainiu  
He thinks the moon is shining  
He thinks the day is reddening  
The evening star is glowing  
The morning star is brightening  
Our windows clean  
Barrels to the room’s eaves  
Pots to the kitchen doorway  
Windows to the garden’s edge

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
If you are willing to be diligent  
And you want to be wise  
Be a busy ant

Pots to the kitchen doorway  
Windows to the garden’s edge  
Tomorrow the bailiff will come to the village  
The old man will come to Vainiu  
He thinks the moon is shining  
He thinks the day is reddening  
The evening star is glowing  
The morning star is brightening  
Our windows clean  
Barrels to the room’s eaves  
Pots to the kitchen doorway  
Windows to the garden’s edge

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Virgin girl, young maiden,  
Fine in handwriting,  
Little piece of runic script,  
If you want to be wise,  
And if you are willing to be diligent;  
Then be industrious in the morning,  
Early before the light,  
Before dawn, bustling about;  
Warm your head and neck,  
Brush under the windows,  
Wash the drinking cups thoroughly.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Already here past midnight  
Without going the other half  
Without the cock's time passing  
This is the Germans' eating time  
The Junkers' drinking time  
The Kilters' walking time  
The peasants' sleeping time  
Soon I will start swearing  
Immediately shouting evil  
Let the devil take the suitor’s eyes  
Let the lame legs wither too  
The two housewives' eyes  
There in the middle of the village  
In this parish's lands.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Hello, dear grown men,  
Come, let us get to know each other,  
Let us accept the greeting,  
Let us give a hand to the guest,  
Let us bow in respect.  
Hello father-in-law, as the master,  
Hello mother-in-law, as the lady,  
Hello on the way when coming in,  
In the turn of the path,  
Who greets you on the road  
Will give you a hand in return.  
Do not forbid the engaged,  
Do not separate the married,  
Who had placed a pair of rings,  
Announced by the church leader,  
Wearing a black robe.  
Young lad, little boy,  
Already from here he has gone.  
He struck his mark into the turf,  
On the crossway of Riga road,  
He struck a piece into the clouds,  
He has spent nights awake,  
Sharing gifts in Aru village,  
Turning heads in Seidla.  
He has gone away to the city,  
Sent to Tallinn as a servant.  
Young lad, little boy,  
If you want to chase after him,  
Take the mark from the turf's edge,  
From the crossway of Riga road,  
Take a piece from the clouds,  
Chase after him with a gray horse,  
Rush with a fast horse,  
Catch him at Võigula.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Little sister, little elf,  
Come here, show yourself?  
What did you knit on the knee,  
Stacked until the evening,  
Long Sundays,  
Weekday meal times.  
Where did you burn the pair,  
In the countless candle sheds.  
If you could give a burn,  
Give it with a whip lashing.  
On the village road I would go,  
On Suanikol hill I would peel bark.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Hello room, greeting in the room  
Hello barn, greeting in the barn  
May I come into the room  
To drive down the rows  
Do you perhaps have tobacco smoke in the room  
In front of the stove you have alder smoke  
Birch smoke in the chamber  
The mother-in-law hears, answers back  
You may come into the room  
To drive down the rows  
We do not have tobacco smoke in the room  
No willow smoke in front of the stove  
No alder smoke in front of the stove  
No birch smoke in the chamber  
Probably the smokes have gone from the room  
The vapors have gone through the window  
The soots between the branches. -  
You come into this room  
You drive the cattle to the barn  
You sit on the bench.  
You bring the horses to this yard  
You bring the oxen to this pole.  
The front goes to the first wall  
The back to the rear wall  
Lean against the tar bucket in the corner  
Dear stove, give room.  
Bring the small table, bring the fabric bolt  
Bring the oilcloth bag inside the room  
In front of the stove, the oil bag  
On the stove, the seed bag.  
The bride’s brother was a brother  
Be sure about Kerstu  
Choose the sister’s box  
Put a stone on Kerstu  
Put a ruble piece on the lock  
The other one to the other corner.  
Maiden, young girl  
Pull up the praise to Kerstu  
Start on the grain box  
What have you dipped your trowel in  
At twilight you have wriggled  
The millers have paid for it  
The flax workers have compensated  
Ellakene, brother  
No glove is made of stone  
Stockings do not sprout moss  
The belt does not sprout twigs  
Decorate the maiden if you can  
The horse shivers from the cold  
The saddle creaks  
Ellakene, brother  
If you gave time to grow  
Then give time to decorate  
I have no one to decorate me  
Nor a father to ask  
Village mothers decorate  
But old women prepare.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Come out, our auntie  
Come out, our uncle  
Come out and see  
If it pleases me  
The son’s wife, the refugee  
Is she quick to strike  
Swift to weave cloth  
Nimble to pick threads  
Is she liked by the father-in-law  
Is she liked by the mother-in-law  
You go, witch, you do the work  
Push the swing to rock  
From the stove comes smoke but no smell  
From the chimney comes no smoke but smoke  
Then you are liked by the mother-in-law  
Then you are liked by the father-in-law  
In the eyes of the hunchback  
In the eyes of the funny people.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Oh, how I would like some sausage  
The key to the sausage pantry is lost  
But yesterday I was given some ribbons  
Ribbons – gray stockings  
The crane’s shoes, garters  
My field is quite festive.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
You will see, you will find out,  
If there is smoke in the house,  
There are coals on the hearth,  
Where my brother throws the felt,  
Where my sister beats the flax.  
My brother’s felt is wet,  
My sister’s flax is damp.  
You will see, you will find out,  
Maybe there is chimney smoke,  
Before the fire is alder smoke,  
In front of the stove is aspen smoke.  
The sister dies from smoke,  
Bursts into the heat,  
Suffocates in the flame.  
You will see, you will find out  
If there are shirts on the groom,  
Groom’s shirts, mother-in-law’s shirts,  
Tattered sleeves on the face,  
Tatters tied around the calves.  
Here are shirts on the groom,  
Groom’s shirts, mother-in-law’s shirts,  
There are ties on the calves,  
Tattered sleeves on the face.  
- -  
You have long made it known,  
Long ago brought to light,  
That you have too many naked,  
Far too many unruly people.  
I would gather sackcloth,  
Get them tied together,  
For your ten villages,  
For your struggling parishes.  
You will see, you will find out,  
If your father-in-law will give a wake,  
Father-in-law’s wake, mother-in-law’s cow,  
Many white-headed calves,  
To give fuel for the fire.  
This one has not praised the cradle,  
Honored the start of the cradle,  
This has praised the kerchief cradle,  
Honored the beginning of kerchief.  
This girl went to the hill,  
To places full of nettles,  
A handsome young man,  
A red-coated lad,  
A hundred—yes—a hundred crowns in gold,  
Save this sister from flax,  
Clothe the poor prisoner;  
My sister’s shoulders are sagging,  
The sides are bursting with heat.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
I must not go,  
I must not dear one disappear,  
I must not leave the people's footsteps,  
From better places,  
Nor swim from the swamp,  
Leave the grouse behind,  
Nor fly away from the pair of ducks.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Swing the beautiful girl  
Dance the gentleman  
Roll after the tune  
Turn after the count  
So the bird ribbons don’t move  
Nor the apron ribbons tighten  
Sparrows between the belt  
There are tiny birds under the wing  
Buds under the arm.  
Bring a bowl, I will drink  
Bring a lamp, I will pour  
I will let the men lean  
Let the young women sniff  
Have I been pushed by the bulls  
Or have I been kissed by the oxen.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
The evening comes late,  
The rooster sings at dawn,  
May we be home by dawn.  
At dusk to father-in-law’s yard,  
At dark to mother-in-law’s yard,  
To the white village between.  
How do I recognize father-in-law’s yard?  
The upset cow moos,  
The ugly dog barks,  
The cheerful rooster crows,  
Because I recognize father-in-law’s yard,  
I remember mother-in-law’s yard.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Let us begin to go,  
The people begin to drive.  
The room begins to beat,  
The stove crackles,  
The oven gives words,  
The window doors howl,  
The handcuffs scream,  
The door handles move.  
The wind outside is very good,  
So it may blow over the great sea,  
Then our troubles will end.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Oh, you sawmill attendants  
Wedding guests on the groom's side  
Why are you standing here  
He has long since gone away from here  
In Paide, he shaved his head  
Asked for his shoes to be laced  
Had a wedding in Petersburg  
A dowry was arranged in Tallinn  
The groom’s beard has certainly been washed here  
The bridegroom’s beard has been washed  
The master’s vessel has been cleaned  
We shall see  
Whether the groom’s beard has been washed here  
Whether the master’s dagger has been cleaned  
Young maiden  
Why do you listen in the chamber  
Standing by the walls  
Among the weak ones  
Come inside, my acquaintance  
Take to the woods, my acquaintance  
A big greeting to you, my love  
Take strangers as your own  
Leave your own as strangers  
Young maiden  
I do not come to eat out of hunger  
Nor to drink wine  
I came because of stories  
I came to do beauty  
Women’s fun  
I came to receive a full measure  
I come to gather skulls  
A full measure for my brother's shoulders  
So let me tell again  
Let me sing back again  
Dear one, brother dear  
Do not curse me  
Swear by the holy angels  
Do not curse the girls’ fathers,  
Do not curse the girls’ mothers  
Swear by the old women,  
Who made the girls tall  
Tall and dear  
Before I had forty girls  
With me I had eighty  
Weddings worth half a thousand  
Now you have forty girls  
But not eighty with you  
Weddings worth half a thousand.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud kodust ja lapsepõlvest
translation:
Devil take the suitor’s eyes  
Let the boys’ eyes be driven away  
Let the feet of the suitor be crippled  
The belt weaver brought the songs  
The belt weaver’s song creator  
Reader of the wide book  
Writer of wheel script  
The room was left empty for the daughter  
The barn was left for storing clothes  
The yard left ugly from the fabric  
The stable bare from the sheep  
When the daughter was married off  
Then the songs were taken from the house  
A beautiful arch before the table  
A suitable place by the field  
So far there has been noise in the village  
When the daughter was married off  
The linen head scarf  
Then there was noise in the village  
Men’s gathering in the alder grove  
When the daughter was married off  
Then beauty was taken from the house  
Linen head scarf before  
Then the noise disappeared from the village  
Men’s gathering from the alder grove  
I raised a daughter  
I raised, I caressed  
I loved, I nurtured  
I took her in my arms, I soothed her  
I put her down, I played with her  
Secretly  
I thought of coming to her aid  
I thought of helping her  
Support goes to the one you don’t know  
Help to the one you haven’t thought of.  
The room remains my support  
The stove remains my help  
The trough longer in reach  
Support remains with the poor one  
Help with the one who is helpless  
Mother’s soul, dear old woman  
When will I repay grandmother’s trouble  
Grandmother’s trouble, mother’s trouble  
Grandfather’s trouble, dear trouble  
Grandmother’s soul, motherly care  
Carrying on the lap and neck  
Led me on the apron field  
On the arm outside  
Thistle made the old woman cry  
Rosehip made her sway  
The thistle did not cry  
The rosehip did not sway  
In the berry garden it did not quiet down  
Oh what a poor child’s game  
It walked under the cradle  
I had three cradles  
Five were weak bows  
One was below the table  
Another by the field’s edge  
The third at home under the bench  
What was below the table  
Moved the birds  
What was by the field’s edge  
Swayed the wind  
What was at home under the bench  
Swayed the smoke  
It was grandmother’s rocking.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Here I bring my beautiful sister  
Here I bring Mari, the humble  
Here I bring Kaie, the cunning  
Here I bring Anne, the diligent  
Here I bring Liisa, the wise  
Here Eeva, the knowledgeable about the world  
Do not put it in the pot  
Do not cook it in the cauldron  
A little to put in the pot  
A scarce amount to put in the cauldron  
To lay it on the table  
Put it into the pot  
Cook the chicken in the cauldron.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Töölaulud  ; Murelaulud  ; Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Young family daughter,  
Farm daughter, little plant  
If you are willing to be alert  
And if you want to be wise  
Clean the room, the glass, the table  
Under the window pane  
Turn the cellar entrance  
Wash the jugs in the pantry  
Bring them to the corner of the room  
Clogs to the kitchen roof ridge  
Buckets to the well edge  
Here comes the bailiff to the village  
The old master to the widow  
He thinks the moon is shining  
He thinks the holy light is shining  
Our ladle handles gleamed  
Our shining tables gleamed  
Our bucket tables gleamed  
Our jugs in the pantry  
Bring them to the corner of the room  
Clogs to the kitchen roof ridge  
Young family daughter  
Farm daughter, little plant  
When she hears the holidays are coming  
You drive the calves in  
The Easter eggs are sliding  
The moon pushes the weaving  
The holy pushes the pearl maiden  
The year decorates itself  
Begins to ask the maidens  
Begins to ask the old men  
Argues with the elders  
Where is my brooch, where is my clasp  
Where are my embroidered gloves

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Pulmalaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Go to the corner, young men,  
To the dark pipe men,  
Lazy men under the table.  
From my brother’s sword’s path,  
My brother is coming to roll,  
Sinisukk is coming to serve.  
There is a long reed,  
Longer than my own brother.  
There is white sea foam;  
Whiter than my own brother,  
He threw a throw to the meadow,  
To the field of the vilevitsa (a folk instrument),  
Who cut their finger yesterday?  
It was that young lad.  
Who jumped to swaddle?  
It was that young maiden.  
Where did she get the scarf?  
She took a neckerchief from the neck,  
Tore the jaw cloth,  
Turned the head scarf to bits.  
Where did she get the ribbon then?  
She took a leg ribbon from the calf;  
That ribbon vanished,  
The village lad will get a new one!  
That scarf vanished,  
The village suitor will buy a new one:  
The Russian boy brings a striped one,  
The German boy brings a velvet one,  
The country boy brings a mud-patterned one,  
The Swedish boy brings a checked one,  
Finnish patterns are woven in,  
Magazines are included,  
Logical writings are woven in.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Let's put beads around the neck  
Pearls on the halter of the ox  
But money around the broad forehead  
Ruble pieces on the mare's neck  
Before the neck of that maiden  
Who was half promised  
Loved too early  
Otherwise, my black horse could run  
The boat could rock on the pole  
The kite could circle ten times  
The donkey could shout below  
Before the kite flies around  
Through the town's streets  
Over the swampy blackberries  
Over the bog's corpse berries  
Behind Paide, at the palm grove.

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Rise up, young people  
The stove has long been burning,  
Smoke has gone out the big door,  
Soot has gone through the vent,  
Steam has gone out the window.  

"Young maiden,  
If you want to be a housewife,  
You must sleep a little  
Under the threshold of the barn door,  
Put your head on the doorstep.  

When you hear the rooster crowing  
And the hen squawking,  
Then quickly without hesitation,  
Jump up without shouting,  
Then walk quietly through the yard,  
Running to the pen.  

See what you find there—  
You will find seven piglets,  
Put those piglets in your apron.  
Then go to show your father-in-law,  
Show your father-in-law, your mother-in-law,  
Show your young husband,  
Then you will be valued by your father-in-law,  
Valued by your mother-in-law,  
Valued by your young husband."

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
You wanted to go to the farm  
To the farm to the peasant  
To the plowman and sower  
To the turner of black soil  
Be diligent in the morning  
Early before dawn  
Pitchforks from the barnyard  
Bundles from the stacks  
Buckets to the well’s edge  
Whoever comes early to the village  
Thinks of the moon shining  
The edge of the straw mattress shining  
Your warm pitchfork handles  
Your shining willow rods  
Your willow branches  
Your shining bucket handles  
When the father-in-law comes from church  
Be diligent and run to meet him  
Undress him  
Take off the plague scarf  
Take gloves from his hands  
Thus, you seek the father-in-law’s favor  
The mother-in-law goes to fetch water from the well  
Be diligent and run to meet her  
Take the bucket from the mother-in-law’s hands  
Pour the water into the kettle  
Then you seek the mother-in-law’s favor  
Your husband comes from the forest  
Be diligent and run to meet him  
Take the horse out of the harness  
Take gloves from his hands  
Undress him  
The child comes from the meadow  
Be diligent and run to meet him  
Oh you lambs to the barn  
Bring the child food and bread  
Thus, you seek the child’s favor  
The spindle was in the cradle  
If you work or wander off  
Push the cradle to swing  
The bowstring to bend weakly  
Thus, you seek the spindle’s favor  
The plowman comes from the field plowing  
Oxen frosted in front  
The chains in the harness jingling  
Be diligent and run to meet him  
Take the oxen from the yoke  
Oh you to the lower cellar  
Out to the yard to the hayfield  
Thus, you seek the plowman’s favor!

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
A young virgin girl,  
A little bird with flaxen hair,  
You think of beauty without a husband,  
A white girl without a husband,  
You think of pure boys without a husband,  
You think of bashful men without a husband.  
You are not beautiful without a husband,  
Not a white bride without a husband,  
Nor a pure boy without a husband,  
You are strange without a husband,  
A thief without a husband,  
Half a girl without a boy,  
A fat woman without men.  
The young maiden understood and said,  
"There is no pot for me to pee in,  
No corner for me to let go,  
I would give boys a chance to sniff,  
Young men to take a whiff,  
And old maids to envy.  
Are you not pure without a boy,  
Are you not a white bride either,  
Are you not beautiful at all,  
Or are you clever in some way?"

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
May one come indoors  
Or drive them outside?  
Blow this fire indoors,  
A warm fire down below,  
Perhaps the springs are flowing,  
Cloths on the floor?  
Maybe I’ll water the neckerchief,  
Ruin new felt boots?  
Yes, one may come indoors  
Or drive them outside.  
Do not sweep the floor  
Or damage the storage barn!  
People have lived here before,  
Drunk five, six shots of vodka,  
Eaten hundreds of loaves of bread,  
This is the front wall,  
Behind is the back wall.  
The drum comes indoors,  
An ass-wipe on the floor.  
Quietly, quietly, wisely, wisely,  
The Maldu people’s meeting.  
Perhaps a baby under the wing here  
A belt between thieves?  
There is no baby under the wing,  
No belt between thieves.  
Mother-in-law brought an awkward one on the road,  
There are two sets of twins, –  
I have no one or nothing!

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Little business, little mother-in-law!  
May I come into the room,  
Under the eaves to take a rest,  
On the roof to lie down?  
Perhaps in your room there is tobacco smoke,  
By the stove there is wood smoke,  
In front of the fire there is elm smoke,  
Birch smoke in the chamber?

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
A young maiden  
A slender writing place  
Don’t take it badly  
Don’t get angry  
That there is a humpback  
That there is a mote on the tassel-necklace  
That there is a father-in-law with a duck’s beak  
That there is a mother-in-law with a crusty jaw  
Because it’s no trouble, it’s no strain  
That there is a humpback  
The farm is full of stones  
Because it’s no trouble, it’s no strain  
That there is a mote on the tassel-necklace  
Sweeping the room with a broom without a handle  
Because it’s no trouble, it’s no strain  
That there is a father-in-law with a duck’s beak  
He stokes the stove without a fork  
Because it’s no trouble, it’s no strain  
That there is a mother-in-law with a crusty jaw  
She boils the broth with a ladle

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
You nursed a baby boy  
You nursed, you raised:  
You grew up and hugged  
To bring the firstborn to the mother  
To the breeder’s embrace  
The time came, the day arrived  
The son brought to the mother a lady  
To the breeder, a helper

label: Pulmalaulud
translation:
Let's go to the chewy tobacco room
To a previously seen life
Already lived here before
Drunk and thought about it some time
A hundred baskets of bread eaten
So much time spent without. -
Little doves, little birds
Do you have a hundred rods in the room
To burn the ears together
Where I toss my brother's quilt
Spread my sister's hem
Reach out a sibling's whiff. -
Little doves, little birds
Yes, we have a hundred rods in the room
To burn the ears together
We have four beds in the room.
The father-in-law's bed is the chief's
päg. 84 The mother-in-law's bed is lively
The groom's bed is on the claws
The bride's bed is on the handles. -
Can you, at the river, go
Settle the stallion's debts in the stable
Lay down the tools in the fields
You cannot take them to the street
The gnomes eat from the street
You cannot take them to the hunter
Thieves take from the hunter. -
And can you go to the river
Settle the stallion's debts in the stable
Lay down the tools in the fields
Go down to the garden, put blankets on the table. -
Little doves, little birds
Give me my own hand
Roll out the brother's wife
Here my brother has given gifts:
Given gifts, placed pledges
Here given a cow's heat
Reconciled the cow's heat
Here brought a sieve ring
The old chest in the balance. -
Little doves, little birds
You do not belong here
The maiden has already left
She had her period in Paide
Given gifts in Aniküla
Stopped by the bridge
If you want to chase after her
Give the all-powerful
Black otherwise will turn away
Kimmelilla's retreat. -
Let the groom cry, let the boy cry
Let the father-in-law cry first
Let the wedding crowd cry in the field
The maiden wants to be sent away
Orders to give the engagement rings. -
Let the groom laugh, let the boy laugh
Let the father-in-law laugh first
Let the wedding crowd laugh in the field
The maiden is already to our liking
To our liking, to our language's liking
We went to the silk oven
To Kalev's chamber
There we were sent silk
Red ribbons were braided for us
Embroidery thread was woven. -
Little doves, little birds
Put a little bird on the linen
Put a little one on the tunic
Ani to the lover's cloth
The bird wants the linen
The little one wants the tunic
Ani to the lover's cloth
The room is full of gossip
The table full of bread crusts
There’s no one to pick clothes
Nor anyone to save from the heat
My sister sits inside
Poor in laborer's clothes
A maiden, a young girl
Don't cry, don't grumble
Don't tremble like a flash
You weren't brought lace ribbons
Nor were you brought a groom’s apron
Ribbon brought for the city-dweller
Tunic brought for the tailor
Apron brought for the weaver. -
The feast must end in truth
The wedding must end incomplete
Bread to the comb’s edge
Beer is finished and drying up
The daughter doesn’t leave the room
Doesn't go to the brothers' house
Tie the daughter some beer
A full oven, some aid bread
Seven round black breads
Then the daughter will leave the room
There is talk about the eidel
Talks as daughters go home
The beautiful door is for the chief
A quick bundle goes out the door
A smooth sickle is by the wall
The talks are smooth
The sheep's wools are tight
When the daughter goes to the farm
Then it’s not she who dies
The sheep’s wool fades
A beautiful team goes to the chief
A quick bundle goes out the door
A smooth sickle goes by the wall
Suitable piglet from the field.

label: Murelaulud  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:
A riderless mare passed by  
A horse without reins  
Strike strongly and hold the reins  
Don’t worry about the whip  
Take care of the rider’s heel

label: Murelaulud  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:
A riderless mare passed by  
A horse without reins  
Strike strongly and hold the reins  
Don’t worry about the whip  
Take care of the rider’s heel

label: Töölaulud  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:
Chopping wood on the hearth,
Won't my gray one start on it?
In the forest, wood and firewood,
From the clearing, boards and planks.
On the black one, I put wood anyway,
On the ear, I put all the wood.
Otherwise, my black one could have run away,
The gray one could have cried on the threshing floor?
Before those maidens,
Before the girl with tin hair,
Who were partly kept,
Who stood by the wall,
Who walked by the tavern.

label: Töölaulud  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:

Before those maidens,

Who were partly kept,

Who walked by the tavern.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Little John, little sleigh!
You made a sleigh in the barn,
A bed-warming one in the spruce grove;
Mirje-Märje in the pine forest.
Every noon you hit a slat,
Every day you added a tongue!
Every month you made a spoke,
You finished the sleigh
You took the sleigh down the German stairs.
What was missing from the sleigh?
A harness, yoke;
Horse bit, reins:
On top of the golden driver’s seat
Inside a silk maiden’s cloak

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let's go to the creator for a story,
To Maria's hayfield!
I took a bow, I took an arrow.
What did I find under the bow?
I found an eagle under the bow
What is under the eagle's wing?
A wounded eagle under the wing.
What is on the wounded corner?
A shingle was on the wounded corner.
What was cut from that shingle?
Jaan's barn was cut:
What is inside Jaan's barn?
Beds inside Jaan's barn.
What is under the beds?
Four young maidens
One is folding canvases;
Another is pounding papers;
The third is weaving a golden belt,
The fourth is crying for a young man!
Don't cry, little maiden!
They are already said to be coming,
The ringing of tin utensils:
The clatter of iron utensils,
The ringing of silver utensils;
The jingling of golden utensils
Be silent, little maiden,
They already seem to be coming!
A head is appearing above the forest;
Feet below seldom out of the forest,
The body in the middle of the forest!

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
