label: Töölaulud
translation:
Up, up, rakers  
Wake up, parents  
Go to the field, young ones  
All the field walkers  
Now drink the sieve, stand on the edges  
The moon will turn upside down  
Stakes against the white fence.  
Take the stakes from the sheath  
Rake the field in order  
The field was not beaten by me  
The stake was not struck harshly  
Even if the field was harshly raked  
The ear did not reach the ear canal.  
Bread is eaten in secret  
Beers are drunk secretly.  
Cakes without the knowledge of the wife  
Honey breads without tasting  
But the field was not beaten by me  
The stake was not struck harshly  
Even if the field was harshly raked  
The ear did not reach the ear canal.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Little Jan, little sled  
Make me a little wheelbed  
So I can roll into the barn  
In winter, by the stove's warmth  
Little Jan, little sled  
Jan made the sled in the forest  
At the edge of the clearing, in the spruce grove  
In the damp pine forest  
Each month he made a spoke  
Each day he put a rim  
With every strike  
He finished making the sled  
Brought the sled home  
What the sled needed  
Bows, braces  
A front panel with a harness  
Inside, a silk maiden  
Riding the sturdy Kalevipoeg

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Dawn is already breaking from the crown side,  
Brightening from the hoarfrost side.  
The moon rises, glowing from the river,  
From the grey ash of Pääva,  
From the stars of the starry sky.  

Rise up, household,  
Walk to the threshing barn, threshers.  
Go to the threshing barn, those who thresh.  
Awaken, heads of the household.  

The sieve is already standing on its edge,  
Against the rod, the white ones,  
For the moon is in front of the house.  

Rise to milk the cows,  
To touch the horns of the bulls.  

Thresh, thresh, little cow,  
Thresh from your mouth, thresh from your head;  
Thresh from your head the little harness,  
Top with the small noseband.  

Give milk to my household,  
Give butter to the table.  
My household craves milk,  
Butter is needed on the table.  

I do not want a churnful of buttermilk,  
Nor a barrelful of whey.  
I want six ladlefuls,  
Five measures of wine honey.  

A thousand of those to pound,  
A hundred gallons of German brandy,  
Ten villages’ worth of lungs (drinking power).

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Thus sings the lazy woman,  
Thus the lazy woman’s children:  
"If only the barley were at the barn,  
The oats at the threshing floor,  
The flax on the rack,  
I would cut two sheaves with a sickle,  
Wet three sheaves in the dew,  
Burn firewood in the stove.  
For our men were crazy,  
To have made the barley into a valley,  
And to have taken the oats far away,  
The flax halfway to the town road,  
To bow to my dear one,  
To soothe his tender back,  
To break his back,  
To break his neck.  
To him, out on the great field,  
On the bottomless field."

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Let's set off, dear sisters  
Let's set off, let's pull together.  
Pull after truth,  
Drag after anger.  
Let the herd have two hands,  
Two hands, ten nails,  
Roll five finger buds.  
So that we swim to the end,  
And reach the island's point,  
Out into the wide water  
At the end is red beer.  
On the island are slender loaves,  
Milk breads in the barn,  
Roosters in the stone harness.  
Let me say it again,  
I'll say it properly:  
Perhaps at the end are slave vines,  
In the barn are rye vines.  
A stone harness on the mast,  
The grain is between moments.  
Why are girls beaten,  
Young women snatched away?

label: Töölaulud
translation:
O, you boys of Udeva,
Young men from the Norwegian district.
They built bridges from planks,
Laid logs from carrots.
So that a woman wouldn’t get her foot wet,
A woman’s foot, a girl’s burden.
A pig doesn’t wear silk shoes,
A sheep doesn’t wear new boots,
A goat doesn’t cook cabbage.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The mother told me to spin,
The father told me to card wool.
I am grumbling in the corner,
Mumbling with a carding stick.
The village boy is at the flax field,
The wool would become matted,
The cards got tangled in the story.
The wool matted, the cards tangled,
The bagcloth near the rooster.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
From the beginning I start,
From the gate I take out.
A little field, a little long,
A little clearing, a little wide.
I will not leave you before.
Perhaps I weave with knees,
Cut a four-joy patch,
Gather a two-hand patch.
Woven are the worn fields,
Lazy fields are cleared.
There are unweaved fresh fields.
Now I have gathered the golden field
Into a wide heap outside;
Together in dry weather,
In bright weather,
In hot weather.
No hair left struck by the wind,
No flock struck by rain,
No hailstorm of frozen graupel.
Now the herd can go out,
The horse can run at a gallop.
Open pastures for the sheep to graze,
Turn the field for the piglet.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Come on, we sisters are tender,
Let's come, let's go.
Come after the truth,
Come after anger:
So we swim to the end,
And reach the island's point,
To the long edge of the field,
To the wide clearing of the meadow.
At the end is red beer,
On the island are slender loaves,
In the clearing are milk breads,
Roosters in the rocky forest,
Sandwiches in between.
Let me tell, I'll answer soon,
I'll tell the flowers up high:
Perhaps at the end are slave's whips,
In the clearing are Piia's rods.
Sandwiches in between,
In the rocky forest are sheaves,
Why is the boy beaten,
Young women are being snatched.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
All the gold goes home,  
All the berries fall asleep.  
Every man with his own,  
Every partner with her chicken.  
Brother goes with a young maiden,  
I go crying towards the corner,  
Towards an empty bed.  
I try to talk with the bedbug,  
To strike a deal with the louse.  
I turn over, find cold;  
I turn my back, find a wall.  
The stepmother says: take a wife,  
But my own says: be without!  
When I get angry, where can I put it?  
You cannot sell off the bull,  
Nor take the cow to town.  
You cannot put the sheep in the barn,  
Nor give away the goat as a gift,  
You cannot trade the horse.  
My wife is too lazy,  
She hasn’t bothered to shear the wool,  
She wanted to pick ticks.  
The wool is getting old and moth-eaten,  
The ticks gnaw on the flannel,  
The wool she took to the village,  
The ticks ended up in the hands of single women.  
That’s how my salts dissolve,  
That’s how my fortunes sink.  
Pieces of meat slip away,  
Bread jars endure,  
The goat trays balance out,  
The millers pay their debts,  
The eucharistic followers scribble.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Ui, ui, ui, ui, in the town of Juuru
Today I went to the town of Tänni,
Tomorrow I will go to Uni church.
The day after tomorrow to Toome town.
To see the maidens,
To listen to the curly necks,
To row with the brooch breasts.
This maiden by my name
My wreath head from my family:
Whoever has twisted shoelaces
Snow-white legs.
Around the pitch-black cloak.
Uidu-tuidu, boys of Toome,
Oh, you boys of Udeva!
Boys of Kärü village herdsmen,
Young men from Norra parish,
Boys of Udeva's hoost!
Big ones of Sõidla, thick ones of Paide,
Straw horses of Ahula.
Viru vikings, Harju woodcutters,
Baggy ones from Kolu point:
Red boys of Järvamaa,
Slender girls of Järva.
Women of Järva with studs.
Wool of Viru, Harju’s woollocks;
Sheets of Laijus laid out nicely:
Bring our Kärt
Kärt, swift to dance,
Quick to tread the boots
Summer makes the wool, winter makes the locks,
Spring the beautiful hemp.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Ui, ui, ui, ui, in the town of Juuru

My wreath head from my family:

Snow-white legs.
Around the pitch-black cloak.

Boys of Kärü village herdsmen,

Kärt, swift to dance,
Quick to tread the boots
Summer makes the wool, winter makes the locks,
Spring the beautiful hemp.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The drowsy girls were napping  
Both Malled were sleeping.  
Former old ladies’ maids  
The old men’s wise stable boys  
A little sleep, a little doze  
A little nap, a little dullness:  
Don’t lull me to sleep,  
Don’t trouble the poor child.  
I have plenty of time to drowse,  
To lie down, to lie about,  
To keep my belly warm.  
Go to the village, go to the neighbors.  
To the village’s young men,  
To the young men’s young lads,  
To the old bachelors and widowers.  
They have time to drowse,  
To lie down, to lie about,  
To keep their bellies warm.  
Don’t cast the sleep spell,  
Let the wheel of power turn.  
Sleep has a low cart,  
The powerful wheel is yellow-gold.  
Sleep sends one to wander,  
Sleeping sends one to march,  
To walk the path of napping,  
Sleep won’t give a new coat,  
Sleeping gives a shirt to the ground.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Surely I will reap, but I am exhausted  
I do not understand what is with me  
Has my strength been taken  
Or are my hands tied  
Fingers tied in knots,  
Is my head braided?  
Or is there rust on this sickle?  
The sickle was made by an evil smith  
The sickle is wicked, not his whole  
The iron is rusty, not his cutting,  
The sickle was made by an evil smith,  
The iron has become rusted,  
A crooked paw has broken.  
Oh, you barley squirrel,  
And you, cunning oats.  
And you, rye rummager,  
And you, leats, pig herders:  
Why did you grow wicked,  
Why did you rise from low ground  
To bow to my gold,  
To bend your gentle back?  
May your back break,  
May your neck crack.  
On your large fields,  
On the bottomless fields.  
Here the hunchbacked have plowed,  
The edge has nibbled the root,  
Sown the piglets,  
And driven the rowdies wild.  
If only they had plowed a gentle brother,  
And tilled a humble sister.  
Then willows would grow thicker  
And reeds lie down.  
Now I reap the pig’s mess,  
The goose’s bad trampling,  
The crane’s evil turning,  
For you have only the crane’s quilt left,  
The crane’s quilt, the grouse’s cover,  
The gray goose’s mantle.  
Here pigs have slept,  
Geese have swum for a year,  
A flock of ducks have paddled.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Neither fieldwork exhausts me,  
Nor outdoor work tires me,  
Neither thigh scabies harm me,  
Nor plow crickets wear me out.  
I make the fields to suffer,  
I make the outdoors to tire,  
The plow crickets to chatter,  
The thigh scabies to flutter.  
But I am ruined by Riga beers,  
By bottle bottoms of shop boys,  
By jug bottoms of merchants,  
By the master's sweet drinks.  
But these ruin a young maiden,  
They cool yet a warm heart.  
Work does not tire me,  
Nor do the fields wear me out.  
I plow the swamps, I cultivate the hay meadows,  
I plow the moss, I cultivate the bilberries,  
I plow the meadows, I cultivate the pines,  
I plow the valleys, I cultivate the barleys.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
I am the son of a sailor man,  
The dear son of a fisherman man,  
I sit on the boat's bench:  
I pick sprats from the mud,  
I put the bigger ones in my mouth,  
I throw the smaller ones into the water;  
There they grow among the fish,  
They move upward, -  
Here are big whitefish, black-spotting herrings,  
Wide salmon, seals at the end.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
I am the son of a sailor man,  
The dear son of a fisherman man,  
I sit on the boat's bench:  
I pick sprats from the mud,  
I put the bigger ones in my mouth,  
I throw the smaller ones into the water;  
There they grow among the fish,  
They move upward, -  
Here are big whitefish, black-spotting herrings,  
Wide salmon, seals at the end.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
What use is to lull with sleep,  
To dream of sleeping;  
Sleep does not bring new moons,  
Sleeping does not brighten the earth, —  
Sleep leads to wandering,  
Sleeping leads to marching,  
To treading the path of slumber.  
Do not put yourself on the track of sleep,  
Do not let the wheel run —  
Sleep has a low stigma,  
The wheel runs towards the slope!

label: Töölaulud
translation:
For I am dear in the old woman's mind,  
For I am dear in the old man's mind:  
That I am a beautiful hay cutter, –  
I make swaths like German boys,  
I make stacks like kings,  
The flies on the field are thick,  
I make neat haycocks in the field.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
This was laughed at by the Germans of Narva,  
Mocked by the lords of Iru:  
That they were rags made by women,  
Heaps gathered by daughters.  
One rag's woman, another's another,  
The young maid was a stack—  
Hatred was of five lazy boys!

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Surely I reap, I do not falter  
Surely I winnow, I do not diminish  
The crop is rare, I do not manage  
The sickle is sharp, I do not gather.  
The crop is rare, a man’s sowing  
The sickle is sharp, made by the smith,  
Hardened by strong men,  
Hammered by crooked hands.  
I cannot go among men  
Nor mingle among boys  
Laughing, go among women  
I cannot come to the girls:  
My sickle is flawed  
The sickle has seven notches.  
I asked my brother’s favor:  
“Kind one, dear brother  
“Go quickly to the hiis’ realm  
“Ride to the smith in Viru  
“To the sharpest, the wisest  
“Make me a whole sickle  
“Let copper casting be poured  
“Smooth the blade in front  
“Drip tin behind  
“Purify the middle with gold  
“Then I can go among men  
“Mingle among boys  
“Come laughing among women.”

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Let's warm up, dear sisters  
Let's warm up and cheer,  
Let's weave mighty bundles  
Let's put on better flies  
Feet full of strength:  
So that everyone here can gather,  
To snap nails in the embrace  
To roam well by the father-in-law.  
The stove warms the households,  
The father-in-law’s good pair of oxen,  
The fishing nets break themselves,  
My bundle has five sticks,  
The sheaf into a golden stack,  
Flies upon the flies I place.  
Let's make heaps of golden combs  
Forelegs of silver combs  
Joints of copper combs:  
The heaps shone in Kuusalu  
Flyaways seen in Narva.  
For that, the Narva Germans laughed  
The lords of Viru marveled  
And the gentlemen of Moora pondered:  
That the flyaways were made by women  
The sheaves gathered by daughters.  
The joints were made by a maiden  
Flyaways by one woman, another by another,  
A bundle by five lazy boys  
A sheaf by six bad boys.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Evening comes, arrives late  
The day rolls down low,  
Evening comes unexpectedly  
Twilight comes uninvited,  
Evening comes accompanied by the outdoors  
Twilight comes with the grain,  
Evening brings straw to the barn  
Twilight puts to sleep,  
The spirit awakens,  
Turns up the dawn  
And spreads the white wide.  
When this sieve stands on its edges,  
The poles against the white side,  
When dawn breaks on the Koigi side  
And whitens from the Varangu side,  
Then we are sent to work:  
One there, another here,  
Some to the manor fields.  
Slave labor is sought  
Wage workers are thrown in.  
If nothing else, to carry mud,  
To clear stones in the field,  
To roll stones together,  
To place stones in position.  
Shoes break the stone quarry,  
The stones place the stones in rows,  
Charcoal remains in the skirt.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Hundred, hundred, warm rain,  
Come home, black cow,  
The old lady makes me sheep's milk,  
For herself, pea milk,  
For the frozen one, warm milk,  
For the head of the household, sour milk,  
For the brother, beautiful butter,  
Come home, vissi-vissi.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Our poor manor spirits,  
Late master's spirits,  
Spirits of the old lord,  
We wander at night,  
In the moonlight we rove,  
At night we cut hay,  
In the moonlight we make heaps,  
We make large heaps in the peat bog,  
From the field slender sheep,  
From the field swift goats,  
Under the village high heaps,  
On the land happier pustules;  
God has a drunken cow,  
Mary has a strong cow,  
They ate large heaps from the peat bog,  
From the field slender sheep,  
From the field swift goats,  
Under the village high heaps,  
On the land happier pustules.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I would live to myself,
I would work for others to know,
I would roll with the knowledge of five fingers,
Not letting the village live,
Not letting the village fools settle.
Oh, you jealous villagers,
Your own great witches,
Brought salt from Finland,
Salt across the seas,
Brought it to our pasture.
The salts began to melt,
The salt dissolved.
Then the herd began to disappear,
The blue pigs to die,
The fat cows to sleep,
The gray bulls to yawn,
The mares to quickly breathe their last,
The studs to completely rage;
Oh, you jealous villagers,
Your own great witches.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
So we had our days of joy  
When the master gave the work party  
And our young master gave the drink  
The day after the work party  
When we were in the barn  
In the evening a beer barrel was brought  
At twilight a vodka barrel  
The third barrel of the rooster's song.  
All was consumed by morning  
All was consumed completely  
To the last stalk, to the last white one  
Anneds, Marids, Liisuds, Leenus  
Lifted their legs, white stockings  
The dust of the work party did not harm them.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Let's go to the maker for the hay  
To Maarja's meadow  
Golden rakes in hand  
Silver scythe handles  
The maker's song to sing  
Maarja's hay to mow

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Dear little sister,  
We gather strong bundles,  
We make proper sheaves,  
So that the village can collect them,  
To snap nails freely,  
If then the neighbors spoil,  
The hail breaks itself,  
Into my bundle to put,  
To assemble from the sheaf.  
So be it, let it remain,  
Let it end by tomorrow,  
By the coming Friday,  
By Easter Saturday,  
By Pentecost, a holy day.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
We drink, girls, this autumn  
This light spring  
Now there is a grain crop in the field  
In the hayfield, there is a hay crop.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Jaan made a sled for the old woman  
A golden skull in the shed  
Every day he put a plank  
And six spokes in the wheel  
Every noon he cut the shingles  
He made the sled ready  
Then he brought the stove  
Called the old woman to look  
Called the old man to look  
What is missing on the sled  
The old woman understood, immediately answered  
Five things are missing on the sled  
Runners are needed, a horse is needed  
Slats are needed, a bow is needed  
Then the maiden sat in it  
Then the sled would be complete.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The herd is sent to the pasture
A switch is lit in hand
A lump is placed in the throat
I keep the old woman’s sheep
I protect the lady’s herd
The old woman makes me new socks
The old man makes me new shoes
A snow-white coat
Go home, little herd
Go home, little herd
Take home the little flock
The mistress’s little milk

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
The herd is sent to the pasture
A switch is lit in hand
A lump is placed in the throat
I keep the old woman’s sheep
I protect the lady’s herd
The old woman makes me new socks
The old man makes me new shoes
A snow-white coat
Go home, little herd
Go home, little herd
Take home the little flock
The mistress’s little milk

label: Töölaulud
translation:
End, end, little field  
Come to an end, little island  
You have long been started.  
And long ago they began cutting you  
Every day they came to you.  
Every hour they came to your roots  
Rise up, sisters, truly  
Hold up the anger because  
We swim to the ends  
We reach the island’s edge  
The red beer is finished  
There are milk cakes in the barn.  
There are slender breads on the island  
So let me say it again  
Let me sing the words back.  
Maybe the slave whip is at an end  
There is a slave whip in the barn  
There are slave pains on the island.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
End, end, little field  
Come to an end, little island  
Little river, little meadow  
We cut the strong branches  
We put the handfuls in order  
The bundles become tighter  
The sheaves become faster  
Then we go to help the flax  
To cover the fine head  
The soil has gone due to eating and drinking  
Not from the fish  
Hurrying because of rye bread  
Help is needed for the flax  
A cover is needed for the fine head  
When the cutting ends here  
Then the midday bread ends here  
The slices in between cry out  
The fur coats stay far away.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Little John, little sledge  
Make me a little wheel-bed  
So I can roll in the cold  
In winter, by the stove's warmth  
Little John, little sledge  
John made the sledge in the forest  
In the spruce grove of Kirje-Kärje  
In the pine forest of Mirje-Märje:  
On every spruce he made a spoke  
On every pine he put a rim  
From every south wind he made a board  
He finished making the sledge  
He brought the sledge in front of the house  
What was missing on the sledge  
Branches, rods,  
To guide the front,  
Inside a silk maiden,  
On the heels, Kalev's boy.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
To the scythe, to the scythe, to the rods,  
To the basket, to the basket, to the golds  
Willow ducks on the marsh.  
We cut the mighty scythes  
Put the worms (pests) better  
Handfuls faster:  
So that I get the gold field  
Layered widely outside,  
Tied around this field.  
Pulled barren fields,  
Interwoven barren fields,  
Laid out is the lazy meadow.  
Ungathered field not woven,  
Ungathered meadows not laid out,  
Now the sickle can stand for mowing,  
Home with this ugly iron,  
With the deceitful copper of Varnane,  
Made on the wall by the smith,  
The mother-in-law cannot sit indoors,  
The daughter cannot rest indoors,  
Cannot bark near the stove.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Thus says the lazy woman,  
Thus the lazy woman's children:  
"If only my field were plowed  
A wide field of grain  
In front of the door,  
Mowed for hay.  
If only there were barley in front of the door,  
Oats by the doorstep:  
I would cut sheaves at the granary,  
Water two sheaves,  
Three sheaves at dawn,  
And bake in the oven.  
Because our men were foolish:  
They made our barley into a valley,  
And the oats far away,  
Flax halfway to the town road.  
May their ships sink,  
May they break on my neck,  
On the big field,  
On the cursed field.  
But let me say it again,  
Let me sing it back again:  
No harm shall come to my back.  
My back will be healthy,  
Healthy and strong,  
Like the back of a young horse,  
The back of a lively foal.  
May great men die,  
May dear men disappear,  
Sink with white beards.  
Before I grow old and clucking  
Like a hen raised by grandmother  
Like a little bird found by grandfather."

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Oh, you barley squirrel,  
And you cunning oats,  
Or you rye wanderer.  
Oh, you leats, swineherd!  
Why did you grow wild,  
Why did you rise so low from the ground,  
To bow to my dear,  
To straighten a tender back?  
Here in this place pigs have slept,  
Geese have swum for a year,  
Ducks have waddled,  
Storms of wind have blown,  
Rustling showers have fallen,  
Rain showers have rolled.  
From where should I cut my sorrow,  
Where should I begin my sadness,  
To mow the meadow grass?  
If you had plowed, dear brother,  
If you had bordered, low sister,  
Then the willow would be thick,  
And the reeds would lie peacefully.

label: Töölaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
How long I tended my herd
How long I guarded the flock,
Snow remained on my trees,
Red on the bushes,
Beauty on the grass,
Strength remained on me,
I trudged to the evil pasture,
Oxen to the meadow,
I turned the herd to the middle,
Who came to take the oxen,
Brother came to take the oxen,
Still, a golden shoulder,
Iron claws like hail,
Words golden-haired,
I asked my brother:
What is the noise in Father’s room?
What is the commotion in Mother’s house?
What is being done at home?
The brother’s voice answered,
What is it, what is it, my sister?
Are you selling the house?
Are you giving it away out of grace?
Young brother,
Are you asked for elsewhere?
And how much is being offered?
The brother understood immediately and answered,
Oh, sister, low grass,
A small drop of dew,
Sister, a big eye,
From your mouth they will ask,
From your mouth much will be offered,
A thousand tons of wheat,
A hundred bushels of rye in the barn,
Father still has oxen,
Mother cows for milking,
Brother a riding horse,
Sister large bundles of straw,
A little one long aprons,
Käl (girl) quick shoes,
I understood and replied,
Let it be, let it be, I curse,
Let it be, let it be, uncle,
When you will go plowing the field,
May your oxen sink into the bushel,
One to the other side,
This is what you have become to me,
Let it be, let it be, grandmother,
When you will go milking the cow,
The first to water the nose
The second to bleed,
The third to milk the breast,
The fourth to whiten the butter,
This is what you have become to me,
Young brother,
When you go to war,
May the horse under you run wild,
The saddle on your back be covered with moss,
May you get sunstroke,
May your hat freeze on your head,
This is what you have become to me,
Dear sister,
When you go to dip the bread,
May you have large bundles of straw,
May you burn a long apron,
This is what you have become to me,
Little girl,
When you go to herd the flock,
May your shoe heels break,
May your shoe tips disappear,
May your shoe laces snap,
This is what you have become to me.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
If I were to go home,
No one would ask for a doorstep,
Nor speak of the eaves rain,
The straw stalks would not talk,
The manure heap would not tell,
The room would start beating,
The floor would start pounding,
The bellows would roar.
The stove would refuse to speak.
At home, all six are angry,
At home, all five are furious,
At home, all seven are confused.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Ann was a young girl,  
She went to the barn to feed the cattle.  
To offer them fodder.  
Ann's brooches broke from her chest,  
Ann's beads broke from her neck,  
Ann's cross-shaped heels broke.  
Ann goes to the blacksmith's workshop:  
"Blacksmith, dear blacksmith,  
Take my beads and fix them,  
Clean the silver beads,  
Adorn the cross-shaped heels."  
The blacksmith hears and replies:  
"If you come to me,  
And come to my son:  
Then I will fix the beads,  
Clean the silver beads,  
Adorn the cross-shaped heels."  
I will not come to you,  
Nor to your son:  
Blacksmith's children are pitch black,  
Soot-covered, scoundrels.  
The blacksmith himself is as dark as the world.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Little Jan, little sled,  
Made a sled in the forest,  
In the damp pine forest,  
In the dense spruce thicket.  
Every noon he chopped wood chips,  
Every six he made a spoke,  
Every day he put a hoop on.  
When he had finished the sled,  
Then he went sledding:  
Put it on gray shafts,  
Jerked ear to ear,  
Ran like a black river,  
Girls ran after him,  
Kaie ran up to the heels,  
Mai was left behind running,  
Anne's mouth stayed open wide.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Indeed, the plant lads wanted me  
And the nettle boys caught me,  
The ravine drivers courted me,  
The Maidu boys were delighted,  
The old woman didn’t give, the old man didn’t allow,  
And I myself didn’t go.  
The old woman didn’t give a sly kiss,  
The old man didn’t give a bare kiss,  
Seeing my eyes, the hungry one,  
Hearing with the ear, the schoolboy,  
That I was successful at haymaking,  
A slender maker of flails,  
A clumsy stacker,  
I made big stacks in the marsh,  
Slender stacks on the field,  
Strong stooks on the meadow.  
The stacks were visible from the secret place,  
The legs of the cellar stretched,  
The stooks seemed all the way to Narva,  
The Germans of Narva laughed at this,  
The Viru gentlemen were amazed  
That the stooks were made by women,  
The stacks gathered by daughters,  
The heap of six maidens,  
The scorn was of five lazy boys.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Indeed, the plant lads wanted me  
And the nettle boys caught me,  
The ravine drivers courted me,  
The Maidu boys were delighted,  
The old woman didn’t give, the old man didn’t allow,  
And I myself didn’t go.  
The old woman didn’t give a sly kiss,  
The old man didn’t give a bare kiss,  
Seeing my eyes, the hungry one,  
Hearing with the ear, the schoolboy,  
That I was successful at haymaking,  
A slender maker of flails,  
A clumsy stacker,  
I made big stacks in the marsh,  
Slender stacks on the field,  
Strong stooks on the meadow.  
The stacks were visible from the secret place,  
The legs of the cellar stretched,  
The stooks seemed all the way to Narva,  
The Germans of Narva laughed at this,  
The Viru gentlemen were amazed  
That the stooks were made by women,

label: Töölaulud
translation:
This is not boys' wedding drink,  
Nor the poor man's tavern drink,  
These are slightly strong drinks  
Brewed by the lady of the house.  
Now the meals have been eaten,  
Now the drinks have been drunk,  
Only the cook's praise  
And the cook's wife’s thanks remain.  
Good is the broth the cook has made,  
Salt is added just right,  
The herbs wisely balanced,  
Brought from Sweden,  
Salts from Germany,  
Peppers from Russia.  
The herbs speak Swedish,  
The salts speak German,  
The peppers speak Russian.  
Thanks to the head of the family,  
Thanks to the lady of the house,  
Thanks for the food and the drink,  
For the good send-off:  
The meals have been eaten,  
The drinks have been drunk.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I rushed to come early,  
To dry before dawn,  
To wash my head before the day.  
When the time came, I lagged behind,  
The day rose, I went back to sleep.  
While the month of distress lulled me,  
A woolen carpet pressed down,  
Sleep took the little fool,  
Sleeping for the little drunkard,  
Lying down for the little bird.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The field is ending, ending  
The little island is running out  
The small field is sparse  
So that we row to the end  
And we reach the island  
The red beer is finished  
There are horned buns on the island  
Gentle sister Anneke  
Listen, dear Maiekene  
Maybe the slave bonds are ending  
In fine dust, there is a long whip  
There is a slim sugar on the island.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Niece Gingerbread
Praised her secret trick
That her trick was from a linden tree
The tip was from an apple tree
She had dozens of suitors,
One was Intu from Hiiumaa
Another Antu from Harjumaa
Third the village blacksmith’s son
Fourth from Neeruti meadow
Fifth Villem the hooligan
Seventh the blacksmith’s apprentice
Eighth the fisherman’s son
Ninth the upper Ants
Tenth the king of Kuuremaa
I have golden mine cakes
Silver barn doors
I build bridges over marshes
Barriers over rivers,
Where a woman’s foot does not sound
The woman’s foot on an ox’s hoof
A maiden’s silk boot barn
A horse’s golden stable.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Niece Gingerbread
Praised her secret trick
That her trick was from a linden tree
The tip was from an apple tree
She had dozens of suitors,
One was Intu from Hiiumaa
Another Antu from Harjumaa
Third the village blacksmith’s son
Fourth from Neeruti meadow
Fifth Villem the hooligan
Seventh the blacksmith’s apprentice
Eighth the fisherman’s son
Ninth the upper Ants
Tenth the king of Kuuremaa
I have golden mine cakes
Silver barn doors
I build bridges over marshes
Barriers over rivers,
Where a woman’s foot does not sound
The woman’s foot on an ox’s hoof
A maiden’s silk boot barn
A horse’s golden stable.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I, Madis, son of Malle  
Tõnis, son of the second wife  
I know how to put weir traps in the river  
I place the mouth of the weir towards the manor  
The tail of the weir towards the Germans.  
From there I bring many fish:  
Pike, eels,  
Wide salmon, bream  
Whitefish and big fish  
From there dad can pat  
Mom can smack her mouth  
And always have something to eat for oneself.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Thank God!
My cheeks are full, my head is full,
My cheeks are full of sugars,
My head is full of nuts,
My lips are meaty,
I'm fed up with chicken eggs.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Our men and Miku's men,  
Vadu and Vainu's men,  
Lokuta and Toravere boys:  
They built bridges over the swamps,  
They built dams over the lands;  
So that the woman would not wet her foot,  
Woman's foot, ox's hoof,  
Kalev's gold horseshoe,  
Pig's silky tail.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
"Let’s pull, let’s drag,
Let’s hurry, let’s row,
So that we reach the end
And sink into the swamp
And arrive at the island’s point,
To the long edge of the field.
At the end there’s red beer,
From the edge comes bitter bread."

- "I will reap, but I am tired,
I do not understand, then I have it!
My hands are tied,
Fingers bound behind,
Thumbs pulled like thread,
Or are these ribbons given by Anne,
Words uttered by Kaia cunningly,
Words spoken by Maie sweetly.
Take the cakes from the pasture path,
The bear eats the cakes from the pasture path."

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I slept with a melancholy mind,  
And would nap on a sharp foot.  
Worry does not let me sleep,  
Sleep does not bring new dreams,  
Sleeping in a linen shirt.  
Sleep sends wandering houses,  
Sleeping sends marching houses,  
I think of yawning by the stove,  
In front of the flame, moving it  
I yawn into the gooseberry bush  
I move the sheet to the flame.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Surely I hurry, surely I pull,
Surely I rush, surely I scavenge,
There is no end to it,
Our rye breads are finished,
Pieces of white bread in the field.
I do not understand what I have,
Whether my hands are tied,
Fingers bound with silk,
Fingers tied with thread,
My sickle is made by a foul blacksmith,
My iron forged from corpses.
Who is waiting for me at home?
The dog is waiting for me at home.
Who is calling me into the house?
The cat is calling me into the house.
I bring the dog crumbs,
For the cat, meat scraps.
No one is seen to give
Lunch bread to be cut.
Viisu parish eats five times,
Kuura parish eats six times,
Seinapalu seven times,
We still have no meal,
Our children no lunch.
I take it in hand and get angry,
I throw the sickle here in place,
Fists full scattered,
I throw the leftovers to the mouse.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Kärt was quick to spin:
by noon, she had spun half the yarn,
by evening, a whole skein.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Our granny has several hats,  
The village granny has ten hats,  
The sauna granny has a hundred hats.  
The first is a hay hat,  
The second is a rye ear hat,  
The third is a barn animal hat,  
The fourth is a cow milking hat,  
The fifth is a rain rod hat,  
The sixth is a bad weather hat,  
The seventh is a manure hauling hat,  
The eighth is a cattle driving hat,  
The ninth is a night hat,  
The tenth is a visiting hat.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The herders, the hungry herders
killed the village bull-calf,
the bull of Laeksaare meadow,
the black bull of Mustassaare,
the dark bull of Tõrvassaare.
The lads placed the guts in a swamp hole,
the legs in Jaani spring,
the head under the linden tree root,
the eyes in an ant hill,
the ears on top of a tall stump.
The tail was placed on the barn manure,
the horns on the sauna roof.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
What lulls this sleep to rest,  
What tethers the act of sleeping.  
Sleep does not grant new growth to the moon,  
Sleeping clothes the earth.  
Sleep sends one wandering,  
Sleeping sends one marching,  
Treading a path ahead.  
Those who sleep are fire starters,  
Those who sleep are house keepers,  
The slave child does not sleep soundly:  
comes tired from work,  
comes weary from the field,  
withered from the haystack...

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Oh, that poor shepherd boy!  
Water below him, water above him,  
water between his toes.  
There is no dry place anywhere,  
mosquito bite on the shin,  
and across the finger onto the knee.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Sleep, come through the door,  
fall on the child,  
let the eye rest on the table.  
But sleep comes howling,  
singing through the groves.  
Heaps of straw on his shoulder,  
a bundle of oats in his pocket;  
he seeks to let the dreamy child sleep,  
the little sleeping boy.  
But sleep says: wander, wander,  
sleeping: march, march.  
Sleep does not give a new hat,  
sleeping, a shining shirt to the ground.

label: Lastelaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Sleep, come through the door,  
fall on the child,  
let the eye rest on the table.  
But sleep comes howling,  
singing through the groves.  
Heaps of straw on his shoulder,  
a bundle of oats in his pocket;  
he seeks to let the dreamy child sleep,  
the little sleeping boy.  
But sleep says: wander, wander,  
sleeping: march, march.  
Sleep does not give a new hat,  
sleeping, a shining shirt to the ground.

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Our road is unmeasured,  
the planks unlaid,  
the milestones unset.  
Our road leads toward the sea,  
toward the great harbor,  
where crown ships gather,  
crown ships, merchant ships,  
and also large warships,  
and also long sailing ships.  
We raise the masts,  
as one would stack wood in the forest,  
we raise the sails,  
like aprons on women;  
we pull the ropes,  
like strings on a kantele...

label: Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Our road is unmeasured,  
the planks unlaid,  
the milestones unset.  
Our road leads toward the sea,  
toward the great harbor,  
where crown ships gather,  
crown ships, merchant ships,  
and also large warships,  
and also long sailing ships.  
We raise the masts,  
as one would stack wood in the forest,  
we raise the sails,  
like aprons on women;  
we pull the ropes,  
like strings on a kantele...

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The piglet comes, the bonfire blazes,  
the woman makes a cake, I go to the herd,  
the granny makes a bull, I go to the forest,  
but A'a drives the bulls to the barn,  
I twist the bulls to the herd,  
but I drive the cows to the alder grove.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Sleep does not bring new wisdom,  
sleeping is earthly clothing;  
but sleep makes one wander,  
sleeping makes one march.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Who binds these sheaves?
Let's go cut the oats,
I will cut, I have the strength,
Neither my front moves forward,
Nor does the field run away,
The oat field shrinks,
The grain plot decreases.
We cut the mighty sheaves,
We place the sturdy staffs,
We bind together the treasure
For the tie of this field,
For the wide layer outside,
The field weavers come,
The field runners appear.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Sister dear, can't you hear,
Sister beloved, do you hear!
Don't stay on the sleepy track,
The carriage of sleeping.
Sleep's gate to the cradle,
The carriage of dreaming to the slope,
The carriage of sleeping is strong!
Sleep doesn't bring any new cubes,
Sleeping is a shining shirt
Sleep sends wandering,
Sleeping is marching,
Dreaming is tuning the path.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
End, end, little field.  
Come to an end, little island.  
Little and little field.  
We cut down the strong sheaves,  
Put the best ears together,  
The plumpest and most wholesome.  
Then we go to help with the grain,  
With the fine shelter overhead.  
We no longer rush because of eating and drinking,  
Nor because of lack of fish,  
Nor because of rushing for rye bread.  
The grain needs help.  
The fine shelter is needed.  
When the cutting ends here,  
Then the midday bread ends here.  
Small slices are small,  
Furs and skins last longer.

label: Töölaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let there be a healthy little bull,  
Another dear little lady.  
Who had made stakes in the field  
And had held a wedding in the meadow.  
It was she who killed that bull,  
Who has never walked the earth,  
Nor labored in the fields for a hundred years.  
Ten cubits of side length,  
A hundred spans of horn length.  
The feeding piece was a feeding table,  
The garden bed a bench to sit on.  
The alder had broad leaves,  
The birch was a dear companion.  
The willow a long pipe blower.

label: Töölaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let there be a healthy little bull,  
Another dear little lady.  
Who had made stakes in the field  
And had held a wedding in the meadow.  
It was she who killed that bull,  
Who has never walked the earth,  
Nor labored in the fields for a hundred years.  
Ten cubits of side length,  
A hundred spans of horn length.  
The feeding piece was a feeding table,  
The garden bed a bench to sit on.  
The alder had broad leaves,  
The birch was a dear companion.  
The willow a long pipe blower.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Cloudy-cloudy patch hat  
I used to have several hats  
One was a hay hat,  
Another a hat for going to the market,  
A third was a suitor’s hat,  
The fourth was a cow-milking hat,  
The fifth was a rain-day hat,  
The sixth was a hat for bad weather,  
The seventh was a poop-smell hat,  
The eighth was a hat for going to the herd,  
The ninth was a night hat,  
The tenth was a hat for going to the village.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
If not even given a bite to eat  
Not even a morsel offered;  
Then I will take it and get angry  
Throw bundles here and there  
Fistfuls scattered about.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
One thousand one hundred
There we had thick forest
Bent birches grew
There I cut wheel wood
Father’s ox yoke wood
Old mare’s rein wood.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:
The whole world was sighing  
The whole people were resting  
We, the poor, were walking  
Our roads were unknown  
Our lands were unmeasured  
Ditches un-dug at the edges  
Bridge pillars unbuilt  
The boy's path leads to the girl  
Our path leads to the sea  
To that big harbor  
And onto that ship’s deck  
I patch the ship, mend the ship  
But I make the ship whole  
I put sails on the ship  
Like aprons on the bride  
When I start to leave  
The storm begins to come  
Water rolls down the eyes  
But the waves break the plank  
Eyebrows drip with tears  
Then the young girls began to cry  
Regretting the wide shirts  
Mourning the linen shirts  
The young girls also cried for Sõnn  
Sõnn, when the ship’s sails appeared  
At last the ship’s flag flickers  
The ship’s journey shines.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud sõjast ja nekrutist
translation:

Sõnn, when the ship’s sails appeared

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Understand, sisters, understand, brothers,  
What is being done to us there?  
In the shade of Viru spruces,  
Behind the oaks of Harju.  
There our ship is being built,  
The ship was completed,  
It was taken to the water.  
Old rowers were placed,  
The old rowed, heads trembled.  
Young rowers were placed,  
The young rowed, the sails reached.  
They rowed the ship below Narva,  
From below Narva to Vyborg.  
From Vyborg to the Russian path.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud abielust
translation:
Jaan made a sled in the forest  
Gold for the skull in the trough,  
Silver into the ice down below,  
Every noon he hammered a board,  
Every evening he set a peg,  
Every day he placed a stake,  
Every month he dug a spoke.  
Then the sled was finished—  
What does the sled need?  
It needs ice, it needs a horse,  
It needs a bridle, it needs a trace;  
Inside, a silk maiden's dress,  
From Kalev, a precious jewel.  
Then he goes to the father-in-law's yard  
And goes to the mother-in-law's yard,  
Under the mother-in-law's window.  
Thunder rumbles, mother-in-law  
Come out and see  
If I am likeable,  
As a daughter-in-law in apron.  
If I am likeable,  
A daughter-in-law in apron,  
Send me back with the sled.  
Now my horses will take me,  
My steeds will walk,  
My mounts will be harnessed.  
Called for a household helper,  
Father-in-law, to help the mother-in-law,  
A young maiden for chores,  
The housekeeper daughter.  
I do not have such a helper  
As a household helper,  
A young maiden for chores,  
The housekeeper daughter.  
Father never gives a helper,  
Brother will not lend the sled's harness.  
I take my horse,  
My saddle with saddle blanket,  
A shaggy whip,  
A rowan twig for the lash,  
I strike the iron from the peace,  
On the horse, a ruble coin,  
Then the maiden comes in response.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where the three of us go  
out five of us go  
We go  
to Maria’s hayfield,  
I take a scythe, I take two,  
I start to take the third,  
what I found under the scythe,  
an eagle I found under the scythe,  
what under the eagle’s wing,  
an axe under the eagle’s wing,  
what in the axe’s blade,  
a splinter in the axe’s blade,  
what will be made from that splinter,  
they will cut John’s barn,  
what will go inside that barn,  
a bed inside that barn,  
what will go inside that bed,  
straw inside that bed,  
what goes on top of that straw,  
linen on top of that straw,  
what goes on top of that linen,  
pillows on top of that linen,  
what goes on top of those pillows,  
four young maidens,  
one returns home with a golden belt,  
the second,  
the third is a kitten in the hand,  
the fourth cries for a young man.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where the three of us go  
out five of us go  
We go  
to Maria’s hayfield,  
I take a scythe, I take two,  
I start to take the third,  
what I found under the scythe,  
an eagle I found under the scythe,  
what under the eagle’s wing,  
an axe under the eagle’s wing,  
what in the axe’s blade,  
a splinter in the axe’s blade,  
what will be made from that splinter,  
they will cut John’s barn,  
what will go inside that barn,  
a bed inside that barn,  
what will go inside that bed,  
straw inside that bed,  
what goes on top of that straw,  
linen on top of that straw,  
what goes on top of that linen,  
pillows on top of that linen,  
what goes on top of those pillows,  
four young maidens,  
one returns home with a golden belt,  
the second,  
the third is a kitten in the hand,  
the fourth cries for a young man.

label: Töölaulud
translation: I happily thresh the grain.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Ants was from Harju, Jaan was from Järva,  
Mart was a little low boy.  
He made ships from swan feathers,  
From swan feathers and poplar boards;  
The ship's masts from rowan wood,  
The ship's rudders from turnip wood;  
I sew the ship, I patch the ship,  
I put on the mast stain,  
Just as on a spruce twig stain.  
I put on the thread stain,  
Just like on a kantele string;  
I put on the sail stain,  
Just like on a maiden's apron;  
I send the ships to Finland,  
Half of the ships to the Land of Cranberries,  
The big ships to Germany.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Oh, those gentlemen of ours  
Our ladies are better  
Young gentlemen with golden hair  
Ladies in lace aprons  
Honeyberry mamselles  
They killed that bull  
That has never walked on earth  
Has not plowed for a hundred years  
Has not tilled the field up to the knees  
Then they held a wedding on the field  
Organized work at the far end  
From the bull, the pot was filled with meat to the handle  
All around quite fatty  
The crow got a bellyful  
The willow bird got a flapful  
The raven was filled with meat up to the throat

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Sirise-Sirise, little sickle!  
Clanging crooked iron,  
Whoever’s sickle reaches us first,  
That groom will buy a hat.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Thank you, thank God.  
Now I gathered the field dry,  
Under clear weather,  
Under a hot day.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Hiss, hiss, little grasshopper,  
Creak, creak, crooked iron,  
Whoever reaches the sickle,  
Will have the pitchfork come to their head.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Let our lord be healthy,  
Who has held weddings in the field,  
Weddings in the field, haystacks in the meadow,  
Let our lady be healthy,  
Who has held weddings in the field,  
Weddings in the field, haystacks in the meadow,  
Silver-breasted young ladies,  
Golden-nosed young gentlemen,  
Copper-nosed old gentlemen.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
End, end, little field,  
come to an end, little island!

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Eevakene, veevakene  
boiled a pot of hot broth,  
burned the servant’s mouth,  
the household son’s long mustache,  
his own child’s wide jaws.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Together, together, little cream!
On the child's piece of bread,
On the happiness of the herdsman's child...

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud laulust
translation:
Should I burn the field without a household,  
Or burn the wasteland without a crowd?  
There is no singer in the field –  
Today I harvest this field,  
Tomorrow I harvest another field,  
The day after tomorrow the one beyond.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Oh, poor Tõnu of the flock,  
I always received harsh words.  
Why did you stay at home for so long?  
I admired the iris buds,  
Raised kitten cubs,  
Nurtured puppy pups.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
A woman makes the breads lumpy,
family breads elongated.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
But evening falls on the forehead  
and Sunday lasts longer.  
Others go to church,  
I, poor, go to the pasture.  
Others hear the church bell,  
I hear the cowbell in the meadow.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
But evening is coming on Monday  
and a longer little Sunday.  
Others go on the way to church,  
I, poor one, on the way to the pasture.  
Others hear the church bell,  
I hear the bell of the cow in the meadow.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I rush, I pull,
I hurry, I steal,
it never ends.
Our rye breads are gone,
only pieces of white bread remain.
I do not understand what is wrong with me.
Are my hands tied,
are my fingers bound with silk,
are my fingers entwined with thread,
my sickle made by a lousy smith,
my iron forged by dead men.
Who waits for me at home?
A dog waits for me at home.
Who calls me into the house?
A cat calls me into the house.
I bring crusts for the dog,
meat scraps for the cat.
No one can be seen giving,
cutting bread for lunch,
Viisu parish eats five times,
Kuura parish eats six times,
Seinapalu seven times,
while we have nothing to eat,
our children have no lunch.
I take it in my hands and get angry,
I throw the sickle here,
handfuls scattered,
scraps I give to the mouse.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
If only there were a wide field for the lazy  
and a long field for the weak,  
they would not allow work to be done  
nor let harshness come.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
If it were my being,  
and secondly my doing,  
I would build bridges over the swamps,  
causeways over the rivers.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
If it were my being,  
if it were my doing,  
I would build bridges over the swamps,  
causeways over the rivers,  
where a woman does not dip her foot,  
a woman’s foot, nor a finger,  
the hoofprint of a golden apple.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Uius-tuius, a tufted cap,  
we have several caps:  
one was a hayfield cap,  
another a log-taking cap,  
the third a pile-animal cap,  
the fourth a cow-milking cap,  
the fifth was still a rain cap,  
the sixth ...(?) ...  
the seventh a fodder-feeding cap,  
the eighth a herding cap,  
the ninth was a night cap,  
the tenth a visiting cap.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Together, together, little cream!  
Together for the dog's luck,  
for the poor child's toil!

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Sizzle, sizzle, little cicada,  
jingle, little bent wire!  
Whoever gets in front of your path,  
the master hunts against them.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Together, together, little cream,  
old granny's berry,  
mistress's little milk!

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Harvest, harvest, little field,  
when you are harvested, I will leave you here,  
leave you here to grow food,  
to grow food, to go to market.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Thanks be to the great Creator,  
honor to the brave God,  
for not complaining about dry weather,  
for the clear weather standing still,  
for the skies not being laden.  
Let us be firmly bound,  
let us be gathered on dry land,  
put together in a better place.  
I have gathered a precious field,  
a silk edge in the bond,  
a wide field in the layer.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Flee, field, flee,  
flee, field, to the shed,  
jump, field, to the grove,  
fly, field, to the alder wood!  
In the field, binder, bring the fire,  
under the bent iron shoulder.  
Buzz, buzz, little sickle,  
rattle, little bent rod!

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Whoever responds first,  
buys a hat for the groom...

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Together, together, little cream,  
old granny’s berry,  
let it come from the sky, let it go to the churn!

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Kiisa Jüri was a handsome man,  
he made his (pipe) stem from alder,  
a small hat from the top of a birch,  
a little tuft from a spruce grove.  
He did not neigh like a donkey  
nor shout like a bull,  
the riders neighed,  
the herdsmen shouted.

label: Töölaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Thanks to the master,  
who held a wedding in the field,  
a wedding in the field, stakes in the meadow.  
Who called me to the sad place,  
who drove me to the forest clearing.  
He did not kill that elk,  
who has a thousand spans of back,  
a hundred spans of long antlers,  
whose flesh was motionless,  
whose stomach was unwavering,  
whose fat was untouched.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Our gentlemen were good,  
Our ladies even better,  
Young gentlemen picked gold,  
Silver and lead were plentiful,  
They held weddings out in the field,  
And organized communal work there.  
They did not kill the ox  
That had never roamed the world,  
Had not plowed for a hundred years,  
Nor tilled for ten years.  
Buzz, buzz, little sickle,  
Clink, clink, crooked iron,  
Whoever gets their sickle in first,  
Their bridegroom arrives first.  
Let the field remain for the herd to graze,  
The meadow to hold the sheep.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Grandmother told me to card wool  
Grandfather told me to pick the ticks out of wool  
I didn’t want to card wool  
I didn’t want to pick the ticks out of wool  
I took the wool to Viru  
And the ticks to a wise woman,  
Virre made sashes from the wool  
The wise woman made narrow ribbons from the ticks.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Patch the ship, patch the ship  
Patch the ship with bird bones  
Patch the ship with crow bones  
Fix the ship with board bones  
Put covers on with bear bones  
Floor it with elk bones  
Doors with wolf bones  
Oars with duck bones.  
The ship was finished  
Roll the ship onto the water  
The water carried it down to the city of Riga  
There were quarrelsome maidens there  
In front were quarrelsome young men  
At the back quarrelsome elders  
In between were quarrelsome poor children.  
Drive off the young men  
Step down the elders  
Beat down the youth  
Take some enemies from time to time  
From poor comes a thief  
From rich, a keeper in the village.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Patch the ship, patch the ship  
Patch the ship with bird bones  
Patch the ship with crow bones  
Fix the ship with board bones  
Put covers on with bear bones  
Floor it with elk bones  
Doors with wolf bones  
Oars with duck bones.  
The ship was finished  
Roll the ship onto the water  
The water carried it down to the city of Riga  
There were quarrelsome maidens there  
In front were quarrelsome young men  
At the back quarrelsome elders  
In between were quarrelsome poor children.  
Drive off the young men  
Step down the elders  
Beat down the youth  
Take some enemies from time to time  
From poor comes a thief  
From rich, a keeper in the village.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I fell asleep on the hill of dreams  
I ended up below a steep shore  
Here is a room made of tulips  
Eaves made of curls.  
Here is a ceiling of glass  
Walls of silk ribbons  
Repairs here are made of paper.  
Here I lie down to rest  
To rest for a moment  
To doze for an hour or two  
To strengthen myself with a scrap,  
Now is the time for warmth  
I will rise with the morning  
Then will come winter snow and cold  
My horse is still unfound  
German sleigh not prepared.  
White reins disregarded.  
Then I went to find the stable  
I found the horse in the field  
The German sleigh on the manure  
White reins at the end of the pole.  
Then I put on the mouse-gray ones  
Pulled the crane-neck over the ear  
Then I went to drive on the bog  
And to make a path to the field  
Whether bridges were made over the bog  
Paths made to the field  
Bridges made from onions  
Half the bridges from carrots  
No one gets their foot wet there  
No one gets their foot wet, no ox gets dirty  
The rooster does not wear golden spurs  
The hen does not have copper combs  
Then I rode along the Finnish bridge  
My horse underneath like a furnace  
On the groom like this day  
Bridge beams for the breakers  
Under beams for the cracklers  
Groom’s hat for the drummers  
Wide ribbons waving.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Tuit, tuit, a cap for a novice,
I used to have several caps before:
one was a hay cap,
another a threshing cap,
a third also a courting cap,
the fourth a milking cap,
the fifth a rainy day cap,
the sixth was an ill-weather cap,
the seventh a manure-carrying cap,
the eighth a shepherding cap,
the ninth was a night cap,
the tenth a visiting cap.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Sisters, dear ones,
I had only one brother,
but he went off to war.
When my brother was leaving,
I did try to teach him:
Brother dear, beloved,
do not go to war in front,
not in front of war, not behind war,
stay in the middle of the war.
The ones in front are thrown away,
the ones behind are killed,
the ones in the middle come home.
Then my brother came home.
Dear one, brother dear,
we patch the ship, we mend the ship,
we send the ship to the waves,
we take reeds for the masts,
sails on top like those aprons,
like those aprons of our maiden.
Row the ship, reach the ship,
row to the big sea,
from there row down to the city of Riga,
bring back a lively woman from there,
from beyond the port,
who has not slept with men,
who has not been with boys.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Hiss, hiss, little sickle,  
rattle, crooked little iron!  
Whoever’s sickle reaches here first,  
that groom will buy a hat.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Sleep does not bring a new moon  
Sleeping in the shirt to the ground  
But sleep sends one to wander  
Sleeping sends one to march  
To trample the path of drowsiness.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Who, who little girl  
send food to the young man,  
men are coming from the forest,  
boys are coming to chop wood,  
children are coming to pick leaves.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Hey hey little milk pail,  
send food for the strong man,  
the men are coming back from the forest,  
the boys are cutting wood,  
the children are picking bark.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Who, which little boy  
goes to get bread with a handle,  
men come from the forest to hunt,  
boys to chop wood,  
children to pick sticks.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
But I am Matsi Malle's son,  
Äbarik Anne's son,  
I know how to make fish traps,  
I know how to put fish traps in the river.  
I put the fish traps toward the manor,  
The tails of the traps facing the German side.  
Then pike can be caught without counting,  
Burbot without measuring,  
Then you can slap the mammal,  
Pat the old priest.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Tattered and torn girl’s hat,  
Our granny has several hats,  
One was a nightcap,  
Another a work hat,  
The third a home hat,  
The fourth was a hay hat,  
The fifth a grain threshing hat,  
The sixth a village visit hat,  
The seventh a pig feeding hat,  
The eighth for driving chickens,  
The ninth a very beautiful one,  
The tenth a church hat.  
Surely our granny has many hats?!

label: Töölaulud
translation:
If it were my being,  
If it were my doing,  
I would surely know what I would do:  
I would build bridges over the swamps,  
Drums on the roads,  
Where the awakened would not scatter dung,  
Would not scatter dung, woman’s foot,  
Shoulders golden horseshoes.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Eat the herd, with the herd,
drink the herd, with the herd,
lay the herd down to rest,
let it rest for the time being,
until the sock lace forms.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Little Hellake, little brother,  
make me a wheel-sled.  
I will roll it into the cold,  
in winter to the oven's warmth.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The shepherd lost the flock,  
sent me to search for them,  
in the foggy morning,  
in the dewy evening.  
The fog ruined the new moon for me,  
the dew the dear coat.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Töölaulud
translation:
I don’t have to go,  
don’t, dear, disappear,  
don’t leave the people’s homeland,  
the better place.  
I knew the life where I lived,  
the fertile soil where I grew,  
I did not know a home,  
how I must go home,  
to the miserable howlers.  
The home was angrily scolding,  
the home was fiercely harsh,  
the home was confusing,  
the home was jealous.  
Leenu traps from the bottom of the barrel,  
My uncle gives encouraging words,  
Jüri ticks with bitterness,  
Who sings my kind song?  
Liisu sings the kind song.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Töölaulud
translation:
I don’t have to go,  
don’t, dear, disappear,  
don’t leave the people’s homeland,  
the better place.  
I knew the life where I lived,  
the fertile soil where I grew,  
I did not know a home,  
how I must go home,  
to the miserable howlers.  
The home was angrily scolding,  
the home was fiercely harsh,  
the home was confusing,  
the home was jealous.  
Leenu traps from the bottom of the barrel,  
My uncle gives encouraging words,  
Jüri ticks with bitterness,  
Who sings my kind song?  
Liisu sings the kind song.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Hiss, crackle, sickle,  
Rattle of crooked iron.  
Whoever runs into the sickle first,  
That one will become a man.  
---  
We finish this field,  
We reduce this gap.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Viru wool, Arju flax,
bring them to our Kärt.
Kärt was quick to spin
and flowed to walk to the cottage.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Sleep does not bring a new moon,  
sleeping in a shirt to the ground.  
Sleep made me howl,  
sleeping made me march.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
A lazy girl’s daughter,
She didn’t bother to spin wool,
She didn’t want to tease the wool;
She took the wool to the village,
The tufts to the village grandmother.
I sat in the corner,
Wondering when the wool would be spun—
At twilight the wool was taken,
Smoke fading as the time came.
My salts melted,
Bread rolls waiting,
The space between slices settled,
Thread holders tumbling.
I throw wool pins into the flax,
I lifted pins from the flax,—
Surely there the mice wander,
Cats weave the cloth,
Lizards twitch here and there,
Rats count the threads there.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Ketra, Liisu  
Ketra, Liisu, grandmother’s daughter

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Indeed, the tub smiths tried to catch me,
Tub smiths, muff smiths.
They wanted a blacksmith from me.
In my mouth I melt iron,
On my tongue I boil it shut.
My teeth are for blacksmithing,
My jawbones are bellows.
The woman didn't give, didn't set a fire,
And I didn't want either.

label: Laulud noorrahva elust  ; Töölaulud
translation:

label: Töölaulud
translation:
A young girl, a maiden,  
A slender angel girl,  
Comes to me for clothes,  
Steals clothes onto her face,  
Jewelry for herself,  
Fine shirts for the boy.  
If you don’t have linen  
Buy a dress from me,  
Make a pocket on the edge,  
Sew on wool by the nail’s length.  
Then comes to me for clothes,  
Steals clothes onto her face,  
Makes jewelry for herself,  
Fine shirts for the boy.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Flee to the field  
Flee to the field, to the willow thicket  
Fly to the field, to the alder grove.  
Jump to the field, to the birch forest.  
The field binder is coming  
Under the crooked iron's edge.  
Cut the field to the end,  
Gather it all up.  
If you don’t cut it to the end,  
You will stay here growing,  
Here to gather cranes,  
To sound the skylarks' song,  
To come upon the swallows.  
The cranes do not eat the hay,  
The skylarks do not gather hay,  
The swallows do not take the straw.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Thanks be to the great Creator,  
Glory to the merciful God,  
For not complaining about dry weather,  
For not standing in clear weather,  
For not sending hail from the sky.  
Let us gather on dry land,  
Let us bind ourselves clearly,  
To put things together better.  
I have put together a golden field  
In a binding of silk field,  
In a stack of a wide meadow.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Let us go along this winding path,  
This carved trail  
Where God has walked before,  
Where Mary came to the land,  
Holy cross on her chest,  
On her chest, came heavy;  
On her chest, heavier still  
On her chest came more heavily.  
What was left again on top?  
Gold was left again on top.  
What fell upon the step?  
Silver fell upon the step.  
Who madly took the gold?  
I madly took the gold.  
Who wickedly took the silver?  
I wickedly took the silver,  
I locked the gold in the chest,  
Turned the silver into bracelets,  
Turned behind the bracelets.  
I took it home to my brother,  
Set it on the brother’s table,  
On a fine jug’s cover.  
Swept the brother’s table,  
Dripped from the pewter jug.  
The jug broke, the cover broke,  
The brother’s wide table broke.  
“Brother, dear little brother,  
Take the gold to Viljandi,  
Take the silver to Tarvastu,  
Viljandi has a skillful blacksmith,  
Tarvastu has a wise smith,  
Make a ring for my finger,  
Make a fibula for my finger.”

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The herd grazes in the yard,  
Mother carries the little bird.  
Come here, little herd,  
Here on the big feed,  
Here on the bare ground.  
Here the herd eats without protection,  
Without taking the rod,  
Without breaking a tree branch,  
Without moving an alder branch,  
Without peeling the birch bark.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let’s go to the meadow at dusk,  
To Maria’s hayfield.  
I take a bow, took two bows  
To gather the third,  
To name the fourth.  
What did I find under the bow?  
I found an eagle under the bow.  
What is under the eagle’s wing?  
A whole eagle under the wing.  
What is whole in the nostril?  
A bone whole in the nostril.  
What is carved from that bone?  
John’s barn is carved.  
What is inside the barn?  
Four young maidens.  
One wears a golden belt,  
Another rattles an iron belt,  
The third has a cat-o’-nine-tails,  
The fourth weeps for a young man.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where do the three of us go,  
Over the field, the five of us!  
We go to the sunset song  
To Marialla’s hayfield.  
I took the bow, left the other  
I began to take the third,  
I should start the fourth,  
I should take the fifth tune.  
What did I find under the bow?  
I found an eagle under the bow.  
What is under the eagle’s wing?  
A chick under the eagle’s wing.  
What is a chip under the chick’s beak?  
A chip is under the chick’s beak.  
What is made from that chip?  
Jaani’s barn is made.  
The base beam is set.  
What is inside Jaani’s barn?  
Salve (ointment) is inside Jaani’s barn.  
What is on the edge of the salve?  
A bed is on the edge of the salve.  
What is in the bed?  
Straw is in the bed.  
What is on the straw?  
A quilt on the straw,  
What is on the quilt?  
Pillows on the quilt.  
What is on the pillows?  
Four young maidens.  
One wears a golden belt,  
The second wears a green ribbon,  
The third wears a wavy one,  
The fourth weeps for a young man.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where do the three of us go,  
Over the field, the five of us!  
We go to the sunset song  
To Marialla’s hayfield.  
I took the bow, left the other  
I began to take the third,

Straw is in the bed.

A quilt on the straw,  
What is on the quilt?  
Pillows on the quilt.

Four young maidens.  
One wears a golden belt,  
The second wears a green ribbon,  
The third wears a wavy one,

label: Töölaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
To the Attüma family father,  
To the Attüma family mother,  
To feed, to give drink,  
To receive well,  
To grant a place at the feast table;  
Who had a wedding in the field  
And had made stacks in the meadow,  
Promised to pour vodka  
Into the barrel without beer.  
He had not killed the bull  
That had plowed the field on its knees,  
Nor ever seen an yoke in the world, –  
Had seen the plow only after a hundred years.  
I have eaten my fill, I have drunk my fill  
I am full to the brim without beer,  
Up to the top of the chest,  
Up to the neck of eggs.  
Give me hay to sleep on  
Give me straw to thrash.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
At the Mede manor, across the wide field  
At Lord Mede's great estate  
When will we reach the shore,  
When will we roll down the slope?  
The nephew waits at the shore,  
The young fellows wait by the slope  
The maid moves the stick unsteadily  
The girl swings the stick restlessly.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The senses thought towards the manor,  
Speeches: without going to the tavern.  
I do not fear the hairy one,  
The thief of Viru grain,  
The predator of the Harju granary,  
The milker of Lääne cows.  
I am a man, son of a sailor,  
A dear man, son of a fisherman.  
But I am Madis, son of Malle,  
Tõnu, son of the second wife.  
But I know how to set a fish trap,  
I know how to put fish traps in the river.  
I place the fish trap mouth towards the manor,  
The edge of the trap towards the lord.  
I tried to catch golden fish,  
Good eels from the town.  
Large pikes went into the trap,  
Large pikes, with black backs.  
They struck a wide splash on the loan.  
They stood at the edge into the sea,  
In the uneven shallows of the loan.

label: Töölaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
I wanted to go to the work party  
I wanted to go without work party beer  
There were ladles for washing the dogs  
The broth was for moving the roughage.  
Here I will leave this place as well,  
Instead, I give you  
Pasturelands for the bear to graze,  
Fields for the moose to trample,  
And meadows for the hares to run.  
I took a gun from the boot  
Bear rifles from the window,  
Bear rifles from the chamber;  
I went to hunt moose,  
To shoot bears from the oats,  
To catch the lumps from the grain.  
I put the moose in the backside,  
The bear between the hairs,  
The moose went to the forest here and there,  
The bear went to the forest in zigzags,  
The lumps flashing behind.

label: Töölaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:

Bear rifles from the window,

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I was a herdboy before:
I drove the bulls to the barn
I turned the cows to the hayfield
The yearlings to the grassland,
The sheep to the flower pasture
The roosters to the clover patch;
Where the herdboys praised me
The sheepboys did not blame me
I was indeed a worthy cattle herder
I was a diligent stable keeper.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
In the village, the kitchen smokes with horehound,  
The village field with sulfur,  
The garden bed with pepper,  
The field edges with vinegar,  
The riverbanks with cranberries.  
I plow the marshes, bluebells grew,  
On the hilltop, wet pines,  
In the grove, good barleys grew,  
In the granary, indeed, curled oats;  
I plow the fallow, pour into the hollow,  
To the old red hayfield.  
Surely I plow the fire land,  
But I also plow the ale furrows.  
Where good barleys grew for me,  
Good barleys, curled oats,  
On the field, mighty wheats.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I wanted to go to a work party,
To marry a rye bride,
But father did not give permission.
Brother would not take the tools out to the barn,
Sister would not give the silver ring.
I wanted to go to a rich suitor
To court a family daughter,
To want a farm daughter,
The family daughter jumped away and
The blame was put on Sandi.
It is not Sandi's fault,
It is Sassi Mats's fault.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
We pull and rush,  
We cut and finish  
We want to finish,  
To put an end to the present.  
But we are awaited at the end,  
But we are watched in the stake.  
We have run out of rye bread at the end,  
At the stake, we have interim slices.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Our little tug captain,
a quirky spotted little bird,
a pretty helper in the garden,
young man, little cheese flower!
Don’t be too angry,
don’t be mad,
that we slightly twisted,
cut a little out!
Now the straw didn’t swing,
the small leaves didn’t move.
What was left of the straw didn’t swing,
the small leaf stayed still,
the dewberry kept growing —
it had secretly remained,
hidden above the rye,
shining through the sickle.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Our noble little lamb,
a quirky colorful little bird,
a pretty helper at the farm,
a young man, little cheese blossom!

label: Töölaulud
translation:
What is the poor farmer to do,
if the oxen don’t pull the plow?
A piece of bread in his sack,
a little bit of salt in his pocket,
still a strong farmer!
I am going to the village for sowing,
only carrying an axe,
the oxen are harnessed,
the whip was dusty in the bag,
hoes in the sticks in the arm,
the pail rattled in the bucket,
water glistened in the trough,
a piece of bread in his sack,
a little bit of salt in his pocket!
Still a strong farmer!

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
My wife is a little delicate one,  
a fine cloth, a little swift one:  
alone sturdy, two strong,  
an ell on ten weeks old!  
She has no end to the threads on the loom,  
nor end to the wool on the loom,  
the pouch cloth is near the rooster!  
They creak and clatter like that,  
the cloth squeaks and rattles!  
Threads are thick, the parts wide!  
Threads thick – received from father-in-law,  
parts wide – made by mother-in-law,  
cloth legs received along,  
the sitting bench was received from the groom.  

I am going to the groom’s gate,  
to accompany (him) to the bedroom;  
I am going in to look,  
how many beds they have there.  
They have seven beds there:  
father’s bed was for sitting,  
mother’s bed was for living,  
the groom’s bed was for courting,  
cook’s bed was for keeping,  
servant’s bed was for fastening,  
mine is for washing,  
the boys’ bed was for stick-like (sleeping)!

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where shall the three of us go
Over the field as five
Let’s go to Looja for a tune
Maria to the hayfield
Looja to take the tune
Mari to mow the hay.
What is given to us there,
Six golden belts
Seven silver rings
Eight necklaces
Nine heart eggs
Ten plowing charms
Where do we weave the gold
Where do we pour the copper
Let's go on top of the hat
Let's go on top of the gate
On grandmother’s pillowcase
On father-in-law’s barn door
The pillow caught fire
Whom do I call to put it out,
I call the moon, I ask the day,
Neither the moon came when called
Nor the day when asked
Then came a little cloud boy
Who brought the clouds while coming
Take the clouds as you go
From the cloud comes a drop of water
From the drop came a long rod
From the rod came a red duck
From the duck came a blue tail
Under the tail was a German bed
What was in that bed
Four young maidens
One washed a golden belt
The second tied a silk belt
The third threaded a thread belt
The fourth cried in the corner
They already had the gifts ready.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
