label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Last year we had black bulls,  
The year before, other black ones. -  
There were no bull killers,  
No slayers of great beasts.  
A man came over the seas,  
A handsome man over the waves.  
A great bull's horn,  
A broad sole of a foot.  
With his fist he crushed the bull,  
With his palm he squeezed the fat.  
Ellakene, vennakene.  
Put this blood into the vein,  
Put this blood into the vein,  
Then I put the old ones to row.  
The old ones rowed, the ships reached,  
Kunni's masts appeared here,  
They waved flags on the city edge.  
Long ago the gray bull of Riga,  
Sang the speckled rooster of Riga,  
Barked the black dog of Riga.  
He did not ruin the city of Riga,  
He did not kill Tallinn,  
Broke the church of Kadrina,  
Broke pots on the fire,  
Pots broken on the porch.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Some think of us as manor lords.  
The gentleman thinks of us as barn workers.  
We are blacksmith apprentices,  
Hard workers of iron.  
I melt iron in my mouth,  
I boil it down on my tongue.  
Teeth are gritted,  
The dear tongue hardens the box.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The scythe was a neighbor here,  
The scythe was here, the scythe was a pig,  
The scythe is in a strange hand.  
Which one do I cut, which do I leave  
Which one do I grind away  
Both are dear  
So let me tell the sickle too  
Speak to the curved iron  
Surely the crane will harvest the sickle  
Sharp iron on the other side  
Curved iron is the best of all.  
The sickle was bad, it did not gather  
The iron was weak, did not respond  
The sickle was made by an evil smith  
The iron was made by weak ones  
Rolled by fluid hands.  
Tapped by crooked hands.  
May that smith be taken by the shores  
May that smith’s fingers be taken  
Let both hands wither,  
So I don’t burn my wood  
Don’t waste the charcoal  
Don’t waste good iron  
Don’t ruin the blade.  
Burned the woods, wasted the feed  
Didn’t spare a piece of iron  
Made the sickle defective  
The sickle with seven extensions  
I go to my brother’s request.  
My own dear brother  
Take down the grey one  
The golden earpiece for the ear  
Between them blood red  
Go to the smith in Viru  
To the smartest tailors,  
Make me a sharp sickle  
Have the copper head cast  
Silver put on the front edge  
Tin dripped on the back  
Boil gold to the middle edge  
Trim it to fit my hand.  
I weave long fields  
I clear wide ridges  
Our fields stretch to the sea,  
The ridges cleared and open  
Tails stretch to the islands  
Corners into the meadow’s ash.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Our manor workers both  
Cut down alder trunks together  
We fell wet pines,  
Wet pines, dry spruces.  
Long rows of rowan trees  
Longer rows of rowan trees  
Slender hazels  
More delicate bird cherries  
Pile the branches into a heap  
We stack the beams properly  
From alder came withes,  
From hazel came firewood  
A long pile from rowan  
The wet pines, those were mine.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Foggy-foggy baby's cap,  
Our grandma has several caps,  
Village grandma has ten caps. -  
One was a hay cap,  
Second a hay meadow cap,  
Third a livestock heap cap,  
Fourth a cow milking cap,  
Fifth a rainy weather cap,  
Sixth a dry weather cap,  
Seventh a manure carrying cap,  
Eighth a herding cap,  
Ninth was a night cap,  
Tenth a visiting cap.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I wanted to go to the work party  
I wanted to work at the party without oil  
To stop the barley pulping  
To wrap the rye stalks  
No, father did not give a horse  
Mother did not sew a dress  
Brother did not make a copper harness  
Sister did not knit a mitten.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The master was angry with me  
The mistress was loud with me  
I have not done wrong  
To anyone excessively. -  
I plowed the oxen well  
I quickly harnessed the horses  
I plowed the soil deeply  
I did not spoil the grass roots  
I gave the grass room to grow  
To keep a family on the turf. -  
The tree had large roots  
The steppe horse had wise offspring  
The turf had a proper family. -

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Sisters, dear little souls  
Young maidens, tender youths  
Slender penmanship  
Let us finish this field  
Let us reduce this clearing  
Without beating with a whip  
Without firing wide pink shots  
Without punishing the stick  
The little titmouse knows this  
The gray little bird thinks  
That we have whips in the pen  
Sticks in the stone trough  
Our strong little bull  
The colorful patterned little bird  
Takes the whip from the pen  
The stick from the stone trough  
Beats me along the writing  
Along the edges of the back field  
Right along the shoulder  
Across the top of the head  
So that my envelope is peeled off  
The bib strings are snapped  
The wide linen is slapped  
My girl was a little old woman  
My boy was a little dawn  
You heat the room with wood  
Honor the stove’s beginnings  
Patch up the cozy bed  
I am going to whistle to Virve  
Yes, I will go to the auditory  
Honor with whip strokes from the waist  
From the arm strokes from the wrist  
Those blue strokes from the back  
That the angry strong man had given  
Chosen by the village chief  
The colorful one knocked in anger.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
How I must go home
Home full of angry trouble
Bad words in the house
Hurtful words flying around. -
Who else but me
Must endure it all dearly
The poor must accept it
The dear must give in
Neither evil woman, nor evil man
My loud older one’s anger.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Eat herd, herd with a seal  
Drink herd, herd with a blush  
Lay the herd down to breathe  
Let them rest during the break  
As long as the herd grass grows  
A dark bull rises to the grass  
As long as I tighten the pig's piglet straps  
Fastening shoelaces  
I make a fluttering belt  
I make a roach-tail shirt  
I knit a cat's paw.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Spring comes and the swallow sings  
The girl makes a cuckoo in the west pasture  
I call the herd on a beautiful island  
I myself sit in the quiet island  
But I make fire in the windy island  
Sibri's fleece, Sibri's fleece  
I made slippers for my mother-in-law  
Mother-in-law threw the slippers into the oven  
I looked out the window  
Mother-in-law grabbed my tail  
Tail torn to pieces  
Someone asked whose wheel it was  
Grandmother's wheel, grandfather's wheel  
Old woman's spinning wheel.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The field is long, the young are many  
The wide field, the children are young  
The field of long weaving  
The wide field of braiding  
The field weavers suffer  
The field braiders grow tired.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I wanted, I wanted, dear brothers  
I wanted to go to heaven  
To the great father's wedding  
To take part in the creator’s festival  
To the hayfield under the hill  
But I could not join the heaven's fellowship  
Nor the great father's wedding  
Nor take part in the creator’s festival  
To the hayfield under the hill  
The barren swamp and ruined fence  
A crying child and a drunken woman  
Because I could not join the heaven's fellowship  
Nor the great father's wedding  
Nor the hayfield under the hill

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Hans was the son of Old Hans  
All the people spoke wonders of Hans  
What did that scamp do  
When he went to his uncle's manor  
On his way, he entered a tavern  
Took several shots of vodka  
Then his bones became joyful  
On his way, he went to the straw oven  
Found there a lump of fat  
Because I, a corpse, went to ruin  
Because I spent a long time in the herd  
From the wolf, I got a wild mind  
From the bear, a strong heart  
From the fox, a slippery tongue  
From the hare, dreadful tracks

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
Let's go, young men,  
The boys of Võnnu with the nets,  
Let's go fishing for vendace,  
To catch crayfish,  
Let's go to the lake, to the big spring in the meadow.  
There I got a hundred eels,  
A thousand fat burbot.  
Then I came home from fishing.  
I laid the fish out to dry;  
Who came to steal the fish?  
The cat came to steal the fish.  
I began to chase the cat,  
The cat went to the well to splash about.  
I went to the village to get ropes.  
From Miguel I brought lime rope,  
From Tõnu I brought tar rope,  
From Kangur I brought hemp rope.  
I began to pull the cat.  
A hundred men were after the cat's tail,  
A thousand men were on the cat's back,  
Then I got the cat into the stocks;  
The hemp ropes broke,  
The lime ropes snapped,  
But the tar rope held fast.  
Then I got the cat into the stocks.  
The mice sang in the corner:  
Well, well, little cat,  
Where is my six golden sons,  
Nine brave fathers,  
Ten village maidens.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Neighbor, neighbor little day,  
A moment of golden time;  
The lazy one waits for evening,  
The sluggish one for the day to pass,  
The diligent one waits to celebrate  
The rise of the next day,  
To turn to the third dawn,  
To name the fourth.

label: Laulud laulust  ; Murelaulud  ; Töölaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
I would sing, I would be laid down,  
I would be silent, I would be sworn,  
Otherwise I would sit, I am bored,  
Otherwise I would walk, I am weary.  
Whom I bore by sitting,  
Whom I weary by walking,  
Whom I grieve with sad minds,  
Whom I humble with sorrow?  
To whom else, if not stone,  
To stone at the church,  
To the lime on top of the mortar.  
The stone does not understand the maiden’s language,  
The limestone does not understand the maiden’s prayer.  
Little stone, little gold,  
Little limestone, little thick one,  
You could sleep in the sea.  
Shout from the sea shore,  
Move in the sea sand.  
Before you came to our land,  
Our land, our island,  
To torment us,  
To trouble our hands and arms;  
If we had rowan-berries on our yards,  
Maple leaves on our arms,  
Fingers picking currants,  
Then we would beat with whips,  
We would endure with clubs.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Today the researchers are diligent  
The expellers are mighty,  
The local researchers are sleepy,  
The expellers are tired.  
The old ewes have gone ahead,  
The young plants have stayed behind;  
Their bellies run along the hut,  
Their stomachs run along the ground,  
The udder runs along the snow.  
Be good, our girls,  
So that we can swim to the ends,  
We will settle behind the island,  
We will throw into the clover,  
The red beer is finished,  
On the island are skinny breads,  
In the clover are milk cakes;  
God knows this,  
Maybe the whips are also in the clover,  
The cudgel on the edge of the ditch;  
But the time will come to be donated,  
The hour will come to be drawn.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Hiss, hiss little scythe  
Rattle, rattle crooked iron  
Ring, ring lively iron,  
Listen, listen little moon  
Guess the new little gift:  
Who among us will be left behind:  
Let them be left behind the scythe!  
Who among us will become a man:  
Let them go before the scythe!  
Who among us will die away:  
Let them sink into the scythe!  
Who among us will slide from a whore  
And become a floating bachelor.  
Let them break the scythe! -

label: Töölaulud
translation:
In summer, I am sulking in sadness  
In winter, I am swanning in the snow  
Buckets of water on my shoulder  
Neck cakes around my neck  
I melt water from outside  
I draw water from the well  
I trample angels from the street

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Run, run to the field  
The little soldier marches out  
The field weavers are coming  
Seem to be the meadow rakers  
They have border irons on their faces  
On their wrists are wrist irons  
In their hands, irons you hold falsely  
Why should I border the long field  
Why should I rake the broad meadow  
The field wants to expand by hand  
With finger-binding by the hand  
With the thumb-side of the longer ones  
Along went the mound cutters  
The crooked bag cutters  
The burnt edges turned sideways  
Did not greet a count  
Did not give God's help  
Thanks be to God  
Now I have rye on the field  
Tied the meadow with a bond  
Layered the broad field  
Without whipping with a whip  
Without punishment with a club  
Without raking the dog-rose.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Tuidu-tuidu baby bonnet,  
Our granny’s old bonnet,  
Let me start guessing,  
Following the little swan and reading aloud:  
One was a nightcap,  
The second was a work cap,  
The third was a domestic cap,  
The fourth was a hayfield cap,  
The fifth was a rain cap,  
The sixth was a bad weather cap,  
The seventh was a dung carting cap,  
The eighth was a herding cap,  
The ninth was a good cap,  
The tenth was a church cap.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Some think of me as a manor lord,  
Some believe I am a farmhand.  
I am a humble blacksmith,  
A servant who forges iron.  
With my breath, I warm the iron,  
On the tip of my tongue, I heat the iron.  
My bellows are at the bottom,  
My jaws are the bellows’ boards,  
My lips are fine little pincers.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Surely I was pursued by the eager Jürjes,  
They wanted the plant's blossoms of gold,  
They yearned for the bundles of grain.  
The woman did not give the room, nor did she let in.  
And I myself did not go:  
The woman did not give Annekest,  
Did not let Marikest in the room.  
Ann was a famous pile maker:  
A slender maker of yields,  
A skillful caster of bundles.  
She made the yields spiked:  
Bundles four-cornered,  
Piles hexagonal.  
In front was the hay cutter,  
In back the mower,  
On top the head pile maker,  
Below was the giver of awns.  
It was a beautiful sight to the father,  
A beautiful sight to the mother,  
A beautiful sight for the brothers,  
The fellow is a marvel to the people,  
It was splendid for all to see.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
End, end, little field,  
End of the ridge, little ridge,  
You are little, you are barren.  
I have already been avoiding you  
Every noon with lukewarmness,  
Every hour with harshness,  
Every year stepping on your surface.  
End the field from the knees,  
From the fingers of the sandy ridge,  
From the black earth of the hills,  
So that I could come inside to work,  
And watch the house flax.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Thank God, thank the Lord!  
The end was finally beautiful,  
The field got a long furrow,  
The meadow a broad pasture.  
Without the whip striking,  
Without the guardian punishing,  
Without shooting the wide pink.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Sizzle sizzle scythe,  
Speak of bent iron.  
Listen listen, little moon,  
Show the week,  
Guess the new little year,  
Bring news, little star.  
Who among us will become a man!  
That scythe goes first.  
Who among us will be left behind!  
That one stays after the scythe.  
Who among us will die,  
Will be cut down by the scythe,  
Who among us will become a whore,  
That scythe goes upside down.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I once had a dog named Krants,  
He brought six puppies:  
One was a deer dog, another a boar dog,  
The third was a hare dog.  
They brought six ducks,  
I took the ducks to Paide,  
The ducks paid six coins.  
I bought a new little pipe.  
I started playing the pipe,  
Playing the pajopill (a type of traditional pipe),  
Cutting Swedish hay.  
Father heard the sound of the pipe,  
Then he started making beer.  
Brother heard the sound of the pipe,  
Brother started fetching water.  
Mother heard the sound of the pipe,  
Started churning cream,  
Sister heard the sound of the pipe,  
Sister salted the brine.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Dear man, let's go fishing,  
Let's go catch smelt,  
Let's take out the crayfish,  
Let's break the salmon roe.  
Let's catch sacred fish.  
On Monday I brought herring,  
On Tuesday I brought cuttlefish,  
Wednesday had good fish.  
On Thursday I brought cod,  
Friday Riga sprats,  
Saturday flounder and bream,  
On Sunday nothing was caught.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Eat, herd, herd with a seal,  
Drink, herd, redden, herd,  
Lie down, herd, to breathe,  
Rest during rest time,  
To become a dew drop on the willow.  
As long as I tie the garter,  
I tie the shoelace,  
I weave a new apron string.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Himuta Amuta boys,  
Timiko Tamiko men,  
Metsla boys, sons of men,  
Gentle brothers of Hellavere.  
Let us make bridges over the swamps.  
Causeways over the rivers.  
So that a woman does not wet her foot,  
A woman’s foot, an ox does not trample.  
A horse does not nuzzle the hoof shell,  
A pig does not pull on the silk purse.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
How should I go home?
To the gate to roll,
Home to angry words,
To cruel hands.
At home everyone is hateful,
At home everyone is angry,
At home everyone is confused,
At home everyone is jealous.
At home everyone is superior.
At home everyone is cold!
Everyone comes against me!
They rain down on me directly.
The lower ones oppress,
The lower ones whip,
The upper ones are tough dogs,
The rear ones are hoofs in the mud,
The middle ones are wheel spokes.
The lady struck me with a club,
The master with a fire shovel,
The lord with a farting joke,
The girl with a firestick,
Toomas with a clod of earth,
Hinn with an ox horn,
The children with pine treetops.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
We have a few pranksters,  
A few fart-slingers,  
We have few scoundrels,  
Few scoundrel-breakers,  
Sparsely birch wood,  
Very much asp wood.  
The village is good for kindling,  
There are plenty of kindling makers.  
We set the kindling on fire,  
Rendered fat in the pan,  
Rendered fat burns well,  
Nicely sheep fat,  
Smoothly pig fat,  
Whiteness of veal fat.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Ants was from Harju, Jaan was from Jerva,  
Ants was a diligent man from Harju,  
Jaan was a rough man from Järva,  
Ants was a blacksmith from Harju,  
Jaan was a wheelwright from Järva,  
They made fences for the parish,  
For the community to recover,  
For the maidens in the field to prosper.  
The parish did not praise the fences,  
The community did not recover,  
The maidens in the field suffered.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Viru wool, Tartu flax,
Tallinn linen fine.
Bring our Kärt here,
Kärt is quick to spin,
Diligent in treading the distaff.
Before Christmas thick,
After Christmas thin.
Spins nights and spins mornings:
Spins spring, autumn,—
Spins in the warm summer time,
Even after winter.
Cannot put a shirt on the back,
Nor sleeves on the arms.
The yoke shows a bare backside,
Revealing beautiful skin.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Töölaulud
translation:
The home is awaited,  
At the gate awaited:  
Is the fire blower coming,  
Or the water carrier rolling,  
The spoon rinsing,  
The plates lighting,  
The tub shining,  
The milk mug washing,  
The beer cup decorating,  
The water bucket rolling,  
The tubs bristling.  
I put the mugs on the rack,  
The cups on the kitchen roof,  
The buckets by the well's edge,  
The tubs white on the pole end.  
When the brewer came to the village,  
The old man to the mourner,  
He thought of the shining moon,  
The faint white glow spilling,  
The rainbow shimmering,  
The echo star shining.  
The bucket tables glimmered,  
The cup handles gleamed,  
The tub bottoms shone,  
The tub branches flickered,  
The bath edges brightened,  
Here the daughter has done the work,  
The foster child has put in the effort.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
A hundred little rains,  
Let it rain, so I could sleep,  
Let a shower come, to nap.  
If only I could fall asleep,  
Breathing the haystack air.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Others walk the church road,
Greet other people,
Give kisses even to boys.
I walk the herding path,
Greet your barn,
Give kisses to the cow.
I drive the cows to the grove,
Send the bulls to the meadow,
The oxen to the hayfield,
The pigs to the bluebells. -
In the grove the cows are fat,
In the meadow the bulls are beautiful,
In the hayfield the oxen are thick,
On the bluebells the pigs are big.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Not to bow here,  
Not to bend a bad head here,  
To this evil crane,  
To the wicked oats,  
To the envious barley,  
To the foolish rye.  
To let the wicked oats spoil,  
To let the cunning oats spoil,  
To let the foolish rye spoil,  
To let the calm buckwheat spoil,  
The maiden to harvest the wheat.  
Wait-wait, barley stalk butt,  
Reap-reap, oat armpit,  
Who will reap you a second time?  
Who will harvest you a third?  
I must die!  
Others are taken to be married,  
To Odra Annus’ son,  
To Kaera Käsper.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The sickle mows the grain field,  
The scythe rusts in the pasture dew.  
The sickle mows, I whistle,  
The sickle cuts, I tie,  
The shovel digs lightly,  
Throws wood in long stacks,  
Starts the row of fires,  
Puts bundles on the rack.  
So that ten ovens could be fired,  
Seven ovens warmed.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The Võka mill runs slowly,  
Steadily at every time.  
It made barley flakes,  
Rye flour flakes.  
Thin oat flours.  
The woman wanted to joke.  
She wanted to make porridge from the barley flakes,  
Kissel from the oats,  
Rye bread from the rye flour.  
She made the breads lumpy,  
The family’s breads long and thin.  
But the man mocked the woman:  
"She made the breads lumpy,"  
The woman retorted mockingly:  
"It's not your fault anymore,  
You gave me sour flour."

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Töölaulud  ; Murelaulud  ; Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Family daughter, maiden,  
Farm daughter, a little plant.  
If you care to be diligent  
And you want to be wise,  
Clean the room, the window, the table,  
The farm by the windowsill,  
Turn the cellar entrance,  
Wash the small cabinets in the pantry,  
Bring them to the corner of the room,  
The buckets to the kitchen roof,  
Buckets to the well area.  
Someone will come to the village bucket carrier,  
Old man to the beer brewer,  
He thinks about the moon shining,  
He thinks about a bright morning.  
Our ladles shone,  
Our bright tables glistened,  
Our bucket tables rested,  
Our small cabinets in the pantry,  
Bring them to the corner of the room,  
Buckets to the kitchen roof.  

Family daughter, maiden,  
Farm daughter, a little plant,  
When you hear the holidays coming,  
You start to prepare gifts,  
Easter sliding in,  
The moon pushes the curls,  
The day pushes the pearl maiden,  
The year builds itself up,  
Asks the elders,  
Asks the old men,  
Questions the parents,  
Where is my brooch, where is my pin,  
Where are my embroidered handkerchiefs,  
Where is my belt with maple embroidery,  
Where are my socks with Finnish patterns,  
Shoes with cross-stone patterns.  

Mother, old lady,  
Give me the key to the barn,  
Give me the key to the chest.  
I will go to decorate the barn,  
To smooth the top of the kerchief,  
Underneath I put gold, above silver,  
In between I put white ribbons,  
A skirt with embroidery on,  
Belt on the waist with maple embroidery,  
Socks on the feet with Finnish patterns,  
Shoes with cross-stone patterns.  

Then I go under the village swing,  
The village young men,  
Ponder and think,  
Whose beauty queen,  
Whose lovely maiden,  
Town beauty queen,  
Paide’s suitable maiden,  
From whom should I ask for a servant,  
From whom should the poor argue,  
Ask the servant,  
Discuss with the arm,  
Have your hands worked for the village,  
Have your arms endured hardship.  

Mother, old lady,  
Some got a place to bake bread,  
Others found a place to make bread,  
You put the beginning far away,  
Far away to the weaver,  
To the town road to the turner,  
To other lands to the miller.  

Mother, old lady,  
I have not gone to the miller,  
The miller’s stone roars,  
I have not gone to the weaver,  
The weaver’s fabric rustles,  
I have not gone to the smith either,  
Black children of the smith,  
Sooty children of the hammerer.  

I want clean woodworkers,  
Lovers, readers of beginnings,  
Strong wide breakers,  
Long furrow carriers,  
A little for us to furrow,  
A little furrow carrier,  
Surely there are workers for us,  
Surely there are makers for the nursery,  
Let the nurseries be set on fire,  
Let the stirring fat burn.

Black children of the smith,  
Sooty children of the hammerer.  

I want clean woodworkers,  
Lovers, readers of beginnings,  
Strong wide breakers,  
Long furrow carriers,  
A little for us to furrow,  
A little furrow carrier,  
Surely there are workers for us,  
Surely there are makers for the nursery,  
Let the nurseries be set on fire,  
Let the stirring fat burn.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Töölaulud  ; Murelaulud  ; Kalendrilaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Family daughter, maiden,  
Farm daughter, a little plant.  
If you care to be diligent  
And you want to be wise,  
Clean the room, the window, the table,  
The farm by the windowsill,  
Turn the cellar entrance,  
Wash the small cabinets in the pantry,  
Bring them to the corner of the room,  
The buckets to the kitchen roof,  
Buckets to the well area.  
Someone will come to the village bucket carrier,  
Old man to the beer brewer,  
He thinks about the moon shining,  
He thinks about a bright morning.  
Our ladles shone,  
Our bright tables glistened,  
Our bucket tables rested,  
Our small cabinets in the pantry,  
Bring them to the corner of the room,  
Buckets to the kitchen roof.  

Family daughter, maiden,  
Farm daughter, a little plant,  
When you hear the holidays coming,  
You start to prepare gifts,  
Easter sliding in,  
The moon pushes the curls,  
The day pushes the pearl maiden,  
The year builds itself up,  
Asks the elders,  
Asks the old men,  
Questions the parents,  
Where is my brooch, where is my pin,  
Where are my embroidered handkerchiefs,  
Where is my belt with maple embroidery,  
Where are my socks with Finnish patterns,  
Shoes with cross-stone patterns.  

Mother, old lady,  
Give me the key to the barn,  
Give me the key to the chest.  
I will go to decorate the barn,  
To smooth the top of the kerchief,  
Underneath I put gold, above silver,  
In between I put white ribbons,  
A skirt with embroidery on,  
Belt on the waist with maple embroidery,  
Socks on the feet with Finnish patterns,  
Shoes with cross-stone patterns.  

Then I go under the village swing,  
The village young men,  
Ponder and think,  
Whose beauty queen,  
Whose lovely maiden,  
Town beauty queen,  
Paide’s suitable maiden,  
From whom should I ask for a servant,  
From whom should the poor argue,  
Ask the servant,  
Discuss with the arm,  
Have your hands worked for the village,  
Have your arms endured hardship.  

Mother, old lady,  
Some got a place to bake bread,  
Others found a place to make bread,  
You put the beginning far away,  
Far away to the weaver,  
To the town road to the turner,  
To other lands to the miller.  

Mother, old lady,  
I have not gone to the miller,  
The miller’s stone roars,  
I have not gone to the weaver,  
The weaver’s fabric rustles,  
I have not gone to the smith either,  
Black children of the smith,  
Sooty children of the hammerer.  

I want clean woodworkers,  
Lovers, readers of beginnings,  
Strong wide breakers,  
Long furrow carriers,  
A little for us to furrow,  
A little furrow carrier,  
Surely there are workers for us,  
Surely there are makers for the nursery,  
Let the nurseries be set on fire,  
Let the stirring fat burn.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Always I am sorrowful  
Every night is tedious  
Every day is long  
To whom do I plant boredom  
To whom do I set sorrow  
To whom do I complain a sad mind  
To the stone on the church path  
To the rope outside the pasture  
The stone does not speak  
The thick limestone does not tell  
The stone does not understand a maiden's language  
The limestone does not understand a maiden's prayer  
Little stone, dear stone  
Little limestone, thick little one  
You could roar in the sea  
Shout on the seashores  
Move in the sea's sand  
Could bounce on the sea bottom  
Before you came to our land  
To our yard for torment  
To the crook of the arm for suffering  
Our yards are thin  
Shoulders are slender  
If my yards were rowanberry  
Shoulders were apple tree  
Fingers crushed currants  
Then I should endure the whip  
Bear the club  
Answer with the rod.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
How should I go home,
Home to the angry word,
To the hollow hole.
Everyone at home was angry,
The mood at home was all angry,
Everyone at home was confused.
The cold benches were cold,
The edge boards were frozen.
Who am I talking to?
I am talking to Kerbuga.
Who am I having fun with?
I have fun with Lutikaga.
I brush off the old coat:
Oh you old coat!
There was life in the wake,
Lovely life in the herd,
Joyful life in the valley.
I turn to the side, I find the cold,
I turn my back, I find the wall.
Fleas walked in the stems of the mouth,
Lice went to the corner, draped in the sheaths,
Lice had nests on their backs.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The salmon splashes in the river,  
The fish thrashes near the shore.  
The salmon awaits a splitter,  
The fish a cutter.  
There is no such man here,  
But there was a little boy,  
Who breaks the salmon from the sea,  
Cuts the fish from the shore.  
I had a little brother,  
A cruel little rogue,  
He breaks the salmon from the river,  
Cuts the fish from the shore.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
The shepherd loses the flock,  
But Aab goes to look for me,  
Into the misty hill,  
Into the distant dew.  
The mist breaks my new month,  
The dew in the variegated hands.  
I searched for the flock far away,  
Far away, from the high place,  
From the lords’ pasture,  
From the kings’ fold,  
From the great German forest.  
Eat the flock, blue flock,  
Eat the flock, red flock,  
Old woman’s flock to wander,  
To rest for a short time,  
Until I braid the leg strap,  
Braid the old woman’s apron strap,  
The old woman’s sack strap.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
One was my little brother  
A wreath of learning, cruel  
He also became a coachman in Viru  
His superior was Tarvandulla  
That makes a healthy woman in the village  
Nurm's girls are swift  
The village does not praise the healthy woman  
The parish does not praise the field helper  
Nurm's girls are swift  
They leave blocks in the hills  
They leave gaps in the fields  
Buttons on the girls' blades  
I understand, I hear again  
Throw the coachman into the marsh  
Under the under-coachmen  
Tear apart the bellows boards  
Ashes are fiery coals  
Take to praise the plowing lands  
Burn the fields  
Drive the lower lands  
The village does not praise the healthy woman  
The parish does not praise the field helper  
Nurm's girls are swift.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Jaan is making a sled in secret,  
A nice sled in the spruce forest.  
Every spruce made a spoke,  
Every southern wind cut the shingles.  
He finished making the sled,  
Brought the sled into the room.  
Look, maiden, look, old man,  
Look, both of you, old ones.  
What was the carriage for the sled?  
The axle was for the sled.  
What was for the axles?  
The poles were for the axles.  
What was for the poles?  
They were maple rods.  
What was for the rods?  
It was a bent limewood stick.  
The maiden understood, the old man answered:  
Where did you get the maple rods?  
From the maple tree by the gate.  
The lime tree was between the village,  
From there you get the bent limewood stick.  
Where do you get the rowan whip?  
From behind the rowan tree.  
Where do you get the sled riders,  
The spruce ear sitters?  
Grow up first, young lad,  
Then you can get the sled riders,  
The spruce ear sitters.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Slowly slowly wisely wisely  
Madalaste murmurs  
That we have just begun  
We howl in turn  
We end laziness  
From low ground I plow the soil  
I do not damage the root of the jumik  
I do not scatter the willow’s family  
The jumik had large roots  
The willow had a fitting family  
The burdock had wise children  
Sister Ella is not clear  
Sister is either a pin or not clear

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The whole world was breathing  
The entire people were resting  
We poor ones were walking  
Our paths were unknown  
Our lands were unmeasured  
Ditches beside the ear un-dug  
Bridge pillars not preserved  
The boy’s way goes to the girl  
Our way goes to the sea  
To that large harbor  
And onto that ship’s deck  
I patch the ship, patch the ship  
But I make the ship whole  
I will put sails on the ship  
Just like a bride wears an apron  
When I begin to go  
Then the ale will start flowing  
Tears will roll down the eyes  
The table will be rocked by the waves  
Eyebrows like drops of gold  
Then the young girls started to cry  
Regretting the wreaths of karrads  
Sighing the garden shirts  
Mourning the linen shirts  
The bulls, but the young girls cried  
The bulls when the ship’s sails appeared  
The bulls when the ship’s flag flashed  
Glistening at the ship’s wave crest.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
A Little Stone, a Little Lad  
The stone twig was a little brother  
You could have stirred up the sea  
Run down the shores  
Before you came to our land  
But you only tormented us  
Tortured us little ones  
Our shoulders are thin  
Our arms are weak  
If only the shoulders were apple-like  
The arms like maple trees  
Fingers like rowan branches  
Then I could reach around the meadow  
To turn the wrist stones.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Hans was from Harju, Jaan was from Järva.  
Mart was a short little boy.  
He makes the parish’s plowshare,  
The parish’s axe blade,  
The meadow girls’ knives.  
The parish praises him for the plowshare,  
The parish praises him for the axe blade,  
The meadow girls’ knives.  
He makes ships from swan feathers,  
From swan feather rods,  
Ship masts from rowan wood,  
Ship keels from stone.  
Toomas was not allowed to strike,  
Hans was not allowed to pierce,  
Peeter was not allowed to turn his head,  
Rein was not allowed to move the belt,  
Mihkel was not allowed to twist the stick.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I go to the western fox to the threshing barn  
The little duck goes west to the pile  
The little child goes west onto the cart  
I take a cudgel from the corner  
From the barn's quarrel  
From the rake near the barn wall  
I beat the beams into a pile  
The walls become seed rye  
The hanging rods become resonant sticks  
I thresh the barn with joy  
I strike the cart with a song  
I trample the pile with a dance.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Oio, Oio Oisu boys  
Boys of Vaivasvere parish  
Build bridges over the ditches  
Over the lowlands  
Where a duckwon't wet its feet  
A bull won't stomp its feet  
A rooster won’t spur with golden spurs  
A hen won’t scratch with copper toes.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Töölaulud
translation:
The master was angry with me  
The mistress was loud with me  
I am not angry with anyone  
Nor unfair to anyone  
I drove the bulls well  
I harnessed the mares gently  
I plowed the land shallowly  
I did not damage the grass roots  
Nor the thistle root  
I scattered willows without family  
There were many willows without family  
The thistle had many children  
The alder had many mourners  
The spruce had many listeners  
The birch had many criers.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Töölaulud
translation:

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Then we will house the ewes  
Then we will house the hens  
We shelter the wild birds  
If the herd is near the well  
Cattle to the water stream,  
Pag. 761 Bulls to the sea grass  
Oxen to the spring  
If they are not good feeders  
Nor good waterers  
Nor wise herders,  
Who then feeds the large herd,  
Who then waters the large herd  
The forehead large to the barn  
Gives the grey one a seat  
On the thyme a stone spike  
On the black hill a building’s child  
The ears hold higher ground.  
If they are not good feeders  
Nor good waterers  
Nor wise herders.  
I had only one brother alone,  
He was alone, worth two  
He stood for seven  
He bore the hats of ten men;  
He indeed feeds the large herd  
Feeds the herd, waters the herd  
The forehead large to the barn  
Gives the oxen a seat  
On the thyme a stone spike  
On the black hill a building’s child  
The ears hold higher ground.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Our road is full of thistles  
Thistly, needle-like  
Rough, uneven  
A stony hill.  
Here bridges should be built  
Bridges built, rafts put in place.  
Where do we build our bridges,  
Where do we put our rafts?  
We men will build the bridges  
We boys will put in the rafts.  
From men come smooth bridges  
From boys come better rafts  
Where a woman does not wet her foot  
A woman’s foot, an ox does not paw  
Nor a horse with golden hooves.  
One could walk on the stone path  
Clothes up to Malle’s waist  
Shoes on the feet of Anne.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
But I am a smith's apprentice,  
A foster child of the strong boy,  
In my mouth I melted iron,  
On my tongue I sealed it tight,  
My horns were bent backwards,  
My jawbones were flint blades,  
My eyes were a tempering pit.

label: Töölaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud
translation:
Thank you to the father of the family  
Thank you to the mother of the family  
Who made the field weddings  
Field weddings, light rods  
Did not kill that alder tree  
Who has never walked in the world  
For a hundred years plowed the field  
A thousand measures of broad fields  
Seven fathoms wide branches  
A hundred measures of long horns  
Eat the downfalls, drink the downfalls  
Do not put the downfalls into your belly  
I am diligent in searching  
Swift to test through  
I leaf through and tear the shirt fronts  
I touch the fur coat fronts  
I touch the fronts of the new quilt.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
When we run out of slices  
And gathering them together ends  
Then the lunch bread is finished  
The half slices have sunk  
The crusty crusts have disappeared.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Hiss, hiss, the sickle  
Rattles the curved iron  
Whoever’s sickle gets here first  
Whoever buys a cap for the peidmies  
Will ride after that peidmies.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Thank you to the father of the family  
Thank you to the mother of the family  
Who joined the three together  
From one came strength  
From the second came powerful water  
From the third came strong oats  
From the fourth came fearful little birds

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I know how to make weirs  
I know how to put weirs in the river  
I place the weir’s mouth towards the manor  
The weir’s tail towards the German side  
The fish went in both ways  
The salmon were wide at the shallow ends  
The whitefish large, black stripes.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I went to the manor to plow  
To work the soil on windy days  
To help on busy days  
I plow the hill into furrows  
Clods between the rows  
Wide valleys between the ridges  
Clods as tall as pine trees  
There I left space for grass  
Shelter for thistles  
Immediately they drove me home  
Then I said to the master’s household  
To the master and the mistress:  
Master, little master  
Mistress, little lady  
Put some bread in my bag  
A lunch in a small bag  
For tomorrow morning  
Then to the western manor to plow  
To work the soil on windy days  
To help on busy days  
I plow the hills out of the furrows  
Clods between the rows  
There is no wide valley between the ridges  
Clods as tall as pine trees  
Leaves no space for grass  
No shelter for thistles.  
Master, little master  
Mistress, little lady  
They put bread in my bag  
Lunch in a small bag  
For tomorrow morning  
Then I went to plow  
To work the soil on windy days  
To help on busy days  
I do not plow the hills into furrows  
Clods between the rows  
No wide valleys between the ridges  
Clods as tall as pine trees  
There I do not leave space for grass  
No shelter for thistles  
I plow the hill beautiful and straight  
Valleys wide as ditches  
I received praise from the bailiff  
Respect from the assistant  
Okmann said, a worthy man!  
I was no beggar before the Germans.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
If I lived for my own sake,
I would work for others’ sake,
I would walk with both hands’ knowledge;
I would not let the village live,
Nor let newcomers settle;
Oh, you jealous village,
Your elders are great witches,
Let the little one live—
The tiny one work,
The lowly one walk humbly.
Herbs were brought from Sweden,
Salt was brought from Finland,
pag. 84 Placed in our livestock pen,
The salts began to melt,
The fresh ones began to sink,—
Then the herd began to vanish,
Pigs went here and there dying,
The cows lay down heavy with sleep,
The herders left crying.
Then I was left completely poor,
Completely lifeless,
Then I became like a saint,
A carrier of two staffs.
Oh God Almighty,
If only I were a black corpse,
A stinger under the pile,—
So I would not prick the witch’s eyes,
Nor be a remedy for secret jealousies.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:

pag. 84 Placed in our livestock pen,

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Understand, sisters, understand brothers,  
Do you think we are close siblings?  
What will be done to us -  
Behind the Viru spruce trees,  
Behind the wise oaks?  
They will build a ship for us.  
The ship was completed,  
The ship was taken away;  
The old were set to row: -  
The old rowed, the oars creaked,  
The young were set to row,  
The young rowed, the sails caught the wind,  
They rowed the ship down to the town,  
From the town down to Narva.  
From Narva down to Viborg,  
From Viborg to the Russian border. -

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Run, run, little basket  
Run quickly, walk briskly  
Next to the little wall  
I will quickly make for you  
A lid to be put on the box  
The master wants a small beauty  
The lady wants a small board  
Don’t widen the bottom  
The lady wants a long thin one.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Monday to do oiling  
Tuesday to go to the hayfield  
Wednesday to carry the firewood  
Thursday to make wheat  
Friday evening to the meadow  
Saturday to split firewood  
Sunday to observe the holiday

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Töölaulud
translation:
I plowed very early  
Quickly harnessed the oxen  
I put the oxen to the yoke  
Attached the shafts to the cart  
Put collars on the horses  
Hung bells on the oxen  
I myself wanted to plow  
I turned the cart into the field  
The old red ox groaned  
The cart plowed with a jerk on the back  
The cart stepped into the field with a thud  
The strong ox pulled, the horse trembled  
I plowed the fields very well  
The lady of the estate’s fields  
I didn’t damage the grass roots  
I trod on the working man’s heart too  
The young man was left without his father  
The thistles had big tops  
The young man is left with no shelter  
The working man has smart children

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Töölaulud
translation:
I plowed very early  
Quickly harnessed the oxen  
I put the oxen to the yoke  
Attached the shafts to the cart  
Put collars on the horses  
Hung bells on the oxen  
I myself wanted to plow  
I turned the cart into the field  
The old red ox groaned  
The cart plowed with a jerk on the back  
The cart stepped into the field with a thud  
The strong ox pulled, the horse trembled  
I plowed the fields very well  
The lady of the estate’s fields  
I didn’t damage the grass roots  
I trod on the working man’s heart too  
The young man was left without his father  
The thistles had big tops  
The young man is left with no shelter  
The working man has smart children

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Joan made a sled out of fir wood  
A golden skull into the pit,  
A fir branch down to the yard,  
Every noon he struck the pole,  
Every time he put the handle,  
Every month he dug the shaft,  
And the sled was finally ready.  
What is needed for this sled?  
Fir is needed, willow is needed,  
Rowan is needed, birch is needed,  
Inside, a silky little maiden,  
From Kalev’s precious one.  
Then he drives the sled to the father-in-law’s yard,  
Under the mother-in-law’s window:  
“Little mother-in-law, dear mother-in-law,  
Come outside and take a look,  
Is the daughter-in-law to my liking,  
The daughter-in-law with an apron?  
If she is not to my liking,  
The daughter-in-law with an apron,  
Send her back with the sled.  
Now my horses will lead,  
My ears will walk,  
My steeds will harness,  
Called for the help of the home man,  
To the father- and mother-in-law’s aid,  
The head of the family daughter,  
The adviser of the young maiden.  
Father will not dare to buy her,  
Brother will not from the sled’s shaft,  
I will take my horse,  
My saddle from Saaremaa,  
A tunic of linden,  
A whip of rowan.  
I will drive the irons into the ground,  
Then the maiden will come forward,  
A blue-eyed little child.”

label: Töölaulud
translation:
For I am dear in the old woman's eyes,  
In the old woman's eyes, in the old man's eyes,  
That I am a beautiful hay cutter,  
Hay cutter, praiser of work:  
I make stacks like German boys,  
I make heaps like kings,  
Thick haycocks on the field,  
Neat sheaves in the field.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Who was the famous pig trimmer?  
Joan was the famous pig trimmer.  
Every month he made a spoke,  
Every day he set the rim,  
Every noon he struck his hammer.  
Then his pig was ready,  
Immediately after asking:  
Where to ride the pig?  
In the parish grew a white maiden,  
With cheeks like a red rose;  
There the pig rides,  
Frequently traveled.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Viru girls, neat girls;  
Järva girls, sturdy girls:  
They did not bother to beat butter,  
To knock cream,  
They ate the cream, drank the milk,  
Threw pine branches into the pine forest,  
Children singing behind them.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Thank you to the father of the family!  
Thank you to the mother of the family:  
Who has held field weddings,  
Field weddings, out in the yards:  
Has killed the ox for this:  
Who has not plowed for ten years,  
Has not seen the plow for a hundred years.  
Ten ells of thick linen,  
A hundred fathoms of thick skoops,  
A thousand fathoms of thick turja.  
The cook had not boiled the broth,  
The cook had boiled the praises:  
There were medicinal herbs brought from Sweden,  
Pinning herbs brought from the Land of the Bible,  
Secret herbs from Germany.  
Thank you to the father of the family,  
Thank you to the mother of the family.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Why lull sleep from dreams,
And waste time on sleeping!
Sleep does not bring new months,
Sleeping wastes the shirt!
Sleep encourages wandering,
Sleeping encourages marching
On the path of dozing.
Don’t go onto the sleep rail,
Don’t let the wheel of motion:
Sleep has a low frost,
The wheel of motion slopes.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
But I, the smith apprentice,  
A foster child of the strong boy,  
I melted iron in my mouth,  
Sealed it on my tongue,  
My jaws were bellows,  
My cheekbones were bellows boards,  
My eyes were tempered wells.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The poor rabbit is a meek bird,
Because the hunter tries to catch it with a gun,
And bites it with dogs.
He went to shoot birds,
But the cranes fly high,
The woodcocks through the forest tops.
The lapwing on the crane’s eyebrow,
The woodcock on the broad temple,
The grouse on the other wing,
The crow managed to escape past the trap.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Mother told me to spin flax,
Father told me to comb flax.
I did not want to spin flax,
I wanted to comb flax.
Father made me plow the field,
Mother made me sow the field,
My mind went to stealing,
To breaking the barn door,
To tightening the coffin box,
To opening the chest,
To looking for silver money,
To gathering gold.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
What does worrying cost,
The harm of enduring anxiety?
Sleep does not bring new months,
Sleeping in a shining shirt;
Sleep sends me to howl,
Sleeping to march,
To travel the road of naps.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I go west, fox to the threshing floor  
Little duck goes west into the barn  
Little child goes west onto the cart  
I take a club from the corner  
From the threshing floor’s edge  
From the rack by the threshing floor  
I beat the boards into the barn  
The walls into bundles of rye  
Beams into rattling rods  
I beat the threshing floor with joy  
I strike the carts with song  
I trample the barn with dance.

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
You wanted to go to the farm
To the farm, to the farmboy
To the plowman, to the sower
To the turner of the black soil
Be diligent in the morning
Early before daylight
Take the sheaves from the barn loft
The sacks from the stacks
The pitchers to the edge of the well
Who comes early to the village
Thinks of the moon shining
The moon’s edge flickering
Your ladle handles are hot
Your shiny rods gleamed
Your bucket rods shone
Your pitcher ears gleamed
When the father-in-law comes from church
Be diligent, jump to meet him
Take off his clothes
The plague neckerchief
Take the gloves off his hands
In this way, you earn the father-in-law’s favor
The mother-in-law goes to fetch water from the well
Be diligent, jump to meet her
Take the bucket from the mother-in-law’s hands
Pour water into the kettle
Then you earn the mother-in-law’s favor
Your husband comes from the forest too
Be diligent, jump to meet him
Take the horse from the harness
Take the gloves off his hands
Take off his clothes
Your child comes from the field too
Be diligent, jump to meet them
Oh, you lambs to the barn
Or give the child bread
Thus you earn the child’s favor
The calving child was in the cradle
You either work or go along
Push the cradle to rock
The bow weakly to bend
Thus you earn the calving’s favor
The plowman comes from the field
The bulls frosty ahead
The plow handles in arm's reach
Be diligent, jump to meet him
Take the bulls out of the harness
Oh, you down to the cellar
Out to the yard and meadow
Thus you earn the plowman’s favor!

label: Pulmalaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
You wanted to go to the farm
To the farm, to the farmboy
To the plowman, to the sower

Out to the yard and meadow

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Eat the cattle, blue cattle  
Drink the cattle, red cattle  
Old woman's cattle, Ingamaa  
For a little while, to wander  
For an hour, to take a nap  
Until I tie the shoe laces  
Fastening the shoe laces  
I weave the old woman's apron ribbon  
Under the apron, a little garden  
On the apron, a little ribbon  
I make a pattern on the belt  
I touch the cat's paw pattern  
I weave on the shirt's tail  
I make holes in the weaving  
I put ribbons into the work  
I insert the ends of the frame.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Evening comes, arriving late  
Lies low beside the day  
Evening brings happiness along  
Twilight along with brother  
Darkness along with lad  
With whom shall I stay warm  
Whose wing shall I be, a titmouse  
Whose shoulder shall I be, a little apple.  
Evening brings straw indoors  
Twilight leads to sleep  
Dusk wakes us up  
Dawn separates us  
Some to a manor, some to home.  
I linger towards the manor  
Swaying into the small hill  
Frowning at the smith’s anvil.  
I went home wandering  
Calling in vain  
I heard a maiden crying  
A caller begging.  
I asked the maiden  
Why do you cry, little maiden?  
The maiden heard and answered:  
Why shouldn’t I cry, young daughter  
Always lingering at the manor  
Swaying into the small hill  
Frowning at the smith’s anvil  
Among the spirits’ space.  
I fear deceiving small shirts  
Burying beneath the coats  
Covering the torn fur coats  
Hiding the white crane.  
I heard and answered back  
Pressed against the leg:  
Not me, not me, little maiden  
I don’t deceive small shirts  
Nor bury beneath the coats  
Nor cover torn fur coats  
Nor hide the white crane;  
I am hidden by fine sand  
And buried by black earth  
Covered by the torn church  
Hidden by the white little tower.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
How I, poor, became poorer  
And a bad man became a sled boy  
Because of my lazy wife  
She didn’t bother to card the wool  
She wanted to make twine  
She took the wool to a busy woman  
The busy woman made the wool into cloth  
The wise woman made the twine into fabric.  
I set the bread to knead  
The breads kneaded and spilled flour  
I set the child to feed  
The feeding put something in the child’s eye  
Because I, poor, became poorer  
And a bad man became a sled boy.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Our master gave the work party,  
Our squire gifted the drink,  
These must be days of joy:  
The beer mug is in my hand,  
I wet my dry tongue.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Little Jaan, little sled;  
Make me a little wheeled bed?  
What shall I take and roll?  
In summer I roll to the barn,  
In winter to the oven's warmth.  
Little Jaan, little sled:  
Did not make the wheeled bed:  
Jaan made a sled in the forest.  
A colorful skull in the spruce grove.  
There he carved the runners,  
Planed the ends beautifully.  
With the moon he polished the spokes,  
Put a brace on the hub,  
Every noon he shaved shavings.  
Then the sled was ready.  
What was missing for the sled?  
Was something missing from the shafts?  
Jaan brought the shafts from the wilderness,  
Put them in their place.  
What was missing for the shafts?  
Something gray was missing for the shafts?  
Inside a silk maiden,  
On the spoke a boy from Kalev;  
Who knows how to drive a horse,  
To handle the whip carefully,  
To take care of the shafts;  
So it does not drive into drifts,  
Or roll into water puddles.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Joanikin the cowbell  
Make me a little dill cart  
I am going to Narva to bring a wife.  
The wife died on the way to Narva  
Threw her apron into the bush  
To the thorny devil's nettle.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
End, end, little field,  
Small, that little clearing.  
The field was long and narrow,  
And the meadow wide and patchy.  
The field wants harvesting true,  
The meadow wide is for mowing.  
The field is long and narrow,  
The meadow wide and patchy.  
Today I mow this field,  
Tomorrow I mow another field.  
The day after tomorrow the one behind the house,  
Then our grain will be finished.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
It would rain so that the moss could sleep,  
Not letting it sleep dry,  
Not even breathing in the light.....

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Sisters, little flowers,  
Let's go to the threshing floor  
The threshing floor will not beat me,  
The flail will not strike without argument,  
I thresh joyfully  
And I beat the piles with song.  
Threshing floor men,  
Open wide the broad doors,  
Open the gates of Viru yard.  
Silky ones are coming in,  
The herdsmen of Kalev,  
The herdsmen of Kalev.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh dear, brothers,
The master was mean to me,
The mistress was loud to me.
I had oxen
Fit for their yokes.
I yoked strong oxen,
I harnessed calm mares.
I plowed low and slow,
The devil’s root was not spoiled.
The tall one did not stop my heart,
The willow did not touch the house,
The devil had large roots,
The willow had wise children,
The thorn had a mad wife,
The willow had a better family.
The wolf ate my big oxen,
The wolf ate my strong mares,
The bear ate the combed horses.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Oh dear, brothers,
The master was mean to me,
The mistress was loud to me.
I had oxen
Fit for their yokes.
I yoked strong oxen,
I harnessed calm mares.
I plowed low and slow,
The devil’s root was not spoiled.
The tall one did not stop my heart,
The willow did not touch the house,
The devil had large roots,
The willow had wise children,
The thorn had a mad wife,
The willow had a better family.
The wolf ate my big oxen,
The wolf ate my strong mares,
The bear ate the combed horses.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Oh dear, little brothers,  
Young maidens, young girls,  
Why do you maidens smile,  
Why do you sneer among yourselves?  
There are indeed maidens laughing in the corner,  
That I might  
From the oily  
Blackbird's  
Dewgrass straw crossbow  
Let me come a second time,  
A second time in autumn!  
I have a horse under me like this oven,  
A man was a spear like this fireside,  
A hat like the servant of the moon.  
A wreath like a boy of the fireside.  
The maidens flew into the shade,  
Behind the bush, behind the stump,  
Behind the bush, behind the forest thicket,  
Behind the stump, behind the forest thicket,  
On the four-legged paw.  
"If only that man were mine,  
If only he were my companion,  
Who goes through the villages  
Before him rides the sacred grey horse,  
Golden ears running by his side,  
Behind him runs a dancing dagger,  
Rides across the Finnish bridge,  
Along the gold-covered shore of Kudru.  
Counts the Finnish bridge,  
Counts the man's hat,  
Counts the base logs,  
The cuckoo fell on the roof beam,  
The second on the other roof beam,  
The third on the bonfire beam,  
The fourth in the maiden's embrace."

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Virgin girl, young maiden,  
Do not throw yourself onto the rail of sleep,  
Do not put yourself under the wheel of the chaff,  
sleep will not bring a new moon,  
Sleeping in a nightgown...

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Understand, sisters, understand, brothers,  
What is being done to us there  
Beyond the Viru spruce trees  
Behind the Harju oaks.  
There, they are building a ship for us,  
The ship was prepared,  
The ship was taken away,  
Placed on old rowing houses.  
The old rowers rowed, heads trembled,  
Placed on young rowing houses,  
The young rowed, sails reached strength,  
Rowed the ship down to Narva,  
From under Narva to Vyborg,  
From Vyborg to the Russian coast.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Hello, hello black rooster,  
Bee, the fine bird  
Brought honey to the fire  
Carrying eight legs  
Why do I wipe the gunstock  
Of the bee’s buzzing gun?!  
The neck pouch is stiff  
The legs are tired - exhausted  
What advice do I have as a man?  
I throw the gun into the stove  
Tutsere - towel holder.  
From the buzzing gun - from the window  
From the velvet bag - from the veil  
I started brewing beer  
Plowing mossy soil:  
Then I got food, then I got drink,  
Then I could go from tavern to tavern!  
For five days we drink vodka  
We walk like burghers  
Fair men of Tallinn  
Often buying beer  
For Margal - paid for the road  
For Kilimitu - half a load.  
If you don’t pay - you get beaten!  
The village pocket pays off  
And then - the purse listens to the word  
The wallet - half empty.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Ui, tui, tui, tui lively fellow  
I have a robust spirit:  
A coat made of fish skin,  
A shirt from fish scales,  
Pants made from peasant cloth,  
A hat like a miller’s,  
A cap like a church sexton’s,  
That neither moves in the town  
Nor yields before a nobleman,  
Nor stands in a gathering hall.  
Let a man who strikes me be,  
Let another come and punch me,  
Making a rude face.  
I will knock the fellow down,  
Knock the thigh guard over,  
Throw the old gentleman to the ground—  
Then I’ll leave the enemy dumbfounded,  
The man with long trousers tangled up,  
Sometimes passing the time,  
When I get home  
To my own farmstead,  
Then I’ll start making a room  
From logs and beams  
And the boards of a reed pipe.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The evening comes late,
The low sun sets.
The evening comes unexpectedly.
The twilight comes without notice.
The evening comes to the yard,
The twilight comes to the crops.
The evening brings straw to the barn,
The twilight puts to sleep,
Twilight awakens faintly.
It turns up the dawn,
The white spreads apart,
One here, the other there,
Upon some manor field,
Work is sought by the servant,
By the wage earner in the farmstead.
When there is nothing else to haul,
To make a fence in the field,
Stones break the shoes,
Limestone quarries I tied with straps,
The warm mud ate the skirt.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
A blacksmith is hammered on the anvil,  
A rod holder is argued over,  
The blacksmith must burn the good anvil,  
Nor loudly the rod holder,  
I beat the patch on the roof,  
Diagonally onto the floor,  
Along the edges by the walls.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Eat, herd, - the herd with the seal,  
Drink, herd, - the herd with the redness,  
Let the herd rest,  
For a short while to rest,  
For an hour to doze off,  
To become a bit drowsy;  
Until I tie my shoe lace,  
Fix the shoe lace,  
Weave the old woman's apron lace,  
Braide the patch lace.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Töölaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Let's start, men, let's go  
Let's light the pipes  
All the village ovens are burning  
The sauna ovens are smoking  
When I start to go  
Then sorrow begins to come  
Tears roll from my eyes  
I feel sorrow for Anne  
Sorrow for Anne's children  
How am I supposed to go home?  
Answer by staring at the wall  
Home tune - everyone is angry  
Home six - everyone was mad  
Home seven - everyone was confused  
Home eight - everyone was jealous  
Home nine - everyone was superior  
Home ten - everyone was cold  
These come upon me  
The lower are whip branches  
The upper are linden barks  
Growing and raining on my eyes.  
May the sky year come a hundred times  
May the rain year pour  
I still have to go  
I do not care about the proud  
I ignore the tall ones  
I do not boast proudly  
I tear apart the rich  
Called me a manure bottom  
Slandered me, the one who sleeps in hay  
Keeper of tavern benches  
Wanderer of tavern doors  
Mover of the tavern's tongue  
I was not a howler at the doors  
Nor the mover of the tongue  
Nor the keeper of tavern benches  
Nor the one who sleeps in hay.

label: Laulud meelelahutamiseks  ; Töölaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
Let's start, men, let's go  
Let's light the pipes  
All the village ovens are burning  
The sauna ovens are smoking  
When I start to go  
Then sorrow begins to come  
Tears roll from my eyes  
I feel sorrow for Anne  
Sorrow for Anne's children  
How am I supposed to go home?  
Answer by staring at the wall  
Home tune - everyone is angry  
Home six - everyone was mad  
Home seven - everyone was confused  
Home eight - everyone was jealous  
Home nine - everyone was superior  
Home ten - everyone was cold  
These come upon me  
The lower are whip branches  
The upper are linden barks  
Growing and raining on my eyes.  
May the sky year come a hundred times  
May the rain year pour  
I still have to go  
I do not care about the proud  
I ignore the tall ones  
I do not boast proudly  
I tear apart the rich  
Called me a manure bottom  
Slandered me, the one who sleeps in hay  
Keeper of tavern benches  
Wanderer of tavern doors  
Mover of the tavern's tongue  
I was not a howler at the doors  
Nor the mover of the tongue  
Nor the keeper of tavern benches  
Nor the one who sleeps in hay.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Eat, eat, little herd  
Eat the herd, the bluish herd,  
Drink the herd, the reddish herd,  
Lay the herd to rest,  
Let them rest for a while,  
Take a nap for an hour,  
Until I set the leg straps,  
Weave the old woman's apron straps,  
But create the twisted belt,  
Touch the cat's paw string,  
Set the perch tail string,  
Catch the sail-edge string.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Shine, shine little sun!  
Shine your light on us,  
Do not shine on the shore!  
The shore men eat fish,  
The shore children eat roach -  
We poor ones eat water bread,  
The older ones eat salt bread,  
The smaller ones eat milk bread,  
The stingier ones eat kvass bread.  
Keep shining, little sun,  
Keep shining on us,  
Do not shine on the shore!

label: Looduslaulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Shine, shine little sun!  
Shine your light on us,  
Do not shine on the shore!  
The shore men eat fish,  
The shore children eat roach -  
We poor ones eat water bread,  
The older ones eat salt bread,  
The smaller ones eat milk bread,  
The stingier ones eat kvass bread.  
Keep shining, little sun,  
Keep shining on us,  
Do not shine on the shore!

label: Laulud abielust  ; Töölaulud
translation:
How I must go home!  
When home is angry,  
Words begin to scatter.  
To whom else but to me,  
To the daughter’s troubles,  
To the child without a father,  
To the child without a mother.  
I coil the words into a ball,  
Put the bad ones into a braid.  
Let the water come to our land,  
Water under our yard,  
Then I throw the ball away,  
Let the braid loosen.  
Water tells the brother,  
The wind tells the sisters,  
The braid taps on the father,  
The braid taps on the mother.  
Then the wicked old woman heard,  
Then the wicked old man heard,  
The sick sister’s troubles,  
The brother’s anger was loosed.  
Then came kin with a great sword,  
Then came people with an iron sword,  
My brothers bled for the sword—  
They came to hear gold,  
To see the inheritance,  
Whether the gold’s eyes are dry,  
Whether the duck’s palate is hardened.  
Before, before, old woman!  
Before the roots of the spruce dry,  
The roots of the oak harden,  
The roots of the pine rot,  
Before the gold’s eyes dry,  
The duck’s palate hardens.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Little John, little sled  
Tie a little bed on wheels for me  
So I can roll into the corner  
In winter, to the stove's warmth  
Little John, little sled  
John made a sled in the forest  
In the spruce forest of Kirje-Kärje  
In the pine forest of Mirje-Märje  
He made spokes for every wheel  
He put sideboards on every day  
He nailed the shingles every noon  
He finished making the sled  
Brought the sled to the edge of the yard  
What is missing from this sled?  
Reins full of knots  
A front bar to hold  
A silk maiden inside  
To the heel, Kalev’s boy

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Little Jan, little sled,  
Make me a wheelbed:  
So I can roll into the slope,  
In winter to the oven pies,  
Little Jan, little sled!  

Jan made a sled into the forest,  
Into the frost-covered spruce grove,  
Into the damp pine forest;  
He made a spoke for every spruce,  
Put a bracket on every day,  
Every noon he cut a shingle,  
He finished making the sled,  
Brought the sled to the edge of the fields,  
What does this sled lack?  
Bows of bent wood, bindings of bark,  
A seat woven from willow,  
On the runners, the son of Kalev.

label: Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest  ; Töölaulud
translation:
One of our doers  
We drink in the tavern until dawn  
We lick it wide and white  
The word is if the purse listens to the word  
Our wallet gives strength  
If the purse does not listen to the word  
The wallet did not give strength  
Then we drink at the gentleman’s granary  
At the lady's strong chest  
Then we go to start tearing the granary  
To pull the lady’s chest  
The watchman happened to see  
The watchman ran to the squire  
The squire ran to the gentleman  
The gentleman steps toward the granary  
I step toward the gentleman  
Listen, dear gentleman  
Blue Stockings lady  
We drink in the tavern with the farmer’s son  
The word is if the purse listens to the word  
If the purse did not listen to the word  
The wallet did not give strength  
Then we drank at the gentleman’s granary  
At the lady’s strong chest  
We came to start tearing the granary  
To pull the lady’s chest  
The watchman happened to see  
The watchman ran to the squire  
The squire ran to the gentleman  
The gentleman steps toward the granary  
I step toward the gentleman  
Listen, dear gentleman  
Blue Stockings lady.  
Be quiet, you scoundrel boy  
You do us this wrong  
In summer you plow large fields  
In winter you chop a lot of wood  
The nights are long, you beat the thigh  
The days are short, you chop wood  
I beat the thigh with joy  
Raindrops  
I beat the thigh, I forget that  
I sing a song, I worry about another.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Little Jan 
Make me a small wheeled bed 
Which I can roll to the grain 
In winter to the oven stove 
Jan secretly made a sleigh 
To the berry berry forest 
To the edge-edge claws 
Every noon he struck a plank 
Every day he put on braces 
He finished making the sleigh 
Took the sleigh between the village 
Look at the village elders 
What is wrong with that sleigh 
The village women laughed at the sleigh 
Oh you crazy young men 
Oh you crazy and foolish 
There are five faults of the sleigh 
First are the apple wood shafts 
Second are the maple wood rails 
Third the curved buckthorn wood 
Fourth is the gray shaft bar 
Fifth is the silk maiden inside 
But Jan heard again 
Soon he said in response 
Apple tree oil to the hill 
From there come the apple wood shafts 
Maple tree between village 
From there come the maple wood rails 
Buckthorn tree in the village street 
From there come the curved buckthorn wood 
Gray sucks mother’s grain 
Maiden swings in the hideout 
Bends in the weak bow string 
Under the plank in the ribboned place 
From there comes the silk maiden inside

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Let's go to the meadow for hay  
To Maria's hayfield  
The mower has a beautiful song  
Mari has a pretty bow  
I mow with a scythe while whistling  
I take the hay with a rake and sing  
The scythe mows beautifully  
The rake hums on the ground  
Let's make the bows pretty  
Bundles full of seeds  
Then let's make the stacks slender  
Let's make the piles higher

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Here has plowed the humped back  
With a freighted horse's foot  
A mischievous calf has driven  
The piglet has been impregnated  
Now I wander among thistles  
Through the cliff's dew grass  
I speak among the ferns  
If here had plowed a dear brother  
With a freighted low sister  
The father’s house impregnated  
Then a beautiful crop would have grown  
A good rye would have ripened  
There would be bread to eat, there would be bread to sell  
There would be bread to give to Harjumaa.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
First, wide children young  
Long heights, spirits weak  
Let the sisters go  
Let the two hands go  
Two hands, ten fingers  
So that we search at the ends  
And we push the poles  
And reach the island’s point  
To the long garden of the field  
When the end leads to beer  
From the garden flows milk and bread  
From the island’s point, bread rolls

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I surely cannot keep up with the sheaves!  
I surely bind sheaves of rye  
And I pile up stacks of barley,  
I bundle loads of oats.  
My sheaf appears in Viljandi,  
The barley stack appears in Paide,  
The axles go to Ani village,  
The boards are seen near Narva.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
We girls, sisters,  
The most beautiful in the village,  
We come together as a trio,  
Consult as a quartet.  
We go to the field to bind sheaves,  
Out to the wide, open fields,  
To begin at this end,  
We cut the mighty sheaves,  
Put aside the best sheaves,  
The fullest armfuls,  
The most beautiful handfuls,  
So that the harvest can be gathered,  
Carried on father-in-law’s oxen,  
And displayed on brother’s steed.  
The farmer’s wealth is the harvest’s sheaves,  
The father-in-law’s wealth is the pair of oxen.  
The frost broke, itself,  
Its own good horse:  
To carry the sheaf,  
To set aside the best sheaves,  
To gather a handful.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I thresh the grain, you rake it  
I gather the sheaves, you carry them  
I thresh the grain into chaff  
The granary walls into seed  
The granary beams into husks  
The poles with the awns into husks  
The stalks alone, the stalks in pairs  
The stalks under the dress  
The people mending a patchwork on the bench  
A handful of threshing flails won’t break me  
Nor will it tire the thick husks.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud ühiskondlikest vahekordadest
translation:
The evening comes, dusk arrives  
The low sun lies beside the day  
The evening comes with luck  
For whom do I hold the hen  
For whom do I spread the wings  
For whom do I hold the apple  
The evening brings straw to the barn  
Twilight leads to sleep  
The dusk awakens  
The dawn scatters us  
To some manor, to some home  
I linger towards the manor  
I howl towards the field  
I swing into the slope  
I sway to the smith's house  
I went home howling  
I heard the old woman crying  
Calling out to the old woman  
I asked the old woman  
Why do you cry, old woman?  
The old woman hears and replies  
Why do you cry, daughter young  
I fear burying small shirts  
Covering mantle underclothes  
Covering worn sheepskins  
I heard and answered in return  
The colt responded in reply  
Not me, not me, old woman  
Not me who buries small shirts  
Covers mantle underclothes  
Hides worn sheepskins  
The little sand will hide me  
And the black earth will bury me  
Will cover with worn church  
Will hide white little tower.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Understand us, understand brothers  
Guess, comrades, sisters  
What will be done to us there  
In the shade of the Viru spruces  
Behind the Harju oaks?  
There, the ship was prepared.  
Then the ship was taken.  
The old rowed, heads shaking  
The young were put to row  
The young rowed, the sails gathered strength  
They rowed the ship down to the town  
From Narva to Viipuri  
From Viipuri to Russia  
To Kalev's home.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The fire came to the stove to warm,
The moon came to listen by the spruce,
Who is hammering in the wise smith’s workshop,
In the heavenly blacksmith’s workshop,
In the famous smith’s forge,
Who is working endlessly.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud abielust  ; Murelaulud
translation:
Little virgin, young maiden,  
You go to the home of a sweetheart,  
To the silver courtyard,  
To the copper gate.  
You had victories, bread to win,  
Given some forgiveness.  
I go to the home of evil,  
To the malicious courtyard,  
To the angry gate.  
My home is sad, my home is bad,  
My home has harsh doors,  
Given harsh words.  
I roll the evil into a ball,  
Lay it on a birch bark,  
Tie it in shirt sleeves,  
Press it between the belt.  
When I go to fetch water from the well,  
I throw this ball into the water,  
Onto the birch bark raft.  
The water carries my brother knowledge,  
The water carries my sister-in-law knowledge,  
The feathers carry knowledge to relatives,  
The straw carries knowledge to the sister.  
To whom should I complain? To whom should I grieve?  
To whom should I cause hurt?  
To the stone on the church road,  
To the limestone on the priest's head!  
The stone was silent, does not speak,  
The limestone was thick, does not tell.  
The stone does not understand the maiden’s denial,  
The limestone does not heed the maiden’s plea.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Let's go to work in the swamp  
To the wet meadow  
Let's go to mow hay  
Then we will make slender stacks  
We will pile up the haystacks

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Evening comes, arrives late
Evening brings you home
Twilight takes to sleep
Dusk wakes up
White spreads out
Family scattered here and there
Children scattered far and wide
One ran to the hayfield
Another disappeared to the pasture road
The third to the manor field
The family girl stayed in the crying house
By the stove, in the leaning house
All my children scattered
All my family here and there
One ran to the hayfield
Another disappeared to the pasture road
The third to the manor field
The sack down, the sack up
The third with head down.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Hiss, hiss, little sickle,  
Rattle, crooked iron.  
Whoever’s sickle gets here first  
That groom will answer.  
Let's go singing through the forest,  
Whose voice sounds well here  
That groom will buy a hat.  
Take up the curved sickle,  
Don’t let others go ahead.  
Look, the field rises and moves,  
Each stalk sways and moves.  
This year, God willing,  
Every stalk will carry a man.  
Make the ridges high,  
Just like the rows in the field.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I wanted to go to the work party,  
But deception came to meet me.  
They said the beer had gone sour,  
The beer tap was stuck.  
I went back from the road.  
I went to the field to take a shortcut,  
To cut through the meadow.  
I did not cut across the long field,  
Nor trampled the wide meadow.  
I did not let the barn sway,  
Nor did I disturb the butterflies.  
I made a heap like that of a woman,  
A pile like that of an unmarried maiden,  
A stack like that of a mistress.  
Below I put alder branches,  
Above I put linden bark,  
In the middle cherry tree branches,  
Between maple leaves.  
Below it did not get moldy,  
Above it did not dry out,  
In the middle it did not crack.  
Master, little master!  
Mistress, little mistress!  
Let me try, I’ll see,  
Is the lamb on the table?  
Is the young lamb in place?  
Is the round lump in the center?  
Are the spoons counted?  
Are the linens neatly folded?  
Are the bowls turned upside down?  
Let me try, I’ll see,  
Are your tunes flowing?  
Are the island narcissi blooming?  
Let me try, I’ll see,  
Is your task progressing?  
Are your tunes elegant?  
Let me try, I’ll see,  
Is there a party with the instruments?  
Are there many instruments with you?  
Can you dance to the music?  
To hold a music party,  
To cool many feet.  
There the instrument leaps in dance rhythm,  
Calling Anu, Mare, Liisa,  
Lift your legs, white socks,  
The party dust will not harm them.

label: Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
The herd disappeared into the grove  
The cows into the town's poplar grove.  
I went home with a sad mind.  
Little sisters, little birds  
Where do we drive the herd  
Where shall we fold this letter?  
Drive the herd to the pasture  
Fold the letter on the meadow  
At the edge of our hayfield  
By the village meadow’s side  
Between the parish meadows  
On the lord’s unplowed field.  
Let’s go along this road  
These cut paths  
Let’s go to the beautiful hayfield  
To the dear cherished grove,  
Little sisters, little birds  
Drive the herd that way  
Here the herd grazes unprotected  
Apparently the forest uncut  
Eat, herd, eat  
Eat, red herd.

label: Töölaulud  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:
Help brought onto the stove,  
Will my gray not start on it?  
Wood and timber brought from the forest,  
Boards and planks from the clearing.  
On the black one I otherwise placed firewood,  
On the ears I placed all the firewood.  
Would my black one otherwise have run away,  
The gray one have shouted onto the beam?  
Before those maidens,  
The daughters of Tinatu,  
Who were half-done,  
Stopped by the wall,  
Walked by the tavern.

label: Töölaulud  ; Pulmalaulud
translation:

Wood and timber brought from the forest,

Before those maidens,
