Who were half-done,  
Stopped by the wall,  
Walked by the tavern.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I would live in my own knowledge,  
I would work for others to know,  
I would roll with the knowledge of five fingers,  
To settle the village alp.  
Oh, you jealous villagers,  
Your own great witches,  
Bring salt from Finland,  
Salt across the sea  
To our pasture fence;  
The salt began to melt,  
The salt began to sink down,  
Then the herd began to disappear,  
The pigs blue and dying.  
The cows sluggishly sleeping,  
The horses gray and howling,  
The mares quickly exhaling their breath,  
The stallions completely moaning.  
Oh, you jealous villagers,  
Your own great witches!

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Let us be kin,
Let us gather, flock birds,
Away to the herd by the well,
Delicate by the water stream,
Heifers to the seaweed,
Gray bulls by the spring.
What does the herd do by the well,
Delicate by the water stream,
Heifers to the seaweed,
If there is no good feeder
Nor good water-giver,
Nor wise payer.
Who feeds the large herd,
Who feeds, who waters,
Who pays the wise?
Summer feeds, summer waters,
Winter pays the wise;
It must go to the enclosure,
The ridges in the barn,
To the colorful oat field,
To the grass eyes above the fodder.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
/ : A little pebble, a little piece, :/  
/ : A little thick limestone :/  
/ : You could have played in the sea :/  
/ : Moved in the sea sand :/  
/ : Shouted on the sea shores. :/  
/ : Before you come to our land :/  
/ : To torment our chests :/  
/ : To trouble our arms :/  
/ : (Oh!) if our chests were rowanberry red! :/  
/ : Our arms maple-colored. :/  
/ : The finger joints marshy. :/  
/ : Then you could come to our land. :/

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The landlord was angry with me  
The landlady was loud with me;  
In no time, they made me stack straw  
And also cut firewood,  
Digging up spruce roots  
And beating underbrush.  
I went knee-deep into the mud  
And waist-deep into the water;  
Then came a sharp frost  
That took both my knees.  
But I turned to the blacksmith's prayer:  
Dear blacksmith, gentle blacksmith,  
Screw a nut onto my knee  
And also onto my waist,  
I will make you a dill lock,  
I will cast you a brass lock.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I am the son of a seaman man  
Dear man, son of a fisherman  
I push another man into the sea  
I drive after the tar vessels  
But I go to the island on the sea  
I make a small sauna there  
A poor child's shelter  
Where passenger ships pass by  
Every Friday ships from Riga  
Every month royal ships  
Every day ships from Pärnu  
Already those Germans are guessing  
The lords are wondering  
The ladies are whispering  
Whose building could this be?  
I certainly hear and answer:  
This is my little sauna  
A poor child's shelter  
The floor is made of moose knee bone  
The walls are made of moose shin bone  
The ceiling is made of egret feathers  
The roof is made of eggshell  
The eaves are made of pork fat.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I wanted to go to the work party  
To join the barley wedding  
To run to the rye watering place  
False guides came to meet me  
They said: the spoons are washed badly  
The steps are poorly cultivated  
But I went anyway,  
I was not one to believe in nonsense  
I did not take weak advice  
I went anyway  
I found the spoons well washed  
The steps carefully cultivated  
The tables thoroughly rinsed.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I wanted to go to the work party,  
I wanted to go without oil at the work party,  
To spend a few days.  
I would ask my father for a coat,  
My brother for a copper grain barn,  
My mother for wedding clothes,  
My sister for knitted mittens.  
Father did not give the coat,  
Brother did not give the copper grain barn,  
Mother did not give the wedding clothes,  
Sister did not give the knitted mittens.  
I built my own,  
I built a horse out of straw,  
I put metal rings on its neck,  
Silken reins inside,  
With a strap on top of the saddle,  
Like a Russian carriage,  
I sat inside with the master's style  
Then I rode to Courland.  
Young men live in Courland,  
Who are rich in money  
And who have a famous money bag?  
May a maiden come to demand,  
To buy a silver necklace!  
The maiden's hair costs a thousand,  
The maiden's braid costs a hundred,  
The maiden's hat costs fifty.  
Let the poor boys of Courland take it,  
They were not rich in money,  
They did not have famous money bags!  
I myself was the rich brother,  
I myself was rich in money,  
I myself had the famous money bag.  
I went to demand the maiden,  
To buy the silver necklace;  
I gave a thousand for the maiden's hair,  
A hundred for the maiden's braid,  
Fifty for the maiden's hat.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Young village girls,  
Beautiful as apple blossoms,  
Red as lingonberries.  
Straight as bluebell stems.  
They do not go to plowing,  
Nor to threshing,  
Nor to mowing rye hay,  
Nor to fetching mares.  
They go to the plowed fields' village,  
Rolling furrows to the meadows,  
Before the frost-covered oxen,  
With yokes on their shoulders,  
The soil stick behind them,  
The whip in the soil fist,  
The bucket in the soil handle.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I do cut, I do wait,  
There’s no food to be given here,  
Not even a little bit offered.  
I take hold and get angry:  
I throw the sickle here and there,  
A handful of bits and pieces,  
I throw the gnawed husks to the mice,  
The stalks go to the brooms!

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The evening brings happiness along,  
The twilight brings fruit along.  
The evening brings straw to the barn,  
The twilight leads to sleep.  
The gloom wakes one up,  
The morning comes with great care,  
Commands the hands to move quickly,  
To pinch with fingertips,  
To tap with finger tips.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
You surely are, you surely are, little Leena,  
You surely are in the pleasant manor,  
You surely are in the strong yard!  
At home, you are bustling about,  
Working hard, fussing,  
Striving with legwork.  
You handled the linen with ease,  
You washed the clothes thoroughly,  
You cooked the broth while squatting,  
You heated the stove in the corner,  
You searched for the broom in the ashes,  
You swept the floor in the dust.  
You didn't bother to card the wool,  
Nor to pick the knots together.  
You carried the wool to the vigorous hands,  
The knots to the hands of a wise woman.  
The energetic one made the wool into cloth,  
The wise one made the knots into fabric.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud meelelahutamiseks
translation:
I am a man, son of a sailor,  
Dear man, son of a fisherman!  
I pushed the other man to the sea,  
I drove the tar barrels after him:  
Then I went to the big sea  
And made a little sauna there  
A poor child’s shelter  
The walls made of reindeer dung  
The roof made of eggshells  
Lined with pork  
Where the patrol ships come  
Every Friday the Riga ships  
Every month the king’s ships  
Every day even the Pärnu ships.  
Then the Germans wondered,  
The lords marveled,  
The ladies wondered aloud,  
Whose building could this be!  
I hear and answer:  
This is my little sauna  
A poor child’s shelter.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
I wanted to go to the work party,  
I sneaked into the barley bread celebration,  
To dash to the oat stalk,  
To run to the rye bread celebration.  
A false messenger came to meet me:  
That the ladles are badly washed,  
The boots poorly cleaned,  
The tables lazily wiped.  
I went anyway:  
I found the ladles washed like gold,  
The boots thoroughly cleaned,  
The tables diligently wiped.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
If I had my own horse
Like father has a horse
I would ride to Tõnismägi,
From there I would bring a girl with side curls.
A silver-breasted little flower
A slender speckled one
A tiny upright one
With a sleepy night, a two-day,
A week-long visit, a month-long;
She understands how to weave fabric,
How to jingle the loom,
How to pluck silk threads,
How to crack paper.
In the pattern appear upright eyes
On the edge lively peas eyes
In the middle fabric curved eyes.
With the brush she goes shush-shush
And then goes nika-naka,
Clappers clatter rattle-roll.

label: Töölaulud
translation:
When the daughter was taken away from home  
The room remained empty of the daughter  
The barn and the shed full of clothes.  
The room cries into its four corners  
The chamber shouts to its two walls  
Five beauties sit  
Our famous, our dear,  
Our famous stacker  
The maker of slender yields  
In the marsh, she made large stacks  
In the barley, the yields are slender  
Below the hill, more uncertain.

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Where do we go three
Out through five
We go to Looja for a story
To Mary’s hayfield
I take a bow, I take two.
I will take the third one
What did I find under the bow?
I found an eagle under the bow.
What is under the eagle’s wing?
An axe under the eagle’s wing.
What is under the wedge and the tip?
A chip under the wedge and the tip.
What will be made from the chip?
John’s barn is being cut down
But what is inside the barn?
A bed inside the barn.
Straw inside the bed.
What is on top of the straw?
A plank on top of the straw.
What is on the plank?
Pillows on the plank.
What is on the pillows?
Four young maidens.
One carries a golden belt
The second wears an iron belt.
The third wears a cat’s belt
The fourth is crying for a young man.

label: Töölaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
If it would rain, one could sleep  
If it came, one could nap  
Take the rain to Viga  
Drive the rain to Arjumaa  
In Arjumaa, the men were black  
The men were black, the women lazy  
The girls were fiery  
The boys were half-witted  
They played music carefree  
They carried the kannel without noise  
There was no way to light the stove  
Nor to throw in a handful of oats.  
In the room, the smoke forest was visible  
The fire glowing in the fire forest  
Behind the stove, the land of Aru forest.

label: Looduslaulud  ; Lüroeepilised laulud  ; Töölaulud
translation:
Roll, roll little day  
Roll because of the evening of the day  
Because of the gold of kings  
Because of the money of the rich  
Kings have a lot of gold  
The rich have a lot of money  
But we, the poor, have a lot of hardship  
There is no time to roll during the day  
The day deceives the heads of servants  
Combs the heads of herdsmen  
Burden the heads of tall maidens  
The broom scrubbed into the sea  
Andres, the poor boy  
Go take the broom to the sea  
But Andres did not take the word  
I went myself slowly  
Along the narrow roads  
Along the low land  
Along the water’s edges  
Along the wave’s patches  
The lazy one waits for the evening  
The lazy one for the rolling day  
I wait for the morning  
For the rising of the other day  
For the rising of the third dawn  
Evening comes unexpectedly  
Twilight comes unannounced  
Evening brings the hay into the barn  
Twilight puts to sleep  
Dusk awakens  
Dawn sends us far away  
White spreads apart  
The day sends us scattered.

label: Töölaulud  ; Murelaulud
translation:
The shepherd had a hard life  
Water was sometimes under, sometimes over  
Water between the toes  
The elbow was under water by the side  
The elbow above water by the side  
Where are the dry homes  
Water had churned white  
Rain had mossed the walls  
Fog had blocked the doors

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Cry in the meadows  
Chirp in the hayfields  
Into the dense thicket  
Into the pale aspen grove  
Into the ugly spruce forest  
Into the soft pine forest  
Where you don’t hear the rooster’s crow  
Nor the young child’s crying  
Nor the lazy bird’s singing

label: Töölaulud
translation:
The shepherd had a hard life  
Water up, water down  
Water between the toes  
Big soggy excrements  
Wide messy droppings  
Shepherd boys like chicks  
They eat gold, they reach gold  
Put gold in the purse  
Silver over the shoulders  
Copper between the toes

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Hans, you were a cunning man  
You built me a wooden ship  
A wooden ship, a rickety ship  
I go up on the ship's deck  
To the top of the tall mast  
I go to look around  
To see if there is a building there  
There was no building there  
The sea is black and deep  
The sky is wide and the land is open

label: Töölaulud  ; Laulud noorrahva elust
translation:
Let's go to the Creator for a story  
Out to the yard, to the meadow  
I take one arch, leave the other  
I begin to gather the third  
What do I find under the arch?  
I find an eagle under the arch  
What is under the eagle's wing?  
An axe under the eagle's wing  
What is on the axe's nose?  
A shaving on the axe's nose  
What is carved from that shaving?  
Jaan's barn is carved from it  
What is put in that barn?  
A bed is put in that barn  
What is inside the bed?  
Straw is inside the bed  
What is on the straw?  
A blanket is on the straw  
What is on the blanket?  
A pillow is on the blanket  
What is on the pillow?  
Two young girls as a pair  
One sews a ribbon  
The other ties a silk ribbon  
The third weaves a golden belt  
The fourth makes a braided ornament  
The fifth sets a straw ornament  
The sixth makes a cat's paw ornament

label: Töölaulud  ; Looduslaulud
translation:
To sleep until a hundred rains fall  
I would take a nap with great energy  
The slave can sleep a little  
Sleep a little but lull to sleep  
Still leaning against the walls  
On the axe handle  
On the base log pole  
A hundred rains to Harju County  
Take the rain towards Vigala  
Do not rain on our land  
In Harju County, the men were black  
The men were black – the women black  
The boys were fireflies  
The children were coal tips  
In our land, the men were white  
The men white – the women white  
The boys golden-haired  
The children like cowslips  
The girls beautifully fiery

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Little Jaan, little Kaan  
Make me a little German sled  
A little bed with wheels  
So I can roll into the cold  
In winter onto the oven's warmth  
In spring onto the hay  
He made his sled ready  
A meadow in front of the father-in-law's door  
A meadow in front of the mother-in-law's door  
But what is needed for this sled  
A horse in front not bought  
Reins not tested on the neck  
A blanket not made in front  
A maiden not demanded upon

label: Töölaulud
translation:
Let the gentleman be healthy,  
Let the lady be healthy,  
Who have held a wedding in the field,  
Everyday hay drinks in the meadow.  
The gentleman killed the ox,  
That had never walked the earth,  
Had not seen plowing in a hundred years,  
Had not done any sowing in ten years.  
A hundred spans long were its horns,  
There was room for a squirrel to run on the tips,  
To write during the day.  
The ox slayer could not be found,  
The killer of the great animal,  
Then the fellow came from the sea, from the land,  
From the land of the great ox horn,  
Brought knives from Sweden,  
But saws from Germany,  
Killed the ox,  
That had never walked the earth,  
Had not seen plowing in a hundred years,  
Had not done any sowing in ten years.  
Let the gentleman be healthy,  
Let the lady be healthy,  
The young gentleman with a golden button,  
The young lady with a silver ribbon.  
What do I wish for the gentleman?  
A golden button on his hat,  
A knitter of the golden button,  
A golden sword for the knitter.
